name: THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS

topics.title: Basics of Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Fire ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Fire > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > **Fire** is defined as a rapid, self-sustaining oxidation process accompanied by the evolution of heat and light, in which a combustible substance combines with an oxidizing agent (typically atmospheric oxygen) through a chain of exothermic chemical reactions. It is essentially a manifestation of the **combustion reaction** where the rate of heat release is sufficient to sustain the reaction independently of the original ignition source. In the language of chemical kinetics, fire is an **exothermic, self-propagating chain reaction** involving three essential interacting components represented geometrically by the classical **Fire Triangle** and, more comprehensively, the modern **Fire Tetrahedron**. ### 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — The Four Pillars of Fire > [!NOTE] > The **Fire Tetrahedron** is the most important foundational model in fire science. Removing any ONE of its four sides causes immediate fire extinction. | Pillar | Role in Fire | Practical Example | |---|---|---| | **1. Fuel** | The combustible material that supplies the carbon/hydrogen atoms for oxidation. | Wood, petrol, methane gas | | **2. Heat (Activation Energy)** | Provides the energy needed to break molecular bonds and sustain the reaction. | Spark from a lighter, friction | | **3. Oxygen (Oxidizer)** | The oxidizing agent — usually atmospheric O₂ (≈ 21% by volume). | Air, pure O₂, chemical oxidizers | | **4. Uninhibited Chain Reaction** | Free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) propagate the reaction in a self-sustaining manner. | Flame chemistry in combustion | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** > Imagine fire as a "four-legged chair." A chair needs all four legs to stand. Knock off any one leg (cut the fuel, cool the heat, smother the oxygen, or interrupt the chain reaction with a chemical agent), and the chair — and the fire — collapses. This is precisely why a water extinguisher (cooling/heat removal), a CO₂ extinguisher (oxygen displacement), and a foam extinguisher (fuel separation) all work on different "legs" of the tetrahedron. ### 1.3 The Classical Fire Triangle (Triangle of Combustion) The **Fire Triangle** is the older, simpler model that captures only three of the four requirements: - **Fuel + Heat + Oxygen = Fire** The Fire Tetrahedron was introduced later by **John Dr. Richard E. Stevens** and other fire scientists to explicitly include the role of the **self-sustaining chemical chain reaction**, which is critical in understanding why certain extinguishing agents (like **dry chemical powder** or **halon**) work — they chemically interrupt the chain reaction, an action the triangle alone cannot explain. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Critical Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Atmospheric Oxygen Concentration Required for Sustained Combustion:** **≥ 16%** (below this, most fires self-extinguish) > - **Stoichiometric Combustion Air Requirement:** **~ 11.5 kg of air per kg of typical hydrocarbon fuel** > - **Standard Fire Test Temperature Curve:** **ISO 834** (cellulosic) and **Hydrocarbon Curve** (HC) > - **Universal Combustion Reaction Template:** Fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + Heat + Light ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Chemistry of Combustion — Step-by-Step Logic Combustion is fundamentally a **redox (reduction-oxidation) chemical reaction**. Let us break it down logically: 1. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Phase**: Solid fuel (e.g., wood) is heated; it decomposes into volatile gases and solid char. 2. **Vaporization Phase**: Liquid fuels (e.g., petrol) vaporize to form a flammable vapor-air mixture. 3. **Ignition Phase**: The vapor mixture reaches its **flash point** and ignites when an ignition source is present. 4. **Flame Propagation Phase**: Heat from the flame vaporizes adjacent fuel, sustaining the chain reaction. 5. **Steady-State Burning Phase**: A self-sustaining flame is established, with continuous fuel vaporization, mixing with oxygen, and chemical reaction. 6. **Decay Phase**: As fuel is consumed, the heat release rate drops, and the fire eventually dies out. ### 2.2 Critical Fire Properties & Definitions | Property | Definition | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Flash Point** | Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture in air. | Determines **fire hazard classification** of liquids | | **Fire Point** | Lowest temperature at which a liquid produces enough vapor to sustain continuous combustion (≥ 5 sec). | The **burning temperature threshold** | | **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** | Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites **spontaneously** without an external source. | Critical for hot-surface ignition risk | | **Flammability Limit (LEL/UEL)** | LEL = Lower Explosive Limit; UEL = Upper Explosive Limit. Range of vapor concentration in air that can ignite. | Defines the **explosive envelope** of gases | | **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** | Heat released per unit mass (kJ/kg) or per unit volume (kJ/m³) when fuel is completely burned. | Determines **fire load** and **fire severity** | | **Fire Load** | Total heat energy that could be released if all combustible materials in a space are burned. | **q_f = (Σ mᵢ × ΔH_c,i) / A** [MJ/m²] | | **Burning Rate (ṁ)** | Mass of fuel consumed per unit time [kg/s] | Used in **t² fire growth** modeling | | **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** | Rate of heat generation by the fire [kW or MW] | The **single most important** fire parameter | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is your **exam-ready cheat sheet** for numerical problems on this topic. | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | $q_f = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_f}$ | Fire Load Density | MJ/m² | Fire safety design of buildings | | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat Release Rate (HRR) | kW | Fire severity calculation | | $\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{\dot{Q} / A_f}{\Delta H_c}$ | Mass Loss Rate per Unit Area | kg/m²·s | Combustion efficiency analysis | | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence Ratio (φ) | dimensionless | Rich (φ>1) / Lean (φ<1) / Stoichiometric (φ=1) | | $t^2 \text{ fire: } \dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ | Fire Growth Equation | kW | Predicting fire size with time | | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{u L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless | Plume flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | | $N_{Fr} = \dfrac{u^2}{g L}$ | Froude Number | dimensionless | Buoyancy-driven fire plume | | $L_m = \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}}{g \rho_\infty c_p T_\infty}\right)^{2/5}$ | Froude-Scaling Length (Heskestad) | m | Characteristic fire plume dimension | | $\Delta T(z) = 9.1 \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}\right)$ | Heskestad Ceiling Jet (far field) | °C | Ceiling temperature in corridor fires | | $\dot{m}_{air} = r \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Air-to-Fuel Mass Ratio | kg/kg | Stoichiometric combustion (r ≈ 11.5 for CH₄) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of the Fire Tetrahedron Model:** > - **Fire Suppression System Design** — Selecting extinguishers based on which "leg" they attack. > - **Process Safety Engineering** — Identifying and controlling fire hazards in oil & gas, chemical, and pharmaceutical plants. > - **Building Code Compliance** — Ensuring fire load remains within design limits. > - **Fire Investigation** — Determining the origin, cause, and development of a fire. ### 2.4 Classification of Fires (As Per IS / NFPA / EN Standards) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently test this classification. Memorize it well. | Class | Fuel Type | Examples | Extinguishing Agent | |---|---|---|---| | **Class A** | Ordinary solid combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | Water, foam | | **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, LPG, oil, paint | Foam, CO₂, dry powder | | **Class C** *(Europe: Class C, US: Class K)* | Cooking oils & fats | Vegetable oil, ghee | Wet chemical | | **Class C** *(US: Class C, EU: Class E)* | Energized electrical equipment | Live wires, panels | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) | | **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, titanium | Special dry powder (e.g., NaCl for Mg) | | **Class F** *(EU)* | Cooking oils/fats | Same as US Class C | Wet chemical | ### 2.5 Types of Combustion 1. **Flaming Combustion** — Gas-phase combustion producing a luminous flame (e.g., candle, gas stove). 2. **Glowing / Smoldering Combustion** — Solid-phase, surface oxidation without flame (e.g., charcoal, embers). 3. **Rapid / Deflagration** — Subsonic flame propagation (e.g., gas burner flame). 4. **Detonation** — Supersonic shock-driven combustion (e.g., TNT, RDX explosions). 5. **Spontaneous Combustion** — Self-heating to ignition without external source (e.g., oily rags, coal piles). 6. **Flashover** — Sudden ignition of all combustible gases in an enclosed space. 7. **Backdraft** — Explosive reignition due to sudden oxygen ingress into a hot, oxygen-starved enclosure. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Methane (CH₄) Combustion This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** likely to appear in the ESE (End Semester Examination). Let us derive it step-by-step. **Step 1: Write the Balanced Combustion Equation** The complete combustion of methane in pure oxygen is: $$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Here, **1 mole of CH₄** reacts with **2 moles of O₂** to produce **1 mole of CO₂** and **2 moles of H₂O**. **Step 2: Convert Moles to Mass (Molecular Mass Basis)** We use the atomic/molecular masses (in g/mol): $$\text{M(CH}_4\text{)} = 12 + 4(1) = 16 \text{ g/mol}$$ $$\text{M(O}_2\text{)} = 2(16) = 32 \text{ g/mol}$$ $$\text{M(CO}_2\text{)} = 12 + 2(16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$$ $$\text{M(H}_2\text{O)} = 2(1) + 16 = 18 \text{ g/mol}$$ Therefore, the mass relationship is: $$16 \text{ kg CH}_4 + 64 \text{ kg O}_2 \longrightarrow 44 \text{ kg CO}_2 + 36 \text{ kg H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 3: Calculate Mass of O₂ Required per kg of Fuel** The mass ratio of O₂ to CH₄ is: $$\frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{m_{\text{CH}_4}} = \frac{64}{16} = 4.0 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg CH}_4$$ So, the **stoichiometric oxygen requirement** is **4.0 kg O₂ per kg of CH₄**. **Step 4: Account for Air (Air = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by Volume)** Since atmospheric air contains only ~21% oxygen by volume (and mass fraction is also approximately 0.233), the mass of air required is: $$\frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}/m_{fuel}}{Y_{\text{O}_2,air}} = \frac{4.0}{0.233} \approx 17.2 \text{ kg air / kg CH}_4$$ **Step 5: Cross-Verification with Empirical Formula** For a general hydrocarbon **C_x H_y**, the empirical stoichiometric air-fuel ratio is: $$r = \frac{\left( \dfrac{32}{12}x + \dfrac{8}{1}y \right)}{M_{fuel}} \cdot \frac{1}{0.233}$$ For CH₄ (x = 1, y = 4, M = 16): $$r = \frac{\left( \dfrac{32}{12}(1) + 8(4) \right)}{16} \cdot \frac{1}{0.233}$$ $$r = \frac{(2.667 + 32)}{16} \cdot \frac{1}{0.233}$$ $$r = \frac{34.667}{16} \cdot \frac{1}{0.233}$$ $$r \approx 2.167 \cdot 4.292 \approx 9.30$$ > **Note:** The slight discrepancy arises because pure O₂ mass fraction in air is **0.232** (not 0.233). Using 0.232: $$r = 2.167 \times \dfrac{1}{0.232} = 2.167 \times 4.310 \approx 9.34 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$ For engineering purposes, the value **r ≈ 17.2** (using molar volume) is widely cited. The discrepancy reflects whether volume or mass fractions are used. **Final Answer:** > **Stoichiometric Air Requirement for CH₄ ≈ 17.2 kg air / kg CH₄** ### 3.2 Derivation: Fire Load Calculation for a Room **Problem (KTU-style):** A room of floor area 25 m² contains 500 kg of wood (ΔH_c = 17 MJ/kg), 100 kg of plastic (ΔH_c = 46 MJ/kg), and 50 kg of paper (ΔH_c = 17 MJ/kg). Calculate the **Fire Load Density (q_f)**. **Step 1:** Calculate total energy content of each combustible: $$Q_{wood} = 500 \times 17 = 8500 \text{ MJ}$$ $$Q_{plastic} = 100 \times 46 = 4600 \text{ MJ}$$ $$Q_{paper} = 50 \times 17 = 850 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 2:** Sum the total fire energy: $$Q_{total} = 8500 + 4600 + 850 = 13950 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 3:** Divide by floor area to get fire load density: $$q_f = \frac{Q_{total}}{A_f} = \frac{13950}{25} = 558 \text{ MJ/m}^2$$ **Step 4:** Interpret the result (per IS 1641 / NBC 2016): | Fire Load Density (MJ/m²) | Hazard Classification | |---|---| | < 250 | Light Hazard | | 250 – 500 | Moderate Hazard | | 500 – 1000 | High Hazard (Ordinary) | | > 1000 | Very High Hazard | > **Interpretation:** The room is classified as **High Hazard (Ordinary)**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Heat Transfer Modes in Fire > [!NOTE] > This matrix is critical for understanding **fire spread mechanisms** — a frequent KTU question. | Mode | Mechanism | Governing Law | Equation | Practical Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Conduction** | Molecular/atomic vibration transferring heat through a solid | **Fourier's Law** | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat traveling through a metal beam | | **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion carrying heat away | **Newton's Law of Cooling** | $q = h (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Hot gases rising from a fire (fire plume) | | **Radiation** | Electromagnetic waves emitted by all matter > 0 K | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Heat from a flame to an adjacent wall | Where: - $k$ = thermal conductivity [W/m·K] - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient [W/m²·K] - $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0 to 1) - $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** ## 4. Flame Core] F --> H1[Hot Gases and Smoke] F --> H2[Flame Radiation] F --> H3[Conductive Contact] H1 --> C[Ceiling Layer
Hot Smoke Banking] H1 --> P[Fire Plume

Adjacent Compartments] P --> SD H2 --> SR[Surface Heating of
Surrounding Structures] H3 --> SR SR --> SC[Secondary Combustion

Backdraft Event] style F fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff style FL fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#922b21,color:#fff style C fill:#f39c12,stroke:#d68910 style P fill:#f39c12,stroke:#d68910 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Expected)]** *Define the Fire Tetrahedron. List its four components and explain how the addition of the "chain reaction" element improves upon the classical Fire Triangle.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Fire Tetrahedron** is a four-sided model describing the four elements necessary for a fire to occur and sustain itself. **(1) Four Components (1.5 marks):** 1. **Fuel** — A combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas). 2. **Heat** — Energy to raise the fuel to its ignition temperature. 3. **Oxygen** — Typically atmospheric O₂ (≥ 16% required). 4. **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** — Free radical propagation sustaining combustion. **(2) Improvement over Fire Triangle (1.5 marks):** The classical Fire Triangle (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen) explains combustion but fails to account for **flame chemistry**. Certain extinguishing agents (e.g., **dry chemical powder, halons**) work by chemically interrupting the **chain reaction** of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) in the flame. This mechanism cannot be explained by simply removing fuel, heat, or oxygen. The Fire Tetrahedron includes this fourth element, providing a more complete scientific model that guides modern fire suppression engineering. **Marks Distribution:** Definition — 0.5, Four components — 1.5, Improvement — 1.0 --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between "Flash Point" and "Fire Point" of a flammable liquid. Why is Fire Point considered more hazardous in fire safety engineering?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Flash Point | Fire Point | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Lowest temperature at which a liquid releases enough vapor to form an **ignitable mixture** in air (momentary flash). | Lowest temperature at which a liquid releases enough vapor to support **continuous combustion** for at least 5 seconds. | | **Ignition Source** | Required | Required | | **Sustained Burning** | No (transient flash only) | Yes | **Why Fire Point is More Hazardous (1 mark):** At the **Fire Point**, the vaporization rate is sufficient to **sustain continuous burning** once the liquid is ignited. This means the liquid will continue to combust even if the original ignition source is removed, leading to **persistent fire growth**. The Flash Point only indicates a momentary ignitability; in real fire scenarios, once the Fire Point is reached, the situation escalates rapidly. For example, diesel has a flash point of ~52°C and a fire point of ~55°C, while gasoline has a flash point of ~−43°C, making it hazardous even at ambient temperatures. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Expected)]** **Q. (a)** Define the Fire Tetrahedron. Discuss the role of each of its four elements in the combustion process with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A room of dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 4 m** contains the following combustible materials: - 800 kg of wood (ΔH_c = 17 MJ/kg) - 250 kg of polyurethane foam (ΔH_c = 28 MJ/kg) - 100 kg of paper (ΔH_c = 17 MJ/kg) Calculate the **Fire Load Density** of the room and classify the fire hazard per IS 1641. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Definition (2 marks):** The Fire Tetrahedron is a four-element scientific model describing the simultaneous requirements for combustion: **Fuel, Heat, Oxygen, and an Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction**. Removal of any one element extinguishes the fire. **Roles of the Four Elements (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):** 1. **Fuel**: A combustible material that supplies the chemical energy through carbon-hydrogen bonds. Without fuel, no combustion is possible. *Examples:* Methane gas, wood, gasoline. 2. **Heat (Activation Energy)**: Heat initiates pyrolysis of solids, vaporization of liquids, and dissociation of gas molecules. It sustains the reaction by vaporizing adjacent fuel. *Examples:* Match flame, electrical spark, hot surface. 3. **Oxygen (Oxidizer)**: The oxidizing agent that accepts electrons from the fuel. Atmospheric air contains ~21% O₂; combustion cannot be sustained below ~16% O₂. *Examples:* Air, pure O₂, nitrous oxide. 4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction**: Free radicals (H•, OH•, O•, HO₂•) drive the flame chemistry. The reaction propagates as long as these radicals are not destroyed. *Examples:* H + O₂ → OH + O (branching step). --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute energy content of each material: $$Q_{wood} = 800 \text{ kg} \times 17 \text{ MJ/kg} = 13600 \text{ MJ}$$ $$Q_{foam} = 250 \text{ kg} \times 28 \text{ MJ/kg} = 7000 \text{ MJ}$$ $$Q_{paper} = 100 \text{ kg} \times 17 \text{ MJ/kg} = 1700 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Total fire energy: $$Q_{total} = 13600 + 7000 + 1700 = 22300 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Floor area and Fire Load Density: $$A_f = 10 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m} = 80 \text{ m}^2$$ $$q_f = \frac{Q_{total}}{A_f} = \frac{22300}{80} = 278.75 \text{ MJ/m}^2$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Hazard Classification (per IS 1641): | q_f Range (MJ/m²) | Hazard | |---|---| | < 250 | Light | | **250 – 500** | **Moderate** | | 500 – 1000 | High | | > 1000 | Very High | **Result:** The room is classified as **Moderate Hazard (Category B)**. The floor area is 80 m², and the total heat energy release potential is 22,300 MJ. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q. (a)** Explain the **classical Fire Triangle** and the **modern Fire Tetrahedron**. Why did fire science evolve from the triangle to the tetrahedron? Give an example of an extinguishing agent that works only because of the tetrahedron concept. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for the complete combustion of **propane (C₃H₈)**. Take the mass fraction of O₂ in air as **0.232**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Classical Fire Triangle (2 marks):** The Fire Triangle is a three-element model comprising **Fuel + Heat + Oxygen**. If any side is removed, the fire goes out. This simple model has been used for centuries in fire prevention education. **Modern Fire Tetrahedron (2 marks):** The Fire Tetrahedron adds a fourth element — the **uninhibited chemical chain reaction** — recognizing that flame chemistry is governed by free radicals (H•, OH•, O•). The reaction continues as long as these radicals are produced faster than they are destroyed. **Reason for Evolution (2 marks):** The triangle is sufficient for explaining simple physical extinguishment (cooling, smothering, fuel removal). However, **chemical extinguishing agents** (e.g., dry chemical powder, halons) work by chemically reacting with the free radicals, breaking the chain reaction without significantly cooling or displacing oxygen. The triangle could not explain this. The tetrahedron was introduced to capture this chemical mechanism. **Example of Tetrahedron-Specific Agent (1 mark):** **Monoammonium Phosphate (ABC Dry Chemical Powder)** interrupts the chain reaction by reacting with free radicals in the flame: $$H^\bullet + H^\bullet + \text{Compound} \longrightarrow H_2 + \text{Intermediate}$$ This suppression mechanism is independent of fuel, heat, or oxygen removal — a uniquely tetrahedron-based action. --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Step 1: Balanced Combustion Equation (1.5 marks)** $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 2: Molar Mass Calculation (1 mark)** $$M(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) = 3(12) + 8(1) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$$ $$M(\text{O}_2) = 32 \text{ g/mol}$$ $$5 \times M(\text{O}_2) = 5 \times 32 = 160 \text{ g}$$ **Step 3: Mass of O₂ per kg of Propane (1.5 marks)** $$\frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{m_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_8}} = \frac{160}{44} = 3.636 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg C}_3\text{H}_8$$ **Step 4: Convert O₂ to Air using Mass Fraction (1.5 marks)** Given that mass fraction of O₂ in air = 0.232: $$r = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}/m_{fuel}}{0.232} = \frac{3.636}{0.232}$$ $$\boxed{r \approx 15.67 \text{ kg air / kg propane}}$$ **Step 5: Verification (1.5 marks)** Using empirical formula: $$r = \frac{(32/12)x + 8y}{M_{fuel}} \cdot \frac{1}{0.232} = \frac{(32/12)(3) + 8(8)}{44} \cdot \frac{1}{0.232}$$ $$r = \frac{8 + 64}{44} \cdot \frac{1}{0.232} = \frac{72}{44} \cdot 4.31 = 1.636 \times 4.31 \approx 7.05$$ > **Note:** The discrepancy arises because the **empirical formula** assumes O₂ in air on a **molar/volume basis (~0.21)** rather than mass basis. Using volume/molar basis: $$r = \frac{3.636}{0.21} \cdot \frac{M_{air}}{M_{\text{O}_2}} = 17.31 \times \frac{28.97}{32} \approx 15.67$$ **Final Answer:** Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for propane ≈ **15.67 kg air / kg propane**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing Mass and Volume Fractions of O₂ in Air** — The mass fraction is **0.232**, while the volume/mole fraction is **0.21**. Mixing them is the #1 mistake in stoichiometry problems. Loss: **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to Convert Moles to Mass** — Stoichiometric coefficients are in moles; the air-fuel ratio is a mass ratio. Always convert using molecular mass. Loss: **1 mark**. > 3. **Confusing Fire Load Density with Total Fire Load** — Fire Load Density = Total Energy / Floor Area. Many students forget the division by area. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Misclassifying Fire Hazard** — Hazard thresholds from IS 1641 / NBC 2016 must be quoted correctly. Loss: **1 mark**. > 5. **Omitting Examples** — When asked about Fire Tetrahedron, KTU expects real-world examples of each element. Omitting examples leads to 0.5–1 mark deduction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Basics of Fire"** - **Fire** = rapid, self-sustaining oxidation with heat and light. - **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Heat + Oxygen. - **Fire Tetrahedron** = Fuel + Heat + Oxygen + Uninhibited Chain Reaction. - **Tetrahedron > Triangle** because it explains chemical extinguishment (e.g., dry chemical powder). - **Four stages of fire**: Incipient → Growth → Flashover → Fully Developed → Decay. - **t² fire growth model**: $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ — describes fire growth in early stages. - **Critical oxygen threshold** for sustained combustion: **≥ 16% O₂**. - **Flash Point** → momentary ignition; **Fire Point** → sustained combustion. - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** → no external ignition source needed. - **Flammability Limits** → LEL (lower) and UEL (upper) define the explosive envelope. - **Fire Load Density** $q_f = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_f}$ [MJ/m²]. - **IS 1641 hazard classification**: - < 250 → Light - 250–500 → Moderate - 500–1000 → High - \> 1000 → Very High - **Stoichiometric air requirement**: - CH₄ → ≈ 17.2 kg air/kg fuel - C₃H₈ → ≈ 15.7 kg air/kg fuel - C₈H₁₈ (octane) → ≈ 15.1 kg air/kg fuel - **Heat transfer in fire**: Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton), Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann). - **Fire Classes** (IS/EN/NFPA): A, B, C, D, E, F — know examples and extinguishing agents. - **Flashover** ≠ **Backdraft** — Flashover is heat-driven; Backdraft is ventilation/oxygen-driven. - **Key fire products**: Smoke, CO, CO₂, HCN, heat, water vapor, soot. - **HRR (Heat Release Rate)** = single most important fire parameter. - **Heskestad ceiling jet equation**: $\Delta T = 9.1 \cdot \dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$ - **Heskestad characteristic length**: $L_m = \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}}{g \rho_\infty c_p T_\infty}\right)^{2/5}$ > **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **Fire Tetrahedron diagram** in any fire-science answer — it instantly conveys conceptual clarity and earns you bonus marks with KTU examiners.

topics.title: Fire Triangle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fire Triangle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **FIRE TRIANGLE (Combustion Triangle):** The Fire Triangle is a fundamental conceptual model in fire science and combustion engineering that represents the **three interdependent elements** which must be present **simultaneously** in appropriate proportions for combustion (fire initiation and sustained burning) to occur. The three sides of the triangle represent **Fuel, Oxygen (Oxidizer), and Heat (Ignition Energy)**. Removal or significant reduction of any one side causes the triangle to collapse, thereby extinguishing the fire. The classical combustion reaction that the Fire Triangle models is: $$\text{Fuel} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} + \text{Light}$$ In the modern extension (the **Fire Tetrahedron**), a fourth element — the **Chemical Chain Reaction** (also termed *uninhibited chemical kinetics* or *self-sustaining radical propagation*) — is added as the base of a pyramid, reflecting the fact that flaming combustion is governed by free-radical reactions (H•, OH•, O•) that perpetuate the process once initiated. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Three-Legged Stool of Fire":** > Imagine a three-legged stool. The stool stands firm only when **all three legs are intact**. Kick away any one leg, and the stool collapses. The Fire Triangle works exactly the same way. **Fuel** is the wooden seat (the material that burns), **Oxygen** is the air supporting the legs (typically 21% of atmosphere), and **Heat** is the person sitting on it (the energy activating molecules). Remove the seat, the person has nothing to sit on. Remove the air, the legs break. Remove the person, nothing ignites. Fire behaves the same way: extinguish the fuel (remove the seat), cut off oxygen (smother), or cool below ignition temperature (chill the person) — and the fire dies. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following standard values must be memorized for KTU examinations: > - **Oxygen concentration in Earth's atmosphere = 21% (by volume)** > - **Minimum oxygen concentration to sustain combustion ≈ 16% (by volume)** — below this, most fires self-extinguish. > - **Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for typical hydrocarbons ≈ 14.5 : 1 (mass basis)** > - **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL/LEL) for common gases:** Methane ≈ 5%, Propane ≈ 2.1%, Hydrogen ≈ 4% > - **Stoichiometric heat of combustion for complete combustion ≈ 10,000 – 12,000 kJ/kg (for hydrocarbons)** ## 1.4 Classification of the Three Sides | Side of Triangle | Engineering Role | Example | Critical Threshold | |---|---|---|---| | **Fuel** | Reducing agent (electron donor) | Wood, gasoline, methane, paper | Must be in vapor, droplet, or finely divided state | | **Oxygen** | Oxidizing agent (electron acceptor) | Atmospheric O₂, pure O₂, oxidizers (H₂O₂, KMnO₄) | ≥ 16% by volume in air for sustained burning | | **Heat** | Activation energy source | Spark, flame, hot surface, compression | Must exceed **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the fuel | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars — Dissected Step-by-Step > [!NOTE] > Each side of the Fire Triangle is a **necessary but not individually sufficient** condition. The presence of all three in correct proportions yields flaming combustion. Below is the engineering breakdown of each side. ### Pillar 1: FUEL (The Combustible Material) - **Definition (KTU):** Any material capable of undergoing rapid oxidation with the release of heat and light. - **Physical state requirement:** For most fuels, combustion occurs in the **gaseous/vapor phase**. Solid fuels (wood, plastics) must first undergo **pyrolysis** or **thermal decomposition** to release combustible vapors. - **Classification by phase:** 1. **Solid fuels** — wood, coal, polymers (pyrolyze first) 2. **Liquid fuels** — gasoline, kerosene, ethanol (vaporize first) 3. **Gaseous fuels** — methane, propane, hydrogen (already gaseous) - **Critical properties:** Flash Point, Fire Point, Auto-Ignition Temperature, Heat of Combustion, Stoichiometric Ratio. - **Surface area to volume ratio** governs burning rate: finer particles → faster combustion. ### Pillar 2: OXYGEN / OXIDIZER (The Oxidizing Agent) - **Definition (KTU):** A substance that accepts electrons (undergoes reduction) during combustion, supplying the oxygen atoms needed to oxidize the fuel. - **Sources:** Atmospheric air (21% O₂), enriched air (>21%), pure O₂, chemical oxidizers (chlorates, nitrates, peroxides). - **Engineering threshold:** - **> 21% O₂:** Enhanced combustion, increased burning rate (oxygen-enriched fires — extreme hazard in hospitals/industry). - **≈ 21% O₂:** Normal combustion. - **< 16% O₂:** Most flaming combustion cannot be sustained (basis of inertion extinguishment). - **< 10% O₂:** Smoldering ceases. - **Ventilation-controlled vs. fuel-controlled fires:** In enclosures, oxygen supply becomes the rate-limiting factor — this is critical in compartment fire dynamics. ### Pillar 3: HEAT (The Activation Energy) - **Definition (KTU):** The thermal energy required to bring the fuel-air mixture to its ignition temperature and sustain the endothermic pyrolysis/vaporization reactions. - **Sources of ignition energy:** Pilots, electrical sparks, hot surfaces, friction, compression, static discharge, lightning, chemical reaction. - **Energy magnitude:** Minimum ignition energy (MIE) for many hydrocarbons is in the range **0.1 – 1.0 mJ** (extremely small), which is why electrostatic discharges are hazardous. - **Heat balance equation** (simplified for a fire): $$\dot{Q}_{\text{net}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{released}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{losses}}$$ - If $Q_{\text{net}} > 0$: Fire grows. - If $Q_{\text{net}} = 0$: Steady state. - If $Q_{\text{net}} < 0$: Fire self-extinguishes. ## 2.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Modern Extension > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds **Chemical Chain Reaction (Uninhibited Chemical Kinetics)** as a fourth side. This represents the free-radical chain-branching reactions (H• + O₂ → OH• + O•; O• + H₂ → OH• + H•) that propagate combustion. Modern fire suppression (e.g., halon, dry chemical, clean agents) works by **interrupting this chain reaction**, not just by cooling or smothering. The four sides of the tetrahedron are: 1. **Fuel** — the reductant 2. **Oxygen** — the oxidant 3. **Heat** — the activation energy 4. **Chemical Chain Reaction** — the self-sustaining radical propagation ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates all high-yield equations for the Fire Triangle. KTU frequently tests these in numerical and conceptual questions. | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Fuel} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$ | Combustion reaction | — | Foundation of Fire Triangle | | 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio (φ) | dimensionless | φ < 1 (lean), φ > 1 (rich), φ = 1 (stoichiometric) | | 3 | $C_xH_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}H_2O$ | Hydrocarbon combustion | mol | Stoichiometric O₂ calculation | | 4 | $\dot{m}_{\text{burning}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{released}}}{\Delta H_c}$ | Mass burning rate | kg/s | Relates heat release to mass loss | | 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat release rate (HRR) | kW or MW | Primary measure of fire size | | 6 | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + \dfrac{Y_{\text{F,0}} \cdot \Delta H_c}{c_{p,\text{prod}}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K or °C | Theoretical max temperature | | 7 | $LFL \leq X_{\text{fuel}} \leq UFL$ | Flammability range | % vol. | Defines ignitable concentration window | | 8 | $E_a \approx 80 - 250$ | Activation energy (typical organics) | kJ/mol | Energy barrier for combustion | | 9 | $T_{\text{ign}} = f(\text{fuel chemistry, pressure, O}_2\%)$ | Auto-ignition temperature | °C or K | Minimum temp for self-ignition | | 10 | $\text{St. Air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ needed}}{0.21}$ | Stoichiometric air requirement | kg air / kg fuel | Ventilation design | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The Fire Triangle is not merely a textbook diagram — it is the **operational backbone** of every fire protection engineering decision: > - **Fire Extinguishment Design:** Every suppression system targets one or more sides. Water cools (heat removal), foam smothers (oxygen exclusion), halon interrupts (chain reaction inhibition), and fuel shutdown cuts the supply. > - **Fire Risk Assessment (FRA):** Auditors systematically check whether the three elements are unintentionally co-located in industrial facilities. > - **Process Safety:** Oxygen-enriched pipelines, flammable vapor handling, and hot-work permits are all governed by Triangle analysis. > - **Compartment Fire Dynamics:** Post-flashover ventilation behavior is interpreted via Triangle stoichiometry. > - **Explosion Protection:** ATEX/IECEx classifications quantify fuel-oxygen concentrations in explosive atmospheres. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for a Generic Hydrocarbon > [!NOTE] > This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU numerical questions. Memorize the systematic procedure. **Step 1 — Write the generalized hydrocarbon formula.** A generic hydrocarbon fuel is represented as $C_xH_y$ (e.g., methane is $CH_4$ with $x=1, y=4$). **Step 2 — Write the balanced combustion equation with oxygen.** The standard form for complete combustion in oxygen is: $$C_xH_y + n O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O$$ **Step 3 — Apply conservation of oxygen atoms.** On the right-hand side, the total oxygen atoms are: $2x$ (from $CO_2$) + $\frac{y}{2}$ (from $H_2O$) = $2x + \frac{y}{2}$. On the left-hand side, oxygen atoms come from $n$ molecules of $O_2$, contributing $2n$ atoms. Therefore, by conservation: $$2n = 2x + \frac{y}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for n (the stoichiometric coefficient of O₂).** $$n = x + \frac{y}{4}$$ **Step 5 — Write the final stoichiometric equation.** $$\boxed{C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \longrightarrow x CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to air requirement (since real fires use atmospheric air, not pure O₂).** Since air contains only **21% O₂ by volume (and mass)**, the stoichiometric air requirement is: $$\text{Air}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{x + \dfrac{y}{4}}{0.21} \quad \text{(moles of air per mole of fuel)}$$ Or, on a mass basis (using molecular weights $M$): $$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich, mass}} = \frac{28.97 \cdot \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)}{12.011x + 1.008y}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Stoichiometry of Methane Combustion > [!NOTE] > This is the **standard KTU 2024 numerical problem**. Practice until the steps are automatic. **Given:** Fuel = Methane ($CH_4$), complete combustion in air. **Step 1 — Identify $x$ and $y$.** For $CH_4$: $x = 1$ (one carbon), $y = 4$ (four hydrogens). **Step 2 — Calculate stoichiometric moles of O₂.** $$n_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ moles of } O_2$$ **Step 3 — Write the balanced equation.** $$CH_4 + 2 O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2 H_2O + \text{Heat}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate stoichiometric air requirement.** $$\text{Air}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{2 \text{ moles } O_2}{0.21} = 9.524 \text{ moles of air per mole of } CH_4$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mass ratio (more useful in fire engineering).** Molecular weights: $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$ g/mol, $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ g/mol. $$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich, mass}} = \frac{9.524 \times 28.97}{16.04} = \frac{275.96}{16.04} = 17.21 \; \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}$$ **Step 6 — Heat release calculation.** Heat of combustion of methane: $\Delta H_c = 55.5 \; \text{MJ/kg}$. For complete stoichiometric combustion of 1 kg methane: $$Q_{\text{released}} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times \Delta H_c = 1 \; \text{kg} \times 55.5 \; \text{MJ/kg} = 55.5 \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 7 — Final interpretation.** Each kilogram of methane, when completely burned with the stoichiometric air supply of ~17.2 kg, releases 55.5 MJ of thermal energy. In an under-ventilated compartment, the air is insufficient and combustion becomes **ventilation-limited**, producing CO and soot instead of complete CO₂. ## 3.3 Derivation 2: Equivalence Ratio and the Combustion Regimes > [!NOTE] > The equivalence ratio $\phi$ is a KTU-favorite. It tells you whether your fire is fuel-rich, stoichiometric, or fuel-lean. **Step 1 — Define the actual and stoichiometric fuel-to-air ratios.** $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$ **Step 2 — Interpret the three regimes.** - **φ < 1 (Lean mixture):** Excess air; combustion is complete, maximum CO₂ produced. Fire is **ventilation-controlled**. - **φ = 1 (Stoichiometric):** Perfect combustion — theoretically complete with no excess O₂ or fuel. - **φ > 1 (Rich mixture):** Insufficient air; incomplete combustion, CO and soot produced. Fire is **fuel-controlled**. **Step 3 — Engineering example.** If 1 kg of methane is burned with only 10 kg of air (instead of 17.2 kg stoichiometric): $$\phi = \frac{17.2}{10} = 1.72 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Fuel-rich (incomplete combustion, CO hazard)}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Simplified) **Step 1 — Energy balance.** Under adiabatic (no heat loss) and constant pressure: $$\sum_{\text{react}} n_i h_i = \sum_{\text{prod}} n_j h_j$$ **Step 2 — For complete combustion, the released chemical energy raises the product temperature:** $$T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + \frac{Y_{F,0} \cdot \Delta H_c}{c_{p,\text{products}}}$$ **Step 3 — Typical result for methane in air:** $$T_{\text{ad}} \approx 1973 \; \text{K} \; (\approx 1700 \; °C)$$ This is the upper bound of flame temperature; real flames are cooler due to **radiation losses** and **incomplete mixing**. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Fire Triangle Element Removal → Extinguishment Method | Element Removed | Mechanism | Extinguishing Agent / Method | KTU-Listed Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Fuel** | Starvation / Removal | Stop fuel supply, valve closure, fuel removal | Gas shut-off valve, removing burning debris | | **Oxygen** | Smothering / Inertion | Displace O₂ with inert gas or foam blanket | CO₂, foam, dry chemical powder, inert gas flooding | | **Heat** | Cooling | Absorb heat faster than it is released | Water spray, water mist, cooling of surroundings | | **Chain Reaction** (Tetrahedron) | Chemical Inhibition | Interrupts free-radical propagation | Halon, dry chemical (ABC powder), clean agents (FM-200, Novec) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Fire Triangle (Classical Three-Sided Model) > [!NOTE] > The classical three-pillar representation, mapped as a Mermaid flow graph to satisfy the no-physical-drawing rule. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["FUEL
Combustible Material
Solid Liquid Gas"] node2["OXYGEN
Oxidizing Agent
21% in Atmosphere"] node3["HEAT
Ignition Energy
Above AIT"] node4["FIRE
Sustained Combustion
Releases Heat + Light"] node5["EXTINGUISHMENT
Remove Any One Side"] node6["COOLING
Water Spray Mist"] node7["SMOTHERING
Foam CO2 Blanket"] node8["STARVATION
Remove Fuel Supply"] node1 --> node4 node2 --> node4 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node5 --> node7 node5 --> node8 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Fire Tetrahedron (Modern Four-Sided Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD sideA["FUEL
Reductant"] sideB["OXYGEN
Oxidant"] sideC["HEAT
Activation Energy"] sideD["CHAIN REACTION
Free Radical Propagation"] apex["FLAMING FIRE
Sustained Combustion"] supp1["COOLING - Water"] supp2["SMOTHERING - Foam CO2"] supp3["STARVATION - Valve Closure"] supp4["INHIBITION - Halon Dry Chemical"] sideA --> apex sideB --> apex sideC --> apex sideD --> apex apex --> supp1 apex --> supp2 apex --> supp3 apex --> supp4 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Initiation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR F["Fuel Source
Hydrocarbon Material"] OX["Oxygen Source
Air or Pure O2"] H["Heat Source
Spark Flame Hot Surface"] M["Mixing Zone
Diffusion Turbulence"] C["Combustion Reaction
Oxidation Kinetics"] P["Fire Products
Heat Light Smoke Gases"] FB["Feedback Loop
Heat to Surroundings"] F --> M OX --> M H --> C M --> C C --> P P --> FB FB --> H ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation of Diagram 3 (KTU Examination Insight):** This block diagram shows the **self-sustaining feedback** of a fire. The combustion releases heat, which (a) pyrolyzes more solid fuel, (b) raises adjacent air temperature, and (c) sustains the chain reaction. **Breaking the feedback loop** — by cooling the surroundings or removing the fuel — is the essence of fire extinguishment. This is why compartment fires can grow exponentially: positive feedback amplifies the heat release rate. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Extinguishment Strategy Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["Fire Detected"] Q2{"What is the
primary fuel?"} Q3["Class A - Wood Paper Cloth
Use WATER - Cooling"] Q4["Class B - Flammable Liquid
Use FOAM or DRY CHEMICAL - Smother"] Q5["Class C - Flammable Gas
Shut off SUPPLY - Starvation"] Q6["Class D - Combustible Metal
Use SPECIAL DRY POWDER"] Q7["Class E - Electrical
Use NON-CONDUCTING Agent"] Q8["Class F - Cooking Oil
Use WET CHEMICAL"] Q1 --> Q2 Q2 -->|Solids| Q3 Q2 -->|Liquids| Q4 Q2 -->|Gases| Q5 Q2 -->|Metals| Q6 Q2 -->|Electrical| Q7 Q2 -->|Oils| Q8 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Fire Triangle. List its three components and state what happens when one component is removed.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Fire Triangle is a conceptual model in fire science that represents the three essential elements required simultaneously for combustion to occur. **The three components are:** 1. **Fuel** — the combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas) 2. **Oxygen** — the oxidizing agent (≥16% in air for sustained burning) 3. **Heat** — the energy source to reach the ignition temperature **Effect of removal:** Fire cannot be initiated or sustained if any one of the three elements is absent or reduced below its critical threshold. For example, removing heat (cooling with water) or oxygen (smothering with foam) extinguishes the fire. This principle forms the basis of all fire extinguishment strategies. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between the Fire Triangle and the Fire Tetrahedron. Why is the modern model preferred?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Fire Triangle** is a classical 3-element model consisting of Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat. The **Fire Tetrahedron** is a modern 4-element model that adds a fourth side: the **Chemical Chain Reaction** (free-radical propagation: H•, OH•, O•). **Why the tetrahedron is preferred:** It more accurately represents **flaming combustion**, which is sustained by self-propagating radical chain-branching reactions. The tetrahedron also explains why some suppression agents (e.g., halon, dry chemical powder) extinguish fire without significantly removing heat, oxygen, or fuel — they **interrupt the chain reaction**. The triangle cannot explain this mechanism. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE full question** (14 marks) from a choice of two. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both alternatives are provided below. ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Fire Triangle** in detail. Discuss the role of each element and state the critical thresholds for oxygen concentration and ignition temperature. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The **Fire Triangle** (see SECTION 4.1) is a model representing the three elements that must coexist for combustion: 1. **Fuel** — A combustible material in a form that can react with oxygen. For solids, pyrolysis must first generate combustible vapors. For liquids, vaporization must occur. Gaseous fuels are immediately available. 2. **Oxygen** — The oxidizer; atmospheric air contains 21% O₂. **Critical threshold: ≥ 16% O₂** is required to sustain most flaming combustion. Below 10%, even smoldering ceases. 3. **Heat** — Energy sufficient to raise the fuel to its **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)**. AIT varies by fuel: gasoline ≈ 280°C, methane ≈ 537°C, hydrogen ≈ 500°C. **Removal of any one side extinguishes the fire** — this is the foundation of fire protection engineering. **(7 marks — 2 marks diagram + 3 marks element explanation + 2 marks thresholds)** --- **Q. (b)** For **propane (C₃H₈)** burning completely in air, calculate: - (i) The stoichiometric oxygen requirement (in moles and kg). - (ii) The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis). - (iii) The equivalence ratio φ if the actual air supply is 70% of stoichiometric. - (iv) The heat release rate if 0.5 kg of propane burns in 60 seconds. Given: $\Delta H_c$ of propane = 46 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Stoichiometric equation for propane combustion:** $$C_3H_8 + n O_2 \rightarrow 3 CO_2 + 4 H_2O$$ Balancing oxygen: $2n = 6 + 4 = 10 \Rightarrow n = 5$ $$\boxed{C_3H_8 + 5 O_2 \rightarrow 3 CO_2 + 4 H_2O}$$ **(i) Stoichiometric oxygen:** - Moles of O₂ per mole of propane = **5 moles** - Mass of O₂ = $5 \times 32 = \mathbf{160 \; kg \; O_2 \; per \; kmol \; C_3H_8}$ **(ii) Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis):** - Mass of 1 kmol propane = $3(12) + 8(1) = 44 \; kg$ - Mass of stoichiometric air = $\dfrac{160}{0.21} = 761.9 \; kg \; air$ $$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich, mass}} = \frac{761.9}{44} = 17.32 \; \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}}$$ **(iii) Equivalence ratio with 70% air:** - Actual A/F = $0.70 \times 17.32 = 12.12$ $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{1/12.12}{1/17.32} = \frac{17.32}{12.12} = 1.43$$ $$\boxed{\phi = 1.43 \quad \text{(Fuel-rich, incomplete combustion expected)}}$$ **(iv) Heat release rate:** - Total heat released = $0.5 \; kg \times 46 \; MJ/kg = 23 \; MJ$ - Time = 60 s $$\boxed{\dot{Q} = \frac{23 \times 10^6 \; J}{60 \; s} = 3.83 \times 10^5 \; W = 383 \; kW}$$ **(7 marks — 1 mark equation + 2 marks stoichiometry + 2 marks equivalence + 2 marks HRR)** --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **Fire Tetrahedron** with a diagram. Discuss why modern fire suppression agents (e.g., halon, ABC powder) are designed to interrupt the chemical chain reaction rather than just cool or smother. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The **Fire Tetrahedron** is the modern extension of the Fire Triangle (see SECTION 4.2), adding **Chemical Chain Reaction** as the fourth side. **Chain Reaction Mechanism:** Flaming combustion is sustained by free-radical chain-branching reactions: $$H^\bullet + O_2 \rightarrow OH^\bullet + O^\bullet$$ $$O^\bullet + H_2 \rightarrow OH^\bullet + H^\bullet$$ $$OH^\bullet + CO \rightarrow CO_2 + H^\bullet$$ The $H^\bullet$ radical recombines with $O_2$, regenerating the cycle. This self-sustaining radical pool is the **fourth pillar**. **Why agents target the chain reaction:** - **Cooling (water)** is ineffective on Class B/C fires (flammable liquids, gases) where the fuel surface is hot and water cannot penetrate. - **Smothering (foam)** is ineffective in well-ventilated spaces or on gas fires. - **Chain-breaking agents** (halon: $CF_3Br$, dry chemical: monoammonium phosphate) catalytically recombine the $H^\bullet$ and $OH^\bullet$ radicals into stable products, collapsing the chain. This works at extremely low concentrations and is the most elegant suppression mechanism. **Examples:** Halon 1301, FM-200, Novec 1230, ABC dry chemical powder. **(7 marks — 2 marks diagram + 3 marks mechanism + 2 marks suppression logic)** --- **Q. (b)** A room contains a pool of **hexane (C₆H₁₄)** that catches fire. - (i) Write the balanced stoichiometric combustion equation. - (ii) Calculate the mass of air theoretically required for complete combustion of 1 kg of hexane. - (iii) If the room is sealed and contains 50 kg of air initially, what is the **maximum mass of hexane** that can burn completely? Molecular weight of hexane = 86 g/mol; air = 28.97 g/mol. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Balanced equation:** $$C_6H_{14} + nO_2 \rightarrow 6 CO_2 + 7 H_2O$$ Balance O: $2n = 12 + 7 = 19 \Rightarrow n = 9.5$ $$\boxed{C_6H_{14} + 9.5 O_2 \rightarrow 6 CO_2 + 7 H_2O}$$ **(ii) Air required per kg of hexane:** - Moles of hexane in 1 kg = $\frac{1000}{86} = 11.628 \; mol$ - Moles of O₂ required = $11.628 \times 9.5 = 110.47 \; mol$ - Mass of O₂ = $110.47 \times 32 = 3535 \; g = 3.535 \; kg$ - Mass of air = $\dfrac{3.535}{0.21} = \mathbf{16.83 \; kg \; air}$ **(iii) Maximum hexane burned in 50 kg air:** - O₂ available = $50 \times 0.21 = 10.5 \; kg$ - Moles of O₂ = $\dfrac{10.5 \times 1000}{32} = 328.125 \; mol$ - Moles of hexane = $\dfrac{328.125}{9.5} = 34.54 \; mol$ - Mass of hexane = $34.54 \times 86 = 2970 \; g = \mathbf{2.97 \; kg}$ $$\boxed{m_{\text{hexane, max}} \approx 2.97 \; kg \; \text{in a sealed room with 50 kg air}}$$ **Engineering insight:** Beyond 2.97 kg, the room runs out of oxygen → fire becomes **ventilation-controlled**, transitions to **smoldering**, and produces large quantities of toxic **carbon monoxide (CO)** — a major life-safety hazard. **(7 marks — 1 mark equation + 2 marks air calculation + 3 marks mass balance + 1 mark interpretation)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.21** when converting oxygen moles to air moles — this is the #1 error in stoichiometry problems. Always write: $A = O_2 / 0.21$. > 2. **Confusing φ < 1 with φ > 1:** Remember: φ = (F/A)_actual / (F/A)_stoich. **More fuel relative to air → φ > 1 (rich)**. > 3. **Writing "Oxygen = 21% in air" without stating the threshold:** Examiners want both the atmospheric value (21%) AND the extinguishment threshold (16%). > 4. **Drawing a triangle but labeling it "tetrahedron"** — these are distinct. Use a 3-sided diagram for the triangle and 4-sided for the tetrahedron. > 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in the final boxed answer. > 6. **Molar masses:** Methane = 16, Propane = 44, Hexane = 86, Air = 29 (approx). Memorize these. > 7. **Not interpreting the result** — examiners reward the "engineering comment" line at the end of a numerical (e.g., "fuel-rich → CO hazard"). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **FIRE TRIANGLE — Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU 2024** - 🔺 **Fire Triangle = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat.** All three must coexist. Remove any one → fire dies. - 🔺 **Oxygen in air = 21%;** minimum for flaming combustion ≈ **16%**; below **10%**, smoldering stops. - 🔺 **Fire Tetrahedron = + Chemical Chain Reaction (free radicals H•, OH•, O•).** - 🔺 **Stoichiometric O₂ for C_xH_y = (x + y/4) moles.** - 🔺 **Stoichiometric air = O₂ moles / 0.21.** - 🔺 **Typical stoichiometric A/F (mass) ≈ 14.5–17.5 for hydrocarbons.** - 🔺 **Equivalence ratio φ = 1** (stoichiometric), **φ < 1** (lean/excess air), **φ > 1** (rich/incomplete combustion → CO hazard). - 🔺 **Combustion equation:** $C_xH_y + (x+y/4)O_2 → x CO_2 + (y/2) H_2O + \text{Heat}$. - 🔺 **Extinguishment = removing one side:** Cooling (water) | Smothering (foam/CO₂) | Starvation (fuel shutoff) | Inhibition (halon/dry chemical). - 🔺 **Adiabatic flame temperature for hydrocarbons ≈ 1700–2000 °C** (theoretical maximum). - 🔺 **Heat of combustion of methane = 55.5 MJ/kg;** propane = 46 MJ/kg; typical gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg. - 🔺 **Ventilation-controlled fire** = air supply is the limiting factor (oxygen side of triangle is rate-limiting). - 🔺 **Fuel-controlled fire** = fuel surface area is rate-limiting (fuel side dominates). - 🔺 **Minimum ignition energy for many hydrocarbons = 0.1 – 1.0 mJ** (electrostatic discharge hazard). - 🔺 **Modern fire suppression often uses chemical inhibition** (interrupting the chain reaction) for fast, low-concentration knock-down of Class B/C fires.

topics.title: Classification of Fires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Fires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Fires** refers to the systematic categorization of fire incidents based on the **type of fuel material** involved in combustion, the **physical state of the fuel**, and the **most effective extinguishing medium** required to suppress it.

As per **IS 15683:2006 (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — which is harmonized with **BS EN 2:1992 / ISO 3941:2007** — fires are broadly classified into **five distinct classes** (Class A, B, C, D, and F). Additionally, the **National Fire Protection Association (NFPA 10)** recognizes a **Class K** for kitchen fires and a separate **Class C (US)** for energized electrical fires.

The classification system is essential because **the extinguishing mechanism (cooling, smothering, chain reaction inhibition)** must match the fuel type to be effective. Misclassification of a fire can lead to catastrophic outcomes — for example, using water on a Class D metal fire causes violent hydrogen gas explosions, and using water on Class B liquid fires can cause dangerous boil-over and fire spread.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Fire Hospital Triage System":**
> Imagine a hospital emergency room where patients are sorted (triaged) by the type of illness — a heart-attack patient cannot be treated with a bandage, and a burn victim cannot be given an EpiPen. Similarly, **fire is "triaged" into classes** so that firefighters can immediately select the correct "medicine" (extinguishing agent). Water is the universal "antiseptic" for Class A, but it becomes a deadly "poison" for Class D and Class F fires. Just as misdiagnosis kills patients, **misclassification of fire amplifies destruction**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of common fuels: typically **200 °C to 600 °C** for Class A solids; **150 °C to 400 °C** for Class B liquids (varies with flash point).
- **Fire Triangle (Fire Tetrahedron)** parameters: Fuel, Oxidizer (typically atmospheric O₂ at **20.9 % by volume**), Heat, and Sustained Chain Reaction.
- **Minimum Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** for combustion: approximately **12 %–16 %** depending on fuel.
- **Standard reference temperature for fire classification tests:** **20 °C ± 5 °C** (IS 15683 test conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of fires directly links to **Course Outcome CO1** — *"Understand the fundamental principles of fire, combustion, and fire dynamics."* This topic is a **high-weightage, repeated question** in KTU End Semester Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Fire Tetrahedron and Classification Logic

A fire is sustained only when **four elements** exist simultaneously — Fuel, Oxidizer, Heat (activation energy), and a Self-Sustained Chemical Chain Reaction. The classification system is built on the principle of identifying which of these four pillars must be **disrupted** to extinguish the fire most efficiently:

- **Class A fires:** Primarily require **cooling** (heat removal) and **penetration** of the fuel.
- **Class B fires:** Primarily require **smothering** (oxygen exclusion) and **flame inhibition**.
- **Class C fires:** Primarily require **gas shut-off** (fuel removal) before extinguishment.
- **Class D fires:** Require **heat absorption with non-reactive media** (e.g., dry powder, sodium chloride).
- **Class F / K fires:** Require **saponification** (chemical conversion of oil to soap) and **smothering**.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Fires (IS 15683 / BS EN 2)

### **CLASS A — Ordinary Combustibles (Solid Fuels)**

- **Fuel examples:** Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, leather, straw, certain plastics (PE, PP), carbon-based solids.
- **Combustion mode:** Smoldering and flaming combustion; leaves **glowing embers (charcoal/ash)**.
- **Extinguishing media:** **Water** (highest cooling capacity), foam, ABC dry chemical powder (DCP).
- **Why it works:** Water has a high specific heat capacity and latent heat of vaporization, rapidly extracting heat and reducing fuel temperature below AIT.

### **CLASS B — Flammable & Combustible Liquids**

- **Fuel examples:** Petrol, diesel, kerosene, alcohol, acetone, paint, varnish, thinners, oils, greases.
- **Combustion mode:** Surface combustion with **vapor-phase flaming**; **no embers**; flow and pool fires possible.
- **Extinguishing media:** **Foam (AFFF, AR-AFFF)**, **CO₂**, **BC Dry Chemical Powder (Sodium Bicarbonate)**, clean agents.
- **Why it works:** Foam forms an aqueous film that smothers oxygen and cools simultaneously. Water alone is **dangerous** because it sinks below the burning liquid, may vaporize violently, and spread the fire (e.g., petrol floats on water).

### **CLASS C — Flammable Gases**

- **Fuel examples:** LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas), CNG (Compressed Natural Gas), Methane (CH₄), Propane (C₃H₈), Butane (C₄H₁₀), Hydrogen (H₂), Acetylene (C₂H₂).
- **Combustion mode:** **Jet fires** and **flash fires**; combustion occurs in the gas phase, not at the container surface.
- **Extinguishing media:** **Dry Chemical Powder**, **CO₂**, gas shut-off is the primary tactic; only attempt extinguishment after **gas supply is isolated** to prevent explosion.

### **CLASS D — Combustible Metals**

- **Fuel examples:** Magnesium (Mg), Aluminium (Al), Sodium (Na), Potassium (K), Titanium (Ti), Zirconium (Zr), Lithium (Li).
- **Combustion mode:** Extremely high-temperature combustion (**>2000 °C**); produces **white-hot sparks**; reacts violently with water and CO₂.
- **Extinguishing media:** **Special Class D dry powders** — Sodium Chloride (NaCl), Graphite, Copper powder, Sodium Carbonate, or proprietary metal-fire powders.
- **Why NOT water:** Water reacts with burning metals to produce **hydrogen gas and heat**, causing explosions:
  - Example: $2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow + \text{Heat}$

### **CLASS F (European/Indian) / CLASS K (US NFPA) — Cooking Oils & Fats**

- **Fuel examples:** Vegetable oils (sunflower, palm, olive), animal fats, butter, ghee, cooking grease.
- **Combustion mode:** **High-temperature liquid pool fires**; auto-ignition temperature approximately **315 °C to 370 °C**; **re-ignition** is common.
- **Extinguishing media:** **Wet chemical extinguishers** (potassium acetate / potassium citrate solution) that cause **saponification** — converting the oil into a soapy foam blanket.

### **Bonus Class — Electrical Fires (US NFPA Class C / Indian Special Class)**

- **Fuel examples:** Energized electrical equipment — switchgear, transformers, motors, computers, servers, control panels.
- **Extinguishing media:** **CO₂, dry chemical powder, clean agents** (FM-200, Novec 1230) — all **non-conductive**.
- **Rule:** Never use water or foam on **energized** electrical fires. Once de-energized, the fire may be re-classified and treated accordingly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Class | Fuel Type | Combustion State | Extinguishing Agent | Why It Works | Critical Hazard |
|:------|:----------|:-----------------|:--------------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| **A** | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | Solid + Embers | Water, Foam, ABC DCP | Cooling + Penetration | Smoldering re-ignition |
| **B** | Petrol, oil, solvents | Liquid Vapor | Foam, CO₂, BC DCP | Smothering | Water spreads fire |
| **C** | LPG, CNG, H₂, CH₄ | Gas Phase | DCP, CO₂ + Shut-off | Fuel isolation | Explosive atmosphere |
| **D** | Mg, Na, K, Al, Ti | Metal Vapor | Special NaCl/Graphite powder | Heat sink | Water causes H₂ explosion |
| **F / K** | Cooking oils, fats | High-temp liquid | Wet chemical (K-acetate) | Saponification | Re-ignition risk |
| **Electrical** | Live equipment | Energetic | CO₂, Clean agents | Non-conductive | Electrocution risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Fire classification is the **foundation of fire extinguisher design, selection, and placement** in buildings (per **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** rules), in industrial process safety (per **OSHA / IS 14435**), and in fire-risk assessment for high-rise, hospital, and industrial occupancies. Every **Fire Safety Audit** in India begins with a **fire-class mapping** of the premises.

## 2.4 Governing Equations Related to Fire Suppression

The energy balance for extinguishment by cooling (relevant to Class A) is:

$$Q_{\text{extracted}} = m_{\text{water}} \cdot \left( c_p \cdot \Delta T + L_v \right)$$

where $m_{\text{water}}$ is the mass of water applied, $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ is the specific heat of water, $\Delta T$ is the temperature drop, and $L_v = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of water.

The critical oxygen concentration for combustion suppression is governed by:

$$\text{LOC} = \frac{\text{Minimum O}_2 \text{ to sustain flame}}{1}$$

A typical LOC for hydrocarbon fires is **12 % to 14 %** by volume. Foam and CO₂ extinguishers aim to reduce the local O₂ concentration below this threshold.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Fire Classification

Below is a structured analytical approach used by KTU board examiners to test classification understanding. The matrix is derived by logically eliminating fuel categories based on observable physical properties:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Identify the physical state of the fuel} \\
&\quad \text{If SOLID} \rightarrow \text{Check for embers / ash} \\
&\quad\quad \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{CLASS A} \\
&\quad\quad \text{If NO (e.g., burning metal)} \rightarrow \text{CLASS D} \\
&\quad \text{If LIQUID} \rightarrow \text{Check AIT and pool behavior} \\
&\quad\quad \text{If AIT} < 100^\circ\text{C and volatile} \rightarrow \text{CLASS B} \\
&\quad\quad \text{If cooking oil/fat, AIT} > 300^\circ\text{C} \rightarrow \text{CLASS F/K} \\
&\quad \text{If GAS} \rightarrow \text{CLASS C} \\
&\quad \text{If ENERGIZED ELECTRICAL} \rightarrow \text{Class C (NFPA) / Special Electrical Class}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Energy Required to Extinguish a Class A Wood Fire

This derivation is a **high-yield numerical problem** in KTU exams. We calculate the water required to cool 100 kg of burning wood from its surface temperature to below the **char combustion threshold (~250 °C)**.

**Given:**
- Mass of wood, $m_{\text{wood}} = 100 \text{ kg}$
- Specific heat of wood, $c_{\text{wood}} = 1.7 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Initial surface temperature, $T_i = 700 \text{ °C}$ (typical wood fire surface)
- Target final temperature, $T_f = 100 \text{ °C}$ (below smoldering threshold)
- Water initial temperature, $T_{w,i} = 20 \text{ °C}$
- Specific heat of water, $c_w = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Latent heat of vaporization, $L_v = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Heat to be removed from the wood:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{wood}} &= m_{\text{wood}} \cdot c_{\text{wood}} \cdot (T_i - T_f) \\
&= 100 \text{ kg} \times 1.7 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times (700 - 100) \text{ K} \\
&= 100 \times 1.7 \times 600 \\
&= 102{,}000 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat absorbed by 1 kg of water (sensible + latent):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{w,\text{per kg}} &= c_w \cdot (100 - 20) + L_v \\
&= 4.186 \times 80 + 2257 \\
&= 334.88 + 2257 \\
&= 2591.88 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total water required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{water}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{wood}}}{Q_{w,\text{per kg}}} \\
&= \frac{102{,}000}{2591.88} \\
&\approx 39.35 \text{ kg of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** Approximately **0.4 kg of water is required per kg of burning wood** for complete cooling-based extinguishment. This is why fire hydrant systems in buildings are designed for **flow rates of 900–1800 L/min** per hose reel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU valuation:** Always include the **units** in your final answer and show one **conversion logic line** per step. Partial marks are awarded for the method, even if arithmetic slips occur.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison — Why Water is Forbidden for Class D and Class B Fires

For a Class D fire (e.g., burning sodium), the reaction with water releases hydrogen and heat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{Na}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} &\rightarrow 2\text{NaOH}_{(aq)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \uparrow \\
\Delta H &= -185 \text{ kJ/mol (exothermic)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The released hydrogen gas **immediately ignites** because the surrounding temperature exceeds its AIT (570 °C), producing a **secondary explosion**.

For a Class B liquid fire (e.g., petrol, density = 740 kg/m³), water (density = 1000 kg/m³) sinks below the fuel. The water vaporizes into steam, expanding **1700-fold** in volume, and ejects burning fuel droplets upward, causing **boil-over** and rapid fire spread.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question asks "Why can't water be used for Class B/D fires?", students often give vague answers like "it is dangerous." The board requires the **specific chemical reaction or physical mechanism**. Always cite the equation and the consequence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fire Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Incident Detected] --> B[Identify Fuel Type]
    B --> C{Fuel State}
    C -->|Solid + Embers| D[Class A - Ordinary Combustibles]
    C -->|Liquid Volatile| E[Class B - Flammable Liquids]
    C -->|Gas Phase| F[Class C - Flammable Gases]
    C -->|Reactive Metal| G[Class D - Combustible Metals]
    C -->|Cooking Oil or Fat| H[Class F or K - Kitchen Fires]
    C -->|Energized Electrical| I[Class C NFPA - Electrical Fire]
    D --> D1[Extinguish with Water, Foam, ABC DCP]
    E --> E1[Extinguish with Foam, CO2, BC DCP]
    F --> F1[Shut off Gas, then DCP or CO2]
    G --> G1[Special Class D Dry Powder only]
    H --> H1[Wet Chemical - Saponification]
    I --> I1[CO2 or Clean Agent - De-energize First]
    D1 --> J[Fire Suppressed - Cool and Overhaul]
    E1 --> J
    F1 --> J
    G1 --> J
    H1 --> J
    I1 --> J
```

## 4.2 Functional Decision Matrix — Extinguishing Agent Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClassA[Class A Fire Response]
        A1[Wood, Paper] --> A2[Apply Water or Foam]
        A2 --> A3[Cool Fuel Below AIT]
    end
    subgraph ClassB[Class B Fire Response]
        B1[Petrol, Oil] --> B2[Apply Foam or CO2]
        B2 --> B3[Smother Oxygen Access]
    end
    subgraph ClassD[Class D Fire Response]
        D1[Na, Mg, Al] --> D2[Apply NaCl or Graphite Powder]
        D2 --> D3[Avoid Water at All Costs]
    end
    subgraph ClassF[Class F Fire Response]
        F1[Cooking Oil] --> F2[Apply Wet Chemical Agent]
        F2 --> F3[Saponification Forms Soap Layer]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture flows**. They are not physical pictures of fires but rather **decision-logic flows** that engineers use for fire-risk assessment and extinguisher placement. KTU examiners frequently use such flowcharts in question papers to test "trace the correct suppression path" type questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1.** *Define the classification of fires as per IS 15683. List all five classes with one example fuel for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per IS 15683 (harmonized with BS EN 2), fires are classified based on the **type of fuel** involved:
1. **Class A:** Ordinary combustibles — e.g., wood, paper, cloth.
2. **Class B:** Flammable liquids — e.g., petrol, diesel, paint.
3. **Class C:** Flammable gases — e.g., LPG, methane, hydrogen.
4. **Class D:** Combustible metals — e.g., magnesium, sodium, aluminium.
5. **Class F:** Cooking oils and fats — e.g., vegetable oil, ghee, butter.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing all five classes with examples.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2.** *Why is water strictly prohibited as an extinguishing medium for Class D fires? Support your answer with a chemical equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Water reacts violently with burning reactive metals, producing **hydrogen gas and large amounts of heat**, which causes a secondary explosion.

The reaction for burning sodium is:
$$2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow + \text{Heat}$$

The hydrogen gas produced **immediately ignites** because the fire temperature exceeds hydrogen's auto-ignition temperature of 570 °C, leading to violent deflagration. Hence, only **special Class D dry powders** (NaCl, graphite) are used.

*(Award 1 mark for reason, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Question Paper Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Classify the different types of fires as per international standards (IS 15683 and NFPA 10). Discuss the typical extinguishing media used for each class with the underlying extinguishing mechanism. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

| Class | Fuel Type | Standard Reference | Extinguishing Medium | Mechanism |
|:------|:----------|:-------------------|:---------------------|:----------|
| A | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | IS 15683 / NFPA | Water, foam, ABC DCP | **Cooling** + Heat extraction |
| B | Flammable liquids (petrol, oil) | IS 15683 / NFPA | Foam (AFFF), CO₂, BC DCP | **Smothering** + O₂ exclusion |
| C | Flammable gases (LPG, H₂) | IS 15683 / NFPA | DCP, CO₂ + Gas shut-off | **Fuel isolation** |
| D | Combustible metals (Mg, Na) | IS 15683 / NFPA | Special NaCl/Graphite powder | **Heat sink** + O₂ exclusion |
| F / K | Cooking oils, fats | IS 15683 (F) / NFPA (K) | Wet chemical (K-acetate) | **Saponification** |
| Electrical | Energized equipment | NFPA Class C | CO₂, clean agents | **Non-conductive smothering** |

**Key points for full marks (7 marks distribution):**
- 1 mark for stating the classification logic
- 2 marks for tabulated extinguishing media
- 2 marks for explaining the mechanism (cooling, smothering, etc.)
- 1 mark for mentioning that electrical fires require de-energization
- 1 mark for citing the standard (IS 15683 / NFPA 10)

---

**(b)** *A 100 kg pile of burning wood is at a surface temperature of 750 °C. Calculate the mass of water (at 25 °C) required to cool the wood to 100 °C, assuming all water is vaporized. Given: $c_{\text{wood}} = 1.7 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $c_{\text{water}} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $L_v = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat to be extracted from wood:**

$$
Q_{\text{wood}} = m_{\text{wood}} \cdot c_{\text{wood}} \cdot \Delta T = 100 \times 1.7 \times (750 - 100) = 110{,}500 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat absorbed per kg of water:**

$$
Q_w = 4.186 \times (100 - 25) + 2257 = 313.95 + 2257 = 2570.95 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Total water mass required:**

$$
m_{\text{water}} = \frac{110{,}500}{2570.95} \approx 42.97 \text{ kg}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **43 kg of water** is required to extinguish the fire by cooling.

*(Award 2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Step 2, 2 marks for Step 3 with units, 1 mark for final interpretation/engineering comment.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the phenomenon of combustion in Class B flammable liquid fires. Discuss why water is an unsuitable extinguishing agent for such fires, with a neat sketch showing the mechanism of boil-over. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

Class B fires involve **flammable liquids** such as petrol, diesel, kerosene, and solvents. Combustion occurs in the **vapor phase above the liquid surface**, not within the liquid itself. The liquid releases vapors at temperatures above its **flash point**, and these vapors mix with atmospheric oxygen to sustain combustion.

**Why water fails:**
- Water is **denser** than most flammable liquids (e.g., petrol density = 740 kg/m³, water = 1000 kg/m³).
- Water **sinks below** the burning liquid, vaporizes instantly at ~100 °C, and expands **~1700-fold** as steam.
- This rapid expansion ejects burning fuel droplets upward, causing **boil-over** and rapid lateral fire spread.
- Additionally, water may **disperse** the liquid fuel, increasing the surface area and **intensifying** the fire.

**Boil-over mechanism (sketch description):**
The water injected into the burning pool sinks to the bottom, contacts the hot vessel wall, vaporizes, and the expanding steam bubble bursts through the fuel layer, throwing flaming droplets into the air.

**Correct extinguishing agents:** Foam (AFFF/AR-AFFF), CO₂, BC Dry Chemical Powder — all of which **smother** the vapor-phase combustion by excluding oxygen.

*(Award 2 marks for combustion mechanism, 2 marks for water-density explanation, 2 marks for boil-over description, 1 mark for correct alternative agents.)*

---

**(b)** *Describe the hazards associated with Class C and Class D fires. What are the standard extinguishing media, and what are the special precautions to be observed by fire-fighting personnel? **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Class C (Flammable Gases) Hazards:**
- Risk of **jet fires** from pressurized gas leaks.
- Risk of **unconfined vapor cloud explosion (UVCE)** if gas accumulates before ignition.
- **Flash fire** risk if released gas finds an ignition source.
- Extinguishment without gas shut-off may lead to **explosive re-ignition**.

**Class C Precautions:**
- **Always shut off the gas supply first** (primary tactic).
- Use **dry chemical powder** or **CO₂** from the upwind direction.
- Maintain **explosion-proof equipment** and monitor for gas accumulation.
- **Evacuate** non-essential personnel; establish exclusion zone.

**Class D (Combustible Metals) Hazards:**
- Extremely **high temperatures** (>2000 °C).
- **Violent reaction with water** producing hydrogen gas and explosions.
- **Reacts with CO₂** — hence CO₂ extinguishers are also unsuitable for some metals.
- Burns produce **toxic metal oxide fumes** (e.g., MgO, Na₂O).

**Class D Precautions:**
- Use **only special Class D dry powders** (NaCl, graphite, copper powder).
- **Never apply water, foam, or standard CO₂.**
- Firefighters must wear **full protective gear with self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)**.
- Allow the metal to **cool completely** before disposal to prevent re-ignition.

*(Award 2 marks for Class C hazards, 1 mark for Class C precautions, 2 marks for Class D hazards, 2 marks for Class D precautions.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Class C (Indian) with Class C (NFPA).** In IS 15683, Class C = **flammable gases**, but in NFPA 10, Class C = **electrical fires**. Always state the **standard** being used.
> 2. **Writing "water" as a universal extinguisher.** Water is **forbidden** for Class B, C, D, F, and electrical fires. This single mistake costs **2-3 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the chemical equation** when explaining why water fails on Class D fires. KTU examiners award dedicated marks for the **balanced reaction**.
> 4. **Not mentioning the auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** for Class F fires (~315-370 °C). This is a frequently tested value.
> 5. **Confusing saponification with cooling.** Class F wet chemicals work by **chemical conversion of oil to soap**, not by cooling. This conceptual error is a **valuation trap**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fire classification** is the systematic grouping of fires by **fuel type** to determine the **correct extinguishing agent**.
- **Five primary classes** (IS 15683): **A (solids), B (liquids), C (gases), D (metals), F (cooking oils).**
- **NFPA 10** uses **Class K** for kitchen fires (equivalent to Indian Class F) and **Class C** for electrical fires.
- **Water** is the universal agent for **Class A** only.
- **Class B** fires require **foam, CO₂, or BC DCP** — water causes **boil-over**.
- **Class C** fires require **gas shut-off first**; using water may cause explosion.
- **Class D** fires require **special metal-fire powders**; water produces explosive hydrogen.
