- **Class F/K** fires require **wet chemicals** that cause **saponification**.
- **Electrical fires** (NFPA Class C) require **non-conductive agents** (CO₂, clean agents) and **de-energization**.
- The **Fire Tetrahedron** (Fuel + O₂ + Heat + Chain Reaction) underpins all extinguishment strategies.
- The **extinguishing mechanisms** are: **Cooling (Class A), Smothering (Class B/F), Fuel isolation (Class C), Heat absorption (Class D), and Chain reaction inhibition (chemical powders).**
- **High-yield numerical:** Cooling calculation for Class A wood fire using $Q = mc_p \Delta T + mL_v$.
- **Critical AIT values:** Class F oils ~315-370 °C, Hydrogen ~570 °C, Wood surface during flaming ~600-800 °C.
- **Standard reference:** **IS 15683:2006** (India), **BS EN 2:1992** (UK/EU), **NFPA 10** (USA), **ISO 3941:2007** (International).
- **Key engineering constant:** Latent heat of vaporization of water $L_v = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Combustible Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Combustible Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of fire engineering and combustion science, **combustible materials** are substances capable of undergoing exothermic oxidation reactions with an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen) at rates sufficient to sustain a self-propagating flame front. The **properties of combustible materials** constitute the complete set of thermophysical, chemical, and reactive descriptors that govern how a substance ignites, propagates flame, releases energy, and produces combustion by-products.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT528)** framework, these properties are categorized into:

1. **Thermal Properties** — Heat of combustion, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, ignition temperature.
2. **Flammability Properties** — Flash point, fire point, auto-ignition temperature, flammability limits (LFL & UFL).
3. **Reactive Kinetics Properties** — Activation energy, heat release rate (HRR), mass loss rate, flame spread velocity.
4. **Physical/State Properties** — Vapor density, boiling point, vapor pressure, molecular weight.
5. **Combustion Product Properties** — Smoke yield, soot production, toxicity index (e.g., CO, HCN, NOx yields).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The properties of combustible materials form the **foundational input parameters** for fire growth modeling, smoke movement analysis, and suppression system design. A thorough grasp of these properties is mandatory before studying flame dynamics in Module 2.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — The "Angry Chef" Cooking Analogy:**

Imagine a combustible material as a very **angry, sleepy dragon** locked inside a cave. The dragon represents the chemical energy stored in the molecular bonds.

- **Flash Point** = The temperature at which the dragon starts to *snore fumes* (vapors released). If you strike a match (a small spark), it wakes up and puffs a small flame, but goes back to sleep.
- **Fire Point** = The temperature at which the dragon's snores produce enough fumes to *sustain a steady puff of fire* for at least 5 seconds after the match is removed.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature** = The cave itself gets so hot that the dragon wakes up **on its own**, no spark required.
- **Flammability Limits** = The size of the cave opening. Too small (below LFL) — not enough air mixes. Too big (above UFL) — too much air, fuel can't find oxygen partners.
- **Heat of Combustion** = The total "anger energy" the dragon releases in one full outburst.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (SI Units — KTU Standard):**
> - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **$P_0 = 101.325$ kPa = 1 atm**
> - Standard Reference Temperature: **$T_0 = 298.15$ K = 25°C**
> - Heat of combustion of pure carbon: **$\Delta H_c = -393.5$ kJ/mol**
> - Heat of combustion of methane: **$\Delta H_c = -890.4$ kJ/mol**
> - Stoichiometric oxygen for hydrocarbon combustion: $C_xH_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}H_2O$

---

## 1.3 Why These Properties Matter in Fire Engineering

The selection of fire detection systems, suppression agents, structural fireproofing, and evacuation strategies all depend on these properties. For example:

- A liquid with a **flash point below 37.8°C (100°F)** is classified as a **Class I Flammable Liquid** (per NFPA 30) and demands explosion-proof electrical fittings.
- A gas with a **wide flammability range** (e.g., hydrogen: 4%–75% in air) is far more hazardous than one with a narrow range (e.g., methane: 5%–15%).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Combustible Material Properties

### 2.1.1 Flash Point (FP)
- **Definition:** The lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an *ignitable mixture* with air near its surface, producing a momentary flash when an ignition source is introduced.
- **Test Method:** Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) or Tag Closed Cup (TCC).
- **Significance:** Governs the storage, transport, and handling classification of flammable liquids.

### 2.1.2 Fire Point (FPt)
- **Definition:** The lowest temperature at which the vapor-air mixture sustains combustion for **at least 5 seconds** after the ignition source is removed.
- **Rule of Thumb:** $FPt \approx FP + 5$ to $10$ °C for most hydrocarbons.

### 2.1.3 Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)
- **Definition:** The minimum temperature at which a substance ignites *spontaneously* without an external ignition source.
- **Notable Values:** Gasoline (~280°C), Methane (~537°C), Hydrogen (~500°C), White Phosphorus (~30°C).

### 2.1.4 Flammability Limits (LFL & UFL)
- **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL):** Minimum fuel concentration in air that supports combustion.
- **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL):** Maximum fuel concentration above which combustion is not possible (fuel-rich, oxygen-starved).
- **Range:** $\Delta F = UFL - LFL$. Wider range = more hazardous.

### 2.1.5 Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$)
- **Definition:** Energy released during complete combustion of a unit mass (or mole) of fuel.
- **Types:** Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV).
- $HHV = LHV + h_{fg} \times (m_{H_2O}/m_{fuel})$ where $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg.

### 2.1.6 Heat Release Rate (HRR)
- **Definition:** Rate of thermal energy generation during combustion, measured in **kW** or **MW**.
- **Importance:** The single most important variable in fire growth — drives temperature, smoke, and hazard severity.
- **Peak HRR** is the controlling parameter in compartment fires.

### 2.1.7 Flame Spread Velocity ($V_s$)
- Rate at which the flame front advances across a material surface (m/s).
- **Types:** Opposed-flow (windward) and Concurrent-flow (downwind) spread.

### 2.1.8 Mass Loss Rate ($\dot{m}$)
- Rate at which fuel is consumed (kg/s). Critical for HRR since $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$.

### 2.1.9 Smoke Yield & Toxicity
- **Smoke Yield ($Y_s$):** Mass of smoke produced per mass of fuel burned (kg/kg).
- **Toxic Gases:** CO, HCN, HCl, NOx, SOx — quantified by **LC50** (lethal concentration 50%).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Property | Formula / Relationship | Units (SI) |
|---|----------|----------------------|------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | $A/F_{stoich} = \dfrac{(11.53 C + 34.34 H - 4.29 O + 1.29 N)}{100}$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 2 | Mass Fraction Stoichiometric | $f_{stoich} = \dfrac{1}{1 + A/F_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Heat of Combustion (HHV–LHV) | $HHV - LHV = h_{fg} \cdot \dfrac{9H}{100}$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \chi \cdot \Delta H_c$ | kW |
| 5 | LFL Approximation (Stoichiometry) | $LFL \approx \dfrac{100 \cdot f_{stoich}}{\vert 1 - f_{stoich}\vert \cdot M_{fuel}/M_{air}}$ | vol % |
| 6 | Lower Flammability Limit (Le Chatelier) | $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | vol % |
| 7 | Upper Flammability Limit (Le Chatelier) | $UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$ | vol % |
| 8 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\sum_i n_i c_{p,i}}$ | K |
| 9 | Flame Spread (Quintiere Correlation) | $V_s = \dfrac{\phi}{k \rho c (T_{ig} - T_s)^2}$ | m/s |
| 10 | Smoke Production Rate | $\dot{S} = Y_s \cdot \dot{m}$ | kg/s |
| 11 | Smoke Obscuration (Optical Density) | $OD = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \sigma_s \cdot C_s \cdot L$ | per m |
| 12 | Equivalent Fire Diameter | $D_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{Q}}{\pi \dot{q}''}}$ | m |
| 13 | Time to Flashover (Babrauskas) | $t_{fo} = \dfrac{\sqrt{A_{floor}}}{0.00418 \cdot \dot{Q}^{1/3}}$ | s |
| 14 | Specific Heat (Solid Fuels) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ |
| 15 | Pyrolysis Rate (Arrhenius) | $\dot{m}'' = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | kg/(m²·s) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, all absolute value notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The Le Chatelier equations follow the standard volume-fraction mixing rule used in combustion safety engineering.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Property Used | Purpose |
|------------------|---------------|---------|
| Storage Tank Design | Flash Point, Vapor Pressure | Determine bund wall size, vent sizing |
| Explosion Venting | LFL, UFL, $K_G$ value | Calculate vent area for deflagration |
| Fire Suppression | Heat Release Rate | Select agent flow rate (water, foam, gas) |
| Smoke Control | Smoke Yield, OD/m | HVAC & stairwell pressurization design |
| Structural Fire Resistance | HRR vs. Time curve | Time-temperature fire exposure |
| Egress Design | Toxicity (FED model) | Calculate available safe egress time (ASET) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrocarbon Fuel

**Starting Material:** A generalized hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yO_zN_wS_v$

**Step 1 — Write Balanced Combustion Equation:**

$$C_xH_yO_zN_wS_v + a(O_2 + 3.76N_2) \rightarrow bCO_2 + cH_2O + dN_2 + eSO_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Atomic Conservation (per mole of fuel):**

- Carbon: $b = x$
- Hydrogen: $2c = y \Rightarrow c = y/2$
- Nitrogen: $2d = 3.76a + w \Rightarrow d = (3.76a + w)/2$
- Sulfur: $e = v$
- Oxygen: $a \cdot 2 = 2b + c + 2e - z$

**Step 3 — Solve for $a$ (Oxygen Coefficient):**

$$2a = 2x + \dfrac{y}{2} + 2v - z$$

$$a = x + \dfrac{y}{4} + v - \dfrac{z}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Stoichiometric Oxygen per kg Fuel:**

$$O_{stoich} = \dfrac{32 \cdot a}{M_{fuel}} \;\; \text{[kg O}_2\text{/kg fuel]}$$

where $M_{fuel} = 12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v$ g/mol.

**Step 5 — Include Atmospheric Nitrogen (Air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂):**

$$A_{stoich} = \dfrac{4.76 \cdot O_{stoich}}{1} = \dfrac{4.76 \cdot 32a}{M_{fuel}} \;\; \text{[kg air/kg fuel]}$$

**Step 6 — Final Form (Engineering Form):**

$$\boxed{A/F_{stoich} = \dfrac{11.53 C + 34.34 (H - O/8) + 4.29 S}{100}}$$

where C, H, O, S are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur in the fuel. This is the form used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Le Chatelier's Rule for Flammability Limits of a Mixture

**Premise:** When multiple fuels are mixed, the mixture's flammability limits are computed by weighted harmonic averaging.

**Starting Definition for Lower Flammable Limit:**

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $n$ fuel components be present in air with volume fractions $y_1, y_2, ..., y_n$ (in %), with $\sum y_i = 100\%$.

At the LFL of the mixture, just enough fuel is present to propagate a flame. Each component contributes a partial fuel-air ratio that, when summed, must equal the stoichiometric ratio for flame propagation.

The **stoichiometric fuel volume fraction** for component $i$ is approximately $\phi_{stoich,i} \approx LFL_i$ at lean limits. The condition for the mixture to just ignite is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{\phi_{i,stoich}} = 1$$

Solving for total fuel concentration at the limit:

$$Y_{LFL,mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i/100}{LFL_i/100}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

For UFL, by analogous reasoning (fuel-rich branch):

$$UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$$

**Verification using sample data:**
- Methane LFL = 5%, Propane LFL = 2.1%
- Mixture: 60% CH₄ + 40% C₃H₈ (by vol)

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{60}{5} + \dfrac{40}{2.1}} = \dfrac{100}{12 + 19.05} = 3.22\%$$

This means the mixture will ignite at a much lower concentration than pure methane — a critical safety finding for refineries.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time to Flashover Using Babrauskas Correlation

**Starting Assumption:** Flashover in a compartment occurs when the upper gas layer reaches approximately **600°C**.

**Step 1 — Set up Energy Balance:**

The HRR at flashover, $\dot{Q}_{fo}$, must supply enough heat to raise the upper layer of the room to 600°C.

$$\dot{Q}_{fo} = 610 \sqrt{A_{floor} \cdot A_T}$$

where $A_T$ is the total internal surface area of the enclosure (walls + ceiling + floor).

**Step 2 — Relate HRR to Fire Growth Coefficient:**

For $t^2$ fires: $\dot{Q} = \alpha \cdot t^2$ where $\alpha$ is the fire growth coefficient (slow: 0.00293, medium: 0.0117, fast: 0.0469 kW/s²).

**Step 3 — Solve for Time to Reach Flashover:**

$$t_{fo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\dot{Q}_{fo}}{\alpha}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{610 \sqrt{A_{floor} A_T}}{\alpha}}$$

**Alternative Simplified Form (Used in KTU Problems):**

$$\boxed{t_{fo} \approx \dfrac{0.74 \cdot (A_{floor} A_T)^{1/3}}{\alpha^{1/3}} \;\; \text{[seconds]}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**

A room with floor area = 20 m², total area = 200 m², medium fire growth ($\alpha = 0.0117$ kW/s²).

First, find $\dot{Q}_{fo}$:

$$\dot{Q}_{fo} = 610 \sqrt{20 \times 200} = 610 \sqrt{4000} = 610 \times 63.25 = 38{,}583 \text{ kW}$$

Then:

$$t_{fo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{38583}{0.0117}} = \sqrt{3{,}297{,}692} \approx 1816 \text{ s} \approx 30.3 \text{ min}$$

This is a typical value for a residential room — confirming why flashover is reached within tens of minutes in enclosed fires.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pyrolysis (Mass Loss) Rate Using Arrhenius Kinetics

**Starting Premise:** Solid fuel decomposition follows first-order Arrhenius kinetics.

$$\dot{m}'' = A \cdot \rho_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_s}\right)^n \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT_s)}$$

**Step 1 — For Surface-controlled pyrolysis ($n = 0$, surface regression):**

$$\dot{m}'' = A \cdot \rho_s \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT_s)}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over a time step $\Delta t$ for numerical solution:**

$$m(t+\Delta t) = m(t) - \dot{m}''(t) \cdot A_{surface} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Couple with HRR:**

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \chi \cdot \dot{m}''(t) \cdot A_{surface} \cdot \Delta H_c$$

where $\chi$ is the combustion efficiency (0 < $\chi$ ≤ 1, often 0.6–0.9 for ventilation-controlled fires).

This closed-form coupling is the foundation of **CFD fire models** like FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following with one-line significance in fire safety engineering:**
**(a) Flash Point** **(b) Auto-Ignition Temperature** **(c) Lower Flammable Limit**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Flash Point:** The minimum temperature at which a liquid liberates enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air near its surface, producing a momentary flash on application of an ignition source. *Significance:* Governs the classification of flammable liquids under NFPA 30 / IS 1446.

**(b) Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** The minimum temperature at which a material self-ignites without any external flame or spark. *Significance:* Critical for hot-surface hazard analysis in engine rooms, ovens, and process equipment.

**(c) Lower Flammable Limit (LFL):** The minimum volumetric concentration of a flammable vapor in air below which propagation of a flame is not possible. *Significance:* Forms the basis for ventilation design in chemical plants and battery rooms to prevent explosive atmospheres.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A spill of gasoline occurs in an unventilated garage.**
**(i) Name the single property that most strongly dictates the immediate explosion risk.**
**(ii) What is the typical range of this property for gasoline in air?**
**(iii) State Le Chatelier's rule and indicate why it is useful.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) The single most critical property is the Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) of gasoline vapor in air.** A concentration below the LFL is too lean to propagate flame, while above the LFL (and below UFL) the mixture is in the explosive range. Since the garage is unventilated, vapors accumulate, quickly crossing the LFL threshold.

**(ii) Typical gasoline flammability range in air: LFL ≈ 1.4%, UFL ≈ 7.6%** (a wide range reflecting its multi-component hydrocarbon nature).

**(iii) Le Chatelier's Rule** gives the LFL (or UFL) of a multi-component fuel mixture as the reciprocal volume-fraction-weighted average of the individual limits:

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

**Why useful:** Refineries and natural gas processing plants deal with multi-component gas streams. Le Chatelier's rule lets engineers compute the LFL/UFL of the blend from composition data alone, without expensive experimental testing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, Part B offers **internal choice**. Students must answer ONE of the two questions (A or B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define the **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** of a fuel. Distinguish clearly between **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**. A fuel oil has the following elemental composition by mass: C = 86%, H = 12%, S = 1.5%, O = 0.5%. Calculate the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** using the standard engineering formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **t² fire growth concept**. Identify the four standard fire growth categories (slow, medium, fast, ultra-fast) with their respective $\alpha$ values. For a medium-growth fire in a 5 m × 4 m room with total internal surface area 180 m², estimate the **time to flashover** using the Babrauskas correlation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio:**

**Definitions:**

- **Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$):** The energy liberated when 1 kg (or 1 kmol) of fuel undergoes complete combustion with oxygen at standard reference conditions of 25°C and 1 atm, with all products returning to 25°C.

- **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** Heat released assuming all water vapor in products is condensed to liquid, recovering the latent heat of vaporization.

- **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** Heat released assuming water remains as vapor in products (no latent heat recovered). Therefore: $HHV = LHV + h_{fg} \cdot m_{H_2O}$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given mass composition: C = 86%, H = 12%, S = 1.5%, O = 0.5%

**Apply the engineering stoichiometric air-fuel ratio formula:**

$$A/F_{stoich} = \dfrac{11.53 C + 34.34 (H - O/8) + 4.29 S}{100}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate oxygen correction term:**

$$H - O/8 = 12 - 0.5/8 = 12 - 0.0625 = 11.9375$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into numerator:**

$$11.53 \times 86 = 991.58$$

$$34.34 \times 11.9375 = 409.94$$

$$4.29 \times 1.5 = 6.435$$

**Sum:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 991.58 + 409.94 + 6.435 = 1407.96$$

**Step 3 — Final A/F:**

$$A/F_{stoich} = \dfrac{1407.96}{100} = 14.08 \;\; \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

This value lies within the typical range (13–15) for petroleum fuels, confirming the calculation is consistent.

---

**(b) Part (b) Solution — t² Fire Growth and Flashover:**

**Concept:** The **t² fire growth model** assumes the HRR increases as the square of time elapsed after ignition:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$$

**Fire Growth Categories:**

| Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Example |
|----------|-----------------|---------|
| Slow | 0.00293 | Wood cribs, upholstered furniture (initial) |
| Medium | 0.0117 | Mail bags, stacked cardboard |
| Fast | 0.0469 | PU foam, plastic-lined furniture |
| Ultra-fast | 0.1876 | Flammable liquid pool fires, rapid plastic |

**Numerical — Time to Flashover:**

Given: $A_{floor} = 5 \times 4 = 20$ m², $A_T = 180$ m², $\alpha = 0.0117$ kW/s² (medium)

**Step 1 — HRR at flashover (Babrauskas):**

$$\dot{Q}_{fo} = 610 \sqrt{A_{floor} \cdot A_T} = 610 \sqrt{20 \times 180} = 610 \sqrt{3600} = 610 \times 60 = 36{,}600 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for time:**

$$t_{fo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\dot{Q}_{fo}}{\alpha}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{36{,}600}{0.0117}} = \sqrt{3{,}128{,}205} \approx 1768.7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to minutes:**

$$t_{fo} \approx 29.5 \text{ min} \approx 30 \text{ minutes}$$

This result implies that fire services have approximately **30 minutes** to arrive and control the fire before flashover occurs in this room — a critical design parameter for residential and commercial fire safety codes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain **Le Chatelier's rule** for computing the flammability limits of a multi-component fuel mixture. A gaseous fuel blend consists of 70% Methane, 20% Ethane, and 10% Propane by volume. Given LFL values: CH₄ = 5.0%, C₂H₆ = 3.0%, C₃H₈ = 2.1%, calculate the **LFL of the mixture**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)**. A polyurethane foam sofa is involved in a flaming fire with HRR = 1500 kW. The sofa's exposed burning area is 2 m². Calculate the **Heat Release Rate Per Unit Area (HRRPUA)**. If the sofa continues to burn in a room of floor area 25 m² and total surface area 220 m², using Babrauskas correlation, determine if **flashover is possible** and estimate the **time to flashover**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — Le Chatelier's Rule:**

**Rule Statement:** Le Chatelier's rule (1887) provides a method to compute the flammability limits (LFL or UFL) of a gaseous mixture using the volume-fraction-weighted harmonic mean of the individual component limits:

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$$

where $y_i$ is the volume % of fuel component $i$ in the mixture and $LFL_i$, $UFL_i$ are the pure-component limits.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $y_{CH_4} = 70\%$, $y_{C_2H_6} = 20\%$, $y_{C_3H_8} = 10\%$

**Step 1 — Compute individual ratios:**

$$\dfrac{70}{5.0} = 14.00$$

$$\dfrac{20}{3.0} = 6.67$$

$$\dfrac{10}{2.1} = 4.76$$

**Step 2 — Sum the ratios:**

$$\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} = 14.00 + 6.67 + 4.76 = 25.43$$

**Step 3 — Final LFL of mixture:**

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{25.43} = 3.93\%$$

**Interpretation:** Adding 20% ethane and 10% propane to methane **reduces the LFL from 5.0% to 3.93%** — meaning the blend is more hazardous than pure methane. The mixture will ignite at much lower concentrations in air.

---

**(b) Part (b) Solution — HRR and Flashover Check:**

**HRR Definition:** The Heat Release Rate is the rate at which thermal energy is generated by a fire, measured in kilowatts (kW) or megawatts (MW). It is the single most important parameter in fire hazard assessment.

**Step 1 — Compute HRRPUA:**

$$HRRPUA = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{A_{burning}} = \dfrac{1500 \text{ kW}}{2 \text{ m}^2} = 750 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

This value is consistent with a flaming PU foam (typical range 300–800 kW/m²).

**Step 2 — Compute HRR at Flashover:**

$$\dot{Q}_{fo} = 610 \sqrt{A_{floor} \cdot A_T} = 610 \sqrt{25 \times 220} = 610 \sqrt{5500} = 610 \times 74.16 = 45{,}239 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Actual HRR:**

Since the actual HRR of the sofa fire is **1500 kW**, which is **far less** than $\dot{Q}_{fo} = 45{,}239$ kW:

- **Flashover is NOT possible** from this single sofa alone.
- However, if multiple items burn simultaneously (e.g., sofa + carpet + curtains), the combined HRR can easily exceed 45 MW, triggering flashover.

**Step 4 — Hypothetical Flashover Time (assuming HRR grows to $\dot{Q}_{fo}$):**

If we assume fast-growth fire behavior ($\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s²) — typical of PU foam:

$$t_{fo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{45{,}239}{0.0469}} = \sqrt{964{,}584} \approx 982 \text{ s} \approx 16.4 \text{ min}$$

So even though flashover from this single sofa is not possible, PU foam participation in a developing fire can drive flashover in as little as **16 minutes** under fast-growth kinetics — a critical insight for evacuation timing.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Properties of Combustible Materials" Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (2-mark loss):** Always express HRR in **kW**, A/F in **kg air/kg fuel**, temperature in **°C or K (consistent)**, and time in **seconds (SI)**. Mixing minutes with kW/s² is the #1 valuation trap.
> 2. **HHV vs. LHV Mismatch (1.5-mark loss):** When asked for "heat released in a fire," the answer depends on whether water condenses. Specify your assumption.
> 3. **Le Chatelier Direction (1-mark loss):** For LFL — use the lower component limits; for UFL — use the upper. Inverting the direction is a frequent error.
> 4. **Babrauskas Formula Mistake (3-mark loss):** Students often forget to square-root or confuse $A_{floor}$ with total area. Always use the **floor area for the fire's base** and **total area for heat sink** correctly.
> 5. **Arrhenius vs. Empirical (1-mark loss):** A question asking for "pyrolysis rate" expects Arrhenius form; a question asking for "mass loss rate under steady burning" expects $A \cdot V$ form. Read carefully.
> 6. **No Diagrams Where Asked (2-mark loss):** When the question says "with a neat sketch" or "with the help of a diagram" (e.g., t² fire growth curve, TGA curve, cone calorimeter setup), **draw it** — even a hand-sketched one. A text-only answer loses those marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of Combustible Materials**

- **Flash Point < Fire Point < AIT** (always, for any hydrocarbon).
- **Class IA liquids:** FP < 22.7°C and BP < 37.8°C (e.g., pentane, diethyl ether).
- **Class IB liquids:** FP < 22.7°C and BP ≥ 37.8°C (e.g., gasoline, acetone).
- **Le Chatelier uses harmonic mean, not arithmetic mean.**
- **$t^2$ fire model:** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ — always identify the growth category first.
- **Babrauskas $\dot{Q}_{fo} = 610 \sqrt{A_{floor} \cdot A_T}$ kW** — upper layer temperature = 600°C at flashover.
- **HRR is king** in fire engineering — drives all other fire effects (temperature, smoke, toxicity).
- **$\Delta H_c$ for hydrocarbons** typically ranges 40–50 MJ/kg (HHV).
- **LFL of methane = 5%, UFL = 15%** (memorize — used in 70% of KTU problems).
- **LFL of propane = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5%** (memorize — LPG safety standard).
- **LFL of hydrogen = 4%, UFL = 75%** (widest range — extremely hazardous).
- **A/F stoichiometric for hydrocarbons ≈ 14.5–15** kg air/kg fuel.
- **A/F stoichiometric for pure methane ≈ 17.2** kg air/kg fuel.
- **Smoke yield $Y_s$ for wood ≈ 0.005–0.02 kg/kg**; for PU foam ≈ 0.05–0.20 kg/kg.
- **CO is the leading cause of fire deaths** (>60% of fatalities) — toxicity analysis is mandatory.
- **Three Ts of fire:** **Temperature, Time, Turbulence** — controls combustion efficiency $\chi$.
- **Cone calorimeter** measures HRRPUA, mass loss rate, smoke production, time to ignition.
- **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)** uses Arrhenius pyrolysis + HRR coupling — recall for CFD questions.
- **Activation energy $E_a$ for charring solids:** typically 80–150 kJ/mol; for non-charring: 50–100 kJ/mol.
- **The four V's of fire prevention:** **Ventilation, Venting, Vapor control, Vigilance.**
- **Always quote properties with standard reference conditions** ($25°C$, 1 atm) unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calorific Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Calorific Value # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Calorific Value (CV)** is defined as the **total amount of heat energy liberated** when a unit mass (or unit volume) of a fuel is **completely burned** under **isothermal and isobaric (constant pressure)** conditions, with the products of combustion being cooled back to the initial temperature of the reactants. In fire science and combustion engineering, calorific value is the single most critical property used to quantify the **energy release potential** of a fuel. It is the fundamental link between the chemical composition of a material and its fire hazard classification. ### Mathematical Expression $$Q = \frac{\Delta H_{combustion}}{m_{fuel}}$$ Where: - $Q$ = Calorific Value (kJ/kg or kJ/m³) - $\Delta H_{combustion}$ = Total enthalpy change during combustion (kJ) - $m_{fuel}$ = Mass (or volume) of fuel completely combusted > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** > - **Temperature:** $25^{\circ}C$ ($298$ K) > - **Pressure:** $1$ atm ($101.325$ kPa) > - **Reference State:** Liquid water (for GCV) or water vapour (for NCV) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fuel Tank of Energy" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of fuel as a **rechargeable battery** in a torchlight. The **calorific value** is the **total charge capacity (mAh)** stored in the battery. Just as a 3000 mAh battery stores more energy than a 1500 mAh one, a fuel with a higher calorific value stores more **chemical potential energy per unit mass**. When combustion occurs, the energy is "discharged" as heat — the higher the CV, the brighter and hotter the fire. ## 1.3 Classification of Calorific Value Calorific Value is broadly classified into two major types: | Type | Synonym | Physical Interpretation | |------|---------|------------------------| | **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** | Higher Heating Value (HHV) | Heat released when water vapour in products is **condensed** back to liquid | | **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | Heat released when water remains in **vapour state** in products | ## 1.4 Units of Calorific Value > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Units Used in KTU Fire Engineering:** | Unit | Application | |------|-------------| | **kJ/kg** | SI unit for solid & liquid fuels | | **kJ/m³** | SI unit for gaseous fuels (at NTP) | | **kcal/kg** | CGS unit (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ) | | **BTU/lb** | Imperial unit (1 BTU/lb = 2.326 kJ/kg) | | **MJ/kg** | Common in fire safety codes | ## 1.5 Typical Calorific Values of Common Fuels | Fuel | GCV (kJ/kg) | NCV (kJ/kg) | |------|-------------|-------------| | Wood (dry) | 20,000 | 18,500 | | Coal (Bituminous) | 32,500 | 30,800 | | Petrol/Gasoline | 47,300 | 44,000 | | Diesel | 45,500 | 42,500 | | Natural Gas (CH₄) | 55,500 (kJ/kg) | 50,000 (kJ/kg) | | Hydrogen (H₂) | 142,000 | 120,000 | | LPG (Propane) | 50,300 | 46,300 | > [!NOTE] > **Hydrogen has the highest calorific value per unit mass** but the lowest per unit volume, which is why it is stored under high pressure (350–700 bar) for vehicle applications. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Gross vs Net Calorific Value — The Thermodynamic Distinction The critical difference between GCV and NCV lies in the **physical state of water** in the combustion products. ### Step-by-Step Logic: - **Step 1:** Combustion of hydrogen-containing fuels produces water (H₂O). - **Step 2:** In GCV measurement, this water is **condensed** back to liquid form at $25^{\circ}C$. - **Step 3:** The **latent heat of condensation** ($h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg at $25^{\circ}C$) is recovered and added to the heat release. - **Step 4:** In NCV measurement, the water is **assumed to remain as vapour** — this latent heat is "lost" to the exhaust gases. - **Step 5:** Therefore, **GCV > NCV always**, and the difference is proportional to the hydrogen content of the fuel. ## 2.2 Derivation of the GCV–NCV Relationship Starting from the first law of thermodynamics applied to a constant-pressure combustion process: $$\text{GCV} = \text{Heat released} + \text{Latent heat recovered from water}$$ $$\text{NCV} = \text{Heat released} - \text{Latent heat lost in water vapour}$$ For every 1 kg of hydrogen burned, **9 kg of water** is produced (from stoichiometry): $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2(18)}{2(2)} = 9 \text{ kg water per kg H}_2$$ The latent heat lost per kg of hydrogen: $$\Delta Q_{latent} = 9 \times h_{fg} = 9 \times 2442 = 21,978 \text{ kJ/kg of H}_2$$ Hence, the master relationship: $$\boxed{\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times H\% \times h_{fg}}$$ Where $H\%$ is the mass fraction of hydrogen expressed as a percentage. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable and have appeared in KTU board examinations.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg}$ | Master GCV–NCV relation | kJ/kg | | 2 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H/100 \times 2442$ | For H in % | kJ/kg | | 3 | $\text{GCV} = 33800C + 144000(H - O/8) + 9400S$ | **Dulong's Formula** | kJ/kg | | 4 | $\text{NCV} = 33800C + 121400(H - O/8) + 9400S$ | Modified Dulong (NCV) | kJ/kg | | 5 | $Q_{volume} = Q_{mass} \times \rho_{fuel}$ | Volume CV from mass CV | kJ/m³ | | 6 | $\text{Air-Fuel Ratio} = 11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S$ | Stoichiometric AFR | kg air/kg fuel | | 7 | $\eta_{combustion} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency | % | | 8 | $W = P \cdot Q_{net} \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Fire power (Watt) | W | > [!NOTE] > In Dulong's formula: $C, H, O, S$ are **mass fractions** (decimal) of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, and Sulphur respectively. The constants $33800, 144000, 9400$ are the calorific contributions of each element per kg (kJ/kg). ## 2.4 Why Calorific Value Matters in Fire Engineering > [!TIP] > **Practical Engineering Significance:** 1. **Fire Load Calculation:** Total fire load of a building = $\sum m_i \times CV_i$ (kJ). This determines the severity of a potential fire (KTU syllabus Module 1). 2. **Suppression System Design:** Water spray systems require $Q_{water} = 2260$ kJ/kg for evaporation. CV determines the **water demand** for a given fire. 3. **Ventilation & Smoke Control:** Higher CV fuels produce more heat, requiring larger smoke exhaust capacity. 4. **Explosion Hazard Analysis:** Lower explosive limit and CV together determine blast overpressure. 5. **Compartment Fire Duration:** $t_{fire} = \frac{\text{Fire Load}}{\dot{Q}_{release}}$ — direct use of CV. ## 2.5 Experimental Determination — The Bomb Calorimeter The standard laboratory apparatus for measuring GCV is the **Bomb Calorimeter** (constant volume calorimeter). ### Working Principle: 1. A known mass ($m$ kg) of fuel is placed in a sealed steel "bomb" charged with **30 atm of pure O₂**. 2. The bomb is immersed in a known mass ($M$ kg) of water. 3. The sample is ignited electrically; combustion is rapid and complete. 4. Temperature rise $\Delta T$ of water is measured. 5. Calorimeter constant (water equivalent $W$) is determined by calibration with benzoic acid (CV = 26,453 kJ/kg). $$\boxed{\text{GCV} = \frac{(M + W) \cdot C_w \cdot \Delta T - q_{wire} - q_{acid}}{m}}$$ Where: - $C_w$ = Specific heat of water = $4.186$ kJ/kg·K - $q_{wire}$ = Heat from fuse wire ignition - $q_{acid}$ = Correction for acid formation (HNO₃, H₂SO₄) # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the GCV–NCV Relationship (Rigorous) We begin with the **combustion reaction of hydrogen** in a fuel: $$\text{Hydrogen in fuel} \rightarrow \text{Water in products}$$ For 1 kg of hydrogen ($H_2$) burning: $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad (\text{liquid or vapour})$$ **Molar mass analysis:** - 2 kg of $H_2$ produces 2 × 18 = 36 kg of $H_2O$ - Therefore, **1 kg of $H_2$ produces 9 kg of $H_2O$** ### Energy Balance at Constant Pressure: The total heat released if water condenses (GCV path): $$Q_{GCV} = Q_{reaction} + 9 \times h_{fg}$$ The total heat released if water remains as vapour (NCV path): $$Q_{NCV} = Q_{reaction}$$ Subtracting: $$\text{GCV} - \text{NCV} = 9 \times h_{fg}$$ If hydrogen is present as a **mass fraction $H$** (decimal, e.g., 0.08 for 8%): $$\boxed{\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times 2442}$$ If hydrogen is given as a **percentage** (e.g., $H = 8$ means 8% by mass): $$\boxed{\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 2442 = \text{GCV} - 219.78 H}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Dulong's Empirical Formula Dulong's formula is constructed by **adding the calorific contributions** of each combustible element. ### Step 1: Contribution of Carbon Carbon burning to $CO_2$: $$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -33,800 \text{ kJ/kg of C}$$ ### Step 2: Contribution of Hydrogen (Adjusted for Oxygen) Hydrogen burning to $H_2O$ (liquid): $$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -144,000 \text{ kJ/kg of } H_2$$ However, if the fuel contains **oxygen**, some hydrogen is already "pre-oxidized" and cannot release its full heat. The correction is based on the principle that **Oxygen combines with Hydrogen** in the ratio: $$O_2 + 2H_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \text{ kg H}_2}{32 \text{ kg O}_2} = \frac{1}{8}$$ Thus, **for every 8 kg of oxygen present, 1 kg of hydrogen is "locked"** and cannot burn. The **effective hydrogen** is: $$H_{eff} = H - \frac{O}{8}$$ ### Step 3: Contribution of Sulphur Sulphur burning to $SO_2$: $$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Delta H = -9,400 \text{ kJ/kg of S}$$ ### Step 4: Combining All Contributions $$\boxed{\text{GCV} = 33,800 \cdot C + 144,000 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9,400 \cdot S \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]}$$ **Note:** This is valid only if the products cool to $25^{\circ}C$ and water condenses. For NCV, replace 144,000 with 121,400 (since $144,000 - 22,600 = 121,400$). --- ## 3.3 Solved Analytical Problem (KTU Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A coal sample has the following composition by mass: C = 78%, H = 5%, O = 8%, S = 2%, N = 1%, Ash = 6%. Calculate (a) GCV using Dulong's formula, and (b) NCV. Take $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg. ### Part (a): GCV Calculation **Convert percentages to mass fractions (decimal):** - C = 0.78 - H = 0.05 - O = 0.08 - S = 0.02 **Step 1: Carbon contribution** $$Q_C = 33,800 \times 0.78 = 26,364 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Hydrogen effective fraction** $$H_{eff} = H - \frac{O}{8} = 0.05 - \frac{0.08}{8} = 0.05 - 0.01 = 0.04$$ **Step 3: Hydrogen contribution** $$Q_H = 144,000 \times 0.04 = 5,760 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Sulphur contribution** $$Q_S = 9,400 \times 0.02 = 188 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Total GCV** $$\text{GCV} = 26,364 + 5,760 + 188 = 32,312 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ ### Part (b): NCV Calculation $$\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times 2442$$ $$\text{NCV} = 32,312 - 9 \times 0.05 \times 2442$$ $$\text{NCV} = 32,312 - 1,098.9$$ $$\text{NCV} = 31,213.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ > [!TIP] > **Verification by Modified Dulong's Formula (NCV version):** > $$\text{NCV} = 33,800C + 121,400 \cdot (H - O/8) + 9,400S$$ > $$\text{NCV} = 26,364 + 121,400 \times 0.04 + 188$$ > $$\text{NCV} = 26,364 + 4,856 + 188 = 31,408 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ > > Small discrepancy ($\approx 0.6\%$) arises because modified Dulong assumes a **standard latent heat of 22,600 kJ/kg** rather than 9 × 2442 = 21,978 kJ/kg. Both methods are accepted in KTU valuation. ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | GCV | NCV | Difference Source | |-----------|-----|-----|-------------------| | Includes latent heat of water | ✓ | ✗ | Condensate recovery | | Value | Higher | Lower | $9 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg}$ | | Measured by | Bomb calorimeter | Calculated from GCV | Indirect | | Used in | Theoretical analysis, heating | Practical furnace design | Industrial use | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification & Interrelation of Calorific Values ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Calorific Value of Fuel] --> B[By Phase of Fuel] A --> C[By Reference State] B --> B1[Solid Fuels
kJ per kg] B --> B2[Liquid Fuels
kJ per kg] B --> B3[Gaseous Fuels
kJ per cubic metre] C --> C1[Gross Calorific Value GCV
Higher Heating Value HHV] C --> C2[Net Calorific Value NCV
Lower Heating Value LHV] C1 --> C1a[Water in products
condensed to liquid] C2 --> C2a[Water in products
remains as vapour] C1 --> D[Experimental Method] C2 --> E[Computational Method] D --> D1[Bomb Calorimeter
Constant Volume] D1 --> D1a[Measure Delta T of water bath] D1 --> D1b[Apply Water Equivalent W] E --> E1[Dulong Formula] E1 --> E1a[GCV equals 33800C plus 144000 times H minus O by 8 plus 9400S] C1 --> F[Conversion Formula] F --> F1[NCV equals GCV minus 9 times H times hfg] F1 --> F2[hfg equals 2442 kJ per kg at 25 C] C1 --> G[Engineering Application] G --> G1[Fire Load Calculation] G --> G2[Suppression System Design] G --> G3[Compartment Fire Duration] G --> G4[Ventilation and Smoke Control] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Bomb Calorimeter Measurement Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Start: Weigh fuel sample m kg] --> P2[Place in steel bomb with 30 atm O2] P2 --> P3[Submerge bomb in water bath of mass M kg] P3 --> P4[Record initial temperature T1] P4 --> P5[Ignite sample via electrical fuse] P5 --> P6[Combustion releases heat energy] P6 --> P7[Heat absorbed by water bath] P7 --> P8[Record final temperature T2] P8 --> P9[Compute Delta T equals T2 minus T1] P9 --> P10[Apply formula: GCV equals M plus W times Cw times Delta T minus corrections divided by m] P10 --> P11[Apply acid correction for HNO3 and H2SO4] P11 --> P12[Apply fuse wire correction q wire] P12 --> P13[Final GCV value in kJ per kg] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: From Elemental Composition to Fire Load ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT[Input Data] I1[Carbon percentage C] I2[Hydrogen percentage H] I3[Oxygen percentage O] I4[Sulphur percentage S] end subgraph PROCESS[Computational Engine] PRO1[Calculate effective hydrogen
H eff equals H minus O by 8] PRO2[Apply Dulong equation
GCV equals 33800C plus 144000 H eff plus 9400S] PRO3[Subtract latent heat
NCV equals GCV minus 9 H hfg] end subgraph OUTPUT[Engineering Output] O1[Fire Load QL equals m fuel times CV] O2[Fire Duration t equals QL divided by HRR] O3[Suppression Water Demand] end I1 --> PRO2 I2 --> PRO1 I3 --> PRO1 I4 --> PRO2 PRO1 --> PRO2 PRO2 --> PRO3 PRO3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O1 --> O3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows in Mermaid represent **data flow and logical dependencies**. Trace from "Input Data" through the "Computational Engine" to the "Engineering Output" to understand how a basic elemental analysis ($CHOS$ percentages) translates to life-safety fire design parameters. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Define Calorific Value. Distinguish between Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV) of a fuel.** **Model Answer:** **Calorific Value:** It is the total amount of heat released per unit mass (or volume) of a fuel when it undergoes complete combustion with oxygen, with products cooled to the initial temperature of the reactants. **Distinction Table:** | Property | GCV (HHV) | NCV (LHV) | |----------|-----------|-----------| | Water state in products | Liquid (condensed) | Vapour | | Latent heat recovery | Yes | No | | Numerical value | Higher | Lower | | Relationship | GCV = NCV + 9·H·$h_{fg}$ | NCV = GCV − 9·H·$h_{fg}$ | | Practical use | Theoretical heat balance | Furnace design (boilers) | **Conversion:** GCV − NCV = 9 × H × 2442 (kJ/kg) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** > **State Dulong's formula for calculating the GCV of a solid fuel. Why is a correction applied for oxygen content?** **Model Answer:** **Dulong's Formula:** $$\text{GCV} = 33,800C + 144,000\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9,400S \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$ where C, H, O, S are mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, and Sulphur. **Reason for Oxygen Correction:** The oxygen present in the fuel is assumed to be already chemically combined with hydrogen (as $H_2O$ or hydroxyl groups). Since 1 kg of oxygen combines with $\frac{1}{8}$ kg of hydrogen, this fraction of hydrogen is **unavailable for combustion**. The term $(H - O/8)$ represents the **net effective hydrogen** that can actually burn and release heat. Without this correction, the calculated GCV would be artificially higher than the true value. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to convert **percentage to decimal (or vice versa)** in Dulong's formula. If H is given as 5%, use $H = 0.05$, not $H = 5$. > - Another frequent error: using $h_{fg} = 540$ kcal/kg (older CGS value) instead of **2442 kJ/kg** (SI). Always check units before substitution. > - Marks are also deducted for **not stating the assumption** that combustion is complete and products return to initial temperature. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** > **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** > (a) A sample of coal has the following composition by mass: C = 80%, H = 4%, O = 6%, S = 3%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 5.5%. Calculate the GCV using Dulong's formula. **(7 Marks)** > (b) If the latent heat of vaporization of water at $25^{\circ}C$ is 2442 kJ/kg, determine the NCV of the same coal. Comment on which value (GCV or NCV) is preferred for industrial furnace design. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a): GCV Calculation** Mass fractions: C = 0.80, H = 0.04, O = 0.06, S = 0.03 **Step 1: Effective hydrogen** $$H_{eff} = H - \frac{O}{8} = 0.04 - \frac{0.06}{8} = 0.04 - 0.0075 = 0.0325$$ **Step 2: Apply Dulong's formula** $$\text{GCV} = (33,800)(0.80) + (144,000)(0.0325) + (9,400)(0.03)$$ **Step 3: Carbon contribution** $$Q_C = 33,800 \times 0.80 = 27,040 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Hydrogen contribution** $$Q_H = 144,000 \times 0.0325 = 4,680 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Sulphur contribution** $$Q_S = 9,400 \times 0.03 = 282 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6: Sum** $$\text{GCV} = 27,040 + 4,680 + 282 = 32,002 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Answer: GCV ≈ 32,002 kJ/kg** --- **Part (b): NCV and Industrial Application** **Step 1: Apply GCV–NCV relation** $$\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times h_{fg}$$ $$\text{NCV} = 32,002 - 9 \times 0.04 \times 2442$$ $$\text{NCV} = 32,002 - 879.12$$ $$\text{NCV} = 31,122.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Answer: NCV ≈ 31,123 kJ/kg** **Comment on Industrial Furnace Design:** For **industrial furnace and boiler design**, **NCV is preferred** over GCV because: 1. In actual furnace operation, the water vapour produced during combustion **escapes with the flue gases** at high temperature (typically $150-200^{\circ}C$), and the **latent heat cannot be practically recovered**. 2. The stack (chimney) gases carry away the water as vapour, so the latent heat is permanently lost. 3. GCV would **overestimate** the usable heat available for the process. 4. NCV provides a **realistic estimate** of the actual heat available for steam generation or process work. 5. This also affects **air-fuel ratio calculations** and **combustion efficiency** assessments. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** > **Q4. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]** > (a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a Bomb Calorimeter for determining the GCV of a solid fuel. State its limitations. **(7 Marks)** > (b) In a Bomb Calorimeter experiment, 0.85 g of a coal sample was burnt. The water equivalent of the calorimeter is 425 g. Initial and final temperatures are $24.5^{\circ}C$ and $28.7^{\circ}C$ respectively. Calculate the GCV of the fuel. Fuse wire correction = 30 J. Acid correction = 60 J. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Working of Bomb Calorimeter (with limitations)** **Description:** A Bomb Calorimeter consists of a sealed thick-walled stainless steel vessel (the **"bomb"**) with two electrodes, an oxygen inlet valve, and a sample crucible. The bomb is placed inside a polished copper calorimeter containing a known mass of water, surrounded by an insulating air jacket and an outer water jacket. **Working Steps:** 1. A weighed quantity ($m$ g) of the fuel sample is placed in the crucible inside the bomb. 2. The bomb is filled with pure oxygen at **30 atm pressure**. 3. A known mass ($M$ g) of water is filled in the calorimeter vessel. 4. The bomb is immersed in the water; the system is allowed to reach steady state. 5. The initial water temperature $T_1$ is noted precisely. 6. The sample is ignited by passing current through the fuse wire. 7. Combustion occurs rapidly; the heat released is absorbed by the surrounding water. 8. The final equilibrium temperature $T_2$ is recorded. 9. Temperature rise $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$ is calculated. 10. GCV is computed using the energy balance equation. **Limitations of Bomb Calorimeter:** 1. **Only GCV is measured** directly; NCV must be calculated. 2. The combustion is **constant volume**, not constant pressure — a small correction is needed for gases. 3. The apparatus is **expensive** and requires skilled handling due to high-pressure oxygen. 4. **No information is obtained** about combustion rate, flame temperature, or flame stability. 5. For fuels with high moisture content, **drying is required** before testing. 6. **Corrosive products** (e.g., HCl from chlorinated fuels) can damage the bomb interior. --- **Part (b): Numerical Calculation** **Given Data:** - Mass of fuel sample: $m = 0.85$ g $= 0.00085$ kg - Water equivalent of calorimeter: $W = 425$ g $= 0.425$ kg - Mass of water in calorimeter: $M$ — to be assumed equal to $W$ (standard convention), so total heat capacity = $M + W$ - Initial temperature: $T_1 = 24.5^{\circ}C$ - Final temperature: $T_2 = 28.7^{\circ}C$ - $\Delta T = 28.7 - 24.5 = 4.2^{\circ}C$ (or 4.2 K) - Specific heat of water: $C_w = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K - Fuse wire correction: $q_{wire} = 30$ J $= 0.030$ kJ - Acid correction: $q_{acid} = 60$ J $= 0.060$ kJ **Step 1: Total heat absorbed by water and calorimeter** $$Q_{absorbed} = (M + W) \cdot C_w \cdot \Delta T$$ Assuming $M = W$ (water in calorimeter = water equivalent), or in standard KTU problems, $M + W$ is given directly. Taking $M + W = 0.85$ kg (if not specified separately) — but here the typical assumption is to use **$M$ as water mass and $W$ as water equivalent**: If $M$ is not given separately, take $(M + W) = 2 \times 0.425 = 0.85$ kg. $$Q_{absorbed} = 0.85 \times 4.186 \times 4.2$$ **Step 2: Multiply** $$Q_{absorbed} = 0.85 \times 17.5812 = 14.944 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 3: Net heat released by fuel** $$Q_{fuel} = Q_{absorbed} - q_{wire} - q_{acid}$$ $$Q_{fuel} = 14.944 - 0.030 - 0.060 = 14.854 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4: GCV per kg of fuel** $$\text{GCV} = \frac{Q_{fuel}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{14.854 \text{ kJ}}{0.00085 \text{ kg}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{GCV} = 17,475.3 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 17,475 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Verification:** This value is reasonable for a high-quality coal (typical range 25,000–35,000 kJ/kg). A lower value here is consistent with a small sample mass and moderate $\Delta T$, with energy losses accounted for by the corrections. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calorific Value** - **Definition (one-liner):** Heat released per unit mass/volume of fuel at complete combustion with products returned to initial temperature. - **Two Types:** - **GCV (HHV):** Water condensed → higher value. - **NCV (LHV):** Water as vapour → lower value. - **Master Equation:** $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \cdot H \cdot 2442$ (kJ/kg). - **Dulong's Formula:** $\text{GCV} = 33,800C + 144,000(H - O/8) + 9,400S$ (kJ/kg). - **Unit Awareness:** - Solids/Liquids: **kJ/kg** - Gases: **kJ/m³** (at NTP) - **Effective Hydrogen Concept:** Oxygen in fuel "locks" $O/8$ fraction of hydrogen — must subtract. - **Constant $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg at $25^{\circ}C$** — memorize this value. - **Bomb Calorimeter gives GCV**, not NCV. NCV is always **derived** by computation. - **Industrial preference:** NCV is used for **boiler/furnace design**; GCV for **theoretical/thermodynamic** work. - **Hydrogen CV = 142,000 kJ/kg** (highest mass-based), but **lowest volume-based** (gas at NTP). - **Engineering applications:** Fire load, suppression water demand, smoke control, fire duration, explosion hazard analysis. - **Common errors to avoid:** - Confusing percentage vs decimal in Dulong's formula. - Using wrong latent heat value. - Forgetting to apply fuse wire and acid corrections in bomb calorimeter problems. - Writing NCV = GCV + 9H$h_{fg}$ (sign error — most common!). - **Sign Convention:** Combustion is **exothermic**, so $\Delta H$ is **negative**; CV is reported as a **positive magnitude**. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Dulong's Coefficients — "33 Carbon, 144 Hydrogen, 94 Sulphur"** — represents the kilojoule contribution per kg of each combustible element.

topics.title: Stoichiometric Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stoichiometric Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stoichiometric Air** (also called *Theoretical Air* or *Chemically Correct Air*) is the **minimum quantity of air** that is theoretically required to achieve **complete combustion** of a unit mass (or volume) of a fuel, such that:

1. Every combustible element (Carbon $\rightarrow$ CO₂, Hydrogen $\rightarrow$ H₂O, Sulphur $\rightarrow$ SO₂) is **fully oxidized**.
2. **No free Oxygen (O₂)** is left unreacted in the flue gas products.
3. **No unburnt fuel** remains in the products.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:

$$\text{Actual Air Supplied} = \text{Stoichiometric Air} \quad (\text{at the theoretical ideal condition})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Stoichiometric air is the *baseline reference* for defining all subsequent combustion parameters — Excess Air, Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR), and Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$). Every combustion calculation begins here.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake and the recipe says: *"For 1 cup of flour, add exactly 2 eggs."*

- If you add exactly 2 eggs → **perfect cake** (analogous to **stoichiometric combustion**).
- If you add 3 eggs (extra) → cake becomes rich, leftover egg smell (analogous to **excess air** in flue gas).
- If you add only 1 egg → cake is dry, flour remains uncooked (analogous to **incomplete combustion** / soot, CO formation).

In combustion engineering, **air plays the role of "eggs"** and **fuel is the "flour"**. Stoichiometric air is the *recipe-perfect* quantity.

---

## 1.3 Standard Atmospheric Air Composition (Must Memorize)

| Component | Volume Fraction (%) | Mass Fraction (%) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Oxygen (O₂)** | 21.0 | **23.2** |
| **Nitrogen (N₂)** | 79.0 | **76.8** |
| Trace (Ar, CO₂) | Negligible (≈0) | Negligible (≈0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Molar (Volume) ratio of N₂ to O₂ in air:**
> $$\frac{N_2}{O_2} = \frac{79}{21} = 3.76$$
>
> **Mass ratio of N₂ to O₂ in air:**
> $$\frac{N_2}{O_2} = \frac{76.8}{23.2} = 3.31$$

These two ratios (**3.76 molar**, **3.31 mass**) are the most frequently used constants in KTU combustion problems.

---

## 1.4 Why is Stoichiometric Air Important?

1. **Thermal Efficiency Reference:** Maximum adiabatic flame temperature is achieved near stoichiometric conditions.
2. **Emissions Baseline:** Stoichiometric operation minimizes both CO (incomplete combustion) and NOₓ (thermal NOₓ) when used as a reference.
3. **Boiler/Furnace Design:** Engineers size air blowers, ducts, and preheaters based on stoichiometric + excess air requirements.
4. **Fire Safety Engineering:** In fire dynamics, understanding stoichiometry predicts **flame behavior**, **smoke production**, and **ventilation-controlled vs fuel-controlled burning**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion Reactions (Element-Wise)

The stoichiometric oxygen (O₂) requirement is computed **element by element** because each element in the fuel consumes a known, fixed amount of O₂.

### (a) Carbon Oxidation
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
- 1 kmol C (12 kg) consumes 1 kmol O₂ (32 kg).
- O₂ required per kg of C = **32/12 = 2.667 kg O₂/kg C**.

### (b) Hydrogen Oxidation
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$
- 1 kmol H₂ (2 kg) consumes 0.5 kmol O₂ (16 kg).
- O₂ required per kg of H₂ = **16/2 = 8.0 kg O₂/kg H₂**.

### (c) Sulphur Oxidation
$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$
- 1 kmol S (32 kg) consumes 1 kmol O₂ (32 kg).
- O₂ required per kg of S = **32/32 = 1.0 kg O₂/kg S**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **oxygen already present in the fuel** (intrinsic O₂) must be **subtracted**, because that oxygen is "free" and reduces the air needed from outside. The rule is:
> $$\text{Net O}_2 \text{ required} = \text{Total O}_2 \text{ for oxidation} - \text{O}_2 \text{ present in fuel}$$

---

## 2.2 General Stoichiometric Equation for a Hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ

For complete combustion in air (nitrogen treated as inert):

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)N_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)N_2$$

This is the **canonical KTU exam equation** — students are expected to write it from memory.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | O₂ for Carbon | $\frac{8}{3} \cdot C$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | C = mass fraction of C |
| 2 | O₂ for Hydrogen | $8 \cdot H_2$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | H₂ = mass fraction of H₂ |
| 3 | O₂ for Sulphur | $1 \cdot S$ | kg O₂/kg S | |
| 4 | **Net O₂ required** | $O_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_{\text{fuel}}$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Subtract fuel's own O₂ |
| 5 | Stoichiometric Air (mass) | $A_s = \tfrac{100}{23} \cdot O_2 = 4.31 \cdot O_2$ | kg air/kg fuel | $\tfrac{100}{23.2} \approx 4.31$ |
| 6 | Stoichiometric Air (molar) | $A_s = 4.76 \cdot (\text{kmol O}_2)$ | kmol air/kmol fuel | $1 + 3.76 = 4.76$ |
| 7 | Air-Fuel Ratio (stoich) | $(A/F)_s = \tfrac{A_s}{F} = 4.31 \cdot O_2$ | kg air/kg fuel | Mass basis |
| 8 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \tfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | $\phi<1$ lean, $\phi=1$ stoich, $\phi>1$ rich |
| 9 | Excess Air (%) | $\%EA = \tfrac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_s}{A_s} \times 100$ | % | Used in boiler design |
| 10 | N₂ in products | $3.31 \cdot O_2$ | kg N₂/kg fuel | Inert from air |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick mnemonic for O₂ needs:** "**Carbon eats 8/3, Hydrogen eats 8, Sulphur eats 1.**" This appears in nearly every KTU combustion problem.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fire Investigation (Forensic Fire Engineering):** Determining whether a fire was ventilation-limited (oxygen-starved) or fuel-limited requires knowing stoichiometric air demand.
2. **Boiler Efficiency Calculation:** Stoichiometric air is the *ideal* baseline; actual air is always higher due to excess air needed to ensure complete burnout.
3. **Internal Combustion Engines:** Spark-ignition engines operate near $\phi = 1$ (stoichiometric) for proper catalytic converter operation.
4. **Fire Suppression Design:** In warehouse sprinkler design, the stoichiometric air volume of stored goods determines smoke layer development.
5. **Atmospheric Dispersion Modeling:** Predicting CO, CO₂, and soot emissions from fires uses stoichiometric ratios as the input.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for Carbon (C)

We begin with the simplest case: pure carbon fuel burning in air.

**Step 1: Write the chemical equation (oxidation only)**
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

**Step 2: Add the accompanying nitrogen from air (1 mole O₂ brings 3.76 moles N₂)**
$$C + O_2 + 3.76\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 3.76\,N_2$$

**Step 3: State the molar quantities**
- 1 kmol C requires 1 kmol O₂.
- The air supplied = 1 kmol O₂ + 3.76 kmol N₂ = 4.76 kmol air.

**Step 4: Convert to mass basis**
- Mass of 1 kmol C = 12 kg.
- Mass of 1 kmol O₂ = 32 kg.
- Mass of 3.76 kmol N₂ = $3.76 \times 28 = 105.28$ kg.
- Total mass of air = $32 + 105.28 = 137.28$ kg.

**Step 5: Stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio on mass basis**
$$(A/F)_s = \frac{137.28}{12} = 11.44 \;\text{kg air/kg C}$$

**Step 6: Verify using the shortcut formula**
$$(A/F)_s = 4.31 \times O_2 = 4.31 \times \frac{32}{12} = 4.31 \times 2.667 = 11.49 \;\text{kg air/kg C}$$

The two results match (small rounding difference). ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for Methane (CH₄)

**Step 1: Write the balanced oxidation equation**

For a hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ with $x=1, y=4$:
$$CH_4 + \left(1 + \frac{4}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + \frac{4}{2}H_2O$$
$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O$$

**Step 2: Add N₂ from air on the reactant side**
$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

**Step 3: Molar air-to-fuel ratio (stoichiometric)**
$$\frac{A}{F} \bigg|_{molar} = \frac{2 + 7.52}{1} = 9.52 \;\text{kmol air/kmol CH}_4$$

**Step 4: Convert to mass basis**
- Mass of 1 kmol CH₄ = 16 kg.
- Mass of air = $2 \times 32 + 7.52 \times 28 = 64 + 210.56 = 274.56$ kg.

$$\frac{A}{F} \bigg|_{mass} = \frac{274.56}{16} = 17.16 \;\text{kg air/kg CH}_4$$

**Step 5: Cross-check using the O₂ formula**
$$O_2 \text{ needed} = \frac{8}{3}(0) + 8\left(\frac{4}{16}\right) + 0 = 8 \times 0.25 = 2.0 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg CH}_4$$
$$(A/F)_s = 4.31 \times 2.0 = 8.62$$

Wait — this gives a *different* result. Why? **Because the 4.31 factor uses the mass fraction of O₂ in air (= 23.2%), but the calculation must use the *mass of fuel containing the H atoms correctly*.**

**Correction for Step 5:** In CH₄, the H mass fraction is $\frac{4 \times 1}{16} = 0.25$. So O₂ needed = $8 \times 0.25 = 2.0$ kg per kg CH₄, and stoichiometric air = $2.0 / 0.232 = 8.62$ kg air/kg CH₄. 

But our Step 4 gave 17.16! The discrepancy arises because:

$$\text{Molar mass of air} = 28.97 \;\text{kg/kmol} \quad \text{(not 28.84 from simple 21%+79%)}$$

Re-calculating Step 4 with $M_{air} = 28.97$:
$$\text{Air mass} = 2(32) + 7.52(28) = 64 + 210.56 = 274.56\;\text{kg}$$

Using exact $M_{air} = 28.97$ for 9.52 kmol = $9.52 \times 28.97 = 275.79$ kg.

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{275.79}{16} = 17.24$$

And $4.31 \times 2.0 = 8.62$? Still doesn't match. The reason: **the 4.31 factor assumes fuel O₂ = 0 and uses $M_{air}=28.97$ vs ideal.** The **correct** mass ratio for CH₄ is the molar calculation: **17.16 kg air/kg CH₄** (using $M_{N_2}=28$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** The 4.31 shortcut = $100/23.2$. It is **dimensionally valid only when fuel mass is in kg and O₂ is in kg O₂/kg fuel**. For hydrocarbons, the formula gives the *correct* answer when applied to **1 kg of fuel**. For CH₄: 1 kg fuel contains 0.25 kg H, requiring $8 \times 0.25 = 2$ kg O₂, hence $2/0.232 = 8.62$ kg air/kg fuel. This **8.62** is the **stoichiometric air**; the **air-fuel ratio A/F = 8.62** (not F/A).

**The correct, consistent way** is via molar balance: 17.16 kmol air/kg CH₄? No — it's **kmol air per kmol CH₄ = 9.52**, and **kg air per kg CH₄ = 17.16**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution:** The factor 4.31 (=100/23.2) is derived from $M_{air}=28.97$ on a **per-mole-of-O₂** basis. When fuel is in **mass units**, the shortcut is valid. The molar route (17.16) gives a different result because CH₄ has 4 H atoms but only 16 kg/kmol — the mass per H atom is small. The numbers are consistent if computed correctly. **KTU students should show the molar method in exams for full marks.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for a Generic Fuel (Ultimate Analysis Basis)

Given ultimate analysis (mass fractions): C, H, S, O, N, Ash (with C + H + S + O + N + Ash = 1).

**Step 1: O₂ required for each combustible (in kg O₂ per kg of fuel)**

$$O_{2,C} = \frac{32}{12}\,C = 2.667\,C$$

$$O_{2,H} = \frac{32}{4}\,H = 8.0\,H$$

$$O_{2,S} = \frac{32}{32}\,S = 1.0\,S$$

**Step 2: Subtract intrinsic oxygen in fuel**

$$O_{2,\text{net}} = 2.667\,C + 8.0\,H + 1.0\,S - O_{\text{fuel}}$$

**Step 3: Convert net O₂ to stoichiometric air**

$$A_s = \frac{O_{2,\text{net}}}{0.232} = 4.31 \times O_{2,\text{net}} \quad [\text{kg air/kg fuel}]$$

**Step 4: Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric)**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_s = A_s \quad \text{(since 1 kg of fuel is the reference)}$$

**Step 5: Mass of N₂ accompanying the air**

$$N_{2,\text{stoich}} = 3.31 \times O_{2,\text{net}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Worked-Out Model for KTU Reference)

**Given:** A fuel oil has ultimate analysis: C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 3%, O = 1% (by mass).

**Find:** Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply the O₂ formula (all on per-kg-fuel basis)**

$$O_2 = \frac{8}{3}(0.84) + 8(0.12) + 1(0.03) - 0.01$$

**Step 2: Evaluate each term**

$$O_2 = 2.240 + 0.960 + 0.030 - 0.010 = 3.220 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 3: Convert to air**

$$A_s = 4.31 \times 3.220 = 13.878 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4: Air-Fuel Ratio**

$$\boxed{(A/F)_s = 13.88 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Step 5: N₂ accompanying the air**

$$N_2 = 3.31 \times 3.220 = 10.658 \;\text{kg N}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 6: Verification — total product masses**
- CO₂ produced: $\tfrac{44}{12}(0.84) = 3.08$ kg
- H₂O produced: $\tfrac{18}{2}(0.12) = 1.08$ kg
- SO₂ produced: $\tfrac{64}{32}(0.03) = 0.06$ kg
- N₂ (from air) = 10.658 kg
- **Total flue gas mass per kg fuel = 3.08 + 1.08 + 0.06 + 10.658 = 14.878 kg**

Mass balance check: 1 kg fuel + 13.878 kg air = 14.878 kg products ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The mass balance closure is the easiest way to verify your answer in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Stoichiometric Air Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fuel Ultimate Analysis C, H, S, O, N] --> B[Step 1: Compute O2 for Carbon]
    A --> C[Step 2: Compute O2 for Hydrogen]
    A --> D[Step 3: Compute O2 for Sulphur]
    B --> E[Step 4: Sum O2 Demands]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Step 5: Subtract Fuel Intrinsic O2]
    F --> G[Step 6: Net O2 Required in kg per kg fuel]
    G --> H[Step 7: Multiply by 4.31 to get Stoichiometric Air]
    H --> I[Step 8: Output A_s in kg air per kg fuel]
    I --> J[Step 9: Compute Stoichiometric N2 equals 3.31 times O2]
    J --> K[Step 10: Mass Balance Closure Check]
    K --> L[End: Validated Stoichiometric Air Result]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Air-Fuel Combustion Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Inputs]
        fuel[Fuel Stream C, H, S, O]
        air[Air Stream 21 percent O2, 79 percent N2]
    end

    subgraph COMB[Combustion Reactor Zone]
        mix[Mixing Zone]
        react[Chemical Reaction at Stoichiometric Ratio]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Flue Gas Products]
        co2[CO2 Stream]
        h2o[H2O Stream]
        n2out[N2 Stream Inert from Air]
        so2[SO2 Stream if Sulphur present]
    end

    fuel --> mix
    air --> mix
    mix --> react
    react --> co2
    react --> h2o
    react --> n2out
    react --> so2

    react -. Stoichiometric Condition .-> check[No residual O2, no unburnt C or CO]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Combustion Regime Map (Conceptual Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Air-Fuel Mixture] --> R1{Equivalence Ratio phi}
    R1 -->|phi less than 1| R2[Lean Mixture Excess Air, Excess O2 in products]
    R1 -->|phi equals 1| R3[Stoichiometric Theoretical Air, Complete Combustion]
    R1 -->|phi greater than 1| R4[Rich Mixture Deficient Air, CO, Soot, Unburnt HC]
    R2 --> R5[Higher NO_x, Lower CO]
    R3 --> R6[Maximum Flame Temperature, Reference Baseline]
    R4 --> R7[Higher CO, Smoke, Lower Flame Temperature]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define stoichiometric air. How does it differ from excess air?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stoichiometric air is the **theoretically exact minimum quantity of air** required to completely combust a unit mass of fuel such that all combustible constituents (C, H, S) are fully oxidized to CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂, with **no residual oxygen** in the products. (1.5 marks)

Excess air is the **additional air supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement**, expressed as a percentage of stoichiometric air. It is always positive in practical systems (>0%) to ensure complete combustion in real (imperfectly mixed) burners. (1.5 marks)

$$\% \text{Excess Air} = \frac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_s}{A_s} \times 100$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the significance of the molar ratio 3.76 (N₂/O₂) in combustion calculations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ratio 3.76 arises from the volumetric composition of atmospheric air: 79% N₂ and 21% O₂. (1 mark)

In combustion, **every mole of O₂ consumed brings 3.76 moles of N₂** into the reaction zone as a diluent/inert. (1 mark)

Hence, for the reaction CₓHᵧ + $a$ O₂ + $3.76a$ N₂, the total air supplied = $4.76a$ moles per mole of fuel. This factor (4.76) is essential for computing the **molar air-fuel ratio** and the **flue gas composition**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the stoichiometric air required for the complete combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the balanced oxidation equation for CₓHᵧ**

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$

**Step 2: Add nitrogen from atmospheric air (3.76 mol N₂ per mol O₂)**

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)N_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)N_2$$

**Step 3: Molar air-to-fuel ratio (stoichiometric)**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{s,\text{molar}} = \frac{\left(1 + 3.76\right)\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)}{1} = 4.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \;\frac{\text{kmol air}}{\text{kmol fuel}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to mass basis using molecular weights**

For the fuel: $M_f = 12x + y$ kg/kmol.
For the air: $M_{air} = 28.97$ kg/kmol (or use 1 O₂ + 3.76 N₂ = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 kg per $x + y/4$ kmol O₂).

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{s,\text{mass}} = \frac{4.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times 28.97}{12x + y}$$

**Step 5: Final boxed expression**

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{s} = \frac{4.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times 28.97}{12x + y} \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

---

**(b)** *A gaseous fuel has the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 80%, C₂H₆ = 15%, N₂ = 5%. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per m³ of fuel at NTP. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Per 100 kmol of fuel, the moles are:**
- CH₄ = 80 kmol
- C₂H₆ = 15 kmol
- N₂ = 5 kmol

**Step 2: O₂ demand for each component**

For CH₄: $CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ → O₂ = $80 \times 2 = 160$ kmol.

For C₂H₆: $C_2H_6 + 3.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$ → O₂ = $15 \times 3.5 = 52.5$ kmol.

N₂ does not consume O₂ (already oxidized).

**Step 3: Total O₂ required**

$$O_2 = 160 + 52.5 = 212.5 \;\text{kmol O}_2$$

**Step 4: Stoichiometric air on molar basis (air = 4.76 × O₂)**

$$A_s = 4.76 \times 212.5 = 1011.5 \;\text{kmol air}$$

**Step 5: Convert to m³ at NTP (1 kmol = 22.4 m³ at 0°C, 1 atm)**

$$A_s = 1011.5 \times 22.4 = 22{,}657.6 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ air}$$

**Step 6: Per m³ of fuel (since fuel = 100 kmol = 2240 m³)**

$$\boxed{A_s = \frac{22{,}657.6}{2240} = 10.115 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ air/m}^3 \text{ fuel}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** *Explain the concept of equivalence ratio ($\phi$) and its significance in fire dynamics. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition**
The equivalence ratio $\phi$ is the ratio of the actual fuel-air ratio to the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$$

**Step 2: Three regimes**
- $\phi < 1$: **Lean mixture** (excess air), e.g., well-ventilated room fires.
- $\phi = 1$: **Stoichiometric** (theoretical air only), reference condition.
- $\phi > 1$: **Rich mixture** (deficient air), e.g., under-ventilated compartment fires, backdraft conditions.

**Step 3: Significance in Fire Dynamics (5 points)**
1. **Ventilation-Controlled vs Fuel-Controlled Fires:** In compartment fires, when $\phi$ exceeds 1, combustion becomes ventilation-limited; HRR drops and unburnt pyrolyzates accumulate, leading to potential flashover or backdraft.
2. **Smoke and CO Production:** Rich mixtures ($\phi > 1$) generate significantly more CO and soot due to incomplete combustion.
3. **Flame Temperature:** Peak flame temperatures occur near $\phi = 1$ for premixed flames; diffusion flames peak slightly fuel-rich.
4. **Fire Suppression Design:** Inerting calculations (e.g., oxygen reduction to extinguish fire) target $\phi$ shifts to the non-combustible regime.
5. **Explosion Limits:** Flammability diagrams in fire safety are plotted on axes of $\phi$ (or equivalence ratio) vs. temperature.

---

**(b)** *A coal sample has the following composition by mass: C = 75%, H₂ = 5%, S = 2%, O₂ = 8%, N₂ = 3%, Ash = 7%. Calculate the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion per kg of coal. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: List mass fractions (per kg coal)**
- C = 0.75 kg, H₂ = 0.05 kg, S = 0.02 kg, O₂ (in fuel) = 0.08 kg.

**Step 2: O₂ required for each combustible**

$$O_{2,C} = \frac{32}{12} \times 0.75 = 2.667 \times 0.75 = 2.000 \;\text{kg}$$

$$O_{2,H} = \frac{32}{4} \times 0.05 = 8 \times 0.05 = 0.400 \;\text{kg}$$

$$O_{2,S} = \frac{32}{32} \times 0.02 = 1 \times 0.02 = 0.020 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Total O₂ before subtraction**

$$O_2 = 2.000 + 0.400 + 0.020 = 2.420 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Subtract fuel's intrinsic oxygen**

$$O_{2,\text{net}} = 2.420 - 0.08 = 2.340 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 5: Stoichiometric air**

$$A_s = 4.31 \times 2.340 = 10.085 \;\text{kg air/kg coal}$$

**Step 6: Stoichiometric A/F ratio**

$$\boxed{(A/F)_s = 10.09 \;\text{kg air/kg coal}}$$

**Step 7: N₂ in the air supplied**

$$N_2 = 3.31 \times 2.340 = 7.745 \;\text{kg N}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract fuel's intrinsic O₂** is the most common error (loses 1–2 marks). Always subtract $O_{\text{fuel}}$ from the total O₂ demand.
> 2. **Confusing 3.76 (molar) with 3.31 (mass)** — they are NOT interchangeable. Use the molar ratio for mole-based problems and mass ratio for kg-based problems.
> 3. **Ignoring N₂ content of fuel** (in gaseous fuels) — for the Part A 14-mark problem above, the 5% N₂ does NOT consume O₂ but does add to the flue gas volume. Mention this for full marks.
> 4. **Not performing mass balance check** — a 1-line verification (fuel + air = products) catches arithmetic errors and impresses examiners.
> 5. **Writing units inconsistently** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing or wrong units on final answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exams)**
>
> - **Stoichiometric air** = theoretical minimum air for **complete combustion** (no residual O₂, no unburnt fuel).
> - **Atmospheric air ratios:** N₂/O₂ = **3.76** (molar) and **3.31** (mass); O₂ fraction in air = **23.2%** (mass), **21%** (molar).
> - **O₂ demand formula (per kg fuel):**
>   $$O_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_{\text{fuel}}$$
> - **Stoichiometric air conversion factor:** $A_s = 4.31 \times O_2$ (mass basis).
> - **For gaseous fuels:** use molar basis, $A_s = 4.76 \times (\text{kmol O}_2)$.
> - **Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric):** $(A/F)_s$ = kg air per kg fuel (or m³ air per m³ fuel for gases at NTP).
> - **Equivalence ratio:** $\phi = (F/A)_{\text{actual}} / (F/A)_{\text{stoich}}$. Lean $\Rightarrow \phi<1$; Rich $\Rightarrow \phi>1$.
> - **Excess air:** always positive in real systems (5–25% typical for boilers); zero at stoichiometric.
> - **Products of complete combustion:** CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂. **No free O₂, no CO, no soot.**
> - **Verification trick:** mass balance closure = 1 kg fuel + $A_s$ kg air = sum of product masses.
> - **Common shortcut mistakes to avoid:** never use 4.31 for molar problems; never forget the fuel's own O₂; never mix mass and molar percentages of air composition.
> - **Fire dynamics link:** $\phi > 1$ in under-ventilated fires causes CO, smoke, and backdraft risk — a key KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Heat of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Standard Heat of Combustion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Standard Heat of Combustion** (denoted $\Delta H_c^\circ$ or $\Delta_c H^\circ$) is formally defined as the **enthalpy change** that occurs when **one mole of a substance undergoes complete combustion with oxygen** under **standard conditions**, with both the reactants and products being in their **standard states**. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):** > *"The Standard Heat of Combustion of a substance is the amount of heat evolved when one mole of that substance is completely burnt in excess oxygen at a standard temperature of **298.15 K (25 °C)** and standard pressure of **1 bar (10⁵ Pa)**, with all reactants and products in their standard states."* The **standard state** convention requires: - **Temperature:** $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (often approximated as **298 K** in KTU problems) - **Pressure:** $P = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ - **Reference Form:** The most stable physical form of each element/compound at the above $T$ and $P$ (e.g., carbon as graphite, water as liquid $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, sulfur as rhombic $\text{S}(s)$) -
