**Complete Oxidation:** Carbon → $\text{CO}_2(g)$, Hydrogen → $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, Sulfur → $\text{SO}_2(g)$, Nitrogen → $\text{N}_2(g)$ > [!NOTE] **Units used in KTU exam scripts:** > $\Delta H_c^\circ$ is conventionally reported in **kJ/mol** (SI fire engineering standard) or sometimes **kcal/mol** (legacy unit). $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$. ### 1.2 Gross vs. Net Heat of Combustion — A Critical Distinction | Term | Water Product State | Symbol | |------|---------------------|--------| | **Gross (Higher) Heat of Combustion** | $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ (condensed liquid) | $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}}$ or HHV | | **Net (Lower) Heat of Combustion** | $\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$ (vapour) | $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}}$ or LHV | The two are related by the latent heat of vaporisation of water at 298 K, which equals **44.01 kJ/mol** (or roughly 40.7 kJ/mol if using $100^\circ\text{C}$ as reference, depending on convention). > [!TIP] **Fire Engineering Context:** > In real compartment fires, water is always produced as **vapour** because flame temperatures exceed 1000 K. Hence, the **Net Heat of Combustion (LHV)** is the practically relevant value for fire-load and energy-release calculations. The Gross value is used in calorimetric laboratory measurements (bomb calorimeter) where water is condensed. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Burn" Imagine you have exactly **one slice of bread (1 mole)** and you want to know the *total chemical energy stored inside it*. You place it in a perfectly insulated, oxygen-rich oven at room temperature (25 °C, 1 atm — the standard state) and let it burn completely to ash, water vapour, and $\text{CO}_2$. - If you **catch and condense** all the water vapour back into liquid (like a dehumidifier), you measure the **Gross Heat of Combustion** — this is the *maximum possible* energy release. - If you **let the water vapour escape** out of the chimney (like a real fire), you measure the **Net Heat of Combustion** — this is the *actually usable* energy in a fire scenario. > The difference between the two is precisely the **energy needed to vaporise** the water that was chemically formed. This is why fire engineers almost always use the **Net** value for design calculations. ### 1.4 Why "Standard" Conditions Matter Performing measurements at a fixed reference state (298 K, 1 bar) eliminates ambiguity caused by: 1. **Temperature variations** — enthalpy is temperature-dependent. 2. **Phase changes** — solids, liquids, and gases release different amounts of heat. 3. **Incomplete combustion** — any CO or soot formation reduces measured heat. A standardised definition enables **fair comparison** between fuels (methane vs. propane vs. octane) and provides a **universal thermodynamic baseline** for tabulated data in the **NFPA Fire Protection Handbook** and **SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Standard Heat of Combustion is Determined The topic can be decomposed into five logical pillars, each addressing a specific "why" and "how": **Pillar 1 — Writing the Stoichiometric Combustion Equation** - Identify the molecular formula of the fuel. - Balance the reaction so that exactly **1 mole of fuel** reacts with the stoichiometric amount of $\text{O}_2$. - All products must be in their **fully oxidised standard states**. Example for methane ($\text{CH}_4$): $$ \text{CH}_4(g) + 2\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$ > The coefficient "1" before the fuel is *mandatory* — by definition, combustion is per mole of **fuel**, not per mole of oxygen. **Pillar 2 — Application of Hess's Law (The Core Theorem)** Hess's Law states that the total enthalpy change is **independent of the pathway** between initial and final states. This allows us to compute $\Delta H_c^\circ$ from tabulated **standard heats of formation** $\Delta H_f^\circ$: $$ \Delta H_c^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{f,\text{reactants}}^\circ - \sum_j \nu_j \Delta H_{f,\text{products}}^\circ $$ where $\nu$ denotes the stoichiometric coefficient. By convention, the standard heat of formation of an element in its reference form is **zero** (e.g., $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2) = \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{N}_2) = 0$). > [!IMPORTANT] **Sign Convention Alert (Common KTU Pitfall):** > Heat of combustion is **always exothermic** for real fuels, hence $\Delta H_c^\circ < 0$. However, many engineering tables (and SFPE) report the magnitude as a **positive** value, calling it the "heat of combustion." Always check the sign convention in the data source. **Pillar 3 — Relationship Between Gross and Net Values** If the fuel contains hydrogen (mass fraction $w_H$), the difference is governed by the latent heat of vaporisation of water at 298 K, $h_{fg} \approx \mathbf{44.01 \text{ kJ/mol}}$. For a fuel of generic formula $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e$: $$ \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} = \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} - \frac{b}{2} \cdot h_{fg}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, 298\text{ K}) $$ where $\frac{b}{2}$ is the moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced per mole of fuel. **Pillar 4 — Dulong's Empirical Formula (Estimation for Solid Fuels)** For approximate estimation of combustion heats of solid fuels (coal, biomass, polymers) when experimental data is unavailable, **Dulong's formula** is widely used in fire-load calculations: $$ \text{HHV} = 33800\, w_C + 144200\left(w_H - \frac{w_O}{8}\right) + 9400\, w_S \quad [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ where $w_C, w_H, w_O, w_S$ are the mass fractions of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur respectively. > [!NOTE] **KTU Note:** Dulong's formula assumes the fuel's oxygen is bound to hydrogen as water. The term $\frac{w_O}{8}$ corrects for the hydrogen "already oxidised" within the fuel matrix. **Pillar 5 — Experimental Determination (Bomb Calorimeter)** In the laboratory, $\Delta H_c^\circ$ is measured using a **constant-volume bomb calorimeter**: - A known mass $m$ of fuel is ignited in pure $\text{O}_2$ at ~30 atm inside a sealed steel "bomb" immersed in water. - Temperature rise $\Delta T$ of the water (mass $M_w$, specific heat $c_w$) is recorded. - The heat released equals $Q = (C_{\text{bomb}} + M_w c_w)\Delta T$, where $C_{\text{bomb}}$ is the calorimeter constant determined by benzoic acid calibration. - This gives the **internal energy change** $\Delta U_c$. To convert to $\Delta H_c$ (constant pressure): $\Delta H_c = \Delta U_c + \Delta n_{gas} RT$. ### 2.2 Real-World Utility in Fire Engineering | Application | Quantity Used | |-------------|---------------| | **Fire Load Calculation** (MJ/m²) | $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}}$ per kg of combustible | | **HRR (Heat Release Rate)** estimation | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ of dominant fuel | | **Compartment Fire Temperature** modelling | $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}}$ + ventilation factor | | **Explosion / BLEVE** analysis | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ for total energy budget | | **Suppression System Design** (water, foam) | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ for fuel-specific agent quantity | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units | |---|------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{reactants}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{products})$ | Hess's Law (fuel side minus product side) | kJ/mol | | 2 | $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} = \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} - \dfrac{b}{2} h_{fg}$ | Gross–Net relation (b = H atoms in fuel) | kJ/mol | | 3 | $\Delta H_c = \Delta U_c + \Delta n_{gas} RT$ | Bomb-calorimeter constant-$P$ correction | kJ/mol | | 4 | $Q = m \cdot \Delta H_c^\circ$ | Total heat released from $m$ moles of fuel | kJ | | 5 | $\text{HHV} = 33800\, w_C + 144200\!\left(w_H - \dfrac{w_O}{8}\right) + 9400\, w_S$ | Dulong's empirical formula | kJ/kg | | 6 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c^\circ$ | Heat Release Rate (HRR) from mass burning rate | kW | | 7 | $T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H_c^\circ}{\sum \nu_j c_{p,j}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature estimate | K | | 8 | $\Delta H_c^\circ(\text{element in std. state}) = 0$ | Convention for $\text{O}_2, \text{N}_2, \text{C}(\text{graphite})$ | kJ/mol | > [!TIP] **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Tip:** Of the eight equations above, **(1) Hess's Law** appears in ~70% of board exam problems, followed by **(2) Gross–Net** and **(5) Dulong's formula** in design-oriented questions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Full Derivation — Hess's Law Application to Combustion We will derive the relationship between heat of combustion and heat of formation rigorously using a thermodynamic cycle. **Given:** A generic hydrocarbon fuel $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b$ burns in oxygen. The reaction is: $$ \text{C}_a\text{H}_b(g) + \left(a + \frac{b}{4}\right) \text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow a\,\text{CO}_2(g) + \frac{b}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$ **Step 1 — Define the target quantity** (per mole of fuel): $$ \Delta H_c^\circ = H^\circ(\text{products}) - H^\circ(\text{reactants}) $$ This is the standard heat of combustion we wish to compute. **Step 2 — Construct the Hess's Law cycle.** Imagine an alternative two-step path from the same initial state (elements in their standard states) to the same final state (combustion products): $$ \text{Path A (Direct): Elements} \xrightarrow{\Delta H_c^\circ \text{ of fuel}} \text{Products} $$ $$ \text{Path B (Two-step): Elements} \xrightarrow{\text{Step (i): Form the fuel}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step (ii): Burn the fuel}} \text{Products} $$ **Step 3 — Write the enthalpy of each step in Path B.** - Step (i): Formation of 1 mole of fuel from elements: $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_a\text{H}_b, g) = a\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C, graphite}) + \frac{b}{2}\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2, g) \rightarrow \text{C}_a\text{H}_b(g) $$ But since $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of elements in their standard states is **zero** by convention, this simply yields the heat of formation of the fuel itself. - Step (ii): Combustion of 1 mole of fuel — this is exactly $\Delta H_c^\circ$ (our target). **Step 4 — Apply Hess's Law (state-function property).** Since enthalpy is a state function, the total enthalpy change along Path B must equal that along Path A. But Path A is the *sum* of two conceptual sub-paths going from elements to products: - Sub-path α: Elements → Fuel ($\Delta H_f^\circ$ of fuel) - Sub-path β: Fuel → Products ($\Delta H_c^\circ$ of fuel) For the products directly from elements: - Formation of $a$ moles of $\text{CO}_2(g)$: $a \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, g)$ - Formation of $\frac{b}{2}$ moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$: $\frac{b}{2} \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)$ **Step 5 — Equate the two expressions and solve.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{fuel}) + \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{fuel}) &= a\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + \frac{b}{2}\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}) \\ \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{fuel}) &= a\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + \frac{b}{2}\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}) - \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{fuel}) \end{aligned} $$ Generalising to any combustion reaction with arbitrary stoichiometry: $$ \boxed{\;\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum_j \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{reactants})\;} $$ > **Conversion Logic Used:** We exploited the **state-function property of enthalpy** ($H$ depends only on state, not path) and the **convention** that $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of elements in reference form is zero. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Combustion of Propane > **Problem:** Calculate the standard heat of combustion of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$, $g$) at 298 K, given: > $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8, g) = -104.7$ kJ/mol > $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, g) = -393.5$ kJ/mol > $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.8$ kJ/mol > $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241.8$ kJ/mol **Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation.** $$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) + 5\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$ **Step 2 — Identify stoichiometric coefficients.** Reactants: $\nu(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) = 1$, $\nu(\text{O}_2) = 5$. Products: $\nu(\text{CO}_2) = 3$, $\nu(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = 4$. **Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law (Gross value, water as liquid).** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} &= \left[3 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + 4 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)\right] - \left[1 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) + 5 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2)\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.** Note that $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2) = 0$ (element in standard state). $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} &= \left[3(-393.5) + 4(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-104.7) + 5(0)\right] \\ &= \left[-1180.5 + (-1143.2)\right] - (-104.7) \\ &= (-2323.7) - (-104.7) \\ &= -2323.7 + 104.7 \\ &= -2219.0 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate the Net Heat of Combustion (water as vapour).** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} &= \left[3(-393.5) + 4(-241.8)\right] - (-104.7) \\ &= [-1180.5 + (-967.2)] - (-104.7) \\ &= (-2147.7) - (-104.7) \\ &= -2043.0 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Verify with the Gross–Net relation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} - \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} &= -2043.0 - (-2219.0) = 176.0 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ Theoretical check using $h_{fg} = 44.01$ kJ/mol and $b/2 = 4$ moles of water: $$ \frac{b}{2} \cdot h_{fg} = 4 \times 44.01 = 176.04 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad \checkmark $$ The values match within rounding error. **Result: $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} = -2219$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} = -2043$ kJ/mol.** ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Sign and Magnitude Comparisons | Fuel | Formula | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ (gross, kJ/mol) | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ (net, kJ/mol) | Heat Released per kg of fuel (MJ/kg) | |------|---------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | Hydrogen | $\text{H}_2$ | $-285.8$ | $-241.8$ | $119.9$ (net) | | Methane | $\text{CH}_4$ | $-890.4$ | $-802.3$ | $50.0$ (net) | | Propane | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | $-2219.2$ | $-2043.0$ | $46.4$ (net) | | Octane (gasoline surrogate) | $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ | $-5470.7$ | $-5074.4$ | $44.4$ (net) | | Methanol | $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ | $-726.1$ | $-638.1$ | $19.9$ (net) | | Carbon monoxide | $\text{CO}$ | $-283.0$ | $-283.0$ | $10.1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] **Observation:** The energy *per mole* increases with carbon number, but the energy *per unit mass* decreases — a critical reason why **light fuels (hydrogen, methane) are favoured for high-energy-density applications**, while heavier hydrocarbons are preferred where volumetric energy matters. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hess's Law Cycle for Standard Heat of Combustion The following Mermaid block illustrates the thermodynamic pathway used to derive the heat of combustion from heats of formation. The visualisation emphasises the **state-function nature** of enthalpy and the role of the **elements in their standard states** as a common reference. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ELEMENTS IN STANDARD STATE
C graphite + H2 gas + O2 gas
all at 298 K and 1 bar"]:::startNode B["1 MOLE OF FUEL
CaHb gas or liquid
at 298 K and 1 bar"]:::fuelNode C["COMBUSTION PRODUCTS
a CO2 gas + b/2 H2O liquid
at 298 K and 1 bar"]:::productNode A -- "Path alpha
Delta Hf of fuel
(Fuel Formation)" --> B B -- "Path beta
Delta Hc of fuel
(Target Quantity)" --> C A -- "Path gamma direct
Sum of Delta Hf of products" --> C A:::startNode B:::fuelNode C:::productNode classDef startNode fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fuelNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef productNode fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Nodes** represent thermodynamic states at the same reference conditions (298 K, 1 bar). - **Edges** represent enthalpy changes for each conceptual pathway. - By Hess's Law: $\text{Path } \alpha + \text{Path } \beta = \text{Path } \gamma$, which directly gives the boxed formula in Section 3.1. ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Pathways for Determining $\Delta H_c^\circ$ ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT DATA"] I1["Fuel molecular formula"] I2["Standard heats of formation
from thermodynamic tables"] I3["Bomb calorimeter
experimental data"] I4["Ultimate analysis
mass fractions C, H, O, S"] end subgraph METHODS["COMPUTATIONAL METHODS"] M1["Method 1
Hess Law Analytical
Delta Hc equals sum of products minus sum of reactants"] M2["Method 2
Bomb Calorimeter
Q equals C effective times Delta T"] M3["Method 3
Dulong Empirical
HHV equals 33800 wC plus 144200 wH minus wO/8 plus 9400 wS"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT QUANTITIES"] O1["Delta Hc gross
water as liquid
kJ per mol"] O2["Delta Hc net
water as vapour
kJ per mol"] O3["Fire load
MJ per m squared"] O4["HRR per unit mass
kW per kg"] end I1 --> M1 I2 --> M1 I1 --> M3 I4 --> M3 I3 --> M2 M1 --> O1 M1 --> O2 M2 --> O1 M2 --> O2 M3 --> O1 O1 --> O3 O2 --> O3 O2 --> O4 O1 --> O4 ``` **Interpretation of the block diagram:** - **Input layer** lists the three primary data sources (molecular identity, tabulated formation enthalpies, calorimeter data, ultimate analysis). - **Methods layer** maps each input to its appropriate analytical route. - **Output layer** enumerates the engineering quantities derived from $\Delta H_c^\circ$, demonstrating its central role in fire-protection design. > [!NOTE] **Why a block diagram and not a physical picture?** > The concept of "standard heat of combustion" is a thermodynamic *state* property — it does not have a physical shape to draw. A functional block architecture correctly captures its **data flow** and **interactions** with adjacent concepts in fire engineering, which is the academically appropriate schematic. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Style]** **Q1. Define the term "Standard Heat of Combustion" of a fuel. State its standard state conditions clearly.** *(Module 1, CO1, Remember/Understand Level)* **Model Answer:** The **standard heat of combustion** ($\Delta H_c^\circ$) is defined as the enthalpy change when **one mole of a fuel** undergoes **complete combustion with oxygen** under **standard-state conditions**: temperature **298.15 K** and pressure **1 bar (10⁵ Pa)**, with all reactants and products in their thermodynamically most stable reference forms (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ gas, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ liquid, C graphite). It is conventionally reported in **kJ/mol** and is always a **negative (exothermic)** quantity for true fuels. --- **Q2. Distinguish between Gross (Higher) Heat of Combustion and Net (Lower) Heat of Combustion. Which one is used in fire engineering design and why?** *(Module 1, CO1, Understand Level)* **Model Answer:** The **Gross Heat of Combustion (HHV)** assumes the water produced during combustion is in the **liquid state**, recovering its latent heat of condensation. The **Net Heat of Combustion (LHV)** assumes the water remains as **vapour**, with the latent heat *not* recovered. The numerical relationship is: $$ \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} = \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} - \frac{b}{2} \cdot h_{fg} $$ **Fire engineering design uses the Net (LHV) value** because, in real compartment fires, flame temperatures (typically 800–1200 °C) far exceed 100 °C, so water is always produced as vapour and exits with the smoke. Using HHV would overestimate the available energy and lead to **unsafe under-design** of suppression systems. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation, Model 2024 Scheme]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Combustion Thermodynamics of Liquid Hydrocarbons* **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 1, ESE Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between the standard heat of combustion and the standard heats of formation of reactants and products using **Hess's Law**. Clearly state the assumptions and conventions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the relation derived in (a), calculate the **Gross** and **Net** standard heats of combustion of **liquid ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$) at 298 K**, given the following data: $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l) = -277.0 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, g) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ $$ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ State the balanced combustion equation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Step 1 — Consider a generic fuel $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c$ burning in oxygen: $$ \text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c + \left(a + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow a\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{b}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ Step 2 — Construct a Hess's Law cycle. Let State 1 = **elements in standard state**, State 2 = **1 mole of fuel**, State 3 = **combustion products**. Step 3 — The direct path (1 → 3) gives the sum of heats of formation of products: $$ \Delta H_{1\to 3} = a\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + \frac{b}{2}\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}) $$ Step 4 — The two-step path (1 → 2 → 3) gives: $$ \Delta H_{1\to 3} = \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{fuel}) + \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{fuel}) $$ Step 5 — Equating the two expressions: $$ \boxed{\;\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum_j \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{reactants})\;} $$ **Assumptions/Conventions:** Standard state is 298 K and 1 bar; elements in their reference form have $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$; combustion is complete and stoichiometric; products are in their standard states. **(b) Numerical Calculation:** Step 1 — Balanced equation (water initially taken as liquid for gross value): $$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$ Step 2 — Apply Hess's Law (Gross): $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} &= \left[2(-393.5) + 3(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-277.0) + 3(0)\right] \\ &= \left[-787.0 - 857.4\right] - (-277.0) \\ &= -1644.4 + 277.0 \\ &= -1367.4 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — For Net value, replace $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)$ with $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} &= \left[2(-393.5) + 3(-241.8)\right] - (-277.0) \\ &= \left[-787.0 - 725.4\right] + 277.0 \\ &= -1512.4 + 277.0 \\ &= -1235.4 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ Step 4 — Sanity check with the Gross–Net relation (3 moles of water): $$ \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} - \Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} = -1235.4 - (-1367.4) = 132.0 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ Theoretical: $3 \times 44.01 = 132.03$ kJ/mol. ✓ **Final Answer:** $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{gross}} = -1367.4$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H_c^{\circ,\text{net}} = -1235.4$ kJ/mol. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Estimation of Combustion Heat for Solid Fuels* **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 1, ESE Pattern]** **(a)** What is **Dulong's formula**? State it and explain the physical meaning of each term. State two limitations of the formula when applied to modern polymeric fuels. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A furniture warehouse contains **500 kg** of wooden pallets with the following ultimate analysis: $w_C = 0.50$, $w_H = 0.06$, $w_O = 0.43$, $w_S = 0.01$. Using **Dulong's formula**, estimate the **total fire load (in MJ)** contributed by the pallets. Also compute the **net heat of combustion** per kg, assuming the latent-heat correction reduces HHV by $4.18$ MJ per kg of hydrogen content. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Dulong's Formula — Explanation:** Dulong's formula is an empirical correlation used to estimate the **Higher (Gross) Heat of Combustion** of solid carbonaceous fuels (coal, biomass, wood, plastics) from their **ultimate (elemental) analysis** when direct calorimetric data is unavailable. The formula is: $$ \text{HHV} = 33800\, w_C + 144200\left(w_H - \frac{w_O}{8}\right) + 9400\, w_S \quad [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ - **$33800\, w_C$**: Heat released by the complete oxidation of carbon to $\text{CO}_2$ (≈ 32.8 MJ/kg of pure C). - **$144200\left(w_H - \frac{w_O}{8}\right)$**: Heat from hydrogen oxidation, **corrected** for oxygen already bound as $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ within the fuel (the term $\frac{w_O}{8}$ is the hydrogen equivalent of the bound oxygen, assuming 1 O atom ties up 2 H atoms). - **$9400\, w_S$**: Heat released from sulfur oxidation to $\text{SO}_2$ (≈ 9.4 MJ/kg of pure S). **Two Limitations:** 1. **No chlorine or nitrogen correction**: Modern polymers (PVC, polyamides) contain significant Cl or N, whose oxidation products (HCl, $\text{NO}_x$) deviate from the simple S/C/H/O model. Dulong's formula systematically overpredicts HHV for these fuels. 2. **Ash and moisture ignored**: The formula assumes the entire mass is combustible C/H/O/S. High-ash coals (ash > 15%) or high-moisture biomass (moisture > 30%) yield significant overestimation, as non-combustibles absorb heat without contributing. **(b) Numerical Calculation:** Step 1 — Verify that mass fractions sum to 1: $0.50 + 0.06 + 0.43 + 0.01 = 1.00$ ✓. Step 2 — Apply Dulong's formula: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HHV} &= 33800(0.50) + 144200\left(0.06 - \frac{0.43}{8}\right) + 9400(0.01) \\ &= 16900 + 144200\left(0.06 - 0.05375\right) + 94 \\ &= 16900 + 144200(0.00625) + 94 \\ &= 16900 + 901.25 + 94 \\ &= 17895.25 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ &\approx 17.9 \text{ MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Apply latent-heat correction to get the net value: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net heat of combustion} &= \text{HHV} - 4.18 \cdot w_H \\ &= 17895.25 - 4.18 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.06 \quad [\text{converting 4.18 MJ/kg to kJ/kg}] \\ &= 17895.25 - 250.8 \\ &= 17644.45 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 17.64 \text{ MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ Step 4 — Total fire load of 500 kg of pallets: $$ Q_{\text{total}} = m \times \text{NHV} = 500 \times 17.64 = 8820 \text{ MJ} = 8.82 \text{ GJ} $$ **Final Answer:** HHV ≈ 17.9 MJ/kg, NHV ≈ 17.64 MJ/kg, **Total fire load ≈ 8820 MJ**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Heat-of-Combustion Problems:** > 1. **Sign-Convention Confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting the sign convention. Heat of combustion is **exothermic** → $\Delta H_c^\circ < 0$. Some texts quote it as a positive magnitude; always declare which convention you are using. > 2. **Phase of Water Not Specified (–2 marks):** Failing to clarify whether water is **liquid (gross)** or **vapour (net)**. This single omission can change the answer by 100+ kJ/mol for hydrogen-rich fuels. > 3. **Wrong Stoichiometry (–3 marks):** A common error is writing $\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (unbalanced). Always balance the equation **first**; the coefficient of the fuel **must be 1**. > 4. **Forgetting $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2) = 0$** — students sometimes include $\text{O}_2$ with a non-zero formation enthalpy. The standard heat of formation of any element in its reference state is **zero by definition**. > 5. **In Dulong problems, forgetting the $\frac{w_O}{8}$ correction** — this leads to gross overestimation of HHV (typically 20–40% error) for oxygen-rich biomass. > 6. **Unit mismatch** — using kcal in the calculation but reporting kJ in the final answer. **Always show the unit in every intermediate step.** ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Standard Heat of Combustion** is the enthalpy change per mole of fuel for complete combustion at **298 K, 1 bar**, with products in their standard states. - **Sign**: Always **negative** (exothermic). Magnitudes range from ~−240 kJ/mol (hydrogen) to ~−5500 kJ/mol (heavy hydrocarbons). - **Gross (HHV)** vs. **Net (LHV)**: Difference = latent heat of water produced. LHV is the **fire-engineering design value**. - **Hess's Law (the single most-tested formula):** $\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum \nu_{\text{prod}} \Delta H_f^\circ - \sum \nu_{\text{react}} \Delta H_f^\circ$. - **Bomb calorimeter** measures $\Delta U_c$ (constant volume); convert to $\Delta H_c$ using $\Delta n_{\text{gas}}RT$. - **Dulong's formula** is for solid fuels with mass-fraction analysis: HHV = $33800 w_C + 144200(w_H - w_O/8) + 9400 w_S$ kJ/kg. - **Stoichiometric combustion** demands complete oxidation — no CO, no soot. This is the "theoretical maximum" heat release. - **Elemental convention**: $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ for $\text{O}_2(g)$, $\text{N}_2(g)$, $\text{C}(\text{graphite})$, $\text{S}(\text{rhombic})$, $\text{H}_2(g)$. - **Per-mass comparison**: Hydrogen > methane > propane > octane (in MJ/kg). This is why rocket engines use $\text{H}_2$ and **not** gasoline for highest specific impulse. - **Per-mole comparison**: Octane > propane > methane > hydrogen (in MJ/mol). Important for stoichiometric air-supply calculations. - **KTU numerical format**: Always (i) write the balanced equation, (ii) tabulate $\Delta H_f^\circ$ values, (iii) substitute with units, (iv) report answer with **sign and unit (kJ/mol)**.

topics.title: Standard Enthalpy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Standard Enthalpy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Standard Enthalpy** (denoted as $\Delta H^{\circ}$ or $\Delta H^{\ominus}$) is the enthalpy change accompanying a physical or chemical process when the **initial substances and the final substances are in their standard states**, and the reaction is carried out under standard conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard State Convention (IUPAC / KTU Reference):** > - **Temperature:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ (i.e., $25^{\circ}\text{C}$) > - **Pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 1 \, \text{bar}$ (exactly $10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$) > - **Concentration (for solutions):** $c^{\circ} = 1 \, \text{mol/L}$ > - **Physical State:** Pure substance in its most stable form at $T^{\circ}$ and $P^{\circ}$ The general mathematical expression for the standard enthalpy change of any process is: $$\Delta H^{\circ} = H^{\circ}_{\text{final}} - H^{\circ}_{\text{initial}}$$ For a chemical reaction: $$aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD$$ The standard enthalpy of reaction is: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_i \, H^{\circ}_{m,i}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_j \, H^{\circ}_{m,j}(\text{reactants})$$ where $\nu_i$ and $\nu_j$ are the stoichiometric coefficients and $H^{\circ}_{m}$ is the **molar enthalpy in the standard state**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bank Account of Heat" > [!NOTE] > **Think of a fuel molecule as a "Heat Bank Account."** > - When atoms bond together to form a molecule, they **deposit** energy into the bond (energy is released, $\Delta H < 0$, **exothermic** — like depositing money in a bank). > - To **break** a bond, the molecule must **withdraw** energy from the surroundings (energy absorbed, $\Delta H > 0$, **endothermic** — like withdrawing cash). > - The **standard enthalpy of formation** of a fuel tells you the "balance" of energy stored in forming that fuel from its basic elements (in their standard states). A fuel with a **large negative** $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ holds a lot of "locked" chemical energy ready to be released as fire. In the context of **fire propagation**, standard enthalpy values are the **fundamental input data** for predicting: - **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of a fire - **Adiabatic Flame Temperature** - **Fire Growth and Spread Rate** ## 1.3 Classification of Standard Enthalpies in Fire Science The following sub-types of standard enthalpy are critical for combustion and fire dynamics: | Symbol | Name | Definition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}$ | Standard Enthalpy of Formation | Enthalpy change when **1 mole** of a compound is formed from its elements in standard states. | | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{c}$ | Standard Enthalpy of Combustion | Enthalpy change when **1 mole** of a substance undergoes complete combustion with $O_2$ at standard state. | | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r}$ | Standard Enthalpy of Reaction | Enthalpy change for a reaction where all reactants and products are in standard states. | | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{fus}$ | Standard Enthalpy of Fusion | Enthalpy change for melting 1 mole of solid at its melting point. | | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{vap}$ | Standard Enthalpy of Vaporization | Enthalpy change for vaporizing 1 mole of liquid at its boiling point. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Convention:** By IUPAC definition, the standard enthalpy of formation of any **element in its standard state is ZERO** (e.g., $O_2(g)$, $N_2(g)$, $C(\text{graphite})$, $S(\text{rhombic})$ all have $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = 0$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundations Enthalpy $H$ is a **state function** — it depends only on the current state of the system, not on the path taken. This crucial property gives rise to **Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation**, which is the cornerstone of thermochemical calculations. For a system at constant pressure: $$H = U + PV \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta H = Q_{p}$$ where $Q_{p}$ is the heat exchanged at constant pressure (which is precisely the condition in most fire/combustion processes occurring in open atmosphere). ## 2.2 Hess's Law — The Logical Breakdown Hess's Law states that: > *"The total enthalpy change in a chemical reaction is independent of the route taken, provided the initial and final conditions (temperature, pressure, physical state) are the same."* ### Why Hess's Law Works: 1. Enthalpy ($H$) is a **state function** — path-independent. 2. Therefore $\Delta H$ between two states is always the same. 3. This allows us to calculate hard-to-measure enthalpies by adding/subtracting easier-to-measure reactions. ### Standard Enthalpy of Reaction (Generic Formula): $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum_{i} \nu_{i} \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,i}(\text{products}) - \sum_{j} \nu_{j} \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,j}(\text{reactants})}$$ This is the **most important formula** for KTU numerical problems in this module. ## 2.3 Standard Enthalpy of Combustion and Fire Engineering For a hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_y$ undergoing complete combustion: $$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$$ The **heat released per mole of fuel** is $|\Delta H^{\circ}_c|$. In fire engineering, the more practical quantity is the **heat of combustion per unit mass**: $$H_c = \frac{\vert \Delta H^{\circ}_{c} \vert}{M}$$ where $M$ is the molar mass of the fuel in $\text{kg/mol}$, and $H_c$ is in $\text{J/kg}$ or $\text{kJ/kg}$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In fire dynamics, the **effective heat of combustion** ($\Delta H_{eff}$) is often slightly less than $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ because combustion in real fires is rarely 100% complete. Typical values used in KTU-aligned fire engineering problems are $\Delta H_{eff} \approx 0.7 \times \Delta H^{\circ}_c$ for many organic fuels. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table summarizes every formula you must memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic.** | # | Formula / Relation | Description / Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,p} - \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,r}$ | Calculate reaction enthalpy from formation enthalpies | | 2 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,r} - \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,p}$ | Calculate reaction enthalpy from combustion enthalpies | | 3 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{element, std. state}) = 0$ | Reference baseline for all elements | | 4 | $\Delta H = Q_p$ (at constant $P$) | Heat absorbed = enthalpy change | | 5 | $H_c = \dfrac{\vert \Delta H^{\circ}_c \vert}{M}$ | Mass-basis heat of combustion (fire engineering) | | 6 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat equation (for fire temperature rise) | | 7 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{net} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ (Kirchhoff's Law) | Temperature correction of $\Delta H$ | | 8 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{r}(\text{reverse})$ | Sign reversal rule for reverse reactions | | 9 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r}(\text{multiplied by } n) = n \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{r}$ | Scaling rule for stoichiometric coefficients | | 10 | Hess's Law: $\Delta H_{\text{total}} = \sum \Delta H_{\text{steps}}$ | Path-independent heat summation | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Standard enthalpy data is used by fire protection engineers to: - **Size fire suppression systems** (water spray, foam) based on expected heat release. - **Design ventilation** in tunnels, car parks, and buildings to control smoke temperature. - **Predict flame spread rates** over solid fuels using the **Babrauskas correlation** for HRR. - **Conduct calorimetric analysis** (cone calorimeter tests) of materials. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ and $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ **Statement to prove:** $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_{i} \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,i}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_{j} \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,j}(\text{reactants})$$ **Proof using Hess's Law (energy-cycle method):** Consider a generic reaction: $$aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD$$ By definition, the standard enthalpy of formation of each species corresponds to the reaction forming it from its constituent elements. So we can write four formation reactions: $$\text{(i)} \quad aA: \quad a \, (\text{elements of } A) \longrightarrow aA \quad \Delta H^{\circ} = a \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(A)$$ $$\text{(ii)} \quad bB: \quad b \, (\text{elements of } B) \longrightarrow bB \quad \Delta H^{\circ} = b \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(B)$$ $$\text{(iii)} \quad cC: \quad c \, (\text{elements of } C) \longrightarrow cC \quad \Delta H^{\circ} = c \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(C)$$ $$\text{(iv)} \quad dD: \quad d \, (\text{elements of } D) \longrightarrow dD \quad \Delta H^{\circ} = d \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(D)$$ **Step 1 — Build a thermochemical cycle:** We can write the required reaction as a two-step process: - **Path 1 (Direct):** $aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD$, with $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r}$. - **Path 2 (Indirect via elements):** $aA + bB \longrightarrow \text{elements} \longrightarrow cC + dD$. **Step 2 — Quantify Path 2:** The enthalpy change for breaking $aA$ and $bB$ into their elements is: $$\Delta H_{\text{decomposition}} = -a \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(A) - b \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(B)$$ The enthalpy change for forming $cC$ and $dD$ from the same elements is: $$\Delta H_{\text{formation}} = c \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(C) + d \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(D)$$ **Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law:** Since enthalpy is a state function, both paths must yield the same total enthalpy change: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \Delta H_{\text{decomposition}} + \Delta H_{\text{formation}}$$ Substituting: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = -a \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(A) - b \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(B) + c \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(C) + d \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(D)$$ Rearranging: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \left[ c \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(C) + d \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(D) \right] - \left[ a \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(A) + b \, \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(B) \right]$$ In compact notation: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{reactants})$$ This is the **fundamental relation** you will use in nearly every numerical problem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation verified.** The result is dimensionally consistent ($\text{kJ/mol}$) and is path-independent by Hess's Law. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Step-by-Step Solution ### Problem Statement The standard enthalpies of combustion of $C(\text{graphite})$, $H_2(g)$, and $CH_4(g)$ are respectively $-393.5$, $-285.8$, and $-890.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ at $298.15 \, \text{K}$. Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of methane ($CH_4$) using Hess's Law and the thermochemical data given. ### Given Data $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(C, \text{graphite}) = -393.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(H_2, g) = -285.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(CH_4, g) = -890.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ ### Required Find $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_4, g)$, i.e., the enthalpy change for the reaction: $$C(\text{graphite}) + 2H_2(g) \longrightarrow CH_4(g)$$ ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Write the three combustion reactions:** $$\text{(R1)} \quad C(\text{graphite}) + O_2(g) \longrightarrow CO_2(g) \quad \Delta H_1 = -393.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\text{(R2)} \quad H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \longrightarrow H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H_2 = -285.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\text{(R3)} \quad CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \longrightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H_3 = -890.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the target reaction:** The formation of $CH_4$ is: $$\text{(Target)} \quad C(\text{graphite}) + 2H_2(g) \longrightarrow CH_4(g) \quad \Delta H_{f} = ?$$ **Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law — Combine R1, R2, and (reversed) R3:** To obtain the target, we perform: - Keep R1 as is: $C + O_2 \to CO_2$ - Multiply R2 by 2: $2H_2 + O_2 \to 2H_2O$ - **Reverse R3** (so $CH_4$ appears as a product): $CO_2 + 2H_2O \to CH_4 + 2O_2$ **Step 4 — Sum the modified reactions:** Adding (R1) + 2×(R2) + Reverse(R3): $$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS: } & C + O_2 + 2H_2 + O_2 + CO_2 + 2H_2O \\ \text{RHS: } & CO_2 + 2H_2O + 2H_2O + CH_4 + 2O_2 \end{aligned}$$ Cancelling common species on both sides ($O_2$, $CO_2$, $2H_2O$): $$C + 2H_2 \longrightarrow CH_4$$ This is **exactly** the target reaction. ✓ **Step 5 — Sum the corresponding enthalpies:** $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_4) = \Delta H_1 + 2 \cdot \Delta H_2 - \Delta H_3$$ Substituting values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_4) = (-393.5) + 2 \times (-285.8) - (-890.4)$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_4) = -393.5 - 571.6 + 890.4$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_4) = -965.1 + 890.4$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_4, g) = -74.7 \, \text{kJ/mol}}$$ ### Physical Interpretation > [!NOTE] > The **negative** sign indicates that forming methane from its elements is an **exothermic process** — the bonds in $CH_4$ are stronger (more stable) than the separate $C$ and $H$ bonds. This is why methane is such an excellent fuel: it stores approximately **$74.7 \, \text{kJ}$** of chemical potential energy per mole that is rapidly released during combustion, driving the fire propagation process. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Calculation Patterns | Problem Pattern | Strategy | Key Equation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Find $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ from $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ data | Hess's Law: form target by combining combustions | $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{react}) - \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{prod})$ | | Find $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ from $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ data | Direct subtraction of products minus reactants | $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{react})$ | | Find heat released per kg of fuel (fire engineering) | Convert molar to mass basis | $H_c = \vert \Delta H^{\circ}_c \vert \, / \, M$ | | Temperature correction | Kirchhoff's Law | $\Delta H_T = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | | Bond enthalpy estimation | Sum of bond energies broken minus formed | $\Delta H \approx \sum BE(\text{broken}) - \sum BE(\text{formed})$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Thermochemical Cycle — Hess's Law Visualization The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the energy cycle used in Section 3.1 to derive the relationship between $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ and $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart(["Elements in Standard State
C graphite, H2 gas, O2 gas"]) nodeReact["Reactants
aA + bB
a moles of A and b moles of B"] nodeProd["Products
cC + dD
c moles of C and d moles of D"] nodeReverse["Decompose Reactants to Elements
H1 = minus sum of nu_r times dHf react"] nodeForm["Form Products from Elements
H2 = sum of nu_p times dHf prod"] nodeDirect["Direct Reaction Path
dHrxn = H overall"] nodeStart -- "Path A: Decompose" --> nodeReverse nodeReact -- "Reverse Arrow" --> nodeStart nodeStart -- "Path B: Form" --> nodeForm nodeForm -- "Build Up" --> nodeProd nodeReact -- "Direct Single Step" --> nodeDirect nodeDirect -- "Yield" --> nodeProd nodeReverse -. "dH = negative sum of dHf reactants" .-> nodeForm ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** > - **Horizontal axis (Left → Right):** the chemical transformation. > - **Vertical axis (Up → Down):** the thermochemical path. > - The **sum of enthalpies** going via the top path (reactants → elements → products) must equal the direct path (reactants → products) — this is **Hess's Law**. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fire Energy Release Pipeline This diagram shows how **standard enthalpy data flows** through a fire-engineering calculation pipeline to produce actionable fire-protection design parameters. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeInput["THERMOCHEMICAL DATABASE
Tabulated dHf and dHc values
at 298.15 K and 1 bar"] nodeStoich["STOICHIOMETRY MODULE
Balance combustion equation
Determine air fuel ratio"] nodeEnth["ENTHALPY CALCULATOR
Apply Hess Law or direct subtraction
Compute dHrxn in kJ per mol"] nodeConv["UNIT CONVERSION BLOCK
Convert per mole to per kg
Hc equals dHc absolute divided by M"] nodeHRR["HEAT RELEASE RATE
Multiply Hc by mass loss rate
Q dot equals m dot times Hc"] nodeDesign["FIRE PROTECTION DESIGN OUTPUT
Ventilation sizing, suppression agent quantity, structural fire resistance"] nodeInput --> nodeStoich nodeStoich --> nodeEnth nodeEnth --> nodeConv nodeConv --> nodeHRR nodeHRR --> nodeDesign ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** Every block in this pipeline depends on the **accuracy of standard enthalpy data** (the very first block). A small error in $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ propagates through stoichiometry, mass conversion, and HRR calculation — ultimately affecting the safety factor of an entire fire-protection system. ## 4.3 Comparative Enthalpy Levels — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeRef(["REFERENCE ZERO
Pure Elements in Standard State
dHf equals zero"]) nodeForm["STANDARD ENTHALPY OF FORMATION
dHf of compound
Negative for stable compounds"] nodeReact["STANDARD ENTHALPY OF REACTION
dHrxn for any transformation
Calculated from dHf values"] nodeComb["STANDARD ENTHALPY OF COMBUSTION
dHc of fuel with O2
Always large and negative"] nodeFire["FIRE DYNAMICS OUTPUT
Flame temperature, HRR, smoke"] nodeGas["GAS PHASE ENERGETICS
dHvap, dHfus for phase changes"] nodeRef -- "Build molecule" --> nodeForm nodeForm -- "React or rearrange" --> nodeReact nodeReact -- "Burn with O2" --> nodeComb nodeComb -- "Drives" --> nodeFire nodeForm -- "Phase change" --> nodeGas ``` > [!NOTE] > This conceptual map shows the **hierarchical relationship** between different types of standard enthalpies. Notice that all roads lead back to the **elemental reference state** where $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = 0$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **Define the term "Standard Enthalpy of Formation" ($\Delta H^{\circ}_f$). Why is the standard enthalpy of formation of $O_2(g)$ equal to zero?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **standard enthalpy of formation** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_f$) is defined as the enthalpy change accompanying the formation of **one mole** of a compound from its constituent elements, when the compound and all elements are in their **standard states** (i.e., at $T = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ and $P = 1 \, \text{bar}$). For $O_2(g)$: Since $O_2(g)$ is itself the **standard state of the element oxygen**, the reaction "$O_2(g) \to O_2(g)$" involves no chemical change, and hence $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = 0$. This convention provides a **consistent thermodynamic reference baseline** for all formation enthalpies. > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > **State Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation. Mention any two of its applications in fire engineering.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Hess's Law:** *"The total enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction is the same, regardless of the path by which the reaction is carried out, provided the initial and final states (temperature, pressure, and physical states) are the same."* **Two applications in fire engineering:** 1. **Calculating heat of combustion of complex fuels:** Direct experimental measurement of $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ for large hydrocarbons is difficult; Hess's Law allows its computation from tabulated $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ data of products ($CO_2$, $H_2O$). 2. **Predicting adiabatic flame temperature:** Using Hess's Law with Kirchhoff's correction, fire engineers can estimate the maximum theoretical temperature a fuel can reach, which determines material selection for fire-resistant structures. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the relationship between the standard enthalpy of a reaction and the standard enthalpies of formation of reactants and products. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The standard enthalpies of formation of $C_2H_4(g)$, $CO_2(g)$, and $H_2O(l)$ are $+52.3$, $-393.5$, and $-285.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ respectively. Calculate the standard enthalpy of combustion of ethylene ($C_2H_4$) and also determine the heat released when $5.6 \, \text{kg}$ of ethylene is burnt completely at $298.15 \, \text{K}$ and $1 \, \text{bar}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** This is precisely the derivation shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must: - State that enthalpy is a state function. - Write the generic reaction $aA + bB \to cC + dD$. - Construct a Hess's Law cycle via the elements. - Combine formation reactions and apply sign rules. - Arrive at the boxed result: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_{i} \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,i}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_{j} \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,j}(\text{reactants})$$ **Assumption:** All species must be in their standard states at $298.15 \, \text{K}$ and $1 \, \text{bar}$ for the formula to apply directly. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation:** $$C_2H_4(g) + 3O_2(g) \longrightarrow 2CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the standard formula:** $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = \left[ 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CO_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(H_2O) \right] - \left[ 1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(C_2H_4) + 3 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(O_2) \right]$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values** (noting $\Delta H^{\circ}_f(O_2) = 0$): $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = \left[ 2(-393.5) + 2(-285.8) \right] - \left[ (+52.3) + 0 \right]$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = \left[ -787.0 - 571.6 \right] - 52.3$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = -1358.6 - 52.3$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(C_2H_4) = -1410.9 \, \text{kJ/mol}}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate heat released for $5.6 \, \text{kg}$ of ethylene:** Molar mass of $C_2H_4$: $$M = 2(12) + 4(1) = 28 \, \text{g/mol} = 0.028 \, \text{kg/mol}$$ Number of moles: $$n = \frac{5.6 \, \text{kg}}{0.028 \, \text{kg/mol}} = 200 \, \text{mol}$$ Total heat released: $$Q = n \cdot \vert \Delta H^{\circ}_{c} \vert = 200 \times 1410.9$$ $$\boxed{Q = 2.822 \times 10^{5} \, \text{kJ} = 282.18 \, \text{MJ}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a)** Explain the concept of standard enthalpy of combustion. Why is it always negative for organic fuels? Discuss its significance in the context of fire dynamics. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Methanol ($CH_3OH$) has a standard enthalpy of formation of $-238.7 \, \text{kJ/mol}$. Given that $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CO_2, g) = -393.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(H_2O, l) = -285.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$, calculate: - (i) The standard enthalpy of combustion of methanol. - (ii) The mass of methanol that must burn to release $1.0 \times 10^{6} \, \text{kJ}$ of heat. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):** **Standard Enthalpy of Combustion** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_c$) is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of a substance undergoes **complete combustion** with oxygen, with both reactants and products in their standard states at $298.15 \, \text{K}$ and $1 \, \text{bar}$. **Why is it always negative for organic fuels?** - Combustion breaks relatively **weak C–C and C–H bonds** in the fuel and forms very strong **C=O bonds** (in $CO_2$, ~$799 \, \text{kJ/mol}$) and **O–H bonds** (in $H_2O$, ~$463 \, \text{kJ/mol}$). - Since the bonds formed are much stronger than the bonds broken, the excess energy is released to the surroundings. - This makes combustion **highly exothermic**, hence $\Delta H^{\circ}_c < 0$ always. **Significance in Fire Dynamics:** - It directly gives the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** when multiplied by mass loss rate, governing fire growth, flashover, and smoke production. - Critical for **fire load calculations** in buildings. - Used in the design of **fire suppression systems** (water, foam, $CO_2$). **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation:** $$CH_3OH(l) + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2(g) \longrightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$ **Step 2 — (i) Calculate $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$:** $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = \left[ 1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CO_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(H_2O) \right] - \left[ 1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(CH_3OH) + \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot 0 \right]$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = \left[ (-393.5) + 2(-285.8) \right] - (-238.7)$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = \left[ -393.5 - 571.6 \right] + 238.7$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{c} = -965.1 + 238.7$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(CH_3OH) = -726.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}}$$ **Step 3 — (ii) Calculate the mass of methanol required:** Molar mass of $CH_3OH$: $$M = 12 + 4(1) + 16 = 32 \, \text{g/mol} = 0.032 \, \text{kg/mol}$$ Heat released per mole of methanol = $726.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}$. Moles required to release $1.0 \times 10^{6} \, \text{kJ}$: $$n = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{6} \, \text{kJ}}{726.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}} = 1376.7 \, \text{mol}$$ Mass required: $$m = n \cdot M = 1376.7 \times 0.032$$ $$\boxed{m = 44.05 \, \text{kg}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to set $\Delta H^{\circ}_f(O_2) = 0$** → 1 mark lost. > 2. **Wrong sign convention** in the formula "products minus reactants." Some students do "reactants minus products" and report an incorrect (positive) answer. > 3. **Unit mismatch:** reporting heat in J instead of kJ, or per gram instead of per mole. > 4. **Stoichiometric coefficient error:** forgetting to multiply $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of $H_2O$ by 2 in the combustion of methane/methanol. > 5. **In Part (a) derivations, skipping Hess's Law statement** — the examiner expects it as the first line. > 6. **Confusing molar mass units:** $M$ in kg/mol vs. g/mol — this propagates to the final mass. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Standard Enthalpy** - **Standard state** is defined as $T = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ and $P = 1 \, \text{bar}$, with species in their **most stable physical form**. - **Standard enthalpy of formation** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_f$) = enthalpy change for forming **1 mole** of compound from its elements in standard states. - **For any element in its standard state, $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = 0$** — this is the reference baseline. - **Standard enthalpy of combustion** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_c$) = enthalpy change for **complete combustion of 1 mole** of substance with $O_2$. Always **negative** for organic fuels. - **Hess's Law:** $\Delta H$ is path-independent because enthalpy is a **state function**. - **Master formula #1:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,p} - \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,r}$ - **Master formula #2:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,r} - \sum \nu_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,p}$ - **Reverse reaction rule:** $\Delta H_{\text{reverse}} = -\Delta H_{\text{forward}}$. - **Scaling rule:** Multiplying a reaction by $n$ multiplies $\Delta H$ by $n$. - **Fire engineering conversion:** $H_c = \vert \Delta H^{\circ}_c \vert / M$ gives **heat released per unit mass** (used for HRR and suppression design). - **Kirchhoff's Law** (for non-298 K): $\Delta H_T = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$. - **Sign discipline:** Always label whether your answer is for formation, combustion, or reaction — examiners **deduct marks** for ambiguity. - **Always write units** (kJ/mol, J/kg, etc.) — a numerical answer without units is considered **incomplete** in KTU valuation. > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip:** When solving KTU problems, draw the **Hess's Law cycle diagram first**, label all $\Delta H$ values, and then apply algebraic combination. This structured approach matches the **valuation key pattern** and almost guarantees full marks on the 7-mark sub-parts.

topics.title: Adiabatic Flame Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Flame Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** is defined as the temperature attained by the products of a combustion process when:
1. The process is **adiabatic** (no heat is lost or gained from the control volume, i.e., $\dot{Q} = 0$).
2. Combustion is **complete** (unless dissociation is explicitly considered).
3. The system operates at **steady state**.
4. The reference enthalpy state is fixed (typically **298.15 K** and **1 atm**).

Mathematically, the AFT ($T_{ad}$) is found by solving the steady-flow energy equation (First Law of Thermodynamics) for the products:

$$\sum_{products} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i (T_{ad}) \right] = \sum_{reactants} n_j \left[ h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \Delta h_j (T_{in}) \right]$$

where:
- $n_i$ = moles of product species $i$
- $h_{f,i}^{\circ}$ = standard enthalpy of formation of species $i$ at **298.15 K**
- $\Delta h_i(T)$ = sensible enthalpy change of species $i$ from 298.15 K to temperature $T$
- $T_{in}$ = initial temperature of reactants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Adiabatic Flame Temperature is the **theoretical upper limit** of the temperature achievable in a combustion process. In real fires, actual flame temperatures are **always lower** due to radiative heat losses, incomplete combustion, and dissociation of products.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sealed Pressure Cooker"

Imagine a perfectly insulated pressure cooker (adiabatic walls) placed on a powerful gas stove. Inside, you have a stoichiometric mixture of methane and oxygen. The moment combustion begins, the cooker's insulation **forbids any heat from escaping**. All the chemical energy released by the burning methane has nowhere to go except into heating the resulting hot gases (CO₂ and H₂O vapor) inside the cooker.

As more fuel burns, the temperature of the trapped gases skyrockets until all the fuel is consumed. The **final, peak temperature** of the gases inside this imaginary sealed cooker is the **Adiabatic Flame Temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** Think of AFT as a "best-case scenario" temperature. It represents what combustion *could* achieve if nature were perfectly efficient at retaining all the released heat within the reaction products. In fire safety engineering, AFT is used as a conservative reference for evaluating material ignition thresholds, structural fire resistance, and explosion severity.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Conditions

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference Temperature $T_{ref}$ | **298.15 K** (25 °C) | Universal thermodynamic baseline |
| Reference Pressure $P_{ref}$ | **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** | Standard atmospheric pressure |
| Universal Gas Constant $R_u$ | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** | Used in ideal gas relations |
| Combustion Mode | Complete (stoichiometric) | Unless fuel-lean/rich specified |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Reasoning Behind AFT

The calculation of AFT is fundamentally an **enthalpy balance** exercise. The First Law of Thermodynamics for a steady-flow, adiabatic, open system with no work (other than flow work) reduces to:

$$H_{reactants} = H_{products}$$

The total enthalpy of reactants is the sum of their **sensible enthalpy** (heat content due to being above reference temperature) plus their **chemical enthalpy** (enthalpy of formation, which is zero for elements in their standard states). When combustion occurs, the chemical energy locked in the fuel is released, but for an adiabatic system, this energy is not lost — it is absorbed entirely by the product gases, raising their temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the temperature stop rising?** It stops when the **total sensible enthalpy gained by the products** exactly equals the **heat of combustion released** by the reaction. Beyond this point, there is no more chemical energy to convert into thermal energy of the products.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The systematic approach to computing AFT involves the following operational steps:

- **Step 1: Write the balanced stoichiometric equation** for complete combustion of the fuel with the chosen oxidizer (typically O₂ from air).
- **Step 2: Introduce the equivalence ratio $\phi$** if non-stoichiometric conditions are being analyzed:
$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$$

  - $\phi = 1$ → Stoichiometric
  - $\phi < 1$ → Fuel-lean (excess air)
  - $\phi > 1$ → Fuel-rich (excess fuel)

- **Step 3: Determine the product composition** including any excess oxidizer or unburned fuel.
- **Step 4: Set up the enthalpy balance equation** equating total reactant enthalpy (at $T_{in}$) to total product enthalpy (at $T_{ad}$).
- **Step 5: Solve iteratively** (or using tabulated enthalpy data / NASA polynomials) for the unknown $T_{ad}$ because $\Delta h_i(T_{ad})$ is generally a nonlinear function of temperature.
- **Step 6: Account for dissociation** at very high temperatures (above ~2000 K) by including equilibrium reactions such as:
$$\text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2, \quad \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2, \quad \text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{H}, \quad \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{O}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Governing Equation | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Enthalpy Balance (Adiabatic) | $H_{R}(T_{in}) = H_{P}(T_{ad})$ | Energy conservation, basis: per kmol of fuel |
| Sensible Enthalpy (Ideal Gas) | $\Delta h_i(T) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_{p,i}(T)\, dT$ | Units: kJ/kmol |
| Molar Enthalpy Form | $h_i(T) = h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T)$ | $h_{f}^{\circ}$ at 298.15 K |
| Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Adiabatic Combustion Energy Balance | $\sum_{P} n_i \, u_i(T_{ad}) = \sum_{R} n_j \, u_j(T_{ref}) + Q_{combustion}$ | $u$ = internal energy (for constant $V$) |
| Constant-Volume AFT (CV-AFT) | $U_{R}(T_{in}) = U_{P}(T_{ad,CV})$ | Closed system, no flow work |
| Constant-Pressure AFT (CP-AFT) | $H_{R}(T_{in}) = H_{P}(T_{ad,CP})$ | Open system / flow reactor |
| Dissociation Correction | $\text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ | Reduces AFT above 2000 K |
| Relation Between CP-AFT and CV-AFT | $T_{ad,CV} \geq T_{ad,CP}$ for same reactants | Because $H = U + PV$ and $P\Delta V > 0$ for combustion |
| Average $c_p$ Method (Approximate) | $T_{ad} = T_{in} + \dfrac{Q_{comb}}{\sum n_i \, \bar{c}_{p,i}}$ | Approximate, assumes constant $\bar{c}_p$ |
| Air Composition (Mole Basis) | O₂ : 3.76 N₂ | 1 kmol O₂ + 3.76 kmol N₂ = 4.76 kmol air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Character Substitution Rule:** In all rendered math above, notice that absolute-value bars such as $\vert x \vert$ have been written using `\vert` to avoid breaking KTU's markdown-table rendering pipeline. Always substitute plain `$\vert$` for `$\mid$` if needed; never use the raw `|` inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The AFT concept is indispensable in:

- **Fire Dynamics & Safety Engineering:** Estimating the thermal severity of compartment fires; used in standard fire curves (e.g., ASTM E119, ISO 834) and in **compartment fire modeling** (zone models, CFD codes like FDS).
- **Gas Turbine & Rocket Engine Design:** Predicting maximum turbine-inlet temperatures and combustion-chamber wall heat fluxes.
- **Explosion Analysis:** AFT sets the upper bound for the **constant-volume explosion temperature** in deflagration calculations, used in vent-area design for explosion relief.
- **Pollutant Formation Modeling:** Thermal NOₓ formation is exponentially sensitive to peak flame temperature (Zeldovich mechanism).
- **Process Safety:** Predicting runaway reaction temperatures in chemical reactors and storage vessels (adiabatic decomposition calorimetry).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Adiabatic Flame Temperature Equation (Constant-Pressure Case)

We begin with the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** for a control volume enclosing the combustion zone. For an adiabatic, steady-flow system with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes and no shaft work:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{shaft} = \sum_{out} \dot{m}_i \left( h_i + \frac{V_i^2}{2} + g z_i \right) - \sum_{in} \dot{m}_j \left( h_j + \frac{V_j^2}{2} + g z_j \right)$$

Applying the conditions $\dot{Q} = 0$, $\dot{W}_{shaft} = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$:

$$0 = \dot{H}_{products} - \dot{H}_{reactants}$$

Therefore, on a per-mole basis:

$$H_{reactants}(T_{in}) = H_{products}(T_{ad})$$

Expanding the total enthalpy as the sum of formation enthalpy plus sensible enthalpy:

$$\sum_{R} n_j \left[ h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \Delta h_j(T_{in}) \right] = \sum_{P} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T_{ad}) \right]$$

Rearranging to isolate the unknown $T_{ad}$:

$$\sum_{P} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T_{ad}) \right] - \sum_{R} n_j \left[ h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \Delta h_j(T_{in}) \right] = 0$$

Since most combustion calculations start with $T_{in} = 298.15$ K, the sensible enthalpy of the reactants vanishes ($\Delta h_j = 0$), simplifying to:

$$\sum_{P} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T_{ad}) \right] = \sum_{R} n_j \, h_{f,j}^{\circ}$$

This is the **canonical form** of the AFT equation evaluated per kmol of fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** The derivation traces a clean line: *First Law* → *Steady-state SFEE* → *Adiabatic + no-work assumptions* → *Enthalpy equality* → *Decompose into formation + sensible* → *Cancel initial sensible enthalpy* → *Final implicit equation for $T_{ad}$*.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Stoichiometric Combustion of Methane

**Problem Statement:** Determine the adiabatic flame temperature (constant pressure) of a stoichiometric mixture of methane (CH₄) and air, both initially at 298.15 K and 1 atm. Assume complete combustion with no dissociation (frozen chemistry).

**Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate standard enthalpies of formation $h_{f}^{\circ}$ (kJ/kmol):**

| Species | $h_{f}^{\circ}$ (kJ/kmol) |
| :--- | :--- |
| CH₄ (g) | $-74{,}850$ |
| O₂ | $0$ |
| N₂ | $0$ |
| CO₂ | $-393{,}520$ |
| H₂O (g) | $-241{,}820$ |

**Step 3 — Compute the heat of combustion at standard conditions:**

The standard heat of combustion is:

$$\Delta H_c^{\circ} = \sum_{P} n_i h_{f,i}^{\circ} - \sum_{R} n_j h_{f,j}^{\circ}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta H_c^{\circ} = \left[ (-393{,}520) + 2(-241{,}820) \right] - \left[ (-74{,}850) + 0 + 0 \right]$$

$$\Delta H_c^{\circ} = (-393{,}520 - 483{,}640) - (-74{,}850)$$

$$\Delta H_c^{\circ} = -877{,}160 + 74{,}850 = -802{,}310 \text{ kJ/kmol fuel}$$

The negative sign indicates an **exothermic** reaction. The **heat released** is **802,310 kJ per kmol of CH₄**.

**Step 4 — Set up the enthalpy balance for $T_{ad}$:**

Since $T_{in} = 298.15$ K, reactant sensible enthalpies are zero. The energy balance becomes:

$$\Delta h_{\text{CO}_2}(T_{ad}) + 2 \, \Delta h_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}(T_{ad}) + 7.52 \, \Delta h_{\text{N}_2}(T_{ad}) = 802{,}310 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Iterative solution using tabulated enthalpy data:**

Using ideal-gas sensible enthalpy data (relative to 298.15 K), we iterate on $T_{ad}$:

| $T_{ad}$ (K) | $\Delta h_{\text{CO}_2}$ (kJ/kmol) | $\Delta h_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ (kJ/kmol) | $\Delta h_{\text{N}_2}$ (kJ/kmol) | Sum (kJ) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1500 | 71,460 | 47,940 | 36,920 | 514,160 |
| 1800 | 91,440 | 61,830 | 46,150 | 654,610 |
| 2000 | 105,200 | 71,400 | 52,400 | 755,200 |
| 2200 | 119,470 | 81,470 | 58,830 | 855,150 |
| 2222 | 121,070 | 82,580 | 59,640 | 863,500 |

We need the sum to equal **802,310 kJ**. Linear interpolation between 2000 K (755,200 kJ) and 2200 K (855,150 kJ):

$$T_{ad} \approx 2000 + 200 \times \frac{802{,}310 - 755{,}200}{855{,}150 - 755{,}200}$$

$$T_{ad} \approx 2000 + 200 \times \frac{47{,}110}{99{,}950} \approx 2000 + 94.2$$

$$\boxed{T_{ad} \approx 2094 \text{ K} \approx 1821 \text{ °C}}$$

This agrees well with the standard textbook value (~2222 K from more accurate NASA polynomial data, or ~2090 K with simpler tabulated data). The discrepancy underscores why **iterative solver accuracy** and **data source** are critical in KTU board answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **What if we include dissociation?** At this temperature (~2094 K), some CO₂ and H₂O begin to dissociate. The Zeldovich-like endothermic dissociation absorbs a portion of the released energy, reducing the actual AFT by approximately **50–150 K** depending on pressure. At 1 atm, dissociation is moderate; at high pressure (e.g., rocket chambers at 100 atm), dissociation is suppressed and AFT rises.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Key Parameters on AFT

| Parameter | Effect on AFT | Quantitative Trend | Physical Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Increasing $\phi$ (toward rich) from $\phi = 1$ | **Decreases** | $T_{ad, \phi=1.5} \approx 1500$–$1700$ K | Excess unburned fuel absorbs heat without contributing combustion energy |
| Decreasing $\phi$ (toward lean) from $\phi = 1$ | **Decreases** | $T_{ad, \phi=0.5} \approx 1700$–$1900$ K | Excess air acts as a thermal sink, diluting the products |
| Increasing $T_{in}$ of reactants | **Increases** | Roughly $T_{ad} \approx T_{ad,std} + (T_{in} - 298)$ | Preheated reactants already carry sensible enthalpy into the balance |
| Constant-Volume vs Constant-Pressure | CV-AFT > CP-AFT | $\Delta T \approx 50$–$200$ K | No flow-work loss in CV; all combustion energy stays as internal energy |
| Including dissociation (T > 2000 K) | **Decreases** AFT by 50–300 K | At 1 atm, ~50–100 K correction | Endothermic reverse reactions absorb heat |
| Increasing pressure | **Slightly increases** AFT | Higher $P$ suppresses dissociation | Le Chatelier's principle shifts equilibrium toward products |
| Higher H/C ratio in fuel | **Increases** AFT | H₂ ~2400 K, CH₄ ~2094 K, C₈H₁₈ ~2280 K | More H₂O produced (high $c_p$ benefit) per mole O₂ |
| Presence of inert diluents (N₂, CO₂) | **Decreases** AFT | Air-AFT < pure-O₂-AFT by ~700 K | Inerts absorb heat without participating in reaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** When asked "How does excess air affect AFT?" a complete answer must state (i) the direction (decrease), (ii) the physical reason (thermal dilution), and (iii) reference to the principle of energy partitioning — not just a one-line "it decreases." Examiners award partial credit for each of the three elements.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Methodological Flowchart — Calculation of AFT

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **operational decision flow** that a fire engineer or student must follow when computing the AFT for a given fuel-oxidizer system. It captures the iterative thermodynamic logic step-by-step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA0([Start: Fuel and Oxidizer Identified]) --> stepA1
    stepA1[Step 1: Define Reactant Composition]
    stepA1 --> stepA2{Step 2: Stoichiometric or Non-Stoichiometric}
    stepA2 -->|Stoichiometric phi = 1| stepA3a[Compute Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio]
    stepA2 -->|Non-Stoichiometric| stepA3b[Specify Equivalence Ratio phi]
    stepA3a --> stepA4
    stepA3b --> stepA4
    stepA4[Step 3: Write Balanced Combustion Equation]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Step 4: Determine Product Mole Fractions]
    stepA5 --> stepA6{Temperature Regime}
    stepA6 -->|T less than 2000 K| stepA7a[Use Frozen Chemistry Assumption]
    stepA6 -->|T greater than 2000 K| stepA7b[Include Dissociation Equilibrium]
    stepA7a --> stepA8[Step 5: Set Up H balance: H_R = H_P]
    stepA7b --> stepA8
    stepA8 --> stepA9[Step 6: Apply Tabulated Enthalpy Data or NASA Polynomials]
    stepA9 --> stepA10[Step 7: Solve Iteratively for T_ad]
    stepA10 --> stepA11{Convergence Check}
    stepA11 -->|Converged| stepA12[Report T_ad and Product Composition]
    stepA11 -->|Not Converged| stepA10
    stepA12 --> stepA13([End: Adiabatic Flame Temperature Computed])
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — The Three Energy Reservoirs of Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockR[Block R: Reactant Reservoir at T_in]
    blockC[Block C: Chemical Energy Release at T_ref]
    blockP[Block P: Product Reservoir at T_ad]
    blockR -- "Sensible Enthalpy" --> blockC
    blockC -- "Heat of Combustion minus Dissociation Losses" --> blockP
    blockR -- "Initial Chemical Energy" --> blockC
    blockC -- "Sensible Enthalpy of Products" --> blockP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Block R represents the **incoming enthalpy** of unreacted fuel and oxidizer at the inlet temperature. Block C captures the **chemical-to-thermal conversion** — this is where the energy balance ties together the standard heats of formation and any dissociation energy losses. Block P represents the **final state** of the combustion products, whose temperature $T_{ad}$ is the quantity being solved. The arrows depict the energy fluxes that the AFT calculation reconciles into a single scalar temperature.

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison Diagram — CP-AFT vs CV-AFT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepB1([Reactants at T_in]) --> stepB2{Process Path}
    stepB2 -->|Constant Pressure Open System| stepB3[CP-AFT: H_R equals H_P]
    stepB2 -->|Constant Volume Closed System| stepB4[CV-AFT: U_R equals U_P]
    stepB3 --> stepB5[Flow Work P times Delta V is Performed]
    stepB4 --> stepB6[No Flow Work, All Energy Retained Internally]
    stepB5 --> stepB7[Lower T_ad due to flow work loss]
    stepB6 --> stepB8[Higher T_ad because no flow work]
    stepB7 --> stepB9([Final T_ad CP-AFT])
    stepB8 --> stepB10([Final T_ad CV-AFT])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define the term *Adiabatic Flame Temperature*. Mention the assumptions made in its theoretical calculation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** is the temperature attained by the products of a combustion process when the entire enthalpy released by the reaction is utilized to raise the temperature of the products, with **no heat loss** to the surroundings ($\dot{Q} = 0$).

The principal assumptions are:
1. The combustion chamber is **perfectly insulated** (adiabatic walls), so no heat escapes.
2. Combustion is **complete**; there is no unburned fuel or oxidizer (unless dissociation is modeled).
3. The process is at **steady state**.
4. All gases behave as **ideal gases**.
5. Reference temperature is **298.15 K** and pressure is **1 atm**.

*(Examiner's Note: Each assumption carries ~0.5 mark; complete definition ~0.5 mark.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q2. Why is the actual flame temperature in a fire always lower than the adiabatic flame temperature? List any four reasons.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The actual flame temperature is always lower than the theoretical AFT due to the following real-world irreversibilities:
1. **Radiative heat losses** from the flame to surrounding surfaces and gases.
2. **Convective heat losses** to the combustor walls and entrained cold air.
3. **Incomplete combustion**, leaving behind unburned CO, soot, or hydrocarbons.
4. **Dissociation of products** (CO₂ → CO + ½ O₂; H₂O → H₂ + ½ O₂) at high temperatures, which absorbs energy.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 0.75 mark per correctly stated reason. The question demands reasoning, not just listing.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model ESE Question, 14 Marks, with Choice]**

### Question A: (a) Derive the energy balance equation used for determining the adiabatic flame temperature under constant-pressure conditions. State clearly the assumptions and the role of sensible vs chemical enthalpy. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the First Law for an Open System:**

For a steady-flow control volume enclosing the combustion zone:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \left( h_i + \frac{V_i^2}{2} + g z_i \right) = \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \left( h_e + \frac{V_e^2}{2} + g z_e \right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Adiabatic Flame Assumptions:**

- $\dot{Q} = 0$ (adiabatic)
- $\dot{W}_{cv} = 0$ (no shaft work)
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$

This collapses the SFEE to:

$$\sum_{in} \dot{m}_i h_i = \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e h_e$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a Molar Basis (per kmol of fuel):**

$$H_{R}(T_{in}) = H_{P}(T_{ad})$$

where $H_{R}$ and $H_{P}$ are total molar enthalpies of reactants and products.

**Step 4 — Decompose Each Total Enthalpy:**

Each species' molar enthalpy is split into a **chemical (formation) part** at 298.15 K and a **sensible part** representing heating from 298.15 K to the relevant temperature:

$$H_{R} = \sum_{R} n_j \left[ h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \Delta h_j(T_{in}) \right]$$

$$H_{P} = \sum_{P} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T_{ad}) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Set Up the Final Energy Balance:**

$$\sum_{P} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T_{ad}) \right] = \sum_{R} n_j \left[ h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \Delta h_j(T_{in}) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Simplify for Standard Inlet Conditions:**

When $T_{in} = 298.15$ K, $\Delta h_j(T_{in}) = 0$, and the equation reduces to:

$$\sum_{P} n_i \left[ h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \Delta h_i(T_{ad}) \right] = \sum_{R} n_j \, h_{f,j}^{\circ}$$

This is the **canonical AFT equation**. The **chemical enthalpy** (heats of formation) acts as the energy source, while the **sensible enthalpy** of products absorbs that energy and converts it into the temperature rise captured by $T_{ad}$.
