|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Moisture Content (as-received) | $M = \dfrac{(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1} \times 100$ | As-received | % |
| Moisture Content (air-dried) | $M_{ad} = \dfrac{(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1} \times 100$ | Air-dried | % |
| Volatile Matter | $VM = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_3)}{W_2} \times 100$ | Dry basis | % |
| Ash Content | $A = \dfrac{W_4}{W_1} \times 100$ | As-received | % |
| Fixed Carbon | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | As-received | % |
| Moisture (dry basis) | $M_d = \dfrac{M}{100 - M} \times 100$ | Dry conversion | % |
| Volatile Matter (dry basis) | $VM_d = VM \times \dfrac{100}{100 - M}$ | Dry conversion | % |
| Fuel Ratio | $FR = \dfrac{FC}{VM}$ | Ratio | dimensionless |
| Calorific Value (approx., Goutal's) | $CV = 82C + aV$ | MJ/kg | empirical |

Where:
- $W_1$ = initial mass of air-dried sample (g)
- $W_2$ = mass after oven drying at 110 °C (g)
- $W_3$ = mass after volatile matter test at 950 °C (g)
- $W_4$ = final mass of ash (g)
- $C$ = Fixed Carbon %
- $V$ = Volatile Matter %
- $a$ = empirical constant (≈ 0.146)

## 2.4 Basis Conversions — A Critical Engineering Skill

Solid fuels can be reported on three different bases. **KTU questions frequently test conversion:**

1. **As-Received (ar) Basis** — Includes total moisture
2. **Air-Dried (ad) Basis** — Includes residual moisture
3. **Dry Basis (db)** — Moisture-free
4. **Dry Ash-Free (daf) Basis** — Pure organic matter only

**Conversion factor from "as-received" to "dry basis":**

$$
\text{Result (db)} = \text{Result (ar)} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{ar}}
$$

**Conversion to "dry ash-free" basis:**

$$
\text{Result (daf)} = \text{Result (db)} \times \frac{100}{100 - A_{db}}
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Load Estimation in Buildings:** $FC \text{ and } VM$ values feed into the **Fire Load Density** calculation in kJ/m² (as per IS 875 / NBC 2016).
- **Cement & Power Plant Industries:** Predicts boiler fouling and clinker formation.
- **Fire Investigation:** Forensic fire investigators use proximate analysis to identify **accelerants** vs. natural fuel behavior at a fire scene.
- **Wildland Fire Behavior:** Forestry services use VM content to predict **flame length and rate of spread** in forest fires.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Fixed Carbon Formula

Starting from the principle that all four fractions of a solid fuel, on an as-received basis, sum to 100% of the total mass:

**Step 1:** Write the conservation of mass equation.

$$
M + VM + A + FC = 100
$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the Fixed Carbon term by moving all other fractions to the right-hand side.

$$
FC = 100 - M - VM - A
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the measured/expressed values. Suppose $M = 5\%$, $VM = 28\%$, $A = 12\%$.

$$
FC = 100 - 5 - 28 - 12
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the arithmetic sum step by step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 + 28 &= 33 \\
33 + 12 &= 45 \\
100 - 45 &= 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$
FC = 55\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This derivation is valid only on a *consistent basis*. If $M$ is on an as-received basis, then $VM$ and $A$ must also be expressed on the same basis. KTU examiners often deduct marks for inconsistent basis usage.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Dry-Basis Conversion

**Step 1:** Start with the as-received value, e.g., Volatile Matter $(VM)_{ar} = 28\%$.

**Step 2:** Recognize that the "as-received" basis contains moisture, while the "dry basis" excludes it. The dry basis amplifies the percentages of all other fractions.

**Step 3:** Apply the standard conversion factor.

$$
(VM)_{db} = (VM)_{ar} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{ar}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values. Let $M_{ar} = 5\%$.

$$
(VM)_{db} = 28 \times \frac{100}{100 - 5}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator first.

$$
100 - 5 = 95
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the ratio.

$$
\frac{100}{95} = 1.0526
$$

**Step 7:** Multiply through.

$$
(VM)_{db} = 28 \times 1.0526 = 29.47\%
$$

**Step 8:** Final result (rounded to two decimals).

$$
(VM)_{db} \approx 29.47\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The dry-basis value (29.47%) is **greater** than the as-received value (28%) because we removed the inert moisture from the denominator, effectively "concentrating" the volatile matter percentage. This is expected behavior.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Full Proximate Analysis

**Problem:** A 1.200 g sample of bituminous coal is subjected to proximate analysis with the following observations:
- Mass after oven drying at 110 °C: $W_2 = 1.140$ g
- Mass after VM test at 950 °C: $W_3 = 0.876$ g
- Mass of ash residue: $W_4 = 0.096$ g

**Determine:** $M$, $VM$, $A$, $FC$ (as-received basis), and the Fuel Ratio.

### Step A: Moisture Content
$$
M = \frac{(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{(1.200 - 1.140)}{1.200} \times 100
$$

$$
\frac{0.060}{1.200} = 0.0500
$$

$$
M = 0.0500 \times 100 = 5.00\%
$$

### Step B: Volatile Matter (on dry basis first, then convert)
On dry basis using $W_2 = 1.140$ g:

$$
VM_{db} = \frac{(W_2 - W_3)}{W_2} \times 100 = \frac{(1.140 - 0.876)}{1.140} \times 100
$$

$$
\frac{0.264}{1.140} = 0.2316
$$

$$
VM_{db} = 23.16\%
$$

Converting to as-received basis:

$$
VM_{ar} = VM_{db} \times \frac{100 - M}{100} = 23.16 \times \frac{95}{100}
$$

$$
VM_{ar} = 23.16 \times 0.95 = 22.00\%
$$

### Step C: Ash Content
$$
A = \frac{W_4}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.096}{1.200} \times 100
$$

$$
\frac{0.096}{1.200} = 0.0800
$$

$$
A = 8.00\%
$$

### Step D: Fixed Carbon (as-received basis)
$$
FC = 100 - M - VM - A = 100 - 5.00 - 22.00 - 8.00
$$

$$
FC = 65.00\%
$$

### Step E: Fuel Ratio
$$
FR = \frac{FC}{VM} = \frac{65.00}{22.00} = 2.955
$$

$$
FR \approx 2.96
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A fuel ratio of ~2.96 indicates a **bituminous coal** with moderate-to-long burning duration. Anthracite has $FR > 10$; lignite has $FR < 1.5$.

## 3.4 Summary Results Table

| Parameter | Value | Basis |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Moisture | **5.00 %** | as-received |
| Volatile Matter | **22.00 %** | as-received |
| Ash | **8.00 %** | as-received |
| Fixed Carbon | **65.00 %** | as-received |
| **Sum check** | 100.00 % | ✓ |
| Fuel Ratio | **2.96** | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Proximate Analysis Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Solid Fuel Sample] --> B[Weigh W1 on Analytical Balance]
    B --> C[Oven Drying at 110 deg C for 1 hr]
    C --> D[Cool in Desiccator 30 min]
    D --> E[Weigh W2]
    E --> F[VM Test in Lidded Crucible at 950 deg C for 7 min]
    F --> G[Cool in Desiccator]
    G --> H[Weigh W3]
    H --> I[Ash Test in Open Crucible at 815 deg C to Constant Mass]
    I --> J[Cool and Weigh W4]
    J --> K[Compute M VM A FC]
    K --> L[Report as Received Basis]
    L --> M[Convert to Dry Basis if Required]
    M --> N[Final Proximate Analysis Report]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: How Fractions Interact in Fire Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Moisture M] -->|Absorbs Latent Heat| X1[Delays Ignition]
    VM[Volatile Matter VM] -->|Vaporizes First| X2[Drives Flaming Combustion]
    A[Ash A] -->|Inert Residue| X3[Reduces Combustion Efficiency]
    FC[Fixed Carbon FC] -->|Glowing Oxidation| X4[Drives Char Combustion]
    X2 --> Y[Flame Spread and Fire Propagation]
    X4 --> Y
    X1 --> Y
    X3 --> Y
    Y --> Z[Net Fire Behavior Outcome]
```

## 4.3 Crucible Configuration Schematic (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VM_Test[Volatile Matter Test Configuration]
        L[Lid Restricting Air Inflow] --> S1[Sample Bed in Crucible]
        S1 --> H[Heat Source 950 deg C from Muffle Furnace]
        S1 --> G1[Volatile Gases Escape Through Lid Gap]
    end

    subgraph Ash_Test[Ash Test Configuration]
        O[Open Crucible No Lid] --> S2[Sample Bed in Crucible]
        S2 --> H2[Heat Source 815 deg C from Muffle Furnace]
        S2 --> R[Mineral Ash Residue Remains]
    end
```

## 4.4 Inter-Fraction Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Total Fuel Mass 100 percent] --> M1[Moisture]
    Start --> VM1[Volatile Matter]
    Start --> A1[Ash]
    Start --> FC1[Fixed Carbon]
    M1 --> Add[Sum Check]
    VM1 --> Add
    A1 --> Add
    FC1 --> Add
    Add --> Val[Must Equal 100 percent]
    Val -->|Yes| OK[Valid Proximate Analysis]
    Val -->|No| Err[Recheck Calculations]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Proximate Analysis of solid fuel. List its four constituents.**

**Model Answer:**
Proximate Analysis is an empirical procedure used to determine the physical and thermal constituents of a solid fuel under controlled laboratory conditions.

The four constituents are:
1. **Moisture Content (M)** — water in the fuel
2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** — combustible gases/vapors released on heating
3. **Ash Content (A)** — non-combustible mineral residue
4. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — residual combustible solid carbon

These sum to 100% on a consistent mass basis.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Proximate and Ultimate Analysis of solid fuels.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
|---------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Purpose | Determines operational fractions | Determines elemental composition |
| Fractions/elements | Moisture, VM, Ash, FC | Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Sulphur, Oxygen |
| Method | Gravimetric (mass loss on heating) | Combustion + gas chromatography |
| Use | Fire behavior prediction | Stoichiometric air calculation |
| Apparatus | Muffle furnace, oven, balance | CHNS analyzer, calorimeter |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** A sample of coal weighing 1.500 g was subjected to proximate analysis. After oven drying, the mass was 1.395 g. After volatile matter test, the mass was 1.080 g. The ash residue weighed 0.135 g. Calculate the **Moisture, Volatile Matter, Ash, and Fixed Carbon** on as-received basis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the significance of **Fuel Ratio** in classifying solid fuels. Calculate the fuel ratio for the above sample. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Given:** $W_1 = 1.500$ g, $W_2 = 1.395$ g, $W_3 = 1.080$ g, $W_4 = 0.135$ g.

**Step 1: Moisture Content (M)**

$$
M = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{1.500 - 1.395}{1.500} \times 100
$$

$$
M = \frac{0.105}{1.500} \times 100 = 7.00\%
$$

**Step 2: Volatile Matter on Dry Basis**

$$
VM_{db} = \frac{W_2 - W_3}{W_2} \times 100 = \frac{1.395 - 1.080}{1.395} \times 100
$$

$$
VM_{db} = \frac{0.315}{1.395} \times 100 = 22.58\%
$$

**Step 3: Convert VM to As-Received Basis**

$$
VM_{ar} = VM_{db} \times \frac{100 - M}{100} = 22.58 \times \frac{93}{100}
$$

$$
VM_{ar} = 21.00\%
$$

**Step 4: Ash Content**

$$
A = \frac{W_4}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.135}{1.500} \times 100 = 9.00\%
$$

**Step 5: Fixed Carbon**

$$
FC = 100 - M - VM - A = 100 - 7.00 - 21.00 - 9.00
$$

$$
FC = 63.00\%
$$

**Final Results:** $M = 7.00\%$, $VM = 21.00\%$, $A = 9.00\%$, $FC = 63.00\%$ (Sum = 100% ✓)

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

The **Fuel Ratio** is defined as the ratio of Fixed Carbon to Volatile Matter:

$$
FR = \frac{FC}{VM} = \frac{63.00}{21.00} = 3.00
$$

**Classification Table (KTU Expected Answer):**

| Fuel Ratio | Coal Type | Combustion Behavior |
|------------|-----------|---------------------|
| $< 1.5$ | Lignite | Smoky, fast-burning |
| $1.5 - 2.5$ | Sub-bituminous | Moderate |
| $2.5 - 5.0$ | **Bituminous** | **Our sample falls here** |
| $> 5.0$ | Anthracite | Slow, low flame |

**Significance:** Higher fuel ratio means more char combustion (glowing) and less flaming combustion. The sample (FR = 3.0) is a bituminous coal with **moderate flame and long burn duration**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Define the terms **Moisture Content, Volatile Matter, Ash, and Fixed Carbon** with their significance in fire dynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel has the following as-received proximate analysis: $M = 8\%$, $VM = 30\%$, $A = 6\%$. Convert all values to the **dry basis** and **dry ash-free (daf) basis**. Comment on how these conversions affect fire propagation behavior. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

- **Moisture Content (M):** Mass percentage of water in the fuel. In fire dynamics, moisture absorbs latent heat of vaporization, **delaying ignition** and reducing flame spread rate.
- **Volatile Matter (VM):** Combustible gases and vapors released upon heating (excluding moisture). VM drives **flaming combustion** and is a key predictor of **ignitability** and **flame length**.
- **Ash Content (A):** Non-combustible inorganic mineral residue. Ash reduces combustion efficiency, contributes to smoke, and indicates potential **slagging** in fires.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Solid combustible carbon remaining after devolatilization. FC drives **glowing/smoldering combustion** and prolongs fire duration.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Fixed Carbon on as-received basis**

$$
FC_{ar} = 100 - 8 - 30 - 6 = 56\%
$$

**Step 2: Convert to Dry Basis (db) using $M_{ar} = 8\%$**

$$
M_{db} = 0\% \quad \text{(by definition of dry basis)}
$$

$$
VM_{db} = VM_{ar} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{ar}} = 30 \times \frac{100}{92}
$$

$$
VM_{db} = 30 \times 1.0870 = 32.61\%
$$

$$
A_{db} = 6 \times \frac{100}{92} = 6.52\%
$$

$$
FC_{db} = 56 \times \frac{100}{92} = 60.87\%
$$

**Verification:** $0 + 32.61 + 6.52 + 60.87 = 100.00\%$ ✓

**Step 3: Convert to Dry Ash-Free (daf) Basis**

The dry ash-free basis normalizes to the combustible organic fraction:

$$
VM_{daf} = VM_{db} \times \frac{100}{100 - A_{db}} = 32.61 \times \frac{100}{93.48}
$$

$$
VM_{daf} = 32.61 \times 1.0697 = 34.88\%
$$

$$
FC_{daf} = FC_{db} \times \frac{100}{100 - A_{db}} = 60.87 \times 1.0697
$$

$$
FC_{daf} = 65.12\%
$$

**Verification:** $0 + 34.88 + 65.12 = 100.00\%$ ✓

**Step 4: Fire Propagation Comment**

On the **daf basis**, the values reflect only the combustible organic matter. The daf VM (34.88%) is the most accurate indicator of **flame-driven fire propagation**, since the inert moisture and ash are removed. The higher the daf VM, the **more ignitable** and **faster-spreading** the fire.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Basis Inconsistency** — Mixing as-received and dry basis values in one equation is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always declare the basis first.
> 2. **Lidded Crucible Misuse** — If you forget to mention the *lidded* crucible in VM, you will lose the 1 mark for "procedure awareness".
> 3. **Cooling Step Omission** — Always state "cooled in a desiccator" before weighing; not mentioning it costs a mark.
> 4. **Sum Check** — End your numerical answers with a "Sum = 100%" verification line. Examiners reward this habit.
> 5. **Unit Reporting** — Report values in **% by mass**, not just numbers. Marks are deducted for missing units.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

- ✅ Proximate Analysis measures **four** fractions: $M$, $VM$, $A$, $FC$.
- ✅ Sum check: $M + VM + A + FC = 100\%$ (on any consistent basis).
- ✅ **VM test:** 950 °C, 7 min, **lidded** crucible.
- ✅ **Ash test:** 700–815 °C, **open** crucible, to constant mass.
- ✅ **Moisture test:** 105–110 °C, 1 hour oven, cool in **desiccator**.
- ✅ **Fixed Carbon is by difference** — it is never directly measured.
- ✅ **Fuel Ratio** = $FC / VM$; classifies coal from lignite to anthracite.
- ✅ **Three reporting bases:** as-received, dry, dry ash-free (daf).
- ✅ **Conversion to dry basis:** multiply by $\dfrac{100}{100 - M_{ar}}$.
- ✅ **Conversion to daf basis:** multiply by $\dfrac{100}{100 - A_{db}}$.
- ✅ High VM fuels → **fast ignition, high flame spread** (fire hazard ↑).
- ✅ High FC fuels → **long burn duration, charring fires** (fire persistence ↑).
- ✅ High Moisture → **delays ignition, reduces fire load**.
- ✅ High Ash → **reduces calorific value, increases smoke**.
- ✅ Standards: **IS 1350 (Part-I)** and **ASTM D3172**.
- ✅ Proximate analysis ≠ Ultimate analysis (elements vs. fractions).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat of Pyrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heat of Pyrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Module 1 — Heat of Pyrolysis: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat of Pyrolysis ($L_p$)** is defined as the **specific energy (per unit mass)** that must be supplied to a condensed-phase combustible solid to drive its **thermal decomposition** into volatile combustible gases, flammable tars, and a solid carbonaceous **char residue**, under conditions where the combustible products are prevented from reacting with an oxidizer.

It is an **endothermic** quantity, expressed in **kJ/kg** (or sometimes **kJ/mol** when reported per mole of fuel). It represents the cumulative enthalpy change associated with:
1. Heating the solid from ambient to the **pyrolysis temperature** ($T_p$),
2. Breaking the primary chemical bonds in the polymer/cellulosic network,
3. Vaporising any condensable tars and volatiles released.

---

### 🌟 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are frying a thick slab of cheese on a pan.

- At first, the pan transfers heat to the cheese, and the cheese warms up — this is **sensible heating**.
- Suddenly, the cheese begins to **bubble, melt, brown, and release oily vapours**. The energy absorbed at *this exact moment of bubbling* — energy that does *not* raise the temperature of the cheese but instead drives a **chemical/physical transformation** — is exactly the **Heat of Pyrolysis**.
- It is the *hidden energy cost* of turning a solid into a combustible gas.

In a fire, this "gas" is what then meets oxygen and flames. **Without enough Heat of Pyrolysis being supplied, no fuel gases are released, and the fire simply dies out.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (at 25 °C):**
> - **Cellulosics (wood, paper, cotton):** $L_p \approx 200$ – $500$ kJ/kg
> - **PMMA (Plexiglas):** $L_p \approx 250$ – $350$ kJ/kg
> - **Polyethylene (PE):** $L_p \approx 500$ – $700$ kJ/kg
> - **Polypropylene (PP):** $L_p \approx 600$ – $800$ kJ/kg
> - **Polystyrene (PS):** $L_p \approx 800$ – $1000$ kJ/kg
> - **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** $L_p \approx 1000$ – $1500$ kJ/kg
>
> These are tabulated against **ASTME E84 / ISO 17554** standard test methods using a **Cone Calorimeter** or **TGA-DSC** instruments.

---

### 🎯 Why Heat of Pyrolysis Matters in KTU Fire Dynamics

The **mass loss rate** of a solid fuel — the most fundamental parameter that controls the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of a fire — is **inversely proportional** to $L_p$ (and its companion, the **Heat of Gasification, $L_g$**). A material with a *high* Heat of Pyrolysis will pyrolyse *slowly* and produce a *smaller* fire, while a low-$L_p$ material will pyrolyse rapidly and aggressively.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Favourite Fact:** *Pyrolysis is NOT combustion.* It is the **fuel-generation** step that *precedes* combustion. A fire cannot exist until pyrolysis has produced flammable volatiles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Mechanism of Pyrolysis — Step-by-Step

1. **External Heat Flux Incidence:** Radiative and convective flux from the flame or external source strikes the solid surface. Net heat flux reaching the surface is denoted $q''_e$.
2. **Conductive Heat Penetration:** Heat conducts into the solid bulk, raising the temperature gradient. The depth of the heated layer is the **thermal penetration depth** $\delta_T = \sqrt{k_s \rho_s c_s \, t}$.
3. **Sensible Heating Phase:** The solid absorbs energy $Q_{sens} = m \, c_s \, (T_p - T_s)$ to reach the pyrolysis temperature $T_p$.
4. **Pyrolysis Reaction Front:** Once the local temperature reaches $T_p$, **thermochemical decomposition** begins. This absorbs the **Heat of Pyrolysis** $L_p$ (kJ/kg).
5. **Volatile Release:** Combustible vapours diffuse outwards; **char** remains behind.
6. **Re-radiation & Re-convection Losses:** Part of the incoming flux is lost from the hot surface as radiation ($\epsilon \sigma T_s^4$) and convection ($h_c (T_s - T_\infty)$), reducing the net energy available for pyrolysis.

---

## 2. Relationship Between Heat of Pyrolysis and Heat of Gasification

The **Heat of Gasification** $L_g$ is the *total* energy needed to convert a unit mass of solid into combustible vapour (i.e. the *sum* of the sensible heating energy and the chemical bond-breaking energy).

$$
L_g \;=\; L_p \;+\; \int_{T_s}^{T_p} c_{p,s}(T)\,dT
$$

where $c_{p,s}$ is the **specific heat of the solid** (J/kg·K).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, the integral is usually approximated by a constant average value:
>
> $$
> L_g \;\approx\; L_p \;+\; \bar{c}_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)
> $$

This is a high-yield substitution. Examiners regularly test it.

---

## 3. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $L_g = L_p + \int_{T_s}^{T_p} c_{p,s}\, dT$ | Heat of gasification from pyrolysis + sensible heat | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $L_g \approx L_p + \bar{c}_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)$ | Engineering approximation form | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $q''_{net} = q''_e - q''_{rr} - q''_{rc}$ | Net heat flux to solid | kW/m² |
| 4 | $q''_{rr} = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Re-radiative loss from surface | kW/m² |
| 5 | $\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{q''_{net}}{L_g}$ | **Surface mass loss rate (Babrauskas formula)** | kg/(m²·s) |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat release rate from burning solid | kW |
| 7 | $\delta_T = \sqrt{\alpha_s \, t} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{\rho_s c_{p,s}}\,t}$ | Thermal penetration depth | m |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{k_s \rho_s c_{p,s} (T_p - T_s)^2}{(q''_{net})^2}$ | Time to ignition | s |
| 9 | $L_p = L_g - \bar{c}_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)$ | Reverse extraction of $L_p$ from $L_g$ | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $T_p \approx 300$ – $400$ °C (cellulosics); $T_p \approx 350$ – $500$ °C (thermoplastics) | Typical pyrolysis temperatures | °C |

> ⚠️ **Table Formatting Note:** Vertical bars (used for absolute values in some texts) are written as $\vert x \vert$ in the LaTeX column to avoid breaking the markdown grid.

---

## 4. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Safety Engineering:** $L_p$ values feed directly into **CFD fire models** (FDS, PyroSim) to predict flame spread rates over cable trays, façade panels, and structural timber.
- **Material Selection:** Fire-retardant coatings work by *raising the effective $L_p$* of the underlying material, slowing pyrolysis.
- **Forensic Fire Investigation:** A burned polymer's residual char can be matched against a known $L_p$/$T_p$ fingerprint to identify the first fuel ignited.
- **Wildland Fire Modelling:** The Rothermel spread model uses an analogous "heat of pre-ignition" parameter to predict wildland fire rate of spread.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Heat of Gasification from First-Principles Energy Balance

Consider a control volume enclosing a unit area of a thermally-thick solid fuel surface. The energy inputs and outputs during steady-state pyrolysis are:

- **Energy In:** External heat flux $q''_e$.
- **Energy Out:** Re-radiation $q''_{rr}$, re-convection $q''_{rc}$, and energy consumed in pyrolysing mass at rate $\dot{m}''$ with energy demand $L_g$.

**Step 1 — Write the Surface Energy Balance:**

$$
q''_e \;=\; q''_{rr} \;+\; q''_{rc} \;+\; \dot{m}'' \, L_g
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Net Heat Flux to the Surface:**

$$
q''_{net} \;=\; q''_e - q''_{rr} - q''_{rc}
$$

So:

$$
q''_{net} \;=\; \dot{m}'' \, L_g
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Mass Loss Rate (Babrauskas Equation):**

$$
\dot{m}'' \;=\; \dfrac{q''_{net}}{L_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $L_g = L_p + \int_{T_s}^{T_p} c_{p,s}\, dT$:**

$$
\dot{m}'' \;=\; \dfrac{q''_{net}}{L_p + \displaystyle\int_{T_s}^{T_p} c_{p,s}\, dT}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Approximation (Constant $\bar{c}_{p,s}$):**

$$
\dot{m}'' \;=\; \dfrac{q''_{net}}{L_p + \bar{c}_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Heat Release Rate (Multiplying by Heat of Combustion $\Delta H_c$ and Burning Area $A$):**

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}'' \, A \, \Delta H_c \;=\; \dfrac{q''_{net} \, A \, \Delta H_c}{L_p + \bar{c}_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)}
$$

> 💡 **Why this matters:** The denominator contains $L_p$. As $L_p$ rises (more energy needed to decompose), $\dot{Q}$ falls — the fuel resists the fire. This is the mathematical reason fire-retardant additives that increase $L_p$ are so effective.

---

## Derivation 2 — Time-to-Ignition from Heat of Pyrolysis

**Step 1 — Thermal Penetration Depth for a Semi-Infinite Solid:**

$$
\delta_T \;=\; \sqrt{\alpha_s \, t} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{\rho_s \, c_{p,s}} \, t}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface Heat Balance for an Inert-Heated Solid (Pre-Pyrolysis):**

The conductive flux into the solid from a constant imposed surface flux $q''_{net}$ is:

$$
q''_{cond}(t) \;=\; \dfrac{k_s (T_s - T_\infty)}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha_s t}}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Conservation in the Heated Layer:**

$$
q''_{net} \sqrt{t} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s \rho_s c_{p,s}}{\pi}}\,(T_p - T_s)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Time-to-Ignition $\tau$:**

$$
\tau \;=\; \dfrac{k_s \rho_s c_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)^2}{\pi \,(q''_{net})^2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The familiar KTU expression omits $\pi$ by absorbing it into the engineering constant. The key insight is that **$\tau \propto 1/(q''_{net})^2$** — doubling the heat flux cuts ignition time to one-quarter.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A horizontal PMMA slab is exposed to a Cone Calorimeter flux of $q''_e = 35$ kW/m². The surface temperature stabilises at $T_s = 360$ °C = 633 K. Given $L_p = 300$ kJ/kg, $\bar{c}_{p,s} = 1500$ J/kg·K, $T_p = 380$ °C = 653 K, and $\epsilon = 0.95$, calculate the steady mass loss rate. Take $T_\infty = 293$ K.

**Step 1 — Compute the Re-radiation Loss:**

$$
q''_{rr} = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)
$$

Inserting $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-11}$ kW/(m²·K⁴):

$$
q''_{rr} = 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-11} \times (633^4 - 293^4)
$$

$$
= 5.39 \times 10^{-11} \times (1.605 \times 10^{11} - 7.36 \times 10^{9})
$$

$$
= 5.39 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.532 \times 10^{11} \approx 8.26 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Heat of Gasification:**

$$
L_g = L_p + \bar{c}_{p,s}\,(T_p - T_s)
$$

$$
L_g = 300 + 1.5 \times (653 - 633) = 300 + 30 = 330 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Heat Flux to the Surface (ignoring re-convection for simplicity):**

$$
q''_{net} = q''_e - q''_{rr} = 35 - 8.26 = 26.74 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Mass Loss Rate:**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{q''_{net}}{L_g} = \dfrac{26.74}{330} \approx 0.0810 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- Roughly 81 grams per square metre per second of PMMA is being gasified.
- With $\Delta H_c \approx 25$ MJ/kg for PMMA, the heat release rate per m² is $0.081 \times 25{,}000 \approx 2025$ kW/m² — typical pool-fire intensity of a burning plastic slab.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Pyrolysis Energy & Mass Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Heat Source] --> B[Incident Flux q double prime e]
    B --> C[Solid Fuel Surface]
    C --> D[Re radiation Loss q double prime rr]
    C --> E[Re convection Loss q double prime rc]
    C --> F[Conductive Flux into Solid]
    F --> G[Sensible Heating to Tp]
    G --> H{Pyrolysis Temperature Reached}
    H -->|Yes| I[Endothermic Decomposition]
    I --> J[Volatile Combustible Vapours]
    I --> K[Char Residue]
    J --> L[Mix with Oxidizer]
    L --> M[Diffusion Flame Combustion]
    M --> N[Flame Heat Feedback q double prime f]
    N --> B
    K --> O[Char Oxidation at Later Stage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the closed feedback loop from the diffusion flame (M → N → B). This feedback is the *physical reason* a fire sustains itself after ignition — the flame's own heat flux replaces the external $q''_e$ from the Cone Calorimeter or any other ignition source.

## Diagram 2 — Energy Partitioning at the Pyrolysing Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[q double prime e External Flux kW per m2] --> B[Pyrolysing Solid Surface]
    B --> C[q double prime rr Re radiation]
    B --> D[q double prime rc Re convection]
    B --> E[m double dot prime times L_g Energy absorbed by mass loss]
    C --> F[Energy Out]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Volatiles leave surface]
```

## Diagram 3 — Hierarchy of Pyrolysis Energy Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Heat of Gasification Lg] --> B[Heat of Pyrolysis Lp]
    A --> C[Sensible Heat c p s times Tp minus Ts]
    B --> D[Chemical Bond Breaking Energy]
    B --> E[Phase Change of Tars Volatiles]
    C --> F[Heating Solid from Ts to Tp]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (A, B, C, ... ) — no reserved words. All labels are raw uppercase text — no markdown formatting inside the quotes. This guarantees clean rendering in any Markdown viewer.
