Higher TRP = better fire resistance, slower flame propagation. ### 2.4 Order-of-Magnitude Reference Values (Engineering Data) | Material | $k$ (W/m·K) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c$ (J/kg·K) | $\sqrt{k\rho c}$ | TRP* (kW·s⁰·⁵/m²) | Comment | |----------|-------------|----------------|---------------|--------------------|----------------------|---------| | Thin paper / tissue | 0.10 | 600 | 1300 | 279 | 90.6 | Very easy to ignite | | Softwood (pine) | 0.13 | 450 | 1500 | 296 | 96.3 | Low–moderate TRP | | Hardwood (oak) | 0.16 | 700 | 1600 | 423 | 137.5 | Moderate TRP | | PMMA (acrylic) | 0.19 | 1180 | 1500 | 580 | 188.5 | High TRP, melts first | | Gypsum board | 0.17 | 950 | 1090 | 420 | 136.4 | Good passive protection | | Concrete | 1.00 | 2300 | 880 | 1423 | 462.4 | Excellent resistance | | Structural steel | 45.0 | 7850 | 460 | 12 748 | 4143 | Heat-sinks; non-piloted-ignition applies | *Computed with $T_{ig} = 350^\circ\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$ — a representative piloted-ignition value for cellulosics. The TRP for steel is illustrative only; steel is *non-combustible* and does not undergo piloted ignition in the conventional sense — it loses mechanical strength at $\sim 550^\circ\text{C}$ and is governed by the *critical temperature* criterion, not TRP. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Every algebraic transition below is written out in full. You should be able to reproduce each line on the KTU answer script for full marks. ### 3.1 Derivation — Surface Temperature Rise of a Semi-Infinite Solid **Governing PDE** (1-D, constant properties, no source): $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad \alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c}$$ **Initial condition (IC):** $$T(x, 0) = T_{\infty} \quad \text{for} \quad x \ge 0$$ **Boundary condition (BC) at the exposed surface $x = 0$** (constant incident heat flux $\dot{q}''_e$, no re-radiation at $t = 0$): $$-k \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = \dot{q}''_e$$ **Boundary condition at infinity:** $$T(x \to \infty, t) = T_{\infty}$$ The standard closed-form solution (obtainable by Laplace transform, similarity variable $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{\alpha t})$, or by reference to Carslaw & Jaeger) is: $$T(x, t) - T_{\infty} = \frac{2 \, \dot{q}''_e \, \sqrt{\alpha t}}{k} \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4 \alpha t}\right) - \frac{x}{2 \sqrt{\alpha t}} \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{\alpha t}}\right) \right]$$ **Evaluate at the surface $x = 0$:** The exponential term becomes 1, the complementary error function of 0 is 1, and the second term vanishes because of the factor $x$: $$T(0, t) - T_{\infty} = \frac{2 \, \dot{q}''_e \, \sqrt{\alpha t}}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$ **Substitute $\alpha = k/(\rho c)$:** $$T(0, t) - T_{\infty} = \frac{2 \, \dot{q}''_e}{k \sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k t}{\rho c}} = \frac{2 \, \dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{t}}{\sqrt{k \rho c}}$$ This is the celebrated **incident-heat-flux surface-temperature-rise law**: $$\boxed{\,T(0, t) - T_{\infty} = \frac{2 \, \dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi \, k \rho c}} \cdot \sqrt{t}\,}$$ Note the $\sqrt{t}$ scaling — heat penetrates diffusively, not linearly, into a semi-infinite solid. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Time to Piloted Ignition in Terms of TRP **Ignition criterion:** the surface reaches the piloted-ignition temperature at $t = t_{ig}$: $$T(0, t_{ig}) = T_{ig}$$ **Substitute into the surface-rise law:** $$T_{ig} - T_{\infty} = \frac{2 \, \dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi \, k \rho c}} \cdot \sqrt{t_{ig}}$$ **Isolate $\sqrt{t_{ig}}$:** $$\sqrt{t_{ig}} = \frac{(T_{ig} - T_{\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\pi \, k \rho c}}{2 \, \dot{q}''_e}$$ **Square both sides:** $$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi \, (T_{ig} - T_{\infty})^{2} \, k \rho c}{4 \, (\dot{q}''_e)^{2}}$$ **Recognize that $\sqrt{k \rho c} \cdot (T_{ig} - T_{\infty}) \equiv TRP$:** $$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{TRP^{2}}{(\dot{q}''_e)^{2}}$$ $$\boxed{\,t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(\frac{TRP}{\dot{q}''_e}\right)^{2}\,}$$ Equivalently, the **reciprocity relation** is: $$\boxed{\,\dot{q}''_e \sqrt{t_{ig}} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot TRP \approx 0.886 \, TRP\,}$$ This is a *constant* for a given material at a given $T_\infty$ and surface condition — a beautiful result that lets engineers plot $1/\sqrt{t_{ig}}$ versus $\dot{q}''_e$ and extract TRP from a straight-line fit. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Rate of Opposed-Flow Flame Spread on a Thermally Thick Fuel **Conceptual setup:** A flame spread on the surface of a combustible. The flame heat flux $\dot{q}''_f$ preheats the virgin fuel ahead of the flame front. Local ignition at a point occurs once that point has been heated for the *time to ignition* $t_{ig}$. The flame front thus moves at a speed set by how quickly the heat can penetrate a depth of order $\delta = \sqrt{\alpha t_{ig}}$. **Heat-penetration depth:** $$\delta = \sqrt{\alpha \, t_{ig}} = \sqrt{\frac{k \, t_{ig}}{\rho c}}$$ **Spread velocity** is the depth divided by the time to ignition (order-of-magnitude argument, factor of order unity absorbed into the proportionality constant $\lambda_g$): $$V = \frac{\lambda_g \, \delta}{t_{ig}} = \frac{\lambda_g}{t_{ig}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \, t_{ig}}{\rho c}} = \lambda_g \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{\rho c \, t_{ig}}}$$ **Substitute $t_{ig} = (\pi/4) (TRP/\dot{q}''_f)^2$:** $$V = \lambda_g \cdot \sqrt{\frac{4 k (\dot{q}''_f)^2}{\pi \, \rho c \, TRP^{2}}} = \frac{2 \lambda_g}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{\dot{q}''_f}{TRP} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{\rho c}}$$ Taking $\lambda_g = 1$ (a common engineering approximation for thermally thick materials): $$\boxed{\,V = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{\dot{q}''_f \cdot \sqrt{k/(\rho c)}}{TRP}\,}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** - $V \propto \dot{q}''_f$ — *stronger flame radiation → faster spread* (linear dependence). - $V \propto 1/TRP$ — *higher TRP → slower spread* (inverse dependence). - $V \propto \sqrt{k/(\rho c)} = \sqrt{\alpha}$ — *higher thermal diffusivity → faster spread* (good conductors push the heat front along). --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Time to Piloted Ignition of Softwood **Given (typical KTU data for a softwood plank):** $$k = 0.13 \;\text{W/(m·K)}, \quad \rho = 450 \;\text{kg/m}^3, \quad c = 1500 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$$ $$T_{ig} = 350^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_{\infty} = 25^\circ\text{C}, \quad \dot{q}''_e = 25 \;\text{kW/m}^2 = 25\,000 \;\text{W/m}^2$$ **Step 1 — Compute thermal inertia:** $$k \rho c = 0.13 \times 450 \times 1500 = 87\,750 \;\text{W}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{m}^{-4} \cdot \text{K}^{-2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute thermal effusivity (square root of inertia):** $$\sqrt{k \rho c} = \sqrt{87\,750} = 296.23 \;\text{W} \cdot \text{s}^{0.5} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Compute TRP:** $$TRP = (350 - 25) \times 296.23 = 325 \times 296.23 = 96\,275 \;\text{W} \cdot \text{s}^{0.5} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$$ $$TRP = 96.28 \;\text{kW} \cdot \text{s}^{0.5} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$$ **Step 4 — Compute time to piloted ignition:** $$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(\frac{TRP}{\dot{q}''_e}\right)^{2} = 0.7854 \times \left(\frac{96.28}{25}\right)^{2} = 0.7854 \times (3.851)^{2}$$ $$t_{ig} = 0.7854 \times 14.83 = 11.65 \;\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Verification via reciprocity relation:** $$\dot{q}''_e \sqrt{t_{ig}} = 25 \times \sqrt{11.65} = 25 \times 3.413 = 85.32 \;\text{kW} \cdot \text{s}^{0.5} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$$ $$0.886 \times TRP = 0.886 \times 96.28 = 85.30 \;\text{kW} \cdot \text{s}^{0.5} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \;\;\checkmark$$ > **Interpretation for the engineer:** Under a 25 kW/m² radiant panel (typical of a fully developed room fire at the upper layer), this softwood specimen reaches piloted ignition in **≈ 11.6 s**. If the heat flux is doubled to 50 kW/m², the ignition time drops to ≈ 2.9 s — a 4× reduction for a 2× increase in flux, exactly as the $1/\dot{q}''^{2}_e$ scaling predicts. ### 3.5 Limiting-Case Sanity Check — TRP at $t \to 0$ In the limit $t \to 0$, the surface temperature should equal $T_\infty$ (no heat has arrived yet). Substituting $t = 0$ into the surface-rise formula: $$\lim_{t \to 0} T(0, t) = T_\infty + \frac{2\dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi k \rho c}} \cdot \sqrt{0} = T_\infty \;\;\checkmark$$ This confirms the formula is dimensionally and physically consistent. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TRP in the Fire-Propagation Causal Chain (Block-Level Architecture) The following Mermaid block diagram traces how the Thermal Response Parameter sits in the broader fire-propagation causal chain, from the physics of heat transfer to the engineering consequence of flame spread. Use this as a mental model for KTU short-answer and 14-mark questions on Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Heat Flux qe
kW per m2] --> B[Semi Infinite Solid
Conduction Model] C[Material Properties
k rho c] --> B D[Ignition Temperature
Tig minus Tinf] --> E[TRP Definition
TRP = deltaT times sqrt k rho c] C --> E B --> F[Surface Temperature
Rise sqrt t Law] F --> G[Piloted Ignition
Criterion Tig reached] E --> H[Time to Ignition
tig = pi by 4 times TRP by qe squared] G --> H H --> I[Flame Spread Rate
V proportional to qf by TRP] E --> I I --> J[Fire Propagation
Across Surface] H --> K[Fire Resistance
Classification] E --> K E --> L[Material Selection
for Construction] style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a83232,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a07a00,color:#000 style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000 style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a6e3a,color:#000 style E fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#a04a00,color:#000 style F fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a07a00,color:#000 style G fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a6e3a,color:#000 style H fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#a04a00,color:#000 style I fill:#e2c5ff,stroke:#5a1f9a,color:#000 style J fill:#ff9aa2,stroke:#a01a30,color:#000 style K fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000 style L fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 How to Read the Diagram 1. **Inputs to TRP** (top-left cluster): The material's $k\rho c$ and the ignition-temperature gap $\Delta T$ are the only physics inputs required. 2. **Independent path** (left): The external heat flux drives the *transient conduction* problem in parallel, producing the surface-temperature history. 3. **Convergence at ignition** (centre): The moment $T(0,t)$ crosses $T_{ig}$ is governed by both paths simultaneously — and that moment is captured by the simple algebraic relation $t_{ig} = (\pi/4) (TRP/\dot{q}''_e)^2$. 4. **Downstream engineering uses** (right): Once $t_{ig}$ is known, the flame spread rate follows, and with it the macroscopic fire-propagation prediction and the material-selection decision. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that TRP is *the convergence point*. You do **not** need to solve the PDE for every fire scenario — once TRP is known from a single laboratory test (e.g., ASTM E1321, LIFT apparatus), it is portable to any other incident heat flux via the $1/\dot{q}''^{2}_e$ scaling. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the Thermal Response Parameter (TRP) of a combustible solid. State its physical significance and SI units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 m):** $TRP = (T_{ig} - T_\infty)\cdot\sqrt{k \rho c}$, where $k$ is thermal conductivity, $\rho$ is density, $c$ is specific heat, $T_{ig}$ is the piloted-ignition surface temperature, and $T_\infty$ is the ambient temperature. - **Physical significance (1 m):** It is a *lumped material property* that quantifies the resistance of a thermally thick solid to piloted ignition. Higher TRP implies longer ignition time and slower flame spread. - **SI units (0.5 m):** $\text{kW}\cdot\text{s}^{0.5}/\text{m}^{2}$ (or equivalently $\text{W}\cdot\text{s}^{0.5}/\text{m}^{2}$). --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024] **Differentiate between *thermal inertia*, *thermal effusivity*, and *thermal diffusivity*. State the SI unit of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Interpretation | |----------|---------|---------|----------------| | Thermal inertia $I_t$ | $k\rho c$ | $\text{W}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{m}^{-4}\cdot\text{K}^{-2}$ | Total resistance to surface heating | | Thermal effusivity $e$ | $\sqrt{k\rho c}$ | $\text{W}\cdot\text{s}^{0.5}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ | Rate of heat absorption at the surface | | Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ | $k/(\rho c)$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Speed of heat propagation into the solid | (1 mark per quantity, with formula and unit; 0.5 mark extra for the qualitative interpretation that links them.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the 1-D transient heat-conduction equation for a semi-infinite solid with a constant surface heat flux $\dot{q}''_e$, derive the expression for the time to piloted ignition $t_{ig}$ of a thermally thick combustible in terms of the Thermal Response Parameter (TRP). **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Governing PDE and boundary conditions (1 mark):** $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad \alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c}$$ with $T(x,0) = T_\infty$, $-k(\partial T/\partial x)_{x=0} = \dot{q}''_e$, $T(\infty, t) = T_\infty$. **Step 2 — Standard closed-form solution (1 mark):** $$T(x,t) - T_\infty = \frac{2\dot{q}''_e\sqrt{\alpha t}}{k}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{4\alpha t}\right) - \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)\right]$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate at the surface $x = 0$ (1 mark):** $$T(0, t) - T_\infty = \frac{2\dot{q}''_e\sqrt{\alpha t}}{k\sqrt{\pi}} = \frac{2\dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi k \rho c}}\sqrt{t}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the piloted-ignition criterion (1 mark):** $T(0, t_{ig}) = T_{ig}$, hence $$T_{ig} - T_\infty = \frac{2\dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi k\rho c}}\sqrt{t_{ig}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $t_{ig}$ and identify TRP (2 marks):** $$\sqrt{t_{ig}} = \frac{(T_{ig} - T_\infty)\sqrt{\pi k\rho c}}{2\dot{q}''_e} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{ig} = \frac{\pi(T_{ig} - T_\infty)^{2}\,k\rho c}{4(\dot{q}''_e)^{2}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\!\left(\frac{TRP}{\dot{q}''_e}\right)^{2}$$ because $TRP \equiv (T_{ig} - T_\infty)\sqrt{k\rho c}$. $\blacksquare$ **(b) [7 marks]** A sample of PMMA (poly-methyl-methacrylate) has the following properties: $k = 0.19 \;\text{W/(m·K)}$, $\rho = 1180\;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $c = 1500\;\text{J/(kg·K)}$, $T_{ig} = 300^\circ\text{C}$. It is exposed in still air at $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$ to a constant radiant heat flux of $\dot{q}''_e = 30 \;\text{kW/m}^{2}$. Calculate (i) the TRP and (ii) the time to piloted ignition. **Model Solution:** **(i) TRP (3.5 marks):** $$k \rho c = 0.19 \times 1180 \times 1500 = 336\,300\;\text{W}^{2}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{m}^{-4}\cdot\text{K}^{-2}$$ $$\sqrt{k\rho c} = \sqrt{336\,300} = 579.91\;\text{W}\cdot\text{s}^{0.5}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$$ $$TRP = (300 - 25)\times 579.91 = 275 \times 579.91 = 159\,475\;\text{W}\cdot\text{s}^{0.5}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}$$ $$\boxed{TRP \approx 159.48 \;\text{kW}\cdot\text{s}^{0.5}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}}$$ **(ii) Time to piloted ignition (3.5 marks):** $$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{TRP}{\dot{q}''_e}\right)^{2} = 0.7854 \times \left(\frac{159.48}{30}\right)^{2}$$ $$= 0.7854 \times (5.316)^{2} = 0.7854 \times 28.26$$ $$\boxed{t_{ig} \approx 22.2 \;\text{s}}$$ **Verification (cross-check):** $\dot{q}''_e\sqrt{t_{ig}} = 30 \times \sqrt{22.2} = 30 \times 4.71 = 141.4$ kW·s⁰·⁵/m², and $0.886 \times TRP = 0.886 \times 159.48 = 141.3$ kW·s⁰·⁵/m² ✓ --- #### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024] — (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** Explain, with the aid of the derived relations, the role of the Thermal Response Parameter in (i) the piloted-ignition delay and (ii) the opposed-flow flame-spread rate on a thermally thick combustible solid. **Model Solution:** **(i) Role in piloted-ignition delay (3.5 marks):** The time to piloted ignition is given by $t_{ig} = (\pi/4)(TRP/\dot{q}''_e)^{2}$. Three engineering observations follow: 1. *Quadratic suppression by TRP:* For a fixed external heat flux, doubling the TRP increases the ignition delay by a factor of 4. This makes TRP the **dominant material lever** for fire-resistance enhancement — for example, increasing the bulk density of a foam by 50 % quadruples $k\rho c$ and therefore quadruples the ignition time. 2. *Inverse-square reduction with heat flux:* Halving the incident heat flux (e.g., by adding a fire-resistant coating that reflects IR) quadruples the ignition time, independently of TRP. 3. *Reciprocity law:* $\dot{q}''_e\sqrt{t_{ig}} = (\sqrt{\pi}/2) \cdot TRP$ is a *material constant* at fixed $T_\infty$. This is the basis of the **ASTM E1321 (LIFT) test method** — measure $t_{ig}$ at several $\dot{q}''_e$, fit a straight line on a $\dot{q}''_e$ vs $1/\sqrt{t_{ig}}$ plot, and read off TRP from the slope. **(ii) Role in flame-spread rate (3.5 marks):** For opposed-flow flame spread on a thermally thick fuel, the spread velocity is: $$V = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{\dot{q}''_f \sqrt{k/(\rho c)}}{TRP}$$ - $V \propto 1/TRP$ — a *higher* TRP (e.g., concrete, thick hardwood) gives *slower* spread; this is why fire-resistant walls are specified by a minimum effective TRP, not a minimum thickness alone. - $V \propto \sqrt{\alpha} = \sqrt{k/(\rho c)}$ — heat must *diffuse forward* into virgin fuel; good thermal conductors spread flame faster, all else being equal. - $V \propto \dot{q}''_f$ — stronger flame radiation pulls the front forward; in fire-scale flashover conditions $\dot{q}''_f$ rises sharply, and so does $V$ — the **positive feedback** that drives fire to flashover. > **[!WARNING]** > **Common Pitfall in 14-mark answers:** Students often write $V = q''/(TRP)$ and forget the $\sqrt{k/(\rho c)}$ factor and the $2/\sqrt{\pi}$ prefactor. This causes a 2-mark penalty. Always include the *full* spread-rate expression with all dimensional constants. **(b) [7 marks]** Two materials, **softwood** ($k = 0.13\;\text{W/m·K}$, $\rho = 450\;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $c = 1500\;\text{J/kg·K}$, $T_{ig} = 350^\circ\text{C}$) and **PMMA** ($k = 0.19\;\text{W/m·K}$, $\rho = 1180\;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $c = 1500\;\text{J/kg·K}$, $T_{ig} = 300^\circ\text{C}$), are both exposed at $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$ to $\dot{q}''_e = 25\;\text{kW/m}^{2}$. (i) Compute the TRP of each material. (ii) Compute the ignition time of each. (iii) Comment on the fire-propagation implication of the difference. **Model Solution:** **(i) TRP values (2 marks):** *Softwood:* $$k\rho c = 0.13 \times 450 \times 1500 = 87\,750$$ $$\sqrt{k\rho c} = 296.23 \;\text{W·s}^{0.5}\text{/m}^2\text{·K}$$ $$TRP_{\text{wood}} = (350 - 25)\times 296.23 = 325 \times 296.23 = 96\,275\;\text{W·s}^{0.5}\text{/m}^{2} = 96.28\;\text{kW·s}^{0.5}\text{/m}^{2}$$ *PMMA:* $$k\rho c = 0.19 \times 1180 \times 1500 = 336\,300$$ $$\sqrt{k\rho c} = 579.91 \;\text{W·s}^{0.5}\text{/m}^{2}\text{·K}$$ $$TRP_{\text{PMMA}} = (300 - 25)\times 579.91 = 275 \times 579.91 = 159\,475\;\text{W·s}^{0.5}\text{/m}^{2} = 159.48\;\text{kW·s}^{0.5}\text{/m}^{2}$$ **(ii) Ignition times (3 marks):** *Softwood:* $$t_{ig,\text{wood}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{96.28}{25}\right)^{2} = 0.7854 \times 14.83 = 11.65\;\text{s}$$ *PMMA:* $$t_{ig,\text{PMMA}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{159.48}{25}\right)^{2} = 0.7854 \times 40.69 = 31.95\;\text{s}$$ **(iii) Fire-propagation commentary (2 marks):** Although PMMA has *higher* thermal inertia than softwood (336 300 vs 87 750 in absolute units — a factor of ≈ 3.83), the ratio of TRP values is ≈ 1.66 because PMMA's *lower* $T_{ig}$ ($300^\circ$C vs $350^\circ$C) compresses the temperature gap $\Delta T$. The net effect is that **softwood ignites in 11.6 s — roughly 2.7 times faster than PMMA** at the same incident heat flux. From a fire-propagation standpoint, softwood is the *more readily ignitable* fuel and the flame-spread velocity $V$ is correspondingly higher. This explains why timber-clad compartments reach flashover earlier than compartments finished with PMMA panels of the same thickness. > **[!WARNING]** > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students will state that "PMMA has higher thermal inertia, so it is safer." This is **incomplete**. TRP includes $\Delta T$, and PMMA's lower $T_{ig}$ partly cancels its higher $k\rho c$. Always report the *full TRP*, not just the inertia. Marks are lost for confusing thermal inertia and TRP. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — keep this card for Module 1 quick reference.** - **TRP definition:** $TRP = (T_{ig} - T_\infty)\,\sqrt{k\rho c}$. Memorize the formula and the units $\text{kW·s}^{0.5}/\text{m}^{2}$. - **Ignition-time law:** $t_{ig} = (\pi/4)\,(TRP/\dot{q}''_e)^{2}$. Know the $1/\dot{q}''^{2}_e$ scaling. - **Reciprocity relation:** $\dot{q}''_e\sqrt{t_{ig}} = (\sqrt{\pi}/2)\,TRP \approx 0.886\,TRP$. Useful for extracting TRP from $\dot{q}''_e$ vs $1/\sqrt{t_{ig}}$ plots. - **Surface temperature rise:** $T(0,t) - T_\infty = (2\dot{q}''_e/\sqrt{\pi k\rho c})\,\sqrt{t}$ — the *diffusive* $\sqrt{t}$ law. - **Spread rate (thermally thick):** $V \approx (2/\sqrt{\pi})\,\dot{q}''_f\,\sqrt{k/(\rho c)}/TRP$ — linear in flame flux, inverse in TRP. - **Thermal inertia vs effusivity vs diffusivity:** know the difference. Inertia has units of $\text{W}^{2}\text{·s/m}^{4}\text{·K}^{2}$; effusivity is its square root; diffusivity is $k/(\rho c)$. - **"Thermally thick" criterion:** the back face is far enough that $\sqrt{\alpha t} \ll L$ (depth of heat penetration much less than sample thickness). If violated, use the *thermally thin* ignition model instead. - **TRP as a *lumped* property:** assumes constant $k$, $\rho$, $c$. For *charring* materials (wood, char-forming polymers) the residual char layer modifies the effective values; use the *charring-front* correction in advanced work. - **High TRP = safer:** materials with high TRP (concrete, gypsum, thick hardwood) take longer to ignite and propagate flame more slowly — the basis of *passive fire protection* design. - **Piloted vs auto-ignition:** the $T_{ig}$ used in TRP is the *piloted* (small external flame) ignition temperature. For *auto*ignition, use a higher $T_{ig,auto}$ and recompute. - **Numerical safety:** always convert kW → W or W → kW consistently; always state the value to 3–4 significant figures and the units.

topics.title: Smoldering and Factors Affecting Smoldering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Smoldering and Factors Affecting Smoldering # 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Smoldering ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Smoldering combustion** (also called *smouldering combustion* or *non-flaming combustion*) is a slow, low-temperature, flameless, and heterogeneous form of combustion that is sustained by the heat evolved when oxygen directly attacks the surface of a condensed-phase porous solid fuel. It is a surface-type oxidation reaction where fuel and oxidizer exist in different phases, with the reaction propagating through the porous solid via conductive, convective, and radiative heat transfer mechanisms. Mathematically, the global smoldering reaction can be expressed as: $$\text{Fuel (s)} + \nu_{O_2} \cdot O_2 (g) \rightarrow \text{Char (s)} + \text{Volatiles (g)} + \text{Heat (q)}$$ Where: - $\nu_{O_2}$ is the stoichiometric oxygen coefficient - The reaction typically occurs at temperatures between **450 °C and 700 °C** (well below flaming combustion temperatures of **1000 °C–1500 °C**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Smoldering is a fundamentally *heterogeneous* (solid-gas) reaction, distinguishing it from *homogeneous* flaming combustion (gas-gas). This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU examinations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **charcoal grill after the flames have died down**. The glowing red embers that continue to produce heat without visible flames — that's smoldering. The charcoal is being attacked by oxygen molecules on its surface, and the heat generated at the surface is being conducted inward to drive further reactions deeper into the fuel bed. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world example:** The persistent burn in a discarded cigarette butt on a sofa, the slow burn of peat in bogs, and the dangerous subsurface fires in coal mines and silos are all classic smoldering scenarios. In fact, the **2010 Moscow Domodedovo International Airport fire** was initiated by smoldering. ## 1.3 Characteristic Properties of Smoldering | Property | Smoldering | Flaming Combustion | |---|---|---| | **Temperature Range** | **450 °C – 700 °C** | **1000 °C – 2000 °C** | | **Reaction Type** | Heterogeneous (s–g) | Homogeneous (g–g) | | **Propagation Velocity** | **0.1 mm/s – 10 mm/s** (very slow) | **0.1 m/s – 10 m/s** | | **Visible Signature** | Glowing, no flame | Visible flame | | **Fuel State** | Porous solid (charcoal, foam) | Volatiles/gases | | **Activation Energy** | **80–120 kJ/mol** | **120–200 kJ/mol** | | **Stoichiometry** | Inadequate air (oxygen-limited) | Adequate air | | **Toxic Emissions** | High CO yield (toxic) | Lower CO, more CO₂ | ## 1.4 Stages of Smoldering Combustion The smoldering process typically evolves through the following stages: 1. **Initiation Stage** — A small heat source ignites the porous solid (e.g., cigarette ember, hot ash, electrical heating). 2. **Inception Stage** — A localized, oxygen-limited reaction zone forms. 3. **Sustained Smoldering Stage** — Self-sustaining reaction front propagates through the fuel. 4. **Transition Stage** — Under proper conditions, smoldering may transition to flaming combustion. 5. **Decay/Extinction Stage** — Insufficient fuel, oxygen, or excessive heat losses cause self-extinguishment. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Concept:** The smoldering-to-flaming transition is critical in fire safety engineering because smoldering can persist for **days, weeks, or even months** before suddenly transitioning to flaming — making it a major hazard in upholstered furniture, dust accumulations, and industrial bulk solids. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Governing Physical Mechanisms of Smoldering The smoldering phenomenon is governed by the **coupled interaction of four fundamental transport and reaction processes**: ### 🔬 Mechanism 1: Heterogeneous Oxidation Reaction The surface of the solid fuel reacts directly with oxygen in the gas phase. The rate of this reaction is typically modeled using an **Arrhenius-type expression**: $$\dot{R}_{ox} = A_s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_s}\right) \cdot C_{O_2}^n$$ Where: - $\dot{R}_{ox}$ = Rate of oxidation (kg/m³·s) - $A_s$ = Pre-exponential factor (kg/m³·s) - $E_a$ = Activation energy (**kJ/mol**) - $R$ = Universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**) - $T_s$ = Surface temperature (K) - $C_{O_2}$ = Oxygen concentration (mol/m³) - $n$ = Reaction order with respect to O₂ (typically **0.5 to 1.0**) ### 🌡️ Mechanism 2: Heat Transfer Heat generated by the oxidation reaction must be transported forward to preheat adjacent virgin fuel, sustaining the propagation. Three modes are involved: - **Conduction** through the solid matrix (dominant in chars) - **Convection** via the inflow of oxidizer (dominant in forced flow smoldering) - **Radiation** from glowing surfaces (significant at T > **600 °C**) The 1D heat conduction equation in the solid: $$\rho_s c_s \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_s \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} + \dot{q}_{rxn} - \dot{q}_{loss}$$ ### 💨 Mechanism 3: Oxygen Diffusion (Mass Transfer) Oxygen must diffuse from the bulk gas through the porous structure to the reaction site. For porous media, **Fick's Law** with effective diffusivity applies: $$\dot{m}_{O_2} = -D_{eff} \cdot \frac{\partial C_{O_2}}{\partial x}$$ Where $D_{eff}$ is the **effective diffusion coefficient** in the porous medium (m²/s). ### ⚖️ Mechanism 4: Heat Losses Heat is lost to the surroundings via convection, radiation, and conduction through boundaries. These losses determine whether smoldering self-sustains or extinguishes. ## 2.2 The Three Smoldering Fronts (KTU Critical Concept) A self-sustaining smoldering wave is composed of three distinct thermal/chemical zones: | Zone | Temperature | Function | |---|---|---| | **Pre-heat / Drying Zone** (upstream) | $T < 100°C$ | Moisture evaporation, in-depth heating | | **Pyrolysis / Reaction Zone** (middle) | **400 °C – 700 °C** | Char formation, oxidation, heat release | | **Char / Ash Zone** (downstream) | $T > 700°C$ | Residual char oxidation, glowing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw and label the smoldering wave structure," always show these **three zones** with proper temperature ranges and label the **reaction front** clearly. This is worth up to **5 marks** in ESE. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Arrhenius Oxidation Rate | $\dot{R}_{ox} = A_s \cdot e^{-E_a/RT_s} \cdot C_{O_2}^n$ | kg/m³·s | | 2 | Smoldering Velocity (1D, simplified) | $v_s = \frac{\lambda_{eff}}{\rho c_p} \cdot \frac{\dot{q}_{rxn} \cdot \xi}{\rho \cdot \Delta H_{pyr}}$ | mm/s | | 3 | Critical Heat Loss Criterion (Quenching) | $\dot{q}_{loss} \geq \dot{q}_{rxn}$ | W/m³ | | 4 | Péclet Number (for forced flow smoldering) | $Pe = \frac{\rho_g u L c_{p,g}}{\lambda_{eff}}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Damköhler Number (reaction vs transport) | $Da = \frac{\tau_{res}}{\tau_{rxn}} = \frac{L \cdot A_s e^{-E_a/RT}}{u}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Smoldering-to-Flaming Transition Criterion | $T_{s} \geq T_{ign,volatiles}$ (≈ 300 °C–500 °C for volatiles) | K | | 7 | Critical Oxygen Concentration (extinction) | $Y_{O_2,crit} \approx 12\% - 16\%$ by volume | mol/mol | | 8 | Char Oxidation Energy | $\Delta H_c \approx 30 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (for carbon) | kJ/kg | > [!NOTE] > **Formula Selection Guidance:** For KTU problems, you will typically use Equation 1 (Arrhenius) to compute oxidation rates, and Equation 3 (Critical Heat Loss) to explain extinction conditions. The Damköhler number (Eq. 5) is the most important dimensionless parameter for distinguishing **reaction-limited** ($Da \ll 1$) vs **transport-limited** ($Da \gg 1$) smoldering regimes. ## 2.4 Engineering Significance - **Fire Safety Engineering:** Smoldering is responsible for over **25% of residential fire fatalities** because it produces **high yields of CO** (carbon monoxide) and other toxic gases, often during sleep when victims are unaware. - **Industrial Hazards:** Coal storage, grain silos, sawdust piles, and dust collectors are susceptible to smoldering-induced fires. - **Environmental Applications:** Smoldering is intentionally used for **soil remediation** (thermal treatment of contaminated soils) and **biochar production** for carbon sequestration. - **Energy Applications:** Smoldering combustion of biomass is being explored for low-temperature, controlled energy release. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Utility:** Understanding smoldering factors is critical in **fire investigation** — investigators must determine whether a smoldering source (e.g., electrical fault, cigarette) led to a flaming fire, and the physics of the smoldering wave is used to reconstruct the timeline of the fire. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Smoldering Propagation Velocity (1D Forced Flow) We will derive the **smoldering wave velocity** for a 1D forced-flow smoldering system — a classic KTU 14-mark problem. ### Step 1 — Define the System and Coordinates Consider a 1D porous solid fuel slab of length $L$ in a wind channel. A forced oxidizer flow of velocity $u$ passes through the slab. Let $x$ be the coordinate along the flow direction. The smoldering front is assumed to be a thin, planar reaction zone propagating at velocity $v_s$ in the direction opposite to the flow (i.e., upstream propagation — *forward smoldering*). ### Step 2 — Apply the Steady-State Energy Balance In the **moving frame** attached to the smoldering front, the steady-state energy balance per unit cross-sectional area $A_c$ is: $$ \rho u c_{p,g} (T_{ad} - T_u) = \rho_s v_s \Delta H_{eff} $$ Where: - $\rho$ = gas density (kg/m³) - $u$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s) - $c_{p,g}$ = gas specific heat (J/kg·K) - $T_{ad}$ = adiabatic smoldering temperature (K) - $T_u$ = upstream (inlet) gas temperature (K) - $\rho_s$ = bulk solid density (kg/m³) - $v_s$ = smoldering velocity (m/s) - $\Delta H_{eff}$ = effective heat of reaction per unit mass of solid (J/kg) ### Step 3 — Solve for $v_s$ Rearranging for $v_s$: $$ v_s = \frac{\rho u c_{p,g} (T_{ad} - T_u)}{\rho_s \Delta H_{eff}} $$ ### Step 4 — Express $T_{ad}$ in Terms of Heat of Reaction If we assume **adiabatic conditions** (no heat loss), the adiabatic temperature is determined by enthalpy balance: $$ T_{ad} = T_u + \frac{Y_{O_2} \Delta H_{rxn} \sigma}{\rho c_{p,g}} $$ Where: - $Y_{O_2}$ = oxygen mass fraction in the inflow - $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = heat of reaction per unit mass of O₂ consumed (J/kg O₂) - $\sigma$ = stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel mass ratio ### Step 5 — Final Form of Smoldering Velocity Substituting Step 4 into Step 3 yields the **final propagating velocity equation**: $$ v_s = \frac{Y_{O_2} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot \sigma \cdot u}{\Delta H_{eff}} $$ ### Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation (Example for Cellulose Char) Assume the following properties for **charcoal smoldering**: - $Y_{O_2} = 0.23$ (atmospheric) - $\Delta H_{rxn} = 9.1 \times 10^6$ J/kg O₂ - $\sigma = 1.5$ (typical for char) - $u = 0.05$ m/s (low forced flow) - $\Delta H_{eff} = 1.2 \times 10^7$ J/kg (effective char oxidation energy) Substituting: $$ v_s = \frac{0.23 \times 9.1 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 0.05}{1.2 \times 10^7} $$ $$ v_s = \frac{1.56975 \times 10^5}{1.2 \times 10^7} $$ $$ v_s = 0.01308 \text{ m/s} \approx 13.1 \text{ mm/s} $$ This is in excellent agreement with experimentally observed smoldering velocities of **5–20 mm/s** for char in low-velocity forced flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Solution Pattern:** When solving such problems, always: > 1. State the **assumptions** (1D, steady-state, adiabatic, thin reaction zone). > 2. Draw a **schematic** of the control volume. > 3. Write the **conservation equation** clearly. > 4. Solve step-by-step with **substituted numerical values**. > 5. Conclude with a **physical interpretation** of the result. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Parameter on Smoldering The following matrix summarizes the sensitivity of smoldering behavior to each physical parameter (↑ = increase, ↓ = decrease): | Parameter | Increase Effect on Velocity | Increase Effect on Peak Temperature | Effect on Extinction Risk | Mechanism | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Oxygen Concentration ($Y_{O_2}$)** | ↑↑ Significantly | ↑↑ Significantly | ↓ Decreases | Higher reaction rate, more heat release | | **Air Velocity ($u$)** | ↑↑ Significantly | ↑ Moderate | ↓ Decreases (initially) | Enhanced O₂ supply, convective heat | | **Fuel Porosity ($\phi$)** | ↑↑ (optimum exists) | ↑ Moderate | ↓ (downstream) | Better O₂ access, but more heat loss | | **Moisture Content ($M$)** | ↓↓ Significantly | ↓↓ Significantly | ↑↑ Increases | Heat sink from evaporation | | **Particle Size ($d_p$)** | ↓ (smaller→faster) | ↑ Moderate | ↓ Decreases | Larger surface area for smaller particles | | **Inorganic Content (ash)** | ↓↓ Significantly | ↓ Significantly | ↑↑ Increases | Ash blocks O₂ diffusion | | **Ambient Pressure ($P$)** | ↑↑ Significantly | ↑↑ Significantly | ↓ Decreases | More O₂ mass per unit volume | | **Heat Loss Coefficient ($h$)** | ↓↓ Significantly | ↓↓ Significantly | ↑↑ Increases | Direct energy loss to surroundings | | **Activation Energy ($E_a$)** | ↓ Decreases | — | ↑↑ Increases | More temperature-sensitive reaction | | **Bulk Density ($\rho_s$)** | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Slightly | ↑ Increases | Less oxygen penetration per unit volume | | **Volatile Content** | ↑↑ (preheat effect) | ↑↑ Significantly | ↓ Decreases | Volatiles enhance upstream heat transfer | | **Catalysts (e.g., Cu, K)** | ↑↑ Significantly | ↑↑ Significantly | ↓ Decreases | Lower effective activation energy | ## 3.3 Critical Extinction Diameter (Derivation Sketch) For an infinite cylindrical fuel bed, there exists a **critical diameter** below which smoldering cannot self-sustain because surface heat losses dominate. The criterion is: $$ \frac{\dot{q}_{rxn} \cdot d_{crit}^2}{k_{eff}} \approx \text{Biot number threshold} \approx 4 $$ Solving: $$ d_{crit} \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{k_{eff} \cdot (T_{rxn} - T_{\infty})}{\dot{q}_{rxn}}} $$ For typical char with $k_{eff} = 0.2$ W/m·K, $T_{rxn} - T_{\infty} \approx 600$ K, $\dot{q}_{rxn} \approx 50$ kW/m³: $$ d_{crit} \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.2 \times 600}{50000}} = 2 \sqrt{0.0024} \approx 0.098 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ cm} $$ This explains why thin layers of dust or sawdust cannot sustain smoldering (extinction), while thicker piles can. # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Architecture of the Smoldering Process ```mermaid flowchart TB A[External Heat Source
e.g., Cigarette, Spark, Hot Ash] --> B[Initiation Stage
Localized Ignition] B --> C[Pre-heat Zone
T less than 100 degC
Moisture Evaporation] C --> D[Pyrolysis and Reaction Zone
T equals 450 to 700 degC
Char Formation and Oxidation] D --> E[Char and Ash Zone
T greater than 700 degC
Residual Oxidation] E --> F{Heat Loss vs Heat Release} F -->|Heat Release Dominates| G[Self-Sustaining Smoldering
v_s equals 0.1 to 10 mm per sec] F -->|Heat Loss Dominates| H[Extinction
Smoldering Stops] G --> I{Oxygen Supply Sufficient} I -->|Yes| J[Transition to Flaming
T greater than 1000 degC] I -->|No| K[Continued Smoldering] H --> L[End of Process] J --> M[Flaming Fire] K --> F ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Factors Affecting Smoldering (Hierarchical View) ```mermaid flowchart LR SM[Smoldering Behavior] --> F1[Fuel Properties] SM --> F2[Environmental Conditions] SM --> F3[Geometric Factors] SM --> F4[Chemical Additives] F1 --> F1a[Porosity and Permeability] F1 --> F1b[Moisture Content] F1 --> F1c[Particle Size] F1 --> F1d[Volatile Matter Content] F1 --> F1e[Inorganic Ash Content] F1 --> F1f[Chemical Composition] F2 --> F2a[Oxygen Concentration] F2 --> F2b[Air Flow Velocity] F2 --> F2c[Ambient Temperature] F2 --> F2d[Ambient Pressure] F2 --> F2e[Relative Humidity] F3 --> F3a[Sample Geometry] F3 --> F3b[Sample Thickness] F3 --> F3c[Orientation - Vertical or Horizontal] F3 --> F3d[Boundary Conditions - Insulated or Open] F4 --> F4a[Catalysts - K, Cu, Fe] F4 --> F4b[Inhibitors - Borates, Phosphates] F4 --> F4c[Retardants - Halogenated Compounds] F4 --> F4d[Water and Steam Addition] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Reaction Zone Structure of a Smoldering Wave ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Virgin Fuel Region
T equals T_ambient] --> P2[Pre-heat Zone
T equals 25 to 100 degC
Moisture evaporation] P2 --> P3[Pyrolysis Zone
T equals 200 to 400 degC
Volatile release and char formation] P3 --> P4[Reaction Front
T equals 450 to 700 degC
Char oxidation and heat release] P4 --> P5[Char and Ash Zone
T greater than 700 degC
Residual combustion] P5 --> P6[Inert Ash Region
T decreasing to ambient] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Regime Map for Smoldering vs Flaming vs Extinction ```mermaid flowchart TB ST[Smoldering System] --> R1{Oxygen Supply Level} R1 -->|Low less than 12 percent| EX[Extinction Zone] R1 -->|Moderate 12 to 21 percent| SM[Smoldering Zone] R1 -->|High greater than 21 percent| FL[Flaming Combustion Zone] SM -->|High heat retention| TR[Transition to Flaming] TR --> FL EX -->|Re-ignition possible| SM ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The **Regime Map** above is a high-yield concept. The boundaries between **Extinction**, **Smoldering**, and **Flaming** are determined by oxygen concentration, heat losses, and temperature. The critical oxygen threshold for smoldering is typically **12–16%** by volume. Below this, the reaction cannot sustain itself. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, Q1.a]` **Define smoldering combustion. List any four characteristic features that distinguish it from flaming combustion.** **Model Answer:** Smoldering combustion is a slow, low-temperature, flameless form of combustion that is sustained by the heterogeneous oxidation of a porous solid fuel on its surface, with heat propagating through the solid by conduction, convection, and radiation. **Four distinguishing features:** 1. **Reaction type:** Heterogeneous (solid-gas) in smoldering vs homogeneous (gas-gas) in flaming. 2. **Temperature range:** **450–700 °C** for smoldering vs **1000–2000 °C** for flaming. 3. **Propagation velocity:** Very slow (**0.1–10 mm/s**) for smoldering vs rapid (**0.1–10 m/s**) for flaming. 4. **Oxygen requirement:** Smoldering occurs under **oxygen-limited** conditions, while flaming requires near-stoichiometric or oxygen-rich conditions. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, Q1.b]` **Explain the role of moisture content in determining whether smoldering can be sustained in a porous solid fuel.** **Model Answer:** Moisture content plays a **critical inhibitory role** in smoldering because: 1. **Latent heat consumption:** Water evaporation requires **2257 kJ/kg** of latent heat, which is drawn directly from the heat released by the oxidation reaction. This acts as a thermal sink. 2. **Temperature suppression:** High moisture keeps the local temperature below the **ignition threshold** (~**300–400 °C**) needed for char oxidation, leading to extinction. 3. **Critical moisture content:** For most porous fuels, smoldering cannot be sustained if moisture content exceeds **30–60%** by mass (the exact value depends on fuel porosity and ambient conditions). 4. **Drying front dynamics:** A moisture-induced **drying front** must propagate ahead of the reaction front. If this drying process is too slow, the reaction cannot advance. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that "moisture helps smoldering because water vapor is reactive." This is **incorrect** — water is a strong **thermal sink and inhibitor**. Marks will be deducted. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### ⭐ Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, Q2.a — Internal Choice Option A]` **Q2(a).** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the three characteristic zones of a propagating smoldering wave in a porous solid fuel. Discuss how the propagation velocity depends on (i) oxygen concentration, and (ii) ambient humidity. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** A propagating smoldering wave consists of three distinct zones (from upstream/cool side to downstream/hot side): 1. **Pre-heat/Drying Zone:** Temperature rises from ambient to ~**100 °C**. Moisture evaporates; the solid is warmed by conduction from the reaction zone ahead. 2. **Pyrolysis/Reaction Zone:** Temperature ranges from **400–700 °C**. Char is oxidized heterogeneously with oxygen; maximum heat release occurs here. 3. **Char/Ash Zone:** Temperature > **700 °C**; residual char continues to oxidize; ash accumulates. A schematic representation: ``` ┌─────────────┬─────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┐ │ Virgin │ Pre-heat │ Reaction │ Char/Ash │ │ Fuel │ Zone │ Zone │ Zone │ │ T = T_amb │ T < 100°C │ 400-700°C │ T > 700°C │ └─────────────┴─────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┘ ←─── Direction of propagation ``` **(i) Effect of Oxygen Concentration:** Increasing oxygen concentration increases the **Arrhenius reaction rate** ($C_{O_2}^n$ dependence) and the **adiabatic temperature** $T_{ad}$, which in turn increases $v_s$ as per: $$ v_s \propto Y_{O_2}^n \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_{ad}}\right) $$ **Doubling** atmospheric oxygen can increase the smoldering velocity by **2 to 5 times** in many fuels. Below a **critical $Y_{O_2}$ of ~12–16%**, smoldering extinguishes. **(ii) Effect of Ambient Humidity:** Higher ambient humidity increases the **moisture content** of the fuel and the air, acting as a **thermal sink** through evaporation. This **lowers the local reaction temperature**, reducing the reaction rate and propagation velocity. In extreme cases, sustained smoldering becomes impossible. --- **Q2(b).** Derive the steady-state 1D smoldering propagation velocity expression for a forced-flow system. Show that the velocity is linearly proportional to the superficial gas velocity. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** Refer to **SECTION 3.1** for the full step-by-step derivation. The key steps are: 1. **Steady-state energy balance** in the moving frame: $$ \rho u c_{p,g} (T_{ad} - T_u) = \rho_s v_s \Delta H_{eff} $$ 2. **Solving for $v_s$:** $$ v_s = \frac{\rho c_{p,g} u (T_{ad} - T_u)}{\rho_s \Delta H_{eff}} $$ 3. **Linear dependence on $u$:** All other parameters being constant, $v_s$ is **linearly proportional to the superficial gas velocity $u$**. This is one of the most distinctive features of **forward forced-flow smoldering** and is experimentally validated. 4. **Substituting numerical values** (cellulose char example from §3.1): - $u = 0.05$ m/s, $T_{ad} - T_u = 650$ K, $\rho c_{p,g} = 0.6$ kJ/m³·K (air at 600 °C), $\rho_s \Delta H_{eff} = 1.2 \times 10^7$ kJ/m³. $$ v_s = \frac{600 \times 0.05 \times 650}{1.2 \times 10^7} = 0.0163 \text{ m/s} \approx 16.3 \text{ mm/s} $$ This is consistent with experimental values. --- ### ⭐ Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, Q2.b — Internal Choice Option B]` **Q2(a).** Identify and explain **six major factors** that affect smoldering combustion in porous solid fuels. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | # | Factor | Effect on Smoldering | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Oxygen concentration** | Higher O₂ → faster smoldering; below 12–16% O₂ → extinction | | 2 | **Moisture content** | Higher moisture → slower smoldering; >40% → extinction | | 3 | **Porosity** | Higher porosity → better O₂ access → faster smoldering (up to optimum) | | 4 | **Particle size** | Smaller particles → larger surface area → faster smoldering | | 5 | **Air flow velocity** | Higher $u$ → more O₂ supply → faster forward smoldering | | 6 | **Inorganic (ash) content** | Higher ash → blocks O₂ diffusion → slower smoldering | | 7 | **Ambient temperature** | Higher T_amb → preheating → faster ignition and propagation | | 8 | **Heat losses** | Higher losses → greater extinction risk | --- **Q2(b).** With a suitable diagram, explain the **smoldering-to-flaming transition** phenomenon. Under what conditions is this transition most likely to occur? **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The **smoldering-to-flaming transition (SFT)** occurs when the smoldering reaction zone generates enough heat to ignite the **volatile gases** released by the pyrolysis zone. Once ignited, these volatiles produce a **flame** that can rapidly escalate the fire. **Schematic:** ``` Smoldering Phase Transition Flaming Phase ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Reaction │ ──────────▶ │ Volatile│ ─────▶ │ Flame Front │ │ Zone (T) │ Heat │ Ignition│ Flame │ T > 1000°C │ │ 450-700°C │ Transfer │ T>400°C │ │ High HRR │ └──────────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────────┘ ``` **Conditions favoring SFT:** 1. **High oxygen concentration** (typically > **21%** — oxygen-enriched atmosphere) 2. **High external heat flux** (radiation from nearby fires, hot surfaces) 3. **High volatile content** of the fuel (e.g., polyurethane foam, wood) 4. **Favorable geometry** (fuel pile with chimney effect promoting air inflow) 5. **High porosity** allowing rapid volatile transport 6. **Low heat losses** (insulated surroundings) 7. **Strong forced ventilation** providing abundant oxidizer > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to **omit the volatile ignition step** in explaining SFT. Students often jump directly from smoldering to flaming without explaining that volatiles (from pyrolysis) must first be heated, mixed with air, and ignited. This omission costs **2–3 marks** in ESE evaluation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Smoldering and Factors Affecting Smoldering** - ✅ **Definition:** Smoldering is a slow, low-temperature (**450–700 °C**), flameless, heterogeneous (solid-gas) combustion of porous fuels. - ✅ **Distinguishing features:** Slow velocity (0.1–10 mm/s), high CO yield, low activation energy, oxygen-limited. - ✅ **Three zones of smoldering wave:** Pre-heat/Drying Zone, Pyrolysis/Reaction Zone, Char/Ash Zone. - ✅ **Arrhenius rate law** governs the oxidation kinetics. - ✅ **Linear dependence** of smoldering velocity on superficial gas velocity in forced-flow systems. - ✅ **Critical oxygen threshold:** ~**12–16%** by volume; below this, smoldering extinguishes. - ✅ **Critical moisture content:** Typically > **30–60%** mass → extinction. - ✅ **Six key factors:** Oxygen concentration, moisture, porosity, particle size, air flow, ash content. - ✅ **SFT (smoldering-to-flaming transition):** Requires volatile ignition when surface T exceeds ~**400–500 °C** in the presence of sufficient O₂. - ✅ **Engineering significance:** Major cause of fire fatalities (CO poisoning), industrial hazards (silos, coal piles), and emerging applications (biochar, soil remediation). - ✅ **Damköhler number** $Da$ governs whether smoldering is reaction-limited or transport-limited. - ✅ **Critical diameter** concept: Below a threshold (~10 cm for char), heat losses dominate and smoldering cannot self-sustain. > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions, always combine a **diagram** (3 marks), **theory explanation** (4 marks), and **quantitative derivation/calculation** (7 marks). This is the most reliable scoring strategy in ESE.

topics.title: Backdraft
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Backdraft

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BACKDRAFT (Definition per NFPA 921 & IFE):**
> A **Backdraft** (also termed a *smoke explosion* or *deflagration ventilation event*) is a rapid, explosive combustion phenomenon that occurs when **unburned, superheated, pyrolyzed fire gases** accumulated within a confined or under-ventilated compartment are suddenly introduced to a fresh supply of **oxygen (O₂)** through an unplanned or improperly executed ventilation opening. The result is a near-instantaneous deflagration that propagates through the pre-mixed fuel-rich atmosphere.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Theory of Fire Propagation and Dynamics**, a backdraft is classified as a **ventilation-controlled fire phenomenon** — distinct from a flashover — and is governed by the principles of **thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, and combustion chemistry**.

### 1.1.1 Key Operational Parameters (Standard Metrics)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Upper Gas Layer Temperature** | **593 °C – 1100 °C** (1100 °F – 2000 °F) | Indicates sufficient pre-heating for ignition upon oxygen contact |
| **Smoke Density (Optical)** | **Light to Moderate** (color may be yellow/grey/white) | A deceptive indicator — smoke may *appear* to clear just before backdraft |
| **Oxygen Concentration at Floor** | **< 16 % by volume** | Critically oxygen-depleted atmosphere |
| **Pressure Differential (Pre-Vent)** | **Slight positive pressure** at higher openings | Smoke may be "pulsing" or "breathing" at openings |
| **Critical Ignition Energy** | **0.2 mJ – 1.0 mJ** (typical for hot fuel-air mixture) | Extremely low — even static spark can trigger event |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressurised Pressure Cooker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a **pressure cooker** that has been heating for a long time on the stove. Inside, the air is superheated and filled with vapour (analogous to **pyrolysed, unburned fuel gases**). A safety valve keeps the cooker sealed. The moment you crack the lid open (the **ventilation opening**), a sudden inrush of fresh air (oxygen) meets the superheated fuel vapours, causing a violent whoosh of flame. A backdraft behaves identically — except the lid is a closed door or window of a burning room, and the "vapour" is a lethal cocktail of carbon monoxide, hydrogen cyanide, methane, and unburned hydrocarbons.

### 1.3 The Distinguishing Difference: Backdraft vs. Flashover

> [!CAUTION]
> This distinction is a **favourite KTU question**. Do not confuse:
> - **Flashover** → *Fuel-controlled*, simultaneous ignition of *all* exposed surfaces in a compartment (radiation-dominated).
> - **Backdraft** → *Ventilation-controlled*, sudden ignition of *accumulated gases* upon oxygen introduction (convection + deflagration).

### 1.4 The Fire Triangle / Tetrahedron Context

For combustion to occur, the following must coexist:
- **Fuel** (hydrocarbon gases/vapours)
- **Oxidiser** (O₂ ≥ ~16 % under standard conditions)
- **Heat** (≥ **500 °C** ignition temperature for most pyrolysates)
- *(Tetrahedron adds:)* **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction**

In a backdraft, **Fuel + Heat** exist abundantly. **Oxygen is the missing reactant**. The moment O₂ is introduced, all four tetrahedron components are satisfied simultaneously, and a deflagration ensues.

---

## 1.5 The Five Classic Warning Signs of a Backdraft

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise these — KTU frequently tests the indicators of backdraft:**
>
> 1. **Smoke "pulsing" or "breathing"** at door/window cracks (visible pressure cycling).
> 2. **Smoke appearing to be drawn back into the structure** (negative pressure pull).
> 3. **Yellow, grey, or unusually light-coloured smoke** (incomplete combustion signature).
> 4. **Black, sooty deposits** around door/window frames (condensed heavy hydrocarbons).
> 5. **Confined, hot fire with little or no visible flame** inside the compartment.
> 6. **Audible whistling or rushing sounds** at openings (gas being forced through narrow gaps).
> 7. **High heat felt on the closed door** (telltale of superheated gas layer above).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Backdraft Development — A Structured Framework

The evolution of a backdraft can be broken down into **five sequential stages**. Each stage must be understood for both short-answer and 14-mark KTU questions.

### Stage 1 — Sealed Compartment Fire Initiation
- Fire begins in a **closed/under-ventilated room**.
- Available O₂ is consumed rapidly (drops from **20.9 % → < 16 %**).
- Combustion becomes **fuel-rich (vitiation regime)**.

### Stage 2 — Pyrolysis & Gas Accumulation
- With O₂ insufficient, fuel surfaces undergo **pyrolysis** (thermal decomposition).
- Volatile pyrolysates accumulate in the **upper gas layer**.
- Layer temperature rises to **593 °C – 1100 °C**.

### Stage 3 — Pressure & Composition Equilibrium
- The compartment reaches a quasi-steady state.
- Slight positive pressure builds due to **thermal expansion of gases**.
- **CO, HCN, CH₄, H₂, and higher hydrocarbons** dominate.
- Free O₂ concentration: **~ 0 % to 16 %**.

### Stage 4 — Ventilation Event (Trigger)
- A door is opened, a window breaks, or firefighters ventilate.
- **Pressure differential** drives fresh air into the lower portion of the compartment.
- O₂ mixes with superheated fuel-rich layer.

### Stage 5 — Deflagration
- Mixture auto-ignites or is ignited by an external source.
- **Deflagration wave** propagates through the compartment.
- **Overpressure** can reach **10 kPa to 40 kPa** — sufficient to blow out windows and walls.

---

## 2.2 Governing Thermodynamic Principles

### 2.2.1 Ideal Gas Law Application (Pre-Ventilation State)

The accumulated gases obey the ideal gas relation:

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume of compartment (m³)
- $n$ = moles of gas
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.2.2 Mass Conservation During Inflow

For an opening of area $A_o$ during ventilation, the mass flow rate of incoming air is governed by:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{air} \cdot \Delta P}$$

where:
- $C_d$ = **discharge coefficient** (~ 0.6 – 0.65 for typical door/window openings)
- $\rho_{air}$ = density of ambient air (~ **1.225 kg/m³** at 15 °C, 1 atm)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference across opening (Pa)

### 2.2.3 Energy Released During Deflagration

The total heat release rate during backdraft deflagration is:

$$Q_{total} = \int_{V} \rho_{mix} \cdot Y_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c \, dV$$

where:
- $\rho_{mix}$ = density of pre-mixed fuel-air layer (kg/m³)
- $Y_{fuel}$ = mass fraction of fuel in mixture
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (J/kg) — for typical pyrolysates ≈ **20 – 45 MJ/kg**

### 2.2.4 Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) — The Critical Threshold

A backdraft only occurs if the accumulated gases lie **above the LFL** once mixed with incoming air:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$$

where $\phi$ is the **equivalence ratio**. Backdraft typically requires $\phi \geq 1$ (fuel-rich) to be present in the upper layer, while the incoming air brings $\phi$ toward stoichiometric ($\phi \approx 1$).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table — All formulas you need for Backdraft problems:**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P V = n R T$ | $P$: Pa, $V$: m³, $n$: mol, $T$: K | Pre-ventilation gas state |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{in} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | $\dot{m}$: kg/s, $C_d \approx 0.6$ | Air inflow through opening |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | $Q$: W, $\Delta H_c$: J/kg | Heat release from deflagration |
| 4 | $\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Equivalence ratio |
| 5 | $\rho = \frac{P M}{R T}$ | $M$: kg/mol | Gas density vs. temperature |
| 6 | $T_{ad} = T_i + \frac{Y_f \Delta H_c}{c_p}$ | $T$: K, $c_p$: J/(kg·K) | Adiabatic flame temperature estimate |
| 7 | $P_{over} = \frac{n_{prod} R T_{ad}}{V} - P_{amb}$ | Pa | Peak overpressure estimate |
| 8 | $LFL_{CH_4} = 5.0 \,\%$, $LFL_{CO} = 12.5 \,\%$, $LFL_{H_2} = 4.0 \,\%$ | % by volume in air | Critical flammability limits |
| 9 | $v_{def} = \sqrt{\frac{R T}{\gamma M} \cdot \frac{2 \gamma^2}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | m/s | Deflagration velocity (Chapman-Jouguet-like) |

> **CRITICAL SYLLABUS NUMBERS TO MEMORISE:**
> - LFL of **methane (CH₄) = 5.0 %**
> - LFL of **carbon monoxide (CO) = 12.5 %**
> - LFL of **hydrogen (H₂) = 4.0 %**
> - Autoignition of **typical pyrolysates ≈ 500 °C – 600 °C**
> - **O₂ threshold** for sustained combustion: **≈ 16 %**

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive** and **algebraically complete**. No steps are skipped. Suitable for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Overpressure Inside a Confined Compartment During Backdraft

### Problem Setup
A sealed compartment of volume $V$ contains pyrolysed gases at temperature $T_i$ and pressure $P_i$. An opening admits ambient air at $T_{\infty}$ and pressure $P_{\infty}$. The mixture undergoes **adiabatic, isochoric** combustion. Derive the peak overpressure.

### Step 1 — Initial State of the Compartment
Before ventilation, the hot gas layer obeys the ideal gas law:

$$P_i V = n_i R T_i$$

Solving for the initial moles present:

$$n_i = \frac{P_i V}{R T_i}$$

**Textual Logic:** This counts all gaseous moles (mostly pyrolysates + residual N₂ + trace O₂) occupying the compartment before the event.

### Step 2 — Adiabatic Combustion Assumption
We assume **constant-volume adiabatic combustion** (worst-case, no venting of products). Energy conservation gives:

$$n_i \int_{T_i}^{T_{ad}} c_{v,m} \, dT = -n_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c,m}$$

where:
- $c_{v,m}$ = molar specific heat at constant volume (J/(mol·K))
- $\Delta H_{c,m}$ = molar heat of combustion (J/mol, negative for exothermic)

For a constant $c_{v,m}$ (idealisation):

$$n_i c_{v,m} (T_{ad} - T_i) = -n_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c,m}$$

**Textual Logic:** Heat released by combustion is absorbed entirely by all gas products, raising temperature from $T_i$ to $T_{ad}$.

### Step 3 — Expressing the Final State
After combustion, total moles of gas products $n_f$ occupy volume $V$ at temperature $T_{ad}$:

$$P_f V = n_f R T_{ad}$$

Therefore:

$$P_f = \frac{n_f R T_{ad}}{V}$$

### Step 4 — Peak Overpressure
The peak overpressure is the difference between the final combustion pressure and the ambient pressure:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{peak} = P_f - P_{\infty} = \frac{n_f R T_{ad}}{V} - P_{\infty}}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Approximation
If we approximate $n_f \approx n_i$ (combustion conserves moles for many hydrocarbons), and substitute $T_{ad} = T_i + \dfrac{n_{fuel} \Delta H_{c,m}}{n_i c_{v,m}}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{peak} \approx \frac{n_i R}{V} \left( T_i + \frac{n_{fuel} (-\Delta H_{c,m})}{n_i c_{v,m}} \right) - P_{\infty}}$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Illustration

Given:
- $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$ (small bedroom-sized compartment)
- $T_i = 800 \text{ °C} = 1073 \text{ K}$
- $T_{\infty} = 27 \text{ °C} = 300 \text{ K}$
- $P_{\infty} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- Fuel: methane (CH₄), $\Delta H_{c,m} = -802 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $Y_{CH_4} = 0.05$ (mass fraction in upper layer)
- $c_{v,m} \approx 25 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ (approx. for hot combustion products)

Compute $n_i$:

$$n_i = \frac{P_i V}{R T_i} = \frac{101325 \times 50}{8.314 \times 1073}$$

$$n_i = \frac{5.066 \times 10^6}{8921.9} \approx 567.9 \text{ mol}$$

Compute $n_{fuel}$ (assuming 5 % of total moles are CH₄):

$$n_{fuel} = 0.05 \times 567.9 \approx 28.4 \text{ mol}$$

Compute $T_{ad}$:

$$T_{ad} = 1073 + \frac{28.4 \times 802000}{567.9 \times 25}$$

$$T_{ad} = 1073 + \frac{2.278 \times 10^7}{14198} = 1073 + 1604.5 = 2677.5 \text{ K}$$

Compute $P_f$ (assuming $n_f \approx n_i$):

$$P_f = \frac{567.9 \times 8.314 \times 2677.5}{50} = \frac{1.264 \times 10^7}{50} \approx 2.528 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

Compute $\Delta P_{peak}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{peak} = 252800 - 101325 = 151.5 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A peak overpressure of **~ 1.5 atm** is sufficient to **blow out standard window glass and structurally damage lightweight partitions**. This shows why backdrafts are among the most dangerous ventilation events firefighters encounter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time Required for Critical Oxygen Influx

The air inflow through a door opening of area $A_o$ under pressure differential $\Delta P$ is:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_{air} \Delta P}$$

For a 1 m × 2 m door ($A_o = 2 \text{ m}^2$), $C_d = 0.6$, $\Delta P = 50 \text{ Pa}$ (typical):

$$\dot{m}_{in} = 0.6 \times 2 \times \sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 50}$$

$$\dot{m}_{in} = 1.2 \times \sqrt{122.5} = 1.2 \times 11.07 = 13.28 \text{ kg/s}$$

The volume of air required to bring compartment O₂ from 0 % to 16 % in a 50 m³ space:

$$V_{air} = \frac{0.16 \times 50}{0.21} = 38.1 \text{ m}^3$$

At air density 1.225 kg/m³, mass needed:

$$m_{air} = 38.1 \times 1.225 = 46.7 \text{ kg}$$

Time to reach critical O₂ concentration:

$$\boxed{t_{crit} = \frac{m_{air}}{\dot{m}_{in}} = \frac{46.7}{13.28} \approx 3.5 \text{ s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why backdrafts occur with **little warning** — once a door is opened, critical oxygen concentration can be reached in **3 – 5 seconds**. This is the engineering basis for indirect ventilation tactics in firefighting.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Differentiating Fire Phenomena

| Parameter | Flashover | Backdraft | Smoke Explosion (True) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ventilation State** | Adequate | Severely restricted | Confined pre-mixed layer |
| **O₂ in Hot Zone** | > 16 % | < 16 % | Variable |
| **Smoke Colour** | Black, dense | Light grey/yellow/white | Yellow-orange |
| **Pressure Signs** | Minimal | Pulsing at openings | None typically |
| **Trigger** | Heat radiation reaching floor | Sudden O₂ introduction | Spark/ignition source |
| **Propagation** | Surface-to-surface | Deflagration wave | Detonation potential |
| **Speed** | Seconds (slow build) | Sub-second | Sub-second to instant |
| **Acoustic Signature** | Rollover, no blast | Loud **WHOOSH** | Sharp **BANG** |
