# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1
**Q: Define "Backdraft" in fire dynamics. Differentiate it from flashover in two key aspects.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A backdraft is a **rapid explosive combustion of accumulated superheated pyrolysed gases** that occurs when a sudden introduction of fresh air (oxygen) into an oxygen-deficient, ventilation-controlled compartment creates an ignitable fuel-air mixture.

**Two Key Differences from Flashover:**

| Aspect | Backdraft | Flashover |
|---|---|---|
| Controlling Factor | Ventilation-controlled | Fuel-controlled |
| Mechanism | Sudden O₂ influx ignites pre-mixed gases | Radiative heat ignites all exposed surfaces |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two differentiating aspects = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**Q: List any FIVE visible indicators that warn a firefighter of an impending backdraft.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Smoke "pulsing" or "breathing"** at cracks around doors and windows. *(0.6 marks)*
2. **Smoke drawn back into the structure** (indicating negative pressure inside). *(0.6 marks)*
3. **Yellow, grey, or unusually light-coloured smoke** (incomplete combustion). *(0.6 marks)*
4. **Black sooty deposits** around openings. *(0.6 marks)*
5. **Audible whistling or air-rushing sounds** at gaps. *(0.6 marks)*

*(Total 5 × 0.6 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> **Note:** KTU ESE convention provides **internal choice** between two questions on the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choose this OR Question B

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **five-stage development process** of a backdraft. Discuss the **thermodynamic and chemical conditions** that must be satisfied at each stage. *(7 marks)*

**Q: (b)** Derive an expression for the **peak overpressure** generated inside a sealed compartment of volume V during a backdraft event. Using this expression, compute the peak overpressure for a 60 m³ compartment containing methane-rich pyrolysates with 5 % fuel mole fraction, initial temperature 900 °C, and methane heat of combustion 802 kJ/mol. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Stage 1: Sealed Fire Initiation (1 mark)**
A fire begins in a closed compartment. Combustion rapidly consumes available O₂. The atmosphere becomes oxygen-depleted (O₂ < 16 %).

**Stage 2: Pyrolysis and Gas Accumulation (1.5 marks)**
With insufficient oxygen, fuel surfaces undergo **pyrolysis**, releasing volatile hydrocarbons. These accumulate in the **upper hot gas layer**, reaching temperatures of **593 °C – 1100 °C**. Major species: CO, HCN, CH₄, H₂, and higher hydrocarbons.

**Stage 3: Pressure and Composition Equilibrium (1 mark)**
The compartment reaches a quasi-steady state. Slight **positive pressure** develops due to thermal expansion. The mixture is highly fuel-rich.

**Stage 4: Ventilation Event (1.5 marks)**
A door is opened or window breaks. Fresh air rushes in due to pressure differential. O₂ mixes with the superheated fuel-rich layer. A pre-mixed ignitable atmosphere forms.

**Stage 5: Deflagration (2 marks)**
Upon auto-ignition (~ 500 °C) or an external spark, **deflagration** propagates through the pre-mixture. Peak overpressure of **10 – 40 kPa** develops, blowing out windows and ejecting fireballs.

*(Schematic from Section 4.1 should accompany this answer for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State initial conditions (1 mark):**
The pre-ventilation gas state obeys:
$$n_i = \frac{P_i V}{R T_i}$$

**Step 2 — Apply energy conservation under constant-volume combustion (2 marks):**
$$n_i c_{v,m} (T_{ad} - T_i) = -n_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c,m}$$
Solving for $T_{ad}$:
$$T_{ad} = T_i + \frac{n_{fuel} (-\Delta H_{c,m})}{n_i c_{v,m}}$$

**Step 3 — Express final pressure (1 mark):**
$$P_f = \frac{n_f R T_{ad}}{V}$$

**Step 4 — Peak overpressure (1 mark):**
$$\Delta P_{peak} = P_f - P_{\infty} = \frac{n_f R T_{ad}}{V} - P_{\infty}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Computation (2 marks):**
Given $V = 60 \text{ m}^3$, $T_i = 900 + 273 = 1173 \text{ K}$, $P_i = P_{\infty} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$, $\Delta H_{c,m} = 802000 \text{ J/mol}$, $c_{v,m} = 25 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$:

$$n_i = \frac{101325 \times 60}{8.314 \times 1173} = \frac{6.08 \times 10^6}{9752} \approx 623.4 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{fuel} = 0.05 \times 623.4 = 31.17 \text{ mol}$$

$$T_{ad} = 1173 + \frac{31.17 \times 802000}{623.4 \times 25} = 1173 + \frac{2.5 \times 10^7}{15585} = 1173 + 1604 = 2777 \text{ K}$$

$$P_f = \frac{623.4 \times 8.314 \times 2777}{60} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^7}{60} \approx 2.398 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{peak} = 239800 - 101325 = 138.5 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Many students forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin when computing $n_i$. Always use **absolute temperature in Kelvin** in the ideal gas equation. Losing this conversion will cost you **1 mark** immediately.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **physics of smoke behaviour** in a pre-backdraft compartment, including the layering phenomenon, the role of **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)**, and why the smoke may appear light-coloured. *(7 marks)*

**Q: (b)** A 2 m × 1 m door is opened in a sealed compartment with internal pressure 200 Pa below ambient. Calculate: (i) the **mass flow rate** of air entering the compartment, and (ii) the **time required** to deliver enough oxygen to raise the compartment O₂ from 5 % to 16 %. Assume compartment volume 75 m³, $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Smoke Layering (2 marks):**
In a sealed compartment, hot combustion products rise due to buoyancy, forming a distinct **two-layer stratification**:
- **Upper Layer:** Hot (600 – 1100 °C), fuel-rich, low O₂
- **Lower Layer:** Cooler, relatively fresh air (until vitiation progresses)

The interface descends as the upper layer grows.

**Role of LFL (3 marks):**
The **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** is the minimum fuel concentration in air that supports combustion. For methane it is **5.0 %**, for CO it is **12.5 %**, for H₂ it is **4.0 %**. In a pre-backdraft compartment, accumulated gases are typically **above LFL** but cannot burn due to O₂ deficiency. Upon ventilation, the mixing zone achieves both LFL and sufficient O₂, satisfying the fire tetrahedron.

**Why Smoke Appears Light (2 marks):**
Normally, black smoke indicates **fuel-rich, oxygen-starved combustion** with abundant soot. In a pre-backdraft scenario, the smoke appears **yellow, grey, or white** because:
- Combustion has already self-extinguished due to O₂ depletion
- The visible "smoke" is mostly **pyrolysed, unburned volatiles** (not soot)
- These vapours reflect light differently, producing lighter hues

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Door area (0.5 marks):**
$$A_o = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate (3 marks):**
$$\dot{m}_{in} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_{air} \Delta P}$$
$$\dot{m}_{in} = 0.62 \times 2 \times \sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 200}$$
$$\dot{m}_{in} = 1.24 \times \sqrt{490} = 1.24 \times 22.14 = 27.45 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Required O₂ mass (2 marks):**
O₂ needed to raise from 5 % to 16 % in 75 m³:
$$\Delta \text{O}_2 \text{ fraction} = 0.16 - 0.05 = 0.11$$
$$\text{O}_2 \text{ volume needed} = 0.11 \times 75 = 8.25 \text{ m}^3$$
$$\text{Air volume needed} = \frac{8.25}{0.21} = 39.29 \text{ m}^3$$
$$\text{Air mass needed} = 39.29 \times 1.225 = 48.13 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Time to critical O₂ (1.5 marks):**
$$t_{crit} = \frac{m_{air}}{\dot{m}_{in}} = \frac{48.13}{27.45} = 1.75 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{crit} \approx 1.75 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** The most common error is using the **pressure differential incorrectly** — some students use 200 Pa as gauge pressure on the wrong side. Recall: $\Delta P$ is the **absolute difference** between outside (higher) and inside (lower) pressure. Mis-signing this will yield an incorrect square root. Deducted: **1.5 marks**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Backdraft**

- 🔥 **Definition:** Explosive combustion of accumulated superheated gases when O₂ is suddenly introduced into a sealed/under-ventilated compartment.
- 🔥 **Type:** **Ventilation-controlled** fire phenomenon (NOT fuel-controlled like flashover).
- 🔥 **Key Indicator Temperatures:** Upper gas layer reaches **593 °C – 1100 °C**.
- 🔥 **O₂ Threshold:** Combustion ceases below **~ 16 %** O₂; backdraft possible when O₂ falls below this.
- 🔥 **Critical LFL Values:** CH₄ = **5.0 %**, CO = **12.5 %**, H₂ = **4.0 %**.
- 🔥 **Five Stages:** Initiation → Pyrolysis → Equilibrium → Ventilation → Deflagration.
- 🔥 **Peak Overpressure Range:** **10 – 40 kPa** — sufficient to shatter glass and damage walls.
- 🔥 **Critical Time to O₂ Influx:** **3 – 5 seconds** after a door/window is opened.
- 🔥 **Telltale Signs:** Pulsing smoke, light-coloured smoke, soot at openings, high door heat, whistling sounds.
- 🔥 **Fire Tetrahedron:** Backdraft occurs when O₂ (the missing side) is supplied and the chain reaction is enabled.
- 🔥 **Tactical Response:** **NEVER** open directly; use indirect ventilation; cool the gas layer with water fog.
- 🔥 **Governing Equations:** Ideal Gas Law ($PV = nRT$); Mass Inflow ($\dot{m} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2\rho \Delta P}$); Peak Overpressure ($\Delta P = \frac{n_f R T_{ad}}{V} - P_\infty$).
- 🔥 **Always convert °C → K** when using $PV = nRT$.
- 🔥 **NFPA Reference:** NFPA 921 (Guide for Fire & Explosion Investigations) classifies backdraft separately from flashover.

> **END OF TOPIC NOTES — BACKDRAFT | PESFT528 | KTU 2024 SCHEME | MODULE 1**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoke
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoke

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — SMOKE: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / NFPA / ISO Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Smoke Definition (NFPA 92B / SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering):**
> **Smoke** is the airborne solid and liquid particulates (predominantly carbonaceous soot, tars, and condensed moisture) and **gaseous products of incomplete combustion** that, together with the entrained air, form a visible (or partially visible) buoyant column rising above and spreading from a fire source.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, smoke is treated as a **two-phase airborne suspension** consisting of:
1. A **continuous gas phase** (entrained air + combustion gases such as CO, CO₂, HCN, HCl, NOₓ, water vapour).
2. A **dispersed particulate phase** (soot agglomerates, aerosols, condensed hydrocarbon droplets).

The particulate phase is what makes smoke **optically active** (i.e., able to obscure light), while the gas phase is responsible for its **toxicity, corrosivity, and thermal content**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Smoke is not just a nuisance. In fire safety engineering, smoke is the **dominant cause of fire fatalities** (>70% globally, per NFPA fire death statistics), primarily because of **toxic gas inhalation** and **disorientation due to loss of visibility**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a **fuming piece of burning incense stick** in a closed room:
- The visible grey trail is the **particulate phase** (suspended soot/aerosols) — this is what **blocks light**.
- The invisible hot gases riding along with the trail are the **gas phase** — this is what **carries heat and poisons**.
- The whole mixture **rises** (because it is hot and buoyant), spreads along the ceiling, and gradually **sinks** as it cools.

In an actual fire, this same incense phenomenon is **magnified by a factor of thousands**, and instead of smelling pleasant, the gas phase carries **carbon monoxide (CO)** and **hydrogen cyanide (HCN)** — both lethal at sufficient concentration.

## 1.3 The Three Engineering "Threats" Posed by Smoke

| Threat | Physical Mechanism | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Toxic Threat** | CO, HCN, reduced O₂ | LC₅₀ concentration (ppm·min) |
| **Visibility Threat** | Light extinction by soot | Optical Density $D_o$, Visibility $V$ (m) |
| **Thermal Threat** | Hot smoke burns the upper layer | Layer temperature $T_s$, Radiative flux $\dot{q}''$ |

## 1.4 The Five Fundamental Properties of Smoke (KTU Board Favourite)

1. **Smoke Yield ($y_s$)** — kg of smoke per kg of fuel burned.
2. **Optical Density ($D_o$)** — measure of light obscuration.
3. **Specific Extinction Area ($\sigma_s$)** — $\text{m}^2$ of light blocked per kg of smoke.
4. **Visibility ($V$)** — distance at which a reference sign can be seen through smoke.
5. **Smoke Production Rate ($\dot{m}_s$)** — kg of smoke generated per second.

These five properties are the **only quantities a KTU examiner will ask numerical questions on** for Module 1 smoke.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of Smoke (Qualitative Analysis)

Smoke is a complex **aerosol** produced by **incomplete combustion**. The composition depends on:
- Fuel chemistry (C/H/O ratio)
- Local equivalence ratio ($\phi$)
- Temperature history
- Oxygen availability

**Solid Phase (Particulates):**
- **Soot (elemental carbon)** — typically **0.1 – 1 μm** diameter agglomerates.
- **Tar aerosols** — high-molecular-weight condensed hydrocarbons.
- **Mineral ash** (for solid fuels).
- **Droplets** of condensed water and partially combusted fuel.

**Gas Phase (Gaseous Species, by approximate descending order of toxicity):**
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — primary toxicant, target for $\text{Hb}$ binding.
- **Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN)** — produced from N-containing polymers (wool, silk, PU, ABS, nylon).
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — asphyxiant + respiratory stimulant.
- **Hydrogen Chloride (HCl)** — from PVC combustion, causes pulmonary oedema.
- **Hydrogen Fluoride (HF), NOₓ, SO₂, acrolein, formaldehyde** — irritants.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question: *"Why is CO the dominant cause of smoke inhalation deaths?"* — Because it has an affinity for haemoglobin that is **200–250 times** that of O₂, and it produces **carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)** which cannot transport oxygen.

## 2.2 Soot Formation Mechanism (The Pyrolysis–Soot Chain)

The formation of soot particles in a diffusion flame proceeds through the following sequence:

- **Step 1 — Fuel Pyrolysis:** Parent fuel thermally decomposes into smaller hydrocarbon radicals (CH₃·, C₂H₂, etc.).
- **Step 2 — Nucleation:** Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) — primarily **pyrene, coronene** — form via HACA (H-Abstraction–C₂H₂-Addition) mechanism.
- **Step 3 — Coagulation:** Primary particles (~10 nm) collide and form fractal aggregates (~0.1–1 μm).
- **Step 4 — Surface Growth & Oxidation:** Aggregates grow by surface HACA addition, while incipient soot may be oxidized to CO by OH· radicals (the **soot–oxidation battle** determines the **smoke point** of a flame).
- **Step 5 — Aggregation:** Final soot agglomerates detach from the flame and become airborne smoke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoke Point** is a flame property: it is the **minimum fuel flow rate at which a luminous (sooting) tip first appears** on a diffusion flame. Fuels with **low smoke point = sooty fuels** (e.g., toluene, benzene). **High smoke point = clean fuels** (methane, methanol).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Smoke (Module 1)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Smoke Yield** | $y_s = \dfrac{m_{smoke}}{m_{fuel}}$ | kg/kg | 0.005 – 0.20 |
| 2 | **Specific Extinction Area** | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{A_{blocked}}{m_{smoke}}$ | m²/kg | 50 – 1500 (wood ≈ 100, PU ≈ 1100) |
| 3 | **Extinction Coefficient** | $K = \sigma_s \cdot \rho_s$ | m⁻¹ | 0.1 – 10 |
| 4 | **Optical Density (per path length)** | $D_o = K \cdot L = \ln \left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)$ | dimensionless | 0.1 – 5 |
| 5 | **Specific Optical Density** (NIST chamber) | $D_s = \dfrac{V}{A L} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) \approx 132 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | dimensionless | 1 – 1000 |
| 6 | **Visibility Through Smoke** | $V = \dfrac{C}{K} = \dfrac{C}{\sigma_s \cdot \rho_s}$ | m | 1 – 30 m |
| 7 | **Smoke Mass Production Rate** | $\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | kg/s | — |
| 8 | **Smoke Volume Production Rate** | $\dot{V}_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s}$ | m³/s | — |
| 9 | **Layer Descent Velocity (Smoke Filling)** | $v_z = \dfrac{\dot{V}_s}{A_{floor}}$ | m/s | 0.01 – 0.3 |
| 10 | **Convective Heat Release Rate** | $\dot{Q}_c = \chi \cdot \dot{Q}$ (where $\chi \approx 0.6 - 0.7$) | kW | — |
| 11 | **Buoyancy Flux of Fire Plume** | $B = \dfrac{g \cdot \dot{Q}_c}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty}$ | m⁴/s³ | — |
| 12 | **Layer Interface Height (Mass Balance)** | $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = -\dfrac{\dot{V}_s}{A_{floor}}$ | m/s | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical constant — Visibility Coefficient $C$:**
> - For **reflective / photoluminescent signs**: $C \approx 3$ m
> - For **luminous / illuminated signs**: $C \approx 5$ m
> - For **familiar building features (doors, walls)**: $C \approx 8$ m
> This is a **favourite KTU numerical question**.

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Fire Engineering

- **Visibility formulas** dictate **wayfinding signage placement** (NFPA 101 means of egress design).
- **Optical density equations** are the basis of **smoke detector design** (photoelectric detectors use the Lambert-Beer law internally).
- **Smoke yield tables** are inputs to **zone smoke models** (CFAST, OZone) and **CFD models** (FDS) for performance-based design.
- **Buoyancy flux** governs **smoke management / smoke exhaust system sizing** (NFPA 92B).

## 2.5 Optical Density — Theoretical Foundation (Lambert–Beer Law)

The **extinction of a parallel light beam** through a smoke-laden medium is governed by the **Lambert–Beer law**:

- The fractional reduction in intensity over a path length $dL$ is proportional to the local extinction coefficient $K(x)$:

$$\dfrac{dI}{I} = -K(x)\,dL$$

- Integrating over a uniform smoke layer of thickness $L$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I}\dfrac{dI}{I} = -K\int_{0}^{L} dL$$

- This yields the fundamental **transmission equation**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-K L}$$

- Taking the natural logarithm gives the **optical density per unit path length**:

$$D_o = K \cdot L = \ln\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> $D_o$ is **dimensionless** because $K$ has units m⁻¹ and $L$ has units m. In NIST smoke chamber tests, the **specific optical density** $D_s$ is used because it normalizes the test geometry: $D_s = (V / (A L)) \log_{10}(I_0/I)$, where $V$ is chamber volume, $A$ is heated area, $L$ is light path length. For the standard NIST chamber, $V/(A L) \approx 132$, which is why the coefficient "132" appears in many smoke problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Visibility and Mass Concentration of Smoke

**Given:** A compartment of length $L$ contains smoke of uniform mass concentration $\rho_s$ (kg/m³). The smoke has a known specific extinction area $\sigma_s$ (m²/kg). A light source of intensity $I_0$ is observed through the layer, and the transmitted intensity is $I$.

**To Prove:** $V = C / (\sigma_s \rho_s)$ for visibility $V$ through the smoke.

**Step 1:** Start with the **extinction coefficient** definition. The extinction coefficient $K$ is the cross-sectional area of smoke per unit volume of smoke-cloud (units: m² of light blockage per m³ of cloud, i.e., m⁻¹). It is the product of specific extinction area and mass concentration:

$$K = \sigma_s \cdot \rho_s$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Lambert–Beer law** to find the optical density over a path of length $L$:

$$D_o = K \cdot L = \sigma_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot L$$

**Step 3:** By definition, an occupant can see an object when the **transmitted intensity $I$** is greater than or equal to the **contrast threshold** $C_{th}$ times the emitted or reflected intensity. Mathematically, the visibility $V$ is the path length over which the contrast $C$ is reduced to the threshold $C_{th}$.

**Step 4:** The contrast $C$ of a target decreases exponentially with optical density:

$$C = C_0 \cdot e^{-K L}$$

**Step 5:** Setting $C = C_{th}$ (the minimum visible contrast, typically 0.01 to 0.1) and $L = V$ (the visibility distance):

$$C_{th} = C_0 \cdot e^{-K V}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $V$:

$$V = -\dfrac{1}{K} \ln\left(\dfrac{C_{th}}{C_0}\right)$$

**Step 7:** The logarithm term is absorbed into a single empirically determined constant $C$ (units of metres, *not* concentration):

$$V = \dfrac{C}{K}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $K = \sigma_s \rho_s$:

$$\boxed{\,V = \dfrac{C}{\sigma_s \cdot \rho_s}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked KTU formula in Module 1**. The constant $C$ is *not* a contrast — it is an empirically fitted coefficient that depends on the **type of sign being viewed through the smoke**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Smoke Layer Descent (Mass Balance in a Compartment)

**Given:** A fire of smoke volume flow rate $\dot{V}_s$ (m³/s) discharges into a compartment of floor area $A_f$ (m²) and ceiling height $H$ (m). The smoke accumulates as a hot upper layer of thickness $(H - z)$ where $z$ is the **clear height (interface) measured from the floor**.

**Step 1:** Assume that the smoke is hot and therefore does not mix significantly with the cool lower layer (this is the **two-zone assumption** used in CFAST / OZone).

**Step 2:** The total mass of smoke in the upper layer is:

$$m_s(t) = \rho_s \cdot A_f \cdot (H - z(t))$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to time to get the rate of change of mass:

$$\dfrac{dm_s}{dt} = -\rho_s \cdot A_f \cdot \dfrac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Mass conservation requires the rate of smoke mass entering the layer equals the rate of accumulation:

$$\dot{m}_s = -\rho_s \cdot A_f \cdot \dfrac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\dot{V}_s = \dot{m}_s / \rho_s$ (volume flow = mass flow / density):

$$\rho_s \cdot \dot{V}_s = -\rho_s \cdot A_f \cdot \dfrac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\rho_s$ and rearrange for the layer interface descent velocity:

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{dz}{dt} = -\dfrac{\dot{V}_s}{A_f}\,}$$

**Step 7:** The time for the smoke interface to descend from the ceiling ($z = H$) to a height $z$ is obtained by separation of variables:

$$dt = -\dfrac{A_f}{\dot{V}_s}\,dz$$

$$t = \dfrac{A_f}{\dot{V}_s}(H - z)$$

**Step 8:** The **time to reach floor level** ($z = 0$, full smoke filling) is therefore:

$$t_{fill} = \dfrac{A_f \cdot H}{\dot{V}_s} = \dfrac{V_{compartment}}{\dot{V}_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **real compartment with a leakage path (door / vent)** of area $A_v$ and discharge coefficient $C_d$, the filling time becomes longer. The corrected expression used in SFPE/NFPA 92B is:
>
> $t_{fill} \approx \dfrac{V_{compartment}}{\dot{V}_s + 0.5 C_d A_v \sqrt{2 g H}}$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Smoke Plume Mass Flow Rate (Zukoski / Heskestad)

**Given:** A non-flame fire of diameter $D^*$ (or convective heat release $\dot{Q}_c$) producing a buoyant plume rising in quiescent air.

**Step 1:** Define the **characteristic fire diameter**:

$$D^* = \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g}}\right)^{2/5}$$

**Step 2:** The **Zukoski correlation** for plume mass flow rate at height $z$ above the fire is:

$$\dot{m}_p = 0.071 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3}$$

where $\dot{Q}_c$ is in kW and $z$ is in metres. The constant $0.071$ has absorbed $g$, $\rho_\infty$, and $c_p$ at standard ambient.

**Step 3:** The corresponding **plume volume flow rate** (smoke volumetric flow) at height $z$ is:

$$\dot{V}_p = \dfrac{\dot{m}_p}{\rho_s}$$

**Step 4:** For **plume temperature rise** at height $z$:

$$\Delta T_p = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_c}{0.071 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3} \cdot c_p}$$

Simplifying:

$$\Delta T_p = \dfrac{14.08 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the visibility equation to find **smoke concentration at height $z$**:

$$\rho_s(z) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s(z)}{\dot{V}_p(z)} = \dfrac{y_s \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{V}_p(z)}$$

This gives a **closed-form estimate of the local smoke optical density** at any height in the plume.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linking All Quantities

The following dependency chain summarises how the four "smoke inputs" ($y_s$, $\sigma_s$, $\dot{m}_{fuel}$, $\dot{Q}_c$) determine all the "smoke outputs" (optical density, visibility, layer descent):

$$\dot{Q} \;\to\; \dot{Q}_c = \chi \dot{Q} \;\to\; \dot{m}_p(z),\;\Delta T_p(z) \;\to\; \dot{V}_p(z) \;\to\; \rho_s(z) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{V}_p}$$

$$\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} \;\to\; K = \sigma_s \rho_s \;\to\; V = \dfrac{C}{K} \;\to\; D_o = K L$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often confuse $D_o = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ (which is the **base-10** form, dimensionless per unit length) with $D_o = \ln(I_0/I)$ (the **natural-logarithm** form). The two differ by a factor of $\ln 10 \approx 2.303$. Always read the problem statement carefully to see whether the **natural log** or **common log** is implied.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Smoke Composition and Threat Triad


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 pattern]**

### Q1. Define smoke. List any two gaseous toxicants present in it.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Smoke is the airborne mixture of solid particulates (predominantly soot) and gaseous products of incomplete combustion, together with the entrained air, that rises and spreads as a buoyant column from a fire source.
- **Gaseous toxicants (1 mark — any two):** Carbon Monoxide (**CO**) and Hydrogen Cyanide (**HCN**). [Other valid: HCl, NOₓ, acrolein, formaldehyde.]

---

### Q2. State Lambert–Beer law as applied to smoke optical density. What is its significance?
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** Lambert–Beer law states that the transmitted intensity $I$ of a light beam through a smoke-laden medium decays exponentially with path length $L$ and extinction coefficient $K$:
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-K L}$$
- **Significance (1 mark):** It is the **fundamental basis** for (i) quantifying smoke obscuration via the optical density $D_o = K L = \ln(I_0/I)$, (ii) sizing wayfinding signage visibility, and (iii) the operating principle of **photoelectric smoke detectors**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 1, Internal Choice A or B]**

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **two-zone smoke filling model** for a fire in a closed compartment. Derive the expression for the **rate of descent of the smoke layer interface** $dz/dt$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
       CEILING =============================
                  | SMOKE LAYER (HOT)      |
                  | Temp T_s, Depth d      |
                  | Density rho_s          |
                  |---------------- INTERFACE z
                  | CLEAR LAYER (COOL)     |
                  | Temp T_0, Density rho_0|
       FLOOR  =============================
                     [FIRE SOURCE]
```

**2. Two-Zone Model Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Smoke and air are assumed to be separated by a **sharp horizontal interface** at height $z$.
- Each layer is **well-mixed internally** (uniform temperature and concentration).
- Buoyancy dominates; mixing is neglected.

**3. Derivation (3 marks):**
The smoke layer mass at any time $t$ is:
$$m_s = \rho_s \cdot A_f \cdot (H - z)$$
Mass balance (mass in = rate of accumulation):
$$\dot{m}_s = -\rho_s A_f \dfrac{dz}{dt}$$
Using $\dot{m}_s = \rho_s \dot{V}_s$:
$$\dfrac{dz}{dt} = -\dfrac{\dot{V}_s}{A_f}$$
**Result:** The interface descends at a rate equal to the **smoke volume flow rate divided by the floor area**. The negative sign indicates $z$ decreases with time.

---

**Q.A (b)** A polyurethane foam (PUF) sofa fire in a room produces a **specific extinction area** of $\sigma_s = 1100\ \text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ and a **smoke mass concentration** in the upper layer of $\rho_s = 0.008\ \text{kg}/\text{m}^3$. Calculate:
(i) the **extinction coefficient** $K$, (ii) the **optical density** $D_o$ over a layer thickness of $L = 2.5\ \text{m}$, and (iii) the **visibility** $V$ for a reflective exit sign. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Extinction Coefficient (2 marks):**
$$K = \sigma_s \cdot \rho_s = 1100 \times 0.008 = 8.8\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Optical Density (2 marks):**
$$D_o = K \cdot L = 8.8 \times 2.5 = 22.0$$
$$\boxed{D_o = 22.0 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**(iii) Visibility (3 marks):**
For a **reflective exit sign**, $C = 3$ m.
$$V = \dfrac{C}{K} = \dfrac{3}{8.8} = 0.341\ \text{m}$$
$$\boxed{V \approx 0.34\ \text{m}}$$

> **Physical interpretation (extra credit):** At this smoke concentration, an occupant can see a reflective exit sign only from **about 34 cm away** — well below the **10 m tenability limit** prescribed by NFPA 101. **Egress would be impossible** without intervention. This is why PUF furniture is **banned in public occupancies** in many jurisdictions.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B (a)** Explain with a labelled diagram the **mechanism of soot formation** in a diffusion flame. Briefly discuss the role of the **smoke point**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Soot Formation Stages (4 marks):**
- **Fuel Pyrolysis:** Parent hydrocarbon thermally decomposes into C₁/C₂ radicals (CH₃·, C₂H₂).
- **Nucleation:** Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) — pyrene, coronene — form via the HACA mechanism. These act as soot nuclei (~1–2 nm).
- **Coagulation:** Primary soot particles collide and form fractal aggregates (~30–50 nm).
- **Surface Growth & Oxidation:** Aggregates grow by surface HACA; simultaneously, incipient soot is oxidized by OH· radicals. The **net rate = growth – oxidation** determines whether soot escapes the flame.
- **Agglomeration:** Final soot agglomerates (~0.1–1 μm) are carried by the buoyant flow into the smoke plume.

**2. Labelled Diagram (2 marks):**
```
              FLAME SHEATH (LUMINOUS)
              |                        |
   FUEL -->  |  PYROLYSIS ZONE         |
              |  PAH NUCLEATION         |
              |  SURFACE GROWTH         |
              |  AGGLOMERATES DETACH    |  --> SOOT INTO SMOKE PLUME
              |  OXIDATION (OH)         |
   AIR   --> |  (BURN-OUT FRONT)        |
              |________________________|
                       ANCHOR
```

**3. Smoke Point (1 mark):**
- The **smoke point** is the **minimum fuel flow rate at which a sooting (luminous) tip first appears** on a diffusion flame.
- It characterises the **sootiness** of a fuel. Fuels with **low smoke point** (toluene, benzene) produce **more smoke per unit mass burned**. Fuels with **high smoke point** (methane, methanol) burn cleanly.
- Smoke point is a **design input** for selecting clean-burning fuels in **tunnels, aircraft cabins, and submarines**.

---

**Q.B (b)** A fire produces a **mass loss rate of fuel** of $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.025\ \text{kg/s}$ of polyurethane foam with a **smoke yield** of $y_s = 0.040\ \text{kg/kg}$ and a **specific extinction area** of $\sigma_s = 1100\ \text{m}^2/\text{kg}$. The compartment has floor area $A_f = 50\ \text{m}^2$ and ceiling height $H = 4\ \text{m}$. Calculate:
(i) the **smoke mass production rate** $\dot{m}_s$, and (ii) the **time** for the smoke layer to fill the compartment to a level $z = 1\ \text{m}$ above the floor, assuming the smoke density is $\rho_s = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Smoke Mass Production Rate (3 marks):**
$$\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.040 \times 0.025 = 0.001\ \text{kg/s}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_s = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}}$$

**(ii) Time to Reach Interface Height $z = 1$ m (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Smoke volume flow rate:**
$$\dot{V}_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s} = \dfrac{0.001}{1.2} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Descent rate of interface:**
$$\dfrac{dz}{dt} = -\dfrac{\dot{V}_s}{A_f} = -\dfrac{8.33 \times 10^{-4}}{50} = -1.67 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Time to descend from ceiling ($H = 4$ m) to $z = 1$ m:**
$$t = \dfrac{A_f (H - z)}{\dot{V}_s} = \dfrac{50 \times (4 - 1)}{8.33 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{150}{8.33 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t = 180000\ \text{s} \approx 50\ \text{hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 1.8 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \approx 50\ \text{hours}}$$

> **Physical interpretation (extra credit):** The very long filling time indicates that for the **small mass loss rate** assumed, smoke accumulation in the upper layer is **slow**. In a real PUF sofa fire, $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ is **100–500 times larger** during peak burning, making the filling time **a few minutes** — consistent with the **tenability limits** of fire engineering design.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Smoke Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing $D_o$ bases:** Using $D_o = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ when the problem expects $D_o = \ln(I_0/I)$ (or vice versa). Always read the problem.
> 2. **Wrong $C$ constant:** Using $C = 8$ for an exit sign question when the sign is **luminous** ($C = 5$) or **reflective** ($C = 3$).
> 3. **Sign error in $dz/dt$:** The interface **descends** as smoke accumulates, so $dz/dt$ is **negative** if $z$ is measured from the floor. Always state direction.
> 4. **Forgetting $\chi$ in $\dot{Q}_c$:** Convective fraction is $\chi \approx 0.6 - 0.7$, **not** 1.0. Using total HRR instead of convective HRR overestimates plume temperature and mass flow.
> 5. **Units of $\sigma_s$:** Often confused as m⁻¹ instead of **m²/kg**. Remember: $\sigma_s$ is extinction area per unit mass of smoke.
> 6. **NIST chamber constant "132":** Many KTU problems assume the standard NIST smoke chamber where $V/(AL) = 132$. If a problem gives a non-standard chamber, compute $D_s$ using the **actual geometry**, not 132.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Smoke is a **two-phase aerosol** of **particulates** (soot, tar, droplets) and **gases** (CO, HCN, HCl, CO₂, H₂O).
- ✅ The **three threats** from smoke are: **toxicity**, **loss of visibility**, and **thermal exposure** to the upper layer.
- ✅ **Lambert–Beer Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-K L}$ is the **master equation** for smoke obscuration.
- ✅ **Optical Density:** $D_o = K L = \ln(I_0/I)$; **Specific Optical Density (NIST):** $D_s = 132 \log_{10}(I_0/I)$.
- ✅ **Visibility:** $V = C / K = C / (\sigma_s \rho_s)$, with $C = 3$ (reflective), $5$ (luminous), $8$ (familiar feature) m.
- ✅ **Smoke Yield:** $y_s = m_{smoke}/m_{fuel}$ (kg/kg). PU foam ≈ 0.04, wood ≈ 0.015.
- ✅ **Specific Extinction Area:** $\sigma_s$ ranges **50–1500 m²/kg**. Wood ≈ 100, PU foam ≈ 1100.
- ✅ **Smoke Production Rate:** $\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}$.
- ✅ **Layer Descent Velocity:** $dz/dt = -\dot{V}_s / A_f$ (two-zone assumption).
- ✅ **Filling Time:** $t_{fill} = A_f (H - z) / \dot{V}_s$.
- ✅ **Zukoski Plume:** $\dot{m}_p = 0.071 \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} z^{5/3}$.
- ✅ **Convective HRR:** $\dot{Q}_c = \chi \dot{Q}$, with $\chi \approx 0.6 - 0.7$.
- ✅ **Buoyancy Flux:** $B = g \dot{Q}_c / (\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty)$.
- ✅ **CO** is the dominant toxicant — Hb affinity is **200–250×** that of O₂.
- ✅ **Smoke Point** quantifies fuel sootiness; **low smoke point = sooty fuel**.
- ✅ Soot forms via **Pyrolysis → PAH Nucleation → Coagulation → Surface Growth → Oxidation/Aggregation** (HACA mechanism).
- ✅ KTU favourite **3-mark questions:** define smoke, list toxic gases, state Lambert–Beer law.
- ✅ KTU favourite **14-mark questions:** derive $dz/dt$, compute $K$ / $D_o$ / $V$, or solve filling-time problems.
- ✅ Always use **natural log** unless the problem specifies $\log_{10}$.
- ✅ Always state the **value of $C$** explicitly in visibility problems to get full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Heat Flux
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flame Heat Flux

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flame Heat Flux** is defined as the rate of thermal energy transferred per unit area from a flame (or fire plume) to an exposed target surface, quantified in **kilowatts per square meter (kW/m²)**. In fire dynamics engineering, it is the governing parameter that dictates the rate of pyrolysis of solid combustibles, the probability of auto-ignition or piloted ignition of adjacent fuel packages, the speed of flame spread across surfaces, and the thermal loading on structural members.

Mathematically, the **total flame heat flux** ($q''_{total}$) arriving at a target is the sum of two distinct modes of energy transport:

$$q''_{total} = q''_{conv} + q''_{rad}$$

where:
- $q''_{conv}$ = Convective heat flux (energy carried by the bulk motion of hot combustion gases)
- $q''_{rad}$ = Radiative heat flux (energy transmitted as electromagnetic radiation, dominated by infrared and visible spectra in fires)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fire propagation analysis *must* separate the convective and radiative components because they respond differently to scale, fuel type, and ventilation. Radiative flux governs long-distance ignition and flame spread; convective flux governs plume temperature and direct flame impingement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine standing near a **campfire on a cold winter night**. The heat you feel on the *front* of your body is **radiative** — it travels in straight invisible rays from the glowing embers and flames, much like the warmth from the sun. If you place your hand *above* the fire, you feel a rising stream of hot air — that is **convective** heat, carried by the upward motion of buoyant combustion gases.

**Flame Heat Flux** is the engineering quantification of this phenomenon. The two components dominate in different scenarios:

| Scenario | Dominant Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Standing away from a large pool fire | Radiative | Distance attenuates convection more than radiation |
| Sitting directly above a small candle | Convective | Plume impinges directly; radiative cross-section is tiny |
| Flame spread across a wall | Radiative (from pre-heating) | Pre-heating of unburnt fuel ahead of flame is by IR |
| Firefighter engulfed in flashover | Both (≈ 50:50) | Massive, optically-thick flame emits and convects strongly |

### 1.3 Critical Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU-listed constants for flame heat flux calculations:
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Emissivity of luminous (sooty) flame:** $\varepsilon_f = 0.8$ to $1.0$ (often taken as ≈ 0.9 for design)
> - **Emissivity of non-luminous (blue) flame:** $\varepsilon_f = 0.1$ to $0.3$
> - **Radiative fraction ($\chi_r$):** typically 0.20 to 0.60 for hydrocarbon pool fires
> - **Auto-ignition threshold flux:** $q'' \approx 20$ to $25\ \text{kW/m}^2$ (for cellulosic solids)
> - **Critical piloted ignition flux:** $q'' \approx 10$ to $13\ \text{kW/m}^2$ (for cellulosic solids)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Heat Transfer from a Flame

#### 2.1.1 Convective Heat Flux ($q''_{conv}$)

The convective component arises from the bulk flow of hot combustion products. It is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q''_{conv} = h_c \left( T_{flame} - T_{surface} \right)$$

where:
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), typically **10–100 W/m²K** for fire plumes
- $T_{flame}$ = adiabatic flame temperature (K), for hydrocarbons ≈ **1200–1500 K**
- $T_{surface}$ = temperature of the receiving surface (K)

The convective heat transfer coefficient depends on the **Reynolds number**, geometry, and whether the flame is impinging (forced/plume impingement gives much higher $h_c$ than a quiescent ambient).

#### 2.1.2 Radiative Heat Flux ($q''_{rad}$)

The radiative component dominates in large fires. For an optically-thin or optically-thick flame, the radiative heat flux to a small target is given by:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon_f \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4 \cdot F_{12}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_f$ = flame emissivity (dimensionless)
- $T_f$ = effective flame temperature (K)
- $F_{12}$ = configuration view factor from flame (1) to target (2)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant

For a **non-impinging, distant target**, the view factor $F_{12}$ becomes the **solid angle subtended** by the flame at the target, normalized by $4\pi$.

### 2.2 Flame Emissivity — The Soot Connection

The emissivity of a flame is **not** a constant; it depends on the **soot volume fraction ($f_v$)** and the **optical path length ($L$)** through the flame. Soot particles (carbonaceous particulates formed in fuel-rich zones) are extremely efficient radiators because of their high absorption cross-section in the infrared.

The **emissivity of a homogeneous, isothermal, soot-containing flame** is given by the exponential attenuation law:

$$\varepsilon_f = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$$

where $\kappa$ is the **absorption coefficient** of the flame (m⁻¹), related to soot volume fraction by:

$$\kappa \approx 7.0 \, f_v$$

A typical pool fire of heptane has $f_v \approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$, and for a path length of 1 m, this gives $\varepsilon_f \approx 0.7$ to $0.99$, justifying the **luminous (yellow) flame** observation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are gas flames (methane) blue, and oil fires yellow?** Because methane is a *small* hydrocarbon with low soot yield → low $\kappa L$ → low $\varepsilon_f$ → blue (non-luminous). Heavy hydrocarbons pyrolyze soot abundantly → high $\varepsilon_f$ → yellow luminous flame. This single principle is asked frequently in KTU questions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q''_{total} = q''_{conv} + q''_{rad}$ | Total heat flux (always required first step) |
| 2 | $q''_{conv} = h_c (T_f - T_s)$ | Convective flux (Newton's Law) |
| 3 | $q''_{rad} = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 F_{12}$ | Radiative flux to a target |
| 4 | $q''_{rad} = \chi_r \dot{Q} / (4 \pi r^2)$ | Point-source model (far field, free-burning) |
| 5 | $q''_{rad} = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4$ | Flame to large, close, parallel target (F ≈ 1) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_f = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$ | Emissivity from optical thickness |
| 7 | $\kappa = C \cdot f_v$, $C \approx 7$ | Absorption coefficient from soot volume fraction |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat release rate (HRR) of the fire |
| 9 | $L_{flame} \approx 0.235 \dot{Q}^{2/5} - 1.02 D$ | Thomas flame height correlation (pool fire) |
| 10 | $D^* = \left( \dot{Q} / (\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g}) \right)^{2/5}$ | Characteristic fire diameter (scale analysis) |
| 11 | $\chi_r \approx m_f / (\Delta H_c \cdot \dot{m}'')$ | Radiative fraction from mass loss & fuel property |
| 12 | $F_{12} = \frac{1}{A_1} \int_{A_1} \int_{A_2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi s^2} dA_2 dA_1$ | General view factor definition (N-fold integral) |
| 13 | $F_{12} = \sin^2\alpha$ (point to disk) | View factor: small source on axis to a circular disk of half-angle $\alpha$ |
| 14 | $q''_{ig} = \int_0^{t_{ig}} \dot{q}''_{net} dt$ | Cumulative flux for ignition (thermal response) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** $r$ in equation (4) is the **slant distance** from the *center* of the fire to the target, not the vertical height. Use the Pythagorean relationship $r = \sqrt{x^2 + H^2}$ for an elevated target.

### 2.4 The Three Canonical Engineering Models

In KTU examinations, students are expected to choose between three standard models based on the **ratio of the flame diameter to the target distance**:

**(a) Point Source Model** — valid when target distance $r \gg$ flame diameter $D$:
$$q''_{rad} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q}}{4 \pi r^2}$$

**(b) Solid Flame Model** — valid when target is close, $r \approx D$:
$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 F_{12}$$
The flame is treated as a uniform cylindrical (or rectangular) emitter of temperature $T_f$ and emissivity $\varepsilon_f$.

**(c) Shallow Flame / Receiver Model** — used for surface-to-surface flame spread along walls; the flame is treated as a 2-D sheet radiator.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fire Spread Modeling (Hasemi / Heskestad / FDSS):** Wall and corner fires are sized by incident flux from the flame — the criterion is $q''_{target} \geq q''_{ig,crit}$ of adjacent fuel.
2. **Detector Activation:** Smoke/heat detector thresholds (e.g., 8.3 kW/m² for a fixed-temperature heat detector) are calibrated to incident heat flux.
3. **Structural Fire Engineering:** Steel members lose strength rapidly above 550°C — flux-time history is converted to temperature history via lumped-mass FE analysis.
4. **Firefighter Safety:** Tenability limits (pain threshold ≈ 2.5 kW/m², second-degree burn in 30 s ≈ 5 kW/m²) are flux-based criteria.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Point Source Model for Radiative Heat Flux

The point source model assumes the entire fire can be represented as a **point** emitting total radiative power $\dot{Q}_{rad}$ isotropically into a hemisphere. The total radiative power is:

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{total} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$$

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.** Consider a small elemental area $dA$ on a target surface at slant distance $r$ from the fire source. The total radiative power $\dot{Q}_{rad}$ is distributed uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $r$ enclosing the source.

**Step 2 — Flux on a hypothetical sphere of radius r.** The spherical surface area is $4\pi r^2$, so the radiative flux that *would* impinge on a unit area of this sphere is:

$$q''_{sphere} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi r^2} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q}}{4 \pi r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Accounting for the target orientation (view factor).** The target is a real surface, not a part of a sphere. Only the component of radiation *normal* to the target surface contributes to energy absorption (assuming a diffuse, opaque surface). For a small flat target oriented with its normal pointing toward the source, the view factor $F_{12} \approx 1$ and we recover:

$$\boxed{q''_{rad} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q}}{4 \pi r^2}}$$

**Step 4 — For a vertically-mounted target at elevation H and horizontal offset x** (e.g., a façade above a pool fire), the slant distance is $r = \sqrt{x^2 + H^2}$:

$$q''_{rad} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q}}{4 \pi (x^2 + H^2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The point source model is the **most-tested** correlation in KTU Module 1. The chief failure condition is at *near-field* ($r < 2.5D$) where the point assumption breaks down; the solid flame model is then required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Flame Emissivity from Optical Thickness

**Step 1 — Beer-Lambert law for absorption through a homogeneous medium.** Let $I_0$ be the incident spectral intensity and $I$ the transmitted intensity over a path length $L$. The spectral absorption coefficient is $\kappa_\lambda$:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}$$

**Step 2 — Integrating over all wavelengths** (assuming $\kappa$ is grey, i.e., wavelength-independent over the IR):

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\kappa L} = \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the **transmissivity** of the flame. For an *opaque* surface we treat the flame as a blackbody enclosed by a transmitting cover, so:

$$\varepsilon_f + \tau = 1 \quad \text{(Kirchhoff's law for grey media, no reflection)}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for emissivity:**

$$\varepsilon_f = 1 - \tau = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$$

This is the **fundamental emissivity equation** for a homogeneous, non-scattering, isothermal flame. For soot-laden flames:

$$\kappa \approx 7.0 \, f_v \quad \text{[Heskestad, 1988]}$$

where $f_v$ is the soot volume fraction (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ for hydrocarbon pool fires).

> [!NOTE]
> A **criterion for optically thick (re-radiating) flames** is $\kappa L \geq 3$ (i.e., $\varepsilon_f \geq 0.95$). Below this, the flame is *optically thin* and the emissivity depends strongly on path length.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Pool Fire Radiative Flux on Adjacent Wall (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem Statement.** A circular heptane pool fire has diameter $D = 1.0\ \text{m}$. The heat of combustion of heptane is $\Delta H_c = 44.6\ \text{MJ/kg}$ and the mass burning rate is $\dot{m}'' = 0.108\ \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$. The radiative fraction is $\chi_r = 0.35$. Calculate the radiative heat flux on a vertical wall located at horizontal distance $x = 2.0\ \text{m}$ from the center of the fire, at a height $H = 1.5\ \text{m}$ above the pool lip. Use the point source model.

**Step 1 — Heat Release Rate.**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p \cdot \Delta H_c$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.7854\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.108 \times 0.7854 = 0.0848\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q} = 0.0848 \times 44.6 \times 10^6 = 3.78 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 3.78\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 2 — Slant distance from fire center to target point.**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + H^2} = \sqrt{(2.0)^2 + (1.5)^2} = \sqrt{4.0 + 2.25} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Radiative flux via point source model.**

$$q''_{rad} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q}}{4 \pi r^2} = \frac{0.35 \times 3.78 \times 10^6}{4 \pi (2.5)^2}$$

$$q''_{rad} = \frac{1.323 \times 10^6}{4 \pi \times 6.25} = \frac{1.323 \times 10^6}{78.54} \approx 16{,}840\ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q''_{rad} \approx 16.8\ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.** A flux of 16.8 kW/m² is *well above* the piloted ignition threshold of cellulosic solids (≈ 12 kW/m²) and *approaching* the auto-ignition threshold (≈ 20–25 kW/m²). Therefore, the wall would undergo **piloted ignition** if a flame were present, and **spontaneous ignition** is *imminent* under sustained exposure. This is a classic pre-flashover or room-corner fire scenario.

**Step 5 — Convective flux (bonus).** Using $T_f = 1300\ \text{K}$, $T_s = 293\ \text{K}$, $h_c = 25\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ (typical far-field plume value):

$$q''_{conv} = 25 \times (1300 - 293) = 25{,}175\ \text{W/m}^2 = 25.2\ \text{kW/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Total flux.**

$$q''_{total} = 16.8 + 25.2 = 42.0\ \text{kW/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> In *far-field* fire problems, $q''_{conv}$ is often 30–60% of $q''_{total}$ depending on wind, plume entrainment, and whether the flame impinges. Always include both unless the problem explicitly states "radiative flux only."

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: When to Use Which Model

| Criterion | Point Source | Solid Flame | Modified Hasemi |
|---|---|---|---|
| Valid range (r/D) | $r > 2.5D$ | $r < 2.5D$ | $r \approx 0.5D$ to $5D$ |
| Flame shape assumed | Point | Cylinder/Slab | Empirical correlation |
| View factor required | No (cosine baked in) | Yes (geometric calc.) | Built into empirical eq. |
| Accuracy | ±25% (far field) | ±15% (mid field) | ±10% (engineering design) |
| KTU exam preference | **Highest** (numerical) | Medium (derivation) | Low (mention only) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Components of Flame Heat Flux to a Target

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FIRE SOURCE"] --> B["COMBUSTION ZONE"]
    B --> C["FLAME PLUME T_f"]
    C --> D["MODE SPLIT"]
    D --> E["RADIATIVE PATH Emitted as EM radiation in IR and visible bands"]
    D --> F["CONVECTIVE PATH Carried by hot gas bulk motion"]
    E --> G["ATMOSPHERIC ATTENUATION"]
    F --> H["PLUME ENTRAINMENT AND MIXING"]
    G --> I["INCIDENT FLUX ON TARGET q_rad"]
    H --> J["CONVECTIVE FLUX ON TARGET q_conv"]
    I --> K["TARGET SURFACE"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["THERMAL RESPONSE Surface heating pyrolysis ignition"]
    L --> M["FIRE PROPAGATION Spread to adjacent fuel"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Analytical Architecture of Heat Flux Modeling

```mermaid

        A1["Fuel Properties H_c, m_dot"]
        A2["Geometric Inputs D, H, x, r"]

    end
    subgraph MODELCORE
        B1["Model Selection Logic"]
        B2["Point Source Model"]
        B3["Solid Flame Model"]
        B4["Emissivity Model eps_f = 1 - exp(-kappa L)"]
    end

        C1["Radiative Flux q_rad"]
        C2["Convective Flux q_conv"]
        C3["Total Flux q_total"]
    end
    subgraph ENGINEERING
        D1["Ignition Criterion q_ig_crit"]
        D2["Tenability Check q_pain"]
        D3["Structural Loading q_struct"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4

    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C3
    A3 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

        P1A["Burning heptane D = 1.0 m, x = 0"]
    end

    end

```

### 4.4 Soot–Emissivity–Radiation Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Fuel chemistry"] --> S2["Pyrolysis produces soot"]
    S2 --> S3["Soot volume fraction f_v approx 10^-5"]
    S3 --> S4["Absorption coefficient kappa = 7 f_v"]
    S4 --> S5["Optical thickness kappa L"]
    S5 --> S6["Emissivity eps_f = 1 - exp(-kappa L)"]
    S6 --> S7["Radiative flux q_rad = eps_f sigma T_f^4 F_12"]
    S7 --> S8["Luminous yellow appearance of flame"]
```
