<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define flame heat flux. Distinguish clearly between the radiative and convective components, citing typical magnitudes and the fuels where each dominates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Flame Heat Flux** is the rate of heat energy transferred per unit area from a flame to a target, expressed in **kW/m²**, and is the sum of convective and radiative contributions: $q''_{total} = q''_{conv} + q''_{rad}$.

**Distinction:**

| Property | Convective ($q''_{conv}$) | Radiative ($q''_{rad}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier | Bulk hot gas | Electromagnetic radiation (IR) |
| Governing law | $h_c(T_f - T_s)$ | $\varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 F_{12}$ |
| Range | Short range (decay ∝ 1/r) | Long range (decay ∝ 1/r² isotropic) |
| Dominant fuel | Gaseous (methane) — small luminous zone, hot plume | Liquid/solid hydrocarbons — sooty, luminous flames |
| Typical fraction | 30–80% (small fires) | 30–60% (large fires) |

For **methane (gas)** flames, $q''_{conv}$ dominates; for **heptane/oil pool fires**, $q''_{rad}$ can reach 50–60% of the total.

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Explain the role of soot in determining the radiative heat flux of a flame. Justify why gas flames appear blue and oil fires appear yellow using the emissivity equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Soot particles (carbonaceous particulates) are the **primary emitters of thermal radiation** in diffusion flames. Their presence dramatically increases the absorption coefficient $\kappa$ of the flame medium, and since $\varepsilon_f = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$, the emissivity increases exponentially with soot volume fraction $f_v$ via the relation $\kappa \approx 7.0 f_v$.

**Gas flames (methane, propane):** Small hydrocarbon molecules crack into very little soot. Typical $f_v \approx 10^{-7}$, giving $\kappa L \approx 0.001$ and $\varepsilon_f \approx 0.1$–$0.2$. The flame is *optically thin* and emits *little* thermal radiation; what is emitted appears at the **blue/UV end** of the spectrum from CH and C₂ radical chemiluminescence, hence the **blue color**.

**Oil/pool fires (heptane, kerosene):** Large hydrocarbon molecules pyrolyze readily to form abundant soot. Typical $f_v \approx 10^{-5}$, giving $\kappa L \approx 1$–$3$ and $\varepsilon_f \approx 0.7$–$0.95$. The flame is *optically thick*; it glows by **blackbody-like thermal emission** peaking in the **red/IR** band, perceived by the eye as a **luminous yellow** flame. The same physics yields the much higher radiative heat flux from oil fires.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Part B questions in Module 1 carry 14 marks and follow a two-sub-part structure of 7 + 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A square heptane pool fire of side $1.5\ \text{m}$ burns in the open. Given: heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 44.6\ \text{MJ/kg}$, mass burning rate $\dot{m}'' = 0.10\ \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$, radiative fraction $\chi_r = 0.30$, and the empirical flame height $L_f = 2.5\ \text{m}$. Calculate the **total heat release rate** and the **radiative heat flux** at a target point located at horizontal distance $x = 3.0\ \text{m}$ and elevation $H = 2.0\ \text{m}$ using the **point source model**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vertical wall surface is exposed to a steady radiative flux of $25\ \text{kW/m}^2$ from an adjacent compartment fire. The wall is made of gypsum plasterboard of thickness $t = 12\ \text{mm}$, density $\rho = 950\ \text{kg/m}^3$, specific heat $c_p = 1090\ \text{J/kg·K}$, and ignition temperature $T_{ig} = 300°\text{C}$. Assuming lumped-mass behavior and neglecting rear-face losses, estimate the **time to piloted ignition**. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Pool Fire Radiative Flux (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pool area.**

$$A_p = a^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mass burning rate.**

$$\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p = 0.10 \times 2.25 = 0.225\ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Heat release rate (HRR).**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = 0.225 \times 44.6 \times 10^6 = 10.035 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 10.04\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 4 — Radiative power emitted.**

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.30 \times 10.04 = 3.012\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Slant distance to target.**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + H^2} = \sqrt{(3.0)^2 + (2.0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} = 3.606\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Radiative flux (point source).**

$$q''_{rad} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{3.012 \times 10^6}{4 \pi (3.606)^2}$$

$$q''_{rad} = \frac{3.012 \times 10^6}{4 \pi \times 13.00} = \frac{3.012 \times 10^6}{163.36} \approx 18{,}440\ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q''_{rad} \approx 18.4\ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 7 — Validity check.** Slant-to-pool-side ratio: $r/a = 3.606/1.5 = 2.40$, which is on the borderline of point source validity. The point source model is acceptable (errors ≈ 20%) for this distance.

#### Part (b) — Time to Piloted Ignition (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Energy balance for lumped mass.** The rate of temperature rise under constant incident flux $q''$ absorbed entirely at the surface (assuming surface absorptivity = 1) is:

$$\rho c_p t \frac{dT}{dt} = q''$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $T_\infty$ to $T_{ig}$.**

$$\Delta T = T_{ig} - T_\infty = 300 - 25 = 275°\text{C} = 275\ \text{K}$$

$$t_{ig} = \frac{\rho c_p t \Delta T}{q''}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting values.**

$$t_{ig} = \frac{950 \times 1090 \times 0.012 \times 275}{25{,}000}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$950 \times 1090 = 1{,}035{,}500$$

$$1{,}035{,}500 \times 0.012 = 12{,}426$$

$$12{,}426 \times 275 = 3{,}417{,}150\ \text{J/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$t_{ig} = \frac{3{,}417{,}150}{25{,}000} = 136.7\ \text{s} \approx 2.28\ \text{min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ig} \approx 137\ \text{s} \approx 2.3\ \text{minutes}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This short ignition time demonstrates why compartment fires can rapidly propagate to adjacent walls once the incident flux exceeds ~20 kW/m², and is the basis of **flashover** criteria in fire codes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** A turbulent diffusion flame has a soot volume fraction of $f_v = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ uniformly distributed over a cylindrical flame of diameter $D = 0.8\ \text{m}$ and height $L_f = 1.6\ \text{m}$. Assuming a path length equal to the flame diameter and an effective flame temperature $T_f = 1200\ \text{K}$, calculate: (i) the **absorption coefficient** $\kappa$, (ii) the **flame emissivity** $\varepsilon_f$, and (iii) the **radiative heat flux** to a small target placed perpendicular to the flame axis at a distance $r = 2.0\ \text{m}$ from the flame center (assume the flame can be approximated as a point source for the view factor). Use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Two walls of an uninsulated compartment are lined with **PMMA (poly-methyl methacrylate)** of thickness $t = 6\ \text{mm}$, density $\rho = 1180\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1500\ \text{J/kg·K}$, and ignition temperature $T_{ig} = 280°\text{C}$. A growing fire in the compartment produces a linearly increasing incident flux: $q''(t) = 5 + 10t\ \text{kW/m}^2$, where $t$ is in seconds. Determine the **time to piloted ignition** of the PMMA lining. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Soot-based Emissivity Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Absorption coefficient.**

$$\kappa = 7.0 \cdot f_v = 7.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Optical thickness and emissivity.** Path length $L = D = 0.8\ \text{m}$:

$$\kappa L = 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.8 = 2.8 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\varepsilon_f = 1 - e^{-\kappa L} = 1 - e^{-2.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2.8 \times 10^{-5}$$

> **Important observation:** This is essentially **zero**. A diameter of 0.8 m is *not* an optically thick path for $f_v = 5 \times 10^{-6}$. In real fires, $L$ is taken as the *path through the flame* seen by the target, which for a target at $r = 2.0$ m is dominated by the projected flame area, not the diameter.

**Re-evaluation using the correct path length (through flame along the line of sight).** For a target on the flame axis at $r = 2.0$ m, the path length through the flame is approximately the flame diameter $D$ seen edge-on, plus contribution from cylindrical geometry. Taking the average path length as the flame radius times a geometric factor ≈ 1.5 (engineering approximation):

$$L_{eff} \approx 1.5 \times D = 1.2\ \text{m}$$

$$\kappa L_{eff} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.2 = 4.2 \times 10^{-5}$$

Still optically thin. **For a 1 MW scale fire, $f_v$ should be ~$10^{-5}$ for $\varepsilon_f \approx 0.1$ at 1 m path length.** Proceeding formally with the point source and the calculated $\varepsilon_f$:

**Step 3 — Radiative flux.** Total radiative power from the flame is approximated from blackbody emission:

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 \cdot A_{flame}$$

$$A_{flame} = \pi D L_f = \pi \times 0.8 \times 1.6 = 4.02\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 2.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1200)^4 \times 4.02$$

$$(1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}\ \text{K}^4$$

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 2.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12} \times 4.02$$

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 2.8 \times 5.67 \times 2.0736 \times 4.02 \times 10^{-5-8+12}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 2.8 \times 5.67 \times 2.0736 \times 4.02 \times 10^{-1} \approx 13.24\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Point source flux at $r = 2.0$ m:**

$$q''_{rad} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi r^2} = \frac{13{,}240}{4 \pi \times 4.0} = \frac{13{,}240}{50.27}$$

$$\boxed{q''_{rad} \approx 263\ \text{W/m}^2 \approx 0.26\ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is a very low flux — below detection threshold and far below ignition. A real pool fire of this size would have $\varepsilon_f$ closer to 0.5–0.8 (due to higher $f_v$ in turbulent diffusion flames), giving realistic fluxes of 5–10 kW/m². The exercise highlights how sensitive predictions are to assumed $f_v$.

#### Part (b) — Time to Ignition Under Linearly Increasing Flux (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Energy balance with time-dependent flux.**

$$\rho c_p t \frac{dT}{dt} = q''(t) = (5{,}000 + 10{,}000\, t)\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from $T_0 = 25°\text{C}$ to $T_{ig} = 280°\text{C}$.**

$$\int_{T_0}^{T_{ig}} \rho c_p t \, dT = \int_0^{t_{ig}} (5{,}000 + 10{,}000\, t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate left side.**

$$\rho c_p t \cdot (T_{ig} - T_0) = 1180 \times 1500 \times 0.006 \times (280 - 25)$$

$$= 1180 \times 1500 \times 0.006 \times 255$$

$$= 10{,}620 \times 255 = 2{,}708{,}100\ \text{J/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate right side.**

$$\int_0^{t_{ig}} (5{,}000 + 10{,}000\, t)\, dt = 5{,}000\, t_{ig} + 5{,}000\, t_{ig}^2$$

**Step 5 — Equate and solve the quadratic.**

$$5{,}000\, t_{ig} + 5{,}000\, t_{ig}^2 = 2{,}708{,}100$$

Divide by 5{,}000:

$$t_{ig} + t_{ig}^2 = 541.62$$

$$t_{ig}^2 + t_{ig} - 541.62 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Quadratic formula:**

$$t_{ig} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 \times 541.62}}{2} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{2167.48}}{2} = \frac{-1 + 46.56}{2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ig} \approx 22.78\ \text{s}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Under a rapidly growing fire, PMMA lining ignites in under 25 seconds — demonstrating why **interior finishes in compartments** are tightly regulated by codes (IS 15105, NBC 2016). Thin thermoplastic linings are particularly hazardous due to their low thermal inertia.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Flame Heat Flux Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4π factor:** Students often write $q''_{rad} = \chi_r \dot{Q}/(r^2)$. The correct denominator is $4\pi r^2$. This single error costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using vertical height instead of slant distance:** For an elevated target, $r = \sqrt{x^2 + H^2}$, not $H$ alone.
> 3. **Ignoring convective flux:** Many KTU questions ask for "heat flux" without specifying radiative only. Always report **both components** unless the problem says "radiative flux."
> 4. **Mixing units:** $\dot{Q}$ must be in Watts (W) for the formula to give flux in W/m². Mixing kW and m² is the most common dimensional error. Convert: $1\ \text{MW} = 10^6\ \text{W}$.
> 5. **Using point source in near-field:** The point source model is invalid for $r/D < 2.5$. State this validity check explicitly in the answer; it is a **mark-saver** statement.
> 6. **Confusing emissivity with absorptivity:** Kirchhoff's law states $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for a surface in thermal equilibrium — but only if the surface is opaque. For transparent media like flame gases, this is *not* strictly applicable. The flame emissivity equation $\varepsilon_f = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$ is the **correct** form, not $\varepsilon = \alpha$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flame Heat Flux (Module 1)**
> - [ ] **Definition:** $q''_{total} = q''_{conv} + q''_{rad}$ — always start here.
> - [ ] **Convective:** $q''_{conv} = h_c (T_f - T_s)$ — short range, dominant in small fires and gas flames.
> - [ ] **Radiative:** $q''_{rad} = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 F_{12}$ — long range, dominant in large sooty fires.
> - [ ] **Point source:** $q''_{rad} = \chi_r \dot{Q} / (4\pi r^2)$ — valid for $r > 2.5D$.
> - [ ] **Slant distance:** Always $r = \sqrt{x^2 + H^2}$ for an elevated target.
> - [ ] **Emissivity equation:** $\varepsilon_f = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$, with $\kappa \approx 7 f_v$ for soot.
> - [ ] **Soot drives emissivity:** Blue gas flames ($\varepsilon_f \approx 0.1$) vs. yellow pool fires ($\varepsilon_f \approx 0.9$).
> - [ ] **HRR definition:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \Delta H_c = \dot{m}'' A_p \Delta H_c$.
> - [ ] **Radiative fraction** $\chi_r$ = 0.30–0.60 for hydrocarbons; lower for non-sooty fuels.
> - [ ] **Critical fluxes to memorize:** Piloted ignition ≈ 12 kW/m²; auto-ignition ≈ 20 kW/m²; pain threshold ≈ 2.5 kW/m²; second-degree burn (30 s) ≈ 5 kW/m².
> - [ ] **Lumped mass ignition time:** $t_{ig} = \rho c_p t \Delta T / q''$ for constant flux.
> - [ ] **Validity of point source:** Always state $r/D$ and justify model choice.
> - [ ] **Units discipline:** Convert kW → W, m² → correct area; never mix.
> - [ ] **FDSS / Hasemi model** is the empirical improvement over point source for façade fires (mention in design answers).
> - [ ] **Always include convective flux** unless told otherwise — KTU valuation penalizes missing components.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Spread Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flame Spread Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flame Spread Rate (V)** is defined as the velocity at which a flame front propagates across the surface of a combustible material, measured relative to the unburnt fuel. It is a vector quantity with units of **m/s (meters per second)** and is one of the most critical parameters in fire dynamics because it governs:

- The **Time to Flashover** in compartment fires
- The **Fire Growth Rate** in building codes
- The **Surface Area Involvement** of a developing fire
- The **Mass Burning Rate (ṁ″)** when combined with the pyrolysis length

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Flame spread rate is a fundamental parameter that couples heat transfer, fluid dynamics, and solid-phase pyrolysis. Examiners frequently test the distinction between *spread rate over solids* and *spread rate over liquid pools*.

## 1.2 Mathematical Form (Core Definition)

$$V_f = \frac{dx_f}{dt}$$

where $x_f$ is the flame front position measured along the fuel surface and $t$ is time.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Matchstick Race"

Imagine striking a match on a rough surface and holding it horizontally. The flame doesn't just sit at the tip — it races back along the wooden stick toward your fingers. That visible "race" is the **flame spread rate**.

**Plain-English Explanation:**
Think of the unburnt fuel as a line of sleeping dominos. The flame front is the first falling domino. As heat conducts forward (radiatively and convectively), it preheats the next "domino" (fuel element) until it ignites. The speed at which this chain reaction moves is the **flame spread rate**.

- If the wind blows *toward* the flame (opposed flow): the spread is **slow** because fresh fuel is being pushed away.
- If the wind blows *with* the flame (concurrent flow): the spread is **fast** because hot gases preheat fuel ahead.
- If there's **no wind** (still air): buoyancy drives the flow — this is the most common laboratory condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Flame spread rate ($V_f$) is NOT the same as burning rate (ṁ). $V_f$ describes surface propagation; ṁ describes fuel consumption per unit area per unit time.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Values (Bold Constants)

- **Density of typical solid fuel (PMMA, wood):** **ρ = 800–1200 kg/m³**
- **Thermal conductivity of charring solid:** **k ≈ 0.1–0.2 W/(m·K)**
- **Ignition temperature of cellulose:** **T_ig ≈ 573 K (300 °C)**
- **Typical horizontal flame spread rate on PMMA:** **V_f ≈ 1–5 mm/s**
- **Typical wind-aided upward spread on wood:** **V_f up to 0.5 m/s**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flame Spread (Taxonomy)

Flame spread phenomena are classified along **three primary axes**:

### A. By Flow Direction Relative to Spread

| Type | Flow Direction | Typical V_f | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Opposed Flow Spread** | Opposite to spread direction | Very low (mm/s) | Flame spreading against the wind on a horizontal surface |
| **Concurrent Flow Spread** | Same as spread direction | High (cm/s to m/s) | Upward flame spread on a vertical wall (buoyancy-aided) |
| **Still Air / Buoyancy-Driven** | Vertically upward due to gravity | Moderate | Horizontal surface flame in calm air |

### B. By Material Phase

1. **Solid Surface Spread** — Thermally thick vs. thermally thin fuels
2. **Liquid Pool Spread** — Governed by liquid viscosity and surface tension
3. **Smoldering Spread** — Solid-phase heterogeneous reaction, much slower

### C. By Orientation

- **Horizontal (ceiling/floor)**
- **Vertical upward** (fastest, most dangerous)
- **Vertical downward** (slowest, often self-extinguishing)
- **Inclined** (intermediate)

## 2.2 Governing Physics — The "Why" Behind Flame Spread

Flame spread is fundamentally a **heat transfer problem** with three coupled mechanisms:

1. **Conduction** through the solid fuel layer ahead of the flame
2. **Convection** from hot combustion gases to the unburnt surface
3. **Radiation** from the flame and hot soot to the unburnt surface

The flame front advances when the local heat flux at position $x$ ahead of the pyrolysis zone exceeds the critical value required to raise the solid to its **ignition temperature**.

## 2.3 Thermal Theory of Flame Spread (The de Ris Framework)

J. de Ris (1973) pioneered the modern treatment using a **thermal ignition theory**. The key result for **thermally thin fuels** (e.g., paper, thin fabrics):

$$V_f = \frac{\sqrt{2} \, k_g \, (T_f - T_s)}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_s - T_\infty)} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{Nu}^*}$$

For **thermally thick fuels** in opposed flow (the classical configuration), the result simplifies to:

$$V_f = \frac{\lambda \, \delta \, g}{k_s \, \rho_s \, c_s} \cdot \frac{1}{(T_{ig} - T_\infty)^2}$$

where the symbols are defined in the table below.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and memorize the column headers** — KTU examiners award partial marks purely for correct symbol identification even when the final numerical answer is wrong.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V_f$ | Flame spread rate | m/s |
| $T_f$ | Flame temperature | K |
| $T_s$ | Surface temperature (pyrolysis) | K |
| $T_\infty$ | Ambient temperature | K |
| $T_{ig}$ | Ignition temperature | K |
| $k_s$ | Solid thermal conductivity | W/(m·K) |
| $k_g$ | Gas-phase thermal conductivity | W/(m·K) |
| $\rho_s$ | Solid density | kg/m³ |
| $c_s$ | Solid specific heat | J/(kg·K) |
| $\delta$ | Thermal penetration depth (or thickness for thin fuel) | m |
| $\lambda$ | Effective flame heat transfer coefficient | W/(m²·K) |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| $\text{Nu}^*$ | Modified Nusselt number for stagnation point | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Thermal diffusivity of solid $= k_s / (\rho_s c_s)$ | m²/s |

## 2.5 The Quintessential Spread Rate Expression (KTU Gold)

The most frequently cited expression in KTU examinations is the **inverse relationship between spread rate and thermal thickness**:

$$V_f \propto \frac{1}{\delta} \quad \text{(thermally thin regime)}$$

$$V_f \propto \frac{1}{\delta^2} \quad \text{(thermally thick regime, opposed flow)}$$

This means **thinner fuels burn faster laterally** — a critical fire-safety principle.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

1. **Building Code Compliance** — ASTM E84 tunnel test, ISO 5658 lateral spread test both quantify $V_f$.
2. **Fire Retardant Design** — Intumescent coatings slow $V_f$ by 5–10×.
3. **Forest Fire Prediction** — Rothermel spread model uses $V_f$ as the central output.
4. **Cable Fire Safety** — IEC 60332 measures $V_f$ along cable trays.
5. **Compartment Fire Modeling** — Input to FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator) via $V_f$ boundary conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Flame Spread Rate on a Thermally Thin Solid (de Ris Thin-Fuel Model)

**Assumption Set (stated explicitly for KTU partial credit):**

1. The solid is **thermally thin**: $Bi = h\delta/k_s \ll 1$ (Biot number small)
2. Heat loss to the unburnt side is negligible
3. The flame acts as a **line heat source** at temperature $T_f$
4. Quasi-steady propagation (ignition zone thin compared to flame length)

### Step 1 — Energy Balance at the Flame Front

The rate of energy delivered to the unburnt fuel element per unit width must equal the rate of energy stored in raising that element from $T_\infty$ to $T_{ig}$:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{delivered}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{stored}}$$

The convective heat flux from flame to surface is $q'' = h(T_f - T_s)$. The element being heated has width $V_f \, dt$ and thickness $\delta$.

### Step 2 — Mass of Fuel Element Per Unit Width

$$dm = \rho_s \cdot \delta \cdot V_f \cdot dt$$

### Step 3 — Energy Required to Ignite the Element

$$dE = \rho_s \cdot \delta \cdot V_f \cdot c_s \cdot (T_{ig} - T_\infty) \cdot dt$$

### Step 4 — Equating Delivered and Stored Energy

$$h(T_f - T_s) \, dx = \rho_s \, \delta \, c_s \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty) \, dx$$

(The $dx$ and $dt$ cancel via $dx = V_f dt$.)

### Step 5 — Solve for Spread Rate

$$\boxed{V_f = \frac{h \, (T_f - T_s)}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Stagnation-Point Nusselt Number

For a stagnation-point flow, $h = \text{Nu}^* \cdot k_g / L$, where $L$ is the characteristic length (flame standoff distance). The final KTU-canonical form becomes:

$$V_f = \frac{\text{Nu}^* \, k_g \, (T_f - T_s)}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, L \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When the question states "derive the thin-fuel flame spread rate", examiners look for Steps 1–5 with the energy balance explicitly written. The Nusselt substitution in Step 6 earns the final 2 marks.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A thermally thin PMMA sheet ($\delta = 2$ mm, $\rho_s = 1190$ kg/m³, $c_s = 1500$ J/kg·K) burns in a horizontal orientation in still air. Given:
- $T_f = 1800$ K, $T_s = 700$ K
- $T_\infty = 298$ K, $T_{ig} = 580$ K
- Heat transfer coefficient $h = 50$ W/(m²·K)

**Find:** The flame spread rate $V_f$ in m/s and mm/s.

### Solution

**Step 1** — Identify the governing equation (thin-fuel):

$$V_f = \frac{h \, (T_f - T_s)}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}$$

**Step 2** — Compute numerator:

$$h(T_f - T_s) = 50 \times (1800 - 700) = 50 \times 1100 = 55{,}000 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 3** — Compute denominator:

$$\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty) = 1190 \times 1500 \times 0.002 \times (580 - 298)$$

Intermediate:
$$1190 \times 1500 = 1{,}785{,}000$$

$$1{,}785{,}000 \times 0.002 = 3570$$

$$580 - 298 = 282$$

$$3570 \times 282 = 1{,}006{,}740$$

**Step 4** — Divide:

$$V_f = \frac{55{,}000}{1{,}006{,}740} = 0.0546 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5** — Convert to mm/s:

$$V_f = 0.0546 \times 1000 = 54.6 \text{ mm/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Validation:** A value of ~55 mm/s is physically reasonable for horizontal PMMA spread in still air. If your answer comes out as 0.055 m/s **without unit conversion**, the examiner will deduct 1 mark. Always state units.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Thin vs Thick vs Liquid Pool)

| Parameter | Thermally Thin Solid | Thermally Thick Solid | Liquid Pool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | $V_f = h \Delta T / (\rho_s c_s \delta \Delta T_{ig})$ | $V_f \propto \alpha_g (T_f - T_s)^2 / [k_s (T_{ig} - T_\infty)^2]$ | $V_f \propto \sqrt{\sigma g / \rho_l}$ (capillary-gravity) |
| Thickness dependence | $V_f \propto 1/\delta$ | $V_f \propto$ constant (independent) | $V_f \propto$ independent of depth |
| Dominant heat transfer | Conduction through sheet | Conduction into solid substrate | Convection from flame lip |
| Controlling property | $\rho_s c_s \delta$ (heat capacity per area) | $\alpha_s$ (thermal diffusivity) | $\mu_l, \sigma_l$ (viscosity, surface tension) |
| Typical V_f | cm/s to dm/s | mm/s to cm/s | cm/s (pool diameter growth) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flame Spread Mechanism Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram maps the physical interactions governing flame spread rate across a thermally thick solid in opposed flow. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** adaptation recommended when physical drawings (e.g., boundary-layer profiles) are not directly renderable in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion Zone Flame T_f High] --> B[Heat Transfer to Surface]
    B --> C1[Convective Heat Flux q_conv]
    B --> C2[Radiative Heat Flux q_rad]
    B --> C3[Conductive Heat Flux q_cond]
    C1 --> D[Preheated Zone Ahead of Flame]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E{Surface Temperature Reaches T_ig}
    E -->|Yes| F[Pyrolysis Occurs Fuel Vapors Released]
    E -->|No| D
    F --> G[Flame Anchors at New Position x_f + dx]
    G --> H[Flame Front Advances by dx]
    H --> A
    H --> I[Output Flame Spread Rate V_f dx over dt]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each loop iteration represents an incremental advance of the flame. The flame heats the unburnt surface; when that surface reaches $T_{ig}$, pyrolysis begins; vapors ignite; the flame anchors at the new front; the cycle repeats. The rate of cycle completion is the macroscopic $V_f$.

---

## 4.2 Opposed-Flow vs. Concurrent-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPP[Opposed Flow Spread Slow]
        OF1[Air Flow Right to Left] --> OF2[Flame Front] --> OF3[Unburnt Fuel]
    end
    subgraph CON[Concurrent Flow Spread Fast]
        CF1[Air Flow Left to Right] --> CF2[Unburnt Fuel] --> CF3[Flame Front]
    end
    OPP --- CON
```

**Why concurrent flow is faster:** Hot combustion products travel *with* the spread direction, preheating the unburnt fuel. In opposed flow, the oncoming air *cools* the unburnt fuel and dilutes the pyrolysis gases.

---

## 4.3 Spread Rate Dependency Map (Effect of Each Parameter)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Flame Spread Rate V_f] --> P1[Fuel Thickness delta]
    V --> P2[Fuel Density rho_s]
    V --> P3[Solid Specific Heat c_s]
    V --> P4[Ignition Temperature T_ig]
    V --> P5[Flow Direction Forced vs Buoyancy]
    V --> P6[Ambient Oxygen Concentration]
    P1 -->|Thinner fuel| V1[Higher V_f Thin regime]
    P2 -->|Denser fuel| V2[Lower V_f More thermal inertia]
    P3 -->|Higher c_s| V3[Lower V_f More energy to ignite]
    P4 -->|Higher T_ig| V4[Lower V_f Harder to ignite]
    P5 -->|Concurrent with flame| V5[Higher V_f]
    P6 -->|Higher O2| V6[Higher V_f up to stoichiometric limit]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define flame spread rate. Distinguish between opposed-flow and concurrent-flow flame spread with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Flame spread rate $V_f$ is the velocity at which a flame front advances over the surface of a combustible material, defined as $V_f = dx_f / dt$, measured in m/s.

- **Opposed-flow spread (1 mark):** The gas-phase flow is directed **opposite** to the direction of flame propagation. The flame is bent backward and spread rates are very low (typically 1–5 mm/s). *Example:* Flame on a horizontal surface spreading against a wind.

- **Concurrent-flow spread (1 mark):** The gas-phase flow is in the **same** direction as the flame propagation. Preheating of unburnt fuel is enhanced and spread rates are high (cm/s to m/s). *Example:* Upward flame spread on a vertical wall driven by buoyancy.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the de Ris expression for flame spread rate on a thermally thin fuel. List the three physical mechanisms by which heat is transferred from the flame to the unburnt surface.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Expression (1.5 marks):**

$$V_f = \frac{h \, (T_f - T_s)}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}$$

- **Three heat transfer mechanisms (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Conduction** through the solid fuel layer ahead of the flame
  2. **Convection** from hot combustion gases in the flame boundary layer
  3. **Radiation** from luminous soot and the flame envelope to the unburnt surface

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for flame spread rate over a thermally thin solid fuel using the energy balance approach. Clearly state all assumptions.**

*(See full derivation in Section 3.1, Steps 1–6, of these notes.)*

**Mark Allocation Breakdown (for student self-evaluation):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Stating the 4 key assumptions | 1 |
| Energy balance equation (Step 4) | 2 |
| Algebraic manipulation (Step 5) | 2 |
| Final boxed expression | 1 |
| Physical interpretation of result | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A horizontal PMMA sheet of thickness 3 mm is tested in still air. Given:**
- $\rho_s = 1190$ kg/m³, $c_s = 1500$ J/(kg·K)
- $T_f = 1900$ K, $T_s = 720$ K, $T_\infty = 300$ K, $T_{ig} = 600$ K
- $h = 45$ W/(m²·K)

**Calculate the flame spread rate in m/s. Comment on the effect of doubling the sheet thickness.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Write the governing thin-fuel equation:

$$V_f = \frac{h(T_f - T_s)}{\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}$$

**Step 2** — Numerator:

$$h(T_f - T_s) = 45 \times (1900 - 720) = 45 \times 1180 = 53{,}100 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 3** — Denominator (with $\delta = 0.003$ m):

$$\rho_s c_s \delta = 1190 \times 1500 \times 0.003 = 5355 \text{ J/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$$

$$(T_{ig} - T_\infty) = 600 - 300 = 300 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 5355 \times 300 = 1{,}606{,}500 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4** — Compute $V_f$:

$$V_f = \frac{53{,}100}{1{,}606{,}500} = 0.0331 \text{ m/s} = 33.1 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 5** — Effect of doubling thickness:

Since $V_f \propto 1/\delta$ in the thin regime, doubling $\delta$ halves $V_f$ to approximately **16.5 mm/s**.

*(1 mark for the inverse proportionality statement + 1 mark for the final halved value.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute the denominator incorrectly by forgetting to convert thickness from **mm to m**. The most common mark-losing error is using $\delta = 3$ directly instead of $\delta = 0.003$. The 0.003 factor changes the answer by **1000×**. Always convert SI units FIRST before any calculation begins.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the three classifications of flame spread based on the relative direction of gas flow. With the help of suitable diagrams, describe how flow direction affects the spread rate. Discuss the role of buoyancy in vertical upward spread.**

**Model Answer Outline (mark-allocating):**

1. **Classification (3 marks):** Define and sketch opposed, concurrent, and still-air / buoyancy-driven flow. (1 mark per category.)
2. **Effect of flow direction on spread rate (2 marks):** Concurrent flow is 10–100× faster than opposed flow due to enhanced preheating. Cite the de Ris result.
3. **Role of buoyancy in vertical upward spread (2 marks):** In vertical upward configuration, buoyancy-induced flow is concurrent with the spread. The flow velocity is:

$$u_{\text{buoy}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 g x_f (T_f - T_\infty)}{T_\infty}}$$

   This Froude-scaled velocity is responsible for the characteristic acceleration of vertical wall fires.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The flame spread rate over a thermally thick fuel in opposed flow is given by:**

$$V_f = \frac{\lambda \, \delta \, g}{k_s \, \rho_s \, c_s \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)^2}$$

**A fire safety engineer tests a sample with the following properties:**
- $\lambda = 0.05$ W/(m·K), $\delta = 0.005$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $k_s = 0.15$ W/(m·K), $\rho_s = 750$ kg/m³, $c_s = 1700$ J/(kg·K)
- $T_{ig} = 580$ K, $T_\infty = 300$ K

**Compute $V_f$ and explain qualitatively how the spread rate would change if the ambient oxygen concentration were reduced to 15% (from 21%).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute numerator:

$$\lambda \, \delta \, g = 0.05 \times 0.005 \times 9.81 = 0.002453$$

**Step 2** — Compute denominator:

$$k_s \rho_s c_s = 0.15 \times 750 \times 1700 = 191{,}250$$

$$(T_{ig} - T_\infty)^2 = (580 - 300)^2 = 280^2 = 78{,}400$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 191{,}250 \times 78{,}400 = 1.4994 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 3** — Compute $V_f$:

$$V_f = \frac{0.002453}{1.4994 \times 10^{10}} = 1.636 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m/s}$$

*(This very small value indicates that the constant $\lambda$ as given must be interpreted as a composite flame heat-transfer parameter; in practice, $\lambda$ is much larger. Students should still present the numerical result without altering the given data.)*

**Step 4** — Effect of reducing $O_2$ to 15% (qualitative, 3 marks):

- Lower $O_2 \Rightarrow$ lower flame temperature $T_f$ (and hence lower $\lambda$, which depends on $T_f - T_\infty$)
- Lower $T_f \Rightarrow$ lower heat feedback to the surface
- Lower heat feedback $\Rightarrow$ lower $V_f$
- At $O_2 \approx 16\%$ and below, flames may not propagate at all (extinction limit)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error Trap:** Students frequently write $V_f$ in scientific notation without stating the units. Always write "**$V_f = 1.636 \times 10^{-13}$ m/s**" — losing the units costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Flame spread rate $V_f$** is the velocity of flame front propagation along the fuel surface; units: **m/s**.
- ✅ Three heat transfer modes: **conduction, convection, radiation** — all contribute to preheating unburnt fuel.
- ✅ **Opposed flow** = slow (mm/s); **Concurrent flow** = fast (cm/s to m/s); **Still air** = intermediate.
- ✅ **Thermally thin fuel:** $V_f \propto 1/\delta$ — halving thickness doubles spread rate.
- ✅ **Thermally thick fuel (opposed flow):** $V_f \propto 1/\delta^2$ — strong thickness sensitivity.
- ✅ **Vertical upward spread is the most dangerous** in real fires because buoyancy-driven flow is concurrent.
- ✅ The **de Ris thin-fuel equation** is the most-cited formula; memorize it symbol-by-symbol.
- ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², ignition of cellulose ≈ 573 K, flame temperatures ≈ 1500–2000 K.
- ✅ A **lower $O_2$ environment reduces $V_f$**; below ~16%, flames cannot propagate.
- ✅ **Units conversion is the #1 mark-losing step** in numerical problems — always convert mm to m BEFORE substitution.
- ✅ **Dimensional consistency check:** A quick sanity check on any answer is to verify $[V_f] = $ m/s.
- ✅ The fire spreads **outward from the ignition source**, not inward — the unburnt region ahead is what gets preheated.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Propagation Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flame Propagation Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Flame Propagation Index (FPI)** is a dimensionless, experimentally derived numerical parameter that quantifies the rate and propensity of a flame front to advance over the surface of, or through, a combustible material under prescribed thermal exposure. It is the **standardized fingerprint** of how aggressively a material supports surface fire growth and is the most widely used single-number metric in regulatory fire safety engineering for ranking materials by their contribution to early-stage fire propagation.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT528)**, the Flame Propagation Index is taught under two globally recognized test standards:

1. **Fire Propagation Index (I)** — British Standard **BS 476 : Part 6** (*Method of test for fire propagation for products*).
2. **Flame Spread Index (FSI)** — American Standard **ASTM E84** (*Standard Test Method for Surface Burning Characteristics of Building Materials*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for Examiners:** Do *not* conflate the two. The **Fire Propagation Index (BS 476 Pt. 6)** is a **temperature/time integral** that measures the material's *self-heating contribution* to a growing fire. The **Flame Spread Index (ASTM E84)** is a **flame-front distance/time integral** that measures the *physical speed* of flame front migration along a tunnel. They measure different physics but both answer the same engineering question: *"How fast does fire grow on this material?"*

Mathematically, both indices are normalized such that a **red-oak calibration specimen** returns a benchmark value:

| Reference Material | FSI (ASTM E84) | FPI (BS 476 Pt. 6) |
|---|---|---|
| **Red Oak Flooring** | **100** | ≈ 12 – 15 |
| **Asbestos Cement Board** | **0** | **0** (baseline) |
| Inorganic Cement Board | **0** | **0** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Explanation

Imagine a **lit candle placed on the floor of a long, dark corridor**. The candle flame is your ignition source. The corridor floor is your material specimen. Now imagine two scenarios:

* **Scenario A — A polished marble floor:** You place the candle, the wax burns, the floor never ignites, the flame never moves. The "index" of marble is **zero** — it does not propagate fire at all.
* **Scenario B — A trail of gunpowder leading away from the candle:** Touch the candle to one end of the powder trail — the flame *races* down the corridor in milliseconds, far faster than the candle itself. The "index" of gunpowder is **very high** — it propagates fire extremely rapidly.

The **Flame Propagation Index** is simply the engineering version of this thought experiment, but rigorously measured under controlled heat-flux exposure inside a calibrated furnace. The higher the index, the more "gunpowder-like" the material behaves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Slogan to Remember:** *"The Index is the 'fire speedometer' of a material — zero means it refuses to spread, 100 (or higher) means it propagates as fast as red-oak lumber."*

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Units

The following primary measured quantities feed into both indices:

* **Time to Ignition, $t_{ig}$** — seconds (s) — the time from first heat exposure to sustained flaming combustion on the specimen surface.
* **Heat Release Rate, $\dot{Q}$** — kilowatts (kW) or kW/m² — the rate at which the material releases chemical energy upon combustion.
* **Total Heat Released, $Q_{tot}$** — kilojoules (kJ) — the integrated heat release over the test duration.
* **Flame Front Distance, $L_f$** — meters (m) — the furthest point reached by the visible flame front.
* **Exhaust Gas Temperature Rise, $\Delta T$** — Kelvin (K) or degrees Celsius (°C) — the temperature elevation of the specimen's effluent above baseline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Highlight:** The **Flame Propagation Index is NOT a single, universal physical constant like the speed of light.** It is a *standardized empirical score* that depends on the specific test apparatus, the heat-flux profile, and the calibration baseline. Always quote the standard (e.g., *FPI per BS 476 Pt. 6* or *FSI per ASTM E84*) alongside the numerical value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The BS 476 Part 6 — Fire Propagation Index (FPI)

### 2.1.1 The Operating Principle

The BS 476 Part 6 apparatus uses a **small gas-fired tubular furnace** in which a vertical specimen (228 mm × 228 mm) is exposed to a **pre-programmed, monotonically rising thermal exposure** for a total of **20 minutes**. The specimen is held in a frame, and a **specified electric heating element** provides an additional *radiant* heat flux on top of the gas flame — the combination mimics a developing fire.

The exhaust gases from the specimen are ducted through a chimney, and the **temperature rise of the effluent** above the inlet air is continuously recorded. This temperature rise ($\Delta T_s$ for the specimen, $\Delta T_c$ for the asbestos-cement calibration specimen) is the **raw data** of the test.

The genius of the method is the **subtraction of the calibration temperature** $(\Delta T_s - \Delta T_c)$. Because the asbestos-cement board does not contribute any combustion heat, this subtraction **isolates the specimen's own exothermic contribution** to the fire.

### 2.1.2 The Three Sub-Indices

The total 20-minute test is partitioned into **three discrete time windows**, and a sub-index is computed in each window:

| Sub-Index | Time Window (minutes) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $i_1$ | **0 → 0.25** (first 15 s) | Early ignitability — does the material catch fire rapidly? |
| $i_2$ | **0.25 → 1.0** (next 45 s) | Initial fire growth — how quickly does the flame build? |
| $i_3$ | **1.0 → 2.0** (next 60 s) | Sustained propagation — is the fire self-sustaining? |

> [!NOTE]
> The first 2 minutes dominate the index because the test standard was designed to evaluate **early fire growth**, which is the period most critical for **life safety evacuation** in buildings.

### 2.1.3 The Total Fire Propagation Index

$$
\boxed{
I \;=\; i_1 \;+\; i_2 \;+\; i_3
}
$$

The total $I$ value is the single number that regulators use to classify materials. For example, the **UK Building Regulations Approved Document B** sets $I \leq 12$ (with $i_1 \leq 6$) for **"Class 0"** limited-propagability materials used on internal walls.

## 2.2 The ASTM E84 — Flame Spread Index (FSI)

### 2.2.1 The Operating Principle

The ASTM E84 apparatus — the famous **Steiner Tunnel** — uses a **7.62 m (25 ft) long horizontal tunnel** with the specimen mounted as the *roof* of the tunnel. A **gas burner** at one end provides a **82 kW** controlled flame, and an **induced draft** pulls the hot gases over the specimen. The flame front distance $L_f(t)$ is observed through view-ports and recorded every few seconds.

### 2.2.2 The Area-Under-Curve Method

The FSI is computed by integrating the **flame front distance versus time** curve:

$$
\boxed{
FSI \;=\; 0.515 \,\times\, \frac{A_{specimen}}{A_{red\,oak}} \,\times\, 100
}
$$

where $A_{specimen}$ and $A_{red\,oak}$ are the areas under the respective $L_f$ vs. $t$ curves over the standardized 10-minute test duration. The constant **0.515** normalizes the red-oak reference to exactly **FSI = 100**.

### 2.2.3 The Three NFPA Classes

| NFPA Class | FSI Range | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A (I)** | **0 – 25** | Brick, gypsum board, cement board, fiber-cement |
| **Class B (II)** | **26 – 75** | Treated lumber, fire-retardant plywood |
| **Class C (III)** | **76 – 200** | Standard plywood, low-density fiberboard |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you need to answer PESFT528 Module 1 examination problems. Memorize the unit conventions — the examiners are strict.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i = \dfrac{1}{t - t_0} \sum_{k} \bigl( T_{s,k} - T_{c,k} \bigr) \, \Delta t$ | Sub-index for one time window (discrete) | BS 476 Pt. 6 |
| 2 | $I = i_1 + i_2 + i_3$ | Total Fire Propagation Index | BS 476 Pt. 6 |
| 3 | $FSI = 0.515 \cdot \dfrac{A_{sp}}{A_{ro}} \cdot 100$ | Flame Spread Index | ASTM E84 |
| 4 | $V_f = \dfrac{\Phi_{net}}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)}$ | Theoretical flame spread velocity (energy-balance) | de Ris / Quintiere |
| 5 | $V_f = \dfrac{\lambda_g \, \Delta T_{ig}}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)^{2}}$ | Opposed-flow flame spread (thin solid) | de Ris (1969) |
| 6 | $FPI_{cone} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{max} \times 10^{3}}{t_{ig}^{2}}$ | Cone-calorimeter empirical FPI | ISO 14692 variant |
| 7 | $FIGRA = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{max}}{t_{max}}$ | Fire Growth Rate (Eurocode classification) | EN 13823 (SBI) |
| 8 | $\Phi = h_c \, (T_f - T_{ig})$ | Critical heat flux for ignition (Babrauskas) | Piloted ignition |
| 9 | $t_{ig} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, k \, \rho \, c \, \left( \dfrac{T_{ig} - T_s}{\dot{q}'' - \dot{q}''_{cr}} \right)^{2}$ | Inertial ignition time (semi-infinite solid) | Quintiere |
| 10 | $\delta = \sqrt{\alpha_s \, t}$ | Thermal penetration depth ($\alpha_s$ = thermal diffusivity) | Heat-conduction theory |

**Nomenclature used in the table above:**

* $T_{s,k}$ = specimen temperature rise reading at time step $k$ (K or °C)
* $T_{c,k}$ = calibration (asbestos) temperature rise at the same time step (K or °C)
* $\Delta t$ = sampling interval (s or min)
* $A_{sp}, A_{ro}$ = area under flame-front distance vs. time curve for specimen and red-oak (m·s)
* $\Phi_{net}$ = net heat flux to unburned surface ahead of flame (kW/m²)
* $\rho_s, c_s$ = solid density (kg/m³) and specific heat (J/kg·K)
* $\delta$ = thermal penetration depth (m)
* $T_{ig}, T_s$ = ignition temperature and virgin surface temperature (K)
* $\lambda_g$ = gas-phase thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
* $\dot{Q}_{max}$ = peak heat release rate (kW)
* $t_{ig}$ = time to ignition (s)
* $h_c$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
* $T_f$ = adiabatic flame temperature (K)
* $\dot{q}'', \dot{q}''_{cr}$ = imposed and critical heat flux (kW/m²)
* $\alpha_s$ = solid thermal diffusivity (m²/s)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Flame Propagation Index is **not** an academic curiosity. It is the bedrock of three global regulatory regimes:

1. **Building Code Compliance** — Determines whether a wall lining can be used in a high-rise escape corridor (e.g., UK Class 0, US NFPA Class A).
2. **Material Selection in Transport** — Aircraft cabin panels, railway seat trims, and ship bulkheads are all screened by ASTM E84 or BS 476 Pt. 6 equivalents.
3. **Insurance Underwriting** — Factory Mutual (FM Global) and LPCB (Loss Prevention Certification Board) use the index to set property-insurance premiums on industrial materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The FPI is *complementary* to the Heat Release Rate (HRR). HRR tells you how much energy a material releases when it burns; the FPI tells you how *fast* the burning front travels. A material can have low HRR but very high FPI (e.g., a thin tissue paper), or high HRR but moderate FPI (e.g., a thick charring timber). The two parameters together fully describe the early-stage fire hazard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BS 476 Part 6 Sub-Index Formula

The BS 476 Part 6 sub-index $i_s$ for a given time window is derived from a **time-averaged excess-temperature integral**. The logic is presented below in exhaustive step-by-step form.

### Step 1 — Define the physical observable

For each time $t$ during the test, the **exhaust-gas temperature rise** is measured for the specimen ($\Delta T_s$) and for the asbestos-calibration board ($\Delta T_c$). The quantity of interest is the **net excess temperature** contributed by the specimen's own combustion:

$$
\Delta T_{excess}(t) \;=\; \Delta T_s(t) - \Delta T_c(t)
$$

This step removes the *background* thermal exposure that the asbestos board (which does not burn) experiences, leaving only the temperature rise attributable to the specimen's chemical heat release.

### Step 2 — Define the total thermal energy contribution over a window

The total thermal "input" of the specimen to the fire over a time window $[t_0, t]$ is proportional to the **time integral** of the excess temperature:

$$
E_{thermal} \;=\; \int_{t_0}^{t} \Delta T_{excess}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

This integral is a direct proxy for the integrated heat release during that window — higher and longer temperature rise means more energy released.

### Step 3 — Normalize by the window duration

To express the sub-index as a **mean excess temperature** (analogous to an average power density), divide by the window length:

$$
\overline{\Delta T}_{excess} \;=\; \frac{1}{t - t_0} \int_{t_0}^{t} \Delta T_{excess}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### Step 4 — Express in discrete summation form

In practice, the temperature is recorded at discrete time steps $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ separated by an interval $\Delta t$. The continuous integral becomes a discrete sum:

$$
\boxed{
i_s \;=\; \frac{1}{t - t_0} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bigl( T_{s,k} - T_{c,k} \bigr) \, \Delta t
}
$$

The summation index $k$ runs over all measurement points within the time window $[t_0, t]$.

### Step 5 — Assemble the total Fire Propagation Index

The total index is the linear sum of the three sub-indices:

$$
\boxed{
I \;=\; i_1 + i_2 + i_3
}
$$

This linear-additive form assumes that the three time windows measure **independent physical phenomena**: (1) ignitability, (2) initial growth, (3) sustained propagation.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing a Fire Propagation Index

A specimen was tested in the BS 476 Part 6 apparatus. The following discrete temperature-rise data were recorded (temperatures in °C, time in minutes):

| Time (min) | $T_{s,k}$ (Specimen) | $T_{c,k}$ (Calibration) | Excess $T_{s,k} - T_{c,k}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0.10 | 12 | 4 | 8 |
| 0.20 | 30 | 8 | 22 |
| 0.25 | 45 | 10 | 35 |
| 0.50 | 78 | 18 | 60 |
| 0.75 | 110 | 26 | 84 |
| 1.00 | 135 | 32 | 103 |
| 1.50 | 160 | 42 | 118 |
| 2.00 | 175 | 50 | 125 |

**Step 1 — Compute $i_1$ for window [0 → 0.25 min]:**

$$
i_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{0.25 - 0.00} \Bigl[ (0)(0.10) + (8)(0.10) + (22)(0.05) + (35)(0.0) \Bigr]
$$

Using a trapezoidal-style sum: $\bigl[(0+8)/2 \times 0.10\bigr] + \bigl[(8+22)/2 \times 0.10\bigr] + \bigl[(22+35)/2 \times 0.05\bigr]$

$$
i_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{0.25} \Bigl[ 0.4 + 1.5 + 1.425 \Bigr] \;=\; \frac{3.325}{0.25} \;=\; 13.3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $i_2$ for window [0.25 → 1.0 min]:**

The points within this window are at $t = 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00$ min with excess values $35, 60, 84, 103$ °C.

Trapezoidal integration:

$$
\int_{0.25}^{1.0} = 0.25 \times \frac{35+60}{2} + 0.25 \times \frac{60+84}{2} + 0.25 \times \frac{84+103}{2}
$$

$$
= 0.25 \times 47.5 + 0.25 \times 72.0 + 0.25 \times 93.5
$$

$$
= 11.875 + 18.0 + 23.375 = 53.25
$$

$$
i_2 \;=\; \frac{53.25}{1.0 - 0.25} \;=\; \frac{53.25}{0.75} \;=\; 71.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $i_3$ for window [1.0 → 2.0 min]:**

The points within this window are at $t = 1.0, 1.5, 2.0$ min with excess values $103, 118, 125$ °C.

Trapezoidal integration:

$$
\int_{1.0}^{2.0} = 0.5 \times \frac{103+118}{2} + 0.5 \times \frac{118+125}{2}
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 110.5 + 0.5 \times 121.5
$$

$$
= 55.25 + 60.75 = 116.0
$$

$$
i_3 \;=\; \frac{116.0}{2.0 - 1.0} \;=\; 116.0
$$

**Step 4 — Total Fire Propagation Index:**

$$
\boxed{
I \;=\; i_1 + i_2 + i_3 \;=\; 13.3 + 71.0 + 116.0 \;=\; 200.3
}
$$

**Interpretation:** A total FPI of $I = 200.3$ is **extremely high** (well above the red-oak reference of ~12–15). This material would be classified as a **severe fire-propagation hazard** and would be banned from most regulated applications.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Theoretical Flame Spread Velocity (de Ris Model)

For a thin combustible solid sheet heated from the side and ignited at one edge, the **steady opposed-flow flame spread velocity** can be derived from an energy balance on the unburned material just ahead of the flame.

### Step 1 — Identify the control volume

Consider a small element of unburned material of thickness $\delta$ (the thermal penetration depth) located just ahead of the flame front. The flame supplies a net heat flux $\Phi_{net}$ to this element.

### Step 2 — Apply the energy balance

The energy required to raise the element from the ambient temperature $T_s$ to the ignition temperature $T_{ig}$ is:

$$
Q_{req} \;=\; \rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)
$$

where $\rho_s c_s \delta$ is the **thermal mass per unit area** of the preheat zone.

### Step 3 — Equate supplied energy to required energy

The flame supplies this energy at the rate $\Phi_{net}$ over a time interval $dt$. The element of unburned material is exposed for a time $dt = \delta / V_f$ (the time for the flame front to traverse one thermal-penetration-depth). Therefore:

$$
\Phi_{net} \cdot \frac{\delta}{V_f} \;=\; \rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $V_f$

Cancel $\delta$ on both sides and rearrange:

$$
V_f \;=\; \frac{\Phi_{net}}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)}
$$

This is the **simplest energy-balance flame spread velocity** (Quintiere's form). Substituting the de Ris expression for $\Phi_{net} = \lambda_g \Delta T_{ig} / \delta$ (in the limit of a thin solid with a thin gas-phase flame) gives:

$$
\boxed{
V_f \;=\; \frac{\lambda_g \, \Delta T_{ig}}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)^{2}}
}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The flame spreads *faster* when the material has **lower thermal mass** ($\rho_s c_s$), **lower ignition temperature** (small $T_{ig} - T_s$), or **higher gas-phase conductivity** ($\lambda_g$). This is why thin, low-density materials (tissue, foam) propagate flame rapidly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Index Changes with Material Properties

| Material Property | Direction of Change | Effect on FPI / FSI | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density $\rho_s$** | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** | Dense materials resist flame spread |
| **Specific heat $c_s$** | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** | High heat capacity absorbs preheat energy |
| **Thermal conductivity $k_s$** | $\uparrow$ | **Complex** | Conducts heat ahead — may increase or decrease spread |
| **Ignition temperature $T_{ig}$** | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** | Harder to ignite = slower spread |
| **Thickness $\tau$** | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** (beyond $\delta$) | Thick samples behave as semi-infinite solids |
| **Surface emissivity $\varepsilon$** | $\uparrow$ | **Increases** | Better radiative feedback to unburned surface |
| **Char yield** | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** | Char layer insulates underlying material |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Propagation Test Procedure — BS 476 Part 6 Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional block architecture** of the BS 476 Part 6 test and index-calculation procedure, from specimen mounting to final classification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Specimen Preparation 228 mm x 228 mm] --> A2[Mount in Furnace Frame]
    A2 --> A3[Start Gas Burner + Electric Radiant]
    A3 --> A4[Record Specimen Exhaust Temp Ts vs t]
    A3 --> A5[Record Calibration Exhaust Temp Tc vs t]
    A4 --> A6[Compute Excess Temp Tsk minus Tck]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> B1[Window 1: 0 to 0.25 min]
    A6 --> B2[Window 2: 0.25 to 1.0 min]
    A6 --> B3[Window 3: 1.0 to 2.0 min]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Sub Index i1 by Trapezoidal Integration]
    B2 --> C2[Compute Sub Index i2 by Trapezoidal Integration]
    B3 --> C3[Compute Sub Index i3 by Trapezoidal Integration]
    C1 --> D1[Total Fire Propagation Index I]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{I less than 12 AND i1 less than 6}
    E1 -- YES --> E2[Class 0 Limited Propagability]
    E1 -- NO --> E3[Reject or Higher Class]
```

## 4.2 Flame Spread Physics — Energy Flow Block Diagram

The diagram below traces the **energy flows** that govern flame propagation on a solid surface, mapping the de Ris / Quintiere theoretical framework into a process flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Flame Front Heat Source] --> P2[Convective Heat Flux to Solid]
    P1 --> P3[Radiative Heat Flux to Solid]
    P2 --> P4[Unburned Material Preheat Zone]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Thermal Penetration Depth delta]
    P5 --> P6{Temperature Reaches T ign}
    P6 -- YES --> P7[Local Pyrolysis Gas Release]
    P7 --> P8[New Flame Front Advances by Vf]
    P6 -- NO --> P4
    P8 --> P1
```

## 4.3 ASTM E84 Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Material Tested in Steiner Tunnel] --> S1[Compute Area Under Lf vs t Curve]
    S1 --> S2[Normalize to Red Oak Reference FSI equals 100]
    S2 --> Q1{FSI less than or equal to 25}
    S2 --> Q2{FSI between 26 and 75}
    S2 --> Q3{FSI between 76 and 200}
    Q1 --> R1[NFPA Class A or I]
    Q2 --> R2[NFPA Class B or II]
    Q3 --> R3[NFPA Class C or III]
```

## 4.4 Index Comparison — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Fire Propagation Indices] --> H2[Flame Spread Index FSI from ASTM E84]
    H1 --> H3[Fire Propagation Index FPI from BS 476 Part 6]
    H1 --> H4[Fire Growth Rate FIGRA from EN 13823]
    H1 --> H5[Cone FPI from ISO 14692]
    H2 --> H6[Measures Flame Front Distance vs Time]
    H3 --> H7[Measures Exhaust Temperature Rise vs Time]
    H4 --> H8[Measures Peak HRR Divided by Time to Peak]
    H5 --> H9[Uses Peak HRR and Ignition Time]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting**
> The single most common reason students lose marks on this topic is **mixing up the two indices**. The examiner will *deliberately* test whether you know that:
> * **FSI (ASTM E84)** is based on **flame-front distance** $\Rightarrow$ answer in **meters** / **dimensionless score 0–200**.
> * **FPI (BS 476 Pt. 6)** is based on **exhaust temperature rise** $\Rightarrow$ answer in **°C or K** / **additive sub-index score 0–∞**.
> A second common error is **forgetting the calibration-subtraction step** $T_{s,k} - T_{c,k}$ in BS 476 Pt. 6. The calibration removes the *background furnace temperature*; without it, the index is meaningless. Always write $T_s - T_c$, never just $T_s$.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Flame Propagation Index" as per BS 476 Part 6. State its classification threshold for a Class 0 material.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fire Propagation Index (FPI)**, as defined in BS 476 Part 6, is a numerical score that quantifies a material's contribution to the early growth of a fire, derived from the time integral of the excess exhaust-gas temperature rise during a standardized 20-minute furnace test.

The excess temperature is computed as the specimen's temperature rise minus the asbestos-calibration board's temperature rise at each time step. The index is the linear sum of three sub-indices: $I = i_1 + i_2 + i_3$, corresponding to the time windows [0–0.25 min], [0.25–1.0 min], and [1.0–2.0 min].

For a material to be classified as **Class 0 (Limited Propagability)** under UK Building Regulations, the total index must satisfy $I \leq 12$ **and** the first sub-index $i_1 \leq 6$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Flame Spread Index (ASTM E84) and the Fire Propagation Index (BS 476 Part 6) with respect to (i) the physical quantity measured, and (ii) the calibration reference material.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Physical quantity measured:**
* **FSI (ASTM E84):** Measures the **flame-front distance** $L_f$ (in meters) as a function of time inside the Steiner Tunnel. The index is proportional to the area under the $L_f$ vs. $t$ curve.
* **FPI (BS 476 Pt. 6):** Measures the **exhaust-gas temperature rise** $\Delta T$ (in °C or K) above baseline as a function of time. The index is the integral of excess temperature over the first 2 minutes.

**(ii) Calibration reference:**
* **FSI (ASTM E84):** **Red-oak flooring** is the primary reference (FSI = 100); **cement board** is the zero reference (FSI = 0).
* **FPI (BS 476 Pt. 6):** **Asbestos-cement board** is the calibration material (used to subtract the background furnace contribution); **red-oak** is used as a positive benchmark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **any one** of the following. Each carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the BS 476 Part 6 sub-index formula $i_s = \dfrac{1}{t - t_0} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bigl( T_{s,k} - T_{c,k} \bigr) \, \Delta t$, starting from the definition of the excess exhaust temperature. Explain the physical significance of the three time windows [0–0.25], [0.25–1.0], and [1.0–2.0] minutes. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A new polymer composite material is tested in the BS 476 Part 6 apparatus. The excess temperature data $T_{s,k} - T_{c,k}$ in °C at one-minute sampling intervals are:

| Time (min) | 0.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 3.0 | 4.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Excess (°C) | 0 | 18 | 55 | 92 | 120 |

Compute the **total Fire Propagation Index** $I$ (assuming only the 0–4 min integral, with three sub-windows of equal length). Comment on whether this material can be classified as **Class 0**. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(a)

**Derivation** — The sub-index $i_s$ is defined as the **time-averaged excess temperature** within a specified time window. The derivation proceeds as follows:

* **Step 1:** At any instant $t$, the specimen exhaust shows a temperature rise $\Delta T_s(t)$, while the asbestos-calibration board shows $\Delta T_c(t)$. The **excess** rise attributable to the specimen's own combustion is $\Delta T_s(t) - \Delta T_c(t)$.
* **Step 2:** The total thermal contribution over a window $[t_0, t]$ is the time integral: $\int_{t_0}^{t} \bigl[\Delta T_s(\tau) - \Delta T_c(\tau)\bigr] d\tau$.
* **Step 3:** Dividing by the window length gives a **mean excess temperature**, which is the sub-index: $i_s = \dfrac{1}{t - t_0} \int_{t_0}^{t} \bigl[\Delta T_s - \Delta T_c\bigr] d\tau$.
* **Step 4:** Discretizing with sampling interval $\Delta t$ over $N$ data points yields $i_s = \dfrac{1}{t - t_0} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (T_{s,k} - T_{c,k}) \Delta t$.

**Physical significance of the three windows:**

* **Window 1 [0 – 0.25 min]:** The **ignitability** window. It tests whether the material ignites easily when first exposed — the most critical period for early fire initiation.
* **Window 2 [0.25 – 1.0 min]:** The **initial growth** window. It captures how rapidly the flame builds once the material is alight, reflecting the rate of heat release during incipient fire.
* **Window 3 [1.0 – 2.0 min]:** The **sustained propagation** window. It evaluates whether the material supports a self-sustaining propagating flame — analogous to whether the material behaves as a "fuel track" for the fire.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(b)

The data are sampled at $\Delta t = 1$ min intervals from 0 to 4 min. We are told to assume three **equal sub-windows**, so we partition the 0–4 min data into three windows of 1 min each (this is a slight non-standard adaptation):

* **Window 1:** 0 → 1 min, data points (0, 18) — trap rule: $\int = 0.5 \times (0 + 18) = 9.0$
* **Window 2:** 1 → 2 min, data points (18, 55) — trap rule: $\int = 0.5 \times (18 + 55) = 36.5$
* **Window 3:** 2 → 3 min — using excess values (55, 92) — trap rule: $\int = 0.5 \times (55 + 92) = 73.5$

(We will use only the first 3 minutes; the 4-min data point is a distractor or for cross-verification.)

Now compute sub-indices:

$$
i_1 = \frac{9.0}{1 - 0} = 9.0
$$

$$
i_2 = \frac{36.5}{2 - 1} = 36.5
$$

$$
i_3 = \frac{73.5}{3 - 2} = 73.5
$$

**Total Fire Propagation Index:**

$$
\boxed{
I = i_1 + i_2 + i_3 = 9.0 + 36.5 + 73.5 = 119.0
}
$$

**Classification commentary:**

For Class 0 status, the BS 476 criteria require $I \leq 12$ **and** $i_1 \leq 6$.

Here $I = 119.0 \gg 12$ and $i_1 = 9.0 > 6$. **Therefore, this material CANNOT be classified as Class 0.** It is a high fire-propagation-hazard material and would be rejected for use in regulated applications such as internal corridor linings or escape-route enclosures.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a clear block diagram, explain the functional architecture of the BS 476 Part 6 Fire Propagation Test, from specimen preparation to final classification. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the de Ris / Quintiere theoretical framework, derive the **opposed-flow flame spread velocity** $V_f$ for a thin combustible solid. Clearly state the assumptions. Discuss how the FPI is empirically related to the heat release rate and time to ignition in the cone-calorimeter formulation. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(a)

The functional block architecture of the BS 476 Part 6 test is:

1. **Specimen Preparation Block:** A 228 mm × 228 mm specimen of the test material is conditioned and mounted in a standard frame.
2. **Heat Exposure Block:** A gas-fired tubular furnace is ignited, and an electric radiant element is energized. The combination delivers a pre-programmed, monotonically rising thermal exposure for 20 minutes.
3. **Temperature Acquisition Block:** Two thermocouples continuously record the exhaust-gas temperature: one for the specimen ($\Delta T_s$) and one for the asbestos-calibration board ($\Delta T_c$).
4. **Signal Processing Block:** The excess signal $\Delta T_s - \Delta T_c$ is computed at each time step and stored digitally.
5. **Window Selection Block:** The first 2 minutes of the test signal are partitioned into three sub-windows.
6. **Sub-Index Computation Block:** A trapezoidal numerical integration is performed within each sub-window to yield $i_1, i_2, i_3$.
7. **Index Aggregation Block:** The total $I = i_1 + i_2 + i_3$ is computed.
8. **Classification Block:** The decision logic applies the UK Class 0 threshold: $I \leq 12$ AND $i_1 \leq 6$ $\Rightarrow$ Class 0; otherwise the material is rejected for limited-propagability use.

(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for a visual representation of this architecture.)

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(b)

**Derivation of the opposed-flow flame spread velocity:**

* **Assumption 1:** Steady-state, one-dimensional flame spread along a thin solid.
* **Assumption 2:** The solid is thermally thin — its thickness $\tau$ is much less than the thermal penetration depth $\delta = \sqrt{\alpha_s t}$.
* **Assumption 3:** The dominant heat-transfer mechanism from flame to unburned solid is convection (with a radiative correction absorbed into $\Phi_{net}$).
* **Assumption 4:** The flame front advances at a constant velocity $V_f$ in the laboratory frame.

**Energy balance on a control volume of unburned material just ahead of the flame:**

The flame supplies a net heat flux $\Phi_{net}$ to the preheat zone. The time for the flame to traverse one thermal penetration depth is $dt = \delta / V_f$. The energy required to heat the preheat zone from $T_s$ to $T_{ig}$ is:

$$
Q_{req} = \rho_s c_s \delta \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)
$$

Equating supplied and required energies:

$$
\Phi_{net} \cdot \frac{\delta}{V_f} = \rho_s c_s \delta \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)
$$

Solving:

$$
V_f = \frac{\Phi_{net}}{\rho_s c_s \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)}
$$

For the **de Ris limit** (thin solid, thin gas-phase flame), the net heat flux simplifies to $\Phi_{net} = \lambda_g \Delta T_{ig} / \delta$, yielding:

$$
\boxed{
V_f = \frac{\lambda_g \Delta T_{ig}}{\rho_s c_s \delta \bigl( T_{ig} - T_s \bigr)^{2}}
}
$$

**Cone-calorimeter FPI (empirical formulation):**

The cone-calorimeter (ISO 5660) provides an empirical FPI for screening polymer materials. The widely used form is:

$$
FPI_{cone} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{max} \times 10^{3}}{t_{ig}^{2}}
$$

where $\dot{Q}_{max}$ is the peak heat release rate (kW/m²) and $t_{ig}$ is the time to sustained ignition (s). The numerator represents the **energy output**; the denominator represents the **ignition delay** squared, which weights the index heavily against materials that ignite quickly. This is the *practical* index used in petrochemical plant safety assessments (e.g., the API 2218 standard for offshore fire protection).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

* **The Flame Propagation Index is an empirical, standardized score** — not a fundamental physical constant. Always quote the standard (BS 476 Pt. 6 or ASTM E84).
* **BS 476 Part 6 → FPI** uses **exhaust temperature rise**; three time windows [0–0.25], [0.25–1.0], [1.0–2.0] min; $I = i_1 + i_2 + i_3$.
* **ASTM E84 → FSI** uses **flame-front distance** in the Steiner Tunnel; red-oak = 100, cement board = 0.
* **Class 0 (UK):** $I \leq 12$ **AND** $i_1 \leq 6$.
* **NFPA Class A:** FSI 0–25; **Class B:** FSI 26–75; **Class C:** FSI 76–200.
* **Calibration subtraction is essential** in BS 476 — always compute $T_s - T_c$, not just $T_s$.
* **Cone-calorimeter FPI:** $FPI_{cone} = \dot{Q}_{max} \times 10^{3} / t_{ig}^{2}$.
* **Theoretical flame spread (de Ris):** $V_f = \lambda_g \Delta T_{ig} / [\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_s)^2]$.
* **Higher density, higher specific heat, higher ignition temperature → LOWER index** (safer material).
* **Higher emissivity, higher surface temperature, lower thickness → HIGHER index** (more hazardous).
* **FPI and HRR are complementary:** FPI measures *how fast* fire grows; HRR measures *how much* energy is released.
* **Engineering applications:** building code compliance, transport safety (aircraft/rail/ship), insurance underwriting, petrochemical facility design.
* **Common exam trap:** Mixing up FSI and FPI units (meters vs. °C) and forgetting the calibration subtraction. Always check both!

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Release Rate (HRR or Q̇)** is the rate at which thermal energy (heat) is generated by a fire as a function of time, expressed in **watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**. Mathematically, it represents the instantaneous derivative of total heat energy released with respect to time:
> 
> $$\dot{Q}(t) = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, HRR is the **single most influential parameter** in characterizing a fire's hazard, growth behavior, and propagation potential. It governs temperature development, smoke production, flame spread, and ultimately tenability of escape routes.

The fundamental governing relationship that defines HRR from first principles is:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_{c} \cdot \chi_{c}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = **mass loss rate** of the fuel (kg/s)
- $\Delta H_{c}$ = **heat of combustion** of the fuel (kJ/kg or MJ/kg)
- $\chi_{c}$ = **combustion efficiency** (dimensionless, 0 ≤ χ ≤ 1)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Burning Campfire**
> 
> Imagine a campfire. The rate at which it "pours out" heat is the HRR. Now think of three things:
> 1. **How fast are you throwing wood in?** → That's the *mass loss rate* (ṁ). More wood per second = more heat.
> 2. **What type of wood is it?** → That's the *heat of combustion* (ΔHc). Dry hardwood like oak burns hotter per kilogram than soft pine.
> 3. **Is the fire well-ventilated or choking on its own smoke?** → That's the *combustion efficiency* (χ). In an open fire, almost all vapor burns (χ ≈ 0.9). In a sealed fireplace, much energy escapes as unburned smoke (χ drops to 0.5 or below).
> 
> The HRR is the *product* of these three effects. Doubling any one of them doubles the heat output.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Values for KTU Examinations:**
> 
> | Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
> |-----------|---------------|-------|
> | Heat of combustion of wood (cellulose) | **17.5** | MJ/kg |
> | Heat of combustion of heptane | **44.4** | MJ/kg |
> | Heat of combustion of PMMA | **24.9** | MJ/kg |
> | Heat of combustion of polyurethane foam | **23.0 – 28.0** | MJ/kg |
> | Heat of combustion of methane | **50.0** | MJ/kg |
> | Heat of combustion of hydrogen | **142.0** | MJ/kg |
> | Heat released per kg O₂ consumed | **13.1 × 10³** | kJ/kg O₂ |
> | Stoichiometric O₂/fuel ratio for most hydrocarbons | **≈ 1.3 – 1.5** | dimensionless |

The constant **13.1 MJ per kg of O₂ consumed** is the cornerstone of *Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry* — the most widely used laboratory technique for measuring HRR (Huggett's constant).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the HRR Equation

The classical expression $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$ is deceptively simple. Each term is governed by its own physics:

- **Mass Loss Rate (ṁ):** The mass of pyrolyzing fuel per unit time. It is a function of:
  - Surface heat flux
  - Material thermal properties (k, ρ, cₚ)
  - Pyrolysis temperature
  - External heat feedback (re-radiation from flame, convection from hot gas layer)

- **Heat of Combustion (ΔHc):** A thermodynamic property of the fuel describing the total chemical energy released if all the fuel were perfectly oxidized. It is a *fuel property*, not a fire property. Two versions exist:
  - **Higher Heating Value (HHV):** Includes latent heat recovered from water vapor condensation.
  - **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Excludes water vapor condensation. **For fire calculations, LHV is conventionally used.**

- **Combustion Efficiency (χ):** The fraction of pyrolyzate that actually reacts with oxygen to release energy. The remainder escapes as unburned hydrocarbons (smoke, soot, CO). χ depends on **ventilation conditions**:
  - **Well-ventilated (fuel-controlled):** χ ≈ 0.7 – 0.95
  - **Under-ventilated (ventilation-controlled):** χ can fall below 0.5

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive HRR Formula Reference (Board-Exam Ready):**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$ | HRR from mass loss rate | kW |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{conv} = \chi_{conv} \cdot \dot{Q}$ | Convective heat release rate | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_{rad} \cdot \dot{Q}$ | Radiative heat release rate | kW |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \frac{E}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \dot{Q} \, dt}{t_2 - t_1}$ | Time-averaged HRR | kW |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2} \cdot \rho_{O_2} \cdot \Delta H_{O_2}}{M_{O_2}}$ | HRR via O₂ consumption | kW |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} \approx 13.1 \times \dot{m}_{O_2}$ | Huggett's constant (oxygen calorimetry) | kW |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$ | t² fire growth model | kW |
| 8 | $t_g = \sqrt{\frac{\dot{Q}_0}{\alpha}}$ | Time to reach HRR = $\dot{Q}_0$ | s |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Mass loss rate from density, area, regression velocity | kg/s |
| 10 | $\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{q}''_{net}}{\Delta H_v}$ | Surface mass flux via net heat flux | kg/(m²·s) |

## 2.3 t-Squared Fire Growth Curves (NFPA 72 / ISO 16732)

For design and risk-assessment purposes, fires are characterized by standardized growth rates that follow the parabolic law:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Growth Coefficients (α in kW/s²):**
> 
> - **Slow (e.g., heavy timber):** α = **0.00293** kW/s² → 1000 kW in ~583 s (≈ 10 min)
> - **Medium (e.g., wood pallets stacked):** α = **0.01172** kW/s² → 1000 kW in ~292 s (≈ 5 min)
> - **Fast (e.g., upholstered furniture):** α = **0.0469** kW/s² → 1000 kW in ~146 s (≈ 2.4 min)
> - **Ultra-fast (e.g., high-rack storage, flammable liquids):** α = **0.1876** kW/s² → 1000 kW in ~73 s (≈ 1.2 min)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HRR is the *master input* in every fire-engineering model:

- **CFD fire simulation** (FDS, Fluent) — HRR vs. time curves drive temperature and velocity fields.
- **Smoke layer interface descent** — Calculated using the convective HRR fraction.
- **Detection and sprinkler activation** — Detector trip time depends on ceiling jet temperature, which scales with Q̇^(2/3).
- **Structural fire engineering** — Steel temperature evolution uses the compartment HRR.
- **ASET / RSET analysis** — Available Safe Egress Time is bracketed using HRR-based hazard calculations.
