<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Radiative Heat Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Radiative Heat Release

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiative Heat Release (Q̇_rad)** is defined as the rate at which thermal energy is emitted from a fire in the form of electromagnetic radiation (predominantly in the infrared and visible spectrum, wavelengths 0.4–100 μm). It is a constituent component of the **Total Heat Release Rate (THRR / Q̇_total)** of a fire, alongside the convective heat release (Q̇_conv) and the conductive heat release (Q̇_cond). Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$Q_{\text{rad}} = \chi_r \cdot Q_{\text{total}}$$

where **χ_r (chi-r)** is the **radiation fraction** of the fire, a dimensionless empirical parameter ranging from 0 (non-radiative, e.g., hydrogen flames) to nearly 0.5–0.6 (highly luminous, heavily sooting flames such as pool fires of gasoline or kerosene).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing near a bonfire on a cold winter night. You feel warmth on your face *without* the hot air actually moving to reach you. This invisible, line-of-sight warmth is exactly **thermal radiation**. The bonfire is releasing energy in two ways:
1. The hot gases and flame rise upward, heating the air around them (this is *convection*).
2. The luminous, glowing soot particles inside the flame emit infrared rays that travel in straight lines and strike your skin directly (this is *radiation*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Radiative heat release is the "glow energy" of a fire — the part of the heat that reaches you through light-like rays from the flames and hot soot, *not* the part carried away by the rising hot air. It is the dominant mechanism for **ignition of distant targets**, **spread of fire across compartments**, and **tenability of occupants** in enclosed fire scenarios.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | σ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | Fundamental constant for blackbody emission |
| Radiation Fraction | χ_r | 0 to ~0.6 | dimensionless | Depends heavily on fuel sooting tendency |
| Flame Emissivity | ε | 0 to 1 | dimensionless | Effectiveness of surface as a radiator |
| Soot Volume Fraction | f_v | 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ | — | Controls emissivity in luminous flames |
| Luminous Flame Temperature | T_f | 1000–1700 | K | Drives 4th-power dependence |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Fire Radiation

Thermal radiation from a fire originates from three distinct sources:
1. **Luminous Soot Particles** — The dominant radiator in hydrocarbon diffusion flames. Soot volume fraction (f_v) and the mean soot path length (L) determine the optical thickness.
2. **Combustion Gases (CO₂ and H₂O vapor)** — Emit/absorb in discrete infrared bands. Their contribution is significant in large-scale fires where the optical path length is long.
3. **Hot Smoke Layer** — In compartment fires, the accumulated hot smoke beneath the ceiling radiates downward as a semi-infinite gray gas surface.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does soot radiate so effectively?**
Soot particles are sub-micron carbonaceous spheres (typical diameter ~10–50 nm). Their size is comparable to the wavelength of thermal infrared radiation (~1–10 μm), which puts them in the **Rayleigh scattering regime**. They are extremely efficient broadband absorbers and emitters across the entire infrared spectrum.

**How is radiative heat release estimated?**
Estimation follows three logical layers:
- **Layer 1 — Thermal Source:** Determine the flame's emissive power (E) using Stefan-Boltzmann and emissivity.
- **Layer 2 — Geometric Coupling:** Compute the **view factor (F₁₂)** between the fire source and the target using shape, distance, and orientation.
- **Layer 3 — Atmospheric Attenuation:** Apply the transmissivity (τ) of the intervening smoke/air to account for energy lost along the path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental radiative flux at a target is always:
> **Heat Flux = Emissive Power × View Factor × Transmissivity**
> This is the cornerstone relation for ALL KTU problems on this topic.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{rad}} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{total}}$ | Radiation fraction definition | kW or MW | Direct HRR partition |
| 2 | $\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (emitted flux) | kW/m² | Blackbody/gray body flux |
| 3 | $E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4$ | Flame Emissive Power | kW/m² | Fire plume surface emission |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$ | Mean Beam Emissivity (optically thin/thick) | — | Gases and luminous flames |
| 5 | $\dot{q}_r'' = E \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau$ | Radiative flux at a target | kW/m² | Point source / point target |
| 6 | $F_{12} = \frac{1}{A_1}\int\!\!\int \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\, dA_1 dA_2$ | View Factor (configuration factor) | dimensionless | Geometric coupling |
| 7 | $\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}}{4\pi r^2}$ | Point Source Model | kW/m² | Far-field radiation in open fires |
| 8 | $\tau = e^{-\kappa_s L_s}$ | Smoke Transmissivity (Beer-Lambert) | — | Attenuation through smoke |
| 9 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}} = \dot{m} \cdot \chi_r \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Radiative HRR from mass burn rate | kW | Pool fire / crib fire analysis |
| 10 | $E_f = E_{\text{exp}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\kappa L}\right)$ | Luminous Flame Emissive Power | kW/m² | Sooty diffusion flame |

**Notes on Table Notation:**
- In Formula 6, $\cos\theta_1$ and $\cos\theta_2$ are the angles between the normal to each surface and the line connecting the two differential areas separated by distance $r$.
- In Formula 4, $\kappa$ is the absorption coefficient (m⁻¹) and $L$ is the path length (m).
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg); $\dot{m}$ = mass loss rate (kg/s).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Q̇_rad Estimation |
|---|---|
| **Fire Protection Engineering** | Sizing of separation distances between buildings (e.g., NFPA 80A) |
| **Compartment Fire Design** | Predicting flashover via ceiling radiation feedback |
| **Industrial Safety** | Designing thermal shields around LNG pool fires and JP-8 spills |
| **Wildland-Urban Interface (WUI)** | Estimating ember shower and brand ignition distances |
| **Forensic Fire Investigation** | Reconstructing fire size from burn patterns and radiation marks |
| **Spacecraft & Aircraft Safety** | NASA AST Materials ignitability testing under radiant flux |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Distribution

The **spectral radiance** of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$ is given by Planck's Law:

$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$$

where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = Speed of light = **3.0 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

The total emitted flux (integrated over all wavelengths and the entire hemisphere) is:

$$E_b = \pi \int_0^{\infty} B_\lambda(T)\, d\lambda$$

Performing this integration (a classical result requiring residue calculus or direct evaluation):

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- Planck's law was empirically constructed to resolve the **ultraviolet catastrophe** of classical Rayleigh-Jeans theory.
- The integration over wavelength collapses the spectral distribution into a single 4th-power law in $T$.
- For a **gray body** (real surface with emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$): $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
- This shows why a small temperature rise dramatically increases radiation — a 100 K rise from 1000 K → 1100 K increases emission by $(1.1)^4 = 1.464$, i.e., a **46% increase**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Beam Emissivity (Wide-Band Model)

For a non-blackbody (real gas or luminous flame), the spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ varies with wavelength. The **mean beam emissivity** is the spectrally averaged emissivity over the path length $L$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{\sigma T^4} \int_0^{\infty} \left[1 - e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}\right] E_{b\lambda}(T)\, d\lambda$$

If the absorption coefficient $\kappa$ is treated as a constant (wide-band approximation, valid when the band is narrow compared to the spectrum or for soot-dominated radiation):

$$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$$

**Derivation step-by-step:**

1. Start with the spectral transmissivity at wavelength $\lambda$ over a path of length $L$ (Beer-Lambert law):
   $$\tau_\lambda = e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}$$

2. Spectral emissivity is by Kirchhoff's law equal to spectral absorptivity:
   $$\varepsilon_\lambda = 1 - \tau_\lambda = 1 - e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}$$

3. Weight by the spectral blackbody intensity $E_{b\lambda}$ to obtain the mean value:
   $$\varepsilon = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} \varepsilon_\lambda E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda}{\int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} \left[1 - e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}\right] E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda}{\sigma T^4}$$

4. For constant $\kappa$ (gray gas assumption): numerator becomes $\sigma T^4 (1 - e^{-\kappa L})$, yielding:
   $$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}}$$

This is a **Killer Result** for KTU exams: as $L \to \infty$ (optically thick), $\varepsilon \to 1$ (blackbody), and as $L \to 0$ (optically thin), $\varepsilon \to \kappa L$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Point Source Approximation for Far-Field Radiation

When the target is **far from the fire** (distance $r$ much greater than the fire diameter $D$), the flame can be modeled as a **point source** of total radiative power $Q_{\text{rad}}$ located at the flame's centroid.

**Step 1: Energy spreading.** The total radiative power $Q_{\text{rad}}$ is distributed uniformly over a sphere of radius $r$ centered at the source. The surface area of this sphere is $4\pi r^2$.

**Step 2: Incident flux at the target.** The flux (power per unit area) at distance $r$ is:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{total}}}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 3: Accounting for orientation and atmospheric loss.** In a more refined model, multiply by the view factor $F_{12}$ (cosine of incidence angle) and the atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{total}} \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 4: Validation against experimental data.** This model is empirically validated for hydrocarbon pool fires when $r/D > 5$. Below this limit, near-field effects (flame shape, non-uniform soot distribution) require a **solid flame model** instead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The point source model is *only* valid in the far field. For a small fire close to a wall, you must use the **solid flame model** (e.g., the Shokri-Beyler correlation or Mudan-Croce correlation). Many students lose marks by applying the point source model universally.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Solid Flame Model (Cylindrical Flame Approximation)

For a cylindrical pool fire of diameter $D$ and visible flame height $L_f$, the radiative flux to an external target at distance $r$ is:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = E_f \cdot F_{12}$$

where $E_f$ is the flame emissive power (typically 20–150 kW/m² for hydrocarbon pool fires) and $F_{12}$ is the view factor from a vertical cylindrical flame to a small target on the ground:

$$F_{12} = \frac{1}{\pi n} \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt{(s^2 - 1)}}\right) + \frac{(a^2 - (s+n)^2)}{\pi n \cdot s} \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{(a^2 - s^2 + 1) \cdot h}{\sqrt{(s^2 - 1)(a^2 - 1)}}\right) \cdots$$

This is the standard **Mudan-Croce correlation**, simplified for a vertical, tilted-cylinder flame. For KTU purposes, the **simplified version** is:

$$F_{12} \approx \frac{1}{4\pi} \cdot \left[\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D/2}{r}\right) + \frac{(D/2)}{r \cdot \sqrt{(D/2)^2 + r^2}}\right] \cdot \text{(geometric correction)}$$

**Practical takeaway for KTU numericals:** The view factor decays as $\sim 1/r^2$ at large $r$, transitioning to $\sim 1/r$ at intermediate distances.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Radiative Heat Release Estimation Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Fire Scenario Identified] --> B{Determine Total HRR Q total via Cone Calorimeter or Mass Loss Method}
    B --> C[Select Fuel Type: Pool, Jet, Compartment, Crib]
    C --> D{Far Field or Near Field?}
    D -- Far Field r/D greater 5 --> E[Apply POINT SOURCE MODEL]
    D -- Near Field r/D less 5 --> F[Apply SOLID FLAME MODEL]
    E --> G[Compute chi r from Empirical Correlation]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute E flame from epsilon sigma T power 4]
    H --> I[Calculate View Factor F12 using geometry]
    I --> J[Apply Atmospheric Transmissivity tau]
    J --> K[Compute Radiative Flux q double prime r in kW per m2]
    K --> L[Compare with Target Ignition Threshold]
    L -- Above Threshold --> M[CRITICAL: Ignition Predicted]
    L -- Below Threshold --> N[SAFE: No Ignition]
    M --> O[END: Design Fire Protection]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Partitioning in a Fire

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Chemical Energy in Fuel delta Hc times m dot] --> COMBUST[Combustion Zone]
    COMBUST --> QTOT[Total Heat Release Rate Q total]
    QTOT --> QCONV[Convective Heat chi c times Q total: Heats Gases and Smoke]
    QTOT --> QRAD[Radiative Heat chi r times Q total: Emitted via EM Waves]
    QTOT --> QCOND[Conductive Heat chi cond times Q total: Heats Adjacent Solids]
    QRAD --> SOOT[Soot Particle Emission: Dominant Source]
    QRAD --> GAS[Combustion Gas Emission: CO2 and H2O Bands]
    QRAD --> SMOKE[Hot Smoke Layer: Ceiling Radiation]
    SOOT --> TARGET[Radiative Flux to Target: q double prime r]
    GAS --> TARGET
    SMOKE --> TARGET
    TARGET --> IGNITE[Possible Ignition if q double prime r exceeds q double prime crit]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Layered Logic of Radiative Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Thermal Source: Flame Emissive Power E equals epsilon sigma T power 4] --> L2[Layer 2 Geometric Coupling: View Factor F12 from Fire to Target]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Path Attenuation: Smoke Transmissivity tau equals exp minus kappa L]
    L3 --> OUT[Output Radiative Flux at Target in kW per m2]
    L1 -.Requires.-> R1[Temperature and Emissivity Data]
    L2 -.Requires.-> R2[Geometry: Distance, Orientation, Dimensions]
    L3 -.Requires.-> R3[Smoke Optical Properties]
    R1 --> L1
    R2 --> L2
    R3 --> L3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Components in Radiative Transfer

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Fuel as Fuel Source
    participant Flame as Luminous Flame
    participant Smoke as Smoke Plume
    participant Target as Remote Target
    Fuel->>Flame: Releases Combustion Energy
    Flame->>Flame: Generates Soot Particles
    Flame->>Smoke: Emits IR and Visible Radiation
    Flame->>Target: Direct Line of Sight Radiation
    Smoke->>Smoke: Absorbs and Reemits along Path
    Smoke->>Target: Attenuated Transmitted Radiation
    Target->>Target: Net Incident Flux equals E times F12 times tau
    Target-->>Flame: If Flux exceeds Critical Value: Ignition Loop
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the radiation fraction (χ_r) of a fire and state its typical range for (i) methanol, (ii) methane, and (iii) toluene pool fires.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **radiation fraction χ_r** is the dimensionless ratio of the radiative heat release rate to the total heat release rate of a fire:

$$\chi_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{total}}}$$

- **(i) Methanol pool fire:** χ_r ≈ **0.10 to 0.15** (low soot, nearly non-luminous, blue flame).
- **(ii) Methane (LNG) pool fire:** χ_r ≈ **0.15 to 0.20** (moderate, mostly non-luminous).
- **(iii) Toluene pool fire:** χ_r ≈ **0.40 to 0.55** (heavily sooting, luminous yellow/orange flame).

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for reasoning (soot content dependence), 1 mark for values.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why are soot particles the dominant radiators in hydrocarbon diffusion flames?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Soot particles dominate radiation in hydrocarbon flames for three reasons:
1. **Size comparable to infrared wavelength:** Soot particles are typically 10–50 nm in diameter, comparable to thermal IR wavelengths (1–10 μm), placing them in the **Rayleigh regime** where absorption and emission cross-sections are extremely high.
2. **High volume fraction in luminous flames:** Soot volume fraction $f_v$ in luminous flames reaches 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵, leading to large optical thickness and emissivity approaching 1.
3. **Broadband continuum emission:** Unlike CO₂ and H₂O which emit only in narrow bands, soot radiates across the entire IR spectrum, making it an efficient broadband emitter.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per point. Keyword "Rayleigh regime" is a KTU favourite and earns bonus credit.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — kW vs MW, mm vs m. Marks deducted for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Applying point source model near-field** — Valid only for $r/D > 5$. Examiners will explicitly check this.
> 3. **Ignoring view factor** — Even for point source, $F_{12}$ must appear in the full formulation.
> 4. **Mixing χ_r with ε (emissivity)** — These are different quantities. ε is a property of surface/gas; χ_r is a global fire property.
> 5. **Skipping final statement of units** — Always end the solution with "Therefore, the radiative flux = ___ kW/m²."

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** A circular heptane pool fire of diameter **1.0 m** is burning in the open with a total heat release rate of **1500 kW**. The radiation fraction for heptane is **0.35**. Compute the radiative heat flux incident on a vertical target surface placed at a horizontal distance of **3.0 m** from the center of the pool. Use the point source approximation, and validate that the model is appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch how the radiative flux at the target would change if a smoke layer of optical thickness κL = 1.5 were to develop between the fire and the target. Compute the new attenuated flux. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1: Validate the point source assumption.**
$$D = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad r = 3.0 \text{ m}$$
$$\frac{r}{D} = \frac{3.0}{1.0} = 3.0$$

Since $r/D = 3.0 < 5$, the point source model is at the **edge of validity**. For KTU purposes, we will proceed with the point source model, but note that the **solid flame model** would be more accurate. *(Examiner accepts both, but award 0.5 mark for noting the limitation.)*

**Step 2: Compute the total radiative power.**
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{total}} = 0.35 \times 1500 = 525 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Apply the point source formula with view factor F₁₂ = 1 (face-on perpendicular target).**
$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{total}}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{525}{4\pi (3.0)^2} = \frac{525}{113.10}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{q}_r'' = 4.64 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1: Compute the smoke transmissivity using Beer-Lambert law.**
$$\tau = e^{-\kappa L} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

**Step 2: Compute the attenuated flux at the target.**
$$\dot{q}_{r,\text{attenuated}}'' = \dot{q}_r'' \cdot \tau = 4.64 \times 0.2231$$

$$\boxed{\dot{q}_{r,\text{attenuated}}'' = 1.035 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 3: Percentage reduction in flux.**
$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left(1 - 0.2231\right) \times 100 = 77.69\%$$

The smoke layer absorbs nearly 78% of the incident radiation, reducing the flux from 4.64 kW/m² to about 1.04 kW/m². This is a critical safety consideration: smoke can both obscure vision **and** significantly reduce thermal radiation exposure to fire-fighters.

> **Marking Scheme (7 + 7):** Each sub-part carries 7 marks distributed across definition, calculation, units, and final statement.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the Stefan-Boltzmann law $E = \sigma T^4$ from Planck's spectral distribution. State the value of σ with units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wood crib fire in a compartment has a measured flame temperature of **1200 K** and a mean beam emissivity of **0.45**. The visible flame surface area is **2.5 m²**. Calculate: (i) the total radiative power emitted, and (ii) the radiative flux incident on a small target located **5 m** away, assuming the flame behaves as a point source and the view factor is 0.08. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Start from Planck's spectral radiance:
$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$$

The total emissive power (integrating over hemisphere and all wavelengths):
$$E = \pi \int_0^{\infty} B_\lambda(T)\, d\lambda$$

Substituting and performing the integration:
$$E = \pi \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1)}\, d\lambda$$

Let $u = hc/(\lambda k_B T)$, so $du = -hc/(\lambda^2 k_B T)\, d\lambda$, which gives $d\lambda = -hc/(k_B T u^2)\, du$. The integral transforms to:
$$E = \frac{2\pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1}\, du$$

The integral is a known result equal to $\pi^4/15$:
$$E = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

**Stefan-Boltzmann constant:**
$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

**(i) Total radiative power emitted by the flame surface:**

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.45 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1200)^4$$

$$(1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

$$E = 0.45 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E = 52{,}915 \text{ W/m}^2 = 52.92 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

Total radiative power:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{rad,surface}} = E \times A = 52.92 \times 2.5 = 132.3 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Radiative flux at the target:**

For a point source located at the flame centroid, with view factor F₁₂ = 0.08:
$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rad,surface}} \cdot F_{12}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{132.3 \times 0.08}{4\pi (5)^2}$$

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{10.584}{314.16} = 0.0337 \text{ kW/m}^2 = 33.7 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\dot{q}_r'' = 33.7 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

This is well below the typical **critical flux for piloted ignition of wood (~12–15 kW/m²)**, indicating that at 5 m distance, the target will not ignite from this crib fire.

> **Marking Scheme (7 + 7):** Derivation steps for Part (a) and explicit calculations for Part (b).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- [x] **Radiation fraction χ_r** is fuel-dependent: methanol (low) → gasoline → toluene (high).
- [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁴** — memorize exactly.
- [x] **Emissive power E = εσT⁴** — 4th-power law means temperature is the most sensitive variable.
- [x] **Mean beam emissivity** $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\kappa L}$ — transitions from optically thin (ε = κL) to optically thick (ε → 1).
- [x] **Point source model** valid only when **r/D > 5**; below that, use solid flame model.
- [x] **View factor F₁₂** couples geometry to radiation — must include in all calculations.
- [x] **Beer-Lambert transmissivity** $\tau = e^{-\kappa L}$ — smoke attenuates radiation exponentially.
- [x] **Soot particles** (10–50 nm) are dominant radiators due to Rayleigh regime + broadband emission.
- [x] **Critical ignition flux** for wood: ~12–15 kW/m² (piloted), ~25–30 kW/m² (auto-ignition).
- [x] **Combined formula:** $\dot{q}_r'' = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau$ — master this for any KTU numerical.
- [x] **Distinguish χ_r (global fire property) from ε (local surface/gas property).**
- [x] **For compartment fires:** include ceiling smoke layer radiation feedback.
- [x] **For LNG/heavy hydrocarbon pool fires:** χ_r can exceed 0.4 — never assume small.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Smoke Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Smoke Point # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Smoke Point (SP)** in fire dynamics and propagation theory is defined as the **critical threshold condition** in a developing smoke layer at which the optical density of the accumulated smoke becomes sufficient to drastically impair visibility, hinder occupant escape, and endanger fire-fighter operations. Mathematically, it is the height (or concentration) within a compartment or plume at which the **extinction coefficient ($K$)** of the smoke reaches a critical value ($K_c$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Definition:** The Smoke Point is the height of the smoke layer interface at which the **smoldering-to-optically-dense transition** occurs, conventionally defined as the elevation where $K = K_c \approx \mathbf{0.08 \; m^{-1}}$ (for green light, $\lambda = 632.8 \; nm$). In combustion engineering, the same term is also used for liquid fuels (per **ASTM D1322**), where it represents the **maximum flame height (in mm)** at which a fuel burns without producing continuous smoke. However, within the KTU PESFT528 syllabus (Module 2: Smoke Movement and Visibility), the term refers to the **fire-engineering definition** related to visibility hazard in enclosures. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing in a room filling with **thick fog from a smoke machine**. Initially, you can see the ceiling and walls clearly. As more smoke accumulates near the ceiling, the air becomes hazy, then milky, then opaque. There is a precise moment when the layer of dense smoke descends past your eye level and your visibility drops to a few meters — that descent interface is essentially the **"smoke point."** > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a transparent glass of water being slowly mixed with milk. The "smoke point" is the depth in the glass below which the milk-water mixture becomes so cloudy that you can no longer see through it. That boundary is the **smoke point height ($y_{sp}$)**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Critical Extinction Coefficient ($K_c$):** $\mathbf{0.08 \; m^{-1}}$ (for green light, $632.8 \; nm$) - **Specific Extinction Area of Soot ($\sigma_s$):** $\mathbf{8.7 \; m^2/g}$ (typical) - **Smoke Production Rate (SPR):** Measured in $\mathbf{m^2/s}$ (optical area per unit time) - **Mass Optical Density ($D_m$):** Dimensionless, expressed per meter - **Standard Visibility Criterion:** **$\mathbf{10 \; m}$** for occupants, **$\mathbf{5 \; m}$** for fire-fighters (per ISO 13571 / BS 7974) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: From Light Attenuation to Smoke Point The estimation of the smoke point proceeds through a logical chain of optical and mass-conservation principles. The reasoning unfolds in **four cumulative stages**: ### Stage 1 — Light Attenuation (Beer–Lambert Law) When a beam of light of intensity $I_0$ passes through a smoke-laden medium of path length $L$, the transmitted intensity $I$ is governed by: $$I = I_0 \, e^{-KL}$$ - $K$ = extinction coefficient of the smoke (m⁻¹) - $L$ = optical path length through the smoke (m) ### Stage 2 — Optical Density and Specific Extinction Area The **optical density** $D$ is the logarithmic measure of light blockage: $$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{KL}{\ln 10}$$ The **specific extinction area** $\sigma_s$ (m²/kg) links the extinction coefficient to the mass concentration $m$ (kg/m³) of suspended soot particles: $$K = \sigma_s \, m$$ So, the **mass optical density** is: $$D_m = \frac{\sigma_s}{\ln 10} \approx 2.303 \, \sigma_s$$ ### Stage 3 — The Critical Threshold (Smoke Point Condition) The smoke point is reached when: $$K = K_c \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; m_{sp} = \frac{K_c}{\sigma_s}$$ This is the **mass concentration of smoke** at the smoke point. ### Stage 4 — Conservation of Mass in the Upper Layer The mass of smoke accumulated in the upper layer at time $t$ equals the integrated smoke production: $$m_{total}(t) = \int_0^t \dot{m}_s \, dt = \dot{m}_s \cdot t \quad (\text{steady fire})$$ This mass is distributed in the volume $V_{upper} = A \cdot (H - y_{sp})$, so: $$m = \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t}{A \, (H - y_{sp})}$$ Setting $m = m_{sp}$ and solving for $y_{sp}$: $$y_{sp} = H - \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c}$$ This is the **Master Equation for Smoke Point Height Estimation** in a closed compartment. ## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Egress Design:** Architects and fire-safety engineers use $y_{sp}$ to determine the time at which the smoke layer descends below the heads of evacuating occupants, dictating **Safe Available Egress Time (SAET)**. - **Vent Design:** Smoke point calculations drive the **size and placement of smoke vents** and **smoke-management HVAC systems** in atriums, malls, and tunnels. - **Detector Siting:** Optical smoke detectors are positioned based on the expected descent profile of the smoke layer. - **Material Selection:** $\sigma_s$ values for various fuels (plastics, woods, PU foam) help select low-smoke materials in transit and aviation applications. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | **#** | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Standard Unit** | **Typical Value** | |------|--------------|------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------| | 1 | Transmitted Intensity | $I$ | $I = I_0 \, e^{-KL}$ | W/m² | — | | 2 | Extinction Coefficient | $K$ | $K = -\ln(I/I_0)/L$ | m⁻¹ | — | | 3 | Specific Extinction Area | $\sigma_s$ | $\sigma_s = K/m$ | m²/kg | 8.7 (soot) | | 4 | Optical Density | $D$ | $D = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ | — | — | | 5 | Mass Optical Density | $D_m$ | $D_m = \sigma_s / \ln 10$ | m⁻¹ | ≈ 10 (PU foam) | | 6 | Critical Extinction Coeff. | $K_c$ | Defined constant | m⁻¹ | 0.08 | | 7 | Mass Conc. at Smoke Point | $m_{sp}$ | $m_{sp} = K_c / \sigma_s$ | kg/m³ | ≈ 9.2 × 10⁻⁶ | | 8 | Smoke Point Height | $y_{sp}$ | $y_{sp} = H - \dot{m}_s t \sigma_s / (A K_c)$ | m | — | | 9 | Smoke Yield | $y_s$ | $y_s = \dot{m}_s / \dot{m}_f$ | kg/kg | 0.01–0.20 | | 10 | Smoke Production Rate | SPR | $\text{SPR} = K \cdot \dot{V}$ | m²/s | — | | 11 | Visibility | $V$ | $V = 8 / K$ (green light) | m | — | | 12 | Soot Volume Fraction | $f_v$ | $f_v = m / \rho_{soot}$ | — | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for KTU Examinations:** When substituting $|K|$, $\vert L \vert$ etc., use the LaTeX delimiters `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables. ## 2.4 Boundary Conditions and Physical Limits - For $K < K_c$: visibility > 10 m (safe for evacuation) - For $K = K_c$: smoke point reached (design threshold) - For $K > K_c$: visibility < 5 m (tenable limit exceeded) - $y_{sp}$ cannot exceed $H$ (ceiling) and cannot be negative (the fire has consumed the compartment) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Smoke Point Height in a Closed Compartment ### Given: - Compartment floor area: $A$ (m²) - Compartment ceiling height: $H$ (m) - Steady smoke mass production rate: $\dot{m}_s$ (kg/s) - Specific extinction area: $\sigma_s$ (m²/kg) - Critical extinction coefficient: $K_c$ (m⁻¹) - Time elapsed: $t$ (s) ### Step 1 — State the Beer–Lambert Law of Light Attenuation The intensity of monochromatic light after passing through a smoke column of path length $L$ is: $$I = I_0 \, e^{-KL}$$ *Explanation:* This is the foundational optical law relating transmitted light to smoke attenuation. The exponent $K \cdot L$ is the "optical thickness" of the smoke. ### Step 2 — Express Optical Density in Terms of Extinction Coefficient $$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{KL}{\ln 10}$$ *Explanation:* Optical density is the base-10 logarithm of the inverse transmittance. The factor $\ln 10 \approx 2.303$ converts natural log to base-10. ### Step 3 — Introduce the Specific Extinction Area The extinction coefficient is the product of specific extinction area and the mass concentration of smoke: $$K = \sigma_s \, m$$ *Explanation:* $\sigma_s$ is an intrinsic material property of the smoke aerosol; it has the same value for a given fuel regardless of concentration. ### Step 4 — Identify the Critical Concentration at the Smoke Point The smoke point is reached when the extinction coefficient reaches the critical value $K_c$: $$K_c = \sigma_s \, m_{sp} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{sp} = \frac{K_c}{\sigma_s}$$ *Explanation:* This is the "trigger concentration" at which visibility becomes critically impaired. ### Step 5 — Apply Conservation of Mass to the Upper Smoke Layer For a steady fire, the smoke mass accumulating in the upper layer (between the smoke interface $y_{sp}$ and the ceiling $H$) over time $t$ is: $$M_{smoke}(t) = \dot{m}_s \cdot t$$ *Explanation:* Integration of a constant rate yields a linear accumulation of mass. ### Step 6 — Relate Total Mass to Local Concentration The smoke is distributed uniformly in the upper layer of volume $V_{upper} = A \cdot (H - y_{sp})$: $$m = \frac{M_{smoke}(t)}{V_{upper}} = \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t}{A \, (H - y_{sp})}$$ *Explanation:* Dividing total mass by the layer volume gives the average mass concentration in the upper layer. ### Step 7 — Set Concentration Equal to Smoke Point Value and Solve Equating step 6 to step 4: $$\frac{\dot{m}_s \, t}{A \, (H - y_{sp})} = \frac{K_c}{\sigma_s}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s = A \, (H - y_{sp}) \, K_c$$ Dividing both sides by $A \cdot K_c$: $$\frac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c} = H - y_{sp}$$ Isolating $y_{sp}$: $$\boxed{\, y_{sp} = H - \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c} \,}$$ *Explanation:* This is the closed-form master equation showing the smoke point descends linearly with time. The term $\dot{m}_s \sigma_s / (A K_c)$ has units of m/s and represents the **rate of descent of the smoke interface**. ### Step 8 — Express in Terms of Smoke Yield $y_s$ and Fuel Burn Rate $\dot{m}_f$ Since $\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_f$: $$y_{sp} = H - \frac{y_s \, \dot{m}_f \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c}$$ *Explanation:* This alternate form is useful in **Zone-Model Fire Simulations** (CFAST, OZone) where fuel properties are inputs. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Visibility–Extinction Relationship ### Given: - Critical extinction coefficient $K_c = 0.08$ m⁻¹ ### Derivation: Empirically, for green light at $632.8 \; nm$, visibility $V$ (m) and $K$ are inversely related: $$V = \frac{C}{K}$$ For evacuation safety, $V_{min} = 10$ m and $C = 8$: $$V = \frac{8}{K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \frac{8}{V}$$ For $V = 10 \; m$: $$K = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 \; m^{-1} \quad \text{(NOT used)}$$ For KTU standard, the smoke point uses the **empirical constant $C = 8$ m · m⁻¹** yielding $K_c = 0.8 \; m^{-1}$ for the **10-m visibility criterion**; the 0.08 m⁻¹ value corresponds to a **100-m visibility** (used in some international codes). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Preference:** Indian codes (NBC 2016, IS 14435) generally use $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$ for green light. Students should state this clearly in answers. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Typical Specific Extinction Areas | **Fuel Material** | **$\sigma_s$ (m²/kg)** | **$y_s$ (kg/kg)** | **Notes** | |-------------------|----------------------|------------------|-----------| | Wood (Douglas Fir) | ~ 4.0 | 0.005 | Low smoke | | Polyurethane Foam | ~ 10.0 | 0.10 | High smoke | | PVC | ~ 7.0 | 0.06 | Toxic HCl | | Polystyrene | ~ 12.0 | 0.13 | Heavy soot | | Heptane | ~ 5.0 | 0.04 | Pool fire | | Toluene | ~ 9.0 | 0.18 | Sooty diffusion | | Methane | ~ 0.5 | 0.001 | Almost no soot | ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A compartment of floor area $A = 50 \; m^2$ and ceiling height $H = 4 \; m$ contains a steady fire producing smoke at $\dot{m}_s = 0.005 \; kg/s$. The specific extinction area of the soot is $\sigma_s = 8.7 \; m^2/kg$ and the critical extinction coefficient is $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$. Find the **time at which the smoke point reaches a height of $2 \; m$** above the floor. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Use the master equation: $$y_{sp} = H - \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the given values (with $y_{sp} = 2$ m): $$2 = 4 - \frac{(0.005)(t)(8.7)}{(50)(0.08)}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the denominator: $$(50)(0.08) = 4.0$$ **Step 4:** Compute the numerator coefficient: $$(0.005)(8.7) = 0.0435$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back: $$2 = 4 - \frac{0.0435 \, t}{4.0}$$ **Step 6:** Rearrange: $$\frac{0.0435 \, t}{4.0} = 4 - 2 = 2$$ **Step 7:** Solve for $t$: $$0.0435 \, t = 8.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{8.0}{0.0435}$$ $$\boxed{\, t \approx 183.9 \; \text{seconds} \approx 3.07 \; \text{minutes} \,}$$ *Interpretation:* Fire-fighters and occupants have roughly **3 minutes** before the smoke layer descends to eye level. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Smoke Point Formation in a Compartment (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fire Ignition Source] --> B[Combustion Process] B --> C[Soot Particle Generation] C --> D[Hot Smoke Plume Rise] D --> E[Ceiling Impingement] E --> F[Smoke Layer Accumulation] F --> G[Upper Layer Thickness Grows] G --> H{Smoke Interface y_sp Descent} H --> I[Compare K with K_c Critical] I --> J{K reaches K_c?} J -->|No| K[Visibility Still Safe] J -->|Yes| L[SMOKE POINT REACHED] L --> M[Visibility V less than 10 m] M --> N[Egress and Rescue Becomes Critical] N --> O[Activate Smoke Vents and Detectors] K --> P[Continue Monitoring] O --> Q[Tenable Conditions Restored] P --> Q ``` ## 4.2 Logical Flow of Smoke Point Calculation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inputs: A, H, m_dot_s, sigma_s, K_c] --> B[Apply Beer Lambert Law] B --> C[Compute Extinction Coefficient K] C --> D[Set K equal to K_c] D --> E[Compute Critical Mass Concentration m_sp] E --> F[Apply Mass Conservation in Upper Layer] F --> G[Solve for y_sp] G --> H[Output: Smoke Point Height and Arrival Time] H --> I[Compare with Occupant Eye Level] I --> J{Is y_sp less than 1.8 m?} J -->|Yes| K[Tenable Limit Exceeded] J -->|No| L[Continue Safe Egress] ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of Smoke Layer Descent ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COMP[Compartment Cross Section] direction TB CEIL[Ceiling at height H = 4 m] ZONE1[Upper Hot Smoke Layer
K greater than K_c
Optical Density High] SP[SMOKE POINT INTERFACE y_sp] ZONE2[Lower Clear Air Layer
K less than K_c
Safe Visibility] FLOOR[Floor Level y = 0] CEIL --- ZONE1 ZONE1 --- SP SP --- ZONE2 ZONE2 --- FLOOR end FIRE[Fire Source at Floor Center] -. Plume .-> ZONE1 ``` ## 4.4 System Input–Output Diagram for Smoke Point Estimator ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUTS[Input Parameters] I1[Floor Area A in m squared] I2[Ceiling Height H in m] I3[Smoke Production Rate m_dot_s in kg per s] I4[Specific Extinction Area sigma_s in m squared per kg] I5[Critical Extinction K_c in per m] I6[Time t in s] end INPUTS --> CORE[Smoke Point Master Equation] CORE --> OUT1[Smoke Point Height y_sp in m] CORE --> OUT2[Critical Time t_c in s] CORE --> OUT3[Visibility V in m] OUT1 --> DEC{Decision Module} OUT2 --> DEC OUT3 --> DEC DEC -->|Unsafe| ALARM[Trigger Alarm and Vents] DEC -->|Safe| MON[Continue Monitoring] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "Smoke Point" as applied in fire dynamics. State its significance in fire safety design.** **Model Answer:** The Smoke Point is the height within a fire compartment at which the accumulated smoke becomes optically dense enough to cause a **critical reduction in visibility** for occupants attempting egress. It is defined quantitatively as the elevation at which the **extinction coefficient $K$ of the smoke equals the critical value $K_c$** (conventionally $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$ for green light, $\lambda = 632.8 \; nm$). **Significance:** - Determines the **time available for safe evacuation** before visibility drops below 10 m. - Governs the **design of smoke vents, smoke-management HVAC, and detector placement.** - Used in zone-model simulations (CFAST, OZone) to compute **ASET vs. RSET** comparisons. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State and explain Beer–Lambert's law as applied to smoke optical measurements.** **Model Answer:** Beer–Lambert's law states that the intensity of a light beam transmitted through a smoke-laden medium decays exponentially with the path length and the extinction coefficient of the medium: $$I = I_0 \, e^{-K L}$$ Where: - $I_0$ = intensity of incident light (W/m²) - $I$ = intensity of transmitted light (W/m²) - $K$ = extinction coefficient (m⁻¹) - $L$ = path length through the smoke (m) **Application:** This law forms the basis of all optical smoke measurement techniques, including the **laser-extinction photometer** used in the cone calorimeter (ISO 5660) and the **NIST smoke density chamber**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **smoke point height ($y_{sp}$)** in a closed compartment with a steady fire, in terms of floor area $A$, ceiling height $H$, smoke mass production rate $\dot{m}_s$, specific extinction area $\sigma_s$, critical extinction coefficient $K_c$, and time $t$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A 60 m² room with a 3.5 m ceiling has a steady polyurethane-foam fire producing smoke at $\dot{m}_s = 0.008 \; kg/s$. For $\sigma_s = 10.0 \; m^2/kg$ and $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$, determine **(i)** the time at which the smoke point reaches 2.0 m above the floor and **(ii)** the visibility at that instant. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Derivation of Smoke Point Height:** Step 1 — Beer–Lambert law: $$I = I_0 \, e^{-K L}$$ Step 2 — Optical density: $$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{KL}{\ln 10}$$ Step 3 — Specific extinction area: $$K = \sigma_s \, m$$ Step 4 — Critical condition: $$K_c = \sigma_s \, m_{sp} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{sp} = \frac{K_c}{\sigma_s}$$ Step 5 — Mass conservation in the upper layer (steady fire): $$M_{smoke}(t) = \dot{m}_s \, t$$ Step 6 — Average concentration in upper layer of volume $A(H - y_{sp})$: $$m = \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t}{A(H - y_{sp})}$$ Step 7 — Equate and solve: $$y_{sp} = H - \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c}$$ **(b) Numerical Computation:** Given: $A = 60 \; m^2$, $H = 3.5 \; m$, $\dot{m}_s = 0.008 \; kg/s$, $\sigma_s = 10.0 \; m^2/kg$, $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$, $y_{sp} = 2.0 \; m$. **Sub-part (i): Time at which $y_{sp} = 2.0 \; m$** $$2.0 = 3.5 - \frac{(0.008)(t)(10.0)}{(60)(0.08)}$$ Simplify denominator: $$(60)(0.08) = 4.8$$ Simplify numerator coefficient: $$(0.008)(10.0) = 0.08$$ Therefore: $$2.0 = 3.5 - \frac{0.08 \, t}{4.8}$$ $$\frac{0.08 \, t}{4.8} = 1.5$$ $$0.08 \, t = 7.2$$ $$\boxed{\, t = 90 \; \text{seconds} = 1.5 \; \text{minutes} \,}$$ **Sub-part (ii): Visibility at that instant** Using $V = 8 / K$ and $K = K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$: $$V = \frac{8}{0.08} = 100 \; m$$ $$\boxed{\, V = 100 \; m \text{ (at the smoke point interface)} \,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse the visibility formula ($V = 8/K$) with the wrong constant (sometimes using $C = 2$ or $C = 3$ for different light sources). **Always state the light wavelength and the empirical constant** in your answer. Green light → $C = 8$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the role of **specific extinction area ($\sigma_s$)** in smoke point estimation. Tabulate typical $\sigma_s$ values for at least **five common fuels**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A compartment of dimensions 8 m × 10 m × 4 m (L × B × H) experiences a steady fire with smoke yield $y_s = 0.10 \; kg/kg$ and fuel burn rate $\dot{m}_f = 0.05 \; kg/s$. For $\sigma_s = 8.7 \; m^2/kg$ and $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$, compute the **time required for the smoke point to descend to a height of 1.5 m above the floor.** **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Role of Specific Extinction Area ($\sigma_s$):** The specific extinction area $\sigma_s$ is an **intrinsic optical property of the smoke aerosol** that quantifies the **cross-sectional area of light extinction per unit mass of smoke produced** (units: m²/kg). It is a critical parameter in the Beer–Lambert law: $$K = \sigma_s \, m$$ **Physical Significance:** - It is **independent of smoke concentration** — a material constant. - High $\sigma_s$ materials (e.g., PU foam, polystyrene) produce **optically dense smoke** that severely impairs visibility. - Low $\sigma_s$ materials (e.g., methane, wood) produce **optically thin smoke** that allows better visibility. **Tabulated Values:** | **Fuel** | **$\sigma_s$ (m²/kg)** | **Smoke Character** | |----------|----------------------|---------------------| | Methane | 0.5 | Practically invisible | | Heptane | 5.0 | Light grey | | Wood (Douglas Fir) | 4.0 | Light grey-brown | | Polyurethane Foam | 10.0 | Dark, dense black | | Polystyrene | 12.0 | Heavy, sooty black | | PVC | 7.0 | Grey with HCl fumes | | Toluene | 9.0 | Sooty yellow flame | | Cotton | 3.0 | Light haze | **(b) Numerical Computation:** Given: $A = 8 \times 10 = 80 \; m^2$, $H = 4 \; m$, $y_s = 0.10$, $\dot{m}_f = 0.05 \; kg/s$, $\sigma_s = 8.7 \; m^2/kg$, $K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$, $y_{sp} = 1.5 \; m$. **Step 1:** Compute smoke production rate: $$\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_f = (0.10)(0.05) = 0.005 \; kg/s$$ **Step 2:** Apply the smoke point equation: $$y_{sp} = H - \frac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute numerical values: $$1.5 = 4 - \frac{(0.005)(t)(8.7)}{(80)(0.08)}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify: $$(80)(0.08) = 6.4$$ $$(0.005)(8.7) = 0.0435$$ $$1.5 = 4 - \frac{0.0435 \, t}{6.4}$$ **Step 5:** Solve: $$\frac{0.0435 \, t}{6.4} = 2.5$$ $$0.0435 \, t = 16.0$$ $$\boxed{\, t = \frac{16.0}{0.0435} \approx 367.8 \; \text{seconds} \approx 6.13 \; \text{minutes} \,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to compute $\dot{m}_s$ from $y_s \cdot \dot{m}_f$ and directly substitute incorrect values. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Floor area $A$ is computed incorrectly for a 3D compartment (use $L \times B$, not $L \times B \times H$). > - **Common Mistake 3:** Unit mismatch — $K_c$ is in m⁻¹, but some texts use cm⁻¹. **Always convert to SI units (m⁻¹).** > - **Mark Loss Tip:** KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark per sub-step** for missing units in the final answer. Always box the answer with units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points (MUST MEMORIZE for KTU 2024):** - **Smoke Point Definition:** Height at which $K = K_c = 0.08 \; m^{-1}$ (green light, $\lambda = 632.8 \; nm$). - **Master Equation:** $y_{sp} = H - \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \, t \, \sigma_s}{A \, K_c}$ - **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 \, e^{-K L}$ (foundation of optical smoke measurement). - **Optical Density:** $D = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = KL / \ln 10$ - **Specific Extinction Area:** $\sigma_s = K / m$ (m²/kg), intrinsic material property. - **Mass Optical Density:** $D_m = \sigma_s / \ln 10$ - **Visibility Relation:** $V = 8 / K$ (for green light) - **Critical $K_c$ values:** $0.08 \; m^{-1}$ (KTU/Indian) vs. $0.2 \; m^{-1}$ (some international standards) - **Smoke Yield Range:** $y_s = 0.001$ (methane) to $0.20$ (toluene) - **Units Check:** $K$ in m⁻¹, $L$ in m, $\sigma_s$ in m²/kg, $m$ in kg/m³ - **Closed Compartment Assumption:** Uniform mixing in upper layer (valid for $\dot{m}_s < 0.1 \; kg/s$ and $t < 600$ s). - **Tenable Limits:** Visibility > 10 m (occupants), > 5 m (fire-fighters) - **Detector Trigger:** Optical smoke detector threshold typically corresponds to $K \approx 0.015 \; m^{-1}$ - **Difference from Combustion Smoke Point (ASTM D1322):** Refers to **flame height (mm)** for liquid fuels in a wick lamp — do NOT confuse in answers. > **End of Topic: Estimation of Smoke Point — KTU PESFT528 Module 2**

topics.title: Non-Dimensional Numbers for Fire Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Dimensional Numbers for Fire Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Non-Dimensional Number** (also called a **dimensionless group** or **similarity parameter**) is a pure number formed by combining physical variables such that all units of measurement (length, mass, time, temperature, etc.) cancel out, leaving a unitless ratio. In fire science and combustion engineering, non-dimensional numbers represent the relative magnitude of competing physical phenomena such as inertia versus viscosity, buoyancy versus diffusion, or chemical reaction rate versus transport rate. They are derived using two principal methods:

1. **Dimensional Analysis** (Buckingham Pi Theorem) — used when the governing equations are unknown or overly complex.
2. **Non-Dimensionalization of Governing Equations** — used when the partial differential equations (mass, momentum, energy, species) are known.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Non-dimensional numbers are the cornerstone of **scaling laws** in fire research. They allow laboratory-scale experiments (e.g., a 1:10 scale model of a building) to predict full-scale fire behavior. This is the principle of **Froude-scale modeling** used by NIST, BRE, and IIT fire laboratories.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking pasta in two different pots — a tiny one and a giant industrial one. The physics governing how the water boils, how steam rises, and how heat distributes is **identical** at both scales, provided certain ratios remain the same. These ratios are the non-dimensional numbers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of a non-dimensional number as a **"score"** comparing two rival forces. For example, the **Reynolds number** is the score in a tug-of-war between *inertia* (the tendency of a fluid to keep moving) and *viscosity* (the fluid's internal friction). A high Reynolds number means inertia is winning; a low number means viscosity dominates. In fire plumes, the **Froude number** compares *forward momentum* of fire gases to *buoyancy lift*. If Froude is low, buoyancy dominates — which is why smoke rises vertically in a quiescent room.

### 1.3 Why Non-Dimensional Numbers Matter in Fire Study

| Engineering Utility | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Scale Modeling** | Enables burning a small model to predict full-scale fire behavior |
| **Equation Simplification** | Reduces PDEs to ODEs when certain terms are negligibly small |
| **Regime Identification** | Classifies fires as laminar/turbulent, buoyancy-driven/forced, etc. |
| **Similarity Preservation** | Ensures dynamic, thermal, and chemical similarity between model and prototype |
| **Universal Correlations** | Permits empirical correlations (e.g., Nusselt vs. Re) that apply across all scales |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention (KTU):** All non-dimensional numbers are **pure ratios** and therefore have **no units (dimensionless)**. In SI notation, they are reported as a numerical value only (e.g., Re = 2300, not Re = 2300 m/s).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Non-Dimensional Numbers in Fire Science

Non-dimensional numbers in fire studies are systematically grouped by the **physical phenomena** they compare:

#### 2.1.1 Fluid Mechanics Group (Flow Dynamics)
- **Reynolds Number (Re)** — Inertia vs. Viscous Forces
- **Froude Number (Fr)** — Inertia vs. Gravitational/Buoyancy Forces
- **Grashof Number (Gr)** — Buoyancy vs. Viscous Forces
- **Mach Number (Ma)** — Flow Velocity vs. Speed of Sound (compartment overpressure)
- **Richardson Number (Ri)** — Buoyancy vs. Shear (defines plume behavior)

#### 2.1.2 Thermal/Heat Transfer Group
- **Nusselt Number (Nu)** — Convective vs. Conductive Heat Transfer
- **Biot Number (Bi)** — Internal Conduction vs. Surface Convection
- **Fourier Number (Fo)** — Heat Conduction Rate vs. Heat Storage Rate
- **Peclet Number (Pe)** — Advective vs. Diffusive Heat Transport
- **Stanton Number (St)** — Heat Transfer Coefficient Normalized

#### 2.1.3 Combustion/Chemical Reaction Group
- **Damköhler Number (Da)** — Chemical Reaction Rate vs. Transport Rate
- **Karlovitz Number (Ka)** — Chemical Reaction Time vs. Kolmogorov Time Scale
- **Damköhler Number I (Da$_I$)** — Reaction vs. Convection
- **Damköhler Number II (Da$_{II}$)** — Reaction vs. Diffusion
- **Damköhler Number III (Da$_{III}$)** — Reaction vs. Conduction
- **Zeldovich Number (Ze)** — Activation Energy / (Molar Gas Constant × Temperature)

#### 2.1.4 Property Group
- **Prandtl Number (Pr)** — Momentum Diffusivity vs. Thermal Diffusivity
- **Schmidt Number (Sc)** — Momentum Diffusivity vs. Mass Diffusivity
- **Lewis Number (Le)** — Thermal Diffusivity vs. Mass Diffusivity
- **Specific Heat Ratio ($\gamma$)** — $C_p / C_v$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The following table is the single most important reference for Module 2 numerical problems. Memorize definitions, not just formulas — KTU theory questions (10-mark) frequently ask *"Why is the Froude number critical in fire scaling?"*

| # | Non-Dimensional Number | Symbol | Defining Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Fire Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds | Re | $\dfrac{\rho u L}{\mu}$ | Inertia / Viscosity | 10$^2$ – 10$^6$ |
| 2 | Froude | Fr | $\dfrac{u}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Inertia / Buoyancy | 0.1 – 10 |
| 3 | Grashof | Gr | $\dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Buoyancy / Viscosity | 10$^6$ – 10$^{10}$ |
| 4 | Richardson | Ri | $\dfrac{Gr}{Re^2}$ | Buoyancy / Inertia | $< 0.1$ (forced), $> 10$ (buoyancy) |
| 5 | Nusselt | Nu | $\dfrac{h L}{k}$ | Convection / Conduction | 10 – 1000 |
| 6 | Prandtl | Pr | $\dfrac{\nu}{\alpha} = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$ | Momentum Diff. / Thermal Diff. | Air $\approx$ 0.71 |
| 7 | Peclet (Thermal) | Pe | $Re \cdot Pr$ | Advection / Heat Diffusion | > 1 |
| 8 | Biot | Bi | $\dfrac{h L_c}{k_s}$ | Internal Cond. / Surface Conv. | < 0.1 (lumped valid) |
| 9 | Fourier | Fo | $\dfrac{\alpha t}{L^2}$ | Heat Conducted / Heat Stored | 0.1 – 10 |
| 10 | Damköhler I | Da$_I$ | $\dfrac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}}$ | Reaction / Convection | 0.01 – 100 |
| 11 | Damköhler II | Da$_{II}$ | $\dfrac{L \cdot \dot{r}}{u}$ | Reaction / Diffusion | < 1 (mixing-limited) |
| 12 | Karlovitz | Ka | $\dfrac{\tau_{chem}}{\tau_{Kolm}}$ | Flame Stretch Effect | < 1 (wrinkled) |
| 13 | Lewis | Le | $\dfrac{\alpha}{D}$ | Thermal / Mass Diffusivity | Le = 1 (stoichiometric) |
| 14 | Zeldovich | Ze | $\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}$ | Reactivity Measure | > 5 (ignition possible) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Standard:** $u$ = velocity (m/s), $L$ = characteristic length (m), $g$ = gravity (9.81 m/s$^2$), $\rho$ = density (kg/m$^3$), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m$^2$/s), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m$^2$/s), $D$ = mass diffusivity (m$^2$/s), $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (1/K), $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R_u$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K).

### 2.3 Why and How — Deeper Insights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter in fire research:** Each non-dimensional number answers a binary engineering question — *which physical effect dominates?* For example, a low **Froude number (Fr < 1)** in a doorway jet confirms that the smoke layer is **buoyancy-driven** (flows vertically, follows the ceiling), which dictates ventilation-limited fire modeling. A high **Re (> 4000)** confirms **turbulent flow**, validating the use of RANS/LES CFD models over laminar codes.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Critical Non-Dimensional Number | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Smoke Movement in Buildings | **Froude Number** | Doorway jet velocity, layer descent rate |
| Ceiling Jet Temperature | **Nusselt / Re** | Correlation: $\Delta T_{max} \propto Re^{1/2}$ |
| Compartment Fire Stability | **Damköhler** | Predict flashover, extinction |
| Detector Response Time | **Fourier** | Solid heat-up delay for thermocouples |
| Suppression Design | **Karlovitz, Da$_{II}$** | Aerosol/water mist effectiveness |
| Pool Fire Modeling | **Froude, Re** | Flame length correlation: $L_f/D \propto Fr^n$ |
| Wildland Fire Spread | **Byram number (dimensionless intensity)** | Crown fire initiation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Foundation: Buckingham Pi Theorem Application

The **Buckingham Pi Theorem** states: If a physical problem involves $n$ variables in $k$ fundamental dimensions, the relationship can be expressed in $(n - k)$ independent non-dimensional groups ($\Pi$ terms).

> [!NOTE]
> **General Procedure (to be applied in derivations below):**
> 1. List all variables governing the phenomenon.
> 2. Determine the number of fundamental dimensions (M, L, T, $\Theta$).
> 3. Compute the number of $\Pi$ groups = (variables) − (dimensions).
> 4. Choose repeating variables and form dimensionless products.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 1 — Reynolds Number (Re)

**Step 1 — Identify the variables and dimensions:**

The inertial-to-viscous force ratio depends on:
- Fluid density $\rho$ [M L$^{-3}$]
- Velocity $u$ [L T$^{-1}$]
- Characteristic length $L$ [L]
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ [M L$^{-1}$ T$^{-1}$]

**Step 2 — Form a dimensionless $\Pi$ group:**

We seek $\Pi = \rho^a u^b L^c \mu^d$ such that the product is dimensionless.

The dimensional matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi &: \rho^a u^b L^c \mu^d \\
\text{Mass (M)} &: a + d = 0 \\
\text{Length (L)} &: -3a + b + c - d = 0 \\
\text{Time (T)} &: -b - d = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system:**

From the Time equation: $b = -d$.
From the Mass equation: $a = -d$.
Substituting into the Length equation:

$$
-3(-d) + (-d) + c - d = 0
$$

$$
3d - d + c - d = 0
$$

$$
c + d = 0 \implies c = -d
$$

Let $d = -1$ (arbitrary choice — any value works as a scaling factor):

$$
a = 1, \quad b = 1, \quad c = 1, \quad d = -1
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the result:**

$$
\Pi = \frac{\rho u L}{\mu}
$$

$$
\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho u L}{\mu} = \frac{u L}{\nu}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $Re$ is the ratio of **convective momentum flux** ($\rho u^2$) to **viscous shear stress** ($\mu u / L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Engineering Threshold:** Flow is **laminar** for $Re < 2300$, **transitional** for $2300 < Re < 4000$, and **turbulent** for $Re > 4000$. Most compartment fire flows are highly turbulent ($Re > 10^5$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Froude Number (Fr)

**Step 1 — Variables and dimensions:**

Buoyancy-driven flow depends on:
- Velocity $u$ [L T$^{-1}$]
- Gravitational acceleration $g$ [L T$^{-2}$]
- Characteristic length $L$ [L]
- Density difference $\Delta \rho$ [M L$^{-3}$] (optional — sometimes subsumed)

For a fire plume, the simplest Froude definition uses only $u$, $g$, and $L$.

**Step 2 — Form the $\Pi$ group:**

$\Pi = u^a g^b L^c$ must be dimensionless.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length (L)} &: a + b + c = 0 \\
\text{Time (T)} &: -a - 2b = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

From Time: $a = -2b$.
From Length: $-2b + b + c = 0 \implies c = b$.

Choose $b = 1$:

$$
a = -2, \quad b = 1, \quad c = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$
\Pi = u^{-2} g^1 L^1 = \frac{g L}{u^2}
$$

For convenience, the **Froude number** is defined as the inverse square root:

$$
\boxed{Fr = \frac{u}{\sqrt{g L}}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $Fr$ is the ratio of **flow inertia** ($\rho u^2$) to **buoyancy/hydrostatic pressure** ($\rho g L$). For a hot smoke layer:

$$
Fr = \frac{u_{jet}}{\sqrt{g H \Delta T / T_{amb}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Froude dominates fire scaling:** In low-Mach, buoyancy-driven flows, gravitational acceleration $g$ is the only relevant body force. **Froude scaling** (and the Reynolds number is preserved only if the model is in fully turbulent flow, $Re > 4000$) is the standard for compartment fire modeling (Zukoski, 1986; Quintiere, 2006).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Nusselt Number (Nu)

**Step 1 — Variables and dimensions:**

Convective heat transfer depends on:
- Convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ [M T$^{-3}$ $\Theta^{-1}$]
- Characteristic length $L$ [L]
- Thermal conductivity $k$ [M L T$^{-3}$ $\Theta^{-1}$]

**Step 2 — Form the $\Pi$ group:**

$\Pi = h^a L^b k^c$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass (M)} &: a + c = 0 \\
\text{Length (L)} &: b + c = 0 \\
\text{Time (T)} &: -3a - 3c = 0 \\
\text{Temperature (\Theta)} &: -a - c = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

From the Time and $\Theta$ equations (which are dependent), we get $a + c = 0$, hence $a = -c$. From Length: $b = -c$. Choose $c = 1$: $a = -1, b = -1$.

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{Nu = \frac{h L}{k}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $Nu$ is the ratio of **convective** to **conductive** heat transfer across a fluid boundary layer. $Nu = 1$ corresponds to pure conduction (no convection enhancement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire correlation example:** The ceiling jet temperature rise under a fire is correlated as $Nu_{ceiling} \propto Re^{1/2}$ (Alpert correlation), directly used in smoke detector activation modeling.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Prandtl Number (Pr)

**Step 1 — Variables and dimensions:**

- Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ [L$^2$ T$^{-1}$]
- Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ [L$^2$ T$^{-1}$]

**Step 2 — Form the ratio:**

Since $\nu$ and $\alpha$ have identical dimensions, their ratio is automatically dimensionless:

$$
\boxed{Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $Pr$ compares **momentum diffusion** to **thermal diffusion** in a fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key values for fire studies:**
> - Air: $Pr \approx 0.71$
> - Hot combustion gases: $Pr \approx 0.65$–$0.7$
> - Liquid water: $Pr \approx 7$
> - Engine oil: $Pr \approx 100$–$1000$
>
> Most fire applications assume $Pr = 0.7$.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 5 — Grashof Number (Gr)

**Step 1 — Variables and dimensions:**

Natural convection depends on:
- Gravitational acceleration $g$ [L T$^{-2}$]
- Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient $\beta$ [$\Theta^{-1}$]
- Temperature difference $\Delta T$ [$\Theta$]
- Characteristic length $L$ [L]
- Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ [L$^2$ T$^{-1}$]

**Step 2 — Form the $\Pi$ group:**

$\Pi = g^a \beta^b \Delta T^c L^d \nu^e$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length (L)} &: a + d + 2e = 0 \\
\text{Time (T)} &: -2a - 2e = 0 \\
\text{Temperature (\Theta)} &: b + c = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

From Time: $a = -e$. From Length: $-e + d + 2e = 0 \implies d = -e$. From $\Theta$: $b = -c$.

Choose $e = -1$ (to get positive exponents on $g$, $L$, $\beta \Delta T$):

$$
a = 1, \quad d = 3, \quad e = -1, \quad b = 1, \quad c = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{Gr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $Gr$ is the ratio of **buoyancy forces** to **viscous forces** in natural (un-forced) convection. The **Rayleigh number** $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ sets the onset of turbulence in buoyancy-driven flows.

---

### 3.7 Derivation 6 — Damköhler Numbers (Da$_I$, Da$_{II}$, Da$_{III}$)

**Damköhler I — Reaction vs. Convection:**

$$
\boxed{Da_I = \frac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{L/u}{1/\dot{r}} = \frac{L \cdot \dot{r}}{u}}
$$

where $\dot{r}$ is the chemical reaction rate (1/s).

**Damköhler II — Reaction vs. Species Diffusion:**

$$
\boxed{Da_{II} = \frac{\tau_{diff}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{L^2/D}{1/\dot{r}} = \frac{L^2 \dot{r}}{D}}
$$

**Damköhler III — Reaction vs. Heat Conduction:**

$$
\boxed{Da_{III} = \frac{\tau_{cond}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{L^2/\alpha}{1/\dot{r}} = \frac{L^2 \dot{r}}{\alpha}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $Da_I \ll 1$: **Mixing-limited (kinetically frozen)** — fuel and oxidizer coexist without burning (typical in dilute systems).
- $Da_I \gg 1$: **Reaction-limited** — burning rate is chemistry-controlled (typical in well-stirred reactors, engines).

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion regime diagram:** Da$_I$ vs. Ka (Karlovitz) classifies flames into **premixed** (wrinkled, corrugated, broken) and **diffusion** regimes — central to turbulent combustion modeling (Peters, 2000).

---

### 3.8 Derivation 7 — Karlovitz Number (Ka)

**Step 1 — Define the Kolmogorov time scale:**

$$
\tau_{Kolm} = \sqrt{\frac{\nu}{\varepsilon}}
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the turbulent dissipation rate (m$^2$/s$^3$).

**Step 2 — Define the chemical time scale:**

$$
\tau_{chem} = \frac{\delta_L}{S_L}
$$

where $\delta_L$ is the laminar flame thickness and $S_L$ is the laminar burning velocity.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\boxed{Ka = \frac{\tau_{chem}}{\tau_{Kolm}} = \frac{\delta_L / S_L}{\sqrt{\nu/\varepsilon}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regime significance:** $Ka < 1$ defines the **wrinkled flamelet regime** (most practical fire flames). $Ka > 1$ indicates **distributed reaction zones** and **broken reaction zones** where chemistry is no longer separable from turbulence.

---

### 3.9 Derivation 8 — Biot Number (Bi)

**Step 1 — Variables:**

- Convective coefficient $h$ [W/m$^2$·K]
- Characteristic length $L_c$ (volume/surface area) [m]
- Solid thermal conductivity $k_s$ [W/m·K]

**Step 2 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k_s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lumped capacitance validity:** If $Bi < 0.1$, the solid heats uniformly — internal conduction is much faster than surface convection, justifying a single-temperature ODE for the solid (used in fire detector modeling, thermal element method).

---

### 3.10 Derivation 9 — Fourier Number (Fo)

$$
\boxed{Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L^2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** $Fo$ is the ratio of **heat conducted through** a solid to **heat stored** within it. $Fo = 0.1$ is often used as the "characteristic time" for thermally-thick solids; $Fo > 0.1$ implies significant penetration of heat, and $Fo \to \infty$ is steady state.

---

### 3.11 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Reference Table

| Number | Primary Variables | Physical Contrast | Fire Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Re | $u, L, \nu$ | Inertia vs. Viscosity | Plume turbulence classification |
| Fr | $u, g, L$ | Inertia vs. Buoyancy | **Fire scaling laws** |
| Gr | $g, \beta, \Delta T, L, \nu$ | Buoyancy vs. Viscosity | Natural convection in compartments |
| Nu | $h, L, k$ | Convection vs. Conduction | Ceiling jet correlations |
| Pr | $\nu, \alpha$ | Momentum vs. Thermal diffusion | Fluid property selection |
| Da$_I$ | $\dot{r}, L, u$ | Chemistry vs. Convection | Burning rate regime |
| Ka | $\delta_L, S_L, \nu, \varepsilon$ | Chemistry vs. Turbulence | Flamelet theory |
| Bi | $h, L_c, k_s$ | Internal vs. Surface | Detector/solid heat-up |
| Fo | $\alpha, t, L$ | Conduction vs. Storage | Time to thermal penetration |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
