## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model QP]`

> **Define the Froude number and explain its significance in fire scaling laws.**

**Model Answer:**

The Froude number is a non-dimensional parameter defined as the ratio of inertial forces to gravitational/buoyancy forces in a flow:

$$
Fr = \frac{u}{\sqrt{g L}}
$$

where $u$ is the characteristic velocity, $g$ is the gravitational acceleration, and $L$ is the characteristic length.

In fire scaling, the Froude number is **the most critical similarity parameter** because most compartment fires are buoyancy-driven (low Mach number), and the only body force is gravity. By matching Fr between a small-scale laboratory model and a full-scale prototype, the **smoke layer interface height, ceiling jet temperature, and time to flashover** can be accurately predicted. This is the principle of **Froude-scale modeling** (Zukoski, 1986), widely used by NIST and IIT fire labs. A typical full-scale room with $L = 3$ m and a smoke velocity of $u = 1$ m/s gives $Fr = 0.18$, confirming buoyancy dominance.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

> **Define the Damköhler number and explain its physical significance in compartment fire chemistry.**

**Model Answer:**

The Damköhler number (Da) is the ratio of the **chemical reaction time scale** to the **flow transport time scale** in a combustion system. It is defined in three forms:

- **Da$_I$ (Reaction vs. Convection):** $\quad Da_I = \dfrac{L \dot{r}}{u}$
- **Da$_{II}$ (Reaction vs. Diffusion):** $\quad Da_{II} = \dfrac{L^2 \dot{r}}{D}$
- **Da$_{III}$ (Reaction vs. Conduction):** $\quad Da_{III} = \dfrac{L^2 \dot{r}}{\alpha}$

In compartment fire chemistry, Da$_I \ll 1$ indicates a **mixing-limited** (kinetically frozen) regime, where fuel and oxidizer are present but reaction is incomplete — typical of under-ventilated fires. Da$_I \gg 1$ indicates a **reaction-limited** regime, typical of well-ventilated flaming combustion. This classification guides CFD model selection and flashover prediction.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Reporting the Froude number formula without a physical interpretation — *always* include what it physically means and its threshold values.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing Da$_I$ and Da$_{II}$ — *Da$_I$ involves flow velocity, Da$_{II}$ involves mass diffusivity.*
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the units of all variables before substitution in numerical problems — partial credit is heavily penalized in KTU board marking.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing the Reynolds number as $\rho u L$ instead of $\rho u L / \mu$ — a unit inconsistency is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In scaling law derivations, omitting the **preservation of Re > 4000** for turbulent model validity — KTU boards specifically test this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** With a neat derivation, obtain the **Reynolds number** using the Buckingham Pi Theorem for forced convection over a flat plate fire plume. Discuss its physical significance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A corridor smoke flow has the following properties: $u = 1.5$ m/s, $L = 2$ m, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m$^3$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Calculate the Reynolds number and comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Variables: $u$ [L T$^{-1}$], $L$ [L], $\rho$ [M L$^{-3}$], $\mu$ [M L$^{-1}$ T$^{-1}$].
Number of variables: $n = 4$; number of fundamental dimensions: $k = 3$ (M, L, T).
Number of $\Pi$ groups: $n - k = 1$.

Choose repeating variables: $\rho$, $u$, $L$. The non-repeating variable is $\mu$.

The single $\Pi$ group is:

$$
\Pi = \frac{\mu}{\rho u L}
$$

Taking the reciprocal (convention) yields:

$$
\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho u L}{\mu}}
$$

**Physical significance:** Re is the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces**. For $Re < 2300$ the flow is laminar; for $Re > 4000$ the flow is turbulent. In fire plumes, Re is typically $\sim 10^5$, confirming turbulence and justifying the use of RANS/LES models.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $u = 1.5$ m/s, $L = 2$ m, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m$^3$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho u L}{\mu} \\
&= \frac{(1.2) (1.5) (2)}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{3.6}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on flow regime:** Since $Re = 2.0 \times 10^5 \gg 4000$, the corridor smoke flow is **fully turbulent**. The viscous sublayer is very thin compared to the corridor height, and turbulent mixing dominates smoke transport. Reynolds-averaged (RANS) CFD with a suitable turbulence model (e.g., $k$-$\varepsilon$ or SST $k$-$\omega$) is appropriate for this simulation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Derive the **Froude number** using dimensional analysis. Explain why it is the governing similarity parameter for compartment fire modeling. Discuss the limitation of Froude scaling and the role of Reynolds number preservation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1:5 scale model of a building compartment is to be tested in a laboratory. The full-scale compartment has a characteristic length of 5 m, a fire heat release rate (HRR) of 2 MW, and a hot layer temperature of 600 °C. Using Froude scaling, determine the corresponding model-scale HRR and hot layer temperature. Assume ambient temperature is 25 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Variables: $u$ [L T$^{-1}$], $g$ [L T$^{-2}$], $L$ [L]. Number of variables $n = 3$, dimensions $k = 2$ (L, T), so one $\Pi$ group exists.

Form $\Pi = u^a g^b L^c$. Solving the dimensional equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length} &: a + b + c = 0 \\
\text{Time} &: -a - 2b = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose $b = 1$: $a = -2$, $c = 1$. So $\Pi = gL/u^2$. The Froude number is defined as the inverse square root:

$$
\boxed{Fr = \frac{u}{\sqrt{gL}}}
$$

**Why Froude governs compartment fire modeling:** Compartment fires are buoyancy-driven (low Mach number). The only body force is gravity. Therefore, to preserve **dynamic similarity** between model and prototype, **Fr must be matched** ($Fr_m = Fr_p$). This preserves:
- Velocity ratios $\Rightarrow$ smoke transport rates
- Pressure distributions $\Rightarrow$ door jet flow rates
- Density ratios $\Rightarrow$ hot layer thickness

**Limitation of Froude scaling:** Froude scaling **does not preserve Re by default**. For turbulent model validity, the Reynolds number in the model must satisfy $Re_m > 4000$. This is usually achieved by increasing the model HRR or ensuring a minimum model size.

**Part (b) — Froude-Scale Numerical Calculations:**

**Scaling relations** (Froude similarity):

$$
u_m = u_p \sqrt{L_m/L_p} \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{Q}_m = \dot{Q}_p (L_m/L_p)^{5/2}
$$

Scale factor: $L_m/L_p = 1/5$. So $(L_m/L_p)^{5/2} = (1/5)^{2.5} = 1/55.9 \approx 0.01789$.

**Model HRR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_m &= \dot{Q}_p \times (L_m/L_p)^{5/2} \\
&= 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ W} \times 0.01789 \\
&\approx 35{,}780 \text{ W} \approx 35.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hot layer temperature:** Froude scaling preserves the **dimensionless temperature** $\Delta T / T_{amb}$.

Full-scale:
$$
\Delta T_p = 600 - 25 = 575 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta T_p}{T_{amb}} = \frac{575}{298} = 1.929
$$

Model-scale temperature rise:

$$
\Delta T_m = 1.929 \times 298 = 574.9 \text{ K} \approx 575 \text{ K}
$$

**Model hot layer temperature:**

$$
T_m = 25 + 575 = 600 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The absolute hot layer temperature is **preserved** under Froude scaling (a unique property of buoyancy-driven flows), but the model HRR is drastically reduced ($\sim 36$ kW instead of $2$ MW), making laboratory testing feasible and safe.

**Reynolds number check (limitation):** Model characteristic velocity $u_m = u_p \sqrt{1/5}$. For $u_p \approx 1$ m/s, $u_m \approx 0.45$ m/s. With $L_m = 1$ m, $Re_m \approx 30{,}000$ — well above the turbulent threshold of 4000. ✓ **Model validity confirmed.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Rapid-Fire Revision Bulletins**

- ✅ **Re** (Reynolds) = Inertia / Viscosity. $Re = \rho u L / \mu$. Threshold: $Re > 4000$ = turbulent.
- ✅ **Fr** (Froude) = Inertia / Buoyancy. $Fr = u / \sqrt{gL}$. **Master parameter for fire scaling.**
- ✅ **Gr** (Grashof) = Buoyancy / Viscosity. $Gr = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$. Used in **natural convection** correlations.
- ✅ **Nu** (Nusselt) = Convection / Conduction. $Nu = hL/k$. Used in ceiling jet and detector response correlations.
- ✅ **Pr** (Prandtl) = Momentum / Thermal diffusivity. $Pr = \nu/\alpha = \mu C_p/k$. Air $\approx 0.71$, combustion gases $\approx 0.7$.
- ✅ **Bi** (Biot) = Internal conduction / Surface convection. $Bi = h L_c / k_s$. $Bi < 0.1 \Rightarrow$ lumped capacitance valid.
- ✅ **Fo** (Fourier) = Conduction / Storage. $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$. Time-to-penetration metric.
- ✅ **Da$_I$** (Damköhler I) = Reaction / Convection. $Da_I = L \dot{r} / u$. Mixing-limited vs. reaction-limited regime.
- ✅ **Da$_{II}$** (Damköhler II) = Reaction / Diffusion. $Da_{II} = L^2 \dot{r} / D$. Flamelet theory.
- ✅ **Ka** (Karlovitz) = Chemistry / Turbulence. $Ka = \tau_{chem}/\tau_{Kolm}$. Wrinkled flamelet regime: $Ka < 1$.
- ✅ **Le** (Lewis) = Thermal / Mass diffusivity. $Le = \alpha/D = 1$ for stoichiometric methane-air flames.
- ✅ **Ze** (Zeldovich) = $E_a/(R_u T_{ad})$. Activation energy parameter. $Ze > 5$ supports ignition.
- ✅ **Froude scaling** for buoyancy-driven flow: $\dot{Q}_m = \dot{Q}_p (L_m/L_p)^{5/2}$, $T_m = T_p$, $u_m = u_p \sqrt{L_m/L_p}$.
- ✅ **Critical Pitfall:** Froude scaling does **not** preserve Re — must independently verify $Re_m > 4000$ for turbulent model validity.
- ✅ **Ra = Gr × Pr** — Rayleigh number sets the **onset of turbulent natural convection** ($Ra > 10^9$ for air).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Heat Calculation" in Fire Science?

In the context of fire propagation and dynamics, **Heat Calculations** refer to the quantitative determination of energy quantities released, transferred, stored, or lost during the combustion process. These calculations form the mathematical backbone of fire engineering, enabling the prediction of:
- How much thermal energy is released when a fuel burns
- How fast that energy is delivered (rate effects)
- How that energy propagates through solids, fluids, and gases
- The temperature rise of surrounding materials leading to flashover

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *"Heat Calculations encompass the quantitative analysis of calorific value, heat release rate, sensible and latent heat effects, conductive, convective, and radiative heat transfer, and the energy balance equations governing fire growth, ignition, and spread in compartments."*

## 1.2 Three Pillars of Heat Calculation in Fires

| Pillar | What it Measures | Practical Question Answered |
|--------|------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Calorimetry** | Total energy content of fuel | *"How much energy is locked inside this material?"* |
| **Heat Transfer** | Energy movement mechanisms | *"How does fire heat reach a remote object?"* |
| **Heat Balance** | Net energy accumulation | *"Will the surrounding temperature reach ignition?"* |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Bonfire Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **🔥 Real-World Analogy — The Tea Kettle on a Campfire**
>
> Imagine you are boiling water on a wood campfire:
>
> 1. **Calorific Value (CV)** = The total chemical energy stored in the wood (like the total battery capacity of a power bank).
> 2. **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** = How fast the battery is being drained — burning a match releases energy very fast; a log releases it slowly.
> 3. **Conduction** = The handle of the kettle getting hot because metal physically transfers vibrations (energy) molecule by molecule.
> 4. **Convection** = Hot air rising from the flame and warming your face.
> 5. **Radiation** = The invisible infrared heat you feel on your skin even when the flame is several feet away.
>
> **Heat Calculation** is the engineering science of *quantifying each of these phenomena with numbers*, so that fire safety designs can be made before a real fire happens.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants Used

- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$
- **Absolute zero:** $0 \ \text{K} = -273.15 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Standard reference temperature:** $T_{\text{ref}} = 25 \ ^\circ\text{C} = 298.15 \ \text{K}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m·s}^{-2}$ (used in plume dynamics)

## 1.5 Why Heat Calculations Matter in Fire Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every fire protection decision — from choosing fire-resistant cable insulation, sizing sprinkler flow rates, designing smoke vents, to setting structural fire resistance times — is fundamentally rooted in a heat calculation. Without these calculations, fire safety would be guesswork.

---

## 1.6 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for This Topic

| KTU 2024 CO | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Topic Mapping |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| **CO2** | Apply, Analyze | Compute calorific values and HRR for common fuels |
| **CO3** | Analyze, Evaluate | Apply heat transfer equations to fire scenarios |
| **CO5** | Evaluate | Use heat balance to predict ignition and flashover |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Heat Quantities in Fire

Fire energy analysis breaks down into the following hierarchical structure:

### A. Thermochemical Quantities (per unit mass / per unit mole)
- **Calorific Value (CV)** — also called **Heat of Combustion**
- **Heat of Reaction**
- **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)**

### B. Rate-Based Quantities (power)
- **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** — measured in Watts (W) or kiloWatts (kW)
- **Heat Flux (q″)** — measured in W/m² or kW/m²

### C. Transfer Mechanism Quantities
- **Conductive Heat Flux** — through solids
- **Convective Heat Flux** — through fluids
- **Radiative Heat Flux** — through electromagnetic waves

## 2.2 Calorific Value — Detailed Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Define the fuel:** Identify mass $m$ (kg) or moles $n$ (kmol) of fuel.

**Step 2 — Define combustion:** Complete oxidation yielding CO₂, H₂O (vapor or liquid), SO₂, N₂.

**Step 3 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics:**

$$\Delta H_c = \sum H_{\text{products}} - \sum H_{\text{reactants}}$$

- Since reactants (O₂, fuel at 25 °C) have reference enthalpies, the magnitude $|\Delta H_c|$ gives the energy released.

**Step 4 — Distinguish Higher vs Lower Heating Value:**

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** Water in products is **condensed** to liquid → latent heat of vaporization is recovered.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV):** Water leaves as **vapor** → latent heat is **lost** in the exhaust.

The relationship:

$$\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + h_{fg} \cdot m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$$

where $h_{fg} \approx 2260 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ at 100 °C.

**Step 5 — Why two values?**
- **HHV** is measured in a **bomb calorimeter** (water condenses).
- **LHV** is closer to real fire situations, where hot gases escape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For most solid fuels, $\text{HHV} \approx 1.10 \times \text{LHV}$ for hydrogen-rich fuels; for pure carbon, HHV = LHV.

## 2.3 Heat Release Rate (HRR) — The Most Important Quantity in Fire

**Definition:** The rate at which thermal energy is generated by combustion.

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \Delta H_c \times \chi_c$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}$ = mass loss rate of fuel (kg/s)
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg)
- $\chi_c$ = combustion efficiency (0 to 1)

**Why HRR Dominates Fire Engineering:** The HRR curve (HRR vs time) defines the entire fire timeline — growth, flashover, fully developed, decay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fire with double the HRR is **not** twice as dangerous — it produces roughly 4× the radiative fraction to distant objects, because radiation scales with the 4th power of flame temperature.

## 2.4 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer

### (i) Conduction — Fourier's Law

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

For a plane wall of thickness $L$ and constant $k$:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \frac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

The transient form (semi-infinite solid, used in fire-side ignition):

$$T(x,t) = T_s + (T_i - T_s) \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$

where $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity**.

### (ii) Convection — Newton's Law of Cooling

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{conv}} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature
- $T_\infty$ = bulk fluid (gas) temperature

**Typical h values in fires:**
- Natural convection (hot gases): $h \approx 5\text{–}25 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- Forced (wind-driven): $h \approx 10\text{–}100 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- Impinging flame: $h \approx 50\text{–}200 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$

### (iii) Radiation — Stefan–Boltzmann Law

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$$

The net radiation between two surfaces ($T_1$ hotter than $T_2$):

$$\dot{q}_{\text{rad}}'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0 to 1)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$
- $T$ in **Kelvin** (mandatory!)

> [!NOTE]
> **View Factor ($F_{12}$):** In real fires, the receiver sees only a fraction of the flame. The general form becomes:
> $$\dot{q}_{\text{rad}}'' = \varepsilon \sigma F_{12} (T_f^4 - T_s^4)$$

## 2.5 Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)

**Definition:** The theoretical maximum temperature achieved when all heat of combustion goes into heating the products, with no losses.

$$T_{\text{AFT}} = T_{\text{ref}} + \frac{\Delta H_c}{\sum n_i c_{p,i}}$$

- $n_i$ = moles of product $i$ per mole of fuel
- $c_{p,i}$ = specific heat of product $i$ (averaged over the temperature range)

**Typical AFTs:**
- Methane: $\approx 1970 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Propane: $\approx 1980 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Wood: $\approx 1200\text{–}1500 \ ^\circ\text{C}$

## 2.6 Fire Load and Fire Load Density

- **Fire Load (Q):** Total energy content of all combustible materials in a compartment (MJ)
- **Fire Load Density (q):** Fire load per unit floor area

$$q = \frac{\sum_i m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_{\text{floor}}} \quad \left[\text{MJ/m}^2\right]$$

## 2.7 Time to Ignition (Critical for Fire Spread)

For a thermally thick solid exposed to constant heat flux $\dot{q}_e''$:

$$t_{\text{ig}} = \frac{\pi}{4} k \rho c_p \left(\frac{T_{\text{ig}} - T_s}{\dot{q}_e''}\right)^2$$

This is the **Quintiere / Babrauskas ignition model** — high-yield for KTU derivations.

## 2.8 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Where Used |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Heat of Combustion (HHV) | $\Delta H_c = \sum H_p - \sum H_r$ | kJ/kg | Bomb calorimetry |
| 2 | HHV vs LHV | $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + m_{w} h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Energy audit |
| 3 | Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \Delta H_c \chi_c$ | kW | Fire growth |
| 4 | Conduction (plane wall) | $\dot{Q} = kA \Delta T / L$ | W | Wall heating |
| 5 | Transient conduction | $T(x,t) = T_s + (T_i - T_s)\text{erf}(x/2\sqrt{\alpha t})$ | K | Pre-heating of solids |
| 6 | Convection | $\dot{Q} = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | Hot gas heating |
| 7 | Radiation (surface) | $\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | W | Flame-to-target |
| 8 | Net radiation exchange | $\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \sigma F(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W/m² | Remote ignition |
| 9 | Adiabatic Flame Temp | $T_{af} = T_{ref} + \frac{\Delta H_c}{\sum n_i c_{p,i}}$ | K | Combustion efficiency |
| 10 | Fire Load Density | $q = \sum m_i \Delta H_{c,i} / A$ | MJ/m² | Compartments |
| 11 | Ignition time (thick) | $t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4}k\rho c_p \left(\frac{T_{ig}-T_s}{\dot{q}''}\right)^2$ | s | Spread prediction |
| 12 | Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | m²/s | Transient heating |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Building Code Compliance (NBC 2016, IS 15105):** Fire load density is used to classify occupancy hazard.
- **Cone Calorimeter Testing (ISO 5660):** HRR is measured directly to rank material fire performance.
- **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator):** All three heat transfer modes are solved numerically using the formulas above.
- **Sprinkler System Design:** Heat release rate determines required water flow rate.
- **Structural Fire Engineering:** Steel temperature rise is computed using transient conduction formulas.

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between HHV and LHV

**Starting Premise:** Combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yO_z$ produces CO₂ and H₂O.

**Stoichiometric Reaction:**

$$C_xH_yO_z + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Moles of water produced per kg of fuel:**

For a generic fuel with mass fractions $C$, $H$, $O$, $S$, the mass of water per kg fuel is:

$$m_{w} = 9 H \quad \text{[kg water per kg fuel]}$$

where $H$ is the mass fraction of hydrogen in the fuel.

**Step 2 — Energy difference:**

The latent heat recovered when water condenses from vapor to liquid at 100 °C:

$$h_{fg} = 2257 \ \text{kJ/kg} \quad \text{(at 1 atm)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + 9 H \cdot h_{fg}}$$

**Verification Example (Methane, CH₄):**
- $H = 4/16 = 0.25$
- LHV = 50,016 kJ/kg
- $9 \times 0.25 \times 2257 = 5078$ kJ/kg
- HHV (predicted) = 50,016 + 5078 = **55,094 kJ/kg**
- Literature HHV ≈ **55,530 kJ/kg** ✓ (within 1%)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Release Rate from Mass Loss

**Premise:** A wooden chair of mass 5 kg burns. The mass loss rate is 0.02 kg/s. The combustion efficiency is 0.85. The effective heat of combustion is 17,000 kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Write the HRR equation:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\dot{Q} = (0.02 \ \text{kg/s}) \times (17{,}000 \ \text{kJ/kg}) \times 0.85$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step:**

$$\dot{Q} = 0.02 \times 17{,}000 = 340 \ \text{kJ/s (per efficiency unit)}$$

$$\dot{Q} = 340 \times 0.85 = 289 \ \text{kJ/s} = 289 \ \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 289 \ \text{kW}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is roughly the heat release of a small office wastebasket fire — easily capable of igniting nearby furniture.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Flame Temperature of Methane

**Stoichiometric Combustion of CH₄:**

$$CH_4 + 2 O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2 H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Energy balance:**

All chemical energy of methane → sensible heating of products.

$$\Delta H_{c,\text{CH}_4} = \sum n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{af}} c_{p,i}\, dT$$

**Step 2 — Assume constant $c_p$ (approximation):**

For product mixture: 1 mole CO₂ + 2 moles H₂O = 3 moles total.

Using average $c_p$ values (in kJ/kmol·K):
- CO₂: $c_p \approx 44$
- H₂O (vapor): $c_p \approx 40$

Total heat capacity of products:

$$\sum n_i c_{p,i} = 1(44) + 2(40) = 124 \ \text{kJ/K}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T_{af}$:**

$$\Delta H_c = \sum n_i c_{p,i} \cdot (T_{af} - T_{ref})$$

$$T_{af} - 298 = \frac{802{,}300 \ \text{kJ/kmol}}{124 \ \text{kJ/K·kmol}}$$

$$T_{af} - 298 = 6470 \ \text{K}$$

This is unrealistically high because $c_p$ is not constant. With temperature-dependent $c_p$:

$$\boxed{T_{af} \approx 2223 \ \text{K} \approx 1950 \ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

(Literature: ~1970 °C ✓)

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Time to Ignition of a Wooden Slab

**Given:**
- Density: $\rho = 700 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat: $c_p = 1500 \ \text{J/kg·K}$
- Conductivity: $k = 0.15 \ \text{W/m·K}$
- Surface temp before heating: $T_s = 25 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Ignition temp: $T_{ig} = 300 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Incident heat flux: $\dot{q}_e'' = 25 \ \text{kW/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute thermal diffusivity:**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{0.15}{700 \times 1500} = 1.43 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Babrauskas ignition correlation:**

$$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \left(\frac{T_{ig} - T_s}{\dot{q}_e''}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (units first!):**

- $k\rho c_p = 0.15 \times 700 \times 1500 = 157{,}500 \ \text{W}^2\text{·s/(m·K}^2\text{)}$
- $(T_{ig} - T_s) = 300 - 25 = 275 \ \text{K}$
- $\dot{q}_e'' = 25{,}000 \ \text{W/m}^2$

**Step 4 — Compute the squared term:**

$$\left(\frac{275}{25{,}000}\right)^2 = (0.011)^2 = 1.21 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^4\text{·K}^2\text{/W}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 157{,}500 \times 1.21 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_{ig} = 0.7854 \times 19.06$$

$$\boxed{t_{ig} \approx 14.97 \ \text{s} \approx 15 \ \text{seconds}}$$

**Interpretation:** Under 25 kW/m² (typical of a nearby burning chair), wood ignites in **15 seconds** — justifying rapid sprinkler response requirements.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Radiative Heat Flux from a Flame to a Target

**Given:** Flame at $T_f = 900 \ ^\circ\text{C} = 1173 \ \text{K}$, emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.9$, view factor $F = 0.4$. Target at $T_s = 25 \ ^\circ\text{C} = 298 \ \text{K}$.

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:**
- $T_f = 1173 \ \text{K}$
- $T_s = 298 \ \text{K}$

**Step 2 — Apply the net radiation formula:**

$$\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \sigma F (T_f^4 - T_s^4)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_f^4$ and $T_s^4$:**

$$T_f^4 = (1173)^4 = 1.892 \times 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_s^4 = (298)^4 = 7.886 \times 10^{9} \ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_f^4 - T_s^4 \approx 1.884 \times 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by constants:**

$$\dot{q}'' = 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.4 \times 1.884 \times 10^{12}$$

$$= 0.9 \times 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 1.884 \times 10^{4}$$

$$= 0.36 \times 10.685 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{q}'' \approx 38.5 \ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Interpretation:** This is **above** the critical flux for piloted ignition of wood (~12 kW/m²) → **auto-ignition imminent.**

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Solving Strategy for KTU Problems

| Problem Type | Identify | First Equation | Common Mistake |
|--------------|----------|----------------|----------------|
| Compute total energy | Look for "MJ", "kJ" → Calorific value | $Q = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Forgetting LHV vs HHV |
| Compute rate | Look for "kW", "W", "/s" | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \Delta H_c \chi_c$ | Using total energy as rate |
| Conduction through wall | Look for "wall", "thickness", "$k$" | $\dot{Q} = kA \Delta T / L$ | Confusing $k$ (conductivity) with convective $h$ |
| Transient heating | Look for "time", "depth" | erf function or ignition formula | Using steady-state formula |
| Flame radiation | Look for "flame", "T", "emissivity" | Stefan–Boltzmann | Using °C instead of K |
