# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define: (i) Higher Heating Value, (ii) Lower Heating Value, and (iii) State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Higher Heating Value (HHV):** The amount of heat released per unit mass of fuel when it undergoes complete combustion and the water vapor in the products is **condensed** to liquid, thereby recovering the latent heat of vaporization. Measured in a **bomb calorimeter**.

**(ii) Lower Heating Value (LHV):** The amount of heat released per unit mass of fuel when it undergoes complete combustion and the water in the products remains in the **vapor** state (i.e., the latent heat is not recovered). It represents the energy available in real fire situations.

**(iii) Relationship:**

$$\boxed{\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + 9H \cdot h_{fg}}$$

where $H$ is the mass fraction of hydrogen in the fuel and $h_{fg} \approx 2257 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Stefan–Boltzmann Law and write the equation for net radiative heat flux between two surfaces.**

**Model Answer:**

**Stefan–Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a black body is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

For a **black body**:

$$\dot{E}_b = \sigma T^4 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

For a real surface with emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$\dot{E} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

For **net radiative heat exchange** between a hot surface at $T_1$ and a cooler surface at $T_2$ (with view factor $F_{12}$):

$$\boxed{\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \sigma F_{12} (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$ and temperatures are in **Kelvin**.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A wooden crib of mass 8 kg burns in a room. The mass loss rate is constant at 0.025 kg/s and the effective heat of combustion is 18,000 kJ/kg. Combustion efficiency is 90%. Calculate:
(i) The Heat Release Rate (HRR).
(ii) The total energy released if the crib burns completely.
(iii) The radiative heat flux received by a wall 2 m away if the flame emissivity is 0.85 and view factor is 0.3. Flame temperature = 850 °C, wall temperature = 30 °C.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the three modes of heat transfer during a fire. State the governing equations and identify the dominant mode in (i) a smouldering fire, (ii) a pool fire, (iii) a flashover condition.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 8$ kg, $\dot{m} = 0.025$ kg/s
- $\Delta H_c = 18{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $\chi_c = 0.90$
- $T_f = 850 + 273 = 1123$ K
- $T_w = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $\varepsilon = 0.85$, $F = 0.3$

**Part (i) — HRR:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$$

$$\dot{Q} = 0.025 \times 18{,}000 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 405 \ \text{kW}}$$

**Part (ii) — Total Energy:**

$$Q = m \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c = 8 \times 18{,}000 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{Q = 129{,}600 \ \text{kJ} = 129.6 \ \text{MJ}}$$

**Part (iii) — Radiative Flux:**

$$\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \sigma F (T_f^4 - T_w^4)$$

Compute $T_f^4$ and $T_w^4$:

$$T_f^4 = (1123)^4 = 1.589 \times 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_w^4 = (303)^4 = 8.43 \times 10^{9} \ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_f^4 - T_w^4 \approx 1.580 \times 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$

Now substitute:

$$\dot{q}'' = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.3 \times 1.580 \times 10^{12}$$

$$= 0.85 \times 0.3 \times 5.67 \times 1.580 \times 10^{4}$$

$$= 0.255 \times 8.96 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{q}'' \approx 22.84 \ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

This exceeds the **critical flux for piloted ignition of wood (~12 kW/m²)** → wall will ignite.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

| Fire Type | Dominant Mode | Reasoning |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Smouldering fire** | **Conduction** + slow convection | Low temperatures; heat moves through porous solid |
| **Pool fire** | **Convection** + radiation | Hot buoyant plume + luminous flame radiation |
| **Flashover** | **Radiation dominates** | All surfaces near 600 °C → intense re-radiation |

**Governing Equations:**

- **Conduction (Fourier):** $\dot{q}'' = -k \, dT/dx$
- **Convection (Newton):** $\dot{q}'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$
- **Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann):** $\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$

**Sketch Description (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FLAME[Fire / Flame Source]:::a
    FLAME -->|Conduction| SOLID[Adjacent Solid]
    FLAME -->|Convection| GAS[Hot Gas Plume]
    FLAME -->|Radiation| FAR[Remote Surfaces]

    classDef a fill:#ff5722,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff
```

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A compartment of floor area 25 m² contains wooden furniture with total mass 400 kg. The effective heat of combustion is 17 MJ/kg. Calculate:
(i) Fire load (in MJ)
(ii) Fire load density (in MJ/m²)
(iii) The expected fire severity category as per IS 15105 / NBC 2016.
(iv) If the HRR is 1500 kW, estimate the approximate time to flashover using a simple energy balance, assuming the upper gas layer must reach 600 °C with an effective heat capacity of 0.05 kJ/m³·K and layer volume of 50 m³.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between Higher Heating Value and Lower Heating Value for a hydrocarbon fuel. Show all steps and verify the result numerically for methane (CH₄) with H = 25% by mass and $h_{fg}$ = 2257 kJ/kg.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Given:** $A = 25$ m², $m = 400$ kg, $\Delta H_c = 17$ MJ/kg, $\dot{Q} = 1500$ kW, $V = 50$ m³, $c_v = 0.05$ kJ/m³·K, $\Delta T = 600 - 25 = 575$ K.

**(i) Fire Load:**

$$Q = m \cdot \Delta H_c = 400 \times 17 = \boxed{6800 \ \text{MJ}}$$

**(ii) Fire Load Density:**

$$q = Q / A = 6800 / 25 = \boxed{272 \ \text{MJ/m}^2}$$

**(iii) Fire Severity (per IS 15105 / NBC 2016):**

| Fire Load Density | Severity |
|------------------|----------|
| $< 250$ MJ/m² | Low |
| $250$–$500$ MJ/m² | **Moderate** |
| $500$–$1000$ MJ/m² | High |
| $> 1000$ MJ/m² | Very High |

$$\boxed{\text{Result: } 272 \ \text{MJ/m}^2 \rightarrow \textbf{Moderate Severity}}$$

**(iv) Time to Flashover:**

$$t_{fo} = \frac{V \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T}{\dot{Q}}$$

$$t_{fo} = \frac{50 \times 0.05 \times 575}{1500} = \frac{1437.5}{1500}$$

$$\boxed{t_{fo} \approx 0.96 \ \text{minutes} \approx 57.5 \ \text{seconds}}$$

**Critical insight:** The compartment reaches flashover in **under 1 minute** → automatic sprinkler / detection systems are essential.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Step 1 — Combustion Equation:**

For a generic hydrocarbon $C_xH_yO_z$:

$$C_xH_yO_z + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water per kg fuel:**

If mass fraction of hydrogen is $H$, then hydrogen mass in 1 kg fuel = $H$ kg.
Moles of H = $H/2$ kmol.
Moles of H₂O = $H/4$ kmol.
Mass of H₂O = $9H$ kg.

**Step 3 — Energy difference:**

Difference between HHV and LHV = latent heat recovered when this water condenses:

$$\Delta E = m_{w} \cdot h_{fg} = 9H \cdot h_{fg}$$

**Step 4 — Final relationship:**

$$\boxed{\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + 9H \cdot h_{fg}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification for Methane:**

- Molecular formula: CH₄
- Molar mass: 16 kg/kmol
- H mass fraction: $H = 4/16 = 0.25$
- $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg

$$9H \cdot h_{fg} = 9 \times 0.25 \times 2257 = 5078.25 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$

- Literature LHV(CH₄) = 50,016 kJ/kg
- HHV (computed) = $50{,}016 + 5078 = \mathbf{55{,}094 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$
- Literature HHV(CH₄) = 55,530 kJ/kg
- **Error ≈ 0.8%** ✓ (acceptable; small error from $h_{fg}$ value at 25 °C vs 100 °C)

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Using °C in Stefan–Boltzmann:** Temperature MUST be in Kelvin. Mixing units gives answers 1000× too small.
> 2. **Confusing HHV with LHV:** Many students use HHV where the question asks for usable energy (LHV).
> 3. **Forgetting combustion efficiency $\chi_c$:** Real fires are not 100% efficient. A common trap.
> 4. **Wrong units in $t_{ig}$ formula:** Mixing kJ with J, kW with W → off by factor of 1000.
> 5. **Heat release rate vs Total energy:** The presence of "/s" in the question means HRR; otherwise total energy.
> 6. **Forgetting view factor in radiation:** In open geometries, $F < 1$ drastically reduces received flux.
> 7. **Skipping the Energy Balance Sketch in part (b):** Examiners award marks for clear diagrams.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Calorific Value (CV):** Total energy in fuel → HHV (condensed) vs LHV (vapor). Bomb calorimeter gives HHV.
- 🔑 **HHV = LHV + 9H·h_fg:** The single most-tested numerical relation in Module 2.
- 🔑 **HRR (Q-dot):** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \Delta H_c \chi_c$ — the master rate equation; HRR curve drives entire fire timeline.
- 🔑 **Three Modes of Heat Transfer:** Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton), Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann).
- 🔑 **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ — **T must be in Kelvin**; emissivity $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$.
- 🔑 **Adiabatic Flame Temperature:** Theoretical max; real flame is lower due to losses.
- 🔑 **Fire Load Density:** $q = \sum m_i \Delta H_{c,i} / A$ in MJ/m² — used to classify compartment hazard (Low / Moderate / High / Very High).
- 🔑 **Time to Ignition (Babrauskas):** $t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4}k\rho c_p \left(\frac{T_{ig}-T_s}{\dot{q}''}\right)^2$ — inversely proportional to **square** of heat flux.
- 🔑 **Thermal Diffusivity:** $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ — controls how fast heat penetrates a solid.
- 🔑 **Convection Coefficient $h$:** Ranges 5–200 W/m²·K in fires; needed for Newton's law of cooling.
- 🔑 **View Factor $F$:** Geometric reduction in radiation exchange; never assume $F=1$ except for enclosed cavities.
- 🔑 **Combustion Efficiency $\chi_c$:** Real-world fires 0.7–0.95; well-ventilated fires closer to 1.
- 🔑 **Energy Balance for Flashover:** $\dot{Q} \cdot t = V \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T$ — simple but powerful compartment estimate.
- 🔑 **KTU Favorite Numerical:** Methane combustion, time-to-ignition of wood, flame-to-wall radiation, and fire load calculations appear repeatedly.
- 🔑 **Reference Values to Memorize:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$, $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, AFT(CH₄) ≈ 1970 °C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Load (Q):** The total quantity of heat energy (in MJ or kWh) that would be released if *all* combustible materials within a defined space (room, compartment, building) were to undergo complete combustion. It is the **energy content of the fuel inventory**, not the heat actually released during a real fire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Load Density (q):** The fire load per unit floor area, expressed in **MJ/m²** (or **kWh/m²**). It is the governing parameter in fire protection design because it normalises the fuel inventory to the area of fire origin.

$$
q = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \cdot H_{c,i}}{A_{f}}
$$

where $M_i$ is the mass of the *i*th combustible, $H_{c,i}$ is its calorific value (MJ/kg), and $A_f$ is the floor area of the compartment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thermostat of the Fuel Bank"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of a room as a **bank account of stored chemical energy**. Every kilogram of wood, plastic, paper, or textile is like a *currency note* whose value is its calorific content. The **fire load density** is the *account balance per square metre of floor area*. A high balance means a "rich" room that can sustain a long, severe fire; a low balance means a "poor" room that burns out quickly. Engineers use this balance to decide how thick the walls, how big the exits, and how much sprinkler water the room needs — exactly like a banker decides the credit limit based on the balance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Calorific value of oven-dry wood:** $H_{c,\text{wood}} \approx$ **17.5 MJ/kg** (reference standard for wood-equivalent calculations).
- **Typical fire load densities in buildings:** **250 – 2500 MJ/m²**.
- **Conversion:** 1 kWh/m² = **3.6 MJ/m²**; 1 BTU/ft² ≈ **0.01136 MJ/m²**.
- **Opening factor units:** m^(1/2) or m^0.5.

## 1.4 Why Fire Loading Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fire load is the *primary* input to the calculation of **equivalent fire duration (t_e)**, which in turn determines the **required fire resistance rating** of structural elements per IS 1642 / IS 3809 / Eurocode 1991-1-2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Concept

- **Step 1 — Inventory:** Identify *every* combustible item (fixed + movable) in the compartment.
- **Step 2 — Mass × Heat:** For each item, multiply its mass by the corresponding calorific value. This is the **Direct Method** of CIB W14.
- **Step 3 — Sum & Normalise:** Add all heat contributions and divide by the floor area → *Fire load density* **q**.
- **Step 4 — Convert (optional):** Express the fuel inventory as an **equivalent mass of wood** by dividing by $H_{c,\text{wood}}$ → *Wood-equivalent fire load* **q_e**.
- **Step 5 — Severity Mapping:** Combine **q** with the **ventilation factor (w)** and **construction factor (k)** to obtain **equivalent fire duration t_e**, the time a standard furnace test (ISO 834) would produce the same thermal exposure.

## 2.2 Types of Fire Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Permanent (Fixed) Fire Load:** Combustibles that form part of the building fabric and do not change with occupancy — e.g., wooden floors, timber partitions, plastic-insulated cables embedded in walls.
>
> **Variable (Movable) Fire Load:** Combustibles associated with the use/occupancy — furniture, stored goods, documents. This is **occupancy-dependent** and usually the **dominant** contribution.
>
> **Temporary Fire Load:** Seasonal or short-duration items (decorations, festival stock, transient exhibits).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total fire load | $Q = \sum M_i H_{c,i}$ | MJ | Energy content of all fuel |
| 2 | Fire load density | $q = Q / A_f$ | MJ/m² | Normalised to floor area |
| 3 | Wood-equivalent fire load | $q_e = Q / (H_{c,\text{wood}} \cdot A_f)$ | kg/m² | Useful for t_e calculation |
| 4 | Ventilation factor | $w = A_v \sqrt{H_v} \,/\, A_f$ | m^0.5 | $A_v$ = vertical opening area, $H_v$ = weighted mean opening height |
| 5 | Equivalent fire duration (Pettersson/CIB W14) | $t_e = q \cdot w \cdot k$ | min | $k$ = construction/thermal property factor |
| 6 | Conversion to wood basis | $q_{\text{wood}} = q / H_{c,\text{wood}}$ | kg/m² | When $q$ is in MJ/m² and $H_{c,\text{wood}} = 17.5$ MJ/kg |
| 7 | Lie's empirical form | $t_e = 0.067 \cdot q_{\text{wood}} \cdot w \cdot k$ | min | $q_{\text{wood}}$ in kg/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol rule:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ instead to avoid breaking the table layout.

## 2.4 Calorific Values of Common Materials (Reference Table)

| Material | $H_c$ (MJ/kg) | Wood Equivalent Factor |
|----------|---------------|------------------------|
| Wood (dry) | 17.5 | 1.00 |
| Paper / cardboard | 16.5 – 17.0 | 0.95 |
| Cotton / textiles | 17.0 – 18.0 | 1.00 |
| Wool | 20.0 – 25.0 | 1.30 |
| PVC | 20.0 | 1.14 |
| Polyethylene | 46.0 | 2.63 |
| Polystyrene | 40.0 | 2.29 |
| Rubber (natural) | 38.0 | 2.17 |
| Leather | 18.0 | 1.03 |
| Coal | 24.0 – 30.0 | 1.50 |
| Petrol / gasoline | 44.0 | 2.51 |
| Ethanol | 27.0 | 1.54 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fire load is the **cornerstone input** of prescriptive fire safety codes (NFPA, IS 3809, Eurocode 1991-1-2). It is used to:
- Determine **fire resistance rating** of structural members.
- Classify **occupancy hazard** (low / medium / high).
- Size **ventilation openings** and **smoke control systems**.
- Decide on **sprinkler / suppression requirements** in NBC-2016 (India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fire Load Density from First Principles

> **Logic:** Every combustible substance stores chemical energy equal to the product of its mass and its calorific value. Summation over all materials gives the total fire load. Normalising by floor area gives the density.

$$
Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \cdot H_{c,i}
$$

$$
q = \frac{Q}{A_f} = \frac{\sum M_i \cdot H_{c,i}}{A_f}
$$

If we choose wood as the reference (because of its dominant historical use in buildings):

$$
q_{\text{wood}} = \frac{Q}{H_{c,\text{wood}} \cdot A_f} = \frac{\sum M_i H_{c,i}}{17.5 \cdot A_f}
$$

The **wood-equivalent density** $q_{\text{wood}}$ (kg/m²) is the mass of wood that would release the same energy as the actual fuel inventory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Fire Duration (CIB W14 / Pettersson)

The equivalent fire duration $t_e$ is defined as the time of exposure to the **ISO 834 standard fire** that produces the same severity (maximum temperature or heat flux) as the real fire in a compartment. Pettersson (1973) and CIB W14 (1986) proposed:

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot w}{b}
$$

where $b$ is a constant that absorbs the thermal inertia $\sqrt{\lambda \rho c_p}$ of the compartment boundaries. For **normal-weight concrete** enclosure:

$$
b \approx 330 \quad \text{(with } q \text{ in MJ/m}^2,\; w \text{ in m}^{0.5},\; t_e \text{ in minutes)}
$$

Combining with the ventilation factor:

$$
w = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_f}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\;t_e \;=\; \frac{q \cdot A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{330 \cdot A_f} \quad \text{(minutes)}\;}
$$

A more general form includes a **construction factor $k$** (1.0 for dense concrete, ~0.8 for lightweight concrete, ~0.6 for steel-lined compartments):

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot w \cdot k}{330}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A storage room of floor plan **8 m × 6 m** (height 3.5 m) contains:
> - Wooden pallets: **1200 kg** ($H_c = 17.5$ MJ/kg)
> - Cotton textiles: **500 kg** ($H_c = 18.0$ MJ/kg)
> - PVC pipes: **150 kg** ($H_c = 20.0$ MJ/kg)
> - Paper cartons: **200 kg** ($H_c = 16.5$ MJ/kg)
>
> The room has one window of **1.5 m × 2.0 m** (height of opening $H_v = 1.5$ m). The walls are normal-weight concrete ($k = 1.0$).
>
> Calculate: **(a)** Fire load density, **(b)** wood-equivalent fire load, **(c)** equivalent fire duration, **(d)** classification of occupancy.

### Step (a) — Total Fire Load *Q*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{wood}} &= 1200 \times 17.5 = 21\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{cotton}} &= 500 \times 18.0 = 9\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{PVC}} &= 150 \times 20.0 = 3\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{paper}} &= 200 \times 16.5 = 3\,300 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 21\,000 + 9\,000 + 3\,000 + 3\,300 = 36\,300 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (b) — Fire Load Density *q*

Floor area: $A_f = 8 \times 6 = 48$ m²

$$
q = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{A_f} = \frac{36\,300}{48} = 756.25 \text{ MJ/m}^2
$$

### Step (c) — Wood-Equivalent Fire Load *q_wood*

$$
q_{\text{wood}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{H_{c,\text{wood}} \cdot A_f} = \frac{36\,300}{17.5 \times 48} = \frac{36\,300}{840} = 43.21 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

### Step (d) — Ventilation Factor *w*

Opening area: $A_v = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0$ m²; opening height: $H_v = 1.5$ m

$$
w = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_f} = \frac{3.0 \times \sqrt{1.5}}{48} = \frac{3.0 \times 1.2247}{48} = \frac{3.6742}{48} = 0.0765 \text{ m}^{0.5}
$$

### Step (e) — Equivalent Fire Duration $t_e$

Using the CIB W14 form with $k = 1.0$ (normal-weight concrete):

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot w \cdot k}{330} = \frac{756.25 \times 0.0765 \times 1.0}{330} = \frac{57.85}{330} = 0.175 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CIB W14 constant (330) gives a *very low* $t_e$ in this example because the **ventilation factor is small** (only one small window). In practice, this is interpreted as a *ventilation-controlled fire*; the room would need additional openings or mechanical smoke exhaust to achieve realistic severity. For KTU exam purposes, students should present the **methodology clearly** and state the constant used.

If the *direct* $q \cdot w \cdot k$ form (without dividing by 330) is requested:

$$
t_e \;=\; q \cdot w \cdot k \;=\; 756.25 \times 0.0765 \times 1.0 \;\approx\; 57.85 \text{ min}
$$

This corresponds to a **2-hour fire resistance rating** requirement per IS 1642 / IS 3809.

### Step (f) — Classification of Occupancy

| Standard | Threshold | Class for $q = 756$ MJ/m² |
|----------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Eurocode 1 / IS 3809 | $< 500$ → Low; $500-1500$ → Medium; $> 1500$ → High | **Medium Hazard** |

> **Result:** $q = 756.25$ MJ/m² → **Medium fire load**; $t_e \approx 58$ min → **2-hour** structural fire resistance required.

---

## 3.4 Classification Matrix (Per IS 3809 / Eurocode 1991-1-2)

| Class | Fire Load Density (MJ/m²) | Typical Occupancy | Required Fire Resistance |
|-------|---------------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Low Hazard (LH)** | $< 500$ | Offices, schools, hospitals | 1 – 2 h |
| **Medium Hazard (MH-1)** | $500 - 1000$ | Light industry, residential | 2 h |
| **Medium Hazard (MH-2)** | $1000 - 1500$ | Warehouses (low combustible) | 2 – 3 h |
| **High Hazard (HH-1)** | $1500 - 2500$ | Paper stores, printing | 3 – 4 h |
| **High Hazard (HH-2)** | $2500 - 4000$ | Cotton/textile warehouses | 4 h |
| **Very High (HH-3)** | $> 4000$ | Flammable liquid stores | $\geq$ 4 h + sprinklers |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Method | Input Required | Output | Accuracy | Used In |
|--------|----------------|--------|----------|---------|
| Direct (Inventory) | Mass + $H_c$ of every material | $q$ (MJ/m²) | Highest | CIB W14, IS 3809 |
| Indirect (Occupancy-based) | Occupancy type only | $q$ (MJ/m²) | Moderate (statistical averages) | Eurocode 1991-1-2 Annex E |
| Heat Release Rate (HRR) | Burning rate of fuel | $Q^\cdot$ (MW) | Time-dependent | Performance-based design |
| Equivalent Time (Pettersson) | $q$, $w$, $k$ | $t_e$ (min) | Approximate | Structural design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Fire Load Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fire Load Calculation] --> B[Inventory of Combustibles]
    B --> C{Permanent or Movable?}
    C -->|Permanent| D1[Floor, walls, partitions, cable insulation]
    C -->|Movable| D2[Furniture, goods, documents]
    D1 --> E[Tabulate Mass Mi and Calorific Value Hci]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Total Energy Q = Sum Mi x Hci]
    F --> G[Determine Floor Area Af]
    G --> H[Fire Load Density q = Q / Af in MJ per m2]
    H --> I[Wood Equivalent q_wood = Q / 17.5 x Af]
    I --> J[Compute Ventilation Factor w = Av x sqrt Hv / Af]
    J --> K[Select Construction Factor k from material]
    K --> L[Equivalent Fire Duration te = q x w x k divided by 330]
    L --> M[Classify Occupancy per IS 3809]
    M --> N[Determine Structural Fire Resistance Rating]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Fire Load Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Input q in MJ per m2] --> Q{q less than 500}
    Q -->|Yes| R[LOW HAZARD LH 1 to 2 h FR]
    Q -->|No| S{q less than 1000}
    S -->|Yes| T[MEDIUM HAZARD MH1 2 h FR]
    S -->|No| U{q less than 1500}
    U -->|Yes| V[MEDIUM HAZARD MH2 2 to 3 h FR]
    U -->|No| W{q less than 2500}
    W -->|Yes| X[HIGH HAZARD HH1 3 to 4 h FR]
    W -->|No| Y{q less than 4000}
    Y -->|Yes| Z[HIGH HAZARD HH2 4 h FR]
    Y -->|No| AA[VERY HIGH HH3 at least 4 h FR plus sprinklers]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Map: Fire Load to Fire Severity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Combustible Inventory Mass] --> B1[Heat Content Hc in MJ per kg]
    B1 --> C1[Total Fire Load Q in MJ]
    C1 --> D1[Fire Load Density q in MJ per m2]
    A2[Compartment Geometry Av Hv Af] --> B2[Ventilation Factor w in m0.5]
    B2 --> E1[Ventilation Factor w]
    A3[Boundary Material lambda rho c] --> B3[Construction Factor k]
    B3 --> F1[Construction Factor k]
    D1 --> G1[Equivalent Fire Duration te]
    E1 --> G1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve]
    H1 --> I1[Structural Fire Resistance Rating]
    D1 --> J1[Occupancy Hazard Class]
    I1 --> K1[Prescriptive Fire Design]
    J1 --> K1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define fire load. Differentiate between fire load and fire load density.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Fire load (Q):** The total quantity of heat energy (in MJ) released by the complete combustion of *all* combustible materials in a defined space.
- **Fire load density (q):** The fire load per unit floor area, in **MJ/m²**.

| Aspect | Fire Load (Q) | Fire Load Density (q) |
|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| Symbol | $Q$ | $q$ |
| Unit | MJ | MJ/m² |
| Dependence on floor area | No | Yes |
| Use | Total energy budget | Design parameter for hazard classification |

> [!NOTE]
> Both parameters describe the *fuel inventory*, not the actual heat released during a real fire (which is governed by ventilation, geometry, and fire growth).

---

### **Q2. Explain the concept of 'wood-equivalent fire load' with a suitable example.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **wood-equivalent fire load** is the mass of oven-dry wood that would release the same total heat energy as the actual combustible inventory. It allows diverse fuels (plastics, textiles, rubber) to be expressed on a *common reference scale*.

$$
q_{\text{wood}} = \frac{\sum M_i H_{c,i}}{H_{c,\text{wood}} \cdot A_f} = \frac{\sum M_i H_{c,i}}{17.5 \cdot A_f}
$$

**Example:** A room of 50 m² contains 700 kg of furniture (wood + plastic, average $H_c = 19$ MJ/kg). Then

$$
q_{\text{wood}} = \frac{700 \times 19}{17.5 \times 50} = \frac{13\,300}{875} = 15.2 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

This means the room has the same fuel energy as 760 kg of pure wood (15.2 × 50).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern (KTU 2024):** Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Sub-part (a) is *computational*; sub-part (b) is *conceptual/descriptive*.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** A factory storage area of dimensions **15 m × 10 m × 4 m** contains the following combustible materials:

| Material | Mass (kg) | $H_c$ (MJ/kg) |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Wooden crates | 2000 | 17.5 |
| Cotton bales | 1500 | 18.0 |
| Polystyrene packaging | 300 | 40.0 |
| Rubber belts | 250 | 38.0 |

The room has **two windows**, each 2.0 m × 1.5 m, with weighted opening height $H_v = 1.5$ m. Walls are **normal-weight concrete** ($k = 1.0$).

**Calculate:**
1. Total fire load $Q$ (MJ)
2. Fire load density $q$ (MJ/m²)
3. Wood-equivalent fire load $q_{\text{wood}}$ (kg/m²)
4. Ventilation factor $w$ (m^0.5)
5. Equivalent fire duration $t_e$ (min)
6. Occupancy class per IS 3809.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Total Fire Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{wood crates}} &= 2000 \times 17.5 = 35\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{cotton}} &= 1500 \times 18.0 = 27\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{polystyrene}} &= 300 \times 40.0 = 12\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{rubber}} &= 250 \times 38.0 = 9\,500 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 35\,000 + 27\,000 + 12\,000 + 9\,500 = 83\,500 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Fire Load Density**

$$
A_f = 15 \times 10 = 150 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
q = \frac{83\,500}{150} = 556.67 \text{ MJ/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Wood-Equivalent Fire Load**

$$
q_{\text{wood}} = \frac{83\,500}{17.5 \times 150} = \frac{83\,500}{2625} = 31.81 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Ventilation Factor**

$$
A_v = 2 \times (2.0 \times 1.5) = 6.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
w = \frac{6.0 \times \sqrt{1.5}}{150} = \frac{6.0 \times 1.2247}{150} = \frac{7.348}{150} = 0.0490 \text{ m}^{0.5}
$$

**Step 5: Equivalent Fire Duration**

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot w \cdot k}{330} = \frac{556.67 \times 0.0490 \times 1.0}{330} = \frac{27.28}{330} = 0.0827 \text{ min}
$$

(Or using direct $q \cdot w \cdot k \approx 27.3$ min, implying 1-h fire resistance.)

**Step 6: Classification**

$q = 556.67$ MJ/m² lies in the range $500 - 1000$ MJ/m² → **Medium Hazard, Class MH-1** → **2-h fire resistance rating required.**

---

**(b)** Discuss the **CIB W14 method** for determining equivalent fire duration. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the roles of **ventilation factor** and **construction factor**. Comment on the limitations of this method. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

The **CIB W14 (1986) method** expresses the severity of a real compartment fire in terms of an **equivalent time of exposure $t_e$** to the **ISO 834 standard fire curve**. The governing equation is:

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot w \cdot k}{330}
$$

- **Ventilation factor $w$** captures the influence of *air supply* on fire severity. It is given by

$$
w = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_f}
$$

where $A_v$ is the total area of vertical openings (windows, doors), $H_v$ is the weighted mean height of these openings, and $A_f$ is the floor area of the fire compartment. **A room with large windows is well-ventilated and can sustain a high HRR, increasing $t_e$.** A closed room has a *ventilation-controlled* fire that decays quickly.

- **Construction factor $k$** (also called the *thermal property factor*) captures the heat-absorbing capacity of the compartment boundaries. It is derived from the thermal inertia $\sqrt{\lambda \rho c_p}$ of the wall material. **Dense concrete has a high thermal inertia, absorbs more heat, and therefore sustains a longer equivalent fire duration** (larger $k$, typically 1.0). Lightweight partitions (drywall) have low thermal inertia → smaller $k$ → shorter $t_e$.

**Limitations:**

1. The method assumes a **single, well-defined fire compartment**; it does not handle multi-room or smoke-spread scenarios.
2. The ventilation factor is a **static, geometric** quantity — it ignores **dynamic opening behaviour** (e.g., glass cracking at high temperatures).
3. It is valid only for **fuel-controlled and well-defined ventilation-controlled** fires; it cannot predict **flashover** or **backdraft** events.
4. The constant 330 is empirical and **standard-specific** (CIB W14 European data set); Indian or tropical-climate data may differ.
5. It treats all fuel as if released uniformly; in reality, **localised** high-HRR items (e.g., foam stacks) create local severity peaks.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A textile warehouse has floor plan **25 m × 12 m** and contains **6000 kg of cotton bales** ($H_c = 18$ MJ/kg) and **3000 kg of wooden crates** ($H_c = 17.5$ MJ/kg). Calculate:
1. Total fire load $Q$
2. Fire load density $q$
3. Wood-equivalent fire load $q_{\text{wood}}$
4. Classify the occupancy and determine the required fire resistance rating. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Total Fire Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{cotton}} &= 6000 \times 18 = 108\,000 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{wood}} &= 3000 \times 17.5 = 52\,500 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 108\,000 + 52\,500 = 160\,500 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Fire Load Density**

$$
A_f = 25 \times 12 = 300 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
q = \frac{160\,500}{300} = 535 \text{ MJ/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Wood-Equivalent Fire Load**

$$
q_{\text{wood}} = \frac{160\,500}{17.5 \times 300} = \frac{160\,500}{5250} = 30.57 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Classification & Fire Resistance**

$q = 535$ MJ/m² lies in the range $500 - 1000$ MJ/m² → **Medium Hazard (MH-1)** → **2-hour fire resistance rating** required for load-bearing structural elements per IS 1642.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students *forget* to state the **calorific values used** when calculating $Q$. Always show $M \times H_c$ step explicitly. Marks are deducted if $H_c$ is not quoted with units (MJ/kg). Also, do not mix up $q$ in MJ/m² and $q_{\text{wood}}$ in kg/m² — they are *different* quantities and both must be calculated distinctly.

---

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **equivalent fire duration**. How is it used in the **structural fire design** of buildings? Compare the **standard temperature–time curve (ISO 834)** with the **real (natural) fire curve**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Equivalent fire duration ($t_e$)** is the *hypothetical* time of exposure to the **ISO 834 standard furnace test** that would cause the *same* thermal damage to a structural element as the **actual, fully-developed compartment fire**.

**Use in structural design:**

1. The structural element is rated for $t_e$ minutes of fire resistance.
2. The rating determines **column, beam, slab, and wall thicknesses** for the chosen material (concrete, steel, timber).
3. The rating also decides **compartmentation requirements**, **escape route lengths**, and **active system** (sprinkler) design.

**Comparison: ISO 834 vs Natural Fire**

| Feature | ISO 834 Standard Fire | Natural (Real) Compartment Fire |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------------|
| Origin | Furnace, gas-fired | Actual fuel in room |
| Temperature–time curve | Monotonically increasing: $T = 345 \log(8t + 1) + 20$ | Growth → flashover → fully developed → decay |
| Peak temperature | Reaches ~1000 °C in 2 h | May exceed 1100 °C briefly |
| Duration | Open-ended (test-controlled) | Limited by fuel and ventilation |
| Ventilation effect | Not modelled | Central to severity |
| Use | Standardised rating, prescriptive codes | Performance-based design |
| Decay phase | Absent | Present; critical for thermal inertia cooling |

**Significance:** While ISO 834 is convenient for *prescriptive* rating, it is *not* representative of a real fire. For *performance-based* design, **parametric fire curves** (e.g., Eurocode 1991-1-2 Annex A) are used, which incorporate **fire load density, opening factor, and thermal inertia** of the enclosure directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fire Loading**

- **Fire load $Q$** = sum of $M_i H_{c,i}$ for all combustibles; unit **MJ**.
- **Fire load density $q$** = $Q / A_f$; unit **MJ/m²**; the design parameter.
- **Wood-equivalent $q_{\text{wood}}$** = $Q / (17.5 \times A_f)$; unit **kg/m²**; required for $t_e$.
- **Reference calorific value of wood:** $H_{c,\text{wood}} = 17.5$ MJ/kg (always).
- **Ventilation factor $w = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_f$** in m^0.5.
- **Equivalent fire duration:** $t_e = q \cdot w \cdot k / 330$ (CIB W14 form, minutes).
- **Classification (IS 3809):** LH < 500, MH-1 500–1000, MH-2 1000–1500, HH-1 1500–2500, HH-2 2500–4000, HH-3 > 4000 MJ/m².
- **Conversion:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 BTU/ft² ≈ 0.01136 MJ/m².
- **High-$H_c$ materials** (PE 46, PS 40, rubber 38) drastically *increase* $q$ for small masses — beware in plastic stores.
- **Permanent + Variable** fire load must both be tabulated; movable load is usually the larger share.
- **Indirect (occupancy-based) method** is used in Eurocode 1991-1-2 Annex E for *preliminary* design only.
- **Fire severity** depends on $q$, $w$, and $k$ — *not* on $q$ alone.
- **Exam tip:** Always show units in *every* step of the calculation — partial marks are given for unit consistency even if the final number is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Heat of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Heat of Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Standard Heat of Reaction** (denoted $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r}$ or $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}$) is the **enthalpy change** associated with a chemical reaction when both the **reactants** and the **products** are in their **standard states** — that is, the thermodynamically most stable physical form at a pressure of exactly **1 bar (≈ 1 atm = 101.325 kPa)** and a reference temperature of **25 °C (298.15 K)**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the difference between the sum of the standard enthalpies of formation (or equivalently the standard enthalpies of combustion) of the products and reactants, weighted by their respective stoichiometric coefficients.

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum_{products} \nu_i H^{\circ}_{f,i} \; - \sum_{reactants} \nu_j H^{\circ}_{f,j}$$

where $\nu_i, \nu_j$ are the stoichiometric coefficients (positive for products, negative for reactants when using the convention $\Delta H = H_{products} - H_{reactants}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Tip:** Always write the **standard state notation** with a superscript circle (°), e.g., $\Delta H^{\circ}$. The standard state is **NOT** standard temperature and pressure (STP) used in ideal gas problems. Standard state is a **pressure reference (1 bar)**, and 25 °C is the conventional reference temperature for tabulated thermochemical data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bank account** that you open at a specific date (1 bar, 25 °C). Every chemical species has a fixed "energy balance" deposited into this account when it is formed from its elements in their standard states. This balance is called the **Standard Heat of Formation** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_f$).

When a chemical reaction occurs:
- You **close the bank accounts of the reactants** (debit them).
- You **open the bank accounts of the products** (credit them).
- The **net change** in your "energy wallet" is the **Standard Heat of Reaction**.

> **Real-World Fire Analogy:** When methane ($\text{CH}_4$) burns, the bank accounts of $\text{CH}_4$ and $\text{O}_2$ (the reactants) are debited, and the accounts of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (the products) are credited. The "energy bill" paid (heat released) is exactly $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ — the heat of combustion. This is the energy that drives fire propagation.

### 1.3 Standard State Conventions (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams)

| Convention | Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Pressure | **1 bar (10⁵ Pa)** | $P^{\circ}$ |
| Reference Temperature | **25 °C (298.15 K)** | $T^{\circ}$ |
| Standard State of a Gas | Pure gas at 1 bar, behaving ideally | — |
| Standard State of a Liquid | Pure liquid at 1 bar | — |
| Standard State of a Solid | Most stable solid allotrope at 1 bar | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The standard heat of formation of any element in its standard state is **defined as zero**. This is the cornerstone of thermochemistry. For example, $\Delta H^{\circ}_f (\text{O}_2, g) = 0$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_f (\text{C, graphite}) = 0$, but $\Delta H^{\circ}_f (\text{C, diamond}) = +1.895 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (because diamond is NOT the standard state of carbon).

### 1.4 Classification of Reactions Based on $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$

| Type | Sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ | Heat Flow | Fire Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exothermic** | Negative ($\Delta H^{\circ}_r < 0$) | Released to surroundings | Combustion, oxidation, neutralization |
| **Endothermic** | Positive ($\Delta H^{\circ}_r > 0$) | Absorbed from surroundings | Decomposition of fuels at high T |
| **Athermic** | Zero ($\Delta H^{\circ}_r = 0$) | No net heat exchange | Mixing of ideal gases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In fire dynamics, **virtually every combustion reaction is strongly exothermic**. For example, the complete combustion of methane has $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = -890.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$, meaning every mole of methane releases 890.4 kJ of energy — this is the energetic engine of fire spread.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Foundation: Why Do We Need a "Standard" Heat of Reaction?

The enthalpy of reaction depends strongly on:
1. **Temperature** of the system.
2. **Pressure** at which reactants and products are held.
3. **Physical state** (solid, liquid, gas) of each species.
4. **Allotropic form** (e.g., graphite vs. diamond for carbon).

To enable **comparability** between reactions reported in different laboratories, IUPAC established the **standard state** as a universal reference. Without this, comparing combustion data from two different fire research institutes would be meaningless.

### 2.2 The Two Pathways to Calculate $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$

#### **Pathway A: From Standard Heats of Formation ($\Delta H^{\circ}_f$)**

This is the **direct, first-principles method** used in KTU board problems:

$$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum_{i} \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, products} - \sum_{j} \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, reactants}}$$

**Why this works:** Every species in its standard state has a "formation energy" from its constituent elements. By subtracting the formation energy of the reactants from that of the products, we isolate the net bond rearrangement energy — which is the heat of reaction.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Identify the balanced chemical equation.
2. Look up (or be given) $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ for every reactant and product.
3. Multiply each by its stoichiometric coefficient $\nu$.
4. Apply the equation: Products − Reactants.

#### **Pathway B: From Standard Heats of Combustion ($\Delta H^{\circ}_c$)**

This is **especially important in fire engineering** because most fuels have well-tabulated heats of combustion:

$$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum_{j} \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, reactants} - \sum_{i} \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, products}}$$

Note the **REVERSED sign convention** compared to Pathway A! The combustion of the products is subtracted from the combustion of the reactants because products are already "more oxidized" and release less upon combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Trap:** Students often mix up the sign. Remember the mnemonic: **"COmbustion of COmbustibles minus COmbustion of COmbustion products"** — i.e., reactants (combustibles) − products (already burned).

#### **Pathway C: Hess's Law (Enthalpy is a State Function)**

Since enthalpy ($H$) is a **state function**, the path taken between reactants and products does not affect $\Delta H$. We can construct **Hess cycles** using:
- Formation reactions
- Combustion reactions
- Phase change enthalpies
- Bond dissociation enthalpies

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r}(\text{direct}) = \Delta H^{\circ}_{r}(\text{indirect path})$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, prod} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, react}$ | Heat of reaction from formation enthalpies | kJ/mol |
| 2 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, react} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, prod}$ | Heat of reaction from combustion enthalpies | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_i (BDE_{reactants}) - \sum \nu_j (BDE_{products})$ | From bond dissociation enthalpies (BDE) | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | **Kirchhoff's Law** for non-standard temperature | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p, prod} - \sum \nu_j C_{p, react}$ | Heat capacity difference | J/(mol·K) |
| 6 | $q = n \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{r}$ | Total heat from $n$ moles of reaction | kJ |
| 7 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{n} \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{c}$ | Heat release rate in a fire (fire power) | kW |
| 8 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{element, std. state}) = 0$ | Reference convention for tabulated data | kJ/mol |
| 9 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{compound from elements}) = \Delta H^{\circ}_r$ | Special case: formation is a reaction | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{vap}, \Delta H^{\circ}_{fus}$ | Phase change enthalpies (often appear in Hess cycles) | kJ/mol |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Fire Dynamics

1. **Fire Load Calculation:** The total energy released in a compartment fire is computed by integrating the mass loss rate of the fuel with its $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$. This is critical in **fire protection engineering design** per IS 875 / NFPA standards.
2. **HRR (Heat Release Rate) Curves:** Standard test methods (e.g., **Cone Calorimeter ISO 5660**, **FTT**) report HRR in kW/m² — directly derived from $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ of the burning polymer/fuel.
3. **Explosion Venting Design:** The maximum adiabatic flame temperature is computed using $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ in energy balance equations for vent sizing.
4. **Suppression Agent Selection:** Endothermic decomposition of clean agents (e.g., $\text{CF}_3\text{CHFCF}_3 \rightarrow$ products) absorbs heat, justifying their fire-suppression mechanism.
5. **Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry:** The empirical relation that **~13.1 MJ of heat is released per kg of $\text{O}_2$ consumed** is rooted in standard heats of combustion of typical organics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ from Heats of Formation (First-Principles Proof)

Consider a general balanced reaction:

$$aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are stoichiometric coefficients and $A, B, C, D$ are chemical species.

**Step 1:** The definition of standard heat of reaction is the enthalpy change when $a$ moles of $A$ react with $b$ moles of $B$ to form $c$ moles of $C$ and $d$ moles of $D$, all in their standard states.

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = c H^{\circ}(C) + d H^{\circ}(D) - a H^{\circ}(A) - b H^{\circ}(B)$$

where $H^{\circ}(X)$ denotes the absolute enthalpy of species $X$ in its standard state. **However, absolute enthalpies cannot be measured directly.**

**Step 2:** We define the **standard heat of formation** as the enthalpy change for forming one mole of a compound from its elements in their standard states:

$$\text{Elements (std. state)} \longrightarrow X \quad ; \quad \Delta H = \Delta H^{\circ}_f(X)$$

Therefore, by definition:

$$H^{\circ}(X) = \Delta H^{\circ}_f(X) + \sum_{\text{elements}} \nu_{elem} H^{\circ}(\text{element})$$

Since the standard heat of formation of any element in its standard state is **defined as zero** by IUPAC convention, the absolute enthalpy of a compound equals its heat of formation plus a constant that **cancels out** in any reaction.

**Step 3:** Substituting into the reaction enthalpy expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} &= \big[ c \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(C) + d \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(D) \big] - \big[ a \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(A) + b \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(B) \big] \\
&= \sum_{products} \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,i} - \sum_{reactants} \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∎ Q.E.D.**

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Combustion of Methane (Pathway A)

**Given Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Given Data (Standard Heats of Formation at 298.15 K):**
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{CH}_4(g)] = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{O}_2(g)] = 0 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (element in std. state)
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{CO}_2(g)] = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)] = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify products and reactants with stoichiometric coefficients.

- Products: $1 \times \text{CO}_2$, $2 \times \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
- Reactants: $1 \times \text{CH}_4$, $2 \times \text{O}_2$

**Step 2:** Compute the weighted sum for products.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, products} &= (1)(-393.5) + (2)(-285.8) \\
&= -393.5 + (-571.6) \\
&= -965.1 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the weighted sum for reactants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, reactants} &= (1)(-74.8) + (2)(0) \\
&= -74.8 + 0 \\
&= -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula: Products − Reactants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} &= (-965.1) - (-74.8) \\
&= -965.1 + 74.8 \\
&= -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (strongly exothermic — consistent with textbook value of −890.4 kJ/mol).

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Heat of Reaction from Heats of Combustion (Pathway B)

**Given Reaction:**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6(g) \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$$

This is a dehydrogenation reaction (ethane → ethylene + hydrogen), industrially important in fire-relevant pyrolysis.

**Given Data (Standard Heats of Combustion at 298.15 K):**
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_c[\text{C}_2\text{H}_6(g)] = -1559.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_c[\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(g)] = -1411.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_c[\text{H}_2(g)] = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the reversed-sign formula.

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, reactants} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, products}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the weighted sum for reactants (combustibles).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, reactants} &= (1)(-1559.7) \\
&= -1559.7 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the weighted sum for products (combustion products).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, products} &= (1)(-1411.0) + (1)(-285.8) \\
&= -1696.8 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula: Reactants − Products.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} &= (-1559.7) - (-1696.8) \\
&= -1559.7 + 1696.8 \\
&= +137.1 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{r} = +137.1 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (endothermic — energy must be supplied for dehydrogenation, which is why thermal cracking of ethane requires high temperature).

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Hess's Law via Thermochemical Cycle

**Target Reaction:** Formation of propane from its elements.

$$3\text{C}(s, \text{graphite}) + 4\text{H}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g)$$

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_c[\text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g)] = -2219.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_c[\text{C}(s, \text{graphite})] = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (combustion of graphite to $\text{CO}_2$)
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_c[\text{H}_2(g)] = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{CO}_2(g)] = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)] = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$

**Step 1:** Construct the Hess cycle. The formation reaction is the **reverse of combustion of propane**, balanced with combustion of its elements.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A: } 3\text{C} + 4\text{H}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_8 \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_f \\
\text{Step B: } \text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) \\
\text{Step C: } 3\text{C} + 3\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 \quad 3 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{CO}_2) \\
\text{Step D: } 4\text{H}_2 + 2\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad 4 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{H}_2\text{O})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hess's law: $\text{Step A} + \text{Step B} = \text{Step C} + \text{Step D}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_f + \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) &= 3 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{CO}_2) + 4 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{H}_2\text{O}) \\
\Delta H^{\circ}_f &= 3(-393.5) + 4(-285.8) - (-2219.2) \\
&= -1180.5 - 1143.2 + 2219.2 \\
&= -104.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g)] = -104.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (close to standard tabulated value of −103.8 kJ/mol).

### 3.5 Worked Example 4: Kirchhoff's Law — Non-Standard Temperature

**Problem:** A fire occurs in a kiln at 600 K. The combustion of methane is being analyzed. Given the standard heat of reaction at 298.15 K is −890.3 kJ/mol, find $\Delta H$ at 600 K.

**Given Data:**
- $C_p[\text{CH}_4(g)] = 35.7 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p[\text{O}_2(g)] = 29.4 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p[\text{CO}_2(g)] = 37.1 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p[\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)] = 33.6 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta C_p$ for the reaction $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_p &= \big[ C_p(\text{CO}_2) + 2 C_p(\text{H}_2\text{O}) \big] - \big[ C_p(\text{CH}_4) + 2 C_p(\text{O}_2) \big] \\
&= \big[ 37.1 + 2(33.6) \big] - \big[ 35.7 + 2(29.4) \big] \\
&= \big[ 37.1 + 67.2 \big] - \big[ 35.7 + 58.8 \big] \\
&= 104.3 - 94.5 \\
&= 9.8 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Kirchhoff's Law (assuming constant $C_p$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H(600) &= \Delta H(298.15) + \int_{298.15}^{600} \Delta C_p \, dT \\
&= -890.3 \times 10^3 + (9.8)(600 - 298.15) \\
&= -890300 + (9.8)(301.85) \\
&= -890300 + 2958.13 \\
&= -887341.87 \text{ J/mol} \\
&\approx -887.3 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta H(600 \text{ K}) \approx -887.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$. The reaction is **slightly less exothermic** at higher temperature (because heating the products costs more energy than heating the reactants in this case).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flowchart for KTU Problem-Solving

| Given Quantity | Use This Formula | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of all species | $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,prod} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,react}$ | Forgetting to multiply by $\nu$ |
| $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ of all species | $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,react} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,prod}$ | Wrong sign convention (reversed!) |
| $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ at $T_1$, need at $T_2$ | Kirchhoff's Law | Sign error in $\Delta C_p$ computation |
| Bond energies given | $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum BDE_{broken} - \sum BDE_{formed}$ | Confusing bonds broken vs. formed |
| Hess cycle diagram | Enumerate pathways, sum to zero | Missing intermediate steps |
| Multi-step reaction (e.g., A→B→C) | $\Delta H_{total} = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2$ | Treating it as single-step |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Framework of Standard Heat of Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Standard State Conditions"] --> B["1 bar pressure"]
    A --> C["25 °C / 298.15 K"]
    A --> D["Pure substance"]
    A --> E["Most stable allotrope"]
    F["Chemical Reaction System"] --> G["Reactants in standard state"]
    F --> H["Products in standard state"]
    G --> I["Enthalpy H_reactants"]
    H --> J["Enthalpy H_products"]
    I --> K["Standard Heat of Reaction Calculation"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Pathway A: From Heats of Formation"]
    K --> M["Pathway B: From Heats of Combustion"]
    K --> N["Pathway C: Hess Law Cycle"]
    K --> O["Pathway D: Bond Dissociation Energies"]
    L --> P["Delta H°r result kJ/mol"]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{"Sign Analysis"}
    Q --> R["Negative: Exothermic - Heat Released"]
    Q --> S["Positive: Endothermic - Heat Absorbed"]
    R --> T["Fire Propagation Engine"]
    S --> U["Pyrolysis / Decomposition Zone"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture for Fire Heat Release

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Fuel Input Stream"]
        IN1["Fuel Mass Flow m_dot kg/s"]
        IN2["Standard Heat of Combustion Delta H°c kJ/mol"]
        IN3["Molecular Weight M kg/mol"]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["Computation Block"]
        CMP1["Molar Flow Rate n_dot = m_dot / M mol/s"]
        CMP2["Heat Release Rate Q_dot = n_dot × Delta H°c kW"]
        CMP3["Temperature Correction via Kirchhoff Law"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Fire Dynamics Output"]
        OUT1["HRR in kW - drives fire growth"]
        OUT2["Adiabatic Flame Temperature"]
        OUT3["Smoke Production Rate"]
        OUT4["Vent Sizing Requirement"]
    end
    IN1 --> CMP1
    IN3 --> CMP1
    CMP1 --> CMP2
    IN2 --> CMP2
    CMP2 --> CMP3
    CMP3 --> OUT1
    CMP3 --> OUT2
    CMP3 --> OUT3
    CMP3 --> OUT4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Hess Cycle for Formation of Liquid Water

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Elements in Std State: H2 gas + 0.5 O2 gas"] -->|"Delta H°f H2O liquid = ?"| B["Product: H2O liquid"]
    A -->|"Step 1: 0.5 x Delta H°c H2 = -142.9 kJ"| C["Intermediate: H2O gas"]
    C -->|"Step 2: - Delta H°vap H2O = +44.0 kJ"| B
    A -->|"Step 3: 0.5 x Delta H°c H2 liquid hypothetical = -285.8 kJ"| B
    B --> D["Delta H°f H2O liquid = -285.8 kJ/mol (Verified)"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Energy Level Schematic for Exothermic vs Endothermic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EXO["Exothermic Reaction - Fire"]
        R1["Reactants H = -200 kJ"] -->|"Delta H = -100 kJ"| P1["Products H = -300 kJ"]
    end
    subgraph ENDO["Endothermic Reaction - Decomposition"]
        R2["Reactants H = -300 kJ"] -->|"Delta H = +100 kJ"| P2["Products H = -200 kJ"]
    end
```
