<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**"Define the term 'Standard Heat of Reaction'. State the standard state conditions used in thermochemistry."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Standard Heat of Reaction** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_r$) is defined as the enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction when all the reactants and products are in their **standard states** — the most stable form of the pure substance at **1 bar pressure** and conventionally at a reference temperature of **25 °C (298.15 K)**. It is a state function and depends only on the initial and final states of the system, not on the reaction pathway. The standard state conditions are: pressure = 1 bar (10⁵ Pa), temperature = 25 °C (298.15 K), and the substance must be in its pure, most stable physical form (e.g., graphite for carbon, liquid for water at 25 °C).

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]
**"Distinguish between standard heat of formation and standard heat of combustion with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **standard heat of formation** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_f$) is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of a compound is formed from its **constituent elements in their standard states**. Example: $\text{C}(\text{graphite}) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g)$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

The **standard heat of combustion** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_c$) is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of a substance undergoes **complete combustion with oxygen** under standard state conditions, with products being the most stable oxides (CO₂ for C, H₂O(l) for H, etc.). Example: $\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_c = -890.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Heat of formation measures the bond-making energy from elements, while heat of combustion measures the energy release during total oxidation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / ESE Model Paper]**

**(a)** State and explain **Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation**. How is it applied to determine the standard heat of reaction from standard heats of formation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard heats of combustion of **graphite**, **hydrogen**, and **propane** are −393.5 kJ/mol, −285.8 kJ/mol, and −2219.2 kJ/mol respectively. Calculate the **standard heat of formation of propane** $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g)$. Comment on the stability of propane based on your result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Statement of Hess's Law (2 marks):** Hess's Law states that *"the total enthalpy change in a chemical reaction is independent of the pathway or number of intermediate steps taken, provided the initial and final conditions (temperature, pressure, physical states) are the same."* This is a direct consequence of enthalpy being a **state function**.

**Explanation (3 marks):** Enthalpy $H$ is a state function — its value depends only on the state of the system (T, P, composition, phase), not on the history of how that state was reached. Therefore, the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ between any two states is path-independent. Chemically, this means we can compute $\Delta H$ for a reaction that is difficult to measure directly by constructing a **thermochemical cycle** (Hess cycle) of known steps. The standard heat of reaction from formation enthalpies uses the relationship: $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, prod} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, react}$, which is itself derived by treating formation as a hypothetical multi-step Hess pathway.

**Application (2 marks):** For any reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$, the formation reactions of each species are summed, and by Hess's law applied to a cycle going from elements → products and elements → reactants, the standard heat of reaction equals the difference between product formation enthalpies and reactant formation enthalpies (both weighted by stoichiometric coefficients).

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Write the formation reaction of propane:** (1 mark)

$$3\text{C}(s, \text{graphite}) + 4\text{H}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_f = ?$$

**Step 2 — Write combustion reactions for all species involved:** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) &\longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) + 5\text{O}_2(g) &\longrightarrow 3\text{CO}_2(g) + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c = -2219.2 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Hess cycle:** (2 marks)

The desired reaction (formation of propane) is obtained by:
- Combustion of $3$ moles of graphite → $3\text{CO}_2$
- Combustion of $4$ moles of $\text{H}_2$ → $4\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
- **Reverse** combustion of propane (formation = −combustion)

So: $\Delta H^{\circ}_f = 3 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{C}) + 4 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{H}_2) - \Delta H^{\circ}_c(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8)$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{C}_3\text{H}_8] &= 3(-393.5) + 4(-285.8) - (-2219.2) \\
&= -1180.5 - 1143.2 + 2219.2 \\
&= -104.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on stability:** (Bonus / partial mark)

Since $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ is **negative** (−104.5 kJ/mol), propane is **thermodynamically more stable** than its constituent elements (graphite + hydrogen) at standard conditions. Energy must be supplied to decompose it back into elements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Question A(b):**
> - **Sign error** when applying the "reverse combustion" step: forgetting the minus sign.
> - **Forgetting stoichiometric multipliers**: computing for 1 mole of C and 1 mole of H₂ without scaling to 3 and 4 respectively.
> - **Misidentifying combustion products**: using $\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$ instead of $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ changes the answer by ~44 kJ/mol.
> - **Final stability comment** is worth 1 mark — students often skip it.

---

#### **Question B (Option 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary ESE]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, products} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, reactants}$ using the principle of Hess's Law. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard heat of reaction for the combustion of **liquid ethanol** is given by: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = -1366.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Using the following data, verify the result and comment on the sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ for the **reverse reaction** (decomposition of combustion products back to ethanol): **(7 marks)**

- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l)] = -277.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{CO}_2(g)] = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)] = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{O}_2(g)] = 0 \text{ kJ/mol}$

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Statement of Hess's Law assumption (1 mark):** Enthalpy is a state function; therefore, $\Delta H$ depends only on the initial and final states and is independent of the path.

**Step 2 — Define the formation reaction for a generic species (1 mark):** For any species $X$ composed of elements $E_1, E_2, \ldots$:

$$\nu_1 E_1 + \nu_2 E_2 + \ldots \longrightarrow X \quad \Delta H = \Delta H^{\circ}_f(X)$$

**Step 3 — Consider a general reaction:** (1 mark)

$$aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD$$

**Step 4 — Construct Hess cycle:** (2 marks)

- Path 1 (Direct): $A + B \rightarrow C + D$ with $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$.
- Path 2 (Via elements): $A + B \rightarrow$ elements $\rightarrow C + D$.

The enthalpy of Path 2 equals: $[-\Delta H^{\circ}_f(A) - \Delta H^{\circ}_f(B)] + [\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C) + \Delta H^{\circ}_f(D)]$ (formation reverses for reactants, formation proceeds for products).

**Step 5 — Equate paths by Hess's Law:** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_r &= \big[ \Delta H^{\circ}_f(C) + \Delta H^{\circ}_f(D) \big] - \big[ \Delta H^{\circ}_f(A) + \Delta H^{\circ}_f(B) \big] \\
&= \sum_{products} \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,i} - \sum_{reactants} \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:** (implicit) standard states are well-defined, no non-PV work, ideal behavior.

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify stoichiometry and tabulated values (1 mark):**

- Reactants: 1 mol C₂H₅OH(l), 3 mol O₂(g)
- Products: 2 mol CO₂(g), 3 mol H₂O(l)

**Step 2 — Sum of formation enthalpies of products (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, products} &= 2(-393.5) + 3(-285.8) \\
&= -787.0 - 857.4 \\
&= -1644.4 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of formation enthalpies of reactants (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, reactants} &= 1(-277.0) + 3(0) \\
&= -277.0 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula and verify (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^{\circ}_r &= (-1644.4) - (-277.0) \\
&= -1644.4 + 277.0 \\
&= -1367.4 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is in excellent agreement with the given value of −1366.8 kJ/mol (small difference of 0.6 kJ/mol due to rounding).

**Step 5 — Comment on the reverse reaction (1 mark):**

For the reverse reaction $2\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\text{O}_2(g)$:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{r, reverse} = +1367.4 \text{ kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

The reverse reaction is **strongly endothermic**, requiring continuous energy input. This is why combustion products do not spontaneously revert to fuel — making combustion essentially **irreversible** under standard conditions, which has critical implications for fire extinguishment (you cannot "un-burn" the fuel without enormous energy).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Question B:**
> - Students frequently **omit stoichiometric coefficients** when summing, leading to ~30% numerical error.
> - Forgetting that $\Delta H^{\circ}_f[\text{O}_2(g)] = 0$ leads to confusion.
> - **Sign convention for the reverse reaction** is often inverted in the comment.
> - The "verification" step should explicitly **compare both numbers** with a tolerance statement; not just compute and stop.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Standard Heat of Reaction**

- [x] **Definition:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_r$ = enthalpy change at 1 bar, 25 °C (298.15 K), all species in standard states.
- [x] **Standard State ≠ STP:** Standard state is a pressure reference (1 bar), not the IUPAC STP (which is 100 kPa for gas density).
- [x] **Key Formula 1:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, products} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{f, reactants}$ (from heats of **formation**).
- [x] **Key Formula 2:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_r = \sum \nu_j \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, reactants} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H^{\circ}_{c, products}$ (from heats of **combustion** — sign is REVERSED).
- [x] **Key Formula 3:** Kirchhoff's Law — $\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$ for non-standard temperatures.
- [x] **Reference Convention:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_f(\text{element in standard state}) = 0$ — graphite for C, $\text{O}_2$ for O, $\text{N}_2$ for N, $\text{H}_2$ for H, etc.
- [x] **Hess's Law:** Enthalpy is a state function → $\Delta H$ is path-independent → enables thermochemical cycles.
- [x] **Fire Significance:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ directly drives **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** in fire dynamics; $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ relates to **fuel stability** and pyrolysis onset.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Negative = exothermic (fire/combustion); Positive = endothermic (decomposition/pyrolysis).
- [x] **Units:** All $\Delta H^{\circ}$ values in **kJ/mol** (or kcal/mol in older texts); multiply by moles for total heat $q = n \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_r$.
- [x] **Combustion Products Rule:** Heat of combustion assumes products are $\text{CO}_2(g)$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, $\text{SO}_2(g)$, $\text{N}_2(g)$, HCl$(g)$, etc. — most stable oxides under standard state.
- [x] **Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry:** ~13.1 MJ/kg of O₂ consumed (empirical constant derived from $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ of typical organics) — used in cone calorimeter data reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxygen Calorimetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oxygen Calorimetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Oxygen Calorimetry** is an *indirect* calorimetric technique used in fire engineering to determine the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of a burning material or fire, based on the principle that the amount of heat released during the complete combustion of an organic fuel is directly proportional to the **mass of oxygen consumed** from the combustion atmosphere.

The technique was formalized by **Clayton Huggett (1980)** at NIST (formerly NBS), who established that for a wide range of organic fuels (hydrocarbons, polymers, woods, alcohols, etc.), the heat released per kilogram of oxygen consumed is essentially constant:

$$E \approx 13.1 \ \text{MJ/kg of O}_2 \quad (\pm \ 5\%)$$

This principle is now known as **Huggett's Relation** (or the *Oxygen Consumption Principle*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Board Expectation):** In KTU ESE answers, always state Huggett's principle with the value **13.1 MJ/kg O₂** and explicitly mention that the technique is *independent of the chemical nature of the fuel*, which is its greatest engineering advantage.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef cooking a steak, a fish, and a chicken in a closed kitchen. You cannot measure the heat produced by each dish directly (no thermometer in the flame), but you have a *spy* measuring how much oxygen is being drawn out of the room by the exhaust hood.

Oxygen Calorimetry works exactly the same way:

- 🔥 **The fire** is the "cooking process."
- 💨 **The exhaust duct** measures the air coming in and going out.
- 🧪 **Oxygen sensors** act like the "spy" — they measure how much O₂ disappears.
- 📐 **A constant multiplier (13.1)** converts the missing O₂ directly into heat energy, regardless of *what* is burning.

So, if **1 kg of oxygen disappears** during combustion, we can confidently claim that roughly **13.1 MJ of heat** was released — whether the fuel was gasoline, wood, plastic, or methanol. This universal "translator" is what makes oxygen calorimetry the **gold standard** of modern fire testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The reason this principle works universally is that the *stoichiometric oxygen demand* of a fuel is tightly coupled to its chemical energy. Bond-breaking in hydrocarbons requires roughly the same number of O₂ molecules per unit energy released, regardless of the carbon chain length.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Oxygen consumption constant, **E** | **13.1 MJ/kg O₂** |
| Oxygen mass fraction in air, **X₀O₂** | **0.232** (or 23.2%) |
| Molecular weight of O₂ | **32 g/mol** |
| Molecular weight of air | **28.97 g/mol** |
| Combustion air stoichiometric O₂ | 21% by volume (in air) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does the Principle Hold?

The oxygen consumption principle rests on the thermodynamic observation that for complete combustion of organic compounds, the **heat of combustion per unit mass of O₂ consumed is approximately constant** for most fuels.

### Combustion Reactions (Generalized)

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧOᵤNᵥClᵥFᵥ:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_u\text{N}_v\text{Cl}_w\text{F}_z + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O} + v\text{NO}_x + w\text{HCl} + z\text{HF} + \text{Heat}$$

For the canonical methane combustion:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -802 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Per kg of O₂ consumed: (802 kJ/mol) / (2 × 0.032 kg) = 12,531 kJ/kg ≈ 12.5 MJ/kg

For octane (C₈H₁₈):

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 8\text{CO}_2 + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -5,470 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Per kg of O₂: 5,470 / (12.5 × 0.032) = 13,675 kJ/kg ≈ 13.7 MJ/kg

The **average across fuels clusters around 13.1 MJ/kg O₂**, validating Huggett's empirical finding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Validity:** The 13.1 MJ/kg value holds ONLY for **complete combustion of organic fuels**. It fails for:
> - Incomplete combustion (soot, CO formation)
> - Pure metals (e.g., magnesium, aluminum)
> - Inorganic compounds (e.g., silicones, certain polymers)
> - Explosives and propellants

## 2.2 Operating Principle of an Oxygen Calorimeter

An oxygen consumption calorimeter is essentially a **controlled exhaust duct system** with the following operational logic:

1. **Specimen burns** under a conical heater (in cone calorimeter) or within an enclosure.
2. **All combustion products** are captured and drawn through an exhaust duct.
3. **Mass flow rate of exhaust** $\dot{m}_e$ is measured (via orifice plate, bidirectional probe, or Pitot tube).
4. **Oxygen concentration** in the exhaust ($X_{O_2}$) is continuously measured by a **paramagnetic or zirconia sensor**.
5. **The difference** between inlet (ambient) O₂ concentration ($X_{O_2}^0 \approx 0.232$) and exhaust O₂ concentration is used to compute oxygen consumption.
6. **HRR is calculated** in real time using the 13.1 MJ/kg constant.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of Oxygen Calorimetry |
|---|---|
| **Building Material Testing** | Cone calorimeter (ISO 5660) for HRR of wall linings, cables, insulation |
| **Fire Investigation** | Reconstruction of fire development using measured HRR curves |
| **Fire Modeling Validation** | Input data for CFD models (FDS, CFAST) |
| **Consumer Product Safety** | Upholstery, mattresses, electronics flammability |
| **Aircraft & Ship Fire Safety** | OSU calorimeter (FAR 25.853), RHR per unit area |
| **Polymer Industry** | Research & development of fire-retardant materials |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the exact set of equations KTU examiners expect for an Oxygen Calorimetry problem.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}$ | Heat Release Rate (fundamental) | kW |
| 2 | $E = 13.1 \times 10^3$ | Oxygen consumption constant | kJ/kg O₂ |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{O_2} = \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}}$ | Oxygen mass consumption from exhaust analysis | kg/s |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_e = \dfrac{C \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}}{\sqrt{T_e}}$ | Exhaust mass flow rate (orifice equation) | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot (X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2})}{1 - X_{O_2}}$ | Working HRR equation (combined) | kW |
| 6 | $\text{THR} = \displaystyle\int_0^{t_f} \dot{Q}(t)\, dt$ | Total Heat Released (integral of HRR) | kJ |
| 7 | $\text{FIGRA} = \dfrac{\text{HRR}_{\text{peak}}}{t_{\text{peak}}}$ | Fire Growth Rate index (classification metric) | kW/s |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}^{\prime\prime} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{A_s}$ | HRR per unit sample area (cone calorimeter) | kW/m² |
| 9 | $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$ | Initial O₂ mass fraction in ambient air | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{SEA} = \dfrac{\text{Smoke mass}}{\dot{Q} \cdot \Delta t}$ | Specific Extinction Area (smoke yield) | m²/kg |

### Variable Glossary

- $\dot{Q}$ — Heat Release Rate (kW or MW)
- $E$ — Oxygen consumption constant (= 13.1 MJ/kg O₂)
- $\dot{m}_{O_2}$ — Mass flow rate of oxygen consumed (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_e$ — Mass flow rate of exhaust gases (kg/s)
- $X_{O_2}^0$ — Initial oxygen mass fraction (0.232)
- $X_{O_2}$ — Oxygen mass fraction in exhaust duct
- $A_s$ — Exposed surface area of specimen (m²)
- $\Delta P$ — Pressure differential across orifice plate (Pa)
- $T_e$ — Absolute temperature of exhaust gas (K)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From First Principles to the Working HRR Equation

We will derive the canonical **oxygen consumption calorimetry equation** used in ISO 5660 (Cone Calorimeter) and ASTM E1354.

### Step 1 — Conservation of Mass in the Exhaust Duct

The exhaust gas consists of air minus the **consumed oxygen** plus **combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, soot)**. By the principle of mass conservation in a steady flow:

$$\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_a - \dot{m}_{O_2}^{c} + \dot{m}_{products}$$

However, by the experimental simplification of Huggett, the mass flow of gases (excluding the consumed O₂) is approximately conserved through the duct when expressed on a dry basis. Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_e (1 - X_{O_2}) = \dot{m}_a (1 - X_{O_2}^0)$$

### Step 2 — Oxygen Mass Balance

The mass of oxygen entering = mass of oxygen leaving + mass of oxygen consumed by combustion.

$$\dot{m}_a \cdot X_{O_2}^0 = \dot{m}_e \cdot X_{O_2} + \dot{m}_{O_2}^{c}$$

Rearranging for the consumed oxygen:

$$\dot{m}_{O_2}^{c} = \dot{m}_a \cdot X_{O_2}^0 - \dot{m}_e \cdot X_{O_2}$$

### Step 3 — Eliminate $\dot{m}_a$ Using Conservation

From the conservation equation in Step 1:

$$\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{(1 - X_{O_2})}{(1 - X_{O_2}^0)}$$

Substituting into the oxygen balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{O_2}^{c} &= \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{(1 - X_{O_2})}{(1 - X_{O_2}^0)} \cdot X_{O_2}^0 - \dot{m}_e \cdot X_{O_2} \\[6pt]
&= \dot{m}_e \left[ \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 (1 - X_{O_2}) - X_{O_2}(1 - X_{O_2}^0)}{1 - X_{O_2}^0} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \dot{m}_e \left[ \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}^0 X_{O_2} - X_{O_2} + X_{O_2} X_{O_2}^0}{1 - X_{O_2}^0} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \dot{m}_e \left[ \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}^0} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For most engineering calculations, $X_{O_2}^0 \ll 1$ is small, and to a good approximation, $1 - X_{O_2}^0 \approx 1 - X_{O_2}$, yielding the **standard KTU-form expression**:

$$\dot{m}_{O_2}^{c} \approx \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Huggett's Principle

The total heat release rate is the mass of oxygen consumed multiplied by Huggett's constant:

$$\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}^{c}$$

Substituting the expression for $\dot{m}_{O_2}^{c}$:

$$\boxed{\ \dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}}\ }$$

This is the **master equation** of oxygen consumption calorimetry as taught in KTU.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A polymer specimen is tested in a cone calorimeter. The exhaust duct mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_e = 0.024$ kg/s. The oxygen analyzer reads $X_{O_2} = 0.205$ in the exhaust duct. The ambient oxygen mass fraction is $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$. Determine the heat release rate and the total heat released over a 60-second test period. Also calculate the HRR per unit area, given the specimen surface area $A_s = 0.01$ m².

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify the knowns.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_e &= 0.024 \ \text{kg/s} \\
X_{O_2}^0 &= 0.232 \\
X_{O_2} &= 0.205 \\
E &= 13.1 \times 10^3 \ \text{kJ/kg O}_2 \\
t &= 60 \ \text{s} \\
A_s &= 0.01 \ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the oxygen mass fraction difference.**

$$\Delta X = X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2} = 0.232 - 0.205 = 0.027$$

**Step 3 — Apply the working HRR equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \frac{\Delta X}{1 - X_{O_2}} \\[6pt]
&= (13.1 \times 10^3) \cdot (0.024) \cdot \frac{0.027}{1 - 0.205} \\[6pt]
&= (13.1 \times 10^3) \cdot (0.024) \cdot \frac{0.027}{0.795} \\[6pt]
&= 314.4 \cdot 0.03396 \\[6pt]
&= 10.68 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Total Heat Released (THR).**

Assuming steady HRR over the test period:

$$\text{THR} = \dot{Q} \cdot t = 10.68 \ \text{kW} \times 60 \ \text{s} = 640.8 \ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Compute HRR per unit area.**

$$\dot{Q}^{\prime\prime} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{A_s} = \frac{10.68}{0.01} = 1{,}068 \ \text{kW/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A value of $\dot{Q}^{\prime\prime} \approx 1{,}000$ kW/m² corresponds to a **fully developed room fire** in the post-flashover regime, indicating this polymer specimen has a high fire hazard rating.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Energy Density Comparisons

| Fuel | $\Delta H_c$ (MJ/kg) | E (MJ/kg O₂) | Deviation from 13.1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) | 50.0 | 12.54 | −4.3% |
| Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | 26.9 | 13.21 | +0.8% |
| n-Heptane | 44.4 | 12.68 | −3.2% |
| Methanol | 19.9 | 13.27 | +1.3% |
| Polyethylene | 43.6 | 13.65 | +4.2% |
| Polystyrene | 39.2 | 13.32 | +1.7% |
| PMMA (Plexiglass) | 25.2 | 13.00 | −0.8% |
| Cellulose (paper) | 16.2 | 13.35 | +1.9% |
| **Average** | — | **~13.1** | **~0%** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Oxygen Consumption Calorimeter

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **architectural flow** of a standard oxygen calorimeter test setup (e.g., ISO 5660 Cone Calorimeter). This satisfies the KTU 2024 requirement for schematic visualization of fire testing apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Holder 100mm x 100mm] --> B[Conical Heater 0 to 50 kW m2]
    B --> C[Spark Igniter]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber]
    D --> E[Exhaust Hood Capture]
    E --> F[Exhaust Duct Flow Section]
    F --> G[Orifice Plate Flow Meter]
    G --> H[Paramagnetic O2 Analyzer]
    G --> I[NDIR CO2 Analyzer]
    G --> J[Smoke Obscuration Laser]
    H --> K[Data Acquisition System]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Real time HRR Calculator]
    L --> M[Output HRR THR Time Curves]
    M --> N[Engineering Classification FIGRA MARHE]

    P[Ambient Air Supply] --> Q[Blower Fan]
    Q --> F
    R[Thermocouples in Duct] --> K
```

## 4.2 Step-by-Step Data Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Raw XO2 Exhaust Signal] --> S2[O2 Depletion Computation]
    S3[Raw Delta P from Orifice] --> S4[Mass Flow Calculation]
    S2 --> S5[Multiply by Huggett Constant E 13.1]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[HRR Value in kW]
    S6 --> S7[Time Integration yields THR]
    S6 --> S8[Divide by Area yields HRR per m2]
    S7 --> S9[Final Fire Performance Report]
    S8 --> S9
```

## 4.3 Apparatus Subsystem Summary (Engineering Description)

| Subsystem | Function | Typical Sensor / Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Conical Heater** | Provides controlled radiant flux on specimen (0–100 kW/m²) | Electrical resistance coil with shutter |
| **Spark Igniter** | Initiates piloted ignition | 10 kV spark, located above specimen |
| **Exhaust Hood** | Captures all smoke and combustion products | Steel hood with collection duct |
| **Flow Meter** | Measures exhaust mass flow rate | Orifice plate with differential pressure transducer |
| **O₂ Analyzer** | Measures oxygen depletion | Paramagnetic (Servomex) or Zirconia cell |
| **CO/CO₂ Analyzers** | Quantify toxic/combustion gas yields | Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) |
| **Smoke Measurement** | Measures light obscuration due to soot | He-Ne laser + photodiode |
| **Data Logger** | Records all signals at 1 Hz (or faster) | Computer with dedicated DAQ software |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note for KTU students:** The **paramagnetic oxygen sensor** is the most common in modern calorimeters because O₂ is strongly paramagnetic compared to other gases, giving a highly selective and accurate measurement with response times of < 5 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]
**State and explain the Oxygen Consumption Principle. Why is it considered the most reliable method for measuring fire Heat Release Rate?**

**Model Answer:**

The Oxygen Consumption Principle, formalized by **Huggett (1980)**, states that for a wide variety of organic fuels undergoing complete combustion, the heat released per unit mass of oxygen consumed is approximately constant at **E ≈ 13.1 MJ/kg of O₂ (± 5%)**.

It is considered the most reliable HRR measurement method because:

1. **Fuel independence:** The constant 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ is valid across most organic fuels (plastics, woods, liquids, gases) — so we do not need to know the chemical composition of the burning material in advance.
2. **Measurement simplicity:** We only need to measure oxygen concentration in the exhaust — which can be done accurately with paramagnetic analyzers.
3. **Complete mass-energy balance:** All combustion phenomena (flaming, smoldering, vitiated combustion) are captured in the oxygen depletion signal.
4. **Standards compatibility:** Used globally under ISO 5660, ASTM E1354, and ISO 9705.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**A specimen in a cone calorimeter test shows an oxygen mass fraction of 0.198 in the exhaust duct. The duct mass flow rate is 0.03 kg/s. Calculate the HRR, taking $E = 13.1 \times 10^3$ kJ/kg O₂ and $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $\dot{m}_e = 0.03$ kg/s, $X_{O_2} = 0.198$, $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$, $E = 13.1 \times 10^3$ kJ/kg O₂.

**Step 1:** Compute the oxygen mass fraction drop.

$$\Delta X_{O_2} = X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2} = 0.232 - 0.198 = 0.034$$

**Step 2:** Apply the working HRR equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \frac{\Delta X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}} \\[6pt]
&= (13.1 \times 10^3)(0.03)\left(\frac{0.034}{0.802}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 393 \times 0.04239 \\[6pt]
&= 16.66 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\dot{Q} = 16.66$ kW.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 2 Part B has internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Full Module 2 Choice]

**(a)** State and explain **Huggett's Oxygen Consumption Principle** with its thermodynamic basis. Discuss why the heat of combustion per kg of O₂ is approximately constant (≈ 13.1 MJ/kg) for most organic fuels. Give at least two fuel examples with their corresponding E values. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **cone calorimeter** based on oxygen consumption. Derive the standard equation for HRR as a function of exhaust oxygen mass fraction, mass flow rate, and Huggett's constant. **[7 Marks]**

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### **Part (a) — Huggett's Principle (7 Marks)**

**Statement:** Huggett (1980) established empirically that for complete combustion of virtually all organic fuels, the heat released per unit mass of oxygen consumed is approximately **constant at 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ (± 5%)**.

**Thermodynamic Basis:**

For complete combustion of a generic fuel CₓHᵧOᵤ, the stoichiometric equation is:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_u + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{u}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The heat released per mole of O₂ is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\nu_{O_2}}$$

For different fuels, both $\Delta H_c$ and $\nu_{O_2}$ change, but their *ratio* remains nearly constant because the energy released per C–C, C–H bond broken is similar across organic compounds (≈ 400–420 kJ/mol of bond energy, with O₂ providing similar oxidizing power per molecule).

**Examples:**

| Fuel | $\Delta H_c$ (MJ/kg) | E = $\Delta H_c \cdot \nu_{O_2}/M_{O_2}$ (MJ/kg O₂) |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | 50.0 | 12.54 |
| PMMA | 25.2 | 13.00 |
| Polystyrene | 39.2 | 13.32 |

All values cluster near **13.1 MJ/kg O₂**, confirming Huggett's principle.

**Significance:** This constancy makes oxygen consumption calorimetry *fuel-independent* — we do not need to know what is burning to measure HRR accurately.

#### **Part (b) — Cone Calorimeter Working + Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4 Mermaid Diagram)**

**Working:**
1. A 100 mm × 100 mm specimen is exposed to a conical electric heater (radiant flux 0–100 kW/m²).
2. A spark igniter triggers flaming combustion.
3. All combustion products are captured by a hood and drawn through an exhaust duct.
4. The duct contains an orifice plate (for flow rate) and a paramagnetic oxygen analyzer.
5. Oxygen depletion is continuously recorded, and HRR is calculated in real time.

**Derivation:**

From oxygen mass balance on the exhaust duct:

$$\dot{m}_{O_2}^{c} = \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}}$$

Applying Huggett's principle:

$$\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}^{c} = E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}}$$

This is the **standard ISO 5660 / ASTM E1354 equation** used in the cone calorimeter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Alternative Question]

**(a)** A medium-scale oxygen consumption calorimeter is used to test a furniture mock-up. The following data is recorded:

- Exhaust duct mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_e = 0.36$ kg/s
- Initial oxygen mass fraction: $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$
- Exhaust oxygen mass fraction (average over 5 min): $X_{O_2} = 0.187$
- Test duration: $t = 300$ s

Calculate:
  (i) The average heat release rate (HRR) in kW
  (ii) The total heat released (THR) in MJ
  (iii) The corresponding fuel mass loss, if the effective heat of combustion of the polyurethane foam is 28 kJ/g **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations** of the oxygen consumption principle. In which situations does it fail? How are these limitations addressed in modified calorimetry standards (e.g., ISO 9705, ANSI/NFPA 265)? **[7 Marks]**

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### **Part (a) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
$\dot{m}_e = 0.36$ kg/s, $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$, $X_{O_2} = 0.187$, $t = 300$ s, $E = 13.1 \times 10^3$ kJ/kg O₂, $\Delta H_c^{\text{eff}} = 28$ kJ/g = 28,000 kJ/kg.

**(i) Average HRR:**

$$\Delta X = 0.232 - 0.187 = 0.045$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \frac{\Delta X}{1 - X_{O_2}} \\[6pt]
&= (13.1 \times 10^3)(0.36) \left(\frac{0.045}{0.813}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 4{,}716 \times 0.05535 \\[6pt]
&= 261.0 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Heat Released:**

$$\text{THR} = \dot{Q} \cdot t = 261.0 \times 300 = 78{,}300 \ \text{kJ} = 78.3 \ \text{MJ}$$

**(iii) Fuel mass loss:**

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{\text{THR}}{\Delta H_c^{\text{eff}}} = \frac{78{,}300}{28{,}000} = 2.80 \ \text{kg}$$

#### **Part (b) — Limitations (7 Marks)**

**Limitations of Oxygen Consumption Principle:**

1. **Incomplete Combustion:** When significant CO is produced, the effective E value changes (CO formation reduces heat release per O₂ consumed). The 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ is reduced to ~12.3 MJ/kg for fuels where 10% carbon is converted to CO.
2. **Metals and Inorganic Fuels:** Magnesium, aluminum, and other metals release heat via non-oxygen-dependent reactions (e.g., reduction of metal oxides).
3. **Vitiated Combustion:** When $X_{O_2}$ drops below ~15% in the test enclosure, the constant breaks down due to oxygen-limited reaction kinetics.
4. **High Soot Production:** Soot formation changes the apparent oxygen consumption without releasing equivalent heat energy.
5. **Humidity Effects:** Water vapor in the combustion products can interfere with O₂ sensor readings unless compensation is applied.

**How Standards Address These Issues:**

- **ISO 9705 (Room/Corner Test):** Uses a full-scale room with a propane burner as a **reference**. Both test specimen and reference burner exhaust are analyzed, and the HRR is calibrated by comparison.
- **ANSI/NFPA 265:** Specifies correction factors for CO and soot yields in calculating HRR.
- **Modern software** (e.g., ConeTools, FTISC) applies the **Huggett correction factor for CO formation** automatically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to **convert the oxygen consumption constant** properly. E = 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ = **13,100 kJ/kg O₂**, not 13.1 kJ/kg.
> - The denominator in the working equation is **$1 - X_{O_2}$** (the exhaust oxygen fraction), not $1 - X_{O_2}^0$ — this is a frequent error in derivations.
> - When stating Huggett's principle, ALWAYS mention the **±5% tolerance** — failing to do so loses 1 mark in KTU evaluations.
> - For incomplete combustion, the effective E value is **lower** than 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ — do not blindly use 13.1 in CO-rich scenarios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam)**

- ✅ **Huggett's Principle (1980):** E ≈ **13.1 MJ/kg O₂** for complete combustion of most organic fuels.
- ✅ **Universal constant:** Works for ~80% of combustible materials, **independent of fuel chemistry**.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \dfrac{X_{O_2}^0 - X_{O_2}}{1 - X_{O_2}}$
- ✅ **Ambient O₂ mass fraction:** $X_{O_2}^0 = 0.232$ (always use this unless specified).
- ✅ **Denominator trap:** Always use $1 - X_{O_2}$ (exhaust), not $1 - X_{O_2}^0$.
- ✅ **O₂ sensor type:** Paramagnetic — preferred in modern calorimeters.
- ✅ **Standards:** ISO 5660 (cone), ISO 9705 (room corner), ASTM E1354, NFPA 265.
- ✅ **THR** = $\int \dot{Q} \, dt$ (area under HRR curve).
- ✅ **FIGRA** = HRR_peak / t_peak (used in Euroclass classification).
- ✅ **Limitations:** Incomplete combustion, metals, vitiated air, soot, humidity.
- ✅ **Conversion:** 13.1 MJ/kg = 13,100 kJ/kg (mind the unit prefix).
- ✅ **Cone calorimeter specimen:** 100 mm × 100 mm; radiant flux 0–100 kW/m².
- ✅ **Equation derivation origin:** Mass balance + Huggett's empirical constant.
- ✅ **Cone calorimeter HRR per unit area:** Divide total HRR by 0.01 m² (specimen area).
- ✅ **Historic note:** Thornton (1917) first observed the principle; Huggett (1980) formalized it for fire testing.

> **End of Notes — Oxygen Calorimetry | PESFT528 | Module 2**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Calorimetry** is an experimental fire-test methodology that quantifies the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of a burning specimen by continuously measuring the **mass flow rate of combustion products** and the **mole (or mass) fractions of O₂ and CO₂** in the exhaust duct of an enclosed combustion hood. It is a sub-class of the broader family of **Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry** techniques and is governed internationally by standards such as **ISO 5660** (Cone Calorimeter) and **ISO 9705** (Room Corner / Full-Scale Room Test).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Scheme, PESFT528-Module 2 requires the student to derive the governing energy-balance equation for CO₂ calorimetry, identify each measured parameter, and explain the physical basis of **Thornton's Rule** (≈ 13.1 MJ of energy released per kg of O₂ consumed).

The fundamental working premise is that for **complete combustion of virtually all organic fuels (C, H, O, N, S containing)**, the net heat of complete combustion per unit mass of oxygen consumed is remarkably constant. This empirical observation, first quantified by **W.M. Thornton in 1917** and later refined by **C. Huggett (1980)**, eliminates the need to know the exact chemical composition of the burning material in order to compute its HRR.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a stove burning wood. The wood does not "measure" its own heat — but if you could:
1. **Measure** how much fresh air (oxygen) is being sucked into the chimney each second,
2. **Measure** how much oxygen comes *out* the chimney (it should be less, because combustion ate it),
3. **Measure** how much CO₂ comes out (it should be more, because combustion produced it),

then the difference tells you how fast fuel is being burned. Because every kilogram of "oxygen eaten" by a flame always releases **roughly the same amount of energy** (about 13.1 MJ, equivalent to the energy in ~330 mL of petrol), you do not even need to know *what* the fuel is — wood, plastic, or polyurethane foam all behave almost identically. That is the genius of CO₂/O₂ calorimetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "CO₂ Calorimetry" if O₂ is the main measurement?**
> Because the technique was originally developed by simultaneously tracking both **O₂ depletion** and **CO₂ generation** in the duct; modern codes (e.g., ASTM E1354) require the CO₂ signal as a cross-check for ventilation, vitiation, and combustion efficiency. Hence the dual terminology is retained in KTU Module 2.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat released per kg O₂ (Thornton's constant) | E | **13.1 × 10³** | kJ/kg |
| Huggett's universal constant (alternative form) | E | 13.1 | MJ/kg |
| Ambient O₂ mole fraction (dry air) | X_{O₂,a} | 0.2095 | — |
| Ambient O₂ mass fraction | Y_{O₂,a} | 0.232 | — |
| Ambient N₂ mole fraction | X_{N₂,a} | 0.7808 | — |
| Molecular mass of O₂ | M_{O₂} | 32 | g/mol |
| Molecular mass of N₂ | M_{N₂} | 28 | g/mol |
| Molecular mass of air | M_a | 28.97 | g/mol |
| Molecular mass of CO₂ | M_{CO₂} | 44 | g/mol |
| Molecular mass of H₂O | M_{H₂O} | 18 | g/mol |
| Standard density of air (15 °C, 101.325 kPa) | ρ_a | 1.225 | kg/m³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical & Chemical Premise

When a hydrocarbon fuel C\_x H\_y O\_z N\_w S\_v burns completely, the global reaction is:

$$C_xH_yO_zN_wS_v + \nu_{O_2}\,O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{w}{2}\,N_2 + v\,SO_2$$

The **heat of combustion per mole of O₂** is, for any such stoichiometrically balanced reaction, within ±5 % of a universal value. Thornton (1917) and Huggett (1980) demonstrated this constancy by computing ΔH\_c for ~80 common fuels and observing that the standard deviation is below 5 % of the mean. This is the *empirical cornerstone* of every oxygen-consumption calorimeter in use today, including the **cone calorimeter, room calorimeter, and furniture calorimeter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Percent Rule:** If the fuel contains significant halogens (Cl, F, Br) or large ash fractions, the constant E deviates. A correction factor (Φ) is introduced in the KTU governing equation to account for incomplete combustion (soot, CO, unburnt hydrocarbons).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Air Extraction:** A blower draws the entire fire plume through an extraction hood and into a circular exhaust duct of known cross-section (typically 0.25 – 0.4 m diameter).

**Step 2 — Flow Measurement:** A **bi-directional probe** or **orifice plate** mounted in the duct measures the **duct mass flow rate** m\_dot\_e (kg/s). The signal is typically linearised to a 0–10 V analog output.

**Step 3 — Gas Sampling:** A portion of the exhaust is drawn through a water trap (to remove moisture), a particulate filter, and a pump into the analyser rack. Sample conditioning ensures the gas is dry and at constant temperature/pressure.

**Step 4 — Gas Analysis:** Two analysers run in parallel:
* **O₂ Analyser** — *paramagnetic* or *zirconia-cell* type, range 0–25 % O₂, accuracy ±0.01 %.
* **CO₂ Analyser** — *Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)*, range 0–10 % CO₂, accuracy ±0.02 %.

**Step 5 — Smoke Measurement (Optional but Standard):** A *laser photometer* (wavelength 632.8 nm, He-Ne source) measures the **specific extinction area k\_s** for the smoke yield calculation — not used in the HRR equation directly.

**Step 6 — HRR Computation:** The raw analyser signals are fed to a data acquisition system (≥ 1 Hz). The governing Huggett equation (Section 3) is applied in real time and the trace is plotted as HRR (kW) vs. time (s).

**Step 7 — Integration:** Total Heat Released (THR) in kJ/m² is obtained by integrating the HRR curve over the test duration.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Release Rate (Huggett) | $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2,\,consumed}$ | kW |
| 2 | Universal Thornton's constant | $E = 13.1 \times 10^3$ | kJ/kg O₂ |
| 3 | Oxygen depletion form | $\dot{Q} = E \,\dot{m}_e \,\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{1 + \Phi}$ | kW |
| 4 | Equivalence ratio (oxygen) | $\Phi = \frac{\Delta H_{O_2}}{\Delta H_c}\cdot\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{X_{O_2,e}}$ | — |
| 5 | Stoichiometric O₂ factor | $\Delta H_{O_2} = \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\nu_{O_2}\,M_{O_2}}$ | kJ/kg O₂ |
| 6 | Total heat released | $\text{THR} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_f} \dot{Q}(t)\,dt$ | kJ/m² |
| 7 | Effective heat of combustion | $\Delta h_{c,eff} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_f}$ | MJ/kg |
| 8 | Mass loss rate (sample) | $\dot{m}_f = -\dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | kg/(m²·s) |
| 9 | Specific extinction area (smoke) | $k_s = \dfrac{1}{L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)\cdot\dfrac{1}{\rho_s}$ | m²/kg |
| 10 | Exhaust mass flow | $\dot{m}_e = C_d A\sqrt{2\rho_a\,\Delta p}$ | kg/s |
| 11 | Duct velocity (Pitot) | $v_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta p}{\rho_e}}$ | m/s |
| 12 | CO₂ generation rate | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dot{m}_e\,Y_{CO_2,e}$ | kg/s |
| 13 | Air-to-fuel stoichiometric ratio | $r_0 = \dfrac{\nu_{O_2}\,32}{M_f}$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 14 | Heat release per kg fuel burnt | $q'' = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{A_s}$ | kW/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU board papers, the examiner routinely tests the "plug-flow assumption":** gas composition is uniform across the duct cross-section and analyser transport lag is corrected by a time-shift of 5–15 s depending on duct length.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Fire-retardant material certification:** Cone calorimeter (50 kW/m²) data feed FM Global / UL listings of cables, composites, and polymers.
* **Performance-based design (PBD):** Full-scale room tests (ISO 9705, 100/300/600 kW burners) yield **HRR vs. time** curves that drive CFD fire simulations (FDS, PyroSim) for atria, tunnels, and high-rise design.
* **Forensic fire investigation:** Reproduction of an HRR trace allows investigators to pinpoint the first-item-ignited (FII) and reconstruct the timeline.
* **Ship & aerospace certification:** IMO Resolution A.800(19) and FAA aircraft cabin fire tests use oxygen-consumption calorimetry as the *primary* HRR metric.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Huggett Governing Equation (Full KTU Rigor)

We start from an **energy balance on the control volume** bounded by the exhaust hood and the duct cross-section where measurements are taken.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance on O₂

Mass flow of O₂ entering the duct (in fresh air entrained) = Mass flow of O₂ leaving (in exhaust products) + Mass flow of O₂ consumed by combustion.

$$
\dot{m}_a\,Y_{O_2,a} = \dot{m}_e\,Y_{O_2,e} + \dot{m}_{O_2,\,c}
$$

Rearranging for the oxygen consumption rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{O_2,\,c} = \dot{m}_a\,Y_{O_2,a} - \dot{m}_e\,Y_{O_2,e}
$$

**Conversion logic:** In a properly designed calorimeter, virtually all air enters as fresh (ambient) entrained air, so Y\_{O₂,a} = 0.232 (mass fraction of O₂ in dry air). The analyser returns the **dry-basis** mole fraction; conversion to mass fraction uses M\_{O₂}/M\_e where M\_e is the mean molecular mass of the dry exhaust.

### Step 2 — Energy Balance on the Control Volume

The chemical energy released by the fire = sensible enthalpy gained by combustion products (above reference) − sensible enthalpy lost when ambient air is heated (and any convective/radiative heat lost through the walls, lumped into a heat-loss term \dot{Q}_L).

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_e\,h_e - \dot{m}_a\,h_a + \dot{Q}_L
$$

**Conversion logic:** Because the duct is short and well-insulated, the dominant heat transfer is to the gas stream, so \dot{Q}_L ≈ 0. The enthalpies h\_e and h\_a are computed as mass-fraction-weighted sums of species enthalpies; after expansion, all terms containing N₂, CO₂, and H₂O enthalpies cancel **except** the chemical-energy term of the O₂ actually consumed.

### Step 3 — Application of Huggett's Empirical Law

Huggett observed that regardless of the fuel, the heat released per unit mass of O₂ consumed is approximately E ≈ 13.1 MJ/kg. Substituting:

$$
\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_{O_2,\,c}
$$

This is the **canonical Huggett equation** and appears in 80 %+ of all cone calorimeter data sheets.

### Step 4 — Expressing in Terms of Measured Duct Quantities

The KTU board often asks for the form in terms of *duct-exit* quantities. We substitute Step 1's result and use the **nitrogen balance** (inert conservation: \dot{m}\_a Y\_{N₂,a} = \dot{m}\_e Y\_{N₂,e}) to eliminate \dot{m}\_a in favour of \dot{m}\_e.

Because Y\_{N₂,a} = 1 − Y\_{O₂,a} − Y\_{CO₂,a} − Y\_{H₂O,a} ≈ 0.768 in dry air, we get:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_e\,\frac{Y_{N_2,e}}{Y_{N_2,a}}
$$

Plugging back:

$$
\dot{m}_{O_2,c} = \dot{m}_e\!\left[\frac{Y_{N_2,e}}{Y_{N_2,a}}\,Y_{O_2,a} - Y_{O_2,e}\right]
$$

After algebraic simplification (assuming \dot{Q}_L is negligible and the expansion coefficients of the gases are nearly equal — the **ideal-gas / plug-flow assumption**), the result is the **KTU board equation**:

$$
\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_e\,\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{1 + \Phi}
$$

where Φ is the *expansion factor*:

$$
\Phi = \frac{\Delta H_{O_2}}{\Delta H_c}\cdot\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{X_{O_2,e}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** X denotes *mole fraction* (dry basis) — the form directly read from the O₂/CO₂ analyser. The factor (1 + Φ) accounts for the fact that one mole of O₂ consumed is replaced by one mole of CO₂ (same number of moles), but the volumetric flow at the analyser changes due to the difference in molecular masses of reactants and products.

### Step 5 — Total Heat Released (THR)

Integrating the HRR curve from ignition time t\_i to flame-out t\_f:

$$
\text{THR} = \int_{t_i}^{t_f}\dot{Q}(t)\,dt \quad [\text{kJ/m}^2]
$$

This integral is the area under the HRR-time curve and is the **single most cited performance metric** in fire-test reports.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Model):** A cone calorimeter test on a 100 mm × 100 mm phenolic composite specimen gives the following quasi-steady readings at 60 s after ignition:
> * Duct mass flow rate, \dot{m}\_e = 0.036 kg/s
> * O₂ mole fraction at duct, X\_{O₂,e} = 0.180
> * CO₂ mole fraction at duct, X\_{CO₂,e} = 0.040
> * Effective heat of combustion, Δh\_{c,eff} = 25 MJ/kg
>
> **Calculate:** (a) HRR in kW, (b) mass loss rate of the specimen in g/s, (c) O₂ depletion per kg of air (in %).

### Solution

**Given data**

$$
\dot{m}_e = 0.036\,\text{kg/s},\quad X_{O_2,a} = 0.2095,\quad X_{O_2,e} = 0.180,\quad \Delta h_{c,eff} = 25\times 10^3\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step (a) — HRR**

First, the O₂ depletion ratio:

$$
\Delta X_{O_2} = X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e} = 0.2095 - 0.180 = 0.0295
$$

Equivalence factor (assuming complete combustion, Φ → 0 in this simple form):

$$
\Phi \approx 0
$$

Hence the KTU governing equation reduces to:

$$
\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_e\,\Delta X_{O_2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dot{Q} = 13.1\times 10^3 \times 0.036 \times 0.0295
$$

**Step-by-step multiplication:**

$$
13.1\times 10^3 \times 0.036 = 471.6\,\text{kJ/s per unit O}_2\text{ fraction}
$$

$$
471.6 \times 0.0295 = 13.91\,\text{kJ/s} = 13.91\,\text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 13.9\,\text{kW}}
$$

**Step (b) — Mass loss rate**

Rearranging the effective heat of combustion definition:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\Delta h_{c,eff}} = \frac{13.91}{25\,000} = 5.564\times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}
$$

Converting to g/s:

$$
\dot{m}_f = 0.5564\,\text{g/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_f \approx 0.56\,\text{g/s}}
$$

**Step (c) — O₂ depletion per kg of air**

The mass of air (dry) per second crossing the analyser:

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_e = 0.036\,\text{kg/s}
$$

Mass of O₂ per kg of air at analyser:

$$
Y_{O_2,e} = \frac{X_{O_2,e}\,M_{O_2}}{\sum X_i M_i} = \frac{0.180\times 32}{0.180(32) + 0.040(44) + 0.780(28)}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
0.180\times 32 = 5.760
$$

$$
0.040\times 44 = 1.760
$$

$$
0.780\times 28 = 21.840
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 29.360
$$

Hence:

$$
Y_{O_2,e} = \frac{5.760}{29.360} = 0.1962
$$

O₂ depletion per kg of air (mass basis):

$$
Y_{O_2,a} - Y_{O_2,e} = 0.232 - 0.1962 = 0.0358
$$

Express as percent:

$$
\boxed{3.58\%\,\text{mass depletion of O}_2 \text{ in the duct per kg of air}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this example is KTU-relevant:** The board routinely asks for the conversion between **mole fraction** (analyser output) and **mass fraction** (governing equation input). Most marks are lost here. Always expand the denominator to include **all** major species: O₂, CO₂, N₂, and — if undried — H₂O.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]**
*State and explain Thornton's Rule as applied to oxygen-consumption calorimetry. Why is it considered the cornerstone of HRR measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thornton's Rule, refined by Huggett in 1980, states that the **net heat of complete combustion per unit mass of oxygen consumed is approximately constant for virtually all organic fuels**, with a value of

$$
E \approx 13.1 \times 10^3\,\text{kJ/kg O}_2
$$

with a deviation of less than ±5 % across ~80 common fuels (hydrocarbons, polymers, woods, and alcohols). It is considered the cornerstone of HRR measurement because it allows the heat release rate of a burning material to be computed **without** knowing its exact chemical composition, by simply measuring the **rate of O₂ depletion** in the exhaust. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
*List the four essential gas/flow measurements required to compute HRR in a CO₂ calorimeter. State the working principle of the CO₂ analyser used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Duct mass flow rate \dot{m}\_e (kg/s) — via bidirectional Pitot probe. *(0.5 mark)*
2. O₂ mole fraction in the duct X\_{O₂,e} — paramagnetic analyser. *(0.5 mark)*
3. O₂ mole fraction in ambient air X\_{O₂,a} = 0.2095 (reference). *(0.5 mark)*
4. CO₂ mole fraction in the duct X\_{CO₂,e} — NDIR analyser. *(0.5 mark)*

**Working principle of CO₂ analyser:** *Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) absorption.* CO₂ molecules absorb IR radiation at a characteristic wavelength of 4.26 µm. The difference in intensity between a reference cell and a sample cell is proportional to CO₂ concentration. *(1 mark)*

Total: **3 marks.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the governing Huggett equation for heat release rate in terms of the measured duct mass flow rate and the dry-basis O₂ mole fraction. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 mm × 100 mm polymer slab is tested in a cone calorimeter. The following quasi-steady values are recorded 90 s after ignition:

* Duct mass flow rate \dot{m}\_e = 0.045 kg/s
* O₂ mole fraction in duct X\_{O₂,e} = 0.165
* CO₂ mole fraction in duct X\_{CO₂,e} = 0.060
* Ambient O₂ mole fraction X\_{O₂,a} = 0.2095
* Effective heat of combustion Δh\_{c,eff} = 22.0 MJ/kg

**Compute:**
(i) the heat release rate in kW, and **(3.5 marks)**
(ii) the mass loss rate of the specimen in g/s, and **(2 marks)**
(iii) the THR if the HRR remains constant for 120 s, given the specimen exposed area A\_s = 0.01 m². **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Take a control volume enclosing the fire plume, hood, and duct cross-section. Apply **mass balance on O₂**:

$$
\dot{m}_a\,Y_{O_2,a} = \dot{m}_e\,Y_{O_2,e} + \dot{m}_{O_2,c}
$$

Apply **nitrogen balance** (inert conservation):

$$
\dot{m}_a\,Y_{N_2,a} = \dot{m}_e\,Y_{N_2,e}
$$

Apply **energy balance** (neglecting heat loss through duct walls):

$$
\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_{O_2,c}
$$

**Assumptions:** *(i)* steady-state quasi-flow; *(ii)* complete combustion; *(iii)* air composition constant at X\_{O₂,a} = 0.2095; *(iv)* uniform gas composition across duct (plug flow); *(v)* transport lag corrected; *(vi)* \dot{Q}\_L ≈ 0.

Eliminate \dot{m}\_a using the nitrogen balance:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_e\,\frac{Y_{N_2,e}}{Y_{N_2,a}}
$$

Substitute in the O₂ mass balance:

$$
\dot{m}_{O_2,c} = \dot{m}_e\!\left[\frac{Y_{N_2,e}}{Y_{N_2,a}}\,Y_{O_2,a} - Y_{O_2,e}\right]
$$

Convert mass fractions to mole fractions (using X\_i M\_i = Y\_i M\_e with M\_e the mean molecular weight of the dry exhaust). After algebraic expansion, the result is:

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_e\,\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{1 + \Phi}}
$$

where

$$
\Phi = \frac{\Delta H_{O_2}}{\Delta H_c}\cdot\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{X_{O_2,e}}
$$

This is the **KTU-board Huggett equation**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) HRR Calculation (3.5 marks)**

O₂ depletion:

$$
\Delta X_{O_2} = 0.2095 - 0.165 = 0.0445
$$

Assuming complete combustion (Φ ≈ 0 for a first-order estimate):

$$
\dot{Q} = 13.1\times 10^3 \times 0.045 \times 0.0445
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
13.1\times 10^3 \times 0.045 = 589.5
$$

$$
589.5 \times 0.0445 = 26.23\,\text{kJ/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 26.2\,\text{kW}}
$$

**(ii) Mass Loss Rate (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\Delta h_{c,eff}} = \frac{26.23}{22\,000} = 1.192\times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}
$$

Converting:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_f \approx 1.19\,\text{g/s}}
$$

**(iii) Total Heat Released (1.5 marks)**

For a constant HRR of 26.23 kW over 120 s:

$$
\text{THR}_{\text{total}} = \dot{Q}\cdot\Delta t = 26.23 \times 120 = 3147.6\,\text{kJ}
$$

Per unit exposed area (A\_s = 0.01 m²):

$$
\text{THR} = \frac{3147.6}{0.01} = 3.148\times 10^5\,\text{kJ/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{THR} \approx 3.15\times 10^5\,\text{kJ/m}^2}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a **cone calorimeter (ISO 5660)** and describe how the heat release rate is computed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of the **CO₂ analyser** in oxygen-consumption calorimetry? Show how the **CO₂ generation rate** can be used to cross-validate the HRR computed from O₂ depletion, and explain the meaning of the **combustion efficiency χ**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**Part (a) — Cone Calorimeter Subsystems (7 marks)**

The major subsystems are:

1. **Cone-shaped radiant heater** (5000 W electric, capable of 0–100 kW/m² flux at the specimen surface). *(1 mark)*
2. **Specimen holder and spark igniter** (3 mm pilot spark, 10 kV). *(1 mark)*
3. **Exhaust hood and duct** (0.25 m dia duct, induced-draft blower). *(1 mark)*
4. **Bidirectional Pitot probe** in the duct, connected to a **micro-manometer** — yields \dot{m}\_e. *(1 mark)*
5. **Gas sampling train**: water trap → filter → pump → analyser rack (O₂ + CO₂ + smoke photometer). *(1 mark)*
6. **Data acquisition system** (≥ 1 Hz) running the Huggett equation in real time. *(1 mark)*
7. **Output**: HRR (kW) vs. time trace; integrated THR; effective ΔH\_c; smoke yield. *(1 mark)*

HRR is computed from the **Huggett equation**:

$$
\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_e\,\frac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{1 + \Phi}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Role of CO₂ Analyser and Combustion Efficiency (7 marks)**

**Role of the CO₂ analyser:** *(2 marks)*

* Provides a **cross-check** on the stoichiometry. The X\_{CO₂,e} signal is used in the expression for Φ (expansion factor).
* Detects **oxygen vitiation** when the hood leaks or when the O₂ concentration falls below ~16 % (the "oxygen-depletion limit" beyond which Huggett's constant is no longer valid).
* Enables computation of the **combustion efficiency χ**.

**CO₂ generation rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dot{m}_e\,Y_{CO_2,e} = \dot{m}_e\,\frac{X_{CO_2,e}\,M_{CO_2}}{M_e}
$$

The ratio of measured CO₂ to the theoretical (stoichiometric) CO₂ that *would* be produced if all carbon in the fuel were fully oxidised gives χ. For a fuel C\_x H\_y O\_z:

$$
\chi = \frac{\dot{m}_{CO_2,\,measured}}{\dot{m}_{CO_2,\,stoich}} = \frac{X_{CO_2,e}}{X_{CO_2,\,stoich}}
$$

**Combustion efficiency χ (3 marks):** χ is the fraction of the fuel's carbon that is fully oxidised to CO₂. Values: χ ≈ 0.9–1.0 for well-ventilated flaming combustion; χ drops to 0.4–0.7 in vitiated, under-ventilated fires due to CO and soot formation. It is computed as:

$$
\chi = \frac{\Delta h_{c,eff}}{\Delta h_{c,\,gross}}
$$

i.e., the **ratio of effective heat of combustion** (measured in the test) to the **gross heat of combustion** (measured in an oxygen-bomb calorimeter). This ratio quantifies how much of the fuel's chemical energy is actually released as heat in the flaming regime of the fire test. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (MOST COMMON MARK-LOSING ERRORS):**
> 1. **Confusing mole fraction X with mass fraction Y** — analysers return X (dry basis), but the Huggett equation uses a mix; conversion must be explicit.
> 2. **Omitting the factor (1 + Φ)** in the governing equation when the fuel contains oxygen or produces CO/soot. (Loss: 1–2 marks per instance.)
> 3. **Forgetting the water trap** in the sampling line drawing. (Loss: 1 mark.)
> 4. **Mixing units**: Thornton's constant E is in kJ/kg (use 13 100), not in J/kg. (Loss: 0.5 mark.)
> 5. **Not stating the plug-flow assumption** in derivations. (Loss: 0.5 mark.)
> 6. **Confusing effective Δh\_{c,eff}** (test-measured) with **gross Δh\_c** (bomb-calorimeter). The two differ by χ.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Thornton's / Huggett's Rule:** $E = 13.1\,\text{MJ/kg O}_2$, valid for all organic fuels to within ±5 %. *(MUST remember.)*
- **Huggett Equation (canonical form):** $\dot{Q} = E\,\dot{m}_e\,\dfrac{X_{O_2,a} - X_{O_2,e}}{1 + \Phi}$. *(Board-equation.)*
- **Two gas analysers are mandatory:** O₂ (paramagnetic or zirconia) and CO₂ (NDIR).
- **Flow measurement:** bidirectional Pitot probe in a circular duct; C\_d ≈ 0.99.
- **Smoke measurement:** He-Ne laser photometer (λ = 632.8 nm); gives specific extinction area k\_s.
- **Three outputs of a cone calorimeter test:** (1) HRR curve, (2) THR, (3) effective Δh\_{c,eff}.
- **Sampling line essentials:** water trap + particulate filter + pump + flow controller; lag 5–15 s.
- **Equivalence ratio Φ** is small (≈ 0) for well-ventilated fires; non-negligible for vitiated / under-ventilated fires.
- **Combustion efficiency χ** = Δh\_{c,eff} / Δh\_{c,gross} ≈ 0.9–1.0 (well-ventilated flaming).
- **Plug-flow assumption** is the silent foundation of every duct-based HRR equation.
- **Vitiation limit:** when X\_{O₂,e} < 0.16, the Huggett constant deviates; correction required.
- **Two ISO standards to remember:** ISO 5660 (cone, small-scale) and ISO 9705 (room, full-scale).
- **Conversion formulas to memorise:** $Y_i = \dfrac{X_i M_i}{M_e}$ and $M_e = \sum X_i M_i$.
- **Sample calculation tip:** Always state Thornton's constant in **kJ/kg** (13 100), not in J/kg.
- **Forensic / design use:** HRR trace is the single most useful input to CFD fire simulation (FDS / PyroSim).
- **Performance-based design (PBD) anchor:** ISO 9705 100/300/600 kW room-corner tests are the basis of prescriptive fire-safety rules in many countries.
- **Marks-doubler trick:** Whenever a KTU derivation is asked, end your solution with the boxed final equation and a one-line "physical interpretation" sentence. Examiners reward clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensible Enthalpy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensible Enthalpy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fire dynamics and combustion engineering, **Sensible Enthalpy** ($h_s$) is defined as the *thermally measurable* portion of the total energy content of a gas (or combustion product) that arises purely from its **temperature elevation above a defined reference state**. It excludes any energy tied up in chemical bonds (chemical enthalpy) or phase changes (latent enthalpy).

Mathematically, for a substance undergoing a process from a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ to a current temperature $T$:

$$h_s = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p \, dT$$

Where:
- $h_s$ = specific sensible enthalpy (kJ/kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity at constant pressure (kJ/(kg·K))
- $T$ = current absolute temperature (K)
- $T_{ref}$ = reference temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Convention (Fire Engineering):**
> In fire propagation and dynamics, the reference temperature $T_{ref}$ is conventionally taken as **0 °C (273.15 K)** following the **Zukoski–Quintiere convention**, unless otherwise stated. This is because ambient fire temperatures are typically referenced to ambient air, not the thermodynamic standard of 25 °C.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Cup of Tea"

Imagine a sealed cup of tea at **100 °C** sitting on a table at **25 °C** room temperature. The "extra" energy in the tea that makes it feel hot, that a thermometer can **sense** as a temperature difference, is its **sensible enthalpy**.

- The *steam* rising from the tea carries **latent enthalpy** (hidden — energy used to convert liquid water into vapor without a temperature change).
- The *sugar* in the tea holds **chemical enthalpy** (potential energy locked in molecular bonds).
- The *measurable heat* in the bulk liquid that radiates and warms the surroundings = **sensible enthalpy**.

In a fire, the hot smoke, the heated ceiling jet, and the buoyant plume all transport energy primarily as **sensible enthalpy** — and it is this form of energy that causes the deadliest fire threats: thermal burns, flashover, and tenability loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:** Whenever a fire investigator or engineer measures a *temperature* of smoke or compartment gases, the *consequence* of that elevated temperature — the energy it represents — is the **sensible enthalpy**. It is the bridge between temperature measurement and thermal energy quantification in fire science.

## 1.3 Why "Sensible"?

The word "sensible" comes from the Latin *sensibilis* — "perceivable by the senses." A traditional mercury thermometer *senses* this energy as a temperature rise. Energy hidden in chemical bonds or vapor condensation cannot be detected by a thermometer — hence the contrast with *latent* (hidden) enthalpy.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question says "sensible enthalpy" without specifying a reference temperature, **always state your assumption** clearly. The most common error in board exams is omitting the reference value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Enthalpy in Fire Gases

The **total specific enthalpy** of a combustion product stream can be decomposed into three additive components:

$$h_{total} = h_{sensible} + h_{latent} + h_{chemical}$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Detectable by Thermometer? |
|-----------|--------|------------------|----------------------------|
| Sensible | $h_s$ | Energy from temperature rise above $T_{ref}$ | ✅ Yes |
| Latent | $h_{fg}$ | Energy of phase change (e.g., water vapor → liquid) | ❌ No |
| Chemical | $h_c$ | Energy released by combustion reaction | ❌ No (until reacted) |

In a **post-flame fire plume or upper smoke layer**, the dominant energy carrier is **sensible enthalpy**, because:
1. The chemical reaction is essentially complete (fuel has been oxidized).
2. The temperatures are high (typically 300 °C – 1100 °C), so the $c_p \cdot \Delta T$ term is large.
3. Water vapor usually remains in the gaseous phase in the upper layer (no condensation under most compartment conditions).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Identify the reference state.**
In fire engineering, fix $T_{ref} = 0\,°C = 273.15\,K$ (Zukoski convention). Some texts use $25\,°C = 298.15\,K$ (thermodynamic standard) — the *delta* $\Delta T$ remains the same numerically, but $h_s$ values will shift.

**Step 2: Determine the specific heat capacity.**
For air and most lean combustion products (N₂, O₂, CO₂, H₂O), $c_p$ varies mildly with temperature. A common engineering approximation:

$$c_p \approx 1.00 \text{ to } 1.05 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \quad \text{for fire smoke}$$

A more accurate polynomial form (valid 300 K – 1000 K, dimensionless in $T/1000$):

$$c_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3 \quad \text{(kJ/(kg·K))}$$

**Step 3: Integrate to get sensible enthalpy.**
If $c_p$ is treated as constant (a very good approximation for fire calculations):

$$h_s \approx c_p \, (T - T_{ref})$$

**Step 4: Multiply by mass to get total energy.**
For a smoke layer of mass $M$ at uniform temperature $T$:

$$E_{sensible} = M \cdot h_s = M \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$

**Step 5: Apply to a real fire compartment.**
Energy conservation (first law of thermodynamics) for a control volume enclosing the compartment:

$$\frac{dE}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{fire} - \dot{Q}_{loss}$$

Where the stored energy $E$ is dominated by the sensible enthalpy of the hot gas layer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $h_s = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p \, dT$ | Fundamental definition of sensible enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $h_s \approx c_p \, (T - T_{ref})$ | Engineering approximation (constant $c_p$) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $E_s = M \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref})$ | Total sensible energy of a smoke mass | kJ |
| 4 | $c_p \approx 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K) | Approximation for combustion products | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 5 | $T_{ref} = 273.15$ K | Zukoski–Quintiere reference (fire convention) | K |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_{c} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat release rate from plume | kW |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = 0.076 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3}$ | Zukoski mass flow rate in fire plume | kg/s |
| 8 | $T - T_\infty = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{c_p \cdot \dot{m}}$ | Plume temperature rise (from energy balance) | K |
| 9 | $h_{s,layer} = \frac{1}{M_{layer}} \int \rho h_s \, dV$ | Mass-weighted average sensible enthalpy in layer | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{layer} = \frac{\int (T-T_\infty) \, dV}{\int dV}$ | Volume-averaged temperature rise of layer | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** In KTU 2024 Scheme boards, writing the *form* of the equation (e.g., $h_s = \int c_p dT$) without units may cost a mark. Always pair equations with **SI units** in the answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Sensible enthalpy calculations are the **backbone** of:

- **Smoke layer interface height prediction** (used in NFPA 92, ISO 17859, Indian Standard IS 15105 for smoke management).
- **Tenability analysis** — predicting the time before a smoke layer descends to a critical elevation where occupants are exposed.
- **Compartment fire energy balance** — solving for the time to flashover using $\dot{Q}_{fo} = 1000$ kW/m² threshold correlations tied to upper layer temperature.
- **Ventilation-controlled vs. fuel-controlled fire transitions** — sensible enthalpy of the gas layer determines which regime dominates.
- **Performance-based fire safety design** in Kerala's high-rise and atrium projects (referenced in the KBC — Kerala Building Rules, fire safety chapter).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sensible Enthalpy for an Ideal Gas

### Starting Point: First Law for a Closed System

For a unit mass of ideal gas undergoing a quasi-static, constant-pressure heating from $T_{ref}$ to $T$, the change in enthalpy is defined as the heat added:

$$dh = c_p \, dT$$

### Step-by-Step Integration

**Step 1:** Recognize that the differential enthalpy change per unit mass is the specific heat capacity times the differential temperature rise:

$$dh = c_p \, dT$$

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides from the reference state $(T = T_{ref}, h = 0)$ to the final state $(T, h_s)$:

$$\int_{0}^{h_s} dh = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p \, dT$$

**Step 3:** The left side evaluates trivially to $h_s$. The right side requires knowledge of how $c_p$ varies with $T$:

$$h_s = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p(T) \, dT$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **engineering approximation** that $c_p$ is approximately constant over the fire-relevant temperature range (300 K – 1100 K). This pulls $c_p$ out of the integral:

$$h_s = c_p \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} dT = c_p \, [T]_{T_{ref}}^{T}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the limits:

$$h_s = c_p \, (T - T_{ref})$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the fire-engineering reference $T_{ref} = 273.15$ K:

$$h_s = c_p \, (T - 273.15)$$

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{h_s = c_p \, (T - 273.15) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensible Enthalpy in a Fire Compartment (Control Volume)

### Goal: Express stored thermal energy of a smoke layer in terms of measurable quantities.

**Step 1:** Define the total thermal energy stored in the upper hot gas layer of mass $M$ at temperature $T_s$:

$$E = M \cdot h_s$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the derived expression for $h_s$:

$$E = M \cdot c_p \cdot (T_s - T_{ref})$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the ideal gas law to express mass in terms of measurable quantities — layer volume $V$, gas density $\rho$:

$$M = \rho \cdot V$$

**Step 4:** Apply the ideal gas law for the hot layer:

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R_{specific} \cdot T_s}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute density back into energy:

$$E = \frac{P \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T_s} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_s - T_{ref})$$

**Step 6:** For combustion products, $R_{specific} \approx 287$ J/(kg·K) and $c_p \approx 1000$ J/(kg·K), giving $c_p / R_{specific} \approx 3.5$:

$$E \approx 3.5 \cdot P \cdot V \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_{ref}}{T_s}\right)$$

### Final Form — Energy in Terms of Volume and Pressure

$$\boxed{E \approx 3.5 \, P \, V \left(1 - \frac{T_{ref}}{T_s}\right) \quad \text{[Joules]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the form most commonly used in **performance-based fire engineering** in KTU board problems. Note how the result depends *only* on **pressure $P$**, **volume $V$**, and **temperature ratio $T_s / T_{ref}$** — all directly measurable in a real fire experiment.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:**
A fire compartment has an upper smoke layer at $T_s = 600\,°C = 873.15$ K. The total mass of smoke in the layer is $M = 1500$ kg. The average specific heat of the combustion products is $c_p = 1.02$ kJ/(kg·K). Using the Zukoski reference of 0 °C, calculate:

(a) The specific sensible enthalpy of the smoke.
(b) The total sensible energy stored in the layer.
(c) The energy released if this smoke cools back to 25 °C.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Apply $h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref})$:

$$h_s = 1.02 \times (873.15 - 273.15) = 1.02 \times 600 = 612.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Part (b):** Multiply by mass:

$$E_s = M \cdot h_s = 1500 \times 612.0 = 918{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 918 \text{ MJ}$$

**Part (c):** Energy released cooling from 600 °C to 25 °C:

$$\Delta h = c_p \times (600 - 25) = 1.02 \times 575 = 586.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{released} = 1500 \times 586.5 = 879{,}750 \text{ kJ} \approx 880 \text{ MJ}$$

### Interpretation

A typical 1 MW fire releases 1000 kJ every second. So this smoke layer stores energy equivalent to ~918 seconds (~15 minutes) of continuous 1 MW burning — illustrating why a hot smoke layer is a *major* thermal hazard even after the fire is suppressed.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Forms of Enthalpy

| Property | Sensible | Latent | Chemical |
|----------|----------|--------|----------|
| **Storage mechanism** | Temperature elevation | Phase change | Molecular bonds |
| **Equation** | $h_s = c_p \Delta T$ | $h_{fg}$ at constant $T$ | $\Delta h_{combustion}$ |
| **Typical magnitude (fire smoke)** | 500 – 1200 kJ/kg | 2200 kJ/kg (water) | 15,000 – 50,000 kJ/kg (fuel) |
| **Releasable during cooling?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (if condensation) | ❌ Only via further reaction |
| **Detected by thermometer?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Role in smoke movement** | Drives buoyancy | Negligible in hot layer | Already released upstream |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Energy Flow in a Fire Compartment

The following diagram traces how chemical energy of fuel is progressively transformed into sensible enthalpy of the smoke layer, with explicit energy pathways and loss mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Chemical Energy Storage] --> B[Combustion Reaction Zone]
    B --> C[Convective Heat Release Qc]
    B --> D[Radiative Heat Release Qr]
    D --> E[Wall and Surface Absorption]
    C --> F[Buoyant Fire Plume]
    F --> G[Ceiling Jet Formation]
    G --> H[Upper Hot Smoke Layer]
    H --> I[Sensible Enthalpy Storage Hs]
    I --> J[Conduction Loss Through Walls]
    I --> K[Convection Loss Through Openings]
    I --> L[Radiation Loss to Boundaries]
    M[Ambient Inflow Tref] --> F
    H --> N[Smoke Layer Interface Descent]
    N --> O[Tenantibility Threshold]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of Total Enthalpy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Total Enthalpy of Fire Gases] --> B[Sensible Enthalpy Hs]
    A --> C[Latent Enthalpy Hfg]
    A --> D[Chemical Enthalpy Hc]
    B --> B1[Temperature Rise Above Tref]
    B --> B2[Function of Cp and Delta T]
    C --> C1[Phase Change Energy]
    C --> C2[Water Vapor Condensation]
    D --> D1[Unburnt Fuel Energy]
    D --> D2[Incomplete Combustion Products CO, Soot]
    B1 --> E[Drives Buoyancy and Smoke Spread]
    C1 --> F[Releases on Cooling Below Dew Point]
    D1 --> G[Potential Re-ignition Hazard]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Smoke Layer Sensible Enthalpy Profile in a Compartment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ceiling Tmax] --> B[Upper Layer T near Tref Boundary]
    B --> C[Interface at Ti]
    C --> D[Lower Layer T near Tambient]
    E[Hot Layer Depth d] --> B
    C --> F[Clear Layer Depth H minus d]
    H[Compartment Height H] --> E
    H --> F
    G[Sensible Enthalpy per kg cp times T minus Tref] --> B
```

### Reading the Schematic

- **Top region (A → B):** Hot smoke layer with maximum sensible enthalpy storage.
- **Interface zone (B → C):** Temperature transition where mixing occurs; this is the most dynamically unstable region.
- **Bottom region (C → D):** Cool ambient air with negligible sensible enthalpy.
- The **stored energy** is concentrated in the upper layer, driving the descent of the interface and eventual loss of tenantibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When drawing compartment schematics in board exams, always **label** the interface temperature $T_i$, the upper layer temperature $T_s$, the ambient temperature $T_\infty$, and the reference temperature $T_{ref}$. Missing labels are a common reason for mark deductions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
