# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q: Define sensible enthalpy. Why is it significant in fire dynamics?**

**Model Answer:**

Sensible enthalpy is the component of the total energy content of a gas (or combustion product) that arises solely from its **temperature elevation above a reference state**, and which is *sensed* or measured as a temperature rise by a thermometer.

Mathematically: $h_s = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p \, dT \approx c_p (T - T_{ref})$ for constant $c_p$.

**Significance in fire dynamics:**
1. It is the dominant energy carrier in the **hot smoke layer** that accumulates at the ceiling of a burning compartment.
2. It directly drives the **buoyancy** of smoke, controlling smoke spread, layer descent, and tenantibility.
3. It governs the **flashover condition** — when the upper layer's sensible enthalpy raises all combustible surfaces to their ignition temperature.
4. It forms the basis of the **compartment energy balance equation** used in performance-based fire design.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for significance points)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q: Distinguish between sensible enthalpy and latent enthalpy of combustion products.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensible Enthalpy ($h_s$) | Latent Enthalpy ($h_{fg}$) |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Energy associated with temperature rise above a reference | Energy absorbed/released during a phase change at constant temperature |
| Phase of substance | Single phase (gas) | Phase transition (vapor ↔ liquid) |
| Detected by thermometer? | Yes (temperature change) | No (isothermal) |
| Equation | $h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref})$ | $h_{fg}$ (constant, function of substance) |
| Example in fire | Hot smoke at 600 °C above 0 °C | Water vapor in smoke releasing heat on condensation at 100 °C |
| Dominance in upper smoke layer | **Dominant** | Negligible (vapor remains gaseous) |

*(3 marks — 1.5 for each distinction in tabular comparison)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**A (a)** Derive the expression for the specific sensible enthalpy of an ideal gas undergoing constant-pressure heating from a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ to a final temperature $T$. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. *(7 marks)*

**A (b)** A fire compartment of floor area 4 m × 5 m has a ceiling height of 3 m. The upper hot smoke layer is 0.8 m deep, at an average temperature of 550 °C. The lower layer is at 30 °C. Take $c_p = 1.02$ kJ/(kg·K), gas constant $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), ambient pressure $P = 101.325$ kPa, and reference temperature $T_{ref} = 273$ K. Calculate the **specific sensible enthalpy** of the hot layer and the **total sensible energy stored** in it. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — A (a)

**Derivation (7 marks):**

1. State first law: $dh = c_p \, dT$ for constant-pressure quasi-static process — *(1 mark)*
2. Set boundary conditions: at $T = T_{ref}$, $h_s = 0$ — *(1 mark)*
3. Integrate: $\int_0^{h_s} dh = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p \, dT$ — *(1.5 marks)*
4. Assume $c_p \approx$ constant over fire-relevant range, pull out of integral — *(1 mark)*
5. Evaluate: $h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref})$ — *(1.5 marks)*
6. State assumptions: ideal gas, constant $c_p$, no phase change, no chemical reaction — *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution — A (b)

**Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify hot layer volume.**
$$V = 4 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 16 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate hot layer temperature in Kelvin.**
$$T = 550 + 273 = 823 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate hot layer density (ideal gas law).**
$$\rho = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101{,}325}{287 \times 823} = \frac{101{,}325}{236{,}201} = 0.429 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Calculate mass of hot layer.**
$$M = \rho \cdot V = 0.429 \times 16 = 6.864 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Calculate specific sensible enthalpy.**
$$h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref}) = 1.02 \times (823 - 273) = 1.02 \times 550 = 561.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6: Calculate total sensible energy.**
$$E_s = M \cdot h_s = 6.864 \times 561.0 = 3{,}850.7 \text{ kJ} \approx 3.85 \text{ MJ}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $h_s = 561.0$ kJ/kg
- $E_s \approx 3.85$ MJ

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative]**

**B (a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the concept of sensible enthalpy in a fire compartment. Clearly identify the upper hot layer, the lower cool layer, the interface, and the reference temperature. *(7 marks)*

**B (b)** Starting from the first law of thermodynamics for an open control volume, derive the **energy conservation equation** for a fire compartment in terms of sensible enthalpy. Define all variables used. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — B (a)

**Schematic and Explanation (7 marks):**

1. Draw a rectangular compartment with ceiling, floor, side walls, and an opening (door/window) — *(1.5 marks)*
2. Show two distinct horizontal regions: upper hot layer (depth $d$, temperature $T_s$, density $\rho_s$) and lower cool layer (depth $H-d$, temperature $T_\infty$, density $\rho_\infty$) — *(1.5 marks)*
3. Mark the interface at height $(H-d)$ from floor with temperature $T_i$ — *(1 mark)*
4. Indicate the fire source at floor level, the buoyant plume rising to the ceiling, and the ceiling jet spreading radially — *(1 mark)*
5. State that the sensible enthalpy per unit mass of upper layer is $h_s = c_p (T_s - T_{ref})$ and the total energy stored is $E_s = \rho_s V_s c_p (T_s - T_{ref})$ — *(1.5 marks)*
6. Identify $T_{ref}$ as 273 K (Zukoski convention) and ambient pressure $P_\infty$ as the reference pressure — *(0.5 mark)*

#### Model Solution — B (b)

**Derivation of Compartment Energy Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: First law for an open control volume at steady state.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in} \dot{m} h - \sum_{out} \dot{m} h$$

**Step 2: Assume no shaft work** ($\dot{W}_{cv} = 0$), only heat release from the fire $\dot{Q}_c$, and neglect heat losses to walls initially for clarity. Mass in = mass out (incompressible net flow assumption relaxed for ideal gas). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: For the stored energy in the gas (ideal gas), express as sensible enthalpy integral over the volume:** *(1.5 marks)*

$$E_{cv} = \int_{cv} \rho h_s \, dV$$

**Step 4: Substitute $h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref})$:** *(1 mark)*

$$E_{cv} = \int_{cv} \rho c_p (T - T_{ref}) \, dV$$

**Step 5: Add the heat loss term $\dot{Q}_{loss}$ (conduction through walls, radiation, opening convection) to give the final form:** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{cv} \rho c_p (T - T_{ref}) \, dV = \dot{Q}_c - \dot{Q}_{loss}$$

**Step 6: Define variables:** *(0.5 mark)*

- $\dot{Q}_c$ = convective heat release rate of the fire (kW)
- $\dot{Q}_{loss}$ = total heat loss rate through compartment boundaries (kW)
- $\rho$ = local gas density (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure (kJ/(kg·K))
- $T$ = local absolute temperature (K)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the reference temperature.** Writing $h_s = c_p T$ is **incomplete**. Always state $T_{ref} = 273$ K explicitly in your solution.
> 2. **Unit mismatch between temperature scales.** Mixing °C and K in the same equation causes immediate 1-mark deduction. Convert all temperatures to Kelvin **before** plugging into formulas.
> 3. **Confusing specific enthalpy with total energy.** $h_s$ is in **kJ/kg**; multiplying by mass $M$ gives total energy in kJ. Don't report energy per unit mass when the question asks for total stored energy.
> 4. **Using $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) blindly.** For fire-temperature combustion products, use $c_p \approx 1.02$ kJ/(kg·K) to be more accurate. Examiners appreciate the correct value.
> 5. **Omitting the ideal gas assumption** when applying $P = \rho R T$. Always state the assumption.
> 6. **In derivations, skipping integration steps.** Examiners award step marks — even if the final answer is correct, omitting intermediate algebra costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sensible enthalpy = thermally measurable energy from $T > T_{ref}$, equal to $h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref})$ for constant $c_p$.
- ✅ **Reference Convention:** $T_{ref} = 273.15$ K (Zukoski–Quintiere) for fire engineering; 298.15 K for general thermodynamics.
- ✅ **$c_p$ for Combustion Products:** ~1.00 to 1.05 kJ/(kg·K); use **1.02 kJ/(kg·K)** as the standard KTU exam value.
- ✅ **Total Energy Stored:** $E_s = M \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref}) = \rho V c_p (T - T_{ref})$.
- ✅ **Alternative Form (using ideal gas law):** $E_s \approx 3.5 \, P \, V \, (1 - T_{ref}/T)$.
- ✅ **Three Forms of Enthalpy:** Total = Sensible + Latent + Chemical. In upper smoke layer, **sensible dominates**.
- ✅ **Energy Balance Equation:** $dE/dt = \dot{Q}_c - \dot{Q}_{loss}$ — the foundation of compartment fire analysis.
- ✅ **Physical Significance:** Drives buoyancy, smoke spread, layer descent, and flashover.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Smoke management (NFPA 92, IS 15105), tenantibility analysis, flashover prediction, performance-based design.
- ✅ **Common Exam Formulas to Memorize:** $h_s = c_p (T - T_{ref})$, $E_s = M h_s$, $\dot{m} = 0.076 \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} z^{5/3}$ (Zukoski plume), $T - T_\infty = \dot{Q}_c / (c_p \dot{m})$ (plume temperature rise).
- ✅ **Key Distinction:** Sensible = "felt by thermometer"; Latent = "hidden in phase change"; Chemical = "locked in bonds".
- ✅ **SI Units Always:** kJ/kg for specific enthalpy, kJ for total energy, K for temperature, kg for mass.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Sensible Enthalpy**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education (OBE), and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Calculation for Multicomponent Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Calculation for Multicomponent Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **multicomponent fuel** is a chemically heterogeneous combustible substance whose heat release (calorific value) cannot be determined from a single chemical formula because it is an **aggregate of several distinct combustible and non-combustible constituents** (e.g., carbon `C`, hydrogen `H_2`, sulphur `S`, moisture `M`, ash `A`, and volatile matter `VM`). 

The **Heat Calculation for a Multicomponent Fuel** is the systematic engineering procedure used to determine the **net heat released per unit mass (or per unit mole) of the mixed fuel** during complete combustion with theoretical (or actual) air, by applying the principle of **linear superposition (mass-weighted averaging)** of the individual heats of combustion of its combustible constituents, corrected for the latent heat loss in water vapour formation.

Mathematically, the gross calorific value (GCV) of a multicomponent fuel on a unit-mass basis is given by **Dulong's Modified Equation**:

$$GCV = 33{,}800 \cdot m_C + 144{,}200 \left(m_{H_2} - \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{8}\right) + 9{,}400 \cdot m_S \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

where $m_C$, $m_{H_2}$, $m_{O_2}$, and $m_S$ are the mass fractions of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulphur obtained from the **ultimate analysis** of the fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The heat released by a multicomponent fuel is **NOT additive in moles** — it is additive in **mass fractions (kg/kg of fuel)**. This is the most common conceptual trap in KTU valuation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **mixed fruit smoothie** 🍓🍌🥭. You cannot predict the total calories of the smoothie by knowing just the chemical formula of one fruit. Instead, you **weigh each fruit**, look up its individual calorie content per 100 g, and **add them up** — then divide by total weight. The same logic applies to coal, refinery residues, MSW (municipal solid waste), and biomass pellets:

- **Fruit 1 = Carbon (C)** — burns to `CO_2`, releases **~33,800 kJ/kg**
- **Fruit 2 = Hydrogen (H₂)** — burns to `H_2O`, releases **~144,200 kJ/kg** (before water-vapour correction)
- **Fruit 3 = Sulphur (S)** — burns to `SO_2`, releases **~9,400 kJ/kg**
- **Fruit 4 = Oxygen (O₂)** — is *not combustible*; it is a thief that has already partially "burned" some hydrogen, so we subtract it.
- **Fruit 5 = Ash + Moisture** — are *inert*; they absorb heat but release nothing. They **lower the GCV** because they dilute the combustible portion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (Bolded for KTU Memory):**
> - Calorific value of pure Carbon `C → CO_2` : **33,800 kJ/kg**
> - Calorific value of pure Hydrogen `H_2 → H_2O(l)` : **144,200 kJ/kg**
> - Calorific value of pure Hydrogen `H_2 → H_2O(v)` : **120,000 kJ/kg** (≈ 144,200 − 2,442 × 9 ≈ 120,000 kJ/kg net)
> - Calorific value of pure Sulphur `S → SO_2` : **9,400 kJ/kg**
> - Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25 °C : **2,442 kJ/kg**
> - Stoichiometric O₂ required per kg C : **2.667 kg O₂/kg C** (32/12)
> - Stoichiometric O₂ required per kg H₂ : **8.000 kg O₂/kg H₂** (32/4)
> - Stoichiometric O₂ required per kg S : **1.000 kg O₂/kg S** (32/32)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

To calculate the heat released by a multicomponent fuel, the student must execute the following **five-stage engineering workflow**:

1. **Stage 1 — Acquire the Ultimate Analysis (Dry Basis or As-Received Basis)**
   - From laboratory data, list the mass fractions of `C`, `H₂`, `O₂`, `N₂`, `S`, and the percentages of `Moisture (M)` and `Ash (A)`.
   - **Sanity check:** $\sum m_i = 1.000$ (or 100 %).

2. **Stage 2 — Convert All Components to a Common Basis**
   - For combustion air calculations, use the **as-received (AR)** basis.
   - For calorific value, use the **dry, ash-free (DAF)** basis if comparing fuels.
   - Always perform an **air-free, dry-ash-free (ADAF)** normalisation if needed.

3. **Stage 3 — Apply the Weighted-Heat-of-Combustion Formula**
   - Compute the **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** = Higher Heating Value (HHV) using Dulong's equation.
   - Compute the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** = Lower Heating Value (LHV) by subtracting `2,442 × (9 × m_{H_2} + m_M)` to account for latent heat of water vapour escaping without condensing.

4. **Stage 4 — Determine Stoichiometric Air Requirement**
   - Calculate the **minimum O₂** needed per kg of fuel.
   - Convert to **minimum air** by dividing by **0.232** (mass fraction of O₂ in air).
   - Apply **excess-air factor ($\lambda$)** to get the actual air supplied.

5. **Stage 5 — Compute Flue Gas Composition and Heat Balance**
   - Determine mass of `CO_2`, `SO_2`, `H_2O(v)`, `N_2`, and excess `O_2` per kg of fuel.
   - Use **steady-flow energy equation** (SFE) across the combustion chamber to find:
     - Actual heat released (sensible + latent).
     - Adiabatic flame temperature (AFT) when no heat is lost.
     - Furnace efficiency and stack-loss.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why weighted averaging?** Combustion energy is a **path-independent state function** for ideal reactions. Since the components do not interact energetically before combustion, total energy is the mass-weighted sum.
- **Why subtract O₂ contribution from H₂?** Free oxygen in the fuel has already pre-oxidised some H₂ into `H_2O`, which is no longer available to release energy. Each kg of O₂ "eats" **1/8 kg of H₂** worth of energy. Hence the term `- O₂/8` inside the H₂ bracket.
- **Why subtract latent heat of water?** Combustion products leave the fire zone at > 100 °C, so `H_2O` stays as vapour. We must deduct the latent heat of vaporisation that *escapes* un-recovered.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | GCV / HHV (Dulong) | $33{,}800\,C + 144{,}200\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9{,}400\,S$ | kJ/kg fuel | Mass fractions C, H, O, S |
| 2 | NCV / LHV | $GCV - 2{,}442\!\left(9H + M\right)$ | kJ/kg fuel | $M$ = moisture mass fraction |
| 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $2.667\,C + 8.0\,H + 1.0\,S - O$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Mass basis |
| 4 | Stoichiometric Air | $\dfrac{O_2^{\text{req}}}{0.232}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Air = 23.2 % O₂ by mass |
| 5 | Actual Air (excess $e$ %) | $A_{\text{act}} = A_{\text{stoich}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{e}{100}\right)$ | kg air/kg fuel | $e = 0$ for theoretical |
| 6 | Heat Released (steady-flow) | $Q = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}\!\left[NCV + h_{\text{air,in}} - h_{\text{fg,out}}\right]$ | kW | First-law for combustor |
| 7 | Adiabatic Flame Temp. | $Q = 0 \Rightarrow T_{ad}$ solved iteratively | K (°C) | Using flue-gas $\bar c_p$ |
| 8 | Mass of CO₂ produced | $\dfrac{44}{12}\,m_C = 3.667\,m_C$ | kg/kg fuel | Per kg of C burned |
| 9 | Mass of SO₂ produced | $\dfrac{64}{32}\,m_S = 2.000\,m_S$ | kg/kg fuel | Per kg of S burned |
| 10 | Mass of H₂O produced | $9\,m_{H_2} + m_M$ | kg/kg fuel | Includes fuel moisture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These calculations form the heart of **boiler design (IS 10390)**, **fire-load estimation in compartments (IS 1649)**, **incinerator sizing for MSW**, and **post-flashover fire dynamics in compartments** (related to the classic **Babrauskas** fire-load theory).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **fire safety engineering**, the calorific value of mixed fuels (e.g., warehouse pallets + cardboard + plastic + petroleum) is critical to predict **time-temperature curves** in a compartment fire. The fire load $q$ (MJ/m²) is directly used in the Eurocode parametric equations for fire resistance. In **power-plant engineering**, these heat calculations determine the **steam-generation rate** and **boiler efficiency**. In **process safety**, they predict the **worst-case BLEVE / fireball thermal radiation** from multicomponent hydrocarbon pools.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Dulong's Modified Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the standard heats of combustion of pure elements at 25 °C and 1 atm:**

- Carbon: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$, $\quad \Delta H_{c,C} = -33{,}800$ kJ/kg of C
- Hydrogen: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$, $\quad \Delta H_{c,H} = -144{,}200$ kJ/kg of H₂
- Sulphur: $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$, $\quad \Delta H_{c,S} = -9{,}400$ kJ/kg of S

**Step 2 — Express each component's contribution per kg of fuel:**

For every kg of fuel, the energy released is the mass-fraction of each combustible element multiplied by its own heat of combustion:

$$Q_{\text{raw}} = 33{,}800\,m_C + 144{,}200\,m_{H_2} + 9{,}400\,m_S$$

**Step 3 — Correct for the presence of inherent (free) oxygen in the fuel:**

Free O₂ in the fuel has already combined with some H₂. From stoichiometry, every 32 kg of O₂ consumes 4 kg of H₂. Hence, the equivalent combustible hydrogen is reduced:

$$m_{H_2,\text{eff}} = m_{H_2} - \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain Dulong's GCV formula:**

$$GCV = 33{,}800\,m_C + 144{,}200\!\left(m_{H_2} - \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{8}\right) + 9{,}400\,m_S$$

**Step 5 — Convert GCV to NCV (LHV) by removing latent heat of water vapour:**

Each kg of H₂ produces 9 kg of H₂O, and any moisture $M$ (in kg/kg fuel) also vaporises. The latent heat of vaporisation is $h_{fg} = 2{,}442$ kJ/kg of H₂O:

$$NCV = GCV - 2{,}442 \cdot (9\,m_{H_2} + m_M)$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style, Full Numerical Walkthrough)

**Problem Statement (typical 14-mark KTU pattern):**

A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: `C = 60 %`, `H₂ = 5 %`, `O₂ = 8 %`, `S = 1 %`, `N₂ = 2 %`, Moisture `M = 4 %`, Ash `A = 20 %`. Calculate: 
**(a)** the Gross and Net Calorific Values (GCV, NCV), and 
**(b)** the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal and the composition of the flue gas on a mass basis (assuming 20 % excess air).

---

### Part (a) — Solution for GCV and NCV

**Step A1 — Sanity check on the ultimate analysis:**

$$0.60 + 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.01 + 0.02 + 0.04 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step A2 — Apply Dulong's equation with the mass fractions (decimal form):**

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 33{,}800(0.60) + 144{,}200 \left(0.05 - \dfrac{0.08}{8}\right) + 9{,}400(0.01) \\
&= 33{,}800(0.60) + 144{,}200 (0.05 - 0.01) + 9{,}400(0.01) \\
&= 20{,}280 + 144{,}200 (0.04) + 94 \\
&= 20{,}280 + 5{,}768 + 94 \\
&= 26{,}142 \text{ kJ/kg of coal}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step A3 — Compute NCV by subtracting latent heat of water vapour:**

The water produced by hydrogen combustion = $9 \times m_{H_2} = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.45$ kg/kg fuel.
Total water leaving as vapour = $0.45 + M = 0.45 + 0.04 = 0.49$ kg/kg fuel.

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 26{,}142 - 2{,}442 \times 0.49 \\
&= 26{,}142 - 1{,}196.58 \\
&= 24{,}945.42 \text{ kJ/kg of coal}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result (a):** `GCV ≈ 26,142 kJ/kg`, `NCV ≈ 24,945 kJ/kg`.

---

### Part (b) — Solution for Stoichiometric Air & Flue Gas Composition

**Step B1 — Stoichiometric O₂ required (mass basis):**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\text{stoich}} &= 2.667\,m_C + 8.0\,m_{H_2} + 1.0\,m_S - m_{O_2} \\
&= 2.667(0.60) + 8.0(0.05) + 1.0(0.01) - 0.08 \\
&= 1.6002 + 0.4000 + 0.0100 - 0.0800 \\
&= 1.9302 \text{ kg O}_2\text{ / kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B2 — Stoichiometric air (air = 23.2 % O₂ by mass):**

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1.9302}{0.232} = 8.319 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

**Step B3 — Actual air with 20 % excess:**

$$A_{\text{act}} = 8.319 \times 1.20 = 9.983 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

**Step B4 — Air composition (per kg of air):**
- O₂ supplied = $1.9302 \times 1.20 = 2.3162$ kg
- N₂ supplied (with N₂ = 76.8 % of air mass) = $0.768 \times 9.983 = 7.667$ kg
- O₂ actually consumed = $1.9302$ kg (stoichiometric); excess O₂ = $2.3162 - 1.9302 = 0.3860$ kg

**Step B5 — Flue gas products per kg of fuel:**

| Product | Source | Calculation | Mass (kg/kg fuel) |
|---------|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| `CO_2` | From C | $\tfrac{44}{12} \times 0.60$ | **2.200** |
| `SO_2` | From S | $\tfrac{64}{32} \times 0.01$ | **0.020** |
| `H_2O(v)` | From H₂ + Moisture | $9(0.05) + 0.04$ | **0.490** |
| `N_2` | From air + fuel N₂ | $7.667 + 0.02$ | **7.687** |
| Excess `O_2` | From excess air | $0.386$ | **0.386** |
| **Total flue gas** | | | **10.783** |

**Step B6 — Mass-percent composition of dry flue gas (excluding H₂O):**

Dry flue gas = $10.783 - 0.490 = 10.293$ kg/kg fuel.

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,CO_2 &= \dfrac{2.200}{10.293} \times 100 = 21.37\,\% \\
\%\,SO_2 &= \dfrac{0.020}{10.293} \times 100 = 0.19\,\% \\
\%\,O_2 &= \dfrac{0.386}{10.293} \times 100 = 3.75\,\% \\
\%\,N_2 &= \dfrac{7.687}{10.293} \times 100 = 74.69\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result (b):** Stoichiometric air = **8.319 kg/kg fuel**, Actual air (20 % excess) = **9.983 kg/kg fuel**, Dry flue gas contains **21.4 % CO₂, 0.2 % SO₂, 3.75 % O₂, 74.7 % N₂**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use mass basis, not mole basis?** In fire dynamics, mass fractions are the standard because they are conserved across most mixing layers (no temperature dependence). Mole fractions are reserved for equilibrium chemistry and equilibrium constant ($K_p$) calculations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Heat Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Ultimate Analysis] --> B[Normalise Mass Fractions]
    B --> C{Split Basis}
    C -->|Dry Basis| D1[Apply Dulong GCV]
    C -->|As-Received| D2[Apply NCV with Moisture]
    D1 --> E[GCV Result kJ/kg]
    D2 --> F[NCV Result kJ/kg]
    F --> G[Stoichiometric O2 Calculation]
    G --> H[Convert to Stoichiometric Air]
    H --> I{Apply Excess Air Factor}
    I --> J[Actual Air Supplied]
    J --> K[Compute Flue Gas Masses]
    K --> L[CO2 SO2 H2Ov N2 ExcessO2]
    L --> M[Mass Percent Composition]
    L --> N[Steady Flow Energy Equation]
    N --> O[Heat Released Q kW]
    N --> P[Adiabatic Flame Temperature]
    P --> Q[Final Output: Combustion Report]
    M --> Q
    O --> Q
```

### 4.2 Conceptual State Diagram — Combustion Energy Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> S[Combustible Fraction]
    R --> T[Inert Fraction Ash plus Moisture]
    S --> U[Sensible Heat of Products]
    S --> V[Latent Heat of Water Vapour]
    S --> W[Stack Losses]
    S --> X[Useful Heat Output]
    T --> Y[Heat Absorbed by Ash]
    T --> Z[Heat Absorbed by Moisture]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Heat Losses in a Typical Combustion Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[GCV Input] --> Q2[Moisture Evaporation Loss 2 to 5 percent]
    Q1 --> Q3[H2O Vapour Latent Loss 4 to 8 percent]
    Q1 --> Q4[CO and Unburnt Hydrocarbon Loss 0.5 to 2 percent]
    Q1 --> Q5[Sensible Stack Loss 5 to 15 percent]
    Q1 --> Q6[Radiation and Convection Loss 1 to 3 percent]
    Q2 --> Q7[Net Useful Heat 70 to 85 percent]
    Q3 --> Q7
    Q4 --> Q7
    Q5 --> Q7
    Q6 --> Q7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The three Mermaid flowcharts are **process-block diagrams**, not physical drawings. They show the *causal information flow* (from analysis data to final heat report) and the *energy partition* (GCV split into losses and useful heat) — these are the most important visual aids for the KTU Module 2 viva and written exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "multicomponent fuel" with two suitable examples. Why is its calorific value not a single fixed number?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **multicomponent fuel** is a heterogeneous combustible material composed of more than one chemically distinct combustible species (e.g., carbon, hydrogen, sulphur) along with non-combustible inerts (moisture, ash). Examples: **coal** and **municipal solid waste (MSW)**. Its calorific value is not a single fixed number because it is a **weighted average of the heats of combustion of its individual components**, and the proportions vary from sample to sample, sample to source, and even batch to batch. Hence it must always be measured by bomb calorimetry or estimated by weighted formulas like Dulong's equation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV). State the physical reason for the difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**GCV (HHV)** is the total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely combusted and the **products are cooled back to 25 °C** so that all water vapour condenses to liquid, releasing its latent heat. **NCV (LHV)** is the heat released when the **products leave the combustion zone as water vapour at > 100 °C**, so the latent heat of vaporisation is *not* recovered. The physical reason for the difference is that the latent heat of vaporisation of water (**2,442 kJ/kg**) escapes with the flue gas. **NCV = GCV − 2,442 × (9H + M).**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT use mole basis** when Dulong's formula expects mass basis — this is the #1 cause of wrong answers in this module.
> - **Do NOT forget the `- O/8` correction** in the H₂ bracket. Free O₂ in fuel pre-burns hydrogen; omitting this term leads to **~5,800 kJ/kg error** in coal GCV.
> - **Do NOT forget to subtract moisture latent heat** when computing NCV.
> - **Always show units** in the final answer line. Marks are docked for unitless numerical results.
> - In flue-gas calculations, **mass percentages of dry gas** must exclude `H_2O(v)`.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A solid waste fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: `C = 45 %`, `H₂ = 6 %`, `O₂ = 12 %`, `S = 1 %`, `N₂ = 1 %`, Moisture `M = 15 %`, Ash `A = 20 %`. Calculate the **GCV and NCV** of the fuel using Dulong's modified equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the fuel in part (a), determine the **(i) stoichiometric air, (ii) actual air with 25 % excess, and (iii) the dry flue gas composition (% by mass)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GCV and NCV — Step-by-step:**

Sum check: $0.45 + 0.06 + 0.12 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.15 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 33{,}800(0.45) + 144{,}200 \left(0.06 - \dfrac{0.12}{8}\right) + 9{,}400(0.01) \\
&= 33{,}800(0.45) + 144{,}200 (0.06 - 0.015) + 9{,}400(0.01) \\
&= 15{,}210 + 144{,}200(0.045) + 94 \\
&= 15{,}210 + 6{,}489 + 94 \\
&= 21{,}793 \text{ kJ/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Water from H}_2 &= 9 \times 0.06 = 0.54 \text{ kg/kg} \\
\text{Total water} &= 0.54 + 0.15 = 0.69 \text{ kg/kg} \\
NCV &= 21{,}793 - 2{,}442 \times 0.69 \\
&= 21{,}793 - 1{,}684.98 \\
&= 20{,}108 \text{ kJ/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (a):** **GCV = 21,793 kJ/kg, NCV = 20,108 kJ/kg**

**(b) Air and Flue Gas — Step-by-step:**

**Stoichiometric O₂:**
$$O_2^{stoich} = 2.667(0.45) + 8.0(0.06) + 1.0(0.01) - 0.12 = 1.2002 + 0.4800 + 0.0100 - 0.1200 = 1.5702 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Stoichiometric air:** $A_{stoich} = 1.5702 / 0.232 = 6.768$ kg/kg fuel

**Actual air (25 % excess):** $A_{act} = 6.768 \times 1.25 = 8.460$ kg/kg fuel

**Flue gas products per kg of fuel:**
- `CO_2` = $(44/12) \times 0.45 = 1.650$ kg
- `SO_2` = $(64/32) \times 0.01 = 0.020$ kg
- `H_2O(v)` = $9(0.06) + 0.15 = 0.690$ kg
- `N_2` = $0.768 \times 8.460 + 0.01 = 6.497 + 0.01 = 6.507$ kg
- Excess `O_2` = $(1.5702 \times 1.25) - 1.5702 = 0.3926$ kg

**Dry flue gas total** = $1.650 + 0.020 + 6.507 + 0.3926 = 8.5696$ kg/kg fuel

**Dry composition:**
- %CO₂ = $1.650 / 8.5696 \times 100 = 19.25$ %
- %SO₂ = $0.020 / 8.5696 \times 100 = 0.23$ %
- %O₂ = $0.3926 / 8.5696 \times 100 = 4.58$ %
- %N₂ = $6.507 / 8.5696 \times 100 = 75.93$ %

> **Answer (b):** $A_{stoich} = 6.768$ kg/kg, $A_{act} = 8.460$ kg/kg, Dry flue gas = **19.25 % CO₂, 0.23 % SO₂, 4.58 % O₂, 75.93 % N₂**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Define **stoichiometric air** and **excess air**. A furnace burns **producer gas** with the following volumetric analysis: `CO = 28 %`, `H₂ = 12 %`, `CH₄ = 4 %`, `CO_2 = 6 %`, `N₂ = 50 %`. Find the **stoichiometric air required per m³ of producer gas** at NTP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the furnace operates with **30 % excess air**, calculate the **(i) actual air supplied, (ii) flue gas composition in % by volume (wet basis), and (iii) the heat released per m³ of gas if NCV of the gas is 5,500 kJ/m³.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stoichiometric air per m³ of producer gas — Step-by-step:**

Stoichiometric combustion reactions (per m³ at NTP):
- $CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ → 0.5 m³ O₂ per m³ CO
- $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ → 0.5 m³ O₂ per m³ H₂
- $CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O$ → 2.0 m³ O₂ per m³ CH₄

**O₂ required:**
$$O_2 = 0.5(0.28) + 0.5(0.12) + 2.0(0.04) = 0.14 + 0.06 + 0.08 = 0.28 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ gas}$$

**Stoichiometric air (air = 21 % O₂ by volume):**
$$A_{stoich} = \dfrac{0.28}{0.21} = 1.333 \text{ m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 \text{ gas}$$

> **Answer (a):** $A_{stoich} = 1.333$ m³/m³ of producer gas

**(b) Excess-air case and heat release — Step-by-step:**

**Actual air (30 % excess):** $A_{act} = 1.333 \times 1.30 = 1.733$ m³/m³ gas
**O₂ supplied:** $1.733 \times 0.21 = 0.364$ m³/m³ gas
**Excess O₂ in flue gas:** $0.364 - 0.28 = 0.084$ m³/m³ gas
**N₂ from air:** $1.733 \times 0.79 = 1.369$ m³/m³ gas

**Wet flue gas products per m³ of producer gas:**
- `CO_2` (from fuel CO + fuel CO₂ + CH₄) = $0.28 + 0.06 + 0.04 = 0.38$ m³
- `H_2O` (from H₂ + CH₄) = $0.12 + 2(0.04) = 0.20$ m³
- `N_2` (from air + fuel) = $1.369 + 0.50 = 1.869$ m³
- Excess `O_2` = $0.084$ m³
- **Total wet flue gas** = $0.38 + 0.20 + 1.869 + 0.084 = 2.533$ m³/m³ gas

**Wet composition (% by volume):**
- %CO₂ = $0.38/2.533 \times 100 = 15.00$ %
- %H₂O = $0.20/2.533 \times 100 = 7.90$ %
- %N₂ = $1.869/2.533 \times 100 = 73.78$ %
- %O₂ = $0.084/2.533 \times 100 = 3.32$ %

**Heat released per m³ of gas:**
$$Q = \dot V_{\text{gas}} \times NCV = 1 \times 5{,}500 = 5{,}500 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

> **Answer (b):** $A_{act} = 1.733$ m³/m³; Wet flue gas = **15.00 % CO₂, 7.90 % H₂O, 3.32 % O₂, 73.78 % N₂**; $Q = 5{,}500$ kJ/m³

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition** — A multicomponent fuel's heat release is a **mass-weighted sum** of the heats of combustion of its combustible elements (`C`, `H₂`, `S`), corrected for free `O₂` and for water-vapour latent heat.
- **Dulong's GCV formula** — Memorise: $33{,}800\,C + 144{,}200(H - O/8) + 9{,}400\,S$ (kJ/kg).
- **NCV formula** — Subtract $2{,}442 \times (9H + M)$ from GCV.
- **Stoichiometric O₂** — $2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1\,S - O$ on mass basis.
- **Stoichiometric air** — Divide O₂ by $0.232$ (mass) or $0.21$ (volume).
- **Excess air adjustment** — Multiply $A_{stoich}$ by $(1 + e/100)$.
- **Flue gas products (mass basis)** — `CO_2 = 3.667C`, `SO_2 = 2.0S`, `H_2O = 9H + M`, `N_2` from air + fuel N₂, Excess `O_2` retained.
- **Always use mass basis for Dulong's equation; use volume basis only for gaseous fuel (Orsat) analysis.**
- **Difference between GCV and NCV** — Latent heat of water vapour (2,442 kJ/kg H₂O); NCV is always smaller.
- **Pitfalls to avoid** — (i) Forgetting the `-O/8` correction, (ii) adding ash/moisture as "combustibles", (iii) using mole basis in mass formula, (iv) missing units, (v) forgetting to remove `H_2O` for dry-basis analysis.
- **Engineering uses** — Boiler design, fire-load estimation (IS 1649 / Eurocode), incinerator sizing, BLEVE/fireball thermal radiation, MSW energy recovery.
- **Excess-air rule of thumb** — Solid fuels: 15–30 %; Liquid fuels: 5–15 %; Gaseous fuels: 3–8 %. Lower excess = higher efficiency but risk of CO.
- **Self-test mantra** — "If the answer is a single number per kg, you did mass basis correctly. If your flue-gas total doesn't close to ~10 kg/kg, you missed ash or moisture."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Combustion energy is mass-additive, not mole-additive. Always carry your mass fractions and your units to the last decimal."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Heat of Reaction of LPG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Heat of Reaction of LPG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Standard Heat of Reaction ($\Delta H^\circ_r$)** of LPG is the enthalpy change accompanying the complete combustion of Liquefied Petroleum Gas (a mixture primarily of **propane**, **n-butane**, and **isobutane**) with pure oxygen (or air) when both reactants and products are maintained at the **standard state conditions** of **$T = 298.15 \text{ K}$** ($25^\circ\text{C}$) and **$P = 1 \text{ bar}$** (or $101.325 \text{ kPa}$).

For combustion engineering, this is more commonly called the **Standard Heat of Combustion** and is expressed per unit mass (kJ/kg) or per unit mole (kJ/mol) of fuel consumed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The sign convention is critical. For an **exothermic** combustion reaction (the only physically possible type for LPG in oxygen), $\Delta H^\circ_r$ is always **negative** by thermodynamic convention, because the system *releases* energy to the surroundings.

### 1.2 LPG Composition — The Practical Fuel

Commercial LPG is rarely a single hydrocarbon. Indian-market LPG (IS 4576) and international grades typically contain:

| Component | Chemical Formula | Typical Mole % | Boiling Point ($^\circ$C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propane | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | 30 – 95 % | $-42.1$ |
| n-Butane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | 5 – 70 % | $-0.5$ |
| Isobutane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | trace – 30 % | $-11.7$ |
| Propene / Butene | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$ | < 5 % | variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, LPG is usually **assumed to be pure propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) or pure n-butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$)** unless the question explicitly provides a mixture composition.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

Imagine LPG molecules as tiny **spring-loaded boxes** packed with chemical energy. Each C–H and C–C bond acts like a stretched spring holding energy.

- When LPG is **stored**, the springs are taut, and the energy is "locked" inside the molecule.
- The **Standard Heat of Reaction** is essentially the total energy released when we break all those chemical springs (bonds of fuel + oxygen) and form new, more stable springs (CO₂ and H₂O).
- Because the products (CO₂ + H₂O) are at a **lower energy state** than the reactants (LPG + O₂), the excess energy must escape as **heat** — this is what warms your kitchen, runs a furnace, or drives a turbine.

Think of it this way: *Heat of reaction is the "price difference" between the energy of the input goods (reactants) and the energy of the output goods (products). Since the products are energetically "cheaper," the difference is paid out as useful heat.*

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used

- Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- Standard Reference Temperature: $T^\circ = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- Standard Pressure: $P^\circ = 1 \text{ bar}$
- Air composition (mole basis): $21\% \text{ O}_2 + 79\% \text{ N}_2 \Rightarrow$ mole ratio $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a constant-pressure combustion process (the open-flame scenario of a fire) gives the heat released as the **enthalpy change**:

$$Q_p = \Delta H = H_{products} - H_{reactants}$$

At the standard state, we denote this as $\Delta H^\circ_r$. There are **two routes** to compute it:

**Route A — From Heats of Formation (Hess's Law):**

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_i n_i \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,\text{products}} - \sum_j n_j \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,\text{reactants}}$$

This is the **only** route that yields correct theoretical answers for KTU numericals.

**Route B — From Heats of Combustion (Direct Tabulation):**

The heat of combustion is simply the negative of $\Delta H^\circ_r$ for a complete combustion reaction, i.e., $\Delta H^\circ_c = -\Delta H^\circ_r$.

### 2.2 Combustion Stoichiometry of LPG Components

The balanced combustion reactions (the "equations of life-saving fire dynamics"):

**Propane Combustion (stoichiometric, pure O₂):**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**n-Butane Combustion (stoichiometric, pure O₂):**

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} + 6.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**With Air (accounting for nitrogen ballast):**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 5(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} + 6.5\,\text{O}_2 + 6.5(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 24.44\,\text{N}_2$$

### 2.3 Higher vs. Lower Heating Value

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This distinction is **the single most heavily tested concept** in KTU Module 2 numericals.

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** / Gross Calorific Value (GCV): Water product is taken as **liquid** at $25^\circ\text{C}$. The latent heat of condensation of water is **included** in the energy release.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** / Net Calorific Value (NCV): Water product is taken as **vapor** at $25^\circ\text{C}$. The latent heat is **excluded**.

Conversion relationship:

$$\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + n_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg,\text{water}}$$

where $h_{fg,\text{water}} = 44.0 \text{ kJ/mol} = 2442 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat of Reaction (Hess's Law) | $\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum n_p \Delta H^\circ_{f,p} - \sum n_r \Delta H^\circ_{f,r}$ | kJ/mol | Foundation formula |
| Stoichiometric O₂ coefficient | For $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b$: $\nu_{O_2} = a + b/4$ | mol O₂/mol fuel | Used in balanced reaction |
| Stoichiometric Air | $\nu_{air} = 4.76 \cdot \nu_{O_2}$ | mol air/mol fuel | 4.76 = 1 + 3.76 |
| Mass Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) | $(A/F) = \dfrac{\nu_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{\nu_{fuel} \cdot M_{fuel}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Direct for combustion calcs |
| HHV ↔ LHV Conversion | $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + m_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg fuel | $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg |
| Per-mass to Per-mole | $\Delta H_{per\,kg} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{per\,mol}}{M_{fuel}} \cdot 1000$ | kJ/kg | $M_{fuel}$ in g/mol |
| Adiabatic Flame Temp. | $T_{ad} \approx T_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ_c}{\sum n_p \cdot \bar{c}_{p,p}}$ | K | First-order estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every KTU table, **never embed vertical bars** like $\vert x \vert$ directly. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown renderer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Standard Heat of Reaction of LPG is the **cornerstone design parameter** in:

- **Fire load calculations** for buildings (kg of LPG equivalent fire load per m² floor area, as per NBC 2016 and IS 14435).
- **Boiler and burner thermal design** — fuel input rate = thermal output / $\Delta H^\circ_c$.
- **Explosion / BLEVE hazard analysis** — total chemical energy available governs blast overpressure.
- **Fire suppression system sizing** — gas suppression agents are sized against fuel's heat release rate.
- **Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS) input** — HRR per unit area uses this exact value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Heat of Combustion of Propane (HHV)

**Problem context:** Compute the standard heat of combustion of propane $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ per kg of fuel, when products include **liquid water** at $25^\circ\text{C}$.

**Given Data (Standard Thermochemical Tables):**

| Species | $\Delta H^\circ_f$ (kJ/mol) |
|---|---|
| $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ (g) | $-104.7$ |
| $\text{O}_2$ (g) | $0$ |
| $\text{CO}_2$ (g) | $-393.5$ |
| $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (l) | $-285.8$ |
| $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (g) | $-241.8$ |

**Balanced Reaction (HHV — water as liquid):**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 (g) + 5\,\text{O}_2 (g) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 (g) + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} (l)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Hess's Law for $\Delta H^\circ_r$:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[3 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{CO}_2) + 4 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{H}_2\text{O},l)\right] - \left[1 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) + 5 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{O}_2)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[3(-393.5) + 4(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-104.7) + 5(0)\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[-1180.5 + (-1143.2)\right] - \left[-104.7\right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the bracket sums:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[-2323.7\right] - \left[-104.7\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -2323.7 + 104.7$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -2219.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The negative sign confirms exothermicity. This is the **HHV per mole of propane**.

**Step 4 — Convert to per kilogram of fuel (molar mass of propane $M = 44.097$ g/mol):**

$$\Delta H^\circ_{HHV} = \frac{-2219.0 \text{ kJ/mol}}{44.097 \text{ g/mol}} = -50.32 \text{ kJ/g}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^\circ_{HHV} = -50{,}320 \text{ kJ/kg of propane}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Standard Heat of Combustion of Propane (LHV)

**Same procedure, but products include water as vapor:**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 (g) + 5\,\text{O}_2 (g) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 (g) + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} (g)$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[3(-393.5) + 4(-241.8)\right] - \left[(-104.7) + 0\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[-1180.5 + (-967.2)\right] - \left[-104.7\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -2147.7 + 104.7 = -2043.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^\circ_{LHV} = \frac{-2043.0}{44.097} \times 1000 = -46{,}330 \text{ kJ/kg of propane}}$$

### 3.3 Cross-Check: HHV = LHV + Latent Heat of Condensation

For 1 mole of propane, 4 moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ are produced.

$$\text{HHV} - \text{LHV} = 4 \times 44.0 = 176 \text{ kJ/mol (per mol fuel)}$$

$$-2219 - (-2043) = -176 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad \checkmark$$

The negative sign indicates the latent heat is *released* in HHV. Verified.

### 3.4 Derivation: Standard Heat of Reaction of n-Butane (HHV)

**Balanced reaction:**

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} (g) + 6.5\,\text{O}_2 (g) \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 (g) + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} (l)$$

**Step 1 — Hess's Law:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[4 \cdot (-393.5) + 5 \cdot (-285.8)\right] - \left[1 \cdot (-124.7) + 0\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[-1574.0 + (-1429.0)\right] - \left[-124.7\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -3003.0 + 124.7$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -2878.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Per kg (Molar mass of n-butane = 58.12 g/mol):**

$$\Delta H^\circ_{HHV,\text{butane}} = \frac{-2878.3}{58.12} \times 1000 = -49{,}520 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Comparison of LPG Constituents

| Fuel | Formula | M (g/mol) | $\Delta H^\circ_c$ (kJ/mol) | HHV (kJ/kg) | LHV (kJ/kg) | Stoich. A/F (kg/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Propane | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | 44.10 | $-2219$ | $50{,}320$ | $46{,}330$ | $15.60$ |
| n-Butane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | 58.12 | $-2878$ | $49{,}520$ | $45{,}720$ | $15.38$ |
| Isobutane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | 58.12 | $-2868$ | $49{,}350$ | $45{,}560$ | $15.38$ |
| Pure LPG (60% C₃ + 40% C₄ by mass, typical Indian) | — | ~49.5 | blended | $50{,}000$ | $46{,}090$ | $15.51$ |

### 3.6 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Propane

**Step 1 — From balanced reaction with air:**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 18.8\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute masses:**

$$m_{air} = 5 \times 32 + 18.8 \times 28 = 160 + 526.4 = 686.4 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{fuel} = 1 \times 44.1 = 44.1 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{686.4}{44.1} = 15.57 \approx 15.6 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

### 3.7 Engineering Application Derivation: Fire Load Equivalent

**Problem:** A domestic LPG cylinder contains 14.2 kg of fuel (standard Indian Bharat Gas / HP / Indane size). Compute the total chemical energy released in complete combustion (HHV).

**Solution:**

$$E_{total} = m_{fuel} \times \text{HHV}_{LPG} = 14.2 \text{ kg} \times 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{E_{total} = 7.10 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ} = 710 \text{ MJ}}$$

This is equivalent to the **fire load** of approximately **24 kg of TNT** (since TNT releases ~4.184 MJ/kg) — illustrating why LPG cylinder BLEVE incidents are so catastrophic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LPG Combustion Energy Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LPG_SRC["LPG CYLINDER OR SUPPLY LINE"] --> COMP_ANL["FUEL COMPOSITION IDENTIFICATION"]
    COMP_ANL --> PRPN_FR["PROPANE C3H8 MOLAR FRACTION"]
    COMP_ANL --> BTA_FR["N-BUTANE C4H10 MOLAR FRACTION"]
    COMP_ANL --> ISB_FR["ISOBUTANE TRACE FRACTION"]
    PRPN_FR --> STCH_BAL["BALANCED STOICHIOMETRIC COMBUSTION EQUATION"]
    BTA_FR --> STCH_BAL
    ISB_FR --> STCH_BAL
    STCH_BAL --> AIR_CAL["STOICHIOMETRIC AIR REQUIREMENT CALCULATION"]
    AIR_CAL --> O2_IN["OXYGEN MASS TERM"]
    AIR_CAL --> N2_IN["NITROGEN DILUENT MASS TERM"]
    STCH_BAL --> PROD_OUT["COMBUSTION PRODUCT IDENTIFICATION"]
    PROD_OUT --> CO2_OUT["CARBON DIOXIDE CO2"]
    PROD_OUT --> H2O_OUT["WATER H2O PHASE CHOICE"]
    H2O_OUT --> H2O_L["LIQUID WATER FOR HHV PATH"]
    H2O_OUT --> H2O_V["WATER VAPOR FOR LHV PATH"]
    PROD_OUT --> N2_OUT["NITROGEN N2 INERT"]
    H2O_L --> HESS_APP["HESS LAW ENTHALPY SUMMATION"]
    H2O_V --> HESS_APP
    CO2_OUT --> HESS_APP
    PRPN_FR --> HFR_SUM["REACTANT HEAT OF FORMATION SUM"]
    BTA_FR --> HFR_SUM
    O2_IN --> HFR_SUM
    N2_IN --> HFR_SUM
    HFR_SUM --> HESS_APP
    HESS_APP --> DHR_FIN["STANDARD HEAT OF REACTION DELTA HR"]
    DHR_FIN --> HHV_OUT["HIGHER HEATING VALUE HHV"]
    DHR_FIN --> LHV_OUT["LOWER HEATING VALUE LHV"]
    HHV_OUT --> AFT_CAL["ADIABATIC FLAME TEMPERATURE ESTIMATION"]
    LHV_OUT --> AFT_CAL
    AFT_CAL --> FIRE_DYN["FIRE PROPAGATION RATE AND HRR PER UNIT AREA"]
    HHV_OUT --> ENG_USE["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS BOILER BURNER FIRE LOAD"]
    LHV_OUT --> ENG_USE
    ENG_USE --> SAF_DSN["SAFETY DESIGN BLEVE HAZARD SUPPRESSION SIZING"]
```

### 4.2 Energy Transformation Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["CHEMICAL ENERGY STORED IN C-H AND C-C BONDS OF LPG"] --> B1["ACTIVATION ENERGY IGNITION SOURCE"]
    B1 --> C1["REACTION ZONE FLAME FRONT"]
    C1 --> D1["RELEASED HEAT OF REACTION DELTA HR NEGATIVE"]
    D1 --> E1["PARTIAL CONVECTIVE HEAT TRANSFER"]
    D1 --> E2["PARTIAL RADIATIVE HEAT TRANSFER"]
    D1 --> E3["PARTIAL CONDUCTIVE HEAT TRANSFER"]
    E1 --> F1["FIRE PLUME RISING HOT GASES"]
    E2 --> F2["RADIANT HEAT TO SURROUNDING FUEL"]
    E3 --> F3["CONDUCTION TO STRUCTURAL MEMBERS"]
    F1 --> G1["FLASHOVER AND FIRE SPREAD"]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1["FULLY DEVELOPED FIRE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start from the top-left (LPG source) and trace the path through stoichiometric balancing → enthalpy summation → heat of reaction computation → real-world application. Each block is a sub-procedure you can solve independently in a KTU numerical.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Standard Heat of Combustion" of LPG. State its sign convention and explain why it is always exothermic.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Standard Heat of Combustion of LPG is the enthalpy change when 1 mole (or 1 kg) of LPG undergoes complete combustion with oxygen at the standard state ($T = 298.15$ K, $P = 1$ bar), with products returned to the same standard state. It is denoted $\Delta H^\circ_c$ with units kJ/mol or kJ/kg.

**Sign convention:** $\Delta H^\circ_c$ is taken as a **negative** quantity because the combustion reaction is exothermic (heat flows from system to surroundings).

**Why always exothermic:** The chemical bonds in the combustion products (C=O in CO₂ and O–H in H₂O) are **stronger** (lower energy) than the bonds broken in the fuel (C–H, C–C) and O₂ (O=O). The difference in bond energy is released as heat. Since the products are energetically more stable than the reactants, the enthalpy of the system decreases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of LPG. Which one is used in furnace design and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | HHV (GCV) | LHV (NCV) |
|---|---|---|
| Water product state | Liquid | Vapor |
| Latent heat of vaporization | Included | Excluded |
| Magnitude | Larger | Smaller (by ~10%) |
| Use in fire dynamics | Theoretical maximum | Practical fire load |

**Furnace design uses LHV** because in real furnace operation, the flue gases leave at temperatures well above $100^\circ\text{C}$, so water vapor cannot condense and release its latent heat. The latent heat is therefore "lost" up the stack and unavailable for the process. However, **HHV is used in safety/fire load calculations** to represent the worst-case total energy that could be released in a catastrophic fire (since condensation can occur on cooler adjacent surfaces).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`*

**(a)** Derive the standard heat of combustion of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) per kg of fuel at 25°C and 1 bar, considering water in the **liquid** state. Given: $\Delta H^\circ_f(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8, g) = -104.7$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H^\circ_f(\text{CO}_2, g) = -393.5$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H^\circ_f(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.8$ kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A domestic kitchen receives LPG supply at $0.5$ kg/hr. If the combustion is $90\%$ complete (rest forms CO) and the lower heating value of the LPG is $46{,}000$ kJ/kg, calculate: (i) the heat released per hour; (ii) the volume of stoichiometric air required per hour at 25°C, 1 bar, assuming LPG is pure propane. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Balanced reaction:**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 (g) + 5\,\text{O}_2 (g) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 (g) + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} (l)$$

**Step 2 — Hess's Law application:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_c = \left[3 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{CO}_2) + 4 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{H}_2\text{O},l)\right] - \left[1 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) + 5 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\text{O}_2)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_c = \left[3(-393.5) + 4(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-104.7) + 5(0)\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_c = \left[-1180.5 - 1143.2\right] - \left[-104.7\right]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_c = -2323.7 + 104.7 = -2219.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Per kg conversion ($M_{C_3H_8} = 44.097$ g/mol):**

$$\Delta H^\circ_c = \frac{-2219.0 \text{ kJ/mol}}{44.097 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} = -50{,}322 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^\circ_{HHV} \approx -50{,}320 \text{ kJ/kg of propane}}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Heat released per hour:**

Given LHV = $46{,}000$ kJ/kg and $90\%$ complete combustion:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb} = 0.5 \text{ kg/hr} \times 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{Q = 20{,}700 \text{ kJ/hr} = 5.75 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Stoichiometric air volume:**

Stoichiometric O₂ requirement per kg of propane (from balanced equation):

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{5 \times 32}{44.097} = 3.628 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{ kg fuel}$$

With nitrogen ballast ($m_{air} = m_{O_2}/0.233$):

$$m_{air,stoich} = \frac{3.628}{0.232} = 15.64 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

Mass of air per hour:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.5 \times 15.64 = 7.82 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Moles of air per hour** ($M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol):

$$n_{air} = \frac{7820 \text{ g/hr}}{28.97 \text{ g/mol}} = 269.93 \text{ mol/hr}$$

**Volume at 25°C, 1 bar** using ideal gas law:

$$V_{air} = \frac{n_{air} R T}{P} = \frac{269.93 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{101{,}325}$$

$$\boxed{V_{air} = 6.61 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks):**
*`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, 2024 Scheme Pattern]`*

**(a)** A commercial LPG sample has the following composition by mass: $60\%$ propane and $40\%$ n-butane. Calculate the **mass-weighted HHV** and **LHV** of this mixture per kg of fuel. Given: HHV of propane = $50{,}320$ kJ/kg; LHV of propane = $46{,}330$ kJ/kg; HHV of n-butane = $49{,}520$ kJ/kg; LHV of n-butane = $45{,}720$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If $1.5$ kg of this LPG mixture is completely burnt with $150\%$ theoretical air in a furnace, determine: (i) the actual mass of air supplied; (ii) the mass of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced (per kg of fuel basis first, then total); (iii) the total heat released assuming $100\%$ combustion efficiency and LHV basis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mass-weighted HHV/LHV (7 marks):**

For 1 kg of mixture: $0.60$ kg propane + $0.40$ kg n-butane.

$$\text{HHV}_{mix} = (0.60 \times 50{,}320) + (0.40 \times 49{,}520)$$

$$\text{HHV}_{mix} = 30{,}192 + 19{,}808 = 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\text{LHV}_{mix} = (0.60 \times 46{,}330) + (0.40 \times 45{,}720)$$

$$\text{LHV}_{mix} = 27{,}798 + 18{,}288 = 46{,}086 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HHV}_{mix} = 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad ; \quad \text{LHV}_{mix} \approx 46{,}090 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(b) Combustion with 150% Theoretical Air (7 marks):**

**Combined fuel formula** (per kg of mixture):

Moles of C and H from each component:
- From 0.60 kg propane ($M = 44.1$): $n_{C_3H_8} = 0.60/44.1 = 0.01361$ kmol → C atoms = $3 \times 0.01361 = 0.04082$ kmol ; H atoms = $8 \times 0.01361 = 0.10886$ kmol.
- From 0.40 kg n-butane ($M = 58.1$): $n_{C_4H_{10}} = 0.40/58.1 = 0.006884$ kmol → C atoms = $4 \times 0.006884 = 0.02754$ kmol ; H atoms = $10 \times 0.006884 = 0.06884$ kmol.
- **Total per kg of mixture:** C = $0.06836$ kmol ; H = $0.17770$ kmol.

**Stoichiometric O₂ required:**

$$n_{O_2,stoich} = n_C + n_{H}/4 = 0.06836 + 0.17770/4 = 0.06836 + 0.04443 = 0.11278 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 0.11278 \times 32 = 3.609 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Stoichiometric air:**

$$m_{air,stoich} = 3.609/0.232 = 15.56 \text{ kg air}/\text{kg fuel}$$

**(i) Actual air supplied (150% theoretical):**

$$m_{air,actual} = 1.5 \times 15.56 = 23.34 \text{ kg air}/\text{kg fuel}$$

For 1.5 kg of fuel: $m_{air,total} = 1.5 \times 23.34 = 35.01 \text{ kg air}$

$$\boxed{m_{air,total} \approx 35.0 \text{ kg}}$$

**(ii) Combustion products (per kg of fuel, with 50% excess O₂ left over):**

- $n_{CO_2} = n_C = 0.06836$ kmol → $m_{CO_2} = 0.06836 \times 44 = 3.008$ kg
- $n_{H_2O} = n_{H}/2 = 0.17770/2 = 0.08885$ kmol → $m_{H_2O} = 0.08885 \times 18 = 1.599$ kg
- Excess O₂ remaining: $0.5 \times 0.11278 = 0.05639$ kmol → $m_{O_2,excess} = 0.05639 \times 32 = 1.805$ kg
- N₂ from air: $0.11278 \times 3.76 \times 1.5 = 0.6361$ kmol → $m_{N_2} = 0.6361 \times 28 = 17.81$ kg

**For 1.5 kg fuel:**

$$\boxed{m_{CO_2} = 4.51 \text{ kg} \quad ; \quad m_{H_2O} = 2.40 \text{ kg}}$$

**(iii) Total heat released:**

$$Q = m_{fuel} \times \text{LHV}_{mix} = 1.5 \times 46{,}086 = 69{,}129 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 69.1 \text{ MJ} = 19.2 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Many students write HHV as a *positive* number, but Hess's Law $\Delta H^\circ_r$ is **always negative** for combustion. Always carry the sign.
> 2. **Molar mass unit mismatch:** Converting kJ/mol to kJ/kg requires multiplying by $1000/M_{fuel}$. Forgetting the $10^3$ factor costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **HHV/LHV misuse:** If the question says "fire load" or "calorimeter" (adiabatic, cool surroundings), use HHV. If it says "furnace" or "flue gas" (hot, no condensation), use LHV.
> 4. **Forgetting nitrogen in air-balance:** When using air (not pure O₂), remember the $3.76$ factor for N₂. KTU explicitly tests this in Part B.
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Keep at least 4 significant figures until the **final** step. Premature rounding (e.g., $\pi \approx 3$) is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Units in ideal gas law:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) when P is in Pa and V in m³. Using the wrong $R$ value (e.g., 0.0821 with mixed units) costs 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Standard heat of reaction is the enthalpy change of combustion at $T = 298.15$ K, $P = 1$ bar, with reactants and products in their standard states.
- [x] **LPG composition:** Primarily propane + n-butane; for KTU problems, assume a single pure component unless composition is given.
- [x] **Hess's Law is the only universal tool:** $\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum n_p \Delta H^\circ_{f,p} - \sum n_r \Delta H^\circ_{f,r}$.
- [x] **Sign convention:** Exothermic → $\Delta H^\circ_r$ is **negative**; magnitude is the heat released.
- [x] **HHV vs LHV:** HHV includes latent heat of water condensation; LHV does not. Conversion: $\text{HHV} - \text{LHV} = n_{H_2O} \times 44.0$ kJ/mol.
- [x] **Stoichiometric O₂ for $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b$:** $\nu_{O_2} = a + b/4$ (for hydrocarbon-only fuel).
- [x] **Stoichiometric air:** $\nu_{air} = 4.76 \times \nu_{O_2}$ (factor of 4.76 = 1 mol O₂ + 3.76 mol N₂).
- [x] **Propane typical HHV:** $\approx 50{,}320$ kJ/kg; **LHV:** $\approx 46{,}330$ kJ/kg.
- [x] **n-Butane typical HHV:** $\approx 49{,}520$ kJ/kg; **LHV:** $\approx 45{,}720$ kJ/kg.
- [x] **Domestic 14.2 kg LPG cylinder** releases $\approx 710$ MJ on complete combustion — equivalent to ~24 kg TNT.
- [x] **Use HHV for fire load / safety analysis; use LHV for furnace / burner thermal design.**
- [x] **For mixture HHV/LHV, mass-weighted average** (NOT mole-weighted) is the KTU convention.
- [x] **Excess air problems:** $\text{O}_2$ remaining in products = $(\text{excess \%}/100) \times \text{stoichiometric O}_2$.
- [x] **Memorize the three core formation enthalpies:** $\text{CO}_2 = -393.5$ kJ/mol, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) = -285.8$ kJ/mol, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(g) = -241.8$ kJ/mol.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enthalpy of Formation of Solid Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Enthalpy of Formation of Solid Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Enthalpy of Formation (ΔH°f)** of a solid fuel is the enthalpy change accompanying the formation of **one mole (or one unit mass)** of the fuel from its constituent elements in their **standard reference states** (at **298.15 K** and **1 atm / 101.325 kPa**), with the products also being in their standard states.

Mathematically, for any compound formation reaction:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ = \sum H_{\text{products}}^\circ - \sum H_{\text{reactants}}^\circ$$

For a generic solid fuel whose empirical formula (on a 1 kg basis) contains C, H, O, N, and S, the formation reaction is written as:

$$C_{(s)} + H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)} + N_{2(g)} + S_{(s, rhombic)} \longrightarrow \text{Solid Fuel}_{(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reference (standard) states are: **Carbon → Graphite (s)**, **Hydrogen → H₂ gas**, **Oxygen → O₂ gas**, **Nitrogen → N₂ gas**, **Sulfur → Rhombic S (s)**.

By convention, the **standard enthalpy of formation of every element in its reference state is ZERO (0 kJ/mol)**. Hence the enthalpy of formation of a compound is essentially the **net internal chemical energy stored within its bonds** relative to its elements.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a brick wall (the solid fuel) that is built from individual loose bricks (elements in standard states). The process of "forming" the wall represents the formation reaction.

- Building the wall (formation) **releases** energy as mortar sets and bonds form → this would be a **negative ΔH_f (exothermic formation)**.
- Pulling the wall apart back into loose bricks would require you to put in **energy** to break the bonds → this would be the **reverse**, a positive enthalpy.

For most solid fuels (coal, wood, biomass), the **ΔH_f is negative** (between **−1000 to −10,000 kJ/kg**), meaning the fuel is in a *lower energy state* than its separated elements. This stored chemical potential energy is precisely what we release during combustion to power turbines, boilers, and engines.

> [!NOTE]
> A more **negative ΔH_f** does NOT mean a more energetic fuel. A more energetic fuel is one whose combustion releases a **larger magnitude of heat**, i.e., a more negative **heat of combustion (ΔH_c)**. The two quantities are inversely linked through Hess's Law.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

| Species | Standard Enthalpy of Formation (ΔH°f) | Physical State |
|---|---|---|
| **C (graphite)** | **0 kJ/mol** | Solid (reference) |
| **H₂ (g)** | **0 kJ/mol** | Gas (reference) |
| **O₂ (g)** | **0 kJ/mol** | Gas (reference) |
| **N₂ (g)** | **0 kJ/mol** | Gas (reference) |
| **S (rhombic)** | **0 kJ/mol** | Solid (reference) |
| **CO₂ (g)** | **−393.5 kJ/mol** | Gas |
| **H₂O (l)** | **−285.8 kJ/mol** | Liquid |
| **H₂O (g)** | **−241.8 kJ/mol** | Gas (vapor) |
| **CO (g)** | **−110.5 kJ/mol** | Gas |
| **SO₂ (g)** | **−296.8 kJ/mol** | Gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These tabulated values form the **thermodynamic backbone** of every fire dynamics and combustion calculation. KTU examiners expect students to know at least the CO₂, H₂O(l), H₂O(g), and CO values by memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

#### A) Why ΔH_f of Solid Fuels is Important in Fire Dynamics

1. **Fire Load Calculation:** The total fire load of a compartment is the total ΔH_c of all combustible contents. Knowing ΔH_f of the original solid fuel is essential to derive ΔH_c.
2. **Pyrolysis Modeling:** When a solid fuel heats up, it decomposes (pyrolysis) into volatile gases and char. Each product has its own ΔH_f, and the difference determines endothermicity of pyrolysis.
3. **Ignition Energy Prediction:** The ΔH_f of a fuel dictates its chemical potential, governing the minimum energy needed to initiate self-sustained combustion.
4. **Smoke and Toxic Gas Yield:** Incomplete combustion of solid fuel yields CO, soot, and hydrocarbons — each of which has its own ΔH_f used in energy balance equations.

#### B) Determination of ΔH_f for Solid Fuels

Unlike pure compounds whose ΔH_f is tabulated, solid fuels (coal, biomass, MSW) are **complex polymeric mixtures** with no single molecular formula. The ΔH_f is determined via three approaches:

1. **Experimental Bomb Calorimetry** (direct measurement of ΔH_c, then back-calculated).
2. **Group Contribution Methods (GCM)** — Benson, Joback, or van Krevelen methods.
3. **Correlation from Ultimate Analysis** — Dulong's Formula and its modifications.

#### C) Dulong's Formula (for Heat of Combustion)

For a solid fuel whose ultimate analysis (mass %) gives **C, H, O, N, S**, the higher heating value (HHV) is:

$$HHV = 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 94\,S \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

Where each elemental symbol represents the **mass percentage** of that element in the fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **H − O/8** corrects for the oxygen already present in the fuel (which is assumed to be bound to hydrogen as water). Dulong's formula is most accurate for **bituminous and anthracite coals**; it overestimates HHV for high-oxygen fuels like biomass and lignite.

#### D) Relationship Between ΔH_f and ΔH_c (Hess's Law)

The combustion reaction of a solid fuel can be split into two virtual steps:

- **Step 1:** Elements → Solid Fuel (ΔH_f of fuel)
- **Step 2:** Solid Fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O (ΔH_c)

By Hess's Law, both pathways yield the same total energy change:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{fuel}) = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{fuel})$$

On a **per kg of fuel basis**, the relationship becomes:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{fuel, kJ/kg}) = \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2)\cdot \frac{C}{100} + \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O,l})\cdot \frac{9H}{100} - \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{fuel, kJ/kg})$$

Where the **9H/100** term arises from the molecular weight ratio (18/2 = 9 g of H₂O per gram of H burned).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta H_f^\circ = \sum H_{\text{prod}}^\circ - \sum H_{\text{react}}^\circ$ | Definition of standard enthalpy of formation | kJ/mol |
| 2 | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2,\ \Delta H = -393.5$ | Combustion of graphite | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO,\ \Delta H = -110.5$ | Formation of CO (incomplete) | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)},\ \Delta H = -285.8$ | Formation of liquid water | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)},\ \Delta H = -241.8$ | Formation of water vapor | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $\Delta H_c = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{prod}) - \sum \Delta H_f(\text{react})$ | Hess's Law for combustion | kJ/mol or kJ/kg |
| 7 | $HHV = 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 94\,S$ | Dulong's formula | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $LHV = HHV - 2442\!\left(\dfrac{9H}{100}\right)$ | Lower heating value (latent heat subtracted) | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{fuel, kJ/kg}) = -\tfrac{32.67\,C}{12} - \tfrac{285.8 \cdot 9H}{100 \cdot 2} - \Delta H_c$ | ΔH_f back-calculated from ΔH_c | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\Delta n_{gas} = n_{gas}(\text{prod}) - n_{gas}(\text{react})$ | Change in gaseous moles (for ΔH→ΔU) | mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the conversion between **HHV and LHV**, and the **Hess's Law** back-calculation. Memorize the multipliers **338, 1442, 94, 2442** — they are universally used in solid-fuel calorimetry.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Boiler Design:** Knowing ΔH_f of coal allows engineers to size heat exchangers and predict flue gas temperatures.
- **Fire Safety Engineering (SFPE Handbook):** Fire load density of a building is the ΔH_c of all contents per unit floor area.
- **Pyrolysis Reactor Design:** Biomass conversion to biochar requires accurate ΔH_f data to design endothermic/exothermic reactor profiles.
- **Cement Kilns & Metallurgy:** Pulverized coal injection rates are computed using ΔH_f and ΔH_c data for efficient combustion.
- **Waste-to-Energy Plants:** Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) has a complex composition; ΔH_f correlations help predict energy recovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: ΔH_c from ΔH_f via Hess's Law (Per Mole Basis)

**Given:** Combustion of methane: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O_{(l)}$

We want to express ΔH_c in terms of standard ΔH_f values.

**Step 1:** Write the general combustion reaction in a balanced form (per mole of fuel).

$$CH_{4(g)} + 2O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow CO_{2(g)} + 2H_2O_{(l)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hess's Law: the total enthalpy change equals the enthalpy of products minus the enthalpy of reactants.

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[\Delta H_f^\circ(CO_2) + 2\,\Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O,l)\right] - \left[\Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4) + 2\,\Delta H_f^\circ(O_2)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute standard reference values. Note that **ΔH_f°(O₂) = 0** by convention since O₂ is an element in its reference state.

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)\right] - \left[\Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4) + 0\right]$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = -393.5 - 571.6 - \Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4)$$

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = -965.1 - \Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4)$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to back-calculate ΔH_f(CH₄) if ΔH_c is known (experimentally, ΔH_c°(CH₄) = **−890.4 kJ/mol**).

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4) = -965.1 - (-890.4) = -74.7\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

This matches the tabulated value of ΔH_f°(CH₄) = **−74.8 kJ/mol**, confirming the methodology. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Back-Calculation of ΔH_f for a Solid Fuel (Per kg Basis)

**Problem:** A bituminous coal has the following ultimate analysis (mass % dry basis): C = 75%, H = 5%, O = 10%, N = 1.5%, S = 1.0%, Ash = 7.5%. The higher heating value measured in a bomb calorimeter is **30,500 kJ/kg**. Determine the enthalpy of formation of the coal on a kJ/kg basis (water produced in liquid form).

**Step 1:** Normalize the ultimate analysis. Since ash does not combust, we typically work on a dry-ash-free (daf) or as-received basis. Here we'll work on the **as-received (ar)** basis.

C, H, O, N, S are already given in mass %.

**Step 2:** Express the combustion reaction on a 1 kg fuel basis.

For 1 kg of coal, mass of elements:
- C: 0.750 kg → moles = 750/12 = 62.5 mol
- H: 0.050 kg → moles = 50/2 = 25 mol
- O: 0.100 kg → moles = 100/32 = 3.125 mol
- S: 0.010 kg → moles = 10/32 = 0.3125 mol

**Step 3:** Construct the balanced combustion equation.

$$C + H_2 + O_2 + S + (\text{ash, N}_2) \longrightarrow \text{Fuel (coal)} \xrightarrow{+ O_2} CO_2 + H_2O + SO_2 + N_2$$

The "fuel → products" reaction (per 1 kg coal) is:

$$\text{Coal}_{(s)} + a\,O_2 \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,SO_2 + e\,N_2$$

Where:
- $b = 62.5$ mol (all C → CO₂)
- $c = 25$ mol (all H → H₂O, since we use HHV: liquid water)
- $d = 0.3125$ mol
- $e = 0.75$ mol (N is inert, forms N₂)

**Step 4:** Compute the oxygen balance.

- O atoms required on RHS: $2(62.5) + 25 + 2(0.3125) = 125 + 25 + 0.625 = 150.625$ mol O atoms
- O atoms already in fuel: 3.125 × 2 = 6.25 mol O atoms
- Net O atoms needed from O₂: 150.625 − 6.25 = 144.375 mol O atoms → 72.1875 mol O₂

So **a = 72.1875 mol**.

**Step 5:** Apply Hess's Law to the combustion reaction.

$$\Delta H_c = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$$

$$\Delta H_c = \left[62.5\,\Delta H_f(CO_2) + 25\,\Delta H_f(H_2O,l) + 0.3125\,\Delta H_f(SO_2) + 0\right] - \left[\Delta H_f(\text{coal}) + 72.1875 \times 0\right]$$

(N₂ and O₂ have ΔH_f = 0 as elements in their reference states)

$$\Delta H_c = 62.5(-393.5) + 25(-285.8) + 0.3125(-296.8) - \Delta H_f(\text{coal})$$

**Step 6:** Numerically evaluate the products' enthalpy.

$$62.5 \times (-393.5) = -24{,}593.75\ \text{kJ}$$

$$25 \times (-285.8) = -7{,}145.0\ \text{kJ}$$

$$0.3125 \times (-296.8) = -92.75\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 7:** Sum and rearrange.

$$\sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) = -24{,}593.75 - 7{,}145.0 - 92.75 = -31{,}831.5\ \text{kJ}$$

$$\Delta H_f(\text{coal}) = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \Delta H_c$$

$$\Delta H_f(\text{coal}) = -31{,}831.5 - (-30{,}500) = -1{,}331.5\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Conclusion:** The enthalpy of formation of the bituminous coal is **−1,331.5 kJ/kg**. The negative sign confirms the fuel is thermodynamically more stable than its elements.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Dulong's Formula Energy Contribution by Each Element

**Step 1:** The combustion enthalpies per kg of each element in its standard state (HHV basis, liquid water):

- C: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$, $\Delta H = -32{,}790$ kJ/kg of C $\approx -32{,}800$ kJ/kg
- H: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$, $\Delta H = -142{,}900$ kJ/kg of H₂ → per kg of H = $2 \times -142{,}900 = -285{,}800$? No, careful: per kg of atomic H is **−142,900 kJ/kg**, but conventionally we keep it in elemental H form (mass of H atoms = H₂ × 0.5 in mass).
- H (per kg of H atoms, atomic mass 1): 1 kg of H atoms = 1 kg-equivalent of H₂O formed → 142,900 kJ/kg H

**Step 2:** The "−O/8" correction.

For every 8 kg of oxygen present in the fuel, 1 kg of hydrogen is "tied up" as water of constitution (e.g., in cellulose –OH groups). This hydrogen is **not available for combustion** and does not contribute to HHV. Hence we subtract O/8 from H.

**Step 3:** The empirical coefficients (per kg of element, in kJ/kg of fuel):
- 338 kJ per kg of C → reflects $32{,}790 / (12/12) \times 0.1233$... Actually 338 = (393.5 × 1000)/12 × (1/100)... The derivation:
  - Per mole C: 393.5 kJ → per kg: $393.5/0.012 = 32{,}792$ kJ/kg, but the coefficient 338 is empirical and slightly different due to using the gross average.
- 1442 for H: $285.8 \times 1000 / 2 \times (1/100) \approx 1429$... The coefficient 1442 reflects energy per kg of H in the HHV formulation: $285.8/(2/1000) = 142{,}900$ per kg of H₂ → divide by 100 and multiply by ~1 (giving 1429); the 1442 arises from standard convention.
- 94 for S: $296.8 \times 1000 / 32 \times (1/100) = 92.75 \approx 94$ kJ per kg of S (rounded up empirically).

> [!NOTE]
> Dulong's formula is **semi-empirical**; the coefficients (338, 1442, 94) are derived by dividing the molar enthalpy of combustion of each element by its atomic mass, with small empirical corrections. KTU does not require students to derive these constants, but understanding their origin is important for problem-solving.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Estimating ΔH_f of Biomass

**Given:** A sample of rice husk has ultimate analysis: C = 38%, H = 5%, O = 36%, N = 0.5%, S = 0.1%, ash = 20.4%. Using Dulong's formula, estimate HHV, then estimate ΔH_f.

**Step 1:** Apply Dulong's formula.

$$HHV = 338(38) + 1442\!\left(5 - \frac{36}{8}\right) + 94(0.1)$$

$$HHV = 12{,}844 + 1442(5 - 4.5) + 9.4$$

$$HHV = 12{,}844 + 1442(0.5) + 9.4 = 12{,}844 + 721 + 9.4 = 13{,}574.4\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Compute ΔH_f per kg of biomass (using products on a per kg basis).

Mass of C in 1 kg = 0.38 kg → mol C = 380/12 = 31.667 mol
Mass of H in 1 kg = 0.05 kg → mol H₂ = 50/2 = 25 mol
Mass of S in 1 kg = 0.001 kg → mol S = 1/32 = 0.03125 mol

$$\sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) = 31.667(-393.5) + 25(-285.8) + 0.03125(-296.8)$$

$$= -12{,}460.97 - 7{,}145.0 - 9.275 = -19{,}615.25\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Hess's Law to back-calculate ΔH_f.

$$\Delta H_f(\text{biomass}) = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \Delta H_c$$

$$\Delta H_f(\text{biomass}) = -19{,}615.25 - (-13{,}574.4) = -6{,}040.85\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Conclusion:** The enthalpy of formation of rice husk is approximately **−6,041 kJ/kg**. The higher (more negative) value compared to coal reflects the higher oxygen content (more bonds already formed in the fuel, more chemical "stability" relative to free elements, less combustion energy available).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hess's Law Cycle for Solid Fuel Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Elements in Standard States: Graphite C(s) + H2(g) + O2(g) + N2(g) + S(s)"]
    B["Standard Enthalpy of Formation Pathway: Elements to Fuel with Enthalpy dHf"]
    C["Solid Fuel in Standard State (C, H, O, N, S mixture)"]
    D["Combustion Reaction with Oxygen Pathway: Enthalpy dHc"]
    E["Combustion Products: CO2(g) + H2O(l) + SO2(g) + N2(g)"]
    F["Direct Formation to Products Pathway: Elements to Products via Enthalpy dHfp"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    A --> F
    F --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Hess's Law cycle states that the **direct path** (Elements → Products, enthalpy = ΔH_fp) must equal the **two-step path** (Elements → Fuel → Products, enthalpy = ΔH_f + ΔH_c). Therefore: **ΔH_fp = ΔH_f + ΔH_c**, which rearranges to the working formula for back-calculating ΔH_f of the solid fuel.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Workflow for Determining ΔH_f of a Solid Fuel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Sample Acquisition and Preparation: Grind and Dry the Solid Fuel"]
    P2["Step 2: Ultimate Analysis: Determine C, H, O, N, S mass percent"]
    P3["Step 3: Bomb Calorimetry: Measure HHV experimentally"]
    P4{"Step 4: Choose Method for Estimation"}
    P5["Path A: Dulong's Formula: HHV = 338C + 1442(H minus O divided by 8) + 94S"]
    P6["Path B: Group Contribution Method: Benson or Joback correlations"]
    P7["Step 5: Apply Hess's Law: dHf = sum dHf of Products minus dHc"]
    P8["Step 6: Output: Final dHf of Solid Fuel in kJ per kg"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Transformation Sequence in Solid Fuel Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["Chemical Potential Energy Stored in Fuel: |dHf| around 1000 to 10000 kJ per kg"]
```
