```mermaid
    E2["Energy Released by Combustion: |dHc| around 10000 to 35000 kJ per kg"]
    E3["Thermal Energy: Heat of Combustion converted to Temperature Rise"]
    E4["Mechanical Work: Turbine or Engine converts Heat to Work"]
    E5["Electrical Energy: Generator converts Mechanical to Electrical"]

    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E4 --> E5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram clarifies a critical conceptual point that KTU examiners love to test: the **enthalpy of formation of a solid fuel** is **NOT** the energy released during combustion. The energy released is the **heat of combustion (ΔH_c)**, which is a **different** (and typically much larger in magnitude) quantity. ΔH_f is the energy difference between the fuel and its elements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** Define the term *Standard Enthalpy of Formation* of a solid fuel. Why is the value taken as **zero** for elements in their standard states?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f) of a solid fuel is the enthalpy change when **one mole (or unit mass) of the fuel is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states (298.15 K, 1 atm)**. For elements in their most stable reference state at standard conditions, the enthalpy of formation is assigned a value of **zero** by convention. This is a thermodynamic reference frame: it provides a baseline against which the chemical potential of all compounds can be compared. Without this convention, absolute enthalpies of formation would be undefined.

#### **Question 2:** State **Hess's Law** as applicable to the combustion of solid fuels. Write the relationship between ΔH_f and ΔH_c of a fuel.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Hess's Law states that the total enthalpy change in a chemical reaction is **independent of the pathway** taken, depending only on the **initial and final states**.

For combustion of a solid fuel:

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{reactants})$$

Rearranged to back-calculate the enthalpy of formation of the fuel:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ (\text{fuel}) = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{products}) - \Delta H_c^\circ$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **QUESTION A: (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A sample of bituminous coal has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): C = 72%, H = 4.8%, O = 9.6%, N = 1.4%, S = 1.2%, Ash = 11%. The higher heating value (HHV) was determined experimentally in a bomb calorimeter as **29,800 kJ/kg**. Calculate the **enthalpy of formation of the coal in kJ/kg** using Hess's Law. Assume water is in liquid form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using **Dulong's formula**, estimate the HHV of the same coal and compare it with the experimentally measured value. Comment on the deviation. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Identify moles of each element per kg of fuel.**
- C: 720/12 = 60 mol
- H: 48/2 = 24 mol
- O: 96/32 = 3 mol
- S: 12/32 = 0.375 mol

**Step 2: Combustion reaction per kg of coal.**

$$\text{Coal} + a\,O_2 \rightarrow 60\,CO_2 + 24\,H_2O + 0.375\,SO_2$$

**Step 3: Oxygen atom balance.**

O atoms on RHS = $2(60) + 24 + 2(0.375) = 144.75$ mol O atoms
O atoms in fuel = 3 × 2 = 6 mol O atoms
O atoms needed from O₂ = 144.75 − 6 = 138.75 mol → a = 69.375 mol

**Step 4: Apply Hess's Law.**

$$\Delta H_c = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \Delta H_f(\text{coal})$$

$$-29{,}800 = 60(-393.5) + 24(-285.8) + 0.375(-296.8) - \Delta H_f(\text{coal})$$

$$-29{,}800 = -23{,}610 - 6{,}859.2 - 111.3 - \Delta H_f(\text{coal})$$

$$-29{,}800 = -30{,}580.5 - \Delta H_f(\text{coal})$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_f(\text{coal}) = -30{,}580.5 + 29{,}800 = -780.5\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Apply Dulong's formula.**

$$HHV = 338(72) + 1442\!\left(4.8 - \frac{9.6}{8}\right) + 94(1.2)$$

$$HHV = 24{,}336 + 1442(4.8 - 1.2) + 112.8$$

$$HHV = 24{,}336 + 1442(3.6) + 112.8 = 24{,}336 + 5{,}191.2 + 112.8$$

$$\boxed{HHV = 29{,}640\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**Step 2: Compare with experimental value.**

Experimental HHV = 29,800 kJ/kg
Dulong's estimate = 29,640 kJ/kg
**Deviation = (29,800 − 29,640)/29,800 × 100% ≈ 0.54%**

**Comment:** The Dulong estimate is in excellent agreement with the experimental HHV (deviation < 1%) because the coal has relatively low oxygen content (9.6%) and is dominated by carbon. Dulong's formula works best for bituminous and anthracite coals with **O < 15%** and gives larger errors for high-oxygen fuels like biomass and lignite.

---

#### **QUESTION B: (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Dulong formula** for estimating the calorific value of a solid fuel. State the role of the **(H − O/8)** term. Derive the contribution of carbon and hydrogen individually to the HHV. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lignite coal sample has ultimate analysis: C = 50%, H = 4%, O = 22%, N = 1%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 22.5%. Determine:
  (i) HHV using Dulong's formula
  (ii) LHV (lower heating value)
  (iii) Enthalpy of formation per kg, given that CO₂ and H₂O(l) are the major products. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1: State Dulong's formula.**

$$HHV = 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 94\,S\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

where C, H, O, S are mass percentages of the corresponding elements.

**Step 2: Role of (H − O/8).**
The term (H − O/8) corrects for **oxygen already bound** to hydrogen in the fuel (e.g., in hydroxyl groups of cellulose, lignin, or moisture). For every **8 kg of oxygen** present, 1 kg of hydrogen is assumed to be in the form of H₂O of constitution (organic hydroxyl). This hydrogen is **not available for combustion** and does not release heat. Hence the oxygen content reduces the effective combustible hydrogen, and we subtract O/8 from H.

**Step 3: Derive the contribution of carbon.**
The combustion of carbon: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$, ΔH = −393.5 kJ/mol = −32,792 kJ per kg of C.
Dulong's empirical coefficient = **338 kJ per kg of fuel per mass% of C**, i.e., 338 × 100 = 33,800 kJ per kg of pure C (a close approximation of the theoretical 32,792 with a safety/correction factor of ~3%).

**Step 4: Derive the contribution of hydrogen.**
The combustion: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$, ΔH = −285.8 kJ/mol = −142,900 kJ per kg of H₂ = **−142,900 kJ per kg of H atoms**.
Dulong's coefficient: 1442 × 100 = 144,200 kJ per kg of pure H atoms (matches the theoretical 142,900 to within ~1%).

**Step 5: Coefficient for sulfur.**
Combustion: $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$, ΔH = −296.8 kJ/mol = −9,275 kJ per kg of S = **9,275 kJ per kg**.
Dulong's: 94 × 100 = 9,400 kJ per kg of pure S (matches within ~1.3%).

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**(i) HHV using Dulong's formula:**

$$HHV = 338(50) + 1442\!\left(4 - \frac{22}{8}\right) + 94(0.5)$$

$$HHV = 16{,}900 + 1442(4 - 2.75) + 47$$

$$HHV = 16{,}900 + 1442(1.25) + 47 = 16{,}900 + 1{,}802.5 + 47$$

$$\boxed{HHV = 18{,}749.5\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**(ii) LHV:**

The latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C = 2,442 kJ/kg of water.
Water formed per kg of fuel = 9 × H/100 = 9 × 4/100 = 0.36 kg H₂O per kg of fuel.

$$LHV = HHV - 2442 \times 0.36 = 18{,}749.5 - 879.12$$

$$\boxed{LHV = 17{,}870.38\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**(iii) Enthalpy of formation per kg:**

Per kg of fuel:
- Mol C = 500/12 = 41.667 mol
- Mol H = 40/2 = 20 mol
- Mol S = 5/32 = 0.15625 mol

**Oxygen balance (H₂O is liquid):**
O atoms on RHS = 2(41.667) + 20 + 2(0.15625) = 83.333 + 20 + 0.3125 = 103.646 mol
O atoms in fuel = 22/32 × 2 = 1.375 mol
Net O atoms from O₂ = 103.646 − 1.375 = 102.271 mol → mol O₂ = 51.135

**Sum of ΔH_f of products:**

$$\sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) = 41.667(-393.5) + 20(-285.8) + 0.15625(-296.8)$$

$$= -16{,}395.94 - 5{,}716.0 - 46.375 = -22{,}158.32\ \text{kJ}$$

**Apply Hess's Law (using LHV or HHV consistently with state of water):**

Since water is liquid (HHV basis), we use HHV = 18,749.5 kJ/kg.

$$\Delta H_f(\text{fuel}) = -22{,}158.32 - (-18{,}749.5) = -3{,}408.82\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_f(\text{lignite}) \approx -3{,}409\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$

The more negative value (compared to bituminous coal at −780.5 kJ/kg) confirms that lignite has higher oxygen content, more pre-formed bonds, and lower energy yield on combustion.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert elemental mass % to moles** before substituting into Hess's Law equation. The ΔH_f values are in **kJ/mol**, so **mass % must be divided by atomic mass**.
> 2. **Mixing up HHV and LHV** in the latent heat subtraction: use **2,442 kJ/kg of H₂O**, and the H₂O mass is **9H/100** per kg of fuel, NOT H/100.
> 3. **Using H₂O(g) instead of H₂O(l)** without realizing it: the difference is **44 kJ/mol (latent heat of vaporization)**. Bomb calorimeters yield HHV (liquid), so use **−285.8 kJ/mol**.
> 4. **Sign convention errors:** ΔH_f of the **fuel** is **negative** (fuel is more stable than elements). ΔH_c is also negative (combustion is exothermic). The sign must be tracked carefully when rearranging Hess's Law.
> 5. **Ignoring ash and moisture** in the ultimate analysis. Dulong's formula uses only combustible C, H, O, S; ash and moisture are **inert**.
> 6. **Using O₂'s ΔH_f as nonzero** in Hess's Law: it is **0 kJ/mol** because O₂ is an element in its reference state. Same for N₂.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Enthalpy of Formation of Solid Fuels**

- ✅ **Definition:** ΔH_f° = enthalpy to form 1 mole/unit mass of fuel from elements in standard states (298.15 K, 1 atm).
- ✅ **Reference values (memorize):** C(gr) = 0, H₂ = 0, O₂ = 0, N₂ = 0, S(rh) = 0, CO₂ = −393.5, H₂O(l) = −285.8, H₂O(g) = −241.8, CO = −110.5, SO₂ = −296.8 (all kJ/mol).
- ✅ **Hess's Law for combustion:** ΔH_c = ΣΔH_f(products) − ΣΔH_f(reactants). Rearranged: ΔH_f(fuel) = ΣΔH_f(products) − ΔH_c.
- ✅ **Dulong's Formula (HHV in kJ/kg):** $HHV = 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 94\,S$.
- ✅ **LHV formula:** $LHV = HHV - 2442 \times \dfrac{9H}{100}$.
- ✅ **Conversion rule:** mass % of element X → moles of X per kg of fuel = (mass % × 10) / (atomic mass of X).
- ✅ **Solid fuels are negative ΔH_f** (between **−1,000 to −10,000 kJ/kg**). Bituminous coal ≈ −1,300; lignite ≈ −3,400; biomass ≈ −6,000.
- ✅ **Most negative ΔH_f ≠ most energetic fuel.** More negative ΔH_f means more pre-formed bonds; **less energy is released on combustion.**
- ✅ **Dulong's formula accuracy:** Best for bituminous/anthracite coals; **less accurate for high-oxygen fuels** (biomass, lignite, MSW).
- ✅ **In KTU exams, always state the standard state of water** (liquid → HHV; vapor → LHV) explicitly to avoid losing marks.
- ✅ **ΔH_f of O₂, N₂, and elements in their standard states is zero** — a frequently tested trick question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Flame Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Flame Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Equilibrium Flame Temperature** (also called the **Adiabatic Flame Temperature**, AFT) is defined as the temperature attained by the products of combustion when the combustion process is assumed to be:

1. **Adiabatic** (no heat loss to surroundings, $Q = 0$),
2. **At constant pressure** (for open flames, typical engineering assumption) or **at constant volume** (for closed systems like internal combustion engines),
3. **Occurs under chemical equilibrium conditions** — meaning dissociation reactions are accounted for and the Gibbs free energy is minimized at the final state.

Mathematically, the Equilibrium Flame Temperature ($T_{ad}$ or $T_{eq}$) is the final temperature at which the **total enthalpy of the products** equals the **total enthalpy of the reactants** (since $\Delta H = 0$ for an adiabatic, non-work process at constant pressure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (verbatim for exam):** *"The adiabatic flame temperature is the temperature of the products of combustion when the combustion is complete and no heat is lost to the surroundings, but the composition of products corresponds to chemical equilibrium at the final temperature and pressure."*

The two key assumptions that distinguish it from other flame temperature concepts:
- **Complete combustion** is replaced by **equilibrium combustion** (allowing for dissociation).
- The **chemical equilibrium composition** is used (not just stoichiometric complete combustion products).

> [!NOTE]
> The term "Adiabatic Flame Temperature" is technically the upper bound (assuming complete combustion, no dissociation). The strictly correct thermodynamic name is "Equilibrium Flame Temperature" because it includes the effects of chemical dissociation that occur at high temperatures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sealed Pressure Cooker Analogy

Imagine you are cooking rice in a **sealed pressure cooker** (this is your adiabatic, constant-volume system). You burn a small amount of fuel inside. Because no heat escapes, **all the chemical energy released by combustion is trapped and converted into thermal energy of the gases**. The temperature inside rises dramatically. But here's the twist — as the temperature climbs to extreme values (say above **2000 K**), some of the CO₂ and H₂O molecules are so energetic that they **"fall apart"** back into CO, H₂, and O₂ (this is called **dissociation**). This "falling apart" actually **absorbs** some of the heat, preventing the temperature from reaching its theoretical maximum. The final temperature you measure in the cooker is the **Equilibrium Flame Temperature** — where energy released by combustion exactly equals the energy stored in heating all the gas AND the energy used to break apart some molecules.

## 1.3 Two Distinct Flame Temperature Concepts in KTU Syllabus

| Concept | Dissociation Considered? | Typical Value (CH₄ + Air) |
|---|---|---|
| **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** | No — assumes 100% complete combustion | ~**2222 K** |
| **Equilibrium Flame Temperature (EFT)** | Yes — accounts for dissociation at high T | ~**1995 K** to ~2200 K depending on dissociation level |

## 1.4 Standard Reference State and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> All thermodynamic evaluations use the **standard reference state** of **298.15 K** and **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**. Standard enthalpies of formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$) are tabulated at this reference.

Key constants used:
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- Standard pressure: $P^\circ = 1 \; \text{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
- Air composition (mole basis): 1 mol O₂ + **3.76 mol N₂** (which gives 4.76 mol air)
- Reference temperature: $T_{ref} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$

## 1.5 Significance in Fire Science and Engineering

- Determines the **maximum temperature a fire can reach** — critical for structural fire resistance design.
- Governs the **rate of heat release** and **radiative output** of fires.
- Influences **dissociation limits** — explains why very rich or very lean mixtures produce lower temperatures.
- Essential for sizing **fire protection systems**, **ventilation design**, and **explosion venting** calculations.
- Used in **combustion efficiency** and **emission (NOx, CO)** prediction models.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Methods to Determine Equilibrium Flame Temperature

### Method 1: The **Enthalpy Balance Method** (Direct Method)
This method uses the First Law of Thermodynamics for a steady-flow, adiabatic, constant-pressure process:

$$\sum_{reactants} n_i \, \bar{h}_i (T_{in}) = \sum_{products} n_j \, \bar{h}_j (T_{ad})$$

Where $\bar{h}_i$ is the **molar enthalpy** of species $i$, and $n$ represents moles.

Each molar enthalpy is decomposed into:
$$\bar{h}_i(T) = \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ + \left[ \bar{H}_T^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right]_i$$

Where:
- $\Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ$ = Standard enthalpy of formation at 298 K (kJ/kmol)
- $\left[ \bar{H}_T^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right]_i$ = Sensible enthalpy from 298 K to $T$ (kJ/kmol)

The energy balance then becomes:
$$\sum_{products} n_j \left[ \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right]_j = \sum_{reactants} n_i \bar{h}_i (T_{in}) - \sum_{products} n_j \Delta \bar{H}_{f,j}^\circ$$

The right side simplifies to the **Heat of Combustion** (with sign convention) plus any sensible enthalpy of reactants.

### Method 2: The **Equilibrium Constant Method** (Iterative)
This method considers dissociation reactions. For each dissociation reaction, the equilibrium constant $K_p(T)$ must be satisfied:

$$K_p = \prod_i \left( \frac{p_i}{P^\circ} \right)^{\nu_i}$$

The procedure:
1. Assume complete combustion (no dissociation) → get a first guess of $T_{ad}$.
2. At this temperature, evaluate $K_p$ for the dissociation reactions.
3. Solve the simultaneous equations for the equilibrium composition.
4. Re-evaluate $T_{ad}$ from the energy balance with the new composition.
5. Iterate until convergence.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THESE — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum n_i h_i^{react} = \sum n_j h_j^{prod}$ | First Law for adiabatic flame (energy conservation) | kJ |
| 2 | $h_i(T) = \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ + (H_T^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_i$ | Total molar enthalpy of species $i$ | kJ/kmol |
| 3 | $\Delta H_c = -\sum_{prod} n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ + \sum_{react} n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ$ | Standard heat of combustion (at 298 K) | kJ/kmol fuel |
| 4 | $\sum n_j (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_j = -\Delta H_c + \sum n_i (H_{T_{in}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_i$ | Working equation for AFT (no dissociation) | kJ/kmol fuel |
| 5 | $K_p(T) = \prod (p_i/P^\circ)^{\nu_i}$ | Equilibrium constant expression | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ(T)}{R \, T}$ | Van't Hoff relation linking $K_p$ to Gibbs energy | dimensionless |
| 7 | $p_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} \cdot P$ | Partial pressure from mole fraction | atm or kPa |
| 8 | $\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio (stoichiometric basis) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{ad}(\text{stoich}) > T_{ad}(\text{lean}) > T_{ad}(\text{rich})$ | Maximum AFT occurs at stoichiometric mixture | — |
| 10 | $T_{ad} \uparrow$ as $T_{preheat} \uparrow$ | Preheat of reactants raises flame temperature | — |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown: Enthalpy Method

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric combustion equation** for 1 kmol of fuel.
- Include the N₂ from air (3.76 mol N₂ per mol O₂).

**Step 2 — Define the energy balance equation**.
- At constant pressure, adiabatic: $H_{reactants}(T_{in}) = H_{products}(T_{ad})$.

**Step 3 — Substitute enthalpy expressions** using the sensible enthalpy form.
- For reactants at $T_{in}$: $h_i = \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ + (H_{T_{in}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_i$.
- For products at $T_{ad}$: $h_j = \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ + (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_j$.

**Step 4 — Rearrange** so the unknown $T_{ad}$ is on the left and known quantities on the right.

**Step 5 — Tabulate the sensible enthalpy** $(H_T^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)$ for each product gas at trial temperatures.
- These tables are called **Ideal Gas Property Tables** or **JANAF Tables**.

**Step 6 — Iterate** by guessing $T_{ad}$, computing LHS, comparing to RHS, and adjusting.

**Step 7 — Interpolate** between two trial temperatures for final accuracy.

## 2.4 The Dissociation Phenomenon — Why Equilibrium ≠ Adiabatic

At high temperatures (typically **> 1800 K**), a fraction of the combustion products "dissociate" back into reactants, absorbing energy:

- $\text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ — Carbon dioxide dissociation
- $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ — Water vapor dissociation
- $\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{H}$ — Hydrogen dissociation (very high T)
- $\text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{O}$ — Oxygen dissociation (very high T)
- $\text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}$ — NO formation (Zeldovich mechanism)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** Dissociation is **endothermic** → it absorbs energy → reduces $T_{ad}$. The higher the temperature, the more dissociation, the lower the effective $T_{ad}$ would have been. Equilibrium gives the **true** final temperature accounting for this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire protection engineering:** Determines the upper bound for compartment fire temperatures (e.g., **ASTM E119**, **ISO 834** fire curves).
- **Boiler and furnace design:** Sets material selection limits (refractory lining choice).
- **Gas turbine combustor design:** Peak temperature must be limited to prevent blade failure.
- **Pollutant formation modeling:** Thermal NOx formation is exponentially sensitive to $T_{ad}$ (Zeldovich mechanism).
- **Explosion venting:** $T_{ad}$ of a fuel-air mixture determines the peak explosion pressure in a vented enclosure.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The General Energy Balance for Adiabatic Flame Temperature

**Starting Point — First Law of Thermodynamics** (steady-flow, no work, adiabatic):

$$dH = \delta Q - \delta W_{non-PV} \quad \text{(neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes)}$$

For an adiabatic process, $\delta Q = 0$, and if we assume no shaft work and negligible $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$:

$$dH = 0 \implies H_{reactants} = H_{products}$$

**Expressed in molar form:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{R} n_i \, \bar{h}_i (T_{in}) = \sum_{j=1}^{P} n_j \, \bar{h}_j (T_{ad})$$

Where $R$ = number of reactant species, $P$ = number of product species.

**Now substitute the standard enthalpy decomposition:**

$$\bar{h}_i(T) = \underbrace{\Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ}_{\text{chemical}} + \underbrace{\left( \bar{H}_T^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i}_{\text{sensible (thermal)}}$$

**Substituting back:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{R} n_i \left[ \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ + \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i \right] = \sum_{j=1}^{P} n_j \left[ \Delta \bar{H}_{f,j}^\circ + \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_j \right]$$

**Rearranging to isolate the unknown $T_{ad}$ on one side:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{P} n_j \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_j = \sum_{i=1}^{R} n_i \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ - \sum_{j=1}^{P} n_j \Delta \bar{H}_{f,j}^\circ + \sum_{i=1}^{R} n_i \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i$$

**Recognizing the right side:**

- The first two terms together = **Standard Heat of Combustion** = $\Delta \bar{H}_c^\circ$ (note sign convention: combustion is exothermic, so $\Delta H_c^\circ$ is negative for the products-from-reactants convention; the heat **released** = $-\Delta H_c^\circ$).
- The third term = **Sensible enthalpy of preheated reactants**.

**Final Working Equation:**

$$\boxed{ \sum_{j=1}^{P} n_j \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_j = -\Delta \bar{H}_c^\circ + \sum_{i=1}^{R} n_i \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i }$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Methane Combustion with Air

**Problem:** Determine the **adiabatic flame temperature** (assuming complete combustion, no dissociation) when methane (CH₄) is burned stoichiometrically with air, both initially at **298 K**. Use the data provided below.

**Given Data (Standard Enthalpies of Formation at 298 K):**

| Species | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ/kmol) |
|---|---|
| CH₄ (g) | **−74,850** |
| O₂ (g) | 0 |
| N₂ (g) | 0 |
| CO₂ (g) | **−393,520** |
| H₂O (g) | **−241,820** |

**Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation with air:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard heat of combustion:**

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}) \right] - \left[ \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_4) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2) + 7.52 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{N}_2) \right]$$

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[ (-393{,}520) + 2(-241{,}820) \right] - \left[ (-74{,}850) + 0 + 0 \right]$$

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[ -393{,}520 - 483{,}640 \right] - \left[ -74{,}850 \right]$$

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = -877{,}160 + 74{,}850 = -802{,}310 \; \text{kJ/kmol CH}_4$$

> The negative sign indicates the reaction is exothermic. The **heat released** is **802,310 kJ per kmol of CH₄**.

**Step 3 — Apply the energy balance equation** (reactants at 298 K → no preheat, last term = 0):

$$1 \cdot \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_{\text{CO}_2} + 2 \cdot \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} + 7.52 \cdot \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_{\text{N}_2} = 802{,}310 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Set up iteration.** Use tabulated values of $(\bar{H}_T^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ)$ for each species.

**Trial 1: Assume $T_{ad} = 2000$ K**

From ideal gas property tables:

| Species | $(\bar{H}_{T}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ)$ at 2000 K (kJ/kmol) | Moles | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | **91,440** | 1 | 91,440 |
| H₂O | **72,790** | 2 | 145,580 |
| N₂ | **56,140** | 7.52 | 422,173 |
| **Total** | | | **659,193** |

Compare: 659,193 < 802,310 → **Temperature must be higher.**

**Trial 2: Assume $T_{ad} = 2300$ K**

| Species | $(\bar{H}_T^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ)$ at 2300 K (kJ/kmol) | Moles | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | **112,940** | 1 | 112,940 |
| H₂O | **89,140** | 2 | 178,280 |
| N₂ | **67,950** | 7.52 | 510,984 |
| **Total** | | | **802,204** |

Compare: 802,204 ≈ 802,310 → **Very close! Slight difference of 106 kJ.**

**Step 5 — Linear interpolation between 2300 K and 2320 K.**

At 2300 K: Energy absorbed = 802,204 kJ (short by 106 kJ)
At 2320 K: Assume energy absorbed ≈ 802,204 + (Δ from tables) ≈ 810,000 kJ (estimate)

A more precise interpolation:
$$T_{ad} \approx 2300 + \frac{802{,}310 - 802{,}204}{810{,}000 - 802{,}204} \times 20 \approx 2300.27 \; \text{K}$$

$$\boxed{ T_{ad} \approx 2300 \; \text{K} \;\; \text{(or 2027°C)} }$$

This is the **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** assuming complete combustion. With dissociation, the **Equilibrium Flame Temperature** would be lower, approximately **2180–2200 K**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Parameters on $T_{ad}$

| Parameter Change | Effect on $T_{ad}$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Increase in **excess air** (lean mixture) | **Decrease** | More N₂ and O₂ to heat up; O₂ has low $\Delta H_f^\circ$ |
| Increase in **preheat temperature** of reactants | **Increase** | Reactants carry sensible enthalpy into the system |
| Increase in **fuel-richness** (equivalence ratio > 1) | **Decrease** (for most hydrocarbons) | Incomplete combustion; unburned fuel absorbs heat |
| Increase in **pressure** (constant volume) | **Increase** (slightly) | Dissociation is suppressed at high pressure |
| **Dissociation** allowance | **Decrease** | Endothermic dissociation absorbs energy |
| **Heat loss** to surroundings | **Decrease** | Energy leaves the system |

## 3.4 Effect of Dissociation: Quantitative Demonstration

For CH₄ + 2O₂ at high T, the dissociation of CO₂ and H₂O is significant. Let's say at the equilibrium temperature, **8% of CO₂ dissociates** and **5% of H₂O dissociates**.

**Modified reaction equation** (using extent of dissociation variables $x_1$ and $x_2$):

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 7.52\text{N}_2 \to (1-x_1)\text{CO}_2 + x_1 \text{CO} + \tfrac{x_1}{2}\text{O}_2 + (2-x_2)\text{H}_2\text{O} + x_2 \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{x_2}{2}\text{O}_2 + 7.52\text{N}_2$$

**Total O₂ in products:** $\tfrac{x_1}{2} + \tfrac{x_2}{2} = \tfrac{x_1 + x_2}{2}$

**Equilibrium constraints** (two equations):
$$K_{p,1}(T) = \frac{(p_\text{CO}) (p_{\text{O}_2})^{1/2}}{(p_{\text{CO}_2}) \, P^{\circ \, 1/2}}, \quad K_{p,2}(T) = \frac{(p_{\text{H}_2}) (p_{\text{O}_2})^{1/2}}{(p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}) \, P^{\circ \, 1/2}}$$

**Energy balance with dissociation:**

$$\sum n_j (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_j = -\Delta H_c^\circ - x_1 \cdot \Delta H_{diss,\text{CO}_2} - x_2 \cdot \Delta H_{diss,\text{H}_2\text{O}}$$

> Where $\Delta H_{diss}$ is the enthalpy of dissociation (positive value, since dissociation is endothermic).

**Iterative procedure:**
1. Guess $T_{ad}^{(0)}$.
2. Look up $K_p$ for both dissociation reactions at this T.
3. Solve simultaneous equations for $x_1, x_2$.
4. Compute new LHS of energy equation; solve for new $T_{ad}^{(1)}$.
5. Repeat until $|T_{ad}^{(k+1)} - T_{ad}^{(k)}| < \epsilon$.

**Typical result for CH₄ at 1 atm:** $T_{eq} \approx 2200 \; \text{K}$ (compared to AFT ≈ 2300 K).
**At 10 atm:** Dissociation suppressed → $T_{eq} \approx 2280 \; \text{K}$.

## 3.5 Special Case: Constant-Volume Adiabatic Flame Temperature ($T_{ad,v}$)

For a closed system (e.g., bomb calorimeter, internal combustion engine cylinder), the energy balance uses **internal energy** instead of enthalpy:

$$\sum_{i} n_i \, \bar{u}_i (T_{in}) = \sum_{j} n_j \, \bar{u}_j (T_{ad,v})$$

Using the relation $\bar{u} = \bar{h} - P\bar{v} = \bar{h} - RT$ for ideal gases:

$$\sum_{j} n_j \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad,v}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ - R T_{ad,v} \right)_j = \sum_i n_i \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i + (-\Delta H_c^\circ) - (n_R - n_P) R T_{in}$$

Where $n_R$ and $n_P$ are total moles of reactants and products.

> [!NOTE]
> For most hydrocarbon-air combustion, $n_P > n_R$ (more moles of products), so the $-RT$ term reduces the available energy → $T_{ad,v} < T_{ad,p}$ in some cases. However, the lack of flow work often makes $T_{ad,v} > T_{ad,p}$ in practice. **KTU frequently tests this distinction.**

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Determining Equilibrium Flame Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Add N2 from Air: 3.76 mol N2 per mol O2]
    C --> D[Step 1: Standard Enthalpy Calculation]
    D --> D1[Compute Standard Heat of Combustion ΔHc°]
    D1 --> E[Step 2: Energy Balance Setup]
    E --> E1[Apply First Law: H_reactants = H_products]
    E1 --> F[Step 3: Initial Guess of Tad]
    F --> F1[Assume complete combustion, no dissociation]
    F1 --> G[Step 4: Tabulate Sensible Enthalpies]
    G --> G1[Look up HT° - H298° for each product gas]
    G1 --> H[Step 5: Iterate with Method Choice]
    H --> H1{Choose Method}
    H1 -->|Method 1| I1[Enthalpy Balance Method]
    H1 -->|Method 2| I2[Equilibrium Constant Method]
    I1 --> I1a[Compute LHS of energy equation]
    I1a --> I1b[Compare to -ΔHc° = RHS]
    I1b --> I1c{Converged?}
    I1c -->|No| I1d[Adjust T and Re-tabulate]
    I1d --> I1a
    I1c -->|Yes| J[Report Tad]
    I2 --> I2a[Evaluate Kp for dissociation reactions at trial T]
    I2a --> I2b[Solve simultaneous equations for x1, x2, ...]
    I2b --> I2c[Re-compute LHS with new product composition]
    I2c --> I2d{Converged?}
    I2d -->|No| I2e[Adjust T and Re-evaluate Kp]
    I2e --> I2a
    I2d -->|Yes| J
    J --> K[Apply Corrections]
    K --> K1[Excess air effect]
    K --> K2[Preheat effect]
    K --> K3[Dissociation correction]
    K --> K4[Heat loss correction]
    K1 --> L[Final Equilibrium Flame Temperature Teq]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Engineering Application: Fire Protection, Combustor Design, Emission Modeling]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram: Factors Influencing Equilibrium Flame Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equilibrium Flame Temperature T_eq] --> B[Composition of Fuel-Air Mixture]
    A --> C[Initial Temperature of Reactants]
    A --> D[System Pressure]

    A --> F[Equivalence Ratio phi]
    B --> B1[Lean: Excess air → Lower T_eq]
```

## 4.5 Diagram: Iterative Solution Procedure for Enthalpy Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Known fuel, air, T_in] --> B[Compute ΔHc° from formation enthalpies]
    B --> C[Assume trial T_ad value]
    C --> D[Tabulate HT° - H298° for all products at T_ad]
    D --> E[Compute LHS = sum of n_j times HT° - H298°]
    E --> F{Compare LHS to -ΔHc°}
    F -->|LHS less than -ΔHc°| G[T_ad too low → Increase T]
    F -->|LHS greater than -ΔHc°| H[T_ad too high → Decrease T]
    F -->|LHS equals -ΔHc°| I[Converged → T_ad is final answer]
    G --> C
    H --> C
    I --> J[Report T_ad with units K or °C]
```
