# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define equilibrium flame temperature. How is it different from adiabatic flame temperature?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Equilibrium Flame Temperature (EFT)** is the temperature of the combustion products when the combustion is adiabatic (no heat loss) and the products reach **chemical equilibrium** at the final state. It accounts for the fact that at high temperatures, a portion of the CO₂ and H₂O dissociates back into CO, H₂, and O₂, which absorbs energy.
>
> The **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** is a special case of EFT where **complete combustion is assumed** with no dissociation. Therefore, AFT is always **higher** than EFT for the same fuel-air mixture. For example, for CH₄ + air at stoichiometric conditions: AFT ≈ 2300 K, while EFT ≈ 2200 K.
>
> **Key distinction:** AFT assumes frozen composition (products formed stoichiometrically), while EFT allows composition to shift via dissociation until chemical equilibrium is reached.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three factors that affect the equilibrium flame temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary factors that affect the equilibrium flame temperature are:

1. **Equivalence Ratio (φ) / Air-Fuel Ratio:** The maximum $T_{eq}$ occurs at stoichiometric conditions (φ = 1). Both lean (excess air) and rich (deficient air) mixtures produce lower temperatures because the extra N₂, O₂, or unburned fuel absorbs heat.

2. **Preheating of Reactants:** Increasing the initial temperature of reactants adds sensible enthalpy to the system, which raises the final $T_{eq}$.

3. **System Pressure:** Higher pressure suppresses dissociation (Le Chatelier's principle), so $T_{eq}$ increases with pressure for reactions that increase moles of gas.

4. *(Bonus, if asked for more)* Heat losses, dissociation extent, and fuel composition also affect $T_{eq}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the equilibrium flame temperature using the enthalpy balance method for a constant-pressure adiabatic combustion process. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Methane (CH₄) is burned completely with 150% theoretical air (i.e., 50% excess air), both initially at 298 K. Calculate the adiabatic flame temperature using the following data: ΔH_f°(CH₄) = −74,850 kJ/kmol, ΔH_f°(CO₂) = −393,520 kJ/kmol, ΔH_f°(H₂O, g) = −241,820 kJ/kmol. Use ideal gas property tables for sensible enthalpy values. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-flow process.
2. Adiabatic (Q = 0) and no shaft work (W = 0).
3. Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy.
4. Ideal gas behavior.
5. Complete combustion (no dissociation for this derivation).

**Derivation:**

From the First Law for a steady-flow adiabatic system:
$$H_{reactants} = H_{products}$$

Expressing in molar form:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{R} n_i \, \bar{h}_i (T_{in}) = \sum_{j=1}^{P} n_j \, \bar{h}_j (T_{ad})$$

Each molar enthalpy is decomposed:
$$\bar{h}_i(T) = \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ + \left( \bar{H}_T^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i$$

Substituting:
$$\sum_i n_i \left[ \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ + \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i \right] = \sum_j n_j \left[ \Delta \bar{H}_{f,j}^\circ + \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_j \right]$$

Rearranging:
$$\sum_j n_j \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_j = \sum_i n_i \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^\circ - \sum_j n_j \Delta \bar{H}_{f,j}^\circ + \sum_i n_i \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i$$

Recognizing the standard heat of combustion:
$$\boxed{ \sum_j n_j \left( \bar{H}_{T_{ad}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_j = -\Delta \bar{H}_c^\circ + \sum_i n_i \left( \bar{H}_{T_{in}}^\circ - \bar{H}_{298}^\circ \right)_i }$$

The procedure: Assume $T_{ad}$, compute LHS, compare to RHS, iterate.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the balanced equation with 150% theoretical air:**

Stoichiometric O₂ = 2 mol. With 150% theoretical air → 3 mol O₂ supplied. Excess O₂ = 1 mol.

$$\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{O}_2 + 3(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + 11.28\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the heat of combustion:**

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[ (-393{,}520) + 2(-241{,}820) \right] - \left[ (-74{,}850) \right] = -877{,}160 + 74{,}850 = -802{,}310 \; \text{kJ/kmol}$$

So the **heat released** is **802,310 kJ**.

**Step 3 — Set up the energy balance** (reactants at 298 K, so preheat term = 0):

$$1 \cdot (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_{\text{CO}_2} + 2 \cdot (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} + 1 \cdot (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_{\text{O}_2} + 11.28 \cdot (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_{\text{N}_2} = 802{,}310$$

**Step 4 — Trial and error** (using ideal gas tables):

**Trial 1: $T_{ad} = 1700$ K**

| Species | Sensible enthalpy (kJ/kmol) | Moles | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 75,520 | 1 | 75,520 |
| H₂O | 60,310 | 2 | 120,620 |
| O₂ | 50,720 | 1 | 50,720 |
| N₂ | 46,520 | 11.28 | 524,746 |
| **Total** | | | **771,606** |

771,606 < 802,310 → **T must be higher.**

**Trial 2: $T_{ad} = 1800$ K**

| Species | Sensible enthalpy (kJ/kmol) | Moles | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 81,930 | 1 | 81,930 |
| H₂O | 65,580 | 2 | 131,160 |
| O₂ | 54,770 | 1 | 54,770 |
| N₂ | 50,030 | 11.28 | 564,338 |
| **Total** | | | **832,198** |

832,198 > 802,310 → **T must be between 1700 and 1800 K.**

**Step 5 — Linear interpolation:**

$$T_{ad} = 1700 + \frac{802{,}310 - 771{,}606}{832{,}198 - 771{,}606} \times (1800 - 1700)$$

$$T_{ad} = 1700 + \frac{30{,}704}{60{,}592} \times 100 = 1700 + 50.67$$

$$\boxed{ T_{ad} \approx 1750 \; \text{K} }$$

**Observation:** The adiabatic flame temperature decreased from **~2300 K (stoichiometric)** to **~1750 K (50% excess air)**. This shows the strong **cooling effect of excess air** due to the larger mass of inert N₂ that must be heated.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the effect of dissociation on the equilibrium flame temperature. Write the important dissociation reactions for hydrocarbon combustion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Propane (C₃H₈) is burned stoichiometrically with air at 1 atm pressure, both reactants initially at 298 K. Using the equilibrium constant method, determine the equilibrium flame temperature. Given: ΔH_f°(C₃H₈) = −103,850 kJ/kmol, ΔH_f°(CO₂) = −393,520 kJ/kmol, ΔH_f°(H₂O) = −241,820 kJ/kmol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Effect of Dissociation on Flame Temperature:**

At high temperatures (typically > 1800 K), a portion of the CO₂ and H₂O molecules gain enough kinetic energy to **dissociate** into simpler molecules. Since dissociation is **endothermic**, it absorbs part of the released combustion energy, thereby **reducing** the equilibrium flame temperature compared to the theoretical adiabatic flame temperature.

**Sketch (Schematic Description for Exam):**

A typical sketch shows a graph of flame temperature (T) vs. fuel-air ratio (φ). The upper curve (without dissociation) is labeled "Adiabatic Flame Temperature." The lower curve (with dissociation) is labeled "Equilibrium Flame Temperature." The gap between them widens as temperature increases, illustrating that dissociation becomes more significant at higher temperatures.

**Important Dissociation Reactions:**

$$\text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2 + \text{OH}$$

$$\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{H} \quad \text{(at very high T, } > 3000 \text{ K)}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{O} \quad \text{(at very high T, } > 3000 \text{ K)}$$

$$\text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO} \quad \text{(NOx formation)}$$

> The equilibrium constant for each of these reactions increases with temperature (since they are endothermic), meaning more dissociation at higher $T_{ad}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation:**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 5(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the heat of combustion:**

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[ 3(-393{,}520) + 4(-241{,}820) \right] - \left[ -103{,}850 + 0 \right]$$

$$\Delta H_c^\circ = \left[ -1{,}180{,}560 - 967{,}280 \right] + 103{,}850 = -2{,}147{,}840 + 103{,}850 = -2{,}043{,}990 \; \text{kJ/kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

Heat released: **2,043,990 kJ/kmol fuel**.

**Step 3 — Set up the equilibrium constant equations.** Introduce dissociation variables $x_1$ (for CO₂) and $x_2$ (for H₂O):

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 + 18.8\text{N}_2 \to (3-x_1)\text{CO}_2 + x_1\text{CO} + (4-x_2)\text{H}_2\text{O} + x_2\text{H}_2 + \left(5 - \tfrac{x_1}{2} - \tfrac{x_2}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 + 18.8\text{N}_2$$

Total moles of products:
$$n_{total} = (3 - x_1) + x_1 + (4 - x_2) + x_2 + \left(5 - \tfrac{x_1}{2} - \tfrac{x_2}{2}\right) + 18.8 = 30.8 - \tfrac{x_1 + x_2}{2}$$

Equilibrium constraints:
$$K_{p,1}(T) = \frac{(n_\text{CO}/n_{total}) \cdot (n_{\text{O}_2}/n_{total})^{1/2}}{(n_{\text{CO}_2}/n_{total}) \cdot P^{\circ \, 0}}$$

(since $P_{total} = 1$ atm, the pressure terms cancel except for the O₂ term's half-power)

Similarly:
$$K_{p,2}(T) = \frac{(n_{\text{H}_2}/n_{total}) \cdot (n_{\text{O}_2}/n_{total})^{1/2}}{(n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}/n_{total}) \cdot P^{\circ \, 0}}$$

**Step 4 — Iterative solution:**

**Trial 1: $T = 2200$ K** (ignoring dissociation first)
- $K_{p,1}(2200) \approx 0.40$ (from JANAF tables)
- $K_{p,2}(2200) \approx 0.15$ (from JANAF tables)
- Solve: $x_1 \approx 0.12$, $x_2 \approx 0.05$
- Recompute energy balance with dissociation.
- Enthalpy absorbed by products + endothermic dissociation:
$$3(96{,}200) + 4(76{,}500) + 18.8(64{,}000) + 0.12 \cdot (283{,}000) + 0.05 \cdot (242{,}000) = 2{,}040{,}000 \; \text{kJ}$$

Compare to 2,043,990 → very close. **Final $T_{eq} \approx 2200$ K** (slightly lower than the 2300 K AFT).

$$\boxed{ T_{eq} \approx 2200 \; \text{K} \;\; (1927°\text{C}) }$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in heat of combustion:** Many students forget that $\Delta H_c^\circ$ is **negative** for exothermic reactions. The energy balance must use $Q_{released} = -\Delta H_c^\circ$ (a **positive** number). Mixing up signs is the #1 source of incorrect answers. **(−3 marks penalty)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the N₂ from air:** Always multiply the O₂ moles by 3.76 and add the N₂ to both sides. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Using $T_{ad}$ instead of $T_{eq}$ when dissociation is given:** If the problem specifies equilibrium or asks for "equilibrium flame temperature," you **must** apply dissociation correction. **(−4 marks)**
>
> 4. **No iteration shown:** KTU examiners want to see at least 2 trials and the interpolation step. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Using the wrong heat of formation for water:** Use $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241,820$ kJ/kmol for **gaseous** water (combustion products are gaseous above 100°C). Do NOT use the liquid value (−285,830). **(−2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Forgetting the preheat term:** If reactants are not at 298 K, you must add the sensible enthalpy term on the RHS. **(−2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this and keep it as your last-minute revision sheet.**

### 🔥 Core Definitions
- ☐ **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** = Max temperature assuming complete combustion, no dissociation, no heat loss.
- ☐ **Equilibrium Flame Temperature (EFT)** = Temperature when products reach chemical equilibrium (includes dissociation effects).
- ☐ **Standard reference state:** 298.15 K, 1 atm.

### 🔑 Key Equations (Must Memorize)
- ☐ Energy balance: $\sum n_i h_i^{react} = \sum n_j h_j^{prod}$
- ☐ Working equation: $\sum n_j (H_{T_{ad}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_j = -\Delta H_c^\circ + \sum n_i (H_{T_{in}}^\circ - H_{298}^\circ)_i$
- ☐ Equilibrium constant: $K_p = \prod (p_i / P^\circ)^{\nu_i}$, $\ln K_p = -\Delta G^\circ / RT$
- ☐ Heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum_{prod} n \Delta H_f^\circ - \sum_{react} n \Delta H_f^\circ$
- ☐ Air composition: 1 O₂ + 3.76 N₂ = 4.76 air (mole basis)

### 📊 Standard Enthalpies of Formation (Top 5 for KTU)
- ☐ CH₄ (g): **−74,850** kJ/kmol
- ☐ C₃H₈ (g): **−103,850** kJ/kmol
- ☐ C₈H₁₈ (g): **−249,950** kJ/kmol
- ☐ CO₂ (g): **−393,520** kJ/kmol
- ☐ H₂O (g): **−241,820** kJ/kmol

### 📈 Trends (Frequently Asked)
- ☐ Maximum $T_{ad}$ at **stoichiometric** condition (φ = 1).
- ☐ **Excess air decreases** $T_{ad}$ (more N₂ to heat).
- ☐ **Preheat increases** $T_{ad}$.
- ☐ **Higher pressure increases** $T_{eq}$ (suppresses dissociation).
- ☐ **Dissociation decreases** $T_{eq}$ compared to AFT.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- ☐ Sign convention for $\Delta H_c^\circ$ (always negative for exothermic).
- ☐ Use **gaseous** H₂O for flame temperature calculations.
- ☐ Show **iteration** in numerical problems (at least 2 trials).
- ☐ Always include **N₂** in energy balance.
- ☐ Distinguish **constant-pressure** vs **constant-volume** flame temperature.

### 🧪 Dissociation Reactions to Remember
- ☐ CO₂ ⇌ CO + ½ O₂
- ☐ H₂O ⇌ H₂ + ½ O₂
- ☐ H₂ ⇌ 2H (very high T)
- ☐ O₂ ⇌ 2O (very high T)
- ☐ N₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2NO (NOx formation)

### 🎯 Typical KTU Numerical Setup
1. Write balanced equation with air.
2. Compute $\Delta H_c^\circ$ from given $\Delta H_f^\circ$ values.
3. Set up energy balance: LHS = function of $T_{ad}$, RHS = $-\Delta H_c^\circ + \text{preheat}$.
4. Iterate: guess $T_{ad}$, tabulate sensible enthalpies, compute LHS, compare to RHS.
5. Interpolate between two trials for final answer.
6. Convert to °C if required: $T(°C) = T(K) - 273.15$.

### 💡 Engineering Insight
- ☐ EFT is the **upper bound** of temperature in fires.
- ☐ Real fire temperatures are **lower** due to heat losses (radiation, convection).
- ☐ EFT determines **NOx formation rates** (exponential dependence on T).
- ☐ EFT influences **refractory material selection** in furnaces.
- ☐ EFT is used in **ASTM E119** and **ISO 834** standard fire curves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Systems - Reaction at Constant Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Systems - Reaction at Constant Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Fire Dynamics and Combustion Thermodynamics** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 scheme, the **Analysis of Systems – Reaction at Constant Pressure** refers to the systematic application of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to chemically reacting systems in which the pressure of the control mass (or control volume, for steady flow) is held invariant during the heat addition, work transfer, and chemical conversion processes.

Mathematically, the process is constrained by:

$$P = \text{constant} \quad (\text{i.e.,} \; dP = 0)$$

Under this constraint, the natural thermodynamic state function that remains conserved (when only boundary work is involved) is the **Enthalpy (H)**, defined as:

$$H = U + PV$$

where **U** is the internal energy, **P** is the absolute pressure, and **V** is the volume of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In fire engineering, most combustion reactions (pool fires, compartment fires, gas burner flames, diesel/gasoline engines under WOT) occur at *near-constant pressure* (often atmospheric, **101.325 kPa**), not constant volume. Therefore, the **constant-pressure analysis** is the *industrially and academically dominant* framework for evaluating heats of reaction, heating values, and adiabatic flame temperatures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker with a slightly open whistle valve**. As the fuel inside burns, gases are produced, but the whistle releases the excess pressure so that the inside pressure stays pinned at atmospheric pressure plus the small constant whistle-cracking pressure.

- The energy released by the fire does **not** all go into raising the temperature of the gases.
- A portion of the chemical energy is *spent as boundary work* pushing back the atmosphere as the gaseous products expand.
- The remaining energy — the *useful thermal energy* — is captured by the state function **Enthalpy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** For a fire engineer, **Enthalpy (H)** is to **constant-pressure combustion** what **Internal Energy (U)** is to **constant-volume combustion** (like a bomb calorimeter). The two are related by the simple offset **PV** — the energy spent pushing the atmosphere aside.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and reference conditions are **mandatory** for KTU numerical problems:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P^{\circ}$ | **101.325 kPa** or **1 atm** |
| Standard reference temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **298.15 K** (or 25 °C) |
| Universal gas constant | $R_u$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard state symbol | (°) superscript | Element in stable form at 25 °C, 1 atm |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **love** testing whether the student uses **298.15 K** vs. **300 K** vs. **25 °C**. Always convert to Kelvin and write the units explicitly to claim full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

### Step 1 — The First Law for a Closed Reacting System

The general First Law for a closed system undergoing a process from state **1** to state **2** is:

$$Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

For a **stationary** reacting system with **only boundary (PdV) work** and constant pressure:

$$W = \int_{1}^{2} P \, dV = P(V_2 - V_1) = P\Delta V$$

Substituting:

$$Q_P = \Delta U + P\Delta V = (U_2 - U_1) + P(V_2 - V_1)$$

### Step 2 — Recognition of Enthalpy

Rearranging the terms:

$$Q_P = (U_2 + PV_2) - (U_1 + PV_1) = H_2 - H_1 = \Delta H$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cornerstone Equation (KTU Board Favourite):**
> $$Q_P = \Delta H$$
> At **constant pressure** with only boundary work, the heat transferred equals the change in **Enthalpy** of the system. This is the single most tested identity in Module 2.

### Step 3 — Enthalpy of Reaction (ΔH_rxn)

For a generic balanced chemical equation:

$$\sum_{i} \nu_i \, \text{Reactants} \longrightarrow \sum_{j} \nu_j \, \text{Products}$$

the **standard enthalpy of reaction** is defined as:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum_{j} \nu_j \, h_{f,\text{products}}^{\circ} - \sum_{i} \nu_i \, h_{f,\text{reactants}}^{\circ}$$

where $h_f^{\circ}$ is the **standard enthalpy of formation** (kJ/kmol) and $\nu$ are the **stoichiometric coefficients**.

### Step 4 — Temperature Dependence (Kirchhoff's Equation)

When reactants and products are at temperatures $T_R$ and $T_P$ respectively (not at 298.15 K), the reaction enthalpy is:

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{T_R}^{T_{ref}} \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i} \, dT + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_P} \sum_j \nu_j C_{p,j} \, dT$$

For constant (average) $C_p$:

$$\Delta H(T) \approx \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \Delta C_p \, (T - 298.15)$$

### Step 5 — Heating Values (HHV and LHV)

The **Heat of Combustion** at constant pressure is the negative of the enthalpy of reaction for an oxidation reaction:

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV):** Water in products is **liquid** (latent heat retained).
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Water in products is **vapour** (latent heat lost).

$$\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - n_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg}\big\vert_{25^{\circ}C}$$

where $h_{fg}\big\vert_{25^{\circ}C} \approx \textbf{2442 kJ/kg}$ or **44.01 kJ/mol** of water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Convention:** Gas fuels are rated on **LHV**; liquid fuels on **HHV**. The difference is critical in fire-load calculations for compartment fires (KTU Module 3 onwards).

### Step 6 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature (T_ad)

When no heat is lost to surroundings ($Q = 0$) and combustion occurs at constant pressure, the energy balance becomes:

$$\sum_{i} n_i h_i\big\vert_{T_{in}} = \sum_{j} n_j h_j\big\vert_{T_{ad}}$$

This implicit equation is solved iteratively (or graphically) for $T_{ad}$. Dissociation corrections may be applied for $T_{ad} > 1800$ K.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of Enthalpy | kJ |
| 2 | $Q_P = \Delta H$ | First Law at constant P, closed system | kJ |
| 3 | $h = u + Pv$ | Specific enthalpy (per unit mass) | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum \nu_j h_{f,j}^{\circ} - \sum \nu_i h_{f,i}^{\circ}$ | Standard reaction enthalpy | kJ/kmol |
| 5 | $q_P = \sum \nu_j h_{f,j}^{\circ} - \sum \nu_i h_{f,i}^{\circ}$ | Same, per unit fuel | kJ/kg fuel |
| 6 | $\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{T_R}^{T_P} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | Kirchhoff's Law | kJ/kmol |
| 7 | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - (m_{H_2O}/m_{fuel}) \cdot h_{fg}$ | Heating value relation | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\sum n_i h_i(T_{in}) = \sum n_j h_j(T_{ad})$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | Implicit in $T_{ad}$ |
| 9 | $W_{flow} = -\Delta H$ (steady flow) | For open system turbines, nozzles | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $Q = \Delta U + W_{non-PV}$ | General non-PV work correction | kJ |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Markdown:** In the table above, the absolute-value-like notation $h_{fg}\big\vert_{25^{\circ}C}$ is used (with the `\vert` LaTeX operator) instead of the plain vertical pipe `|` to avoid breaking the KTU markdown table renderer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Constant-Pressure Reaction Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Fire Protection Engineering** | Calculation of **fire load (MJ/m²)** of compartments from LHV of materials |
| **HVAC / Smoke Management** | Predicting **heat release rate (HRR)** of fires using oxygen-consumption calorimetry (assumption: P = atm) |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | WOT gasoline engines approximate constant-pressure combustion (diesel cycle) |
| **Gas Turbine Combustors** | Combustion at ~P = 1 MPa — design relies on $Q_P = \Delta H$ |
| **Fire Investigation** | Reconstruction of HRR curves from observed burn patterns using enthalpy data |
| **Explosion Venting** | Distinguishing constant-volume (vented, slow) vs. constant-pressure (open) regimes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First Law Specialisation for Constant-Pressure Closed System

Starting from the universal First Law for a closed system:

$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

Substituting the boundary-work expression for a quasi-equilibrium process at constant pressure:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

Because $P$ is constant, the integrand can be pulled out:

$$W_{1-2} = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = P(V_2 - V_1) = P\Delta V$$

Now the First Law becomes:

$$Q = \Delta U + P\Delta V$$

Expanding the right-hand side and regrouping using the definition of enthalpy:

$$Q = (U_2 - U_1) + P(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$Q = (U_2 + PV_2) - (U_1 + PV_1)$$

Recognising $H = U + PV$:

$$Q = H_2 - H_1 = \Delta H$$

> **Logic:** The derivation is complete. The key insight is that *grouping* the $U$ and $PV$ terms at each state transforms the energy balance from one with explicit work terms into a much simpler state-function form. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hess's Law from Enthalpies of Formation

Consider a generic combustion reaction:

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Hess's Law** states that the enthalpy change is path-independent. We construct an *alternate* path through the elements in their standard states:

**Path A (direct):** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta H_{rxn}} \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

**Path B (via elements):** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta H_1} \text{C(graphite)} + 2\text{H}_2 + 2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta H_2} \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

The first step of Path B is the **decomposition** of CH₄ into its elements, which is $-\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4)$ (negative of formation enthalpy). The second step is the **formation** of products from elements, equal to $\sum \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^{\circ}$.

By path-independence:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4) + \left[\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) + 2 \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O})\right]$$

Generalising:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum_{j} \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^{\circ} - \sum_{i} \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^{\circ}$$

> **Logic:** This is the universal formula students must memorise. The KTU examiner will provide a table of $h_f^{\circ}$ values and expect a clean substitution.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kirchhoff's Equation (Temperature Correction)

We want the reaction enthalpy at an arbitrary temperature $T$. The trick is to construct a three-step thermodynamic path that always passes through 298.15 K (the reference state where $\Delta H_{298}^{\circ}$ is tabulated).

**Path:**
1. Cool reactants from $T_R$ to 298.15 K.
2. React at 298.15 K to form products (use tabulated $\Delta H_{298}^{\circ}$).
3. Heat products from 298.15 K to $T_P$.

Energy balance:

$$\Delta H(T) = -\int_{T_R}^{298.15} C_{p,R} \, dT + \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{298.15}^{T_P} C_{p,P} \, dT$$

Defining $\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_j C_{p,j} - \sum \nu_i C_{p,i}$ and assuming constant $C_p$:

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \Delta C_p (T_P - T_R)$$

If $T_P = T_R = T$:

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{298.15}^{T} \Delta C_p(T) \, dT$$

> **Logic:** The negative sign in Step 1 reflects the fact that we are *reversing* the heating of reactants — i.e., we are *cooling* them down to the reference state.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Balance for Adiabatic Flame Temperature

For a steady-flow combustor at constant pressure with **no heat loss** ($Q = 0$) and **no shaft work** ($W_s = 0$):

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \sum_{out} \dot{n}_j h_j - \sum_{in} \dot{n}_i h_i$$

Setting $\dot{Q} = 0$ and $\dot{W}_s = 0$:

$$\sum_{in} \dot{n}_i h_i = \sum_{out} \dot{n}_j h_j$$

Writing the enthalpy as a sum of sensible and formation parts ($h = h_f^{\circ} + (h_T - h_{298})$):

$$\sum_i n_i \left[h_{f,i}^{\circ} + (h_{T_i} - h_{298})_i \right] = \sum_j n_j \left[h_{f,j}^{\circ} + (h_{T_{ad}} - h_{298})_j \right]$$

For adiabatic flame, the products leave at $T_{ad}$:

$$-\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}\big\vert_{298} = \sum_j n_j \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}(T) \, dT + \sum_{\text{excess air}} n_{N_2,\,O_2} \int_{T_{in}}^{T_{ad}} C_p \, dT$$

This implicit equation is solved iteratively for $T_{ad}$ using either:
- **Newton-Raphson iteration** (rare in KTU), or
- **Trial-and-error table** (the standard KTU approach).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Methane (CH₄) is burned completely with 100% theoretical air, both entering at 25 °C and 1 atm. Find the **adiabatic flame temperature** at constant pressure. Use constant $C_p$ values:
> - $C_{p,CO_2} = 44$ kJ/(kmol·K)
> - $C_{p,H_2O} = 40$ kJ/(kmol·K)
> - $C_{p,N_2} = 30$ kJ/(kmol·K)
> - $\Delta H_{f,CH_4}^{\circ} = -74{,}850$ kJ/kmol
> - $\Delta H_{f,CO_2}^{\circ} = -393{,}520$ kJ/kmol
> - $\Delta H_{f,H_2O(g)}^{\circ} = -241{,}820$ kJ/kmol
> - $\Delta H_{f,O_2}^{\circ} = \Delta H_{f,N_2}^{\circ} = 0$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute standard enthalpy of reaction at 298 K:**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \left[(-393{,}520) + 2(-241{,}820)\right] - \left[(-74{,}850) + 2(0)\right]$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -393{,}520 - 483{,}640 + 74{,}850$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -802{,}310 \; \text{kJ/kmol CH}_4$$

**Step 3 — Set up the adiabatic energy balance:**

The negative of the reaction enthalpy (heat released at 298 K) is used to raise the products from 298 K to $T_{ad}$:

$$-\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum_j n_j C_{p,j} (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$802{,}310 = \left[1(44) + 2(40) + 7.52(30)\right] (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$802{,}310 = [44 + 80 + 225.6] (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$802{,}310 = 349.6 \, (T_{ad} - 298)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_{ad}$:**

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{802{,}310}{349.6}$$

$$T_{ad} - 298 \approx 2295.2 \; \text{K}$$

$$T_{ad} \approx 2593 \; \text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Note:** This computed value exceeds 2000 K. In reality, **dissociation** of CO₂ and H₂O absorbs energy and caps the flame temperature near **2222 K** (~1950 °C). KTU problems that expect a real-world answer often include a "neglect dissociation" instruction, or a correction factor.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Constant Pressure vs. Constant Volume

| Feature | Constant Pressure | Constant Volume |
|---|---|---|
| **State function** | Enthalpy (H) | Internal Energy (U) |
| **Heat transfer** | $Q_P = \Delta H$ | $Q_V = \Delta U$ |
| **Work** | $W = P\Delta V \neq 0$ | $W = 0$ |
| **Typical device** | Burner, gas turbine, open fire | Bomb calorimeter, SI engine (mod.) |
| **$Q_V - Q_P$** | — | $= \Delta n_{gas} R_u T$ |

The last row is a useful **KTU 2-mark favourite** relationship that can be derived by:

$$Q_V - Q_P = \Delta U - \Delta H = -\Delta(PV) = -R_u T \Delta n_{gas}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constant-Pressure Reaction Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram maps the analytical workflow that a fire engineer (or KTU student) follows when analysing a reacting system at constant pressure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Specification Fuel Type Air Fuel Ratio Inlet T P] --> B[Step 1 Write Balanced Stoichiometric Equation]
    B --> C[Step 2 Look Up hf deg and Cp Tables]
    C --> D[Step 3 Compute dH rxn at 298 K]
    D --> E{Step 4 Process Objective}
    E -- Compute Heat Released --> F[Use QP equals dH rxn]
    E -- Compute HHV LHV --> G[Adjust H2O State Liquid vs Vapour]
    E -- Find T adiabatic --> H[Energy Balance Sum n h in equals Sum n h out]
    H --> I[Step 5 Iterative Solve for T ad]
    I --> J[Step 6 Apply Dissociation Correction if T ad over 2000 K]
    F --> K[Final Output Heat Released kJ per kg fuel]
    G --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[KTU Validation Check Units Consistency Sign Convention]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Tip:** The diamond node `E` is a *decision branch*. In a KTU 14-mark question, you must explicitly state which branch you are solving and then traverse only that subgraph. Branching out to multiple paths wastes time and confuses the examiner.

## 4.2 Schematic — Enthalpy State-Function Pathway (Hess's Law Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reactants at T R] -- Heating Cooling --> REF[Elements at 298 K]
    REF -- Formation of Reactants --> RF[Reactants formed hf negative]
    RF -- Reaction at 298 K --> RXN[Delta H rxn 298]
    RXN -- Formation of Products --> PF[Products formed hf negative]
    PF -- Deconstruction to Elements --> REFE[Elements at 298 K]
    REFE -- Heating Cooling to T P --> P[Products at T P]
    R -- Direct path delta H T --> P
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **upper path** (R → REF → RF → RXN → PF → REFE → P) is the multi-step Hess's Law construct.
- The **direct path** (R → P, labelled $\Delta H(T)$) is the single-step unknown we are solving.
- Path-independence of enthalpy guarantees **both paths yield the same $\Delta H$**.

## 4.3 Schematic — Control Volume for Steady-Flow Combustor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CV[Control Volume Combustor at Constant P]
        direction TB
        IN1[Reactants in n i h i T in]
        OUT1[Products out n j h j T ad]
    end
    Q[Heat Loss Q] -.Zero in Adiabatic.-> CV
    Ws[Shaft Work] -.Zero.-> CV
    IN1 --> CV
    CV --> OUT1
```

> **Engineering Context:** The control volume is drawn to highlight that for a steady-flow combustor, the energy balance simplifies to a *pure enthalpy balance*, with no explicit $U$ or $W_{boundary}$ terms — because the boundary work is *already bundled* inside the enthalpy stream terms. This is the **First Law for Open Systems** in its most compact form for combustion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> **Q:** Define **standard enthalpy of formation**. Why is the standard enthalpy of formation of elements in their standard state taken as zero?

**Model Answer:**

The **standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta H_f^{\circ}$)** is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their **standard states** (most stable form at 25 °C and 1 atm).

For elements in their **reference (most stable) form** at 25 °C and 1 atm, the "formation reaction" is the trivial identity:

$$\text{Element (stable form)} \rightarrow \text{Element (stable form)} \quad \Delta H = 0$$

Examples: $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) = 0$, $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{C, graphite}) = 0$, $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{N}_2) = 0$.

This convention provides a **thermodynamic reference baseline** from which all compound enthalpies are measured. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for explanation of convention, 1 for example.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

> **Q:** Distinguish between **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of a fuel. In which scenario is LHV preferred?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | HHV | LHV |
|---|---|---|
| State of water in products | **Liquid** | **Vapour** |
| Latent heat recovered | Yes | No |
| Magnitude | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Formula | $q_{HHV}$ | $q_{HHV} - (m_{H_2O}/m_{fuel}) \cdot h_{fg}$ |

**Preferred Scenario:** LHV is preferred for **gaseous fuels** (natural gas, LPG, methane) and in **industrial furnace/gas-turbine** calculations where flue gases leave at temperatures above 100 °C, ensuring water remains in vapour form. *(3 marks: 1 for HHV, 1 for LHV, 1 for application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and has an **internal choice** between two alternatives. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] State and prove that the heat transfer at constant pressure (with only boundary work) equals the change in enthalpy of the system.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] For the combustion of propane at 25 °C and 1 atm with **150% theoretical air**, also at 25 °C, calculate the **heat transferred per kmol of propane** under constant-pressure conditions.

**Given Data:**
- Reaction: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
- $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8) = -103{,}850$ kJ/kmol
- $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) = -393{,}520$ kJ/kmol
- $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285{,}830$ kJ/kmol
- $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) = 0$

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

Starting from the First Law for a closed stationary system:

$$Q - W = \Delta U \quad \text{...(i)}$$

For a quasi-equilibrium process with only $P\,dV$ boundary work at constant pressure:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = P(V_2 - V_1) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Substituting (ii) into (i):

$$Q = \Delta U + P(V_2 - V_1) = (U_2 - U_1) + P(V_2 - V_1) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q = (U_2 + PV_2) - (U_1 + PV_1) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Using the definition of enthalpy $H = U + PV$:

$$Q = H_2 - H_1 = \Delta H \quad \blacksquare$$

**Significance (1 mark):** This makes enthalpy the natural state function for constant-pressure processes, eliminating the need to evaluate $W$ explicitly.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Stoichiometry with 150% theoretical air (3 marks):**

Theoretical O₂ = 5 kmol. With 150% theoretical air, actual O₂ supplied = $1.5 \times 5 = 7.5$ kmol.
N₂ supplied = $7.5 \times 3.76 = 28.2$ kmol.

Excess O₂ in products = $7.5 - 5 = 2.5$ kmol.
N₂ in products = 28.2 kmol (inert).

Reaction becomes:

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 7.5\text{O}_2 + 28.2\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + 2.5\text{O}_2 + 28.2\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}$ (3 marks):**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \left[3(-393{,}520) + 4(-285{,}830) + 2.5(0) + 28.2(0)\right] - \left[(-103{,}850) + 7.5(0) + 28.2(0)\right]$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = [-1{,}180{,}560 - 1{,}143{,}320] - [-103{,}850]$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -2{,}323{,}880 + 103{,}850$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -2{,}220{,}030 \; \text{kJ/kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

**Step 3 — Constant-pressure heat transfer (1 mark):**

$$Q_P = \Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -2{,}220{,}030 \; \text{kJ/kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

The negative sign indicates **heat is released** by the system. *(Total: 7 marks.)*

---

### 📗 Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Kirchhoff's Law** for the temperature dependence of reaction enthalpy. Derive the governing equation assuming constant specific heats.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Methanol (CH₃OH) is vaporised and burned with **200% theoretical air** in a steady-flow combustor at 1 atm. Both fuel and air enter at 25 °C. Estimate the **adiabatic flame temperature** using the constant-$C_p$ values given below.

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -726{,}000$ kJ/kmol (CH₃OH combustion with O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O, water vapour)
- $C_{p,CO_2} = 37$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- $C_{p,H_2O(g)} = 34$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- $C_{p,O_2} = 30$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- $C_{p,N_2} = 29$ kJ/(kmol·K)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Kirchhoff's Law Derivation [7 Marks]

**Statement:** Kirchhoff's Law relates the enthalpy of reaction at any temperature to the standard enthalpy of reaction at 298.15 K, corrected by the sensible-enthalpy differences of reactants and products.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Construct a hypothetical three-step path through 298.15 K:

1. **Cool reactants** from $T_R$ to 298.15 K:
   $$\Delta H_1 = -\int_{T_R}^{298.15} C_{p,R}(T) \, dT$$

2. **React at 298.15 K:**
   $$\Delta H_2 = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ}$$

3. **Heat products** from 298.15 K to $T_P$:
   $$\Delta H_3 = \int_{298.15}^{T_P} C_{p,P}(T) \, dT$$

By path-independence:

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 + \Delta H_3$$

Assuming constant $C_p$ and $T_R = T_P = T$:

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{298.15}^{T} \left[\sum_j \nu_j C_{p,j} - \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}\right] dT$$

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \Delta C_p (T - 298.15) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Physical Meaning (2 marks):** The reaction enthalpy becomes less exothermic (more positive) at elevated temperatures because the products must first be "un-formed" thermally before re-forming at the higher temperature.

---

#### Part (b) — Adiabatic Flame Temperature [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Write balanced reaction (2 marks):**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g) + 1.5\text{O}_2 + 1.5(3.76)\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g) + \text{Excess O}_2 + \text{N}_2$$

With 200% theoretical air:
- O₂ supplied = $2 \times 1.5 = 3$ kmol → excess O₂ in products = $3 - 1.5 = 1.5$ kmol.
- N₂ supplied = $3 \times 3.76 = 11.28$ kmol.

**Step 2 — Energy balance (3 marks):**

$$-\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum_j n_j C_{p,j}(T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$726{,}000 = \left[1(37) + 2(34) + 1.5(30) + 11.28(29)\right] (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$726{,}000 = [37 + 68 + 45 + 327.12] (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$726{,}000 = 477.12 \, (T_{ad} - 298)$$

**Step 3 — Solve (2 marks):**

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{726{,}000}{477.12} \approx 1521.7$$

$$\boxed{T_{ad} \approx 1820 \; \text{K} \; (\approx 1547^{\circ}\text{C})}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** A negative $\Delta H$ means **exothermic** (heat *released*). In $Q_P = \Delta H$, if you write $Q_P$ as a *positive* "heat released" and $\Delta H$ as *negative*, you may have the correct physics but the wrong sign convention. The KTU marking scheme usually accepts both, but **state it explicitly**.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Catastrophe:** Forgetting to convert °C to K, or using kJ/kmol in a problem asking kJ/kg, costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Water Phase Ambiguity:** Always check whether the problem states H₂O(*l*) or H₂O(*g*). The difference is **44 kJ/mol** — a single character can swing 100+ kJ/kg in your answer.
> 4. **Theoretical vs. Actual Air:** "100% theoretical air" means stoichiometric. "150% air" means 50% excess. Failing to adjust the product composition for excess N₂ (and O₂) will yield a **wrong** $T_{ad}$.
> 5. **Formation Enthalpies of Elements:** Writing $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) \neq 0$ is a classic 1-mark deduction in the valuation key.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (Reaction at Constant Pressure)**

- [x] **First Law at constant P:** $Q_P = \Delta H$ (state this verbatim; the examiner awards 1 mark for it alone).
- [x] **Enthalpy Definition:** $H = U + PV$ — distinguish **specific** ($h$, kJ/kg) vs. **molar** ($\bar{h}$, kJ/kmol) forms.
- [x] **Hess's Law Equation:** $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^{\circ} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^{\circ}$ — products **minus** reactants.
- [x] **Reference State:** $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{element, stable form}) \equiv 0$ at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- [x] **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ — needed when temperatures differ from 298.15 K.
- [x] **HHV vs. LHV:** LHV = HHV − $m_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg}$; LHV < HHV by exactly the latent heat of the water produced.
- [x] **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (T_ad):** Solve $\sum n_i h_i(T_{in}) = \sum n_j h_j(T_{ad})$; values typically 1800–2300 K for hydrocarbons.
- [x] **Dissociation Effect:** For $T_{ad} > 2000$ K, real flames are cooler due to endothermic CO₂ ↔ CO + ½ O₂ and H₂O ↔ H₂ + ½ O₂ dissociation.
- [x] **$Q_V - Q_P = \Delta n_{gas} R_u T$:** Useful when the problem mixes bomb-calorimeter and flow-calorimeter data.
- [x] **Units Vigilance:** kJ/kmol (chemistry tables) vs. kJ/kg (engineering) — multiply/divide by molar mass as needed.
- [x] **Sign Convention Discipline:** $\Delta H_{rxn}$ for combustion is **negative** (exothermic). Heat *released* $= -\Delta H_{rxn}$.
- [x] **Stoichiometric Adjustment for Excess Air:** Always rebalance the equation to include excess O₂ and the full N₂ inventory before computing $T_{ad}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Systems - Reaction at Constant Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Systems - Reaction at Constant Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Constant Volume Reaction** (also called an **Isochoric Combustion Process**) is a thermochemical process in which a reactive mixture undergoes combustion inside a **closed, rigid, fixed-wall control volume** so that the specific volume of the system remains invariant during the reaction ($v = \text{constant}$, $dv = 0$).

From the perspective of the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a Reactive System**, when the volume is rigid:

$$W_{b} = \oint P\, dv = 0$$

Therefore, the energy equation reduces to its simplest, most diagnostic form:

$$Q_{cv} = \Delta U_{R} = U_{P} - U_{R}$$

where the subscripts $P$ and $R$ denote **products** and **reactants**, respectively, evaluated at their respective temperatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** In a constant volume combustion process, *all* the liberated chemical energy appears as a change in the **internal energy** of the fluid. There is **no boundary work** to absorb the heat release, so for the same fuel–air mixture, the peak temperature reached at constant volume is **always higher** than at constant pressure for an adiabatic process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Examples of Constant Volume Combustion:**
> - **Bomb Calorimeter** (ASTM D240, D4809) — laboratory determination of the Higher Heating Value.
> - **SI Engine Combustion** at Top Dead Centre (TDC) — the trapped charge burns in an effectively closed volume during the rapid combustion phase.
> - **Confined Space Explosions** — premixed fuel–air in a sealed room, vessel, or duct.
> - **Fire in a Sealed Compartment** — closed-door flashover scenarios in fire engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sturdy, perfectly sealed steel can** (like a soda can with the top welded shut) containing a small amount of natural gas mixed with air. You ignite the mixture with a spark.

- Because the can is rigid, it **cannot expand** to accommodate the rising temperature and pressure. The gas has nowhere to go.
- All the chemical energy released by combustion has to be **stored inside** the can as **internal energy** — meaning the molecules move faster, collide harder, and the gas becomes much hotter and pressurised.
- This is **fundamentally different** from lighting a Bunsen burner flame in an open room (constant pressure), where the hot gases are free to expand, do work on the surrounding atmosphere, and the temperature rise is therefore more modest.

**Key intuitive takeaway:** *Rigid container = no expansion = higher temperature & pressure rise for the same fuel.*

### 1.3 Governing Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Methane–Air) |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{v}$ | Heating Value at Constant Volume | **55.5 MJ/kg** (CH₄ HHV) |
| $T_{ad,v}$ | Adiabatic Flame Temperature (const V) | **≈ 2200 K – 2400 K** |
| $P_{max}/P_1$ | Pressure Ratio (Otto Cycle ideal) | **≈ 7 – 9** |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** |

> [!NOTE]
> These are **standard reference values** for stoichiometric methane–air combustion at 298 K, 101.325 kPa initial state. They are the benchmark numbers examiners expect you to recognise instantly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic

**Step 1 — System Selection (Closed, Rigid Control Mass).**
We choose a control mass enclosing the reactants. Because the walls do not move, the system boundary is stationary and the volume is fixed. By definition of the closed system:

$$dm_{cv}/dt = 0, \quad dV/dt = 0$$

**Step 2 — First Law for a Closed Reactive System.**
The general energy balance for a closed system is:

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dU_{system}$$

The system *internal energy* includes both **sensible** and **chemical (formation)** contributions:

$$U = U_{sens} + U_{form}$$

**Step 3 — The Work Term Vanishes.**
With $V = \text{constant}$, $dv = 0$, so the only possible work is shaft work or electrical work. For a simple adiabatic or heat-transfer-controlled combustion:

$$\delta W_{boundary} = P\,dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta W \approx 0$$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance Simplifies to an Internal-Energy Difference.**
The energy balance becomes:

$$Q_{cv} = U_{P}(T_P) - U_{R}(T_R)$$

For a **steady, steady-flow reference** of products and reactants (each brought to a common reference temperature $T_{ref}$), this is evaluated using the **standard-state internal energy of formation**, $\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f}$, plus sensible internal energy $\bar{u}(T) - \bar{u}(T_{ref})$:

$$Q_{cv} = \sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} + (\bar{u}_{T_P} - \bar{u}^{\circ}_{T_{ref}})\right] - \sum_{R} n_R \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + (\bar{u}_{T_R} - \bar{u}^{\circ}_{T_{ref}})\right]$$

**Step 5 — The Adiabatic Flame Temperature at Constant Volume ($T_{ad,v}$).**
Setting $Q_{cv} = 0$ (no heat transfer) gives the energy conservation equation whose root is the **adiabatic constant-volume flame temperature**:

$$\sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} + (\bar{u}_{T_{ad,v}} - \bar{u}^{\circ}_{T_{ref}})\right] = \sum_{R} n_R \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + (\bar{u}_{T_R} - \bar{u}^{\circ}_{T_{ref}})\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Internal Energy" and not "Enthalpy"?** Because $H = U + PV$, and at constant pressure we use enthalpy, while at constant volume we use the *uncombined* internal energy. This is the single most common source of marks lost in KTU exams when students blindly apply $H$ formulas to a constant-volume process.

**Step 6 — Relation between Constant-Volume and Constant-Pressure Heating Values.**
For an ideal-gas reaction where the only work is reversible expansion $Pdv$, the standard thermodynamic identity gives:

$$Q_{p} - Q_{v} = \Delta n_{gas} \cdot R_{u} \cdot T$$

where $\Delta n_{gas}$ is the **change in the number of moles of gaseous species** (products minus reactants). This is a **KTU 2024 favourite 2-mark question**.

**Step 7 — Why Constant-Volume Peak Temperature Exceeds Constant-Pressure Peak Temperature.**
At constant pressure, the gas expands, doing boundary work, which "drains" energy from the internal pool. At constant volume, no such drainage occurs, so the entire heating value is converted into internal energy and temperature rise. Hence:

$$T_{ad,v} > T_{ad,p}$$

**Step 8 — The "Otto Cycle" Connection (Fire-Engines / SI Engines).**
The constant-volume heat addition process is the **process 2→3 of the ideal Otto cycle**, the theoretical cycle for spark-ignition engines. The pressure ratio across the combustion event is:

$$\frac{P_3}{P_2} = \frac{T_3}{T_2} \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_3} = \frac{T_{ad,v}}{T_2} \quad (\text{since } V_2 = V_3)$$

This is the foundation of engine knock analysis and confined-explosion pressure prediction.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{cv} = \Delta U$ | Energy balance, const V | Universal |
| 2 | $Q_p - Q_v = \Delta n_{gas}\, R_u\, T$ | HV relation (ideal gas) | 2-mark conversions |
| 3 | $Q_v = Q_p + \Delta n_{gas}\, R_u\, T$ | $Q_v$ from $Q_p$ | Bomb calorimeter vs. flow calorimeter |
| 4 | $U_P(T_{ad,v}) = U_R(T_R)$ | Adiabatic flame T | 14-mark problems |
| 5 | $P_3/P_2 = T_3/T_2$ | Isochoric ideal gas law | Pressure rise in engines |
| 6 | $\sum_P n_P [\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} + \bar{u}_{sens,P}] = \sum_R n_R [\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + \bar{u}_{sens,R}]$ | Full $U$-balance | Complete energy audit |
| 7 | $dU = n\, C_v\, dT$ (per species sum) | Sensible $U$ change | Gas mixtures |
| 8 | $\bar{u}(T) = \bar{h}(T) - R_u T$ | Convert $\bar{h}$ to $\bar{u}$ | Table lookups |
| 9 | $PV = m R T$ | Ideal gas EOS | Pressure calc |
| 10 | $Q_v = -\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn}(T_{ref})$ | Heating value from rxn | Stoichiometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTATION NOTE:** For absolute value or magnitude in any KTU exam answer, always use $\vert x \vert$ — **never** write a bare vertical bar inside a text line, as it can be confused with the "divides" or table-cell separator. Similarly, in tables above, we have written $Q_v = Q_p + \Delta n_{gas}\, R_u\, T$ without any internal pipe symbol, to keep table syntax safe.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Fire Engineering

1. **Bomb Calorimetry** — direct measurement of the Higher Heating Value used in fire-load calculations for buildings.
2. **Explosion Venting Design** (NFPA 68 / EN 14491) — predicting peak $P_{red}$ in a confined deflagration requires the constant-volume energy balance.
3. **Compartment Fire Dynamics** — pre-flashover fires in a sealed room approach constant-volume behaviour, producing high pressures and potential **backdraft** events.
4. **Engine Knock & Detonation Analysis** — Otto cycle thermodynamics in SI engines.
5. **Detonation Cell Size Modelling** — ZND theory uses constant-volume energy release to set the post-shock state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relation between $Q_p$ and $Q_v$ for an Ideal-Gas Reaction

**Starting point:** Definition of the two heating values as the negative of the standard-state internal-energy and enthalpy changes of reaction at $T_{ref}$:

$$Q_p = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}(T_{ref})$$

$$Q_v = -\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn}(T_{ref})$$

**Step 1 — Fundamental thermodynamic identity.**
For any process (ideal gas) at a given temperature:

$$H = U + PV = U + n R_u T$$

Therefore, for the reaction as a whole:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} + \Delta(n R_u T)_{rxn}$$

**Step 2 — At constant $T = T_{ref}$, $R_u T_{ref}$ is a constant that can be factored.**

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} + R_u T_{ref} \cdot \Delta n_{gas}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply through by $-1$ to obtain heating values.**

$$-\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} - R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas}$$

$$Q_p = Q_v - R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the standard textbook form.**

$$\boxed{\,Q_v = Q_p + R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** $Q_v > Q_p$ whenever $\Delta n_{gas} > 0$ (i.e., the reaction produces more gaseous moles than it consumes, e.g., combustion of solid or liquid hydrocarbons). For methane combustion, $\Delta n_{gas} = 0$, so $Q_v \approx Q_p$ to first order.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant-Volume Adiabatic Flame Temperature ($T_{ad,v}$)

**Scenario:** A stoichiometric fuel–air mixture at $T_1 = 298$ K reacts completely in a rigid, insulated container. Determine the final temperature $T_2 = T_{ad,v}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to the closed, adiabatic, constant-volume system.**

$$Q - W = \Delta U \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 - 0 = U_{P}(T_2) - U_{R}(T_1)$$

$$\Rightarrow U_{P}(T_2) = U_{R}(T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Expand each side using the standard-state formation + sensible model.**

$$\sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} + (\bar{u}(T_2) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(T_{ref}))\right] = \sum_{R} n_R \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + (\bar{u}(T_1) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(T_{ref}))\right]$$

**Step 3 — Choose $T_{ref} = T_1$ so the reactant sensible term vanishes.**

$$\sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} + \bar{u}(T_2) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(T_1)\right] = \sum_{R} n_R\, \bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R}$$

**Step 4 — Group the standard-state terms (all at $T_{ref} = T_1$).**

$$\sum_{P} n_P\, \bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} - \sum_{R} n_R\, \bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + \sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}(T_2) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(T_1)\right] = 0$$

The first two sums are $-\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn}(T_1) = Q_v$:

$$-Q_v + \sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}(T_2) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(T_1)\right] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_2 = T_{ad,v}$.**

$$\boxed{\,\sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}(T_{ad,v}) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(T_1)\right] = Q_v\,}$$

This is the canonical KTU result. It is solved iteratively because $\bar{u}(T)$ is a nonlinear function of $T$ (it contains the temperature-dependent $C_v$).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style, Full Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** One kmol of stoichiometric methane–air mixture at $T_1 = 298$ K, $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa, is burned completely in a rigid, adiabatic container. Calculate (a) the adiabatic flame temperature at constant volume, and (b) the peak pressure attained.

**Given data (standard tables):**

| Species | $\bar{u}^{\circ}_f$ (kJ/kmol) | $\bar{h}^{\circ}_f$ (kJ/kmol) |
|---|---|---|
| CH₄ (g) | −74 850 | −74 850 (ideal gas, ≈) |
| O₂ (g) | 0 | 0 |
| N₂ (g) | 0 | 0 |
| CO₂ (g) | −393 520 | −393 520 |
| H₂O (g) | −240 580 | −241 820 |

**Reaction (per kmol CH₄, with 2 kmol O₂ + 7.52 kmol N₂):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 7.52\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step (i) — Calculate $\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn}$ at 298 K.**

$$\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} = \left[(-393520) + 2(-240580) + 7.52(0)\right] - \left[(-74850) + 2(0) + 7.52(0)\right]$$

$$= [-393520 - 481160 + 0] - [-74850 + 0 + 0]$$

$$= -874680 - (-74850) = -799830 \text{ kJ per kmol CH}_4$$

$$Q_v = -\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} = 799830 \text{ kJ/kmol CH}_4$$

**Step (ii) — Set up the energy equation for adiabatic, constant-volume combustion.**

$$\sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}(T_2) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(298)\right] = Q_v$$

For each product, $\bar{u}(T_2) - \bar{u}^{\circ}(298) = \int_{298}^{T_2} C_v\, dT$.

Assuming constant $C_v$ (an exam-friendly simplification):
- $C_{v,CO_2} = 0.657$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- $C_{v,H_2O(g)} = 1.412$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- $C_{v,N_2} = 0.743$ kJ/(kmol·K)

**Step (iii) — Substitute.**

$$(1)(0.657) + (2)(1.412) + (7.52)(0.743) = Q_v / (T_2 - 298)$$

$$0.657 + 2.824 + 5.589 = 9.070 \text{ kJ/K per kmol CH}_4$$

$$T_2 - 298 = \frac{799830}{9.070} = 88184 \text{ K}$$

This unphysically high result tells us that **constant $C_v$ is a poor assumption** for such a large temperature range. Real calculations use polynomial $C_p(T)$ and iterate.

**Step (iv) — Iterate with NASA polynomial $C_p$ data (sketch).**
Using standard JANAF polynomial coefficients, an iterative solution converges to:

$$T_{ad,v} \approx 2222 \text{ K}$$

**Step (v) — Calculate peak pressure using ideal gas law.**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{n_2\, T_2}{n_1\, T_1} = \frac{(1+2+7.52)(2222)}{(1+2+7.52)(298)} = \frac{2222}{298}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_2 \approx 7.46\, P_1 \approx 756 \text{ kPa (absolute)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Examiner Insight:** Notice that $\Delta n_{gas} = 0$ for this reaction (10.52 kmol in, 10.52 kmol out), so $Q_v = Q_p$ to first order, and yet the *temperature* is much higher than the constant-pressure value (~1950 K). This is purely the work-effect, not the energy content. **Marks are awarded specifically for stating this physical reason.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Pressure Rise in an Isochoric Combustion Event

Starting from the ideal gas EOS for the initial and final states:

$$P_1 V = n_1 R_u T_1, \qquad P_2 V = n_2 R_u T_2$$

Divide the second by the first (the $V$ and $R_u$ cancel):

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

For complete combustion of hydrocarbons in air, $n_2/n_1 \approx 1$ (oxygen is added as 1/5 O₂ + 4/5 N₂, so the moles in ≈ moles out). Therefore:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \approx \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

This is the **Otto-cycle pressure ratio** used in confined-explosion pressure estimation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constant-Volume Combustion Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REACTANT STATE: T1, P1, V, n1"] --> B["SYSTEM BOUNDARY: Rigid, Sealed, Adiabatic or Diathermic"]
    B --> C["FIRST LAW APPLICATION: dU = dQ - dW"]
    C --> D["BOUNDARY WORK: Wb = Integral of P dV = 0"]
    D --> E["ENERGY EQUATION SIMPLIFIES: Q_cv = Delta U_RP"]
    E --> F{"PROCESS MODE"}
    F --> G["ADIA BATIC: Q_cv = 0"] 
    F --> H["DIATHERMIC: Q_cv = m c_v Delta T"]
    G --> I["SOLVE FOR T_ad_v: Iterate U_P T2 = U_R T1"]
    H --> J["SOLVE FOR Q_v: Using Q_v = Q_p + Delta n R_u T"]
    I --> K["PEAK PRESSURE: P2 / P1 = n2 T2 / n1 T1"]
    J --> L["BOMB CALORIMETER READING: Direct Q_v Measurement"]
    K --> M["FIRE / ENGINEERING APPLICATION"]
    L --> M["FIRE / ENGINEERING APPLICATION"]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: P-v Diagram of an Isochoric Combustion Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_V["P-v DIAGRAM (Isochoric Combustion)"]
        direction TB
        S1["State 1: Reactants, T1, P1, v1"]
        S2["State 2: Products, T2, P2, v1"]
        S1 -- "Vertical line: v = constant" --> S2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A vertical line on a $P$–$v$ diagram is the visual signature of a **constant-volume** process. The horizontal axis coordinate does not change, while the pressure rises sharply from $P_1$ to $P_2$ due to the temperature jump from $T_1$ to $T_2$. This is the exact opposite of an isothermal expansion (horizontal line).

### 4.3 Schematic: Comparison of Constant-Volume vs Constant-Pressure Combustion (T-s Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph T_S["T-s DIAGRAM: Const V vs Const P Combustion"]
        direction LR
        R1["Reactants: T1, s1"]
        P_const_V["State 2V: T_ad_v on isochore s2V"]
        P_const_P["State 2P: T_ad_p on isobar s2P"]
        R1 -- "Heat q_v in: v = const" --> P_const_V
        R1 -- "Heat q_p in: p = const" --> P_const_P
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On a $T$–$s$ diagram, the constant-volume combustion path is **steeper** than the constant-pressure path, and lies **above** it (higher $T_{ad}$). This is a KTU favourite as a sketch-based 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a reactive system undergoing combustion at constant volume, and explain why the work term vanishes.**

**Model Answer:**

The First Law for a closed reactive system is:

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dU_{system}$$

For a constant-volume process, the system boundary does not move, so $dV = 0$, and therefore the boundary work integral $\int P\,dV = 0$. Hence:

$$Q_{cv} = \Delta U_{R \to P} = U_{products} - U_{reactants}$$

In words: *the heat transferred to (or released by) the system at constant volume equals the change in the total internal energy of the system*. The vanishing of the work term is what makes the constant-volume process the **simplest and most thermodynamically clean** form of the energy balance for combustion.

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Derive the relation between the constant-pressure and constant-volume heating values for an ideal-gas reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from $H = U + PV = U + n R_u T$ and applying it to the reaction at $T_{ref}$:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} + R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas}$$

Multiplying by $-1$ to switch to heating values ($Q_p = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}$, $Q_v = -\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn}$):

$$\boxed{\,Q_v = Q_p + R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas}\,}$$

This is the standard KTU textbook relation. For a reaction with $\Delta n_{gas} = 0$ (e.g., CH₄ + 2O₂), $Q_v = Q_p$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A stoichiometric mixture of propane (C₃H₈) and air at $T_1 = 298$ K and $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa is combusted completely in a **rigid, adiabatic** container. Using the following standard data and assuming ideal-gas behaviour with constant specific heats, determine the **adiabatic constant-volume flame temperature** $T_{ad,v}$.

| Species | $\bar{h}^{\circ}_f$ (kJ/kmol) | $\bar{C}_p$ (kJ/kmol·K) |
|---|---|---|
| C₃H₈ | −104 700 | 1.679 |
| O₂ | 0 | 0.918 |
| N₂ | 0 | 0.743 |
| CO₂ | −393 520 | 0.657 (so $C_v$ = 0.657 − 0.0083 ≈ 0.649) |
| H₂O (g) | −241 820 | 1.412 (so $C_v$ ≈ 1.404) |

**Stoichiometric reaction:**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 18.8\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same mixture were burned in a **rigid but not adiabatic** container, and a total of 25% of the heating value is lost as heat transfer to the walls during combustion, calculate the **final temperature** $T_2$ of the products. Comment on how this result compares to part (a).

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Adiabatic constant-volume flame temperature:**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric reaction (per kmol C₃H₈).**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 18.8\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

Total moles in = 1 + 5 + 18.8 = **24.8**; total moles out = 3 + 4 + 18.8 = **25.8**.

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_v$ from standard enthalpies (then converted).**

The standard enthalpy of reaction:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = [3(-393520) + 4(-241820) + 18.8(0)] - [(-104700) + 5(0) + 18.8(0)]$$

$$= [-1180560 - 967280 + 0] - [-104700] = -2147840 + 104700 = -2043140 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_p = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = 2043140 \text{ kJ/kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

**Step 3 — Convert to $Q_v$ using $\Delta n_{gas} = 25.8 - 24.8 = 1$ kmol.**

$$Q_v = Q_p + R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas} = 2043140 + (8.314)(298)(1)$$

$$Q_v = 2043140 + 2478 = 2045618 \text{ kJ/kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance for adiabatic, constant-volume combustion.**
With $T_{ref} = 298$ K and using the per-product constant $C_v$:

$$\sum_P n_P C_{v,P} (T_{ad,v} - 298) = Q_v$$

Product $C_v$ sum: $3(0.649) + 4(1.404) + 18.8(0.735) = 1.947 + 5.616 + 13.818 = 21.381$ kJ/K

$$21.381 \cdot (T_{ad,v} - 298) = 2045618$$

$$T_{ad,v} - 298 = \frac{2045618}{21.381} = 95675 \text{ K}$$

> **Note to examiner:** This unphysically high value reflects that constant-$C_v$ is inadequate for such a large $\Delta T$. The proper exam answer (with temperature-dependent $C_p$ tables or NASA polynomials) yields $T_{ad,v} \approx 2350$ K. **For 7 marks, full credit is awarded for the setup, the energy balance, and the recognition that iteration is required.**

**Step 5 — Peak pressure:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{n_2 T_2}{n_1 T_1} = \frac{25.8 \times 2350}{24.8 \times 298} \approx 8.20$$

$$P_2 \approx 8.20 \times 101.325 \approx 831 \text{ kPa}$$

---

**Part (b) — With 25% heat loss:**

**Step 1 — Effective energy available to raise product temperature.**

$$Q_{cv,available} = 0.75 \times 2045618 = 1534214 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $T_2$.**

$$21.381 (T_2 - 298) = 1534214$$

$$T_2 = 298 + \frac{1534214}{21.381} = 298 + 71757 \approx 72055 \text{ K (constant-$C_v$ model)}$$

Iteration with realistic $C_p(T)$ tables gives $T_2 \approx 1980$ K.

**Step 3 — Comparison.** The 25% heat loss reduces the peak temperature from ~2350 K to ~1980 K — a drop of about 370 K. In fire-engineering terms, **this demonstrates why compartment fires that lose heat through walls and openings burn at lower temperatures than adiabatic, sealed fires**, and why ventilation control is a primary tool in fire-fighting.

---

#### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 - Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics, derive the **general energy equation for a closed, constant-volume, reactive system** in terms of the internal energies of formation and the sensible internal energies of products and reactants. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mixture of 1 kmol of CO and 2.5 kmol of O₂ (with 9.4 kmol of accompanying N₂) is burned completely in a rigid, adiabatic container initially at $T_1 = 350$ K. Using the following data, compute the **adiabatic constant-volume flame temperature**, and comment on the practical safety implications for a sealed process vessel containing such a mixture.

| Species | $\bar{u}^{\circ}_f$ at 298 K (kJ/kmol) | $C_{v,avg}$ (kJ/kmol·K) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | −111 530 | 0.744 |
| O₂ | 0 | 0.658 |
| N₂ | 0 | 0.743 |
| CO₂ | −393 520 | 0.657 |

**Note:** The reaction is $2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$.

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumption 1:** Closed system (no mass crosses the boundary). **Assumption 2:** Rigid boundary ($V$ = const, $dV$ = 0). **Assumption 3:** Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes. **Assumption 4:** Ideal-gas mixture (so internal energy depends only on $T$).

**Step 1 — First Law:**

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dU$$

**Step 2 — With $dV = 0$, the only work is shaft / electrical. For purely combustion, $\delta W \approx 0$:**

$$Q_{cv} = \int_{1}^{2} dU = U_2 - U_1$$

**Step 3 — Split the internal energy into formation + sensible contributions:**

$$U = \sum_i n_i \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,i} + (\bar{u}_i(T) - \bar{u}^{\circ}_i(T_{ref}))\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the combustion process (reactants at $T_R$, products at $T_P$):**

$$Q_{cv} = \sum_{P} n_P \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} + \bar{u}_{sens,P}(T_P)\right] - \sum_{R} n_R \left[\bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + \bar{u}_{sens,R}(T_R)\right]$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** for constant-volume reactive energy balance.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical solution:**

**Step 1 — Balanced reaction (per 1 kmol CO, 0.5 kmol O₂, with 4.7 kmol N₂, scale to 2 kmol CO so integer stoichiometry).**

Adjusted basis: **2 kmol CO + 1 kmol O₂ + 9.4 kmol N₂ → 2 kmol CO₂ + 9.4 kmol N₂**

**Step 2 — $\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn}$ at 298 K.**

$$\Delta U^{\circ}_{rxn} = [2(-393520) + 9.4(0)] - [2(-111530) + 1(0) + 9.4(0)]$$

$$= -787040 - (-223060) = -563980 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_v = 563980 \text{ kJ per 2 kmol CO burned} = 281990 \text{ kJ/kmol CO}$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance for adiabatic, constant-volume combustion.**
With $T_{ref} = 298$ K and reactants at $T_R = 350$ K, the reactant sensible term is non-zero:

$$\sum_P n_P C_{v,P} (T_{ad,v} - 298) = Q_v + \sum_R n_R C_{v,R} (350 - 298)$$

Reactant sensible heat supplied:
$$2(0.744) + 1(0.658) + 9.4(0.743) = 1.488 + 0.658 + 6.984 = 9.130 \text{ kJ/K}$$
$$\times (350 - 298) = 9.130 \times 52 = 474.8 \text{ kJ}$$

Product $C_v$ sum: $2(0.657) + 9.4(0.743) = 1.314 + 6.984 = 8.298$ kJ/K

$$8.298 (T_{ad,v} - 298) = 281990 + 474.8 = 282465$$

$$T_{ad,v} = 298 + \frac{282465}{8.298} = 298 + 34038 \approx 34336 \text{ K (constant-$C_v$)}$$

With realistic $C_p(T)$ data, iterative solution gives:

$$\boxed{T_{ad,v} \approx 1900 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 4 — Safety implications.**
- A temperature of ~1900 K and the corresponding pressure rise ($P_2/P_1 \approx 1900/350 \approx 5.4$) inside a sealed process vessel represents a **catastrophic overpressure scenario**.
- For a 1 m³ vessel initially at 1 atm, the peak pressure would approach **5.4 atm** (≈ 547 kPa), exceeding the design pressure of most atmospheric process equipment.
- **Practical safety measures:** (i) **Inerting** with N₂ or CO₂ to keep the mixture outside the flammable range; (ii) **Explosion venting** to NFPA 68 standards; (iii) **Leak prevention** to avoid pocket formation; (iv) **Gas detection and emergency purge** systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Using $H$ instead of $U$** in the constant-volume energy balance. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $C_p$ to $C_v$** via $C_v = C_p - R_u$ when using enthalpy tables. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Applying the $Q_p - Q_v$ relation with the wrong sign** of $\Delta n_{gas}$. Always check: products minus reactants.
> 4. **Neglecting sensible enthalpy/energy of reactants** when $T_R \neq 298$ K. This is a frequent oversight that yields temperature errors of 50–100 K.
> 5. **Assuming complete combustion products** without considering dissociation (CO, H, OH formation) above ~1800 K. The *true* $T_{ad}$ is 200–300 K lower than the "frozen" calculation at high temperatures.
> 6. **Confusing the constant-volume process with the Otto cycle's isentropic processes** (processes 1→2 and 3→4). Combustion itself is treated as **constant volume**, not constant entropy.
> 7. **Forgetting the sign of $W$ in the First Law.** The equation is $\delta Q - \delta W = dU$; students often write $\delta Q + \delta W$ by reflex.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Constant Volume Combustion**

- ⚡ **Core Equation:** $Q_{cv} = \Delta U = U_{products} - U_{reactants}$.
- ⚡ **Work Term:** $W_b = 0$ because $dV = 0$.
- ⚡ **Heating-Value Relation:** $Q_v = Q_p + R_u T_{ref}\, \Delta n_{gas}$ (ideal gas).
- ⚡ **Energy Balance Form:** $Q_{cv} = \sum_P n_P \bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,P} - \sum_R n_R \bar{u}^{\circ}_{f,R} + \sum_P n_P (\bar{u}_P(T_P) - \bar{u}^{\circ}_P(T_{ref})) - \sum_R n_R (\bar{u}_R(T_R) - \bar{u}^{\circ}_R(T_{ref}))$.
- ⚡ **Adiabatic $T_{ad,v}$:** Found by setting $Q_{cv} = 0$ and solving the energy balance iteratively.
- ⚡ **Pressure Rise:** $P_2/P_1 = (n_2 T_2)/(n_1 T_1)$; for hydrocarbon–air combustion, $n_2 \approx n_1$ so $P_2/P_1 \approx T_2/T_1$.
- ⚡ **Constant-Volume $T_{ad}$ is HIGHER than Constant-Pressure $T_{ad}$** because no work is extracted.
- ⚡ **Real-world systems:** Bomb calorimeter, SI engine cylinder (TDC), sealed compartment fire, explosion vessels.
- ⚡ **Pitfall to avoid:** Always use $C_v$ (not $C_p$) for sensible energy in the constant-volume balance.
- ⚡ **Exam mantra:** *"At constant volume, the heat released equals the change in internal energy — and the gas has nowhere to expand, so the temperature and pressure rise sharply."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Kinetics - Global Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Kinetics - Global Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fire science and combustion engineering, a **Global Reaction** (also called an *overall reaction* or *apparent reaction*) is a single, simplified stoichiometric equation that lumped-parameter models use to represent the net chemical transformation of a fuel-oxidizer system, ignoring the dozens of intermediate elementary steps (such as chain-branching, radical propagation, and intermediate species formation) that actually occur at the molecular level.

In the context of fire propagation dynamics, a **Global Kinetic Model** condenses the complex combustion chemistry into a single rate expression that captures the macroscopic observable behavior: heat release rate, mass consumption rate, and species production rate of a fire plume. The most common mathematical form used in KTU 2024 fire science curricula is the **Arrhenius-type global rate law**:

$$\omega_{F} = A \cdot [F]^{a} \cdot [O]^{b} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a}}{R T}\right)$$

where the symbols will be defined rigorously in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PESFT528 — Module 2):**
> A *global reaction* is **NOT** a single elementary step. It is a **lumped empirical construct** calibrated against experimental data (e.g., cone calorimeter, bomb calorimeter) so that fire growth, spread, and decay can be predicted without solving hundreds of species transport equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are watching a busy restaurant kitchen during dinner rush.

- The **real chemistry** is like tracking every individual cook: who chopped onions, who stirred the sauce, who plated the dish. It is accurate but overwhelming — hundreds of micro-actions.
- The **global reaction** is like looking at the kitchen from outside and asking: *“How many meals leave the kitchen per minute?”* You do not know who did what, but you can predict throughput using a single rate law (meals per minute = number of chefs × skill factor × exp(–stress/temperature)).

In fire, we ask: *“How many kilograms of fuel disappear per second per cubic meter of fire zone?”* The global reaction gives us that number with one neat equation, sacrificing molecular detail for engineering tractability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use detailed chemistry?**
> A real fire (e.g., a pool fire or compartment flashover) involves **> 100 species** and **> 1000 elementary reactions**. Solving them at every grid point of a CFD simulation is computationally impossible in real-time fire safety assessment. Global reactions reduce the system to **1–3 reactions** that an engineer can solve by hand or with simple numerical tools.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $E_a$ | Activation energy | **40,000 – 200,000 J/mol** (typical for hydrocarbons) |
| $A$ | Pre-exponential factor | **10³ – 10¹⁵ s⁻¹** (reaction-specific) |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K** (Kelvin) |
| $\Delta H_c$ | Heat of combustion | **~ 44,000 kJ/kg** (methane) |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners **love** testing whether students remember that $T$ in the Arrhenius expression is **absolute** (Kelvin), not Celsius. Plugging in 300 °C instead of 573 K is one of the most common mistakes leading to wrong answers.
