## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Global Combustion Reaction

A general hydrocarbon fuel undergoing global combustion with oxygen in air is written as:

$$C_{x}H_{y}O_{z}N_{w} + \nu_{O_{2}} \, O_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; \nu_{CO_{2}} \, CO_{2} + \nu_{H_{2}O} \, H_{2}O + \nu_{N_{2}} \, N_{2} + \nu_{CO} \, CO + \nu_{C} \, C(s)$$

For a clean, well-ventilated methane fire, this collapses to the familiar stoichiometric form:

$$CH_{4} + 2\,O_{2} \longrightarrow CO_{2} + 2\,H_{2}O \quad \Delta H_{c} = -802.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 2.2 Components of the Global Rate Law

The full global rate expression is built from four multiplicative components:

1. **Pre-exponential Factor $A$** — captures the *frequency* of molecular collisions and the *orientational probability* of a successful collision. Think of it as the "ceiling" of the reaction rate.
2. **Concentration Dependence $[F]^a [O]^b$** — captures how the rate scales with fuel and oxidizer availability. The exponents $a$ and $b$ are the *partial orders*.
3. **Arrhenius Exponential $\exp(-E_a/RT)$** — captures the *fraction of molecules* that possess enough thermal energy to overcome the activation energy barrier at temperature $T$.
4. **Overall Reaction Order $n = a + b$** — determines the mathematical form of the integrated rate law.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Framework (Bulleted)

- **Step 1 — Identify the limiting reactant:** In a diffusion-controlled fire, fuel or oxygen may be the bottleneck. The global rate is then driven by the rate at which the limiting species is *supplied* (mixing-controlled) rather than the chemical rate.
- **Step 2 — Determine the reaction order from experiments:** In a well-stirred reactor, vary $[F]$ at constant $T$ and plot $\ln \omega$ vs. $\ln [F]$; the slope is $a$. Repeat for $[O]$ to find $b$.
- **Step 3 — Fit $A$ and $E_a$ from temperature sweep data:** Plot $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ (Arrhenius plot). Slope = $-E_a/R$, intercept = $\ln A$.
- **Step 4 — Couple to heat release:** Multiply the mass consumption rate by the heat of combustion to get the volumetric heat release rate $\dot{Q}'''$ in kW/m³.
- **Step 5 — Validate against fire test data:** Compare predicted HRR curve with cone calorimeter output; refine $A$ and $E_a$ if needed.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ or m³/(mol·s) |
| 2 | $\omega_{F} = k \, [F]^a [O]^b$ | Global reaction rate | kg/(m³·s) |
| 3 | $n = a + b$ | Overall reaction order | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}''' = \omega_{F} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Volumetric heat release rate | kW/m³ |
| 5 | $t_{c} = [F]_0 / (\omega_{F} \cdot n)$ | Characteristic reaction time | s |
| 6 | $\delta_{\text{chem}} = \alpha / u_{\infty}$ | Chemical reaction zone thickness | m |
| 7 | $\tau_{\text{chem}} = 1/k$ | Chemical time scale | s |
| 8 | $Da = \tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ | Damköhler number (kinetics vs. flow) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k(T+10)/k(T) \approx 2\text{–}3$ | Rule of thumb: 10 K temperature rise doubles rate | — |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{ignition}} \propto \exp(E_a/2RT)$ | Piloted ignition delay (Arrhenius-like) | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Arrhenius law is the *backbone* of fire growth modeling. Codes like NIST's **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)** and the **SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering** use global kinetics to predict flame spread, ignition time, and HRR — the three quantities that determine life safety in a building fire.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Safety Engineering:** Predict time to flashover in compartments (typically 600 °C upper layer).
- **Suppression System Design:** Calculate the minimum agent concentration to interrupt the global chain reaction.
- **Compartment Fire Modeling:** Drive zone models (CFAST, OZone) with a single HRR input.
- **Wildland-Urban Interface (WUI) Fire:** Estimate rate of spread using Rothermel's model, which embeds a global reaction rate for cellulosic fuels.
- **Propellant & Explosive Safety:** Assess deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT) using global decomposition kinetics.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** For any 7 or 14 mark question, the examiner looks for a *clean, line-by-line derivation* with explicit transitions. The derivations below are written exactly as a model answer script should be.

---

### 3.1 Derivation I — Integrated First-Order Global Rate Law

**Starting premise:** A unimolecular global decomposition of fuel with rate law:

$$\omega_{F} = -\frac{d[F]}{dt} = k\,[F]$$

**Step 1 — Separate the variables** to bring all $[F]$ terms to the left and $t$ terms to the right:

$$\frac{d[F]}{[F]} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides** from initial concentration $[F]_0$ at $t=0$ to $[F]$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{[F]_0}^{[F]} \frac{d[F]'}{[F]'} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals** (the left side is a natural logarithm):

$$\ln [F] - \ln [F]_0 = -k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Combine the logarithms** and exponentiate both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{[F]}{[F]_0}\right) = -k\,t \quad \Longrightarrow \quad [F](t) = [F]_0 \exp(-k\,t)$$

**Step 5 — Express the half-life** $t_{1/2}$ by setting $[F] = [F]_0/2$:

$$\frac{[F]_0}{2} = [F]_0 \exp(-k\,t_{1/2}) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

> **Key result:** For a first-order global reaction, the half-life is **independent of initial concentration**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Integrated Second-Order Global Rate Law

**Starting premise:** A bimolecular global rate where rate is proportional to $[F]^2$:

$$\omega_{F} = -\frac{d[F]}{dt} = k\,[F]^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables** (note: $k$ has units of m³/(mol·s) here, *not* s⁻¹):

$$\frac{d[F]}{[F]^{2}} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate** with limits $[F]_0$ to $[F]$:

$$\int_{[F]_0}^{[F]} \frac{d[F]'}{[F]'^{2}} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate** the left side using the standard integral $\int x^{-2}dx = -x^{-1}$:

$$\left[-\frac{1}{[F]'}\right]_{[F]_0}^{[F]} = -k\,t \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{[F]} - \frac{1}{[F]_0} = k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Half-life expression** by substituting $[F] = [F]_0/2$:

$$\frac{2}{[F]_0} - \frac{1}{[F]_0} = k\,t_{1/2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k\,[F]_0}$$

> **Key result:** For a second-order global reaction, the half-life is **inversely proportional to $[F]_0$** — diluting the fuel dramatically extends reaction time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Temperature Sensitivity of the Global Rate

We want to know how the rate constant changes when temperature changes from $T_1$ to $T_2$.

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius law at both temperatures:**

$$k_1 = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_1}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad k_2 = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio** to eliminate the unknown $A$:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{\exp(-E_a/R T_2)}{\exp(-E_a/R T_1)} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm** to get a linearized form suitable for data fitting:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange** to solve for activation energy from two experimental points:

$$E_a = \frac{R \, \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example:** Suppose a fuel has $k_1 = 0.01 \text{ s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 600$ K and $k_2 = 0.10 \text{ s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 650$ K. Find $E_a$.

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \cdot \ln(0.10/0.01)}{(1/600) - (1/650)} = \frac{8.314 \cdot \ln(10)}{0.001282}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 2.3026}{0.001282} = \frac{19.146}{0.001282} \approx 14{,}937 \text{ J/mol} \approx 14.9 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Link from Global Rate to Heat Release Rate (HRR)

The HRR is the single most important engineering output of a fire model.

**Step 1 — Start from the mass conservation of fuel** in a control volume:

$$\dot{m}_{F} = -\int_{V} \omega_{F} \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Assume a well-mixed control volume** so that $\omega_F$ is spatially uniform, and let the volume be $V$:

$$\dot{m}_{F} = -\omega_{F} \cdot V = A \exp(-E_a/RT) \cdot [F]^a [O]^b \cdot V$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the heat of combustion** $\Delta H_c$ (J/kg) to obtain the heat release rate:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{F} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi \cdot \eta$$

where $\chi$ is the combustion efficiency (typically 0.7–0.9 for ventilation-controlled fires) and $\eta$ is a radiative fraction modifier.

**Step 4 — Express per unit area** (the form used in fire growth correlations like the $t^2$ fire):

$$\dot{Q}'' = \frac{\dot{Q}}{A_{\text{floor}}} = \dot{m}_{F}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$$

**Step 5 — For the standard $t^2$ fire**, the HRR grows as $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$, where the growth coefficient $\alpha$ is empirically tied to the global kinetic parameters through:

$$\alpha \approx \rho_{\infty} c_p T_{\infty} \sqrt{\frac{g}{c_p T_{\infty}}} \cdot \frac{\dot{Q}_0^{1/3}}{...} \quad \text{(semi-empirical form)}$$

> **Physical insight:** A 10 K increase in compartment temperature can cause a **2× to 3× jump** in the global reaction rate, which propagates into a $\sim 1.5$× to $2$× rise in HRR — this is why flashover is so catastrophic once initiated.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Reaction Order Effects

| Property | Zero-Order ($n=0$) | First-Order ($n=1$) | Second-Order ($n=2$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rate law | $\omega = k$ | $\omega = k[F]$ | $\omega = k[F]^2$ |
| Integrated form | $[F] = [F]_0 - kt$ | $[F] = [F]_0 e^{-kt}$ | $1/[F] = 1/[F]_0 + kt$ |
| Half-life | $t_{1/2} = [F]_0/2k$ | $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ | $t_{1/2} = 1/(k[F]_0)$ |
| Half-life depends on $[F]_0$? | **Yes (linear)** | **No** | **Yes (inverse)** |
| Units of $k$ | mol/(m³·s) | s⁻¹ | m³/(mol·s) |
| Common in fire | Surface ablation | Pyrolysis | Soot oxidation |
| KTU typical weight | 1 mark (definition) | 5 marks (derivation) | 5 marks (comparison) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions for students (as per KTU 2024):** *Each question carries 3 marks. Answer in 2–3 sentences or with a single labeled diagram. Length: ~50–80 words.*

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set B]**
**Define a "global reaction" in the context of fire chemistry. Why is it preferred over detailed elementary mechanisms in fire modeling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **global reaction** is a single, lumped stoichiometric equation that represents the net chemical transformation of a fuel-oxidizer system, bypassing the many intermediate elementary steps that actually occur. It is preferred in fire modeling because detailed mechanisms involve hundreds of species and thousands of reactions, which are computationally intractable in CFD-based fire simulations. The global reaction reduces this to typically 1–3 equations with kinetic parameters ($A$, $E_a$) calibrated against experimental HRR data, enabling real-time life-safety assessment.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — explanation of elementary vs. global, 1 mark — engineering justification)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Arrhenius equation and explain the physical significance of (i) the pre-exponential factor $A$, and (ii) the activation energy $E_a$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Arrhenius equation is $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$.

- **(i) Pre-exponential factor $A$:** Represents the *frequency of molecular collisions* with the correct orientation for reaction. It sets the upper limit of the rate constant as $T \to \infty$. Typical units are s⁻¹ (first order) or m³/(mol·s) (second order).
- **(ii) Activation energy $E_a$:** The minimum *energy barrier* (in J/mol) that reactant molecules must overcome to transform into products. Higher $E_a$ means greater temperature sensitivity.

*(1 mark — equation, 1 mark — $A$ significance, 1 mark — $E_a$ significance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** *Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **one** of the two alternatives.*

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Module 2 / 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the integrated rate law for a **first-order global reaction** of the form $\omega_F = k[F]$. Show that the half-life is independent of the initial concentration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The rate constant of a global reaction is $k_1 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹ at $T_1 = 700$ K and $k_2 = 4 \times 10^{-2}$ s⁻¹ at $T_2 = 800$ K. Calculate the **activation energy** $E_a$ and the **pre-exponential factor** $A$. Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from:
$$\omega_F = -\frac{d[F]}{dt} = k[F]$$

Separating variables:
$$\frac{d[F]}{[F]} = -k\,dt$$

Integrating from $[F]_0$ at $t=0$ to $[F]$ at time $t$:
$$\int_{[F]_0}^{[F]} \frac{d[F]'}{[F]'} = -k \int_0^t dt'$$

$$\ln[F] - \ln[F]_0 = -kt \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\boxed{[F](t) = [F]_0 \exp(-kt)} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

For half-life, substitute $[F] = [F]_0/2$ into Eq. (1):
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k\,t_{1/2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Since the right-hand side of Eq. (3) contains **no $[F]_0$ term**, the half-life is independent of initial concentration. This is a **unique signature of first-order kinetics** and is used experimentally to identify reaction order.

*(2 marks — separation of variables, 2 marks — integration, 2 marks — integrated form, 1 mark — half-life independence statement with physical interpretation)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

From the Arrhenius equation:
$$E_a = \frac{R \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

Compute the ratio:
$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 8 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln(8) = 2.0794$$

Compute the temperature term:
$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{700} - \frac{1}{800} = \frac{800 - 700}{700 \times 800} = \frac{100}{560{,}000} = 1.7857 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

Compute $E_a$:
$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 2.0794}{1.7857 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{17.288}{1.7857 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 96{,}810 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 96.8 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

Now compute $A$ from $k_1 = A \exp(-E_a/RT_1)$:
$$A = k_1 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T_1}\right) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp\!\left(\frac{96{,}810}{8.314 \times 700}\right)$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R T_1} = \frac{96{,}810}{5819.8} = 16.636 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \exp(16.636) = 1.66 \times 10^{7}$$

$$A = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.66 \times 10^{7} = 8.3 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 8.3 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**Verification:** Plug $T_2$ back in:
$$k_2 = 8.3 \times 10^{4} \times \exp(-96{,}810/(8.314 \times 800)) = 8.3 \times 10^{4} \times \exp(-14.556)$$
$$= 8.3 \times 10^{4} \times 4.83 \times 10^{-7} = 4.01 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1} \;\checkmark$$

*(2 marks — $E_a$ formula, 2 marks — substitution and result, 2 marks — $A$ computation, 1 mark — verification)*

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 2 / 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **five stages of compartment fire development** and identify the stage at which the global reaction rate transitions from **kinetically controlled** to **ventilation controlled**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyurethane foam slab (heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 28{,}000$ kJ/kg) burns in a small compartment. The global rate law is $\omega_F = 5 \times 10^8 \exp(-90{,}000/RT)$ kg/(m³·s), with $[F] = 0.05$ kg/m³. The compartment volume is $V = 30$ m³ and ambient temperature is 300 K. Estimate the **peak HRR** in kW when the fire reaches $T = 900$ K. State clearly any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Stages of Compartment Fire (7 marks):**

1. **Incipient Stage** — Small flame at the ignition source, $T$ ≈ ambient, HRR < 50 kW. The reaction is kinetically controlled.
2. **Growth Stage** — HRR follows the empirical $t^2$ law, $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$. The flame spreads along the fuel surface; this is still **kinetically controlled** (Arrhenius regime).
3. **Flashover** — Sudden transition where the upper hot layer reaches ~ 600 °C and all combustible surfaces ignite. This is the **transition point** where the global reaction shifts from kinetic control to **ventilation (mixing) control**.
4. **Fully Developed Stage** — All fuel is involved, HRR is roughly constant and limited by the air supply through openings. This is **ventilation-controlled**; the global rate is bounded by the oxygen inflow rate $\dot{m}_{O_2}$, not by chemistry.
5. **Decay Stage** — Fuel is consumed, HRR decays exponentially; transitions to smoldering.

*(2 marks — naming the 5 stages, 2 marks — sketch description, 2 marks — identifying flashover as transition, 1 mark — ventilation-controlled justification)*

---

**(b) Peak HRR Calculation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions stated:**
- Well-mixed control volume
- Steady-state at $T = 900$ K
- Stoichiometric oxygen available
- Combustion efficiency $\chi = 1$ (clean burn)

**Step 1 — Evaluate the rate constant at $T = 900$ K:**

$$k = 5 \times 10^{8} \exp\!\left(-\frac{90{,}000}{8.314 \times 900}\right) \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{90{,}000}{7482.6} = 12.027 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \exp(-12.027) = 5.96 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$k = 5 \times 10^{8} \times 5.96 \times 10^{-6} = 2980 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute volumetric mass consumption rate** (assumed first-order in $[F]$):

$$\omega_F = k \cdot [F] = 2980 \times 0.05 = 149 \text{ kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 3 — Total mass consumption rate** in the compartment:

$$\dot{m}_F = \omega_F \cdot V = 149 \times 30 = 4470 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to HRR** using $\Delta H_c = 28{,}000$ kJ/kg:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_F \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi = 4470 \times 28{,}000 \times 1.0 = 1.252 \times 10^{8} \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 1.25 \times 10^{8} \text{ kW} \;\; (\text{or } 125 \text{ GW})}$$

**Reality check (Examiner's comment):** This number is *physically absurd* for a 30 m³ compartment, because the limiting factor in reality is the **ventilation-controlled oxygen supply**, not the chemical rate. The exercise illustrates that *the kinetic rate alone can grossly overestimate HRR*; an upper bound must be applied using:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{max, vent}} = \dot{m}_{O_2, \text{inflow}} \cdot \Delta H_c / r_O$$

where $r_O$ is the stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel mass ratio. KTU 2024 expects students to *recognize and comment* on this limitation.

*(2 marks — $k$ calculation, 2 marks — $\omega_F$ and $\dot{m}_F$, 2 marks — HRR conversion, 1 mark — physical sanity check / ventilation-limited commentary)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (Module 2 — Global Reaction):**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute temperature:** Using $T = 900$ instead of $T = 900$ K in the Arrhenius expression. → *Loss: 2 marks*
> 2. **Confusing the direction of the inequality in $k_2/k_1$:** Always verify whether the numerator is the *higher* or *lower* temperature rate constant before taking ln. → *Loss: 1 mark*
> 3. **Mixing up units of $k$:** First-order $k$ has units s⁻¹; second-order $k$ has units m³/(mol·s). Plugging the wrong units loses 1 mark in the final numerical answer.
> 4. **Ignoring the difference between kinetic and ventilation control:** Many students quote a kinetic HRR without ever mentioning the oxygen-supply cap. → *Loss: 1–2 marks in 14-mark answers*
> 5. **Sign convention in $\Delta H_c$:** Combustion is **exothermic**, so $\Delta H_c < 0$ thermodynamically, but the **magnitude** $|\Delta H_c|$ is what you multiply with the mass consumption. → *Loss: 1 mark*
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always state the units of $E_a$ (J/mol or kJ/mol), $A$ (s⁻¹ or m³/(mol·s)), and $\dot{Q}$ (kW or MW). The KTU model answer key explicitly *deducts 0.5 mark* for missing units.
> 7. **Writing global reaction rate as $\omega_F$ but using $\dot{m}_F$ in the next step without conversion:** $\omega_F$ is volumetric (kg/m³·s), $\dot{m}_F$ is total (kg/s). They are related by $\dot{m}_F = \omega_F \cdot V$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] A **global reaction** is a single lumped equation representing the net transformation of a fuel-oxidizer system; it is *not* an elementary step.
- [ ] The **Arrhenius equation** is $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and $T$ is in Kelvin.
- [ ] **Pre-exponential factor $A$** = collision frequency × orientation probability (units: s⁻¹ for unimolecular, m³/mol·s for bimolecular).
- [ ] **Activation energy $E_a$** = the energy barrier that reactant molecules must overcome (J/mol).
- [ ] **Reaction order $n = a + b$** where $a$ and $b$ are partial orders in fuel and oxidizer concentrations.
- [ ] **First-order kinetics** ⇒ $[F](t) = [F]_0 \exp(-kt)$ ⇒ half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ (independent of $[F]_0$).
- [ ] **Second-order kinetics** ⇒ $1/[F] = 1/[F]_0 + kt$ ⇒ half-life $t_{1/2} = 1/(k[F]_0)$ (inversely dependent on $[F]_0$).
- [ ] **Temperature jump rule:** A 10 K rise typically **doubles or triples** the rate constant.
- [ ] **Two-temperature formula:** $E_a = R \ln(k_2/k_1) / \left[(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)\right]$.
- [ ] **HRR link:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_F \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$ where $\chi$ is the combustion efficiency.
- [ ] **Fire stages:** Incipient → Growth ($t^2$ law) → Flashover → Fully Developed (ventilation-controlled) → Decay.
- [ ] **Kinetic vs. ventilation control:** Kinetic rates can vastly overestimate HRR if oxygen is not available; the smaller of the two determines the actual rate.
- [ ] **Damköhler number** $Da = \tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ — high $Da$ means chemistry is fast, mixing controls; low $Da$ means kinetics controls.
- [ ] **SFPE / NIST FDS** use global reaction models with calibrated $A$ and $E_a$ for practical fire simulations.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** $\Delta H_c < 0$ thermodynamically, but engineering HRR uses the magnitude $|\Delta H_c|$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elementary Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elementary Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **elementary reaction** is a chemical transformation that occurs in a **single molecular event** without any detectable intermediate species. Unlike an overall (or net) reaction, which merely represents the stoichiometric balance between reactants and products, an elementary reaction describes the *actual* microscopic pathway by which reactant molecules collide, rearrange their bonds, and form products in one concerted step.

In the context of **fire propagation and combustion dynamics**, the study of elementary reactions is foundational because all practical hydrocarbon combustion processes (e.g., methane oxidation, CO burnout, soot formation) are macroscopic manifestations of a large network of elementary reactions, commonly referred to as a **reaction mechanism**.

$$ \text{Elementary Reaction} \equiv \sum_{i} \nu_i' X_i \xrightarrow{k} \sum_{j} \nu_j'' X_j $$

where $X_i$ are reactant species, $X_j$ are product species, $\nu_i', \nu_j''$ are stoichiometric coefficients, and $k$ is the **rate constant** (a function of temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For an *elementary* reaction, the reaction order is *numerically equal* to its molecularity. This one-to-one correspondence holds **only** for elementary reactions — never for net reactions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded college canteen during the lunch break.

- A **net (overall) reaction** is like a complex transaction: a student pays ₹100 → gets a samosa, a chai, and change back. We only see the *before* and *after* states.
- An **elementary reaction** is like watching a *single handshake* between two specific people in that canteen. It is the smallest, indivisible social interaction that actually takes place.

The canteen is full of thousands of these handshakes (collisions) per minute. Some lead to a meal (successful reaction), most do not. Each *successful* handshake is one elementary event. A fire is essentially a *staggeringly large synchronized orchestra* of such elementary events, where heat release from each "handshake" sustains the next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid — The Three Pillars of an Elementary Reaction:**
> 1. **No intermediate** detectable on the reaction coordinate.
> 2. **Order = Molecularity** (a direct consequence of collision statistics).
> 3. The **rate law can be written directly from the stoichiometry** without experimental determination.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology — Must Memorize for KTU

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Molecularity** | Number of molecules/atoms participating in the elementary step | Single, double, or triple |
| **Order of Reaction** | Exponent of concentration in the rate law | Determined experimentally (or = molecularity for elementary steps) |
| **Rate Constant ($k$)** | Proportionality constant in the rate expression | Strongly temperature-dependent |
| **Activation Energy ($E_a$)** | Minimum energy barrier for the reaction to proceed | Given in **J/mol** or **kJ/mol** |
| **Pre-exponential Factor ($A$)** | Frequency of properly oriented collisions | Units depend on overall order |
| **Reaction Coordinate** | The 1-D path from reactants → transition state → products | Central to transition state theory |

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Gas Constant (Standard Value):** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$. This value appears in the Arrhenius equation and is **non-negotiable** in calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by Molecularity

Elementary reactions are classified by the number of reacting particles in the single molecular event. This classification is **rigorously defined** and forms a frequent KTU short-answer topic.

### 2.1.1 Unimolecular Reactions (Molecularity = 1)
A single molecule decomposes or rearranges. In flames, these often represent **thermal dissociation** steps.

$$ \text{AB} \xrightarrow{k} \text{A} + \text{B} $$

**Example in fire chemistry:**
$$ \text{N}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{k_1} \text{N}_2 + \text{O} $$
$$ \text{HO}_2 + \text{M} \xrightarrow{} \text{H} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \quad (\text{M is third-body collider}) $$

Rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[\text{AB}]$ → **First order overall**.

### 2.1.2 Bimolecular Reactions (Molecularity = 2)
Two species collide and react. The vast majority of flame chemistry elementary steps are bimolecular.

$$ \text{A} + \text{B} \xrightarrow{k} \text{Products} $$

**Example in fire chemistry:**
$$ \text{H} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH} + \text{O} \quad (k_1 \text{ in many mechanisms}) $$
$$ \text{CO} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H} \quad (k_8 \text{ — critical for CO burnout}) $$

Rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}][\text{B}]$ → **Second order overall**.

### 2.1.3 Termolecular Reactions (Molecularity = 3)
Three bodies must simultaneously interact. These are **statistically rare** in the gas phase.

$$ \text{A} + \text{B} + \text{C} \xrightarrow{k} \text{Products} $$

**Example:**
$$ \text{H} + \text{H} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{M}^* $$
$$ \text{O} + \text{O} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{M}^* $$

The third body **M** removes excess energy to stabilize the product. Rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}][\text{B}][\text{C}]$ → **Third order overall**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** Termolecular reactions are often *written* as pseudo-bimolecular when one species (typically M) is in large excess (e.g., atmospheric nitrogen). The rate constant is then re-defined to absorb the constant $[M]$.

---

## 2.2 The Why Behind Elementary Reaction Rate Laws

For an elementary reaction, the rate is proportional to the **frequency of productive collisions** between the reacting species. The kinetic theory of gases tells us that the bimolecular collision frequency is proportional to the product of concentrations. Thus, for:

$$ \text{A} + \text{B} \rightarrow \text{Products} $$

$$ \text{Rate} = -\frac{d[\text{A}]}{dt} = k[\text{A}]^m[\text{B}]^n $$

where, for an **elementary** step, $m$ and $n$ are exactly the stoichiometric coefficients (i.e., $m = 1$, $n = 1$).

---

## 2.3 The Arrhenius Equation — The Heart of Fire Kinetics

The temperature dependence of the rate constant $k$ for an elementary reaction is given by the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$ k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

### 2.3.1 Component-by-Component Meaning

- **$A$ (Pre-exponential Factor):** The maximum possible rate if no energy barrier existed. Determined by collision frequency, orientation, and steric factors. Units: $\text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ for bimolecular.
- **$E_a$ (Activation Energy):** The minimum *threshold* energy the colliding pair must possess. Units: $\text{J/mol}$ or $\text{kJ/mol}$.
- **$R$ (Universal Gas Constant):** $8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$.
- **$T$ (Absolute Temperature):** In **Kelvin (K)**.

### 2.3.2 Linearized Form (For Data Plotting)

$$ \ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

A plot of $\ln k$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line** with:
- Slope = $-E_a / R$ → used to extract $E_a$
- Intercept = $\ln A$ → used to extract $A$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $k = A e^{-E_a / (RT)}$ | Arrhenius rate law | All thermal reactions |
| 2 | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius ratio | Comparing rates at 2 temps |
| 3 | $E_a = -R \cdot (\text{slope of } \ln k \text{ vs } 1/T)$ | Slope method | Experimental $E_a$ |
| 4 | Rate $= k[\text{A}]^m[\text{B}]^n$ | General rate law | For elementary steps, $m,n$ = stoich. coeffs |
| 5 | Molecularity = Order | For elementary reactions ONLY | Key identity |
| 6 | $\Delta t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life (1st order) | Decay calculations |
| 7 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k[\text{A}]_0}$ | Half-life (2nd order) | Bimolecular half-life |
| 8 | $Z_{AB} = N_A \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu}} [A][B]$ | Collision frequency (kinetic theory) | Pre-exponential interpretation |
| 9 | $k = p \cdot Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$ | Collision theory $k$ | Where $p$ is steric factor |
| 10 | $k = \kappa \frac{k_B T}{h} \frac{Q^{\ddagger}}{Q_A Q_B} e^{-E_0 / (RT)}$ | Transition state theory $k$ (Eyring) | Activated complex theory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant ($1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$), $h$ = Planck constant ($6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$), $N_A$ = Avogadro number, $\mu$ = reduced mass, $Q$ = partition function, $\kappa$ = transmission coefficient.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Elementary reaction kinetics are the **building blocks** of every practical fire engineering tool:

1. **CFD Fire Simulation (FDS):** Software like NIST's *Fire Dynamics Simulator* uses detailed chemical mechanisms (e.g., GRI-Mech 3.0 with **325 elementary reactions** for methane) to predict flame structure, CO yield, and pollutant formation.
2. **Fire Suppression Design:** Inert gas suppressants work by *third-body collision* with radical chain carriers (H, OH, O), terminating elementary chain-branching steps.
3. **Explosion Limits:** Flammability limits are governed by the competition between elementary chain-branching (e.g., H + O₂ → OH + O) and chain-terminating reactions.
4. **Engine Knock & Detonation:** Auto-ignition in SI engines is dictated by the temperature sensitivity of elementary decomposition reactions of peroxides.
5. **Cone Calorimeter Data Reduction:** Although the experiment measures global heat release, the *interpretation* of results uses elementary kinetic frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Arrhenius Equation from Collision Theory

**Goal:** Show why $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ naturally arises from kinetic theory.

### Step 1 — Bimolecular Collision Frequency
For two species A and B, kinetic theory gives the number of collisions per unit volume per unit time as:

$$ Z_{AB} = N_A \, \sigma_{AB} \, \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu}} \, [A][B] $$

### Step 2 — Fraction with Sufficient Energy
Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics show the fraction of collisions whose relative kinetic energy exceeds the threshold $E_a$ along the line of approach is:

$$ f = e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

### Step 3 — Steric (Orientation) Factor
Not all sufficiently energetic collisions are *oriented* correctly. Let $p$ (with $0 \le p \le 1$) denote the **steric factor**.

### Step 4 — Rate of Successful Collisions
The reaction rate equals the collision frequency times the fraction that is energetic times the fraction properly oriented:

$$ \text{Rate} = p \cdot Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

### Step 5 — Extract the Rate Constant
Comparing with Rate $= k[A][B]$, we identify:

$$ k = p \cdot N_A \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu}} \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

The first three terms are often lumped into the pre-exponential factor $A$ (which is weakly temperature-dependent compared to the exponential):

$$ \boxed{k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R T)}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** The pure collision theory slightly underestimates $A$ for complex polyatomic species. *Transition state theory* (Eyring) corrects this by considering the *activated complex* on the reaction coordinate. The result has the same Arrhenius *form* but a more physically meaningful $A$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Temperature Form of the Arrhenius Equation

**Given:** $k_1 = A e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}$ and $k_2 = A e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$

**Step 1:** Take natural logarithm of both equations.

$$ \ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T_1} $$
$$ \ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T_2} $$

**Step 2:** Subtract the two equations ($\ln k_2 - \ln k_1$).

$$ \ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{R T_2} + \frac{E_a}{R T_1} $$

**Step 3:** Factor the right-hand side.

$$ \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$

**Step 4:** The boxed result, used in KTU numerical problems:

$$ \boxed{\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Validation:** If $T_2 > T_1$, then $1/T_1 > 1/T_2$, so the right side is positive → $k_2 > k_1$. ✅ A higher temperature *always* increases $k$ for an endothermic- or even mildly exothermic elementary step.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Integrated Rate Law for a First-Order Elementary Reaction

Consider the elementary step:

$$ \text{A} \xrightarrow{k} \text{Products} \quad \text{(unimolecular)} $$

**Step 1:** Write the differential rate equation.

$$ -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k [A] $$

**Step 2:** Separate variables.

$$ \frac{d[A]}{[A]} = -k \, dt $$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = t$.

$$ \int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]}{[A]} = -k \int_0^t dt $$

**Step 4:** Evaluate both integrals.

$$ \ln [A] - \ln [A]_0 = -k t $$

**Step 5:** Final linearized form.

$$ \boxed{\ln [A] = \ln [A]_0 - k t \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad [A] = [A]_0 \, e^{-k t}} $$

A plot of $\ln [A]$ versus $t$ gives a straight line with slope $-k$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Integrated Rate Law for a Second-Order Elementary Reaction

Consider:

$$ \text{A} + \text{B} \xrightarrow{k} \text{Products} $$

Let $[A]_0 = a$ and $[B]_0 = b$, with $a \ne b$.

**Step 1:** Let $x$ be the extent of reaction at time $t$.

$$ [A] = a - x, \quad [B] = b - x $$

**Step 2:** Rate law.

$$ \frac{dx}{dt} = k (a - x)(b - x) $$

**Step 3:** Partial-fraction decomposition yields:

$$ \frac{1}{b - a}\left[\frac{1}{a - x} - \frac{1}{b - x}\right] dx = k \, dt $$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $0$ to $x$.

$$ \frac{1}{b-a} \ln\left(\frac{a(b-x)}{b(a-x)}\right) = k t $$

**Step 5:** Boxed integrated form (the form used in KTU numericals):

$$ \boxed{\frac{1}{b - a} \ln\left(\frac{[A][B]_0}{[A]_0 [B]}\right) = k t} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case** $a = b$: $\frac{1}{[A]} - \frac{1}{[A]_0} = k t$ (used when reactants start at stoichiometric ratio).

---

## 3.5 Sample Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** For an elementary decomposition, $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 700 \, \text{K}$ and $k = 4.8 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 900 \, \text{K}$. Determine $E_a$ and $A$.

**Step 1:** Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius form.

$$ \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio of $k$ values.

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 19.2 $$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm.

$$ \ln(19.2) = 2.955 $$

**Step 4:** Compute the temperature reciprocal difference.

$$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{700} - \frac{1}{900} = \frac{900 - 700}{700 \times 900} = \frac{200}{630000} = 3.175 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $E_a$.

$$ E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 2.955}{3.175 \times 10^{-4}} $$

**Step 6:** Numerical evaluation.

$$ E_a = \frac{24.567}{3.175 \times 10^{-4}} = 77,376 \, \text{J/mol} \approx 77.4 \, \text{kJ/mol} $$

**Step 7:** Solve for $A$ using $k_1 = A e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}$.

$$ A = k_1 \cdot e^{+E_a / (R T_1)} $$

**Step 8:** Compute the exponential argument.

$$ \frac{E_a}{R T_1} = \frac{77376}{8.314 \times 700} = \frac{77376}{5819.8} = 13.295 $$

**Step 9:** Compute the exponential.

$$ e^{13.295} = 5.89 \times 10^{5} $$

**Step 10:** Final value of $A$.

$$ A = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.89 \times 10^{5} = 1472.5 \, \text{s}^{-1} \approx 1.47 \times 10^{3} \, \text{s}^{-1} $$

**Final Answer:** $E_a = 77.4 \, \text{kJ/mol}$, $A = 1.47 \times 10^{3} \, \text{s}^{-1}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** Fire-related elementary reactions typically have $E_a$ between **40 and 250 kJ/mol**. Our answer lies comfortably in this range, confirming physical plausibility.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Identifying an Elementary Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Chemical Equation] --> B{Does it occur in a SINGLE molecular event with NO intermediate?}
    B -- No --> C[Net Overall Reaction]
    B -- Yes --> D[Confirmed Elementary Reaction]
    D --> E{Count reacting species}
    E -- 1 species --> F[Unimolecular — First order]
    E -- 2 species --> G[Bimolecular — Second order]
    E -- 3 species --> H[Termolecular — Third order]
    F --> I[Rate = k A]
    G --> J[Rate = k A B]
    H --> K[Rate = k A B C]
    I --> L[Write rate law directly from stoichiometry]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Reaction Coordinate Diagram for an Elementary Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reactants] -- Energy barrier Ea --> TS[Transition State]
    TS -- Energy release DeltaH --> P[Products]
    R -->|Lower energy| R
    P -->|Higher or lower| P
    TS -->|Peak of potential energy surface| TS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The vertical distance from reactants to the **transition state (TS)** peak equals $E_a$ (activation energy).
> - The vertical distance from **reactants to products** equals $\Delta H$ (enthalpy of reaction).
> - A **negative** $\Delta H$ means an **exothermic** elementary step (typical of combustion chain-branching reactions).

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Elementary Steps to Fire Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph M1[Layer 1 - Microscopic]
    A1[Elementary Reaction Event] --> A2[Single Collision Molecules]
    end
    subgraph M2[Layer 2 - Mesoscopic]
    B1[Reaction Mechanism Network] --> B2[Dozens to Hundreds of Elementary Steps]
    end
    subgraph M3[Layer 3 - Macroscopic]
    C1[Fire Phenomena] --> C2[Flame Speed, Heat Release, Species Yields]
    end
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    A2 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
```

## 4.4 Schematic of a Typical Combustion Elementary Reaction Pathway (H2 / O2 System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2O2[Reactants H2 plus O2] --> R1[Step 1 H plus O2 gives OH plus O]
    R1 --> R2[Step 2 O plus H2 gives OH plus H]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3 H2 plus OH gives H2O plus H]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4 H plus OH plus M gives H2O plus M]
    R4 --> PROD[Final Products H2O plus Heat]
    R1 -.Chain branching.-> R2
    R2 -.Chain branching.-> R3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** Each arrow in this diagram is an *elementary* reaction. Notice how Steps 1 and 2 are *chain-branching* (one radical produces two). This exponential growth in radical concentration is what gives flames their characteristic rapid energy release.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an elementary reaction. Why does the rate law of an elementary reaction follow directly from its stoichiometry?  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **elementary reaction** is a chemical transformation that occurs in a single molecular event, proceeding through one transition state without any stable intermediate species. (1 mark)

For an elementary reaction, the rate is determined by the frequency of productive collisions between reactant molecules. According to kinetic theory, the collision frequency between two species is proportional to the product of their concentrations. Therefore, the rate law can be written directly from the stoichiometric coefficients as the exponents. (1 mark)

For example, for the elementary step $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$, the rate is $\text{Rate} = k[A][B]$. The exponents 1 and 1 correspond exactly to the stoichiometric coefficients, a property exclusive to elementary reactions. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between molecularity and order of reaction. Under what condition are they equal?  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Molecularity | Order |
|--------|--------------|-------|
| **Definition** | Number of molecules participating in a single elementary event (1, 2, or 3) | Sum of concentration exponents in the rate law |
| **Theoretical/Experimental** | Theoretical (from mechanism) | Experimental (from data) |
| **Can be zero or fractional?** | Always a small positive integer (1, 2, 3) | Can be 0, fractional, or negative |
| **Depends on mechanism** | Yes | Not always |

**Condition for equality (1 mark):** For an *elementary* reaction, the order of reaction is numerically equal to its molecularity. For non-elementary (overall) reactions, they generally differ.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**a)** Starting from the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, derive the Arrhenius equation $k = A e^{-E_a / (RT)}$ for a bimolecular elementary reaction. Clearly state the role of the **steric factor** $p$ and the **activation energy** $E_a$. (7 marks)

**b)** For the elementary decomposition of acetone in the gas phase, the rate constant is $3.7 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $600 \, \text{K}$ and $1.05 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $700 \, \text{K}$. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Bimolecular collision frequency per unit volume from kinetic theory of gases. (1 mark)

$$ Z_{AB} = N_A \, \sigma_{AB} \, \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu}} \, [A][B] $$

**Step 2:** Fraction of collisions with energy $\ge E_a$ (Maxwell-Boltzmann). (2 marks)

$$ f = e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

**Step 3:** Multiply by steric factor $p$ to account for proper molecular orientation. (1 mark)

$$ \text{Successful collision rate} = p \cdot Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

**Step 4:** Identify $k$ from $\text{Rate} = k[A][B]$. (1 mark)

$$ k = p \cdot N_A \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu}} \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} $$

**Step 5:** Group weakly $T$-dependent prefactor into $A$. (1 mark)

$$ \boxed{k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / (R T)}} $$

**Step 6:** Concluding remark. (1 mark) The **steric factor $p$** accounts for the probability that colliding molecules are properly oriented. The **activation energy $E_a$** is the minimum energy threshold that must be overcome for bond rearrangement.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 600 \, \text{K}, \; k_1 = 3.7 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{s}^{-1}$
- $T_2 = 700 \, \text{K}, \; k_2 = 1.05 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}$
- $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1:** Apply the two-temperature form. (1 mark)

$$ \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$

**Step 2:** Calculate the ratio. (1 mark)

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-2}}{3.7 \times 10^{-5}} = 283.78 $$

**Step 3:** Take the natural log. (1 mark)

$$ \ln(283.78) = 5.648 $$

**Step 4:** Compute the reciprocal-temperature difference. (1 mark)

$$ \frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{700} = \frac{700 - 600}{600 \times 700} = \frac{100}{420000} = 2.381 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $E_a$. (1 mark)

$$ E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 5.648}{2.381 \times 10^{-4}} = 197{,}213 \, \text{J/mol} $$

**Step 6:** Convert to kJ/mol. (0.5 mark)

$$ \boxed{E_a \approx 197.2 \, \text{kJ/mol}} $$

**Step 7:** Calculate $A$ using $k_1 = A e^{-E_a/(RT_1)}$. (1 mark)

$$ A = k_1 \cdot e^{+E_a/(R T_1)} $$

**Step 8:** Compute the exponent. (0.5 mark)

$$ \frac{E_a}{R T_1} = \frac{197213}{8.314 \times 600} = 39.53 $$

$$ A = 3.7 \times 10^{-5} \times e^{39.53} = 3.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.49 \times 10^{17} $$

$$ \boxed{A \approx 5.5 \times 10^{12} \, \text{s}^{-1}} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)  `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**a)** What is an elementary reaction? Explain unimolecular, bimolecular, and termolecular reactions with **two examples each** from combustion chemistry. (7 marks)

**b)** A first-order elementary gas-phase reaction has $k = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 500 \, \text{K}$ and $k = 2.7 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 600 \, \text{K}$. (i) Calculate the activation energy. (ii) What is the rate constant at $T_3 = 750 \, \text{K}$? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** An elementary reaction is a chemical change that occurs in a single, indivisible molecular event with no intermediate species, where the rate law can be written directly from the balanced equation.

**Unimolecular (Molecularity = 1) — 2 marks:**
A single molecule undergoes rearrangement or decomposition.
- $\text{N}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}$
- $\text{HO}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{H} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M}$
- Rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[A]$ → first order.

**Bimolecular (Molecularity = 2) — 2 marks:**
Two species collide and react.
- $\text{CO} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}$
- $\text{H} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH} + \text{O}$
- Rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[A][B]$ → second order.

**Termolecular (Molecularity = 3) — 2 marks:**
Three-body collision; statistically rare; needs a third body **M** to remove excess energy.
- $\text{H} + \text{H} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{M}^*$
- $\text{O} + \text{O} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{M}^*$
- Rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[A][B][M]$ → third order.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}^{-1}, \; T_1 = 500 \, \text{K}$
- $k_2 = 2.7 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}, \; T_2 = 600 \, \text{K}$

**Step 1: Compute $E_a$ — Apply two-temperature Arrhenius. (2 marks)**

$$ \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-2}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 15.0 $$

$$ \ln(15.0) = 2.708 $$

$$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{600} = \frac{600 - 500}{500 \times 600} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 2.708}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 67{,}520 \, \text{J/mol} \approx 67.5 \, \text{kJ/mol} $$

**Step 2: Find $A$. (1 mark)**

$$ A = k_1 e^{+E_a/(R T_1)} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times e^{67520 / (8.314 \times 500)} $$

$$ \frac{E_a}{R T_1} = \frac{67520}{4157} = 16.24 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{16.24} = 1.12 \times 10^{7} $$

$$ A = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.12 \times 10^{7} = 2.01 \times 10^{4} \, \text{s}^{-1} $$

**Step 3: Calculate $k_3$ at $T_3 = 750 \, \text{K}$. (3 marks)**

$$ k_3 = A e^{-E_a / (R T_3)} $$

$$ \frac{E_a}{R T_3} = \frac{67520}{8.314 \times 750} = \frac{67520}{6235.5} = 10.83 $$

$$ e^{-10.83} = 1.97 \times 10^{-5} $$

$$ k_3 = 2.01 \times 10^{4} \times 1.97 \times 10^{-5} = 0.396 \, \text{s}^{-1} $$

**Final Answer:** $E_a \approx 67.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$, $A \approx 2.01 \times 10^{4} \, \text{s}^{-1}$, $k_3(750 \, \text{K}) \approx 0.396 \, \text{s}^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T$ to Kelvin** when applying the Arrhenius equation. Using Celsius is an *automatic zero* for that numerical.
> 2. **Mixing up the order of subtraction** in $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ vs $\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$. A sign error in $E_a$ is a frequent mark-deduction point.
> 3. **Claiming that order = molecularity for *all* reactions.** It is *strictly true only for elementary reactions* — examiners love to test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring units of $k$ and $A$.** For a first-order reaction, units are $\text{s}^{-1}$; for second-order, $\text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. Stating units incorrectly can lose **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **In two-part questions, writing out the full derivation but skipping the numerical substitution.** Always show the *plug-in* step.
> 6. **In transition-state theory, confusing $E_a$ with $E_0$ (the energy barrier at 0 K).** The Arrhenius $E_a$ is *experimentally* defined from the slope of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Elementary Reaction:** Occurs in a single molecular event; no detectable intermediates.
- **Molecularity vs Order:** Equal *only* for elementary reactions; molecularity is theoretical, order is experimental.
- **Three Molecularities:**
  - Unimolecular → 1st order → Rate $= k[A]$
  - Bimolecular → 2nd order → Rate $= k[A][B]$
  - Termolecular → 3rd order → Rate $= k[A][B][C]$ (very rare)
- **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a / (RT)}$, with $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$.
- **Linearized Arrhenius:** $\ln k = \ln A - (E_a / R) \cdot (1/T)$ → straight-line plot.
- **Two-Temperature Formula:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a / R)\left[(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)\right]$.
- **First-Order Integrated Law:** $\ln [A] = \ln [A]_0 - k t$.
- **Second-Order Integrated Law (unequal initial):** $\dfrac{1}{b - a} \ln\left(\dfrac{[A][B]_0}{[A]_0 [B]}\right) = k t$.
- **Combustion Examples to Memorize:**
  - $\text{H} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH} + \text{O}$ (chain branching)
  - $\text{CO} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}$ (CO burnout)
  - $\text{H} + \text{OH} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{M}$ (termination)
- **Units Discipline:** Always write units of $k$ and $A$ in answers.
- **Temperature Discipline:** $T$ *must* be in Kelvin.
- **Typical $E_a$ range for fire-related elementary steps:** **40 – 250 kJ/mol**.
- **Engineering Connect:** CFD codes (FDS), suppression design, flammability limits, detonation chemistry all use elementary reaction frameworks.
- **Avoid Confusion:** Don't equate $E_a$ (Arrhenius) with $E_0$ (potential energy at 0 K from TST).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "ELMO"** for Elementary reactions:
> **E** = Event is single-step, **L** = Law of rate matches stoichiometry, **M** = Molecularity equals Order, **O** = One transition state — *no* intermediate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecularity of Elementary Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Molecularity of Elementary Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **chemical kinetics** applied to combustion and fire propagation, the **molecularity of an elementary reaction** is defined as the **total number of reactant species (atoms, molecules, or ions)** that are involved in a single, indivisible reaction step (the *elementary step*) as written in the stoichiometric equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 – Reaction Kinetics in Fire):**
> "The molecularity of an elementary reaction is the exact number of reacting particles (molecules, atoms, or ions) that collide and undergo a chemical transformation in a single reaction event."

Mathematically, for an elementary step:
$$aA + bB \longrightarrow \text{Products}$$
The molecularity is given by:
$$n = a + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the stoichiometric coefficients of the reactants appearing in the balanced elementary step.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of molecularity like a **group handshake at a party**:
- A **unimolecular reaction** is like one person clapping their own hands — only one particle is doing something.
- A **bimolecular reaction** is like two people shaking hands — exactly two participants must meet.
- A **termolecular reaction** is like three friends doing a triple high-five — three participants must all be at the exact same spot at the exact same instant.

In a fire, consider the combustion of methane. The **overall** reaction looks like:
$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$
But the **elementary step** actually involves a single oxygen molecule and a single methane molecule colliding. So the *elementary* molecularity is **2 (bimolecular)**, even though the *overall stoichiometry* is 3.

## 1.3 Classification by Molecularity

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Molecularity is always a **whole, positive integer** (1, 2, 3, ...). It can never be zero, fractional, or negative.

| Type | Molecularity | General Form |
|------|:---:|:---|
| Unimolecular | **1** | $A \rightarrow Products$ |
| Bimolecular | **2** | $A + A \rightarrow Products$ or $A + B \rightarrow Products$ |
| Termolecular | **3** | $A + B + C \rightarrow Products$ |

## 1.4 Why Molecularity Matters in Fire Dynamics

In fire propagation, most combustion chain reactions propagate via **bimolecular elementary steps** (e.g., $H^\cdot + O_2 \rightarrow OH^\cdot + O^\cdot$). The molecularity dictates the **frequency of effective collisions** in the flame front and ultimately the **laminar flame speed**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification & Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Unimolecular Elementary Reactions ($n = 1$)
- **Definition:** A single molecule undergoes internal rearrangement, decomposition, or dissociation.
- **Fire context example:** $O_3 \rightarrow O_2 + O^\cdot$ (ozone decomposition in upper flame zones).
- **Rate Law:** For elementary unimolecular reaction $A \rightarrow P$:
$$\text{Rate} = k[A]$$
- **Mechanism interpretation:** A single energized molecule passes over the activation energy barrier.

### 2.1.2 Bimolecular Elementary Reactions ($n = 2$)
- **Definition:** Two species (either same or different) must collide simultaneously.
- **Fire context example:** The famous **Hydrogen-Bromine chain initiation**: $Br_2 + M \rightarrow 2Br^\cdot + M$ (where $M$ is a third-body collision partner, making the elementary step *quasi-bimolecular*).
- **Rate Law:** For $A + B \rightarrow P$:
$$\text{Rate} = k[A][B]$$
- For $2A \rightarrow P$:
$$\text{Rate} = k[A]^2$$

### 2.1.3 Termolecular Elementary Reactions ($n = 3$)
- **Definition:** Three species must collide simultaneously in a single event.
- **Rate Law:** For $A + B + C \rightarrow P$:
$$\text{Rate} = k[A][B][C]$$
- **Fire context example:** The **third-body recombination** $H^\cdot + H^\cdot + M \rightarrow H_2 + M$ where $M$ removes excess energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probability Insight:** The probability of a *simultaneous three-body collision* is roughly **1000× lower** than a two-body collision. This is why termolecular elementary steps are **rate-limiting** in many combustion mechanisms, often requiring a third-body "chaperone" $M$.

## 2.2 Molecularity vs. Order of Reaction — The Critical Distinction

This is a **favourite KTU examiner's question** and a common source of marks lost.

| Property | Molecularity | Order of Reaction |
|----------|:---:|:---:|
| **Definition** | Number of particles in an elementary step | Sum of exponents of concentration terms in rate law |
| **Applies to** | Elementary reactions *only* | Both elementary and overall (empirical) reactions |
| **Value** | Always a positive integer (**1, 2, or 3**) | Can be zero, fractional, or negative |
| **Determination** | From the balanced elementary equation | From experimental rate data |
| **Dependence on mechanism** | Independent | Highly dependent |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Many students assume that the stoichiometric coefficients in the overall reaction give the molecularity. **This is FALSE.** Molecularity is defined only for *elementary steps*. The overall reaction order must be determined experimentally.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units of $k$ | Notes |
|---------|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Unimolecular rate | $r = k[A]$ | $s^{-1}$ | First-order kinetics |
| Bimolecular (same) | $r = k[A]^2$ | $L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$ | Second-order, identical reactants |
| Bimolecular (different) | $r = k[A][B]$ | $L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$ | Second-order, distinct reactants |
| Termolecular | $r = k[A][B][C]$ | $L^2 \cdot mol^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$ | Extremely rare; needs third body |
| Molecularity number | $n = \sum_{i} \nu_i$ (reactants only) | dimensionless | $\nu_i$ = stoichiometric coefficient of reactant $i$ |
| Pseudo-molecularity | $n = 1$ (apparent) | varies | One reactant in large excess |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **fire safety engineering** and **combustion modelling**, the molecularity of elementary steps in a mechanism (such as the GRI-Mech 3.0 for methane combustion) directly determines:
1. **Rate constants** at a given temperature (via Arrhenius law $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$).
2. **Laminar burning velocity** of the fuel-air mixture.
3. **Auto-ignition delay** times in knock-resistance engine design.
4. **Pollutant formation rates** (e.g., $NO_x$ via the Zeldovich mechanism involves termolecular steps).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Molecularity and Rate Law (Collision Theory Basis)

The **Collision Theory** of chemical kinetics provides a quantitative foundation linking molecularity to the observed rate law for an elementary step.

### Step 1: Collision Frequency for Bimolecular Reaction ($A + B \rightarrow$ Products)

Consider two species $A$ and $B$ with number densities $N_A$ and $N_B$ (particles per unit volume), mean velocities $\bar{c}_A$ and $\bar{c}_B$, and molecular diameters $d_A$ and $d_B$.

The **relative velocity** between the colliding pair is:
$$\bar{c}_{rel} = \sqrt{\bar{c}_A^2 + \bar{c}_B^2}$$

The **collision cross-section** $\sigma_{AB}$ is:
$$\sigma_{AB} = \pi d_{AB}^2 = \pi \left(\frac{d_A + d_B}{2}\right)^2$$

The **collision frequency per unit volume**, $Z_{AB}$ (number of A–B collisions per second per cm³), is:
$$Z_{AB} = N_A N_B \sigma_{AB} \bar{c}_{rel}$$

### Step 2: Applying the Boltzmann Energy Criterion

Not every collision is effective. According to the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, the fraction of collisions possessing energy greater than the activation energy $E_a$ is given by:
$$f = e^{-E_a / RT}$$

The **rate of effective collisions per unit volume** is therefore:
$$Z_{AB}^{eff} = Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} = N_A N_B \sigma_{AB} \bar{c}_{rel} \, e^{-E_a/RT}$$

### Step 3: Identifying the Rate Constant

By definition, the rate of reaction for an elementary bimolecular step is:
$$\text{Rate} = k \cdot [A][B]$$

Comparing with the effective collision expression, and converting number density to molar concentration using $N_A = N_{Avogadro} \cdot [A]$:

$$k = \underbrace{N_{Avogadro} \cdot \sigma_{AB} \bar{c}_{rel}}_{\text{pre-exponential factor } A} \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$

This recovers the famous **Arrhenius equation**:
$$k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where the pre-exponential factor $A$ encodes the **molecularity information** (collision geometry) and the exponential encodes the **activation energy barrier**.

### Step 4: Generalization to Termolecular Reactions

For a termolecular reaction $A + B + C \rightarrow P$, the rate constant expression becomes:
$$k = N_{Avogadro}^2 \cdot \sigma_{ABC} \cdot \bar{c}_{rel,ABC} \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$

with units of $L^2 \cdot mol^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$, confirming the **third-order nature** of the rate law for a termolecular elementary step.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Determining Molecularity of a Combustion Step

**Problem:** Consider the elementary step in a hydrocarbon flame:
$$H^\cdot + O_2 \rightarrow OH^\cdot + O^\cdot$$
Find the molecularity and write the rate law.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Count the reacting particles on the left-hand side.
- Hydrogen radical: **1**
- Oxygen molecule: **1**
- Total particles: $1 + 1 = 2$

**Step 2:** Therefore, the molecularity is:
$$n = 2 \quad (\text{bimolecular})$$

**Step 3:** The rate law for this elementary step (by definition, no need for experiments) is:
$$\text{Rate} = k[H^\cdot][O_2]$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: This is a **second-order reaction** with respect to these species. It is one of the most important chain-branching reactions in the **H₂–O₂ combustion mechanism**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because the rate law is dictated directly by molecularity (and molecularity is determined from the mechanism), we know the rate law *without* performing any kinetic experiments — provided we are dealing with an elementary step.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Molecularity | Rate Law Form | Half-life $t_{1/2}$ | Integrated Rate Law |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (Unimolecular) | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$ | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | $[A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$ |
| 2 (Bimolecular, A+B) | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A][B]$ | depends on initial conc. | $\ln\frac{[A][B]_0}{[B][A]_0} = ([B]_0-[A]_0)kt$ |
| 2 (Bimolecular, 2A) | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2$ | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | $\frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$ |
| 3 (Termolecular) | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A][B][C]$ | Complex; depends on all three conc. | No closed form in general |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Molecularity Classification Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram depicts the logical classification pathway and rate-law mapping for elementary reaction molecularity, incorporating fire-propagation context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Elementary Reaction Identified]) --> readEq[Read the balanced elementary equation]
    readEq --> countReactants[Count reactant species on LHS]
    countReactants --> branchOne{Number of particles?}

    branchOne -- n = 1 --> unimolA[Unimolecular Step]
    branchOne -- n = 2 --> bimolA[Bimolecular Step]
    branchOne -- n = 3 --> termolA[Termolecular Step]
    branchOne -- n > 3 --> rareA[Essentially Never Observed]

    unimolA --> unimolEx[Fire Example: O3 to O2 plus O radical]
    unimolEx --> unimolRate[Rate Law: r = k times A]
    unimolRate --> unimolOrder[Reaction Order: 1]

    bimolA --> bimolEx[Fire Example: H radical plus O2 gives OH plus O]
    bimolEx --> bimolRate[Rate Law: r = k times A times B]
    bimolRate --> bimolOrder[Reaction Order: 2]

    termolA --> termolEx[Fire Example: H radical plus H radical plus M gives H2 plus M]
    termolEx --> termolRate[Rate Law: r = k times A times B times C]
    termolRate --> termolOrder[Reaction Order: 3]

    rareA --> rareNote[Probability of simultaneous collision is vanishingly small]

    unimolOrder --> finalOut([Order equals Molecularity for ELEMENTARY steps])
    bimolOrder --> finalOut
    termolOrder --> finalOut
    rareNote --> finalOut

    finalOut --> ktuCheck[KTU Key Insight: For elementary steps only, the reaction order equals the molecularity. For overall reactions, this need not be true.]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mermaid Map: Molecularity vs. Reaction Order Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startB([Given a reaction]) --> q1{Is the reaction ELEMENTARY?}
    q1 -- YES --> path1[Molecularity read directly from stoichiometry]
    path1 --> path1b[Order equals Molecularity automatically]
    q1 -- NO --> path2[Must determine Order EXPERIMENTALLY]
    path2 --> path2b[Order may differ from stoichiometric sum]
    path1b --> endB([Apply to fire model: laminar flame speed and ignition delay])
    path2b --> endB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "molecularity of an elementary reaction". Give one example relevant to combustion chemistry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Molecularity of an elementary reaction is the number of reactant species (atoms, molecules, or ions) that participate in a single, indivisible reaction step as represented by the balanced chemical equation. It is always a positive integer (1, 2, or 3) for observable reactions.
- **Example (1 mark):** In the hydrogen–oxygen combustion chain, the elementary step $H^\cdot + O_2 \rightarrow OH^\cdot + O^\cdot$ is **bimolecular** (molecularity = 2), as it involves one hydrogen radical and one oxygen molecule.

---

### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the "order" and "molecularity" of a reaction. State one key difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Order | Molecularity |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Sum of exponents in experimental rate law | Number of reacting species in an elementary step |
| Applicable to | Overall and elementary reactions | Elementary reactions only |
| Value | Can be zero, fractional, or negative | Always a positive integer |

- **Key difference (1 mark):** The order of a reaction is determined **experimentally** from rate data, whereas molecularity is a **theoretical construct** deduced from the reaction mechanism. They coincide *only* for elementary reactions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define molecularity of an elementary reaction. With suitable combustion examples, classify reactions based on molecularity and derive the corresponding rate law expressions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following elementary steps in a methane–air flame, identify the molecularity of each step and write the rate law:
  - (i) $CH_4 + M \rightarrow CH_3^\cdot + H^\cdot + M$
  - (ii) $CH_3^\cdot + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3O_2^\cdot$
  - (iii) $H^\cdot + H^\cdot + M \rightarrow H_2 + M$  
Where $M$ is a third-body collision partner. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — Definition, Classification & Rate Law (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The molecularity of an elementary reaction is the number of reactant species that must collide simultaneously in a single reaction event to produce products. For a generic elementary step:
$$aA + bB + cC + \ldots \rightarrow \text{Products}$$
the molecularity is $n = a + b + c + \ldots$ (summing stoichiometric coefficients of **reactants only**).

**Classification & Rate Laws (5 marks):**
1. **Unimolecular (n = 1):** $A \rightarrow P$. The single species undergoes internal rearrangement. Rate law: $r = k[A]$. *Combustion example:* $O_3 \rightarrow O_2 + O^\cdot$ (ozone decomposition in flame front).
2. **Bimolecular (n = 2):** $A + B \rightarrow P$. Two species must collide. Rate law: $r = k[A][B]$. *Combustion example:* $CO + OH^\cdot \rightarrow CO_2 + H^\cdot$ (key $CO$-to-$CO_2$ oxidation step).
3. **Termolecular (n = 3):** $A + B + C \rightarrow P$. Three species must collide simultaneously. Rate law: $r = k[A][B][C]$. *Combustion example:* $O^\cdot + O^\cdot + M \rightarrow O_2 + M$ (radical recombination needing a third body to carry away excess energy).

**Why termolecular is rare:** The probability of three bodies meeting at a single point in space at the same instant is statistically low; in many flames the third body $M$ is provided by abundant $N_2$ or $H_2O$ molecules.

---

**(b) — Analysis of Methane Combustion Steps (7 marks):**

**Step (i):** $CH_4 + M \rightarrow CH_3^\cdot + H^\cdot + M$
- **Reactant particle count:** 1 (methane). The third body $M$ appears on both sides (catalyst) and is **not** consumed; it is conventionally **excluded** from the molecularity count.
- **Molecularity:** **1 (unimolecular)** — sometimes called a *pseudo-unimolecular* or *Lindemann* step.
- **Rate law:** $r = k[CH_4][M]$. The observed order is **2** even though molecularity is 1, because of the $M$ dependency.

*(2 marks for step i)*

**Step (ii):** $CH_3^\cdot + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3O_2^\cdot$
- **Reactant particle count:** 2 (methyl radical + oxygen).
- **Molecularity:** **2 (bimolecular)**.
- **Rate law:** $r = k[CH_3^\cdot][O_2]$.

*(2 marks for step ii)*

**Step (iii):** $H^\cdot + H^\cdot + M \rightarrow H_2 + M$
- **Reactant particle count:** 2 distinct $H$ radicals (counted as a collision of two $H$ species) + 1 third body $M$.
- **Strictly speaking, molecularity = 3 (termolecular)**, since three independent species must meet in one event.
- **Rate law:** $r = k[H^\cdot]^2[M]$.
- **Note:** The third body $M$ carries away the bond-formation energy; without it, the newly formed $H_2$ would immediately redissociate.

*(3 marks for step iii)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain why the probability of termolecular elementary reactions is far lower than bimolecular ones. Estimate the ratio of three-body to two-body collision frequencies in a typical flame ($T = 1500\,K$, $P = 1\,atm$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following rate data were obtained for an elementary gas-phase reaction $A + B \rightarrow P$ at $1000\,K$:
| $[A]_0$ (mol/L) | $[B]_0$ (mol/L) | Initial Rate (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.10 | 0.10 | $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.20 | 0.10 | $5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.10 | 0.20 | $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.30 | 0.30 | $7.5 \times 10^{-3}$ |

Determine the molecularity of the reaction and calculate the rate constant $k$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — Three-Body vs. Two-Body Collisions (7 marks):**

**Conceptual reasoning (3 marks):**
For a two-body collision, two molecules must simply be within a "collision cylinder" of each other during a time interval. For a three-body collision, *three* molecules must simultaneously be within mutual interaction range — a volume defined by two intersecting collision cylinders. The geometric/statistical probability of a third molecule being present in that small overlap volume during the brief two-body encounter is very small.

**Quantitative estimate (4 marks):**
Using hard-sphere kinetic theory, the ratio of three-body to two-body collision frequencies scales as:
$$\frac{Z_{ABC}}{Z_{AB}} \approx N_C \cdot d_{ABC} \approx N_C \cdot d$$
where $N_C$ is the number density of the third body and $d$ is the molecular diameter.

At $T = 1500\,K$ and $P = 1\,atm$, using the ideal gas law:
$$N_C = \frac{P}{k_B T} = \frac{101325}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(1500)}$$

$$N_C = \frac{101325}{2.0715 \times 10^{-20}} \approx 4.89 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules/m}^3$$

Converting to mol/L: $N_C \approx 8.13 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L.

With $d \approx 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m, the dimensionless ratio is:
$$N_C \cdot d \approx (4.89 \times 10^{24})(3 \times 10^{-10}) \approx 1.47 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-1} \cdot \text{m} = \text{??}$$

Re-evaluating with proper units: the ratio of three-body to two-body collision *probability per pair* is $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ in atmospheric-pressure flames. This means three-body events are roughly **1000 to 10,000 times rarer** than two-body events, justifying their role as rate-limiting steps.

---

**(b) — Determining Molecularity from Rate Data (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compare experiments to find order in A (2 marks):**
Comparing experiments 1 and 2: $[A]$ doubles, $[B]$ constant, rate doubles (from $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ to $5.0 \times 10^{-3}$).
$$\text{Rate} \propto [A]^1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{order in } A = 1$$

**Step 2: Compare experiments to find order in B (2 marks):**
Comparing experiments 1 and 3: $[A]$ constant, $[B]$ doubles, rate is unchanged ($2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ in both).
$$\text{Rate} \propto [B]^0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{order in } B = 0$$

**Step 3: Verify with experiment 4 (1 mark):**
With order 1 in $A$ and 0 in $B$, predicted rate at $[A]=0.30$ is:
$$r = k[0.30]$$
The experimental rate $7.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is consistent (since $[B]$ is also tripled but order in $B$ is 0, this doesn't affect rate). For example, using experiment 1: $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} / 0.10 = 0.025\,s^{-1}$. Then at $[A]=0.30$: $r = 0.025 \times 0.30 = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$ ✓

**Step 4: Molecularity and rate constant (2 marks):**
- **Total order** = 1 + 0 = 1.
- Since the reaction is stated to be **elementary**, the molecularity equals the reaction order.
- **Molecularity = 1 (unimolecular)** — and the rate law is $r = k[A]$.
- **Rate constant:** $k = 0.025\,s^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Mixing up "overall" and "elementary" reactions** — A student who writes "the molecularity of $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ is 3" loses 2–3 marks. The overall stoichiometry has 3 reactant molecules, but this is *not* an elementary step. Always check whether the reaction is elementary first.
> 2. **Counting $M$ (third body) in molecularity** — Convention: $M$ acts as a collision partner/catalyst. In strict molecularity, $M$ may or may not be counted depending on context; state your assumption clearly. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to *acknowledge* $M$ and explain its role.
> 3. **Confusing rate law with stoichiometry** — The rate law is $r = k[A][B]$ for a *bimolecular elementary step*, NOT derived from the overall stoichiometry. Marks are lost if no justification is given.
> 4. **Forgetting units of $k$** — Always quote the units of the rate constant consistent with the molecularity (1st order: $s^{-1}$, 2nd order: $L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$, 3rd order: $L^2 \cdot mol^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Molecularity of Elementary Reactions**

- **Core definition:** Molecularity = number of reactant species in *one elementary step*.
- **Allowed values:** **1, 2, or 3** (positive integers only). Higher values are statistically negligible.
- **Three categories:**
  - **Unimolecular (1):** $A \rightarrow P$, rate $= k[A]$
  - **Bimolecular (2):** $A + B \rightarrow P$, rate $= k[A][B]$
  - **Termolecular (3):** $A + B + C \rightarrow P$, rate $= k[A][B][C]$
- **Equivalence rule:** For an **elementary** reaction, **order = molecularity**. For overall (non-elementary) reactions, this need not hold.
- **Third body $M$:** Acts as an energy sink; appears unchanged on both sides; may be excluded from or included in molecularity count depending on convention — always state assumption.
- **Combustion context:** Most chain-propagation steps in flames are bimolecular; chain-termination steps are often termolecular involving a third body.
- **Units check:** $k$ units depend on order: $s^{-1}$ (1st), $L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$ (2nd), $L^2 \cdot mol^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$ (3rd).
- **Pseudo-molecularity:** When one reactant is in large excess, the apparent order may be less than the true molecularity (e.g., pseudo-first-order kinetics).
- **Arrhenius link:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$, where the pre-exponential factor $A$ encodes the collision frequency determined by molecularity.
- **Examiner hot-spot:** "Distinguish order and molecularity" is a KTU favourite — always present the comparison as a **two-column table** for clarity.
- **Probability hierarchy:** Bimolecular $\gg$ Termolecular (by a factor of $\sim 10^3$–$10^4$ at atmospheric pressure and flame temperatures).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Law of Mass Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Law of Mass Action

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Law of Mass Action** (LoMA) is a fundamental chemical kinetics principle, originally proposed by Norwegian mathematicians **Cato Maximilian Guldberg** and **Peter Waage** between **1864–1879**, which states that the **rate of a chemical reaction at a constant temperature is directly proportional to the product of the active masses (concentrations or partial pressures) of the reacting substances, each raised to a power equal to its stoichiometric coefficient in the balanced chemical equation**.

In the context of **Fire Propagation and Dynamics**, the Law of Mass Action serves as the mathematical foundation for describing the *microscopic chemistry of combustion*, governing the rate at which fuel vapors and oxidizers consume each other in the reaction zone of a flame, and ultimately determining the **flame speed, heat release rate, and fire growth**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This principle is essential for deriving the *overall reaction order* of combustion, the *kinetic control regime* (as opposed to the mixing/diffusion control regime), and forms the basis of the **Arrhenius-type combustion models** used in CFD fire simulation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded classroom where students (molecules) are throwing paper balls (reactions) at each other.
