The more students you pack into the room, the more likely paper balls will be thrown, caught, and cause a "reaction." If you double the number of students, you roughly double the rate of paper-throwing chaos. The Law of Mass Action says exactly this: **more molecules crammed together means more collisions, and therefore a faster reaction rate**.

In a fire scenario:
- **Fuel molecules (CH₄, C₈H₁₈, etc.)** and **oxygen molecules (O₂)** are the "students."
- **Heat (activation energy)** is the "energy needed to throw the paper."
- The **reaction rate** is the speed of the combustion "chaos" that sustains the flame front.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** 8.314 **J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Pressure (p°):** 1 **atm** = 101,325 **Pa** (reference for partial pressures in dimensionless activity)
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** 6.022 × 10²³ **mol⁻¹**
- **Typical Activation Energy (Eₐ) for hydrocarbon combustion:** 100–250 **kJ/mol**
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for methane (CH₄):** 17.2:1 by mass

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in Fire Engineering:** Without the Law of Mass Action, we could not mathematically predict *how fast* a flame propagates through a fuel-air mixture, nor could we design fire suppression systems that interrupt specific elementary reaction steps (e.g., dry chemical agents that scavenge free radicals like H• and OH•).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Statement

Consider a generalized homogeneous reaction (typical of gas-phase combustion):

$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

Where:
- $a, b, c, d$ are the stoichiometric coefficients
- $A, B$ are reactants (e.g., fuel + O₂)
- $C, D$ are products (e.g., CO₂ + H₂O)

**Forward Rate of Reaction (R_f):**

$$R_f = k_f \cdot [A]^a \cdot [B]^b$$

**Reverse Rate of Reaction (R_r):**

$$R_r = k_r \cdot [C]^c \cdot [D]^d$$

**Net Rate of Reaction (R_net):**

$$R_{net} = R_f - R_r = k_f \cdot [A]^a \cdot [B]^b - k_r \cdot [C]^c \cdot [D]^d$$

The constants $k_f$ and $k_r$ are the **forward and reverse rate constants**, which are temperature-dependent (typically described by the **Arrhenius equation**).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Identify the Elementary Reaction:** In combustion, the *overall* reaction (e.g., CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O) is actually a sum of dozens of elementary steps (the radical chain mechanism: H•, O•, OH•, CH₃•). The Law of Mass Action applies to *each elementary step* individually.
- **Step 2 — Assign Reaction Orders:** The exponent of each concentration term is the *order* with respect to that species. The sum of all exponents is the *overall order*. For combustion, the global reaction is often modeled as first-order in fuel and first-order in O₂, giving an overall second-order kinetics.
- **Step 3 — Apply Temperature Dependence:** As temperature rises, $k$ rises exponentially via Arrhenius behavior, making combustion self-accelerating — a key reason fires spread rapidly once ignition occurs.
- **Step 4 — Equilibrium Condition:** At dynamic equilibrium, $R_f = R_r$, leading to the **Law of Mass Action for Equilibrium (Guldberg-Waage Law)**, which defines the equilibrium constant $K$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Application in Fire Dynamics** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward reaction rate | $R_f = k_f \cdot [A]^a \cdot [B]^b$ | $k_f$: rate constant (m³/mol·s); $[A]$: mol/m³ | Rate of fuel consumption in flame |
| Net reaction rate | $R_{net} = k_f[A]^a[B]^b - k_r[C]^c[D]^d$ | All in SI | Determines flame propagation speed |
| Equilibrium constant (concentration) | $K_c = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ | Dimensionless | Predicts combustion completeness |
| Equilibrium constant (pressure) | $K_p = \dfrac{(p_C/p°)^c(p_D/p°)^d}{(p_A/p°)^a(p_B/p°)^b}$ | Dimensionless | Used in high-pressure flames |
| Arrhenius rate constant | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $A$: pre-exponential (m³/mol·s); $E_a$: J/mol | Temperature dependence of combustion |
| Relationship $K_c$ to $K_p$ | $K_p = K_c \cdot (RT)^{\Delta n}$ | $\Delta n = (c+d)-(a+b)$ | High-pressure combustion correction |
| Overall reaction order | $n = a + b$ | Dimensionless integer | Determines kinetic regime |
| Specific reaction rate | $k = \dfrac{R}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ | Variable | Back-calculated from experimental data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always use the **dimensionless activity** form (concentration divided by a standard reference state, usually 1 mol/L or 1 atm) when computing equilibrium constants. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for failing to apply this normalization.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Suppression Design:** Inert gas systems (CO₂, N₂) work by reducing the partial pressure of O₂, thereby reducing $[O_2]^b$ in the rate equation and slowing the combustion rate exponentially.
- **CFD Fire Modeling:** Codes like **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)** by NIST use Law of Mass Action-based finite-rate chemistry sub-models to predict species concentrations in compartment fires.
- **Explosion Hazard Analysis:** The Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) and Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) are partially defined by concentrations at which the rate equation predicts a self-sustaining branched-chain reaction.
- **Engine Combustion:** Spark-ignition and diesel engine design relies on the mass-action principles of flame kernel growth and knock suppression.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Equilibrium Constant from the Law of Mass Action

**Starting Point:** Consider a generic reversible elementary reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

**Step 1 — Write the rate expressions using the Law of Mass Action:**

The forward reaction rate (consumption of A and B) is proportional to the frequency of molecular collisions between A and B, which scales with their concentrations:

$$R_f = k_f \cdot [A]^a \cdot [B]^b$$

Similarly, the reverse reaction rate is:

$$R_r = k_r \cdot [C]^c \cdot [D]^d$$

> *Reasoning:* Each collision requires one molecule of A and one of B (per stoichiometric unit), so the probability of a successful collision is the joint probability, which equals the product of individual concentrations raised to their stoichiometric powers.

**Step 2 — Apply the condition of dynamic equilibrium:**

At equilibrium, the macroscopic concentrations remain constant because the forward and reverse rates are exactly balanced:

$$R_f = R_r$$

Therefore:

$$k_f \cdot [A]^a \cdot [B]^b = k_r \cdot [C]^c \cdot [D]^d$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the ratio of rate constants:**

$$\dfrac{k_f}{k_r} = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$$

**Step 4 — Define the equilibrium constant K_c:**

Since the ratio $k_f / k_r$ is itself a constant at a given temperature (both $k_f$ and $k_r$ depend only on temperature via Arrhenius), we define:

$$K_c = \dfrac{k_f}{k_r} = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$$

> *This is the classical Guldberg-Waage expression for the equilibrium constant.*

**Step 5 — Converting to partial pressure form (for gaseous systems, typical in fire):**

For ideal gases, concentration is related to partial pressure by $[X] = p_X / RT$. Substituting:

$$K_c = \dfrac{(p_C/RT)^c (p_D/RT)^d}{(p_A/RT)^a (p_B/RT)^b}$$

Simplifying the RT terms:

$$K_c = \dfrac{p_C^c \cdot p_D^d}{p_A^a \cdot p_B^b} \cdot (RT)^{-(c+d)+(a+b)}$$

$$K_c = \dfrac{p_C^c \cdot p_D^d}{p_A^a \cdot p_B^b} \cdot (RT)^{-\Delta n}$$

where $\Delta n = (c+d) - (a+b)$ is the change in moles of gas.

**Step 6 — Define the pressure-based equilibrium constant K_p:**

$$K_p = \dfrac{(p_C)^c (p_D)^d}{(p_A)^a (p_B)^b}$$

Therefore the relationship between $K_p$ and $K_c$ is:

$$K_p = K_c \cdot (RT)^{\Delta n}$$

> *Note:* When $\Delta n = 0$ (e.g., H₂ + Cl₂ → 2HCl), $K_p = K_c$ exactly.

### 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of the Rate Constant (Arrhenius Form)

The rate constant $k$ for a combustion reaction increases dramatically with temperature. Svante Arrhenius proposed (1889) the empirical relation:

$$k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 1 — Define the pre-exponential factor A:**

$A$ represents the maximum theoretical rate constant at infinite temperature, when every collision has sufficient energy. For hydrocarbon combustion, $A$ is typically $10^{10}$ to $10^{14}$ m³/(mol·s).

**Step 2 — Define the activation energy E_a:**

$E_a$ (in J/mol) is the minimum kinetic energy threshold a colliding pair of molecules must possess to react. For methane oxidation, $E_a \approx 200$ kJ/mol.

**Step 3 — Derive the temperature sensitivity:**

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$

Differentiating with respect to temperature (useful for finding ignition temperature):

$$\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2}$$

> *Interpretation:* A small change in temperature near the ignition point causes a huge change in reaction rate — this is the *thermal runaway* phenomenon central to fire growth.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application to a Typical Combustion Reaction

Consider the complete combustion of methane in air:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

| **Quantity** | **Value** | **Derivation Logic** |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric coefficients (a, b, c, d) | 1, 2, 1, 2 | Read from balanced equation |
| $\Delta n$ (change in gas moles) | $(1+2) - (1+2) = 0$ | Products minus reactants |
| $K_p / K_c$ relation | $K_p = K_c$ (since $\Delta n = 0$) | Substituting into $(RT)^{\Delta n}$ |
| Overall reaction order (assumed elementary) | $1 + 2 = 3$ | Sum of stoichiometric coefficients |
| $R_{net}$ | $k_f[CH_4][O_2]^2 - k_r[CO_2][H_2O]^2$ | Mass action applied to both directions |
| Temperature effect (at 298 K vs 1500 K) | $k$ increases by factor of $\sim 10^{8}$ | Arrhenius ratio with $E_a = 200$ kJ/mol |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Application of Law of Mass Action in Fire Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactants enter reaction zone] --> B[Determine concentrations of fuel and O2]
    B --> C[Apply Law of Mass Action]
    C --> D[Compute forward rate Rf = kf times A to power a times B to power b]
    C --> E[Compute reverse rate Rr = kr times C to power c times D to power d]
    D --> F[Net reaction rate Rnet = Rf minus Rr]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Temperature above ignition threshold?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Arrhenius rate constant k = A exp negative Ea over RT]
    G -->|No| I[Reaction negligible, no combustion]
    H --> J[Heat release and radical chain propagation]
    J --> K[Flame front advances, fire spreads]
    J --> L[Equilibrium reached if Rf equals Rr]
    L --> M[Equilibrium constant Kc defines combustion completeness]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Architecture of Mass Action in Combustion Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Macro["MACROSCOPIC FIRE BEHAVIOR"]
        A1[Flame Spread Rate]
        A2[Heat Release Rate]
        A3[Smoke Production]
    end

    subgraph Meso["MESOSCALE TRANSPORT"]
        B1[Fuel Vapor Concentration]
        B2[Oxygen Partial Pressure]
        B3[Temperature Field]
    end

    subgraph Micro["MICROSCOPIC KINETICS"]
        C1[Elementary Reactions]
        C2[Law of Mass Action]
        C3[Arrhenius Rate Laws]
        C4[Equilibrium Constant]
    end

    Micro --> Meso
    Meso --> Macro
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C2 -.controls.-> B1
    C3 -.modulates.-> B3
    C4 -.sets.-> A2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Rate vs. Concentration Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start with balanced equation aA + bB to cC + dD] --> P2[Define rate constant k via Arrhenius]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Rf from Law of Mass Action]
    P2 --> P4[Compute Rr from Law of Mass Action]
    P3 --> P5[Rnet equals Rf minus Rr]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6{System at equilibrium?}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Kc = kf / kr = ratio of concentration products]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Net reaction proceeds, fire grows or decays]
    P7 --> P9[Use Kp equals Kc times RT to the power delta n for gases]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These schematics illustrate the *causal chain* by which the Law of Mass Action — a microscopic kinetic principle — propagates upward to determine macroscopic fire behavior. KTU examiners often expect students to draw or describe such flow chains in long-answer questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the Law of Mass Action. How is it applied to express the rate of a homogeneous chemical reaction?

**Model Answer:**
The Law of Mass Action states that *at a constant temperature, the rate of a chemical reaction is directly proportional to the product of the active masses (concentrations) of the reacting substances, each raised to a power equal to its stoichiometric coefficient.*

For a reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow \text{products}$, the rate is expressed as:
$$R = k \cdot [A]^a \cdot [B]^b$$
where $k$ is the rate constant dependent on temperature (Arrhenius), and $[A]$, $[B]$ are the molar concentrations. This principle governs the *elementary step kinetics* of combustion reactions in fire zones. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is the relationship between $K_p$ and $K_c$ for a gaseous reaction? Under what condition are they equal?

**Model Answer:**
The relationship is:
$$K_p = K_c \cdot (RT)^{\Delta n}$$
where $\Delta n = \sum n_{\text{products}} - \sum n_{\text{reactants}}$ is the change in number of gaseous moles, $R$ is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/(mol·K)), and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

$K_p = K_c$ when $\Delta n = 0$, i.e., when the total number of moles of gaseous products equals that of gaseous reactants (e.g., $H_2 + Cl_2 \rightarrow 2HCl$). **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the equilibrium constant $K_c$ from the Law of Mass Action for the reaction:
$$N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$$
Also, write the expression for $K_p$ in terms of $K_c$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A methane-air mixture is burned in a combustion chamber. The partial pressures at equilibrium at 1000 K are: $p_{CH_4} = 0.05$ atm, $p_{O_2} = 0.10$ atm, $p_{CO_2} = 0.20$ atm, $p_{H_2O} = 0.40$ atm. For the reaction $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$, calculate the equilibrium constant $K_p$ and comment on the completeness of combustion. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Apply Law of Mass Action to the forward and reverse rates:**

For $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$:

$$R_f = k_f \cdot [N_2]^1 \cdot [H_2]^3$$

$$R_r = k_r \cdot [NH_3]^2$$

**Step 2 — At equilibrium, $R_f = R_r$:**

$$k_f \cdot [N_2] \cdot [H_2]^3 = k_r \cdot [NH_3]^2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to define $K_c$:**

$$K_c = \dfrac{k_f}{k_r} = \dfrac{[NH_3]^2}{[N_2] \cdot [H_2]^3}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to $K_p$ form using $[X] = p_X / RT$:**

$$K_c = \dfrac{(p_{NH_3}/RT)^2}{(p_{N_2}/RT)(p_{H_2}/RT)^3} = \dfrac{p_{NH_3}^2}{p_{N_2} \cdot p_{H_2}^3} \cdot (RT)^{-(2-1-3)} = \dfrac{p_{NH_3}^2}{p_{N_2} \cdot p_{H_2}^3} \cdot (RT)^{2}$$

So:
$$K_p = K_c \cdot (RT)^{\Delta n}$$
with $\Delta n = 2 - 4 = -2$, giving $K_p = K_c \cdot (RT)^{-2}$. **(7 marks — full marks breakdown: 2 for rate expressions, 2 for equilibrium, 2 for $K_c$ derivation, 1 for $K_p$ conversion)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Write the reaction and identify stoichiometry:**

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

Here $a=1$, $b=2$, $c=1$, $d=2$. Total gas moles on each side: $1+2=3$ reactants; $1+2=3$ products. So $\Delta n = 0$.

**Step 2 — Write the $K_p$ expression (dimensionless, using $p° = 1$ atm):**

$$K_p = \dfrac{(p_{CO_2}/p°)^1 \cdot (p_{H_2O}/p°)^2}{(p_{CH_4}/p°)^1 \cdot (p_{O_2}/p°)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values:**

$$K_p = \dfrac{(0.20/1)^1 \cdot (0.40/1)^2}{(0.05/1)^1 \cdot (0.10/1)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator:**

$$K_p = \dfrac{0.20 \times 0.16}{0.05 \times 0.01} = \dfrac{0.032}{0.0005} = 64$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$K_p = 64 \gg 1$$

A large $K_p$ value means the equilibrium strongly favors the products. This indicates **highly complete combustion** of methane under the given conditions, with most of the fuel converted to CO₂ and H₂O. The reaction is product-favored and nearly complete. **(7 marks — full marks breakdown: 2 for setting up $K_p$, 2 for numerical substitution, 2 for evaluation, 1 for engineering interpretation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the Arrhenius equation. Discuss how the rate constant of a combustion reaction changes with temperature, and compute the ratio of rate constants at 1500 K and 800 K for a reaction with $E_a = 180$ kJ/mol. Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the reaction $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3$ at 1000 K, the equilibrium concentrations are: $[SO_2] = 0.20$ mol/L, $[O_2] = 0.10$ mol/L, $[SO_3] = 0.80$ mol/L. Calculate $K_c$ and $K_p$ (using $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K)). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — State the Arrhenius equation:**

$$k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 2 — Discuss temperature dependence:**

As $T$ increases, the exponent $-E_a/RT$ becomes less negative, so $e^{-E_a/RT}$ increases. Therefore $k$ increases *exponentially* with temperature. This explains why combustion accelerates rapidly once the ignition temperature is exceeded — a key factor in fire spread.

**Step 3 — Write the ratio of rate constants:**

$$\dfrac{k(T_2)}{k(T_1)} = \dfrac{A e^{-E_a/RT_2}}{A e^{-E_a/RT_1}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:** $E_a = 180{,}000$ J/mol, $T_1 = 800$ K, $T_2 = 1500$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{800} - \dfrac{1}{1500} = 0.001250 - 0.000667 = 0.000583 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right) = \dfrac{180{,}000}{8.314} \times 0.000583 = 21{,}650 \times 0.000583 \approx 12.62$$

$$\dfrac{k(1500)}{k(800)} = e^{12.62} \approx 3.04 \times 10^5$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**

The reaction rate increases by a factor of approximately **300,000** when temperature rises from 800 K to 1500 K. This huge temperature sensitivity is why fire dynamics exhibits thermal runaway. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify stoichiometry for $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3$:**

$a=2$, $b=1$, $c=2$. Total gas moles: reactants = 3, products = 2. So $\Delta n = 2 - 3 = -1$.

**Step 2 — Write the $K_c$ expression:**

$$K_c = \dfrac{[SO_3]^2}{[SO_2]^2 \cdot [O_2]}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute equilibrium concentrations:**

$$K_c = \dfrac{(0.80)^2}{(0.20)^2 \cdot (0.10)} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.04 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.004} = 160 \text{ (mol/L)}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to $K_p$ using $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}$:**

$$K_p = 160 \times (0.0821 \times 1000)^{-1} = 160 \times (82.1)^{-1} = \dfrac{160}{82.1} \approx 1.95$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**

$K_c = 160$ (L/mol) is large, indicating the forward reaction (formation of SO₃) is favorable at this temperature. The negative $\Delta n$ (moles of gas decrease in forward direction) is consistent with Le Chatelier's principle: high pressure would shift the equilibrium further toward SO₃. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency trap:** In $K_p$ expressions, students often forget to divide partial pressures by the standard state $p° = 1$ atm, making the equilibrium constant appear to have units. Always use dimensionless form.
> 2. **Arrhenius arithmetic trap:** When computing $k(T_2)/k(T_1)$, students frequently flip the sign of the exponent or invert the temperature difference. Memorize: $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
> 3. **Reaction order trap:** For non-elementary combustion reactions, the Law of Mass Action exponents do *not* equal stoichiometric coefficients. Always verify whether the reaction is elementary before applying power-law kinetics.
> 4. **Common 1-mark deduction:** Failing to explicitly state the temperature at which $K_c$ or $K_p$ is reported.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Law of Mass Action** = rate $\propto$ (concentration)$^{\text{stoichiometric coefficient}}$ — applicable to *elementary* reactions.
- **Rate Law Form:** $R = k[A]^a[B]^b$, where $k$ is temperature-dependent (Arrhenius).
- **Equilibrium Constant:** $K_c = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ — derived by setting $R_f = R_r$.
- **Pressure Form:** $K_p = K_c \cdot (RT)^{\Delta n}$ — essential for gas-phase combustion.
- **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — explains thermal runaway in fires.
- **Combustion Context:** Hydrocarbon combustion is typically modeled as first-order in fuel and first-order in O₂, giving an overall second-order rate.
- **$K \gg 1$** → reaction product-favored (complete combustion); **$K \ll 1$** → reactant-favored (incomplete).
- **Stoichiometry vs. Order:** Stoichiometric coefficients are *not* the same as reaction orders unless the reaction is elementary.
- **Fire Engineering Applications:** Suppression (lowering $[O_2]$), CFD modeling (FDS), explosion limits (LEL/UEL), and engine knock analysis.
- **Standard State Convention:** Use dimensionless activity ($a_X = [X]/[X]°$ or $p_X/p°$) — KTU examiners check this.
- **Units of $K_c$:** Vary with overall order; always reduce to standard SI (mol/m³) for combustion problems.
- **Sign of $\Delta n$:** Negative $\Delta n$ → high pressure favors products (Le Chatelier).
- **Activation Energy Range:** For hydrocarbon combustion, $E_a$ is typically 100–250 kJ/mol.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecularity and Order
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Molecularity and Order

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: MOLECULARITY AND ORDER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Molecularity — The Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molecularity** of a chemical reaction is defined as the **number of atoms, ions, or molecules** of the reactants that must collide simultaneously in an **elementary step** to bring about a chemical transformation.

In the context of fire dynamics, molecularity dictates the *minimum mechanistic collaboration* required between reacting species (fuel + oxidizer) before a combustion event can be initiated. It is a **theoretical construct** based on the reaction mechanism and applies *strictly* to elementary reactions.

The general classification used by KTU 2024 examiners is:

| Type | Reacting Species Count | General Form |
|---|---|---|
| Unimolecular | 1 | A → Products |
| Bimolecular | 2 | A + A → Products or A + B → Products |
| Termolecular | 3 | A + B + C → Products |

> [!NOTE]
> Reactions with molecularity greater than **3 are considered negligible** because the simultaneous collision of more than three molecules in the correct orientation with sufficient activation energy is statistically almost impossible.

### 1.2 Order of Reaction — The Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of a reaction** is defined as the **sum of the exponents (powers) of the concentration terms** to which the rate of reaction is directly proportional, as expressed in the experimentally determined rate law.

Mathematically, for a general reaction:

$$aA + bB + cC \rightarrow \text{Products}$$

The empirical rate law is written as:

$$\text{Rate} = k[A]^x[B]^y[C]^z$$

The **overall order** is:

$$n = x + y + z$$

Where:
- **x** = order with respect to A
- **y** = order with respect to B
- **z** = order with respect to C
- **k** = rate constant (specific reaction rate)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cooking Kitchen:**
>
> Imagine you are a chef trying to prepare a dish (the **reaction**).
> - **Molecularity** is the *minimum number of chefs* who must simultaneously touch the pan to flip a delicate item. It is the **mechanistic requirement** — a single chef can do some flips, two chefs are needed for others, and you would never realistically ask 5 chefs to do a single coordinated flip.
> - **Order of reaction** is *how the cooking time actually depends on the number of chefs in the kitchen*. You might think 4 chefs would be 4× faster, but in practice, congestion makes the relationship non-linear. Sometimes the order is 1 (linear), sometimes 2 (quadratic dependence on staff concentration), and occasionally fractional (e.g., 1.5, where overcrowding paradoxically slows things down).
>
> **Key Insight:** Molecularity is a *theoretical integer* (1, 2, or 3), while order is an *experimentally determined quantity* that can be zero, fractional, or even negative.

In combustion engineering, this distinction is critical: the **ignition delay time** of a hydrocarbon fuel in air is governed by the **order** of the overall oxidation reaction, not merely by the molecularity of the elementary radical chain-propagation steps (H + O₂, OH + CO, etc.).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Pressure** $P^\circ = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ bar}$
- **Activation Energy Units:** $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ or $\text{kcal mol}^{-1}$ (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 scheme valuation, examiners frequently award full marks when students explicitly mention that **order is an experimental quantity** while **molecularity is a theoretical quantity**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Molecularity

### 2.1.1 Unimolecular Reactions (Molecularity = 1)
A single molecule undergoes rearrangement, decomposition, or isomerization.

**Examples in Fire Dynamics:**
- Thermal decomposition of fuel vapors: $\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}$
- Radioactive decay during fire detection sensor activation
- Unimolecular bond fission of large hydrocarbon radicals

**Operational Logic:**
- Only one species needs to acquire sufficient activation energy
- These follow **first-order kinetics** in nearly all practical scenarios

### 2.1.2 Bimolecular Reactions (Molecularity = 2)
Two atoms, ions, or molecules collide to yield products. This is the **most common** molecularity in combustion chemistry.

**Examples in Fire Dynamics:**
- The classic ignition reaction: $\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Radical recombination: $\text{H}^\bullet + \text{OH}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Carbon monoxide oxidation: $\text{CO} + \text{OH}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}^\bullet$

**Operational Logic:**
- Requires geometrically favorable collision
- Rate depends on collision frequency (governed by kinetic theory of gases)

### 2.1.3 Termolecular Reactions (Molecularity = 3)
Three-body collisions are rare but exist; often involve a third inert "chaperone" molecule that absorbs excess energy.

**Example:**
- Ozone formation: $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}^*$
- Here **M** (typically N₂ or O₂) is the chaperone that stabilizes the newly formed ozone

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify that **pseudo-unimolecular reactions** (where one reactant is in vast excess) are still mechanistically bimolecular but kinetically appear first-order.

## 2.2 Classification of Order of Reaction

### 2.2.1 Zero-Order Reactions (n = 0)
Rate is **independent** of reactant concentration.

$$\text{Rate} = k[A]^0 = k$$

**Examples in Fire Engineering:**
- Heterogeneous surface combustion on a catalyst (the surface area is the rate-limiting factor, not concentration)
- Photodecomposition at high light intensity (saturation effect)

### 2.2.2 First-Order Reactions (n = 1)
Rate is directly proportional to the concentration of a single reactant.

$$\text{Rate} = k[A]$$

**Examples in Fire Engineering:**
- Most unimolecular decompositions
- Radioactive decay of fire detector isotopes
- Pseudo-unimolecular hydrolysis

### 2.2.3 Second-Order Reactions (n = 2)
Rate is proportional to either the square of one concentration or the product of two concentrations.

$$\text{Rate} = k[A]^2 \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Rate} = k[A][B]$$

**Examples in Fire Engineering:**
- Bimolecular elementary steps in hydrocarbon combustion
- Most radical + radical terminations in chain reactions

### 2.2.4 Third-Order Reactions (n = 3)
Rare; typically requires specific conditions.

### 2.2.5 Fractional-Order Reactions (n = ½, 3/2, etc.)
Found in heterogeneous catalysis and complex chain reactions.

**Example:**
- $\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$ decomposition on hot wire: $n = 1.5$

### 2.2.6 Negative-Order Reactions
Rate *decreases* with increasing concentration of a particular species (often product inhibition).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** for KTU 2024 scheme valuation. Memorize the integrated forms, half-life expressions, and units.

| Order | Integrated Rate Law | Half-Life ($t_{1/2}$) | Units of $k$ | Linear Plot |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $k = \dfrac{x}{t}$ | $\dfrac{[A]_0}{2k}$ | $\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$ | $[A]$ vs $t$ |
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{2.303}{t} \log\dfrac{a}{a-x}$ | $\dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | $\log[A]$ vs $t$ |
| 2 (same reactant) | $k = \dfrac{1}{t}\left[\dfrac{1}{a-x} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right]$ | $\dfrac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | $\text{L mol}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{[A]}$ vs $t$ |
| 2 (different reactants) | $k = \dfrac{2.303}{t(a-b)} \log\dfrac{b(a-x)}{a(b-x)}$ | Depends on $[A]_0, [B]_0$ | $\text{L mol}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$ | $\log\dfrac{[A]}{[B]}$ vs $t$ |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{1}{2t}\left[\dfrac{1}{(a-x)^2} - \dfrac{1}{a^2}\right]$ | $\dfrac{3}{2k[A]_0^2}$ | $\text{L}^2\text{ mol}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{[A]^2}$ vs $t$ |
| $n$ (general) | $k = \dfrac{1}{(n-1)t}\left[\dfrac{1}{(a-x)^{n-1}} - \dfrac{1}{a^{n-1}}\right]$ | $\dfrac{2^{n-1} - 1}{(n-1)k[A]_0^{n-1}}$ | $(\text{L mol}^{-1})^{n-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{[A]^{n-1}}$ vs $t$ |

### General Unit Formula (Highly Tested!)

$$\boxed{\text{Units of } k = (\text{mol L}^{-1})^{1-n} \cdot \text{time}^{-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General half-life relation:** $t_{1/2} \propto [A]_0^{1-n}$ for $n \ne 1$. For $n = 1$, $t_{1/2}$ is **independent** of initial concentration — this is a unique signature used to identify first-order reactions experimentally.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **fire dynamics**, the order of combustion reactions determines:

1. **Burn rate prediction** — mass loss rate of solid fuels follows $n$-th order kinetics.
2. **Smoldering vs flaming transition** — different apparent orders govern the regimes.
3. **Detonation chemistry** — chain-branching reactions (e.g., $\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$) have well-defined global orders that determine explosion limits.
4. **Suppression design** — understanding the order with respect to inhibitor concentration (e.g., halon) allows for optimal dosage calculation.
5. **Fire retardant chemistry** — intumescent coatings are designed to disrupt specific elementary steps in the pyrolysis chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Integrated Rate Law

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most tested derivation** in KTU 2024 board exams for chemical kinetics modules.

**Step 1: Write the elementary rate expression.**
For a first-order reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$:

$$-\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$$

**Step 2: Separate variables.**

$$-\dfrac{d[A]}{[A]} = k\,dt$$

**Step 3: Integrate between limits.** Let initial concentration at $t=0$ be $a$, and at time $t$ be $(a-x)$, where $x$ is the amount reacted.

$$\int_{a}^{a-x} -\dfrac{d[A]}{[A]} = \int_{0}^{t} k\,dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the left-hand side.**

$$-\big[\ln[A]\big]_{a}^{a-x} = kt$$

$$-\left[\ln(a-x) - \ln a\right] = kt$$

$$\ln\dfrac{a}{a-x} = kt$$

**Step 5: Convert natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm.**

$$\ln\dfrac{a}{a-x} = 2.303 \log_{10}\dfrac{a}{a-x}$$

**Step 6: Equate and solve for $k$.**

$$2.303 \log\dfrac{a}{a-x} = kt$$

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{2.303}{t} \log\dfrac{a}{a-x}}$$

**Step 7: Derive the half-life expression.**

At $t = t_{1/2}$, $x = a/2$, so $a - x = a/2$.

$$k = \dfrac{2.303}{t_{1/2}} \log\dfrac{a}{a/2} = \dfrac{2.303}{t_{1/2}} \log 2$$

$$k = \dfrac{2.303 \times 0.3010}{t_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Rate Law (Same Reactant)

**Step 1:** For $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$:

$$-\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2$$

**Step 2:** Separate and integrate:

$$\int_{a}^{a-x} -\dfrac{d[A]}{[A]^2} = \int_0^t k\,dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$\left[\dfrac{1}{[A]}\right]_{a}^{a-x} = kt$$

$$\dfrac{1}{a-x} - \dfrac{1}{a} = kt$$

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{1}{t}\left[\dfrac{1}{a-x} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right]}$$

**Step 4:** Half-life derivation ($x = a/2$):

$$k = \dfrac{1}{t_{1/2}}\left[\dfrac{1}{a/2} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right] = \dfrac{1}{t_{1/2}}\left[\dfrac{2-1}{a}\right] = \dfrac{1}{a\,t_{1/2}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k\,a}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Zero-Order Rate Law

**Step 1:** For zero order:

$$-\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k$$

**Step 2:** Integrate:

$$[A]_0 - [A] = kt \implies x = kt$$

**Step 3:** Half-life:

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{a}{2k}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: General $n$-th Order Half-Life

For $n \geq 2$:

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k(n-1)} \left[\dfrac{1}{(a/2)^{n-1}} - \dfrac{1}{a^{n-1}}\right]$$

Expanding:

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k(n-1)a^{n-1}}\left[2^{n-1} - 1\right]$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{2^{n-1} - 1}{(n-1)\,k\,[A]_0^{\,n-1}}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Molecularity vs. Order

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is the **definitive reference** for any "distinguish between" question in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Feature | Molecularity | Order of Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Number of molecules colliding in an elementary step | Sum of exponents of concentration terms in the rate law |
| **Source** | Theoretical; from reaction mechanism | Experimental; from kinetic data |
| **Value Range** | Always a positive integer (1, 2, or 3) | Can be zero, positive, negative, or fractional |
| **Applies To** | Elementary reactions only | Overall (complex) reactions |
| **Effect of Concentration** | No effect on value | Can change with concentration conditions |
| **Determination** | By inspecting balanced equation | By graphical / integration methods |
| **Knowledge of Mechanism** | Required | Not required |
| **Numerical Equality** | Equals order *only* for elementary reactions | Can differ from molecularity for complex reactions |

### Worked Example 3A: Pseudo-Unimolecular Reaction

Consider the acid hydrolysis of an ester:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

- **Molecularity:** 2 (bimolecular)
- **True Order:** 2 (second order)
- **Apparent (Pseudo) Order:** 1, because water is in vast excess, so $[H_2O] \approx$ constant.

**Practical Implication:** During fire suppression of a pool fire, water dilution of combustible vapors can shift the apparent order without changing the molecularity.

## 3.6 Method to Determine Order: Initial Rate Method

If the rate law is: $\text{Rate} = k[A]^n$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log(\text{Rate}) = \log k + n \log[A]$$

A plot of $\log(\text{Rate})$ vs $\log[A]$ gives a straight line of slope $n$. This is the **van't Hoff differential method**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["REACTION KINETICS CLASSIFICATION"]
    node2["BY MOLECULARITY"]
    node3["BY ORDER"]
    node4["Unimolecular n equals 1"]
    node5["Bimolecular n equals 2"]
    node6["Termolecular n equals 3"]
    node7["Zero Order n equals 0"]
    node8["First Order n equals 1"]
    node9["Second Order n equals 2"]
    node10["Third Order n equals 3"]
    node11["Fractional Order"]
    node12["Negative Order"]
    node13["Rate equals k"]
    node14["Rate equals k times A"]
    node15["Rate equals k times A squared"]
    node16["Examples CH3CHO decay"]
    node17["Examples CO plus OH radical"]
    node18["Examples O plus O2 plus M"]
    node19["Half life t half equals 0.693 by k"]
    node20["Half life independent of concentration"]
    node21["Half life inversely proportional to initial concentration"]
    node22["Half life inversely proportional to initial concentration squared"]
    node23["Units of k is time inverse"]
    node24["Units of k is L per mol per s"]
    node25["Units of k is L squared per mol squared per s"]
    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node7
    node3 --> node8
    node3 --> node9
    node3 --> node10
    node3 --> node11
    node3 --> node12
    node4 --> node13
    node4 --> node16
    node5 --> node14
    node5 --> node17
    node6 --> node15
    node6 --> node18
    node7 --> node13
    node7 --> node20
    node8 --> node14
    node8 --> node19
    node8 --> node20
    node8 --> node23
    node9 --> node15
    node9 --> node21
    node9 --> node24
    node10 --> node22
    node10 --> node25
    node11 -.-> node23
    node12 -.-> node24
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rate Determination Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0["EXPERIMENTAL DATA"] --> B0["PLOT METHOD"]
    A0 --> C0["INTEGRATION METHOD"]
    A0 --> D0["HALF LIFE METHOD"]
    A0 --> E0["INITIAL RATE METHOD"]
    B0 --> F0["Zero Order A vs t linear"]
    B0 --> G0["First Order log A vs t linear"]
    B0 --> H0["Second Order 1 by A vs t linear"]
    B0 --> I0["Third Order 1 by A squared vs t linear"]
    C0 --> J0["Substitute integrated rate law"]
    C0 --> K0["Calculate k from each dataset"]
    C0 --> L0["Consistent k equals correct order"]
    D0 --> M0["Measure t half at varying a"]
    D0 --> N0["t half constant equals first order"]
    D0 --> O0["t half inversely proportional to a equals second order"]
    D0 --> P0["t half inversely proportional to a squared equals third order"]
    E0 --> Q0["Vary initial concentration"]
    E0 --> R0["Measure initial rate"]
    E0 --> S0["log Rate versus log A"]
    E0 --> T0["Slope equals order n"]
    F0 --> Z0["REPORT ORDER AND k WITH UNITS"]
    G0 --> Z0
    H0 --> Z0
    I0 --> Z0
    L0 --> Z0
    N0 --> Z0
    O0 --> Z0
    P0 --> Z0
    T0 --> Z0
```

## 4.3 Molecularity vs. Order: Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1["LEFT BLOCK MOLECULARITY"]
    R1["RIGHT BLOCK ORDER OF REACTION"]
    L1 --> L1A["Definition Molecules colliding in elementary step"]
    L1 --> L1B["Origin Theoretical from mechanism"]
    L1 --> L1C["Value Type Positive integer only"]
    L1 --> L1D["Scope Elementary reactions only"]
    L1 --> L1E["Example H plus OH radical to give H2O"]
    R1 --> R1A["Definition Sum of exponents in rate law"]
    R1 --> R1B["Origin Experimental from data"]
    R1 --> R1C["Value Type Integer fractional zero or negative"]
    R1 --> R1D["Scope Complex and elementary reactions"]
    R1 --> R1E["Example Decomposition of N2O5 on Pt surface"]
```

## 4.4 Reaction Coordinate Schematic (Activation Energy vs. Progress)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["REACTANTS A plus B"] --> T1["TRANSITION STATE with high energy"]
    T1 --> P1["PRODUCTS C plus D"]
    T1 -.-> A1["Activation Energy Ea"]
    S1 -.-> D1["Energy difference Delta H"]
    P1 -.-> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **bimolecular** reaction, the transition state involves both A and B simultaneously. The activation energy $E_a$ is a key parameter in the Arrhenius equation: $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$, which determines how temperature affects the reaction order behavior in fire dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between molecularity and order of a reaction. Give one example for each where they differ."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Molecularity | Order |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Number of reacting species in an elementary step | Sum of concentration exponents in rate law |
| Nature | Theoretical, integer (1, 2, 3) | Experimental, can be fractional/zero/negative |
| Scope | Only elementary reactions | Overall (complex) reactions |

**Example where they differ:**
Consider the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide on a Pt surface:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$
- **Molecularity** = 1 (unimolecular, single molecule involved)
- **Order** = 0 (zero-order, rate is independent of $[H_2O_2]$ because the Pt surface becomes saturated)

*(1 mark: definition table; 1 mark: clear difference; 1 mark: example with both values stated)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"A reaction is second order with respect to A. How will the rate of reaction change if the concentration of A is doubled? Justify with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a second-order reaction: $\text{Rate} = k[A]^2$

- **Original rate:** $R_1 = k[A]^2$
- **New rate (doubled concentration):** $R_2 = k(2[A])^2 = 4k[A]^2$

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = 4$$

**The rate becomes 4 times the original.**

**Example:** Decomposition of HI at high temperatures: $2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2$

$$(3 \text{ marks: 1 for setup, 1 for calculation, 1 for example})$$

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Complete Model Solution

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a)** Define the order of a reaction. Derive the integrated rate law expression for a first-order reaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The half-life of a first-order reaction is 30 minutes. Calculate the time required for the concentration of the reactant to decrease to 1/8 of its initial value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **order of a reaction** is defined as the sum of the powers of the concentration terms in the experimentally determined rate law. For a reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, if Rate $= k[A]^n$, then $n$ is the order.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For a first-order reaction:
$$-\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$$

Rearranging and integrating:
$$\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \dfrac{d[A]}{[A]} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln[A] - \ln[A]_0 = -kt$$

$$\ln\dfrac{[A]}{[A]_0} = -kt$$

$$\ln\dfrac{[A]_0}{[A]} = kt$$

Converting to log base 10:
$$2.303 \log\dfrac{[A]_0}{[A]} = kt$$

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{2.303}{t} \log\dfrac{[A]_0}{[A]}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $t_{1/2} = 30$ minutes
**To find:** Time $t$ for $[A] = \dfrac{[A]_0}{8}$

**Step 1: Find rate constant $k$.**
$$k = \dfrac{0.693}{t_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{30} = 0.0231 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Apply the first-order rate law.**
$$k = \dfrac{2.303}{t} \log\dfrac{[A]_0}{[A]_0/8} = \dfrac{2.303}{t} \log 8$$

**Step 3: Note that $\dfrac{[A]_0}{8} = \dfrac{[A]_0}{2^3}$ corresponds to 3 half-lives.**

Therefore, $t = 3 \times t_{1/2} = 3 \times 30 = 90$ minutes.

**Verification by direct calculation:**
$$t = \dfrac{2.303}{0.0231} \times \log 8 = 99.69 \times 0.903 = 90 \text{ minutes} \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $t = 90$ minutes

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a)** What is meant by molecularity of a reaction? Explain unimolecular, bimolecular, and termolecular reactions with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a second-order reaction $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, derive the integrated rate equation and the half-life expression. The rate constant for a second-order reaction is $3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$. Calculate the time required for the concentration to fall from 0.20 mol L⁻¹ to 0.10 mol L⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Molecularity is the number of atoms, ions, or molecules that must collide simultaneously in an elementary step to cause a chemical reaction.

**Unimolecular (2 marks):**
- Only one molecule participates in the elementary step.
- Example: Radioactive decay — $_{88}^{226}\text{Ra} \rightarrow _{86}^{222}\text{Rn} + _{2}^{4}\text{He}$

**Bimolecular (2 marks):**
- Two molecules collide to yield products.
- Example: $2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2$ at high temperature

**Termolecular (2 marks):**
- Three molecules collide simultaneously (rare; usually involves a chaperone).
- Example: $2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$:
$$-\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2$$

This simplifies to:
$$-\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = 2k[A]^2 = k'[A]^2$$

where $k' = 2k$. Using the standard derivation (as in Section 3.2):

$$\dfrac{1}{[A]} - \dfrac{1}{[A]_0} = 2kt$$

Or in terms of reacted amount $x$ with initial $a$:
$$k = \dfrac{1}{2t}\left[\dfrac{1}{a-x} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right]$$

**Half-life expression (1 mark):**
$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{2k\,[A]_0}$$

**Numerical Problem (2 marks):**

Given: $k = 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, $a = 0.20 \text{ mol L}^{-1}$, $a-x = 0.10 \text{ mol L}^{-1}$

$$t = \dfrac{1}{2k}\left[\dfrac{1}{a-x} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right]$$

$$t = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-2}}\left[\dfrac{1}{0.10} - \dfrac{1}{0.20}\right]$$

$$t = \dfrac{1}{0.06}\left[10 - 5\right] = \dfrac{5}{0.06}$$

$$\boxed{t = 83.33 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Confusing $k$ and $2k$:** For the reaction $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, students often forget the stoichiometric factor of 2 in the rate law. Always write: $-\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2$.
> 2. **Mixing molecularity and order in definitions:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students use "order" when defining "molecularity" or vice versa.
> 3. **Ignoring the $2.303$ factor:** In first-order rate equations, $\log$ means $\log_{10}$ (which requires the 2.303 conversion from $\ln$). Using $\ln$ without conversion costs the unit-conversion mark.
> 4. **Forgetting units of $k$:** Always state the units of the rate constant in the final answer box.
> 5. **Pseudo-order mistakes:** When a problem says "in excess water," students must explicitly mention the **pseudo-order concept** to receive full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ **Molecularity** = theoretical integer (1, 2, 3) based on elementary step collisions
- ☐ **Order** = experimental quantity that can be 0, fractional, or negative
- ☐ **Pseudo-order reactions:** When one reactant is in large excess, true bimolecular reactions appear first-order
- ☐ **First-order half-life** is **independent** of initial concentration — a unique signature
- ☐ **For $n \neq 1$:** $t_{1/2} \propto [A]_0^{1-n}$
- ☐ **Units of $k$ formula:** $\text{Units} = (\text{mol L}^{-1})^{1-n} \cdot \text{time}^{-1}$
- ☐ **First-order integrated law:** $k = \dfrac{2.303}{t}\log\dfrac{a}{a-x}$
- ☐ **Second-order integrated law (same reactant):** $k = \dfrac{1}{t}\left[\dfrac{1}{a-x} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right]$
- ☐ **General $n$-th order half-life:** $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{2^{n-1} - 1}{(n-1)k[A]_0^{n-1}}$
- ☐ **Order determination methods:** Graphical, Integration, Half-life variation, van't Hoff initial rate
- ☐ **Fire dynamics connection:** Combustion orders govern **burn rate**, **ignition delay**, and **suppressant efficiency**
- ☐ **Complex vs. elementary reactions:** Order of a complex reaction equals the order of the slow (rate-determining) step
- ☐ **Negative orders** indicate product inhibition or catalyst deactivation — common in heterogeneous fire chemistry
- ☐ Always report **$k$ with units**; always state **assumed temperature** (since $k$ depends on $T$)
- ☐ For KTU full marks: **draw the structure of the rate law** before integrating
- ☐ Memorize the **three standard plots** (linearity test):
  - $[A]$ vs $t$ → Zero order
  - $\log[A]$ vs $t$ → First order
  - $\dfrac{1}{[A]}$ vs $t$ → Second order
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate of a Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rate of a Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of fire propagation and combustion dynamics, the **Rate of a Reaction** is formally defined as the change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time during a chemical reaction. It quantifies the *speed* at which a combustion process (such as the oxidation of a fuel) proceeds under specific thermodynamic and chemical conditions.

Mathematically, for a generalized homogeneous reaction:

$$ aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD $$

The reaction rate $r$ is expressed as:

$$ r = -\frac{1}{a}\frac{d[A]}{dt} = -\frac{1}{b}\frac{d[B]}{dt} = +\frac{1}{c}\frac{d[C]}{dt} = +\frac{1}{d}\frac{d[D]}{dt} $$

The negative sign indicates the depletion of reactants, while the positive sign indicates the formation of products. The **units** of reaction rate in SI are $\mathbf{mol \cdot m^{-3} \cdot s^{-1}}$, though in fire engineering, mass loss rate ($kg/m^2/s$) and heat release rate ($kW/m^2$) are more commonly used representations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The rate of a chemical reaction is governed by intrinsic kinetic parameters (activation energy, pre-exponential factor) and extrinsic environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, concentration). In fire dynamics, this directly translates to the **Burning Rate**, a critical parameter determining fire growth, spread, and ultimate hazard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Flow" Model

Imagine a **highway toll booth** where cars (molecules) pass through a narrow gate (activation energy barrier) at a fixed speed.

- **Cars waiting in queue** = Reactant molecules with insufficient energy
- **Cars passing through the gate** = Molecules with energy $\geq E_a$ that successfully react
- **Cars emerging on the other side** = Product molecules (combustion products like $CO_2$, $H_2O$)
- **Width of the gate (number of booths)** = Related to the pre-exponential factor $A$ (frequency of effective collisions)
- **Highway speed/traffic density** = Temperature and concentration of reactants

When a fire ignites, the toll booth opens wide (high temperature increases the number of cars with sufficient energy), and the queue of unreacted fuel is rapidly processed — this is the **acceleration of the reaction rate** with temperature.

A simpler real-world analogy: Compare **burning of wood** vs. **burning of paper**. Paper burns much faster because its chemical structure has a lower activation energy, allowing the reaction to proceed rapidly even at moderate temperatures. Wood requires a higher temperature (more energy input) before its molecules can overcome the activation barrier, resulting in a slower reaction rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Fire Engineers:** In combustion, we are not merely interested in the rate of disappearance of fuel, but in the **volumetric rate of heat release**, which couples the reaction rate with the **heat of combustion** ($\Delta H_c$). This coupling defines fire intensity.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit | Significance in Fire |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Arrhenius equation |
| Activation Energy | $E_a$ | **40–250 kJ/mol** (for fuels) | Energy barrier for combustion |
| Pre-exponential Factor | $A$ | **$10^6$–$10^{20}$ $s^{-1}$** | Collision frequency factor |
| Standard Pressure | $P_0$ | **101,325 Pa** | Reference for state equations |
| Stoichiometric Ratio | $\phi$ | **0.5–2.0** (typical fires) | Defines fuel-air mix |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Governing the Rate of Reaction

The reaction rate in combustion systems is governed by the interplay of multiple physical and chemical parameters. The following structured breakdown presents each factor, its mechanism of action, and its engineering relevance.

#### 2.1.1 Concentration of Reactants
- **Mechanism:** Higher concentration $\Rightarrow$ more molecular collisions per unit volume per unit time.
- **Mathematical Form:** $r = k[A]^m [B]^n$ (Rate Law Expression)
- **Why it matters:** In enclosed compartment fires, oxygen concentration depletes rapidly, directly reducing the reaction rate (ventilation-controlled regime).

#### 2.1.2 Temperature
- **Mechanism:** Governed by the **Arrhenius Equation**. A $10°C$ rise in temperature often doubles or triples the rate.
- **Mathematical Form:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$
- **Why it matters:** Fire propagation is fundamentally a **thermally driven feedback process** — heat from reaction accelerates the reaction, which generates more heat.

#### 2.1.3 Pressure
- **Mechanism:** For gas-phase reactions, higher pressure $\Rightarrow$ higher concentration (via $PV = nRT$).
- **Mathematical Form:** $r \propto P^n$ (where $n$ is the global order)
- **Why it matters:** At elevated pressures (e.g., BLEVE scenarios), reaction rates are dramatically amplified.

#### 2.1.4 Surface Area (Heterogeneous/Solid Fuels)
- **Mechanism:** For solid fuels (wood, polymers), larger exposed surface area increases the rate of pyrolysis and subsequent gas-phase combustion.
- **Mathematical Form:** $r'' \propto A_s$ (mass loss rate per unit area)
- **Why it matters:** Crumbled, powdered, or shredded materials burn explosively fast (dust explosions are an extreme example).

#### 2.1.5 Catalysts and Inhibitors
- **Mechanism:** Catalysts provide alternative pathways with lower $E_a$; inhibitors raise $E_a$ or scavenge radicals.
- **Engineering Relevance:** **Fire retardants** (e.g., halogenated compounds) work by interrupting the radical chain reactions in flames.

#### 2.1.6 Radiation and Heat Feedback
- **Mechanism:** In fire, radiant heat from the flame preheats adjacent fuel, raising its temperature and thus its reaction rate — a self-sustaining feedback loop.
- **Engineering Relevance:** This is the primary mechanism of **flame spread** across surfaces.

### 2.2 Order of Reaction vs. Molecularity

| Property | Order of Reaction | Molecularity |
|----------|------------------|--------------|
| Definition | Sum of exponents in rate law | Number of molecules colliding in elementary step |
| Values | Can be 0, 1, 2, 3, fractional, or negative | Always a whole number (1, 2, rarely 3) |
| Determined by | Experimentally | Stoichiometry of elementary step |
| Applies to | Overall reaction | Single elementary step only |
| KTU Relevance | **High** — used in fire modeling | Moderate — important for mechanism understanding |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all **essential equations** for solving reaction rate problems in KTU examinations. Memorize the forms, the variables, and the units.

| # | Equation / Formula | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $r = -\frac{d[A]}{dt}$ | Differential rate (reactant) | $mol \cdot L^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $r = k[A]^n$ | Rate law (n-th order) | $mol \cdot L^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | **Arrhenius Equation** | $s^{-1}$ (1st order) |
| 4 | $\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{[A]_0}{2k}$ | Half-life (zero order) | $s$ |
| 7 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life (first order) | $s$ |
| 8 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | Half-life (second order) | $s$ |
| 9 | $[A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$ | First-order integrated law | $mol/L$ |
| 10 | $\frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$ | Second-order integrated law | $L/mol$ |
| 11 | $[A] = [A]_0 - kt$ | Zero-order integrated law | $mol/L$ |
| 12 | $Q = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat Release Rate (HRR) | $kW/m^2$ |
| 13 | $T(t) = T_0 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha t}}{erfc(\text{arg})}\right)$ | Solid-phase thermal response | $K$ |

**Boundary Conditions** for typical fire problems:
- Initial condition: At $t=0$, $[A] = [A]_0$ (initial fuel concentration)
- Final condition: As $t \to \infty$, $[A] \to 0$ (complete consumption) for irreversible reactions
- Stoichiometric limit: $\phi = 1$ defines the ideal air-fuel ratio

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of reaction rates underpins multiple fire safety engineering applications:

1. **Fire Growth Prediction:** Models like the **Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS)** use Arrhenius-based chemical sub-models to predict flame spread.
2. **Explosion Analysis:** Rate laws determine the rate of pressure rise in confined vapor cloud explosions.
3. **Fire Retardant Design:** Understanding $E_a$ modification allows chemists to design inhibitors for polymers.
4. **Compartment Fire Design:** The transition from fuel-controlled to ventilation-controlled fires is dictated by oxygen-limited reaction rates.
5. **Suppression System Sizing:** The rate at which a suppressant (e.g., water mist, $CO_2$) reduces reaction kinetics determines system capacity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Integrated Rate Laws

#### 3.1.1 Zero-Order Reaction

**Starting Premise:** The rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of the reactant.

**Step 1 — Write the differential rate law.**
For a zero-order reaction, the rate depends only on the rate constant $k$:

$$ r = -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k $$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to separate variables.**
Multiply both sides by $-dt$ and divide by $k$:

$$ -\frac{d[A]}{k} = dt $$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides with appropriate limits.**
Apply the boundary conditions: at $t = 0$, $[A] = [A]_0$; at time $t$, $[A] = [A]$.

$$ -\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]}{k} = \int_0^t dt $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**
Since $k$ is constant, it comes outside the integral:

$$ -\frac{1}{k}\left([A] - [A]_0\right) = t $$

**Step 5 — Solve for $[A]$.**
Rearranging gives the zero-order integrated rate law:

$$ [A] = [A]_0 - kt $$

**Step 6 — Derive the half-life.**
Set $[A] = \frac{[A]_0}{2}$ and solve for $t_{1/2}$:

$$ \frac{[A]_0}{2} = [A]_0 - k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ k \cdot t_{1/2} = [A]_0 - \frac{[A]_0}{2} = \frac{[A]_0}{2} $$

$$ t_{1/2} = \frac{[A]_0}{2k} $$

This proves that for a zero-order reaction, the half-life is **directly proportional to the initial concentration**.

#### 3.1.2 First-Order Reaction

**Starting Premise:** The rate is directly proportional to the concentration of one reactant.

**Step 1 — Write the differential rate law.**

$$ -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A] $$

**Step 2 — Separate the variables.**
Divide both sides by $[A]$ and multiply by $-dt$:

$$ -\frac{d[A]}{[A]} = k \, dt $$

**Step 3 — Integrate with boundary conditions.**

$$ -\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]}{[A]} = \int_0^t k \, dt $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$ -\left(\ln[A] - \ln[A]_0\right) = kt $$

**Step 5 — Simplify using logarithm properties.**

$$ \ln\left(\frac{[A]_0}{[A]}\right) = kt $$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to obtain $[A]$ as a function of $t$.**

$$ [A] = [A]_0 \, e^{-kt} $$

**Step 7 — Derive the half-life.**
Set $[A] = \frac{[A]_0}{2}$:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{[A]_0}{[A]_0/2}\right) = k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ \ln(2) = k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k} $$

This proves that for a first-order reaction, the half-life is **independent of the initial concentration** — a key result for KTU problems.

#### 3.1.3 Second-Order Reaction (Type I: $2A \rightarrow$ Products)

**Step 1 — Differential rate law.**

$$ -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2 $$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

$$ -\frac{d[A]}{[A]^2} = k \, dt $$

**Step 3 — Integrate.**

$$ -\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} [A]^{-2} \, d[A] = \int_0^t k \, dt $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral using $\int x^{-2}dx = -x^{-1}$.**

$$ -\left(-\frac{1}{[A]} + \frac{1}{[A]_0}\right) = kt $$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$ \frac{1}{[A]} - \frac{1}{[A]_0} = kt $$

$$ \frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt $$

**Step 6 — Half-life derivation.**

$$ \frac{1}{[A]_0/2} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ \frac{2}{[A]_0} - \frac{1}{[A]_0} = k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k[A]_0} $$

For a second-order reaction, half-life is **inversely proportional to initial concentration**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Arrhenius Equation

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.** Svante Arrhenius observed that the rate constant $k$ increases with temperature according to a specific pattern. The fraction of molecules with energy $\geq E_a$ follows a Boltzmann distribution.

**Step 2 — Starting form based on Boltzmann's distribution.**

$$ k \propto e^{-E_a/RT} $$

**Step 3 — Introduce the proportionality constant $A$ (pre-exponential factor).**

$$ k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} $$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$ \ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT} $$

**Step 5 — Recognize the linear form.**
This is in the form $y = c + m \cdot x$, where:
- $y = \ln k$
- $x = 1/T$
- Slope $m = -E_a/R$
- Intercept $c = \ln A$

**Step 6 — Two-temperature form.**
For KTU problems comparing two temperatures:

$$ \ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT_1} $$

$$ \ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT_2} $$

**Step 7 — Subtract the first equation from the second.**

$$ \ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{RT_2} + \frac{E_a}{RT_1} $$

**Step 8 — Factor out $E_a/R$.**

$$ \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$

This is the most-tested form in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Order | Differential Form | Integrated Form | Linear Plot | Half-Life | Slope/Intercept |
|-------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Zero | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k$ | $[A] = [A]_0 - kt$ | $[A]$ vs $t$ | $\frac{[A]_0}{2k}$ | Slope: $-k$ |
| First | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$ | $[A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$ | $\ln[A]$ vs $t$ | $\frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Slope: $-k$ |
| Second | $-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2$ | $\frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$ | $\frac{1}{[A]}$ vs $t$ | $\frac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | Slope: $+k$ |

### 3.4 Connection to Fire Propagation: The Pyrolysis Rate

In solid-phase fire spread, the **mass loss rate per unit area** $\dot{m}''$ follows an Arrhenius-type relationship:

$$ \dot{m}'' = \rho \cdot v_p = A_p \cdot e^{-E_p/RT_p} $$

where:
- $v_p$ = pyrolysis velocity (m/s)
- $A_p$ = pre-exponential factor for pyrolysis
- $E_p$ = activation energy of pyrolysis
- $T_p$ = surface temperature of the pyrolyzing solid

This bridges the abstract chemical kinetics with the **practical fire growth rate**, making it the cornerstone of fire spread modeling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Reaction Rate Analysis Framework in Fire Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[REACTANT INPUT] --> B1[Fuel Molecules A]
    A2[OXYGEN INPUT] --> B2[Oxidizer Molecules B]
    B1 --> C1{COLLISION ZONE}
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Sufficient Energy Ea]
    C1 --> D2[Insufficient Energy]
    D1 --> E1[TRANSITION STATE COMPLEX]
    E1 --> F1[PRODUCT FORMATION]
    F1 --> G1[Heat Release Q]
    F1 --> G2[Combustion Products CO2 H2O]
    G1 --> H1[FEEDBACK LOOP]
    H1 --> A1
    D2 --> Z1[Unreacted Fuel Reservoir]
    H1 --> I1[System Temperature Increase]
    I1 --> J1[Modified Arrhenius Rate Constant k]
    J1 --> C1
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Determination of Reaction Order and Rate Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START Experiment] --> S2[Measure Concentration vs Time Data]
    S2 --> Q1{Linear when plotted as}
    Q1 --> P1[Concentration A vs time t]
    P1 --> R1[ZERO ORDER]
    Q1 --> P2[ln A vs time t]
    P2 --> R2[FIRST ORDER]
    Q1 --> P3[1 over A vs time t]
    P3 --> R3[SECOND ORDER]
    R1 --> S3[Slope equals minus k]
    R2 --> S4[Slope equals minus k]
    R3 --> S5[Slope equals plus k]
    S3 --> END1[Calculate Half Life t half]
    S4 --> END1
    S5 --> END1
    END1 --> FIN[KTU ANSWER: Rate Law and Constants]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Effect of Temperature on Rate Constant (Arrhenius Behavior)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[LOW TEMPERATURE T1] --> K1[Small k1]
    T2[MEDIUM TEMPERATURE T2] --> K2[Larger k2]
    T3[HIGH TEMPERATURE T3] --> K3[Very Large k3]
    K1 --> EQ[Arrhenius Equation k equals A exp minus Ea over RT]
    K2 --> EQ
    K3 --> EQ
    EQ --> OUT[Reaction Rate r equals k times Concentration term]
    OUT --> FIRE[Fire Growth Acceleration]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Energy Profile Diagram of a Combustion Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Reactants A plus B] --> A1[Activation Energy Barrier Ea]
    A1 --> TS[Transition State Peak]
    TS --> P1[Products C plus D]
    P1 --> EN[Energy Released equals Delta H negative]
    R0 -.Heat Absorbed.-> A1
    A1 -.Heat Released.-> P1
    EN --> H1[Net Exothermic Release Feeds Fire]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The vertical axis represents the potential energy of the reacting system. The reactants must first "climb" the activation energy hill ($E_a$) to reach the transition state, after which they "fall" to a lower energy state (products), releasing $\Delta H$ as heat. In fire, this released heat sustains and propagates the reaction.
