<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Rate Coefficient** (also called the **rate constant**, denoted by the symbol **k**) is the proportionality constant that appears in the rate law expression of a chemical reaction. It quantifies the intrinsic speed at which a particular chemical reaction proceeds at a given temperature, independent of the concentrations of the reacting species.

For a general homogeneous gas-phase combustion reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$$

the empirical rate law takes the form:

$$\text{Rate} = k [A]^m [B]^n$$

where the order of the reaction is $m + n$ (often determined experimentally, not directly from stoichiometry). The rate coefficient **k** carries the units that make the rate dimensionally consistent, typically expressed in $\text{s}^{-1}$ (first order), $\text{cm}^3 \text{mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ (second order), or $\text{cm}^6 \text{mol}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ (third order) for gas-phase fire chemistry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In fire dynamics, the rate coefficient governs the rate of fuel oxidation, pyrolysis gas release, and flame propagation chemistry. The student MUST master the Arrhenius formulation since it is the most heavily weighted concept in Module 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of the rate coefficient **k** as the **"speed limit" of a chemical reaction**, while the concentrations [A] and [B] are the **"number of cars on the highway"**.

- A reaction with a **high k** is like a wide, well-paved expressway — even a few cars (low concentration) zoom through quickly.
- A reaction with a **low k** is like a narrow, potholed village road — even many cars (high concentration) crawl through slowly.
- The **rate of reaction** is the **total traffic flow** = (number of cars) × (speed limit).

In fire dynamics, **k** is the "engine" of combustion: it determines how fast a fuel vapor can react with oxygen, dictating whether you get a slow smolder (low k) or a violent deflagration (high k).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
> - Boltzmann Constant: **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J K⁻¹**
> - Planck's Constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J s**
> - Avogadro's Number: **N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
> - Activation Energy (typical hydrocarbon combustion): **E_a ≈ 100–250 kJ mol⁻¹**
> - Pre-exponential Factor (typical): **A ≈ 10⁷–10¹⁴ s⁻¹** (unimolecular) or **A ≈ 10¹⁰–10¹⁴ cm³ mol⁻¹ s⁻¹** (bimolecular)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Governing the Rate Coefficient

The rate coefficient is **NOT a true constant** — it is "constant" only at a fixed temperature. The following structured logic explains why:

- **Step 1 — Temperature Dependence (Dominant Factor):** As temperature rises, the average kinetic energy of molecules increases. A larger fraction of molecular collisions possess energy ≥ E_a (activation energy), exponentially accelerating the reaction. This is captured by the **Arrhenius equation**.
- **Step 2 — Molecular Orientation & Steric Factor:** Not all collisions are geometrically favorable. Only collisions with proper orientation on the molecular surface lead to reaction. This is corrected by the **steric factor (ρ)**, which has values between 0 and 1.
- **Step 3 — Collision Frequency (Z):** The number of molecular collisions per unit volume per unit time depends on the molecular diameter (σ), relative velocity, and number density. Higher collision frequency → higher k.
- **Step 4 — Catalytic Effects (Fire-Relevant):** In fire chemistry, surfaces, metal oxides, and radicals (H•, OH•, O•) act as catalysts, lowering the effective E_a and thereby raising k. This is critical in fire suppression chemistry.
- **Step 5 — Pressure & Third-Body Effects:** For unimolecular and termolecular reactions in flames, pressure influences k through the **Lindemann mechanism** and **Troe falloff behavior**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Rate} = k [A]^m [B]^n$ | Empirical rate law | Rate in mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius Equation** (MOST IMPORTANT) | A = pre-exponential factor, E_a in J mol⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius form | Plot ln k vs 1/T → slope = -E_a/R |
| 4 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius form | Used when A is unknown |
| 5 | $k = Z \rho \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Collision theory form | Z = collision frequency, ρ = steric factor |
| 6 | $Z = N_A \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\dfrac{8\pi k_B T}{\mu}}$ | Collision frequency per mol | σ_AB = collision cross-section, μ = reduced mass |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{k_B T}{h} \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)$ | Eyring equation (TST) | ΔG‡ = Gibbs free energy of activation |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{k_B T}{h} \exp\left(\dfrac{\Delta S^{\ddagger}}{R}\right) \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)$ | Eyring full form | ΔH‡, ΔS‡ from transition state theory |
| 9 | $k = \dfrac{k_{\infty}}{1 + \dfrac{k_{\infty}}{k_0 [M]}}$ | Lindemann falloff (low pressure limit) | [M] = third-body concentration |
| 10 | $\dfrac{d \ln k}{d T} = \dfrac{E_a}{R T^2}$ | Arrhenius temperature sensitivity | Shows exponential growth of k with T |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Formulas (2), (3), and (4) above are **guaranteed high-yield**. Master the linearized form (3) because KTU frequently asks students to determine E_a and A from tabular (T, k) data using a graph or linear regression.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Fire Science

- **Flame Speed Prediction:** Laminar burning velocity depends on the slowest elementary reaction's k, especially the chain-branching reaction H + O₂ → OH + O.
- **Ignition Delay Time:** τ_ign ∝ 1/k; low k (e.g., at low temperature) means long ignition delays, critical in engine knock and fire suppression.
- **Pyrolysis Modeling:** Solid-fuel pyrolysis rate is modeled using Arrhenius k with biomass-specific E_a and A (Broido-Shafizadeh model).
- **Detonation Chemistry:** Branching ratios and rate coefficients determine whether a flame accelerates to detonation (DDT).
- **Fire Suppressant Design:** Halons and clean agents work by scavenging H• and OH• radicals, effectively reducing the net k of the branching reactions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Arrhenius Equation from Maxwell–Boltzmann Statistics

**Starting Premise:** Only molecules possessing kinetic energy greater than or equal to the activation energy E_a can react upon collision.

**Step 1 — Energy Distribution of Molecules (Maxwell–Boltzmann Law):**
The fraction of molecules having energy ≥ E_a at temperature T is given by the Boltzmann factor:

$$f(E \geq E_a) = \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define the Pre-exponential Factor A:**
The pre-exponential factor A represents the maximum possible reaction rate, which would occur if every molecular collision had sufficient energy. Mathematically, A encapsulates:
- Collision frequency Z
- Steric factor ρ
- Number density considerations

**Step 3 — Combine the Two Effects:**
The actual rate coefficient equals the maximum rate (A) multiplied by the fraction of "successful" collisions:

$$k = A \cdot f(E \geq E_a)$$

Substituting the Boltzmann factor:

$$\boxed{k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

This is the **Arrhenius equation** in its canonical form.

**Step 4 — Linearization for Experimental Determination:**
Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

Comparing with the equation of a straight line $y = mx + c$:

$$y = \ln k, \quad x = \frac{1}{T}, \quad m = -\frac{E_a}{R}, \quad c = \ln A$$

Hence, a plot of $\ln k$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line of slope $-E_a/R$ and intercept $\ln A$.

**Step 5 — Two-Point Arrhenius Formula (Eliminates A):**
For two different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T_1}$$

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T_2}$$

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{R T_2} + \frac{E_a}{R T_1}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This is the **two-point form** used universally in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** The rate coefficient for the elementary combustion reaction H + O₂ → OH + O is measured as $k_1 = 2.0 \times 10^7 \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 1000 \text{ K}$ and $k_2 = 8.0 \times 10^7 \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 1200 \text{ K}$. Calculate the activation energy E_a and the pre-exponential factor A.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Write the two-point Arrhenius formula:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio $k_2 / k_1$:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^7}{2.0 \times 10^7} = 4.0$$

**Step 3:** Compute the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(4.0) = 1.3863$$

**Step 4:** Compute the temperature reciprocal difference:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{1200}$$

$$= \frac{1200 - 1000}{1000 \times 1200} = \frac{200}{1.2 \times 10^6}$$

$$= 1.6667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to solve for E_a:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 1.3863}{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{11.526}{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 69,156 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx 69.16 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for A using $T_1$ and $k_1$:

$$A = k_1 \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R T_1}\right)$$

$$A = 2.0 \times 10^7 \times \exp\left(\frac{69,156}{8.314 \times 1000}\right)$$

$$A = 2.0 \times 10^7 \times \exp(8.318)$$

$$A = 2.0 \times 10^7 \times 4107.5$$

$$A = 8.215 \times 10^{10} \text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 69.16 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}, \quad A \approx 8.22 \times 10^{10} \text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Collision-Theory Rate Coefficient

**Step 1 — Total Collision Frequency per Unit Volume:**

$$Z_{AB} = N_A n_A n_B \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi \mu}}$$

where $\sigma_{AB} = \pi (r_A + r_B)^2$ is the collision cross-section and $\mu$ is the reduced molar mass.

**Step 2 — Multiply by Boltzmann Reactive Fraction:**

$$k = Z_{AB} \cdot \rho \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Here $\rho$ (steric factor, $0 \leq \rho \leq 1$) accounts for unfavorable molecular orientations.

**Step 3 — Form for Identical Molecules (A = A):**

$$Z_{AA} = \frac{1}{2} N_A n^2 \sigma_{AA} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

The factor ½ avoids double-counting collisions between identical molecules.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Rate Coefficient

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Fire Chemistry System]) --> reactID[A plus B reacting species]
    reactID --> concID[Concentrations A and B]
    reactID --> orderID[Reaction order m and n]
    concID --> rateID[Reaction rate]
    orderID --> rateID
    rateID --> rateConst[Rate coefficient k]
    rateConst --> arrID[Arrhenius temperature dependence]
    rateConst --> freqID[Collision frequency Z]
    rateConst --> stericID[Steric factor rho]
    rateConst --> tstID[Transition state delta G dagger]
    arrID --> eaID[Activation energy E a]
    arrID --> rConst[R equals 8.314 J per mol K]
    freqID --> crossID[Cross section sigma]
    freqID --> massID[Reduced mass mu]
    stericID --> orientID[Molecular orientation]
    tstID --> eyringID[Eyring equation kT over h]
    rateConst --> fireID1[Flame speed prediction]
    rateConst --> fireID2[Ignition delay time]
    rateConst --> fireID3[Pyrolysis rate modeling]
    rateConst --> fireID4[Detonation chemistry DDT]
    eaID --> outcome[Fire propagation outcome]
    orientID --> outcome
    fireID1 --> outcome
    fireID2 --> outcome
    fireID3 --> outcome
    fireID4 --> outcome
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — Determining E_a and A from Experimental Data

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataNode([Tabular T and k data]) --> lnNode[Take natural log of k values]
    invTNode[Compute 1 over T] --> lnNode
    lnNode --> plotNode[Plot ln k versus 1 over T]
    plotNode --> slopeNode[Determine slope equals negative E a over R]
    plotNode --> intNode[Determine intercept equals ln A]
    slopeNode --> eaOut[E a equals negative slope times R]
    intNode --> aOut[A equals exp intercept]
    eaOut --> kFinal[Reconstruct k as A exp negative E a over RT]
    aOut --> kFinal
    kFinal --> validNode[Validate using additional data points]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of the Energy Profile

The Arrhenius equation is best visualized as an energy barrier:

- Reactants sit in an energy well at the baseline.
- A potential energy **hill of height E_a** separates them from products.
- Only molecules with kinetic energy ≥ E_a (the shaded tail of the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution) can climb the hill and reach the product state.
- As temperature rises, the shaded tail grows exponentially → k rises exponentially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Substitution Note:** A full physical potential-energy diagram is typically hand-drawn by students in the KTU exam. The Mermaid fallback above provides a complete functional mapping of how concentration, order, temperature, E_a, ρ, and Z all converge to define the single quantity **k** that ultimately governs fire propagation dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define the rate coefficient of a chemical reaction. What are its SI units for a first-order reaction?**

**Model Answer:**
The rate coefficient **k** is the proportionality constant in the rate law expression that relates the rate of reaction to the concentrations of the reactants raised to appropriate powers. For the general rate law $\text{Rate} = k [A]^m [B]^n$, **k** is independent of concentration but depends strongly on temperature.
For a **first-order reaction** (m = 1), rate has units of mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ and concentration has units of mol L⁻¹, so the units of k are:

$$[k] = \frac{\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}}{\text{mol L}^{-1}} = \text{s}^{-1}$$

Hence, for first-order reactions, **k has units of s⁻¹ (per second)**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: State the Arrhenius equation and explain the physical significance of the pre-exponential factor A.**

**Model Answer:**
The Arrhenius equation is:

$$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Physical Significance of A:**
- A represents the **maximum possible rate** of the reaction, which would occur only if every molecular collision had sufficient energy to overcome the activation barrier.
- It encapsulates the **collision frequency**, the **steric (orientation) factor**, and the **number density** of reactive molecular pairs.
- Units of A are identical to those of k, but A is much larger in magnitude (typically 10⁷–10¹⁴ s⁻¹ for unimolecular reactions).
- Mathematically, A equals the value of k in the hypothetical limit of infinite temperature (T → ∞, the exponential → 1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a suitable energy profile diagram, derive the Arrhenius equation starting from the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution law. Explain the significance of activation energy. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** For a first-order thermal decomposition reaction, the following rate coefficients were obtained:

| Temperature (K) | k (s⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| 600 | $4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 700 | $6.4 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 800 | $3.6 \times 10^{0}$ |
| 900 | $8.0 \times 10^{1}$ |

Calculate the activation energy E_a (in kJ mol⁻¹) and the pre-exponential factor A. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** State the Maxwell–Boltzmann law for the fraction of molecules with energy ≥ E_a:

$$f = \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The maximum possible rate of reaction is A (when every collision is effective). The actual rate is the maximum rate multiplied by the fraction of effective collisions:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

**Step 4:** Discuss significance of E_a — it is the minimum kinetic energy that reactant molecules must possess upon collision to form products. A high E_a means the reaction is highly temperature-sensitive.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Convert all data to (1/T, ln k):

| T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | k (s⁻¹) | ln k |
|---|---|---|---|
| 600 | 1.667 × 10⁻³ | 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ | -7.824 |
| 700 | 1.429 × 10⁻³ | 6.4 × 10⁻² | -2.749 |
| 800 | 1.250 × 10⁻³ | 3.6 × 10⁰ | 1.281 |
| 900 | 1.111 × 10⁻³ | 8.0 × 10¹ | 4.382 |

**Step 2:** Use the two-point form between T = 600 K and T = 900 K:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^1}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.0 \times 10^5$$

$$\ln(2.0 \times 10^5) = 12.206$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} - 1.111 \times 10^{-3} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:**

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 12.206}{5.556 \times 10^{-4}} = 182,684 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx 182.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for A using T = 600 K:

$$A = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times \exp\left(\frac{182{,}684}{8.314 \times 600}\right) = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times \exp(36.625) = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.92 \times 10^{15}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 3.17 \times 10^{12} \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad E_a \approx 182.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the Collision Theory of reaction rates. Derive the expression for the rate constant based on collision theory and discuss the role of the steric factor. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** The rate coefficient for the gas-phase reaction 2NO₂ → 2NO + O₂ is 0.540 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ at 600 K and 11.5 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ at 700 K. What is the activation energy in kJ mol⁻¹? What is the rate constant at 800 K? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** State the postulates of Collision Theory:
- Molecules must collide to react.
- Only collisions with energy ≥ E_a and proper orientation are effective.
- The reaction rate = (collision frequency) × (reactive fraction) × (orientation factor).

**Step 2:** The collision frequency for two unlike molecules A and B per unit volume per unit time:

$$Z_{AB} = N_A n_A n_B \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi \mu}}$$

**Step 3:** The rate constant expression:

$$k = Z_{AB} \cdot \rho \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Role of the steric factor ρ:
- ρ (0 ≤ ρ ≤ 1) accounts for the fraction of collisions with proper molecular orientation.
- For simple atom-atom reactions, ρ ≈ 1.
- For complex organic molecules, ρ can be as low as 10⁻⁶ because most collisions are geometrically unfavorable.
- It explains why collision theory often overpredicts k for polyatomic reactants.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Apply the two-point Arrhenius formula with $T_1 = 600$ K and $T_2 = 700$ K:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{11.5}{0.540} = 21.296$$

$$\ln(21.296) = 3.0586$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{700} = \frac{700 - 600}{420{,}000} = 2.381 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:**

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 3.0586}{2.381 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{25.429}{2.381 \times 10^{-4}} = 106,800 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 106.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Find k at 800 K using E_a and the data at 600 K:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_3}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3} = \frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{800} = \frac{800 - 600}{480{,}000} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_3}{0.540}\right) = \frac{106{,}800}{8.314} \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} = 12,847 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} = 5.353$$

$$\frac{k_3}{0.540} = \exp(5.353) = 211.5$$

$$\boxed{k_3 \approx 114.2 \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1} \text{ at } 800 \text{ K}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake in E_a:** If you forget to convert E_a from J mol⁻¹ to kJ mol⁻¹, you lose 1 mark. Always state the final answer in **kJ mol⁻¹** for fire-engineering problems.
> 2. **Temperature Reciprocal Sign Error:** The two-point formula uses $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. Swapping this to $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ changes the sign of E_a. Always confirm that E_a > 0 for a normal reaction.
> 3. **Forgetting the Steric Factor:** In collision-theory derivations, students often write $k = Z \exp(-E_a/RT)$ without the factor $\rho$. Examiners will deduct 2 marks for this.
> 4. **Linearization Plot:** When asked to "show graphically," many students plot k vs T (wrong) instead of ln k vs 1/T (correct). Always specify axes clearly.
> 5. **Round-off Errors:** Avoid premature rounding. Carry at least 4 significant figures through intermediate steps, and only round the final answer to 3–4 significant figures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The **rate coefficient k** is the proportionality constant in the rate law, **not a true constant** — it depends strongly on temperature.
- [ ] The **Arrhenius equation** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ is the master equation for temperature dependence of k.
- [ ] **A** (pre-exponential factor) represents the maximum reaction rate at infinite temperature, with units identical to k.
- [ ] **E_a** (activation energy) is the minimum energy barrier that reactant molecules must overcome. Typical hydrocarbon combustion: 100–250 kJ mol⁻¹.
- [ ] **Linearized form:** $\ln k = \ln A - (E_a/R)(1/T)$ — plot ln k vs 1/T to extract E_a and A.
- [ ] **Two-point formula:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ — used when A is unknown.
- [ ] **Collision theory:** $k = Z \rho \exp(-E_a/RT)$ — adds steric factor ρ (0 ≤ ρ ≤ 1) for orientation effects.
- [ ] **Eyring (TST) equation:** $k = (k_B T/h) \exp(-\Delta G^{\ddagger}/RT)$ — more rigorous, accounts for entropy of activation.
- [ ] **Lindemann falloff:** For unimolecular reactions in flames, $k$ is pressure-dependent: $k = k_\infty / (1 + k_\infty/k_0[M])$.
- [ ] **Fire relevance:** k controls flame speed, ignition delay, pyrolysis rate, and DDT — master Arrhenius for guaranteed marks.
- [ ] **Units to remember:** First-order k → s⁻¹; Second-order k → cm³ mol⁻¹ s⁻¹; Third-order k → cm⁶ mol⁻² s⁻¹.
- [ ] **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** is universal — never substitute R = 1.987 × 10⁻³ kJ mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ without unit conversion.
- [ ] **The 10 K Rule (Rule of Thumb):** For E_a ≈ 100 kJ mol⁻¹ near 1000 K, k approximately **doubles for every 10 K rise in temperature** — a quick self-check in exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **fire chemistry and combustion thermodynamics**, the **equilibrium constant** ($K$) of a chemical reaction is a dimensionless quantity that expresses the *ratio of product activities to reactant activities*, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient, when the reaction has reached thermodynamic equilibrium at a specified temperature and pressure.

For a generic homogeneous gas-phase reaction encountered in fire systems,

$$aA + bB \;\rightleftharpoons\; cC + dD$$

the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressures ($K_p$), molar concentrations ($K_c$), and mole fractions ($K_x$) is defined as:

$$K_p \;=\; \frac{(p_C)^{c}\,(p_D)^{d}}{(p_A)^{a}\,(p_B)^{b}} \qquad K_c \;=\; \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \qquad K_x \;=\; \frac{(x_C)^{c}(x_D)^{d}}{(x_A)^{a}(x_B)^{b}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** For fire-engineering calculations, the *thermodynamic* equilibrium constant uses **fugacities** (real gases) and **activities** (non-ideal solutions), but for the temperatures and pressures typical of enclosure fires (300 K – 2500 K, 1 atm – 10 atm), ideal-gas behaviour is an acceptable approximation, and **partial pressures in atm** are used.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Balanced Tug-of-War"

Imagine a tug-of-war contest between two teams (reactants vs. products) on a frictionless, sliding platform. At the very start, the *reactants* pull with full force, the *products* haven't formed yet, and the platform is far on the reactant side. As the contest proceeds, products accumulate and start pulling in the opposite direction. Eventually, the platform settles at a fixed position where both teams pull with exactly equal force. **That final, steady position is the equilibrium state, and the "ratio of forces" describing it is the equilibrium constant.**

If the temperature rises, one team (usually the endothermic side) gains strength, the platform shifts, and the *value of K changes*. This is precisely why flame-product composition shifts with fire temperature — a critical idea in fire dynamics.

### 1.3 Why Equilibrium Constants Matter in Fire Dynamics

| Fire Phenomenon | Role of Equilibrium Constant |
|---|---|
| Adiabatic flame temperature (theoretical upper limit) | Predicts maximum $T_{ad}$ before significant dissociation lowers it |
| Combustion product speciation (CO, CO₂, H₂, H₂O, OH) | Predicts toxic/sooty species at high temperature |
| Water–gas shift in post-flame gases | Explains why CO persists in vitiated or under-ventilated fires |
| Smouldering vs. flaming transition | Equilibrium of char–oxygen reactions governs heat release |
| Suppression chemistry (e.g., halogenated agents) | Determines catalytic recombination rate of H, O, OH radicals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Gas Constant** ($R$): $R = 8.314 \;\mathrm{J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$ — appears in every equilibrium relation.
> **Standard-state pressure**: $P^{\circ} = 1\;\mathrm{atm} = 101.325\;\mathrm{kPa}$.
> **Standard-state temperature**: $T^{\circ} = 298.15\;\mathrm{K}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Meaning of the Three Forms of $K$

In fire-computations we routinely swap between the three forms. The unifying relation is:

$$K_p \;=\; K_c\,(RT)^{\Delta n} \;=\; K_x\,(P)^{\Delta n}$$

where $\Delta n = (c + d) - (a + b)$ is the *change in the number of gaseous moles* per stoichiometric reaction event.

> **Why three forms?**
> - **$K_p$** — most natural for combustion gases (partial pressures tabulated in JANAF tables).
> - **$K_c$** — convenient for kinetic follow-up (rate constants often referenced to molarity).
> - **$K_x$** — used in *closed-vessel* fire calculations where mole fractions are tracked (e.g., zone-model compartments).

### 2.2 Temperature Dependence — The van 't Hoff Equation

The equilibrium constant is *not* a constant with respect to temperature; it is a *function of temperature alone* (for an ideal system, at fixed $P$). The classical **van 't Hoff equation** captures this:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\ln K}{\mathrm{d}T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R\,T^{2}}$$

Its integrated (constant-$\Delta H^{\circ}$) form is:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for combustion:** Combustion reactions are *exothermic* ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$). Therefore, **as temperature rises, $K$ decreases for an exothermic reaction** — meaning equilibrium shifts *toward reactants* (more dissociation of CO₂ and H₂O at very high flame temperatures, the so-called **frozen-chemistry / dissociation limit** of adiabatic flame temperature).

A more accurate temperature dependence uses the **four-coefficient NASA polynomial** for $C_p^{\circ}(T)$:

$$\ln K(T) \;=\; -\frac{1}{R}\!\left[\frac{\Delta a_1}{2}\,T^{-1} + \Delta a_2\,\ln T + \frac{\Delta a_3}{2}\,T + \frac{\Delta a_4}{6}\,T^{2} + \frac{\Delta a_5}{2}\,T^{-2}\right] + \frac{\Delta a_6}{R}$$

This is the form used by **CHEMKIN / Cantera / NASA CEA** codes routinely employed in CFD fire modelling (FDS, FireFOAM).

### 2.3 The Three Most Important Equilibrium Reactions in Fire Engineering

| # | Reaction | Name | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298}$ (kJ/mol) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathrm{CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2}$ | Water–gas shift (WGS) | $-41.2$ | Dominates post-flame CO ⇄ CO₂ inter-conversion in vitiated compartment fires |
| 2 | $\mathrm{CO_2 \rightleftharpoons CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2}$ | CO₂ dissociation | $+283.0$ | Limits adiabatic flame temperature of carbonaceous fuels |
| 3 | $\mathrm{H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2}$ | H₂O dissociation | $+241.8$ | Major endothermic sink in H₂ flames; critical for hydrogen-safety analysis |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$ | Pressure ↔ concentration form | Dimensionless |
| F2 | $K_p = K_x(P)^{\Delta n}$ | Pressure ↔ mole-fraction form | Dimensionless |
| F3 | $\Delta n = \sum \nu_{\text{prod}} - \sum \nu_{\text{react}}$ | Stoichiometric mole change | Dimensionless |
| F4 | $\frac{\mathrm{d}\ln K}{\mathrm{d}T} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$ | van 't Hoff (differential) | $K^{-1}$ |
| F5 | $\ln(K_2/K_1) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | van 't Hoff (integrated) | Dimensionless |
| F6 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT\ln K$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz link | $\mathrm{J \cdot mol^{-1}}$ |
| F7 | $\frac{K_{T_2}}{K_{T_1}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$ | Practical $K(T)$ interpolation | Dimensionless |
| F8 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$ | Free-energy decomposition | $\mathrm{J \cdot mol^{-1}}$ |
| F9 | $K = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}\right)$ | Direct form of F6 | Dimensionless |
| F10 | $T_{ad,\text{equilibrium}} \;<\; T_{ad,\text{frozen}}$ | Dissociation cools the flame | $\mathrm{K}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall (KTU Board favourite trap):** If $\Delta n \neq 0$, the *dimensionless* form of $K$ uses $(P/P^{\circ})^{\Delta n}$ — never the bare $(P)^{\Delta n}$. Always divide by the standard-state pressure to keep $K$ dimensionless.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Protection Engineering:** Predicting CO yield in under-ventilated (fuel-rich) compartment fires. The WGS equilibrium, frozen at ~1000 K, is the accepted *engineering model* for post-flame CO/H₂ ratios (Purser's toxicological yield model).
- **Propulsion & Boiler Design:** Equilibrium composition gives the upper bound on $T_{ad}$, which is the design limit of turbine-inlet and fire-chamber materials.
- **Explosion / Detonation Modelling:** Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) theory requires equilibrium products behind the shock.
- **Hydrogen Safety:** High $K_p$ for H₂O dissociation means hydrogen flames can self-quench less easily; flammability limits widen at high $T$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$

We start from the **ideal-gas law** applied to each gaseous species $i$ in the reaction mixture:

$$p_i \;=\; \frac{n_i}{V}\,RT \;=\; c_i\,RT$$

Here $p_i$ is the partial pressure (Pa or atm), $c_i$ is the molar concentration ($\mathrm{mol/m^{3}}$), and $V$ is the volume of the gas.

**Step 1 — Substitute the ideal-gas law into the definition of $K_p$:**

$$K_p \;=\; \prod_i (p_i)^{\nu_i} \;=\; \prod_i (c_i\,RT)^{\nu_i} \;=\; \prod_i (c_i)^{\nu_i}\,\prod_i (RT)^{\nu_i}$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the first product as $K_c$:**

$$\prod_i (c_i)^{\nu_i} \;=\; K_c$$

**Step 3 — Pull the $RT$ terms out of the product and combine their exponents:**

$$\prod_i (RT)^{\nu_i} \;=\; (RT)^{\sum_i \nu_i} \;=\; (RT)^{\Delta n}$$

**Step 4 — Combine steps 2 and 3:**

$$K_p \;=\; K_c\,(RT)^{\Delta n}$$

$\blacksquare$

> **Engineering caveat:** This derivation uses ideal-gas behaviour. For high-pressure storage-tank fires ($P > 50$ atm) one must replace $p_i$ by fugacity $\phi_i p_i$, which makes the ratio a *fugacity coefficient* correction: $K_{\phi} = K_p^{\text{ideal}} / K_p^{\text{real}}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the van 't Hoff Equation (Differential Form)

We begin with the **fundamental thermodynamic identity** at constant pressure:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:**

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta H^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} - \Delta S^{\circ} - T\,\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta S^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T}$$

**Step 2 — Use Kirchhoff's relation** $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta H^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} = \Delta C_p$ **and** $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta S^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} = \dfrac{\Delta C_p}{T}$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} \;=\; \Delta C_p - \Delta S^{\circ} - T\cdot\frac{\Delta C_p}{T} \;=\; -\Delta S^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — But from the original identity, $\Delta S^{\circ} = (\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ})/T$. Substitute:**

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into a "Gibbs–Helmholtz" form:**

$$\frac{1}{T}\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT\ln K$ and divide both sides by $R$:**

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}T}\!\left(-R\ln K\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\mathrm{d}\ln K}{\mathrm{d}T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: WGS Equilibrium in a Compartment Fire

**Problem (typical KTU-style):**
Methane burns in a closed room. Post-flame gases equilibrate at $T = 1000\;\mathrm{K}$. For the water–gas shift reaction

$$\mathrm{CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2}$$

$\Delta H^{\circ} = -41.2\;\mathrm{kJ/mol}$. At $T_1 = 800\;\mathrm{K}$, $K_{p,1} = 4.0$. Estimate $K_{p,2}$ at $T_2 = 1000\;\mathrm{K}$, and comment on CO concentration in a fuel-lean fire.

**Step 1 — Apply the integrated van 't Hoff equation:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}}\right) \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values (note $\Delta H^{\circ}$ in $\mathrm{J/mol}$):**

$$\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \;=\; \frac{-41\,200}{8.314} \;=\; -4\,955.4\;\mathrm{K}$$

**Step 3 — Temperature bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; \frac{1}{800} - \frac{1}{1000} \;=\; \frac{1000-800}{800\,000} \;=\; 2.5\times 10^{-4}\;\mathrm{K^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply and exponentiate:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}}\right) \;=\; (-4\,955.4)\,(2.5\times 10^{-4}) \;=\; -1.2389$$

$$K_{p,2} \;=\; K_{p,1}\,e^{-1.2389} \;=\; 4.0 \times 0.2893 \;=\; 1.157$$

**Step 5 — Interpret for a fire-leak scenario:**

$$K_{p,2} \;=\; \frac{[{\rm CO_2}][{\rm H_2}]}{[{\rm CO}][{\rm H_2O}]} \;\approx\; 1.16$$

A small $K_p$ means the equilibrium is *not strongly product-favoured*. **In a fuel-lean fire, the abundance of H₂O (excess oxidiser) drives the equilibrium toward the reactant side of the WGS, leaving non-trivial residual CO in the post-flame gas** — a key reason CO toxicity is non-negligible even in well-ventilated fires. This is a routinely examined KTU point under "Fire Effluent Toxicology".

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | $K_p$ (WGS) | $K_p$ (CO₂ ⇄ CO + ½O₂) | $K_p$ (H₂O ⇄ H₂ + ½O₂) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298}$ (kJ/mol) | $-41.2$ | $+283.0$ | $+241.8$ |
| Exo / Endothermic | Exo | Endo | Endo |
| Behaviour as $T \uparrow$ | $K$ falls (eqm. shifts left → more CO) | $K$ rises (more dissociation → more CO) | $K$ rises (more dissociation) |
| Effect on $T_{ad}$ | Negligible | Strongly lowers $T_{ad}$ | Strongly lowers $T_{ad}$ |
| Implication for CO toxicity | Increases with $T$ in vitiated fires | Increases with $T$ in all fires | Indirect (via O/H pool) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Relations Among the Three Forms of $K$

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Define Reaction: aA + bB ↔ cC + dD] --> B[Compute Δn = c+d - a-b]
    B --> C{Known Variable?}
    C -- Partial pressures --> D[Kp = Π pi^νi]
    C -- Concentrations --> E[Kc = Π Ci^νi]
    C -- Mole fractions --> F[Kx = Π xi^νi]
    D --> G[Convert via Kp = Kx · P^Δn]
    E --> H[Convert via Kp = Kc · RT^Δn]
    F --> G
    H --> I[Thermodynamic Consistency Check]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Apply van t Hoff: dlnK/dT = ΔH° / RT²]
    J --> K[Final K T for fire analysis]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top with the balanced equation → compute the stoichiometric mole change $\Delta n$ → pick the form of $K$ you have data for → convert to the desired form → cross-check with van 't Hoff to find $K$ at the fire temperature of interest (e.g., 1000 K post-flame).

### 4.2 Mermaid — Block Diagram of a Fire-Scenario Equilibrium Calculation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fuel Composition CHxOyNz] --> B[Assume Complete Combustion]
    B --> C[Product Set CO2, H2O, N2, O2 surplus]
    C --> D[Energy Balance → Tad frozen chemistry]
    D --> E[Tad approx 2300 K for hydrocarbons]
    E --> F[Apply Equilibrium at Tad]
    F --> G[Dissociation: CO2 → CO + 1/2 O2]
    F --> H[Dissociation: H2O → H2 + 1/2 O2]
    F --> I[WGS: CO + H2O → CO2 + H2]
    G --> J[Recompute Energy Balance]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[New Tad equilibrium - usually 100-300 K lower]
    K --> L[Final Composition for Toxicity / CFD input]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the *engineering iteration* used in adiabatic flame-temperature codes. The "frozen-chemistry" $T_{ad}$ from step E is an *upper bound*; introducing real equilibrium reactions G, H, I in step F and re-solving energy balance in J gives a *realistic* $T_{ad}$ and product mix, which feeds toxic-load calculations in L.

### 4.3 Mermaid — Temperature Dependence Behaviour Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1[T = 298 K] --> Q1[Combustion strongly product-favoured]
    T2[T = 1000 K] --> Q2[Moderate product-favoured, WGS relevant]
    T3[T = 2000 K] --> Q3[Dissociation noticeable, CO rising]
    T4[T = 3000 K] --> Q4[Strong dissociation, Tad capped near 2350 K]
    Q1 --> R[K large, ~ 10^40 typical for oxidation]
    Q2 --> S[K moderate, 1 to 10]
    Q3 --> T[K small but not negligible, 10^-2 to 1]
    Q4 --> U[K very small, 10^-5 to 10^-2]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As temperature climbs, the equilibrium constant for net oxidation shrinks dramatically, demonstrating why even "stoichiometric" flames contain measurable CO and H₂ at very high $T$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the equilibrium constant $K_p$ for a gaseous reaction. Why is it called a "constant" even though its value changes with temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$K_p$ is defined for a gaseous reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ as

$$K_p \;=\; \frac{(p_C)^{c}(p_D)^{d}}{(p_A)^{a}(p_B)^{b}}$$

with all partial pressures expressed in atmospheres (so that $K_p$ is dimensionless). It is called a "constant" because, **at a *fixed* temperature**, it depends *only* on the *standard Gibbs free energy change* of the reaction:

$$K_p \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}\right)$$

which is a single-valued function of $T$ alone (for an ideal system at a specified standard-state pressure). However, when $T$ changes, $\Delta G^{\circ}$ changes, and so does $K_p$ — governed by the van 't Hoff equation:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\ln K_p}{\mathrm{d}T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$

Thus, "constant" refers to *isothermal* constancy, not temperature-independence. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for $\Delta G^{\circ}$ link, 1 mark for explicit temperature-dependence clarification.)

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
**For the reaction $\mathrm{N_2O_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO_2(g)}$ at 298 K, the equilibrium constant $K_p = 0.148$. Calculate $K_c$ at the same temperature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The reaction has $\Delta n = 2 - 1 = +1$. Using the relation $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$:

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{K_p}{(RT)^{\Delta n}} \;=\; \frac{0.148}{(0.08206\;\mathrm{L\,atm\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-1}}\times 298\;\mathrm{K})^{1}}$$

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{0.148}{24.45} \;=\; 6.05\times 10^{-3}\;\mathrm{L\,mol^{-1}}$$

(1 mark for $\Delta n$, 1 mark for relation, 1 mark for numerical evaluation.)

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the van 't Hoff equation* $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\ln K}{\mathrm{d}T} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$ *starting from the definition of Gibbs free energy. State clearly the assumptions made.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *For the dissociation* $\mathrm{CO_2(g) \rightleftharpoons CO(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g)}$ *with* $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298} = +283.0\;\mathrm{kJ/mol}$ *and* $K_{p,298} = 1.0\times 10^{-45}$*, estimate* $K_p$ *at 2000 K, assuming* $\Delta H^{\circ}$ *is temperature-independent. Comment on the consequence for the adiabatic flame temperature of methane.*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the van 't Hoff equation** (7 marks)

1. Start from the Gibbs free energy identity: $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$ — **(1 mark)**.
2. Differentiate with respect to $T$ at constant $P$ — **(1 mark)**:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} = \frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta H^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} - \Delta S^{\circ} - T\,\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta S^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T}$$

3. Apply Kirchhoff relations $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\Delta H^{\circ}}{\mathrm{d}T} = \Delta C_p$ and $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\Delta S^{\circ}}{\mathrm{d}T} = \dfrac{\Delta C_p}{T}$ — **(1 mark)**. After substitution:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} = -\Delta S^{\circ}$$

4. Rearrange using $\Delta S^{\circ} = (\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ})/T$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G^{\circ})}{\mathrm{d}T} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$ — **(1 mark)**

5. Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT\ln K$ and divide by $-R$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\ln K}{\mathrm{d}T} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$ — **(2 marks)**

6. Assumptions: ideal-gas behaviour, $K$ expressed in activities/standard states, $\Delta H^{\circ}$ treated as constant over the integration range (otherwise use the more accurate NASA polynomial form) — **(1 mark)**.

---

**(b) Numerical application** (7 marks)

1. Integrated van 't Hoff: $\ln(K_2/K_1) = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ — **(1 mark)**.
2. Insert $\Delta H^{\circ}/R = 283\,000/8.314 = 34\,034\;\mathrm{K}$ — **(1 mark)**.
3. Temperature bracket: $1/298 - 1/2000 = 3.356\times 10^{-3} - 5.0\times 10^{-4} = 2.856\times 10^{-3}\;\mathrm{K^{-1}}$ — **(1 mark)**.
4. Compute: $\ln(K_2/K_1) = 34\,034 \times 2.856\times 10^{-3} = 97.20$ — **(1 mark)**.
5. $K_2 = K_1 \cdot e^{97.20} = 1.0\times 10^{-45} \times 1.16\times 10^{42} = 1.16\times 10^{-3}$ — **(1 mark)**.
6. **Comment (1 mark):** Even though $K_p$ is *very* small, it has grown by **42 orders of magnitude** between 298 K and 2000 K. This means that at typical adiabatic flame temperatures (~2200 K for methane), a small but non-negligible fraction of CO₂ dissociates into CO + ½O₂. The endothermic dissociation absorbs thermal energy, *capping* the adiabatic flame temperature at around 2225 K — well below the ~2300 K predicted by "frozen-chemistry" calculations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** *With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the relationship among* $K_p$, $K_c$, *and* $K_x$ *for a general gaseous reaction. Apply these to compute the mole-fraction equilibrium constant* $K_x$ *for the reaction* $\mathrm{2SO_2(g) + O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3(g)}$ *at 1000 K, given that* $K_p = 3.4$ *and the total pressure is* $1.5\;\mathrm{atm}$*.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the role of the water–gas shift equilibrium in determining the CO concentration in a vitiated compartment fire. Use the van 't Hoff equation to show quantitatively how* $K_p$ *decreases from 4.0 at 800 K to a value of your calculation at 1200 K, given* $\Delta H^{\circ} = -41.2\;\mathrm{kJ/mol}$*.*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Relations among $K_p$, $K_c$, $K_x$ and numerical computation** (7 marks)

1. **Diagram (3 marks)** — refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.1, redrawn as: a central node showing the balanced equation, three branches $K_p$, $K_c$, $K_x$, and conversion arrows $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$ and $K_p = K_x(P)^{\Delta n}$.

2. For the given reaction, $\Delta n = (2) - (2+1) = -1$ — **(1 mark)**.

3. From $K_p = K_x(P)^{\Delta n}$:

$$K_x \;=\; \frac{K_p}{P^{\Delta n}} \;=\; \frac{K_p}{P^{-1}} \;=\; K_p \cdot P \;=\; 3.4 \times 1.5 \;=\; 5.1$$

**(1 mark)** for the relation, **(1 mark)** for arithmetic, **(1 mark)** for stating the dimensionless / consistent-units reasoning.

---

**(b) WGS equilibrium and CO in vitiated fires** (7 marks)

1. **Physical explanation (2 marks):** In an under-ventilated (vitiated) compartment fire, oxygen is depleted before fuel combustion completes. The post-flame gas contains CO, H₂, CO₂, H₂O, and soot. As this mixture cools from ~1200 K toward 1000 K, the WGS reaction tries to *re-equilibrate*; because the gas is fuel-rich, the equilibrium shifts to the *left*, freezing significant CO and H₂. The "frozen" CO concentration then dictates the toxic hazard.

2. **Quantitative van 't Hoff computation (4 marks):**

   Using the integrated form with $T_1 = 800\;\mathrm{K}$, $K_1 = 4.0$, $T_2 = 1200\;\mathrm{K}$, $\Delta H^{\circ} = -41\,200\;\mathrm{J/mol}$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{4.0}\right) \;=\; \frac{-41\,200}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{800} - \frac{1}{1200}\right) \;=\; (-4955.4)\!\left(4.167\times 10^{-4}\right) \;=\; -2.065$$

$$K_2 \;=\; 4.0 \times e^{-2.065} \;=\; 4.0 \times 0.1267 \;=\; 0.507$$

So $K_p$ *halves* going from 800 K to 1200 K.

3. **Interpretation (1 mark):** A smaller $K_p$ at the higher temperature (typical of post-flame vitiated gases) means the equilibrium is *less product-favoured*, i.e., more CO and H₂ remain. Combined with vitiation (fuel-rich conditions), the CO yield is markedly higher than the stoichiometric value, validating the **Purser / ISO 13571** toxic-yield model used in KTU-referenced fire-safety design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the standard-state pressure ($P^{\circ}$ = 1 atm)**: KTU strictly marks $K$ as *dimensionless*. If you write $K_p = \prod p_i^{\nu_i}$ with $p_i$ in Pa, you lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing $\Delta H^{\circ}$ units**: van 't Hoff demands $\mathrm{J/mol}$, not $\mathrm{kJ/mol}$. Wrong unit → wrong answer → zero marks for the numerical sub-part.
> 3. **Sign of $\Delta n$**: A common error is using $\Delta n = (a+b) - (c+d)$ instead of the correct *products − reactants* order. This inverts $K_p/K_c$ and $K_p/K_x$ relations.
> 4. **Treating $K$ as truly constant**: If the question asks for $K$ at a *new* temperature, the van 't Hoff equation *must* be invoked, or full marks will not be awarded.
> 5. **Ignoring $\Delta S^{\circ}$**: $\ln K = -\Delta G^{\circ}/RT = (\Delta S^{\circ}/R) - (\Delta H^{\circ}/RT)$. Students who equate "equilibrium constant" with "enthalpy change" alone lose 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** $K_p = \prod p_i^{\nu_i}$ with all $p_i$ in atm; $K$ is *dimensionless* by KTU convention.
- **Three forms:** $K_p$, $K_c$, $K_x$ — inter-convertible via $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n} = K_x(P)^{\Delta n}$.
- **$\Delta n$:** Always (sum of product stoichiometric coefficients) − (sum of reactant coefficients), for *gaseous* species only.
- **van 't Hoff (differential):** $\mathrm{d}\ln K/\mathrm{d}T = \Delta H^{\circ}/RT^{2}$.
- **van 't Hoff (integrated, constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$):** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = (\Delta H^{\circ}/R)\,(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **Link to thermodynamics:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT\ln K$ — the *root* link between kinetics-free equilibrium data and energetic spontaneity.
- **Exothermic reactions:** $K$ *decreases* as $T$ rises (Le Chatelier reproduced quantitatively).
- **Endothermic reactions:** $K$ *increases* as $T$ rises.
- **Fire-relevant equilibria:** WGS, CO₂ dissociation, H₂O dissociation — these set the *upper bound* on adiabatic flame temperature.
- **Dissociation limit:** $T_{ad,\text{equilibrium}} < T_{ad,\text{frozen}}$; for hydrocarbons in air, the gap is ~100–300 K.
- **CO toxicity link:** WGS equilibrium in *vitiated* (fuel-rich) compartment fires explains residual CO levels in post-flame gases.
- **CFD link:** NASA 7-coefficient polynomials + CHEMKIN/Cantera implement the temperature-dependent $K(T)$ in modern fire codes (FDS, FireFOAM).
- **Sign trap:** $K_p/K_c = (RT)^{\Delta n}$ — *if* $\Delta n > 0$, then $K_p > K_c$; *if* $\Delta n < 0$, then $K_p < K_c$.
- **Standard state reminder:** $P^{\circ} = 1\;\mathrm{atm}$, $T^{\circ} = 298.15\;\mathrm{K}$ — used for tabulated $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and $\Delta H^{\circ}$ values.
- **Worked typical value:** For WGS, $K_p$ goes from 4.0 at 800 K to ~0.5 at 1200 K, ~0.05 at 2000 K — a clear sign of shifting equilibrium with flame temperature.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Equilibrium constants are the *backbone* of adiabatic flame-temperature calculations, CO yield predictions, and dissociation-limited performance of combustion systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Reaction Rates
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Net Reaction Rates — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Net Reaction Rates

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of fire dynamics and combustion theory, the **Net Reaction Rate** is formally defined as the algebraic sum of the rates of all chemical reactions that produce a given species minus the rates of all reactions that consume it. For a reacting mixture containing $N_s$ species undergoing $N_R$ elementary or global reactions, the **net molar production rate** of species $i$ is expressed as:

$$\dot{\omega}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_R} \nu_{ij} \cdot q_j$$

where:
- $\dot{\omega}_i$ = net molar production rate of species $i$ (kmol/m³·s)
- $\nu_{ij}$ = net stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ in reaction $j$ (positive for products, negative for reactants)
- $q_j$ = rate of progress (or rate of reaction) of the $j^{th}$ reaction (kmol/m³·s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The net reaction rate is the **fundamental coupling term** between chemistry, thermodynamics, and fluid dynamics in any fire propagation problem. Without an accurate model for $\dot{\omega}_i$, predicting flame spread, heat release, and species concentrations becomes impossible.

In fire dynamics, due to the extreme computational cost of detailed chemical kinetics (which can involve hundreds of species and thousands of reactions for hydrocarbon fuels), engineers commonly employ a **global one-step reaction mechanism** of the form:

$$\text{Fuel} + \nu_O \cdot \text{Oxidizer} \rightarrow \nu_P \cdot \text{Products} + \text{Heat}$$

For methane, the canonical global reaction is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}, \quad \Delta H_c = -55.5 \text{ MJ/kmol}$$

For a general hydrocarbon $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conveyor Belt" of a Fire

Imagine a busy factory assembly line where **wooden planks (fuel)** enter from one side and **ash and smoke (products)** exit from the other. Workers (chemical reactions) actively transform planks into ash. The **net reaction rate** is simply **how fast the planks are being turned into ash per second per unit volume of the factory**.

Now, if you place a **thermostat** in the factory, the workers get faster as the temperature rises — this is the **Arrhenius effect**. If you pump in more oxygen (oxidizer), the workers also speed up — this is the **reaction order effect**. The combination of temperature and concentration dependencies gives the **instantaneous production rate** of every species in the fire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** A higher net reaction rate means:
> 1. 🔥 More heat is released per second (higher HRR)
> 2. 💨 More fuel and oxygen are consumed
> 3. ☠️ More toxic species (CO, soot) may be produced (especially in under-ventilated fires)
> 4. 🌬️ The flame spreads faster across the fuel surface

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| $E_a$ | Activation energy | **30,000 – 200,000** | J/mol |
| $A$ | Pre-exponential factor | $10^3$ – $10^{15}$ | varies |
| $\Delta H_c$ | Heat of combustion (CH₄) | **−55.5** | MJ/kmol |
| $\Delta H_c$ | Heat of combustion (C₈H₁₈) | **−5,474** | MJ/kmol |
| $T_{ad}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | **~2,200** | K |
| $Y_O^{\infty}$ | Ambient O₂ mass fraction | **0.233** | — |
| $Y_F^0$ | Initial fuel mass fraction | **1.0** (pure fuel) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All bolded values above are **standard textbook constants** that KTU examiners expect students to recall without derivation.

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Arrhenius Framework — The Heart of Reaction Rates

Every chemical reaction rate constant follows the **Arrhenius Law**, which captures the probabilistic nature of molecular collisions overcoming an energy barrier. The temperature dependence is given by:

$$k(T) = A \cdot T^n \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor — captures collision frequency
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = temperature exponent (often 0 for simple reactions, used to improve fit for complex ones)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) — the energy "hump" molecules must overcome
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

**Why does $k$ increase so dramatically with $T$?**
The exponential term $\exp(-E_a/RT)$ means that small temperature increases produce **enormous** rate increases. For example, doubling $T$ from 500 K to 1000 K (with $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol) increases $k$ by a factor of $e^{9.6} \approx 14,700$.

---

## 2.2 Reaction Rate of Progress ($q_j$)

For a generic irreversible reaction:

$$\sum_i \nu'_i \mathcal{M}_i \rightarrow \sum_i \nu''_i \mathcal{M}_i$$

the rate of progress is:

$$q_j = k_j \prod_{k=\text{reactants}} [C_k]^{\alpha_{kj}}$$

where $[C_k]$ denotes the molar concentration of species $k$ and $\alpha_{kj}$ is the partial reaction order. The **net stoichiometric coefficient** is:

$$\nu_{ij} = \nu''_{ij} - \nu'_{ij}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU standard):** Reactants have $\nu' > 0$, products have $\nu'' > 0$. The net coefficient $\nu_{ij}$ is **negative** for reactants (they are consumed) and **positive** for products (they are produced).

---

## 2.3 🔥 Net Reaction Rate in Fire Dynamics — Practical Form

For a one-step global combustion mechanism with fuel $F$ and oxidizer $O$:

$$F + s\, O \rightarrow (1+s)\,\text{Products}$$

the net rate of fuel consumption (mass basis) is most commonly modeled as:

$$\dot{\omega}_F'' = A \cdot \rho^n \cdot Y_F^a \cdot Y_O^b \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$$

where:
- $\dot{\omega}_F''$ = volumetric fuel consumption rate (kg/m³·s)
- $Y_F, Y_O$ = mass fractions of fuel and oxidizer
- $a, b$ = reaction orders (typically $a=1$, $b=1$ for hydrocarbons)
- $n$ = overall density exponent (often 1 or 2)
- $A, E_a$ = empirical Arrhenius parameters

---

## 2.4 Coupling with Heat Release Rate (HRR)

The **Heat Release Rate per unit volume** is directly tied to the net reaction rate:

$$\dot{Q}''' = -\dot{\omega}_F \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{\omega}_P \cdot \Delta H_c$$

where $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (J/kg). For fire spread modeling, the **mass loss rate per unit area** is often used:

$$\dot{m}'' = \rho_s \cdot v_p = \rho_s \cdot \beta \cdot Y_{O,\infty}^m$$

where $v_p$ is the pyrolysis (surface regression) rate and $\beta$ is the **burning rate parameter** (m/s).

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | (m³/mol)ⁿ/s |
| 2 | $k = A\, T^n \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Modified Arrhenius | (m³/mol)ⁿ/s |
| 3 | $\dot{\omega}_i = \sum_j \nu_{ij} q_j$ | Net species production rate | kmol/m³·s |
| 4 | $q_j = k_j \prod_k C_k^{\alpha_{kj}}$ | Rate of progress of reaction $j$ | kmol/m³·s |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}''' = -\dot{\omega}_F \Delta H_c$ | Volumetric heat release rate | W/m³ |
| 6 | $\dot{m}'' = \rho_s v_p$ | Mass loss rate per unit area | kg/m²·s |
| 7 | $v_p = \beta\, Y_{O,\infty}^m$ | Surface regression rate (de Ris) | m/s |
| 8 | $\nu_O = x + y/4$ | Stoichiometric O₂ for CₓHᵧ | mol O₂ / mol fuel |
| 9 | $\phi = (\nu_O Y_F)/(s\, Y_O)$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{chem}} = 1/k$ | Characteristic chemical time | s |
| 11 | $\text{Da} = \tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ | Damköhler number | dimensionless |
| 12 | $Y_F = Y_F^0 (1 - c)$ | Fuel mass fraction vs. progress $c$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used in the table above (e.g., $\vert\dot{\omega}_F\vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. **Do not use raw `|` inside tables!**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Net reaction rate models are the **backbone** of:
- 🔥 **Fire Safety Engineering** — Predicting HRR for performance-based design (e.g., SFPE Handbook, NFPA standards)
- 🏭 **Industrial Furnace Design** — Optimizing combustion efficiency in boilers and gas turbines
- ✈️ **Rocket Propulsion** — Modeling hybrid solid-propellant regression rates
- 🌲 **Wildfire Behavior Prediction** — Coupling chemistry with atmospheric CFD (e.g., FARSITE, WRF-SFIRE)
- 🚢 **Compartment Fire Modeling** — Zone models (CFAST) and field models (FDS) require submodels for $\dot{\omega}_i$

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Rate from a Two-Step Reaction Mechanism

**Problem Setup:** Consider a simple two-step reaction mechanism for a fire:

$$\text{Step 1 (Pyrolysis):} \quad F \xrightarrow{k_1} I + \text{Volatiles}$$

$$\text{Step 2 (Oxidation):} \quad I + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} P + \text{Heat}$$

Let $C_F$, $C_I$, $C_O$ denote molar concentrations of fuel, intermediate, and oxygen.

---

### Step 1: Write individual rates of progress

For Step 1 (first order in fuel):

$$q_1 = k_1 \cdot C_F$$

For Step 2 (first order in each):

$$q_2 = k_2 \cdot C_I \cdot C_O$$

### Step 2: Build the stoichiometric coefficient matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_{F,1} &= -1, \quad \nu_{I,1} = +1, \quad \nu_{O,1} = 0 \\
\nu_{F,2} &= 0, \quad \nu_{I,2} = -1, \quad \nu_{O,2} = -1, \quad \nu_{P,2} = +1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the net production rate formula

For **fuel (F)** — appears only as reactant in Step 1:

$$\dot{\omega}_F = \nu_{F,1}\, q_1 + \nu_{F,2}\, q_2 = (-1)(k_1 C_F) + (0)(k_2 C_I C_O)$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\omega}_F = -k_1\, C_F}$$

This makes physical sense: fuel is **consumed** (negative sign) at a rate proportional to its own concentration.

For **intermediate (I)** — produced in Step 1, consumed in Step 2:

$$\dot{\omega}_I = (+1)(k_1 C_F) + (-1)(k_2 C_I C_O)$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\omega}_I = k_1\, C_F - k_2\, C_I\, C_O}$$

For **oxygen** — consumed only in Step 2:

$$\dot{\omega}_O = (0)(k_1 C_F) + (-1)(k_2 C_I C_O)$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\omega}_O = -k_2\, C_I\, C_O}$$

For **product (P)** — produced only in Step 2:

$$\dot{\omega}_P = 0 + (+1)(k_2 C_I C_O)$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\omega}_P = k_2\, C_I\, C_O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **intermediate species $I$** is governed by a stiff ordinary differential equation: $\dot{\omega}_I = k_1 C_F - k_2 C_I C_O$. In CFD codes, this requires **implicit time integration** (e.g., BDF methods) for numerical stability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Net Rate and Heat Release

**Given:** A global one-step combustion reaction with stoichiometry $F + sO \rightarrow$ Products and heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ (J/kg of fuel).

**Find:** The volumetric heat release rate $\dot{Q}'''$ in terms of $\dot{\omega}_F$.

### Step 1: Energy conservation for the reaction

Each kilogram of fuel consumed releases $|\Delta H_c|$ joules of chemical energy. By definition:

$$\dot{Q}''' = (-\dot{\omega}_F) \cdot (-\Delta H_c)$$

The double negative arises because:
- $\dot{\omega}_F < 0$ (fuel is consumed)
- $\Delta H_c < 0$ (combustion is exothermic)

### Step 2: Simplify for a sign-convention cleanup

Define the **magnitude** of fuel consumption rate as $r_F = -\dot{\omega}_F > 0$, and the **magnitude** of heat released per kg fuel as $\Delta H_{c,\text{mag}} = -\Delta H_c > 0$. Then:

$$\dot{Q}''' = r_F \cdot \Delta H_{c,\text{mag}}$$

### Step 3: Specialize to Arrhenius kinetics

Substituting $r_F = A\, C_F\, C_O\, \exp(-E_a/RT)$:

$$\dot{Q}''' = A\, C_F\, C_O\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right) \cdot \Delta H_{c,\text{mag}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}''' = A\, \Delta H_c\, C_F\, C_O\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)}$$

This is the **canonical volumetric HRR expression** used in nearly every CFD fire simulation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Equivalence Ratio from Net Rates

The **equivalence ratio** $\phi$ quantifies whether a fire is fuel-rich, stoichiometric, or fuel-lean:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/O)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/O)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{\nu_O\, Y_F}{s\, Y_O}$$

where $s$ is the stoichiometric oxidizer-to-fuel mass ratio:

$$s = \frac{\nu_O\, M_O}{M_F}$$

### Step 1: Derive $s$ for methane (CH₄)

Stoichiometric reaction: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- $\nu_O = 2$
- $M_{\text{CH}_4} = 16$ g/mol
- $M_{\text{O}_2} = 32$ g/mol

$$s = \frac{2 \times 32}{16} = 4.0 \quad \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg CH}_4$$

### Step 2: Air-to-fuel ratio (by mass)

Since air is 23.3% O₂ by mass:

$$A/F = \frac{s}{Y_{O_2,\text{air}}} = \frac{4.0}{0.233} = 17.17 \quad \text{kg air/kg CH}_4$$

This matches the standard textbook value for methane combustion.

### Step 3: Interpret the net rate behavior

The net reaction rate **vanishes** when $\phi \to 0$ (no fuel) or $\phi \to \infty$ (no oxidizer). It peaks at $\phi = 1$ (stoichiometric). This is why **over-ventilated fires** burn leaner, and **under-ventilated fires** (e.g., post-flashover compartments) produce large amounts of **CO and soot** — incomplete combustion dominates because $\dot{\omega}_F$ is limited by $Y_O \to 0$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Reaction Progress Variable (Mixture Fraction Approach)

A powerful simplification in fire modeling is the introduction of a **reaction progress variable** $c$:

$$c = \frac{Y_{F,0} - Y_F}{Y_{F,0} - Y_F^{\text{eq}}}$$

where $Y_{F,0}$ is the initial fuel mass fraction and $Y_F^{\text{eq}}$ is the equilibrium value.

### Step 1: Solve for fuel mass fraction

$$Y_F = Y_{F,0} - c\,(Y_{F,0} - Y_F^{\text{eq}})$$

For a non-equilibrium fire, $0 \le c \le 1$, with $c=0$ (unburnt) and $c=1$ (fully burnt).

### Step 2: Express the net rate in terms of $c$

Since the chemistry occurs on a timescale much faster than the flow timescale (high Da number), one can use a **chemical equilibrium assumption** to close the combustion model:

$$\dot{\omega}_F = -\rho\, \frac{Y_{F,0} - Y_F^{\text{eq}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} \cdot c\,(1-c)$$

The factor $c(1-c)$ captures the physics: reaction is slow at $c=0$ (no fuel) and $c=1$ (no oxidizer), and fast in between.
