# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Define the term "Net Reaction Rate" as applied to combustion. Write the general expression for the net molar production rate of a species $i$ in a reacting mixture.**

**Model Answer:**

The **net reaction rate** is the algebraic sum of the rates of all elementary or global reactions that produce a given species minus the rates of all reactions that consume it. It is a key quantity in fire dynamics, linking chemistry to heat release and species transport.

For a system of $N_R$ reactions involving $N_s$ species, the net molar production rate of species $i$ is:

$$\dot{\omega}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_R} \nu_{ij} \cdot q_j$$

where:
- $\nu_{ij}$ = net stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ in reaction $j$
- $q_j$ = rate of progress of reaction $j$, given by $q_j = k_j \prod_k C_k^{\alpha_{kj}}$
- $C_k$ = molar concentration of reactant $k$
- $k_j = A_j \exp(-E_{a,j}/RT)$ is the Arrhenius rate constant.

The sign convention ensures reactants have negative $\dot{\omega}_i$ (consumed) and products have positive $\dot{\omega}_i$ (produced).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2022]**

**State and explain the Arrhenius equation. Why is the activation energy $E_a$ critical in determining the net reaction rate during fire propagation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Arrhenius equation** relates the rate constant of a chemical reaction to temperature:

$$k(T) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor (collision frequency, m³/mol·s), $E_a$ is the activation energy (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

**Importance of $E_a$ in fire propagation:**

1. **Thermal sensitivity:** The exponential dependence means that small increases in $T$ produce large increases in $k$. For typical hydrocarbon combustion ($E_a \approx 80$–$150$ kJ/mol), a 100 K rise near 800 K can multiply $k$ by 5–10×.
2. **Ignition threshold:** A fuel-air mixture ignites only when the local temperature is high enough that $k$ becomes significant relative to heat losses.
3. **Flame spread control:** The net reaction rate in the preheat zone ahead of a flame depends critically on $E_a$; fuels with high $E_a$ burn slowly, while low $E_a$ fuels (e.g., hydrogen) burn explosively.
4. **Heat feedback coupling:** Higher $E_a$ makes the fire more sensitive to external heating (radiation from the flame), governing whether a propagating flame is **fire-point controlled** or **quenching-controlled**.

---

## 5.2 📗 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has an **internal choice**. The standard split is **Part (a) = 7 marks** + **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Sub-parts may contain numerical sub-divisions.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Derive the net rate of fuel consumption for a global one-step combustion reaction. A methane-air flame at 1500 K has the following kinetic parameters: $A = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ m³/(kmol·s), $E_a = 1.5 \times 10^5$ J/mol, $Y_F = 0.05$, $Y_O = 0.20$, $\rho = 0.25$ kg/m³. Calculate the rate constant and the volumetric fuel consumption rate. Use $\Delta H_c = -55.5$ MJ/kmol for CH₄ and the ideal gas law with $M_{\text{mix}} = 28.5$ kg/kmol.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Net Rate Expression (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Consider the global one-step irreversible reaction:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2:** The net stoichiometric coefficients are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_{\text{CH}_4} &= -1 \\
\nu_{\text{O}_2} &= -2 \\
\nu_{\text{CO}_2} &= +1 \\
\nu_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} &= +2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The rate of progress (first order in fuel, first order in oxidizer):

$$q = k \cdot [\text{CH}_4] \cdot [\text{O}_2]$$

**Step 4:** Converting to mass-fraction form using $[i] = \rho Y_i / M_i$:

$$q = k \cdot \frac{\rho Y_F}{M_F} \cdot \frac{\rho Y_O}{M_O} = \frac{k\, \rho^2\, Y_F\, Y_O}{M_F\, M_O}$$

**Step 5:** The net rate of **fuel consumption** (mass basis) is:

$$\dot{\omega}_F = M_F \cdot \nu_F \cdot q = -\frac{k\, \rho^2\, Y_F\, Y_O}{M_O}$$

**Step 6:** The Arrhenius rate constant is:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Hence, the net rate expression is:

$$\boxed{\dot{\omega}_F = -\frac{A\, \rho^2\, Y_F\, Y_O}{M_O} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $T = 1500$ K, $A = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ m³/(kmol·s), $E_a = 1.5 \times 10^5$ J/mol
- $Y_F = 0.05$, $Y_O = 0.20$, $\rho = 0.25$ kg/m³
- $M_O = 32$ kg/kmol, $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K)

**Step 1: Compute the exponential term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_a}{R\,T} &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{8314 \times 1500} \\
&= \frac{150{,}000}{12{,}471{,}000} \\
&= 0.01203
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\exp(-0.01203) = 0.9880$$

**Step 2: Calculate the rate constant $k$**

$$k = (1.0 \times 10^{10}) \times 0.9880 = 9.880 \times 10^9 \quad \text{m}^3/(\text{kmol}\cdot\text{s})$$

**Step 3: Calculate the volumetric fuel consumption rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\omega}_F &= -\frac{(9.880 \times 10^9)(0.25)^2 (0.05)(0.20)}{32} \\
&= -\frac{(9.880 \times 10^9)(0.00625)(0.01)}{32} \\
&= -\frac{6.175 \times 10^5}{32} \\
&= -1.930 \times 10^4 \quad \text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Volumetric Heat Release Rate**

$$\dot{Q}''' = -\dot{\omega}_F \cdot \Delta H_c = (1.930 \times 10^4)(55.5 \times 10^6)$$

$$\dot{Q}''' = 1.071 \times 10^{12} \quad \text{W/m}^3$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{k = 9.88 \times 10^9 \;\text{m}^3/(\text{kmol}\cdot\text{s}), \quad \dot{\omega}_F = -1.93 \times 10^4 \;\text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s})}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}''' = 1.07 \times 10^{12} \;\text{W/m}^3}$$

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

**Explain the role of the equivalence ratio in determining the net reaction rate in a diffusion flame. For a propane (C₃H₈) fire, calculate the stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio by mass, and comment on the fire behavior when $\phi = 0.7$ (fuel-lean) and $\phi = 1.8$ (fuel-rich). Use $M_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_8} = 44$ kg/kmol, $M_{\text{O}_2} = 32$ kg/kmol.**

#### Part (a) — Theory of Equivalence Ratio and Diffusion Flames (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition of Equivalence Ratio**

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{\nu_O\, Y_F}{s\, Y_O}$$

where $s$ is the stoichiometric oxidizer-to-fuel mass ratio and $\nu_O$ is the stoichiometric coefficient.

**Step 2: Combustion Mechanism for Propane**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3: Net Reaction Rate in a Diffusion Flame**

In a **diffusion flame** (e.g., candle, pool fire), fuel and oxidizer are initially separated. The net reaction rate is controlled by the **mixing rate**, not the chemistry. The local net rate peaks at the **stoichiometric surface** where $Y_F$ and $Y_O$ are both non-zero.

When $\phi = 0.7$ (fuel-lean): Oxidizer is in excess, fuel is the limiting reagent. Net rate depends mainly on $Y_F$ availability.

When $\phi = 1.8$ (fuel-rich): Fuel is in excess, oxidizer is limiting. Net rate is constrained by $Y_O$. Incomplete combustion occurs, producing **CO, soot, and unburnt hydrocarbons**.

---

#### Part (b) — Stoichiometric Calculation for Propane (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify stoichiometric coefficients**

$\nu_O = 5$ mol O₂ per mol C₃H₈

**Step 2: Mass of O₂ required per kg of fuel**

$$s = \frac{\nu_O \cdot M_{O_2}}{M_F} = \frac{5 \times 32}{44} = \frac{160}{44} = 3.636 \quad \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg C}_3\text{H}_8$$

**Step 3: Mass of air required per kg of fuel**

$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{s}{Y_{O_2,\text{air}}} = \frac{3.636}{0.233} = 15.61 \quad \text{kg air / kg C}_3\text{H}_8$$

**Step 4: Comment on $\phi = 0.7$ (fuel-lean)**

$$(F/A)_{\text{actual}} = \phi \cdot (F/A)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{0.7}{15.61} = 0.0449 \quad \text{kg fuel/kg air}$$

**Behavior:** Blue, clean, non-luminous flame. Complete combustion to CO₂ and H₂O. Low soot production. Higher excess air means heat is "diluted" — flame temperature slightly below adiabatic.

**Step 5: Comment on $\phi = 1.8$ (fuel-rich)**

$$(F/A)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1.8}{15.61} = 0.1153 \quad \text{kg fuel/kg air}$$

**Behavior:** Yellow, luminous, sooty flame. **Incomplete combustion** dominates. Significant production of **CO (carbon monoxide — toxic)**, soot (black smoke), and unburnt hydrocarbons. Visible flame is large and radiative.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 15.61 \;\text{kg air / kg C}_3\text{H}_8, \quad s = 3.64 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students often forget that $\dot{\omega}_F$ is **negative** for fuel consumption. Markers deduct 1 mark for missing the sign.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistencies:** Mixing kmol and mol within the same calculation loses 2 marks. Use a **single consistent molar system** throughout.
> 3. **Arrhenius Argument Mistake:** The argument of the exponential must be **dimensionless**. If you use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), use $E_a$ in J/mol; if you use $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K), use $E_a$ in J/kmol.
> 4. **Skipping Intermediate Steps:** Showing only the final number in Part (b) without intermediate calculations forfeits 2–3 marks. Always show $\exp(-E_a/RT)$ explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing $Y_F$ and $[F]$:** Mass fraction $Y_F$ is **dimensionless**; concentration $[F]$ has units of kmol/m³. Conversion: $[F] = \rho Y_F / M_F$.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Quick-Recall Bullet List

- ⚛️ **Net Reaction Rate Definition:** $\dot{\omega}_i = \sum_j \nu_{ij}\, q_j$ — sum of (stoich. coeff × reaction rate) over all reactions.
- 🔥 **Arrhenius Law:** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ — governs **temperature dependence** of all reaction rates.
- 📈 **One-Step Global Mechanism:** $\text{Fuel} + \nu_O \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Products} + \text{Heat}$ — standard simplification in fire CFD.
- ⚖️ **Stoichiometric O₂:** For CₓHᵧ, $\nu_O = x + y/4$.
- 🎯 **Equivalence Ratio:** $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi = 1$ stoichiometric, $\phi > 1$ rich.
- 💨 **HRR Coupling:** $\dot{Q}''' = -\dot{\omega}_F \cdot \Delta H_c$ (volumetric heat release).
- 🪵 **Mass Loss Rate:** $\dot{m}'' = \rho_s\, v_p$ where $v_p = \beta\, Y_{O,\infty}^m$ (de Ris law).
- 🔄 **Reaction Progress Variable:** $c \in [0, 1]$ — tracks completeness of combustion.
- ⏱️ **Damköhler Number:** $\text{Da} = \tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ — high Da ⇒ chemistry-limited; low Da ⇒ mixing-limited.
- 🚫 **Sign Convention:** Reactants → negative $\nu_{ij}$ → negative $\dot{\omega}_i$ (consumed).
- 💧 **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) **or** $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K) — be consistent!
- 🔥 **Adiabatic Flame Temperature:** $T_{ad} \approx 2200$ K for stoichiometric hydrocarbon-air.
- 📊 **Typical $E_a$ Range:** 30,000 – 200,000 J/mol for hydrocarbon combustion.
- 🏭 **Air Composition:** 23.3% O₂ by mass (mass fraction), 21% by volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners **frequently** ask derivations of $\dot{\omega}_i$ for a given mechanism and numerical Arrhenius problems. Practice **2–3 problems** from each type, and always carry units explicitly through every step of your calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Reaction Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Global Reaction Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Global Reaction Rate** (also called the *overall reaction rate* or *macroscopic reaction rate*) in fire dynamics represents the net rate at which a fuel and oxidizer are consumed (or products are formed) in a combustion process, expressed as a single, lumped kinetic expression rather than the sum of individual elementary steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The global reaction rate is the macroscopic rate of the overall fuel-oxidation reaction expressed through a single-step Arrhenius-type kinetic formulation that combines the effects of temperature, species concentrations, and pressure into one governing rate equation used in fire growth and heat release calculations.

Mathematically, for a general fuel + oxidizer combustion:

$$\text{Fuel} + \nu_{O_2}\,O_2 \longrightarrow \text{Products} + \text{Heat}$$

the global reaction rate is written in the canonical Arrhenius-power-law form:

$$\dot{r}'' = A\,[Fuel]^{a}\,[O_2]^{b}\,[H_2O]^{c}\,[CO_2]^{d}\,\ldots \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$$

where each symbol has the standard kinetic meaning (defined rigorously in Section 2).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Single-Lane Toll Booth"**
> Imagine a multi-lane highway collapsing into a **single toll booth** before a bridge. Every car (every elementary chemical step) must squeeze through that one booth. The **Global Reaction Rate is the speed at which the booth processes cars**: it doesn't care which lane they came from, only how fast the bottleneck clears them. The booth's speed depends on:
> - **How many cars arrive together** → *reactant concentrations*
> - **The temperature outside** → *activation energy* (warmer = drivers move faster)
> - **The structural capacity of the booth** → *pre-exponential factor A*
>
> This is exactly how fire models treat combustion: the dozens of elementary radical reactions are "lumped" into a single rate expression so engineers can solve the problem.

## 1.3 Why "Global" and Not "Elementary"?

Combustion of even simple methane involves **hundreds of elementary radical reactions** (H, O, OH, CH₃, HCO, etc.). For fire engineering computations (zone models, CFD, hand calculations), tracking every radical is computationally impossible. The **global reaction** is a *lumped engineering approximation* that captures the **net observable behavior** — fuel disappearance, heat release, smoke generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction Table:**

| Feature | Elementary Reaction | Global Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| **Steps** | Single bond-breaking/forming event | Lumped net of many elementary steps |
| **Order** | 1 or 2 (molecularity) | Empirical (can be fractional) |
| **Mechanism** | Physically exact | Phenomenological approximation |
| **Use** | Detailed chemical kinetics | Fire engineering, CFD, zone models |

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Global Rate Specification

A complete global reaction specification in fire dynamics requires **three pieces of information**:

1. **Stoichiometry** — proportions of fuel, oxygen, and products.
2. **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** — energy released per kg of fuel.
3. **Rate Parameters** — $A$, $E_a$, and concentration exponents $a, b, \ldots$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:**
> Confusing *rate of reaction* (mol/m³·s) with *rate of heat release* (kW). They are related through ΔH_c but are NOT the same physical quantity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Global Rate Expression

The classical global rate is an Arrhenius-power-law expression, and each multiplicative factor carries physical meaning.

### 2.1.1 Pre-Exponential Factor $A$

- **Units**: $(m/s)^{1-n}$ where $n$ is the overall reaction order.
- **Physical Meaning**: Collision frequency, or the "try-rate" — how often reactant molecules *attempt* to react regardless of whether they have enough energy.
- **Strongly temperature-dependent** in reality, but treated as constant in the simple Arrhenius model.

### 2.1.2 Activation Energy $E_a$

- **Units**: $\text{kJ/mol}$ or $\text{J/mol}$.
- **Physical Meaning**: The minimum energy "hump" molecules must climb to rearrange into products.
- A **higher $E_a$** → reaction is more temperature-sensitive.

### 2.1.3 Concentration Exponents $a, b, c, \ldots$

- These are **empirical** — they are NOT the stoichiometric coefficients in general.
- For **methane-air** the well-known **Westbrook-Dryer (1981)** global mechanism uses $a=1.1$, $b=1.1$ — not the stoichiometric 1.0/2.0.

### 2.1.4 The Exponential Term $\exp(-E_a/RT)$

- The **Boltzmann fraction** of molecules possessing energy $\geq E_a$ at temperature $T$.
- The universal gas constant $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.

## 2.2 Common Global Reaction Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three global mechanisms you MUST know for KTU exams:**

### Mechanism 1 — One-Step Irreversible (Simple Hydrocarbons)

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \nu_{O_2}\,O_2 \longrightarrow \nu_{CO_2}\,CO_2 + \nu_{H_2O}\,H_2O$$

- **Rate**: $\dot{r}'' = A\,[C_xH_y]^{a}[O_2]^{b}\exp(-E_a/RT)$

### Mechanism 2 — Two-Step (Dryer & Haas, 1973) — accounts for CO/soot

Step 1 (Fuel → CO):

$$C_xH_y O_z + \nu_1 O_2 \longrightarrow \nu_{CO}\,CO + \nu_{H_2O}\,H_2O$$

Step 2 (CO → CO₂):

$$CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2$$

### Mechanism 3 — Westbrook-Dryer (1981) Methane Mechanism

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

- $\dot{r}_{CH_4} = 1.3\times 10^8\,[CH_4]^{0.5}[O_2]^{1.25}\exp(-E_a/RT)$ for fuel-lean
- $\dot{r}_{CH_4} = 4.4\times 10^8\,[CH_4]^{0.77}[O_2]^{-0.53}\exp(-E_a/RT)$ for fuel-rich

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and memorize the following table before any KTU exam.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol / Expression | Standard Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Global Rate (volumetric) | $\dot{r}''' = A\,[F]^a[O_2]^b\exp(-E_a/RT)$ | $\text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$ | CFD source term |
| 2 | General Global Rate (per unit area) | $\dot{r}'' = A\,[F]^a[O_2]^b\exp(-E_a/RT)$ | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | Surface burning |
| 3 | Arrhenius Constant | $A$ | varies with order | Reaction frequency |
| 4 | Activation Energy | $E_a$ | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | Temp sensitivity |
| 5 | Universal Gas Constant | $R = 8.314$ | $\text{J/(mol·K)}$ | Always used |
| 6 | Mass Consumption Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho\,A_s\,\dot{r}''$ | $\text{kg/s}$ | Fire size |
| 7 | Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\,\Delta H_c$ | $\text{kW}$ or $\text{MW}$ | Fire hazard |
| 8 | Heat Release Rate (volumetric) | $\dot{Q}''' = \dot{r}'''\,\Delta H_c$ | $\text{kW/m}^3$ | CFD fire source |
| 9 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | $\nu = \dfrac{Y_{O_2,\infty}}{\nu_{O_2}\,Y_{F,stoic}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion efficiency |
| 10 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/O)_{actual}}{(F/O)_{stoic}}$ | dimensionless | Mixture classification |
| 11 | Char Oxidation Rate (global) | $\dot{r}''_{char} = A_p\,T_s^{-1/2}\exp(-E_a/RT_s)$ | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | Solid fuels |
| 12 | Damköhler Number | $Da = \dfrac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}}$ | dimensionless | Reaction regime |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fire Growth Curves (t²-fire)**: $Q = \alpha t^2$ — built on global rate assumption.
2. **CFD Fire Simulation (FDS)**: Uses a single-step finite-rate Arrhenius reaction for the combustion source term.
3. **Compartment Zone Models (CFAST, OZone)**: Use global rate to compute upper layer temperature.
4. **Suppression Design**: Inert gas concentrations required are calculated from extinction conditions where global rate $\to 0$.
5. **Explosion Venting**: Deflagration pressure rise depends on global rate under confinement.
6. **Industrial Safety**: Used in API 521, NFPA 68/69 fire and explosion calculations.

## 2.5 Limits of Applicability

> [!WARNING]
> The global rate model **fails** under the following conditions:
> - Very low temperatures (extinction limits not captured by simple Arrhenius).
> - High-pressure, supercritical, or oxy-fuel combustion.
> - Soot/CO-rich under-ventilated fires (requires multi-step or two-step).
> - Auto-ignition chemistry dominated by low-temperature radical chains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Elementary Steps to a Global Rate

Consider a simple fuel oxidation with the following 3 elementary steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & F + M \xrightarrow{k_1} F^* + M \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & F^* + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{Int} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Int} \xrightarrow{k_3} P + \text{Heat}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F$ = fuel, $F^*$ = activated fuel, $M$ = third body, $P$ = products, $Int$ = intermediate.

### Applying the Steady-State Approximation (SSA) for $F^*$ and $Int$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d[F^*]}{dt} &= k_1[F][M] - k_2[F^*][O_2] = 0 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{d[Int]}{dt} &= k_2[F^*][O_2] - k_3[Int] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solving for the Intermediates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From (1):} \quad & [F^*] = \dfrac{k_1[F][M]}{k_2[O_2]} \\[6pt]
\text{From (2):} \quad & [Int] = \dfrac{k_2[F^*][O_2]}{k_3} = \dfrac{k_1[F][M]}{k_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Global Rate = Rate of Product Formation = Rate of Step 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{r}_{global} &= k_3[Int] \\[4pt]
&= k_3 \cdot \dfrac{k_1[F][M]}{k_3} \\[4pt]
&= k_1[F][M] \\[6pt]
&= k_1[M]\,[F]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **collapsed 1-step rate** for the chain. The temperature dependence enters through:

$$k_1 = A_1 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a,1}}{RT}\right)$$

### Final Lumped Global Form:

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{r}_{global} = A_1[M]\,[F]\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a,1}}{RT}\right)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: Even though the original mechanism had three steps, the global rate ends up first-order in fuel and first-order in third-body concentration. The intermediate species cancel out — a beautiful demonstration of why the global approach works.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Release Rate from Global Reaction Rate

Given a global rate $\dot{r}'''$ in $\text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$ of fuel, and a heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ in $\text{kJ/kg}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}''' &= \dot{r}''' \cdot \Delta H_c \quad \text{(volumetric, kW/m}^3) \\[6pt]
\dot{Q}'' &= \dot{r}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \quad \text{(per unit area, kW/m}^2) \\[6pt]
\dot{Q} &= \int_V \dot{r}'''\,\Delta H_c\,dV \quad \text{(total fire, kW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

> **Problem:** A pool fire of liquid methanol burns over a circular pan of diameter $D = 0.5\ \text{m}$. The mass loss rate per unit area is measured to be $\dot{m}'' = 0.012\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$. Methanol has a heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 19.9\ \text{MJ/kg}$. The global combustion reaction is:
> $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 1.5\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O$$
> Calculate: (i) the total mass burning rate, (ii) the total heat release rate, and (iii) the heat release rate per unit volume if the flame volume is $V_f = 0.05\ \text{m}^3$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Total mass burning rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963\ \text{m}^2 \\[6pt]
\dot{m} &= \dot{m}''\cdot A_s = 0.012 \times 0.1963 = 2.36\times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total heat release rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \dot{m}\cdot \Delta H_c \\[4pt]
&= (2.36\times 10^{-3}) \times (19.9\times 10^3) \\[4pt]
&= 46.9\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) HRR per unit volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}''' &= \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{V_f} = \dfrac{46.9}{0.05} = 938\ \text{kW/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Sensitivity — Why $E_a$ Dominates Fire Behavior

Differentiate the Arrhenius form with respect to temperature to find the temperature sensitivity coefficient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{r}'' &= A\exp(-E_a/RT) \\[6pt]
\dfrac{d\dot{r}''}{dT} &= A\exp(-E_a/RT) \cdot \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{1}{\dot{r}''}\dfrac{d\dot{r}''}{dT} &= \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Estimate at $T = 1000\ \text{K}$ with $E_a = 100\ \text{kJ/mol}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\dot{r}''}\dfrac{d\dot{r}''}{dT} &= \dfrac{100{,}000}{8.314 \times (1000)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{100{,}000}{8.314\times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= 0.012\ \text{K}^{-1} \approx 1.2\%\text{ per K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication**: A **12 % increase in temperature produces ~ 12 % increase in reaction rate**. This is why fires accelerate so dramatically — even small temperature rises from radiant feedback create runaway growth.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Damköhler Number — Global Rate vs. Flow Time

The Damköhler number compares the chemical reaction time-scale to the flow/transport time-scale:

$$
Da = \dfrac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \dfrac{L/u}{1/\dot{k}_{global}}
$$

When $Da \gg 1$ — **chemistry-limited (kinetically controlled)** flame.
When $Da \ll 1$ — **mixing-limited** flame; global rate is irrelevant, supply of fuel/oxidizer controls everything.

> [!NOTE]
> Most building-scale fires operate in the **mixing-limited regime** ($Da \ll 1$), which is why the *global rate* is often less important than the *ventilation flow rate* in compartment fire analysis. KTU examiners love this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Global Reaction Rate System

The following Mermaid block diagrams the logical interaction of the four primary inputs to the global rate and its three principal outputs in fire engineering calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL Properties] --> C[Global Reaction Rate Calculator]
    B[OXIDIZER Concentration] --> C
    D[ACTIVATION ENERGY Ea] --> C
    E[PRE-EXPONENTIAL FACTOR A] --> C
    F[SURROUNDING TEMPERATURE T] --> C

    C --> G[Mass Consumption Rate m_dot]
    C --> H[Heat Release Rate Q_dot]
    C --> I[Product Species Generation]

    G --> J[Fire Size Classification]
    H --> K[Compartment Temperature Rise]
    H --> L[Radiant Heat Flux]
    I --> M[Smoke and Toxic Gas Yield]

    K --> N[Structural Integrity]
    L --> O[Tenability Limits]
    M --> P[Evacuation Time]

    style A fill:#f9d6d6
    style B fill:#d6e4f9
    style D fill:#fff3b0
    style E fill:#fff3b0
    style F fill:#d6f9d6
    style C fill:#d6d6f9
    style H fill:#f9b3d6
    style N fill:#ffcccc
    style O fill:#ffcccc
    style P fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Global Rate Specification to Fire Hazard Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Define Stoichiometry] --> S2[Step 2 Choose Kinetic Mechanism]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Assign A and Ea]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Couple with Transport]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Solve for T and Y_i]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compute HRR and Species]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Hazard Assessment]

    style S1 fill:#e0f0ff
    style S7 fill:#ffe0e0
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When is a Global Rate Sufficient?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Fire Scenario} --> Q1[Well-ventilated diffusion flame]
    Q --> Q2[Under-ventilated compartment fire]
    Q --> Q3[Premixed deflagration]
    Q --> Q4[Oxy-fuel or supercritical]

    Q1 --> R1[Global Rate: SUFFICIENT]
    Q2 --> R2[Global Rate: PARTIALLY SUFFICIENT - 2-step recommended]
    Q3 --> R3[Global Rate: INSUFFICIENT - detailed kinetics needed]
    Q4 --> R4[Global Rate: NOT APPLICABLE]

    style R1 fill:#d6f9d6
    style R2 fill:#fff3b0
    style R3 fill:#ffd6b3
    style R4 fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams**: The first diagram is the *information-flow architecture* — four input streams flow into the global-rate calculator, producing three fire-engineering outputs which then drive three hazard endpoints. The second diagram is a *workflow*, the third is a *decision tree* showing applicability limits of the global approach.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *Global Reaction Rate* as applied in fire dynamics. Write its general Arrhenius form.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Global Reaction Rate** is the net, lumped rate at which fuel and oxidizer are consumed (or products formed) in a combustion process, expressed as a single kinetic expression combining the effects of many elementary steps. The general Arrhenius form is:
> $$\dot{r}'' = A\,[Fuel]^{a}[O_2]^{b}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$
> where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ the activation energy, $R$ the universal gas constant, $T$ the absolute temperature, and $a, b$ are empirical concentration exponents. (1 mark definition + 1 mark form + 1 mark terms explanation)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between an *elementary reaction rate* and a *global reaction rate*. Why is the global rate used in fire engineering computations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **elementary reaction rate** describes a single bond-breaking or bond-forming event, with reaction order equal to molecularity (always integer). A **global reaction rate** is a lumped expression describing the net rate of a complex multi-step process, with empirical (often non-integer) reaction orders. Global rates are used in fire engineering because real combustion involves hundreds of elementary steps; tracking them all in CFD or zone models is computationally impossible, while the global form gives the engineer the *net observable behavior* — fuel consumption, heat release, and product formation — with acceptable accuracy. (1 mark distinction + 1 mark use + 1 mark reason)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four primary input factors that determine the Global Reaction Rate of a diffusion flame, and the three principal output quantities derived from it. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A circular pool of heptane (C₇H₁₆) of diameter 1.0 m is burning in the open. The measured mass burning rate per unit area is $\dot{m}'' = 0.030\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$. The heat of combustion of heptane is $\Delta H_c = 41.2\ \text{MJ/kg}$. The global combustion reaction is:
> $$\text{C}_7\text{H}_{16} + 11\,O_2 \longrightarrow 7\,CO_2 + 8\,H_2O$$
> Compute: (i) the total mass burning rate, (ii) the total heat release rate, (iii) the stoichiometric air required per second, and (iv) comment on whether the fire is fuel-controlled or ventilation-controlled. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The four inputs are: **(1)** Fuel properties (composition, mass fraction), **(2)** Oxidizer concentration (typically 21 % O₂ by volume in air), **(3)** Activation energy $E_a$, **(4)** Pre-exponential factor $A$, with **surrounding temperature $T$** acting as the universal modifier. These combine in the Arrhenius expression $\dot{r}'' = A[F]^a[O_2]^b\exp(-E_a/RT)$. The three outputs are: **(1)** mass consumption rate, **(2)** heat release rate $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\cdot\Delta H_c$, and **(3)** product species generation (smoke, CO, CO₂, H₂O). These outputs feed into fire size classification, compartment temperature rise, and tenability analysis. *(2 marks diagram + 3 marks input/output explanation + 2 marks engineering linkage)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total mass burning rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.7854\ \text{m}^2 \\[6pt]
\dot{m} &= \dot{m}''\cdot A_s = 0.030 \times 0.7854 = 0.02356\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total heat release rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \dot{m}\cdot \Delta H_c = 0.02356 \times 41{,}200 = 970.7\ \text{kW} \approx 971\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Stoichiometric air required:**

From stoichiometry, 1 mole heptane (100 g) requires 11 moles O₂. The mass of O₂ required per kg of heptane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass O}_2\text{ per kg fuel} &= \dfrac{11\times 32}{0.100} = 3520\ \text{g O}_2\text{/kg fuel} \\[4pt]
\text{Mass air per kg fuel} &= \dfrac{3520}{0.232} = 15{,}172\ \text{g air/kg fuel} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{air,stoic} &= 15.17 \times 0.02356 = 0.3574\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Fuel vs. Ventilation Controlled:**

Since the fire burns in the open, it has **unlimited oxidizer access**; the rate is governed purely by the fuel's surface vaporization and the global rate. Therefore, the fire is **fuel-controlled (burning rate governed by the liquid heptane mass loss rate)**. *(1 mark setup + 1 mark total mass rate + 1 mark HRR + 1.5 marks stoich air + 1 mark conclusion + 1.5 marks discussion)*

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** Derive the relationship between the volumetric global reaction rate $\dot{r}'''$ and the volumetric heat release rate $\dot{Q}'''$. Explain each variable. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A methane-air diffusion flame is modeled with the Westbrook-Dryer (1981) global mechanism. The fuel-lean form is:
> $$\dot{r}_{CH_4} = 1.3\times 10^8\,[CH_4]^{0.5}[O_2]^{1.25}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$
> where $E_a = 202.6\ \text{kJ/mol}$, and the gas temperature is $T = 1500\ \text{K}$. If $[CH_4] = 0.05$ and $[O_2] = 0.20$ (mole fractions), compute: (i) the Arrhenius factor, (ii) the global rate coefficient, and (iii) the volumetric heat release rate in kW/m³ given $\Delta H_c = 55.5$ MJ/kg. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The volumetric global reaction rate $\dot{r}'''$ gives the mass of fuel consumed per unit volume per unit time, with units $\text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$. To convert this to a heat release rate, multiply by the heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ (energy released per kg of fuel consumed, in kJ/kg):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}''' &= \dot{r}'''\cdot\Delta H_c
\end{aligned}
$$

For an area-based rate, the analogous form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}'' &= \dot{r}''\cdot\Delta H_c
\end{aligned}
$$

The total heat release rate over a fire volume $V$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \int_V \dot{Q}'''\,dV = \int_V \dot{r}'''\,\Delta H_c\,dV
\end{aligned}
$$

*Variables:* $\dot{r}'''$ — fuel mass consumption rate per unit volume; $\Delta H_c$ — heat of combustion (lower or higher, depending on convention); $\dot{Q}'''$ — energy release rate per unit volume. *(2 marks derivation + 2 marks explanation of each variable + 1.5 marks integral form + 1.5 marks units/connection to fire hazard)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Arrhenius factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_a}{RT} &= \dfrac{202{,}600}{8.314 \times 1500} = \dfrac{202{,}600}{12{,}471} = 16.246 \\[6pt]
\exp(-16.246) &= 8.81\times 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Global rate coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{global} &= A\cdot[CH_4]^{0.5}\cdot[O_2]^{1.25}\cdot\exp(-E_a/RT) \\[4pt]
&= 1.3\times 10^8 \times (0.05)^{0.5} \times (0.20)^{1.25} \times 8.81\times 10^{-8} \\[4pt]
&= 1.3\times 10^8 \times 0.2236 \times 0.1337 \times 8.81\times 10^{-8} \\[4pt]
&= 1.3\times 10^8 \times 0.0299 \times 8.81\times 10^{-8} \\[4pt]
&= 1.3\times 10^8 \times 2.635\times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3425\ \text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric heat release rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}''' &= \dot{r}'''\cdot \Delta H_c = 0.3425 \times 55{,}500 = 19{,}008\ \text{kW/m}^3 \approx 19.0\ \text{MW/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark Arrhenius + 1.5 marks rate coefficient + 1.5 marks concentration product + 1 mark final rate + 1 mark HRR conversion + 1 mark unit consistency)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Global Reaction Rate questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of $A$** — the pre-exponential factor has units that depend on reaction order. KTU evaluators check this strictly.
> 2. **Confusing $E_a$ in J/mol vs. kJ/mol** — the gas constant is in J/(mol·K). Mixing units is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Using stoichiometric coefficients as reaction orders** — they are NOT the same. Methane is order 0.5 in fuel, not 1.
> 4. **Skipping the heat of combustion link** — Q-dot problems demand explicit use of $\Delta H_c$. Forgetting it loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not commenting on fuel vs. ventilation control** — KTU examiners always want you to interpret whether the fire is limited by fuel supply or air supply.
> 6. **Failing to state that the global rate is phenomenological** — examiners reward students who note the lumped/approximate nature explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Global reaction rate** = single lumped Arrhenius-power expression representing net fuel oxidation.
- General form: $\dot{r} = A\,[F]^a[O_2]^b\,\exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- **A** = pre-exponential factor (collision frequency analogue).
- **E_a** = activation energy — controls temperature sensitivity.
- **a, b** = empirical (NOT stoichiometric) concentration exponents.
- **R** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** — memorize.
- HRR link: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\cdot\Delta H_c$ (area basis) or $\dot{Q}''' = \dot{r}'''\cdot\Delta H_c$ (volumetric).
- **Westbrook-Dryer (1981)** is the standard methane global mechanism — orders 0.5 and 1.25 in fuel-lean regime.
- **Two-step (Dryer-Haas)** is needed for under-ventilated, CO-producing fires.
- **Damköhler number** decides whether global rate matters: $Da \ll 1$ → mixing-controlled, $Da \gg 1$ → chemistry-controlled.
- Global rate is **fuel-adequate** for well-ventilated diffusion flames; **inadequate** for premixed deflagrations, oxy-fuel, or supercritical combustion.
- **Lower heating value (LHV)** is used in fire engineering, not HHV.
- **Equivalence ratio** $\phi$ separates fuel-lean ($\phi < 1$) from fuel-rich ($\phi > 1$) regimes.
- Common KTU exam trap: asking for **rate constant** vs. **rate of reaction** — they differ by one power of concentration.
- In a t²-fire growth model, the **global rate implicitly drives the t² coefficient α** through heat release accumulation.
- **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)** uses a single-step finite-rate Arrhenius reaction by default — this is the most common practical application of the global rate.
- For char/solid fuels, the surface oxidation rate $\dot{r}''_{char} = A_p\,T_s^{-0.5}\exp(-E_a/RT_s)$ is the standard global form.
- **Be ready to compute**: (i) mass burning rate, (ii) HRR, (iii) stoichiometric air demand, (iv) flame height (via Heskestad), all from a single global rate specification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Premixed and Diffusion Flames
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Premixed Flames — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Premixed and Diffusion Flames

A **Premixed Flame** is a combustion regime in which the **fuel and oxidizer are homogeneously mixed upstream of the flame front**, and the combustion reaction is initiated when this homogeneous mixture is exposed to a source of ignition. The reaction zone is a thin, propagating wave — formally called a **deflagration wave** — that travels through the unburnt mixture at a characteristic velocity known as the **Laminar Burning Velocity** $S_L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristic of a Premixed Flame:**
> Mixing $\rightarrow$ Ignition $\rightarrow$ Reaction (chemistry-controlled)
> The combustion is **kinetically limited** by the chemistry, and the **mixing has already been completed** before combustion begins.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pre-Mixed Cocktail" Analogy

Imagine you are at a party where the bartender has already mixed the **fuel (alcohol) and oxidizer (mixer)** into a uniform cocktail in a glass. The moment a small spark (a matchstick) touches the surface, the *entire uniform mixture* ignites almost simultaneously and burns as a thin blue sheet across the glass. There is **no spatial segregation** between fuel and air — they are molecularly intimate. This is exactly how a **Bunsen burner** or a **gas-stove flame** works: the air-gas mixture is pre-mixed inside the burner nozzle, and combustion occurs as a thin flame sheet.

---

## 1.2 Diffusion Flames — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Diffusion Flame** is a combustion regime in which the **fuel and oxidizer are initially separated** and are brought into contact **only at the reaction zone** through the process of **molecular and turbulent diffusion**. Combustion is therefore **mixing-controlled** (transport-limited), not chemistry-controlled. The classical example is a **candle flame** or a **pool fire**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristic of a Diffusion Flame:**
> Fuel + Oxidizer $\rightarrow$ Mix (in situ via diffusion) $\rightarrow$ Reaction
> The combustion rate is governed by the rate at which the species **diffuse toward each other**, not by chemical kinetics.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Campfire Wood vs. Wick" Analogy

Think of a piece of wood burning in an open fireplace. The **wood (fuel) is solid** and the **oxygen (oxidizer) is in the air surrounding it**. They never mix beforehand. As the wood pyrolyzes, vaporized fuel molecules drift outward; simultaneously, oxygen molecules drift inward. Where these two streams meet at the right temperature, they ignite to form the **luminous yellow flame** you see. Combustion happens *only at the interface* where mixing occurs — a Diffusion Flame in its purest form.

---

## 1.3 The Master Comparison Table

| Property | Premixed Flame | Diffusion Flame |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing before combustion | **Yes (homogeneous)** | **No (heterogeneous)** |
| Controlling process | Chemical kinetics | Molecular/turbulent diffusion |
| Typical color | Pale blue | Yellow/luminous (sooty) |
| Soot formation | Negligible | Significant |
| Flame temperature | Approaches **adiabatic** $T_{ad}$ | Lower (heat loss to fuel) |
| Example | Bunsen burner, gas stove | Candle, pool fire, jet flame |
| Standard flame | Flat flame, Bunsen flame | Burke–Schumann jet flame |
| Burning velocity | Defined ($S_L$) | Not strictly defined |
| Flammability limits | Governed by LFL / UFL | Always within flammable envelope |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Mixed/Partially Premixed Flames:**
> In practice, many real flames (e.g., a Bunsen flame with insufficient primary air) fall between the two extremes. These are called **partially premixed flames**, where some mixing has occurred upstream but additional mixing happens at the reaction zone.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Adiabatic Flame Temperature ($T_{ad}$)** — Stoichiometric methane/air: **~2222 K**
- **Laminar Burning Velocity ($S_L$)** for methane/air: **0.4 m/s**
- **Laminar Burning Velocity ($S_L$)** for propane/air: **0.46 m/s**
- **Flammability Limits (Methane):** LFL = **5%**, UFL = **15%** (by volume in air)
- **Quenching Distance (Methane):** ~ **2.0 mm**
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Methane):** **17.2 : 1** by mass
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Fire Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Significance:**
> Premixed and diffusion flame theory is the **cornerstone of Module 3** because it underpins:
> 1. Fire growth modeling (T-squared fires, flame spread).
> 2. Explosion venting design (deflagration pressure rise).
> 3. Detection and suppression (optical smoke sensors, flame detectors).
> 4. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) — Eddy-Break-Up, Flamelet models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of a Laminar Premixed Flame

A 1-D, adiabatic, planar premixed flame has three distinct zones, as illustrated by the **Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring (ZND)** structure:

### 2.1.1 Zone I — Pre-Heat Zone (Induction Zone)
- Cold unburnt mixture is heated by conduction from the reaction zone.
- Temperature rises from $T_u$ (unburnt) to $T_i$ (ignition temperature).
- **No significant chemical reaction** occurs.
- Dominant transport mechanism: **thermal conduction**.

### 2.1.2 Zone II — Reaction Zone
- Rapid oxidation; temperature surges from $T_i$ to $T_{ad}$.
- **Thinnest** of the three zones (~ 0.1–1 mm for hydrocarbons).
- Radical pool (H, O, OH) peaks here.
- Heat release rate $\dot{q}''$ is maximum.

### 2.1.3 Zone III — Post-Flame (Recombination) Zone
- Slow radical recombination; gradual cooling by radiation.
- Equilibrium products: $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$.
- Temperature slowly approaches final burned-gas temperature.

### 2.1.4 Why and How the Flame Propagates
- The reaction zone **transports heat backward** (into the unburnt gas) by conduction.
- This heat raises the unburnt gas to its auto-ignition temperature.
- The reaction then proceeds in this newly heated layer, propagating the wave.
- The "speed" at which this wave moves relative to the unburnt gas is the **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$.

---

## 2.2 The Premixed Flame Equations — Conservation Laws

For a 1-D, steady, adiabatic premixed flame attached to a coordinate system moving with the flame, the governing equations are:

### 2.2.1 Continuity Equation
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}'' = \rho_u S_L = \rho_b (S_L + V_{products})
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2.2 Species Continuity (for fuel mass fraction $Y_F$)
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho u \frac{dY_F}{dx} = -\frac{d}{dx}\left(\rho D \frac{dY_F}{dx}\right) + \dot{\omega}_F
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2.3 Energy Equation
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho u c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} h_i \dot{\omega}_i
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2.4 The "Why" Behind These Equations
- **Continuity** ensures mass in = mass out (the flame is steady in its own reference frame).
- **Species continuity** tracks the fuel being consumed by chemistry ($\dot{\omega}_F$) and transported by diffusion ($D$).
- **Energy** balances heat conduction ($k\,dT/dx$) against the enthalpy released by the reaction ($\sum h_i \dot{\omega}_i$).

---

## 2.3 Laminar Burning Velocity ($S_L$) — The Most Important Parameter

The **Laminar Burning Velocity** is the speed at which a 1-D planar flame propagates into a quiescent, homogeneous, unburnt mixture. It is a **property of the mixture**, not of the geometry.

### 2.3.1 Empirical Correlation — Metghalchi–Keck
$$\begin{aligned}
S_L = S_{L,ref} \left(\frac{T_u}{T_{ref}}\right)^2 \left(\frac{p}{p_{ref}}\right)^{-0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.3.2 The Markstein Length ($\ell_M$)
- Characterizes flame stretch sensitivity.
- $\ell_M > 0$ (most hydrocarbons): mixture is **stable** to stretch.
- $\ell_M < 0$ (e.g., $\text{H}_2$–air): mixture is **unstable** (cellular instabilities).

---

## 2.4 Flammability Limits — LFL, UFL, and Limits of Flammability

A premixed flame can **only** propagate if the fuel–air ratio lies between the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** and the **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)**.

- **LFL (Lean Limit):** Insufficient fuel; flame extinguishes due to excessive heat loss.
- **UFL (Rich Limit):** Insufficient oxygen; flame extinguishes because the heat release cannot sustain propagation.
- The limits are governed by the **adiabatic flame temperature criterion** — empirically, $T_{ad} \geq 1600$ K is required for sustained propagation (this is the **limiting flame temperature theory** of Zeldovich).

### 2.4.1 Le Chatelier's Rule (for multi-component fuels)
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LFL}_{mix} = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.5 Diffusion Flame Theory — The Burke–Schumann Model

The **Burke–Schumann (1928)** model is the **canonical analytical solution** for a laminar jet diffusion flame. It treats combustion as an infinitely fast, single-step reaction:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel} + \nu \cdot \text{Oxidizer} \rightarrow \text{Products}
\end{aligned}$$

The model assumes:
- Axisymmetric cylindrical geometry.
- Steady, laminar, incompressible flow.
- Equal diffusivities of all species ($D_F = D_O = D_{Th} = \alpha$).
- Chemical reaction is **infinitely fast** (stoichiometric surface).

### 2.5.1 The Mixture Fraction ($\xi$)
The **mixture fraction** is the central conserved scalar of non-premixed combustion:
$$\begin{aligned}
\xi = \frac{s - s_{ox}}{s_{fuel} - s_{ox}}
\end{aligned}$$
where $s$ is a passive scalar proportional to the mass fraction of elements from the fuel stream.

- $\xi = 0$: pure oxidizer stream.
- $\xi = 1$: pure fuel stream.
- $\xi = \xi_{st}$: the **stoichiometric surface** (flame location).

### 2.5.2 The Governing Equation for $\xi$
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho \frac{D\xi}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla \xi)
\end{aligned}$$

Under Burke–Schumann assumptions, the stream function $\psi$ satisfies:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{1}{\rho u r} \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{1}{\rho u r} \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.6 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for KTU 2024 exam preparation on this topic. Memorize all entries with units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass burning rate (premixed) | $\dot{m}'' = \rho_u S_L$ | $\rho_u$: kg/m³, $S_L$: m/s |
| 2 | Adiabatic flame temperature | $T_{ad}$ from energy balance $\sum n_i h_i = 0$ | K |
| 3 | Laminar burning velocity (Metghalchi–Keck) | $S_L = S_{L,0}(T_u/T_0)^2(p/p_0)^{-0.5}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Lewis number | $\text{Le} = \alpha / D = k/(\rho c_p D)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Quenching distance (theory) | $d_q = \text{const} \cdot \alpha/S_L$ | m |
| 6 | Flame thickness | $\delta_L = (T_{ad}-T_u)/(\max \vert dT/dx \vert)$ | m |
| 7 | Mixture fraction | $\xi = (s - s_{ox})/(s_{fuel} - s_{ox})$ | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | Stoichiometric mixture fraction | $\xi_{st} = (Y_{O,\infty}/s Y_{F,\infty}) / [1 + (Y_{O,\infty}/s Y_{F,\infty})]$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Burke–Schumann flame radius | $r_{flame} = r_0 \sqrt{\ln(1/\xi_{st})/\ln(1/\xi_{cl})}$ | m |
| 10 | Soot volume fraction (yellow tip) | $f_v = C \cdot \xi \cdot (1-\xi)$ empirical | — |
| 11 | Le Chatelier's rule | $\text{LFL}_{mix}^{-1} = \sum y_i/\text{LFL}_i$ | volume % |
| 12 | Limiting flame temperature | $T_{ad,lim} \approx 1600$ K (Zeldovich) | K |
| 13 | Mass diffusivity of fuel (air) | $D \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ (CH₄, 300 K) | m²/s |
| 14 | Characteristic Damköhler number | $\text{Da} = \tau_{flow}/\tau_{chem}$ | — |
| 15 | Karlovitz number (turbulence) | $\text{Ka} = \tau_{chem}/\tau_{eddy}$ | — |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Why This Theory Matters |
|---|---|
| **Fire detection sensors** | Yellow luminous diffusion flame produces unique spectral signature (Na-D 589 nm, soot continuum) |
| **Flame arrestors** | Designed using quenching distance $d_q$ of the most easily ignited fuel in the process stream |
| **Gas turbine combustors** | Lean premixed prevaporized (LPP) combustors exploit $S_L$ to ensure flame stability without flashback |
| **Pool fire modeling** | Burke–Schumann-based correlations predict flame height, $L/D$ |
| **Explosion venting** | Deflagration $S_L$ drives pressure rise rate $(dp/dt)$ used in NFPA 68 design |
| **CFD Fire Modeling (FDS)** | Mixture fraction and $S_L$ are the central state variables in FDS reaction sub-models |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Laminar Burning Velocity from Conservation Laws (1-D Steady Flame)

We consider a 1-D, steady, planar premixed flame in its own moving reference frame.

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**
Let $x$ be the coordinate perpendicular to the flame. The unburnt gas moves in the $+x$ direction with velocity $S_L$, and the burned gas moves in the $+x$ direction with velocity $S_L + V_{products}$.

**Step 2 — Continuity equation (steady-state).**
Mass flow rate per unit area is conserved:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}'' = \rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b
\end{aligned}$$
where the subscript $b$ denotes burned-gas conditions. This gives the relation between $S_L$ and $S_b$ (the burned gas velocity in the lab frame):
$$\begin{aligned}
S_b = \frac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} S_L = \frac{T_{ad}}{T_u} \cdot S_L \quad \text{(using ideal gas and constant pressure)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Energy equation (integral form).**
Integrate the energy equation from $x = -\infty$ (unburnt) to $x = +\infty$ (burned):
$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \rho u c_p \frac{dT}{dx} dx = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx
\end{aligned}$$
The right-hand side vanishes because $dT/dx = 0$ at both limits. This yields the trivial integral unless we include the **heat release term**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \rho u c_p \frac{dT}{dx} dx = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \sum_i h_i \dot{\omega}_i \, dx = \dot{Q}''\text{(total)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Define the heat release parameter.**
For an adiabatic flame, all reaction enthalpy becomes sensible enthalpy in the products:
$$\begin{aligned}
c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\rho u} \sum_i h_i \dot{\omega}_i \, dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply to the Rankine–Hugoniot jump (post-flame analysis).**
The post-flame burned gas has constant temperature $T_{ad}$. A global energy balance across the entire flame gives:
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \sum_i h_i \dot{\omega}_i \, dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $S_L$.**
This implicit equation defines $S_L$ in terms of chemistry ($\dot{\omega}_i$), thermodynamics ($T_{ad}$), and transport ($S_L$ appears in $\rho u = \rho_u S_L$). For a first-order Arrhenius chemistry with activation energy $E_a$ and a large activation energy asymptotic analysis (Zeldovich), the analytical result is the **Zeldovich formula**:
$$\begin{aligned}
S_L \sim \sqrt{\frac{2 k B \lambda \, \exp(-E_a/R_u T_{ad})}{\rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)^2} \cdot \frac{R_u T_{ad}^2}{E_a}}
\end{aligned}$$
where $B$ is the pre-exponential factor and $\lambda$ the thermal conductivity. This is the **fundamental link** between chemistry ($E_a, B$), transport ($\lambda$), and flame propagation ($S_L$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Burke–Schumann Solution for a Laminar Jet Diffusion Flame

We derive the famous **flame surface location** for a round laminar jet of fuel issuing into stagnant air.

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
A circular fuel jet of radius $r_0$ issues with uniform velocity $w_0$ into still air. The jet axis is the $z$-axis. We use a stream-function formulation to satisfy continuity identically.

**Step 2 — Stream function definition.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho u r = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} \quad , \quad \rho w r = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — The mixture fraction equation (incompressible, equal diffusivities).**
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho u \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial r} + \rho w \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial z} = \rho D \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial z^2}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the boundary layer approximation.**
For large Reynolds number, the streamwise diffusion term $\partial^2 \xi / \partial z^2$ is negligible compared to radial diffusion:
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho u \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial r} + \rho w \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial z} = \frac{\rho D}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial r}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Transform to similarity variable $\eta$.**
Define $\eta$ such that the governing ODE becomes ordinary. With $z$ as the axial coordinate:
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta = \sqrt{\frac{w_0}{4 D z}} \, r \quad , \quad \xi = f(\eta)
\end{aligned}$$
The conserved scalar equation reduces to:
$$\begin{aligned}
f'' + \eta \, \left(\frac{w}{w_0}\right) f' \cdot \frac{2}{?} &= 0 \quad \text{(simplified form)}
\end{aligned}$$

The analytical solution (Schvab–Zeldovich) for the mixture fraction is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\xi(\eta) = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{\eta}{2}\right)\right] \cdot (\text{profile})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — The exact solution (Burke–Schumann).**
For a round jet with uniform exit profile, the mixture fraction is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\xi(r,z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{r}{2\sqrt{D z/w_0}}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Locate the flame surface ($\xi = \xi_{st}$).**
Setting $\xi = \xi_{st}$ in the above expression and solving for $r$ yields the **flame radius** as a function of height $z$:
$$\begin{aligned}
r_{flame}(z) = 2\sqrt{\frac{D z}{w_0}} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}\left(1 - 2\xi_{st}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

For stoichiometric methane ($Y_F = 1, s = 4$, $Y_{O,\infty}=0.232$):
$$\begin{aligned}
\xi_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + (s\, Y_{O,\infty}/Y_{F,\infty})} = \frac{1}{1 + 4 \cdot 0.232 / 1.0} = 0.055
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Flame height (L/D of jet).**
Setting $r = r_0$ (nozzle exit) and solving for $z$:
$$\begin{aligned}
z_{flame} = \frac{w_0 \, r_0^2}{4 D} \cdot \left[\text{erf}^{-1}(1 - 2\xi_{st})\right]^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the classical **Burke–Schumann flame height** for a round laminar jet. It predicts $H \propto r_0^2$ for fixed $\xi_{st}$ and mixture.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Quenching Distance from Critical Péclet Number

**Step 1 — Define quenching.**
A flame is "quenched" when conductive heat losses to the wall exceed the rate of heat generation by chemistry.

**Step 2 — Energy balance at a parallel-plate channel of gap $d$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} = S_L \cdot A \cdot \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)
\end{aligned}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{loss} = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{(T_{ad} - T_w)}{d/2} \cdot 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Critical condition.** At quenching, $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
S_L \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \frac{4 k (T_{ad} - T_w)}{d_q}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for quenching gap $d_q$.**
Using $T_w \approx T_u$ (cold wall approximation):
$$\begin{aligned}
d_q = \frac{4 k (T_{ad} - T_u)}{S_L \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)} = \frac{4 \alpha}{S_L}
\end{aligned}$$
where $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ is the thermal diffusivity. The factor of 4 is empirical; the simpler form is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{d_q = \frac{C \cdot \alpha}{S_L} \quad , \quad C \approx 10 \text{ (empirical)}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **methane (low $S_L$, high $\alpha$) has a large quenching distance (~2 mm)** — important for flashback safety in burner design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Assumption | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1-D Premixed flame | $\rho u dY_F/dx = \dot{\omega}_F$ | Planar, adiabatic | $S_L, T_{ad}$ |
| Bunsen flame (conical) | $S_L = u_0 \sin(\alpha/2)$ | Conical geometry | Cone half-angle $\alpha$ |
| Flat flame (plug flow) | $S_L = u_0$ | 1-D burner-stabilized | Profile $T(x), Y_i(x)$ |
| Burke–Schumann jet | $\xi(r,z)$ with $D_F = D_O$ | Equal diffusivities, infinite chemistry | Flame surface $r_{flame}(z)$ |
| Pool fire (de Ris) | $\xi$ from convection + conduction | Low Pe, Opposed flow | Flame height, heat flux |
| Turbulent premixed (Damköhler) | $\bar{S}_T = S_L \cdot f(\text{Da}, u'/S_L)$ | Eddy Break-Up | $S_T$ |
| Flamelet (Peters) | $\xi, \chi$ (scalar dissipation) | Thin reaction zone | $T, Y_i(\xi, \chi)$ |
