# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between premixed and diffusion flames. Give two practical examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key format):**

A **premixed flame** is one in which the fuel and oxidizer are **homogeneously mixed** upstream of the flame front, and the combustion rate is governed by **chemical kinetics**. The flame appears as a thin, non-luminous blue sheet. *Examples:* Bunsen burner flame, gas-stove flame, spark-ignition engine combustion.

A **diffusion flame** is one in which the fuel and oxidizer are **separately supplied** and mix only at the reaction zone via molecular and turbulent diffusion. Combustion is **transport-controlled**, the flame is generally luminous yellow due to soot, and is generally larger. *Examples:* Candle flame, wood-fire flame, liquid-pool fire, gas-turbine diffusion combustor.

| Criterion | Premixed | Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing | Pre-mixed | In-situ |
| Rate control | Kinetics | Diffusion |
| Color | Blue (non-luminous) | Yellow (luminous) |
| Examples | Bunsen, gas-stove | Candle, pool fire |

> Each example carries ½ mark; the differentiation table carries the remaining ½ mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
**Define "Laminar Burning Velocity" ($S_L$). State the factors on which $S_L$ depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Laminar Burning Velocity ($S_L$)** is defined as the velocity at which a 1-D, planar, adiabatic flame front propagates **relative to the unburnt gas** ahead of it, into a homogeneous, quiescent, premixed fuel–oxidizer mixture. It is a **thermokinetic property of the mixture**, not of the apparatus.

**Factors affecting $S_L$:**
1. **Equivalence ratio ($\phi$):** Maximum at slightly rich ($\phi \approx 1.05$–1.10) for hydrocarbons.
2. **Unburnt gas temperature ($T_u$):** $S_L \propto T_u^n$, where $n \approx 1.5$–2.0.
3. **Pressure ($p$):** $S_L \propto p^{-n}$, where $n \approx 0$ for methane, $n \approx 0.5$ for higher hydrocarbons.
4. **Fuel type:** Higher activation energy $\Rightarrow$ lower $S_L$ (e.g., $\text{CH}_4$ ~0.4 m/s; $\text{H}_2$ ~2.5 m/s).
5. **Diluent concentration** (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, steam): reduces $T_{ad}$ and $S_L$.
6. **Lewis number (Le):** Le $> 1 \Rightarrow$ preferential diffusion stabilizes flame; Le $< 1$ causes cellular instability.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and **MUST have an internal choice** between two completely independent sub-options. The structure is: Part (a) = **7 marks**, Part (b) = **7 marks**. Students answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Sample]**

#### **Q.A (a) [7 marks]**
**Derive the Burke–Schumann analytical solution for a laminar round jet diffusion flame. State clearly the assumptions. Use the result to determine the flame radius as a function of axial distance $z$ for a methane jet with $\xi_{st} = 0.055$, $D = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $w_0 = 1.5$ m/s, and nozzle radius $r_0 = 5$ mm. Also compute the flame height.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions of the Burke–Schumann model** *(2 marks)*:
1. Axisymmetric, steady, laminar, incompressible flow.
2. **Equal diffusivities** for all species: $D_F = D_O = D_{Th} = D$ (this is the key simplification).
3. **Infinitely fast, single-step chemistry** $\Rightarrow$ reaction occurs on a stoichiometric surface where $\xi = \xi_{st}$.
4. Negligible buoyancy (forced-convection dominant).
5. Boundary layer (parabolic) approximation.

**Step 2 — Governing equation** *(1 mark)*:
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho u \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial r} + \rho w \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial z} = \rho D \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial z^2}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Stream function + similarity transform** *(2 marks)*:
Using $\psi$ and assuming a Schvab–Zeldovich transformation, the equation reduces to a self-similar form. With $\eta = (r/2)\sqrt{w_0/(Dz)}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\xi(r,z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{r}{2\sqrt{Dz/w_0}}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Flame radius derivation** *(1 mark)*:
Set $\xi = \xi_{st}$ and solve for $r$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\xi_{st} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{r_f}{2\sqrt{Dz/w_0}}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
r_f(z) = 2\sqrt{\frac{Dz}{w_0}} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(1 - 2\xi_{st})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical computation** *(1 mark)*:
- $\xi_{st} = 0.055 \Rightarrow 1 - 2\xi_{st} = 0.890$
- $\text{erf}^{-1}(0.890) = 1.097$
- $r_f(z) = 2 \cdot \sqrt{(2.0 \times 10^{-5} \cdot z)/1.5} \cdot 1.097$
- $r_f(z) = 1.007 \times 10^{-2} \sqrt{z} \;\; (\text{with } z \text{ in m, } r_f \text{ in m})$

**Step 6 — Flame height** *(1 mark)*:
At the flame tip, $r_f = r_0 = 0.005$ m. Solve for $z$:
$$\begin{aligned}
z_f = \left(\frac{r_0}{1.007 \times 10^{-2}}\right)^2 = (0.497)^2 \approx 0.247 \text{ m} = 24.7 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

#### **Q.A (b) [7 marks]**
**Explain the three structural zones of a 1-D laminar premixed flame (ZND structure). Sketch the temperature and major species (fuel, oxygen, products) profiles. Discuss how the heat-release profile relates to the structure.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pre-heat Zone** *(2 marks)*:
- Located upstream of the reaction zone.
- Cold unburnt gas ($T_u$, $\rho_u$) is heated by **conduction from the reaction zone**.
- $T$ rises from $T_u$ to $T_i$ (ignition temperature, typically $0.5\,T_{ad}$).
- **Fuel and $\text{O}_2$ remain essentially constant** (no significant reaction).
- Width $\delta_P \sim \alpha / S_L$ where $\alpha$ is thermal diffusivity.

**Step 2 — Reaction Zone** *(2 marks)*:
- Narrowest of the three zones ($\delta_R \sim 0.1$–$1$ mm for hydrocarbons).
- Heat release rate $\dot{q}''$ is **maximum** here (Gaussian-like profile).
- $T$ surges from $T_i$ to $T_{ad}$ (adiabatic flame temperature).
- Fuel and $\text{O}_2$ are rapidly depleted; products ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) form.
- Radical pool (H, O, OH) peaks within this zone.

**Step 3 — Post-Flame (Recombination) Zone** *(2 marks)*:
- Burnt products at $T_{ad}$ slowly cool via **radiative loss** and minor radical recombination.
- Equilibrium chemistry — major species approach their final equilibrium values.
- The temperature profile asymptotically reaches $T_{ad}$.

**Step 4 — Profile sketch** *(1 mark)*:
- $T$ profile: shallow rise in pre-heat, steep rise in reaction, plateau in post-flame.
- Fuel: 1 in unburnt, $\sim$constant in pre-heat, drops sharply in reaction zone, 0 in post-flame.
- $\text{O}_2$: similar to fuel (depends on stoichiometry).
- Products ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$): 0 in unburnt, rise sharply in reaction zone.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Sample]**

#### **Q.B (a) [7 marks]**
**Starting from first principles, derive an expression for the quenching distance $d_q$ of a premixed flame in a parallel-plate channel. Use the result to compute $d_q$ for methane/air ($\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $S_L = 0.4$ m/s) and propane/air ($\alpha = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $S_L = 0.46$ m/s). Which fuel is more hazardous from a flashback standpoint and why?**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition and physical setup** *(1 mark)*:
Quenching distance $d_q$ is the minimum gap between two parallel plates for which a flame can still propagate. Below $d_q$, conductive heat losses to the cold walls dominate over chemical heat release and the flame extinguishes.

**Step 2 — Heat generation rate** *(1 mark)*:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{m}'' c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) A = \rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) A
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss to the walls** *(1 mark)*:
For two plates separated by $d$, with wall temperature $\approx T_u$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{loss} = 2 \cdot k A \cdot \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{d/2} = \frac{4 k A (T_{ad} - T_u)}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Critical condition $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss}$** *(1 mark)*:
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_u S_L c_p d_q = 4 k \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_q = \frac{4 k}{\rho_u c_p S_L} = \frac{4 \alpha}{S_L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Empirical correction factor** *(1 mark)*:
Experimental data yields $d_q \approx 10\,\alpha/S_L$ (factor ~2.5 larger). So:
$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{d_q \approx \frac{10\,\alpha}{S_L}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical calculations** *(1 mark)*:
- **Methane:** $d_q = 10 \cdot 2.2 \times 10^{-5} / 0.4 = 5.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m = **0.55 mm** (idealized); experimental ~2.0 mm.
- **Propane:** $d_q = 10 \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-5} / 0.46 = 3.9 \times 10^{-4}$ m = **0.39 mm** (idealized); experimental ~1.5 mm.

**Step 7 — Hazard discussion** *(1 mark)*:
**Propane is more hazardous** from a flashback standpoint because it has a **smaller quenching distance** (and higher $S_L$). The flame can travel through smaller gaps, making propane systems more prone to flashbacks. This is why natural gas (methane) is preferred for domestic distribution in narrow pipes.

#### **Q.B (b) [7 marks]**
**What are the flammability limits (LFL and UFL)? Explain Zeldovich's "limiting flame temperature" hypothesis. Use Le Chatelier's rule to compute the LFL of a natural gas mixture containing 90% $\text{CH}_4$ (LFL = 5%), 7% $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ (LFL = 3%), and 3% $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ (LFL = 2.1%).**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions** *(1.5 marks)*:
- **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL):** The minimum fuel concentration in air below which the mixture is too lean to propagate flame. Below LFL, heat losses exceed heat release.
- **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL):** The maximum fuel concentration above which the mixture is too rich to propagate flame. Above UFL, there is insufficient $\text{O}_2$ to sustain combustion.

**Step 2 — Zeldovich's Limiting Flame Temperature Hypothesis** *(2 marks)*:
Zeldovich proposed that a premixed flame can **only** propagate if the **adiabatic flame temperature of the mixture** exceeds a critical value $T_{lim} \approx 1600$ K, regardless of fuel type. This unifies LFL and UFL under a single thermodynamic criterion: as fuel is added from 0% upward, $T_{ad}$ rises; the LFL is reached when $T_{ad} = T_{lim}$. Continuing, $T_{ad}$ peaks near stoichiometric, then decreases; the UFL is reached on the rich side when $T_{ad}$ falls back to $T_{lim}$.

**Step 3 — Le Chatelier's Rule statement** *(1 mark)*:
For a multi-component fuel mixture, the LFL of the mixture is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LFL}_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_i y_i / \text{LFL}_i}
\end{aligned}$$
where $y_i$ is the volume fraction of component $i$ in the fuel mixture and $\text{LFL}_i$ is its pure-component LFL.

**Step 4 — Numerical computation** *(1.5 marks)*:
- $\text{CH}_4$ contribution: $0.90 / 5.0 = 0.180$
- $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ contribution: $0.07 / 3.0 = 0.0233$
- $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ contribution: $0.03 / 2.1 = 0.0143$
- Sum: $0.180 + 0.0233 + 0.0143 = 0.2176$
- $\text{LFL}_{mix} = 1 / 0.2176 = 4.60\%$ (by volume in air)

**Step 5 — Interpretation** *(1 mark)*:
The mixture LFL (**4.6%**) is **lower** than pure methane's LFL (5.0%) because ethane and propane have lower individual LFLs. This makes mixed natural gases **slightly more hazardous** at the lean limit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Premixed & Diffusion Flames (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S_L$ with flame speed in the lab frame.** $S_L$ is defined relative to the **unburnt gas** only. Students routinely forget the density ratio factor $\rho_u/\rho_b = T_{ad}/T_u$ when computing absolute flame speed in a lab.
> 2. **Mixing up LFL/UFL definitions.** LFL is the *lean* limit (low fuel), UFL is the *rich* limit (high fuel). Reversing these costs full marks on definition questions.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions in the Burke–Schumann derivation.** KTU examiners award **at least 1.5–2 marks** for clearly stating the five assumptions. Students who write only the final equation lose these marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical problems.** Quenching distance in mm vs m, mixture fraction dimensionless (always between 0 and 1) — wrong unit means **automatic -1 mark penalty** per KTU key.
> 5. **Confusing "flame temperature" with "flame thickness".** Both relate to the ZND structure but are distinct quantities; using one in a formula for the other is a frequent error.
> 6. **Neglecting the soot/luminous aspect of diffusion flames.** KTU examiners look for "yellow luminous flame, soot formation" descriptors — students who only mention "non-premixed" lose conceptual marks.
> 7. **In Le Chatelier's rule, mixing up volume % and mass %.** LFLs are tabulated in **volume %**; the rule is valid in volume fractions. Converting to mass fractions requires molecular weights and is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Quick-Fire Revision — Read This the Night Before Your Exam:**

- ✅ **Premixed flame** = pre-mixed fuel+air, kinetics-controlled, **blue**, non-luminous. Example: Bunsen.
- ✅ **Diffusion flame** = fuel & oxidizer mix in situ, transport-controlled, **yellow**, luminous (soot). Example: candle.
- ✅ **Laminar Burning Velocity $S_L$** is a *property of the mixture*; depends on $\phi, T_u, p, \text{fuel type}$.
- ✅ ZND structure: **Pre-heat → Reaction → Post-Flame**.
- ✅ **Mixture fraction $\xi \in [0, 1]$**; flame surface at $\xi = \xi_{st}$.
- ✅ **Burke–Schumann** assumptions: equal diffusivities, infinite chemistry, axisymmetric jet.
- ✅ **Quenching distance** $d_q \sim \alpha/S_L$; smaller for higher $S_L$ fuels.
- ✅ **LFL** = lean limit; **UFL** = rich limit; **$T_{ad,lim} \approx 1600$ K** (Zeldovich criterion).
- ✅ **Le Chatelier's rule** uses **volume** fractions.
- ✅ Yellow luminous flame $\Leftrightarrow$ diffusion flame $\Leftrightarrow$ soot formation.
- ✅ Adiabatic flame temperature for methane/air $\approx$ **2222 K**; $S_L(\text{CH}_4\text{-air}) \approx$ **0.4 m/s**.
- ✅ Stoichiometric $\xi$ for methane: $\xi_{st} \approx 0.055$.
- ✅ Markstein length $\ell_M > 0$: stable flame; $\ell_M < 0$: cellular instability (e.g., $\text{H}_2$).
- ✅ Flames **outside LFL–UFL envelope** cannot propagate — used in fire prevention by fuel-lean or fuel-rich atmospheres.
- ✅ For KTU 2024: Memorize the formula sheet table (Section 2.7) verbatim — it covers 80% of numerical questions.

---

**End of KTU 2024 Module 3 Notes — Premixed and Diffusion Flames** ✨

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bunsen Burner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bunsen Burner

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bunsen Burner (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Bunsen burner* is a **pre-aerated, atmospheric-pressure, laboratory gas burner** that produces a controlled, laminar, non-luminous diffusion flame by allowing a controlled amount of **primary air** to premix with a fuel gas (typically methane, natural gas, or LPG) at its base, before the mixture is ignited at a *barrel nozzle*. The resulting flame is a classic *anchored, conical, Bunsen-type flame* and serves as a foundational model system in fire dynamics for studying **flame structure, flame speed, stoichiometry, and heat transfer**.

In fire science, the Bunsen burner is treated as a **canonical, axisymmetric, laminar premixed/diffusion flame**, used to study:
- Combustion chemistry (complete vs incomplete combustion)
- Flame temperature distribution
- Aerodynamics of reacting flows
- Effect of air-fuel ratio (λ = actual AFR / stoichiometric AFR)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Bunsen burner as a "controlled campfire with adjustable lungs."**

Imagine a campfire that produces thick black smoke because the fuel (wood) cannot access enough oxygen quickly. Now imagine giving that fire a *valve* to draw in a precise, controlled stream of fresh air at the bottom of the fuel inlet — and a *chimney* (the barrel) to focus the flame into a tight, hot, predictable cone. That is exactly what Robert Bunsen (with Peter Desaga, 1855) invented.

- The **gas jet** = the fuel.
- The **air-inlet collar** at the base = the "lungs" of the fire.
- The **barrel** = the chimney that stabilizes the flame and prevents random drafts.
- The **inner blue cone** = the active reaction zone (where chemistry meets physics).
- The **outer pale-blue mantle** = the burnt gases cooling by mixing with ambient air.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adiabatic flame temp. of CH₄ in air | $T_{ad}$ | **≈ 1950 °C (≈ 2223 K)** | Stoichiometric, 1 atm |
| Visible inner-cone temp. | $T_{cone}$ | **≈ 1500 °C** | Top of inner blue cone |
| Laminar burning velocity (methane/air) | $S_L$ | **≈ 0.40 m/s** | φ = 1.0, 25 °C, 1 atm |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (methane) | $AFR_{st}$ | **17.2 : 1** (by mass) | Air-to-fuel mass ratio |
| Primary air entrainment | — | up to **~60 %** of stoich. need | Limited by injector design |
| Density of methane (STP) | ρ_CH4 | **0.717 kg/m³** | Used in jet velocity calcs. |
| LHV of methane | $\Delta H_c$ | **50.0 MJ/kg** | Lower heating value |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Bunsen burner is the *prototype* for studying **flame structure, primary/secondary air concepts, premixed flame theory, and flame stability** (flashback, blow-off, lift-off) in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Structured Logic

**Step 1 — Fuel delivery:** Natural gas (chiefly CH₄) flows through a **jet** (a small orifice) at the base of the barrel. By Bernoulli's principle, the high-velocity gas jet creates a **local low-pressure region** that entrains **primary air** through the adjustable collar.

**Step 2 — Mixing in the barrel:** The fuel and primary air travel up the barrel, mixing by turbulence and diffusion. The amount of primary air determines the **air–fuel ratio (AFR)** and therefore the flame type.

**Step 3 — Ignition at the rim:** At the top of the barrel, the mixture meets atmospheric oxygen. The flame anchors at the rim because the **flow velocity equals the local laminar burning velocity** — a condition called *flame anchoring*.

**Step 4 — Flame structure:** Three coaxial regions are observed:
1. **Inner dark cone** — unburnt, pre-heated gas–air mixture.
2. **Inner blue cone (reaction zone)** — the thin luminous blue sheet where chemistry occurs. The hottest point is the **tip of the inner cone**.
3. **Outer mantle (post-flame gases)** — products of combustion (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) mixing with surrounding air, dimly visible.

**Step 5 — Flame type selection by AFR:**
- **Luminous (yellow/orange) flame** → fuel-rich (φ > 1, *insufficient* primary air). Soot particles glow by incandescence. Lower temperature, **incomplete combustion**.
- **Non-luminous (blue) flame** → near-stoichiometric or slightly lean (φ ≤ 1). High temperature, **complete combustion**, no soot.
- **Roaring flame** → very lean, lots of secondary air; flame lifts and may blow off.

**Step 6 — Flame stability limits:** The flame exists only if:
$$v_{gas} \ge S_L \quad \text{(prevents flashback)} \qquad \text{and} \qquad v_{gas} \le v_{blow-off}$$
If $v_{gas}$ is too low → **flashback** (flame travels back into the barrel). If too high → **blow-off** (flame detaches and extinguishes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Bunsen flame is the conceptual ancestor of every modern gas appliance — stoves, furnaces, oxy-acetylene torches, gas turbine primary zones, and rocket engine injectors. Understanding its flame-stability map is critical in **fire-suppression engineering** and **compressor/boiler safety**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$ | Stoichiometric combustion of methane in air | — |
| 2 | $AFR_{st} = \dfrac{2(4.76)(M_{air})}{1 \cdot M_{CH_4}}$ | Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (mass basis) | kg/kg |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{st}}{(F/A)_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\phi} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{st}}$ | Excess-air / relative A/F ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta H_c = \sum_i n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,i}(T)\,dT$ | Energy balance for adiabatic flame temp. | J/mol |
| 6 | $v_{jet} = C_d \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{gas}}}$ | Gas exit velocity from the jet (Bernoulli) | m/s |
| 7 | $Q_{primary} = C_d A_{collar} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P_{induced}}{\rho_{air}}}$ | Primary air entrainment rate | m³/s |
| 8 | $S_L = S_{L,0} \left(\dfrac{T_u}{T_0}\right)^\alpha \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_u}\right)^\beta$ | Laminar burning velocity (Metghalchi–Keck) | m/s |
| 9 | $h_c = \dfrac{S_L}{v_{gas}} \cdot d_{barrel}$ | Inner-cone height (Michelson's line) | m |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\, v\, d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for barrel flow | dimensionless |

> **Note on units and constants:** $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol, $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$ g/mol, $C_d$ ≈ 0.6–0.95 depending on geometry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio for Methane

We start from the **balanced global reaction** at standard conditions (1 atm, 298 K):

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Identify mole requirements.**
For complete combustion of **1 mole of CH₄**, we need **2 moles of O₂**.

**Step 2 — Convert O₂ to "air."**
Air is approximated as $O_2 + 3.76\,N_2$ on a mole basis, so **1 mole of O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 moles of N₂**.

**Step 3 — Total moles of air per mole of CH₄.**
$$n_{air} = 2 \times (1 + 3.76) = 2 \times 4.76 = 9.52 \text{ mol air / mol CH}_4$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass basis.**
$$m_{air} = n_{air} \cdot M_{air} = 9.52 \times 28.97 = 275.79 \text{ g}$$
$$m_{fuel} = 1 \times 16.04 = 16.04 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the air–fuel ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{st} &= \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{9.52 \times 28.97}{1 \times 16.04} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{275.79}{16.04} \\[4pt]
&\approx 17.20 \; \text{kg air / kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the Bunsen flame:** A flame with λ = 1 (φ = 1) requires ~17.2 kg of air per kg of CH₄. A *luminous* Bunsen flame operates at λ < 1 (insufficient primary air), while a *roaring blue* flame is at λ > 1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Energy Balance)

We assume **adiabatic, constant-pressure, complete combustion** and use the standard **first-law enthalpy balance**:

$$H_{reactants}(T_{ref}) = H_{products}(T_{ad})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_j n_j \, h_{f,j}^\circ + \sum_j n_j \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,j}(T)\, dT \;=\; \sum_k n_k \, h_{f,k}^\circ + \sum_k n_k \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,k}(T)\, dT
\end{aligned}
$$

For our reaction, $T_{ref} = 298$ K, and the product mole numbers are 1, 2, 7.52 for CO₂, H₂O(g), N₂.

**Step 1 — Standard heats of formation (kJ/mol):**
- $h_f^\circ(\text{CH}_4) = -74.8$
- $h_f^\circ(\text{O}_2) = h_f^\circ(\text{N}_2) = 0$
- $h_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) = -393.5$
- $h_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241.8$

**Step 2 — Heat released (LHV) per mole of CH₄.**
$$\Delta H_{rxn} = [(-393.5) + 2(-241.8)] - [(-74.8) + 0] = -802.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Equate to sensible enthalpy of products at $T_{ad}$.**
Using constant average $c_p$ values (kJ/mol·K): CO₂ = 0.054, H₂O(g) = 0.044, N₂ = 0.033.
$$
\begin{aligned}
802.3 &= (1)(0.054)(T_{ad}-298) + (2)(0.044)(T_{ad}-298) + (7.52)(0.033)(T_{ad}-298) \\[4pt]
802.3 &= (0.054 + 0.088 + 0.248)(T_{ad} - 298) \\[4pt]
802.3 &= 0.390 \,(T_{ad} - 298) \\[4pt]
T_{ad} - 298 &= \frac{802.3}{0.390} \approx 2057 \\[4pt]
T_{ad} &\approx 2355 \text{ K} \approx 2082 \;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Variable $c_p$ and dissociation reduce this to the *real* adiabatic value of **≈ 2223 K**, matching tabulated data.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The *theoretical* adiabatic temperature is higher than the *measured* Bunsen-flame temperature because (i) heat is lost to the burner body, (ii) the burner is *non-adiabatic*, and (iii) at high T, CO₂ and H₂O dissociate, absorbing energy.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bunsen Burner Design vs. Flame Character

| Variable increased | Inner-cone height $h_c$ | Flame color | Flame temperature | Combustion completeness | Stability risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gas flow rate (constant primary air) | **Increases** (taller cone) | unchanged (at fixed AFR) | slight increase | unchanged | **Flashback** at low flow |
| Primary air opening | **Decreases** (shorter, broader cone) | shifts yellow → blue | **Increases** | becomes more complete | **Flashback** at full open |
| Secondary air (drafts) | Slight decrease | shifts yellow → blue | moderate increase | improves | may cause **lift-off** |
| Barrel diameter $d$ | increases linearly (eq. 9) | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | increases $Re$ |
| Excess λ (lean shift) | shorter, lifts | bluish, then translucent | peaks near φ ≈ 1.05 | near complete | **blow-off** at high λ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU valuation tip:** Examiners often ask students to *qualitatively* trace the change in flame as the air collar is opened. Memorize the column "Flame color" and the inverse relationship between $h_c$ and primary air.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bunsen Burner

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of a Bunsen burner — from the gas supply and air entrainment to the multi-zone flame structure. (Adapted as a block-level schematic because a faithful cross-section is best represented as a parts-and-flows map.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GasSupply] --> B[JetOrifice]
    C[AmbientAir] --> D[AirCollar]
    D --> E[VenturiMixingZone]
    B --> E
    E --> F[BarrelRiser]
    F --> G[FlameAnchorRim]
    G --> H1[InnerDarkCone]
    G --> H2[InnerBlueReactionZone]
    G --> H3[OuterMantle]
    H1 --> I[PreheatedGasAir]
    H2 --> J[CombustionChemistry]
    H3 --> K[HotProducts]
    K --> L[BuoyantPlume]
    H2 --> M[TipOfInnerCone_Hottest]
```

### 4.2 Flame Stability Map (Blow-off vs. Flashback)

The next block diagram explains the **flame-stability envelope** of a Bunsen-type premixed flame as a function of mixture velocity and equivalence ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[MixtureVelocity] --> Q{VelocityTest}
    Q -->|v less than SL| R[FlashbackIntoBarrel]
    Q -->|v between SL and VBlowoff| S[StableAnchoredFlame]
    Q -->|v greater than VBlowoff| T[Blowoff]
    U[EquivalenceRatio] --> S
    U --> V{FuelAirMixture}
    V -->|phi greater than 1| W[LuminousYellowFlame]
    V -->|phi approx 1| X[BlueNonLuminousFlame]
    V -->|phi less than 1| Y[LeanRoaringFlame]
    S --> W
    S --> X
    S --> Y
```

### 4.3 Component Map of the Bunsen Burner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[BaseMount] --> A2[GasInletTube]
    A2 --> A3[JetNozzle]
    A3 --> A4[AirRegulationCollar]
    A4 --> A5[BarrelChimney]
    A5 --> A6[FlameAtRim]
    A6 --> A7[InnerBlueCone]
    A6 --> A8[OuterMantle]
    A1 --> A9[Stand]
    A1 --> A10[GasAdjustmentKnob]
    A10 --> A2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is meant by "primary air" in a Bunsen burner? Why is it important for the character of the flame?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Primary air* is the **atmospheric air deliberately entrained at the base** of the Bunsen barrel through the adjustable air-inlet collar, which mixes with the fuel gas **before** the mixture reaches the flame. It is important because **(i) the amount of primary air sets the air–fuel ratio (λ or φ)**, which determines whether the flame is luminous (φ > 1, fuel-rich) or non-luminous blue (φ ≤ 1); **(ii) pre-mixing promotes complete combustion**, raising flame temperature and eliminating soot; and **(iii) primary-air flow affects flame stability**, controlling flashback and blow-off limits. (3 marks for definition + 2 distinct reasons; mention φ or λ for full credit.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Repeat Pattern]**
*Distinguish between a luminous and a non-luminous Bunsen flame. State one practical situation where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **luminous flame** is produced when the primary air is insufficient (φ > 1); combustion is incomplete, soot particles are present, and they glow yellow/orange by incandescence. Temperature is lower (~1000 °C) but the flame is **reducing** — useful in **reducing oxides in qualitative analysis** (e.g., reducing ZnO to Zn). A **non-luminous (blue) flame** is produced when primary air is near-stoichiometric (φ ≈ 1); combustion is complete, no soot, flame is hotter (~1500 °C) and **oxidizing** — preferred for **laboratory heating and sterilization** where high, clean temperatures are needed. (½ mark each for the 4 distinctions + ½ mark each for the practical use = 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal-Choice Format)

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the construction and working of a Bunsen burner. Explain the role of the air-inlet collar in producing different types of flames. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For methane burning in air, derive the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio by mass and calculate the *equivalence ratio* if the actual air supplied is 14 kg air / kg fuel. Comment on the resulting flame character. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**
- *Construction (3 marks):* Label the main parts on a sketch — **(i) base & stand**, **(ii) gas inlet tube with needle valve**, **(iii) jet (orifice)** at top of gas tube, **(iv) air-regulation collar (sleeve) with holes**, **(v) barrel (chimney)**. (½ mark each ≈ 2.5 marks + ½ mark for labeling)
- *Working (2 marks):* Gas flows from inlet through the jet at high velocity. By Bernoulli's principle, low pressure at the jet base entrains **primary air** through the collar. The fuel–air mixture rises through the barrel, exits at the rim, and is ignited. The flame anchors at the rim because **local gas velocity = local laminar burning velocity**.
- *Role of air collar (2 marks):* The collar controls primary-air entrainment. **Closed** → no primary air → **luminous yellow flame** (diffusion flame, soot glowing). **Partially open** → premixed → **non-luminous blue flame** (complete combustion, hot). **Fully open** → very lean, flame may roar, lift, or blow off.

**(b) Stoichiometric AFR & Equivalence Ratio (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Write the balanced equation (1 mark):*
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 7.52\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

*Step 2 — Moles of air per mole of fuel (1 mark):* 9.52 mol air/mol CH₄.

*Step 3 — Mass-based AFR (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{st} &= \frac{9.52 \times 28.97}{1 \times 16.04} = \frac{275.79}{16.04} \approx 17.20
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Equivalence ratio (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}} = \frac{1/14}{1/17.20} = \frac{17.20}{14} \approx 1.23
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Comment on flame character (1 mark):*
Since **φ ≈ 1.23 > 1**, the mixture is **fuel-rich**. Insufficient air for complete combustion → **luminous yellow flame**, presence of soot, lower flame temperature, and possible release of unburnt CO.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **flame stability** in a Bunsen burner. Define *flashback* and *blow-off*, and describe the conditions (in terms of flow velocity and laminar burning velocity) under which they occur. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using an energy balance, derive an expression for the **adiabatic flame temperature** of methane in air, stating clearly the assumptions. Compare this to the *actual* flame temperature in a Bunsen burner and explain why the two differ. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flame Stability (7 marks):**
- *Definition (1 mark):* Flame stability is the ability of a flame to remain anchored at a fixed location (the barrel rim) without being extinguished or propagating back into the premixing zone.
- *Flashback (3 marks):* Occurs when the **mixture velocity at the rim is less than the local laminar burning velocity** ($v < S_L$). The flame then propagates *downstream into the barrel*, often with a popping sound. It is favored by: low gas flow, large primary-air opening, small barrel diameter. **Quantitative condition:** $v_{gas,rim} < S_L(\phi, T_u, P_u)$.
- *Blow-off (3 marks):* Occurs when the mixture velocity **exceeds the blow-off velocity** ($v > v_{BO}$), the flame detaches from the rim and extinguishes. It is favored by: high flow, very lean (λ → upper limit) or very rich mixtures. **Quantitative condition:** $v_{gas,rim} > v_{BO}(\phi)$.

**(b) Adiabatic Flame Temperature (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Energy balance (1 mark):* For an adiabatic, constant-pressure, complete-combustion process,
$$\sum_j n_j h_{f,j}^\circ = \sum_k n_k \left[ h_{f,k}^\circ + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,k}(T)\,dT \right]$$

*Step 2 — Solve for $T_{ad}$ (3 marks):* Rearranging,

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ &= \sum_k n_k \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,k}(T)\,dT \\[4pt]
T_{ad} &= T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ}{\sum_k n_k \, \bar c_{p,k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = 802.3$ kJ/mol and $\sum n_k \bar c_{p,k} \approx 0.39$ kJ/K (from §3.2), we get $T_{ad} \approx 2355$ K (constant-$c_p$ estimate), reduced to **~2223 K** when dissociation and variable $c_p$ are included.

*Step 3 — Assumptions (1 mark):* Adiabatic walls, complete combustion, no dissociation, ideal gas, constant pressure, well-defined reactants/products.

*Step 4 — Comparison to actual Bunsen temperature (2 marks):* The actual Bunsen flame temperature at the tip of the inner cone is **~1773 K (≈ 1500 °C)** — significantly lower than the theoretical $T_{ad} \approx 2223$ K because:
1. **Heat losses** to the metal barrel and ambient surroundings (non-adiabatic walls).
2. **Incomplete combustion** — some CO and soot form in fuel-rich or fuel-lean local regions.
3. **Dissociation** of CO₂ and H₂O at high T absorbs energy.
4. **Radiation losses** from the luminous soot particles (in yellow flames) and from hot CO₂/H₂O bands.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "primary" and "secondary" air.** Primary air = *entrained at the base* (pre-mixed with gas). Secondary air = *diffused from the atmosphere* at the flame's outer mantle. Marking scheme deducts 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Wrong unit on AFR.** Always quote air–fuel ratio as **kg air / kg fuel** unless specifically asked for the volumetric form.
> 3. **Mixing up φ and λ.** φ = (F/A)actual/(F/A)stoich (rich if φ > 1). λ = 1/φ. Examiners explicitly test this swap.
> 4. **In the energy balance, forgetting to account for the sensible enthalpy of reactants** at $T_u \ne 298$ K.
> 5. **Stating "the Bunsen flame is purely premixed."** It is *partially* premixed — primary air is pre-mixed; secondary air diffuses in at the mantle. Mention both for full credit.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of barrel diameter in flame anchoring** ($h_c \propto d$, Michelson's law — eq. 9 in §2.2).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Bunsen burner** is a *partially premixed*, atmospheric, axisymmetric laboratory flame.
- **Primary air** (entrained at the base) is what makes it different from a simple Bunsen-type diffusion flame; **secondary air** diffuses in from the atmosphere at the outer mantle.
- The flame has **three zones**: inner dark cone (unburnt), inner blue cone (reaction zone — *hottest*, ~1500 °C at the tip), outer mantle (products).
- **Luminous yellow flame** ↔ φ > 1 (fuel-rich, soot, lower T, *reducing*). **Non-luminous blue flame** ↔ φ ≤ 1 (stoich./lean, hot, *oxidizing*).
- **Stoichiometric AFR (methane) ≈ 17.2 : 1 (mass).** Equivalence ratio: φ = (F/A)actual / (F/A)stoich. Excess-air ratio: λ = 1/φ.
- **Flame stability** requires the local mixture velocity at the rim to be **between $S_L$ (lower limit, flashback) and $v_{BO}$ (upper limit, blow-off)**.
- **Adiabatic flame temperature of CH₄ in air ≈ 2223 K** (theoretical); **actual Bunsen tip temperature ≈ 1773 K** due to heat loss, dissociation, and incomplete combustion.
- **Laminar burning velocity of methane/air** at 1 atm, 25 °C, φ = 1 is **≈ 0.40 m/s** — small enough that the Bunsen flame is *laminar* under normal operation.
- **Inner-cone height** scales with barrel diameter (Michelson's line): $h_c = (S_L / v_{gas}) \cdot d_{barrel}$ — taller barrel → taller cone at fixed flow.
- The Bunsen burner is the **prototype** of every modern gas appliance: stove, furnace, gas-turbine primary zone, and rocket-injector shear layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Primary air → AFR (φ, λ) → flame color → flame temperature → stability regime.* Memorize this chain and you can answer 80 % of any Bunsen-burner question on a KTU paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Flame Temperature vs. Equivalence Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Flame Temperature vs. Equivalence Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** is defined as the temperature achieved when a fuel and oxidizer mixture undergoes **complete combustion** under **adiabatic conditions** (no heat loss to the surroundings, $Q = 0$) and at **constant pressure** (or constant volume for the isochoric variant), with all the chemical energy of combustion being converted into the **sensible enthalpy** of the combustion products.

$$T_{ad} \;=\; T_{ad}(\phi,\;P,\;\text{initial temperature},\;\text{degree of dissociation})$$

The **Equivalence Ratio** ($\phi$) is the **dimensionless ratio** of the actual fuel–oxidizer mass (or mole) ratio to the stoichiometric fuel–oxidizer ratio:

$$\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\left(F/A\right)_{\text{actual}}}{\left(F/A\right)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT528 – Module 3):** AFT is a *thermodynamic ceiling* on flame temperature. The variation of $T_{ad}$ with $\phi$ is the *single most important combustion chart* a fire-protection engineer must master because it links fuel chemistry to thermal hazard output (fire plume temperature, smoke layer temperature, blast overpressure, etc.).

### 1.2 Classification by Equivalence Ratio

| Regime | Condition | Excess Species | Combustion Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Lean | $\phi < 1$ | Excess **Air/O$_2$** | All fuel burns, products diluted |
| Stoichiometric | $\phi = 1$ | **None** (theoretical ideal) | All fuel + all O$_2$ consumed exactly |
| Rich | $\phi > 1$ | Excess **Fuel** | All O$_2$ consumed, unburnt fuel remains |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker of Combustion"

Imagine striking a match inside a perfectly insulated steel pressure vessel that absorbs zero heat. The flame cannot lose energy to walls, so **every joule of chemical enthalpy released by the fuel–oxygen reaction must end up warming the resulting gas molecules** (N$_2$, H$_2$O, CO$_2$, O$_2$, etc.). The final temperature the gas settles at is the **Adiabatic Flame Temperature**.

Now, if you change the **fuel-to-air mix**:
- **Too much air (lean, $\phi < 1$):** The extra air acts like ice cubes in a hot drink — it absorbs heat but does not participate in the reaction. Temperature drops.
- **Exactly right ($\phi = 1$):** No diluent, no leftover fuel. Maximum possible temperature *if dissociation is ignored*.
- **Too much fuel (rich, $\phi > 1$):** The leftover fuel acts like ice cubes again — it has to be heated but does not react. Temperature drops.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Non-Obvious Twist:** The maximum AFT does **not** occur exactly at $\phi = 1$ but slightly on the **rich side** (typically $\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15$ for hydrocarbons). This is because at very high temperatures, CO$_2$ and H$_2$O partially *dissociate* back into CO, H$_2$, and O$_2$, an endothermic effect that is suppressed when extra fuel is present (rich side) because the partial pressure of O$_2$ drops. This is a favorite KTU question!

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Universal Gas Constant: $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
- Standard Reference Temperature: $T_{ref} = 298.15$ **K** (25 °C)
- Standard Reference Pressure: $P_{ref} = 1$ **atm = 101.325 kPa**
- Air composition (mole basis): **O$_2$ = 21%**, **N$_2$ = 79%** $\Rightarrow$ $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$
- For combustion with air, per mole O$_2$ consumed: $1\,\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2$ enter the reactor.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation — Energy Balance on an Open System

Apply the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to a combustor (no work, no heat transfer, negligible KE and PE changes):

$$\sum_{\text{reactants}} n_i\,h_i \;=\; \sum_{\text{products}} n_j\,h_j$$

Expanding the enthalpy into standard heat of formation plus sensible enthalpy:

$$\sum_{\text{react}} n_i\left[h_{f,i}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{in}} C_{p,i}\,dT\right] \;=\; \sum_{\text{prod}} n_j\left[h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT\right]$$

Rearranging for the unknowns (typically $T_{ad}$ and the equilibrium product distribution):

$$\boxed{\;\sum_{\text{prod}} n_j\!\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT \;=\; \sum_{\text{react}} n_i\!\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{in}} C_{p,i}\,dT \;-\; \sum_{\text{react}} n_i\,h_{f,i}^{\circ} \;+\; \sum_{\text{prod}} n_j\,h_{f,j}^{\circ}\;}$$

Or, more compactly, defining the **Heat of Combustion at the reference state**:

$$H_R \;=\; \sum_{\text{react}} n_i\,h_{f,i}^{\circ}, \qquad H_P \;=\; \sum_{\text{prod}} n_j\,h_{f,j}^{\circ}$$

$$\sum_{\text{prod}} n_j\!\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT \;=\; H_R \;-\; H_P \;+\; \sum_{\text{react}} n_i\!\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{in}} C_{p,i}\,dT$$

If reactants enter at $T_{ref} = 298$ K, the reactant sensible term vanishes and:

$$\sum_{\text{prod}} n_j\!\int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT \;=\; -\,\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}\big|_{298}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign on $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}$ arises because combustion is **exothermic** ($\Delta H < 0$), and the right-hand side must be positive so that the left-hand-side sensible-enthalpy integral of the products is positive.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Solving an AFT Problem

1. **Write the stoichiometric reaction** for 1 kmol of fuel with $\nu_{O_2}$ kmol of O$_2$ producing $\nu_{CO_2}$, $\nu_{H_2O}$, etc.
2. **Scale the reaction** for the actual equivalence ratio $\phi$ by introducing the air ratio $\lambda = 1/\phi$.
   - If $\phi < 1$ (lean): supply more O$_2$ than stoichiometric, i.e., $\lambda$ O$_2$ per kmol fuel, with $\lambda = 1/\phi > 1$.
   - If $\phi > 1$ (rich): supply less O$_2$, i.e., $\lambda = 1/\phi < 1$.
3. **Balance the products** assuming complete combustion (no dissociation) for a first estimate.
4. **Set up the energy balance** equating reactant and product total enthalpies.
5. **Iterate** on $T_{ad}$ using $C_p(T)$ polynomial fits or JANAF/NASA tables until both sides are equal.
6. **Apply a dissociation correction** (advanced step) if $T_{ad} > 1800$ K.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Table 2.1: Master Formula Reference for AFT Computations**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (F/A)_{act}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | $\phi = 1/\lambda$ |
| 2 | Air–Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR = (m_{air}/m_{fuel})$ | kg/kg | $34.3$ for gasoline |
| 3 | Stoichiometric O$_2$ demand | $\nu_{O_2} = x + y/4 - z/2$ per C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$ | kmol/kmol fuel | Hydrocarbon rule |
| 4 | Energy balance (adiabatic) | $\sum n_R h_R = \sum n_P h_P$ | kJ | SFEE with $\dot{Q}=\dot{W}=0$ |
| 5 | Sensible enthalpy integral | $\int_{T_{ref}}^{T} C_p\,dT$ | kJ/kmol | Polynomial NASA-7 form |
| 6 | NASA-7 polynomial form | $C_p/R_u = a_1 + a_2 T + a_3 T^2 + a_4 T^3 + a_5 T^4$ | dimensionless | Two ranges, $T < 1000$ K and $T > 1000$ K |
| 7 | Dissociation effect | $\text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ | — | Endothermic, lowers $T_{ad}$ |
| 8 | Maximum $T_{ad}$ location | $\phi_{\max\,T_{ad}} \approx 1.05 - 1.15$ | — | Slightly rich (CH$_4$, C$_3$H$_8$) |
| 9 | First-law check (open system) | $H_P - H_R = -Q_{released}$ | kJ | Sign convention critical |
| 10 | Mean $C_p$ approximation | $T_{ad} \approx T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{comb}^{\circ}}{\sum n_P \bar{C}_{p,P}}$ | K | Useful for hand-calc |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In the formula table, do **not** confuse the *mole fraction* of O$_2$ in air (0.21) with the *mole ratio* $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$. The number $3.76$ is the correct multiplier for the N$_2$ brought in by air.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the AFT vs. $\phi$ Relationship

- **Fire Hazard Analysis:** Peak temperature of a compartment fire, the temperature of a jet flame, the radiant heat flux — all scale with AFT.
- **Engine Design:** Engineers tune AFR (and thus $\phi$) to balance power, fuel economy, and NO$_x$ emissions.
- **Boiler/Combustor Sizing:** AFT determines the enthalpy of the products driving the gas turbine or steam cycle.
- **Explosion Severity:** The AFT feeds into the calculation of TNT-equivalent blast overpressure and the deflagration index $K_G$.
- **Pollutant Formation:** NO$_x$ and CO formation are highly sensitive to peak $T_{ad}$ (Zeldovich mechanism) and to the fuel-lean/rich local stoichiometry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric AFT for Methane (CH$_4$) in Air — Complete Combustion, No Dissociation

This is the **canonical KTU board problem**. We solve it in full, step by step.

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric reaction per kmol of CH$_4$:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

- Stoichiometric O$_2$ demand: $\nu_{O_2} = 1 + \tfrac{4}{4} = 2$ kmol.
- N$_2$ brought in by air: $2 \times 3.76 = 7.52$ kmol.
- Total products: $1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52$ kmol of gas.

**Step 2 — Tabulate standard heats of formation $h_f^{\circ}$ (at 298 K):**

| Species | $h_f^{\circ}$ (kJ/kmol) |
|:---:|:---:|
| CH$_4$ (g) | $-74,850$ |
| O$_2$ | 0 |
| N$_2$ | 0 |
| CO$_2$ | $-393,520$ |
| H$_2$O (g) | $-241,820$ |

**Step 3 — Compute the heat of reaction at 298 K (the lower heating value, LHV, basis):**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} \;=\; \sum_P n_j\,h_{f,j}^{\circ} \;-\; \sum_R n_i\,h_{f,i}^{\circ}$$

$$= \left[1(-393{,}520) + 2(-241{,}820) + 7.52(0)\right] \;-\; \left[1(-74{,}850) + 2(0) + 7.52(0)\right]$$

$$= (-393{,}520 - 483{,}640) - (-74{,}850)$$

$$= -877{,}160 + 74{,}850$$

$$= -802{,}310 \text{ kJ per kmol CH}_4$$

This is the **LHV of methane** (≈ 50.0 MJ/kg, well known).

**Step 4 — Apply the adiabatic energy balance (reactants at 298 K):**

$$\sum_P n_j\!\int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT \;=\; -\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} \;=\; +802{,}310 \text{ kJ}$$

The product stream contains 1 kmol CO$_2$, 2 kmol H$_2$O, 7.52 kmol N$_2$.

Use polynomial $C_p$ values (kJ/(kmol·K)) — typical averages over 1000–2500 K:

| Species | $C_p$ average (kJ/kmol·K) |
|:---:|:---:|
| CO$_2$ | $\approx 54.3$ |
| H$_2$O | $\approx 45.2$ |
| N$_2$ | $\approx 32.3$ |

**Step 5 — Compute the total product heat capacity:**

$$\sum n_P\,C_{p,P} \;=\; 1(54.3) + 2(45.2) + 7.52(32.3)$$

$$= 54.3 + 90.4 + 242.9$$

$$= 387.6 \text{ kJ/K per kmol CH}_4$$

**Step 6 — First estimate of $T_{ad}$ (mean $C_p$ approximation):**

$$T_{ad} \;\approx\; 298 \;+\; \frac{802{,}310}{387.6} \;\approx\; 298 + 2070 \;=\; 2368 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7 — Refine with temperature-dependent $C_p$ (NASA-7 polynomial).**

Using full polynomial integration (compressed for brevity; shown in a sample call to JANAF-like tables):

$$\int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,\text{CO}_2}\,dT = a_1(T-298) + \tfrac{a_2}{2}(T^2 - 298^2) + \tfrac{a_3}{3}(T^3 - 298^3) + \ldots$$

A converged value is $T_{ad} \approx 2222$ K for **methane at $\phi = 1$** in air. The difference (2368 → 2222) reflects the rise in $C_p$ with temperature that the linear approximation neglects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2222 K and not 2368 K?** As temperature rises, the rotational, vibrational, and electronic modes of CO$_2$, H$_2$O, and N$_2$ absorb more energy per kelvin. The mean $C_p$ at 2000–2200 K is noticeably higher than at 1000 K. Hand-calculation textbooks quote $T_{ad} \approx 2222$ K (Turns, *An Introduction to Combustion*; similar to KTU reference style).

**Step 8 — Apply the dissociation correction.**

Above 1800 K, CO$_2$ and H$_2$O start to dissociate:

$$\text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2, \quad \Delta H = +283 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2, \quad \Delta H = +242 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This is **endothermic** — it absorbs heat and **lowers** the actual flame temperature. The fully-equilibrated adiabatic flame temperature for CH$_4$/air at $\phi = 1$ drops to about **$T_{ad} \approx 1950 - 2000$ K**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Variation of AFT with $\phi$ — General Procedure

For a generic hydrocarbon C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$, the stoichiometric O$_2$ demand is:

$$\nu_{O_2,\text{stoich}} \;=\; x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} \quad \text{(kmol O}_2 \text{ per kmol fuel)}$$

For a given $\phi$, the actual O$_2$ supplied is:

$$\nu_{O_2,\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{\nu_{O_2,\text{stoich}}}{\phi}$$

For a **lean mixture** ($\phi < 1$, $\lambda > 1$):

- All fuel is consumed.
- Excess O$_2$ remains in the products, acting as a heat sink.
- Product moles: $1$ CO$_2$, $y/2$ H$_2$O, $(\lambda - 1)\nu_{O_2,\text{stoich}}$ O$_2$, $3.76\,\nu_{O_2,\text{actual}}$ N$_2$.

For a **rich mixture** ($\phi > 1$):

- All O$_2$ is consumed.
- Excess fuel is **not** burned (in the simple non-dissociation model). The extra carbon ends up as solid **C(s)** soot or as **CO** and H$_2$ depending on the model.
- For the standard textbook model: excess C ends up as **solid carbon (soot)** and excess H ends up as **H$_2$(g)**.

**Energy balance for general $\phi$ (mean $C_p$ approximation):**

$$T_{ad}(\phi) \;\approx\; 298 \;+\; \frac{Q_{\text{released}}(\phi)}{\sum n_P(\phi)\,\bar{C}_{p,P}(\phi)}$$

where $Q_{\text{released}}(\phi)$ is the actual heat released up to the chosen product model (full oxidation for lean, partial for rich).

**Key qualitative result:**

$$\boxed{\;T_{ad}(\phi) \;\text{is maximum at} \;\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15\;\text{(slightly rich)}\;}$$

The peak shifts to the rich side because:

1. On the lean side, extra N$_2$ + O$_2$ dilutes the products.
2. On the rich side, the same dilution effect occurs but is partially offset by the **suppression of dissociation** (lower [O$_2$] shifts the CO$_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ equilibrium to the left, releasing more heat).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Closed-Volume (Constant-Volume) Adiabatic Flame Temperature

For a fire in a sealed compartment (a **backdraft** or **gas explosion** scenario), the combustion is at constant volume, not constant pressure. The relevant energy balance is:

$$U_P(T_{ad,v}) \;=\; U_R(T_{in})$$

$$\sum n_P\!\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad,v}} C_{v,j}\,dT \;=\; \sum n_R\!\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{in}} C_{v,i}\,dT \;-\; \Delta U_{rxn}^{\circ}$$

Note: $\Delta U = \Delta H - \Delta n_{gas} R_u T$ for ideal-gas reactions.

**For methane (constant-volume AFT):**

$$T_{ad,v} \;\approx\; 2900 - 3000 \text{ K}$$

This is **higher** than the constant-pressure AFT (2222 K) because at constant volume **no $P\,dV$ work is done**, so all the released energy stays as internal energy of the gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The constant-volume AFT is the relevant temperature for **explosion/blast pressure** calculations. Use $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ and the isentropic relation $P_2/P_1 = (T_2/T_1)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ to translate the temperature rise into the overpressure that drives the blast wave.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — AFT of Propane (C$_3$H$_8$) at $\phi = 0.9$ (Lean)

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric reaction:**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 5(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Scale for $\phi = 0.9$ (lean, $\lambda = 1/0.9 = 1.111$):**

$$\nu_{O_2,\text{actual}} = 5 / 0.9 = 5.556 \text{ kmol}$$

- Excess O$_2$ = $5.556 - 5 = 0.556$ kmol.
- N$_2$ from air = $5.556 \times 3.76 = 20.89$ kmol.

**Product composition (complete combustion, no dissociation):**

- 3 kmol CO$_2$
- 4 kmol H$_2$O
- 0.556 kmol O$_2$
- 20.89 kmol N$_2$
- **Total = 28.45 kmol**

**Step 3 — Heat of reaction (298 K):**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} \;=\; \left[3(-393{,}520) + 4(-241{,}820) + 0 + 0\right] - \left[1(-104{,}700) + 0 + 0\right]$$

$$= (-1{,}180{,}560 - 967{,}280) - (-104{,}700)$$

$$= -2{,}147{,}840 + 104{,}700 \;=\; -2{,}043{,}140 \text{ kJ per kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

This is the LHV of propane ≈ 46 MJ/kg (well-known value).

**Step 4 — Mean $C_p$ of products (kJ/(kmol·K)) for $T \sim 2000$ K:**

| Species | $n$ (kmol) | $C_p$ (kJ/kmol·K) | $n\,C_p$ (kJ/K) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CO$_2$ | 3 | 54.3 | 162.9 |
| H$_2$O | 4 | 45.2 | 180.8 |
| O$_2$ | 0.556 | 33.0 | 18.35 |
| N$_2$ | 20.89 | 32.3 | 674.7 |
| **Sum** | 28.45 | — | **1036.75** |

**Step 5 — First estimate of $T_{ad}$:**

$$T_{ad} \;\approx\; 298 + \frac{2{,}043{,}140}{1036.75} \;\approx\; 298 + 1971 \;\approx\; 2269 \text{ K}$$

After full temperature-dependent $C_p$ iteration and dissociation correction, a typical result is **$T_{ad} \approx 2050$ K** for propane at $\phi = 0.9$.

---

### 3.5 The Master Qualitative Curve (Analytical Matrix)

| $\phi$ | Regime | O$_2$ status | Fuel status | Product heat capacity | $T_{ad}$ trend | Dissociation effect |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.0 | Pure air | Surplus | None | Very high (lots of N$_2$+O$_2$) | ≈ 298 K | Negligible |
| 0.5 | Lean | Surplus | All burns | High | Rising steeply | Negligible |
| 0.8 | Lean | Surplus | All burns | Moderate-high | Rising | Small |
| **1.0** | **Stoichiometric** | **Exactly consumed** | **All burns** | **Moderate** | **Local high** | **Significant** |
| **1.05–1.15** | **Slightly rich** | **All consumed** | **Trace excess** | **Moderate** | **★ MAXIMUM ★** | **Suppressed** |
| 1.3 | Rich | All consumed | Excess remains | Moderate (extra H$_2$ dilutes) | Falling | Suppressed |
| 2.0 | Very rich | All consumed | Lots of excess | Higher (more gas) | Much lower | Suppressed |
| $\infty$ | Pure fuel | None | None | — | ≈ 298 K | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marker:** The shape of this curve is *more important* than any single numerical answer. Be able to **sketch** $T_{ad}$ vs. $\phi$, mark the stoichiometric point, mark the maximum, and explain the asymmetry using the dilution + dissociation argument.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — AFT Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given fuel, phi, T_in, P] --> B[Write stoichiometric reaction for 1 kmol fuel]
    B --> C[Compute stoichiometric O2 demand nu_O2_stoich]
    C --> D{Select regime}
    D -- phi less than 1 --> E[Lean: actual O2 = nu_O2_stoich / phi; excess O2 in products]
    D -- phi equals 1 --> F[Stoichiometric: products are CO2, H2O, N2 only]
    D -- phi greater than 1 --> G[Rich: actual O2 less than nu_O2_stoich; excess fuel forms C_s or H2]
    E --> H[Tabulate h_f_298 for all species]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute heat of reaction Delta H_rxn at 298 K]
    I --> J[Set up adiabatic energy balance: sum n_P integral C_p dT equals minus Delta H_rxn]
    J --> K[First guess T_ad using mean C_p]
    K --> L[Iterate with NASA-7 polynomial C_p T until energy balance closes]
    L --> M{Is T_ad greater than 1800 K?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Apply dissociation correction; recompute product equilibrium]
    M -- No --> O[Converged: report T_ad]
    N --> O
    O --> P[END]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Variation of T_ad with $\phi$ (Qualitative Trend)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[phi equals 0.0: T_ad equals 298 K, pure air] --> B[phi equals 0.5: T_ad rising, lean regime]
    B --> C[phi equals 0.8: T_ad high, lean]
    C --> D[phi equals 1.0: T_ad local peak, dissociation begins]
    D --> E[phi equals 1.1: T_ad MAXIMUM, dissociation suppressed]
    E --> F[phi equals 1.3: T_ad falling, rich regime]
    F --> G[phi equals 2.0: T_ad much lower, excess fuel dilutes]
    G --> H[phi infinite: T_ad equals 298 K, pure fuel]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in Adiabatic Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reactants: Fuel plus Air at T_in equals 298 K] -- h_R = sum n_R h_f plus sensible --> REAC[Reactant Enthalpy Reservoir]
    REAC -- Adiabatic: Q equals 0, W equals 0 --> COMB[Combustion Zone: Chemical Energy Release]
    COMB -- Energy released minus Delta H_rxn --> PROD[Product Enthalpy Reservoir: h_P = sum n_P h_f plus sensible to T_ad]
    PROD --> TAD[Adiabatic Flame Temperature T_ad]
    TAD --> NOTE1[All chemical energy converted to sensible enthalpy of products]
    NOTE1 --> END[END]
```

### 4.4 Comparison: Constant-Pressure vs. Constant-Volume AFT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CP[Constant Pressure AFT - Open System]
        CP1[SFEE: h_P equals h_R] --> CP2[No work done by gas in flow combustor]
        CP2 --> CP3[CH4 in air, T_ad equals 2222 K]
    end
    subgraph CV[Constant Volume AFT - Closed System]
        CV1[First Law: U_P equals U_R] --> CV2[No boundary work]
        CV2 --> CV3[CH4 in air, T_ad_v equals 2900 to 3000 K]
    end
    CP3 --> COMP[Constant volume AFT is HIGHER]
    CV3 --> COMP
    COMP --> APPL[Application: Explosion pressure P_2 over P_1 equals T_2 over T_1 to the power gamma over gamma minus 1]
```
