## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT). Why does the maximum AFT for hydrocarbon–air combustion occur at an equivalence ratio slightly greater than 1.0 (i.e., on the rich side) rather than exactly at stoichiometric conditions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)** is the temperature attained by the products of combustion when the process is carried out adiabatically ($Q = 0$), with reactants supplied at a specified initial temperature (usually 298 K) and pressure, and the combustion assumed to be complete to stable products (CO$_2$, H$_2$O, N$_2$, etc.). Mathematically, it is the temperature $T_{ad}$ that satisfies the steady-flow energy equation $\sum n_R h_R = \sum n_P h_P(T_{ad})$.

**Reason for off-stoichiometric peak (1.5 marks):**
At very high temperatures (above 1800 K), the combustion products CO$_2$ and H$_2$O begin to **dissociate** endothermically into CO, H$_2$, and O$_2$. This dissociation absorbs a portion of the released chemical energy, lowering the temperature. On the **rich side** ($\phi > 1$), the partial pressure of O$_2$ in the products is essentially zero because all the oxygen is consumed; this **suppresses dissociation** (Le Chatelier's principle) and recovers some of the temperature. On the **lean side** ($\phi < 1$), the excess O$_2$ promotes dissociation, lowering the temperature. The competition between the *dilution effect* of extra air/fuel and the *suppression of dissociation* shifts the maximum to $\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15$.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Distinguish between the constant-pressure and constant-volume adiabatic flame temperatures. State one practical fire-protection scenario in which the constant-volume value is the relevant one.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Constant-Pressure AFT | Constant-Volume AFT |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Thermodynamic process | Isobaric, $P = $ const | Isochoric, $V = $ const |
| Governing equation | $H_P = H_R$ (SFEE) | $U_P = U_R$ (1st Law closed) |
| Work term | $P\,dV$ work done by gas | Zero boundary work |
| Magnitude for CH$_4$/air | ≈ **2222 K** | ≈ **2900–3000 K** |
| Typical application | Furnace, gas turbine, fire plume | Gas explosion, sealed compartment |

**Practical scenario (1 mark):** The constant-volume AFT governs the peak pressure rise in a **gas explosion inside a sealed vessel or room** (e.g., a backdraft event or a methane build-up ignition in a coal mine). Using the ideal-gas isentropic relation, the overpressure is $P_2/P_1 = (T_{ad,v}/T_1)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$, which can exceed 7–8 bar — enough to demolish unreinforced masonry.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **adiabatic flame temperature of methane (CH$_4$) in air at $\phi = 1$** by setting up the first-law energy balance for a steady-flow combustor. Use the mean-$C_p$ approximation to obtain a numerical estimate of $T_{ad}$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The same methane–air mixture is now combusted in a **rigid, insulated bomb** of volume $V = 0.020$ m$^3$ initially at 1.013 bar and 298 K. Estimate the **peak explosion pressure** using the constant-volume AFT and the isentropic relation. Assume products are ideal with $\gamma = 1.32$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric reaction (1 mark):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance equation (1.5 marks):**

For adiabatic, steady-flow combustion ($\dot{Q} = \dot{W} = \dot{KE} = \dot{PE} = 0$), the SFEE gives:

$$\sum_R n_i h_i(T_{in}) \;=\; \sum_P n_j h_j(T_{ad})$$

With reactants at $T_{in} = 298$ K, $h_i = h_{f,i}^{\circ}$, and $h_j = h_{f,j}^{\circ} + \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT$:

$$\sum_P n_j \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT \;=\; \sum_P n_j h_{f,j}^{\circ} \;-\; \sum_R n_i h_{f,i}^{\circ} \;=\; -\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Standard heats of formation (1 mark):**

| Species | $h_f^{\circ}$ (kJ/kmol) |
|:---:|:---:|
| CH$_4$ | $-74{,}850$ |
| CO$_2$ | $-393{,}520$ |
| H$_2$O (g) | $-241{,}820$ |
| O$_2$, N$_2$ | $0$ |

**Step 4 — Heat of reaction (1 mark):**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = [1(-393{,}520) + 2(-241{,}820)] - [1(-74{,}850)]$$
$$= -877{,}160 + 74{,}850 = -802{,}310 \text{ kJ/kmol CH}_4$$

**Step 5 — Mean $C_p$ of products (1.5 marks):**

Using $C_p$ values at ~2200 K: CO$_2$ = 54.3, H$_2$O = 45.2, N$_2$ = 32.3 kJ/(kmol·K).

$$\sum n_P C_p = 1(54.3) + 2(45.2) + 7.52(32.3) = 54.3 + 90.4 + 242.9 = 387.6 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T_{ad}$ (1 mark):**

$$T_{ad} = 298 + \frac{802{,}310}{387.6} \approx 298 + 2070 \approx \mathbf{2368 \text{ K}}$$

(Refined with full $C_p(T)$ iteration: $T_{ad} \approx 2222$ K. Dissociation-corrected: $\approx 1950$ K.)

**Assumptions stated (free distribution across steps):** ideal gas, complete combustion, no dissociation, mean $C_p$, adiabatic walls, no work output, no kinetic energy change.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Constant-volume energy balance (2 marks):**

For adiabatic, rigid, closed system: $U_P(T_{ad,v}) = U_R(T_{in})$.

$$U_P - U_R = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum n_P\!\int_{298}^{T_{ad,v}} C_{v,j}\,dT = -\Delta U_{rxn}^{\circ}$$

For ideal gases, $C_v = C_p - R_u$. Using the mean $C_p$ values from part (a):

$$C_{v,\text{CO}_2} = 54.3 - 8.314 = 45.99, \quad C_{v,\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 45.2 - 8.314 = 36.89$$
$$C_{v,\text{N}_2} = 32.3 - 8.314 = 23.99 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$$

$$\sum n_P C_v = 1(45.99) + 2(36.89) + 7.52(23.99) = 45.99 + 73.78 + 180.4 = 300.2 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 2 — First estimate of $T_{ad,v}$ (1.5 marks):**

For an approximate first-law estimate, we can use the same $\Delta H$ but recognize that at constant volume the energy stays internal. With the relation $\Delta U = \Delta H - \Delta n_{gas} R_u T$:

$\Delta n_{gas} = (1 + 2 + 7.52) - (1 + 2 + 7.52) = 0$ (no change in gas moles for CH$_4$/air at stoichiometric). Therefore $\Delta U \approx \Delta H = -802{,}310$ kJ/kmol.

$$T_{ad,v} \approx 298 + \frac{802{,}310}{300.2} \approx 298 + 2673 \approx \mathbf{2971 \text{ K}}$$

(Compare with $\approx 2900$–$3000$ K for the fully-iterated constant-volume AFT.)

**Step 3 — Compute the peak pressure (2.5 marks):**

Using the isentropic relation for an ideal gas in a rigid container, treating the combustion as equivalent to instantaneous heat addition (the **explosion pressure ratio**):

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{T_{ad,v}}{T_1} \cdot \frac{n_P}{n_R}$$

For stoichiometric CH$_4$/air, $n_P = n_R = 10.52$ kmol, so the mole ratio is unity:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{T_{ad,v}}{T_1} = \frac{2971}{298} \approx 9.97$$

**Alternative (more rigorous) — using $\gamma$ exponent (1 mark):**

$$P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{T_{ad,v}}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = 1.013 \times (9.97)^{1.32/0.32} = 1.013 \times (9.97)^{4.125} \approx 1.013 \times 9200 \approx 9315 \text{ bar}$$

This unrealistic result is because the isentropic formula assumes all gas at uniform $T_{ad,v}$. The correct, more modest estimate using the isochoric-complete-combustion model (no isentropic exponentiation) is:

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx P_1 \cdot \frac{T_{ad,v}}{T_1} \cdot \frac{n_P}{n_R} = 1.013 \times 9.97 \times 1 \approx 10.1 \text{ bar}}$$

So the peak explosion pressure is roughly **10 bar** — about **10 times** atmospheric. This is the level at which standard pressure-vessel relief discs are rated for hydrocarbon explosions.

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**

A 10-fold pressure rise in a sealed room is more than sufficient to shatter windows, blow out doors, and damage masonry. This is why KTU's fire-protection curriculum devotes Module 3 to this exact calculation.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** A stoichiometric mixture of **propane (C$_3$H$_8$) and air** is burned in a furnace at 1 atm with reactants at 298 K. Using the energy balance and the mean-$C_p$ method, compute the **adiabatic flame temperature**. Use the data: $h_f^{\circ}$(C$_3$H$_8$) = $-104{,}700$ kJ/kmol; $h_f^{\circ}$(CO$_2$) = $-393{,}520$ kJ/kmol; $h_f^{\circ}$(H$_2$O, g) = $-241{,}820$ kJ/kmol; $\bar{C}_p$(CO$_2$) = $54.3$, $\bar{C}_p$(H$_2$O) = $45.2$, $\bar{C}_p$(N$_2$) = $32.3$ kJ/(kmol·K). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The same propane–air mixture is now operated at **$\phi = 0.8$** (lean). **Without** doing a full numerical integration, explain **qualitatively** how and why $T_{ad}$ changes compared to stoichiometric. State whether $T_{ad}$ will be higher or lower, and the dominant physical reason. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric reaction (1 mark):**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 + 5(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 18.8\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance (1.5 marks):**

$$\sum n_P \bar{C}_p\,(T_{ad} - 298) = -\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Heat of reaction (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = [3(-393{,}520) + 4(-241{,}820)] - [1(-104{,}700) + 5(0) + 18.8(0)]$$
$$= [-1{,}180{,}560 - 967{,}280] - [-104{,}700]$$
$$= -2{,}147{,}840 + 104{,}700 = -2{,}043{,}140 \text{ kJ/kmol C}_3\text{H}_8$$

This is the LHV of propane.

**Step 4 — Sum of $n_P \bar{C}_p$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\sum n_P \bar{C}_p = 3(54.3) + 4(45.2) + 18.8(32.3)$$
$$= 162.9 + 180.8 + 607.2 = 950.9 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_{ad}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{ad} = 298 + \frac{2{,}043{,}140}{950.9} = 298 + 2149 \approx \mathbf{2447 \text{ K}}$$

(Fully-iterated value with dissociation correction: $\approx 2250$ K; the mean-$C_p$ method gives a *first* estimate of 2447 K.)

**Statement of assumptions (free):** complete combustion, no dissociation, ideal gas, constant mean $C_p$, adiabatic, isobaric, reactants at 298 K.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Recast the problem at $\phi = 0.8$ (1 mark):**

$\lambda = 1/\phi = 1.25$. Excess O$_2$ = $(1.25 - 1) \times 5 = 1.25$ kmol. Extra N$_2$ = $1.25 \times 3.76 \times 5 = 23.5$ kmol — wait, recompute carefully:

- Actual O$_2$ supplied: $5/0.8 = 6.25$ kmol.
- Excess O$_2$ = $6.25 - 5 = 1.25$ kmol.
- N$_2$ from air: $6.25 \times 3.76 = 23.5$ kmol.

**Product moles at $\phi = 0.8$ (1 mark):**
- 3 kmol CO$_2$ + 4 kmol H$_2$O + 1.25 kmol O$_2$ + 23.5 kmol N$_2$ = **31.75 kmol** (vs. 25.8 kmol at $\phi = 1$).

**Step 2 — Energy released (1.5 marks):**

Same amount of fuel is burned as at $\phi = 1$ (all the fuel reacts because we are lean), so $\Delta H_{rxn}$ is the **same** as part (a): 2,043,140 kJ per kmol C$_3$H$_8$.

**Step 3 — Sum of $n_P \bar{C}_p$ at $\phi = 0.8$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\sum n_P \bar{C}_p = 3(54.3) + 4(45.2) + 1.25(33.0) + 23.5(32.3)$$
$$= 162.9 + 180.8 + 41.25 + 759.05 = \mathbf{1144.0 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

(Compare with 950.9 kJ/K at $\phi = 1$. The product heat capacity has **risen by ~20%**.)

**Step 4 — Numerical estimate of $T_{ad}$ at $\phi = 0.8$ (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{ad}(\phi = 0.8) = 298 + \frac{2{,}043{,}140}{1144.0} = 298 + 1786 \approx \mathbf{2084 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and physical interpretation (0.5 mark):**

- At $\phi = 1.0$: $T_{ad} \approx 2447$ K (mean-$C_p$ estimate).
- At $\phi = 0.8$: $T_{ad} \approx 2084$ K (mean-$C_p$ estimate).
- **Drop: ≈ 360 K (~15%).**

**Dominant physical reason (free-distribution):** The lean mixture introduces **1.25 kmol of excess O$_2$ and an additional 4.7 kmol of N$_2$** that did not participate in the reaction but must be heated from 298 K up to $T_{ad}$. This extra inert mass acts as a **thermal diluent** and **absorbs sensible enthalpy** without contributing to the heat release. The released energy (which is essentially unchanged because the same amount of fuel is burned) is therefore spread over a larger product mass with a higher total heat capacity, lowering the final temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3.76 factor for N$_2$** from air. Students often write $1\,\text{O}_2 : 4\,\text{N}_2$ (rounded air composition) and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign error** in the energy balance: writing $\sum n_R h_R - \sum n_P h_P$ when it should be $\sum n_P h_P - \sum n_R h_R = 0$. Worth 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Using the higher heating value (HHV)** instead of LHV, i.e., including the latent heat of condensation of H$_2$O. The LHV is the correct basis when products leave as vapor (the default in AFT calculations).
> 4. **Failing to state assumptions** — for at least 1 mark, the model answer must explicitly list the ideal-gas, complete-combustion, adiabatic, and constant-pressure assumptions.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units consistently** — kJ/kmol with kJ/(kmol·K) for $C_p$ to get K. A frequent slip.
> 6. **Miscounting the stoichiometric O$_2$** for a hydrocarbon — apply the rule $\nu_{O_2} = x + y/4 - z/2$ for C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — KTU PESFT528 Module 3**

- **AFT Definition:** $T_{ad}$ is the temperature when combustion releases its chemical energy entirely as sensible enthalpy of the products, with $Q = W = 0$.
- **Governing equation (open system):** $\sum n_R h_R = \sum n_P h_P$.
- **Governing equation (closed system):** $\sum n_R u_R = \sum n_P u_P$, giving a higher $T_{ad,v}$.
- **Equivalence ratio:** $\phi = (F/A)_{act}/(F/A)_{stoich}$. Lean $\phi<1$, rich $\phi>1$.
- **Stoichiometric O$_2$ demand** for C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$: $\nu_{O_2} = x + y/4 - z/2$.
- **Air composition:** 21% O$_2$, 79% N$_2$; the N$_2$/O$_2$ mole ratio is **3.76**.
- **Mean-$C_p$ shortcut:** $T_{ad} \approx 298 + (-\Delta H_{rxn})/\sum n_P \bar{C}_p$.
- **The $T_{ad}$ vs. $\phi$ curve peaks at $\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15$** (slightly rich), NOT at $\phi = 1$ — because at high $T$, dissociation is suppressed on the rich side.
- **Dissociation reactions** (endothermic): CO$_2 \to$ CO + ½O$_2$; H$_2$O $\to$ H$_2$ + ½O$_2$; H$_2$ $\to$ 2H; O$_2$ $\to$ 2O.
- **Constant-volume AFT > constant-pressure AFT** because no $P\,dV$ work is done.
- **Order of magnitude for CH$_4$/air:** $T_{ad,P} \approx 2222$ K; $T_{ad,V} \approx 2900$ K.
- **Order of magnitude for C$_3$H$_8$/air:** $T_{ad,P} \approx 2250$ K (after dissociation correction).
- **Le Chatelier on rich side:** zero [O$_2$] suppresses CO$_2$/H$_2$O dissociation → more energy retained as sensible heat.
- **Lean side penalty:** excess N$_2$ + O$_2$ dilute the products → more heat capacity to heat.
- **AHFV / LHV of common fuels:** CH$_4$ = 802 MJ/kmol; C$_3$H$_8$ = 2043 MJ/kmol; C$_8$H$_{18}$ = 5074 MJ/kmol (approximate LHV).
- **Explosion overpressure** in a rigid container: $P_2/P_1 \approx T_{ad,v}/T_1$ when moles unchanged.
- **Fire-protection implication:** AFT governs the radiant heat flux from a fire, the convection temperature of a hot smoke layer, and the peak overpressure in a vapour-cloud explosion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar Flame Speed vs. Equivalence Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar Flame Speed vs. Equivalence Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Laminar Flame Speed ($S_L$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Laminar Flame Speed ($S_L$)** is the velocity at which a **one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, unstretched flame front** propagates *relative to the unburned gas mixture* ahead of it, under the idealized assumption of no flow divergence, no preferential diffusion effects on the bulk velocity, and negligible stretch.

- **SI Units:** $\mathbf{m/s}$ (commonly reported in $\mathbf{cm/s}$ in experimental work).
- **Typical Magnitudes:** $\mathbf{0.3 \text{ to } 3.0 \ m/s}$ for most hydrocarbon–air systems.
- **Reference Benchmarks:**
  - Methane ($\text{CH}_4$)–Air: $S_L \approx 0.38 \ m/s$
  - Propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$)–Air: $S_L \approx 0.45 \ m/s$
  - Hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$)–Air: $S_L \approx 2.10 \ m/s$

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** is a dimensionless fuel-to-air normalization parameter that quantifies how the *actual* fuel-oxidizer mass ratio deviates from the *stoichiometric* (chemically ideal) ratio.

The formal expression is:

$$
\phi = \frac{(m_{\text{fuel}}/m_{\text{air}})_{\text{actual}}}{(m_{\text{fuel}}/m_{\text{air}})_{\text{stoichiometric}}}
$$

- $\phi < 1$ → **Lean Mixture** (excess air)
- $\phi = 1$ → **Stoichiometric Mixture** (exact chemical balance)
- $\phi > 1$ → **Rich Mixture** (excess fuel)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toll Booth Highway"

Imagine a highway with two lanes merging into a single toll booth:
- **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** is like the *ratio of cars to toll workers* approaching the booth.
- **Laminar Flame Speed ($S_L$)** is the *rate at which cars clear the booth*.

If you have **too many cars and too few workers** ($\phi > 1$, rich), the queue builds up because the workers (oxidizer) cannot process the cars fast enough. If you have **too many workers and too few cars** ($\phi < 1$, lean), the workers are idle and the throughput is again limited. The **maximum throughput** (peak $S_L$) occurs at an *optimal* — but *not perfectly symmetric* — ratio where the system is best balanced. This is the fundamental reason $S_L$ is a strong function of $\phi$.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Fire Safety Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — PESFT528 (Module 3):**
> The $S_L$–$\phi$ relationship is the *primary input* to all practical fire propagation models (e.g., the Markstein-length framework, the Damköhler-number model, and the eddy-break-up turbulent closure). It governs whether a fire will *propagate*, *flashback*, or *blow-off* in confined and unconfined geometries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Stage Theoretical Framework

The behavior of $S_L$ as $\phi$ varies can be decomposed into three physical regimes:

**Stage 1 — Lean Regime ($\phi < 1$): Thermal Damping by Diluent**
- The *excess air* acts as a **thermal sink** that absorbs sensible heat from the reaction zone.
- This reduces the **adiabatic flame temperature** ($T_{ad}$), which directly lowers the reaction rate via the Arrhenius factor $\exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- Result: $S_L$ decreases monotonically as $\phi$ decreases.

**Stage 2 — Stoichiometric Peak ($\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15$ for hydrocarbons)**
- Combustion is most *thermally efficient* here — maximum heat release per unit mass of mixture.
- The peak is **not** at $\phi = 1.0$ for hydrocarbons; it is shifted **slightly rich**.
- **Physical Reason (Preferential Diffusion):** Hydrocarbon fuels have a higher molecular diffusivity ($\mathcal{D}_{\text{fuel}}$) than oxygen ($\mathcal{D}_{O_2}$). This allows extra fuel to *leak* into the reaction zone, so a slight excess of fuel compensates for the asymmetric transport.
- For hydrogen, the equivalent shift is **lean** ($\phi \approx 0.7 - 0.8$ for peak $S_L$) because $\mathcal{D}_{H_2} \gg \mathcal{D}_{O_2}$.

**Stage 3 — Rich Regime ($\phi > 1$): Oxidizer Starvation**
- Beyond the peak, the **limiting reactant becomes oxygen**.
- Although $T_{ad}$ is still high (rich mixtures are also hot), the *incomplete oxidation* suppresses the volumetric heat-release rate $\dot{Q}'''$.
- Result: $S_L$ decreases, with a more gradual tail than the lean side (because the unburned fuel in the rich case is still combustible downstream).

### 2.2 The Role of Heat-Release Parameter ($\theta$)

The non-dimensional heat-release parameter is a critical scaling variable:

$$
\theta = \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{T_u}
$$

- $T_{ad}$ = adiabatic flame temperature
- $T_u$ = unburned mixture temperature

**Why it matters:** $S_L$ scales roughly as $S_L \propto \sqrt{\theta \cdot \alpha \cdot \tau_{\text{ign}}^{-1}}$ (Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii form), meaning small changes in $T_{ad}$ due to varying $\phi$ produce large changes in $S_L$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are exam-grade. Memorize the units, the boundary conditions, and the signs of the partial derivatives.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = (m_f/m_a)_{\text{act}} \ / \ (m_f/m_a)_{\text{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio definition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\phi = \left( \frac{\nu_{O_2}}{\nu_{O_2,\text{stoich}}} \right)^{-1} \cdot \left( \frac{X_f}{1-X_f} \right)_{\text{actual}}$ | φ in terms of mole fraction $X_f$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $S_L = \dot{m}'' / \rho_u$ | Mass burning flux $\dot{m}''$ over unburned density | $\mathbf{m/s}$ |
| 4 | $\rho_u \, S_L = \rho_b \, S_b$ | Mass-flux continuity across flame | $\mathbf{kg/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ |
| 5 | $\theta = (T_{ad} - T_u)/T_u$ | Heat-release parameter | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_L \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \lambda \, \dot{\omega}''_{\text{ign}} \, \alpha}{\rho_u^2 \, c_p^2 \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2}}$ | Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii form | $\mathbf{m/s}$ |
| 7 | $\alpha = \lambda / (\rho \, c_p)$ | Thermal diffusivity of unburned gas | $\mathbf{m^2/s}$ |
| 8 | $Le = \alpha / \mathcal{D}$ | Lewis number (thermal vs. mass diffusivity) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\delta_L = \alpha / S_L$ | Laminar flame thickness (preheat zone) | $\mathbf{m}$ |
| 10 | $u_t = S_L \cdot \phi_{\text{Damköhler}}^{1/2}$ | Turbulent flame speed (eddy break-up limit) | $\mathbf{m/s}$ |

**Critical Reminder on Symbols:**
- $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity, $c_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure, $\rho$ = density, $\mathcal{D}$ = mass diffusivity, $E_a$ = activation energy, $\dot{\omega}''_{\text{ign}}$ = ignition reaction rate.
- Do **NOT** confuse $\phi$ (equivalence ratio) with $\phi_{\text{Damköhler}}$ (Damköhler number). KTU examiners *will* test this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Safety Design:** $S_L(\phi)$ data feeds the **Babrauskas empirical correlation** for heat release rate $\dot{Q} = \chi \cdot \dot{m}'' \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c$, where $\chi$ depends on the local $\phi$.
- **Gas Turbine Combustors:** Engineers deliberately operate at $\phi \approx 0.4 - 0.6$ (highly lean) to suppress $S_L$ and avoid flashback, even at the cost of flame stability.
- **IC Engine Knock Prediction:** Octane ratings are tied to how $S_L$ varies with $\phi$ under end-gas auto-ignition conditions.
- **Mine Safety:** Methane accumulation shifts the local $\phi$; knowing $S_L(\phi)$ allows prediction of deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Equivalence Ratio from Mole Fractions

**Problem Setup:** For a binary fuel-air mixture with fuel mole fraction $X_f$ and oxidizer mole fraction $X_{ox} = 1 - X_f$, derive the expression for $\phi$ in terms of $X_f$ and the stoichiometric coefficients.

**Step 1:** The global combustion reaction for a generic fuel $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$ is:

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \nu_{O_2} \left( \text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2 \right) \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\,\nu_{O_2}\,\text{N}_2
$$

where $\nu_{O_2} = x + y/4$ is the stoichiometric oxygen requirement (in moles).

**Step 2:** By definition, the equivalence ratio compares the *actual* fuel-oxygen mole ratio to the *stoich* fuel-oxygen mole ratio:

$$
\phi = \frac{(n_f / n_{O_2})_{\text{actual}}}{(n_f / n_{O_2})_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Inversion — since $(n_f/n_{O_2})_{\text{stoich}} = 1/\nu_{O_2}$:

$$
\phi = \nu_{O_2} \cdot \left( \frac{n_f}{n_{O_2}} \right)_{\text{actual}}
$$

**Step 4:** Express $n_f / n_{O_2}$ in terms of mole fractions. For a binary mixture of fuel and air (where air is taken as $X_f$ fuel and $1-X_f$ air):

$$
\left( \frac{n_f}{n_{O_2}} \right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{X_f}{0.21 \cdot (1 - X_f)}
$$

(The factor 0.21 is the oxygen mole fraction in atmospheric air.)

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$
\phi = \frac{\nu_{O_2}}{0.21} \cdot \frac{X_f}{1 - X_f}
$$

This is the final form KTU expects in derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Continuity Across a Steady 1-D Flame

**Problem Setup:** Show that $\rho_u \, S_L = \rho_b \, S_b$ for a 1-D adiabatic flame.

**Step 1:** Apply conservation of mass in a control volume enclosing the flame. For steady flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}
$$

**Step 2:** The unburned gas approaches the flame at speed $S_L$ (the *displacement speed* of the flame in the unburned-gas reference frame) through area $A$:

$$
\dot{m}_u = \rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot A
$$

**Step 3:** The burned gas leaves at speed $S_b$ on the other side:

$$
\dot{m}_b = \rho_b \cdot S_b \cdot A
$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two:

$$
\rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot A = \rho_b \cdot S_b \cdot A
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $A$ and solve:

$$
\boxed{\rho_u \, S_L = \rho_b \, S_b}
$$

This is the **flame-speed continuity condition**. Since $\rho_b \ll \rho_u$ (gas expands dramatically upon heating), $S_b \gg S_L$ — burned gases flow *outward* much faster than the flame front propagates *inward*. This is why a Bunsen burner flame is taller than the tube from which gas issues.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii Scaling for $S_L$

**Problem Setup:** Derive the asymptotic scaling of $S_L$ using activation-energy asymptotics.

**Step 1:** Write the energy equation for the preheat zone of the flame. The heat conduction flux into the preheat zone is $\lambda \, dT/dx$, and the convective transport is $\rho_u \, c_p \, S_L \, (T - T_u)$:

$$
\lambda \frac{dT}{dx} = \rho_u \, c_p \, S_L \, (T - T_u)
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate this first-order ODE from $T_u$ (at $x = -\infty$) to $T_{ad}$ (at the reaction sheet, $x = 0$). The solution is the familiar exponential profile:

$$
T(x) - T_u = (T_{ad} - T_u) \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{\rho_u c_p S_L x}{\lambda} \right) \quad \text{for } x < 0
$$

**Step 3:** The preheat-zone thickness is:

$$
\delta_L = \frac{\lambda}{\rho_u \, c_p \, S_L} = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}
$$

This confirms the table entry.

**Step 4:** Match the heat flux at the reaction sheet. The conductive heat flux from the burned to the unburned side is:

$$
q'' = \lambda \left. \frac{dT}{dx} \right|_{x=0^-} = \lambda \cdot \frac{\rho_u c_p S_L (T_{ad} - T_u)}{\lambda} = \rho_u c_p S_L (T_{ad} - T_u)
$$

**Step 5:** The heat-release rate in the thin reaction zone is:

$$
q'' = \int_{-\delta_r}^{+\delta_r} \dot{\omega}''_{\text{ign}} \, dx
$$

For an Arrhenius rate $\dot{\omega}''_{\text{ign}} = B \exp(-E_a/RT)$, the integral over the thin reaction zone gives:

$$
\int \dot{\omega}''_{\text{ign}} \, dx \approx \frac{\lambda \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2 \, E_a \, B \, \exp(-E_a / R T_{ad})}{R \, T_{ad}^3}
$$

**Step 6:** Equate the two expressions for $q''$ and solve for $S_L$:

$$
S_L = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \lambda \, B \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2 \, E_a \, \exp(-E_a/RT_{ad})}{\rho_u^2 \, c_p^2 \, R \, T_{ad}^3}}
$$

In compact form:

$$
\boxed{S_L \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \alpha \, \tau_{\text{chem}}^{-1} \cdot \theta}{\text{function of }E_a/RT_{ad}}}}
$$

where $\tau_{\text{chem}}$ is the characteristic chemical time at $T_{ad}$.

**Step 7:** **Interpretation — Why $\phi$ matters:** Since $T_{ad}$ and $\theta$ are *strong* functions of $\phi$, even small changes in $\phi$ produce exponential changes in $S_L$ through the $\exp(-E_a/RT_{ad})$ term. This is the analytical origin of the **bell-shape** of $S_L(\phi)$ curves.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How $S_L$ Reacts to $\phi$ for Common Fuels

| **Fuel** | **Peak $S_L$ (m/s)** | **$\phi$ at Peak** | **Lean Limit $\phi_{\text{lean}}$** | **Rich Limit $\phi_{\text{rich}}$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Methane $\text{CH}_4$ | 0.38 | $\approx 1.08$ | 0.50 | 1.70 |
| Propane $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | 0.45 | $\approx 1.05$ | 0.51 | 1.80 |
| n-Heptane $\text{C}_7\text{H}_{16}$ | 0.48 | $\approx 1.10$ | 0.50 | 1.85 |
| Hydrogen $\text{H}_2$ | 2.10 | $\approx 0.75$ | 0.10 | 4.50 |
| Carbon Monoxide $\text{CO}$ | 0.52 | $\approx 0.55$ | 0.20 | 3.00 |

*(Values typical for $T_u = 298$ K, $p = 1$ atm; data rounded to KTU-relevant significant figures.)*

**Key Takeaway:** The peak $S_L$ for **hydrocarbons is rich-shifted**; for **hydrogen it is lean-shifted**; for **CO it is strongly lean-shifted**. The direction of the shift is governed by the Lewis number $Le$ — fuels with $Le < 1$ (high $\mathcal{D}$) peak on the rich side, fuels with $Le > 1$ peak on the lean side.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The $S_L$ vs. $\phi$ Functional Dependence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel-Air Mixture] --> B{Compute phi}
    B -->|"phi < 1"| C[Lean Regime]
    B -->|"phi = 1"| D[Stoichiometric]
    B -->|"phi > 1"| E[Rich Regime]
    C --> F[Excess air acts as thermal sink]
    F --> G[Tad decreases]
    G --> H[Arrhenius rate falls]
    H --> I[SL decreases monotonically]
    D --> J[Maximum Tad peak]
    J --> K[Maximum heat release per unit mass]
    E --> L[Preferential diffusion of fuel]
    L --> M[Effective fuel excess at flame front]
    M --> N[SL peak shifts rich phi approx 1.05 to 1.15]
    I --> O[Combine into SL versus phi curve]
    K --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Bell-shaped curve with rich-shifted peak for hydrocarbons]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of $S_L$ Sensitivity to $\phi$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fuel Composition] --> B1[Compute Stoichiometric Air Requirement]
    B1 --> C1[Local Mixture Composition Xf]
    C1 --> D1[Equivalence Ratio phi]
    A2[Unburned Temperature Tu] --> B2[Adiabatic Flame Temperature Tad]
    B1 --> B2
    D1 --> B2
    B2 --> C2[Heat Release Parameter theta]
    A3[Pressure p and Lewis Number Le] --> B3[Transport Properties alpha lambda D]
    C2 --> D3[Reaction Rate omega via Arrhenius]
    B3 --> D3
    D3 --> E3[Zeldovich Scaling SL]
    B3 --> E3
    E3 --> F3[Final SL output m per s]
    F3 --> G3[Fire Propagation Model Input]
```

### 4.3 Reading the Diagrams

- **Diagram 4.1** traces the *physical chain of causality* that produces the bell-shaped $S_L(\phi)$ curve.
- **Diagram 4.2** emphasizes the *interlocking dependencies*: composition, temperature, transport, and chemistry all feed into $S_L$. KTU examiners often ask students to identify **which block** a perturbation acts on (e.g., "If the air is preheated by 100 K, which block changes?").

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PESFT528]**
*Define Laminar Flame Speed. State two factors on which it depends.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Laminar Flame Speed ($S_L$)** is the velocity at which a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, unstretched flame front propagates relative to the unburned gas mixture. It is expressed in m/s.
>
> It depends on: (1) the **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** of the mixture, and (2) the **Unburned Gas Temperature ($T_u$)** and **Pressure ($p$)**. A third influencing factor is the **Lewis Number ($Le$)** of the fuel-oxidizer pair.

*(Examiner's note: Award full marks only if the student explicitly mentions the reference frame "relative to the unburned gas" — KTU key insists on this phrasing.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PESFT528]**
*Why does the laminar flame speed of hydrocarbon-air mixtures peak at a slightly rich equivalence ratio ($\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15$) rather than exactly at $\phi = 1$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The peak occurs slightly rich because of **preferential diffusion**: hydrocarbon fuels have a higher molecular mass diffusivity ($\mathcal{D}_{\text{fuel}}$) than oxygen ($\mathcal{D}_{O_2}$). This allows extra fuel molecules to leak from the unburned to the burned side, effectively enriching the reaction zone. To compensate, the bulk mixture must be slightly fuel-rich so that the *local* ratio at the reaction sheet is closer to stoichiometric. Hence the *bulk* equivalence ratio at peak $S_L$ is shifted to $\phi > 1$.

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on $S_L(\phi)$ Questions:**
> 1. Confusing **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** with the **Damköhler Number (Da)** — KTU board deducts **2 marks** for this symbol ambiguity.
> 2. Forgetting the **reference frame** of $S_L$ (it is measured *relative to the unburned gas*, not the lab frame).
> 3. Mixing up **Lewis Number (Le)** and **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** in the preferential-diffusion argument.
> 4. Writing $\phi < 1$ as "rich" instead of "lean" — a fatal sign error.
> 5. Omitting units in numerical substitution — KTU deducts **0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the relation $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ for a 1-D steady adiabatic flame. Discuss the physical significance of this relation with reference to Bunsen-burner flame heights. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For methane–air combustion, the following data are given:
- $S_L = 0.38 \ m/s$ at $\phi = 1.0$
- $\rho_u = 1.20 \ kg/m^3$
- $T_{ad} = 2225 \ K$, $T_u = 298 \ K$
- $c_p = 1200 \ J/(kg \cdot K)$

Calculate:
1. The mass burning rate per unit area $\dot{m}''$.
2. The heat-release parameter $\theta$.
3. The flame thickness $\delta_L$ (take $\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-5} \ m^2/s$).
4. Comment on the trend of $S_L$ when $\phi$ is varied from 0.6 to 1.4. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply conservation of mass to a control volume enclosing the 1-D flame at steady state:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}}
$$

**Step 2:** The unburned gas enters the CV at velocity $S_L$ through area $A$:

$$
\dot{m}_u = \rho_u \, S_L \, A
$$

**Step 3:** The burned gas leaves at velocity $S_b$:

$$
\dot{m}_b = \rho_b \, S_b \, A
$$

**Step 4:** Equate:

$$
\rho_u \, S_L = \rho_b \, S_b
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $S_b$:

$$
S_b = S_L \cdot \frac{\rho_u}{\rho_b}
$$

**Step 6 (Significance):** Since $\rho_b \ll \rho_u$ (gas expands due to heating), $S_b \gg S_L$. In a Bunsen burner, the burned gas velocity $S_b$ is what determines the *visible flame height*, not $S_L$. Hence the flame is much taller than the burner tube exit.

**Conclusion:** The continuity condition $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ explains why visible Bunsen-burner flame heights scale as $h \propto S_b \cdot D_{\text{tube}}$, where $D_{\text{tube}}$ is the inner tube diameter.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Mass burning rate per unit area:**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \rho_u \cdot S_L = 1.20 \ kg/m^3 \times 0.38 \ m/s = 0.456 \ kg/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

**(ii) Heat-release parameter:**

$$
\theta = \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{T_u} = \frac{2225 - 298}{298} = \frac{1927}{298} \approx 6.47
$$

**(iii) Flame thickness:**

$$
\delta_L = \frac{\alpha}{S_L} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5} \ m^2/s}{0.38 \ m/s} \approx 5.79 \times 10^{-5} \ m = 57.9 \ \mu m
$$

**(iv) Trend of $S_L$ with $\phi$ from 0.6 to 1.4:**

- For $\phi = 0.6$ (lean): Excess air absorbs heat → $T_{ad}$ lower → $S_L$ is small.
- For $\phi = 1.0$ (stoichiometric): $S_L$ is near-maximum.
- For $\phi = 1.1$ (slightly rich): $S_L$ reaches the **peak** (≈ 0.40 m/s) due to preferential diffusion of methane.
- For $\phi = 1.4$ (rich): Oxidizer becomes limiting; $S_L$ decreases gradually.

**Conclusion:** The curve is **bell-shaped** with the peak shifted slightly rich.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the variation of laminar flame speed with equivalence ratio for a typical hydrocarbon–air system. Explain the physical reason for the rich-shifted peak. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A propane–air mixture at $\phi = 0.9$ has $S_L = 0.40 \ m/s$. The mixture temperature is raised from 298 K to 450 K (preheat). Using the empirical correlation $S_L \propto T_u^{1.75}$ (Metghalchi–Keck), estimate the new $S_L$. Comment on the safety implication for fire propagation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Sketch Description (KTU expects both a verbal sketch and a written trend):**

- The curve is **bell-shaped**, with $S_L$ rising from zero at the lean flammability limit ($\phi \approx 0.51$ for propane), reaching a maximum of ≈ 0.45 m/s at $\phi \approx 1.05$, and falling back to zero at the rich flammability limit ($\phi \approx 1.80$).
- The lean-side slope is **steeper** than the rich-side slope.

**Physical Reason for the Rich-Shifted Peak:**

> Hydrocarbon fuel molecules diffuse faster than oxygen molecules (i.e., $\mathcal{D}_{\text{fuel}} > \mathcal{D}_{O_2}$). This causes preferential diffusion of fuel into the reaction zone. Therefore, a *bulk* mixture that is slightly rich ($\phi > 1$) has a *local* mixture at the reaction sheet that is closer to stoichiometric. This local stoichiometry maximizes the volumetric heat-release rate, which maximizes $S_L$.

**Mathematical Justification (Zeldovich scaling):**

$$
S_L \propto \sqrt{\alpha \cdot \dot{\omega}''(T_{ad}) \cdot \theta}
$$

Both $T_{ad}$ and the local $\dot{\omega}''$ are maximized when the reaction-zone mixture is stoichiometric. Preferential diffusion shifts the bulk $\phi$ required for this condition from 1.0 to ≈ 1.05–1.15.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Apply the Metghalchi–Keck correlation:

$$
S_L(T_u) = S_L(T_{u,0}) \cdot \left( \frac{T_u}{T_{u,0}} \right)^{1.75}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:

$$
S_L(450 \ K) = 0.40 \ m/s \cdot \left( \frac{450}{298} \right)^{1.75}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{450}{298} \approx 1.510
$$

**Step 4:** Raise to the 1.75 power:

$$
(1.510)^{1.75} = \exp(1.75 \cdot \ln 1.510) = \exp(1.75 \cdot 0.4121) = \exp(0.7212) \approx 2.057
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the new $S_L$:

$$
S_L(450 \ K) = 0.40 \ m/s \cdot 2.057 \approx 0.823 \ m/s
$$

**Step 6 — Safety Implication:**

> The laminar flame speed has more than **doubled** (from 0.40 to 0.82 m/s) due to the preheat. In a fire scenario, this means:
> - The flame front will propagate **~2× faster** through the unburned mixture.
> - The **deflagration pressure rise** scales roughly as $S_L^2$, so a 2× increase in $S_L$ can produce a **4× increase in overpressure** in confined geometries.
> - Preheated layers near ceilings or hot surfaces are therefore *critical hotspots* for accelerated fire spread — a key design consideration in tunnel and high-rise fire safety.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **$S_L$ is a velocity relative to the unburned gas** — never the lab frame. (KTU-favorite trap question.)
- **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** is dimensionless: $\phi < 1$ = lean, $\phi = 1$ = stoich, $\phi > 1$ = rich. Mixing up the inequality direction is a **fatal sign error**.
- **The $S_L(\phi)$ curve is bell-shaped** for hydrocarbons, with the peak slightly **rich** ($\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.15$) due to **preferential fuel diffusion**.
- For **hydrogen**, the peak is shifted **lean** ($\phi \approx 0.7 - 0.8$) because $Le_{H_2} \ll 1$.
- The **continuity condition** $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ is the most-rigorous way to derive gas expansion in flames.
- **Heat-release parameter** $\theta = (T_{ad} - T_u)/T_u$ is the non-dimensional anchor of the Zeldovich scaling.
- **Flame thickness** $\delta_L = \alpha / S_L$ gives physical scales of $10 - 100 \ \mu m$ for hydrocarbon-air flames.
- **Higher $T_u$** → higher $S_L$ (e.g., Metghalchi–Keck $T_u^{1.75}$ scaling) — the core reason preheat is dangerous in fire engineering.
- **Higher pressure** → lower $S_L$ for most hydrocarbons (sub-linear scaling) — important for high-pressure fire scenarios like turbine enclosures.
- Do **not** confuse $\phi$ (equivalence ratio) with $\phi_{\text{Da}}$ (Damköhler number); KTU will mark this strictly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar Flame Speed for Various Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar Flame Speed for Various Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laminar Flame Speed (Symbol: $S_L$ or $S_u^0$)** is defined as the speed at which a planar, one-dimensional, adiabatic, premixed flame front propagates *relative to the unburned gas mixture* ahead of it, under the conditions of negligible flame stretch, uniform inlet flow, and zero geometric curvature. The SI unit is **m/s**, and it is a fundamental thermochemical-transport property of the fuel–oxidizer–diluent mixture at a specified initial temperature ($T_u$), pressure ($P$), and equivalence ratio ($\phi$).

In the KTU 2024 scheme notation:
- $S_L$ — **Laminar burning velocity** (also called *fundamental flame speed*).
- $S_T$ — **Laminar flame speed** (sometimes interchangeably used, but in KTU notation $S_T$ denotes the *stretched* flame speed; $S_L$ is the unstretched, intrinsic value).
- $S_b$ — **Burning velocity relative to burned gas**.

The two velocities are related by a mass balance across the flame:

$$
\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b
$$

where $\rho_u$ and $\rho_b$ are the unburned and burned gas densities respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU calls it $S_L$ and not $S_u$:** The classical Russian/Eastern-European literature (Zeldovich, Frank-Kamenetskii) uses $S_n$ (normal speed) and $S_u$ (burning velocity). KTU's 2024 scheme adopts the Western combustion literature convention where $S_L$ is the *laminar* (as opposed to *turbulent*) flame speed, with subscript $L$ denoting *Laminar*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt of Combustion"

Imagine a perfectly flat, infinitely wide sandwich of fuel and air resting on a moving conveyor belt. You light the right edge of the sandwich. A thin, glowing **reaction zone** (the crust) travels leftward at a steady pace. The speed of the crust relative to the *unlit, raw sandwich* ahead of it is the **laminar flame speed**.

Key visual cues from the analogy:
- The crust thickness ≈ the **flame thickness** $\delta_L$ (typically 0.1–1 mm).
- The belt is perfectly flat → no curvature → **zero stretch**.
- The bread is uniform everywhere → **1-D adiabatic**.
- The speed is intrinsic to the ingredients (bread + filling), not to how hard you push the belt.

In real flames:
- Hydrogen–air mixtures can run this "belt" at $S_L \approx 2.5$–$3.0$ m/s.
- Methane–air at stoichiometry crawls at $S_L \approx 0.37$ m/s.
- Ultra-lean propane barely propagates at $S_L \approx 0.05$ m/s near the lean flammability limit.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric methane–air $S_L$ | $S_L^0$ | **0.37 m/s** at 300 K, 1 atm | Benchmark value |
| Stoichiometric propane–air $S_L$ | $S_L^0$ | **0.40 m/s** at 300 K, 1 atm | Slightly higher than CH₄ |
| Stoichiometric hydrogen–air $S_L$ | $S_L^0$ | **2.80 m/s** at 300 K, 1 atm | Highest among common fuels |
| Stoichiometric acetylene–air $S_L$ | $S_L^0$ | **1.58 m/s** at 300 K, 1 atm | Very fast, soot-prone |
| Stoichiometric n-heptane–air $S_L$ | $S_L^0$ | **0.36 m/s** at 300 K, 1 atm | Reference for engine knock |
| Laminar flame thickness (CH₄–air) | $\delta_L$ | **0.4–0.5 mm** | $\delta_L = \alpha_u / S_L$ |
| Thermal diffusivity of air | $\alpha_u$ | **$2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s** | At 300 K, 1 atm |
| Universal gas constant | $R_u$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | |
| Adiabatic flame temperature (CH₄–air) | $T_{ad}$ | **2222 K** | At $\phi = 1.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 highlight:** When the question says "for various fuels", examiners expect you to know (i) the *absolute* value at $\phi = 1$ for at least four fuels, and (ii) the *shape* of $S_L(\phi)$ — symmetric for high-Lewis-number fuels, asymmetric for low-Lewis-number fuels like H₂.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Hierarchy of Flame Speed Models

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3) requires students to know the following five levels of theoretical analysis, listed in order of increasing rigor:

### Level 1 — Thermal Theory (Mallard–Le Chatelier, 1881)

The flame is treated as a **jump discontinuity** that conducts heat from the burned gas into the unburned gas at rate governed by the thermal diffusivity $\alpha_u$.

**Governing equation (1-D energy equation for unburned gas):**

$$
\rho_u c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \lambda_u \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}
$$

**Two boundary conditions:**

- At $x = 0$ (post-flame): $T = T_{ad}$ (adiabatic flame temperature).
- At $x \to \infty$: $T = T_u$ (unburned temperature).
- At $x = 0$: $- \lambda_u (dT/dx)_0 = \rho_u c_p S_L (T_{ad} - T_u)$.

**Result of integration (Mallard–Le Chatelier):**

$$
S_L = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha_u}{\tau_{ig}}}
$$

where $\tau_{ig}$ is the **ignition delay time** — the time a cold gas pocket must spend inside the heated region to reach auto-ignition. This is a rough but historically important estimate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it's a KTU favorite:** Mallard–Le Chatelier has only *one* input that needs experiment (the chemistry lumped into $\tau_{ig}$). It is the model you quote when the examiner asks for a *qualitative* estimate of $S_L$.

### Level 2 — Thermal + Activation-Energy Asymptotics (Zeldovich, 1948)

The reactant is consumed by a one-step Arrhenius reaction. The preheat zone is thin compared with the reaction zone, but the *activation energy* $E_a$ is very large (typical $E_a/RT_{ad} \gg 1$).

**Key result (Zeldovich–Frank–Kamenetskii):**

$$
S_L^2 \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right)
$$

**More explicit form (using adiabatic flame temperature ratio $\theta = T_{ad}/T_u$):**

$$
S_L \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \lambda_u \, c_p \, \Lambda \, \beta}{\rho_u \, c_p \, \beta}} \cdot \text{function}(E_a/R_uT_{ad})
$$

In the limit $E_a \to \infty$:

$$
S_L \sim \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\lambda_u \, c_p \, \Lambda}{\rho_u c_p}} \cdot \frac{1}{\delta_L} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{2 R_u T_{ad}}\right)
$$

where $\Lambda$ is a pre-exponential factor.

### Level 3 — Quasi-1-D Theory with Finite-Rate Chemistry (Mallard–Le Chatelier Revisited)

The flame structure is divided into two zones:
- **Preheat zone** (thickness $\delta_P$): heat conduction dominates, no reaction.
- **Reaction zone** (thickness $\delta_R$): Arrhenius reaction dominates.

$$
\delta_L = \delta_P + \delta_R \approx \delta_P \quad (\text{since } \delta_R \ll \delta_P)
$$

The energy balance across the reaction sheet gives:

$$
\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \rho_u S_L Y_F Q_c
$$

where $Y_F$ is the mass fraction of fuel and $Q_c$ is the heat of combustion per kg of fuel. This just confirms energy conservation; the *rate* comes from matching the gradient at the sheet.

### Level 4 — Lewis-Number Effects (Markstein, Clavin, Joulin)

The flame speed is sensitive to the **Lewis number**:

$$
Le = \frac{\alpha_u}{D_u} = \frac{\lambda_u / (\rho_u c_p)}{D_u}
$$

For $Le \ne 1$, the flame is **non-adiabatic in composition space** — heat and mass diffuse at different rates.

- $Le > 1$ (heavy hydrocarbons, e.g., propane, $Le \approx 1.85$): heat diffuses faster than mass → flame is **thermo-diffusively stable**.
- $Le < 1$ (hydrogen, $Le \approx 0.3$): mass diffuses faster than heat → flame is **thermo-diffusively unstable**, especially on the lean side.

**Markstein length $\mathcal{L}$ quantifies stretch sensitivity:**

$$
S_T = S_L - \mathcal{L} \cdot \kappa
$$

where $\kappa$ is the **stretch rate** (sum of strain and curvature rates). For $Le < 1$ fuels (hydrogen), $\mathcal{L} < 0$ on the lean side — a hallmark of **diffusive-thermal instability**.

### Level 5 — Detailed Chemistry (the "Real" Answer)

Real flames involve hundreds of elementary reactions. Tools used: CHEMKIN, Cantera, FlameMaster. The KTU syllabus does *not* require a student to run these, but you should be able to **interpret** the output.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the 2024 KTU examiner can ask on this topic. Memorize the bolded equations. CRITICAL: I am using `\vert` and `\mid` for all absolute-value and norm symbols so the markdown table does not break.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ | Mass conservation across flame | kg/(m²·s) | ★★★ |
| 2 | $S_L = \sqrt{\alpha_u / \tau_{ig}}$ | Mallard–Le Chatelier (thermal) | m/s | ★★★ |
| 3 | $S_L^2 \propto \exp(-E_a / R_u T_{ad})$ | Zeldovich activation-energy asymptote | m²/s² | ★★★ |
| 4 | $\delta_L = \alpha_u / S_L$ | Pre-heat zone thickness | m | ★★ |
| 5 | $Le = \alpha_u / D_u$ | Lewis number | — | ★★★ |
| 6 | $S_T = S_L - \mathcal{L} \cdot \kappa$ | Markstein stretch relation | m/s | ★★★ |
| 7 | $\mathcal{L} / \delta_L = f(Le)$ | Markstein–Lewis functional form | — | ★★ |
| 8 | $S_L(\phi) = S_L^{\max} \, f(\phi; a, b)$ | Gaussian / polylog fit to data | m/s | ★★ |
| 9 | $S_L(T_u) = S_L^{\text{ref}} \, (T_u / T_{\text{ref}})^{\alpha}$ | Power-law temperature dependence | m/s | ★★★ |
| 10 | $S_L(P) = S_L^{\text{ref}} \, (P / P_{\text{ref}})^{\beta}$ | Power-law pressure dependence | m/s | ★★★ |
| 11 | $S_L(Y_{\text{dil}}) = S_L^0 \, (1 - 2.1 \, Y_{\text{dil}})$ | Metghalchi–Keck diluent correlation | m/s | ★★ |
| 12 | $\phi_m = (Z + 1) / (Z \sqrt{\dots})$ | Equivalence ratio of max $S_L$ | — | ★★ |
| 13 | $S_b = S_L \cdot T_{ad} / T_u$ | Burned-gas flame speed | m/s | ★★ |
| 14 | $\text{Re}_f = S_L L / \nu_u$ | Flame Reynolds number | — | ★★ |
| 15 | $\tau_{chem} = \delta_L / S_L$ | Chemical residence time | s | ★★ |

**Temperature exponent** $\alpha$ (typical values for various fuels):
- **Methane:** $\alpha \approx 2.0$
- **Propane:** $\alpha \approx 1.7$
- **Hydrogen:** $\alpha \approx 1.5$
- **n-Heptane:** $\alpha \approx 1.8$

**Pressure exponent** $\beta$ (typical values):
- **Methane:** $\beta \approx -0.5$ (negative, so $S_L$ decreases with $P$)
- **Propane:** $\beta \approx -0.4$
- **Hydrogen:** $\beta \approx -0.5$ (also decreases, weakly)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** A fire-safety engineer uses the formula $S_L(T_u, P, Y_{\text{dil}})$ to predict whether a flame in a coal-mine tunnel will accelerate or quench when ventilation humidity rises. A gas-turbine designer uses the same to size the flashback arrestor. The Markstein number governs the **Darrieus–Landau instability** — the cellular structure you see on a Bunsen-burner cone.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

1. **Gas Turbine Combustors**: $S_L$ sets the minimum residence time; flashback occurs if the inlet velocity falls below $S_L$.
2. **Fire Safety**: $S_L$ controls the **mass burning rate** $m'' = \rho_u S_L$ in a pool fire, hence the heat-release rate.
3. **SI / HCCI Engines**: The pressure dependence $S_L \propto P^\beta$ governs knock propensity.
4. **Hydrogen Safety**: The very high $S_L$ and $Le < 1$ nature of H₂–air flames is why hydrogen cars need **flame arrestors** with sub-millisecond quenching.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mallard–Le Chatelier Formula (Full, Exhaustive)

### Step 0: Set up the physical picture

A 1-D, planar, steady flame is anchored at the origin. Unburned gas (subscript $u$) flows in from $x = -\infty$ at velocity $S_L$ and exits as burned gas at $x = +\infty$. The reaction is modelled as a **single irreversible step** with finite ignition delay $\tau_{ig}$.

**Assumption (Mallard–Le Chatelier):** A gas element ignites the moment it has spent a time $\tau_{ig}$ in the heated preheat zone. Thus the flame speed equals the preheat-zone length divided by $\tau_{ig}$:

$$
S_L = \frac{\delta_P}{\tau_{ig}}
$$

### Step 1: Write the energy equation in the moving frame

In the coordinate system where the flame is stationary, the steady-state 1-D energy equation in the preheat zone (no reaction) is:

$$
\rho_u c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx} - \lambda_u \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\lambda_u \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = \rho_u c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

### Step 2: Non-dimensionalise

Define $\theta = (T - T_u) / (T_{ad} - T_u)$ (ranges from 0 to 1 across the preheat zone), and the **thermal-diffusion length** $\delta_{th} = \lambda_u / (\rho_u c_p S_L) = \alpha_u / S_L$.

Substituting:

$$
\delta_{th}^2 \frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} = \delta_{th} \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

Let $\xi = x / \delta_{th}$, so:

$$
\frac{d^2 \theta}{d\xi^2} = \frac{d\theta}{d\xi}
$$

### Step 3: Solve the ODE

The general solution is:

$$
\theta(\xi) = C_1 e^{\xi} + C_2
$$

Apply the boundary conditions:
- $\xi \to -\infty$ (far unburned): $\theta \to 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$ (since $e^{\xi} \to 0$).
- $\xi = 0$ (at the reaction sheet): $\theta = 1$ (full adiabatic temperature).

Wait — we need to re-think. The physically correct sign convention is that $x$ is positive downstream. With $C_1 = 0$ from the upstream condition, the solution cannot satisfy $\theta(0) = 1$. We must adopt a *coordinate convention* in which $x$ points **into the unburned gas** (against the flow). Then the ODE becomes:

$$
\delta_{th}^2 \frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} = -\delta_{th} \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

with the same general solution $\theta = C_1 e^{-\xi} + C_2$, but now:
- $\xi \to +\infty$: $\theta \to 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.
- $\xi = 0$: $\theta = 1 \Rightarrow C_2 = 1$.

This gives $\theta(\xi) = e^{-\xi} = \exp(-x / \delta_{th})$. So the temperature profile is a **pure exponential decay** from $T_{ad}$ at the flame to $T_u$ far upstream.

### Step 4: Read off the preheat-zone thickness

By definition, the preheat-zone thickness is the $e$-folding length of this exponential:

$$
\delta_P = \delta_{th} = \frac{\alpha_u}{S_L}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the ignition-delay criterion

The residence time of a gas element in the preheat zone is:

$$
\tau_{ig} = \frac{\delta_P}{S_L} = \frac{\alpha_u}{S_L^2}
$$

Solving for $S_L$:

$$
S_L^2 = \frac{\alpha_u}{\tau_{ig}}
$$

Therefore the **Mallard–Le Chatelier formula** is:

$$
S_L = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha_u}{\tau_{ig}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check (CH₄–air at $\phi=1$):** $\alpha_u \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s; $\tau_{ig}$ for methane–air ≈ 1.8 ms (at 1500 K). Plugging in:
> 
> $$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1.39 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.118 \text{ m/s}$$
> 
> This is **lower** than the experimental 0.37 m/s because (i) Mallard–Le Chatelier assumes the gas must reach the *adiabatic* temperature, which is too pessimistic, and (ii) chemistry is reduced to a single ignition delay. The order of magnitude is correct. This 3× discrepancy is *the* standard KTU follow-up question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Zeldovich Activation-Energy Asymptotic Result

### Step 0: Set up the 1-D reaction-sheet model

The preheat zone is governed by the same energy equation as before. Inside the reaction sheet, the reaction rate is governed by a one-step Arrhenius law:

$$
\dot{\omega} = A \, \rho \, Y_F \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)
$$

The activation energy is large: $\beta = E_a / (R_u T_{ad}) \gg 1$.

### Step 1: Energy balance across the reaction sheet

In the limit $\beta \to \infty$, the reaction sheet collapses to a sink of enthalpy. The energy jump condition is:

$$
\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \rho_u S_L Y_F Q_c
$$

The rate of energy release is balanced by the **conductive flux** from the burned gas to the unreacted side:

$$
\lambda_b \left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{0^+} - \lambda_u \left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{0^-} = 0
$$

(with a sign change depending on convention).

### Step 2: Solve the Arrhenius rate integral

Inside the reaction zone, the temperature rises rapidly from $T_u$ to $T_{ad}$. The integral

$$
I = \int_{T_u}^{T_{ad}} \frac{\lambda_u \, \exp(-E_a / R_u T)}{T - T_u} \, dT
$$

is dominated by the region near $T = T_{ad}$ (Laplace's method). The standard asymptotic evaluation yields:

$$
I \sim \frac{\lambda_u T_{ad}^2 R_u}{E_a (T_{ad} - T_u)} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Equate the conductive flux to the integrated heat release

$$
\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) \sim \rho_u Y_F A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right) \cdot \frac{\lambda_u T_{ad}^2 R_u}{E_a (T_{ad} - T_u)}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $S_L$

After algebra:

$$
S_L^2 \sim \frac{\lambda_u \, c_p \, Y_F \, A \, R_u \, T_{ad}^2}{c_p^2 (T_{ad} - T_u)^2 \, E_a \, \rho_u} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right)
$$

The **dominant dependence** on activation energy is the exponential term:

$$
S_L^2 \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
S_L \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{2 R_u T_{ad}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU takeaway:** Doubling $T_{ad}$ (e.g., from oxygen-enrichment) does NOT double $S_L$. It increases the exponential argument linearly, so $S_L$ scales as $\exp(E_a/2R_uT_{ad}^2 \cdot dT_{ad})$, which is **super-linear**. This is why monopropellant hydrazine flames are ~5× faster than methane flames despite similar $E_a$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Markstein Number from Lewis-Number Theory (Clavin, 1985)

For a *weakly stretched* premixed flame, the burning velocity relative to the unburned gas is:

$$
S_T = S_L \left[ 1 - \frac{\mathcal{L}_b \cdot \kappa}{S_L} \right]
$$

The **Markstein length** (with respect to burned gas) is, in the large-activation-energy limit:

$$
\mathcal{L}_b = \frac{\delta_L}{\beta} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\lambda(T)/\lambda_u - 1}{1 - \theta} \cdot \frac{Le_u - 1}{1} \, d\xi'
$$

For most hydrocarbons, a simpler closed-form approximation holds:

$$
\mathcal{L}_b \approx \delta_L \cdot \frac{Le - 1}{2 \beta}
$$

The **Markstein number** is the non-dimensional form:

$$
\text{Ma} = \frac{\mathcal{L}_b}{\delta_L} = \frac{Le - 1}{2 \beta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For propane** ($Le \approx 1.85$, $\beta \approx 6.5$): $\text{Ma} = (0.85)/(2 \cdot 6.5) \approx +0.065$. **Positive** Ma → flame resists stretch.
> 
> **For hydrogen** ($Le \approx 0.3$, $\beta \approx 5.5$): $\text{Ma} = (-0.7)/(2 \cdot 5.5) \approx -0.064$. **Negative** Ma → flame is stretch-unstable; lean hydrogen flames spontaneously develop cellular structure.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: $S_L$ at Stoichiometry for 10 Common Fuels

| Fuel | Formula | $S_L^0$ (m/s) | $Le$ | $T_{ad}$ (K) | $\delta_L$ (mm) | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | **0.37** | 0.97 | 2222 | 0.45 | Stable |
| Ethane | C₂H₆ | **0.43** | 1.21 | 2259 | 0.50 | Stable |
| Propane | C₃H₈ | **0.40** | 1.85 | 2267 | 0.50 | Very stable |
| n-Butane | C₄H₁₀ | **0.38** | 2.05 | 2273 | 0.55 | Very stable |
| Acetylene | C₂H₂ | **1.58** | 0.95 | 2598 | 0.20 | Marginally unstable |
| Ethylene | C₂H₄ | **0.70** | 1.20 | 2370 | 0.40 | Stable |
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | **0.52** | 1.10 | 2370 | 0.60 | Stable |
| Hydrogen | H₂ | **2.80** | 0.30 | 2400 | 0.30 | Unstable (cellular) |
| n-Heptane | C₇H₁₆ | **0.36** | 2.20 | 2294 | 0.55 | Very stable |
| Methanol | CH₃OH | **0.48** | 1.20 | 2200 | 0.45 | Stable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern recognition (KTU Module 3 focus):**
> 1. **Hydrogen** is the outlier — 7× faster than methane, low Le, cellular structure.
> 2. **Acetylene** is the second-fastest — fast chemistry, high $T_{ad}$, soot-prone.
> 3. **CO** is moderate despite low $T_{ad}$ because of its high thermal diffusivity in N₂-diluted air.
> 4. **Saturated hydrocarbons** (CH₄, C₃H₈, C₇H₁₆) all cluster around **0.36–0.43 m/s**. This is the famous "Lovachev rule": for any saturated hydrocarbon–air flame at $\phi = 1$, $S_L \approx 0.4$ m/s within ±15%.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — $S_L(\phi)$ Polylog Fit (Metghalchi–Keck, 1982)

For a *given* fuel and unburned temperature, the equivalence-ratio dependence is fitted by:

$$
S_L(\phi) = S_L^{\max} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + A \left( \dfrac{1}{\phi} - \phi \right)^2 + B \left( \dfrac{1}{\phi} - \phi \right)^4}
$$

For methane–air at 300 K, 1 atm:
- $S_L^{\max} = 0.392$ m/s
- $A = 0.46$
- $B = -0.05$
- $\phi_m = 1.08$ (slightly rich peak)

The peak $S_L$ typically occurs at $\phi = 1.05$–$1.15$ because the *heat-release rate per unit mass of unburned mixture* is maximised slightly rich of stoichiometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU note:** For hydrogen–air, $S_L$ peaks at $\phi = 1.6$ (very rich) due to high H-atom diffusion on the lean side suppressing $S_L$. This is the canonical "asymmetric" $S_L(\phi)$ curve that the examiner loves to ask about.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — 1-D Steady Laminar Flame Structure

This Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** of a planar 1-D flame, since physical flame drawings are not Mermaid-friendly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Unburned Gas Reservoir<br/>Temperature Tu<br/>Density rho_u<br/>Velocity SL"]
    nodeB["Preheat Zone<br/>Thickness delta_P<br/>0.1 to 1.0 mm<br/>Heat Conduction Dominant<br/>No Reaction"]
    nodeC["Reaction Sheet<br/>Thickness delta_R<br/>0.01 to 0.1 mm<br/>Arrhenius Reaction<br/>Temperature Tad"]
    nodeD["Burned Gas Exhaust<br/>Temperature Tad<br/>Density rho_b<br/>Velocity Sb"]

    nodeA -- "Convective Inflow" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "Conductive Heating" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "Exothermic Jump" --> nodeD
    nodeD -- "Outflow to Ambient" --> nodeA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The flame is a **two-zone structure**: preheat + reaction sheet.
- $S_L$ is the **inlet velocity** of node A (relative to the stationary flame).
- The reaction sheet (node C) is the **rate-limiting bottleneck**; $S_L$ is set by the chemistry there.
- Energy flows: $Tu \to$ (conduction) $\to Tad$ in node C $\to$ (exothermic release) $\to$ node D.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Causal Chain from Chemistry to $S_L$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Fuel-Oxidizer Mixture<br/>Equivalence Ratio phi<br/>Unburned Temperature Tu<br/>Pressure P"]
    node2["Elementary Chemistry<br/>Hundreds of Reactions<br/>Rate Constants kT<br/>Activation Energy Ea"]
    node3["Heat Release Rate<br/>omega dot per unit volume<br/>Adiabatic Flame Temp Tad"]
    node4["Transport Properties<br/>Thermal Diffusivity alpha<br/>Mass Diffusivity D<br/>Lewis Number Le"]
    node5["Flame Structure<br/>delta_P Preheat<br/>delta_R Reaction<br/>Markstein Length"]
    node6["Laminar Flame Speed<br/>SL in m per second<br/>K TU Target Variable"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node1 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
```

**Reading the causal chain:**
- The chemistry fixes the **heat release**.
- The transport properties fix the **diffusion rates**.
- Their interplay sets the **flame structure**.
- The structure, in turn, sets the **laminar flame speed**.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — $S_L(\phi)$ Behaviour for Different Lewis-Number Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["phi less than phi_lean<br/>Quenching<br/>SL approaches 0"]
    nodeB["phi_lean to phi_peak<br/>Rising Branch<br/>Hydrogen SL peaks late at phi approx 1.6"]
    nodeC["phi_peak<br/>Hydrogen peak at phi 1.6<br/>Methane peak at phi 1.08<br/>Most hydrocarbons at phi 1.05"]
    nodeD["phi_peak to phi_rich<br/>Falling Branch<br/>All fuels decline"]
    nodeE["phi greater than phi_rich<br/>Quenching<br/>SL approaches 0"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **logical block diagram**, not a plot. The actual $S_L(\phi)$ plot is sketched verbally:
> - **Methane:** symmetric Gaussian centred at $\phi = 1.08$.
> - **Propane:** symmetric, peak at $\phi = 1.10$, slightly narrower.
> - **Hydrogen:** strongly asymmetric, peak at $\phi = 1.6$, long rich tail, short lean side.
> - **CO–H₂ mixtures:** peak shifts with H₂ fraction.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Engineered System: Flame-Arrestor Geometry (KTU application context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn["Pipe Inlet<br/>Premixed Gas<br/>Velocity U_in"]
    nodeA["Flame Arrestor Core<br/>Crimped Metal Ribbon<br/>Quenching Diameter d_q"]
    nodeB["Quenching Decision<br/>delta_L less than d_q<br/>then flame extinguished"]
    nodeC["Permissive Flow<br/>Gas continues downstream<br/>velocity U_out"]
    nodeD["Blocked Path<br/>Flame cannot propagate<br/>mass flow halts"]

    nodeIn --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "Quench Diameter Met" --> nodeD
    nodeB -- "Quench Diameter Not Met" --> nodeC
```

**Engineering logic:** The arrestor geometry must satisfy $d_q < \delta_L$ so that the conductive cooling by the metal walls outruns the chemical heat release — a direct application of the Mallard–Le Chatelier criterion.
