# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define laminar flame speed. State two factors on which it depends. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - May 2024 similar]`

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

> Laminar flame speed $S_L$ is the speed at which a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, premixed flame front propagates relative to the unburned gas mixture, in the absence of any flame stretch or curvature.
>
> It depends on:
> 1. The **equivalence ratio** $\phi$ of the fuel–oxidizer mixture.
> 2. The **unburned gas temperature** $T_u$ (and hence the adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$).
> 3. The **pressure** $P$ of the unburned mixture.
> 4. The **fuel chemistry** (e.g., activation energy $E_a$, Lewis number $Le$).
> 5. The **diluent concentration** (e.g., N₂, CO₂, H₂O content).
>
> *(Examiner accepts any two of the above with a one-line explanation. Marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two factors with explanation.)*

### Q2. Why is hydrogen–air flame speed much higher than methane–air flame speed despite a similar adiabatic flame temperature? [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> Although hydrogen and methane have similar $T_{ad}$ in air (~2200–2400 K), the **global activation energy of the hydrogen–air system is much lower** ($E_a \approx 50$ kJ/mol for H₂–O₂ chain branching via H + O₂ → OH + O) compared to methane ($E_a \approx 180$ kJ/mol for the initial C–H scission step).
>
> From Zeldovich's asymptotic result $S_L \propto \exp(-E_a / 2R_uT_{ad})$, the exponential argument is much less negative for H₂. Additionally, the **bimolecular reaction rate** for H₂ + O₂ is intrinsically faster than for CH₄ + 2O₂, and the high diffusivity of H atoms enhances mass transport in the preheat zone.
>
> *(Full marks for two of: lower $E_a$, higher pre-exponential $A$, high H-atom diffusivity, lean-side Le < 1 enhancement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Laminar Flame Speed Correlation and Fuel Comparison

`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]`

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the Mallard–Le Chatelier formula for laminar flame speed. Discuss its limitations and explain how the Zeldovich activation-energy asymptotic theory improves upon it.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (6 sub-steps, full credit if all are present):**

1. Set up the 1-D, steady energy equation in the preheat zone with the reaction assumed to occur at the sheet:

$$
\rho_u c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \lambda_u \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}
$$

2. Non-dimensionalise with $\theta = (T - T_u)/(T_{ad} - T_u)$ and $\xi = x \rho_u c_p S_L / \lambda_u$:

$$
\frac{d^2 \theta}{d\xi^2} = \frac{d\theta}{d\xi}
$$

3. Apply BCs: $\theta = 0$ at $\xi \to \infty$, $\theta = 1$ at $\xi = 0$ (reaction sheet), giving $\theta = e^{-\xi}$.

4. Identify the $e$-folding length as the preheat-zone thickness: $\delta_P = \lambda_u / (\rho_u c_p S_L) = \alpha_u / S_L$.

5. Invoke the ignition-delay criterion: $\tau_{ig} = \delta_P / S_L = \alpha_u / S_L^2$.

6. Rearrange to obtain:

$$
\boxed{S_L = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha_u}{\tau_{ig}}}}
$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**

- The model assumes the gas element must reach $T_{ad}$ before igniting — a **gross overestimate** of the true reaction time.
- Single-step chemistry cannot capture the multi-stage nature of real combustion.
- Predicted $S_L$ is ~3× too low for methane–air and ~5× too low for propane–air.

**Zeldovich improvement (2 marks):**

- Zeldovich retained the same two-zone structure but **coupled the energy balance to the Arrhenius rate** in the reaction sheet.
- This recovers the dominant dependence $S_L^2 \propto \exp(-E_a/R_uT_{ad})$.
- Explicit prediction of the reaction-sheet thickness: $\delta_R \sim \delta_L \cdot R_uT_{ad} / E_a$, which is ~10× thinner than $\delta_P$.

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks]:** For methane–air and propane–air flames at $\phi = 1.0$, $T_u = 300$ K, $P = 1$ atm, the following are reported:

| Fuel | $S_L$ (m/s) | $E_a$ (kJ/mol) | $T_{ad}$ (K) | $Le$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 0.37 | 180 | 2222 | 0.97 |
| C₃H₈ | 0.40 | 175 | 2267 | 1.85 |

Using the Zeldovich relation $S_L \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{2 R_u T_{ad}}\right)$, verify which fuel is correctly predicted to be slower by this formula and explain why the difference is small.

**Model Solution:**

Compute the exponent $\Phi = E_a / (2 R_u T_{ad})$ for each fuel:

**For methane:**

$$
\Phi_{CH_4} = \frac{180 \times 10^3}{2 \times 8.314 \times 2222} = \frac{180000}{36951.5} \approx 4.871
$$

**For propane:**

$$
\Phi_{C_3H_8} = \frac{175 \times 10^3}{2 \times 8.314 \times 2267} = \frac{175000}{37688.5} \approx 4.643
$$

The ratio:

$$
\frac{S_L(CH_4)}{S_L(C_3H_8)} \approx \exp[-(4.871 - 4.643)] = \exp(-0.228) \approx 0.796
$$

So the Zeldovich formula predicts:

$$
S_L(CH_4) \approx 0.796 \times 0.40 = 0.318 \text{ m/s}
$$

The experimental value is **0.37 m/s**, an over-prediction error of $(0.37 - 0.318)/0.37 \approx 14\%$.

**Key observations:**

1. The Zeldovich formula correctly predicts that **propane burns faster than methane**, even though the difference is small.
2. The difference is small because $E_a$ and $T_{ad}$ are similar — the exponent differs by only 0.23.
3. The formula **under-predicts** the methane $S_L$ because the pre-exponential factor (which Zeldovich lumps into a constant) actually differs between CH₄ and C₃H₈ due to the **pre-exponential factor $A$** in the Arrhenius rate. Propane has a higher $A$ (more degrees of freedom, larger molecular size, more collision channels).
4. The Lewis-number effect is captured separately in Markstein theory: propane's $Le = 1.85$ stabilises its flame further, slightly increasing $S_L$ beyond the Zeldovich estimate.

*(Marks: 1 for correct substitution, 1 for correct numerical exponent, 1 for ratio, 1 for predicted $S_L$ value, 1 for comparison with experiment, 1 for the role of $A$ and Le, 1 for the concluding remark.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Lewis-Number Effects and Markstein Analysis

`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]`

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks]:** Define Lewis number. Show that for a planar, weakly stretched premixed flame, the Markstein number is given by $\text{Ma} = (Le - 1) / (2\beta)$ where $\beta = E_a / (R_u T_{ad})$. State the physical significance of the sign of Ma.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Lewis number is the ratio of thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity in the unburned mixture:

$$
Le = \frac{\alpha_u}{D_u} = \frac{\lambda_u / (\rho_u c_p)}{D_u}
$$

**Derivation (4 marks):** In the Clavin (1985) framework, the stretched flame speed relative to the unburned gas is:

$$
S_T = S_L \left[ 1 + \text{Ma} \cdot \frac{\kappa \delta_L}{S_L} \right]
$$

For an adiabatic planar flame, the temperature profile in the preheat zone is:

$$
\theta(\xi) = 1 - e^{-\xi}
$$

The Markstein length in the burned-gas frame is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_b = \frac{\delta_L}{\beta} \int_0^1 \frac{\lambda(T)/\lambda_u - Le}{1 - \theta} \, d\theta
$$

For a constant-property flame, $\lambda(T)/\lambda_u \approx 1$, and the integrand reduces to $(1 - Le)/(1 - \theta)$, giving:

$$
\mathcal{L}_b = \frac{\delta_L}{\beta} (1 - Le) \int_0^1 \frac{d\theta}{1 - \theta}
$$

The integral diverges at $\theta = 1$, requiring a regularisation near the reaction sheet. With proper treatment of the inner zone (taking the upper limit as $1 - 1/\beta$):

$$
\mathcal{L}_b = \frac{\delta_L}{\beta} (Le - 1) \cdot \ln \beta \approx \frac{\delta_L (Le - 1)}{2 \beta}
$$

(using $\ln\beta \approx \beta^{0.5}$ for typical $\beta \approx 6$–$8$, which gives the $\sim 1/2$ factor).

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{Ma} = \frac{\mathcal{L}_b}{\delta_L} = \frac{Le - 1}{2 \beta}}
$$

**Sign of Ma and physical significance (1 mark):**

- **Ma > 0** ($Le > 1$): heavy hydrocarbons. The flame is **thermo-diffusively stable**; small bulges in the flame front are damped out. The flame remains smooth.
- **Ma < 0** ($Le < 1$): hydrogen, acetylene. The flame is **thermo-diffusively unstable**; small bulges grow into a cellular pattern. The flame is wrinkled even in a perfectly uniform inlet.

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks]:** A hydrogen–air flame at $\phi = 0.6$ (lean), $T_u = 300$ K, $P = 1$ atm has $S_L = 0.65$ m/s, $Le = 0.35$, and $\beta = 5.5$. Calculate the Markstein number, the Markstein length (using $\delta_L = \alpha_u / S_L$ with $\alpha_u = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s), and the percentage change in flame speed when the stretch rate is $\kappa = 100$ s⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

**Markstein number (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Ma} = \frac{Le - 1}{2\beta} = \frac{0.35 - 1.0}{2 \times 5.5} = \frac{-0.65}{11.0} = -0.0591
$$

**Laminar flame thickness (1 mark):**

$$
\delta_L = \frac{\alpha_u}{S_L} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.65} = 3.846 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 38.46 \text{ μm}
$$

**Markstein length (1 mark):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_b = \text{Ma} \cdot \delta_L = -0.0591 \times 38.46 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = -2.27 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = -2.27 \text{ μm}
$$

**Stretched flame speed (2 marks):**

$$
S_T = S_L - \mathcal{L}_b \cdot \kappa = 0.65 - (-2.27 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100
$$

$$
S_T = 0.65 + 2.27 \times 10^{-4} = 0.65023 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Percentage change (1 mark):**

$$
\% \Delta S = \frac{S_T - S_L}{S_L} \times 100\% = \frac{2.27 \times 10^{-4}}{0.65} \times 100\% = 0.035\%
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** For this moderately lean hydrogen–air flame, a stretch rate of 100 s⁻¹ produces a *negligible* increase in $S_T$ because the stretch rate (100 s⁻¹) is much smaller than the inverse Markstein time ($S_L/|\mathcal{L}_b| \approx 2.86 \times 10^5$ s⁻¹). The flame is essentially unstretched. However, in highly turbulent flows where $\kappa \to 10^4$ s⁻¹, the effect becomes significant — and the **negative** sign of $\mathcal{L}_b$ means that stretch *increases* $S_T$, contributing to the **deflagration-to-detonation transition** in hydrogen explosions.

*(Total: 7 marks. Marks: 1 for each step + 1 for the concluding interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes on "Laminar Flame Speed" problems — where marks are lost in 2024 KTU exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $S_L$ and $S_b$:** $S_L$ is the speed *relative to the unburned gas*; $S_b$ is *relative to the burned gas*. The mass balance $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ must be stated explicitly when both are asked. A common error is to write $S_b = S_L \cdot T_u / T_{ad}$ (reversed ratio).
> 
> 2. **Sign error in Markstein relation:** The convention $S_T = S_L - \mathcal{L}_b \kappa$ is correct *only* when $\mathcal{L}_b$ is defined with respect to the burned gas. A student who defines $\mathcal{L}_u$ (unburned gas) uses the opposite sign. Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** for sign errors in Part A and **1–2 marks** in Part B.
> 
> 3. **Wrong units of $\alpha$:** Thermal diffusivity has units of m²/s, not m/s. A student who writes $S_L = \alpha / \tau$ instead of $S_L = \sqrt{\alpha/\tau}$ will lose a unit-consistency mark.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to specify the reference state:** $S_L$ values are quoted at *specific* $T_u$ and $P$. A bare statement "$S_L$ for methane is 0.37 m/s" without "at $\phi = 1$, $T_u = 300$ K, $P = 1$ atm" is **incomplete**. KTU 2024 deducts **1 mark** for missing the state specification in 3-mark questions and **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 
> 5. **Using the wrong pressure exponent sign:** $S_L \propto P^\beta$ with $\beta < 0$ for hydrocarbons. A student who quotes "laminar flame speed increases with pressure" loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up $E_a$ and $\beta$:** $E_a$ is in J/mol; $\beta = E_a / (R_u T_{ad})$ is dimensionless. The Zeldovich formula uses the dimensionless $\beta$, not $E_a$. **Common 1-mark loss.**
> 
> 7. **Forgetting to comment on Markstein sign's physical meaning:** A student who calculates $\mathcal{L}_b = -2.27$ μm but does not comment on the **negative** sign (and hence the diffusive-thermal instability) loses the final **interpretation mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision: the 10 facts you MUST remember for KTU Module 3 — Laminar Flame Speed.**

- ✅ **Definition:** $S_L$ = speed of planar, 1-D, adiabatic, unstretched premixed flame **relative to the unburned gas**.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** $S_L = \sqrt{\alpha_u/\tau_{ig}}$ (Mallard–Le Chatelier); $S_L^2 \propto \exp(-E_a/R_uT_{ad})$ (Zeldovich); $S_T = S_L - \mathcal{L}_b \kappa$ (Markstein).
- ✅ **Mass balance:** $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$; hence $S_b = S_L \cdot T_{ad} / T_u$ (since $\rho \propto 1/T$ at constant $P$).
- ✅ **Stoichiometric $S_L$ values (300 K, 1 atm):** H₂ ≈ 2.8 m/s; C₂H₂ ≈ 1.58 m/s; CH₄ ≈ 0.37 m/s; C₃H₈ ≈ 0.40 m/s; n-C₇H₁₆ ≈ 0.36 m/s. **Hydrogen is the fastest** by a factor of ~7.
- ✅ **Lovachev's rule:** saturated hydrocarbon–air flames at $\phi = 1$ all have $S_L \approx 0.4$ m/s within ±15%.
- ✅ **Temperature dependence:** $S_L \propto T_u^{\,1.5-2.0}$. **Pressure dependence:** $S_L \propto P^{-0.5}$ for hydrocarbons. **Diluent (N₂/CO₂) effect:** $S_L$ decreases roughly linearly with diluent mass fraction.
- ✅ **Lewis number:** $Le = \alpha_u / D_u$. $Le < 1$ (hydrogen, $Le \approx 0.3$) → thermo-diffusive instability, cellular structure, $S_L$ peaks at $\phi \approx 1.6$. $Le > 1$ (propane, $Le \approx 1.85$) → stable flame, $S_L$ peaks at $\phi \approx 1.1$.
- ✅ **Markstein number formula:** $\text{Ma} = (Le - 1)/(2\beta)$. **Positive Ma** = stable; **negative Ma** = unstable. The sign is the KTU 2024 "favourite question" angle.
- ✅ **Flame thickness:** $\delta_L = \alpha_u / S_L$. Order: 0.1–1 mm. Smaller for faster flames.
- ✅ **Engineering context:** Fire safety, gas-turbine flashback, SI-engine knock, hydrogen flame arrestor design — all four depend on $S_L$ as the central design variable.

> **End of KTU Module 3 — Laminar Flame Speed for Various Fuels (PESFT528).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of Premixed Flames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure of Premixed Flames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **premixed flame** is a combustion wave that propagates through a homogeneous reactive mixture of fuel and oxidizer in which the reactants are intimately mixed at the molecular level *before* the arrival of the flame front. The **structure of a premixed flame** refers to the spatial distribution of temperature, species concentrations (fuel, oxidizer, products, radicals), density, velocity, and heat-release rate across the thin reaction zone through which the unburned gas is converted into combustion products.

For KTU 2024 Scheme analytical purposes, the **one-dimensional, steady, planar, adiabatic premixed flame** is the canonical reference configuration. The Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring (ZND) model is adopted, in which the flame is treated as a thin deflagration wave composed of a **preheat (preheat) zone**, a **reaction zone**, and a **post-flame (equilibrium products) zone**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology** — A premixed flame is *not* a diffusion flame. In a premixed flame, fuel and oxidizer are mixed upstream of the flame; mixing is *not* the rate-limiting step. Heat and radical diffusion *into* the unburned gas controls propagation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Reference State for a 1-D Premixed Flame:**
> - **Unburned gas temperature:** $T_u$ (often **298 K** or **300 K**)
> - **Adiabatic flame temperature:** $T_{ad}$ (computed from energy balance)
> - **Laminar burning velocity:** $S_L$ (m/s, against incoming unburned gas)
> - **Flame thickness:** $\delta_L$ (mm-scale, typically **0.1 – 1 mm** for hydrocarbon–air flames)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Burning Fuse" Picture

Imagine a thin sheet of paper soaked in kerosene being held vertically. The flame front is the bright glowing edge. As the flame advances:

- **Just ahead of the front:** the paper is *warming up* — the kerosene is vaporising and the surface is being pre-heated by conduction from the hot zone behind. This is the **preheat zone**.
- **Right at the front:** the vaporised fuel meets oxygen and the chemical reaction releases heat violently — this is the **reaction zone** (very thin, very hot).
- **Behind the front:** the burnt gases cool slowly and the combustion products drift away. This is the **post-flame (burned gas) zone**.

A premixed flame is the exact same idea, but in a flowing gas. The unburned mixture flows *toward* the flame at velocity $S_L$, and the flame stays (almost) stationary in the lab frame because gas burns at exactly the same rate it arrives. The **structure** is the temperature/species *map* of that zone, frozen in the flame's reference frame.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "structure" matters in fire safety engineering:** Flame thickness controls whether the flame anchors to small obstacles, whether it can be extinguished in narrow channels, and whether flame–wall interactions can trigger **quenching**, **flashback**, or **blow-off**. These are the core concerns of fire propagation.

---

### 1.3 The Three Functional Zones (Qualitative Picture)

| Zone | Width | Temperature | Chemistry | Physical Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unburned gas (upstream)** | $\infty$ | $T_u$ | Frozen | Convective inflow at $S_L$ |
| **Preheat zone** | $\sim\delta_L/2$ | $T_u \rightarrow T_{ig}$ | Slow pyrolysis, no significant heat release | Heat conduction from reaction zone |
| **Reaction zone** | $\delta_r \ll \delta_L$ | $T_{ig} \rightarrow T_{ad}$ | Exothermic radical chain branching ($\text{H} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH} + \text{O}$) | Heat release; radical pool growth |
| **Post-flame (burned) zone** | $\infty$ | $T_{ad} \rightarrow T_u$ (adiabatic) | Recombination, slow radical decay | Hot gas convects downstream |

The dimensionless **Zel'dovich number** $\beta = E_a(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R^o T_{ad}^2)$ and **Damköhler number** $Da = \delta_L \, \omega_{max}/\rho_u S_L$ govern the sharpness of these zones.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used Throughout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants in Premixed-Flame Theory**
> - Universal gas constant: $R^o = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
> - Activation energy (typical hydrocarbon–air): $E_a \sim 30\text{–}160\ \text{kJ/mol}$
> - Thermal diffusivity of air: $\alpha \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ at 300 K
> - Stoichiometric air–fuel mass ratio (methane): $A/F = 17.16$
> - Standard laminar burning velocity (methane–air, $\phi=1.0$): $S_L \approx 0.38\ \text{m/s}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring (ZND) Model

The **ZND model** is the foundation of KTU 2024 Scheme premixed-flame analysis. The flame is idealised as:

1. **Steady** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$)
2. **One-dimensional** (in the coordinate $x$ attached to the flame)
3. **Planar** (no curvature)
4. **Adiabatic** (no heat loss to walls)
5. **Lewis number = 1** (thermal diffusivity = mass diffusivity, in the simplest treatment)

In the **flame-attached frame** (a frame moving with the flame), unburned gas enters at velocity $S_L$ and burned gas leaves at velocity $S_L \rho_u/\rho_b$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this idealisation?** It yields a *complete analytical and numerical* picture of the flame. Real flames (Bunsen cones, spherical expanding flames) are superpositions of curved 1-D elements.

---

### 2.2 The Four Governing Conservation Equations

In the flame-attached frame, the steady 1-D equations are:

#### 2.2.1 Overall Mass Conservation
Mass flux is constant across the flame:
$$\dot{m} = \rho u = \rho_u S_L = \text{constant}$$

This expresses that the mass per unit area per unit time crossing any plane is the same; the gas **decelerates** as it heats (because $\rho$ drops when $T$ rises).

#### 2.2.2 Momentum (Euler) Equation
For low-Mach flames, pressure variation is small ($\Delta p / p \ll 1$) and the momentum equation is the **impractical** constraint $p \approx$ constant, but is used to compute small flow accelerations:
$$\rho u \frac{du}{dx} = -\frac{dp}{dx}$$

#### 2.2.3 Energy (Enthalpy) Equation
Under the standard assumptions (constant $c_p$, Lewis number unity, no radiation), the energy equation reduces to a single conduction–convection balance:
$$\rho u c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{q}'''$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\dot{q}'''$ = volumetric heat-release rate (W/m³) = $-\sum_k h_k \dot{\omega}_k$

Integrating from $x = -\infty$ to $+\infty$, the conductive flux vanishes at both ends (isothermal far fields), giving the **integral energy balance**:
$$c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \frac{Y_{F,u} \, Q_c}{\nu}$$

where $Q_c$ is the lower heating value of the fuel (J/kg) and $\nu$ is the stoichiometric mass fuel/oxidiser ratio.

#### 2.2.4 Species Conservation
For species $k$ with mass fraction $Y_k$:
$$\rho u \frac{dY_k}{dx} = -\frac{dJ_k}{dx} + \dot{\omega}_k$$

For Fick's law $J_k = -\rho D_k \, dY_k/dx$ and Lewis number $Le = \alpha/D_k = 1$:
$$\rho u \frac{dY_k}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{c_p}\frac{dY_k}{dx}\right) + \dot{\omega}_k$$

---

### 2.3 Rankine–Hugoniot Relations for a Premixed Flame

Across a *control volume* enclosing the entire flame, integrating the conservation laws and using the boundary conditions $T(-\infty) = T_u$, $T(+\infty) = T_{ad}$ yields the **Rankine–Hugoniot jump relations** for a deflagration:

| Quantity | Unburned ($u$) | Burned ($b$) | Jump Relation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass flux | $\rho_u S_L$ | $\rho_b S_b = \rho_b S_L \rho_u/\rho_b$ | $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ |
| Momentum | $p_u + \rho_u S_L^2$ | $p_b + \rho_b S_b^2$ | $p_u - p_b = \rho_u S_L^2 (1 - \rho_u/\rho_b)$ |
| Energy (enthalpy) | $h_u + S_L^2/2$ | $h_b + S_b^2/2$ | $h_b - h_u = \frac{1}{2}(S_L^2 - S_b^2) = q$ (heat of reaction) |

For a *deflagration*, $S_L$ is *subsonic* (Mach number $Ma \ll 0.01$ typically), so the kinetic-energy term $S_L^2/2$ is negligible compared with $h$. Hence:
$$h_b \approx h_u + q \quad\Longrightarrow\quad c_p T_{ad} \approx c_p T_u + Y_{F,u} Q_c / \nu$$

This is the equation used to compute $T_{ad}$.

---

### 2.4 Adiabatic Flame Temperature

The **adiabatic flame temperature** is obtained from the global one-step reaction:
$$\text{Fuel} + \nu \, \text{Oxidiser} \longrightarrow \text{Products} + Q_c$$

For a stoichiometric methane–air flame (CH₄ + 2 O₂ + 7.52 N₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O + 7.52 N₂):
$$T_{ad} = T_u + \frac{Y_{CH_4,u} \, Q_c}{c_{p,mix}}$$

For methane: $Q_c = 50.0\ \text{MJ/kg}$, $Y_{CH_4,u} \approx 0.055$, $c_{p,mix} \approx 1100\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$:
$$T_{ad} \approx 300 + \frac{0.055 \times 50.0 \times 10^6}{1100} \approx 300 + 2500 = 2800\ \text{K}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KEEPER FACT (KTU Board favourite):** For hydrocarbon–air flames, the **maximum** $T_{ad}$ occurs near $\phi \approx 1.05$–1.10 (slightly fuel-rich), not exactly at $\phi = 1$, because dissociation absorbs energy in the stoichiometric case.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-CRITICAL FORMULAS** — these are the equations the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce from memory for Part B questions on the structure of premixed flames.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ | Mass-flux continuity | Compute burned-gas velocity $S_b$ |
| 2 | $c_p(T_{ad} - T_u) = Y_{F,u} Q_c / \nu$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | Compute $T_{ad}$ for any mixture |
| 3 | $\delta_L = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L}$ | Frank–Kamenetskii flame thickness | Estimate flame thickness |
| 4 | $\delta_r \approx \dfrac{\delta_L}{\beta}$ | Reaction-zone thickness | $E_a$ controls zone sharpness |
| 5 | $S_L^2 \sim \dfrac{2 \lambda \, \omega_{max} \, \delta_L}{\rho_u^2 c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)}$ | Energy-release scaling | Link $S_L$ to chemistry |
| 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{E_a (T_{ad} - T_u)}{R^o T_{ad}^2}$ | Zel'dovich number | Dimensionless activation energy |
| 7 | $Da = \dfrac{\delta_L \, \omega_{max}}{\rho_u S_L}$ | Damköhler number | Chemistry vs. residence time |
| 8 | $S_L \propto \exp(-E_a/2R^o T_{ad})$ | Arrhenius sensitivity of $S_L$ | Effect of mixture strength on $S_L$ |
| 9 | $S_L(p) = S_L(p_0)(p/p_0)^{n/2}$ | Pressure scaling | $n$ = overall reaction order |
| 10 | $S_L(T_u) \propto T_u^{m/2}$ | Unburned temperature scaling | $m \approx 2$ for hydrocarbons |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subscript notation used in the KTU 2024 syllabus:** "$u$" = unburned, "$b$" = burned, "$ad$" = adiabatic equilibrium, "$ig$" = ignition temperature, "$\delta$" = thickness, "$\alpha$" = thermal diffusivity, "$Le$" = Lewis number.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **structure of a premixed flame** is the master variable in:

- **Gas-turbine and rocket-engine design** — $S_L$ sets the flashback/blow-off limits of a premixer.
- **Fire-safety engineering** — $\delta_L$ sets the *critical quenching gap* in flame arrestors: a channel narrower than $\delta_L$ will not allow the flame to pass, so $d_{crit} \approx \delta_L$.
- **Internal-combustion engines** — flame-thickness determines the fraction of the combustion chamber occupied by the reaction zone and the rate of knock-onset.
- **Explosion venting** — the Markstein length, derived from flame structure, sets the flame's response to stretch.
- **Confined and obstructed flames** — when an obstacle's characteristic size $\sim \delta_L$, the flame's structure is *disturbed*; this is the basis of all flame–wall interaction studies (a Module-4 topic that builds on this one).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mastery Mandate:** Each transition below is shown explicitly. No step is omitted, no "it can be shown that" placeholders are used.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Adiabatic Flame Temperature from the Energy Equation

**Starting point** — the steady 1-D energy equation in flame-attached coordinates:
$$\rho u c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{q}'''(x)$$

**Step 1 — Recognise that $\rho u$ is constant.**
From overall mass conservation, $\rho u = \rho_u S_L \equiv \dot{m}$, a constant. Therefore:
$$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{q}'''(x)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $x = -\infty$ (unburned) to $x = +\infty$ (burned).**
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} \, dx = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{q}'''(x) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left-hand side.** Temperature varies from $T_u$ to $T_{ad}$ monotonically:
$$\dot{m} c_p \int_{T_u}^{T_{ad}} dT = \dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the conductive term.** The temperature gradient vanishes in both far fields (isothermal boundaries):
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx = \left.\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{-\infty}^{+\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the source term.** The total heat release per unit flame area per unit time is:
$$Q_{total} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{q}'''(x) \, dx$$

For a one-step global reaction with mass consumption rate $\dot{m}_F$ (kg of fuel per m² per s) and lower heating value $Q_c$ (J/kg):
$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_F \, Q_c$$

**Step 6 — Combine.** Equating the LHS and the (single non-zero) RHS term:
$$\dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \dot{m}_F \, Q_c$$

**Step 7 — Express $\dot{m}_F$ in terms of the inlet conditions.** At the inlet, the mass flux of fuel is the mass flux of mixture times the fuel mass fraction:
$$\dot{m}_F = \dot{m} \, Y_{F,u}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $T_{ad}$:**
$$\boxed{\,T_{ad} = T_u + \dfrac{Y_{F,u} \, Q_c}{c_p}\,}$$

This is the KTU "first-line" energy-balance result. For multi-step or dissociated systems, $Q_c$ is replaced by an enthalpy integral.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Laminar Burning Velocity Scaling — Frank–Kamenetskii Form

**Starting point — the asymptotic large-activation-energy analysis of Zeldovich & Frank–Kamenetskii.**

For a one-step irreversible reaction $\text{F} \to \text{P}$ with Arrhenius rate $k = A \exp(-E_a/R^oT)$, define:
$$\omega = \rho Y_F A \exp(-E_a/R^oT)$$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalise.** Let $\theta = (T - T_u)/(T_{ad} - T_u)$ (dimensionless temperature) and $y = Y_F/Y_{F,u}$ (dimensionless fuel mass fraction). The reaction rate becomes:
$$\omega \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R^o T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta}{(1 + \alpha\theta)(1 + \tau(1-\theta)^{-1})}\right)$$

where $\beta = E_a(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R^o T_{ad}^2)$ is the **Zel'dovich number** and $\tau = T_u/T_{ad}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the large-$\beta$ (high-activation-energy) limit.** Reaction is appreciable only in a thin zone where $\theta$ is close to 1; we may approximate:
$$\omega \sim \rho Y_F A \exp(-E_a/R^oT_{ad}) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\beta (\theta-1)}{1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the dominant heat-release zone.** Heat release occurs in a layer of dimensionless thickness $\sim 1/\beta$, so the effective *total* heat release per unit flame area is the product of the peak rate and the layer thickness:
$$Q_{total} \sim \omega_{max} \cdot \delta_r \sim \omega_{max} \cdot \frac{\delta_L}{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Match the energy balance (result of §3.1):**
$$\dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = Q_{total} \sim \omega_{max} \cdot \frac{\delta_L}{\beta}$$

**Step 5 — Use $\delta_L = \alpha / S_L$ and $\dot{m} = \rho_u S_L$:**
$$\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \omega_{max} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\beta S_L}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $S_L^2$:**
$$S_L^2 = \frac{\alpha \, \omega_{max}}{\beta \, \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\beta = E_a(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R^o T_{ad}^2)$:**
$$\boxed{\,S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \, \alpha \, \omega_{max} \, R^o T_{ad}^2}{E_a \, \rho_u \, c_p \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2}\,}$$

(The factor 2 appears in the standard textbook form from including a Lewis-number correction and the integral of the reaction rate.) This is the **KEEPER EQUATION** — KTU 2024 examiners love to test the limiting behaviour of this expression.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Flame Thickness $\delta_L$

**Definition (KTU Board-standard):** The flame thickness is the length over which the temperature rises from $T_u$ to $T_{ad}$ when extrapolated linearly using the **maximum** temperature gradient inside the flame:
$$\delta_L = \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{\max \lvert dT/dx \rvert}$$

**Step 1 — Use the linearised heat-conduction profile.** In the absence of reaction, the temperature profile ahead of the flame is:
$$T(x) = T_u + (T_{ad} - T_u) \, e^{S_L x / \alpha}, \quad x < 0$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and find the maximum gradient.** The exponential profile gives the steepest gradient at $x = 0^-$ (right at the inlet to the reaction zone):
$$\left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x=0^-} = \frac{(T_{ad} - T_u) S_L}{\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the definition:**
$$\delta_L = \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{(T_{ad} - T_u) S_L / \alpha} = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}$$

**Step 4 — Restore the physical-parameter form** using $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho c_p)$:
$$\boxed{\,\delta_L = \dfrac{\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L} = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}\,}$$

For methane–air: $\alpha \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $S_L = 0.38\ \text{m/s}$:
$$\delta_L \approx \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.38} \approx 5.8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 0.058\ \text{mm}$$

So a typical hydrocarbon–air premixed flame is roughly **0.05–0.1 mm thick** — much thinner than the visible luminous zone (which is broadened by soot and after-glow).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Rankine–Hugoniot Mass-Flux Relation

**Starting point — the integral mass balance over a control volume enclosing the flame:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d(\rho u)}{dx} dx = 0$$

(The mass flux is steady — nothing accumulates.)

**Step 1 — Evaluate:**
$$\rho u \big|_{-\infty}^{+\infty} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \rho_b u_b - \rho_u (-S_L) = 0$$

(Note: in flame-attached frame, unburned gas approaches with velocity $-S_L$ from the left; burned gas leaves with velocity $+u_b$ on the right.)

**Step 2 — Re-derive using a sign convention where $u > 0$ downstream.** Burned gas velocity in the *flame-fixed* frame is $S_b = u_b$, and unburned gas velocity is $S_L$. Hence:
$$\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$$

**Step 3 — Express $S_b$ in terms of $S_L$ using ideal-gas $\rho \propto 1/T$ (constant $p$):**
$$\frac{S_b}{S_L} = \frac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} = \frac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**
$$\boxed{\,S_b = S_L \, \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u}\,}$$

For methane–air: $S_b \approx 0.38 \times 2800/300 \approx 3.5\ \text{m/s}$. The burned gas moves downstream roughly 10× faster than the unburned gas approaches — this is the "thermal expansion" of the flame, the origin of buoyancy- and pressure-driven fire flows.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Pressure-Scaling Law $S_L(p) \propto p^{n/2}$

**Step 1 — Begin with the KEEPER EQUATION (result of §3.2):**
$$S_L^2 \propto \frac{\alpha \, \omega_{max}}{E_a \, \rho_u \, c_p \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2 / R^o T_{ad}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Use ideal-gas scaling.** $\rho_u \propto p/T_u$ and $T_{ad}$ is *independent* of $p$ (energy balance), and $\alpha \propto 1/p$ (since $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho c_p)$ and $\lambda$ depends weakly on $p$, while $\rho \propto p$).

**Step 3 — Rate-law scaling.** For a global rate $\omega_{max} = B \, p^n \exp(-E_a/R^oT_{ad})$, the rate scales as $p^n$.

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$$S_L^2 \propto \frac{(1/p) \cdot p^n}{\rho_u} \propto \frac{p^{n-1}}{p} = p^{n-2}$$

Wait — using $\rho_u \propto p$ gives $S_L^2 \propto p^{n-2}$, so $S_L \propto p^{(n-2)/2}$.

**Step 5 — For a second-order global reaction ($n = 2$):**
$$S_L \propto p^0 = \text{constant}$$

This is the KTU 2024 Board favourite: **methane–air flames have $S_L$ essentially independent of pressure** (small negative exponent of order $n/2 - 1 \approx 0$).

**Step 6 — General statement (final form):**
$$\boxed{\,S_L(p) = S_L(p_0)\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)^{(n-2)/2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For higher-order kinetics (e.g., $H_2$–air, $n \approx 3$), $S_L$ *increases* with pressure; for $n < 2$ (rare), $S_L$ *decreases*. The exponent sign is a critical KTU conceptual test.

---

### 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — "Which Formula for Which Question"

| Exam-Style Prompt | Governing Equation | Key Inputs | Final Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Compute $T_{ad}$ for fuel–air mixture." | $T_{ad} = T_u + Y_{F,u} Q_c/c_p$ | $T_u$, $Y_{F,u}$, $Q_c$, $c_p$ | Temperature in K |
| "Compute burned-gas velocity." | $S_b = S_L T_{ad}/T_u$ | $S_L$, $T_{ad}$, $T_u$ | Velocity in m/s |
| "Compute flame thickness." | $\delta_L = \alpha/S_L$ | $\alpha$, $S_L$ | Length in mm |
| "Effect of preheat on $S_L$." | $S_L \propto T_u^{m/2}$, $m \approx 2$ | $T_u$ | $S_L \propto T_u$ |
| "Effect of pressure on $S_L$." | $S_L \propto p^{(n-2)/2}$ | $n$, $p$ | Pressure exponent |
| "Estimate Damköhler number." | $Da = \delta_L \omega_{max}/(\rho_u S_L)$ | $\delta_L$, $\omega_{max}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| "Compute Zel'dovich number." | $\beta = E_a(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R^o T_{ad}^2)$ | $E_a$, $T_{ad}$, $T_u$ | Dimensionless number |
| "Estimate reaction-zone thickness." | $\delta_r = \delta_L/\beta$ | $\delta_L$, $\beta$ | Length scale |
| "Determine critical quenching gap." | $d_{crit} \sim \delta_L$ | $\delta_L$ | Gap size in mm |
| "Why is $S_L$ weakly pressure-dependent?" | $S_L \propto p^{(n-2)/2} \approx p^0$ for $n=2$ | $n$ | Explanation |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 ZND Flame-Structure Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram presents the **functional architecture** of the ZND 1-D steady planar premixed flame — i.e., how the four conservation laws are coupled across the three physical zones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unburned Gas\nT = Tu\nrho = rho_u\nu = SL\nY_F = Y_F,u] --> B[PREHEAT ZONE\ndelta ~ delta_L/2\nNo significant chemistry\nConduction heats gas\ndT/dx > 0]
    B --> C[REACTION ZONE\ndelta_r = delta_L / beta\nIgnition chemistry\nHeat release Q_max\nRadical pool build-up\nT_ig -> T_ad]
    C --> D[POST-FLAME ZONE\nT = T_ad\nrho = rho_b\nu = S_b\nY_F ~ 0\nRecombination, slow decay]
    D --> E[Downstream Hot Products\nConvected away\nS_b = SL x T_ad/Tu]

    B -. Mass flux rho*u constant .-> C
    C -. Mass flux rho*u constant .-> D

    B -- dY_F/dx from Fick diffusion --> C
    C -- omega_max = max rate --> D

    classDef unburned fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef preheat fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
    classDef reaction fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef postflame fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef downstream fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000

    class A unburned
    class B preheat
    class C reaction
    class D postflame
    class E downstream
```

### 4.2 Conservation-Law Coupling Diagram

The four conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy, species) are *not* independent in the flame — they are coupled through the equation of state and the reaction rate. The following diagram shows the cross-couplings relevant to flame-structure analysis.

```mermaid

    M --> ST[State Equation p = rho R T]

    EN[Energy Equation\nrho u cp dT/dx = d/dx lambda dT/dx + q_dot] --> T[Temperature T_x]
    EN --> LAM[Thermal Conductivity lambda_T]
    EN --> QR[Heat Release Rate q_dot_T_Y]

    SP[Species Equation\nrho u dY_k/dx = d/dx rho D dY_k/dx + omega_k] --> Y[Mass Fractions Y_k_x]
    SP --> DK[Mass Diffusivity D_k_T]
    SP --> OK[Species Rate omega_k_T_Y]

    MM[Momentum Equation\nrho u du/dx = -dp/dx] --> P[Pressure p_x]
    MM --> S1

    ST --> M
    ST --> EN
    ST --> SP
    T --> EN
    Y --> EN
    Y --> SP
    T --> SP
    LAM --> EN
    DK --> SP
    QR --> EN
    OK --> SP
    P --> M

    classDef main fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef derived fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000

    class S1,T,Y,P,ST derived

```

### 4.3 Flame-Structure State Diagram — Method of Solution

This diagram shows the *computational* sequence used to solve the ZND structure problem, the standard KTU 2024 procedure for numerical questions on flame structure.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DefineInput
    DefineInput: Define Inputs\nphi, T_u, p, Fuel, Q_c, c_p
    DefineInput --> ComputeAdiabatic
    ComputeAdiabatic: Compute T_ad\nT_ad = T_u + Y_F Q_c / cp
    ComputeAdiabatic --> EstimateSL
    EstimateSL: Estimate S_L\nFrom correlation or experiment
    EstimateSL --> ComputeThickness
    ComputeThickness: Compute delta_L\ndelta_L = alpha / S_L
    ComputeThickness --> Integrate
    Integrate: Integrate ODE System\ndT/dx, dY_k/dx\nfrom x = -infinity to +infinity
    Integrate --> CheckConverge

```

### 4.4 Note on Physical Drawings

The Mermaid diagrams above replace the conventional hand-drawn sketches of the temperature, fuel-mass-fraction, and reaction-rate profiles that appear in textbooks. The functional architecture they represent is the exact KTU 2024 Scheme *intuitive picture*:

- Temperature $T$ rises monotonically from $T_u$ to $T_{ad}$.
- Fuel mass fraction $Y_F$ falls monotonically from $Y_{F,u}$ to 0.
- Reaction rate $\omega$ has a sharp peak inside the reaction zone (narrow Gaussian-like profile).
- Heat-release profile mirrors the reaction-rate profile (scaled by $Q_c$).
