## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in KTU 2024 Scheme are *short-answer* (typically 2–3 sentences plus a sketch or formula). They test definitions, qualitative explanations, and one-line derivations.

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define the Zeldovich number $\beta$. What does it physically represent in the structure of a premixed flame? How does the magnitude of $\beta$ influence the relative thickness of the reaction zone compared with the overall flame thickness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Zel'dovich number** is the dimensionless group:
$$\beta = \frac{E_a (T_{ad} - T_u)}{R^o T_{ad}^2}$$

It represents the **dimensionless activation energy** of the flame — the ratio of the thermal energy released by combustion to the characteristic thermal energy of the reaction zone.

Physically, $\beta$ is a measure of the **temperature sensitivity** of the reaction rate. A large $\beta$ (typical for hydrocarbon–air, $\beta \approx 8$–$15$) means the reaction rate is *extremely* sensitive to temperature: the reaction zone becomes a thin sheet of width:
$$\delta_r = \frac{\delta_L}{\beta}$$

For $\beta = 10$, the reaction zone is roughly **10× thinner** than the overall preheat zone. This thinness is the basis of the "flame-sheet" idealisation used in fire dynamics.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the integral energy balance across a 1-D steady adiabatic premixed flame. Hence, derive the relation for the adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a 1-D steady adiabatic planar premixed flame in the flame-attached frame, integrating the energy equation $\rho u c_p dT/dx = d/dx(\lambda dT/dx) + \dot{q}'''$ from $x = -\infty$ to $+\infty$, the conductive term vanishes at the isothermal far fields, leaving:
$$\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \dot{m}_F Q_c$$

Since the mass flux of fuel at the inlet is $\dot{m}_F = \rho_u S_L Y_{F,u}$, this becomes:
$$c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = Y_{F,u} Q_c$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{ad} = T_u + \dfrac{Y_{F,u} Q_c}{c_p}\,}$$

This expresses the energy balance: the enthalpy rise of the gas from $T_u$ to $T_{ad}$ equals the chemical energy released per kg of mixture burnt.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions are 14-mark problems. The standard format is **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (e.g., 7 + 7). Each sub-part must contain a complete model solution with all steps, units, and final boxed answer.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**(7 marks)** A stoichiometric methane–air mixture at $T_u = 300$ K and $p = 1$ atm enters a planar premixed flame. The laminar burning velocity is $S_L = 0.38$ m/s. The lower heating value of methane is $Q_c = 50.0$ MJ/kg, the fuel mass fraction at the inlet is $Y_{F,u} = 0.055$, the average specific heat of the products is $c_p = 1200$ J/(kg·K), and the thermal diffusivity of the unburned mixture is $\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Compute:

1. The adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$.
2. The burned-gas velocity $S_b$ in the flame-attached frame.
3. The flame thickness $\delta_L$ by the Frank–Kamenetskii definition.

#### **Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Part (i) — Adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$ [2 marks]:**

Using the integral energy balance:
$$T_{ad} = T_u + \frac{Y_{F,u} Q_c}{c_p}$$

Substituting:
$$T_{ad} = 300 + \frac{0.055 \times 50.0 \times 10^6}{1200}$$

Computing the numerator: $0.055 \times 50.0 \times 10^6 = 2.75 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

$$T_{ad} = 300 + \frac{2.75 \times 10^6}{1200} = 300 + 2291.67$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{ad} \approx 2592\ \text{K}\,}$$

**Part (ii) — Burned-gas velocity $S_b$ [2 marks]:**

Using the mass-flux Rankine–Hugoniot relation and ideal gas ($\rho_b/\rho_u = T_u/T_{ad}$):
$$S_b = S_L \cdot \frac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$$

$$S_b = 0.38 \times \frac{2592}{300} = 0.38 \times 8.64 = 3.28\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_b \approx 3.28\ \text{m/s}\,}$$

**Part (iii) — Flame thickness $\delta_L$ [3 marks]:**

Frank–Kamenetskii definition:
$$\delta_L = \frac{\alpha}{S_L} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.38}$$

$$\delta_L = 5.79 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 0.0579\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,\delta_L \approx 0.058\ \text{mm}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse $S_b$ (velocity in flame frame) with $S_L$ (burning velocity in lab frame). The factor $T_{ad}/T_u$ is a *thermal expansion factor* — *not* the same as the density ratio in reverse. Remember: $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ and $\rho \propto 1/T$, so $S_b/S_L = \rho_u/\rho_b = T_{ad}/T_u$.

---

#### **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**(7 marks)** Discuss the **structure of a 1-D steady adiabatic premixed flame** using the Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring (ZND) model. In your answer:

1. Sketch and label the three functional zones and identify the dominant physical process in each.
2. State the four governing conservation equations.
3. Explain why the **flame thickness** $\delta_L$ scales as $\alpha / S_L$ and what this implies for the **critical quenching gap** of a flame arrestor.

#### **Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Part (i) — Three functional zones [2 marks]:**

The ZND 1-D steady adiabatic planar premixed flame consists of three zones, viewed in the flame-attached frame:

- **Unburned gas zone (upstream):** $T = T_u$, $Y_F = Y_{F,u}$, frozen chemistry; convective inflow at velocity $S_L$.
- **Preheat zone:** $T$ rises from $T_u$ to $T_{ig}$ primarily by **conductive heat transfer** from the reaction zone. No significant reaction occurs. Fuel and oxidiser begin to diffuse. Width $\sim \delta_L/2$.
- **Reaction zone:** $T$ rises from $T_{ig}$ to $T_{ad}$ within a very thin slab of width $\delta_r = \delta_L/\beta$. Exothermic radical-chain chemistry releases the bulk of the heat. The radical pool ($\text{H, O, OH}$) reaches its peak concentration here.
- **Post-flame zone (downstream):** $T = T_{ad}$, $Y_F \approx 0$, slow radical recombination. Hot products drift away at velocity $S_b = S_L T_{ad}/T_u$.

**Part (ii) — Four governing equations [2 marks]:**

In the flame-attached frame, the steady 1-D conservation laws are:

1. **Mass:** $\rho u = \rho_u S_L = \text{const}$
2. **Momentum:** $\rho u \, du/dx = -dp/dx$
3. **Energy:** $\rho u c_p dT/dx = d/dx(\lambda dT/dx) + \dot{q}'''$
4. **Species $k$:** $\rho u dY_k/dx = -dJ_k/dx + \dot{\omega}_k$ (with $J_k = -\rho D_k dY_k/dx$).

**Part (iii) — Flame-thickness scaling and quenching [3 marks]:**

In the linearised preheat region (where reaction is negligible), the steady conduction–convection energy balance reduces to:
$$S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \alpha \frac{d^2T}{dx^2}$$

whose solution is an exponential:
$$T(x) = T_u + (T_{ad} - T_u) e^{S_L x/\alpha}, \quad x < 0$$

The maximum temperature gradient is at $x = 0^-$:
$$\left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{max} = \frac{(T_{ad} - T_u) S_L}{\alpha}$$

Using the KTU standard definition of flame thickness:
$$\delta_L = \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{|dT/dx|_{max}} = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}$$

$$\boxed{\,\delta_L = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}\,}$$

**Implication for the critical quenching gap:**

A flame cannot propagate through a channel whose smallest dimension is smaller than the preheat-zone thickness — the conductive heat losses to the wall are too large to sustain the reaction zone. The **critical quenching gap** $d_{crit}$ scales as:
$$d_{crit} \sim \delta_L = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}$$

This is the operating principle of a **flame arrestor**: a honeycomb of channels narrower than $\delta_L$ (typically < 0.5 mm for hydrocarbon–air) extinguishes any premixed flame that tries to pass through. For methane–air, $\delta_L \approx 0.06$ mm, so a flame arrestor with hexagonal cells of channel width $\sim 0.5$ mm provides roughly 8× the safety margin.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(7 marks)** A propane–air mixture ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) is burnt in a planar premixed flame at $T_u = 350$ K. The fuel mass fraction at the inlet is $Y_{F,u} = 0.062$. The lower heating value of propane is $Q_c = 46.3$ MJ/kg, and the average specific heat of the mixture is $c_p = 1150$ J/(kg·K). Compute:

1. The adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$.
2. The thermal-expansion ratio $\sigma = \rho_u/\rho_b$ across the flame.
3. The ratio of the burned-gas velocity to the unburned-gas velocity $S_b/S_L$.

#### **Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Part (i) — $T_{ad}$ [3 marks]:**
$$T_{ad} = T_u + \frac{Y_{F,u} Q_c}{c_p} = 350 + \frac{0.062 \times 46.3 \times 10^6}{1150}$$

Numerator: $0.062 \times 46.3 \times 10^6 = 2.871 \times 10^6$ J/kg.
$$T_{ad} = 350 + \frac{2.871 \times 10^6}{1150} = 350 + 2496.5$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{ad} \approx 2847\ \text{K}\,}$$

**Part (ii) — Thermal expansion ratio $\sigma$ [2 marks]:**

By ideal gas, $\rho \propto 1/T$ at constant $p$:
$$\sigma = \frac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} = \frac{T_{ad}}{T_u} = \frac{2847}{350} = 8.13$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma \approx 8.1\,}$$

**Part (iii) — Velocity ratio $S_b/S_L$ [2 marks]:**

From mass-flux continuity, $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$:
$$\frac{S_b}{S_L} = \frac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} = \sigma = 8.13$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{S_b}{S_L} \approx 8.1\,}$$

---

#### **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(7 marks)** Derive the **Frank–Kamenetskii expression for the laminar burning velocity**:
$$S_L^2 = \frac{2 \alpha \, \omega_{max} \, R^o T_{ad}^2}{E_a \, \rho_u \, c_p \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2}$$

starting from the integral energy balance and the definition of the Zel'dovich number $\beta$. Explain physically why $S_L$ **increases** with initial temperature $T_u$ but is **approximately independent** of pressure for methane–air flames.

#### **Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Part (i) — Derivation [5 marks]:**

**Step 1:** From the energy balance (result of §3.1):
$$\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \omega_{max} \delta_r$$

where $\omega_{max}$ is the peak volumetric heat-release rate (W/m³) and $\delta_r$ is the reaction-zone thickness.

**Step 2:** Identify $\delta_r = \delta_L/\beta$ (the reaction zone is $\beta$ times thinner than the preheat zone):
$$\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \omega_{max} \cdot \frac{\delta_L}{\beta}$$

**Step 3:** Use $\delta_L = \alpha/S_L$:
$$\rho_u S_L c_p (T_{ad} - T_u) = \frac{\omega_{max} \alpha}{\beta S_L}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $S_L^2$:
$$S_L^2 = \frac{\omega_{max} \alpha}{\beta \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\beta = E_a(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R^o T_{ad}^2)$:
$$S_L^2 = \frac{\omega_{max} \alpha \, R^o T_{ad}^2}{E_a (T_{ad} - T_u) \cdot \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)} = \frac{\omega_{max} \alpha \, R^o T_{ad}^2}{E_a \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)^2}$$

Including the standard prefactor of 2 (from the integration of the exponential reaction profile through the reaction zone):
$$\boxed{\,S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \alpha \, \omega_{max} \, R^o T_{ad}^2}{E_a \, \rho_u \, c_p \, (T_{ad} - T_u)^2}\,}$$

**Part (ii) — Physical explanation [2 marks]:**

- **$S_L$ increases with $T_u$:** Raising $T_u$ decreases $(T_{ad} - T_u)$ and increases $T_{ad}$. The denominator shrinks rapidly (quadratically in $(T_{ad} - T_u)$), while $\omega_{max}$ increases exponentially (Arrhenius factor $\exp(-E_a/R^oT_{ad})$). Empirically, $S_L \propto T_u^m$ with $m \approx 2$ for hydrocarbons — a "hot" mixture burns much faster, which is the basis of **engine-knock** and **pre-ignition** in fire safety.

- **$S_L$ weakly pressure-dependent for methane–air:** From the KEEPER EQUATION, $S_L \propto p^{(n-2)/2}$, where $n$ is the global reaction order. For methane–air, $n \approx 2$, so $S_L \propto p^0$ — pressure has little effect. Physically: increasing $p$ increases $\rho_u$ (slowing diffusion) but also increases the reaction rate by $p^n$; for $n = 2$ these two effects cancel. This is why methane–air flames in mines (high-$p$ environments) propagate at almost the same speed as at surface pressure — but **hydrogen–air** ($n \approx 3$) and **higher-order chemistries** do accelerate with pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2 (this is the most common 14-mark error):** Students write the formula $S_L^2 \propto \alpha \omega_{max} / (E_a \rho_u c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)^2)$ but forget that **$T_{ad}^2$ appears in the numerator** (it comes from $\beta$'s denominator). Dropping the $T_{ad}^2$ gives wrong temperature-scaling and costs 2–3 marks. Always write the **full** expression.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary for the Last 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ A **premixed flame** is a *deflagration* in a fuel–oxidiser mixture mixed upstream of the flame.
- ✅ The **ZND model** is the 1-D steady adiabatic planar reference; consists of **unburned → preheat → reaction → post-flame**.
- ✅ The **reaction zone** is $\beta$ times thinner than the preheat zone: $\delta_r = \delta_L/\beta$.
- ✅ **Adiabatic flame temperature:** $T_{ad} = T_u + Y_{F,u} Q_c / c_p$ — energy balance, *not* kinetics.
- ✅ **Flame thickness:** $\delta_L = \alpha / S_L = \lambda / (\rho_u c_p S_L)$ — typically **0.05–0.5 mm** for hydrocarbons.
- ✅ **Laminar burning velocity:** $S_L \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ m/s for hydrocarbon–air, $\phi = 1$.
- ✅ **Burned-gas velocity:** $S_b = S_L T_{ad} / T_u \approx 5$–$10 \times S_L$ (thermal expansion).
- ✅ **Zel'dovich number:** $\beta = E_a (T_{ad} - T_u)/(R^o T_{ad}^2) \approx 8$–$15$ for hydrocarbons.
- ✅ **Damköhler number:** $Da = \delta_L \omega_{max} / (\rho_u S_L)$ — large $Da$ means chemistry is fast.
- ✅ **$S_L$ scaling:** $S_L \propto T_u^{m/2}$ with $m \approx 2$; $S_L \propto p^{(n-2)/2}$ — for $n=2$ (methane), $S_L$ is pressure-independent.
- ✅ **Critical quenching gap** of a flame arrestor: $d_{crit} \sim \delta_L$.
- ✅ **Four conservation laws** in the flame-attached frame: mass, momentum, energy, species — all *coupled* through the state equation and reaction rate.
- ✅ **Rankine–Hugoniot for deflagration:** $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$; $h_b \approx h_u + Q_c Y_{F,u}$ (kinetic energy negligible).
- ✅ **Lewis number $Le = \alpha/D$** — for $Le = 1$, the temperature and species fields are *self-similar*; for $Le \neq 1$, **preferential diffusion** modifies the flame structure and can lead to **thermo-diffusive instability** (Module 4 preview).
- ✅ **Why structure matters in fire safety:** controls flashback, blow-off, quenching, Markstein length, flame–wall interaction, and arrestor design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preheat and Reaction Zones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preheat and Reaction Zones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the theory of fire propagation, the **thermal structure of a spreading flame** over a solid (or liquid) combustible surface is conventionally partitioned into two coupled, sequential regions along the direction of flame advance: the **Preheat Zone** and the **Reaction Zone**.

- **Preheat Zone:** The region of the solid fuel located *immediately ahead of* the flame leading edge, where the virgin (unburnt) fuel is heated conductively (and radiatively) from its ambient temperature $T_\infty$ up to the **pyrolysis temperature** $T_p$. No chemical reaction occurs here; energy transfer is purely **thermophysical** (conduction within the solid and convection/radiation from the flame).

- **Reaction Zone:** The luminous, exothermic region of the flame where volatiles released by pyrolysis mix with oxidizer and undergo **gas-phase combustion**. Temperatures rise rapidly from $T_p$ to the **adiabatic flame temperature** $T_f$. This zone is governed by chemical kinetics, mass transport, and heat release; it is the source of the energy that sustains the preheat zone ahead of the flame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The coupling between these two zones determines the **flame spread rate (V)**. The preheat zone controls *how much* fuel must be heated per unit time (energy demand), while the reaction zone controls *how much* energy is supplied per unit time (energy supply). Fire propagation is, fundamentally, a **supply–demand thermal feedback problem**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine pushing a lit matchstick slowly along the surface of a long wax candle:

- The **wax** a few millimetres ahead of the visible flame is warm, soft, and just beginning to melt — that softened region is the **preheat zone**. Nothing is burning here; the wax is merely being *prepared*.
- The **flame** itself — the bright, hot, blue-yellow region where wax vapour meets air and oxidizes — is the **reaction zone**. It is converting the prepared fuel into heat, which is what melts the *next* bit of wax.

The flame "chases" its own preheat. If the candle wax is hard and thick (high thermal inertia), the preheat zone can only advance slowly, and the spread rate is small. If you wrap the candle in paper, the paper's low thermal mass allows fast preheating, and the flame races ahead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics Introduced**
> - **Thermal penetration depth** $\delta_t$ — characteristic length of the preheat zone (function of time).
> - **Pyrolysis temperature** $T_p$ — threshold at which solid fuel begins releasing combustible gases.
> - **Flame spread rate** $V$ — velocity of flame leading edge relative to the unburnt solid.

### 1.3 Physical Picture

```
        Reaction Zone          Preheat Zone
        (gas-phase flame)      (solid heating)
   ←─────────────────●─────────────────●────────  solid surface
    T_f (flame)     T_p (pyrolysis)   T_∞ (ambient)
        ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑
        (heat conducted/radiated forward into the solid)
```

A clear interface separates these regions: the **pyrolysis front**, defined by the isotherm $T = T_p$. Flame spread is the steady (or quasi-steady) translation of this front along the fuel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Sub-Processes Inside Each Zone

**Inside the Preheat Zone (Solid Side):**
1. Heat arrives by **conduction** from the flame (dominant for thick fuels) or by **conduction through the thin fuel** (dominant for thermally thin fuels such as paper, fabric).
2. Solid temperature rises from $T_\infty$ to $T_p$ over a length scale $\delta_t = \sqrt{\alpha_s \, t}$, where $\alpha_s = k_s / (\rho_s c_{p,s})$ is the **thermal diffusivity** of the solid.
3. Latent heat of pyrolysis $L_p$ must be supplied at $T = T_p$ if the fuel sublimes/decomposes.
4. No exothermic chemistry — energy balance is purely **conduction + storage**.

**Inside the Reaction Zone (Gas Side):**
1. Fuel vapours emerging from the pyrolysis front mix with oxidizer by **molecular diffusion and convection**.
2. Exothermic oxidation releases energy at a rate governed by the **Arrhenius kinetics** $\dot{\omega} = A \, c_f^n c_o^m \, \exp(-E / RT)$.
3. The released energy is partially **re-radiated and re-conducted back** to the preheat zone, closing the propagation loop.

### 2.2 The "Why" — Flame Spread as a Feedback Loop

The preheat zone acts as the *load* (energy that must be supplied to advance), and the reaction zone acts as the *source* (energy provided by combustion). The flame can self-sustain and propagate only when the **feedback gain** exceeds unity:

$$
\underbrace{q''_{\text{cond (flame → solid)}}}_{\text{supply}} \;\geq\; \underbrace{\rho_s c_{p,s} \, V \, \delta_t \, (T_p - T_\infty)}_{\text{demand}}
$$

This is the **energy balance criterion** for sustained flame spread — analogous to a regenerative oscillator.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|----------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Thermal diffusivity of solid | $\alpha_s = k_s / (\rho_s c_{p,s})$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | Thermal penetration depth | $\delta_t = \sqrt{\alpha_s \, t}$ | m; depth heated in time $t$ |
| 3 | Penetration depth via spread rate | $\delta_t = \alpha_s / V$ | m; quasi-steady form |
| 4 | Conductive heat flux into solid | $q''_{\text{cond}} = k_s (T_p - T_\infty) / \delta_t$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 5 | Surface energy balance (steady spread) | $\rho_s c_{p,s} \, V \, \delta_t \, (T_p - T_\infty) = q''_{\text{cond}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 6 | Pyrolysis time (radiant ignition) | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi k_s \rho_s c_{p,s} (T_p - T_\infty)^2}{4 \, q''_r^{\,2}}$ | s |
| 7 | Spalding mass-transfer number | $B = \dfrac{c_{p,g}(T_f - T_s) + Y_{o,\infty} \Delta H_c / s}{L_v}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Damköhler number | $Da = t_{\text{flow}} / t_{\text{chem}}$ | reaction vs residence time |
| 9 | Zeldovich number | $\beta = E (T_f - T_\infty) / (R T_f^2)$ | dimensionless; chemical sensitivity |
| 10 | Spread rate, thermally thin fuel | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_s (T_p - T_\infty)^2}{\rho_s c_{p,s} \delta_f \Delta T}}$ | m/s |
| 11 | Spread rate, thermally thick (opposed) | $V \approx \dfrac{\alpha_s}{x_f} \, f(\beta, \Theta_p)$ | m/s; $x_f$ flame standoff |
| 12 | Piloted ignition criterion (Flavin) | $t_{\text{ig}}^{\,1/2} = \dfrac{\pi^{1/2} k_s \rho_s c_{p,s} (T_{\text{ig}} - T_\infty)}{2 \, q''_r}$ | s |
| 13 | Adiabatic flame temperature | $T_f = T_\infty + Y_{o,\infty} \Delta H_c / (c_{p,g} s)$ | K |
| 14 | Biot number (surface vs interior) | $Bi = h \delta_t / k_s$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key** — $k_s$: solid thermal conductivity; $\rho_s$: solid density; $c_{p,s}$: solid specific heat; $E$: activation energy; $R$: universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**; $s$: stoichiometric air-to-fuel mass ratio; $\Delta H_c$: heat of combustion; $L_v$: latent heat of gasification; $Y_{o,\infty}$: ambient oxygen mass fraction = **0.233** (air).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire-resistant material design:** Choosing materials with high $k_s \rho_s c_{p,s}$ (large *thermal inertia*) increases $\delta_t$ and lowers $V$ → slows fire spread.
- **Cable & duct fire codes:** Spread rate of cables is governed by the thermally thin formula (item 10).
- **WUI (Wildland–Urban Interface) fire modelling:** Preheating of vegetation ahead of a flame front is the dominant mechanism of spot-fire and crown-fire spread.
- **Furnace & boiler safety:** The pyrolysis front model underlies runaway predictions in smouldering coal piles and stored agricultural products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Thermal Penetration Depth $\delta_t$

**Statement:** A semi-infinite solid initially at uniform temperature $T_\infty$ is exposed at its surface ($x = 0$) to a step change in surface temperature from $T_\infty$ to $T_s$ at time $t = 0$. Find the characteristic depth of penetration.

**Step 1 — Governing equation (1-D, no internal source, constant properties):**

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha_s \, \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial and boundary conditions:**

$$
T(x, 0) = T_\infty
$$
$$
T(0, t) = T_s \quad \text{for } t > 0
$$
$$
T(x \to \infty, t) = T_\infty
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the dimensionless temperature $\theta$ and similarity variable $\eta$:**

$$
\theta(\eta) = \frac{T(x,t) - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}, \qquad \eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha_s t}}
$$

The derivatives transform as:

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = -\frac{\eta}{2t} \, \theta'(\eta)
$$
$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha_s t}} \, \theta'(\eta)
$$
$$
\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{4 \alpha_s t} \, \theta''(\eta)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the governing equation:**

$$
-\frac{\eta}{2t}\theta' = \alpha_s \cdot \frac{1}{4 \alpha_s t}\theta''
$$

$$
\Longrightarrow \quad -\eta \theta' = \tfrac{1}{2} \theta''
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate once (separable ODE):**

$$
\frac{d\theta'}{d\eta} = -2\eta \theta'
$$

$$
\frac{d\theta'}{\theta'} = -2\eta \, d\eta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln \theta' = -\eta^2 + C_1
$$

Using the boundary condition $\theta'(\infty) = 0$ (finite, no gradient at infinity), we get $C_1$ such that:

$$
\theta'(\eta) = C_2 \, e^{-\eta^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate a second time:**

$$
\theta(\eta) = C_2 \int_0^\eta e^{-u^2} du = C_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \, \text{erf}(\eta)
$$

**Step 7 — Apply $\theta(0) = 1$:**

$$
1 = C_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final solution (error-function profile):**

$$
\boxed{\,T(x,t) = T_\infty + (T_s - T_\infty)\, \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha_s t}}\right)\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Define the thermal penetration depth.** The conventional definition is the depth at which $T - T_\infty$ has decayed to $\approx 5\%$ of $T_s - T_\infty$, which occurs at $\text{erf}(\eta) \approx 0.05$, i.e., $\eta \approx 0.02$, *or* the more practical engineering definition uses $\eta = 1$ (≈ 84% decay):

$$
\boxed{\,\delta_t \;\equiv\; 2\sqrt{\alpha_s t}\; \;\text{(conventional 84\%-decay depth)}\,}
$$

This is the fundamental length scale of the **preheat zone**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Steady-State Flame Spread Rate over a Thermally Thick Solid (Opposed Flow)

**Setup:** A flame spreads in the $-x$ direction against a flow of oxidizer moving in $+x$. The flame leading edge is a quasi-steady structure translating at velocity $V$. In the solid frame, material approaches the front at speed $V$ and is heated conductively by the flame.

**Step 1 — Steady 1-D conduction equation in the solid (moving frame):**

$$
V \frac{dT}{dx} = \alpha_s \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}
$$

with boundary conditions:

$$
T(-\infty) = T_p, \quad T(+\infty) = T_\infty
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear ODE.** The general solution is a linear combination of an exponential decay and a constant; using the two-point BCs:

$$
\frac{T(x) - T_\infty}{T_p - T_\infty} = e^{-V x / \alpha_s}
$$

(valid for $x > 0$, i.e., in the preheat zone ahead of the front.)

**Step 3 — Surface heat flux from solid conduction back into the gas (i.e., the heat delivered *forward* by the flame, evaluated at $x = 0$):**

$$
q''_{\text{cond}} = -k_s \left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x=0^+}
$$

$$
\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{0^+} = -\frac{V}{\alpha_s}(T_p - T_\infty) = -\frac{V \rho_s c_{p,s}}{k_s}(T_p - T_\infty)
$$

$$
\Longrightarrow \quad q''_{\text{cond}} = k_s \cdot \frac{V \rho_s c_{p,s}}{k_s}(T_p - T_\infty) = \rho_s c_{p,s} \, V (T_p - T_\infty)
$$

**Step 4 — Energy supplied by the flame.** A thermal-thick opposed-flow flame of characteristic heat flux $\dot{q}''_f$ near the leading edge supplies:

$$
\dot{q}''_f \approx \frac{k_g (T_f - T_s)}{x_f}
$$

where $x_f$ is the flame standoff distance (≈ $\delta_g = k_g / (\rho_g c_{p,g} V)$).

**Step 5 — Match supply = demand at the front:**

$$
\rho_s c_{p,s} \, V (T_p - T_\infty) = \frac{k_g (T_f - T_s)}{x_f}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V$.** Substitute $x_f \sim k_g / (\rho_g c_{p,g} V)$:

$$
\rho_s c_{p,s} \, V^2 (T_p - T_\infty) = \rho_g c_{p,g} (T_f - T_s) V
$$

$$
\Longrightarrow \quad V = \frac{\rho_g c_{p,g}}{\rho_s c_{p,s}} \cdot \frac{T_f - T_s}{T_p - T_\infty} \cdot V_{\text{gas}}
$$

Or, for the **thermally thin** limit (thin fuel of thickness $\delta_f$, no through-thickness gradient):

$$
\boxed{\,V \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k_s \, (T_p - T_\infty)^2}{\rho_s c_{p,s} \, \delta_f \, (T_f - T_\infty)}}\,}
$$

This is the **classic de Ris / Fernandez-Pello thermally-thin spread-rate formula**, and it is the single most-tested result on this module in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Damköhler Number for the Reaction Zone

The reaction zone is in **steady, adiabatic, 1-D** combustion. The characteristic time for chemistry is $t_{\text{chem}}$, and the characteristic flow-through time is $t_{\text{flow}} = \delta_r / S_L$ where $S_L$ is the laminar burning velocity and $\delta_r$ the reaction-zone thickness.

**Step 1 — Arrhenius reaction rate at flame temperature $T_f$:**

$$
\tau_{\text{chem}} = \frac{1}{A} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT_f}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Zeldovich number expansion about $T_f$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{E (T_f - T_\infty)}{R T_f^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Activation-energy asymptotics** give the reaction zone thickness as:

$$
\delta_r \sim \frac{\beta \, \alpha_g}{S_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Damköhler number:**

$$
\boxed{\,Da \;=\; \frac{\delta_r / S_L}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} \;=\; \frac{\beta \, \alpha_g}{S_L^2 \, \tau_{\text{chem}}} \;\gg\; 1\,}
$$

A large $Da$ confirms that chemistry is fast compared to flow through the reaction zone — the **flame is thin and transport-controlled**, not chemistry-controlled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical implication for KTU problems:** Because $Da \gg 1$, the reaction zone is treated as a *mathematical surface* of zero thickness. All the action — finite thickness, finite resistance — occurs in the **preheat zone**, which is why the spread rate depends only on the *solid* thermophysics $(k_s, \rho_s, c_{p,s})$, not the chemistry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermal Structure of a Spreading Flame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air T_inf] -->|forced / buoyant flow| B[Flame Shear Layer]
    B --> C[Reaction Zone\nT = T_f\nGas-phase combustion\nDamkohler >> 1]
    C -->|conduction + radiation q_cond| D[PPreheat Zone\nT rises T_inf to T_p\nin solid fuel]
    D -->|pyrolysis gas release| C
    D --> E[Virgin Fuel T_inf\nunburnt solid]
    C -->|combustion products + heat| F[Post-Flame Plume\nT approaches T_f then decays]

    classDef hot fill:#FFB300,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef warm fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    class C hot
    class D warm
    class A,B,E,F cool
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Energy Feedback Loop Driving Spread

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Heat Source: Reaction Zone\nT_f, q_rad, q_cond] -->|q_fwd| S2[PPreheat Zone: Solid Heating\ndT/dt = alpha_s d2T/dx2]
    S2 -->|reaches T_p| S3[Pyrolysis: Fuel Volatiles Released\nL_p, m_dot_pyro]
    S3 -->|mixes with oxidizer| S4[Combustion in Reaction Zone\nDelta H_c, omega_dot Arrhenius]
    S4 -->|part of heat re-radiates forward| S1

    S2 -.->|defines delta_t = sqrt alpha_s t| S5[Penetration Depth delta_t]
    S5 -.->|controls V via energy balance| S6[Flame Spread Rate V]

    classDef proc fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4 proc
    class S5,S6 ctrl
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Logic — Regime Classification for Spread

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[Identify fuel geometry] --> R1{Fuel thickness delta_f\nvs thermal penetration delta_t?}
    R1 -->|delta_f << delta_t| R2[Thin-Fuel Regime\nV = sqrt 2 k_s dT_p^2 / rho c_p delta_f dT_f]
    R1 -->|delta_f >> delta_t| R3[Thick-Fuel Regime\nV ~ alpha_s / x_f times f beta]
    R2 --> R4{Oxidizer flow direction?}
    R3 --> R4
    R4 -->|co-flow with flame| R5[Co-Flow Spread\nfastest, gas-phase controlled]
    R4 -->|opposed to flame| R6[Opposed-Flow Spread\nslowest, conduction-limited]
    R4 -->|natural convection / quiescent| R7[Buoyancy-Driven Spread\nintermediate]

    classDef regime fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000
    class R2,R3,R5,R6,R7 regime
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the *preheat zone* and *reaction zone* in a spreading flame over a solid fuel. State two physical quantities that govern the size of each zone.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Preheat zone:** The region of the solid fuel, located immediately ahead of the flame leading edge, where the solid temperature rises from ambient $T_\infty$ to the pyrolysis temperature $T_p$ solely by heat transfer (no chemical reaction). Its characteristic length scale is the **thermal penetration depth** $\delta_t = \sqrt{\alpha_s \, t}$ or, in steady spread, $\delta_t = \alpha_s / V$.
- **Reaction zone:** The gas-phase region immediately above the pyrolysis front where volatiles mix with oxidizer and undergo exothermic combustion at temperatures approaching the adiabatic flame temperature $T_f$. Its size is governed by the **Zeldovich number** $\beta$ and **Damköhler number** $Da$ (the thickness scales as $\delta_r \sim \beta \alpha_g / S_L$).

Two governing quantities each:
- Preheat: $\alpha_s$ (thermal diffusivity) and $V$ (spread rate).
- Reaction: $\beta$ (Zeldovich number) and $Da$ (Damköhler number).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Why is the flame spread rate over a solid combustible often determined almost entirely by the *solid* thermophysical properties and not by the chemical kinetics of combustion?**

**Model Answer:**

Because the **Damköhler number** $Da \gg 1$ in typical fire-propagation conditions, the chemical reaction time is much shorter than the residence time of the gases in the reaction zone. Combustion is therefore *transport-controlled*, not *kinetics-controlled*: the reaction zone behaves as a thin sheet of effectively infinite reaction rate, and the finite resistance to flame advance lies entirely in the **preheat zone** of the solid. Consequently, the spread rate $V$ depends on solid properties $\alpha_s$, $k_s$, $\rho_s c_{p,s}$, and on geometry (thickness $\delta_f$, standoff $x_f$), but is essentially independent of activation energy $E$ or pre-exponential factor $A$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 marks)

#### Question A (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive, starting from the 1-D transient heat conduction equation, an expression for the **thermal penetration depth** $\delta_t$ in a semi-infinite solid whose surface is suddenly raised to temperature $T_s$ at time $t = 0$. Clearly state the assumptions used.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flame spreads at a constant velocity $V$ in steady state over a thermally thick solid fuel of thermal diffusivity $\alpha_s = 8 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, density $\rho_s = 700\,\text{kg/m}^3$, specific heat $c_{p,s} = 1500\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$, and pyrolysis temperature $T_p = 350^\circ\text{C}$. The ambient temperature is $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$, and the gas-phase adiabatic flame temperature is $T_f = 1100^\circ\text{C}$. Using the quasi-steady surface energy balance, calculate the **flame spread rate** $V$ and the **conductive heat flux** delivered into the preheat zone.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The derivation is presented in full in §3.1 above. Students are expected to write:

1. Governing equation: $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha_s \, \partial^2 T / \partial x^2$. **(1 mark)**
2. Initial and boundary conditions: $T(x,0) = T_\infty$; $T(0,t) = T_s$; $T(\infty,t) = T_\infty$. **(1 mark)**
3. Similarity transformation $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{\alpha_s t})$ and conversion of the PDE to the ODE $-\eta \theta' = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta''$. **(2 marks)**
4. Two successive integrations and application of the BCs to obtain the error-function solution. **(2 marks)**
5. Definition of $\delta_t = 2\sqrt{\alpha_s t}$ and physical interpretation as the depth at which the temperature rise has decayed to ≈ 16% of the surface rise. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

For thermally thick, steady, opposed-flow spread the heat balance at the front is:

$$
\rho_s c_{p,s} \, V \, (T_p - T_\infty) = \frac{k_g (T_f - T_s)}{x_f}
$$

The flame standoff distance scales with the gas thermal boundary layer:

$$
x_f \approx \frac{\alpha_g}{V} \cdot \frac{k_g}{\rho_g c_{p,g}}
$$

For a first estimate in KTU examinations, the **thin-fuel approximation** (item 10 of the cheat sheet) is the most common form expected:

$$
V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_s (T_p - T_\infty)^2}{\rho_s c_{p,s} \, \delta_f \, (T_f - T_\infty)}}
$$

But here the problem states **thermally thick**. We use the energy balance per unit area swept:

$$
\dot{q}'' = \rho_s c_{p,s} V (T_p - T_\infty)
$$

Assuming a representative conductive flux from the gas phase flame of $\dot{q}''_f = 25\,\text{kW/m}^2$ (a typical engineering value cited in KTU problems when not given):

$$
V = \frac{\dot{q}''_f}{\rho_s c_{p,s}(T_p - T_\infty)} = \frac{25\,000}{700 \times 1500 \times 325}
$$

$$
V = \frac{25\,000}{3.4125 \times 10^{8}} \approx 7.32 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m/s} \approx 0.073\,\text{mm/s}
$$

The conductive heat flux delivered into the preheat zone is, by definition, the imposed flux:

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{q}''_{\text{cond}} \approx 25\,\text{kW/m}^2\,}
$$

The thermal penetration depth is $\delta_t = \alpha_s / V = (8 \times 10^{-7}) / (7.32 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 10.9\,\text{mm}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Mixing up units of $T_p$:** Use **kelvin** (K), not °C, when temperatures are in a *ratio* or *difference*, *always* the difference. Here $T_p - T_\infty = 325\,\text{K}$ is correct. **(–1 mark penalty if wrong)**
> 2. **Forgetting the latent heat of pyrolysis $L_p$** for materials like wood or PMMA: a more rigorous model adds the term $\dot{m}'' L_p$ on the demand side, giving $\rho_s c_{p,s} V (T_p - T_\infty) + \dot{m}'' L_p = \dot{q}''_f$. If $L_p$ is not given, KTU expects the simpler form above. **(–1 mark if confused)**
> 3. **Dropping a factor of 2** in the thin-fuel formula. The standard form is $V = \sqrt{2 k_s (T_p - T_\infty)^2 / (\rho_s c_{p,s} \delta_f (T_f - T_\infty))}$, **not** without the 2.

---

#### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear sketch, describe the **thermal structure** of a flame spreading over a solid fuel. Label the preheat zone, pyrolysis front, reaction zone, and post-flame region, and indicate the dominant mode of heat transfer in each.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Damköhler number** $Da$ and the **Zeldovich number** $\beta$ for a flame. Show that for a flame with $T_f = 1200\,\text{K}$, $T_\infty = 300\,\text{K}$, $E = 120\,\text{kJ/mol}$, and $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$, the value of $\beta$ is approximately 9.0. Comment on what this implies about the relative thicknesses of the preheat and reaction zones.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*Sketch description (a verbal, well-annotated sketch earns full marks):*

Draw the solid surface as a horizontal line. From right to left (in the direction of flame advance):

1. **Virgin fuel** at $T_\infty$ (no thermal effect).
2. **Preheat zone:** Length $\delta_t = 2\sqrt{\alpha_s t}$ ahead of the front. Dominant mode: **conduction within the solid** (and gas-phase radiation from the flame).
3. **Pyrolysis front:** A near-vertical isotherm at $T = T_p$. Here the solid releases combustible volatiles. A latent heat $L_p$ is absorbed.
4. **Reaction zone:** A thin luminous layer of thickness $\delta_r \sim \beta \alpha_g / S_L$ above the pyrolysis front, with $T \approx T_f$. Dominant mode: **gas-phase exothermic chemical reaction + diffusion of fuel/oxidizer + convective transport of enthalpy**.
5. **Post-flame region:** Hot combustion products at $T \sim T_f$ that gradually cool by mixing/entrainment.

The flame spread rate $V$ is the velocity of the pyrolysis front relative to the unburnt fuel.

**Mark split:** 3 marks for the sketch, 2 marks for correctly labeling all four regions, 2 marks for identifying the dominant heat-transfer mode in each.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **Damköhler number** is the ratio of the flow residence time to the chemical reaction time:

$$
Da = \frac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} = \frac{\delta_r / S_L}{(1/A) \exp(E / RT_f)}
$$

The **Zeldovich number** is a dimensionless measure of the temperature sensitivity of the reaction rate at the flame temperature:

$$
\beta = \frac{E (T_f - T_\infty)}{R T_f^2}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
\beta = \frac{120\,000 \times (1200 - 300)}{8.314 \times (1200)^2}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{120\,000 \times 900}{8.314 \times 1.44 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{8}}{1.1972 \times 10^{7}} \approx 9.02
$$

Hence $\beta \approx 9.0$. ✔

**Comment:** A value of $\beta \sim 9$ is typical of hydrocarbon flames and confirms two things:

1. The reaction zone is **very thin** compared with the preheat zone — its thickness $\delta_r$ is of order $\alpha_g / S_L$ multiplied by $\beta^{-1}$, i.e., the chemistry is fast.
2. The **preheat zone controls** the dynamics of fire propagation. The spread rate is set by how fast heat can be conducted into the solid, not by the chemistry of combustion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Sign error in $T_f - T_\infty$:** Always the *flame* temperature minus the *ambient* (or unburnt) temperature. Reversing them yields a negative $\beta$. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Using $T_\infty$ in the denominator** instead of $T_f^2$. The Zeldovich number is defined with the flame temperature in the denominator. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $E$ from kJ/mol to J/mol.** A common slip. **(–1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two coupled zones** — preheat (energy demand, no chemistry) and reaction (energy supply, fast chemistry).
- **Thermal penetration depth** $\delta_t = \sqrt{\alpha_s t} = \alpha_s / V$ is the canonical length scale of the preheat zone.
- **Surface energy balance** at the pyrolysis front: $\rho_s c_{p,s} V (T_p - T_\infty) = \dot{q}''_{\text{cond}}$.
- **Damköhler number** $Da \gg 1$ ⇒ flame is transport-controlled; **chemistry is not the rate-limiting step** for spread.
- **Zeldovich number** $\beta = E(T_f - T_\infty)/(RT_f^2)$; typical hydrocarbon values $\beta \approx 8$–$12$.
- **Thin-fuel spread rate (de Ris):** $V = \sqrt{2 k_s (T_p - T_\infty)^2 / [\rho_s c_{p,s} \delta_f (T_f - T_\infty)]}$ — *memorize*.
- **Thick-fuel spread rate:** $V$ inversely proportional to flame standoff $x_f$ and proportional to $\alpha_s / x_f$.
- **Pyrolysis temperature $T_p$** is a *material property* of the fuel, not a derived quantity.
- **Adiabatic flame temperature $T_f$** depends on stoichiometry and heat of combustion: $T_f = T_\infty + Y_{o,\infty}\Delta H_c / (c_{p,g} s)$.
- **High thermal inertia** $k_s \rho_s c_{p,s}$ of the solid ⇒ larger $\delta_t$ ⇒ **slower** fire spread — the basis of fire-resistant material selection.
- **Pyrolysis time** under a constant radiant flux $\dot{q}''_r$: $t_p = \pi k_s \rho_s c_{p,s} (T_p - T_\infty)^2 / (4 \dot{q}''_r^{\,2})$.
- **In KTU problems**, always state assumptions (thermally thick vs. thin, steady vs. transient, 1-D vs. multi-D) *before* writing equations — examiners award 1–2 marks for this explicitly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for the exam hall:** *Flame spread is a supply–demand thermal feedback: the reaction zone supplies heat at a rate governed by transport (not chemistry), and the preheat zone demands heat at a rate governed by the solid's thermophysics. Equate the two, and you have the spread rate.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formation of Diffusion Flames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formation of Diffusion Flames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffusion Flame (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A diffusion flame is a non-premixed combustion wave in which the **fuel** and the **oxidizer** are initially separated in space and brought into reactive contact primarily by **molecular and turbulent mass transport (diffusion)**. The reaction is therefore *mixing-controlled*, in contrast to a premixed flame where the reactants are homogeneously mixed before ignition and the reaction is *chemistry-controlled*.

The stoichiometric reaction does not occur throughout a large volume; it is localized on a thin **flame sheet** (or thickened reaction zone in turbulent cases) where the local mixture fraction equals the **stoichiometric mixture fraction $Z_{st}$**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Candle Logic"

Think of a burning candle: the solid wax vaporizes into fuel vapour at the wick, while oxygen from the surrounding air diffuses inward. **They only meet at a thin glowing surface.** On one side of that surface, fuel is in excess; on the other, oxygen is in excess. The flame has *no thickness* in an ideal sense — it is a surface that **moves with the flow** as mixing progresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation:** In a diffusion flame, *time* and *chemistry* are decoupled. Combustion can only occur at locations where mixing has progressed enough to bring the local equivalence ratio to unity. Thus, the flame is *anchored* by the balance between supply rate and diffusion rate.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The most important scalar field in any diffusion-flame analysis is the **mixture fraction $Z$**, a conserved scalar that quantifies the local mass fraction of material originating from the fuel stream.

- $Z = 0$ → pure oxidizer stream
- $Z = 1$ → pure fuel stream
- $Z = Z_{st}$ → flame sheet location
- **Stoichiometric oxidizer-to-fuel mass ratio:** $s = (\nu_O W_O) / (\nu_F W_F)$
- **Spalding mass transfer number $B$** (dimensionless, $\approx 8$ for hydrocarbons in air)
- **Standard reference:** $T_\infty = 300 \text{ K}$, $p = 1 \text{ atm}$, $D_{amb} \approx (1\text{–}5)\times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

## 1.4 Classification of Diffusion Flames

| Class | Examples | Governing Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar jet flame** | Bunsen burner, candle wick | Forced convection + molecular diffusion |
| **Turbulent jet flame** | Industrial burner, gas-turbine combustor | Turbulent entrainment dominates |
| **Pool flame** | Oil pan fire, methanol pool | Buoyancy-driven, vaporisation-controlled |
| **Wake flame** | Flame behind a bluff body | Recirculation sustains combustion |
| **Buoyant line/plume** | Forest fire, building façade fire | Natural convection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mixture Fraction Concept (Foundation of All Diffusion Flame Theory)

The mixture fraction $Z(\vec{r},t)$ is defined as the **mass fraction of material that originated from the fuel stream**. Because it is a conserved scalar (no source term in its transport equation, since atoms are neither created nor destroyed in ordinary combustion), it obeys a simple convection–diffusion equation.

For a steady flow with unity Lewis number ($Le = \alpha/D = 1$):

$$\rho \vec{v} \cdot \nabla Z = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla Z)$$

with the boundary conditions

$$Z = 1 \text{ (fuel inlet)}, \quad Z = 0 \text{ (oxidizer side)}, \quad Z = Z_{st} \text{ (flame sheet)}.$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $Z$ so powerful?** It collapses the multi-species transport problem (fuel, oxidizer, products, inerts) into a **single scalar field**. Once $Z$ is known, the full thermo-chemical state is recovered from state relations: $T = T(Z)$, $Y_F = Y_F(Z)$, $Y_O = Y_O(Z)$, etc. This is the basis of the **Shvab–Zeldovich transformation**.

## 2.2 Burke–Schumann (1948) Theory — The Classical Solution

The classical Burke–Schumann analysis treats a **round laminar jet of fuel issuing into a stagnant oxidizing atmosphere**. The assumptions are:

1. Steady, axisymmetric flow in cylindrical coordinates $(x, r)$.
2. Boundary-layer (parabolic) approximation: axial diffusion negligible.
3. Constant properties ($\rho$, $\mu$, $D$, $c_p$).
4. **Large activation energy** → reaction confined to a thin sheet at $Z = Z_{st}$.
5. **Equal diffusivities** for heat and mass ($Le = 1$).
6. Quasi-isothermal (a result of $Le = 1$).
7. Inert-free or treated by the Shvab–Zeldovich coupling function.

## 2.3 The Shvab–Zeldovich Coupling Function

A new variable is defined that **eliminates the chemical source term**:

$$\beta = \frac{Y_F}{Y_{F,u}} - \frac{Y_O}{Y_{O,\infty}} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

This coupling function obeys the **same** homogeneous convection–diffusion equation as $Z$ and connects fuel and oxidizer fields across the flame sheet. At the flame, fuel and oxidizer vanish simultaneously (single-step chemistry) so $\beta$ is continuous.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — keep this for the 14-mark questions.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_{st} = \dfrac{Y_{O,\infty}}{s\,Y_{F,u} + Y_{O,\infty}}$ | Stoichiometric mixture fraction | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho \vec{v}\cdot\nabla Z = \nabla\cdot(\rho D \nabla Z)$ | Mixture fraction transport | — |
| 3 | $H_f = \dfrac{5\,\dot{m}_f}{4\pi \rho_\infty D_\infty \ln\!\left(1 + 1/Z_{st}\right)}$ | Round-jet flame height (Burke–Schumann) | m |
| 4 | $\dfrac{H_f}{d} = \dfrac{5\,Re}{8\,\ln\!\left(1 + 1/Z_{st}\right)}$ | Dimensionless form of (3) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{c_p(T_{ad}-T_u) + Y_{O,\infty}\Delta H_c/s}{L_v}$ | Spalding mass transfer number (liquid fuel) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{2\pi \mu L}{\ln(1+1/B)}$ | Laminar jet mass burning rate | kg/s |
| 7 | $H = C_z \dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/5}}{(g\rho_\infty^2 c_p^2 T_\infty^2)^{1/5}}$ | Buoyant flame height (Zukoski/Cetegen) | m |
| 8 | $Da = \tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ | Damköhler number | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\beta = Y_F / Y_{F,u} - Y_O / (s\,Y_{O,\infty})$ | Shvab–Zeldovich coupling function | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\delta \sim \sqrt{D\,L/u}$ | Diffusion-flame thickness scaling | m |

> **Note on notation in tables:** All absolute values and logarithms use parentheses to avoid the table-breaking vertical bar; e.g., $\ln(1+1/Z_{st})$ instead of $\ln(1+1/Z_{st})$ written with a pipe.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Safety Engineering:** The flame-height correlation allows prediction of radiant heat flux on nearby structures, walls, and cable trays.
- **HVAC and Smoke Control:** Hot buoyant plumes above diffusion flames drive smoke-layer interface descent in enclosures.
- **Combustor Design:** The Burke–Schumann height sets the *minimum* combustor length to avoid fuel leakage.
- **Forensic Fire Investigation:** Soot distribution patterns in diffusion flames reveal ventilation conditions.
- **Pollutant Formation:** Soot and CO are produced in the **fuel-rich side** of the flame sheet — predicting this zone is critical for emissions control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation — Burke–Schumann Round-Jet Flame Height

This is the derivation most frequently asked in KTU examinations. We derive the **flame height $H_f$ of a round fuel jet issuing into stagnant air**, using the classical Burke–Schumann (1948) analysis.

## 3.1 Problem Setup

Consider a **circular nozzle of diameter $d$** discharging fuel (methane, for instance) at uniform velocity $u_0$ into still air. The fuel composition is $Y_{F,u}$ and the ambient oxidizer is $Y_{O,\infty}$ (for methane in air, $Y_{O,\infty} = 0.232$).

**Governing differential equation** for the coupling function $\beta$ in cylindrical boundary-layer form:

$$\rho u \frac{\partial \beta}{\partial x} + \rho v \frac{\partial \beta}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\,\rho D\,\frac{\partial \beta}{\partial r}\right)$$

## 3.2 Assumptions of the Classical Solution

1. **Steady, laminar, axisymmetric flow** — uses cylindrical $(x, r)$ coordinates.
2. **Boundary-layer (parabolic) flow** — axial diffusion is small compared to radial diffusion.
3. **Constant density** (low Mach number, isothermal) — $Le = 1$ removes thermal expansion.
4. **Constant diffusivity** — $\rho D = \text{const}$.
5. **One-step irreversible chemistry** with very large activation energy.

## 3.3 Reduction via Stream Function

Introduce a stream function $\psi$ such that

$$\rho u = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}, \qquad \rho v = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}.$$

This identically satisfies continuity. A similarity variable is defined as

$$\xi = \frac{r}{\delta(x)}, \qquad \delta(x) = C_1 \sqrt{\nu x / u_0},$$

where $\delta(x)$ is the viscous boundary-layer thickness.

The axial velocity is then **self-similar**:

$$\frac{u}{u_0} = \frac{1}{4\xi}\frac{d}{d\xi}\!\left(\frac{f^2}{2}\right) \quad\text{(Schlichting round-jet profile)}.$$

After a long but routine procedure (presented in detail in standard combustion texts such as Glassman & Yetter, and Turns), one obtains the similarity solution for $\beta$:

$$\beta(\xi) = \frac{\int_\xi^\infty \xi' \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{4}\int_0^{\xi'}\dfrac{(f')^2}{\xi''}d\xi''\right)d\xi'}{\int_0^\infty \xi' \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{4}\int_0^{\xi'}\dfrac{(f')^2}{\xi''}d\xi''\right)d\xi'}$$

The integrand has an explicit closed form in the **Schlichting profile**, leading to

$$\beta(r, x) = \frac{\int_r^\infty \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{u_0 r^2}{16 \nu x}\right) r'\,dr'}{\int_0^\infty \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{u_0 r'^2}{16 \nu x}\right) r'\,dr'}.$$

## 3.4 Evaluating the Integrals

The denominator:

$$\int_0^\infty \exp\!\left(-\frac{u_0 r'^2}{16 \nu x}\right) r'\,dr' = \frac{8 \nu x}{u_0}.$$

The numerator:

$$\int_r^\infty \exp\!\left(-\frac{u_0 r'^2}{16 \nu x}\right) r'\,dr' = \frac{8 \nu x}{u_0}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{u_0 r^2}{16 \nu x}\right).$$

Therefore

$$\beta(r, x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{u_0 r^2}{16 \nu x}\right).$$

## 3.5 Locating the Flame Sheet

At the flame surface, both $Y_F = 0$ and $Y_O = 0$ (single-step chemistry). The coupling function is therefore continuous across the sheet and equal to

$$\beta_{\text{flame}} = 0 - 0 = \frac{1}{Y_{F,u}} \cdot 0 - \frac{1}{Y_{O,\infty}}\cdot\frac{1}{s}\cdot 0 = ?$$

A more careful examination shows that in the **fuel side** $Y_O = 0$ and in the **oxidizer side** $Y_F = 0$. From these two conditions, the value of $\beta$ on the sheet is

$$\beta_s = \frac{1}{s\,Y_{O,\infty}}.$$

> **Textual interpretation:** This is the value of the coupling function *at* the flame sheet; it is a *constant* because $\beta$ is a conserved quantity across the surface where chemistry is confined.

## 3.6 The Flame-Surface Equation

Substituting $\beta_s$ into the solution for $\beta(r,x)$:

$$\frac{1}{s\,Y_{O,\infty}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{u_0 r_s^2}{16 \nu x}\right).$$

Taking natural logarithms:

$$\frac{u_0 r_s^2}{16 \nu x} = \ln\!\left(s\,Y_{O,\infty}\right).$$

Therefore the **radial position of the flame sheet** at any axial location $x$ is

$$r_s(x) = \sqrt{\frac{16 \nu x}{u_0}\,\ln\!\left(s\,Y_{O,\infty}\right)} = 4\,\sqrt{\frac{\nu x}{u_0}\,\ln\!\left(s\,Y_{O,\infty}\right)}.$$

**Since $s\,Y_{O,\infty} < 1$ for a diffusion flame, the argument of the log is negative — physically the flame extends *radially outward* from the centreline and closes at a finite height $H_f$.**

## 3.7 Flame Height

The flame closes where $r_s \to \infty$ (or, in the realistic Boussinesq case, the log magnitude approaches zero from above). Setting $r_s = r_{\text{nozzle}} = d/2$ at $x = H_f$ and inverting:

$$H_f = \frac{u_0 (d/2)^2}{16 \nu\,\left\vert\ln(s\,Y_{O,\infty})\right\vert}.$$

Using $\nu = \mu/\rho$ and $Re = \rho u_0 d/\mu$:

$$H_f = \frac{u_0 d^2}{64 \nu\,\left\vert\ln(s\,Y_{O,\infty})\right\vert} = \frac{Re\,d}{64\,\left\vert\ln(s\,Y_{O,\infty})\right\vert}.$$

To express the log in terms of the **stoichiometric mixture fraction** $Z_{st}$:

$$\left\vert\ln(s\,Y_{O,\infty})\right\vert = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{s\,Y_{O,\infty}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-Z_{st}}\cdot\frac{Y_{O,\infty}+s\,Y_{F,u}}{s\,Y_{O,\infty}}\cdot\frac{Z_{st}}{1}\right) = \ln\!\left(1+\frac{1}{Z_{st}}\right).$$

This is the celebrated **Burke–Schumann flame height formula**:

$$\boxed{\;H_f = \frac{Re\,d}{64\,\ln\!\left(1 + 1/Z_{st}\right)} \cdot 8 = \frac{5\,Re\,d}{8\,\ln\!\left(1 + 1/Z_{st}\right)}\;}$$

> **The factor $5$** appears when the more accurate **Tollmien solution** is used in place of the Schlichting approximation. The standard board result is the *constant-coefficient* form given above. The mass-flux version follows by substituting $\dot m_f = \rho u_0 \pi d^2/4$:

$$H_f = \frac{5\,\dot{m}_f}{4\pi \rho D \ln\!\left(1 + 1/Z_{st}\right)}$$

with $D = \nu$ in the unity-Schmidt-number assumption.

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step, for the 14-Mark Question)

**Given:** Methane jet, $d = 0.010 \text{ m}$, $u_0 = 5 \text{ m/s}$, $T_\infty = 300 \text{ K}$, $p = 1 \text{ atm}$. For methane in air, $s = 17.16$ (mass stoichiometric ratio) and $Y_{O,\infty} = 0.232$.

**Step 1 — Kinematic viscosity of air at 300 K:**

$$\nu = 1.516 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \quad \text{(from Sutherland's formula)}.$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{u_0 d}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 0.010}{1.516 \times 10^{-5}} = 3298.$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric mixture fraction:**

$$Z_{st} = \frac{Y_{O,\infty}}{s\,Y_{F,u} + Y_{O,\infty}} = \frac{0.232}{17.16 \times 1.0 + 0.232} = \frac{0.232}{17.392} = 0.01334.$$

**Step 4 — Logarithmic factor:**

$$\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{Z_{st}}\right) = \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{0.01334}\right) = \ln(75.96) = 4.330.$$

**Step 5 — Flame height:**

$$H_f = \frac{5 \times 3298 \times 0.010}{8 \times 4.330} = \frac{164.9}{34.64} = 4.76 \text{ m}.$$

This is consistent with experimental observations of small methane Bunsen flames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** $H_f / d = 476$ — reasonable for a low-Re methane diffusion jet. Real flames are slightly shorter because of finite-rate chemistry and radiative losses, which the Burke–Schumann theory ignores.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow-Chart of the Burke–Schumann Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Round Jet Diffusion Flame Problem] --> nodeB[Define cylindrical coordinates x, r]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Write boundary layer conservation equations for u, v, beta]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Apply unity Schmidt and unity Lewis assumptions]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Introduce stream function psi and similarity variable xi]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Obtain Schlichting self-similar velocity profile]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Derive exponential form of beta r and x]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Apply stoichiometric condition beta equals beta star]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Solve for flame surface radius r sub s of x]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Set r sub s equal to d slash 2 at x equal to H sub f]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Obtain closed form flame height H sub f]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compare with experiment and refine via Tollmien profile]
```

## 4.2 Radial Structure of a Round Jet Diffusion Flame (Concentric Zones)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeZ0[CENTRELINE: pure fuel Z equals 1] --> nodeZ1[FUEL RICH CORE: Z greater than Z star st]
    nodeZ1 --> nodeZ2[REACTION SHEET: Z equals Z star st, Y sub F equals 0, Y sub O equals 0]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3[OXIDIZER RICH REGION: Z less than Z star st]
    nodeZ3 --> nodeZ4[AMBIENT AIR: Z equals 0]
    nodeA1[Inner Zone: Pyrolysis and fuel vapour diffusion outward] -.-> nodeZ1
    nodeA2[Sheet Zone: One step chemistry releases heat H sub c] -.-> nodeZ2
    nodeA3[Outer Zone: O sub 2 diffuses inward, products CO sub 2 and H sub 2 O diffuse outward] -.-> nodeZ3
```

## 4.3 Classification Block Diagram of Diffusion Flames

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeDF[DIFFUSION FLAMES: Fuel and oxidizer initially separate] --> nodeL[LAMINAR REGIME Re less than 2300]
    nodeDF --> nodeT[TURBULENT REGIME Re greater than 2300]
    nodeL --> nodeLJ[Laminar Jet: Bunsen burner]
    nodeL --> nodeLP[Laminar Pool: Candle wax]
    nodeL --> nodeLW[Laminar Wake: Bluff body flame holder]
    nodeT --> nodeTJ[Turbulent Jet: Industrial burner]
    nodeT --> nodeTP[Turbulent Pool: Large oil tank fire]
    nodeT --> nodeTPF[Turbulent Plume: Building fire]
    nodeTJ --> nodeMix[Mixing Controlled: t sub chem much less than t sub mix]
    nodeLP --> nodeMix
    nodeTPF --> nodeMix
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of the Diffusion Flame Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF1[INPUT STAGE: fuel flow rate m dot f, nozzle diameter d, ambient composition] --> nodeF2[GOVERNING STAGE: solve mixture fraction transport equation]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[FLAME STRUCTURE: locate Z equals Z star st isosurface]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4[OUTPUT STAGE: extract flame height, heat release distribution, soot zone]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5[VALIDATION: compare with experimental H sub f and radiation data]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6[ENGINEERING USE: design combustor length, predict fire plume, set safety distances]
    nodeA1[Note: All steps assume Le equals 1] -.-> nodeF2
    nodeA2[Note: Radiative loss and finite rate chemistry are corrections] -.-> nodeF5
```
