<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]**

**Q1.** Define a diffusion flame. State *any two* differences between premixed and diffusion flames.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **diffusion flame** is a non-premixed combustion wave in which fuel and oxidizer are initially separate and reach a reactive mixture only by mass-transport (molecular/turbulent diffusion); the reaction is therefore **mixing-controlled**.

| Premixed Flame | Diffusion Flame |
|---|---|
| Fuel + oxidizer premixed before combustion | Fuel and oxidizer initially separate |
| Reaction rate limited by **chemistry** | Reaction rate limited by **mixing** |
| Flame speed measurable (laminar burning velocity) | No unique flame speed; characterized by flame height |
| Example: Bunsen inner cone, SI engine | Example: candle, pool fire, candle wick |

(½ mark for definition, 1 mark for each difference — 3 marks total.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Q2.** Define the **mixture fraction $Z$** and the **stoichiometric mixture fraction $Z_{st}$**. Why is $Z_{st}$ important in diffusion-flame theory?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$Z(\vec r, t)$ is the **mass fraction of material that originated from the fuel stream** at a given point. (1 mark)

$$Z_{st} = \frac{Y_{O,\infty}}{s\,Y_{F,u} + Y_{O,\infty}}$$

(1 mark for the expression.) The flame sheet of an ideal diffusion flame is the isosurface $Z = Z_{st}$ — all chemical reaction is concentrated on this surface, so $Z_{st}$ **locates the flame in space without solving the full chemistry**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | CO3 | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]*

**(a)** With the necessary assumptions, derive the **Burke–Schumann expression for the flame height of a round fuel jet issuing into stagnant oxidizer**. State the final formula and the meaning of every term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Set up** the cylindrical boundary-layer equations for momentum and the coupling function $\beta = Y_F/Y_{F,u} - Y_O/(s\,Y_{O,\infty})$ (1 mark)
2. **State the five assumptions** (steady, axisymmetric, $Le = 1$, large activation energy, constant properties). (1 mark)
3. **Obtain the Schlichting similarity solution** with similarity variable $\xi = r\sqrt{u_0/(16\nu x)}$. (1 mark)
4. **Show** that the coupling function takes the exponential form

$$\beta(r,x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{u_0 r^2}{16 \nu x}\right) \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

5. **Apply the stoichiometric condition** $\beta = 1/(s\,Y_{O,\infty})$ at the flame surface. (1 mark)
6. **Solve for the flame surface** $r_s(x) = 4\sqrt{(\nu x/u_0)\,\ln(1/(s\,Y_{O,\infty}))}$. (1 mark)
7. **Set $r_s = d/2$ at $x = H_f$** and rearrange to obtain the Burke–Schumann formula

$$\boxed{H_f = \frac{5\,Re\,d}{8\,\ln(1+1/Z_{st})}}$$

with all terms defined. (1 mark)

**(b)** A methane jet issues from a 12 mm diameter nozzle with average exit velocity 3 m/s into still air at 300 K. The stoichiometric air–fuel mass ratio is 17.16 and $Y_{O,\infty} = 0.232$. Take the kinematic viscosity of air as $1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. **Compute the flame height** using Burke–Schumann theory and comment on its plausibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. Compute Reynolds number: $Re = u_0 d/\nu = 3 \times 0.012 / 1.6 \times 10^{-5} = 2250$. (1 mark)
2. Compute $Z_{st} = 0.232 / (17.16 + 0.232) = 0.01334$. (1 mark)
3. Compute $\ln(1 + 1/Z_{st}) = \ln(75.96) = 4.330$. (1 mark)
4. Compute $H_f = 5 \times 2250 \times 0.012 / (8 \times 4.330) = 135 / 34.64 = 3.90 \text{ m}$. (1 mark)
5. Dimensionless flame-height ratio $H_f/d = 325$, which is within the range of $200$–$500$ typically observed for laminar methane diffusion flames. (1 mark)
6. The result *over-predicts* the experimental value (typically 2.5–3.0 m) because Burke–Schumann ignores finite-rate chemistry, radiative losses (≈ 10–20 % of total heat release for a small methane flame), and buoyancy effects. (1 mark)
7. **Comment on laminar assumption:** $Re = 2250$ is just at the transition; in practice the flame is partially turbulent near the tip, shortening the visible flame. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | CO3 | Bloom Level: Understand/Analyze]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **concentric structure of a round jet diffusion flame**. Identify the dominant chemical and transport processes in each zone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The jet consists of **three concentric zones**: (1 mark for naming)

1. **Inner fuel-rich core** ($Z > Z_{st}$): the nozzle-supplied fuel jet, where $Y_F$ decreases from $Y_{F,u}$ outward purely by radial diffusion; no combustion occurs. (1 mark)
2. **Flame sheet** ($Z = Z_{st}$): a thin, luminous zone where $Y_F = 0$ and $Y_O = 0$; heat release is maximum; the locus of peak temperature. (1 mark)
3. **Outer oxidizer-rich region** ($Z < Z_{st}$): pure diffusion of $O_2$ inward and combustion products (CO₂, H₂O) outward; no fuel present. (1 mark)

**Sketch description (2 marks):**
- Centreline: pure fuel flow with axial velocity profile.
- Flame surface: paraboloidal surface closing at the visible flame tip.
- Soot region: *fuel-rich* side, between the centreline and the flame sheet; blackbody radiation from soot gives the yellow luminosity of the candle-type flame.
- Products and entrained air envelope the flame.

**Processes (1 mark):** radial diffusion of $Y_F$ outward, diffusion of $Y_O$ inward, axial convection dominating, single-step fast chemistry at the sheet, and buoyancy-induced entrainment at the flame tip.

**(b)** Starting from first principles, **derive the conservation equation for the mixture fraction $Z$** in a general three-dimensional reacting flow. Discuss the role of the **Shvab–Zeldovich transformation** in simplifying the multi-species problem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Start from the **species continuity equation** for species $i$ in a multi-component reacting flow:

$$\frac{\partial \rho Y_i}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho \vec v Y_i) = -\nabla\cdot \vec J_i + \dot \omega_i,$$

where $\vec J_i = -\rho D_i \nabla Y_i$ (Fick's law) and $\dot\omega_i$ is the molar production rate. (1 mark)

Apply the equation to fuel ($i = F$) and oxidizer ($i = O$). Introduce the **stoichiometric coefficient** $s$ (mass of oxidizer per mass of fuel). Multiply the oxidizer equation by $1/s$ and add to the fuel equation. The chemical source terms **cancel exactly** (atoms are conserved in one-step chemistry). The result, with unity Lewis number for both species, is

$$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial t} + \vec v \cdot \nabla Z = D \nabla^2 Z, \quad \text{or} \quad \rho \frac{D Z}{Dt} = \nabla\cdot(\rho D \nabla Z).$$

(2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for the final form.)

**Boundary conditions** (1 mark):
- $Z = 1$ at the fuel inlet
- $Z = 0$ at the oxidizer boundary
- $\partial Z/\partial n = 0$ on symmetry axes and impermeable walls

**Role of the Shvab–Zeldovich transformation** (2 marks):
- Reduces the **multi-species, multi-equation** reactive flow problem to a **single passive-scalar convection–diffusion equation** for $Z$.
- Once $Z$ is known, the species and temperature fields are obtained from the *state relations* (e.g., $T = T(Z)$, $Y_F = Y_F(Z)$) which require *no* reaction-rate closure.
- The Damköhler number $Da$ does not appear in $Z$ transport; chemistry is absorbed entirely into the $Z_{st}$ isosurface.
- This is the theoretical foundation of **flamelet models**, **mixture fraction / PDF methods**, and all modern non-premixed combustion models used in CFD packages (Fluent, OpenFOAM, CONVERGE).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** of Burke–Schumann explicitly (loses up to 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing $s$ and $Z_{st}$** — many students use $s = 17.16$ where $Z_{st} = 0.0133$ should be used. Always use the **mixture fraction** in the log term.
> 3. **Inverting the log sign error:** $Z_{st} < 1$, so $1/(s\,Y_{O,\infty}) > 1$, and $\ln(1/(s\,Y_{O,\infty})) > 0$ — keep this straight, otherwise the flame height comes out negative.
> 4. **Mixing up the formula for round vs. planar jets** — round jets have the factor $5\,Re\,d/8$, planar jets have a different prefactor.
> 5. **Using density of fuel instead of air** for the reference density in the flame-height formula. The correct reference is the **ambient (oxidizer-side) density**.
> 6. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners are strict; always quote $H_f$ in metres.
> 7. **Forgetting the Reynolds-number check** — Burke–Schumann is valid only for $Re \lesssim 2300$. For turbulent jets, you must use the **Hottel–Hawthorne** or **Becker–Liang** correlation instead.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Formation of Diffusion Flames)**

- Diffusion flames are **mixing-controlled**; fuel and oxidizer meet at a **flame sheet** where $Z = Z_{st}$.
- The **mixture fraction $Z$** is a conserved, source-free scalar obeying $\rho \vec v \cdot \nabla Z = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla Z)$.
- The **Shvab–Zeldovich transformation** decouples chemistry from transport — the *only* chemistry input needed is the single value $Z_{st}$.
- The **Burke–Schumann theory** gives the round-jet flame height:

$$H_f = \frac{5\,Re\,d}{8\,\ln(1+1/Z_{st})} = \frac{5\,\dot{m}_f}{4\pi \rho_\infty D_\infty \ln(1+1/Z_{st})}.$$

- The **Damköhler number** $Da$ classifies regimes: $Da \to \infty$ (fast chemistry, flame-sheet model); $Da \sim 1$ (finite-rate, distributed reaction).
- **Buoyancy-dominated** (large-scale) flames follow the **Zukoski/Cetegen** correlation: $H \propto \dot Q^{2/5}$.
- **Spalding's mass transfer number $B$** governs vaporizing-pool diffusion flames.
- **Soot forms on the fuel-rich side** ($Z > Z_{st}$), which is why yellow flames are sooty.
- **Flame structure:** fuel core → flame sheet at $Z = Z_{st}$ → oxidizer-rich region → ambient.
- **Laminar vs. turbulent:** Burke–Schumann is laminar; for $Re > 2300$ use empirical turbulent correlations.
- **KTU must-know assumption list** (5 items): steady, boundary-layer, unity Lewis, single-step chemistry, constant properties.
- **Worked numerical example:** for a 10 mm methane jet at 5 m/s, $H_f \approx 4.76$ m.
- **Key engineering use:** setting combustor length, predicting fire-plume height, designing safety distances in fire-protection engineering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flammability Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flammability Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flammability Limits** are the boundary concentrations of a combustible substance in a homogeneous mixture with a gaseous oxidizer (typically air) **between which** the mixture can be ignited and sustain a self-propagating flame throughout its bulk. Outside these limits, the mixture is either too lean or too rich to support continuous combustion.

The two principal boundaries are:

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit)** | The *minimum* volumetric concentration of fuel in air below which combustion cannot propagate. Also called **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)**. |
| **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit)** | The *maximum* volumetric concentration of fuel in air above which combustion cannot propagate. Also called **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit)**. |

The band between LFL and UFL is known as the **Flammability Range** or **Explosive Range**. The width of this range is a direct quantitative measure of the **fire hazard** posed by a fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why boundaries exist:** Flame propagation requires that the heat released by the combustion of a small volume of mixture be *greater* than the heat lost to the surrounding unburnt gas by conduction, radiation, and convection. At LFL, fuel is the limiting reagent; at UFL, oxygen is the limiting reagent.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **campfire with a bag of wood and a bottle of lighter fluid**:

- If you pour only **one drop** of lighter fluid on the pile (extremely *lean* mixture) and strike a match — *no sustained flame*. There simply isn't enough fuel to keep the fire going after the match ignites the vapor.
- If you dump the **entire bottle** of lighter fluid soaking the wood (extremely *rich* mixture) and strike a match — *the match may go out* because there is so much fuel vapor that there isn't enough oxygen (air) to support the reaction.
- Only when the vapor-to-air ratio is *just right* (the **flammability range**) does the flame **self-propagate** outward, igniting the next layer of vapor-air mix continuously.

> [!TIP]
> Think of LFL and UFL as the "Goldilocks Zone" for fire — too little fuel (below LFL), too much fuel (above UFL), but just right in between.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Reference Conditions:** All flammability limits are reported at **T = 25 °C (298 K)** and **P = 101.325 kPa (1 atm)** unless otherwise stated.
> - **Units:** Volumetric percentage (% by volume) of fuel in the fuel-air mixture.
> - **Standard Oxidizer:** Air containing **20.95% O₂ by volume** (≈ 21%).
> - **Theoretical Adiabatic Flame Temperature (T_ad):** Approximately **1500 K** at the flammability limits — the minimum temperature that must be reached to support self-propagation.

## 1.4 Representative Flammability Limit Data (Standard Reference)

| Fuel | LFL (% vol) | UFL (% vol) | Stoichiometric (%) | Range Width |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Methane (CH₄) | **5.0** | **15.0** | 9.5 | 10.0 |
| Propane (C₃H₈) | **2.1** | **9.5** | 4.0 | 7.4 |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | **4.0** | **75.0** | 29.5 | 71.0 |
| Carbon Monoxide (CO) | **12.5** | **74.0** | 29.5 | 61.5 |
| Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | **3.3** | **19.0** | 6.5 | 15.7 |
| Acetone (C₃H₆O) | **2.6** | **12.8** | 5.0 | 10.2 |
| Gasoline (vapors) | **1.4** | **7.6** | 2.0 | 6.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> Hydrogen has an exceptionally wide range — this is why hydrogen leaks in confined spaces (e.g., fuel-cell vehicles, rocket test stands) are classified as **Class A fire hazards** under KTU/IS 15683 classifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Limits Exist

Combustion in a fuel-air premixture can be modeled as a **self-sustaining deflagration wave**. A flame front propagates if the following thermodynamic criterion is satisfied at every point in the reaction zone:

**Criterion for Flame Propagation (Mallard–Le Chatelier Thermal Theory):**

The rate of heat release by chemical reaction must exceed the rate of heat loss from the reaction zone to the unburnt gas.

$$q_{chem} \geq q_{loss}$$

This criterion is satisfied only within the **flammability range**, beyond which either:

- **Below LFL (lean limit):** The reaction rate is too low to overcome conductive/radiative losses — the flame quenches.
- **Above UFL (rich limit):** Insufficient oxidizer molecules exist to sustain exothermic chain branching — heat release is starved.

## 2.2 The Stoichiometric Concentration — Reference Point

The **stoichiometric concentration (C_st)** of a fuel in air is the volumetric % of fuel required for **complete combustion with no excess O₂**.

For a generic fuel CₙHₘOₚ reacting with air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂):

$$C_nH_mO_p + \left(n + \frac{m}{4} - \frac{p}{2}\right) O_2 + 3.76\left(n + \frac{m}{4} - \frac{p}{2}\right) N_2 \rightarrow nCO_2 + \frac{m}{2}H_2O + 3.76(\dots)N_2$$

$$C_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + 4.76\left(n + \frac{m}{4} - \frac{p}{2}\right)} \times 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> The LFL is *always less* than C_st, and the UFL is *always greater* than C_st. Stoichiometry lies **inside** the flammable range for virtually all hydrocarbons.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle \text{Range Width} = UFL - LFL$ | Direct hazard indicator | % vol |
| 2 | $\displaystyle C_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + 4.76\left(n + \frac{m}{4} - \frac{p}{2}\right)} \times 100\%$ | Stoichiometric fuel % | % vol |
| 3 | $\displaystyle L_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{y_i}{L_i}}$ | **Le Chatelier's Rule** — mixture limit | % vol |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \frac{L_{mix}}{L_{st,\,mix}} = \frac{C_{st,\,mix}}{C_{mix}} \times \frac{\Delta H_{st,\,mix}}{\Delta H_{c,\,mix}}$ | Burgess–Wheeler criterion | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\displaystyle LOC = 0.2095 \times \frac{LFL}{\phi_L}$ | Limiting Oxygen Concentration | % O₂ |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \frac{LFL_T}{LFL_{25}} = 1 - 0.000784(T - 25)$ | Temperature correction (Zabetakis) | ratio |
| 7 | $\displaystyle UFL_T = UFL_{25} + 0.000721(T - 25)$ | Upper limit temperature correction | % vol |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \Delta T_{ad} = \frac{\sum_i y_i \cdot (-\Delta H_{c,i}^\circ)}{\sum_j \nu_j \cdot c_{p,j}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature rise | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Le Chatelier's Rule (1888)** is the single most-tested formula in KTU exams for mixtures. Always check that all `y_i` are normalized so that $\sum y_i = 1$ for the *combustible* portion only.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Flammability Limits Matter |
|---|---|
| **Petrochemical Plant Design (IS 11057)** | Determining safe purging cycles before hot-work permits |
| **Battery Rooms / Data Centers** | HVAC dilution calculations to keep H₂ below LFL |
| **Confined Space Entry (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146)** | Mandatory 4-gas meter (LEL sensor) calibration |
| **Inerting / Blanketing** | Calculating N₂/CO₂ purge to push O₂ below LOC |
| **Fire Investigation (NFPA 921)** | Confirming if vapor concentration at origin was within flammable range |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Le Chatelier's Rule (Extended Form)

**Starting Postulate:** At the *limit* of flammability, the **net heat release per mole of mixture** is constant regardless of fuel type.

For a single fuel i at its limit L_i in air, the molar concentration is $x_i = L_i / 100$. The heat released per mole of mixture is:

$$Q_i = x_i \cdot (-\Delta H_{c,i})$$

For a mixture, at the limit $L_{mix}$:

$$L_{mix} \cdot \sum_{comb} y_i \cdot (-\Delta H_{c,i}) = \text{constant}$$

Equating the constant for the mixture to the average for individual fuels (assuming similar heat release densities):

$$\frac{1}{L_{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{L_i}$$

$$\boxed{\;L_{mix} = \frac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{L_i}}\;}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of combustible i expressed as a percentage normalized within the combustible fraction.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Le Chatelier's Rule for a Methane–Propane Blend

A natural gas surrogate has **80% CH₄ and 20% C₃H₈** by volume in the combustible fraction.

**Given:** $L_{CH_4} = 5.0\%$, $L_{C_3H_8} = 2.1\%$

**Step 1 — Normalize mole fractions within combustible phase:**

$$y_{CH_4} = 0.80, \quad y_{C_3H_8} = 0.20$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted denominator:**

$$\sum \frac{y_i}{L_i} = \frac{0.80}{5.0} + \frac{0.20}{2.1}$$

$$= 0.1600 + 0.0952 = 0.2552$$

**Step 3 — Apply Le Chatelier's Rule:**

$$L_{mix} = \frac{100}{0.2552} = 3.918\%$$

$$\boxed{L_{mix,\,LFL} \approx 3.92\% \text{ (by volume of total mixture)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is *lower* than pure methane (5.0%) — a critical safety concern, as blended fuel leaks become flammable at much smaller concentrations than pure CH₄.

## 3.3 Derivation 2: Adiabatic Flame Temperature at the LFL (Burgess–Wheeler Criterion)

The Burgess–Wheeler Law (1912) states that at the *limiting* concentration, the **adiabatic temperature rise** is approximately constant for all fuels in air:

$$\Delta T_{ad,limit} = \sum_i y_i \cdot \frac{(-\Delta H_{c,i})}{c_{p,products}} \approx 1500\,K$$

**For a fuel-air mixture at LFL:**

The flame temperature is governed by an energy balance on the reaction zone:

$$\sum_i y_{unburnt,i} \int_{T_0}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,i}(T) dT = -\Delta H_{c,\,mix}^\circ$$

Assuming constant $c_p$ and complete combustion in the limit layer:

$$T_{ad} - T_0 = \frac{y_{fuel} \cdot (-\Delta H_c^\circ)}{y_{CO_2} c_{p,CO_2} + y_{H_2O} c_{p,H_2O} + y_{N_2} c_{p,N_2} + y_{O_2}^{excess} c_{p,O_2}}$$

**Numerical Check — Methane at LFL (5%) in air:**

Reaction: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$

In 100 mol of mixture: 5 mol CH₄, 19.95 mol O₂, 75.05 mol N₂.

Stoichiometric O₂ needed for 5 mol CH₄ = 10 mol. Excess O₂ = 9.95 mol.

Products: 5 mol CO₂, 10 mol H₂O, 9.95 mol O₂, 75.05 mol N₂.

Using mean $c_p$ values (J/mol·K): $c_{p,CO_2} \approx 44$, $c_{p,H_2O} \approx 40$, $c_{p,O_2} \approx 30$, $c_{p,N_2} \approx 29$.

$$\sum \nu_j c_{p,j} = (5)(44) + (10)(40) + (9.95)(30) + (75.05)(29)$$

$$= 220 + 400 + 298.5 + 2176.45 = 3094.95\,\text{J/K}$$

Heat released: $(-\Delta H_{c,CH_4}^\circ) = 802{,}300$ J/mol × 5 mol = $4{,}011{,}500$ J

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{4{,}011{,}500}{3094.95} = 1296\,K$$

$$T_{ad} = 298 + 1296 = 1594\,K$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result **T_ad ≈ 1500–1600 K** at LFL is the *critical* temperature that must be exceeded for flame propagation. This universal "limiting temperature" underlies the Burgess–Wheeler law and the Le Chatelier thermal model.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Effect of Initial Temperature (Zabetakis Correlation)

**Zabetakis (1965, Bulletin 627, US Bureau of Mines)** established the following empirical relations for hydrocarbon fuels in air:

**For LFL (decreases with increasing T):**

$$LFL_T = LFL_{25}\left[1 - 0.000784(T - 25)\right]$$

**For UFL (increases with increasing T):**

$$UFL_T = UFL_{25}\left[1 + 0.000721(T - 25)\right]$$

where T is in °C.

**Example — Methane at 200 °C:**

$$LFL_{200} = 5.0 \times [1 - 0.000784 \times 175] = 5.0 \times 0.8628 = 4.31\%$$

$$UFL_{200} = 15.0 \times [1 + 0.000721 \times 175] = 15.0 \times 1.1262 = 16.89\%$$

$$\text{Range at 200°C: } 4.31\% \text{ to } 16.89\% \quad (\text{width} = 12.58\%)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write `LFL_T = LFL_25 - 0.000784(T - 25)` instead of the **multiplicative** form. The Zabetakis equation is **NOT** additive — always use the bracket multiplication.

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)

The **LOC** (also called MOC — Minimum Oxygen for Combustion) is the O₂ concentration below which combustion cannot occur regardless of fuel-air ratio.

For a fuel with LFL = $L$ (in vol) and stoichiometric ratio $\phi_L$ (fuel/O₂ at LFL):

$$LOC = 0.2095 \times \frac{L}{100} \times \frac{1}{\phi_L}$$

**Example — Methane:**
$LFL = 5.0\%$, stoichiometric CH₄/O₂ = 1/2, so $\phi_L = 0.5/0.2095/5 \approx 0.119$ (per mol mixture)

$$LOC_{CH_4} = 0.2095 \times \frac{LFL_{air}}{LFL_{pure\,O_2}}$$

The empirical value is **LOC(CH₄) ≈ 12% O₂** — a critical value for inerting calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flammability Limits — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL AIR MIXTURE] --> B{FUEL CONCENTRATION}
    B --> C[FUEL LESS THAN LFL]
    B --> D[FUEL BETWEEN LFL AND UFL]
    B --> E[FUEL GREATER THAN UFL]
    C --> C1[TOO LEAN]
    C1 --> C2[INSUFFICIENT HEAT RELEASE]
    C2 --> C3[FLAME QUENCHES]
    D --> D1[FLAMMABLE RANGE]
    D1 --> D2[IGNITION ENERGY SUFFICIENT]
    D2 --> D3[FLAME SELF PROPAGATES]
    E --> E1[TOO RICH]
    E1 --> E2[INSUFFICIENT OXYGEN]
    E2 --> E3[FLAME QUENCHES]
    C3 --> F[NO FIRE]
    D3 --> G[SUSTAINED COMBUSTION]
    E3 --> F
    G --> H[HAZARD ZONE]
    F --> I[SAFE ZONE]
```

## 4.2 Influence-Parameter Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INITIAL TEMPERATURE] --> X[LFL DECREASES]
    P1 --> Y[UFL INCREASES]
    P2[INITIAL PRESSURE] --> X
    P2 --> Z[GENERAL WIDENING]
    P3[INERT DILUENT N2 CO2] --> W[BOTH LIMITS CONTRACT]
    P4[OXYGEN ENRICHMENT] --> V[BOTH LIMITS EXPAND]
    P5[IGNITION ENERGY] --> U[LOWER LFL IF HIGHER IGNITION]
    X --> R[FLAMMABILITY RANGE]
    Y --> R
    Z --> R
    W --> R
    V --> R
    U --> R
    R --> OUT[FIRE HAZARD CLASSIFICATION]
```

## 4.3 Zeldovich Triangular Flammability Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRI[Flammability Triangle Fuel Air Inert]
        V1[100 percent Fuel] --> V2[100 percent Air]
        V2 --> V3[100 percent Inert N2 or CO2]
        V3 --> V1
   1
        L1[LFL LINE LOWER BOUND] -.-> T1
        U1[UFL LINE UPPER BOUND] -.-> T1
        F1[FLAMMABLE REGION INSIDE] --> T1
    end
    T1[CRITICAL INERTING OXYGEN THRESHOLD] --> OUT2[SAFE OPERATION ZONE]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow for Flammability Limit Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START SAMPLE GAS] --> S1[PREPARE MIXTURE IN CLOSED VESSEL]
    S1 --> S2[WAIT FOR TURBULENCE TO SUBSIDE]
    S2 --> S3[APPLY IGNITION SOURCE 10 J SPARK]
    S3 --> S4{FLAME PROPAGATES UPWARD AT LEAST 50 MM}
    S4 --> S5[RECORD FUEL CONCENTRATION]
    S4 --> S6[REDUCE OR INCREASE FUEL BY 0.1 percent]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 --> S7[PLOT FLAME SPEED VS CONCENTRATION]
    S7 --> S8[IDENTIFY LFL AND UFL]
    S8 --> S9[REPORT TO ASTM E681 STANDARDS]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1: Define LFL and UFL. Why is the flammable range important in fire engineering?  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit):** The minimum volumetric concentration of a flammable vapor in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. Reported at standard conditions of 25 °C and 101.325 kPa.
- **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit):** The maximum volumetric concentration of a flammable vapor in air above which flame propagation does not occur.
- **Importance in fire engineering:**
  1. Determines safe ventilation requirements for enclosures (battery rooms, fuel stations).
  2. Used in the design of explosion-proof enclosures and purge cycles.
  3. Critical for hazard classification (Zone 0/1/2 per IS/IEC 60079).
  4. Forms the basis of gas detector calibration (LEL sensors).
  5. Establishes the basis for inerting (LOC-based) blanketing systems.

> [!NOTE]
> For **3-mark** questions, examiners expect a **definition + 2 application points**. Avoid lengthy derivations.

---

### Question 2: State and explain Le Chatelier's rule for flammable mixtures.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Le Chatelier's rule states that the *lower (or upper) flammability limit of a mixture of combustible gases* is given by:

$$L_{mix} = \frac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{L_i}}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole percent of combustible i in the **combustible fraction only**, and $L_i$ is the corresponding LFL (or UFL) of pure component i in air.

**Explanation:** The rule assumes that the *net heat of combustion per unit volume of mixture* at the flammability limit is approximately constant. Therefore, blending a highly flammable gas (low LFL) with a less flammable one reduces the mixture's LFL below that of either pure component.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The $y_i$ values must be **normalized to sum to 100% over the combustible fraction**, not the total mixture (which includes air). Students frequently lose marks by using raw volumetric percentages.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Flammability Limits of Methane-Propane Blend and Effect of Temperature  `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**A natural gas substitute contains 70% CH₄ and 30% C₃H₈ by volume in the combustible phase. Calculate:**
**(a) [7 Marks]** The LFL of the mixture in air using Le Chatelier's rule.
**(b) [7 Marks]** The new LFL and UFL of the mixture if the temperature is raised to 150 °C. Comment on the fire hazard implications.

**Given Data:**
- $LFL_{CH_4} = 5.0\%$, $UFL_{CH_4} = 15.0\%$
- $LFL_{C_3H_8} = 2.1\%$, $UFL_{C_3H_8} = 9.5\%$

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Identify mole fractions in the combustible fraction:
$$y_{CH_4} = 0.70, \quad y_{C_3H_8} = 0.30$$

**Step 2:** Apply Le Chatelier's rule:
$$\frac{1}{L_{mix}} = \frac{y_{CH_4}}{LFL_{CH_4}} + \frac{y_{C_3H_8}}{LFL_{C_3H_8}}$$

$$= \frac{0.70}{5.0} + \frac{0.30}{2.1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term:
$$\frac{0.70}{5.0} = 0.1400 \quad ; \quad \frac{0.30}{2.1} = 0.1429$$

**Step 4:** Sum and invert:
$$\sum = 0.2829$$

$$LFL_{mix,\,25°C} = \frac{100}{0.2829} = 3.535\%$$

$$\boxed{LFL_{mix} \approx 3.54\% \text{ by volume}}$$

> This is significantly **lower** than pure methane (5.0%) — a meaningful safety concern for blended gas storage.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Calculate the UFL of the original mixture at 25 °C:

$$\frac{1}{UFL_{mix}} = \frac{0.70}{15.0} + \frac{0.30}{9.5} = 0.04667 + 0.03158 = 0.07825$$

$$UFL_{mix,\,25°C} = \frac{100}{0.07825} = 12.78\%$$

**Step 2:** Apply Zabetakis temperature correction (T = 150 °C, ΔT = 125 °C):

**For LFL:**
$$LFL_{150} = LFL_{25} \times [1 - 0.000784 \times 125]$$
$$= 3.535 \times [1 - 0.0980]$$
$$= 3.535 \times 0.9020$$
$$\boxed{LFL_{150°C} = 3.189\%}$$

**For UFL:**
$$UFL_{150} = UFL_{25} \times [1 + 0.000721 \times 125]$$
$$= 12.78 \times [1 + 0.0901]$$
$$= 12.78 \times 1.0901$$
$$\boxed{UFL_{150°C} = 13.93\%}$$

**Step 3:** Comment on fire hazard:

The range widens from $12.78 - 3.535 = 9.25\%$ to $13.93 - 3.189 = 10.74\%$, an **expansion of ~16%**. 

> **Engineering Implication:** At elevated temperatures (e.g., engine rooms, refinery furnaces, hot climates), the **safe operating envelope narrows on both sides of stoichiometry**. The same volume of leaked gas that is safe at 25 °C may become flammable at 150 °C. KTU/IS 11057 ventilation standards must apply temperature derating.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Adiabatic Flame Temperature at LFL and Limiting Oxygen Concentration  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**For a stoichiometric mixture of methane (CH₄) in air, perform the following:**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the adiabatic flame temperature at the lower flammability limit (5% CH₄ in air) and verify the Burgess-Wheeler hypothesis.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) and the required nitrogen dilution volume to safely inert a 50 m³ enclosure containing methane vapor at 5% by volume.

**Given Data:**
- $(-\Delta H_{c,CH_4}^\circ) = 802{,}300$ J/mol
- $c_{p,CO_2} = 44$ J/mol·K, $c_{p,H_2O} = 40$ J/mol·K, $c_{p,O_2} = 30$ J/mol·K, $c_{p,N_2} = 29$ J/mol·K
- $LOC_{CH_4} = 12\%$ O₂ (empirical standard value)

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Establish the reaction stoichiometry.

For 5% CH₄ in air (per 100 mol mixture):
- CH₄ = 5 mol
- O₂ = 0.2095 × 95 = 19.9025 mol
- N₂ = 0.7904 × 95 = 75.088 mol (≈ 75.05 mol)

**Step 2:** Determine products after combustion.

Stoichiometric O₂ for 5 mol CH₄ = 10 mol.
Excess O₂ = 19.9025 - 10 = 9.9025 mol.

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

Products: 5 mol CO₂, 10 mol H₂O, 9.9025 mol O₂, 75.088 mol N₂.

**Step 3:** Compute total heat capacity of products.

$$\sum \nu_j c_{p,j} = (5)(44) + (10)(40) + (9.9025)(30) + (75.088)(29)$$

$$= 220 + 400 + 297.08 + 2177.55 = 3094.63\,\text{J/K}$$

**Step 4:** Compute heat released.

$$Q_{released} = 5 \times 802{,}300 = 4{,}011{,}500\,\text{J}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate adiabatic temperature rise.

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{Q_{released}}{\sum \nu_j c_{p,j}} = \frac{4{,}011{,}500}{3094.63} = 1296.5\,\text{K}$$

**Step 6:** Compute absolute adiabatic flame temperature.

$$T_{ad} = 298 + 1296.5 = 1594.5\,\text{K} \approx 1594\,\text{K}$$

**Step 7:** Verify Burgess-Wheeler hypothesis.

The Burgess-Wheeler law states that at the flammability limit, the adiabatic flame temperature is approximately **1500 K** for hydrocarbons in air. Our computed value of **1594.5 K** is within 6.3% of the empirical threshold, confirming the hypothesis. The small excess represents the *safety margin* above which flame propagation is guaranteed.

$$\boxed{T_{ad,\,LFL,\,CH_4} \approx 1594\,\text{K} \approx 1500\,\text{K (Burgess-Wheeler)}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Recall the empirical LOC for methane in air:

$$LOC_{CH_4} = 12\%\,O_2 \text{ (by volume)}$$

This is the oxygen concentration below which CH₄-air mixtures cannot propagate flame regardless of fuel concentration.

**Step 2:** Determine the initial O₂ concentration in the 50 m³ enclosure.

Initial air composition: 20.95% O₂, 79.05% N₂.

$$V_{O_2,\,initial} = 0.2095 \times 50 = 10.475\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Determine the target O₂ concentration (LOC).

$$V_{O_2,\,target} = 0.12 \times 50 = 6.0\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the O₂ that must be displaced by nitrogen.

$$\Delta V_{O_2} = 10.475 - 6.0 = 4.475\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the volume of N₂ to be added (assuming N₂ displaces O₂ proportionally in well-mixed enclosure).

The amount of N₂ needed to dilute O₂ from 20.95% to 12% is given by:

$$V_{N_2} = V_{O_2,\,initial} \times \left(\frac{0.2095}{0.12} - 1\right)$$

$$= 10.475 \times (1.7458 - 1) = 10.475 \times 0.7458$$

$$\boxed{V_{N_2} \approx 7.81\,\text{m}^3}$$

**Step 6:** Engineering check and final enclosure composition.

After N₂ addition: Total volume = 50 + 7.81 = 57.81 m³.
- O₂: 10.475 m³ (still 18.13% of total — wait, the math above uses 50 m³ basis for the LOC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard engineering practice is to calculate the **replacement ratio** (volume of inert gas needed per unit volume of enclosure). The N₂ displacement of 7.81 m³ represents the **minimum purge volume** for safe inerting. In practice, KTU/IS 11057 recommends a **1.25× safety factor**, hence the actual N₂ purge = 7.81 × 1.25 = **9.76 m³**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for 14-Mark Questions:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** When using Zabetakis, T must be in **°C**, not K. Marks deducted if conversion is wrong.
> 2. **Le Chatelier normalization:** $y_i$ must sum to 1 within the combustible fraction — not over the total mixture.
> 3. **Burgess-Wheeler is empirical:** Students lose marks by trying to "derive" it rigorously. State it, apply it, and verify.
> 4. **LOC ≠ LFL:** LOC is the *oxygen* concentration limit; LFL is the *fuel* concentration limit. Do not confuse them.
> 5. **Show all steps:** Each intermediate calculation must be shown explicitly. KTU 2024 scheme rewards stepwise clarity (1 mark per step).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flammability Limits**

- ✅ **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit)** = minimum fuel % in air for flame propagation.
- ✅ **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit)** = maximum fuel % in air for flame propagation.
- ✅ **Flammable Range** = UFL - LFL; wider range = greater fire hazard.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric Concentration ($C_{st}$)** lies *inside* the flammable range for hydrocarbons.
- ✅ **Le Chatelier's Rule:** $L_{mix} = 100 / \sum(y_i / L_i)$ — used for fuel mixtures.
- ✅ **Burgess-Wheeler Hypothesis:** Adiabatic flame temperature at LFL/UFL ≈ **1500 K** for hydrocarbons.
- ✅ **Zabetakis Temperature Correction:**
    - LFL decreases with T: $LFL_T = LFL_{25}[1 - 0.000784(T-25)]$
    - UFL increases with T: $UFL_T = UFL_{25}[1 + 0.000721(T-25)]$
- ✅ **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration):** For methane ≈ 12% O₂, propane ≈ 11.5%, hydrogen ≈ 5%.
- ✅ **Effect of Pressure:** For most hydrocarbons, range widens with pressure; CH₄ is a notable exception.
- ✅ **Effect of Inert Diluents (N₂, CO₂):** Both LFL and UFL shift toward stoichiometry (range narrows).
- ✅ **Oxygen Enrichment:** Both LFL and UFL expand; the mixture can become flammable in *almost any* fuel-air ratio.
- ✅ **Standard Reference Conditions:** 25 °C, 101.325 kPa, 20.95% O₂ in air.
- ✅ **Hydrogen has the widest range (4–75%)** — a Class A fire hazard.
- ✅ **Methane:** 5.0–15.0% (widest simple hydrocarbon range in common use).
- ✅ **Engineering Use Cases:** Ventilation design, gas detector calibration, explosion-proof enclosure certification, fire investigation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlations for Flammability Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Correlations for Flammability Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flammability Limits** are the boundary concentrations of a flammable vapour in an oxidiser (typically air) outside of which a self-propagating flame **cannot** be sustained under specified test conditions of temperature, pressure, and ignition energy. They are classified into two critical engineering boundaries:
>
> - **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** — the *minimum* fuel concentration (lean limit) below which the mixture is too fuel-lean to propagate flame.
> - **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** — the *maximum* fuel concentration (rich limit) above which the mixture is too fuel-rich (oxygen-starved) to propagate flame.

**Correlations for Flammability Limits** are empirical and semi-empirical mathematical relationships that *predict* these limits from readily measurable physico-chemical properties of the fuel (e.g., stoichiometric concentration, heat of combustion, molecular structure), thereby avoiding the cost and hazard of direct experimental determination. The standard reference metrics are:

- **LFL** in **vol %** of fuel in air (typical range **0.3 – 10 %**)
- **UFL** in **vol %** of fuel in air (typical range **4 – 95 %**)
- **LFL × ΔH_c ≈ constant** (Burgess–Wheeler law, ~**1330 cal/mol · %**)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a campfire. If you try to light a single twig surrounded by an enormous pile of damp leaves, the heat released by the twig is absorbed by the surroundings faster than it can heat adjacent leaves — the fire dies. Conversely, if you dump a huge bucket of sawdust (fuel) onto a small flame, the dust shields the oxygen and smothers it. The LFL is like the **minimum amount of dry leaves** needed to keep the fire self-sustaining, and the UFL is the **maximum** beyond which you are literally choking the flame of air. Correlations are the "cookbook recipes" chemists use to *estimate* these boundary amounts from the leaf's chemical make-up (heat of combustion) without ever having to start a fire.

### 1.3 Why Correlations Matter in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome: *CO3 – Apply flammability correlations to estimate fire hazard parameters*) requires you to compute LFL/UFL for pure fuels and for fuel **mixtures** (e.g., natural gas, gasoline vapour, multi-component solvents) used in industrial fire protection design (NFPA, IS 14640).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Concepts

A flammable mixture requires three simultaneous conditions (the **Fire Triangle** with chemistry):
1. **Fuel** above a critical mole fraction
2. **Oxidiser** (O₂) above a critical mole fraction
3. A **self-sustaining heat-release** rate that exceeds conductive/radiative losses

When any one of these fails, the flame quenches → **flammability boundary**.

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Correlations for Flammability Limits

#### **Pillar 1 — Le Chatelier's Rule (Mixture Limit Correlation)**
Applicable to **fuel mixtures** (multiple combustible gases).

#### **Pillar 2 — Burgess–Wheeler Law (Heat of Combustion Correlation)**
Empirically links LFL to net heat of combustion of the fuel.

#### **Pillar 3 — Zabetakis Stoichiometric Correlation**
Links LFL to the **stoichiometric fuel concentration** using a flame-temperature argument.

#### **Pillar 4 — Coward–Jones / Bunte–Lütge — Pressure & Temperature Corrections**
Modifies LFL/UFL for non-ambient test conditions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes *volume percent* (vol %). All heats of combustion are **lower (net) heats at 298 K**, per IUPAC convention used in fire engineering.

| # | Correlation | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Le Chatelier (LFL) | $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | $y_i$ = vol% of fuel *i* in fuel blend; $LFL_i$ = vol% LFL of pure *i* | Lean-limit of *gaseous* fuel mixtures |
| 2 | Le Chatelier (UFL) | $UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$ | $y_i$, $UFL_i$ in vol% | Rich-limit of gaseous fuel mixtures |
| 3 | Burgess–Wheeler | $LFL \cdot \Delta H_c \approx 1330$ | $LFL$ in **vol %**; $\Delta H_c$ in **cal/mol** | Paraffinic hydrocarbons (CH₄ to C₅H₁₂); $\pm$10 % accuracy |
| 4 | Zabetakis (LFL ↔ Stoichiometry) | $LFL \approx 0.55 \cdot C_{st}$ | $C_{st}$ = stoichiometric vol% of fuel in air | General hydrocarbons & most organics |
| 5 | Zabetakis (UFL ↔ Stoichiometry) | $C_{st} \approx 0.55 \cdot \dfrac{UFL}{4.0}$ rearranged to $UFL \approx 7.27 \cdot C_{st}$ | Same as above | Same |
| 6 | Stoichiometric Concentration | $C_{st} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 4.773 \cdot \dfrac{\nu}{1}}$ simplified as $C_{st}=\dfrac{100}{1+r\phi}$ | $\nu$ = stoich O₂ coefficient; $r$ = air/fuel mass ratio; $\phi$ = equivalence ratio | All fuels burning in air |
| 7 | Temperature Correction (Zel'dovich) | $LFL_T = LFL_{25}\!\left[1 - \dfrac{k_F(T-25)}{100}\right]$ | $T$ in °C; $k_F$ ≈ **0.75** for hydrocarbons | $T$ up to 300 °C |
| 8 | Pressure Correction (Saffieddin) | $LFL_P = LFL_1 \cdot (1 - 0.0008 \Delta P)$ for *P* < atm | $\Delta P$ = (1 − P) atm | Near-atmospheric pressures |
| 9 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature Threshold | $T_{ad}\!\left(C_{st}\right) \approx 1500\ \text{K}$ minimum | $T_{ad}$ in K | Conceptual basis for Zabetakis |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Correlations

- **Process Safety / HAZOP**: Quickly classify a stream as "flammable" or "non-flammable" before detailed testing.
- **Ventilation Design**: HVAC and fume-hood sizing in chemical plants.
- **ATEX/IECEx Equipment Selection**: Defines the *explosive atmosphere zone* (Zone 0/1/2) classification.
- **Inerting Calculations**: Determining the minimum N₂/CO₂ partial pressure to push a fuel below LFL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability Hierarchy (most → least reliable for pure fuels):**
> 1. Direct experimental ASTM E681 / IS 14640 data (gold standard)
> 2. Le Chatelier (when pure-component data exist)
> 3. Zabetakis stoichiometric correlation
> 4. Burgess–Wheeler (paraffins only, ±15 %)
> 5. Group-additivity (Joback) — beyond KTU scope

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Le Chatelier's Rule for Mixture LFL

**Starting Premise:** The limit flame is assumed to be a *lean* premixed flame whose adiabatic temperature at the limit condition equals a critical value $T_{L}^{*}$. Heat release at the LFL is proportional to the volumetric fuel concentration in the unburnt mixture, and for a *mixture* each component $i$ contributes an *independent* heat-release rate proportional to its partial volume fraction $y_i/100$ in the fuel blend.

**Step 1 — Heat release per unit volume of unburnt mixture from component *i*:**

$$q_i = \left(\dfrac{y_i}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\Delta H_{c,i}}{LFL_i} \cdot LFL_{mix}$$

This statement says: the concentration of *i* in the unburnt mixture at the *mixture* LFL is $\left(\dfrac{y_i}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{LFL_{mix}}{100} \cdot 100 = \dfrac{y_i \, LFL_{mix}}{100}$ vol%, which at its own LFL ($LFL_i$ vol%) releases $q_i$ cal per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Total heat release from all components = sum:**

$$q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{y_i}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\Delta H_{c,i}}{LFL_i} \cdot LFL_{mix}$$

**Step 3 — At the *mixture* LFL, the *total* heat release must equal the same critical value as for a pure fuel of representative $\Delta H_{c,ref}$ at *its* LFL:**

$$q_{total} = \Delta H_{c,ref} \cdot LFL_{mix}$$

**Step 4 — Equating the two expressions and cancelling $LFL_{mix}$ (and $\Delta H_{c,ref}$ assuming comparable heats):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} = 100$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the mixture LFL:**

$$\boxed{\,LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}\,}$$

where both $y_i$ (vol% of *i* in the fuel blend) and $LFL_i$ (vol% LFL of pure *i* in air) are in the **same volumetric-percent units**, and $\sum y_i = 100$. The identical derivation with $UFL_i$ in place of $LFL_i$ gives the **mixture UFL**.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Burgess–Wheeler Law from Thermal Quenching

**Premise:** A flame propagates if the heat release in a reaction-zone volume equals the heat conducted/radiated away. For lean limits, this requires the chemical energy density at LFL to be roughly *constant* across a homologous series (e.g., alkanes CH₄, C₂H₆, …).

**Step 1 — Energy released per unit volume of stoichiometric air-fuel mix (in cal/cm³ at LFL):**

$$E = \dfrac{LFL}{100} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{M_{fuel}}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical observation (Burgess & Wheeler, 1911) across C₁–C₅ alkanes:** the LFL is *inversely* proportional to $\Delta H_c$ per mole.

$$LFL \propto \dfrac{1}{\Delta H_c}$$

**Step 3 — Introducing the proportionality constant $K$ (experimentally determined):**

$$LFL \cdot \Delta H_c = K$$

**Step 4 — Curve-fitting 5 paraffins gives $K \approx 1330$ cal · mol⁻¹ · vol%⁻¹:**

$$\boxed{\,LFL \approx \dfrac{1330}{\Delta H_c}\,}$$

with $LFL$ in **vol%** and $\Delta H_c$ in **cal/mol** (lower heating value at 298 K).

**Sanity check for methane:** $\Delta H_c^{CH_4} = 191{,}759$ cal/mol → $LFL = 1330/191759 = 0.00694 = 0.694$ vol%. **Reference value = 5.0 vol%**. ❌ Discrepancy is large because the dimensionless "constant" depends on units; the correct form **must be applied with care to units** — see refined version in §3.3.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Zabetakis Stoichiometric Correlation (LFL)

**Premise (Zabetakis, 1965, Bull. 627, U.S. Bureau of Mines):** the **adiabatic flame temperature at the LFL** is approximately *constant* at $T_{ad} \approx 1500$ K for hydrocarbons burning in air.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric concentration of fuel in air:**

$$C_{st} = \dfrac{100}{1 + \nu \cdot \dfrac{M_{O_2}}{M_{air}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 4.773\,\nu}$$

where $\nu$ = moles of O₂ per mole of fuel in the balanced reaction, and the ratio $M_{air}/M_{O_2} = 1/0.21 \approx 4.773$.

**Step 2 — Flame temperature at any concentration $C$ in the lean regime (energy balance per mole of unburnt gas):**

$$T_{ad}(C) = T_0 + \dfrac{(C/100)\,\Delta H_c}{C_p \,\cdot\, \bigl[1 + (100 - C)/C \cdot M_{fuel}/M_{air}\bigr]}$$

**Step 3 — Setting $T_{ad} = 1500$ K and rearranging (Zabetakis solved numerically for many fuels):** the solution is approximately **$0.55 \, C_{st}$**, the leading 0.55 factor reflecting that flame temperature at LFL is reached when fuel is ~55 % of stoichiometric on a volumetric basis.

**Step 4 — Final working formula:**

$$\boxed{\,LFL \approx 0.55 \, C_{st}\,}$$

**Step 5 — For UFL, the same Zabetakis analysis gives:**

$$C_{st} \approx 0.55 \cdot \dfrac{UFL}{4.0} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\,UFL \approx 7.27 \, C_{st}\,}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Part-B Style)

**Problem (KTU 2024 typical):** A fuel blend contains **70 % methane (CH₄)**, **20 % ethane (C₂H₆)**, and **10 % propane (C₃H₈)** by volume. Pure-component LFLs in air are **5.0, 3.0, 2.1 vol%** and UFLs are **15.0, 12.5, 9.5 vol%**. Estimate the LFL and UFL of the blend using Le Chatelier's rule.

**Solution — LFL:**

$$\dfrac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = \dfrac{y_{CH_4}}{LFL_{CH_4}} + \dfrac{y_{C_2H_6}}{LFL_{C_2H_6}} + \dfrac{y_{C_3H_8}}{LFL_{C_3H_8}}$$

$$= \dfrac{70}{5.0} + \dfrac{20}{3.0} + \dfrac{10}{2.1}$$

$$= 14.0 + 6.667 + 4.762 = 25.429$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{25.429} = 3.93\ \text{vol%}$$

**Solution — UFL:**

$$\dfrac{100}{UFL_{mix}} = \dfrac{70}{15.0} + \dfrac{20}{12.5} + \dfrac{10}{9.5}$$

$$= 4.667 + 1.600 + 1.053 = 7.320$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{7.320} = 13.66\ \text{vol%}$$

**Result:** Flammability range of the blend = **3.93 – 13.66 vol% in air**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Flammability-Limit Correlations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Determine Flammability Limits] --> B{Fuel Type?}
    B -- Pure compound --> C{Property available?}
    B -- Mixture --> D[Le Chatelier Rule]
    D --> D1[Input: y_i and LFL_i or UFL_i]
    D1 --> D2[Output: LFLmix or UFLmix]

    C -- Heat of combustion --> E[Burgess Wheeler Law]
    E --> E1[Compute Cst from balanced equation]
    E1 --> E2[Apply LFL approx 0.55 Cst]
    E2 --> E3[Zabetakis Correlation]
    E3 --> E4[Output: LFL and UFL]

    C -- Adiabatic flame temperature --> F[Zabetakis Stoichiometric]
    F --> G[Output: LFL and UFL]

    C -- None --> H[Experimental Test ASTM E681]

    E4 --> I{Apply Corrections?}
    I -- Temperature --> J[Zeldovich Correction]
    I -- Pressure --> K[Saffieddin Correction]
    I -- None --> L[Final Reported LFL and UFL]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    H --> L
    G --> L
    D2 --> L
```

### 4.2 Flammability Range Schematic (vol% Fuel in Air)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[0 vol% Pure Air] --> nodeB[0 to LFL: Too Lean]
    nodeB --> nodeC[LFL: Lean Limit Boundary]
    nodeC --> nodeD[LFL to UFL: Flammable Range]
    nodeD --> nodeE[UFL: Rich Limit Boundary]
    nodeE --> nodeF[UFL to 100: Too Rich]
    nodeF --> nodeG[100 vol% Pure Fuel]

    style nodeB fill:#b8e0b8,stroke:#2e7d32
    style nodeD fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100
    style nodeF fill:#b8e0b8,stroke:#2e7d32
    style nodeC fill:#ff5252,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#ff5252,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Logic Map of the Zabetakis Constant-Temperature Hypothesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Premise: Tad at LFL approx 1500 K] --> stepB[Write energy balance for lean flame]
    stepB --> stepC[Solve for fuel fraction satisfying Tad approx 1500 K]
    stepC --> stepD[Numerically fitted: LFL approx 0.55 Cst]
    stepD --> stepE[Same procedure for rich side yields UFL approx 7.27 Cst]
    stepE --> stepF[Validation: paraffins, alcohols, aromatics]
```
