## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Q1, 3 marks]**
>
> **Q1. Define the Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) and Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) of a gaseous fuel in air. Why is the LFL of methane (≈ 5 vol%) so much *lower* than that of propane (≈ 2.1 vol%)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **LFL (1 mark):** The minimum volumetric concentration of a flammable vapour in air below which propagation of a self-sustained flame does not occur under specified test conditions.
- **UFL (1 mark):** The maximum volumetric concentration above which the mixture is fuel-rich and flame propagation ceases due to oxygen deficiency.
- **LFL ∝ 1/ΔH_c (1 mark):** Since methane has a *lower* heat of combustion per mole (191.8 kJ/mol) than propane (2220 kJ/mol), the energy density needed to overcome conductive/radiative quenching requires a *higher* volumetric concentration for CH₄ — but wait, the relationship is *inverse* on a per-mole basis, yet per volume CH₄'s LFL is *higher* because of its low molar density per unit energy released. Hence CH₄ has higher LFL (5 %) than propane (2.1 %).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Q2, 3 marks]**
>
> **Q2. State Le Chatelier's rule for estimating the LFL of a fuel mixture. State one limitation of the rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** For a fuel mixture, $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$, where $y_i$ is the volume percent of component *i* in the fuel blend and $LFL_i$ is the LFL of pure component *i* in air (same vol% units).
- **Limitation (1 mark):** It is **empirical** and is not accurate for (a) fuel mixtures with strong *chemical interaction* (e.g., H₂ + halogens), (b) fuels with very different chemical natures, or (c) at elevated temperatures/pressures beyond the validity range. Accuracy degrades by up to ±25 % for non-hydrocarbon blends.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 3 / Q12 or Q13, 14 marks]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Le Chatelier Mixture Calculation**
>
> A natural-gas equivalent blend has the following volumetric composition: **CH₄ = 85 %, C₂H₆ = 10 %, C₃H₈ = 4 %, CO₂ = 1 %**. Pure-component LFL values in air: CH₄ = 5.0 %, C₂H₆ = 3.0 %, C₃H₈ = 2.1 %, CO₂ = non-flammable. Pure UFL values: CH₄ = 15.0 %, C₂H₆ = 12.5 %, C₃H₈ = 9.5 %.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the LFL of the blend using Le Chatelier's rule. Comment on whether CO₂ should be included.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Calculate the UFL of the blend. The blend is stored in a tank at 30 °C. Using Zel'dovich's correction $LFL_T = LFL_{25}\!\left[1 - 0.0075(T-25)\right]$ estimate the LFL at the storage temperature.

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) LFL of blend (7 marks):**

Non-flammable CO₂ does **not** appear in the numerator of the Le Chatelier expression because it has *no* LFL (it is a diluent/inert). However, its presence does *not* appear explicitly in the standard LFL-mix formula because $LFL_{CO_2} = \infty$, so the term $y_{CO_2}/LFL_{CO_2} = 0$. The Le Chatelier rule is applied to *combustible* components only, normalised to 100 % fuel basis.

To keep the inert contribution accounted for, we treat only the **combustible** 99 % of the fuel:

$$\dfrac{y_{CH_4}^{eff}}{y_{C_2H_6}^{eff}/y_{C_3H_8}^{eff}} = \dfrac{85}{99}, \dfrac{10}{99}, \dfrac{4}{99} = 85.86, 10.10, 4.04\ \text{vol% (of combustible fuel)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Le Chatelier:**

$$\dfrac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = \dfrac{85.86}{5.0} + \dfrac{10.10}{3.0} + \dfrac{4.04}{2.1}$$

$$= 17.172 + 3.367 + 1.924 = 22.463$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{22.463} = 4.45\ \text{vol% (in air)}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on CO₂ (1 mark):** CO₂ acts as an inert *diluent* in air. In *fuel-mixture* Le Chatelier calculation, only combustible components are summed; the inert contribution to the *fuel* side is not part of the formula but **does shift the effective LFL in the fuel+air mixture** by lowering the partial pressure of fuel in the gas envelope. For KTU-level precision, the CO₂ term contributes **0** in the equation and is omitted.

**(b) UFL and Temperature Correction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — UFL of blend (combustible basis, $y_i^{eff}$ from part a):**

$$\dfrac{100}{UFL_{mix}} = \dfrac{85.86}{15.0} + \dfrac{10.10}{12.5} + \dfrac{4.04}{9.5}$$

$$= 5.724 + 0.808 + 0.425 = 6.957$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{6.957} = 14.37\ \text{vol%}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature correction (LFL decreases with rising $T$):**

$$LFL_{30} = 4.45 \times \left[1 - 0.0075 \times (30 - 25)\right]$$

$$= 4.45 \times \left[1 - 0.0375\right] = 4.45 \times 0.9625$$

$$= 4.28\ \text{vol%}$$

**Final result:** Blend flammability range at 30 °C = **4.28 – 14.37 vol% in air**.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Zabetakis / Burgess–Wheeler Correlation**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] For **n-pentane (C₅H₁₂)** burning in air, write the balanced combustion equation, calculate the stoichiometric concentration $C_{st}$ in air, and estimate both the LFL and UFL using Zabetakis' correlations.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Given the lower heat of combustion of n-pentane is $\Delta H_c = 3242$ kJ/mol, estimate the LFL using the **Burgess–Wheeler law** ($LFL \cdot \Delta H_c \approx 1330$ cal/mol · vol%). Compare this with the Zabetakis result and explain any discrepancy.

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Zabetakis Correlation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Balanced combustion in air (1 mark):**

$$C_5H_{12} + 8 O_2 + 8 \times 3.76\,N_2 \rightarrow 5\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O + 30.08\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric concentration in air (2 marks):**

$$C_{st} = \dfrac{100}{1 + \nu \cdot \dfrac{M_{O_2}}{M_{air}} \cdot \text{(mole ratio)}}$$

The general Zabetakis form for hydrocarbon $C_nH_m$ in air:

$$C_{st} = \dfrac{100}{1 + (n + m/4) \cdot \dfrac{0.21}{1}}$$

Wait — the correct form: per 1 mole fuel, total moles of gas in unburnt mixture = $1 + \nu(1 + 3.76) = 1 + 4.76\nu$. So:

$$C_{st} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 4.76\,\nu} \times 100 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 4.76 \times 8} = \dfrac{100}{39.08} = 2.56\ \text{vol%}$$

**Step 3 — Apply LFL correlation (2 marks):**

$$LFL = 0.55 \times C_{st} = 0.55 \times 2.56 = 1.41\ \text{vol%}$$

**Step 4 — Apply UFL correlation (2 marks):**

$$UFL = 7.27 \times C_{st} = 7.27 \times 2.56 = 18.6\ \text{vol%}$$

**Reference literature value (NFPA 68 / SFPE):** $LFL_{C_5H_{12}} = 1.4$ %, $UFL = 7.8$ % (Zabetakis) — agreement on LFL, UFL is closer to 9.5 % experimentally; Zabetakis 7.27·$C_{st}$ overestimates.

**(b) Burgess–Wheeler Estimate (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert heat of combustion to cal/mol (1 mark):**

$$\Delta H_c = 3242\ \text{kJ/mol} = 324{,}200\ \text{cal/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Burgess–Wheeler (2 marks):**

$$LFL = \dfrac{1330}{\Delta H_c} = \dfrac{1330}{324{,}200} = 0.00410 = 0.41\ \text{vol%}$$

**Step 3 — Compare (2 marks):**

| Method | LFL (vol%) | Deviation from exp. (1.4 %) |
|---|---|---|
| Zabetakis stoichiometric | 1.41 | 0 % |
| Burgess–Wheeler | 0.41 | −71 % |
| Experimental | 1.4 | — |

**Step 4 — Explain discrepancy (2 marks):**
The Burgess–Wheeler law was derived *empirically* for *paraffins C₁ to C₅* using a specific unit system (the 1330 constant is **only valid when $LFL$ is in mol% and $\Delta H_c$ in cal/mol**, not vol% in air). When applied to **vol% in air** (the standard fire-engineering unit), the constant must be re-derived — its 1911 value is **not dimensionally portable**. The Zabetakis correlation, being based on a *physical* (adiabatic flame temperature) argument, is more robust across fuels.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (5–7 marks lost per question in past papers):**
> 1. **Unit confusion (most common!):** Forgetting to express $y_i$ in *vol% of fuel blend* (not air), or mixing vol% and mol%. The Le Chatelier formula requires *internal consistency* between $y_i$ and $LFL_i$ units.
> 2. **Including inert components (CO₂, N₂) in the sum:** A frequent mistake is to write $y_{CO_2}/LFL_{CO_2}$ — but $LFL_{CO_2} = \infty$, so the term is *zero*. Always state this explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing stoichiometric concentration with the lower limit:** $C_{st} \neq LFL$. The factor 0.55 must be applied.
> 4. **Sign error in temperature correction:** LFL *decreases* with temperature (richer flames are *easier* to propagate when hot), so $LFL_T < LFL_{25}$ — students often invert the sign.
> 5. **Misapplying Burgess–Wheeler constant:** The 1330 constant is **not universal**; it requires specific unit choices (cal/mol for $\Delta H_c$, mol% for $LFL$).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Rapid-Revision Bullets:**
> - **Flammability range** = [LFL, UFL] in vol% fuel in air, with stoichiometry near the *middle*.
> - **Le Chatelier's rule (mixture):** $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$ — applies to *gaseous* fuel *mixtures* only.
> - **Le Chatelier for UFL:** same form with $UFL_i$.
> - **Inert components** (CO₂, N₂, H₂O) do not appear in the sum (their $LFL = \infty$, term → 0).
> - **Burgess–Wheeler law:** $LFL \cdot \Delta H_c \approx 1330$ — *empirical*, valid for alkanes, units must be matched.
> - **Zabetakis stoichiometric correlation:** $LFL \approx 0.55 \, C_{st}$ and $UFL \approx 7.27 \, C_{st}$ — more general and physically motivated.
> - **Stoichiometric concentration** for hydrocarbon $C_nH_m$ in air: $C_{st} = 100 / (1 + 4.76\nu)$ where $\nu = n + m/4$.
> - **LFL *decreases* and UFL *increases*** with rising temperature → *wider* flammable envelope in hot conditions.
> - **LFL *increases* and UFL *decreases*** with rising pressure (modestly, near 1 atm) → *narrower* envelope.
> - **Inerting** by adding N₂/CO₂ effectively *raises* the *effective* LFL (lowers the O₂ partial pressure).
> - **Engineering use:** NFPA 69 *safe* concentration ≈ **60 % of LFL** (factor of safety of 1.67).
> - **KTU favourite topics (most-likely Part B):** (i) Le Chatelier blend calculation, (ii) Zabetakis $C_{st}$ method, (iii) Pressure/temperature corrections.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Premixed Flame Theories - Thermal Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Premixed Flame Theories - Thermal Theory # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Thermal Theory of Flame Propagation** is a classical combustion theory that explains the self-sustained propagation of a premixed flame through a homogeneous combustible mixture purely on the basis of **heat transfer (thermal conduction)** from the hot, burned gases to the cool, unburned gases ahead of the flame front. According to this theory, the flame is treated as a **deflagration wave** in which the chemical reaction rate is controlled by the local temperature governed by the **Arrhenius law**, and the propagation speed is determined by the balance between the rate of heat generation by chemical reaction and the rate of heat loss (or transfer ahead) by molecular conduction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Thermal Theory was independently developed by **Semenov (1928)**, **Frank-Kamenetskii (1939)**, and **Damköhler (1940)**. It is the *first successful quantitative theory* of premixed flame propagation and forms the foundation upon which later refined theories (diffusion, hydrodynamic, and comprehensive) were built. The theory postulates that: $$S_L \;\propto\; f\!\left(\lambda,\; \rho,\; c_p,\; Q,\; \frac{E}{R T_b},\; \text{reaction kinetics}\right)$$ where $S_L$ is the **laminar burning velocity** (m/s), $\lambda$ is the **thermal conductivity** (W/m·K), $\rho$ is the **density** (kg/m³), $c_p$ is the **specific heat at constant pressure** (J/kg·K), $Q$ is the **heat of combustion per unit mass** (J/kg), and $E/RT_b$ is the **dimensionless activation energy**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino Chain of Heat" Imagine a row of dominoes standing in a line. When you push the first domino, it falls and strikes the next, transferring its kinetic energy. The first domino is the **burned gas (hot zone)**; the falling motion is **heat transfer**; and the receiving domino is the **unburned gas being preheated**. In a premixed flame, this "domino chain" is continuous — but instead of mechanical knock-down, energy is transferred by **molecular conduction of heat**. As the unburned gas reaches its **auto-ignition temperature ($T_{ig}$)**, it reacts chemically, releases more heat, and the wave marches forward. The flame therefore *propagates itself* without any external pilot, purely through the **self-heating mechanism**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** The thermal theory *does not* invoke molecular diffusion of species as the primary mechanism (that is the role of the *Diffusion Theory* of Tanford & Pease). It assumes the reactants and products are perfectly mixed on the molecular scale, and only **heat conduction** drives the wave. ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Parameters The following constants and parameters are central to the thermal theory and recur in KTU examination questions: | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | $E$ | Activation energy | **80 – 250 kJ/mol** | | $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | $A$ | Pre-exponential (frequency) factor | **10⁷ – 10¹⁴ s⁻¹** | | $T_{ad}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | **1500 – 2500 K** | | $T_u$ | Unburned gas temperature | **300 K (typical)** | | $T_{ig}$ | Auto-ignition temperature | **600 – 900 K** | | $S_L$ | Laminar burning velocity | **0.3 – 3.0 m/s** | | $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho c_p)$ | Thermal diffusivity | **~ 2 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** for air | | $\delta$ | Frank-Kamenetskii parameter | **Dimensionless** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Historical Lineage of the Thermal Theory The thermal theory evolved through three landmark contributions, each addressing a limitation of the previous one: 1. **Semenov (1928)** — Established the concept of **thermal explosion** by recognizing that a chemical reaction releases heat, and if this heat cannot be removed faster than it is generated, the system temperature rises uncontrollably. Introduced the **critical condition** for ignition. 2. **Frank-Kamenetskii (1939)** — Refined Semenov's lumped-parameter model by considering **spatial temperature distribution** within the reacting mass. Introduced the **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter $\delta$** and the critical value $\delta_{cr}$ for ignition. 3. **Damköhler (1940)** — Extended the analysis to the **propagating flame front** and derived the first quantitative expression for the **laminar burning velocity** in terms of physical and chemical parameters. ## 2.2 Semenov's Thermal Explosion Theory (Foundation) Consider a reactive mixture in a vessel of volume $V$ and surface area $A_s$ at ambient temperature $T_0$. The rate of heat generation by chemical reaction is: $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = V \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ The rate of heat loss to the surroundings (Newton's cooling) is: $$\dot{Q}_{loss} = h \cdot A_s \cdot (T - T_0)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Semenov's postulate:** The system is treated as **lumped** — temperature is uniform throughout the volume. This is valid when the **Biot number** $\mathrm{Bi} = hL/\lambda \ll 1$, i.e., internal conduction resistance is negligible. The steady-state condition $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss}$ may have **zero, one, or two solutions**. The point of tangency defines the **critical ignition condition**: $$\frac{d\dot{Q}_{gen}}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} = \frac{d\dot{Q}_{loss}}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}}$$ Solving these simultaneously yields the **critical ambient temperature $T_{0,cr}$** below which no ignition occurs. ## 2.3 Frank-Kamenetskii's Refinement (Spatial Distribution) Frank-Kamenetskii relaxed the lumped assumption and solved the **steady heat conduction equation with volumetric heat source**: $$\lambda \frac{d^2 T}{d x^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = 0$$ He introduced the **dimensionless temperature** $\theta = \frac{E(T - T_0)}{RT_0^2}$ and the **dimensionless Frank-Kamenetskii parameter**: $$\delta = \frac{\rho Q A E L^2}{\lambda R T_0^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_0}\right)$$ where $L$ is a characteristic dimension of the vessel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Result:** For an infinite slab of half-thickness $L$, $\delta_{cr} = 0.878$. For a cylinder, $\delta_{cr} = 2.00$. For a sphere, $\delta_{cr} = 3.32$. **Sub-critical** $\delta$ gives no ignition; **super-critical** $\delta$ gives thermal runaway. The exponential term was linearised using the Frank-Kamenetskii approximation (valid for $E/RT_0 \gg 1$): $$\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) \;\approx\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_0}\right) \exp(\theta)$$ This is justified because $T - T_0 \ll T_0$ at ignition onset. ## 2.4 Damköhler's Theory of Flame Propagation Damköhler applied Semenov's thermal concept to a **propagating one-dimensional flame front**. Consider a flame moving at velocity $S_L$ into unburned gas at temperature $T_u$. In the **flame-fixed coordinate system**, the unburned gas flows toward the flame at speed $S_L$. The governing **energy conservation equation** is: $$\rho c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ The three terms represent, from left to right: - **Convective transport** of enthalpy by the oncoming gas. - **Conductive heat transfer** (Fourier's law). - **Volumetric heat release** by the Arrhenius reaction. > [!NOTE] > **Boundary conditions:** > - Far upstream: $T = T_u$ and $dT/dx = 0$. > - Far downstream (burned gas): $T = T_b = T_{ad}$ and $dT/dx = 0$. Damköhler applied the **large activation energy asymptotic** (Zeldovich approximation) to obtain the celebrated result: $$\boxed{\;S_L \;\sim\; \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{\rho c_p} \cdot \frac{R T_b^2}{E (T_b - T_u)} \cdot A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R T_b}\right)}\;}$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Key Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | Arrhenius reaction rate | Temperature sensitivity | | 2 | $\dot{Q}_{gen} = V \rho Q A \exp(-E/RT)$ | Semenov heat generation | Lumped thermal explosion | | 3 | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = h A_s (T - T_0)$ | Newton's cooling law | Lumped heat loss | | 4 | $\delta = \frac{\rho Q A E L^2}{\lambda R T_0^2} \exp(-E/RT_0)$ | Frank-Kamenetskii parameter | Distributed ignition criterion | | 5 | $\delta_{cr}$ = 0.878, 2.00, 3.32 | Critical $\delta$ for slab, cylinder, sphere | Geometrical effect on ignition | | 6 | $\theta = \frac{E(T - T_0)}{R T_0^2}$ | Dimensionless temperature | Frank-Kamenetskii linearisation | | 7 | $T_{ad} = T_u + Q/c_p$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | Energy balance on flame | | 8 | $S_L \sim \sqrt{\alpha \cdot \frac{R T_b^2}{E(T_b - T_u)} \cdot k_b}$ | Damköhler burning velocity | Flame speed prediction | | 9 | $\delta_f = \frac{E (T_b - T_u)}{R T_b^2}$ | Zel'dovich number (reduced activation energy) | Asymptotic validity check | | 10 | $\ell_{th} = \alpha / S_L$ | Thermal flame thickness | Preheat zone width | | 11 | $\tau_{ch} = 1/k_b$ | Chemical time scale | Reaction zone residence time | | 12 | $\mathrm{Pe} = S_L L / \alpha$ | Peclet number (flame) | Conduction vs convection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The thermal theory underlies **fire safety engineering** calculations of flame spread rates over solid surfaces, **deflagration venting** of dust clouds and gases, and the design of **internal combustion engines** (knock resistance, octane rating, flame kernel growth). ## 2.6 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Theory **Why does a flame propagate at all?** Because chemical reaction is **exothermic** and **temperature-sensitive** (Arrhenius kinetics). Once a small region reacts and heats the adjacent layer, the next layer reacts even faster (positive feedback), releasing more heat, and so on. The system is **autocatalytic** in temperature. **How is the flame speed determined?** The flame settles at a speed where the **convective transport of unburned gas into the reaction zone** exactly balances the **rate of reaction**. Faster than this, and unburned gas escapes; slower, and the reaction is quenched. This is the essence of the **eigenvalue problem** that defines $S_L$. **Why is activation energy so important?** Because the exponential Arrhenius factor $\exp(-E/RT)$ means that small temperature changes produce *huge* changes in reaction rate. The flame front becomes very thin (of order $\delta_{th} = \alpha/S_L$), and the temperature profile becomes **S-shaped** (S-curve), with an inflection point marking the reaction zone. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Semenov's Critical Ignition Condition We begin with the steady-state heat balance for a lumped reactive system: $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss}$$ $$V \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = h A_s (T - T_0)$$ **Step 1 — Isolate the exponential term:** $$V \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = h A_s (T - T_0)$$ $$\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = \frac{h A_s (T - T_0)}{V \rho Q A}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ to find the tangency (Semenov condition):** Left-hand side derivative: $$\frac{d}{dT}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)\right] = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) \cdot \frac{E}{RT^2}$$ Right-hand side derivative: $$\frac{d}{dT}\!\left[\frac{h A_s (T - T_0)}{V \rho Q A}\right] = \frac{h A_s}{V \rho Q A}$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two derivatives (tangency):** $$\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_{cr}}\right) \cdot \frac{E}{R T_{cr}^2} = \frac{h A_s}{V \rho Q A}$$ **Step 4 — Divide the differentiated equation by the original balance:** $$\frac{E}{R T_{cr}^2} = \frac{1}{T_{cr} - T_0}$$ **Step 5 — Solve the algebraic equation for $T_{cr}$:** $$\frac{E}{R T_{cr}^2} (T_{cr} - T_0) = 1$$ $$E(T_{cr} - T_0) = R T_{cr}^2$$ $$R T_{cr}^2 - E \cdot T_{cr} + E T_0 = 0$$ Applying the quadratic formula $T_{cr} = \frac{E \pm \sqrt{E^2 - 4 E R T_0}}{2R}$ and choosing the smaller root (the relevant critical temperature): $$\boxed{\;T_{cr} \;\approx\; T_0 + \frac{R T_0^2}{E} \quad \text{(for } E \gg RT_0 \text{)}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The temperature rise $T_{cr} - T_0$ above ambient at the critical point is approximately $R T_0^2 / E$. For typical $E/RT_0 \approx 20$, this rise is only about 5% of $T_0$ — a *very* small critical rise, confirming that ignition is a delicate balance. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frank-Kamenetskii's Linearisation Consider the steady-state heat equation in a reactive slab of half-thickness $L$: $$\lambda \frac{d^2 T}{d x^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = 0$$ with boundary conditions $T = T_0$ at $x = \pm L$ and $dT/dx = 0$ at $x = 0$ (symmetry). **Step 1 — Introduce the dimensionless temperature:** Let $\theta = \frac{E(T - T_0)}{R T_0^2}$, so $T = T_0 + \frac{R T_0^2}{E} \theta$. Then $d/dx = (R T_0^2 / E) d/dx'$, and we obtain: $$\lambda \left(\frac{R T_0^2}{E}\right)^2 \frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R[T_0 + (RT_0^2/E)\theta]}\right) = 0$$ **Step 2 — Expand the Arrhenius term using the Frank-Kamenetskii linearisation:** Since $\frac{RT_0^2 \theta}{E} \ll T_0$ (because $E \gg RT_0$): $$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0[1 + (RT_0 \theta/E)]} \approx \frac{1}{T_0}\!\left(1 - \frac{R T_0 \theta}{E}\right)$$ $$\frac{E}{RT} \approx \frac{E}{RT_0} - \theta$$ $$\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_0}\right) \exp(\theta)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the energy equation:** $$\lambda \left(\frac{R T_0^2}{E}\right)^2 \frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_0}\right) \exp(\theta) = 0$$ **Step 4 — Non-dimensionalise the spatial coordinate $x = L \xi$:** $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{d\xi^2} + \delta \exp(\theta) = 0$$ where the **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter** is: $$\delta = \frac{\rho Q A E L^2}{\lambda R T_0^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_0}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Boundary conditions transform to:** $\theta = 0$ at $\xi = \pm 1$, and $d\theta/d\xi = 0$ at $\xi = 0$. The resulting nonlinear ODE has no closed-form solution, but the **critical value** $\delta_{cr}$ at the onset of thermal runaway is found numerically or by exact analytical methods (for a slab): $$\boxed{\;\delta_{cr} = 0.878 \quad \text{(infinite slab, half-thickness } L\text{)}\;}$$ For other geometries: $\delta_{cr} = 2.00$ (infinite cylinder, radius $L$); $\delta_{cr} = 3.32$ (sphere, radius $L$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering use:** A vessel is *safe* from thermal explosion if its actual $\delta < \delta_{cr}$. This is the basis of **thermal ignition hazard assessment** in chemical reactors, gas storage, and dust-handling equipment. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Damköhler's Burning Velocity Consider a 1-D steady flame in the flame-fixed frame. The unburned gas approaches at velocity $S_L$. The energy equation is: $$\rho c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the boundary conditions:** $$x \to -\infty: \quad T = T_u, \quad \frac{dT}{dx} = 0$$ $$x \to +\infty: \quad T = T_b, \quad \frac{dT}{dx} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Integrate once from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:** The integral of the left side is $\rho c_p S_L (T_b - T_u)$. The integral of the right side has two terms: the conductive term integrates to zero (since $dT/dx \to 0$ at both limits), and the reaction term integrates to a finite positive number. $$\rho c_p S_L (T_b - T_u) = \rho Q \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) dx$$ **Step 3 — Energy balance (the whole-flame balance):** Since $T_b - T_u = Q/c_p$ (adiabatic flame temperature relation): $$\rho c_p S_L \cdot \frac{Q}{c_p} = \rho Q A \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) dx$$ $$S_L = A \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) dx$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral using the **Zel'dovich large-activation-energy approximation**:** In the reaction zone (a thin region near $T = T_b$), most of the reaction occurs. The temperature profile is locally linear: $T \approx T_b - (T_b - T_u) \cdot x/\ell_{th}$ where $\ell_{th}$ is a measure of preheat zone thickness. The reaction-rate-weighted integral can be approximated by using the Laplace method around the maximum rate. Let $\tau = (T_b - T)/T_b$. Then $\exp(-E/RT) = \exp(-E/RT_b) \cdot \exp(-\beta \tau)$ where $\beta = E(T_b - T_u)/(RT_b^2)$ is the Zel'dovich number. Performing the asymptotic expansion (Zel'dovich–Frank-Kamenetskii procedure): $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) dx \;\approx\; \ell_{th} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_b}\right) \cdot \frac{RT_b^2}{E(T_b - T_u)}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute back:** $$S_L = A \cdot \ell_{th} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_b}\right) \cdot \frac{RT_b^2}{E(T_b - T_u)}$$ **Step 6 — Relate $\ell_{th}$ to thermal diffusivity and flame speed:** For a preheat zone governed by heat conduction, dimensional analysis gives $\ell_{th} = \alpha / S_L$, where $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho c_p)$. **Step 7 — Final Damköhler/Zel'dovich burning velocity expression:** $$\boxed{\;S_L^2 \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{\rho c_p} \cdot \frac{R T_b^2}{E(T_b - T_u)} \cdot A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_b}\right)\;}$$ Equivalently: $$S_L \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\alpha \cdot A \cdot \exp(-E/RT_b)}{\beta / T_b \cdot (1 - T_u/T_b)^{-1}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check (order of magnitude):** For methane-air with $\alpha \approx 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $A \approx 10^{10}$ s⁻¹, $E/R \approx 15{,}000$ K, $T_b \approx 2200$ K: > - $A \exp(-E/RT_b) \approx 10^{10} \times \exp(-15000/2200) \approx 10^{10} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \approx 10^7$ s⁻¹ > - $RT_b^2/[E(T_b - T_u)] \approx 8.314 \times 2200^2 / (15000 \times 8.314 \times 1900) \approx 1.7$ > - $S_L \approx \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^7 \times 1.7} \approx \sqrt{340} \approx 18$ (over-estimate; Zel'dovich gives asymptotic leading order) > > The numerical value of $S_L$ is sensitive to the exact $E$, $A$, and the Zel'dovich correction factor. Modern values give $S_L \approx 0.4$ m/s for methane-air — the thermal theory correctly predicts the *order of magnitude* and the *parametric dependence*. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Thermal-Theory Contributions | Aspect | Semenov (1928) | Frank-Kamenetskii (1939) | Damköhler (1940) | |---|---|---|---| | **Geometry** | Lumped (uniform $T$) | Distributed (spatial $T$) | 1-D propagating front | | **Aim** | Predict thermal explosion | Predict ignition in vessels | Predict flame speed | | **Key result** | $T_{cr} - T_0 = RT_0^2 / E$ | $\delta_{cr}$ (geometry-dependent) | $S_L = f(\alpha, k_b, \beta)$ | | **Mathematical tool** | Tangency condition | Linearisation + ODE | Asymptotic integration | | **Limitation** | Ignores spatial gradients | Steady-state only; linearised | Thin-flame approximation | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A — Architecture of the Premixed Flame (Thermal View) This diagram shows the **flame as a layered thermal structure** with three physically distinct zones, each governed by a different balance in the energy equation. ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["Unburned Gas Zone

The **thickness of the preheat zone** is $\ell_{th} \approx \alpha / S_L$, and the **thickness of the reaction zone** is much smaller by a factor of order $\beta^{-1}$ (Zel'dovich number inverse). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]** > *"What is meant by the 'thermal theory' of flame propagation? State the two main physical mechanisms on which the theory is based."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The thermal theory of flame propagation explains the self-sustained propagation of a premixed flame by considering **heat transfer by molecular conduction** as the dominant mechanism that drives the reaction wave into the unburned gas. The two main physical mechanisms on which the theory is based are: 1. **Arrhenius temperature dependence** of the chemical reaction rate, given by $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$, which makes the reaction rate extremely sensitive to temperature. 2. **Fourier's heat conduction** from the hot burned gas to the cool unburned gas, raising the unburned gas to the **auto-ignition temperature $T_{ig}$**, where it spontaneously reacts and releases more heat. The theory therefore couples **chemical kinetics** with **heat transport** to predict the steady laminar burning velocity $S_L$. --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set B]** > *"Define the Frank-Kamenetskii parameter $\delta$ and state its critical values for a slab, a cylinder, and a sphere."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter** is a dimensionless group that governs the thermal ignition of a reactive mass when spatial temperature distribution is taken into account. It is defined as: $$\delta = \frac{\rho \, Q \, A \, E \, L^2}{\lambda \, R \, T_0^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R T_0}\right)$$ where $L$ is the characteristic half-thickness or radius, $\rho$ the density, $Q$ the heat of reaction, $A$ the pre-exponential factor, $E$ the activation energy, $\lambda$ the thermal conductivity, $R$ the gas constant, and $T_0$ the ambient temperature. The **critical values** at the onset of thermal ignition are: - **Infinite slab (half-thickness $L$):** $\delta_{cr} = 0.878$ - **Infinite cylinder (radius $L$):** $\delta_{cr} = 2.00$ - **Sphere (radius $L$):** $\delta_{cr} = 3.32$ A system is **safe** (no ignition) if $\delta < \delta_{cr}$ and **prone to ignition** if $\delta \geq \delta_{cr}$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (Alternative 1) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** > *(a) Derive Semenov's criterion for thermal explosion of a lumped reactive system, clearly stating the assumption involved. (7 marks)* > *(b) Using the Zel'dovich–Damköhler thermal theory of flame propagation, derive an expression for the laminar burning velocity $S_L$ in terms of the physical and chemical properties of the mixture. (7 marks)* #### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Assumption (1 mark):** The system is *lumped* — temperature is spatially uniform throughout the reacting mass (valid when the Biot number $\mathrm{Bi} \ll 1$). **Heat generation (2 marks):** The rate of heat generation by the Arrhenius reaction is: $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = V \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ **Heat loss (1 mark):** The rate of heat loss to the surroundings is: $$\dot{Q}_{loss} = h A_s (T - T_0)$$ **Tangency condition (2 marks):** The critical ignition condition is $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss}$ *and* $d\dot{Q}_{gen}/dT = d\dot{Q}_{loss}/dT$. Dividing the differentiated equation by the original gives: $$\frac{E}{R T_{cr}^2} = \frac{1}{T_{cr} - T_0}$$ **Result (1 mark):** Solving yields: $$T_{cr} \approx T_0 + \frac{R T_0^2}{E}$$ The system is safe if $T_0 < T_{0,cr}$ and prone to explosion if $T_0 \geq T_{0,cr}$. #### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Starting equation (1 mark):** The 1-D steady energy equation in the flame-fixed frame: $$\rho c_p S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ **Integration (2 marks):** Integrating from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ with far-field BCs ($dT/dx = 0$ at both ends) eliminates the conductive term and gives: $$S_L \cdot (T_b - T_u) = A \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) dx$$ **Asymptotic evaluation (2 marks):** Using $T_b - T_u = Q/c_p$ and the Zel'dovich large-activation-energy approximation around the reaction zone, the integral evaluates to: $$\int \approx \ell_{th} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_b}\right) \cdot \frac{R T_b^2}{E (T_b - T_u)}$$ with $\ell_{th} = \alpha / S_L$ being the preheat zone thickness. **Final result (2 marks):** Substituting and solving for $S_L$: $$\boxed{\;S_L = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{\rho c_p} \cdot \frac{R T_b^2}{E(T_b - T_u)} \cdot A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_b}\right)}\;}$$ This is the celebrated Zel'dovich–Frank-Kamenetskii–Damköhler expression for the laminar burning velocity. --- ### Question B (Alternative 2) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** > *(a) For a reactive gas in a vessel of characteristic dimension $L$, write down the steady-state heat conduction equation including the Arrhenius reaction term. Show how the Frank-Kamenetskii parameter $\delta$ arises after non-dimensionalisation and linearisation. (7 marks)* > *(b) Explain with suitable equations the role of activation energy in determining (i) the thickness of the preheat zone, (ii) the burning velocity, and (iii) the ignition delay of a premixture. (7 marks)* #### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Governing equation (1 mark):** For a reactive slab with $x \in [-L, L]$: $$\lambda \frac{d^2 T}{d x^2} + \rho Q A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = 0$$ with BCs $T = T_0$ at $x = \pm L$ and $dT/dx = 0$ at $x = 0$. **Non-dimensionalisation (2 marks):** Introduce $\theta = E(T - T_0)/(RT_0^2)$ and $x = L\xi$. The equation becomes: $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{d\xi^2} + \frac{\rho Q A E L^2}{\lambda R T_0^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = 0$$ **Linearisation (2 marks):** Using the Frank-Kamenetskii approximation $E/RT \approx E/RT_0 - \theta$: $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{d\xi^2} + \delta \exp(\theta) = 0$$ where $$\boxed{\;\delta = \frac{\rho Q A E L^2}{\lambda R T_0^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT_0}\right)\;}$$ **Critical condition (2 marks):** With $\theta = 0$ at $\xi = \pm 1$ and $d\theta/d\xi = 0$ at $\xi = 0$, the boundary-value problem has a unique solution only up to $\delta = \delta_{cr} = 0.878$ for a slab. For $\delta > \delta_{cr}$, no steady solution exists and thermal explosion occurs. #### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Activation energy concept (1 mark):** The activation energy $E$ appears in the Arrhenius exponential $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ and controls the *temperature sensitivity* of the reaction. A larger $E$ means a thinner, faster, and more temperature-sensitive reaction zone. **(i) Preheat zone thickness (2 marks):** From the Damköhler relation: $$\ell_{th} = \frac{\alpha}{S_L} \propto \frac{1}{S_L(E)}$$ As $E$ increases, $S_L$ decreases (because $\exp(-E/RT_b)$ becomes smaller), so $\ell_{th}$ **increases**. The flame becomes *thicker* and *more diffuse* for higher activation energy. Numerically, $\ell_{th} \sim 0.1$ – $1$ mm for hydrocarbon-air flames. **(ii) Burning velocity (2 marks):** The Zel'dovich–Damköhler formula: $$S_L \propto \sqrt{A \exp(-E/RT_b) \cdot \frac{RT_b^2}{E(T_b - T_u)}}$$ The factor $\exp(-E/RT_b)$ dominates, so $S_L$ **decreases strongly** with increasing $E$. In the limit $E \to \infty$, $S_L \to 0$ (no propagation). **(iii) Ignition delay (2 marks):** For an isothermal reactive system, the time to reach significant conversion is: $$\tau_{ig} \sim \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{A} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ A larger $E$ means a **much longer** ignition delay, and a stronger temperature dependence ($\tau_{ig}$ halves for every $\sim 10$ K temperature rise when $E$ is large — the well-known "explosion peninsula" in Frank-Kamenetskii plots). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the tangency condition in Semenov's derivation** (loses 1 mark): Students often write only $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss}$ and forget that *two* equations are needed for a unique critical point. > 2. **Missing the Frank-Kamenetskii linearisation step** (loses 1–2 marks): The exponential $\exp(-E/RT)$ MUST be approximated as $\exp(-E/RT_0)\exp(\theta)$ to obtain $\delta$. Skipping this step shows lack of understanding of *why* $\delta$ has its particular form. > 3. **Writing $\delta_{cr}$ without specifying geometry** (loses 1 mark): The numerical value 0.878, 2.00, 3.32 is geometry-specific. Always mention "for an infinite slab", "for a cylinder", etc. > 4. **Confusing the Semenov criterion with the Frank-Kamenetskii criterion** (loses 1 mark): Semenov applies to a *lumped* (well-stirred) system; Frank-Kamenetskii applies to a *distributed* system. They are NOT interchangeable. > 5. **Forgetting the $\beta$ (Zel'dovich) factor in $S_L$** (loses 2 marks): A common error is to write $S_L \sim \sqrt{\alpha k_b}$ without the pre-factor $RT_b^2/[E(T_b - T_u)]$. The full expression must be stated. > 6. **Mixing up adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$ with burnt gas temperature $T_b$**: In the propagation context, $T_b$ is used; in the explosion context, $T_{ad}$ is used. They are the same *numerically* but represent different conceptual anchors. > 7. **Units and dimensional consistency**: The parameter $\delta$ is *dimensionless* — students who don't check dimensions often write it incorrectly with $L$ instead of $L^2$ in the numerator. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Thermal Theory of Premixed Flame Propagation** - [x] The **thermal theory** explains flame propagation as a **heat-conduction-driven** wave, not a diffusion-driven wave. - [x] The theory was developed by **Semenov (1928), Frank-Kamenetskii (1939), and Damköhler (1940)**. - [x] **Arrhenius kinetics** $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ is the heart of all three contributions. - [x] **Semenov's assumption**: lumped system (Bi ≪ 1). Critical result: $T_{cr} - T_0 = RT_0^2 / E$. - [x] **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter**: $\delta = (\rho Q A E L^2)/(\lambda R T_0^2) \cdot \exp(-E/RT_0)$. - [x] **Critical $\delta$ values**: 0.878 (slab), 2.00 (cylinder), 3.32 (sphere). Geometry matters. - [x] The **Frank-Kamenetskii linearisation** $\exp(-E/RT) \approx \exp(-E/RT_0)\exp(\theta)$ is valid for $E/RT_0 \gg 1$. - [x] The **Damköhler/Zel'dovich burning velocity**: $S_L = \sqrt{(\lambda/\rho c_p) \cdot [RT_b^2/(E(T_b-T_u))] \cdot A \exp(-E/RT_b)}$. - [x] The **Zel'dovich number** $\beta = E(T_b - T_u)/(RT_b^2)$ is a measure of flame thinness; large $\beta$ means thin flame. - [x] The **preheat zone thickness** is $\ell_{th} = \alpha / S_L \sim 0.1$ to $1$ mm for typical hydrocarbon-air flames. - [x] The **flame is an eigenvalue problem**: the propagation speed $S_L$ is *uniquely* determined by the chemical, thermal, and transport properties — it cannot be set arbitrarily. - [x] **Higher $E$** → slower flame, thicker preheat zone, longer ignition delay. - [x] **Adiabatic flame temperature** $T_{ad} = T_u + Q/c_p$ — the upper bound on $T_b$. - [x] The thermal theory **ignores species diffusion** and assumes perfect premixing — this is its main limitation, addressed by the diffusion theory and later comprehensive theories. - [x] **Engineering applications**: fire spread over solids, deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT) precursors, IC engine knock, dust-cloud explosion prevention, gas storage safety. - [x] **Memory aid**: "SFD" — **S**emenov, **F**rank-Kamenetskii, **D**amköhler — each built on the previous one's heat-balance concept.

topics.title: Premixed Flame Theories - Diffusion Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Premixed Flame Theories - Diffusion Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Diffusion Theory of Premixed Flame Propagation** (also historically called the **Active-Center Diffusion Theory** or **Maxwell's Diffusion Theory**) is a classical phenomenological model that describes the propagation of a flame front through a homogeneous premixed reactive medium. The fundamental postulate is that flame propagation is governed by the rate at which **chemically active centers** (free radicals such as H, O, OH, and other chain carriers) diffuse from the thin reaction zone forward into the unburned gas mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (KTU 2024):**
> The diffusion theory asserts that the burning velocity of a premixed flame is directly controlled by the molecular diffusion of active radical species from the reaction sheet into the cold, unreacted mixture. The flame advances at a speed dictated by the diffusion coefficient of the most mobile chain carrier (typically the hydrogen atom, H).

Mathematically, the theory treats the flame as a sharp interface across which active centers are produced at a volumetric rate and then diffuse into the upstream unburned gas. The local concentration of these radicals must reach a **critical ignition concentration** ($c^*$) to trigger self-sustained reaction in the fresh mixture, which establishes the physical thickness of the preheat zone and the resultant laminar burning velocity ($S_L$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a crowd of people standing in a long, dark corridor holding candles. The only people who can see are those close to a lit candle. For the "light" to travel down the corridor, we need either:
- **Heat to be conducted** (like a row of candles each lighting the next by their flame touching — this is the *Thermal Theory*), OR
- **Tiny glowing embers to be carried forward by air drafts** so that when they land on a new candle, it lights up. This is exactly the **Diffusion Theory**: the tiny embers are the *active centers* (radicals like H and OH), and the air drafts are *molecular diffusion*. The flame propagates because the embers "leak" forward and ignite the unburned fuel ahead of the visible flame.

In reality, **both heat conduction and radical diffusion occur simultaneously**, but the diffusion theory isolates the diffusion mechanism to predict flame speed.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in Diffusion Theory Calculations:**
> - **Diffusion coefficient of H atoms in air at NTP:** $D_H \approx \mathbf{1.8 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2/s}$
> - **Diffusion coefficient of OH radicals in air at NTP:** $D_{OH} \approx \mathbf{2.5 \times 10^{-5} \ m^2/s}$
> - **Characteristic flame thickness ($\delta$):** $\mathbf{0.1 \ to \ 1.0 \ mm}$ for typical hydrocarbon–air flames
> - **Laminar burning velocity ($S_L$):** $\mathbf{0.3 \ to \ 3.0 \ m/s}$ for hydrocarbon–air premixed flames
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = \mathbf{8.314 \ J/(mol \cdot K)}$
> - **Stoichiometric flame temperature ($T_{ad}$):** $\mathbf{1500 \ to \ 2300 \ K}$ depending on fuel

The remarkable fact that the **H atom**, with its small mass, has a diffusion coefficient roughly **two orders of magnitude greater** than OH is a key reason why hydrogen-atom transport is the dominant propagation mechanism in hydrogen-containing fuels.

### 1.4 Historical Context and Significance

The diffusion theory was first proposed by **J.C. Maxwell (1860s)** and later refined by **P. Daniell, W. Jost, and Ya.B. Zeldovich** in the 20th century. It gained renewed importance when it was discovered that the hydrogen-atom diffusion coefficient is anomalously high, providing a natural explanation for why flame speeds scale as $\sqrt{D}$ and why hydrogen-rich mixtures have very high burning velocities.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework and Operational Concept

The diffusion theory is built on a **chain of four logical postulates**:

1. **Existence of a thin reaction sheet:** The flame is modeled as a geometric surface of negligible thickness, separating unburned gas (region $-\infty \leq x \leq 0$) from burned gas (region $0 \leq x \leq +\infty$). All reaction chemistry is lumped at $x = 0$.

2. **Active-center production:** The reaction sheet continuously generates active centers (free radicals) at a rate $G$ (units: $\text{moles}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$). For hydrogen–oxygen combustion, the key radical is **H**.

3. **Steady diffusion in a moving frame:** In the coordinate frame moving with the flame at speed $S_L$, the active-center concentration $c(x)$ obeys the **steady convection–diffusion equation**.

4. **Critical concentration criterion for ignition:** A point in the unburned gas will ignite when the local active-center concentration reaches a critical value $c^*$. The location where $c(x_{ig}) = c^*$ defines the upstream edge of the preheat zone, and the diffusion time to this point sets the burning velocity.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Governing Diffusion Equation

In the **flame-attached coordinate system** moving at $S_L$, the steady-state conservation equation for an active center species is:

$$D \frac{d^2 c}{dx^2} + S_L \frac{dc}{dx} = 0$$

where:
- $D$ = molecular diffusion coefficient of the active center
- $c(x)$ = molar concentration of the active center
- $x$ = spatial coordinate measured from the reaction sheet (negative upstream)
- $S_L$ = laminar burning velocity

**Boundary conditions:**
- At the reaction sheet ($x = 0$): the active-center source condition specifies a flux or concentration
- Far upstream ($x \to -\infty$): $c \to 0$ (no active centers in fresh mixture)
- The flame fixes the relationship between $S_L$ and the chemistry

### 2.3 Solution and the Resultant Burning Velocity

The general solution of the diffusion equation is:

$$c(x) = A + B \exp\!\left(-\frac{S_L x}{D}\right)$$

Applying the upstream boundary condition $c(-\infty) = 0$ forces $A = 0$, leaving:

$$c(x) = B \exp\!\left(\frac{S_L \, x}{D}\right) \quad \text{for } x < 0$$

Defining the **characteristic diffusion length**:

$$\ell_D = \frac{D}{S_L}$$

This represents the distance ahead of the reaction sheet within which the active-center concentration has decayed to $\approx 37\%$ of its peak value. The condition that a downstream point reaches the critical concentration $c^*$ at the upstream edge of the reaction sheet yields the classical result:

$$\boxed{S_L = \sqrt{\frac{D}{\tau}}}$$

where $\tau$ is the **chemical induction time** (or the time required for the active-center concentration to build up to $c^*$). The term $D/\tau$ has units of $m^2/s^2$, so its square root yields a velocity — exactly the burning velocity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for diffusion-theory questions in KTU 2024 ESE. **Memorize the boxed equations** and the physical meaning of every variable.

| # | Equation / Relation | Variables and Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_L = \sqrt{D / \tau}$ | $S_L$ in $m/s$, $D$ in $m^2/s$, $\tau$ in $s$ | **Master equation:** burning velocity from diffusion theory |
| 2 | $D \, c_{xx} + S_L \, c_x = 0$ | $c$ in $\text{mol/m}^3$ | Steady convection–diffusion equation in flame frame |
| 3 | $\ell_D = D / S_L$ | $\ell_D$ in $m$ | Characteristic diffusion length (preheat-zone thickness) |
| 4 | $c(x) = c_0 \exp(S_L x / D)$ | $x \leq 0$ | Active-center concentration profile upstream of flame |
| 5 | $D_{H} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$ | H-atom in air, 300 K | Reference diffusion coefficient of H atoms |
| 6 | $S_L \propto \sqrt{D \cdot k_0 \, n \, \exp(-E/RT_{ad})}$ | $k_0$ pre-exponential factor | Burning velocity scaling with Arrhenius kinetics |
| 7 | $Pe = S_L \, \delta / D$ | $Pe$: Peclet number | Dimensionless ratio of convection to diffusion |
| 8 | $\delta \sim \sqrt{D \, \tau}$ | $\delta$ in $m$ | Flame thickness from diffusion theory |
| 9 | $S_L \rightarrow \infty$ as $D \rightarrow \infty$ | — | Faster diffusion ⇒ faster flame (key physical insight) |
| 10 | $S_L \propto \sqrt{T_0^2 / T_{ad}}$ (via $D \propto T^{3/2}$) | $T_0$ unburned, $T_{ad}$ flame | Temperature dependence of burning velocity |

### 2.5 Comparison: Diffusion Theory vs. Thermal Theory

A common 3-mark KTU question asks the student to distinguish between the two classical propagation theories. The student must clearly identify **what physical quantity is being transported** in each case.

| Aspect | Thermal Theory (Mallard–Le Chatelier) | Diffusion Theory (Maxwell) |
|---|---|---|
| **Propagating quantity** | Heat (thermal energy) | Active centers (free radicals) |
| **Governing equation** | Energy equation: $\lambda T_{xx} + \rho c_p S_L T_x = 0$ | Species equation: $D c_{xx} + S_L c_x = 0$ |
| **Key transport property** | Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = \lambda / (\rho c_p)$ | Mass diffusivity $D$ |
| **Burning velocity** | $S_L = \sqrt{\alpha / \tau}$ | $S_L = \sqrt{D / \tau}$ |
| **Flame thickness** | $\delta \sim \sqrt{\alpha \, \tau}$ | $\delta \sim \sqrt{D \, \tau}$ |
| **Best suited for** | Slow flames, hydrocarbon–air | Fast flames, H$_2$–O$_2$ |
| **Limitation** | Ignores radical transport | Ignores conductive heating |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The diffusion theory is **not just an academic exercise**. It has direct engineering applications:

- **Fire safety design in nuclear power plants:** Hydrogen deflagration analysis (e.g., post-LOCA scenarios) uses diffusion theory because H-atom transport dominates.
- **Internal combustion engine knock prediction:** Engine knock is governed by the auto-ignition chemistry that the diffusion theory's active-center framework describes.
- **Explosion venting design:** The burning velocity directly determines the rate of pressure rise in vented enclosures, a critical parameter in ATEX/IECEx safety standards.
- **Flame arrestor sizing:** Industrial flame arrestors rely on quenching the diffusion-driven flame propagation in narrow channels.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** A practical engineering application question worth 7 marks can be constructed by asking the student to apply $S_L = \sqrt{D/\tau}$ to estimate flame propagation in a coal-mine methane explosion. Such questions appear frequently in the KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **complete, exhaustive mathematical derivation** of the diffusion-theory burning velocity. No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is explicitly justified. This is the **"show me every line of working"** treatment expected of a top-band KTU answer script.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Active-Center Concentration Profile

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate frame.**
Consider a planar premixed flame propagating in the $+x$ direction at laminar burning velocity $S_L$. Transform to a coordinate frame attached to the flame front:

$$\xi = x - S_L \, t$$

In this frame, the flame is stationary, the unburned gas flows in from the left ($-\infty$ direction) at speed $S_L$, and the burned gas exits to the right.

**Step 2 — Write the steady-state species conservation equation.**
For an active center A (e.g., H atom) with concentration $c(\xi)$, the steady 1-D species balance (no homogeneous reaction in the preheat zone) is:

$$D \frac{d^2 c}{d\xi^2} + S_L \frac{dc}{d\xi} = 0$$

**Explanation of the equation:**
- The first term is the **diffusive flux divergence** (Fick's law applied to the moving frame).
- The second term is the **convective transport** due to the bulk flow of unburned gas toward the flame.
- Setting the sum to zero enforces **steady state** (no accumulation of active centers anywhere in the preheat zone).

**Step 3 — Recognize that this is a second-order linear ODE with constant coefficients.**
Let $y = c(\xi)$. The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{d\xi^2} + \frac{S_L}{D} \frac{dy}{d\xi} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve using the characteristic equation.**
Assume a trial solution of the form $y = e^{m\xi}$:

$$m^2 + \frac{S_L}{D} m = 0$$

$$m\left(m + \frac{S_L}{D}\right) = 0$$

This gives two roots: $m_1 = 0$ and $m_2 = -S_L/D$.

**Step 5 — Write the general solution.**

$$c(\xi) = A \, e^{0 \cdot \xi} + B \, e^{-(S_L/D) \, \xi} = A + B \, e^{-(S_L/D) \, \xi}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the upstream boundary condition.**
Far upstream ($\xi \to -\infty$), the unburned gas contains no active centers, so:

$$\lim_{\xi \to -\infty} c(\xi) = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\lim_{\xi \to -\infty} \left(A + B \, e^{+\infty}\right) = 0$$

This requires $B = 0$ to prevent the solution from blowing up. But this would give $c(\xi) = A$ everywhere — a constant, not a profile. We must therefore re-examine: the exponential in the solution must decay in the upstream direction, meaning the sign convention needs care.

**Step 7 — Re-define the coordinate with the sign convention.**
It is conventional to let $\xi$ point *into the unburned gas* (i.e., opposite to the direction of flame propagation). With this convention, the convection term changes sign and the solution becomes:

$$c(\xi) = c_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{S_L \, \xi}{D}\right), \quad \xi \geq 0$$

**Step 8 — Identify the reference concentration $c_0$.**
At the reaction sheet ($\xi = 0$), the active-center concentration equals the equilibrium flame value $c_0$. This is set by chemical equilibrium in the burned gas and is treated as a **boundary input** to the diffusion problem.

**Step 9 — Final form of the concentration profile.**

$$\boxed{c(\xi) = c_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{S_L \, \xi}{D}\right)}$$

The profile **decays exponentially** as we move away from the reaction sheet into the unburned gas, with characteristic length scale:

$$\ell_D = \frac{D}{S_L}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Burning Velocity from the Critical Concentration Criterion

**Step 10 — Define the critical ignition concentration.**
For self-sustained propagation, the active-center concentration must reach a critical value $c^*$ at a finite distance $\xi^*$ upstream of the reaction sheet. This is the **ignition threshold** below which the chemistry cannot sustain itself.

**Step 11 — Write the critical condition.**

$$c(\xi^*) = c^* \quad \Longrightarrow \quad c_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{S_L \, \xi^*}{D}\right) = c^*$$

**Step 12 — Solve for the upstream ignition distance.**

$$-\frac{S_L \, \xi^*}{D} = \ln\!\left(\frac{c^*}{c_0}\right)$$

$$\xi^* = \frac{D}{S_L} \ln\!\left(\frac{c_0}{c^*}\right)$$

**Step 13 — Relate the upstream distance to the induction time.**
The chemical induction time $\tau$ is the time required for the radical concentration to build up from $c^*$ to $c_0$ at the reaction sheet. For a steady flame, the residence time of a fluid element in the induction zone is exactly:

$$\tau = \frac{\xi^*}{S_L}$$

**Step 14 — Substitute the expression for $\xi^*$.**

$$\tau = \frac{1}{S_L} \cdot \frac{D}{S_L} \ln\!\left(\frac{c_0}{c^*}\right) = \frac{D}{S_L^2} \ln\!\left(\frac{c_0}{c^*}\right)$$

**Step 15 — Solve for $S_L$ in terms of $D$ and $\tau$.**

$$S_L^2 = \frac{D}{\tau} \ln\!\left(\frac{c_0}{c^*}\right)$$

For a strongly exothermic flame where $c_0 \gg c^*$, the logarithmic term is of order unity (typically $\ln(c_0/c^*) \approx 2$ to $5$). Absorbing this order-unity factor into an effective induction time:

$$\boxed{S_L = \sqrt{\frac{D}{\tau_{\text{eff}}}}}$$

**Step 16 — Verify units.**
$[D] = m^2/s$, $[\tau] = s$, so $[D/\tau] = m^2/s^2$, and $\sqrt{m^2/s^2} = m/s$. Units are consistent. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Flame Thickness from Diffusion Theory

**Step 17 — Define the flame thickness.**
The flame thickness $\delta$ is the distance over which the active-center concentration drops from $c_0$ to $c_0/e$:

$$c(\delta) = c_0/e$$

**Step 18 — Apply the profile equation.**

$$c_0 \, e^{-S_L \delta / D} = c_0 \, e^{-1}$$

$$\frac{S_L \, \delta}{D} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{D}{S_L}$$

**Step 19 — Substitute $S_L = \sqrt{D/\tau}$:**

$$\delta = \frac{D}{\sqrt{D/\tau}} = \sqrt{D \, \tau}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{D \, \tau}}$$

**Step 20 — Numerical sanity check.**
For a methane–air flame with $D \approx 2 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$ and $\tau \approx 1 \, ms = 10^{-3} \, s$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{(2 \times 10^{-5})(10^{-3})} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \, m = 0.14 \ mm$$

This is in excellent agreement with the experimentally observed flame thickness of $\sim 0.1$–$0.5 \, mm$ for methane–air premixed flames. ✓

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Temperature Dependence via the Diffusion Coefficient

**Step 21 — Recall the kinetic theory expression for the diffusion coefficient.**

$$D = D_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{P_0}{P}$$

**Step 22 — Substitute into the burning-velocity expression.**

$$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{D_0}{\tau} \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 23 — Recognize that $\tau$ itself follows an Arrhenius law.**

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k_0} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 24 — Combine to obtain the full temperature dependence.**

$$S_L = \sqrt{D_0 \, k_0 \, \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)}$$

**Step 25 — Identify the dominant temperature in the exponential.**
Because the exponential is the strongest function of $T$, the dominant temperature is the **flame (burned-gas) temperature** $T_{ad}$:

$$\boxed{S_L \propto \sqrt{T^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{R \, T_{ad}}\right)}}$$

This explains the **strong sensitivity of $S_L$ to the adiabatic flame temperature**, a key result used in fire-spread modeling and in understanding why enriched-oxygen atmospheres produce dramatically faster flames.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Key Limiting Cases

| Limit | Condition | Physical Consequence | $S_L$ Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D \to 0$ | No diffusion (e.g., very high pressure) | Flame cannot propagate | $S_L \to 0$ |
| $D \to \infty$ | Very high diffusivity (e.g., H atoms) | Active centers spread far ahead | $S_L \to \infty$ (limiting case) |
| $\tau \to 0$ | Infinitely fast chemistry | No induction delay | $S_L \to \infty$ |
| $\tau \to \infty$ | Very slow chemistry | No chain branching | $S_L \to 0$ |
| $T_{ad} \to T_0$ | No heat release | Sub-adiabatic | $S_L \to 0$ (extinction limit) |
| Lean mixture | $\phi \ll 1$ | Insufficient fuel | $S_L$ small |
| Rich mixture | $\phi \gg 1$ | Insufficient oxidizer | $S_L$ small |
| Stoichiometric | $\phi = 1$ | Maximum $T_{ad}$ | $S_L$ maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The symmetric dependence of $S_L$ on equivalence ratio $\phi$ (maximum at stoichiometry, falling on both lean and rich sides) is a fundamental experimental fact that diffusion theory correctly reproduces via the $T_{ad}(\phi)$ dependence.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Diffusion-Theory Flame Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fresh Unburned Gas\nphi mixture at T0] --> B1[Convective Inflow\nvelocity SL]
    B1 --> C1[Pre-ignition Zone\nactive center build-up]
    C1 --> D1[Critical Ignition\nc reaches c-star]
    D1 --> E1[Reaction Sheet\nx = 0\nactive centers produced]
    E1 --> F1[Burned Gas Region\nT = Tad, products]
    E1 --> G1[Active Center Diffusion\nflux opposite to SL]
    G1 --> C1
    F1 --> H1[Heat Conduction\nsecondary effect]
    H1 --> C1
    E1 --> I1[Boundary Condition\nc0 fixed by equilibrium]
    D1 --> J1[Induction Time tau\nresidence time in preheat]
    J1 --> K1[Formula SL = sqrt D over tau]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Concentration Profile and Length Scales

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Coordinate x] --> P2[x less than 0\nunburned side]
    P2 --> P3[c x equals c0 exp SL x over D]
    P3 --> P4[At x equals 0\nc equals c0 peak]
    P4 --> P5[x greater than 0\nburned side]
    P5 --> P6[Equilibrium chemistry\ndominates diffusion]
    P1 --> P7[Length scale lD equals D over SL]
    P7 --> P8[Physical meaning\n37 percent decay distance]
    P8 --> P9[Also equals flame thickness delta]
    P1 --> P10[Induction distance xi-star]
    P10 --> P11[Where c equals c-star]
    P11 --> P12[Drives burning velocity formula]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Two Classical Theories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Flame Propagation Theories] --> T2[Thermal Theory\nMallard Le Chatelier]
    T1 --> T3[Diffusion Theory\nMaxwell Zeldovich]
    T2 --> T4[Transported quantity\nHeat energy]
    T2 --> T5[Governing equation\nlambda Txx plus rho cp SL Tx]
    T2 --> T6[Burning velocity\nsqrt alpha over tau]
    T3 --> T7[Transported quantity\nActive radical species]
    T3 --> T8[Governing equation\nD cxx plus SL cx]
    T3 --> T9[Burning velocity\nsqrt D over tau]
    T4 --> T10[Best for slow hydrocarbon flames]
    T7 --> T11[Best for fast H2 O2 flames]
    T5 --> T12[Energy transport dominates]
    T8 --> T13[Radical transport dominates]
    T6 --> T14[Combined Theory\nZeldovich von Neumann Doering]
    T9 --> T14
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Engineering Decision Tree for Theory Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start: Flame Speed Calculation Needed] --> S2{Fuel Type?}
    S2 --> S3[Hydrogen rich mixture] --> S4[Use Diffusion Theory\nH atom transport dominates]
    S2 --> S5[Hydrocarbon CH4 C3H8] --> S6{Dominant Mechanism?}
    S6 --> S7[Near stoichiometric\nhigh Tad] --> S8[Use Combined Theory]
    S6 --> S9[Lean mixture\nheat losses significant] --> S10[Use Thermal Theory]
    S4 --> S11[Estimate SL using\nsqrt D H over tau]
    S8 --> S12[Estimate SL using\nZND structure model]
    S10 --> S13[Estimate SL using\nsqrt alpha over tau]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Diffusion-Theory Flame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Z1[Input Boundary: Unburned Mixture\nfresh reactants, T0, P0, phi] --> Z2[Convective Transport Module\nbrings reactants to flame at SL]
    Z2 --> Z3[Diffusion Module\nD d2c over dx2 plus SL dc over dx equals 0]
    Z3 --> Z4[Critical Concentration Check\nc reaches c-star]
    Z4 --> Z5[Induction Timer\ntau equals integral over induction zone]
    Z5 --> Z6[Reaction Sheet\nproduces active centers and heat]
    Z6 --> Z7[Output Boundary: Burned Products\nT equals Tad, equilibrium composition]
    Z6 --> Z8[Feedback Loop\ndiffusion of radicals back to Z3]
    Z3 --> Z9[Compute SL = sqrt D over tau]
    Z9 --> Z2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PESFT528, June 2024]`
> **State and briefly explain the fundamental postulate of the diffusion theory of flame propagation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The diffusion theory of premixed flame propagation, originally proposed by Maxwell and later refined by Zeldovich, postulates that **flame propagation is governed by the molecular diffusion of chemically active centers (free radicals such as H, O, and OH) from the reaction zone into the unburned gas mixture**. The active centers are produced continuously in the thin reaction sheet and diffuse upstream against the convective inflow. When the local concentration of these radicals reaches a critical value $c^*$, the fresh mixture ignites, and the flame front advances at a speed $S_L$ determined by the diffusion coefficient $D$ and the chemical induction time $\tau$ via $S_L = \sqrt{D/\tau}$. (3 marks)

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
> **Distinguish between the thermal theory and the diffusion theory of flame propagation in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. | Thermal Theory | Diffusion Theory |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Propagation is controlled by **heat conduction** from the reaction zone to the unburned gas. | Propagation is controlled by **diffusion of active centers** (free radicals). |
| 2 | The burning velocity is given by $S_L = \sqrt{\alpha / \tau}$, where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity. | The burning velocity is given by $S_L = \sqrt{D / \tau}$, where $D$ is the mass diffusivity. |
| 3 | More applicable to **slow hydrocarbon–air flames** where heat transport dominates. | More applicable to **hydrogen–oxygen flames** where H-atom diffusion is the rate-limiting step. |

(3 marks)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, End Semester, Module 3]`
> **(a)** Starting from the steady-state convection–diffusion equation for active centers in the flame-attached coordinate frame, derive the burning velocity $S_L = \sqrt{D/\tau}$ from the diffusion theory. State clearly all boundary conditions and physical assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a hydrogen–air premixed flame, the diffusion coefficient of H atoms is $D_H = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$ and the chemical induction time is $\tau = 0.5 \, ms$. Calculate (i) the laminar burning velocity, (ii) the characteristic diffusion length, and (iii) the flame thickness. Comment on why your result is higher than that of a methane–air flame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumption 1 (1 mark):* The flame is a planar, steady, 1-D structure propagating in the $+x$ direction at laminar burning velocity $S_L$.

*Assumption 2 (1 mark):* In the flame-attached frame, active centers (concentration $c$) obey steady convection–diffusion with no homogeneous reaction in the preheat zone:

$$D \frac{d^2 c}{dx^2} + S_L \frac{dc}{dx} = 0$$

*Step 1 — General solution (1.5 marks):* Let $\xi = -x$ point upstream. The general solution of the ODE is $c(\xi) = A + B \exp(-S_L \xi / D)$.

*Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions (1.5 marks):*
- At $\xi = 0$ (reaction sheet): $c = c_0$, the equilibrium flame value.
- At $\xi \to \infty$ (far upstream): $c \to 0$.

This yields $A = 0$ and $B = c_0$, giving $c(\xi) = c_0 \exp(-S_L \xi / D)$.

*Step 3 — Critical-concentration criterion (1 mark):* Define the induction distance $\xi^*$ where $c(\xi^*) = c^*$. Setting $c_0 \exp(-S_L \xi^* / D) = c^*$ gives $\xi^* = (D/S_L) \ln(c_0/c^*)$.

*Step 4 — Burning velocity formula (1 mark):* With induction time $\tau = \xi^* / S_L$:

$$S_L^2 = \frac{D}{\tau} \ln\!\left(\frac{c_0}{c^*}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{S_L = \sqrt{\frac{D}{\tau}}}$$

(absorbing the order-unity logarithmic factor into $\tau$).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

*Given:* $D_H = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$, $\tau = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, s$.

**(i) Laminar burning velocity (2.5 marks):**

$$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{D}{\tau}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$S_L = \sqrt{3.6} \approx 1.897 \, m/s$$

**(ii) Characteristic diffusion length (2 marks):**

$$\ell_D = \frac{D}{S_L} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{1.897} \approx 9.49 \times 10^{-4} \, m \approx 0.95 \, mm$$

**(iii) Flame thickness (2 marks):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{D \, \tau} = \sqrt{(1.8 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-4})}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{9 \times 10^{-7}} = 3 \times 10^{-3.5} \approx 9.49 \times 10^{-4} \, m \approx 0.95 \, mm$$

*Comment (0.5 mark):* The hydrogen–air flame has a substantially higher burning velocity (~1.9 m/s) compared to methane–air (~0.4 m/s) because the H-atom diffusion coefficient ($1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$) is roughly 100 times larger than that of typical hydrocarbon radicals in methane combustion, and the induction time is also shorter due to the rapid H–O$_2$–OH chain-branching cycle.

---

> **Question 3B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester, Module 3]`
> **(a)** Explain the four physical postulates underlying the diffusion theory of flame propagation. Discuss the role of the critical radical concentration $c^*$ and the chemical induction time $\tau$ in determining the burning velocity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A stoichiometric propane–air flame has a measured burning velocity of $0.46 \, m/s$ and a flame thickness of $0.5 \, mm$. Using diffusion theory, estimate (i) the effective diffusion coefficient of the dominant active center, and (ii) the chemical induction time. Compare your diffusion coefficient with the H-atom value of $1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$ and explain the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Postulates and Role of $c^*$ and $\tau$ (7 marks):**

*Postulate 1 — Thin reaction sheet (1.5 marks):* The flame is idealized as a mathematically sharp interface separating unburned reactants (upstream) from burned products (downstream). All chemical reaction is concentrated at this surface, which is consistent with the experimentally observed flame thickness being much smaller than any macroscopic length scale of the flow.

*Postulate 2 — Active-center generation (1.5 marks):* Within the reaction sheet, free radicals (H, O, OH, CH$_3$, etc.) are produced at a rate determined by the local chain-branching chemistry. The concentration of these species at the sheet is fixed at $c_0$ by chemical equilibrium in the burned gas.

*Postulate 3 — Steady convection–diffusion in preheat zone (1.5 marks):* In the flame-attached frame, the active-center concentration satisfies $D c_{xx} + S_L c_x = 0$ in the upstream region, yielding the exponential profile $c(x) = c_0 \exp(S_L x / D)$.

*Postulate 4 — Critical-concentration ignition criterion (1.5 marks):* A parcel of unburned gas auto-ignites when the local active-center concentration reaches a critical value $c^*$. The induction time $\tau$ is the residence time required for the concentration to build up to $c^*$; this sets the burning velocity via $S_L = \sqrt{D/\tau}$.

*Role of $c^*$:* Sets the upstream location at which the fresh mixture is "activated." A smaller $c^*$ (more easily ignited mixture) means the induction zone is thicker and the burning velocity is higher.

*Role of $\tau:$* Represents the kinetic timescale of chain branching. A shorter $\tau$ (faster chemistry, e.g., in hydrogen flames) yields a higher $S_L$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

*Given:* $S_L = 0.46 \, m/s$, $\delta = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, m$.

**(i) Effective diffusion coefficient (3 marks):**

From $\delta = \sqrt{D \tau}$ and $S_L = \sqrt{D/\tau}$:

$$\delta \cdot S_L = \sqrt{D \tau} \cdot \sqrt{D/\tau} = D$$

$$D = \delta \cdot S_L = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.46) = 2.3 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2/s$$

**(ii) Chemical induction time (2.5 marks):**

From $\delta = \sqrt{D \tau}$:

$$\tau = \frac{\delta^2}{D} = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2.3 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2.3 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.09 \times 10^{-3} \, s \approx 1.1 \, ms$$

**Comparison and explanation (1.5 marks):**
The effective diffusion coefficient for propane–air ($2.3 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2/s$) is roughly **8 times smaller** than the H-atom diffusion coefficient in air ($1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$). This is because in hydrocarbon combustion, the dominant active centers are heavier species (OH, CH$_3$, CHO) with much lower molecular diffusivities than the hydrogen atom. Although H atoms are still produced in hydrocarbon flames, the rate-limiting propagation step is governed by hydrocarbon radicals, and the *effective* $D$ that emerges from the diffusion-theory fit is therefore smaller. This explains why propane–air flames are slower than hydrogen–air flames.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Diffusion-Theory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the coordinate-frame transformation (–1 mark):** Students often write the diffusion equation in the lab frame instead of the flame-attached frame, omitting the convective term $S_L \, dc/dx$. The examiner will deduct marks if the equation lacks this term.
>
> 2. **Confusing thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ with mass diffusivity $D$ (–1 mark):** The thermal theory uses $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho c_p)$ while the diffusion theory uses $D$. Mixing up the two is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Skipping boundary conditions (–1 to –2 marks):** The boundary conditions $c(-\infty) = 0$ and $c(0) = c_0$ are **mandatory** in the derivation. Omitting them costs marks even if the final answer is correct.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in numerical problems (–1 mark):** Always convert $\tau$ from ms to seconds before substituting. A common mistake is to write $\tau = 0.5$ instead of $\tau = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ when the problem states $0.5$ ms.
>
> 5. **Not commenting on the physical significance of the answer (–0.5 mark):** A 14-mark numerical answer that simply states the final number without a physical comment is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. Always end with a one-line interpretation.
>
> 6. **Writing "diffusion of heat" instead of "diffusion of active centers" (–1 mark):** This is a definitional error. The diffusion theory is specifically about *radical species* diffusion, not heat.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Core idea:** Flame propagates because active centers (H, O, OH) **diffuse** from the reaction sheet into the unburned gas.
- [x] **Governing ODE:** $D \, c_{xx} + S_L \, c_x = 0$ in the flame-attached frame.
- [x] **Concentration profile:** $c(x) = c_0 \exp(S_L x / D)$ for $x \leq 0$.
- [x] **Master burning velocity formula:** $S_L = \sqrt{D / \tau}$.
- [x] **Characteristic diffusion length:** $\ell_D = D / S_L$.
- [x] **Flame thickness:** $\delta = \sqrt{D \, \tau}$.
- [x] **Critical concentration $c^*$:** Defines the upstream ignition boundary.
- [x] **Induction time $\tau$:** Sets the chemical timescale; shorter $\tau$ ⇒ higher $S_L$.
- [x] **H-atom diffusivity:** $D_H \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2/s$ — anomalously high, explains fast H$_2$ flames.
- [x] **Comparison with thermal theory:** Replace $\alpha$ with $D$; replace heat with radical species.
- [x] **Equivalence-ratio dependence:** $S_L$ is maximum near $\phi = 1$ (stoichiometric).
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $S_L \to 0$ as $D \to 0$ or $\tau \to \infty$ (extinction).
- [x] **Engineering applications:** H$_2$ deflagration analysis, IC engine knock, explosion venting, flame arrestors.
- [x] **Examiner's red flag:** Always state all boundary conditions explicitly in derivations.
- [x] **Numerical hygiene:** Convert all units to SI before substituting; check that $D/\tau$ has units of $m^2/s^2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (for the exam hall):**
> **"D-$\tau$"** — Diffusion theory gives **S$_L$ = $\sqrt{D/\tau}$** — the "D-Tau" formula.
> Compare with thermal theory: **"$\alpha$-$\tau$"** — S$_L$ = $\sqrt{\alpha/\tau}$.
> Same shape, different transport property. Memorize both, but **don't mix them up!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Premixed Flame Theories - Comprehensive Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Premixed Flame Theories - Comprehensive Theory # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Premixed Flame:** A combustion wave in which the fuel and oxidizer are **pre-mixed at the molecular level** before the arrival of the flame front. The flame propagates as a self-sustaining wave through the homogeneous mixture via the coupled processes of **thermal conduction, mass diffusion, and chemical reaction**. A **premixed flame** is mathematically characterized by the **laminar burning velocity**, $S_L$, defined as the velocity at which a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, stationary flame front propagates *into the unburned mixture*, measured **relative to the unburned gas**. $$\boxed{S_L \equiv -\frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_u}}$$ where: - $\dot{m}$ is the mass burning flux (kg/m²·s) — *conserved across the flame* for a steady 1-D flame - $\rho_u$ is the **unburned gas density** [kg/m³] ## 1.2 The Three Classical Theories (Syllabus Mandate) KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 requires a complete treatment of three historically significant theoretical frameworks. Each addresses a different dominant transport mechanism: > [!NOTE] > **The Three Theories of Premixed Flame Propagation:** > 1. **Thermal Theory (Mallard–Le Chatelier, 1881):** Heat conduction alone drives propagation. > 2. **Diffusion Theory (Tannery & Jost, 1929–1930):** Molecular diffusion of active species (free radicals, atoms) alone drives propagation. > 3. **Comprehensive Theory (Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii–Semenov, 1938):** Both heat conduction and mass diffusion are treated simultaneously in the high-activation-energy asymptotic limit. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chemical Domino Wave" > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a row of upright dominoes. When the first one falls, it transfers mechanical energy to its neighbor, which then transfers energy to the next, and so on. In a premixed flame, the unburned gas molecules adjacent to the flame front are "tipped over" into reaction, releasing heat and active species. The **heat** then conducts forward to pre-heat the next layer (thermal theory), while the **active species** (like H, O, OH radicals) diffuse forward to ignite the next layer chemically (diffusion theory). The real flame uses *both* dominoes — this is the **comprehensive theory**. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | $E$ | Global Activation Energy | **80–200 kJ/mol** for hydrocarbons | | $S_L$ | Laminar Burning Velocity | **0.3–3.0 m/s** for common fuels | | $T_{ad}$ | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | **1500–2400 K** | | $\delta_L$ | Thermal Flame Thickness | **0.1–1.0 mm** | | $\beta$ | Zeldovich Number | **8–15** (large, justifies asymptotics) | | $Le$ | Lewis Number | **0.8–1.2** for most fuels in air | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 One-Dimensional Steady Flame Governing Equations Consider a 1-D steady flame in a coordinate $x$ fixed in the unburned gas. The flame thickness $\delta_L$ is the preheat-zone length scale.
