The governing equations are: **Continuity (mass conservation):** $$\frac{d}{dx}(\rho u) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho u = \rho_u S_L = \dot{m} = \text{const.}$$ **Energy (thermal):** $$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{\omega}_F q$$ **Species (mass fraction $Y_k$):** $$\dot{m} \frac{dY_k}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\rho D_k \frac{dY_k}{dx}\right) - \dot{\omega}_k$ where: - $\dot{\omega}_F$ is the **fuel mass consumption rate** [kg/m³·s] - $q$ is the **heat of reaction per kg of fuel** [J/kg] - $D_k$ is the **mass diffusivity of species $k$** [m²/s] ## 2.2 Boundary Conditions **Upstream (unburned side, $x \to -\infty$):** $$T \to T_u, \quad Y_F \to Y_{F,u}, \quad Y_P \to 0$$ **Downstream (burned side, $x \to +\infty$):** $$T \to T_{ad} = T_u + \frac{q Y_{F,u}}{c_p}, \quad Y_F \to 0, \quad Y_P \to Y_{P,b}$$ **Asymptotic Reaction-Zone Conditions (used in matched asymptotics):** At the upstream edge of the reaction zone: $T \approx T_u$, fuel is unreacted. At the downstream edge: $T \approx T_{ad}$, fuel is fully depleted. ## 2.3 The Peclet Number and Its Physical Meaning The **Peclet number** of the flame measures the ratio of convective heat transport to conductive heat transport: $$Pe_L = \frac{\rho_u c_p S_L \delta_L}{\lambda} = \frac{S_L \delta_L}{\alpha} \sim \mathcal{O}(1)$$ > [!NOTE] > A Peclet number of order unity means the flame thickness is set by the balance between **convection** (carrying reactants into the reaction zone) and **conduction** (pre-heating the unburned gas). ## 2.4 The Damköhler Number The **Damköhler number** compares the **flow residence time** to the **chemical reaction time**: $$Da = \frac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{\delta_L / S_L}{1/k_0 \exp(-E/RT_{ad})}$$ For a self-sustaining premixed flame: $Da \gg 1$ (chemistry is fast enough that reactants are consumed within the flame). ## 2.5 The Zeldovich Number — The Central Non-Dimensional Group $$\boxed{\beta = \frac{E(T_{ad} - T_u)}{R\,T_{ad}^2}}$$ **Physical meaning:** $\beta$ is the **non-dimensional temperature sensitivity** of the reaction rate, evaluated across the flame. Large $\beta$ means: - The reaction is **highly temperature-sensitive** - The reaction zone is **thin** compared to the preheat zone - The **asymptotic (large-$\beta$) analysis** is valid For methane-air: $\beta \approx 8$; for hydrogen-air: $\beta \approx 6$–$10$. ## 2.6 The Lewis Number $$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{\lambda}{\rho c_p D}$$ This is the ratio of thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity. It determines the **relative importance of heat conduction versus species diffusion** in flame propagation. - $Le > 1$ (most hydrocarbon-air flames): heat conduction is *faster* than species diffusion — thermal effects dominate. - $Le < 1$ (hydrogen-air): mass diffusion is *faster* than heat conduction — diffusion effects dominate. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Laminar Burning Velocity Formulas — Exact Form Reference** | Theory / Condition | $S_L$ Formula | Key Assumption | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Thermal (Mallard–Le Chatelier)** | $S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c}$, $\quad \tau_c = 1/k_0 e^{-E/RT_{ad}}$ | Heat conduction only, $\beta \gg 1$ | | **Diffusion (Tannery–Jost)** | $S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \alpha \cdot Le}{\tau_c}$ | Mass diffusion only, $\beta \gg 1$ | | **Comprehensive (Zeldovich–FK)** | $S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{Le - 1 + \beta}$ | Both transport modes, $\beta \gg 1$ | | **Limit: $Le = 1$** | $S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c}$ | Comprehensive $\to$ Thermal (heat and mass in balance) | | **Limit: $Le \to \infty$** | $S_L^2 \to \dfrac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c}$ | Mass diffusion is negligible | | **Limit: $Le \to 0$** | $S_L^2 \to \infty$ (unphysical) | Mass diffusion dominates unrealistically | | **Flame Thickness** | $\delta_L = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L}$ | All theories | | **Mass Burning Flux** | $\dot{m} = \rho_u S_L$ | Continuity | | **Zeldovich Number** | $\beta = \dfrac{E(T_{ad} - T_u)}{R T_{ad}^2}$ | Definition | | **Damköhler Number** | $Da = \dfrac{\delta_L / S_L}{1/k_0 e^{-E/RT_{ad}}}$ | Definition | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why does this matter?** > - **Fire Safety Engineering:** $S_L$ is the foundation for predicting **flame spread rates** over solid fuels and **deflagration pressures** in enclosures. > - **IC Engine Design:** $S_L$ determines the **knock resistance** and the **allowable compression ratio**. > - **Gas Turbine Combustors:** Laminar burning velocity sets the **blow-off and flashback limits**. > - **Explosion Venting:** $S_L$ directly governs the **rate of pressure rise** during a gas explosion. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Theory of Mallard–Le Chatelier (1881) ### Step 1 — Set up the energy integral For a steady 1-D flame, the energy equation (neglecting radiation and pressure work) is: $$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} - \frac{d}{dx}\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) = \dot{\omega}_F q$$ Integrate from $x = -\infty$ (unburned) to $x = +\infty$ (burned): $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx}\,dx - \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d}{dx}\left(\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{\omega}_F q\,dx$$ ### Step 2 — Evaluate the left-hand side The first term: $\dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)$. The second term: $\left[\lambda \frac{dT}{dx}\right]_{-\infty}^{+\infty} = 0$ (gradients vanish at both boundaries). So the LHS becomes: $\dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)$. ### Step 3 — Evaluate the right-hand side (chemical heat release) The right-hand side equals the **total heat release rate per unit flame area**: $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{\omega}_F q\,dx = q \cdot \left(\text{fuel mass consumption rate per area}\right) = \dot{m} \cdot q \cdot Y_{F,u} / q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)$$ This is a **consistency check** — both sides equal the same $\dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)$, providing no direct information about $S_L$. The thermal theory must therefore introduce a *physical model* for the reaction zone. ### Step 4 — Thermal theory's key assumption Mallard and Le Chatelier assumed that the **reaction occurs in a thin zone of thickness $\delta_R$** centered at the temperature $T_{ad}$, and the rest of the flame (the preheat zone) is a **conduction-dominated inert region**. The **conduction solution** in the preheat zone (no reactions, $\lambda = $ const) is: $$T(x) = T_u + (T_{ad} - T_u) \exp\left(\frac{\rho_u c_p S_L x}{\lambda}\right), \quad x < 0$$ The preheat zone thickness is defined by the $e$-folding length: $$\delta_L = \frac{\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L} = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}$$ ### Step 5 — Reaction time approximation The reaction time at the adiabatic flame temperature is taken as: $$\tau_c = \frac{1}{k_0 \exp(-E/R T_{ad})}$$ The fuel consumption requires a time $\sim \tau_c$. The flame is a balance: reactants flow into the reaction zone at speed $S_L$, and they must be consumed within a length $\sim \delta_L$. The mass balance gives: $$\dot{m} = \rho_u S_L \sim \frac{\rho_u \delta_L}{\tau_c} \cdot (\text{number density factor})$$ Averaging over the flame thickness: $$\rho_u S_L \cdot Y_{F,u} = \frac{\rho_u \delta_L}{\tau_c}$$ Substituting $\delta_L = \alpha/S_L$: $$\rho_u S_L Y_{F,u} = \frac{\rho_u \alpha}{S_L \tau_c}$$ $$S_L^2 = \frac{\alpha}{\tau_c \cdot Y_{F,u}}$$ Since $q Y_{F,u} = c_p (T_{ad} - T_u)$ and for large $\beta$ the analysis is refined to give a **factor of 2** from the asymptotic matching: $$\boxed{S_L^2 = \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c} = \frac{2 \alpha k_0 \exp(-E/R T_{ad})}{}}$$ **Units check:** $\alpha$ [m²/s] / $\tau_c$ [s] = m²/s². Square root gives m/s. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Comprehensive Theory (Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii, 1938) ### Step 1 — Outer (preheat zone) solution Neglect reactions in the preheat zone. With constant properties: $$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$ Solution: $T(x) = T_u + (T_{ad} - T_u) e^{x/\delta_L}$ where $\delta_L = \lambda/(\dot{m} c_p)$. ### Step 2 — Inner (reaction zone) scaling Within the reaction zone, define a stretched coordinate $\eta = x/\delta_R$ where $\delta_R \ll \delta_L$. The large-$\beta$ assumption says: $$\delta_R \sim \frac{\delta_L}{\beta}$$ The dominant energy balance becomes (conduction negligible vs. convection in the reaction zone): $$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} \approx \dot{\omega}_F q$$ ### Step 3 — Inner species equation For a global one-step reaction $F + \nu O \to \text{Products}$ with Lewis number $Le$: $$\dot{m} \frac{dY_F}{dx} = \frac{\lambda}{c_p Le} \frac{d^2 Y_F}{dx^2} - \dot{\omega}_F$$ ### Step 4 — Asymptotic matching and the dispersion relation After integration of the inner equations and matching the inner solution to the outer preheat solution at the upstream edge of the reaction zone, the **eigenvalue condition** for $S_L$ emerges: $$\boxed{S_L^2 = \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c} \cdot \frac{\beta}{Le - 1 + \beta}}$$ where: - $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho_u c_p)$ - $\tau_c = 1/(k_0 e^{-E/RT_{ad}})$ - $\beta = E(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R T_{ad}^2)$ ### Step 5 — Limiting cases **Case A: $Le = 1$ (equal thermal and mass diffusivities):** $$S_L^2 = \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\beta} = \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c}$$ This is identical to the **thermal theory** — the factor of $Le$ from the diffusion equation exactly cancels because $Le = 1$. **Case B: $Le \to \infty$ (mass diffusion is slow):** $$S_L^2 \to \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c} \cdot \frac{\beta}{Le} \to 0 \text{ (but finite if } Le \text{ is large, not infinite)}$$ **Case C: $Le < 1$ (mass diffusion is fast, e.g., hydrogen):** $$Le - 1 + \beta < \beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_L^2 > \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c}$$ This is the **diffusion-promoted flame**: $S_L$ is enhanced because radicals reach the next layer of unburned gas faster than heat. Hydrogen-air flames have anomalously high $S_L$ for this reason. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Zeldovich Number and the Activation-Energy Asymptotics The Zeldovich number arises naturally from the high-$\beta$ expansion of the reaction rate. Near $T_{ad}$: $$\exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT_{ad}}\right) \exp\left[\frac{E}{RT_{ad}^2}(T_{ad} - T)\right]$$ Defining $\theta = (T - T_u)/(T_{ad} - T_u)$ (dimensionless temperature, $0 \le \theta \le 1$): $$\exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT_{ad}}\right) \exp\left[\beta(1 - \theta)\right]$$ For $\beta \to \infty$, the reaction is **exponentially confined** to a thin region near $\theta = 1$ (i.e., $T \approx T_{ad}$), with thickness $\sim 1/\beta$ in $\theta$-space. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Flame Thickness and its Physical Meaning The flame thickness is the $e$-folding length of the preheat zone: $$\delta_L = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}$$ Substituting the comprehensive theory result: $$\delta_L = \alpha \sqrt{\frac{\tau_c (Le - 1 + \beta)}{2 \alpha \beta}} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha \tau_c (Le - 1 + \beta)}{2 \beta}}$$ **Physical meaning:** $\delta_L$ is the **length scale over which heat conduction pre-heats the unburned gas to the reaction temperature**. It is a critical length scale for: - **Flame–vortex interactions** (turbulent combustion) - **Quenching distance** (wall quenching) - **Minimum ignition energy** ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Theories | Property | Thermal (MLC) | Diffusion (TJ) | Comprehensive (ZFKS) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Dominant transport | Heat conduction | Mass diffusion | Both | | Mass transport | Neglected | Dominant | Included with $Le$ | | Heat transport | Dominant | Neglected | Included with $1$ | | Burning velocity $S_L$ | $\sqrt{2\alpha/\tau_c}$ | $\sqrt{2\alpha \cdot Le/\tau_c}$ | $\sqrt{(2\alpha/\tau_c) \cdot \beta/(Le-1+\beta)}$ | | Flame thickness | $\alpha/S_L$ | $\alpha/(S_L \cdot Le)$ | $\alpha/S_L$ | | Best for | Hydrocarbon–air ($Le \approx 1$–$1.5$) | Not physical alone | All flames, especially $Le \neq 1$ | | Historical origin | 1881 | 1929 | 1938 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Flame Zone Structure The following block diagram shows the spatial structure of a one-dimensional, steady, planar premixed flame, with the corresponding temperature, reaction rate, and species profiles. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["ZONE 1: UNBURNED GAS
T equals Tu
Y_F equals Y_Fu
Reaction rate zero"] -->|Conduction and Diffusion| B["ZONE 2: PREHEAT ZONE
T rises from Tu to Tad
Thickness delta_L
Reactions negligible"] B -->|Sharp rise| C["ZONE 3: REACTION ZONE
Thin: delta_R approx delta_L/beta
Maximum heat release
Y_F decreases rapidly"] C -->|Decay| D["ZONE 4: POST-FLAME EQUILIBRIUM
T equals Tad constant
Y_F equals zero
Fully burned products"] D --> E["THERMODYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM
Composition fixed
No further change"] ``` **Description of zones:** - **Zone 1 — Unburned Gas:** Reactants at $T_u$ flowing toward the flame front. No chemical reaction occurs here. - **Zone 2 — Preheat Zone:** Heat conducted backward from the reaction zone raises the gas temperature. The thickness is $\delta_L = \alpha/S_L$. Fuel and oxidizer are essentially unreacted. - **Zone 3 — Reaction Zone:** The temperature is close to $T_{ad}$. The reaction rate $\dot{\omega}_F$ reaches its maximum and fuel is consumed. The thickness is $\delta_R \sim \delta_L/\beta$ — much smaller than $\delta_L$ because $\beta \gg 1$. - **Zone 4 — Post-Flame Equilibrium:** Combustion products at $T_{ad}$. Thermodynamic equilibrium is reached; no further reaction. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Flow of the Three Theories ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Premixed Flame Propagation Problem"] --> Q1["Dominant transport mode?"] Q1 -->|Heat conduction| T1["THERMAL THEORY
Mallard and Le Chatelier 1881
S_L squared equals 2 alpha over tau_c"] Q1 -->|Mass diffusion| T2["DIFFUSION THEORY
Tannery and Jost 1929
S_L squared equals 2 alpha Le over tau_c"] Q1 -->|Both combined| T3["COMPREHENSIVE THEORY
Zeldovich Frank-Kamenetskii 1938
S_L squared equals 2 alpha beta over tau_c times Le minus 1 plus beta"] T1 --> CHECK["Compare predictions to experiments"] T2 --> CHECK T3 --> CHECK CHECK --> OUT["Modern asymptotic theory
Large beta limit
Le != 1 corrections"] T1 -.->|Limit Le equals 1| T3 T2 -.->|Limit beta over Le dominant| T3 ``` **Logical flow notes:** - The **thermal theory** is the limit of the comprehensive theory when $Le = 1$. - The **diffusion theory** is the limit when mass diffusion dominates the heat release transport (relevant for very lean flames of large Lewis-number fuels, though this limit is rarely physical). - The **comprehensive theory** unifies both and is the most general framework used in modern combustion analysis. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Flame Coordinate System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Flame_Frame["Flame-Attached Frame"] X1["x is negative infinity
UNBURNED
T equals Tu, u equals S_L"] --> X2["x equals zero
Reference plane
inside reaction zone"] --> X3["x is positive infinity
BURNED
T equals Tad, u equals S_L times rho_u over rho_b"] end subgraph Lab_Frame["Lab-Attached Frame"] L1["Unburned gas stationary"] --> L2["Flame front propagates
at velocity S_L times rho_b over rho_u"] --> L3["Burned gas moves away"] end Flame_Frame ---|"Continuity:
rho times u is constant"| Lab_Frame ``` This diagram shows the two reference frames: - **Flame-attached frame:** The flame is stationary; unburned gas flows in at $u = S_L$ and burned gas flows out at $u = S_L \rho_u/\rho_b < S_L$. - **Lab-attached frame:** The unburned gas is stationary, and the flame front propagates forward at velocity $S_L \rho_b/\rho_u$. The mass flux $\rho u$ is **the same in both frames** by continuity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **Define the term "laminar burning velocity" of a premixed flame. How is it different from the flame speed observed in a laboratory experiment?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Definition:** The laminar burning velocity, $S_L$, is the velocity at which a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, stationary flame propagates **perpendicular to its surface and relative to the unburned gas mixture**. - **(1 mark) Formula:** It is defined as $S_L = \dot{m}/\rho_u$, where $\dot{m}$ is the mass burning flux and $\rho_u$ is the unburned gas density. - **(1 mark) Difference from observed flame speed:** In a real experiment, the observed flame speed in the lab frame is $S_{lab} = S_L \cdot (\rho_u/\rho_b)$ due to thermal expansion of the burned gases (continuity). It also depends on flame geometry (spherical, cylindrical), stretch, and curvature — none of which affect $S_L$. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **What is the Zeldovich number? State its physical significance in the theory of premixed flame propagation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Definition:** The Zeldovich number is the non-dimensional temperature sensitivity of the reaction rate across the flame: $\beta = E(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R T_{ad}^2)$. - **(1 mark) Significance 1:** For $\beta \gg 1$, the chemical reaction is confined to a thin reaction zone, which justifies the **asymptotic (large-$\beta$) analysis** used in deriving the burning velocity. - **(1 mark) Significance 2:** It directly enters the comprehensive-theory burning velocity formula as a multiplicative factor: $S_L^2 = (2\alpha/\tau_c) \cdot \beta/(Le - 1 + \beta)$. For typical hydrocarbon–air flames, $\beta \approx 8$–$12$, and the reaction zone thickness is $\sim \delta_L/\beta$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Premixed Flame Theory Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $S_L$ with $S_{lab}$:** Many students use $S_L = \rho_b S_{lab}/\rho_u$ incorrectly. The correct relationship is $S_{lab} = (\rho_u/\rho_b) S_L$. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The factor of 2 in $S_L^2 = 2\alpha/\tau_c$ comes from the asymptotic matching across the reaction zone. Skipping it loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Mixing $Le$ conventions:** Some books use $Le = \alpha/D$ (thermal/mass), others use $Le^{-1} = \alpha/D$. Be consistent. KTU uses $Le = \alpha/D$. > 4. **Missing boundary conditions:** A full derivation must specify $T(-\infty) = T_u$, $T(+\infty) = T_{ad}$, $Y_F(-\infty) = Y_{F,u}$, $Y_F(+\infty) = 0$. > 5. **Unit errors in $\beta$:** $E$ in J/mol, $R$ in J/(mol·K), $T$ in K. $\beta$ is **dimensionless**. Verify. ### Question B-A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **laminar burning velocity** from the **Thermal Theory of Mallard and Le Chatelier**, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A methane–air mixture has the following properties: $T_u = 300$ K, $T_{ad} = 2225$ K, $E = 180$ kJ/mol, $k_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{8}$ s$^{-1}$, $\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $\lambda = 0.026$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 1100$ J/(kg·K), $\rho_u = 1.16$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the Zeldovich number $\beta$, (ii) the reaction time $\tau_c$, and (iii) the laminar burning velocity $S_L$ using the thermal theory. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-A(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark) — Set up the steady 1-D conservation equations:** For a 1-D steady flame in flame-attached coordinates: - Continuity: $\rho u = \rho_u S_L = \dot{m} = $ const. - Energy: $\dot{m} c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\lambda \dfrac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{\omega}_F q$ - Species: $\dot{m} \dfrac{dY_F}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\rho D \dfrac{dY_F}{dx}\right) - \dot{\omega}_F$ **Step 2 (1 mark) — Assumptions of the Thermal Theory:** - Heat conduction is the **only** transport mechanism that drives propagation (mass diffusion is neglected). - $\lambda, c_p, \rho$ are **constant** across the flame (cold-value approximation). - The reaction zone is **thin** and located at $T = T_{ad}$ (high-$\beta$ asymptotic limit). - A single global one-step reaction $F + \nu O \to P$ is assumed. **Step 3 (1 mark) — Preheat zone (outer) solution:** In the preheat zone, $\dot{\omega}_F \approx 0$: $$\lambda \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = 0$$ The solution satisfying $T(-\infty) = T_u$ and boundedness is: $$T(x) = T_u + (T_{ad} - T_u) \exp\left(\frac{\dot{m} c_p x}{\lambda}\right)$$ Flame thickness: $\delta_L = \lambda/(\rho_u c_p S_L) = \alpha/S_L$. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Reaction zone integral:** Integrate the energy equation across the thin reaction zone of thickness $\delta_R \ll \delta_L$: $$\dot{m} c_p (T_{ad} - T) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} \dot{\omega}_F q\, dx'$$ **Step 5 (2 marks) — Match inner and outer solutions and obtain $S_L$:** In the reaction zone, using the high-$\beta$ approximation $\exp(-E/RT) \approx \exp(-E/RT_{ad}) \exp[\beta(1-\theta)]$ and integrating with the matched boundary condition $T(-\delta_R) \approx T_u$ at the upstream edge: $$S_L^2 = \frac{2 \lambda k_0 e^{-E/RT_{ad}}}{\rho_u^2 c_p^2} = \frac{2 \alpha k_0 e^{-E/RT_{ad}}}{}$$ This can also be written as $S_L = \sqrt{2\alpha/\tau_c}$ where $\tau_c = 1/[k_0 e^{-E/RT_{ad}}]$. **Step 6 (1 mark) — Physical interpretation:** The factor 2 arises from the asymptotic matching of the inner (reaction) and outer (preheat) solutions. Physically, it reflects the fact that the flame thickness is half the integral of the heat-release profile. --- #### Model Solution to B-A(b) — 7 marks **Part (i) — Zeldovich number (2 marks):** $$\beta = \frac{E(T_{ad} - T_u)}{R T_{ad}^2}$$ Substituting the values: $$\beta = \frac{180{,}000 \times (2225 - 300)}{8.314 \times (2225)^2}$$ $$\beta = \frac{180{,}000 \times 1925}{8.314 \times 4{,}950{,}625}$$ $$\beta = \frac{3.465 \times 10^{8}}{4.116 \times 10^{7}} \approx 8.42$$ **Answer: $\beta \approx 8.42$** (dimensionless, justifying large-$\beta$ asymptotics). ✓ **Part (ii) — Reaction time (2 marks):** $$\tau_c = \frac{1}{k_0 e^{-E/(R T_{ad})}}$$ Compute the exponent: $$\frac{E}{R T_{ad}} = \frac{180{,}000}{8.314 \times 2225} = \frac{180{,}000}{18{,}499} \approx 9.73$$ Therefore: $$e^{-9.73} \approx 5.91 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\tau_c = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{8} \times 5.91 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{8865} \approx 1.13 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$ **Answer: $\tau_c \approx 1.13 \times 10^{-4}$ s = 113 $\mu$s.** **Part (iii) — Laminar burning velocity (3 marks):** $$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c}}$$ $$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.13 \times 10^{-4}}}$$ $$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{4.4 \times 10^{-5}}{1.13 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{0.389} \approx 0.624 \text{ m/s}$$ **Answer: $S_L \approx 0.62$ m/s** — consistent with the experimentally observed laminar burning velocity of methane–air mixtures ($\sim 0.4$ m/s for stoichiometric, with the simple one-step model over-predicting as expected). **Flame thickness check (bonus 1 mark):** $$\delta_L = \frac{\alpha}{S_L} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.624} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 35 \text{ μm}$$ (This is the preheat zone thickness, and is consistent with order-of-magnitude expectations.) --- ### Question B-B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Compare the **Thermal Theory, Diffusion Theory, and Comprehensive Theory** of premixed flame propagation. State the key assumption of each and the resulting expression for the laminar burning velocity. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For a propane–air flame, $Le = 1.85$ and $\beta = 9.5$. The thermal diffusivity is $\alpha = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and the chemical time at $T_{ad}$ is $\tau_c = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s. Using the **comprehensive theory**, calculate $S_L$ and comment on whether the **diffusion theory alone** would over- or under-predict it. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-B(a) — 7 marks | Aspect | Thermal Theory | Diffusion Theory | Comprehensive Theory | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Year / Origin** | Mallard–Le Chatelier, 1881 | Tannery & Jost, 1929 | Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii, 1938 | | **Dominant transport** | Heat conduction only | Mass diffusion only | Both, with Lewis number $Le$ | | **Key assumption** | Radicals play no role; heat preheats the next layer | Heat conduction is neglected; active species diffuse to ignite | Both transport modes act simultaneously; $\beta \gg 1$ | | **$S_L$ expression** | $\sqrt{2\alpha/\tau_c}$ | $\sqrt{2\alpha \cdot Le/\tau_c}$ | $\sqrt{(2\alpha/\tau_c) \cdot \beta/(Le-1+\beta)}$ | | **Validity** | $Le \approx 1$ flames | Unphysical limit; $Le \to \infty$ | General, all flames | **(1 mark per row, 5 rows = 5 marks;** plus **2 marks** for correctly writing out the three $S_L$ formulas and the unification principle that the comprehensive theory reduces to the thermal theory when $Le = 1$). **Unification principle (2 marks):** The comprehensive theory **contains** both special theories as limiting cases: - $Le = 1$: $\beta/(Le - 1 + \beta) = \beta/\beta = 1$, so comprehensive $\to$ thermal. - $\beta \gg (Le - 1)$: $\beta/(Le - 1 + \beta) \to 1$, so comprehensive $\to$ thermal regardless of $Le$. - $Le \to \infty$ (with $\beta$ fixed): $\beta/(Le - 1 + \beta) \to 0$, but the diffusion limit has $S_L^2 \sim Le$, requiring a different formulation. --- #### Model Solution to B-B(b) — 7 marks **Comprehensive theory calculation (4 marks):** $$S_L^2 = \frac{2 \alpha}{\tau_c} \cdot \frac{\beta}{Le - 1 + \beta}$$ Substituting the values: $$S_L^2 = \frac{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot \frac{9.5}{1.85 - 1 + 9.5}$$ $$S_L^2 = 0.6 \cdot \frac{9.5}{10.35}$$ $$S_L^2 = 0.6 \times 0.918 = 0.551 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$S_L = \sqrt{0.551} \approx 0.742 \text{ m/s}$$ **Answer: $S_L \approx 0.74$ m/s.** **Diffusion theory prediction (2 marks):** The pure diffusion theory would predict (if it were the only mechanism): $$S_{L,diff}^2 = \frac{2 \alpha \cdot Le}{\tau_c} = \frac{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.85}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.11 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$S_{L,diff} = \sqrt{1.11} \approx 1.054 \text{ m/s}$$ **Comparison and comment (1 mark):** $$S_{L,diff}/S_{L,comp} = 1.054/0.742 \approx 1.42$$ The pure diffusion theory **over-predicts** $S_L$ by a factor of $\sim 1.4$ for propane–air ($Le = 1.85$). This is because the diffusion theory neglects the **thermal losses** that would slow the flame when $Le > 1$ (heat conduction is faster than species diffusion, so heat can preheat the unburned gas efficiently, but the radicals diffuse relatively slowly and the flame cannot propagate purely by diffusion at a higher rate). The comprehensive theory correctly accounts for both effects. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Premixed Flame Theories** - **Laminar burning velocity $S_L$** is the *fundamental* property of a premixed flame: it is the velocity of the flame front **relative to the unburned gas**, in 1-D, planar, adiabatic, steady conditions. - **Three transport modes** in flame propagation: **conduction** (heat), **diffusion** (mass), **convection** (bulk flow). The first two are the dominant molecular-scale mechanisms; the third is the macroscopic gas motion. - **Thermal Theory (MLC, 1881):** Only heat conduction matters. $S_L = \sqrt{2\alpha/\tau_c}$. Valid for $Le \approx 1$. - **Diffusion Theory (TJ, 1929):** Only mass diffusion matters. $S_L = \sqrt{2\alpha \cdot Le/\tau_c}$. Rarely physical on its own. - **Comprehensive Theory (ZFKS, 1938):** Both mechanisms. $S_L = \sqrt{(2\alpha/\tau_c) \cdot \beta/(Le - 1 + \beta)}$. **The general formula.** - **Zeldovich number $\beta = E(T_{ad} - T_u)/(R T_{ad}^2)$** is the **non-dimensional activation energy** across the flame. It governs the **thickness ratio** of reaction zone to preheat zone: $\delta_R/\delta_L \sim 1/\beta$. - **Lewis number $Le = \alpha/D$** governs **which transport mechanism dominates**: - $Le > 1$: heat conduction faster — **thermal-promoted** flame. - $Le < 1$: mass diffusion faster — **diffusion-promoted** flame (e.g., hydrogen–air, $S_L$ is high). - $Le = 1$: balanced — comprehensive theory reduces to thermal theory. - **Flame thickness $\delta_L = \alpha/S_L$** is the preheat-zone length scale, typically **0.1–1 mm** for hydrocarbon–air flames. - **Damköhler number $Da \gg 1$** for self-sustaining flames — chemistry is fast compared to residence time. - **Mass burning flux $\dot{m} = \rho_u S_L$** is **conserved** across the flame (continuity) — this is the fundamental invariant. - **High-$\beta$ asymptotic theory** is the rigorous modern foundation: $\beta \to \infty$ is the mathematical limit that justifies a thin reaction zone and clean matching. - **Common error to avoid:** $S_L$ is **not** the same as the observed flame speed in a Bunsen burner or a spherical flame — those are affected by geometry, stretch, and thermal expansion. - **Rule of thumb:** Stoichiometric hydrocarbon–air flames: $S_L \approx 0.4$–$0.5$ m/s. Hydrogen–air: $S_L \approx 2.5$–$3.0$ m/s. Methane–air: $S_L \approx 0.4$ m/s. - **Units of every quantity:** $E$ [J/mol], $R$ [J/(mol·K)], $T$ [K], $\alpha$ [m²/s], $\lambda$ [W/(m·K)], $c_p$ [J/(kg·K)], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $S_L$ [m/s]. Always check. - **KTI pitfall:** The factor of **2** in $S_L^2 = 2\alpha/\tau_c$ comes from asymptotic matching — **do not omit it** in derivations.

topics.title: Laminar Flame Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar Flame Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laminar Flame Speed** (also called *Laminar Burning Velocity*), denoted as $S_L$ or $S_u$, is defined as the magnitude of the velocity vector of the unburned gas mixture measured normal to a stationary, planar, adiabatic, one-dimensional, freely-propagating premixed flame surface.

In simpler combustion terminology (Turns, Glassman, Kuo framework):

$$S_L = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_u} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass burning flux per unit area [kg/(m²·s)]
- $\rho_u$ = density of the unburned gas mixture [kg/m³]
- The subscript $u$ denotes the **unburned** state
- The subscript $b$ denotes the **burned** state

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Distinction**
> Students frequently confuse **Laminar Flame Speed ($S_L$)** with **Flame Speed** (apparent velocity of a wrinkled/turbulent flame). In Module 3, we strictly deal with the **laminar** case — a smooth, planar, undisturbed flame front. Turbulent effects are introduced in higher modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Reference Frames in Flame Kinematics**
> 1. **Lab frame (fixed observer):** Total flame speed $S_{total}$
> 2. **Burned-gas frame:** Velocity of burned gas = $S_L \cdot (\rho_u / \rho_b)$
> 3. **Unburned-gas frame:** This is what we call $S_L$ — the velocity the unburned gas approaches the flame.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Burning Conveyer Belt"

Imagine a long conveyor belt carrying a thin layer of dry matchstick tips. As the belt moves, only one tip at the leading edge is being lit. The flame slowly *walks* along the belt as each new tip catches fire from its neighbor. The **flame speed** is the rate at which this ignition front creeps forward relative to the *unburned matchstick line*.

In a real combustion system, the *matchstick tips* are reactive fuel-oxidizer molecules, the *conveyor belt speed* is the incoming gas velocity (which must exactly equal $S_L$ for a stationary flame), and the *walking igniter* is the combined effect of **heat conduction** from the hot burned zone and **radical diffusion** (H, OH, O) from the reaction zone back into the fresh mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two physical "carriers" of flame propagation are:
> 1. **Thermal conduction** (heat diffusing from hot → cold gas)
> 2. **Mass diffusion** of active chain carriers (free radicals)
>
> These are quantified by the **Lewis Number** $Le = \alpha / D$, where $\alpha$ is thermal diffusivity and $D$ is mass diffusivity.

## 1.3 Why $S_L$ Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Relevance of $S_L$ |
|------------------|---------------------|
| **Spark-Ignition Engines** | Determines knock tendency, flame kernel growth |
| **Gas Turbines** | Governs flashback and blow-off limits |
| **Fire Safety** | Predicts flame spread rate over solid/liquid fuels |
| **Industrial Burners** | Designs stable, efficient premixed combustors |
| **Detonation Physics** | Chapman-Jouguet velocity depends on $S_L$ via deflagration-to-detonation transition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Magnitudes:** For methane-air at standard conditions ($\phi = 1.0$, $T_u = 298$ K), $S_L \approx 0.38$ m/s. For hydrogen-air at $\phi = 1.0$, $S_L \approx 2.5$ m/s. Hydrocarbon-air mixtures generally fall in the range **0.3 – 0.8 m/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The One-Dimensional Flame Structure

A 1-D adiabatic, planar, steady flame has three canonical zones:

1. **Pre-heat Zone (0 < x < x₁):** Conductive heating of fresh mixture, no significant reaction.
2. **Reaction Zone (x₁ < x < x₂):** Sharp exothermic reaction, strong radical gradients.
3. **Post-flame Zone (x > x₂):** Burned products in thermodynamic equilibrium.

The flame thickness $\delta$ is of order:
$$\delta \sim \frac{\alpha}{S_L} = \frac{\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L}$$

For methane-air: $\delta \sim 0.5$ mm. For very slow flames (lean mixtures): $\delta$ can reach several mm.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Theories of Flame Propagation

### A. Mallard-Le Chatelier Thermal Theory (1883)

> **Core Postulate:** Flame speed is controlled by the rate at which heat conducts forward to raise adjacent unburned gas to ignition temperature.

This treats reaction kinetics as an *inertial* switch — once gas reaches $T_i$ (ignition temperature), reaction is instantaneous.

### B. Diffusion Theory — Tanney & Jost (1930)

> **Core Postulate:** Flame speed is controlled by diffusion of chain carriers (radicals) from reaction zone into fresh mixture.

This was the *competition* theory to Mallard-Le Chatelier for decades.

### C. Comprehensive (Activation-Energy-Asymptotic) Theory — Zeldovich & Frank-Kamenetskii (1938), refined by Bush & Fendell

> **Core Postulate:** Both heat conduction AND species diffusion matter, with a finite-rate Arrhenius reaction of large activation energy.

This is the modern accepted theory, recovering Mallard-Le Chatelier and Tanney-Jost as asymptotic limits.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table.** Every $S_L$ derivation in KTU ESE and internal assessment maps back to one of these formulas.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Reference |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $S_L = \dot{m}/\rho_u$ | Mass-flux definition of flame speed | Universal |
| 2 | $S_L \rho_u = S_b \rho_b$ (continuity) | Burned-gas velocity $S_b = S_L (\rho_u/\rho_b)$ | Conservation of mass |
| 3 | $\delta \sim \alpha/S_L$ | Flame thickness scaling | Heat conduction |
| 4 | $Pe = S_L \delta / \alpha \sim \mathcal{O}(1)$ | **Peclet number** — advection $\approx$ diffusion | Order-of-magnitude |
| 5 | $Le = \alpha/D$ | Lewis number — thermal vs. mass diffusivity | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\tau_{ign}}}$ | Mallard-Le Chatelier (with $\tau_{ign}$ = induction time) | Thermal theory |
| 7 | $S_L^2 \sim \dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u^2 c_p} \cdot \dfrac{(T_b - T_u)}{T_u - T_i}$ | Damköhler's first similarity result | Damköhler (1940) |
| 8 | $\rho_u S_L Y_{O,u} = \rho_b D_{O,b} \left.\dfrac{dY_O}{dx}\right\vert_b$ | Mass-flux balance of oxidizer | Species conservation |
| 9 | $S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \beta^{-1} \cdot Le^{-1} \cdot k \exp(-E_a/2RT_b)$ | Zeldovich-Frank-Kamenetskii (high $E_a$ limit) | ZFK asymptotic theory |
| 10 | $S_L \propto p^{-(n/2)}$ where $n$ is overall reaction order | Pressure dependence (typically $n \approx 1.5 - 2$) | Empirical/theoretical |
| 11 | $S_L \propto T_u^{m}$ where $m \approx 1.5 - 2.0$ | Unburned-temperature dependence (Metghalchi-Keck) | Empirical correlation |
| 12 | $S_L^{\text{peak}} = f(\phi)$ with peak near $\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.10$ for HC | Equivalence-ratio dependence | Experimental correlation |
| 13 | $\beta = \dfrac{E_a(T_b - T_u)}{R T_b^2}$ | Zeldovich number — dimensionless activation energy | ZFK theory |
| 14 | $Ma = \dfrac{S_L}{c}$ (acoustic Mach number for flame) | Acoustic coupling to flame | Thermoacoustic instabilities |

> **Units Note (KTU expects SI everywhere):** $S_L$ in **m/s**, $\lambda$ in W/(m·K), $\rho$ in kg/m³, $c_p$ in J/(kg·K), $E_a$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Markstein length** $L_M$ quantifies how a flame responds to curvature/stretch:

$$L_M = -\dfrac{1}{S_L}\left.\dfrac{\partial S_L}{\partial(\kappa)}\right\vert_{\kappa=0}$$

where $\kappa$ is flame stretch rate. $L_M > 0$ → **thermally thick** (preferential-diffusion stable); $L_M < 0$ → **thermally thin** (lean H₂, lean CO) → preferential-diffusion unstable, leads to **cellular flames** and **self-acceleration**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **Damköhler's first similarity rule**: *flames geometrically similar have the same $S_L$* when non-dimensionalized by thermal diffusivity. This is why **scaling laws** (small lab flames to full industrial burners) work for premixed combustion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Zeldovich–Frank–Kamenetskii (ZFK) Laminar Flame Speed

This is the **flagship derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions on $S_L$.

### Step 1: Governing Equations (1-D, steady, adiabatic, planar)

**Conservation of mass (continuity):**
$$\rho u = \rho_u S_L = \dot{m} = \text{const}$$

**Energy equation** (neglecting radiative heat transfer, pressure work, viscous dissipation):
$$\rho u c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\lambda \dfrac{dT}{dx}\right) + q \, \omega$$

**Species equation** for the limiting reactant (fuel or oxidizer):
$$\rho u \dfrac{dY_k}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\rho D_k \dfrac{dY_k}{dx}\right) - \omega_k$$

**State and thermodynamic closure:**
$$p = \rho \mathcal{R} T / W_{mix}, \quad \lambda = \text{const}, \quad \rho D = \text{const}$$

Here, $\omega$ is the volumetric reaction rate [kg/(m³·s)], and $q$ is the heat of reaction per kg of mixture.

> We use a **single-step irreversible reaction** of the form $F + \nu O \to P$ with Arrhenius kinetics.

### Step 2: Introduce the Mass-Flux Variable

Define $G = \rho u = \rho_u S_L$ (constant). Then:

$$G c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + q \, \omega \tag{1}$$

$$G \dfrac{dY}{dx} = \rho D \dfrac{d^2 Y}{dx^2} - \omega \tag{2}$$

### Step 3: Linear Combination → Shvab–Zeldovich Coupling Function

Multiply Equation (2) by $q$ and add to Equation (1):

$$G c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} + q G \dfrac{dY}{dx} = \lambda \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + q \rho D \dfrac{d^2 Y}{dx^2}$$

For the special case $\lambda / c_p = \rho D$ (i.e., $Le = 1$):

$$G c_p \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(T + \dfrac{q}{c_p} Y\right) = \lambda \dfrac{d^2}{dx^2}\left(T + \dfrac{q}{c_p} Y\right)$$

The right-hand side is $\frac{d^2}{dx^2}(\cdots)$, and the left is $\frac{d}{dx}(\cdots)$ — this is a **diffusion equation** in the form $G c_p \theta' = \lambda \theta''$ where $\theta = T + (q/c_p)Y$.

> **Boundary conditions for a flame fixed in lab frame:**
> - $x \to -\infty$ (far upstream, unburned): $T \to T_u$, $Y \to Y_u$
> - $x \to +\infty$ (far downstream, burned): $T \to T_b$, $Y \to Y_b$
> - Note: $Y_b = 0$ for the limiting reactant (fully consumed)

### Step 4: Integrate Once Across the Flame

Integrating from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, the $\theta'$ terms vanish at both limits (because $\theta$ reaches asymptotes):

$$0 = \lambda \left[\theta'(+\infty) - \theta'(-\infty)\right]$$

This means $\theta'(+\infty) = \theta'(-\infty) = 0$ (no flux at infinity). So the linear combination is invariant — a constant:

$$T + \dfrac{q}{c_p} Y = T_u + \dfrac{q}{c_p} Y_u = T_b + \dfrac{q}{c_p} \cdot 0 = T_b$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = \dfrac{c_p (T_b - T)}{q} \tag{3}$$

> **This is the famous linear $T$–$Y$ coupling for $Le=1$.** It tells us that *temperature and species profiles are linear functions of each other* across the flame when Lewis number is unity.

### Step 5: Eliminate $Y$ in the Energy Equation

Differentiating (3):
$$\dfrac{dY}{dx} = -\dfrac{c_p}{q} \dfrac{dT}{dx}, \quad \dfrac{d^2 Y}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{c_p}{q} \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

Substitute back into the energy equation (1):

$$G c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + q \, \omega \tag{1}$$

And substitute into the species equation to obtain $\omega$:

$$\omega = G \dfrac{c_p}{q} \dfrac{dT}{dx} + \rho D \dfrac{c_p}{q} \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

This is the **Shvab–Zeldovich reduction** — both equations collapse to one for $T$ alone.

### Step 6: High-Activation-Energy Asymptotics

Assume **large Zeldovich number** $\beta = \dfrac{E_a (T_b - T_u)}{R T_b^2} \gg 1$.

This means the reaction is sharply confined to a thin zone near $T \approx T_b$. Most of the flame is *preheat* (conductive warming). The reaction rate is essentially:

$$\omega \approx \begin{cases} 0, & T < T_b - \mathcal{O}(T_b/\beta) \\ \text{very large}, & T \approx T_b \end{cases}$$

The flame becomes a **step** in the energy equation: nearly all heat is released in a vanishingly thin reaction sheet.

### Step 7: Solve the Pre-Heat Zone (x < x_ign)

In the preheat zone, $\omega \approx 0$, so Equation (1) becomes:

$$G c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

This is a linear ODE with solution:

$$T(x) = T_u + (T_b - T_u) e^{G c_p x / \lambda} \quad \text{for } x < x_{ign}$$

The **e-folding length** of the preheat zone is:

$$\delta_p = \dfrac{\lambda}{G c_p} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L} = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}$$

This confirms our earlier scaling: $\delta \sim \alpha/S_L$.

### Step 8: Global Energy Balance Across the Reaction Sheet

Integrate Equation (1) across the entire flame from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:

$$G c_p \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dfrac{dT}{dx} dx = \lambda \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} dx + q \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \omega \, dx$$

$$G c_p (T_b - T_u) = \lambda [\theta'(+\infty) - \theta'(-\infty)] + q \dot{m}_F$$

But $\theta' \to 0$ at both ends, and $\int \omega \, dx = G (Y_u - Y_b) = G Y_u$ (mass of fuel consumed per unit area per unit time). So:

$$G c_p (T_b - T_u) = q G Y_u$$

This is just the enthalpy balance defining $T_b$ — **not** an equation for $G$ yet! We need a *detailed* analysis of the reaction zone.

### Step 9: Reaction Zone Analysis (Zeldovich Number Asymptotics)

In the thin reaction zone near $T_b$, the temperature variation is small: $T = T_b - \mathcal{O}(T_b/\beta)$. Set $\theta = T_b - T$, so $\theta$ is small.

Rescale the energy equation using $\theta$:

$$G c_p \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} + \lambda \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} + q \omega = 0$$

With Arrhenius kinetics: $\omega = A \rho^2 Y_F Y_O e^{-E_a/RT}$.

The dominant balance in the reaction sheet (large $E_a$) is:

$$q \omega \sim -\lambda \dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2}$$

After detailed matched-asymptotic analysis (Zeldovich-Frank-Kamenetskii, 1938), the **jump in temperature gradient** across the reaction sheet is:

$$\left[\lambda \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\right]_{before}^{after} = -2 \lambda \dfrac{G c_p}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta}$$

This produces the classical ZFK result:

$$S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \lambda \beta^{-1}}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \dfrac{k(T_b) \cdot W_F}{\nu_O} \tag{4}$$

Or, in a more common engineering form:

$$\boxed{\; S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{Le} \cdot k_{eff} \, e^{-E_a / 2 R T_b} \;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The $E_a/2$ in the exponent** (rather than $E_a$) is the signature of the ZFK theory — the chemical timescale is *halved* by the temperature rise through the flame, a consequence of large-activation-energy asymptotics.

### Step 10: Specialization to Le = 1 and Stoichiometric Methane-Air

For $CH_4$ + 2 $O_2$ → $CO_2$ + 2 $H_2O$ with $T_u = 298$ K, $\phi = 1$:

- $T_b \approx 2222$ K (adiabatic flame temperature)
- $E_a \approx 200$ kJ/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $\beta = (200{,}000)(1924)/(8.314 \cdot 2222^2) \approx 9.4$
- $\lambda / \rho_u c_p = \alpha \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

Plugging into Equation (4) yields $S_L \sim 0.35 - 0.40$ m/s — in **excellent agreement** with the experimental value of 0.38 m/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Mallard–Le Chatelier Thermal Theory (Simplified)

This derivation appears almost every KTU cycle as a Part-A (3-mark) or sub-part of a 14-mark question.

### Step 1: Setup

A 1-D steady flame with mass flux $G = \rho_u S_L$. In the **preheat zone**, the energy equation with no reaction is:

$$G c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

With boundary conditions $T(-\infty) = T_u$ and $T(0) = T_i$ (ignition temperature at $x = 0$), the solution is:

$$T(x) = T_u + (T_i - T_u) e^{G c_p x / \lambda} \quad \text{for } x < 0$$

The temperature gradient at $x = 0^-$ (just before the reaction zone) is:

$$\left.\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right\vert_{0^-} = \dfrac{G c_p (T_i - T_u)}{\lambda}$$

### Step 2: Heat Flux into the Pre-Heat Zone

$$q_{cond} = -\lambda \left.\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right\vert_{0^-} = -G c_p (T_i - T_u)$$

The negative sign indicates heat flows *backward* into the unburned gas (because $dT/dx > 0$ upstream of the reaction zone — wait, recheck direction).

Actually, $T$ increases as $x$ approaches the reaction zone, so $dT/dx > 0$ for $x < 0$, meaning $\theta = T$ profile is rising toward $T_b$. Conduction flux is $-\lambda dT/dx < 0$ — meaning heat flows in the $-x$ direction, *back* into the fresh gas. This is what heats the unburned mixture. 

The *magnitude* of conductive heat flux into the pre-heat zone is:

$$q_{cond} = \lambda \dfrac{G c_p (T_i - T_u)}{\lambda} = G c_p (T_i - T_u) \quad \text{[W/m²]}$$

### Step 3: Energy Needed to Ignite the Next Slab

In time $dt$, a slab of thickness $S_L dt$ is consumed. Its mass per unit area is $\rho_u S_L dt$. The energy needed to raise it from $T_u$ to $T_i$ is:

$$dQ = \rho_u S_L c_p (T_i - T_u) \, dt$$

### Step 4: Equate Conduction Supply to Ignition Requirement

$$G c_p (T_i - T_u) = \rho_u S_L c_p (T_i - T_u) \cdot \text{(energy per unit length per unit time absorbed)}$$

The rigorous closure introduces the *induction time* $\tau_{ign}$ — the time required for reaction kinetics to consume the slab once it reaches $T_i$. The **Damköhler closure** gives:

$$\boxed{\; S_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\tau_{ign}(T_i)}} \;}$$

Or, in terms of the **ignition delay** (chemical timescale at $T_i$):

$$S_L \sim \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{\tau_{chem}}}$$

> **Physical meaning:** $S_L$ is the *geometric mean* of thermal diffusivity and chemical reaction rate — a beautiful consequence of the competition between heat diffusion and chemistry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Damköhler's First Similarity Rule

**Statement:** Geometrically similar premixed flames have the *same* non-dimensional flame speed $S_L \cdot \delta / \alpha = \mathcal{O}(1)$.

**Proof:** Non-dimensionalize $x$ by $\delta$ and $T$ by $(T_b - T_u)$:

$$x = \delta \tilde{x}, \quad T - T_u = (T_b - T_u) \tilde{\theta}$$

The energy equation becomes:

$$\rho u c_p \dfrac{(T_b - T_u)}{\delta} \dfrac{d\tilde{\theta}}{d\tilde{x}} = \lambda \dfrac{(T_b - T_u)}{\delta^2} \dfrac{d^2 \tilde{\theta}}{d\tilde{x}^2} + q\omega$$

$$\dfrac{\rho u c_p \delta}{\lambda} \dfrac{d\tilde{\theta}}{d\tilde{x}} = \dfrac{d^2 \tilde{\theta}}{d\tilde{x}^2} + \dfrac{q \omega \delta^2}{\lambda (T_b - T_u)}$$

Setting $\rho u c_p \delta / \lambda = \mathcal{O}(1)$ gives $\delta \sim \alpha / S_L$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{S_L \delta}{\alpha} = \mathcal{O}(1) \quad \text{or} \quad S_L \delta / \alpha \sim 1$$

This is the **Peclet-number unity result**: in a propagating premixed flame, advection and diffusion across the flame thickness are of the same order.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Pressure Dependence of $S_L$

From the ZFK formula, $S_L^2 \propto k_{eff} \propto p^{n-1}$ (for an $n$-th order reaction), giving:

$$S_L \propto p^{(n-1)/2} = p^{m/2}$$

For hydrocarbon-air, $n \approx 1.7 - 2.0$, hence $S_L \propto p^{-0.15}$ to $p^{-0.25}$ — a *weak decrease* with pressure.

**Empirical Metghalchi-Keck correlation (KTU favorite):**
$$S_L = S_{L,ref} \left(\dfrac{T_u}{T_{u,ref}}\right)^m \left(\dfrac{p}{p_{ref}}\right)^{n}$$

with $m \approx 1.5 - 2.0$, $n \approx -0.2$ for methane.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Equivalence-Ratio Dependence

Experimentally (and confirmed by detailed chemistry), $S_L(\phi)$ peaks slightly rich for hydrocarbons:

$$\phi_{\text{peak}} \approx 1.05 - 1.10 \text{ for } C_1 - C_3 \text{ hydrocarbons}$$

A useful fitting function (KTU expects familiarity):
$$S_L(\phi) = S_L^{max} \cdot \left[1 - a \left(\ln \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_{\text{peak}}}\right)^2\right]$$

where $a \approx 0.45 - 0.55$ is fitted from data. This captures the **bell-shaped** profile centered slightly rich of stoichiometric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why peak is rich of $\phi = 1$?**
> - HC-air flames are **fuel-side kinetically limited** (fuel pyrolysis, H-atom abstraction)
> - The temperature is also slightly higher rich of $\phi = 1$ for lean HC-air because $C_p$ of products is lower
> - H₂-air has peak *exactly* at $\phi = 1.0$ (different kinetics — diffusion-limited)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Influence Parameters on $S_L$

| Parameter | Effect on $S_L$ | Reason |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|
| $\phi$ ↑ from lean to ~1.1 | $S_L$ ↑ strongly | Higher $T_b$ + faster kinetics |
| $\phi$ ↑ beyond ~1.1 | $S_L$ ↓ | Excess fuel acts as diluent |
| $T_u$ ↑ | $S_L$ ↑ strongly | $k \uparrow \uparrow$ via Arrhenius; $T_b$ also ↑ |
| $p$ ↑ | $S_L$ ↓ (weakly) | 3-body recombination, lower $T_b$ |
| $E_a$ ↑ | $S_L$ ↓ | Reaction more temperature-sensitive |
| $D$ ↑ (mass diffusivity) | $S_L$ ↑ | Faster radical transport |
| $Le$ ↑ | $S_L$ ↑ in lean HC | Less conductive losses relative to mass diffusion |
| Diluent (N₂, CO₂) | $S_L$ ↓ | Reduces $T_b$ (heat capacity effect) |
| Steam dilution | $S_L$ ↓↓ strongly | H₂O has high $c_p$ + 3-body termination |
| Turbulence | $S_T > S_L$ | Wrinkled flame surface area increase |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 1-D Premixed Flame Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *causal flow* of physical processes and governing equations within a 1-D planar premixed flame. It complements the temperature/mass-fraction profiles by showing the **functional coupling** between zones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U0[Unburned Gas Reservoir<br/>T equal to Tu, Y equal to Yu] --> A1[Convective Influx<br/>mass flux G equal to rho_u S_L]
    A1 --> B1[Pre-Heat Zone<br/>0 to x_ign<br/>Energy equation with no reaction<br/>lambda d2T by dx2 minus G cp dT by dx equals 0]
    B1 --> C1[Reaction Zone<br/>x_ign to x_b<br/>Arrhenius kinetics active<br/>omega equals A rho2 YF YO exp negative E by RT]
    C1 --> D1[Post-Flame Zone<br/>x greater than x_b<br/>Burned products equilibrium<br/>T equal to Tb, Y equal to Yb]
    D1 --> E1[Conductive Heat Recycle<br/>lambda dT by dx at x less than 0<br/>feeds back to B1]
    D1 --> E2[Radical Diffusion Recycle<br/>rho D dY by dx at x less than 0<br/>H OH O atoms feed back to B1]
    B1 --> E1
    C1 --> E2
    E1 --> F1[Governing Output<br/>Laminar flame speed S_L]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Mass-Flux Definition<br/>G equal to rho_u S_L]
    G1 --> A1
```

> **How to read this diagram:**
> - **U0 → A1:** The unburned gas approaches at velocity $S_L$.
> - **B1:** Conductive preheating, *no* chemistry — this is where thermal energy is "loaded" into the unburned gas.
> - **C1:** The reaction sheet — site of Arrhenius reaction and radical generation.
> - **D1:** Burned products, with $T = T_b$, $Y = Y_b \approx 0$ for the limiting reactant.
> - **E1 & E2:** The two feedback paths (heat conduction, radical diffusion) that *sustain* flame propagation.
> - **F1:** The output — the magnitude of $S_L$ that self-consistently satisfies the conservation laws.

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — Theories of $S_L$ and Their Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Classical Theories of<br/>Laminar Flame Speed] --> B0[Mallard Le Chatelier 1883<br/>Thermal Conduction Only]
    A0 --> C0[Tanney Jost 1930<br/>Mass Diffusion Only]
    A0 --> D0[Zeldovich Frank Kamenetskii 1938<br/>Combined Thermal plus Diffusion<br/>Large Activation Energy Asymptotics]
    B0 --> E0[Limiting Case Le equals 1<br/>and high Ea]
    C0 --> E0
    D0 --> E0
    D0 --> F0[Modern Predictive Theory<br/>for hydrocarbon and H2 flames]
    E0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Engineering Correlations<br/>Metghalchi Keck<br/>Guenther Else<br/>Law Ozer
    ]
    G0 --> H0[Design of Industrial<br/>Premixed Burners and SI Engines]
```

## 4.3 The Self-Consistent Loop Driving $S_L$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Choose S_L guess] --> B2[Compute delta equal to alpha by S_L]
    B2 --> C2[Solve Energy plus Species<br/>Equation Across Flame]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Heat Release Rate<br/>Q released equals G cp Tb minus Tu]
    D2 --> E2{Check Mass and Energy<br/>Global Balances}
    E2 -- Not converged --> F2[Update S_L<br/>Newton or Shooting Method]
    F2 --> B2
    E2 -- Converged --> G2[Output S_L and Structure]
```

> This iterative structure is what commercial CFD codes (e.g., CHEMKIN-based Cantera, OpenFOAM's `laminarFlameSpeed` utility) implement to compute $S_L$ for user-supplied chemistry and transport.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define laminar flame speed. State any two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

Laminar flame speed $S_L$ is the velocity at which a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, steady premixed flame propagates normal to itself into the unburned gas mixture. Mathematically,

$$S_L = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_u}$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass burning flux per unit area and $\rho_u$ is the unburned-gas density.

**Two factors affecting $S_L$:**

1. **Equivalence ratio $\phi$** — $S_L$ peaks near $\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.10$ for hydrocarbons; it drops sharply on both lean and rich sides due to reduced adiabatic flame temperature $T_b$.

2. **Unburned gas temperature $T_u$** — Raising $T_u$ increases both $T_b$ and the reaction rate $k(T) \propto \exp(-E_a/RT)$, leading to a strong (Metghalchi-Keck exponent $m \approx 1.5 - 2.0$) increase in $S_L$.

> Other valid factors: pressure (weakly inverse), Lewis number, diluent type, fuel chemistry, mass diffusivity of chain carriers.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Damköhler's first similarity rule for premixed flames. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Geometrically similar premixed flames (e.g., two methane-air flames at different pressures but the same equivalence ratio and unburned temperature) have the same non-dimensional flame speed:

$$\dfrac{S_L \delta}{\alpha} = \mathcal{O}(1) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \dfrac{S_L \delta}{\nu} = \text{const}$$

**Physical significance:** It establishes the **Peclet-number unity** condition — in a self-sustaining premixed flame, the rate of *advective* transport of reactants across the reaction zone equals the rate of *diffusive* transport (heat conduction + species diffusion). It enables **scaling laws** — predictions of $S_L$ from small lab flames to full-scale industrial burners — provided the underlying thermo-chemistry is preserved.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the 1-D premixed flame structure, derive the **Zeldovich–Frank–Kamenetskii** expression for the laminar flame speed under the high-activation-energy asymptotic limit. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A methane–air mixture ($\phi = 1.0$, $T_u = 298$ K, $p = 1$ atm) has the following properties:

- Thermal conductivity of unburned gas: $\lambda = 0.026$ W/(m·K)
- Specific heat: $c_p = 1100$ J/(kg·K)
- Density of unburned gas: $\rho_u = 1.16$ kg/m³
- Adiabatic flame temperature: $T_b = 2222$ K
- Activation energy: $E_a = 200$ kJ/mol
- Lewis number: $Le = 1.0$

Compute (i) the Zeldovich number $\beta$, (ii) the laminar flame speed $S_L$ using the ZFK formula. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution Sketch (7 marks)

1. **[1 mark]** Sketch the flame structure: 1-D coordinate $x$, temperature $T(x)$ rising from $T_u$ to $T_b$ across a thin reaction zone, mass-fraction profile $Y(x)$ falling from $Y_u$ to $0$. Label: preheat, reaction, post-flame zones; flame thickness $\delta$.

2. **[1 mark]** Write the governing 1-D steady equations:
   - Continuity: $\rho u = \rho_u S_L = G = \text{const}$
   - Energy: $G c_p T' = \lambda T'' + q \omega$
   - Species: $G Y' = \rho D Y'' - \omega$

3. **[1 mark]** Form the Shvab–Zeldovich coupling function $\theta = T + (q/c_p)Y$ for $Le = 1$.

4. **[1 mark]** Apply boundary conditions ($T \to T_u$, $Y \to Y_u$ as $x \to -\infty$; $T \to T_b$, $Y \to 0$ as $x \to +\infty$) to obtain the linear $T$–$Y$ relationship $T + (q/c_p)Y = T_b$.

5. **[1.5 marks]** Solve the preheat zone (no reaction) to get $T(x) = T_u + (T_b - T_u)\exp(G c_p x / \lambda)$, recovering $\delta_p = \lambda/(G c_p) = \alpha/S_L$.

6. **[1.5 marks]** Carry out the reaction-zone asymptotic analysis using large Zeldovich number $\beta \gg 1$. Match inner and outer solutions to obtain the **temperature-gradient jump** across the reaction sheet, leading to the ZFK result:

$$S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta \cdot Le} \cdot k(T_b) \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{2 R T_b}\right)$$

7. **[Bonus 0.5 mark]** State that the $E_a/2$ in the exponent (vs. $E_a$ in the bulk reaction rate) is the signature of the large-activation-energy asymptotic limit.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
$\lambda = 0.026$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 1100$ J/(kg·K), $\rho_u = 1.16$ kg/m³,
$T_u = 298$ K, $T_b = 2222$ K, $E_a = 2 \times 10^5$ J/mol, $Le = 1$.

**Step (i) — Zeldovich number $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{E_a (T_b - T_u)}{R T_b^2}$$

$$\beta = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^5)(2222 - 298)}{(8.314)(2222)^2}$$

$$\beta = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^5)(1924)}{8.314 \times 4.937 \times 10^6}$$

$$\beta = \dfrac{3.848 \times 10^8}{4.106 \times 10^7} = 9.37$$

**$\beta \approx 9.4$** ✓ (large-activation-energy limit satisfied)

**Step (ii) — Laminar flame speed $S_L$:**

Using the ZFK result with the conventional pre-exponential factor $A = 1.0 \times 10^8$ s⁻¹ (typical for global methane-air oxidation):

$$k(T_b) = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT_b}\right) = 10^8 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{8.314 \times 2222}\right)$$

$$= 10^8 \cdot \exp(-10.82) = 10^8 \times 1.99 \times 10^{-5} = 1.99 \times 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Compute the prefactor:

$$\dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.026}{1.16 \times 1100} = \dfrac{0.052}{1276} = 4.075 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Assemble:

$$S_L^2 = 4.075 \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{1}{9.37 \times 1} \times 1990$$

$$S_L^2 = 4.075 \times 10^{-5} \times 212.4 = 8.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\boxed{S_L = 9.31 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.093 \text{ m/s}}$$

> **Examiner note:** The ZFK formula alone is a *first-order* asymptotic; the simplified form used here under-predicts by a factor of 3–4 compared to the experimental 0.38 m/s because real methane-air chemistry is multi-step with effective activation energy ~ 160 kJ/mol. For higher accuracy, use CHEMKIN's `flame_speed` routine or detailed chemistry with 30+ species. This is a *known* limitation of the global one-step model — KTU accepts the under-prediction with proper explanation.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]

**(a)** State and explain **Mallard–Le Chatelier's thermal theory** of flame propagation. Show that the laminar flame speed is given by

$$S_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p \, \tau_{ign}(T_i)}}$$

where $\tau_{ign}$ is the induction time at the ignition temperature $T_i$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **thermal theory** and the **diffusion theory** of flame propagation. State the **comprehensive (Zeldovich) theory** and discuss how it resolves the apparent contradiction between the two classical theories. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

1. **[1 mark]** State the theory: flame propagates because heat conducts forward from the reaction zone to raise the adjacent unburned gas to its ignition temperature $T_i$. Once $T_i$ is reached, reaction is assumed instantaneous.

2. **[1 mark]** Sketch a 1-D flame: $T(x) = T_u$ at $x \to -\infty$, $T = T_i$ at $x = 0$ (reaction zone edge), $T = T_b$ at $x \to +\infty$.

3. **[1.5 marks]** Solve the energy equation in the preheat zone ($x < 0$, no reaction):

$$G c_p \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \lambda \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

with $T(-\infty) = T_u$, $T(0) = T_i$:

$$T(x) = T_u + (T_i - T_u) \exp\left(\dfrac{G c_p x}{\lambda}\right)$$

4. **[1 mark]** Compute conductive heat flux into the preheat zone:

$$q_{cond} = \lambda \left.\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right\vert_{0^-} = G c_p (T_i - T_u)$$

5. **[1.5 marks]** Equate this flux to the energy needed to ignite a new slab of fresh mixture. A slab of thickness $S_L \cdot \tau_{ign}$ must reach $T_i$ in time $\tau_{ign}$:

$$q_{cond} \cdot \tau_{ign} = \rho_u c_p (T_i - T_u) \cdot S_L \cdot \tau_{ign}$$

Substituting $G = \rho_u S_L$ and simplifying:

$$S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p \, \tau_{ign}(T_i)}$$

6. **[1 mark]** Discuss: $\tau_{ign}(T_i) \propto \exp(E_a / R T_i)$ from Arrhenius. Therefore $S_L \propto \exp(-E_a/2RT_i)$ — the *square-root* dependence on induction time is the thermal-theory signature.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

1. **[1.5 marks]** **Thermal theory (Mallard–Le Chatelier):** Heat conduction is the dominant mechanism of flame propagation. Diffusion of radicals is ignored. Best suited to situations where heat conduction is the rate-limiting process (e.g., very lean or very rich mixtures where the radical pool is small).

2. **[1.5 marks]** **Diffusion theory (Tanney–Jost):** Chain-carrier (H, OH, O atom) diffusion is the dominant mechanism. Heat conduction is secondary. Best suited to situations where thermal diffusivity is much smaller than mass diffusivity (e.g., hydrogen-air flames, where $Le \ll 1$).

3. **[1 mark]** **Apparent contradiction:** Each theory explains *some* flame behaviors but fails for others. The Mallard-Le Chatelier formula under-predicts $S_L$ for H₂-air; the Tanney-Jost formula fails for slow hydrocarbon flames.

4. **[2 marks]** **Comprehensive theory (Zeldovich, 1938; refined by Frank-Kamenetskii, Semenov):** Both heat conduction AND mass diffusion contribute. The actual flame speed is set by the **slower of the two** transport processes for the **limiting** transport mode. For large-activation-energy asymptotics, the ZFK formula:

$$S_L^2 = \dfrac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u c_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta \cdot Le} \cdot k \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{2 R T_b}\right)$$

recovers the thermal limit when $Le = 1$ and the diffusion limit when $Le \to 0$ or $Le \to \infty$.

5. **[1 mark]** **Resolution:** The classical theories are **limiting cases** of the comprehensive theory, valid in different transport regimes defined by the Lewis number. Modern predictive tools (detailed chemistry + multi-species transport) implement the comprehensive theory with no approximation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Mixing up reference frames** — $S_L$ is the speed relative to the **unburned** gas, not the burned gas. If you write $S_L = \dot{m}/\rho_b$, that is the **burned-gas velocity**, NOT laminar flame speed. Marks deducted: typically 1–2 per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Wrong sign in the energy equation** — Conduction flux must be $-\lambda dT/dx$, not $+\lambda dT/dx$. Students often forget the Fourier sign convention.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state the boundary conditions** — A derivation without $\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} T(x)$ and $\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} Y(x)$ is incomplete. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Writing $E_a$ in the Arrhenius exponent of $S_L$ instead of $E_a/2$** — The factor of 2 is the signature of ZFK; if you write $E_a$ instead of $E_a/2$, the formula is wrong (this is the *Thermal Runaway / Adiabatic* value, not the ZFK one).
>
> 5. **Confusing $\alpha$ (thermal diffusivity) with $\nu$ (kinematic viscosity)** — They differ by a factor of $Pr$. $\alpha = \lambda/(\rho c_p)$ and $\nu = \mu/\rho$. Easy trap in numerical problems.
>
> 6. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing SI units.
>
> 7. **Confusing Lewis number regimes** — $Le > 1$ vs. $Le < 1$ have *opposite* preferential-diffusion effects. Always state explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Definition (3-marks favorite):** $S_L = \dot{m}/\rho_u$ is the laminar flame speed, velocity of the unburned gas into a stationary planar flame.
- [ ] **Three reference frames:** Lab, unburned, burned. Know which is which.
- [ ] **Peclet-number unity:** $S_L \delta / \alpha \approx 1$ — flame thickness scales as $\delta \sim \alpha / S_L$.
- [ ] **Three classical theories:** Mallard-Le Chatelier (thermal, 1883), Tanney-Jost (diffusion, 1930), Zeldovich-Frank-Kamenetskii (comprehensive, 1938). Know authors, year, and key postulates.
- [ ] **ZFK formula** (14-marks favorite): $S_L^2 = (2\lambda/\rho_u c_p)(1/\beta)(1/Le)k \exp(-E_a/2RT_b)$. Memorize the $E_a/2$ exponent.
- [ ] **Zeldovich number** $\beta = E_a(T_b - T_u)/(R T_b^2)$. Large $\beta$ validates the asymptotic theory.
- [ ] **Lewis number** $Le = \alpha/D$. $Le = 1$ is the "thermal-diffusion balanced" case.
- [ ] **Equivalence ratio effect:** Peak $S_L$ near $\phi \approx 1.05 - 1.10$ for hydrocarbons; exactly $\phi = 1$ for H₂.
- [ ] **Pressure effect:** $S_L \propto p^{-n/2}$ with $n \approx 1.7 - 2.0$ (weakly decreasing).
- [ ] **Temperature effect:** $S_L \propto T_u^m$ with $m \approx 1.5 - 2.0$ (Metghalchi-Keck).
- [ ] **Damköhler's first similarity:** Geometrically similar flames have the same $S_L$ scaled by $\alpha$.
- [ ] **Mallard-Le Chatelier result:** $S_L = \sqrt{2\lambda/(\rho_u c_p \tau_{ign})}$ — geometric mean of thermal diffusivity and chemical rate.
- [ ] **Typical magnitudes:** Methane-air $S_L \approx 0.38$ m/s, propane-air $\approx 0.46$ m/s, hydrogen-air $\approx 2.5$ m/s (all $\phi = 1$, $T_u = 298$ K).
- [ ] **Flame thickness:** $\delta \sim 0.5$ mm for methane-air, $\sim 0.6$ mm for propane-air, $\sim 0.4$ mm for hydrogen-air.
- [ ] **Markstein length** $L_M$ — quantifies flame stretch sensitivity. Sign determines cellular instability.
- [ ] **Units mastery:** $S_L$ in m/s, $\lambda$ in W/(m·K), $\rho$ in kg/m³, $c_p$ in J/(kg·K), $E_a$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Boundary conditions for a 1-D flame:** $T(-\infty) = T_u$, $T(+\infty) = T_b$, $Y_k(-\infty) = Y_{k,u}$, $Y_k(+\infty) = Y_{k,b}$.
- [ ] **Engineering correlations to remember:** Metghalchi-Keck (pressure-temperature), Guenther-Elson (H₂-air), Law-Ozer (general fuels).
- [ ] **Acoustic coupling:** Ma = $S_L / c$ is small (~10⁻³) for premixed flames — acoustics still drive thermoacoustic instabilities.
- [ ] **Le > 1 fuels** (most hydrocarbons): preferential diffusion is *stabilizing* in lean mixtures.
- [ ] **Le < 1 fuels** (lean H₂, lean CH₄/H₂ blends): preferential diffusion is *destabilizing*, leading to cellular flames.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flame Thickness # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Flame Thickness (δ_L)** is defined as the characteristic distance over which the physical and chemical properties of a premixed reactive mixture (specifically temperature, species concentrations, and density) undergo their principal transition from the unburned (reactant) state to the fully burned (product) state. In the rigorous framework of combustion theory, a one-dimensional adiabatic planar premixed flame is structured into three sequential regions: 1. **Preheat Zone** — where the cold reactants are heated by conduction from the hot burned gases. No significant chemistry occurs here. 2. **Reaction Zone** — the thin region where exothermic chemical reactions release heat at a maximum rate. 3. **Post-Flame (Burned Gas) Zone** — where the reaction is essentially complete and the gas approaches the adiabatic flame temperature. The **Thermal Flame Thickness (δ_T)** is most commonly defined as: $$\delta_T = \frac{T_{ad} - T_u}{\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{max}}$$ where $T_{ad}$ is the adiabatic flame temperature, $T_u$ is the unburned gas temperature, and $(dT/dx)_{max}$ is the maximum temperature gradient (occurring within the preheat zone). A more operationally useful definition, based on the energy conservation equation, is the **Characteristic Laminar Flame Thickness**: $$\delta_L = \frac{\alpha_{th}}{S_L}$$ where $\alpha_{th}$ is the thermal diffusivity of the unburned mixture and $S_L$ is the laminar burning velocity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Flame thickness is a fundamental scaling parameter that links transport properties (heat conduction, species diffusion) with the chemical reaction rate. It governs the resolution requirements for computational fire modeling and the validity of the **Thick Flame / Thin Flame / Distributed Reaction** approximations used in deflagration analysis. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Physical Intuition **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine standing on a road and watching a controlled grass fire (the **flame**) move toward you at a steady walking pace (the **burning velocity**). Just before the flames reach you, you start to feel radiant heat — this is the **preheat zone**. The grass itself only ignites within a very thin band — this is the **reaction zone**. The total distance over which you transition from "cool surroundings" to "actively burning fuel" defines the **flame thickness**. **A Simpler Laboratory Analogy:** Think of a Bunsen burner flame. The luminous blue cone is not infinitely thin — it has a finite, measurable thickness (typically **0.5 – 1.0 mm** for hydrocarbon-air flames at atmospheric pressure). Inside this thin shell, the unburned gas mixture is heated to ignition temperature in a fraction of a millisecond. The thinner the flame, the more intense and dangerous the combustion process — a key consideration in explosion safety engineering. **Order-of-Magnitude Insight:** - **Thermal flame thickness** for methane-air at 1 atm: $\delta_L \sim 0.5\ \text{mm}$ - **Thermal flame thickness** for propane-air at 1 atm: $\delta_L \sim 0.4\ \text{mm}$ - **Thermal flame thickness** for hydrogen-air at 1 atm: $\delta_L \sim 0.3\ \text{mm}$ - **Reaction zone thickness** ($\delta_R$): typically **5 – 10× thinner** than the preheat zone, controlled by the Zeldovich number $\beta$. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used in This Module:** > - $E$ — Activation energy (J/mol) > - $R$ — Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - $T_u$ — Unburned gas temperature (K) > - $T_{ad}$ — Adiabatic flame temperature (K) > - $\beta$ — Zeldovich number (dimensionless), typically 8 – 12 for hydrocarbon-air flames. ## 1.3 Why Flame Thickness Matters in Fire Engineering In fire safety engineering, flame thickness directly determines: - **Critical flame diameter** (the diameter below which a flame cannot propagate — important for explosion venting design). - **Quenching distance** (used to size explosion arrestors in piping systems). - **Mesh / orifice effectiveness** in flame arrestor design. - **Turbulence integral length scale** in turbulent combustion modeling (RANS and LES). - **Grid resolution** in CFD fire simulation (a flame must be resolved by at least 4 – 10 cells for accurate prediction). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of a Premixed Flame The flame thickness problem is approached through the steady, one-dimensional energy and species conservation equations. In the flame-fixed coordinate frame (where the flame is stationary and the unburned gas approaches at velocity $S_L$), the governing equations reduce to: **Continuity:** $\rho u = \rho_u S_L = \text{constant}$ (mass flux conservation) **Energy Equation:** $$\rho_u S_L c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + Q \dot{\omega}$$ **Species Equation (for fuel F):** $$\rho_u S_L \frac{dY_F}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\rho D \frac{dY_F}{dx}\right) - \dot{\omega}$$ where $\dot{\omega}$ is the volumetric reaction rate (kg/m³·s), $Q$ is the heat of reaction per unit mass, and $D$ is the mass diffusivity. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Analysis **Step 1 — Conductive vs Convective Flux Balance:** The flame is sustained by a precise balance between: - **Convective transport** of enthalpy from the burned to the unburned side (governed by $\rho u c_p$). - **Conductive/diffusive transport** across the temperature gradient (governed by $k$). - **Volumetric heat release** from chemical reactions (governed by $Q \dot{\omega}$). **Step 2 — Identify the Dominant Length Scale:** A scale analysis of the energy equation yields a characteristic length: $$\delta_{char} = \frac{\alpha_{th}}{S_L}$$ This is the **fundamental definition of the laminar flame thickness**. **Step 3 — Role of Activation Energy (Asymptotic Analysis):** For large activation energy asymptotics, the temperature profile in the preheat zone is approximately exponential, while the reaction zone collapses to a thin boundary layer whose thickness scales as $\delta_L / \beta$, where $\beta$ is the **Zeldovich number**. **Step 4 — Link to Damköhler Number:** The Damköhler number $Da$ is defined as the ratio of flow time to chemical time: $$Da = \frac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{\delta_L / S_L}{1/\dot{\omega}} = \frac{\delta_L \cdot \dot{\omega}}{S_L}$$ A self-sustaining premixed flame has $Da \gg 1$ (chemistry is fast compared to flow-through time), which justifies the use of the **Burke–Schumann / thin flame limit**. ## 2.3 The Zeldovich Number and Reaction Zone Thickness The **Zeldovich number** $\beta$ is a measure of the temperature sensitivity of the reaction rate and is defined as: $$\beta = \frac{E (T_{ad} - T_u)}{R \, T_{ad}^2}$$ For most hydrocarbon-air flames at standard conditions, $\beta \approx 8 - 12$. This implies that the reaction zone thickness is approximately: $$\delta_R \sim \frac{\delta_L}{\beta}$$ so $\delta_R$ is **one order of magnitude smaller** than the overall flame thickness. ## 2.4 The Lewis Number Connection The **Lewis number** $Le$ compares thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity: $$Le = \frac{\alpha_{th}}{D_{th}}$$ When $Le \neq 1$, the preheat and reaction zones are coupled differently, modifying the effective flame thickness. For $Le < 1$ (light fuels like hydrogen), the flame is **thinner and faster**; for $Le > 1$ (heavy hydrocarbons), the flame is **thicker and slower**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Use | |---|----------|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Characteristic Flame Thickness | $\delta_L = \alpha_{th} / S_L$ | 0.3 – 1.0 mm | CFD grid resolution | | 2 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha_{th} = k / (\rho \, c_p)$ | 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s (air) | Transport property | | 3 | Thermal Thickness (Gradient) | $\delta_T = (T_{ad} - T_u) / (dT/dx)_{max}$ | ~ 0.5 mm | Experimental measurement | | 4 | Reaction Zone Thickness | $\delta_R \sim \delta_L / \beta$ | 0.05 – 0.1 mm | Detailed chemistry modeling | | 5 | Zeldovich Number | $\beta = E(T_{ad} - T_u) / (R \, T_{ad}^2)$ | 8 – 12 | Activation energy sensitivity | | 6 | Damköhler Number | $Da = \delta_L \, \dot{\omega} / S_L$ | $\gg 1$ | Combustion regime identification | | 7 | Peclet Number | $Pe = S_L \, \delta_L / \alpha_{th} = 1$ | exactly 1 | Flame structure verification | | 8 | Lewis Number | $Le = \alpha_{th} / D_{th}$ | 0.3 (H₂) – 2.0 (heavy HC) | Diffusive-thermal instability | | 9 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{ad} = T_u + Q \, Y_F / c_p$ | 1700 – 2300 K | Energy content | | 10 | Quenching Distance | $\delta_q \sim \delta_L$ | 1 – 3 mm | Flame arrestor design | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Fire Engineering - **Explosion Venting:** Industrial vessels handling flammable vapors must be designed so that the vent diameter exceeds the **quenching distance** (proportional to $\delta_L$) to prevent flame propagation through the vent. - **Flame Arrestors:** Used in piping systems carrying combustible gases. The mesh aperture must be smaller than $\delta_L$ to quench the flame. - **Fire Suppression:** Inert gas or water mist suppression works by cooling the preheat zone and effectively increasing $\delta_L$ until the flame cannot sustain itself. - **Detonation-to-Deflagration Transition (DDT):** The ratio of turbulent integral length scale to $\delta_L$ controls the burning regime. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Characteristic Flame Thickness from Energy Equation Scale Analysis **Starting Point:** Steady 1-D energy equation in flame-fixed coordinates: $$\rho_u S_L c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + Q \dot{\omega}$$ **Step 1 — Scale (Order-of-Magnitude) Analysis:** The left-hand side (convective term) scales as: $$\rho_u S_L c_p \frac{\Delta T}{\delta_L} \sim \rho_u S_L c_p \frac{(T_{ad} - T_u)}{\delta_L}$$ The conduction term on the right scales as: $$\frac{k \Delta T}{\delta_L^2} \sim \frac{k (T_{ad} - T_u)}{\delta_L^2}$$ **Step 2 — Equate Convective and Conductive Terms in the Preheat Zone:** In the preheat zone (where $\dot{\omega} \approx 0$), the temperature profile is sustained purely by the balance of convection and conduction. Equating the two: $$\rho_u S_L c_p \frac{(T_{ad} - T_u)}{\delta_L} \sim \frac{k (T_{ad} - T_u)}{\delta_L^2}$$ **Step 3 — Cancel the Common Temperature Difference:** $$\frac{\rho_u S_L c_p}{\delta_L} \sim \frac{k}{\delta_L^2}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the Characteristic Length:** $$\delta_L \sim \frac{k}{\rho_u S_L c_p} = \frac{\alpha_{th}}{S_L}$$ where the thermal diffusivity is defined as $\alpha_{th} = k / (\rho_u c_p)$. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\delta_L = \frac{\alpha_{th}}{S_L}}$$ This is the **characteristic preheat-zone thickness** of a laminar premixed flame. > [!NOTE] > **Dimensional Check:** $[\alpha_{th}] = \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $[S_L] = \text{m/s}$, so $[\delta_L] = \text{m}$. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Exact Solution for the Preheat Zone (No Reaction Assumption) **Assumption:** Within the preheat zone ($x < 0$ in flame-fixed frame, with the reaction zone at $x = 0$), the reaction rate is negligible: $\dot{\omega} \approx 0$. **Governing Equation:** $$\rho_u S_L c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$ **Step 1 — Rearrange as a Second-Order ODE:** $$k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \rho_u S_L c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Assume Exponential Solution Form:** Let $T(x) = A e^{\lambda x} + B$.
