**Conclusion:** Fuels with **higher laminar burning velocities have smaller quenching distances**. This is why hydrogen ($u_L = 3.1$ m/s) has the smallest $d_q$ (~0.6 mm), and methane ($u_L = 0.4$ m/s) has a much larger $d_q$ (~2.5 mm).

**Numerical demonstration:**

$$\frac{d_{q,H_2}}{d_{q,CH_4}} \approx \frac{u_{L,CH_4}}{u_{L,H_2}} = \frac{0.40}{3.10} = 0.129$$

$$d_{q,H_2} \approx 0.129 \times 2.5 = 0.32 \text{ mm (predicted)} \text{ vs. } 0.6 \text{ mm (experimental)}$$

The order-of-magnitude agreement confirms the inverse proportionality to $u_L$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Quenching Diameter for a Cylindrical Tube

For a flame propagating axially through a cylindrical tube of inner diameter $D$, the conducting surface is the cylindrical wall of perimeter $\pi D$. The flame area is $\pi D^2 / 4$. The critical condition becomes:

$$Pe_{cyl} = \frac{u_L \cdot D_q}{\alpha} = \text{critical value}$$

The cylindrical critical Peclet number is empirically larger than the planar one because the heat loss is distributed over the entire circumference. Typically:

$$D_q \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \times d_q$$

**Experimental example:** Propane–air stoichiometric mixture in a tube:
- $D_q \approx 3.6$ mm
- $d_q \approx 1.8$ mm
- Ratio: $D_q / d_q = 2.0$ (consistent with the geometric factor of 2 for circular cross-section)

### 3.5 Pressure Dependence of Quenching Distance

The laminar burning velocity scales with pressure as:

$$u_L \propto p^{(n/2 - 1)}$$

where $n$ is the overall reaction order (typically $n \approx 2$ for many hydrocarbons, giving $u_L \propto p^0$ — pressure-independent).

For a moderate-pressure dependence case ($u_L \propto p^{-0.1}$):

$$d_q \propto \frac{\alpha}{u_L} \propto p^{+0.1} \text{ (mild increase with pressure)}$$

This explains why **high-pressure flame arresters** are designed with slightly tighter gaps than atmospheric-pressure arresters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Flame Quenching Between Parallel Walls

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unburned Gas at T_u] --> B[Conduction Preheat Zone]
    B --> C[Flame Reaction Zone at T_ad]
    C --> D[Burned Gas at T_ad]
    B -. Conductive Heat Loss .-> E[Solid Wall 1 at T_w]
    C -. Conductive Heat Loss .-> E
    B -. Conductive Heat Loss .-> F[Solid Wall 2 at T_w]
    C -. Conductive Heat Loss .-> F
    E --> G[Compare with d_q]
    F --> G
    G --> H{d > d_q?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Flame Propagates Successfully]
    H -- No --> J[Flame Quenches and Extinguishes]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style D fill:#e2e2e2,stroke:#666666
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style J fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Flame Arrester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Incoming Premixed Flame] --> P2{Check Gap d against d_q}
    P2 -- d > d_q --> P3[Flame Passes Through]
    P2 -- d LESS THAN d_q --> P4[Heat Loss Exceeds Heat Release]
    P4 --> P5[Reaction Zone Temperature Falls Below Ignition Temp]
    P5 --> P6[Combustion Halts]
    P6 --> P7[Unburned Gas Reaches Arrester Safely]
    P3 --> P8[Unburned Gas Beyond Arrester Re-ignites Allowed Only if Protected]
    style P1 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600
    style P2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style P4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style P5 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000
    style P6 fill:#999999,stroke:#333333
    style P7 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Influence Parameters on $d_q$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Laminar Burning Velocity u_L] --> X1[Inverse Effect on d_q]
    V2[Thermal Diffusivity alpha] --> X2[Direct Effect on d_q]
    V3[Wall Temperature T_w] --> X3[Cold Wall Increases d_q]
    V4[Mixture Equivalence Ratio phi] --> X4[Off-Stoichiometric Increases d_q]
    V5[Inert Dilution Level] --> X5[Dilution Decreases d_q]
    V6[Wall Material Conductivity] --> X6[High k Wall Increases d_q]
    X1 --> R[Resulting Quenching Distance d_q]
    X2 --> R
    X3 --> R
    X4 --> R
    X5 --> R
    X6 --> R
    style R fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#cc6600
```

### 4.4 Comparative Geometry Schematic (Textual)

```
PARALLEL PLATE GEOMETRY:               CYLINDRICAL TUBE GEOMETRY:
+------------------+                   . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
|  Wall 1 (T_w)    |                  .  +--------------------+  .
|                  |                  .  |    Wall (T_w)       |  .
|       | d        |                  .  |                    |  .
|       v          |                  .  |       | D          |  .
|  +-----------+   |  Unburned -->    .  |       v            |  .
|  |  Preheat  |   |  Flame -->       .  |   +-----------+    |  .
|  |  Zone     |   |  Reaction -->    .  |   |  Preheat |    |  .
|  +-----------+   |  Zone -->        .  |   |  Zone    |    |  .
|  |  Reaction |   |                  .  |   +-----------+    |  .
|  |  Zone     |   |                  .  |   | Reaction |    |  .
|  +-----------+   |                  .  |   | Zone     |    |  .
|                  |                  .  |   +-----------+    |  .
|  Wall 2 (T_w)    |                  .  |                    |  .
+------------------+                  .  +--------------------+  .
                                        . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
  d_q = 1 to 2 mm                         D_q = 2 d_q = 2 to 4 mm
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **quenching diameter** is the smallest tube diameter that supports flame propagation. The **quenching distance** is the smallest plate separation. Geometrically, $D_q \approx 2 d_q$ for a circular cross-section.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

Define *quenching distance* and *quenching diameter* in the context of premixed flame propagation. State the geometric relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

> The **quenching distance** $d_q$ is the minimum gap between two parallel solid walls through which a premixed flame can just propagate. If the gap is smaller, the flame extinguishes because conductive heat loss to the walls exceeds the chemical energy release rate. The **quenching diameter** $D_q$ is the analogous quantity for a cylindrical tube — the minimum internal diameter that allows flame propagation. The geometric relationship is $D_q \approx 2 d_q$ for a circular cross-section. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

Explain why **hydrogen** has a much smaller quenching distance (~0.6 mm) compared to **methane** (~2.5 mm) under identical conditions.

**Model Answer:**

> Hydrogen has a **laminar burning velocity** approximately 7–8 times higher than methane ($u_{L,H_2} \approx 3.1$ m/s vs. $u_{L,CH_4} \approx 0.4$ m/s). Since the quenching distance varies inversely with $u_L$ (via $d_q \approx \alpha / u_L$), hydrogen's much higher flame speed produces a correspondingly smaller pre-heat zone and thus a much smaller quenching distance. The fast kinetics and high thermal diffusivity of the H₂–air system make the flame thinner and more easily arrested. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option for Module 3 Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the quenching distance $d_q$ for a planar premixed flame using the Peclet number criterion, clearly stating the underlying physical assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

We consider a one-dimensional planar premixed flame between two parallel solid walls separated by distance $d$, with the flame moving at the laminar burning velocity $u_L$ in the unburned gas.

**Assumption 1:** Steady-state, one-dimensional, adiabatic (in absence of wall) flame.

**Assumption 2:** The unburned gas has thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$.

**Assumption 3:** The pre-heat zone is governed by conductive-convective energy balance.

**Step 1 — Energy equation in the pre-heat zone:**

$$\rho u_L c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange using $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$:**

$$u_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \alpha \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$$

**Step 3 — Solution of the unperturbed profile (no walls):**

$$T_0(x) = T_u + (T_i - T_u) \exp\left(\frac{u_L x}{\alpha}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic pre-heat thickness:**

$$\delta_p = \frac{\alpha}{u_L}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Peclet number using the wall separation:**

$$Pe = \frac{u_L \cdot d}{\alpha}$$

**Step 6 — Critical condition: flame quenches when $Pe < Pe_{crit}$. Therefore:**

$$\boxed{d_q = \frac{Pe_{crit} \cdot \alpha}{u_L}}$$

For most hydrocarbon–air systems, $Pe_{crit}$ is of order unity (typically 1 to 2), so $d_q \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \times \delta_p$. **(7 marks — 1 mark for assumptions, 4 marks for derivation, 2 marks for physical interpretation)**

---

**(b)** A stoichiometric propane–air mixture at 298 K and 101.325 kPa has a laminar burning velocity of 0.46 m/s and thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. The critical Peclet number for quenching in a planar geometry is found experimentally to be 50. Calculate **(i)** the quenching distance and **(ii)** the quenching diameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $u_L = 0.46$ m/s
- $\alpha = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pe_{crit} = 50$ (planar)

**Part (i) — Quenching distance:**

Apply the master equation:

$$d_q = \frac{Pe_{crit} \cdot \alpha}{u_L}$$

Substitute the values:

$$d_q = \frac{50 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.46}$$

$$d_q = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-3}}{0.46}$$

$$d_q = 2.283 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_q \approx 2.28 \text{ mm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Quenching diameter:**

Using the geometric relationship $D_q = 2 d_q$:

$$D_q = 2 \times 2.283 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{D_q \approx 4.57 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** A flame arrester for propane–air in a circular tube should have an internal diameter less than ~4.57 mm. In practice, the flame arrester gap is designed at approximately **1.8 mm** (using the experimental value) to provide a safety margin. **(7 marks — 3 for part i, 3 for part ii, 1 for design comment)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option for Module 3 Choice**

**(a)** Explain the thermal theory of flame quenching. Using this theory, discuss how the quenching distance varies with **(i)** laminar burning velocity, **(ii)** thermal diffusivity, and **(iii)** mixture equivalence ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Thermal Theory of Flame Quenching:**

A premixed flame propagating through a narrow channel loses heat to the walls by **conduction**. The chemical energy release rate within the thin reaction zone is finite and scales with the volume of the reaction zone. When the gap $d$ is comparable to the **pre-heat zone thickness** $\delta_p = \alpha / u_L$, the wall begins to "see" the unburned gas before it has time to heat up. The wall acts as an infinite heat sink.

The **critical condition** for quenching occurs when the convective heat transport (which is what heats the unburned gas to ignition) is balanced by the conductive heat loss to the wall. This is captured by the **Peclet number criterion** $Pe = u_L d / \alpha < Pe_{crit}$.

**(i) Effect of $u_L$:**

$$d_q = \frac{Pe_{crit} \cdot \alpha}{u_L} \propto \frac{1}{u_L}$$

Faster-burning gases have **smaller** quenching distances. Hydrogen ($u_L = 3.1$ m/s) has $d_q \approx 0.6$ mm; methane ($u_L = 0.4$ m/s) has $d_q \approx 2.5$ mm.

**(ii) Effect of $\alpha$:**

$$d_q \propto \alpha$$

Higher thermal diffusivity (warmer gas, lighter molecules) **increases** $d_q$ because heat is conducted more readily both to the wall and within the gas, but the pre-heat zone becomes thicker, requiring a larger gap to allow propagation.

**(iii) Effect of equivalence ratio $\phi$:**

The quenching distance is **minimum at or near stoichiometric** ($\phi \approx 1$) and increases for both lean ($\phi < 1$) and rich ($\phi > 1$) mixtures. This is because $u_L$ is maximum at stoichiometric, and the flame temperature is highest. As $\phi$ moves away from unity, $u_L$ decreases, and $d_q$ increases roughly as $1/u_L$. **(7 marks — 2 for theory, 1.5 each for i/ii/iii effects)**

---

**(b)** A flame arrester for an LPG pipeline is to be designed. The stoichiometric propane–air mixture has $u_L = 0.46$ m/s, $\alpha = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, and the critical Peclet number for a parallel-plate geometry is 60. Determine the required plate gap. If a corrugated metal matrix flame arrester is to be used instead with an effective cooling perimeter twice that of the planar geometry, what is the new required gap? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $u_L = 0.46$ m/s
- $\alpha = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pe_{crit,planar} = 60$

**Step 1 — Planar plate gap:**

$$d_{q,planar} = \frac{Pe_{crit,planar} \cdot \alpha}{u_L}$$

$$d_{q,planar} = \frac{60 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.46} = 2.739 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.74 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Corrugated matrix gap:**

For a corrugated matrix, the effective heat-loss perimeter is doubled, so the critical Peclet number effectively doubles (more heat is lost per unit flame area). Therefore:

$$Pe_{eff} = 2 \times Pe_{crit,planar} = 120$$

$$d_{q,corrugated} = \frac{120 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.46} = 5.478 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.48 \text{ mm}$$

**Wait — physical correction:** A corrugated matrix has a *higher* heat-loss rate per unit volume, so the critical gap is actually **smaller**, not larger. The effective $Pe_{crit}$ is *lower*. We re-derive:

For a corrugated matrix with twice the cooling perimeter, the heat-loss rate per unit flame volume is doubled. The critical Peclet number is therefore halved:

$$Pe_{eff} = \frac{Pe_{crit,planar}}{2} = 30$$

$$d_{q,corrugated} = \frac{30 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.46} = 1.370 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{q,corrugated} \approx 1.37 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The corrugated matrix allows a much more compact design — approximately **half the gap** of a simple parallel-plate arrester. This is why industrial flame arresters for LPG commonly use corrugated or sintered-metal matrix elements rather than simple parallel plates. **(7 marks — 3 for part 1, 3 for part 2 with the correct physical reasoning, 1 for design conclusion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing quenching distance with quenching diameter.** They are not the same — remember $D_q \approx 2 d_q$.
> 2. **Forgetting the inverse dependence on $u_L$.** Many students incorrectly write $d_q \propto u_L$ instead of $d_q \propto 1/u_L$.
> 3. **Missing units.** Always state $d_q$ in **mm** (or convert to meters carefully) and ensure consistent SI units in the formula.
> 4. **Ignoring the geometry factor.** Planar and cylindrical geometries have different critical Peclet numbers; do not blindly use the same constant.
> 5. **Treating $Pe_{crit}$ as universal.** It is *not* a true constant — it depends on geometry, wall material, and the chemical system. A 10–20% error in $d_q$ is acceptable in KTU evaluations.
> 6. **Forgetting the "minimum" qualifier.** Quenching distance is the *minimum* gap below which the flame fails — not just any arbitrary gap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quenching distance $d_q$** = minimum parallel-plate gap for flame propagation.
- **Quenching diameter $D_q$** = minimum tube diameter for flame propagation, with **$D_q \approx 2 d_q$**.
- **Master equation:** $d_q = Pe_{crit} \cdot \alpha / u_L \approx (1 \text{ to } 2) \times \delta_p$.
- **Pre-heat zone thickness:** $\delta_p = \alpha / u_L$.
- **Inverse relationship with $u_L$:** faster-burning gases → smaller $d_q$.
- **Direct relationship with $\alpha$:** hotter, more diffusive gas → larger $d_q$.
- **Minimum at stoichiometric** ($\phi = 1$); increases for lean and rich mixtures.
- **Hydrogen has the smallest $d_q$ (~0.6 mm)**; methane and propane have larger $d_q$ (~1.8 to 2.5 mm).
- **Quenching is a transport (heat-loss) phenomenon, not a chemical-kinetics one.**
- **Industrial applications:** flame arresters, flashback preventers, gas-burner port design, mine safety.
- **Critical Peclet number $Pe_{crit}$** is geometry-dependent: planar (~1–2 or higher with reaction kinetics), cylindrical (larger), corrugated matrix (smaller).
- **Wall material matters:** high-conductivity walls (copper) quench more effectively than low-conductivity walls (refractory ceramic).
- **Pressure effect:** mild; $d_q$ may decrease slightly with increasing pressure.
- **Temperature effect:** higher preheat temperature increases $\alpha$, which increases $d_q$.
- **Design safety margin:** industrial flame arresters are typically built with a gap of **0.5 to 0.7 × $d_q$** to account for hot-gas ingress, fouling, and material degradation.
- **Verification cross-check:** for propane–air, $d_q \approx 1.8$ mm; for hydrogen–air, $d_q \approx 0.6$ mm; for methane–air, $d_q \approx 2.5$ mm.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** mixing up $d_q$ and $D_q$; using $u_L$ as the denominator in the wrong place; ignoring the geometry correction; not stating the assumption of quasi-steady one-dimensional propagation.
- **Memory hook:** *"Hot gas, cold wall — the wall always wins when the gap is too small."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar Diffusion Flame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar Diffusion Flame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laminar Diffusion Flame:** A diffusion flame is a flame in which the fuel and oxidizer are initially separated, and combustion occurs only at the region where the fuel and oxidizer have intermingled by molecular and turbulent transport processes to reach a combustible mixture. When the flow is **laminar** (Reynolds number $\text{Re} < 2300$), the flame is termed a **Laminar Diffusion Flame (LDF)**.

In a laminar diffusion flame, the rate of mixing between the fuel and oxidizer controls the overall combustion rate. Unlike a premixed flame, no initial mixing of fuel and air occurs. The reaction zone is localized at a stoichiometric surface where the local fuel and oxidizer concentrations are in chemically correct proportions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a lit **incense stick (agarbatti)** in a still room.
- The fuel (incense vapor) comes out of the stick.
- The oxidizer (oxygen in air) surrounds it.
- They meet at the **flame sheet** — a thin glowing zone where they react.
- The flame remains smooth, steady, and well-defined because the air is calm (laminar flow).

Now, blow on it with a hair dryer. The flame becomes chaotic, noisy, and flickers. That is a **turbulent diffusion flame**.

**Key Idea:**
- In laminar diffusion flames, **mixing is governed by molecular diffusion alone**.
- The flame sheet is mathematically tractable using classical boundary-layer theory (Burke–Schumann, 1928).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Methane, CH₄):** $\text{AFR}_{st} = 17.16 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$
> - **Standard Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Propane, C₃H₈):** $\text{AFR}_{st} = 15.13 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$
> - **Laminar Flame Speed** (pre-mixed, for reference): $S_L \approx 0.4 \text{ m/s}$ for methane
> - **Laminar Reynolds Number Threshold:** $\text{Re}_{crit} = 2300$
> - **Thermal Diffusivity of Air:** $\alpha \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - **Schmidt Number for most gases:** $\text{Sc} = \nu/D \approx 0.7 - 1.0$

### 1.4 Classification of Diffusion Flames

| Type | Reynolds Number (Re) | Description |
|------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Laminar Jet Diffusion Flame** | $\text{Re} < 2300$ | Smooth, axisymmetric, analytically solvable |
| **Transition Diffusion Flame** | $2300 < \text{Re} < 5000$ | Onset of instabilities, flickering begins |
| **Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flame** | $\text{Re} > 5000$ | Chaotic, eddies dominate mixing |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 syllabus, the **Laminar Jet Diffusion Flame** modeled by **Burke–Schumann (1928)** is the cornerstone topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Setup and Coordinates

Consider a round laminar jet of fuel emerging from a circular nozzle of radius $R$ with mean exit velocity $U_0$ into stagnant ambient air. We adopt a **cylindrical coordinate system** $(x, r)$:
- $x$ : axial (streamwise) coordinate, $x=0$ at the nozzle exit
- $r$ : radial coordinate, $r=0$ at the centerline
- $u(r,x)$ : axial velocity component
- $v(r,x)$ : radial velocity component
- $Y_F(r,x)$ : mass fraction of fuel
- $Y_O(r,x)$ : mass fraction of oxidizer

### 2.2 Boundary-Layer Approximations (Order of Magnitude Analysis)

For high Reynolds number laminar jets, the boundary-layer equations apply because:
- The jet is thin (radial scale $\ll$ axial scale)
- Radial pressure gradient $\partial p / \partial r \approx 0$ (neglecting buoyancy for forced jets)
- Axial diffusion $\partial^2 / \partial x^2 \ll$ radial diffusion $\partial^2 / \partial r^2$

### 2.3 Governing Equations (Boundary-Layer Form)

**Continuity Equation:**
$$\frac{\partial (ru)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (rv)}{\partial r} = 0$$

**Axial Momentum Equation:**
$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial r} = \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial u}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Fuel Species Conservation:**
$$u\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial r} = \frac{D_F}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Oxidizer Species Conservation:**
$$u\frac{\partial Y_O}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial Y_O}{\partial r} = \frac{D_O}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial Y_O}{\partial r}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schmidt Number Assumption (Schvab–Zeldovich):** $\text{Sc} = \nu/D_F = \nu/D_O = 1$
> This allows a powerful transformation that couples fuel and oxidizer into a single linear equation.

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions

**At the nozzle exit (x = 0):**
- $u(0,r) = U_0$ for $r \leq R$, and $u(0,r) = 0$ for $r > R$
- $Y_F(0,r) = Y_{F,0}$ for $r \leq R$, and $Y_F(0,r) = 0$ for $r > R$
- $Y_O(0,r) = 0$ for $r \leq R$, and $Y_O(0,r) = Y_{O,\infty}$ for $r > R$

**At the centerline (r = 0):**
- $\partial u / \partial r = 0$, $\partial Y_F / \partial r = 0$, $\partial Y_O / \partial r = 0$
- $v(0,x) = 0$

**Far from the jet (r → ∞):**
- $u(\infty, x) = 0$, $Y_F(\infty, x) = 0$, $Y_O(\infty, x) = Y_{O,\infty}$

### 2.5 The Burke–Schumann Transformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The Schvab–Zeldovich transformation combines the fuel and oxidizer equations linearly. With $\text{Sc} = 1$, define:
> $$\beta(x,r) = \frac{Y_F}{Y_{F,0}} - \frac{Y_O}{Y_{O,\infty}}$$
> Then $\beta$ satisfies the **same linear operator** as the momentum equation, allowing a single solution to govern both species.

### 2.6 Flame Sheet Location (Stoichiometric Surface)

The flame sheet is defined as the locus where the **stoichiometric condition** is satisfied:
$$\frac{Y_F}{Y_{O,\infty}} = \nu_s \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{Y_O}{Y_{O,\infty}} = 1 - \nu_s$$

where the **stoichiometric coupling parameter** is:
$$\nu_s = \frac{Y_{F,s}}{Y_{F,0}} = \frac{\text{Mass of fuel reacting}}{\text{Mass of fuel supplied}}$$

The flame surface satisfies:
$$\frac{Y_F(x, r_f)}{Y_{F,0}} = \nu_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta(x, r_f) = 2\nu_s - 1$$

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{2 U_0 R}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless; $\text{Re} < 2300$ for laminar |
| Mass Conservation (Burke–Schumann) | $\beta = \dfrac{Y_F}{Y_{F,0}} - \dfrac{Y_O}{Y_{O,\infty}}$ | Linear combination |
| Flame Sheet Condition | $\beta(x, r_f) = 2\nu_s - 1$ | Defines the reaction surface |
| Centerline Velocity Decay | $u_c(x) = \dfrac{3}{8}\dfrac{J}{\rho \nu x}$ | $J$ = momentum flux per unit length |
| Centerline Fuel Decay | $Y_{F,c}(x) = \dfrac{3}{8}\dfrac{\dot{m}_F}{\rho \nu x} \cdot \dfrac{1}{Y_{F,0}}$ | Mole fraction form |
| **Flame Height (Burke–Schumann)** | $L_f = \dfrac{3}{8}\dfrac{\dot{m}_F}{\rho \nu} \cdot \dfrac{1}{Y_{O,\infty}(\nu_s - 0)}$ | For pure gas fuel in air |
| **Froude Number Effect** | $L_f \propto \text{Fr}^{2/5}$ for buoyant jets | $\text{Fr} = U_0^2/(gR)$ |
| **Hertzberg Flame Height** | $L_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_F}{D_\infty \rho_\infty Y_{O,\infty} \pi R \ln(1 + 1/\nu_s)}$ | Convective-diffusive balance |
| **Dimensional Flame Height** | $L_f/d = \dfrac{6.2}{\nu_s}$ for $0.2 < \nu_s < 1.5$ | Empirical; $d$ = nozzle diameter |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Laminar diffusion flame theory is foundational to:
- **Fire safety engineering** (candle flames, pool fires, accidental gas leaks)
- **Combustor design** in gas turbines and rocket engines
- **Pollutant formation modeling** (soot, NO_x, CO formation zones)
- **CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) reactors** for material synthesis
- **Flame diagnostics** in microgravity combustion research

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Centerline Velocity Decay in a Laminar Round Jet

Starting with the boundary-layer momentum equation:
$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial r} = \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial u}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Step 1: Use the stream function approach.**
Define the Stokes stream function $\psi$ such that:
$$u = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}, \quad v = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2: Substitute and apply similarity.**
Assume the similarity variable:
$$\eta = \frac{r}{\delta(x)}, \quad \delta(x) = \sqrt{\frac{4 \nu x}{U_0}}$$

**Step 3: Seek a self-similar solution of the form:**
$$\psi(x,r) = \nu \cdot \text{function}(\eta) \cdot x$$

After extensive algebra (presented in detail in classic texts by Schlichting, White), one arrives at the **centerline velocity decay**:
$$u_c(x) = \frac{u_0 R^2}{4 \nu x} \cdot \text{(matching coefficient)} = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{J}{\rho \nu x}$$

where $J = \rho \pi R^2 U_0^2 / \pi = \rho R^2 U_0^2$ is the kinematic momentum flux.

In compact form:
$$\boxed{u_c(x) = \frac{3J}{8\rho \nu x}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Centerline Fuel Mass Fraction

For a passive scalar (fuel vapor) with $\text{Sc} = 1$, the scalar and momentum fields are identical. Therefore:
$$Y_{F,c}(x) = \frac{Y_{F,0} \cdot \dot{m}_F / (\rho U_0 \pi R^2)}{\text{normalization}}$$

After matching the source strength to the scalar boundary condition:
$$\boxed{Y_{F,c}(x) = \frac{3 \dot{m}_F}{8 \pi \rho \nu x}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: The Burke–Schumann Flame Height

**Step 1: Express the flame sheet as a surface of revolution.**

The flame sheet in the Burke–Schumann framework is found by setting $\beta(x, r) = 2\nu_s - 1$. Since $\beta$ satisfies the same linear equation as $u$, it inherits the self-similar solution.

**Step 2: Use the stoichiometric mass-flux balance.**

At any axial location, the **mass of fuel supplied up to the flame tip** must equal the **mass of fuel that has diffused outward to mix with the entrained oxidizer**:
$$\dot{m}_F = \dot{m}_{O,\text{entrained}} \cdot s$$

where $s$ is the stoichiometric mass ratio of oxidizer to fuel.

**Step 3: Compute the entrained oxidizer flow rate at axial position $x$.**

The entrained flow grows linearly along the centerline for a laminar round jet:
$$\dot{m}_{O}(x) = \pi \rho D_\infty r_{1/2}(x) Y_{O,\infty}$$

**Step 4: Equate and solve for the flame tip location.**

At the flame tip ($x = L_f$), the centerline oxidizer concentration reaches the stoichiometric value:
$$Y_{O,c}(L_f) = (1 - \nu_s) Y_{O,\infty}$$

Using $Y_{O,c}(x) = Y_{O,\infty} - (\nu_s / \text{some factor}) \cdot (\text{decay})$, we obtain:
$$\boxed{L_f = \frac{3 \dot{m}_F}{8 \pi \rho \nu Y_{O,\infty} \ln\left(1 + \frac{1}{\nu_s}\right)}}$$

**Step 5: Simplification for high Schmidt numbers.**

For the special case $\text{Sc} = 1$ and pure gas fuel ($\nu_s \to 0$ for fuel-lean stoichiometry), the formula reduces to:
$$L_f = \frac{3 \dot{m}_F}{8 \pi \rho \nu Y_{O,\infty}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) issues from a circular nozzle of diameter $d = 1 \text{ cm}$ with exit velocity $U_0 = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$ into still air at $T_\infty = 300 \text{ K}$. Compute the laminar flame height.

**Given Data:**
- $\rho = 1.177 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (air at 300 K)
- $\nu = 1.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $Y_{O,\infty} = 0.232$
- Stoichiometric O/F ratio: $s = 4.0$ (mass)
- $\nu_s = 1/(1 + s) = 0.2$ for methane

**Step 1: Compute the mass flow rate of fuel.**
$$A = \pi d^2/4 = \pi (0.01)^2/4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$
$$\dot{m}_F = \rho U_0 A = 1.177 \times 1.2 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\dot{m}_F = 1.109 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the logarithm term.**
$$\ln(1 + 1/\nu_s) = \ln(1 + 5) = \ln(6) = 1.7918$$

**Step 3: Plug into flame height formula.**
$$L_f = \frac{3 \times 1.109 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \pi \times 1.177 \times 1.57 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.232 \times 1.7918}$$

Computing numerator: $3 \times 1.109 \times 10^{-4} = 3.327 \times 10^{-4}$

Computing denominator:
$$8\pi = 25.133$$
$$25.133 \times 1.177 = 29.581$$
$$29.581 \times 1.57 \times 10^{-5} = 4.644 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$4.644 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.232 = 1.077 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$1.077 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.7918 = 1.930 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Final answer.**
$$L_f = \frac{3.327 \times 10^{-4}}{1.930 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.724 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_f \approx 1.72 \text{ m}}$$

**Verification using dimensional correlation:**
$$L_f/d = 1.72 / 0.01 = 172$$
Theoretical: $6.2/\nu_s = 6.2/0.2 = 31$ — discrepancy indicates jet-momentum effect in real flames.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, real flames are taller than Burke–Schumann predictions due to finite-rate chemistry, radiation losses, and buoyancy. A correction factor of 2–3 is commonly applied.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Flame Height

| Parameter | Effect on $L_f$ | Physical Reason |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| ↑ Fuel flow rate $\dot{m}_F$ | ↑ Increases | More fuel needs more mixing length |
| ↑ Nozzle diameter $d$ | ↑ Increases (weakly) | Wider jet, lower entrainment rate per area |
| ↑ Air co-flow velocity | ↓ Decreases | Increased forced mixing |
| ↑ Fuel reactivity (lower $\nu_s$) | ↑ Increases | Stoichiometric sheet moves outward |
| ↑ Momentum flux $J$ | ↑ Increases | Higher exit velocity stretches flame |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Laminar Jet Diffusion Flame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Ambient Air]:::in --> A1[Quiescent Oxidizer Reservoir]
    A2[Fuel Supply Line]:::in --> A3[Nozzle Exit x=0 r=R]
    A1 --> B1[Entrainment Zone Outer Region r R]
    A3 --> B2[Inner Fuel Jet Core r less than r_f]
    B1 --> C1[Radial Molecular Diffusion Zone]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Stoichiometric Flame Surface r equals r_f x]
    D1 --> E1[Combustion Reaction Zone Infinitely Thin Sheet]
    E1 --> F1[Hot Product Plume Axial Direction]
    F1 --> G1[Far Field Mixing and Cooling]
    G1 --> H1[Ambient Sink]

    classDef in fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Coordinate System and Flame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Jet[Round Laminar Jet at Nozzle]
        N0[Nozzle Exit at x=0] -->|Streamline| N1[Centerline at r=0]
        N0 -->|Outer Edge| NE[Jet Radius R]
        N1 -->|Decay u_c x| CT[Centerline Velocity Decay]
    end
    subgraph FS[Flame Sheet Geometry]
        S1[Flame Anchoring at x=0 r=R] --> S2[Flame Cone r_f x]
        S2 --> S3[Flame Tip at L_f, r=0]
    end
    subgraph Zone[Species Zones]
        Z1[Pure Fuel Core Y_F greater than Y_F,s]
        Z2[Stoichiometric Sheet r=r_f]
        Z3[Pure Oxidizer Annulus Y_O less than Y_O,s]
    end
    Jet --> FS
    FS --> Zone
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Burke–Schumann Solution Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Write Boundary Layer Equations] --> P1[Apply Schvab Zeldovich Transformation]
    P1 --> P2[Define Beta = Y_F/Y_F0 minus Y_O/Y_O_inf]
    P2 --> P3[Assume Sc equals 1]
    P3 --> P4[Combine Fuel and Oxidizer Equations]
    P4 --> P5[Same Operator as Momentum]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Similarity Solution of Round Jet]
    P6 --> P7[Derive Beta x r Closed Form]
    P7 --> P8[Set Beta equals 2 nu_s minus 1]
    P8 --> P9[Solve for Flame Surface r_f x]
    P9 --> P10[Find Flame Tip x equals L_f when r_f equals 0]
    P10 --> P11[Final Expression for Flame Height]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Three-Zone Structure of a Laminar Diffusion Flame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Zone1[Inner Zone: Fuel Rich]
        Z1A[Pure Fuel Vapor] --> Z1B[Y_F greater than Y_F,s]
        Z1B --> Z1C[No Reaction Occurs]
    end
    subgraph Zone2[Reaction Zone: Stoichiometric Sheet]
        Z2A[Y_F equals Y_F,s] --> Z2B[Y_O equals Y_O,s]
        Z2B --> Z2C[Combustion at Flame Surface]
    end
    subgraph Zone3[Outer Zone: Oxidizer Rich]
        Z3A[Pure Ambient Air] --> Z3B[Y_O greater than Y_O,s]
        Z3B --> Z3C[No Reaction Occurs]
    end
    Zone1 --> Zone2
    Zone2 --> Zone3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Three Zones:**
> 1. **Inner Zone (Fuel Side):** Excess fuel, no oxidizer. The reaction cannot proceed without oxygen.
> 2. **Reaction Sheet (Stoichiometric):** A mathematical surface where fuel and oxidizer are in exact stoichiometric proportion. In the Burke–Schumann limit (infinite chemistry), this is infinitely thin.
> 3. **Outer Zone (Oxidizer Side):** Excess air, no fuel. The fuel has been fully consumed at the sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define a laminar diffusion flame. How is it distinguished from a laminar premixed flame?**

**Model Answer:**
A laminar diffusion flame is a combustion system in which the fuel and oxidizer are initially separated and mix only by molecular (and possibly small-scale turbulent) diffusion before reacting. It is distinguished from a premixed flame in that:
- **Premixed Flame:** Fuel and air are mixed before ignition. Combustion is governed by the laminar flame speed $S_L$.
- **Diffusion Flame:** Fuel and air mix during combustion. The reaction rate is controlled by the rate of mixing (diffusion).
The Reynolds number for a laminar jet is $\text{Re} = 2U_0 R/\nu < 2300$.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **State the Schvab–Zeldovich transformation and its significance in the Burke–Schumann solution.**

**Model Answer:**
The Schvab–Zeldovich transformation defines a linear combination of species mass fractions that satisfies a single linear transport equation identical to that for enthalpy (or momentum). For the fuel-oxidizer system, it is:
$$\beta = \frac{Y_F}{Y_{F,0}} - \frac{Y_O}{Y_{O,\infty}}$$
**Significance:** It reduces the coupled non-linear combustion problem to a single linear boundary-value problem, allowing an exact analytical solution by leveraging the well-known similarity solution of a laminar round jet.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the governing boundary-layer equations for a laminar round jet diffusion flame, including the continuity, momentum, and species conservation equations. State the boundary conditions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Continuity Equation:**
For a steady, axisymmetric, incompressible flow in cylindrical coordinates:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (rv)}{\partial r} = 0$$
Equivalently:
$$\frac{\partial (ru)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (rv)}{\partial r} = 0$$

**Axial Momentum Equation:**
Applying the boundary-layer approximation (neglecting $\partial^2/\partial x^2$ terms and radial pressure gradient):
$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial r} = \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial u}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Fuel Species Equation:**
$$u\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial r} = \frac{D_F}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Oxidizer Species Equation:**
$$u\frac{\partial Y_O}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial Y_O}{\partial r} = \frac{D_O}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial Y_O}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
| Location | Velocity | Fuel | Oxidizer |
|----------|----------|------|----------|
| $x=0, r \leq R$ | $u = U_0$ | $Y_F = Y_{F,0}$ | $Y_O = 0$ |
| $x=0, r > R$ | $u = 0$ | $Y_F = 0$ | $Y_O = Y_{O,\infty}$ |
| $r = 0$ | $\partial u/\partial r = 0, v = 0$ | $\partial Y_F/\partial r = 0$ | $\partial Y_O/\partial r = 0$ |
| $r \to \infty$ | $u \to 0$ | $Y_F \to 0$ | $Y_O \to Y_{O,\infty}$ |

**(b)** Apply the Schvab–Zeldovich transformation and hence obtain the Burke–Schumann flame height expression for a laminar round jet diffusion flame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply Schvab–Zeldovich. Define:
$$\beta = \frac{Y_F}{Y_{F,0}} - \frac{Y_O}{Y_{O,\infty}}$$

**Step 2:** Combining the species equations (subtracting one from the other with normalization) and assuming $D_F = D_O = \nu$ (i.e., $\text{Sc} = 1$):
$$u\frac{\partial \beta}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \beta}{\partial r} = \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial \beta}{\partial r}\right)$$

This is the same operator as the momentum equation. The solution is self-similar.

**Step 3:** The boundary conditions on $\beta$ are:
- $\beta(0, r) = 1$ for $r \leq R$, and $\beta(0, r) = -1$ for $r > R$
- $\beta(x, \infty) = -1$, $\partial \beta / \partial r \vert_{r=0} = 0$

**Step 4:** Using the round jet similarity variable $\eta = r\sqrt{U_0/(4\nu x)}$ and the stream function $\psi = \nu x \cdot F(\eta)$:
$$\beta(\eta) = \frac{\int_{\eta}^{\infty} \frac{F'(\xi)}{\xi} d\xi - 1}{\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{F'(\xi)}{\xi} d\xi - 1}$$

For the standard round jet, $F'(\eta) = \frac{1}{(1 + \xi^2/4)^2}$, which gives:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 + \eta^2/4} - 1 = -\frac{\eta^2/4}{1 + \eta^2/4}$$

**Step 5:** The flame sheet is defined by $\beta = 2\nu_s - 1$. Setting equal:
$$2\nu_s - 1 = -\frac{\eta_f^2/4}{1 + \eta_f^2/4}$$
$$\eta_f^2 = \frac{4(1 - 2\nu_s)}{2\nu_s} = \frac{2(1 - 2\nu_s)}{\nu_s}$$

**Step 6:** The flame tip is where $\eta_f = 0$, i.e., $\nu_s = 0.5$ — not generally true. Instead, the flame height is found from conservation:

Integrating the fuel equation over the cross-section and using the Burke–Schumann identity for oxidizer:
$$L_f = \frac{3\dot{m}_F}{8\pi \rho \nu Y_{O,\infty} \ln(1 + 1/\nu_s)}$$

$$\boxed{L_f = \frac{3\dot{m}_F}{8\pi \rho \nu Y_{O,\infty} \ln(1 + 1/\nu_s)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Explain the three-zone structure of a laminar diffusion flame. Sketch the radial profiles of $Y_F$, $Y_O$, and temperature $T$ across the flame at a fixed axial location. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Three-Zone Structure:**

1. **Inner Zone (Fuel-Rich Core):** $0 \leq r \leq r_f(x)$. Here $Y_F > Y_{F,s}$ and $Y_O = 0$. No reaction because oxidizer is absent.

2. **Reaction Zone (Flame Sheet):** $r = r_f(x)$. Infinitely thin in the Burke–Schumann limit. The fuel and oxidizer are in stoichiometric proportion: $Y_F = Y_{F,s}$, $Y_O = Y_{O,s}$.

3. **Outer Zone (Oxidizer-Rich Annulus):** $r \geq r_f(x)$. Here $Y_O > Y_{O,s}$ and $Y_F = 0$. Excess air, no fuel.

**Radial Profiles at a fixed $x$:**

| Quantity | $r = 0$ | $r = r_f$ | $r \to \infty$ |
|----------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| $Y_F$ | Maximum $Y_{F,c}(x)$ | 0 (at sheet) | 0 |
| $Y_O$ | 0 | 0 (at sheet) | $Y_{O,\infty}$ |
| $T$ | $T_c(x) \approx T_{ad}$ (in fully-burnt products) | Peak $T_{ad}$ | $T_\infty$ |

The temperature profile is **double-valued** at the flame sheet because hot products of combustion (originating from the inner side) flow outward into the cold oxidizer stream, while the flame temperature is the maximum.

**(b)** A round laminar jet of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) issues from a nozzle of diameter $d = 5 \text{ mm}$ with a fuel flow rate $\dot{m}_F = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$. Compute the flame height. Given: $\rho = 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $Y_{O,\infty} = 0.232$, $\nu_s = 0.245$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the Burke–Schumann formula:
$$L_f = \frac{3\dot{m}_F}{8\pi \rho \nu Y_{O,\infty} \ln(1 + 1/\nu_s)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the logarithm term:
$$\ln(1 + 1/\nu_s) = \ln(1 + 1/0.245) = \ln(1 + 4.082) = \ln(5.082) = 1.626$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator:
$$3 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator term by term:
- $8\pi = 25.133$
- $25.133 \times 1.18 = 29.657$
- $29.657 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 4.4485 \times 10^{-4}$
- $4.4485 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.232 = 1.0321 \times 10^{-4}$
- $1.0321 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.626 = 1.6782 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 5:** Final answer:
$$L_f = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6782 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.575 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_f \approx 3.58 \text{ m}}$$

**Verification using empirical correlation:**
$$L_f/d = 6.2/0.245 = 25.3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_f = 25.3 \times 0.005 = 0.127 \text{ m}$$
The discrepancy arises because the empirical formula $L_f/d = 6.2/\nu_s$ is for *buoyant* (candle-like) diffusion flames, whereas the analytical formula is for *forced* (momentum-dominated) jets. The actual value depends on the Froude number.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Laminar Diffusion Flame Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing the stoichiometric ratio definitions:** $\nu_s$ is the *fraction of fuel that reacts* (not the inverse stoichiometric ratio $s$). Recall: $\nu_s = 1/(1 + s)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Schmidt number assumption:** The Schvab–Zeldovich combination is valid **only if** $\text{Sc}_F = \text{Sc}_O = 1$ (i.e., $D_F = D_O = \nu$). For $\text{Sc} \neq 1$, separate similarity solutions are required.
> 3. **Sign convention errors in $\beta$:** The flame sheet is at $\beta = 2\nu_s - 1$, not $\beta = \nu_s$. Confusing this yields a factor-of-2 error.
> 4. **Applying the empirical $L_f/d = 6.2/\nu_s$ to forced jets:** This formula is for *buoyant* flames (e.g., candles). For *forced* laminar jets, use the Burke–Schumann expression involving momentum flux and viscosity.
> 5. **Neglecting the three-zone interpretation:** A common error is to assume uniform $Y_F$ across the cross-section. Always emphasize the **stoichiometric surface** as a localized reaction sheet.
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Use **SI units** consistently. Mixing CGS with SI yields zero credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Laminar Diffusion Flame**

- ✅ A **laminar diffusion flame** has $\text{Re} < 2300$ and is mixing-controlled (no premixing of fuel and air).
- ✅ The **Burke–Schumann (1928)** theory is the cornerstone analytical model.
- ✅ The **flame sheet** is the locus where $Y_F/Y_{F,0} = \nu_s$ and $Y_O/Y_{O,\infty} = 1 - \nu_s$.
- ✅ The **Schvab–Zeldovich transformation** $\beta = Y_F/Y_{F,0} - Y_O/Y_{O,\infty}$ requires $\text{Sc} = 1$.
- ✅ The **centerline velocity** decays as $u_c(x) \propto 1/x$ in a laminar round jet.
- ✅ The **flame height** for a forced laminar jet is $L_f = \dfrac{3\dot{m}_F}{8\pi \rho \nu Y_{O,\infty} \ln(1 + 1/\nu_s)}$.
- ✅ The **flame height** for a buoyant jet scales as $L_f \propto \text{Fr}^{2/5}$.
- ✅ The **three-zone structure** is: fuel-rich core, reaction sheet, oxidizer-rich annulus.
- ✅ The **flame sheet is infinitely thin** in the infinite-chemistry limit (Damköhler number $\text{Da} \to \infty$).
- ✅ The **empirical correlation** $L_f/d \approx 6.2/\nu_s$ applies to buoyant laminar diffusion flames.
- ✅ The **Schmidt number** for most gases in air is approximately 0.7–1.0, justifying the $\text{Sc} = 1$ assumption.
- ✅ The **momentum flux** $J = \rho \pi R^2 U_0^2$ is conserved along a laminar round jet.
- ✅ Real flames are **taller** than Burke–Schumann predictions due to finite-rate chemistry, radiation, and buoyancy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **candle flame** is a classical example of a **laminar, buoyancy-driven, non-premixed (diffusion) flame** sustained by the vaporization and combustion of a solid hydrocarbon fuel (paraffin wax, predominantly C₂₅H₅₂) drawn up a porous wick by capillary action. In the formal language of fire dynamics, the candle flame is treated as a **miniature pool fire / diffusion flame system** where:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A candle flame is a steady-state, buoyancy-controlled laminar diffusion flame in which the fuel vapor, generated by the endothermic pyrolysis of solid wax at the wick, mixes with surrounding oxidizer (air) by molecular and convective diffusion, and burns in a thin reaction zone producing a luminous, soot-laden core surrounded by a non-luminous mantle of combustion products.

The flame exhibits a characteristic **three-zone structure** and is governed by the coupled processes of **capillary wicking, vaporization, mixing-controlled combustion, radiative emission from soot, and natural convective entrainment** induced by buoyancy.

## 1.2 The Three Classical Zones of a Candle Flame

> [!NOTE]
> **Zone Structure (Must memorise for KTU exams):**

| Zone | Name | Appearance | Chemistry / Physics | Approx. Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 1** | **Innermost / Dark Zone** (just above wick) | Dark blue / nearly transparent | Unburnt wax vapor + small amount of pyrolysis products; oxygen absent | **~600 °C – 1000 °C** |
| **Zone 2** | **Luminous / Soot Zone** (middle) | Bright yellow | Incomplete combustion; soot particles incandescent by radiation; oxygen limited | **~1000 °C – 1400 °C** |
| **Zone 3** | **Non-luminous / Mantle / Outer Cone** | Faint blue, almost invisible | Complete combustion of CO → CO₂, soot → CO₂; excess oxygen present | **~1200 °C – 1400 °C** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy — "The Candle as a Tiny Volcano"**
> Imagine the wick as a **straw** that sucks melted wax upward by capillary action (just as oil rises in a lamp wick). The wax at the tip of the wick is heated until it **boils into vapor** (like water becoming steam). This vapor then meets oxygen from the surrounding air and **burns**, much like a campfire meets wind. The hot combustion gases rise (because hot air is lighter — this is **buoyancy**), drawing in fresh cool air from below. The yellow glow you see is not the gas itself, but **microscopic soot particles getting so hot they emit light** — the same physical principle as a glowing ember in a fireplace. The faint blue outer mantle is the **clean, complete combustion zone** where the soot finally burns away.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants / Metrics to remember (highlighted in bold):**
> - **Adiabatic flame temperature of paraffin wax in air ≈ 1400 °C (≈ 1673 K)**
> - **Heat of combustion of paraffin wax (C₂₅H₅₂): ΔH_c ≈ 42 – 46 MJ/kg**
> - **Heat of gasification / vaporization of wax: L_v ≈ 1000 kJ/kg (approx.)**
> - **Density of solid paraffin: ρ_wax ≈ 900 kg/m³**
> - **Soot volume fraction in luminous zone: f_v ≈ 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁵**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant: σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
> - **Standard candle mass burning rate: ṁ″ ≈ 1.5 – 2.0 g/hr (≈ 4–6 × 10⁻⁷ kg/s)** for a typical household candle (D ≈ 20 mm).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — "The Life-Cycle of a Candle Flame"

The candle flame is sustained by a self-regulating **feedback loop**. The KTU examiner frequently tests the *sequence* of processes, so memorise this ordered chain:

1. **Wick Drawing by Capillarity** — Molten wax is pulled up the wick fibres by capillary suction (governed by the Young–Laplace equation).
2. **Radiative & Convective Pre-heating of Wick** — The flame above the wick heats the wax-soaked wick by **conduction + radiation**, raising it above the vaporisation temperature.
3. **Pyrolysis / Vaporisation** — Endothermic phase change: solid/liquid wax → gaseous fuel vapour.
4. **Mixing by Buoyancy-Induced Convection** — Hot combustion products rise (buoyancy), drawing in fresh air from the sides; this induces turbulent mixing in large flames (laminar for small candles, $D < 30$ mm).
5. **Diffusion-Controlled Combustion** — Fuel vapour and oxygen meet by molecular diffusion in a thin reaction sheet.
6. **Soot Formation, Growth, Oxidation** — In fuel-rich regions, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) form soot. Soot radiates strongly (yellow light) and finally oxidises in the outer mantle.
7. **Heat Feedback to Fuel Surface** — A portion of flame heat is returned to the wick to sustain vaporisation. This is the **steady-state closure condition**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why capillary action?** Because the wick is porous, surface tension pulls liquid wax up through narrow gaps. Without the wick, the wax cannot be delivered to the flame zone.
- **Why does the flame have a yellow zone?** In fuel-rich, oxygen-poor interior, hydrocarbon pyrolysis produces **soot nanoparticles**. These are heated to incandescence (~1500 K), giving the **blackbody-like yellow emission**.
- **Why a blue outer mantle?** Oxygen from ambient air diffuses inward, completing combustion. The blue colour is due to **CH* and C₂ Swan band chemiluminescence**.
- **Why does the flame point upward?** Buoyancy — the hot gases (lower density than the surrounding air at ≈ 300 K) experience a net upward force per unit volume: $F_b = (\rho_\infty - \rho_f) g$.
- **Why is the flame stable?** The **heat feedback to the wick** exactly matches the heat required to vaporise the fuel at the observed mass burning rate — a self-consistent energy balance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE 2.1 — High-Yield Formulas for the Candle Flame (Module 3)**

| # | Equation | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{Q}''}{L_v + c_p (T_s - T_\infty)}$ | Mass burning rate per unit area from wick energy balance | kg/m²·s |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}''_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma T_f^{4}$ | Radiative heat flux from luminous soot zone | W/m² |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}''_{conv} = h (T_f - T_s)$ | Convective heat flux from flame to wick surface | W/m² |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho_w \, A_w \, \frac{dL}{dt}$ | Candle mass consumption rate (ρ_w = wax density) | kg/s |
| 5 | $Fr = \dfrac{u^2}{g D}$ | Froude number — ratio of inertial to buoyancy forces | dimensionless |
| 6 | $u(z) = \kappa \, \left( \dfrac{g \dot{Q} z}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty} \right)^{1/3}$ | Centerline plume velocity (Heskestad / Zukoski form) | m/s |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{u D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number; $Re < 2300 \Rightarrow$ laminar; typical candle $Re \sim 10$–$100$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta T = T_f - T_s$ | Driving temperature difference for heat feedback | K |
| 9 | $B = \dfrac{c_{p}(T_f - T_\infty) + Y_{O,\infty} H_c / r}{L_v}$ | Spalding mass transfer / B-number for the wick | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{h}{c_p} \ln(1 + B)$ | Standard diffusion-flame mass flux (Spalding form) | kg/m²·s |
| 11 | $L_{flame} \sim D \cdot Fr^{-1/3}$ or $L \sim (\dot{Q}/c_p \rho_\infty T_\infty g)^{2/5}$ | Visible flame height scaling (Zukoski/Heskestad) | m |
| 12 | $\dot{q}''_{cr} = \sigma (T_f^{4} - T_s^{4})$ | Net radiative feedback assuming black surroundings | W/m² |

> **NOTE on notation in Table 2.1:**
> $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporisation, $c_p$ = specific heat, $T_s$ = wick surface temperature, $T_\infty$ = ambient, $D$ = wick/flame diameter, $u$ = velocity, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity, $h$ = heat transfer coefficient, $Y_{O,\infty}$ = ambient oxygen mass fraction (≈ 0.233), $r$ = stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel mass ratio, $H_c$ = heat of combustion, $\kappa$ ≈ 3.4 (Zukoski constant), $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of luminous zone (≈ 0.5–0.8).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study a candle in B.Tech Fire Engineering?**
> 1. **Calibration Standard** — Candle flames are the *benchmark* against which all fire models (CFD, zone models) are validated because they are reproducible, axisymmetric, steady, and small.
> 2. **Pool-Fire Analogue** — The candle's behaviour extrapolates to industrial **pool fires** (oil tank fires, LNG spills). The same B-number formalism applies.
> 3. **Buoyancy-Driven Flow Physics** — A candle is the simplest laboratory apparatus to study **natural convection** plumes, often used to validate correlations for ceiling jets, smoke layer descent, and compartment fire flows.
> 4. **Soot & Radiation Studies** — Combustion chemists use candle flames to study **soot nucleation, growth, and oxidation** — critical for understanding radiative heat transfer in large-scale fires.
> 5. **Detector Testing** — Smoke detectors and flame detectors are routinely tested using standardised candle sources.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Mass Burning Rate of a Candle from Wick Energy Balance

> **Statement (KTU classic):** At steady state, the rate of heat supplied to the wick from the flame equals the rate of energy required to (a) heat the wax from ambient to vaporisation temperature, and (b) vaporise it. Derive an expression for the mass burning rate per unit wick area, $\dot{m}''$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us define:
- $T_\infty$ = ambient temperature (≈ 298 K)
- $T_s$ = wick surface temperature where vaporisation occurs (≈ wax boiling point ≈ 600–700 K)
- $T_f$ = flame temperature in luminous zone (≈ 1400 K)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporisation of wax (J/kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of liquid wax (J/kg·K)
- $\dot{m}''$ = mass burning rate per unit wick area (kg/m²·s)
- $\dot{Q}''_{in}$ = net heat flux delivered to the wick (W/m²)

**Step 1 — Energy conservation at the wick surface (steady state).**

The net heat arriving at the wick surface from the flame is consumed in heating the wax from $T_\infty$ to $T_s$ and in vaporising it. By the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, applied per unit area of wick:

$$
\dot{Q}''_{in} = \dot{m}'' \left[ c_p (T_s - T_\infty) + L_v \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Express the incoming heat flux in terms of flame temperature.**

The dominant mode of heat feedback from a small (laminar) candle flame is **conduction/convection from hot gas** plus **radiation from the luminous soot zone**. For simplicity, we lump these into an effective heat transfer coefficient $h$ (convective) and a radiative term:

$$
\dot{Q}''_{in} = h (T_f - T_s) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_f^{4} - T_s^{4})
$$

For a *first-order* analytical model (KTU-board preferred), we **neglect the radiative term** in small flames and write simply:

$$
\dot{Q}''_{in} \approx h (T_f - T_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\dot{Q}''_{in}$.**

$$
h (T_f - T_s) = \dot{m}'' \left[ c_p (T_s - T_\infty) + L_v \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $\dot{m}''$.**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{h (T_f - T_s)}{c_p (T_s - T_\infty) + L_v}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify this as the Spalding-form mass transfer rate.**

The non-dimensional **B-number (Spalding transfer number)** is defined as:

$$
B = \frac{c_p (T_f - T_\infty) + Y_{O,\infty} H_c / r}{L_v}
$$

And the more general diffusion-flame expression is:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{h}{c_p} \ln(1 + B)
$$

This is the **Spalding mass-transfer equation** for non-premixed combustion. For a candle, the simplified version derived in Step 4 is sufficient and expected by KTU examiners.

> **Physical interpretation:** $\dot{m}''$ scales linearly with $h$ and flame temperature, and inversely with the "energy demand" $c_p \Delta T + L_v$ of vaporising the wax.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buoyant Plume Velocity Above a Candle (Heskestad / Zukoski Correlation)

> **Statement:** The hot gases rising above the candle behave as a **buoyant plume**. Derive the centerline velocity $u(z)$ at height $z$ above the wick, given the convective heat release rate $\dot{Q}$ (W).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the convective heat release rate.**

The total rate at which enthalpy is released into the rising plume:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p (T_f - T_\infty) = \rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \int_0^{\infty} u \, 2\pi r \, \frac{\Delta T}{T_\infty} \, dr
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gaussian (top-hat) profile assumption for velocity and temperature.**

For an idealised **top-hat plume** of radius $b(z)$ with uniform velocity $u(z)$ and uniform temperature rise $\Delta T(z)$:

$$
\dot{Q} = \rho_\infty c_p \, \pi b^2(z) \, u(z) \, \Delta T(z)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of vertical momentum (buoyancy-driven).**

The buoyancy force per unit height is $g \rho_\infty \pi b^2 \Delta T / T_\infty$. Integrating:

$$
\frac{d}{dz} \left( \rho_\infty \pi b^2 u^2 \right) = \rho_\infty \pi b^2 \frac{g \Delta T}{T_\infty}
$$

**Step 4 — Use entrainment hypothesis (Zukoski):** $u \, db/dz = \alpha \, u$, i.e. plume radius grows linearly with height: $b(z) = \alpha z$ (with $\alpha \approx 0.1$–$0.15$ for round plumes).

**Step 5 — Combine into a single ODE for $u(z)$.**

Substituting $b = \alpha z$ and using the energy integral, after some algebra (Heskestad, 1972), we obtain:

$$
u(z) = \kappa \left( \frac{g \dot{Q} z}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty} \right)^{1/3}
$$

where $\kappa \approx 3.4$ is the **Heskestad constant** for round (axisymmetric) plumes.

**Step 6 — Specialise to a candle.**

For a typical candle: $\dot{Q} \approx 50$ W (convective fraction of a 60 W total heat release). At $z = 0.05$ m (5 cm above wick), $T_\infty = 300$ K, $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1000$ J/kg·K:

$$
u(0.05) = 3.4 \left( \frac{9.81 \times 50 \times 0.05}{1.2 \times 1000 \times 300} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
u = 3.4 \times (0.0000682)^{1/3} = 3.4 \times 0.0409 \approx 0.14 \text{ m/s}
$$

This matches experimentally observed values of **plume velocity above a small candle (≈ 0.1–0.3 m/s)**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Froude Number & Regime Identification

> **Statement:** Show that the candle flame is **buoyancy-controlled** (low Froude number).

The Froude number is the ratio of inertial to gravitational (buoyancy) forces:

$$
Fr = \frac{u^2}{g D}
$$

For a candle: $u \sim 0.2$ m/s, $D = 0.02$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
Fr = \frac{(0.2)^2}{9.81 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.04}{0.1962} \approx 0.20
$$

Since **Fr ≪ 1**, buoyancy dominates over inertia ⇒ **the flame is buoyancy-driven (often called the "natural-convection" or "low-Froude" regime)**. This is exactly the same regime as ceiling jets in compartment fires.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

### **Q1. With a neat sketch, describe the three zones of a candle flame and state the dominant chemical/physical process in each zone. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The candle flame consists of three concentric zones:
1. **Inner dark zone** (≈ 600–1000 °C): Contains unburnt wax vapour and pyrolysis products. Temperature too low for soot incandescence; appears dark.
2. **Luminous zone** (≈ 1000–1400 °C): Fuel-rich region where soot particles form by pyrolysis of hydrocarbons and PAH condensation. The soot is heated to incandescence, emitting the characteristic **yellow luminous radiation** (black-body like).
3. **Outer non-luminous mantle** (≈ 1200–1400 °C, faint blue): Oxygen diffuses in from the ambient air and completes combustion — soot oxidises to CO₂, CO oxidises to CO₂. The blue colour is due to CH* and C₂ Swan-band chemiluminescence. (3 Marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

### **Q2. Why is the candle flame classified as a buoyancy-driven diffusion flame? Justify with the Froude number. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A candle flame is a **non-premixed (diffusion) flame** because fuel vapour and oxidiser mix by molecular and convective diffusion rather than being pre-mixed. It is **buoyancy-driven** because the hot combustion products (T_f ≈ 1400 K) have lower density than the ambient air (T_∞ ≈ 300 K), generating a net upward body force. The Froude number is:

$$
Fr = \frac{u^2}{g D} \approx \frac{(0.2)^2}{9.81 \times 0.02} \approx 0.2 \ll 1
$$

Since **Fr ≪ 1**, buoyancy forces dominate over inertial forces, justifying the classification as buoyancy-driven. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU 2024 ESE style):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Energy Balance and Wick Heat Feedback

**(a)** Starting from the first law of thermodynamics applied to the wick surface, derive the **steady-state mass burning rate per unit wick area** for a laminar diffusion flame such as a candle, clearly defining each term. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For a typical household candle, the convective heat transfer coefficient from the flame to the wick is $h = 50 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, the flame temperature is $T_f = 1373$ K, the wick vaporisation temperature is $T_s = 673$ K, the specific heat of liquid wax is $c_p = 2000$ J/kg·K, the ambient temperature is $T_\infty = 300$ K, and the latent heat of vaporisation is $L_v = 1000$ kJ/kg. **Calculate** the mass burning rate per unit area. If the wick diameter is 20 mm, also **estimate the total mass consumption rate (g/hr)**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** per unit area of wick at steady state:

$$
\dot{Q}''_{in} = \dot{m}'' \left[ c_p (T_s - T_\infty) + L_v \right]
$$

The incoming heat flux is dominated by convection from the flame:

$$
\dot{Q}''_{in} = h (T_f - T_s)
$$

Equating and solving for $\dot{m}''$:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{h (T_f - T_s)}{c_p (T_s - T_\infty) + L_v}
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}''$ = mass burning rate per unit area (kg/m²·s)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_f$ = flame temperature (K)
- $T_s$ = wick vaporisation temperature (K)
- $T_\infty$ = ambient temperature (K)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of wax (J/kg·K)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporisation (J/kg)

This is the **Spalding-form** mass burning equation for a diffusion flame. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (b)

Substituting values:

- $T_f - T_s = 1373 - 673 = 700$ K
- $T_s - T_\infty = 673 - 300 = 373$ K
- $c_p (T_s - T_\infty) = 2000 \times 373 = 7.46 \times 10^{5}$ J/kg
- $L_v = 1.0 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg
- $h (T_f - T_s) = 50 \times 700 = 3.5 \times 10^{4}$ W/m²

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{4}}{7.46 \times 10^{5} + 1.0 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{4}}{1.746 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}'' \approx 0.0200 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}
$$

**Total mass consumption rate:**

Wick area: $A = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (0.02)^2 / 4 = 3.14 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

$$
\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \times A = 0.0200 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} = 6.28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}
$$

Convert to g/hr: $6.28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} \approx 22.6$ g/hr.

**This is in the right order of magnitude for a household candle (typically 5–25 g/hr).** ✓ (7 Marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Buoyant Plume Above a Candle and Froude Analysis

**(a)** Derive the **Heskestad / Zukoski correlation** for the centerline velocity of a buoyant plume above a small diffusion flame. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A candle releases $\dot{Q} = 60$ W of convective heat. Using $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1000$ J/kg·K, $T_\infty = 300$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s², **compute the centerline plume velocity at heights** $z = 0.05$ m and $z = 0.20$ m above the wick. **Comment** on the implications for detector placement in a fire-engineering context. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, axisymmetric, turbulent (or laminar) round plume.
2. Top-hat profiles of velocity and temperature in the plume cross-section.
3. Entrainment: plume radius grows as $b(z) = \alpha z$, with $\alpha \approx 0.1$.
4. Pressure is approximately atmospheric throughout.
5. Boussinesq approximation: $\Delta \rho / \rho \ll 1$, treat $\rho$ as constant except in the buoyancy term.

**Derivation outline:**

- **Conservation of mass (entrainment):** $\frac{d}{dz}(\rho u \pi b^2) = 2\pi b \rho v_e$, with entrainment velocity $v_e = \alpha u$.
- **Conservation of vertical momentum:** $\frac{d}{dz}(\rho u^2 \pi b^2) = \rho_\infty g \pi b^2 \Delta T / T_\infty$.
- **Conservation of energy:** $\dot{Q} = \rho_\infty c_p \pi b^2 u \Delta T$ (constant with height).
- Combining the three integrals using $b = \alpha z$ yields:

$$
u(z) = \kappa \left( \frac{g \dot{Q} z}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty} \right)^{1/3}, \quad \kappa \approx 3.4
$$

(7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (b)

Apply the correlation:

$$
u(z) = 3.4 \left( \frac{9.81 \times 60 \times z}{1.2 \times 1000 \times 300} \right)^{1/3} = 3.4 \left( \frac{588.6 \, z}{3.6 \times 10^5} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
u(z) = 3.4 \times (1.635 \times 10^{-3} \, z)^{1/3}
$$

**At $z = 0.05$ m:**

$$
u = 3.4 \times (8.18 \times 10^{-5})^{1/3} = 3.4 \times 0.0434 = 0.148 \text{ m/s}
$$

**At $z = 0.20$ m:**

$$
u = 3.4 \times (3.27 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 3.4 \times 0.0690 = 0.235 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Comment for fire-engineering context:**

- Plume velocity **increases with height** (as $z^{1/3}$) due to mass entrainment.
- For a single candle this is small (~0.1–0.3 m/s), but the correlation **scales to large fires** — in a 5 MW compartment fire, plume velocities near the ceiling can exceed **3–5 m/s**.
- **Detector placement:** smoke and heat detectors are placed on ceilings where the buoyant plume impinges. The candle's plume helps calibrate **smoke transport time** — for a candle, smoke reaches the ceiling in seconds, ensuring rapid activation.
- For very small flames (like a candle), buoyancy may be insufficient to overcome ambient drafts, leading to **flame tilt** or even extinction — hence the candle's **extinction diameter** (≈ 1 mm). (7 Marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 1. **Confusing inner dark zone with luminous zone** — the inner zone is the *unburnt vapour* region; soot formation (luminous) is the *middle* zone, NOT the innermost. Many students invert the order. (**−1 to −2 marks**)
> 2. **Forgetting to define the wick surface temperature $T_s$** separately from the flame temperature $T_f$ in derivations. KTU examiners award 1 mark each for clear definitions. (**−1 mark**)
> 3. **Using $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \Delta H_c$ without specifying "convective fraction"** — in plume correlations, only the convective portion drives the plume. Mentioning this distinction shows depth. (**−1 mark**)
> 4. **Writing $g$ in the wrong units** (cm/s² vs m/s²) — a common numerical error. Always convert to SI. (**−0.5 to −1 mark**)
> 5. **Omitting the radiation term** in the energy balance for large flames — for a candle, convection dominates, but for larger pool fires, radiation can be 30–40%. State the assumption. (**−1 mark**)
> 6. **Mixing up Spalding B-number with the heat of combustion** — they are related but B is dimensionless. (**−0.5 mark**)
> 7. **Drawing the candle flame upside down** (flame pointing downward) in sketches — KTU graders do deduct for this. (**−0.5 mark**)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **KEY TAKEAWAYS — Candle Flame (Module 3)**

- ☐ A **candle flame is a buoyancy-driven, laminar, non-premixed diffusion flame**.
- ☐ **Three zones**: (1) Inner dark zone (vapour, 600–1000 °C), (2) Luminous yellow zone (soot incandescence, 1000–1400 °C), (3) Outer blue mantle (complete combustion, ~1400 °C).
- ☐ **Yellow glow = soot** heated to incandescence; **blue mantle = chemiluminescence** of CH* and C₂.
- ☐ The **wick** delivers liquid wax by **capillarity**; vaporisation requires latent heat $L_v$ ≈ 1000 kJ/kg.
- ☐ **Adiabatic flame temperature** of wax in air ≈ **1400 °C (1673 K)**.
- ☐ Mass burning rate from wick energy balance: $\dot{m}'' = h(T_f - T_s) / [c_p(T_s - T_\infty) + L_v]$.
- ☐ **Spalding B-number**: $B = c_p \Delta T / L_v \approx 30$–$60$ for paraffin; large B → strong vaporisation.
- ☐ **Buoyant plume velocity** (Heskestad): $u(z) = \kappa [g \dot{Q} z / (\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty)]^{1/3}$, $\kappa \approx 3.4$.
- ☐ **Froude number** $Fr = u^2 / gD \ll 1$ for a candle (≈ 0.2) → buoyancy-controlled regime.
- ☐ **Reynolds number** $Re = uD/\nu \sim 50$ for a small candle → laminar.
- ☐ **Radiative heat flux** from luminous zone: $\dot{q}''_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma T_f^4$, with $\varepsilon \approx 0.5$–$0.8$.
- ☐ **Heat feedback loop**: flame → wick (conduction + convection + radiation) → vaporisation → fuel vapour → combustion → flame. Steady state.
- ☐ **Self-extinction limit**: candle flame cannot be sustained below wick/wick-diameter ≈ **1 mm** (conduction losses exceed heat release).
- ☐ The candle is the **benchmark for validating fire models** (CFD, zone models, plume correlations).
- ☐ Candle physics **extrapolates to pool fires** (industrial-scale) — same B-number, same plume scaling, different length scale.

---

> *End of Module 3 — Candle Flame Notes*
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT528) Board Examination Standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of Diffusion Flame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Structure of Diffusion Flame ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Diffusion Flame** is a flame in which the fuel and the oxidizer are **not premixed** prior to ignition. The combustion process is therefore controlled by the rate at which the fuel vapour and the oxidizer (typically air) intermingle through molecular and turbulent **mass diffusion** across a reaction zone, rather than by the rate of chemical reaction (which is assumed infinitely fast in classical theory). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Box — Burke–Schumann Diffusion Flame** > A classical, *steady, 1-D, laminar diffusion flame* in which the fuel and oxidizer are introduced as two separate streams, mix by molecular diffusion, and react at a thin *stoichiometric surface* where the local mixture is in exact chemical-proportion. Outside this surface, only one of the two reactants is present; the temperature rises monotonically from each stream toward the reaction sheet. The **Structure of a Diffusion Flame** refers to the **spatial distribution of temperature, species concentrations, density, and reaction rate** as a function of position across the flame — i.e., the *internal anatomy* of a non-premixed combustion zone. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Two Rivers Meeting at a Burning Curtain"** Imagine two rivers flowing side-by-side in opposite directions. The fuel river is on one side, the air river on the other. They are separated by a thin, invisible membrane. As molecules diffuse across this membrane, the moment a fuel molecule meets an oxygen molecule at exactly the right proportion, they ignite. This forms a **glowing curtain of fire** (the stoichiometric surface) that stands between the two streams. There is fuel on one side of the curtain (unburnt), pure air on the other side (unburnt), and at the curtain itself, complete combustion. **The flame is essentially a thin reaction sheet suspended in a mixing field.** The *structure* of this flame is simply the description of how temperature and species vary from one side of the curtain to the other. ### 1.3 Distinguishing Features of a Diffusion Flame - **Non-premixed** — fuel and oxidizer are initially separate. - **Reaction-limited by transport, not kinetics** (in classical Burke–Schumann). - A **stoichiometric surface (Z = Z_st)** exists where the flame "sits." - **High soot** content — fuel-rich pockets on the fuel side pyrolyse before reaching the sheet. - **Yellow/luminous** in appearance (soot radiation), unlike the blue premixed flame. - Examples: candle flame, pool fire, jet diffusion flame, diesel engine combustion, wildfire spread. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** For PESFT528 Module 3, students are expected to know the **Burke–Schumann solution**, the role of the **mixture fraction Z**, the **stoichiometric mixture fraction Z_st**, the shape of **species profiles**, the **flame-temperature distribution**, and the **mass burning rate per unit area**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Stoichiometric air–fuel mass ratio for CH₄: **s = 17.16** - Stoichiometric mixture fraction (CH₄ in air): **Z_st ≈ 0.055** - Laminar flame thickness δ_f ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 mm** (typical) - Adiabatic flame temperature (CH₄/air): **T_ad ≈ 2223 K** - Lewis number Le = α/D ≈ **1** (for most gaseous fuels in air) - Universal gas constant: **R_u = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework **Step 1 — Identify the controlling transport process.** In a diffusion flame, mixing (diffusion) is rate-limiting. The chemistry is so fast (relative to mixing) that reaction can be modelled as *infinitely fast* — Burke–Schumann assumption. **Step 2 — Introduce the mixture fraction Z (the central scalar).** Z is a *conserved scalar* that is unaffected by chemistry. It represents the mass fraction of material originating from the fuel stream. $$Z(\vec{r}) = \frac{m_{\text{fuel-stream}}(\vec{r})}{m_{\text{total}}(\vec{r})}$$ - Z = 1 in the pure fuel stream - Z = 0 in the pure oxidizer stream - Reaction occurs at a single iso-surface: **Z = Z_st** **Step 3 — Conservation of the conserved scalar.** In the absence of heat losses and for unity Lewis number, Z obeys a steady advection–diffusion equation identical in form to the heat equation. **Step 4 — Solve for Z(r) using similarity (for canonical geometries — flat plate, round jet, counterflow).** Classical analytic solutions exist for: (a) 1-D planar stagnation flow, (b) round jet, (c) counterflow. **Step 5 — Obtain the stoichiometric surface** by setting Z = Z_st. **Step 6 — Map temperature and species fields onto Z via thermodynamic state relationships.** **Step 7 — Compute the local mass burning rate** at the stoichiometric surface using the gradient of Z. ### 2.2 Governing Equations (Steady, Low-Mach, Constant-ρ Boundary-Layer) **Continuity:** $$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{u}) = 0$$ **Momentum:** $$\rho \vec{u} \cdot \nabla \vec{u} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{u}$$ **Energy (in terms of mixture fraction):** $$\rho \vec{u} \cdot \nabla T = \alpha \nabla^{2} T + \frac{\dot{q}_{\text{chem}}}{c_p}$$ **Species (Favre-averaged):** $$\rho \vec{u} \cdot \nabla Y_i = \nabla \cdot (\rho D_i \nabla Y_i) + \dot{\omega}_i$$ **Conserved scalar (mixture fraction):** $$\rho \vec{u} \cdot \nabla Z = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla Z)$$ ### 2.3 Stoichiometric Mixture Fraction (Derivation Outline) For the global reaction: $$\nu_F F + \nu_{O_2} O_2 \rightarrow \text{Products}$$ The stoichiometric condition is: $$Y_{F,st} = \frac{\nu_F W_F}{\nu_{O_2} W_{O_2}} \cdot Y_{O_2,st}$$ At the stoichiometric surface, fuel and oxidizer arrived in stoichiometric ratio from their respective streams: $$Z_{st} = \frac{Y_{F,\infty} / (\nu_F W_F)}{Y_{F,\infty} / (\nu_F W_F) \;+\; Y_{O_2,\infty} / (\nu_{O_2} W_{O_2})}$$ or equivalently: $$Z_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + s}, \quad s = \frac{\nu_{O_2} W_{O_2}}{\nu_F W_F} \cdot \frac{Y_{F,\infty}}{Y_{O_2,\infty}}$$ where **s** is the local stoichiometric air/fuel mass ratio. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---------------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Mixture fraction definition | $Z = \dfrac{Y_F - (Y_O/s)}{Y_{F,0} - (Y_{O,0}/s)}$ | Conserved scalar, dimensionless | | 2 | Stoichiometric Z | $Z_{st} = \dfrac{1}{1+s}$ | $s$ = stoich. air–fuel ratio | | 3 | Burke–Schumann governing equation | $\rho \vec u \cdot \nabla Z = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla Z)$ | Identical to heat eqn. for Le=1 | | 4 | Energy in terms of Z | $T = T_{\infty} + (T_{ad} - T_{\infty}) \cdot \dfrac{Z(1-Z)}{Z_{st}(1-Z_{st})}$ | Peak T at $Z = Z_{st}$ | | 5 | Local fuel mass consumption rate | $\dot m''_F = \rho D \left( \dfrac{\partial Z}{\partial n} \right)_{Z_{st}} \cdot (1 - Z_{st})$ | kg/(m²·s) | | 6 | Local oxygen consumption rate | $\dot m''_O = \rho D \left( \dfrac{\partial Z}{\partial n} \right)_{Z_{st}} \cdot Z_{st}$ | kg/(m²·s) | | 7 | Flame sheet location | $Z(x,y) = Z_{st}$ | Iso-surface (1-D: a point) | | 8 | Flame thickness (Burke–Schumann) | $\delta_f \approx \dfrac{D}{S_L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad} - T_{\infty}}{T_{\infty}}$ | Order ~0.5 mm | | 9 | Jet diffusion flame height (Roper) | $L_f = \dfrac{3.5 \, \dot V \, d}{D}$ | m, for round jet in still air | | 10 | Dimensionless flame height | $L_f^{*}=\dfrac{L_f \, \rho_\infty \, \sqrt{g d}}{(\rho D)_f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\ln(1+1/Z_{st})}$ | − | | 11 | Adiabatic flame temperature | $T_{ad} = T_\infty + \dfrac{Y_{F,\infty} \, \Delta H_c}{c_p (1 + s)}$ | K | | 12 | Mixture-fraction variance (turbulent) | $\widetilde{Z''^{2}}$ | Favre-averaged RMS | | 13 | Reaction progress variable | $c = 1$ in products, $c = 0$ in reactants | Used in turbulent flames | | 14 | Bilger's definition of Z | $Z = \dfrac{2(Z' - Z'_{st})}{1 - 2 Z'_{st}}$ | Normalised, Z'=elemental mass fraction | | 15 | Heat-release per unit volume | $\dot q''' = \rho \, \Delta H_c \, \dot \omega_F$ | W/m³ | > [!NOTE] > **Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\lvert x \rvert$` in your own handwritten exam scripts instead of `|x|`, since Markdown parsers can mis-interpret the vertical bar inside LaTeX tables.** ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The mixture-fraction framework is the **backbone of modern CFD combustion modelling**, used in: - **Fire safety engineering**: simulating compartment fires, smoke movement. - **Gas-turbine combustors**: predicting NOx and soot in non-premixed flames. - **Rocket engine design**: liquid bipropellant injectors (LOX/kerosene, LOX/H₂). - **Wildfire propagation**: modelling buoyant plumes over fuel beds. - **Diesel engine CFD**: non-premixed combustion in direct-injection engines. - **Hydrogen safety**: diffusion flames in accidental releases. --- ## 3.
