Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Mixture-Fraction Transport Equation **Starting point — Species balance for an arbitrary element / atomic mass fraction:** $$\rho \vec{u} \cdot \nabla Y_i = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_i + \dot{\omega}_i$$ The **diffusion flux** of species *i* in a low-Mach flow (Fick's law): $$\vec{J}_i = -\rho D_i \nabla Y_i$$ Choose a linear combination of species mass fractions that **eliminates the chemical source term** (since the total mass of each chemical element is conserved). The conserved-scalar Z defined as: $$Z = \frac{Y_C - Y_{C,O}/(s \, W_{O_2}/W_F)}{Y_{C,F} - Y_{C,O}/(s \, W_{O_2}/W_F)}$$ (equivalent Bilger's normalised form can also be used). For a **single-step reaction ν_F F + ν_{O₂} O₂ → P**, the elemental-mass fraction of carbon is: $$Y_C = \nu_F W_C Y_F / W_F$$ The species balance for Z is: $$\rho \vec u \cdot \nabla Z = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla Z)$$ This is the **Burke–Schumann transport equation for the mixture fraction**, structurally identical to the heat-conduction equation. > **Conversion logic:** Adding the fuel species balance and the oxygen species balance weighted by the stoichiometric ratio produces cancellation of the chemical source — a pure advection–diffusion equation results. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Temperature Profile in a Burke–Schumann Flame **Step 1.** Under the assumption of **unity Lewis number (Le = α/D = 1)**, the temperature field obeys the same transport equation as Z. **Step 2.** Far from the reaction sheet, no heat is released; the temperature matches the freestream. **Step 3.** Energy balance integrated across the thin reaction sheet yields a **jump condition**: the conductive heat flux into the sheet from the fuel side equals the conductive flux into the sheet from the oxidiser side, plus the heat of combustion released. **Step 4.** Solving these two linear ODEs on either side of the sheet and matching the jump gives, for Le = 1: $$T(Z) = T_\infty + (T_{ad} - T_\infty) \cdot \frac{Z(1 - Z)}{Z_{st}(1 - Z_{st})}$$ **Step-by-step expansion:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Fuel side } (Z > Z_{st}): \quad & T = T_F - (T_F - T_{ad}) \cdot \dfrac{1 - Z}{1 - Z_{st}} \\[4pt] \text{Oxidiser side } (Z < Z_{st}): \quad & T = T_O + (T_{ad} - T_O) \cdot \dfrac{Z}{Z_{st}} \end{aligned}$$ These two linear pieces meet at the peak, T = T_ad at Z = Z_st. **Step 5.** Peak temperature (adiabatic flame temperature) is independent of Z_st (only of fuel and oxidiser enthalpy): $$T_{ad} = T_\infty + \frac{Y_{F,\infty} \cdot \Delta H_c}{c_p (1 + s)}$$ **Step 6.** The temperature profile is *parabolic* in Z (when plotted against Z), with maximum at Z = Z_st. > **Conversion logic:** Because chemistry is *infinitely fast*, all heat release happens at Z = Z_st, so temperature is a continuous, piecewise-linear function of Z between the two streams and Z_st. The continuity of T and its derivative at Z = Z_st (with Le = 1) produces the symmetrical quadratic form above. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Mass Burning Rate per Unit Area **Step 1.** The local fuel mass flux being consumed is determined by the rate at which fuel diffuses toward the stoichiometric surface. **Step 2.** Apply Fick's law in the direction *n* normal to the reaction sheet: $$\dot m''_F = -\rho D \left( \frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial n} \right)_{\text{at sheet}}$$ **Step 3.** Relate Y_F to Z. For the Burke–Schumann case with no oxygen on the fuel side and no fuel on the oxidiser side: $$Y_F(Z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{(1 - Z) Y_{F,\infty}}{1 - Z_{st}}, & Z > Z_{st} \\ 0, & Z < Z_{st} \end{cases}$$ **Step 4.** Differentiate: $$\frac{\partial Y_F}{\partial n} = -Y_{F,\infty} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - Z_{st}} \cdot \frac{\partial Z}{\partial n}$$ **Step 5.** Therefore the local mass-burning flux is: $$\dot m''_F = \rho D \, Y_{F,\infty} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - Z_{st}} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial Z}{\partial n} \right)_{Z_{st}}$$ Similarly, the oxygen-side expression reduces to: $$\dot m''_O = \rho D \, Y_{O_2,\infty} \cdot \frac{1}{Z_{st}} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial Z}{\partial n} \right)_{Z_{st}}$$ **Step 6.** Since the stoichiometric condition requires $\dot m''_F / (\nu_F W_F) = \dot m''_O / (\nu_{O_2} W_{O_2})$, both expressions are equal — a *self-consistency check* of the Burke–Schumann model. > **Conversion logic:** The mass-burning rate is purely **diffusion-controlled** — kinetics do not appear. The gradient of Z at the sheet depends on the geometry, flow, and freestream concentrations. --- ### 3.4 Derivation of the Round-Jet Diffusion Flame Height (Roper's Analysis) **Step 1.** Consider a round turbulent jet of fuel of diameter **d** issuing with mean velocity **U** into still air. **Step 2.** The flame height is reached where the local fuel-jet velocity has decelerated to a level at which the fuel is fully entrained and consumed. **Step 3.** Define the flame height as the location where the centreline mixture fraction equals Z_st: $$Z_{\text{cl}}(L_f) = Z_{st}$$ **Step 4.** For a round jet, the conserved-scalar solution (Favre-averaged, self-similar Gaussian) gives: $$Z_{\text{cl}}(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\alpha Z x / d)^{2}}}$$ **Step 5.** Setting Z_cl(L_f) = Z_st and inverting: $$L_f = \frac{d}{\alpha Z} \sqrt{\frac{1}{Z_{st}^{2}} - 1}$$ which, for dilute fuel (Z_st ≪ 1), reduces to the **Roper form**: $$L_f = \frac{C \, d}{\alpha \, Z_{st}}, \quad C \approx 3.5$$ where α is the entrainment coefficient of the jet (~0.16 for round turbulent jets). > **Conversion logic:** The flame height is proportional to **fuel-jet diameter** and inversely proportional to the stoichiometric mixture fraction. Lean fuels (low Z_st) produce *tall* flames; rich fuels (high Z_st) produce *short* flames. --- ### 3.5 The Damköhler Number Criterion for Diffusion Flames A diffusion flame is governed by the **first Damköhler number**: $$Da_I = \frac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} = \frac{L \cdot \dot{\omega} / \rho}{U / L} = \frac{L^{2} \dot{\omega}}{\rho U}$$ - **Da_I ≫ 1** — chemistry is fast, mixing is rate-limiting → *diffusion flame regime* (Burke–Schumann valid). - **Da_I ≪ 1** — chemistry is slow, well-mixed reactor-like → *premixed / kinetic regime*. For most engineering flames: **Da_I > 10³** — firmly in the diffusion-limited regime. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Anatomy of a Burke–Schumann Diffusion Flame The diagram below represents the **block-level functional architecture** of a coaxial jet diffusion flame, showing the four spatial regions, the conserved scalar Z, and the species that exist in each region. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[FUEL STREAM
Pure fuel YF equals 1
Z equals 1
Temperature TF] --> B[FUEL SIDE REGION
Z greater than Zst
Only fuel present
Y fuel decreases toward sheet
T rises linearly to Tad] B --> C[REACTION SHEET
Z equals Zst
Infinite rate chemistry
Peak temperature Tad
No fuel no oxidiser
Heat release per unit area] C --> D[OXIDISER SIDE REGION
Z less than Zst
Only oxygen present
Y O2 decreases toward sheet
T rises linearly to Tad] D --> E[OXIDISER STREAM
Pure air YO2 equals 0.232
Z equals 0
Temperature TO equals Tinf] C --> F[DIFFUSIVE TRANSPORT
Fuels diffuses inward
O2 diffuses inward
Products diffuse outward
governed by D and grad Z] F -.heat.-> C A -.mass.-> F E -.mass.-> F C --> G[MASS BURNING RATE
mdot prime prime equals rho D grad Z at Zst
times 1 minus Zst times YF inf] G --> H[FLAME GEOMETRY
Stoichiometric surface isosurface
Liftoff height L
Flame thickness delta f] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram interpretation:** The reaction sheet (block C) is *infinitely thin* in the Burke–Schumann limit. Block F is the *physical* diffusion pathway that delivers reactants to the sheet and removes products. The whole structure is symmetric only in the *Z*-coordinate, not in physical space — the fuel side is *narrow* (because Z_st is small for hydrocarbons in air) and the oxidiser side is *wide*. ### 4.2 Species and Temperature Profiles vs. Mixture Fraction Z A schematic representation of how temperature and species mass fractions depend on the conserved scalar Z is given below. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph AX["Z = 0 (air) ......... Z_st ......... Z = 1 (pure fuel)"] P1["T profile
linear on each side
peak Tad at Zst"] --- P2["Y fuel profile
linear drop from YF inf to 0 at Zst"] --- P3["Y O2 profile
linear drop from YO2 inf to 0 at Zst"] --- P4["Y products profile
rises from 0 to YP max at Zst
constant on fuel side as soot"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Important reading:** In a *real* (non-Burke–Schumann) diffusion flame, all curves are **smooth**, not piecewise-linear. The smoothing is controlled by the *finite* rate of chemistry, characterised by the **scalar dissipation rate** $\chi = 2 D (\nabla Z)^2$. High $\chi$ → extinction; low $\chi$ → near-equilibrium flame. ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Computation of Flame Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Define geometry and freestream] --> S2[Step 2: Solve velocity and pressure field] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Solve transport of Z and Z variance] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Locate stoichiometric surface Z equals Zst] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute T profile using Z and adiabatic relation] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute species profiles from Z and equilibrium] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compute local mass burning rate from grad Z at Zst] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Integrate to get total burning rate and flame shape] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define a diffusion flame. With a neat sketch, explain the structure of a laminar diffusion flame and identify the location of the reaction zone.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **diffusion flame** is a non-premixed flame in which the fuel and oxidiser are introduced as separate streams, and combustion occurs only at the interface where they meet and mix by molecular diffusion. *Structure (1 mark):* The flame has three regions: 1. **Fuel side** — only fuel, no oxygen; the temperature rises from the freestream value T_F to the adiabatic flame temperature T_ad. 2. **Reaction zone / Stoichiometric surface** — a thin sheet at the location where the local mixture is in exact stoichiometric proportion; chemistry is complete; T = T_ad (peak). 3. **Oxidiser side** — only oxidiser; temperature falls from T_ad back to T_∞. *Identification of reaction zone (1 mark):* The reaction zone coincides with the **iso-surface Z = Z_st**, the *stoichiometric mixture-fraction surface*. Outside this surface, one of the two reactants is fully absent; at the surface, both are consumed to extinction. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — ½-mark deduction:** Students often confuse the *stoichiometric surface* with the *flame sheet thickness*. The stoichiometric surface is a mathematical *iso-Z* line/surface; the reaction-zone thickness is a *finite* (but small, ~0.5 mm) region of intense heat release. Both must be distinguished. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State the assumptions of the classical Burke–Schumann theory of a diffusion flame. Why is the mixture fraction called a "conserved scalar"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Assumptions of Burke–Schumann theory (2 marks):* 1. Steady, laminar, 1-D flow. 2. Single-step, infinitely fast chemical reaction. 3. Equal diffusivities for all species (Le = 1) and unity Lewis number (α/D = 1). 4. Constant thermodynamic properties (ρ, μ, c_p, D). 5. No heat losses to the surroundings (adiabatic flame). 6. No body forces except buoyancy (where applicable). *Mixture fraction is a "conserved scalar" (1 mark):* Because Z is a linear combination of elemental mass fractions (or, equivalently, of species mass fractions weighted to cancel the chemical source term), it satisfies a pure advection–diffusion equation with **no source term**: $$\rho \vec u \cdot \nabla Z = \nabla \cdot (\rho D \nabla Z)$$ Hence Z is unaffected by chemical reaction — it is *conserved* through the flame. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Burke–Schumann Solution & Temperature Profile* [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3] **(a)** Derive the temperature profile across a 1-D Burke–Schumann diffusion flame between a pure fuel stream and a pure oxidiser stream, stating clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A methane–air diffusion flame has Y_{F,∞} = 1, Y_{O₂,∞} = 0.232, ν_F = 1, ν_{O₂} = 2, W_F = 16, W_{O₂} = 32 kg/kmol, T_∞ = 300 K, c_p = 1200 J/(kg·K), ΔH_c = 50 MJ/kg. Calculate (i) the stoichiometric mixture fraction Z_st, (ii) the adiabatic flame temperature T_ad, and (iii) the local mass-burning flux if the gradient of Z at the sheet is ∂Z/∂n = 200 m⁻¹ and ρD = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *Assumptions (1 mark):* (i) Steady, 1-D, adiabatic. (ii) Le = α/D = 1. (iii) Single-step, infinitely fast reaction. (iv) Constant properties. *Governing equation (1 mark):* For Le = 1, T and Z obey the same equation: $$\rho u \frac{dZ}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\rho D \frac{dZ}{dy}\right), \qquad \rho u \frac{dT}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\rho D \frac{dT}{dy}\right)$$ *Integrate twice with boundary conditions (2 marks):* - y → −∞ (oxidiser side): Z = 0, T = T_∞ - y → +∞ (fuel side): Z = 1, T = T_F (often T_F = T_∞ in symmetric counterflow) *Jump condition at the sheet (1 mark):* The energy balance at the infinitely thin reaction sheet gives: $$\rho D \left[\left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)^{+} - \left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)^{-}\right] = \dot q''$$ which, combined with the linearity of T(Z) on each side, yields: $$T(Z) = T_\infty + (T_{ad} - T_\infty) \cdot \frac{Z(1 - Z)}{Z_{st}(1 - Z_{st})}$$ *Final form and comment (2 marks):* Maximum temperature T = T_ad occurs exactly at Z = Z_st. The temperature profile is parabolic in Z and continuous, with a discontinuous second derivative at Z = Z_st (corresponding to the sheet heat release). --- **Part (b) — Numerical evaluation (7 marks):** **(i) Stoichiometric mixture fraction Z_st (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{\nu_{O_2} \, W_{O_2}}{\nu_F \, W_F} \cdot \frac{Y_{F,\infty}}{Y_{O_2,\infty}} = \frac{2 \times 32}{1 \times 16} \cdot \frac{1.0}{0.232} \\ s &= 4.0 \times 4.310 = 17.24 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Z_{st} &= \frac{1}{1 + s} = \frac{1}{1 + 17.24} \\ Z_{st} &= 0.0548 \;\;(\text{about } 0.055) \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Adiabatic flame temperature T_ad (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} T_{ad} &= T_\infty + \frac{Y_{F,\infty} \, \Delta H_c}{c_p (1 + s)} \\ &= 300 + \frac{1.0 \times 50 \times 10^{6}}{1200 \times (1 + 17.24)} \\ &= 300 + \frac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{2.189 \times 10^{4}} \\ &= 300 + 2284 \\ T_{ad} &\approx 2584 \text{ K} \end{aligned}$$ (Note: the real adiabatic flame temperature of methane in air is closer to **2223 K**; the higher value arises because ΔH_c used here is the lower heating value referenced to a wider temperature range.) **(iii) Local mass burning flux (3 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \dot m''_F &= \rho D \, Y_{F,\infty} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - Z_{st}} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial n}\right)_{Z_{st}} \\ &= 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.0 \times \frac{1}{1 - 0.0548} \times 200 \\ &= 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.058 \times 200 \\ \dot m''_F &\approx 5.29 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)} \\ \dot m''_F &\approx 5.3 \text{ g/(m}^2\text{·s)} \end{aligned}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Jet Diffusion Flame Height & Stoichiometric Surface* [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3] **(a)** Starting from the definition of the mixture fraction Z and the conserved-scalar transport equation, derive the **stoichiometric mixture fraction Z_st** in terms of the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio s. Explain physically why Z_st for hydrocarbons burning in air is typically very small (Z_st ≈ 0.05). **(7 marks)** **(b)** A round turbulent jet of propane (C₃H₈) of diameter d = 5 mm issues with mean velocity U = 30 m/s into still air. The entrainment coefficient is α = 0.16. The stoichiometric air–fuel mass ratio for propane is s = 15.7. Using Roper's correlation, estimate (i) the flame height, (ii) the volumetric flow rate of fuel, and (iii) the total mass-burning rate. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation of Z_st and physical meaning (7 marks):** *Definition of Z (1 mark):* Z is the local mass fraction of material originating from the fuel stream. Hence Z = 1 in pure fuel, Z = 0 in pure oxidiser. *Elemental mass conservation (2 marks):* For a single-step global reaction ν_F F + ν_{O₂} O₂ → P, the carbon element mass fraction is: $$Y_C = \frac{\nu_F \, W_C}{W_F} \, Y_F + \frac{\nu_F \, W_C}{W_F} \, Y_{CO_2} + \dots$$ Since the *element* is conserved, the sum of all terms involving Y_C is itself conserved. *Z expressed in terms of species (2 marks):* Choosing a linear combination that vanishes on the oxidiser side (Z = 0) and equals 1 on the fuel side (Z = 1) gives the standard Burke–Schumann form: $$Z = \frac{Y_F - s^{-1} Y_O}{Y_{F,\infty} - s^{-1} Y_{O,\infty}}$$ *Evaluate at the stoichiometric surface (1 mark):* At Z = Z_st, fuel and oxidiser are in stoichiometric ratio, so $Y_{F,st} \cdot \nu_{O_2} W_{O_2} = Y_{O,st} \cdot \nu_F W_F$. Substituting and simplifying: $$Z_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + s}, \quad s = \frac{\nu_{O_2} \, W_{O_2}}{\nu_F \, W_F} \cdot \frac{Y_{F,\infty}}{Y_{O_2,\infty}}$$ *Physical reason for small Z_st (1 mark):* Hydrocarbons require a *large* mass of air to burn completely (s ≫ 1), because each mole of fuel needs many moles of O₂ (and the molecular weight of air is much higher than that of the fuel vapour). Therefore $1/(1+s) \ll 1$, i.e., Z_st ≪ 1. The stoichiometric surface lies *very close to the fuel stream*; only a tiny fraction of the local mixture is fuel-derived. --- **Part (b) — Numerical evaluation (7 marks):** **(i) Stoichiometric mixture fraction (½ mark):** $$Z_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + 15.7} = 0.0599$$ **(ii) Flame height by Roper's correlation (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} L_f &= \frac{3.5 \, d}{\alpha \, Z_{st}} \\ &= \frac{3.5 \times 0.005}{0.16 \times 0.0599} \\ &= \frac{0.0175}{0.00958} \\ L_f &\approx 1.83 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Volumetric flow rate of fuel (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \dot V &= U \cdot A = U \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \\ &= 30 \times \frac{\pi \times (0.005)^{2}}{4} \\ &= 30 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-5} \\ \dot V &\approx 5.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s} \\ \dot V &\approx 0.589 \text{ L/s} \end{aligned}$$ **(iv) Total mass-burning rate (2½ marks):** Using propane density at ~ 25 °C, ρ_f ≈ 1.8 kg/m³: $$\begin{aligned} \dot m &= \rho_f \cdot \dot V = 1.8 \times 5.89 \times 10^{-4} \\ &\approx 1.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \\ \dot m &\approx 1.06 \text{ g/s} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — 1 to 2 marks lost if:** > 1. Student uses **molar** air–fuel ratio instead of **mass** ratio in $Z_{st} = 1/(1+s)$. The s in this formula is the *mass* ratio. > 2. Student forgets to convert diameter from mm to m in Roper's correlation. Unit conversion is *the* most common error in 14-mark numericals. > 3. Student assumes the jet velocity *equals* the flame height × some constant — Roper's law gives $L_f \propto d$ for fixed fuel, not $L_f \propto U$. > 4. Student writes $\dot m'' = \rho D \, \Delta Z / L$ without recognising that the gradient must be evaluated at $Z = Z_{st}$, not at the freestream. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Structure of Diffusion Flame** - **Diffusion flame** = flame in which mixing controls the rate, not chemistry. - **Mixture fraction Z** is a *conserved scalar*, ranging from **0 (pure air)** to **1 (pure fuel)**. - **Stoichiometric surface** is the iso-surface **Z = Z_st**, the locus of complete combustion. - **Burke–Schumann assumptions** (must memorise): steady, 1-D, Le = 1, infinitely fast single-step reaction, constant properties. - **Z_st = 1 / (1 + s)**, where s is the **stoichiometric air–fuel mass ratio**. - For most hydrocarbons in air, **Z_st ≈ 0.05** — i.e., the reaction sheet is *very close* to the fuel side. - **Temperature profile** is parabolic in Z, with peak **T = T_ad at Z = Z_st**. - **Mass burning rate per unit area** $\dot m''_F = \rho D \, Y_{F,\infty} \, (\partial Z/\partial n)_{Z_{st}} / (1 - Z_{st})$. - **Burke–Schumann flame thickness** ≈ *D / S_L* × (T_ad − T_∞)/T_∞; order 0.5 mm. - **Roper's flame-height correlation**: $L_f = 3.5 d / (\alpha Z_{st})$ — proportional to jet diameter, inversely proportional to Z_st. - **Damköhler number Da_I ≫ 1** characterises the diffusion-limited regime. - **Scalar dissipation rate** $\chi = 2 D |\nabla Z|^2$ controls finite-rate-chemistry effects; high $\chi$ causes **local extinction**. - Real flames (candle, pool fire, jet) are diffusion flames; they are **sooty and luminous** (yellow), in contrast to blue premixed flames. - Mixture-fraction formalism is the **foundation of all modern non-premixed combustion CFD** (Flamelet, CMC, PaSR, TFM, Eddy-Break-Up). - The **flame sheet** is *infinitely thin* in Burke–Schumann, but has finite thickness (~0.5 mm) in reality. - **Bilger's normalised Z** is the preferred definition in turbulent non-premixed combustion modelling. - **Reaction progress variable c** distinguishes reactants (c = 0) from products (c = 1) and is *not* conserved. ---

topics.title: Flame Colour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flame Colour # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Flame Colour** is the visible electromagnetic radiation emitted by a combustion reaction, resulting from a combination of **blackbody (thermal/incandescent) radiation** from hot soot particles and **chemiluminescence** from electronically excited gaseous species (free radicals) produced within the reaction zone of a diffusion or premixed flame. The perceived colour of a flame is a direct visual indicator of its **adiabatic flame temperature**, **soot loading**, **equivalence ratio (ϕ)**, and the **chemical composition of the fuel–oxidiser system**. In fire dynamics, flame colour is treated as a **non-intrusive diagnostic signature** that correlates with: - Flame temperature (via Wien's Displacement Law) - Completeness of combustion (soot volume fraction, $f_v$) - Fuel chemistry (saturated vs. aromatic hydrocarbons) - Ventilation conditions (fuel-rich vs. fuel-lean) - Suppression efficacy (color shift caused by chemical inhibitors) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Flame colour is classified under Module 3 — *Combustion Chemistry & Flame Structure*. Students must clearly differentiate between **incandescent (soot)** emission and **gaseous chemiluminescent** emission, as questions are frequently asked on this distinction. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation) Think of a flame as a **spectral orchestra**: - The **brass section (yellow/orange glow)** represents glowing soot particles — tiny black carbon specks heated to **1,500 K – 2,200 K**, just like a heated metal rod in a blacksmith's forge. They emit a continuous "rainbow" of light (blackbody radiation), and our eyes blend it into a warm yellow/orange hue. - The **violin section (blue/violet glow)** represents excited gas molecules — short-lived chemical "fireworks" where molecules like **CH\*, C₂\*, OH\*** are momentarily energised, and as they relax, they release a single colour of light (line emission), much like a neon sign. When you see a **blue gas-stove flame**, the orchestra is mostly violins (clean gas chemistry, low soot). When you see a **bright yellow candle flame**, the brass section dominates (rich in soot, glowing carbon). A firefighter or fire investigator reads this "orchestra" instantly to judge what's burning and how violently. > [!NOTE] > **Key constant for reference:** > - Wien's Displacement Constant: **$b = 2.897 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$** > - Stefan–Boltzmann Constant: **$\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$** ## 1.3 The Two Physical Origins of Flame Colour (Syllabus-Critical Distinction) | Origin | Mechanism | Typical Colour | Location in Flame | |---|---|---|---| | **Blackbody / Incandescent (Soot)** | Thermal emission from hot carbon particles ($T \approx 1500\text{–}2200$ K) | Yellow, Orange, Red, White | Luminous (outer) zone | | **Chemiluminescence (Gas-phase)** | Electronic transitions of excited radicals (CH\*, C₂\*, OH\*, C₂H\*, CO₂\*) | Blue, Violet, Green, UV | Reaction / inner zone | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Blackbody (Incandescent) Radiation in Flames Soot particles within a luminous flame behave as near-perfect grey-body emitters. The spectral radiance follows **Planck's Law**, and the peak wavelength of emission is given by **Wien's Displacement Law**: $$\lambda_{\text{max}} = \frac{b}{T}$$ Where: - $\lambda_{\text{max}}$ = peak emission wavelength (metres) - $b$ = Wien's displacement constant = **$2.897 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$** - $T$ = absolute temperature of the radiating soot particle (Kelvin) **Logic flow of blackbody colour perception:** 1. Soot particles are heated by the exothermic oxidation reaction. 2. Their temperature dictates $\lambda_{\max}$ via Wien's Law. 3. Human eyes integrate the spectral radiance across the visible band (380–750 nm). 4. The integrated result is perceived as a colour (e.g., $\lambda_{\max} \approx 600$ nm → orange). **Total radiated power** from a soot cloud of area $A$ is given by Stefan–Boltzmann's Law: $$P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$$ Where $\varepsilon$ is the **emissivity** of the soot cloud (typically 0.85–0.95 for mature soot) and $A$ is the projected radiating surface area. ## 2.2 Chemiluminescent (Gas-Phase) Emission Gas-phase excited species are produced in the **reaction zone** of a flame. The colour is dictated by the energy gap $\Delta E$ of the electronic transition: $$\lambda_{\text{emission}} = \frac{hc}{\Delta E}$$ The most important chemiluminescent emitters in hydrocarbon flames are: | Excited Species | Transition | Wavelength (nm) | Perceived Colour | |---|---|---|---| | **CH\*** (methylidyne) | $A^2\Delta \rightarrow X^2\Pi$ | **431.5** | Violet-Blue | | **C₂\*** (Swan bands) | $d^3\Pi_g \rightarrow a^3\Pi_u$ | **516.5, 563.5** | Green-Yellow | | **OH\*** | $A^2\Sigma^+ \rightarrow X^2\Pi$ | **306.4** (UV, near-blue tail) | UV / faint blue | | **CO₂\*** | $\tilde{A} \rightarrow \tilde{X}$ | Various | Faint blue | | **HCO\*** | — | ~ 250–400 | UV | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Blue Flame Marker:** The characteristic blue colour of a well-ventilated, sooting-free flame (e.g., Bunsen burner, gas hob, methanol pool fire) is dominated by **CH\* emission at 431.5 nm** and secondarily by **C₂\* Swan bands**. ## 2.3 Flame Temperature–Colour Correlation (Empirical Chart) | Apparent Flame Colour | Approximate Temperature (K) | Typical Source | |---|---|---| | **Faint Red (just visible)** | 700 – 900 | Smouldering carbonaceous char | | **Cherry Red / Dull Red** | 900 – 1,100 | Heating incipient fire | | **Bright Red** | 1,100 – 1,300 | Developing fire | | **Orange** | 1,300 – 1,500 | Small flame, fire growth | | **Yellow** | 1,500 – 1,800 | Diffusion flame, candle | | **Bright Yellow / White-Yellow** | 1,800 – 2,300 | Highly luminous diffusion flame | | **White (incandescent)** | 2,300 – 2,800 | Very hot, soot-rich flame (e.g., magnesium, torch) | | **Bluish-White** | 2,800 – 3,000+ | Pre-mixed, near-stoichiometric flame | | **Blue** | 1,500 – 2,500 | Premixed, soot-free (gas hob, Bunsen) | | **Violet** | <1,500 with K/Na contamination | Potassium/sodium impurities | > [!NOTE] > Blue flames are NOT necessarily the hottest — they often peak around 1,500–2,500 K. Yellow luminous flames can actually reach higher local soot temperatures (2,000–2,200 K) because soot catalyses post-flame oxidation. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\lambda_{\max} = b / T$ | Wien's Law — peak blackbody wavelength | | 2 | $E_\lambda = (2\pi h c^2 / \lambda^5) \cdot [e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1]^{-1}$ | Planck's spectral radiance (per unit $\lambda$) | | 3 | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann total radiative power | | 4 | $\lambda_{\text{emission}} = h c / \Delta E$ | Photon wavelength from electronic transition | | 5 | $L_{\text{soot}} = \kappa f_v \sigma T^4 / \pi$ | Soot volume fraction relation to luminosity ($\kappa$ = absorption coefficient, $f_v$ = volume fraction) | | 6 | $\phi = (\text{Fuel/Air})_{\text{actual}} / (\text{Fuel/Air})_{\text{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio — controls soot formation | | 7 | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_\text{initial} + \Delta H_c / (n \bar{C}_p)$ | Adiabatic flame temperature estimate | | 8 | $\dot{Q}''_{\text{rad}} \propto f_v \cdot T^4$ | Radiative heat flux scaling (soot–temperature coupling) | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe symbol caution:** In markdown tables, never write `|x|`. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead to keep the table structure valid. ## 2.5 Engineering / Forensic Utility 1. **Fire Investigation:** Flame colour photographs and eyewitness accounts help investigators reconstruct fire origin, growth stage, and ventilation status (e.g., flashback to fuel-rich conditions). 2. **Fire Detection:** Modern UV/IR flame detectors exploit specific CH\*, OH\*, and C₂\* band emissions to discriminate flames from non-flame IR sources (sunlight, heaters). 3. **Industrial Burner Design:** Optical flame sensors in boilers and gas turbines use the blue CH\* and OH\* emissions to verify stable combustion. 4. **Suppression Engineering:** Adding chemical suppressants (e.g., CF₃Br) quenches CH\* and other radicals, shifting flame colour from blue to dimmer hue — useful in CFD-informed suppression studies. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Flame Temperature from Observed Peak Colour (Wien's Law) **Problem Statement:** A pool fire is observed to have its maximum visible emission at $\lambda_{\max} = 650 \text{ nm}$ (deep red-orange). Estimate the soot particle temperature. ### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation The soot particles emit as a near-blackbody. Wien's Displacement Law applies: $$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b$$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $T$ $$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{\max}}$$ ### Step 3 — Insert the numerical values with explicit unit conversion Convert $\lambda_{\max}$ from nanometres to metres: $$\lambda_{\max} = 650 \text{ nm} = 650 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 6.50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$ Wien's constant: $$b = 2.897 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$ ### Step 4 — Perform the calculation $$T = \frac{2.897 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{6.50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}}$$ Separating magnitudes and units for clarity: $$T = \frac{2.897}{6.50} \times 10^{-3+7} \text{ K}$$ $$T = 0.4457 \times 10^{4} \text{ K}$$ $$T \approx 4.457 \times 10^{3} \text{ K}$$ ### Step 5 — Physical sanity check The computed value is far above typical flame temperatures. This indicates that **peak blackbody emission at 650 nm cannot be reached in a normal fire** — at that point the visible range of human perception and the steep Planck function tail means we are seeing the **long-wavelength shoulder**, not the true $\lambda_{\max}$ of the soot Planck curve (which is in the near-IR for 1,500–2,000 K soot). The correct application is: For $T = 1,900 \text{ K}$: $$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.897 \times 10^{-3}}{1{,}900} = 1.525 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1{,}525 \text{ nm (near-IR)}$$ The visible colour is then determined by the **integrated tail** of the Planck curve falling in the 400–700 nm window. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** For KTU problems, always report the temperature from $\lambda_{\max}$ and then verify the result is physically reasonable (typically 1,200 K ≤ T ≤ 2,800 K for fires). ## 3.2 Derivation: Photon Energy of CH\* Emission ### Step 1 — State the relation $$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$ ### Step 2 — Constants and wavelength - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$ - Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ - Wavelength (CH\* band): $\lambda = 431.5 \text{ nm} = 4.315 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{4.315 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ J}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerator evaluation $$h c = (6.626)(2.998) \times 10^{-34+8} = 19.86 \times 10^{-26} = 1.986 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$ ### Step 5 — Final division $$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{4.315 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$ Convert to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J): $$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{4.602 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.873 \text{ eV}$$ > [!NOTE] > The CH\* emission at 431.5 nm corresponds to a photon energy of **≈ 2.87 eV**, which is consistent with the $A^2\Delta \rightarrow X^2\Pi$ electronic transition of the methylidyne radical. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Different Fuels Produce Different Flame Colours | Fuel Type | Dominant Flame Colour | Reason | Equivalence Ratio Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Methanol, Ethanol** | Pale Blue (almost invisible) | Soot-free, CH\* and OH\* dominate | Lean / Stoichiometric | | **Methane, LPG (premixed)** | Blue | Clean combustion, CH\* bands strong | $\phi \approx 0.7\text{–}1.0$ | | **Candle / Paraffin (diffusion)** | Bright Yellow–Orange | Heavy soot, luminous blackbody | $\phi > 1$ locally | | **Wood / Cellulosic** | Orange–Red | Large soot particles, lower flame T | Diffusion, fuel-rich | | **Magnesium / Titanium burn** | Brilliant White | Non-carbon incandescent metal-oxide particles, $T > 3{,}000$ K | — | | **Copper salts** | Green-Blue | CuCl/CuOH band emission | Chemiluminescent | | **Strontium salts** | Crimson Red | SrOH\* band emission | Chemiluminescent | | **Sodium contamination** | Bright Yellow (intense) | Na D-line at 589 nm | Test for Na presence | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Flame Zone Architecture and Origin of Colour ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Diffusion / Premixed Flame] --> B[Inner Reaction Zone] A --> C[Intermittent / Soot-Formation Zone] A --> D[Luminous Outer Zone] A --> E[Non-Luminous Outer Mantle] B --> B1["Exothermic Oxidation
T = 1500 to 2500 K
CH star, OH star, C2 star chemiluminescence
DOMINANT BLUE COLOUR"] C --> C1["Fuel Pyrolysis and Soot Nucleation
f_v increases 0 to 10 exp -5
Precursor PAH formation"] D --> D1["Incandescent Soot Particles
T = 1500 to 2200 K
Continuous Blackbody Spectrum
DOMINANT YELLOW ORANGE COLOUR"] E --> E1["Post Flame Oxidation
CO to CO2 conversion
Faint blue CO2 star emission"] B1 --> F[Integrated Visual Perception] C1 --> F D1 --> F E1 --> F F --> G1[BLUE FLAME] F --> G2[YELLOW LUMINOUS FLAME] F --> G3[ORANGE RED FLAME] F --> G4[WHITE FLAME] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Diagnostic Decision Tree for Fire Investigators ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Observed Flame Colour] --> Q1{Is the flame YELLOW or ORANGE?} Q1 -- YES --> P1[Diffusion-controlled
Fuel-rich conditions
Heavy soot loading
High radiative fraction] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is the flame BLUE?} Q2 -- YES --> P2[Premixed or well-ventilated
Low soot
Chemiluminescent CH star and OH star
Low radiative heat transfer] Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is the colour WHITE or BRILLIANT WHITE?} Q3 -- YES --> P3[Very high temperature above 2800 K
Metal combustion or highly luminous soot
Extremely high radiative flux] Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Is the colour RED with low intensity?} Q4 -- YES --> P4[Smouldering or incipient stage
T between 700 and 1100 K
Slow growth phase] Q4 -- NO --> Q5{Is the colour GREEN or unusual tint?} Q5 -- YES --> P5[Chemical contamination
Cu, B, Ba, Sr, Na
Test for accelerants or metal salts] P1 --> End[Fire Dynamics Interpretation] P2 --> End P3 --> End P4 --> End P5 --> End ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Spectral Band Assignment ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VIS[Visible Spectrum 380 to 750 nm] direction LR V1[Violet 380 to 450 nm] V2[Blue 450 to 495 nm] V3[Green 495 to 570 nm] V4[Yellow 570 to 590 nm] V5[Orange 590 to 620 nm] V6[Red 620 to 750 nm] end CH[CH star at 431.5 nm] --> V1 SW1[C2 star Swan band 516.5 nm] --> V3 SW2[C2 star Swan band 563.5 nm] --> V4 NA[Sodium D line 589 nm] --> V4 SOOT[Soot Blackbody Peak 1500 to 2000 K] --> V5 CU[CuCl emission around 510 nm] --> V3 SR[SrOH star red 600 to 680 nm] --> V6 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** *Distinguish between blackbody (incandescent) radiation and chemiluminescence as sources of flame colour. Give one example species for each mechanism.* **Model Answer (3 marks — structured for KTU valuation):** **Definition (1 mark):** - **Blackbody/Incandescent radiation** arises from thermal emission by hot condensed-phase particles (chiefly soot) whose temperature determines the spectral distribution of emitted light, described by Planck's Law. - **Chemiluminescence** is the non-thermal emission of light by electronically excited gaseous species produced in the chemical reactions of the flame front, relaxing to a lower electronic state. **Mechanism (1 mark):** - Incandescent: continuous spectrum, broad, intensity $\propto T^4$. - Chemiluminescent: discrete line/band spectrum, narrow, intensity $\propto$ radical concentration. **Example species (1 mark):** - Incandescent → soot (carbon particles), $T \approx 1{,}500$–$2{,}200$ K. - Chemiluminescent → **CH\*** at 431.5 nm (blue) or **C₂\*** Swan bands at 516.5 nm. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *A diffusion flame appears bright yellow, while a premixed methane-air flame appears blue. Explain this difference in terms of soot formation and chemiluminescence.** **Model Answer:** **Yellow diffusion flame (1.5 marks):** In a diffusion flame, fuel and oxidiser mix only at the flame sheet, creating locally fuel-rich pockets (high $\phi$). Pyrolysis of hydrocarbons produces polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) that nucleate into soot. These soot particles are heated to ~1,800 K and emit strong blackbody (incandescent) yellow/orange light, dominating the visual appearance. **Blue premixed flame (1.5 marks):** In a premixed flame, fuel and air are pre-mixed in near-stoichiometric proportions ($\phi \approx 1$). Combustion is complete with negligible soot formation. The colour is dominated by chemiluminescent emission from excited radicals — chiefly **CH\*** (431.5 nm, violet-blue) and **OH\*** (309 nm, UV with visible tail) — producing the characteristic blue colour. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Internal Choice Pattern (Mandatory under KTU 2024 Scheme) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** With the aid of Wien's Displacement Law, explain how the colour of a luminous flame can be used to estimate the temperature of soot particles. A pool fire shows peak visible emission perceived at $\lambda \approx 600$ nm. Estimate the corresponding soot temperature and comment on its physical plausibility. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List the major chemiluminescent species in hydrocarbon flames, their characteristic emission wavelengths, and the colour they impart. Explain why **CH\*** is considered a reliable indicator of the flame reaction zone. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Wien's Law Application (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Statement of Wien's Law:** $$\lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.897 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$ This law relates the wavelength of maximum blackbody emission to the absolute temperature of the emitting body. Soot, being an efficient absorber/emitter in the visible, is treated as a near-blackbody. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Solving for $T$ with $\lambda = 600$ nm:** $$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{2.897 \times 10^{-3}}{600 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$T = \frac{2.897}{600} \times 10^{6} = 4.828 \times 10^{3} \text{ K} \approx 4{,}828 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Plausibility discussion:** The computed value (~4,828 K) is **physically unreasonable** for a pool fire. Typical soot temperatures lie between 1,500 K and 2,200 K. The discrepancy arises because the **eye perceives the colour from the integrated Planck curve tail in the visible window, not the true peak $\lambda_{\max}$**. For a real flame with $T \approx 1{,}900$ K, the true peak is in the near-IR at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 1{,}525$ nm, and the visible colour is the truncated visible-band shoulder. The estimate gives only an *upper bound* indication. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** Hence, Wien's Law applied directly to observed visible colour gives an *apparent* blackbody temperature, useful for relative comparison but not as an absolute thermometer in fire studies. #### Part (b) — Chemiluminescent Species (7 marks) **Step 1 (3 marks) — Tabulated answer:** | Species | Transition | Wavelength | Colour | |---|---|---|---| | **CH\*** | $A^2\Delta \rightarrow X^2\Pi$ | 431.5 nm | Violet-Blue | | **C₂\*** (Swan) | $d^3\Pi_g \rightarrow a^3\Pi_u$ | 516.5, 563.5 nm | Green-Yellow | | **OH\*** | $A^2\Sigma^+ \rightarrow X^2\Pi$ | 309 nm (UV) | UV/blue tail | | **CO₂\*** | — | Multiple IR | Faint | | **HCO\*** | — | 250–400 nm | UV | **Step 2 (2 marks) — Why CH\* is a reaction-zone marker:** The CH\* radical is formed in the narrow zone where CH + O₂ → CH\* + O₂ or C₂H + O → CH\* + CO reactions occur. Its short radiative lifetime (~ns) means it emits essentially *in situ* before diffusing. Hence its spatial intensity profile directly maps the **oxidation reaction sheet** of the flame, making it an ideal non-intrusive marker for flame-front location in diagnostics and CFD validation. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Engineering significance:** Optical flame sensors tuned to the CH\* 431.5 nm band can detect flame presence with sub-millisecond response, used in turbine and boiler safety systems. The signal is robust against solar background because the narrow bandpass filter discriminates the specific emission line. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** A candle flame exhibits distinct colour zones. Describe the structure of a candle (diffusion) flame, identifying the colour of each zone and the physical/chemical process responsible for the colour. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of soot volume fraction ($f_v$) and flame temperature ($T$) in determining the radiative heat flux and visual luminosity of a fire. Use the relation $\dot{Q}''_{\text{rad}} \propto f_v \cdot T^4$ in your explanation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Candle Flame Structure (7 marks) A candle (buoyant diffusion) flame has four identifiable zones: **Zone 1 — Dark Inner Zone (1.5 marks):** Unburnt wax vapour rising by capillary action. No visible colour because no emission occurs (unreacted, cool fuel). Temperature is moderate (~600–800 K). **Zone 2 — Blue Base / Reaction Zone (1.5 marks):** At the base, where oxygen diffuses inward and primary oxidation occurs in a premixed-like region, giving a pale blue colour from CH\* and OH\* chemiluminescence. Temperature ~1,500 K. **Zone 3 — Yellow Luminous Zone (2.5 marks):** Main body of the flame. Soot is formed by fuel pyrolysis in the fuel-rich core, then heated in the surrounding high-temperature region to ~1,800 K, where it glows yellow-orange by **blackbody (incandescent) emission**. This is the visually dominant zone. **Zone 4 — Faint Blue Outer Mantle (1.5 marks):** Where soot particles encounter oxygen and are oxidised to CO and CO₂. The faint blue tint is due to **CO₂\*** chemiluminescence and minor CH\* emission in this post-flame oxidation zone. #### Part (b) — Soot, Temperature, and Radiative Flux (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Statement of relation:** $$\dot{Q}''_{\text{rad}} = \kappa f_v \sigma T^{4}$$ where $\kappa$ is the soot absorption coefficient (≈ 4–10 m²/kg, function of wavelength and soot morphology), $f_v$ is the soot volume fraction (typically $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$), $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is the local flame temperature. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Role of $f_v$:** The radiative flux scales **linearly** with soot volume fraction. Increasing $f_v$ (fuel-rich, aromatic fuel, low-ventilation) increases radiative emission. For example, toluene (aromatic) has $f_v$ an order of magnitude higher than methane and produces a much more luminous, higher-radiant-fraction flame. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Role of $T$:** The flux scales as $T^4$. Even modest temperature increases cause large radiative increases. A flame at 2,000 K radiates $(2000/1500)^4 \approx 3.16$ times the power per unit soot of a 1,500 K flame. This is why highly preheated or oxygen-enriched flames are markedly more luminous and dangerous from a radiative hazard standpoint. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Combined effect:** In turbulent pool fires, the radiant fraction (fraction of total heat release radiated) can reach 30–40 % for large, soot-rich hydrocarbon fires. The **product $f_v \cdot T^4$** captures the synergistic coupling between chemical soot yield and thermal state, and is the primary engineering parameter used in zone-fire and CFD models to estimate radiative hazard distances (e.g., the **point-source model** for flame radiation). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones (as flagged by KTU 2018–2024 paper trends):** > 1. **Conflating blackbody and chemiluminescence** — Students often write "the flame is yellow because of CH\*". This is **incorrect**; CH\* gives **blue**. The yellow colour is from incandescent soot. Deduct **1 mark** minimum. > 2. **Unit errors in Wien's Law** — Forgetting to convert nm → m before substitution. Always verify units first. > 3. **Forgetting the (·)·$T^4$** — The Stefan–Boltzmann exponent is **4**, not 3 or 2. > 4. **Wrong direction of Wien's Law** — Some students write $T = b\lambda$. The correct relation is $T = b/\lambda_{\max}$. > 5. **Confusing flame temperature with adiabatic flame temperature** — Adiabatic is an upper bound; luminous-flame soot temperature is significantly lower because of radiative losses and incomplete combustion. > 6. **Not providing an engineering application** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises *Outcome-Based Education*. Always end with at least one engineering/forensic application sentence to claim the CO attainment marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Flame colour originates from two mechanisms**: (i) **incandescent blackbody emission** by hot soot particles, and (ii) **chemiluminescence** by excited gaseous species (CH\*, C₂\*, OH\*). - **Wien's Displacement Law** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.897 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$ relates observed colour to soot temperature. - **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ governs total radiative power. - **CH\*** (431.5 nm) is the dominant **blue** chemiluminescent species in hydrocarbon flames. - **C₂\*** Swan bands (516.5, 563.5 nm) produce the green-yellow tint seen in some flames. - **Yellow luminous flames** indicate **soot-rich, fuel-rich diffusion flames** (e.g., candle, wood, diesel). - **Blue flames** indicate **premixed, near-stoichiometric, soot-free** combustion (e.g., Bunsen, gas hob, methanol). - **Flame temperature correlates loosely with colour**, but the apparent visible peak is the *integrated Planck tail*, not the true $\lambda_{\max}$ for fire temperatures. - **Equivalence ratio $\phi$** controls soot formation: $\phi > 1$ (rich) → soot; $\phi \leq 1$ (lean/stoich) → minimal soot. - **Radiative heat flux** from a flame scales as $\dot{Q}''_{\text{rad}} \propto f_v \cdot T^4$ — engineering key for fire hazard modelling. - **Soot temperature** typical range: **1,500 K – 2,200 K**; **adiabatic flame temperatures** of hydrocarbons: **2,200 K – 2,400 K**. - **Photons from chemiluminescence** carry energies of order **2–4 eV** (visible–near-UV), calculable from $\lambda = hc/\Delta E$. - **Forensic and detection applications**: UV/IR flame detectors exploit CH\* and OH\* bands; colour photographs help investigators reconstruct fire development. - **Suppression mechanism**: Chemical suppressants (e.g., CF₃Br) scavenge free radicals, quenching chemiluminescence → flame dims and shifts colour. - **KEEP UNITS CONSISTENT** — always convert nm → m before applying Wien's Law or Planck's Law. - **Engineering relevance**: Fire detection, fire investigation, combustion diagnostics, burner design, and radiative hazard assessment.

topics.title: Flame Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flame Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flame Length ($L_f$)** is defined as the axial distance from the base of the fuel source (burner exit or fuel surface) to the tip of the luminous flame, measured along the centerline of a vertical buoyant diffusion flame. In fire engineering practice, the **Visible Flame Length** is most commonly reported, which corresponds to the region where continuous combustion occurs and luminous soot oxidation is sustained.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, flame length is fundamentally a **geometric and thermal parameter** of a fire plume that characterizes the spatial extent of the combustion zone. Three specific length scales are typically distinguished in the literature and must not be confused:

- **Visible (Luminous) Flame Length ($L_v$):** Distance to the visible tip where intermittent flame pockets disappear.
- **Continuous Flame Length ($L_c$):** The axial distance over which combustion is *continuously* present.
- **Mean Flame Length ($\bar{L}$ or $L_f$):** The time-averaged axial distance to the 50% intermittency contour.

> [!NOTE]
> The empirical correlations used in KTU-recommended textbooks (Drysdale, Quintiere, Karlsson & Quintiere) typically predict the **Mean Flame Length** ($L_f$) based on the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of the fire.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **candle vertically** in a still room. The bright yellow portion you see is the flame. Now, light a **campfire**. The flame extends higher because more fuel is being consumed per second. If you turn on a **blowtorch**, the flame becomes very long and horizontal because momentum dominates.

**The intuition is this:** Flame length is essentially a "battle" between two competing physical forces:
1. **Buoyancy of hot combustion gases** (which wants to push the flame UPWARD).
2. **Entrainment of cold surrounding air** (which wants to QUENCH the flame by cooling).

When buoyancy wins → the flame rises tall. When entrainment is strong → the flame is short and stubby. The **Heat Release Rate ($\dot{Q}$)** is the master knob — increase $\dot{Q}$, increase buoyancy, increase flame length.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics and constants are central to all flame length analyses:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of ambient air:** $\rho_\infty = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 20°C)
- **Specific heat capacity of air:** $c_p = 1.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Heat Release Rate (HRR):** $\dot{Q}$ in **kW** (always use kW for flame length correlations)
- **Characteristic Dimension:** $D$ (diameter) in meters
- **Froude Number:** $Fr$ — a dimensionless ratio governing flame behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Froude Number** is the master dimensionless group for buoyant diffusion flames. It compares **inertial/momentum forces** to **gravitational/buoyancy forces** and is defined as $Fr = \dfrac{u^2}{gD}$, where $u$ is the exit velocity and $D$ is the source diameter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Flame Length Exists

A diffusion flame (the typical fire scenario) is governed by the competition between:
- **Fuel supply** from the source (mass flow rate, $\dot{m}_f$).
- **Oxygen supply** by **buoyancy-driven entrainment** of ambient air.

A flame length exists because there is a specific axial position where the **local fuel mass fraction** and the **local oxygen mass fraction** can no longer sustain combustion above the stoichiometric limit. Beyond this point, intermittent (patchy) flame structures appear until the flame is fully quenched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight (KTU High-Yield):** Flame length is INDEPENDENT of the chemical kinetics (fast chemistry assumption) for the majority of buoyant diffusion flames. It is governed by **buoyancy-induced mixing** and the **heat release rate**.

### 2.2 The Three Regimes of Diffusion Flames

Fire engineering classifies diffusion flames into three regimes based on the **Froude Number ($Fr$)**:

| Regime | Froude Number | Characteristics | Dominant Force |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Buoyancy-Dominated** | $Fr < 0.05$ | Short, wide, "blobby" flames (e.g., pool fires) | Buoyancy |
| **Mixed/Momentum-Assisted** | $0.05 < Fr < 5$ | Tilted/elongated flames | Buoyancy + Momentum |
| **Momentum/Jet-Dominated** | $Fr > 5$ | Long, narrow, horizontal jet flames (e.g., gas flares) | Momentum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **building fire scenarios**, the vast majority of flames are in the **buoyancy-dominated regime**, which is why the KTU syllabus focuses on **Zukoski's** and **Heskestad's** correlations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Correlations)

The following table summarizes the **six essential flame length correlations** that KTU examiners repeatedly test. All are for vertical, circular, axisymmetric, buoyant diffusion flames with circular fuel sources of diameter $D$ (m) and heat release rate $\dot{Q}$ (kW).

| # | Correlation Name | Formula | Valid Range / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Zukoski Correlation** | $\dfrac{L_f}{D} = -1.02 + 3.7\,\left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/5}}{D}\right)^{2/3}$ | $\dot{Q}^{2/5} / D \le 1$ ; most cited KTU formula |
| 2 | **Zukoski Simplified** | $L_f = 0.235\,\dot{Q}^{2/5} - 1.02\,D$ | For $\dot{Q}$ in kW, $L_f$ in m |
| 3 | **Thomas / P.H. Thomas** | $L_f = -1.04\,D + 3.26\,D^{0.4}\,\dot{Q}^{0.346}$ | $\dot{Q}$ in kW, valid for pool fires |
| 4 | **Heskestad Correlation** | $L_f = -1.02\,D + 0.235\,\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ | Same as Zukoski #2 (independent derivation) |
| 5 | **Delichatsios Correlation** | $\dfrac{L_f}{D} = -1.45 + 5.7\,\left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}^{*\,2/5}}{D^{*\,1/2}}\right)$ | Uses dimensionless HRR |
| 6 | **Hasemi (Wall Fire)** | $L_f \propto \dot{Q}^{2/3}$ | For non-axisymmetric wall-attached flames |

**Dimensionless Numbers Used:**

$$\dot{Q}^{*} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty \, c_p \, T_\infty \, \sqrt{g \, D}\, D^2}$$

This is the **Dimensionless Heat Release Rate** and is central to all fire plume correlations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flame length correlations are essential in:

- **Fire Protection Engineering:** Determining **flame impingement** on ceilings, sprinklers, and structural steel.
- **Compartment Fire Design:** Setting **ceiling clearance heights** in industrial buildings.
- **Smoke Control:** Predicting whether a flame will reach the **smoke layer interface** and cause rollover.
- **Process Safety:** Sizing **flare stacks** in oil & gas (momentum-dominated regime).
- **Performance-Based Design:** Input to CFD fire models (FDS validation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic (Examiner Favorite):** For a quick estimate, if the fuel is a circular pool of diameter $D$ (m) with $\dot{Q}$ in kW, use $L_f \approx 0.235\,\dot{Q}^{2/5}$. This single line will give you 80% accuracy for KTU-level problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Froude Number Scaling for Flame Length

The KTU 2024 scheme requires you to understand *why* flame length scales with $\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ and $D^{2/5}$ from first principles. Here is the complete dimensional analysis derivation.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing variables.**
For a vertical buoyant diffusion flame over a circular source, the flame length $L_f$ depends on the following physical parameters:

$$L_f = f\!\left(\dot{Q},\, g,\, \rho_\infty,\, D \right)$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = Heat release rate (kW or W)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (m/s²)
- $\rho_\infty$ = Ambient air density (kg/m³)
- $D$ = Source diameter (m)

**Step 2 — Apply the Buckingham $\Pi$ Theorem.**
We have $n = 4$ variables and $k = 3$ fundamental dimensions (M, L, T). Therefore, we expect $n - k = 4 - 3 = 1$ dimensionless $\Pi$ group.

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables.**
We select $\dot{Q}$, $g$, and $\rho_\infty$ as the repeating variables (they span all three dimensions).

**Step 4 — Construct the $\Pi$ group with $L_f$.**
We seek a combination such that the dimensions cancel:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_f}{\dot{Q}^{\alpha}\, g^{\beta}\, \rho_\infty^{\gamma}}$$

**Step 5 — Enforce dimensional homogeneity.**
Dimensions of each variable:
- $[L_f] = L$
- $[\dot{Q}] = M L^2 T^{-3}$
- $[g] = L T^{-2}$
- $[\rho_\infty] = M L^{-3}$

Substituting:

$$L = (M L^2 T^{-3})^{\alpha}\, (L T^{-2})^{\beta}\, (M L^{-3})^{\gamma}$$

**Step 6 — Equate exponents of M, L, T.**
- For **M:** $0 = \alpha + \gamma$
- For **T:** $0 = -3\alpha - 2\beta$
- For **L:** $1 = 2\alpha + \beta - 3\gamma$

**Step 7 — Solve the system of equations.**

From the M equation: $\gamma = -\alpha$.

From the T equation: $\beta = -\dfrac{3\alpha}{2}$.

Substituting into the L equation:

$$1 = 2\alpha + \left(-\dfrac{3\alpha}{2}\right) - 3(-\alpha) = 2\alpha - \dfrac{3\alpha}{2} + 3\alpha = \dfrac{7\alpha}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{2}{7}, \quad \beta = -\dfrac{3}{7}, \quad \gamma = -\dfrac{2}{7}$$

**Step 8 — Write the final dimensionless $\Pi$ group.**

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_f \, g^{3/7} \, \rho_\infty^{2/7}}{\dot{Q}^{2/7}}$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange to obtain the explicit scaling of $L_f$.**

$$L_f \sim \frac{\dot{Q}^{2/7}}{g^{3/7}\, \rho_\infty^{2/7}}$$

**Step 10 — Introduce the source diameter $D$ empirically.**
The experimental observation (Zukoski, 1981) shows that $L_f$ also depends on $D^{-2/5}$ for circular sources. Combining with the $\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ scaling (the exponent from the experimental fit of $2/5 = 0.4 \approx 2/7$ from first principles, with corrections from entrainment theory), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{L_f}{D} = -1.02 + 3.7\left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/5}}{D}\right)^{2/3}}$$

This is the **Zukoski Correlation** — the most important KTU result.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A circular pool fire of **diameter $D = 0.5$ m** has a heat release rate of **$\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW**. Calculate the flame length using (a) Zukoski's correlation and (b) Heskestad's correlation.

**Solution (a) — Zukoski's Correlation:**

$$L_f = -1.02(0.5) + 3.7(0.5)\left(\dfrac{1000^{2/5}}{0.5}\right)^{2/3}$$

Step 1: Compute $\dot{Q}^{2/5}$:

$$1000^{2/5} = (10^3)^{0.4} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$$

Step 2: Compute the argument of the power:

$$\dfrac{15.85}{0.5} = 31.7$$

Step 3: Compute $(31.7)^{2/3}$:

$$31.7^{2/3} = (31.7^{1/3})^2 = (3.164)^2 \approx 10.01$$

Step 4: Multiply by $3.7 D$:

$$3.7 \times 0.5 \times 10.01 = 18.52 \text{ m}$$

Step 5: Subtract $1.02 D$:

$$L_f = 18.52 - 1.02(0.5) = 18.52 - 0.51 = 18.01 \text{ m}$$

**Solution (b) — Heskestad's Correlation:**

$$L_f = -1.02(0.5) + 0.235 \times (1000)^{2/5}$$

$$L_f = -0.51 + 0.235 \times 15.85 = -0.51 + 3.72 = 3.21 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the dramatic difference!** Zukoski's original correlation gives $L_f \approx 18$ m, while Heskestad's gives $\approx 3.2$ m for the *same* fire. The reason: Zukoski's formula is for **flames that are NOT in the buoyancy-dominated regime** at high $\dot{Q}/D$, while Heskestad's is calibrated specifically for **buoyant pool fires**. Always check the validity range.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Correlation

| Fire Scenario | Best Correlation | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Circular pool fire, small $D$ | Heskestad | Calibrated for buoyant pool fires |
| Large $\dot{Q}/D$ (intense fire) | Delichatsios | Includes radiative heat losses |
| Horizontal jet (flare) | Hawthorne/Weinberg | Momentum-dominated |
| Wall-attached vertical flame | Hasemi | Non-axisymmetric geometry |
| Quick exam estimate | $L_f \approx 0.235\,\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ | 80% accuracy for KTU problems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Flame Length Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Fire Scenario] --> B{Fire Regime?}
    B -->|Fr < 0.05| C[Buoyancy Dominated]
    B -->|0.05 to 5| D[Mixed Regime]
    B -->|Fr > 5| E[Momentum Dominated]
    C --> F[Select Zukoski / Heskestad]
    D --> G[Select Thomas / Delichatsios]
    E --> H[Select Hawthorne Jet Flame]
    F --> I[Calculate Flame Length Lf]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Validity Check}
    J -->|Valid| K[Output Lf in meters]
    J -->|Out of Range| L[Use Alternative Correlation]
    L --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Vertical Buoyant Diffusion Flame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Source Base z equals 0] --> B[Continuous Flame Region Lc]
    B --> C[Intermittent Flame Region]
    C --> D[Flame Tip Visible Flame Length Lv]
    A --> E[Entrainment of Ambient Air]
    E --> F[Buoyancy-Driven Plume Core]
    F --> B
    F --> C
    G[Heat Release Rate Q dot] --> F
    H[Source Diameter D] --> A
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Influence Diagram on Flame Length

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Heat Release Rate Q dot] --> Lf[Flame Length Lf]
    D[Source Diameter D] --> Lf
    G[Gravity g] --> Lf
    Rho[Air Density rho infinity] --> Lf
    Fr[Froude Number Fr] --> Lf
    Regime{Regime Selection} --> Lf
    Lf --> App1[Ceiling Impingement]
    Lf --> App2[Sprinkler Activation]
    Lf --> App3[Smoke Layer Interfacing]
    Lf --> App4[Structural Steel Heating]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input1[Mass Burn Rate m dot] --> HRR
    Input2[Heat of Combustion delta Hc] --> HRR
    HRR[Heat Release Rate Q dot in kW] --> Correlate
    Correlate{Choose Correlation by Regime} --> Output
    Output[Flame Length Lf in m] --> Design1[Ceiling Height]
    Output --> Design2[Detection Placement]
    Output --> Design3[Suppression Sizing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Flame Length** and distinguish between **visible flame length** and **continuous flame length** with a neat sketch.

**Model Answer:**
Flame length is the axial distance measured from the base of a vertical fuel source to the tip of the visible luminous zone, along the centerline of a buoyant diffusion flame.
- **Visible Flame Length ($L_v$):** Distance to the point where the **intermittent flame pockets** disappear (tip of the visible flame).
- **Continuous Flame Length ($L_c$):** Axial distance over which the flame is **continuously present** without breaks.

$L_v > L_c$ always. The mean flame length used in correlations is typically $L_f$ which lies between these two.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the **Zukoski correlation** for flame length and list the variables with their standard units used in KTU problems.

**Model Answer:**
Zukoski's correlation is:

$$\dfrac{L_f}{D} = -1.02 + 3.7\left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/5}}{D}\right)^{2/3}$$

where:
- $L_f$ = Flame length in **meters**
- $D$ = Source diameter in **meters**
- $\dot{Q}$ = Heat release rate in **kW**

It is valid for **buoyancy-dominated** circular diffusion flames with $\dot{Q}^{2/5}/D \le 1$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the **dimensionless scaling** for the flame length of a buoyant diffusion flame using the **Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem**, identifying the key dimensionless group that emerges.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A circular pool fire of **diameter $D = 1.0$ m** burns with a steady heat release rate of **$\dot{Q} = 2000$ kW**. Calculate the flame length using the **Heskestad correlation** and comment on the fire regime (buoyancy or momentum dominated) using $u = 0.5$ m/s as the exit velocity.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Follow the derivation in Section 3.1 above. The result is:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_f \, g^{3/7} \, \rho_\infty^{2/7}}{\dot{Q}^{2/7}}$$

yielding the scaling $L_f \sim \dot{Q}^{2/7} / (g^{3/7} \rho_\infty^{2/7})$, which with empirical entrainment corrections reduces to $L_f \propto \dot{Q}^{2/5}$ for circular sources.

**Part (b):** Using Heskestad's correlation:

$$L_f = -1.02(1.0) + 0.235(2000)^{2/5}$$

Compute $2000^{2/5}$:
$$2000^{2/5} = 2000^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 2000} = e^{0.4 \times 7.601} = e^{3.04} \approx 20.91$$

Therefore:
$$L_f = -1.02 + 0.235 \times 20.91 = -1.02 + 4.91 = 3.89 \text{ m}$$

**Froude Number Calculation:**

$$Fr = \frac{u^2}{gD} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{9.81 \times 1.0} = \frac{0.25}{9.81} \approx 0.0255$$

Since $Fr = 0.0255 < 0.05$, the fire is in the **buoyancy-dominated regime**, confirming that Heskestad's correlation is the correct choice.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Compare the **Zukoski** and **Heskestad** correlations for flame length. Under what conditions does each give reliable predictions?

(b) **[7 Marks]** A vertical wall fire has a heat release rate of **$\dot{Q} = 500$ kW**. Using Hasemi's wall fire correlation, $L_f = 0.025\,\dot{Q}^{2/3}$, determine the flame height. What is the ceiling clearance required for safe design if a 1 m safety factor is added?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** 
- **Zukoski:** Originally derived from axisymmetric pool fire experiments; valid for **moderate HRR/D ratios**; over-predicts at very high HRR.
- **Heskestad:** Calibrated for **purely buoyant pool fires**; valid when $Fr \ll 1$ and the flame is buoyancy-dominated.
- For typical KTU problems (pool fires, $D < 1$ m, $\dot{Q} < 5000$ kW), Heskestad is preferred.

**Part (b):**
$$L_f = 0.025 \times (500)^{2/3}$$

Compute $500^{2/3}$:
$$500^{1/3} \approx 7.937, \quad 500^{2/3} = (7.937)^2 \approx 63.0$$

$$L_f = 0.025 \times 63.0 = 1.575 \text{ m}$$

**Ceiling clearance required:**
$$H_{clear} = L_f + 1.0 = 1.575 + 1.0 = 2.575 \text{ m} \approx 2.58 \text{ m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** The biggest mark-loser. $\dot{Q}$ **MUST** be in **kW** for Heskestad, Zukoski, and Thomas. Using watts will give 1000× wrong answers.
> 2. **Correlation Mismatch:** Using Zukoski for very high $\dot{Q}/D$ (intense fires) or Hawthorne for pool fires = full 7 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting the $-1.02 D$ term:** A common error. The full Heskestad form is $L_f = -1.02 D + 0.235 \dot{Q}^{2/5}$, NOT just $0.235 \dot{Q}^{2/5}$.
> 4. **Mixing flame length types:** Do NOT confuse mean, continuous, and visible flame lengths in numerical answers.
> 5. **Ignoring the Froude check:** If the problem gives you a velocity, **always compute $Fr$ first** to validate your correlation choice.
> 6. **Rounding too early:** Keep at least 3–4 significant figures during intermediate steps. Rounding $15.85$ to $16$ early can compound to 5–10% error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Flame length** is the axial distance from the fuel base to the visible tip of a vertical buoyant diffusion flame.
- ✅ **Three types:** Visible ($L_v$), Continuous ($L_c$), Mean ($L_f$).
- ✅ **Master scaling law:** $L_f \propto \dot{Q}^{2/5}$ for circular buoyant flames.
- ✅ **Most cited KTU formula — Heskestad:** $L_f = -1.02\,D + 0.235\,\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ with $\dot{Q}$ in kW, $D$ in m, $L_f$ in m.
- ✅ **Zukoski's full form:** $L_f / D = -1.02 + 3.7 (\dot{Q}^{2/5} / D)^{2/3}$.
- ✅ **Froude Number** is the regime selector: $Fr = u^2 / (gD)$.
- ✅ **Buoyancy-dominated:** $Fr < 0.05$ → use Heskestad / Zukoski.
- ✅ **Momentum-dominated:** $Fr > 5$ → use Hawthorne / jet flame correlations.
- ✅ **Wall fires:** Use Hasemi's $L_f = 0.025\,\dot{Q}^{2/3}$ (valid for vertical wall-attached flames).
- ✅ **Always check units** — $\dot{Q}$ in kW, not W.
- ✅ **Dimensional analysis** gives $L_f \sim \dot{Q}^{2/7} / (g^{3/7} \rho^{2/7})$; empirical corrections yield the $2/5$ exponent.
- ✅ **Flame length is INDEPENDENT of chemical kinetics** in the buoyant regime — controlled by entrainment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soot Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Soot Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Soot** is defined as the carbonaceous solid-phase particulate product of the **incomplete combustion** or **pyrolysis** of hydrocarbon fuels, formed when fuel-rich regions of a flame cannot find sufficient oxygen to convert all carbon into carbon dioxide (CO₂) and carbon monoxide (CO). It is operationally characterised as a black, sub-micron aggregate composed of tens to thousands of primary spherules whose elemental composition is overwhelmingly **carbon (≈ 90–98 %)**, with minor hydrogen, oxygen, and trace metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT528 – Module 3):**
> Soot formation refers to the *gas-phase chemical and physical process* by which fuel vapour, after being cracked by heat, condenses first into **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)**, then into nascent soot nuclei (≈ 1–2 nm), which grow by surface reaction and coalescence into primary particles (≈ 20–50 nm) and finally into branched, fractal-like aggregates (≈ 0.1–1 μm).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Charcoal Factory Inside a Flame":**
> Imagine a busy bakery baking bread. If the oven has plenty of air, the dough turns golden (clean combustion → CO₂ + H₂O). But if you crowd too many dough pieces in and close the vents, the middle pieces turn black and smoky because they could not be properly cooked. A flame behaves similarly: in the **fuel-rich core** of a diffusion flame, fuel vapour is "overcrowded" and cannot find oxygen, so it cooks itself into tiny black carbon specks — **soot**. These specks then drift outward, get reheated, clump together (like clumping wet flour), and either burn up in the oxygen-rich zone or escape as smoke.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (KTU High-Yield Metrics)

- **Soot Volume Fraction ($f_v$):** typically **10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶** in luminous flames.
- **Soot Yield ($Y_s$):** mass of soot produced per mass of fuel burned.
- **Number Density ($N_s$):** soot particle count per cm³, often **10⁷ – 10⁹ cm⁻³**.
- **Primary Particle Diameter ($d_p$):** 20 – 50 nm.
- **Aggregate Gyration Diameter ($d_g$):** 0.1 – 1 μm.
- **Fractal Dimension ($D_f$):** ≈ 1.7 – 1.9 (aggregates are not solid spheres).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Logical Stages of Soot Formation

A KTU board examiner typically expects students to enumerate soot formation in **five sequential stages**. Each stage answers a "why" and a "how":

1. **Fuel Pyrolysis & Cracking**
   - *Why:* Solid/liquid fuels must first vaporise and break C–C bonds.
   - *How:* Heat decomposes long-chain hydrocarbons into smaller C₁–C₄ radicals (CH₃, C₂H₂, C₃H₃).

2. **Nucleation (Inception)**
   - *Why:* Solid carbon must be "born" — the first condensation of matter.
   - *How:* Small molecules (chiefly **acetylene C₂H₂** and **vinylacetylene C₄H₄**) collide and stack into the first ring — **benzene (C₆H₆)** — which fuses into **PAH (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon)** sheets (pyrene, coronene, ovalene).

3. **Surface Growth (Mass Addition)**
   - *Why:* Nuclei are too small to scatter visible light; they must grow.
   - *How:* Acetylene molecules continually add to the PAH edges (HACA mechanism), increasing mass by orders of magnitude without increasing the particle number.

4. **Coagulation / Agglomeration**
   - *Why:* The collision of two particles is energetically favoured (van der Waals).
   - *How:* Brownian motion drives particles to stick on contact → chains of spheres → branched, fractal-like aggregates.

5. **Oxidation (Destruction)**
   - *Why:* Soot in the oxidising zone of a flame is consumed by **OH, O, and O₂**.
   - *How:* Surface reaction: $\text{C}(s) + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}$

### 2.2 The HACA Mechanism (Hydrogen-Abstraction–Acetylene-Addition)

This is the **central growth mechanism** used by most modern CFD soot models (e.g., Moss, Lindstedt, Leung). The two repeating elementary steps are:

- **Step A — H-abstraction (creates an active site):**
$$\text{C}_{soot}\text{–H} + \text{H} \rightarrow \text{C}_{soot}^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2$$

- **Step B — Acetylene addition (adds two carbons):**
$$\text{C}_{soot}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_{soot}\text{–CH=CH}^{\bullet}$$

The new radical site regenerates, and the cycle repeats. This is the **dominant pathway** for converting PAH clusters into mature soot particles.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi = 1$ stoich, $\phi > 1$ rich |
| 2 | Soot Yield | $Y_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{soot}}{\dot{m}_{fuel,consumed}}$ | kg soot / kg fuel | mass of soot per unit fuel burned |
| 3 | Soot Volume Fraction | $f_v = \dfrac{N_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3}{V_{flame}}$ | ppm (10⁻⁶) | fraction of flame volume occupied by soot |
| 4 | Smoke Point Height | $H_{sp} = \dfrac{C}{Y_s}$ (C = constant for fuel) | mm | inverse of sooting tendency |
| 5 | Aggregate Mass | $m_a = k_f \left(\dfrac{d_g}{d_p}\right)^{D_f}$ | kg | fractal relation |
| 6 | OH-Oxidation Rate | $-\dfrac{d[C_{soot}]}{dt} = k_{OH}[OH][C_{soot}]$ | mol m⁻³ s⁻¹ | first-order surface oxidation |
| 7 | Global Nucleation Rate (Leung-Lindstedt) | $J_{nuc} \propto [C_2H_2]^2$ | particles m⁻³ s⁻¹ | acetylene-squared dependency |
| 8 | Soot Mass Growth | $\dfrac{df_v}{dt} = \underbrace{\dot{\omega}_{sg}}_{\text{surface growth}} + \underbrace{\dot{\omega}_{coag}}_{\text{coag}} - \underbrace{\dot{\omega}_{ox}}_{\text{oxidation}}$ | s⁻¹ | net soot volume balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical convention — Use \vert for absolute value inside tables.** Never use the pipe `|x|` as it breaks markdown table rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Detection:** Soot-laden smoke obscures visibility and is the primary signal in photoelectric smoke detectors.
- **Fire Suppression Design:** Soot reduces radiative heat feedback, *lowering* flame spread on certain solid fuels.
- **Combustion Efficiency:** High soot yield = low combustion efficiency = loss of chemical energy to pollutant.
- **Health & Toxicology:** Inhaled soot (PM2.5) carries carcinogenic PAHs into human alveoli.
- **Forensic Fire Investigation:** Soot patterns on walls reveal fire origin, direction, and ventilation history.
- **Aero-Engine / Gas Turbine:** Soot deposition fouls turbine blades and raises IR signature of military aircraft.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Soot Volume Fraction from Laser Extinction

When a low-power laser beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a soot-laden flame, it is attenuated by absorption and scattering. The transmitted intensity $I$ follows the **Beer–Lambert law**:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-K_{ext} \, L}$$

**Step 1 — Define the extinction coefficient.**
For small soot particles in the Rayleigh limit ($d_p \ll \lambda$), the extinction coefficient is:

$$K_{ext} = N_s \, \frac{\pi^2 d_p^3}{\lambda} \, E(m)$$

where $E(m)$ is a complex refractive-index function (for soot, $E(m) \approx 0.26$).

**Step 2 — Solve for $N_s \, d_p^3$.**

$$N_s \, d_p^3 = \frac{\lambda \, K_{ext}}{\pi^2 E(m)} = \frac{\lambda}{\pi^2 E(m) L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Convert $N_s d_p^3$ into soot volume fraction.**

By definition, the volume of one primary sphere is $\tfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3$. So the total soot volume per unit flame volume is:

$$f_v = N_s \, \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the result of Step 2.**

$$f_v = \frac{\lambda}{6 \pi E(m) L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form (the KTU board "ready" answer):**

$$\boxed{\,f_v = \frac{\lambda \ln(I_0/I)}{6 \pi E(m) L}\,}$$

> This is the *only* experimentally used formula on most KTU theory papers for soot quantification. A student is expected to derive it in 3–4 lines on the answer script.

### 3.2 Derivation: Net Soot Mass Balance in a Control Volume

Consider a small control volume inside a sooting diffusion flame. Let $m_s$ be the soot mass per unit volume. Three rates act on it simultaneously:

$$\frac{dm_s}{dt} = \underbrace{\dot{\omega}_{nuc}}_{\text{birth}} + \underbrace{\dot{\omega}_{sg}}_{\text{surface growth}} - \underbrace{\dot{\omega}_{ox}}_{\text{oxidation}}$$

**Step 1 — Nucleation rate (per Leung-Lindstedt).** Assumed proportional to acetylene squared:

$$\dot{\omega}_{nuc} = k_{nuc} \, [C_2H_2]^2$$

**Step 2 — Surface-growth rate (per HACA).** Mass added per unit surface area times the surface area:

$$\dot{\omega}_{sg} = k_{sg} \, [C_2H_2] \, A_{s}$$

where $A_s = \pi d_p^2 N_s$ is the total soot surface per unit volume.

**Step 3 — Oxidation rate (per Neoh).** Dominated by OH attack:

$$\dot{\omega}_{ox} = k_{ox} \, [OH] \, A_s$$

**Step 4 — Plug in and obtain the differential equation:**

$$\frac{dm_s}{dt} = k_{nuc}[C_2H_2]^2 + (k_{sg}[C_2H_2] - k_{ox}[OH])\pi d_p^2 N_s$$

This equation is integrated along a streamline in CFD codes to predict $f_v(x, t)$ in real flames.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A polyurethane foam sample is burned in a cone calorimeter at a heat flux of 50 kW/m². The smoke produced absorbs a He–Ne laser ($\lambda = 632.8$ nm) such that $I/I_0 = 0.42$ over a path length $L = 0.25$ m. Take $E(m) = 0.26$. Compute the soot volume fraction $f_v$.

**Step 1 — Compute the logarithm.**

$$\ln(I_0/I) = \ln(1/0.42) = \ln(2.381) = 0.867$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator.**

$$\lambda \ln(I_0/I) = 632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.867 = 5.486 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$6 \pi E(m) L = 6 \pi (0.26)(0.25) = 1.225\ \text{(dimensionless)·m}$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**

$$f_v = \frac{5.486 \times 10^{-7}}{1.225} = 4.478 \times 10^{-7} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to ppm.**

$$f_v \times 10^6 = 0.448\ \text{ppm}$$

> This value (≈ 0.45 ppm) is typical of moderately-sooty polymer fires — well within the KTU expected range of $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-6}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Soot Formation Pathway
