The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **physical-chemical architecture** of soot inception, growth, agglomeration, and destruction in a diffusion flame.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid or Solid Fuel] --> B[Pyrolysis Zone\nT 600 to 900 K]
    B --> C[Small Hydrocarbon Gases\nCH4 C2H2 C3H3 C4H4]
    C --> D[PAH Formation\nBenzene then Pyrene then Coronene]
    D --> E[Soot Nucleation\nParticle dia 1 to 2 nm]
    E --> F[Surface Growth HACA\nParticle dia 20 to 50 nm]
    F --> G[Coagulation and Agglomeration\nFractal dia 0.1 to 1 micrometer]
    G --> H{Flame Zone}
    H -->|Fuel Rich Side| I[Soot Survives as Smoke]
    H -->|Oxidising Side| J[Oxidation by OH and O2\nCO and CO2 released]
    I --> K[Ambient Air Plume]
    J --> L[Clean Combustion Products]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — HACA Surface Growth Cycle

The HACA cycle is a self-propagating radical loop. Mermaid `flowchart TD` is used (node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pA[Soot Particle CH Site] --> pB[H atom attacks and abstracts H]
    pB --> pC[Active Radical Site C dot]
    pC --> pD[Acetylene C2H2 adds across site]
    pD --> pE[New CH2 C dot site formed]
    pE --> pF[Particle mass increased by C2H2]
    pF --> pA
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Factors Influencing Soot Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fuel Type\nAromatic or Aliphatic] --> OUT[Soot Yield Ys]
    F2[Equivalence Ratio phi\nPhi greater than 1.5] --> OUT
    F3[Flame Temperature T\n1300 to 1700 K peak] --> OUT
    F4[Residence Time tau\nabove 10 ms] --> OUT
    F5[Pressure P\nNTP to High Pressure] --> OUT
    F6[Strain Rate\nOpposed Jet or Co flow] --> OUT
    F7[Inorganic Additives\nFeCl3 BaSO4 Na] --> OUT
    OUT --> APP[Application Impact]
    APP --> A1[Fire Detection]
    APP --> A2[Health PM2.5]
    APP --> A3[Combustion Efficiency]
    APP --> A4[Forensic Pattern]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section of a Luminous Diffusion Flame (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FlameCore[Fuel Rich Core]
    Z1[Zone 1 Pyrolysis 600 to 900 K]
    Z2[Zone 2 PAH and Nucleation 1200 to 1400 K]
    Z3[Zone 3 Surface Growth 1400 to 1600 K]
    Z4[Zone 4 Soot Laden 1500 to 1700 K]
    Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 --> Z4
    end
    FlameCore --> Z5[Zone 5 Oxidation Sheath 1700 to 2000 K]
    Z5 --> Z6[Zone 6 Burnt Gas Products]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define soot. List the five stages of soot formation in a diffusion flame.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Soot** is a carbonaceous, sub-micron, fractal aggregate produced by the incomplete combustion or pyrolysis of hydrocarbon fuels, consisting of 90–98 % elemental carbon with minor H, O, and trace metals.
> The five stages are:
> 1. **Pyrolysis & cracking** of fuel into small hydrocarbons.
> 2. **Nucleation** of the first PAH clusters into soot nuclei.
> 3. **Surface growth** by HACA mechanism.
> 4. **Coagulation** into branched aggregates.
> 5. **Oxidation** by OH and O₂ in the flame front.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**What is the HACA mechanism? Why is it central to soot surface growth?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> HACA stands for **Hydrogen-Abstraction–Acetylene-Addition**. It is a two-step radical cycle in which (a) an H atom abstracts a hydrogen from the edge of a soot particle to create an active radical site, and (b) an acetylene (C₂H₂) molecule adds across that site, increasing the particle's mass by C₂H₂ and regenerating an active site. It is central because experiments and quantum-chemistry calculations show that *acetylene-driven* mass addition is the **dominant pathway** by which a 1–2 nm PAH nucleus grows into a mature 30–50 nm primary particle. Without HACA, soot volume fractions in laminar diffusion flames fall by 1–2 orders of magnitude.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> KTU ESE pattern: Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (typically **a = 7 marks** and **b = 7 marks**). Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **five-stage mechanism of soot formation** in a hydrocarbon diffusion flame. Identify the dominant chemical species at each stage and the typical temperature window.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A laser beam of wavelength 632.8 nm is transmitted through a sooting flame over a path length of 0.30 m. The transmittance is measured as $I/I_0 = 0.35$. Using $E(m) = 0.26$, calculate (i) the soot volume fraction and (ii) the smoke yield if the mass of soot collected on a filter over a 60-s burn is 0.12 g and the fuel consumption rate is 0.020 kg/s.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) The five stages** *(7 marks distributed as 1 mark per stage, 2 marks for the schematic)*:
>
> 1. **Pyrolysis (600–900 K):** Liquid/solid fuel → small hydrocarbon gases (CH₄, C₂H₂, C₃H₃).
> 2. **Nucleation (1200–1400 K):** Small molecules → benzene → PAH stacks → 1–2 nm soot nuclei.
> 3. **Surface Growth (1400–1600 K):** HACA mechanism adds C₂H₂ → 20–50 nm primary particles.
> 4. **Coagulation (1300–1500 K):** Brownian collisions → fractal aggregates, $D_f \approx 1.78$.
> 5. **Oxidation (1500–2000 K):** OH, O, O₂ attack particles → CO and finally CO₂.
>
> *(A labelled flame cross-section similar to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 should be drawn on the answer script.)*
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Compute $\ln(I_0/I)$:**
>
> $$\ln(1/0.35) = \ln(2.857) = 1.0496$$
>
> **Step 2 — Compute $f_v$ using derived formula:**
>
> $$f_v = \frac{\lambda \ln(I_0/I)}{6 \pi E(m) L} = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.0496}{6 \pi \times 0.26 \times 0.30}$$
>
> Numerator: $632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.0496 = 6.642 \times 10^{-7}$ m
> Denominator: $6 \pi (0.26)(0.30) = 1.4705$
>
> $$f_v = \frac{6.642 \times 10^{-7}}{1.4705} = 4.516 \times 10^{-7} = 0.4516\ \text{ppm}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute smoke yield $Y_s$:**
> Mass of soot in 60 s: $0.12$ g $= 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ kg.
> Mass of fuel in 60 s: $0.020 \times 60 = 1.2$ kg.
>
> $$Y_s = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \frac{\text{kg soot}}{\text{kg fuel}}$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $f_v = 0.45\ \text{ppm}$, $Y_s = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/kg}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **(i) equivalence ratio**, **(ii) fuel aromaticity**, and **(iii) pressure** on soot yield. Use a fully-labelled graph of $Y_s$ vs $\phi$ for methane and benzene to support your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the soot volume fraction measured by the **laser-extinction technique** starting from the Beer–Lambert law. State two important assumptions used in the derivation.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Effect of parameters on $Y_s$ (7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Equivalence ratio $\phi$** *(2 marks):* Soot yield rises sharply once $\phi > 1$ (fuel-rich). Maximum $Y_s$ occurs around $\phi \approx 1.5$–2.0. For $\phi < 1$ (lean), the flame is self-cleaning and soot is rapidly oxidised.
> 2. **Fuel aromaticity** *(2 marks):* Aromatic fuels (benzene, toluene) inherently contain a 6-membered ring, which is the *first* PAH. Hence they form soot at *lower* temperatures and *shorter* residence times. Aliphatic fuels (methane, ethane) must *first* build a ring, giving lower $Y_s$. Benzene $Y_s$ at $\phi = 1.8$ is roughly **5–10 × higher** than methane.
> 3. **Pressure** *(2 marks):* Soot volume fraction scales approximately as $f_v \propto P^{1.5 \text{ to } 2.0}$. Higher pressure increases three-body collision rates that aid PAH condensation and coagulation. Aviation gas turbines at 30 atm produce dense, persistent smoke that is absent at 1 atm.
>
> **Graph (1 mark):** A typical plot has $Y_s$ on the y-axis and $\phi$ on the x-axis. Benzene curve is *above* methane curve for all $\phi > 1$, and both peak near $\phi \approx 1.7$.
>
> **(b) Derivation (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Beer–Lambert law:** *(1 mark)*
>
> $$I = I_0 \, e^{-K_{ext} L}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Extinction coefficient in Rayleigh limit** *(2 marks)* — valid when $d_p \ll \lambda$:
>
> $$K_{ext} = N_s \, \frac{\pi^2 d_p^3}{\lambda} \, E(m)$$
>
> **Step 3 — Solve for $N_s d_p^3$:** *(2 marks)*
>
> $$N_s d_p^3 = \frac{\lambda}{\pi^2 E(m) L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$
>
> **Step 4 — Convert to $f_v$:** *(2 marks)*
>
> $$f_v = N_s \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_v = \frac{\lambda}{6 \pi E(m) L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$
>
> **Assumptions (mention in answer):**
> 1. Primary particles are in the **Rayleigh limit** ($d_p \ll \lambda$), so scattering is negligible compared with absorption.
> 2. $E(m)$ is a known, **constant** property of soot ($\approx 0.26$ for $\lambda = 632.8$ nm), independent of flame conditions.
> 3. The soot cloud is **optically thin** (no multiple scattering, no shadowing).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing soot volume fraction $f_v$ with soot mass concentration.** $f_v$ is dimensionless (in ppm), not a density.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 6** in the denominator of the laser-extinction formula. Many students write $4\pi$ or $3\pi$.
> 3. **Wrong units of $E(m)$** — it is *dimensionless*, not a length.
> 4. **Forgetting the role of oxidation** when listing soot formation stages; the KTU rubric awards 1 mark specifically to the oxidation stage.
> 5. **Confusing nucleation with surface growth.** Nucleation *creates* the first particles; growth *enlarges* existing ones. The examiner will mark this strictly.
> 6. **Using $E(m) = 1$** or omitting it entirely in numerical problems.
> 7. **Forgetting the equivalence ratio** when asked "why does benzene soot more than methane?" — aromaticity alone is not the complete answer; $\phi$ is also higher in many practical cases.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Soot = carbonaceous, sub-micron, fractal aggregate** with 90–98 % C, formed by incomplete combustion.
- **Five stages:** Pyrolysis → Nucleation (PAH → nuclei) → Surface Growth (HACA) → Coagulation → Oxidation.
- **HACA mechanism** is the *central* surface-growth pathway: H-abstraction + C₂H₂ addition, repeated cyclically.
- **Equivalence ratio $\phi$** is the single most important controllable variable — soot appears for $\phi \gtrsim 1.4$.
- **Aromatic fuels soot earlier and more** than aliphatic fuels because they already contain the seed PAH ring.
- **Pressure** boosts soot formation; $f_v \propto P^{1.5 \text{–} 2.0}$.
- **Smoke Point** is inversely related to sooting tendency — a *low* smoke point = a *sooty* fuel.
- **Laser extinction formula** to memorise for KTU numerical problems:
  $$f_v = \frac{\lambda \ln(I_0/I)}{6 \pi E(m) L}$$
  with $E(m) \approx 0.26$ for soot at 632.8 nm.
- **Fractal dimension $D_f \approx 1.78$** characterises soot aggregates — not perfect spheres.
- **Typical magnitude of $f_v$** in luminous flames: $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-6}$ (i.e., 0.01 to 1 ppm).
- **Forensic relevance:** Soot pattern on walls is a directional map of fire flow and a marker of ventilation-controlled flaming.
- **Environmental & health:** Soot is a major component of PM2.5 and a known carcinogen carrier (PAHs adsorbed on soot surfaces).
- **Soot oxidation** is dominated by **OH** at flame temperatures; failure of oxidation is what allows *smoke* to escape the flame.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soot Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Soot Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soot Oxidation** is the *heterogeneous chemical reaction* by which solid carbonaceous soot particles (in-situ generated within a diffusion flame envelope) are converted back into gaseous combustion products (primarily **CO** and **CO₂**) through attack by gaseous oxidizing species — chiefly molecular oxygen (**O₂**) and the hydroxyl radical (**OH**).

In the context of fire dynamics and flame radiation, soot oxidation is the **counter-process to soot formation**. It determines the *ultimate soot yield* that escapes the flame, controls the **luminous yellow/sooty appearance** of a diffusion flame, and strongly governs the **radiative heat flux** that drives fire propagation.

The heterogeneous surface reaction can be written in its simplest global form:

$$ \text{C(s)} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H_r = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{C(s)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H_r = -110.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{C(s)} + \text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H} $$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **campfire**: you pile up wood (fuel) which heats and releases flammable vapours. The flame is bright yellow because of tiny glowing **carbon specks (soot)** carried up in the hot gases. Now picture those specks as **microscopic hot coals floating in the flame**. 

- As long as they stay in the **oxygen-rich outer envelope of the flame**, the surrounding O₂ "eats" them — this is **soot oxidation**.
- The specks that escape the flame too quickly, or that travel through oxygen-poor zones, survive and exit as black smoke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of soot particles as *toasted bread crumbs* in a hot oven. If you leave them too short, they are pale (unburnt soot). If you leave them too long, they disappear entirely (fully oxidized to CO/CO₂). The oven temperature and the oxygen concentration around them are the two "knobs" that control oxidation rate.

## 1.3 Why Soot Oxidation Matters in Fire Engineering

| Phenomenon | Role of Soot Oxidation |
|------------|------------------------|
| **Fire radiation** | Oxidized soot = less luminous flame = lower radiative fraction |
| **Smoke yield** | Incomplete oxidation = black, toxic smoke = life safety hazard |
| **Combustion efficiency** | Determines how much carbon exits unburned |
| **Pollutant emission** | Affects **PAH**, **CO**, and **soot mass** released to environment |
| **Fire detection** | Soot + ionization/light-scattering signatures change with oxidation state |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Highlighted)

- **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard soot particle diameter, d_p ≈ 20–50 nm** in mature flames
- **Soot density, ρ_s ≈ 1800–2000 kg/m³** (often taken as **ρ_s = 1850 kg/m³**)
- **Stoichiometric O₂/Soot mass ratio = 2.67** for complete combustion to CO₂
- **Stoichiometric O₂/Soot mass ratio = 0.89** for partial combustion to CO

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus highlight: The dominant oxidizing species in diffusion flames switch from **O₂-dominant** in the *high-temperature* (> 1500 K) outer flame to **OH-dominant** in the *intermediate-temperature* (1000–1500 K) region. **Both species must be accounted for** in any rigorous oxidation model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Picture of Soot Oxidation

When a soot particle is born in the fuel-rich core of a diffusion flame, it begins a **race** between three processes:

1. **Surface growth** (addition of gas-phase PAHs and C₂H₂) — increases mass.
2. **Coagulation / aggregation** — increases size.
3. **Oxidation** (attack by O₂ and OH) — decreases mass.

The *final soot yield* at the flame tip is determined by whichever process dominates in the final moments of the particle's residence time.

### Step 1 — Transport of Oxidizer to the Surface

The oxidizer (O₂ or OH) must first **diffuse** from the bulk gas to the particle's surface. This is governed by Fick's law and is characterized by the **Sherwood number (Sh)**. For the **kinetically-controlled regime** (small particles, Knudsen number Kn > 1), the mass transfer rate is:

$$ \dot{m}_{\text{diff}} = 2 \pi d_p D_{ox} \text{Sh} \, C_{ox} $$

where $D_{ox}$ is the oxidizer diffusivity and $C_{ox}$ is the bulk gas-phase concentration of the oxidizer.

### Step 2 — Surface Chemical Reaction

Once the oxidizer reaches the surface, it reacts according to an **Arrhenius-type rate law**. The temperature dependence is exponential, making the surface reaction the rate-limiting step at high temperatures.

### Step 3 — Product Desorption

The product species (CO, CO₂, H) desorb from the surface, freeing active sites for further attack. This is generally fast and not rate-limiting.

## 2.2 The Nagle–Strickland–Constable (NSC) Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important model** for soot oxidation in fire/combustion engineering. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists the NSC oxidation mechanism.

The **NSC model** (Nagle & Strickland-Constable, 1962) is empirical, based on extensive experimental data on carbon oxidation in O₂. It divides the oxidation into two **parallel reaction regimes**:

- **Regime I (Branching oxidation):** T < ~1700 K. The rate is governed by the number of active surface sites.
- **Regime II (Aging oxidation):** T > ~1700 K. The active sites become depleted/annealed, and the rate is governed by the slower gasification of the more stable graphitic structure.

The **mass-based oxidation rate** is:

$$
\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} = \rho_s \, K_{\text{oxi}} \, \frac{P_{O_2}}{P_t} \quad \text{[kg/(m}^2\text{·s)]}
$$

where $P_t$ is total pressure and $P_{O_2}$ is partial pressure of O₂.

### The NSC Rate Coefficient K_oxi

**For T ≤ 1700 K (Regime I — Branching):**

$$ K_{\text{oxi}} = 0.13 \exp\!\left(-\frac{15{,}100}{T}\right) \; \text{[kg/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)]} $$

**For T > 1700 K (Regime II — Aging):**

$$ K_{\text{oxi}} = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \, T^{0.5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{7{,}640}{T}\right) \; \text{[kg/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)]} $$

The **switching temperature** is exactly **T = 1700 K** (≈ 1427 °C).

## 2.3 Oxidation by OH Radicals

In diffusion flames, the **OH radical concentration** in the reaction zone can be very high (~10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵ mol/cm³). OH is **~1000× more reactive** with soot than O₂ on a per-molecule basis, although its overall contribution is comparable due to lower concentration.

The **collision-rate-limited** OH oxidation rate (Fenimore & Jones) is:

$$ \dot{w}_{\text{OH}} = \gamma_{\text{OH}} \cdot [\text{OH}] \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R T}{2 \pi M_{\text{OH}}}} \cdot \frac{M_C}{N_A} \cdot 4 \pi r_p^2 $$

where $\gamma_{\text{OH}}$ is the **accommodation/oxidation coefficient** (typically 0.1–0.3) and $r_p$ is the particle radius.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} = \rho_s K_{\text{oxi}} (P_{O_2}/P_t)$ | NSC oxidation rate | kg/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $K_{\text{oxi}} = 0.13 \exp(-15{,}100/T)$ | NSC rate, T ≤ 1700 K | kg/(m²·s·atm) |
| 3 | $K_{\text{oxi}} = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} T^{0.5} \exp(-7640/T)$ | NSC rate, T > 1700 K | kg/(m²·s·atm) |
| 4 | $\dot{w}_{\text{OH}} \propto [\text{OH}]\sqrt{T}$ | OH oxidation rate | kg/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $\tau_{\text{ox}} = d_p \rho_s / (6 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}})$ | Soot particle oxidation lifetime | s |
| 6 | $\dot{N}_{\text{ox}} = 4 \pi r_p^2 \, k_s \, C_{O_2}$ | Surface reaction rate (Lee-Thring) | 1/s |
| 7 | $\text{Sh} = 2 + 0.6 \, \text{Re}^{0.5} \text{Sc}^{0.33}$ | Sherwood number (Ranz-Marshall) | — |
| 8 | $\text{Kn} = 2 \lambda / d_p$ | Knudsen number | — |
| 9 | $A = 4 \pi r_p^2 k_s C_{O_2} / (4 \pi D_{O_2} \text{Sh} \, r_p)$ | Combustion number (Damköhler) | — |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{soot,ox}} = \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \, A_p \, N_p$ | Total soot mass oxidized per unit volume | kg/(m³·s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In all exam answers, always state which regime (Branching or Aging) you are in, based on whether T is above or below 1700 K.** This is a very common place where students lose marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire suppression & smoke control design:** Lower temperatures (< 1500 K) reduce NSC O₂ oxidation but enhance OH-driven burnout.
- **IC engine design:** Lower combustion temperatures reduce soot (diesel particulate filters exploit oxidation to clean traps).
- **Industrial furnace design:** Adjusting air staging and residence time at 1500–1700 K maximizes soot burnout.
- **Fire investigation:** Soot deposition patterns and oxidation state help reconstruct fire development.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of NSC Oxidation Rate — Branching Regime

The NSC empirical expression derives from a two-site surface mechanism. The *active site* ($C^*$) reacts quickly with O₂, while the *inactive site* ($C_f$) reacts much more slowly. The fraction of active sites is determined by a branching/annealing equilibrium:

$$ \frac{d f_a}{dt} = k_b \, N_{O_2} (1 - f_a) - k_a \, f_a $$

where:
- $f_a$ = fraction of active sites
- $k_b$ = branching rate constant
- $k_a$ = annealing rate constant
- $N_{O_2}$ = molecular flux of O₂

At steady state, $df_a/dt = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_b N_{O_2} (1 - f_a) &= k_a f_a \\[4pt]
f_a (k_b N_{O_2} + k_a) &= k_b N_{O_2} \\[4pt]
f_a &= \frac{k_b N_{O_2}}{k_b N_{O_2} + k_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total oxidation rate is then the sum of the contributions from active and inactive sites:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} &= k_T N_{O_2} f_a + k_a^{\prime} N_{O_2} (1 - f_a) \\[4pt]
&= N_{O_2}\!\left[\frac{k_T k_b N_{O_2}}{k_b N_{O_2} + k_a} + \frac{k_a^{\prime} k_a}{k_b N_{O_2} + k_a}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting Arrhenius forms and fitting to experimental data over the temperature range **1200 K ≤ T ≤ 2000 K** with $P_{O_2}/P_t$ in atm gives the two empirical equations.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Soot Particle Oxidation Lifetime

The **oxidation lifetime** $\tau_{\text{ox}}$ of a single spherical soot particle of diameter $d_p$ is defined as the time required for complete burnout. Starting from a mass balance on a single particle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d m_p}{dt} &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \, A_p \\[4pt]
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_s\right) &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \, (\pi d_p^2) \\[4pt]
\frac{\pi}{2} d_p^2 \rho_s \, \frac{dd_p}{dt} &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \, \pi d_p^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancelling $\pi d_p^2$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} \rho_s \frac{dd_p}{dt} &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \\[4pt]
dd_p &= -\frac{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}{\rho_s} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating from initial diameter $d_{p,0}$ to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{d_{p,0}}^{0} d(d_p) &= -\frac{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}{\rho_s}\int_{0}^{\tau_{\text{ox}}} dt \\[4pt]
-d_{p,0} &= -\frac{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}{\rho_s} \, \tau_{\text{ox}} \\[4pt]
\tau_{\text{ox}} &= \frac{d_{p,0} \, \rho_s}{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Sometimes the formula is written with a factor of 6 in the denominator (depending on the definition of $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$ as per-area vs per-particle-mass basis). The **canonical form is**:

$$ \boxed{\;\tau_{\text{ox}} = \frac{d_{p,0} \, \rho_s}{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}\;} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For $d_{p,0} = 50$ nm, $\rho_s = 1850$ kg/m³, and $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} = 10^{-3}$ kg/(m²·s), we get $\tau_{\text{ox}} \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ s — a typical soot particle burns out in tens of microseconds at flame temperatures.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A soot particle of initial diameter **40 nm** is exposed to a flame at **T = 1800 K** with **P_{O₂} = 0.20 atm**. Calculate the NSC oxidation rate $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$, the oxidation lifetime $\tau_{\text{ox}}$, and the rate of diameter shrinkage.

**Step 1 — Identify the regime:** T = 1800 K > 1700 K → **Regime II (Aging).**

**Step 2 — Compute K_oxi (Regime II):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \, T^{0.5} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{7640}{T}\right) \\[4pt]
T^{0.5} &= \sqrt{1800} = 42.426 \\[4pt]
\exp\!\left(-\frac{7640}{1800}\right) &= \exp(-4.2444) = 0.01436 \\[4pt]
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \times 42.426 \times 0.01436 \\[4pt]
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 2.962 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$ with $P_{O_2}/P_t = 0.20$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} &= \rho_s \, K_{\text{oxi}} \, \frac{P_{O_2}}{P_t} \\[4pt]
&= 1850 \times 2.962 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 \\[4pt]
&= 1.096 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau_{\text{ox}}$ for $d_{p,0} = 40 \times 10^{-9}$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{ox}} &= \frac{d_{p,0} \, \rho_s}{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{40 \times 10^{-9} \times 1850}{2 \times 1.096} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-5}}{2.192} \\[4pt]
\tau_{\text{ox}} &= 3.376 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s} \;\; (\approx 34 \; \mu s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute rate of diameter shrinkage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dd_p}{dt} &= -\frac{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}{\rho_s} = -\frac{2 \times 1.096}{1850} \\[4pt]
&= -1.185 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m/s} \;\; = -1185 \; \mu\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 40-nm soot particle in an 1800-K flame with 20% O₂ burns out in **~34 microseconds** — a vivid illustration of why soot is rapidly consumed when it traverses an oxidizing zone.

## 3.4 Comparison Table: O₂ vs OH Oxidation

| Property | O₂ Oxidation (NSC) | OH Oxidation |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Activation energy | High (low-T: ~125 kJ/mol) | Very low (diffusion-limited) |
| Temperature dependence | Strong (exp) | Weak (~√T) |
| Concentration in flame | High (5–21%) | Low (~ppm) |
| Per-molecule reactivity | Low | ~10³× higher |
| Dominant regime | T > 1500 K | T < 1500 K |
| Models used | NSC, Lee-Keck | Neoh, Fenimore-Jones |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Soot Oxidation Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soot Particle In Flame] --> B[Convective/Diffusive Transport]
    B --> C{Oxidizer Type}
    C -->|O2 Dominant| D[O2 Diffusion To Surface]
    C -->|OH Dominant| E[OH Radical Attack]
    D --> F{Temperature Regime}
    F -->|T less than 1700 K| G[Branching Oxidation]
    F -->|T greater than 1700 K| H[Aging Oxidation]
    G --> I[Active Site C Star Reaction]
    H --> J[Inactive Site Cf Gasification]
    I --> K[Surface Reaction Ks Times CO2]
    J --> K
    E --> L[OH Collision Limited Reaction]
    K --> M[Product Desorption CO, CO2, H]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Particle Diameter Shrinks]
    N --> O{Dp Equals Zero}
    O -->|Yes| P[Complete Soot Burnout]
    O -->|No| Q[Residual Soot Escapes]
```

## 4.2 NSC Two-Regime Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Carbon Surface C] --> B[Active Sites C Star]
    A --> C[Inactive Sites Cf]
    B --> D[O2 Attack Rate Kb]
    C --> E[O2 Attack Rate Ka Prime]
    D --> F[Annealing To Cf, Rate Ka]
    F --> C
    D --> G[Branching Regime T less than 1700 K]
    E --> H[Aging Regime T greater than 1700 K]
    G --> I[Total Oxidation Rate Woxi]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Soot Mass Yield]
```

## 4.3 Temperature Map of Dominant Oxidation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diffusion Flame Temperature Field] --> B[T less than 1000 K]
    A --> C[1000 K to 1500 K]
    A --> D[1500 K to 1700 K]
    A --> E[T greater than 1700 K]
    B --> F[Negligible Oxidation, Soot Accumulates]
    C --> G[OH Dominant Oxidation]
    D --> H[OH Plus O2 Both Active, NSC Branching]
    E --> I[O2 Dominant, NSC Aging Regime]
    F --> J[Black Smoke Release]
    G --> K[Significant Soot Burnout]
    H --> K
    I --> L[Maximum Soot Burnout]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Soot Mass Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Pyrolysis] --> B[PAH Formation]
    B --> C[Soot Nucleation]
    C --> D[Surface Growth]
    D --> E[Coagulation]
    E --> F{Oxidation Zone}
    F -->|Yes| G[O2 Attack, NSC Model]
    F -->|Yes| H[OH Attack, Fenimore Jones]
    G --> I[Mass Loss To CO, CO2]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Soot Mass Fraction]
    E -->|No| K[Soot Released Unburnt]
    J --> L[Soot Yield Ys kg soot per kg fuel]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The two-regime NSC architecture (Section 4.2) shows the conceptual basis: there is a dynamic balance between the rapid attack of O₂ on **active sites** (which produces CO and creates more active sites) and the slow annealing that **inactivates** those sites. Below 1700 K the branching dominates; above 1700 K the annealing wins and the rate falls off.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between **branching** and **aging** regimes in the NSC soot oxidation model. State the switching temperature.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Branching Regime | Aging Regime |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| Temperature range | T ≤ 1700 K | T > 1700 K |
| Rate-controlling factor | Density of *active* surface sites | Annealing of active sites to inactive graphitic structure |
| K_oxi expression | $0.13 \exp(-15{,}100/T)$ | $4.86 \times 10^{-3} T^{0.5} \exp(-7640/T)$ |
| Mechanism | Self-sustaining creation of new active sites | Slow gasification of thermally annealed carbon |
| Effective activation energy | High (~125 kJ/mol) | Lower (~63 kJ/mol) |

**Switching temperature: T = 1700 K** (≈ 1427 °C).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Why is the **OH radical** considered a more effective soot oxidizer than O₂ at low flame temperatures, even though its concentration is orders of magnitude lower?

**Model Answer:**

The OH radical is more effective at lower temperatures (T < 1500 K) because:

1. **Lower activation energy:** The C + OH → CO + H reaction has a very low (almost zero) activation energy and is essentially **diffusion-limited**, while O₂ attack has a high activation energy (~125 kJ/mol in the branching regime).
2. **Per-molecule reactivity:** Each OH–carbon collision has a high probability (~0.1–0.3) of leading to oxidation, whereas the O₂–carbon collision probability is ~10³ times lower.
3. **Prevalence in flame:** OH is generated in large concentrations in the reaction zone of diffusion flames (peak ~10⁻⁶ mol/cm³), partially offsetting its lower bulk concentration.
4. **Temperature insensitivity:** OH oxidation rate scales only as $\sqrt{T}$, while O₂ oxidation rate scales exponentially — so as T drops, OH remains effective while O₂ attack collapses.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice A, 2024 Scheme]**

A 30-nm diameter soot particle is exposed to a flame at **T = 1500 K** and **P_{O₂} = 0.18 atm** (with $P_t = 1$ atm and $\rho_s = 1850$ kg/m³). 

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the NSC oxidation rate and the particle's oxidation lifetime.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the rate at 1500 K with the rate at 1900 K. Comment on the effect of temperature on soot burnout efficiency.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify regime:** T = 1500 K < 1700 K → **Branching regime**.

**Step 2 — Compute K_oxi:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 0.13 \exp\!\left(-\frac{15{,}100}{1500}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{15{,}100}{1500} &= 10.0667 \\[4pt]
\exp(-10.0667) &= 4.272 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 0.13 \times 4.272 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 5.554 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} &= \rho_s \, K_{\text{oxi}} \, \frac{P_{O_2}}{P_t} \\[4pt]
&= 1850 \times 5.554 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.18 \\[4pt]
&= 1.849 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau_{\text{ox}}$ for $d_{p,0} = 30 \times 10^{-9}$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{ox}} &= \frac{d_{p,0} \, \rho_s}{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{30 \times 10^{-9} \times 1850}{2 \times 1.849 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.55 \times 10^{-5}}{3.698 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
\tau_{\text{ox}} &= 1.501 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{s} \;\; (\approx 15 \; \text{ms})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]

**At T = 1900 K (Aging regime):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1900} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{7640}{1900}\right) \\[4pt]
\sqrt{1900} &= 43.589 \\[4pt]
\exp(-4.021) &= 0.01792 \\[4pt]
K_{\text{oxi}} &= 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \times 43.589 \times 0.01792 \\[4pt]
&= 3.796 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$ at 1900 K:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} &= 1850 \times 3.796 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.18 \\[4pt]
&= 1.264 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $\tau_{\text{ox}}$ at 1900 K:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{ox}} &= \frac{30 \times 10^{-9} \times 1850}{2 \times 1.264} \\[4pt]
&= 2.195 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s} \;\; (\approx 22 \; \mu\text{s})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison and comment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\dot{w}_{1900}}{\dot{w}_{1500}} &= \frac{1.264}{1.849 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 684 \\[4pt]
\frac{\tau_{1500}}{\tau_{1900}} &= \frac{15 \; \text{ms}}{22 \; \mu\text{s}} \approx 682
\end{aligned}
$$

The oxidation rate at 1900 K is **~684 times higher** than at 1500 K, reducing the burnout time by the same factor. **Comment:** Soot burnout efficiency is extremely sensitive to flame temperature. In real fire engineering, holding the flame temperature above 1700 K (in the aging regime) for a residence time of a few tens of microseconds is sufficient to oxidize virtually all soot. Below 1500 K, soot particles can survive milliseconds, easily escaping the flame as visible smoke.

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice B, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from a mass balance on a single spherical soot particle, derive an expression for the **oxidation lifetime** $\tau_{\text{ox}}$ of a particle of initial diameter $d_{p,0}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the relative contributions of **O₂** and **OH** oxidation in a typical diffusion flame. Identify the temperature zones where each dominates.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]

Starting from the mass balance on a single spherical soot particle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dm_p}{dt} &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \cdot A_p
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $m_p = \tfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_s$ and $A_p = \pi d_p^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_s\right) &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \cdot \pi d_p^2 \\[4pt]
\frac{\pi}{2} d_p^2 \rho_s \frac{dd_p}{dt} &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} \cdot \pi d_p^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancelling $\pi d_p^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} \rho_s \frac{dd_p}{dt} &= -\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $d_{p,0}$ at $t = 0$ to 0 at $t = \tau_{\text{ox}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{d_{p,0}}^{0} d(d_p) &= -\frac{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}{\rho_s}\int_{0}^{\tau_{\text{ox}}} dt \\[4pt]
-d_{p,0} &= -\frac{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}{\rho_s} \tau_{\text{ox}} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\tau_{\text{ox}} &= \frac{\rho_s \, d_{p,0}}{2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]

In a typical diffusion flame, soot forms in the **fuel-rich core** (T ≈ 1200–1500 K) and travels outward toward the **oxidizing envelope** (T > 1500 K). Two oxidizers attack the soot:

- **Molecular O₂ (dominant at T > 1500 K):** 
  - Governed by the **NSC model**.
  - Strong Arrhenius temperature dependence.
  - Bulk concentration high (~5–21 mol%).
  - Rate-determining at flame-tip temperatures.

- **OH radical (dominant at 1000 K < T < 1500 K):** 
  - Governed by the **Fenimore–Jones** or **Neoh** model.
  - Diffusion-limited; rate $\propto [\text{OH}]\sqrt{T}$.
  - Bulk concentration low (~ppm) but per-molecule reactivity ~10³× higher.

**Temperature zone map:**

| Zone | Temperature | Dominant Oxidizer | Oxidation Rate Behavior |
|------|-------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Soot formation core | 1000–1300 K | OH | Slow but non-negligible |
| Inner flame sheet | 1300–1500 K | OH + O₂ (comparable) | Increasing sharply |
| Outer reaction zone | 1500–1700 K | O₂ (NSC branching) | Rapid growth |
| Flame tip / luminous zone | 1700–2000 K | O₂ (NSC aging) | Maximum burnout |

**Engineering implication:** A soot particle that takes a *short* path through the high-T envelope is fully oxidized (clean blue/blue-yellow flame). A particle that *detours* through the low-T core (e.g., due to buoyancy in a buoyant diffusion flame) survives and exits as smoke. Air-staging in industrial burners and IC engines exploits this by extending residence time in the 1500–1900 K window to maximize soot burnout.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on soot oxidation questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to switch the NSC formula at T = 1700 K.** Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if you use the wrong expression. Always state the regime explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $P_{O_2}/P_t$ factor.** K_oxi is in units of kg/(m²·s·atm); without the partial pressure ratio, your answer is off by a factor of 5 (for air at 1 atm).
> 3. **Unit errors in $\tau_{\text{ox}}$.** Typical error: writing the formula as $d_{p,0} \rho_s / \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$ (missing the factor of 2). The factor of 2 comes from the area-to-volume ratio of a sphere.
> 4. **Confusing soot formation with soot oxidation.** Soot formation is a *build-up* process; oxidation is a *burn-down* process. The two share the symbol $\dot{w}$ but represent opposing fluxes.
> 5. **Ignoring OH oxidation.** A complete answer on soot oxidation in real flames must mention the OH radical contribution — examiners reward this with 1–2 marks for completeness.
> 6. **Using $\rho_s = 2200$ kg/m³ (graphite density) instead of 1850 kg/m³ (soot density).** The KTU convention is to use the in-situ measured value 1800–2000 kg/m³.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Soot oxidation** is the *heterogeneous gas–solid* conversion of soot into CO/CO₂ by O₂ and OH attack.
- [x] **NSC model is the standard tool** in fire/combustion engineering for O₂-based oxidation.
- [x] **Two regimes:** Branching (T < 1700 K) and Aging (T > 1700 K). Always identify which one you are in.
- [x] **K_oxi expressions to memorize:**
  - $K_{\text{oxi}} = 0.13 \exp(-15{,}100/T)$ for T ≤ 1700 K
  - $K_{\text{oxi}} = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} T^{0.5} \exp(-7640/T)$ for T > 1700 K
- [x] **NSC oxidation rate:** $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}} = \rho_s K_{\text{oxi}} (P_{O_2}/P_t)$
- [x] **Oxidation lifetime:** $\tau_{\text{ox}} = \rho_s d_{p,0}/(2 \dot{w}_{\text{oxi}})$
- [x] **OH is the dominant oxidizer at T < 1500 K** due to its low activation energy.
- [x] **O₂ is the dominant oxidizer at T > 1500 K** due to its high concentration.
- [x] **Switching temperature: T = 1700 K** (precise NSC value).
- [x] **Soot density: $\rho_s = 1850$ kg/m³** (use this in numerical problems).
- [x] **Soot burnout efficiency** scales exponentially with T — fires that are fuel-rich (low T) generate more smoke.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** IC engine DPF regeneration, industrial furnace air staging, smoke control in tunnels, fire safety in enclosures.
- [x] **Radiative heat flux** from a flame is directly proportional to soot volume fraction $f_v$ — oxidation reduces $f_v$ and hence radiation.
- [x] **Two competing timescales** to remember: soot *formation* timescale (~ms) vs soot *oxidation* timescale (µs at flame tip, ms in cooler zones).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme ESE questions on Module 3, expect at least one 7-mark sub-part asking you to (i) identify the regime, (ii) compute $K_{\text{oxi}}$, (iii) compute $\dot{w}_{\text{oxi}}$, and (iv) compute $\tau_{\text{ox}}$. Practice the numerical pattern shown in Question A above. Always **show the exponential evaluation step explicitly** — examiners award partial credit for that step alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Tip Oscillations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flame Tip Oscillations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flame Tip Oscillations** refer to the periodic, time-dependent fluctuations in the position, shape, and luminous intensity of the tip (apex) of a diffusion flame, typically a buoyant fire plume. These oscillations arise from the intrinsic coupling between **buoyancy-induced flow instabilities**, **vortex shedding** at the flame shear layer, and **acoustic-thermoacoustic feedback** mechanisms within the surrounding fluid medium.

In fire dynamics and combustion theory, the flame tip is the region where the fuel and oxidizer streams have nearly fully reacted and the thermal expansion reaches its maximum. Because the flame tip represents a region of strong **buoyancy gradients** and **shear instabilities**, it is particularly susceptible to oscillations driven by the periodic release of buoyant gas parcels (puffs) and the associated wake dynamics.

The oscillations are typically classified into two dominant mode families:

| Mode Type | Description | Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Axisymmetric (Puffing) Mode** | Periodic shedding of toroidal vortex rings symmetric about the plume axis | Lower frequency |
| **Helical (K-H) Mode** | Asymmetric, spiraling vortex structures with azimuthal wave number $m = 1$ | Higher frequency |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a candle flame in a still room. Watch the tip carefully — you will see a rhythmic "flickering" where the tip appears to bob up and down and sometimes wiggle side-to-side. This is exactly flame tip oscillation! The hot combustion gases rise, and as they do, they create a low-pressure zone that draws in cool air from the sides. This inflowing air periodically forms small eddies (vortices) that pinch off the flame tip like pinching off a water droplet, causing the visible flicker. The same phenomenon is what makes a flag flutter in a steady wind — periodic vortex shedding from the flag's edges creates a regular oscillation pattern.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental constants and scales are central to flame tip oscillation theory:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_\infty = 1.204 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Characteristic velocity scale (buoyancy):** $U_0 = (g Q)^{1/3}$
- **Characteristic length scale:** $\ell_0 = (Q^2 / g)^{1/5}$
- **Froude number:** $Fr = U^2 / (g L)$ — ratio of inertial to buoyant forces
- **Strouhal number:** $St = f L / U$ — dimensionless oscillation frequency

Where $Q$ is the total heat release rate of the fire in Watts, and $f$ is the oscillation frequency in Hertz.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, remember that flame tip oscillations are intrinsically a **buoyancy-dominated** phenomenon. Unlike jet diffusion flames (which are momentum-dominated), fire plumes are dominated by the balance between **buoyancy forces** and **inertial forces**, with the **Froude number** being the key dimensionless parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Mechanism of Flame Tip Oscillations

The flame tip oscillation phenomenon can be decomposed into a sequence of physical processes:

### Step 1: Buoyancy-Driven Plume Formation
- Hot combustion products are less dense than the surrounding cold air.
- The density difference ($\Delta \rho = \rho_\infty - \rho_{hot}$) creates an upward body force per unit volume equal to $g \Delta \rho$.
- This generates a buoyant acceleration field, causing the plume to rise with a characteristic velocity.

### Step 2: Shear Layer Formation at the Flame Tip
- The plume's outer edge is a sharp interface between hot rising gases and cool ambient air.
- This interface is a **shear layer** characterized by strong velocity gradients ($\partial u / \partial r$).
- According to **Kelvin-Helmholtz (K-H) instability theory**, such shear layers are unconditionally unstable to infinitesimal perturbations.

### Step 3: Vortex Roll-Up and Pinch-Off
- Infinitesimal disturbances grow exponentially in the shear layer.
- The disturbances roll up into discrete vortices — toroidal rings (axisymmetric mode) or helical spirals (helical mode).
- These vortices entrain ambient air and periodically pinch off from the flame tip.

### Step 4: Acoustic-Thermoacoustic Coupling
- The periodic vortex shedding generates pressure fluctuations in the surrounding acoustic field.
- These pressure waves can travel back to the flame base, modulating the fuel supply rate.
- This **feedback loop** can either **sustain** the oscillations (positive feedback) or **damp** them (negative feedback).

### Step 5: Mode Selection
- The competition between axisymmetric and helical modes depends on:
  - **Reynolds number** ($Re$): controls viscous damping
  - **Froude number** ($Fr$): controls buoyancy strength
  - **Geometry** of the fuel source (circular, square, line, etc.)
- For typical buoyancy-driven fire plumes, the **axisymmetric (puffing) mode** is dominant at low $Re$, while the **helical mode** becomes increasingly prominent at higher $Re$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Print and Memorize!**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $St = f D / U$ | Strouhal number definition | $St$ is dimensionless, typically $0.15 - 0.21$ for buoyant plumes |
| 2 | $U_0 = (g Q)^{1/3}$ | Buoyant velocity scale | $\text{m/s}$; $Q$ in W |
| 3 | $\ell_0 = (Q^2 / g)^{1/5}$ | Characteristic length scale | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | $D_{eq} = 2 (Q/g)^{1/2}$ | Equivalent plume diameter | $\text{m}$; Cetegen (1980) |
| 5 | $f_a \approx 1.5 \sqrt{g/Q^{1/2}}$ | **Axisymmetric mode frequency** | $\text{Hz}$; Cetegen & Ahmed correlation |
| 6 | $f_h \approx 1.3 \sqrt{g/Q^{1/2}}$ | **Helical mode frequency** | $\text{Hz}$; Hamins et al. correlation |
| 7 | $f \cdot D_{eq} = St \cdot U_0$ | Frequency-velocity-length relation | Dimensionless check |
| 8 | $Re = U_0 D_{eq} / \nu$ | Reynolds number | $\nu \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for air |
| 9 | $Fr = U_0^2 / (g D_{eq})$ | Froude number | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_a = 1/f_a$ | Period of axisymmetric oscillation | seconds |
| 11 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 12 | $\lambda = U_0 / f$ | Wavelength of vortex street | meters |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flame tip oscillation theory is critical in the following engineering domains:

1. **Fire Safety Engineering:** Predicting flicker frequency helps estimate visibility hazard in smoke-filled escape routes (flicker fusion threshold ≈ 8-10 Hz).
2. **Pool Fire Modeling:** Industrial pool fires exhibit strong puffing oscillations that modulate radiative heat flux to nearby equipment.
3. **Combustion Instability Analysis:** Gas turbine and rocket combustors experience analogous thermoacoustic instabilities that must be suppressed.
4. **CFD Validation:** Flame tip oscillation frequency is a benchmark test case for validating large-eddy simulation (LES) codes for fire.
5. **Detector Placement:** Smoke and flame detectors are tuned to flicker frequency spectra; understanding the dominant frequencies (typically 1-10 Hz for fires) helps in detector optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Buoyant Velocity Scale $U_0$

We begin with the governing integral equations for a buoyant plume (Morton, Taylor, Turner 1956):

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left( b^2 w \right) = 2 \alpha b w$$

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left( b^2 w^2 \right) = 2 b^2 g \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_\infty}$$

Where:
- $b(z)$ = plume half-radius at height $z$
- $w(z)$ = centerline vertical velocity
- $\alpha \approx 0.1$ = entrainment constant
- $\Delta \rho$ = density difference between plume and ambient

**Step 1:** For a steady, fully-developed buoyant plume in a quiescent environment, assume self-similar profiles of the form $w(z) \propto z^a$ and $b(z) \propto z^b$.

**Step 2:** The buoyancy flux $F = g \int (\Delta \rho / \rho_\infty) w \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ is conserved along the plume (assuming uniform heating rate $Q$):

$$F = \frac{g Q}{\rho_\infty C_p T_\infty}$$

**Step 3:** Solving the similarity equations yields the classic point-source plume solutions:

$$w(z) = \frac{5}{6 \alpha} \left( \frac{9 \alpha F}{10} \right)^{1/3} z^{-1/3}$$

$$b(z) = \frac{6 \alpha}{5} z$$

**Step 4:** At a characteristic length scale $\ell_0$ where the plume transitions to the **flame tip** region, the velocity reaches a maximum. Setting $z = \ell_0$ and using $Q$ as the total heat release:

$$U_0 = (g Q)^{1/3}$$

This is the fundamental **buoyant velocity scale** used universally in fire dynamics and flame oscillation analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Plume Diameter $D_{eq}$ (Cetegen Model)

Cetegen (1980) proposed that the flame tip oscillation behavior can be characterized by an **equivalent plume diameter** that captures the geometry of the oscillating shear layer.

**Step 1:** Consider a circular fire source of physical diameter $D_s$ and total heat release rate $Q$. Define a buoyancy length scale:

$$L_b = \frac{Q^{2/5}}{g^{1/5}}$$

**Step 2:** The equivalent diameter is defined such that the buoyancy flux through a horizontal cross-section of area $\pi D_{eq}^2 / 4$ equals the total heat release rate $Q$ divided by a characteristic buoyancy velocity:

$$\frac{\pi D_{eq}^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{g D_{eq}} = \frac{Q}{\rho_\infty C_p \Delta T}$$

**Step 3:** For a fire in open atmosphere, the characteristic buoyancy velocity at scale $D_{eq}$ is $\sqrt{g D_{eq}}$ (from the Froude number condition $Fr = 1$).

**Step 4:** Solving for $D_{eq}$:

$$\boxed{D_{eq} = 2 \left( \frac{Q}{g} \right)^{1/2}}$$

**Step 5:** The prefactor of 2 emerges from numerical matching of the model to experimental data for axisymmetric pool fires. This is the form used in all subsequent KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation of Axisymmetric Puffing Frequency

The axisymmetric mode ("puffing") corresponds to the periodic shedding of toroidal vortex rings from the flame tip. The frequency is set by the convective time scale for a buoyant parcel to traverse the equivalent diameter:

**Step 1:** The convective time scale is:

$$\tau_c = \frac{D_{eq}}{U_{conv}}$$

**Step 2:** The convective velocity at the flame tip is approximately equal to the buoyant velocity scale evaluated at the flame height:

$$U_{conv} \approx U_0 = (g Q)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3:** However, Cetegen's experimental correlation for axisymmetric mode used the buoyancy frequency $\sqrt{g / D_{eq}}$:

$$f_a = C_a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g}{D_{eq}}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $D_{eq} = 2(Q/g)^{1/2}$:

$$f_a = C_a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g}{2(Q/g)^{1/2}}} = C_a \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \frac{g^2}{Q} \right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging and matching to data ($C_a = 1.5\sqrt{2}$):

$$\boxed{f_a \approx 1.5 \sqrt{\frac{g}{Q^{1/2}}}}$$

**Verification:** For a small pool fire with $Q = 1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W}$:

$$f_a = 1.5 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{\sqrt{1000}}} = 1.5 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{31.62}} = 1.5 \times 0.557 = 0.836 \text{ Hz}$$

This matches experimental observations of puffing in small diffusion flames.

## 3.4 Derivation of Helical Mode Frequency

The helical (K-H) mode corresponds to asymmetric spiraling structures. Hamins et al. (1992) showed that for a circular pool fire, the helical mode dominates when:

$$\frac{f_h D_{eq}}{U_0} = St_h \approx 0.45$$

**Step 1:** Starting from the Strouhal number definition:

$$f_h = \frac{St_h \cdot U_0}{D_{eq}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $U_0 = (gQ)^{1/3}$ and $D_{eq} = 2(Q/g)^{1/2}$:

$$f_h = \frac{St_h \cdot (gQ)^{1/3}}{2(Q/g)^{1/2}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the exponent of $Q$:

$$\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2} = -\frac{1}{6}$$

$$f_h = \frac{St_h}{2} \cdot g^{1/3 + 1/2} \cdot Q^{-1/6} = \frac{St_h}{2} \cdot g^{5/6} \cdot Q^{-1/6}$$

**Step 4:** Using empirical $St_h \approx 0.86$ from Hamins' experiments and converting to Cetegen's form:

$$\boxed{f_h \approx 1.3 \sqrt{\frac{g}{Q^{1/2}}}}$$

**Note:** The helical mode frequency is slightly **lower** than the axisymmetric mode in Cetegen's original convention, but **higher** in Hamins' data. KTU examinations typically use the Hamins convention where $f_h > f_a$.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Wavelength of the Vortex Street

The von Karman vortex street wavelength is the spatial period of the shed vortices:

**Step 1:** The convective velocity transports each vortex by one wavelength per oscillation period:

$$\lambda = U_0 \cdot T = \frac{U_0}{f}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting axisymmetric mode frequency:

$$\lambda = \frac{U_0}{f_a} = \frac{(gQ)^{1/3}}{1.5 \sqrt{g/Q^{1/2}}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\lambda = \frac{(gQ)^{1/3}}{1.5} \cdot \left( \frac{Q^{1/2}}{g} \right)^{1/2} = \frac{1}{1.5} \cdot g^{1/3 - 1/2} \cdot Q^{1/3 + 1/4}$$

**Step 4:** Final compact form:

$$\lambda = \frac{2}{3} \cdot g^{-1/6} \cdot Q^{7/12}$$

This is the **characteristic wavelength of the puffing vortex street** in a buoyant fire plume.

## 3.6 Sample Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A circular pool fire has a heat release rate of $Q = 50 \text{ kW}$. Calculate:
1. The buoyant velocity scale $U_0$
2. The equivalent plume diameter $D_{eq}$
3. The axisymmetric puffing frequency $f_a$
4. The helical mode frequency $f_h$
5. The vortex street wavelength $\lambda$

**Solution:**

**(1) Buoyant velocity scale:**

$$U_0 = (gQ)^{1/3} = (9.81 \times 50{,}000)^{1/3} = (490{,}500)^{1/3}$$

$$U_0 = 78.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**(2) Equivalent plume diameter:**

$$D_{eq} = 2 \left( \frac{Q}{g} \right)^{1/2} = 2 \left( \frac{50{,}000}{9.81} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$D_{eq} = 2 \times \sqrt{5096.8} = 2 \times 71.39 = 142.8 \text{ mm}$$

**(3) Axisymmetric puffing frequency:**

$$f_a = 1.5 \sqrt{\frac{g}{Q^{1/2}}} = 1.5 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{\sqrt{50{,}000}}}$$

$$f_a = 1.5 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{223.6}} = 1.5 \times 0.2094 = 0.314 \text{ Hz}$$

**(4) Helical mode frequency (Hamins form):**

Using $St_h \approx 0.45$ and $U_0, D_{eq}$:

$$f_h = \frac{St_h \cdot U_0}{D_{eq}} = \frac{0.45 \times 78.78}{0.1428} = 248.3 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the helical mode in low-power fires, the frequency is significantly higher than the axisymmetric mode. The two modes coexist and the dominant mode depends on the **Reynolds number** and **fire geometry**.

**(5) Vortex street wavelength (axisymmetric):**

$$\lambda = \frac{U_0}{f_a} = \frac{78.78}{0.314} = 250.9 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The wavelength of the axisymmetric vortex street is very large (hundreds of meters) for the **axisymmetric** mode, while the helical mode wavelength is much shorter ($U_0 / f_h = 0.317 \text{ m}$). KTU students often confuse these two. The **physical** wavelength of the helical mode is the more relevant one for engineering design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Flame Tip Oscillation Physical Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Buoyant Plume Forms] --> B[Shear Layer at Plume Edge]
    B --> C{Kelvin-Helmholtz Instability}
    C --> D[Infinitesimal Perturbations Grow]
    D --> E[Vortex Roll-Up]
    E --> F{Mode Selection}
    F -->|Axisymmetric m=0| G[Toroidal Vortex Ring Shedding]
    F -->|Helical m=1| H[Spiraling Helical Vortex]
    G --> I[Pressure Fluctuation Generated]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Acoustic Feedback Path}
    J -->|Positive Feedback| K[Oscillation Sustained]
    J -->|Negative Feedback| L[Oscillation Damped]
    K --> M[Steady-State Flame Tip Flicker]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Observable Frequency f_a or f_h]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vortex Shedding at Flame Tip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Fire Source]
        S1[Fuel Vapor]
        S2[Heat Release Q]
    end
    
    subgraph PHYSICS[Buoyancy-Driven Flow]
        P1[Hot Gases Rise]
        P2[Shear Layer Forms]
        P3[KH Instability Develops]
    end
    
    subgraph OSC[Oscillation Modes]
        O1[Axisymmetric Puffing]
        O2[Helical Spiraling]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Observable Quantities]
        R1[Frequency f in Hz]
        R2[Wavelength lambda]
        R3[Strouhal Number St]
        R4[Amplitude of Tip Displacement]
    end
    
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    O1 --> R1
    O1 --> R2
    O1 --> R3
    O1 --> R4
    O2 --> R1
    O2 --> R2
    O2 --> R3
    O2 --> R4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Temporal Evolution of One Oscillation Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T0 as Time t equals 0
    participant T1 as Time t equals T by 4
    participant T2 as Time t equals T by 2
    participant T3 as Time t equals 3T by 4
    participant T4 as Time t equals T
    
    T0->>T1: Vortex begins forming at flame base
    T1->>T2: Vortex grows and travels upward
    T2->>T3: Vortex reaches flame tip
    T3->>T4: Vortex pinches off and is shed
    T4->>T0: Cycle restarts - new vortex forms
    Note over T0,T4: Period T equals 1 over f_a
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Coupling Between Flame Oscillation and Acoustic Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Source Q] --> B[Buoyant Plume]
    B --> C[Shear Layer Instability]
    C --> D[Periodic Vortex Shedding]
    D --> E[Pressure Fluctuations]
    E --> F[Acoustic Waves Propagate]
    F --> G{Acoustic Coupling}
    G -->|In-phase| H[Constructive Feedback]
    G -->|Out-of-phase| I[Destructive Feedback]
    H --> J[Amplified Oscillations]
    I --> K[Damped Oscillations]
    J --> A
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A`, `B`, `P1`, `O1`) to comply with the Mermaid compiler's reserved keyword restrictions. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node identifiers. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.
