Cross-wind tilts either case toward a *cross-flow jet* regime. ### 1.3 Why Jet Flame Height Matters in Fire Engineering - **Thermal radiation hazard distance** (used as a 2D line source in point-source models). - **Thermal Detector (heat/flame) placement** on offshore platforms, LNG terminals, refineries. - **Equipment layout spacing** per **API RP 521** and **NFPA 30**. - **Determines flame impingement zones** on adjacent vessels, pipe racks, structures. - **Inputs to consequence-analysis software** (PHAST, FRED, FLACS) used by Kerala State Pollution Control Board inspectors and IOCL/KSIDC installations. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework A jet flame is governed by the **conservation equations** for mass, momentum, energy, and species. Through dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem) using characteristic variables $[d, u_0, g, \rho, \Delta\rho, c_p, k, \nu, Y_F]$, the non-dimensional flame height becomes a function of two primary groups: $$\frac{L_f}{d} \;=\; f\!\left( Fr,\; Re,\; \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_a},\; Pr,\; \frac{Y_F}{Y_O} \right)$$ In most KTU problems the density ratio and Prandtl effects are folded into the constants, leaving the **engineering-grade correlation form**: $$\boxed{\;\frac{L_f}{d} \;=\; C_1 \cdot Fr^{a_1} \cdot Re^{b_1}\;}$$ ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow (How a Jet Flame Forms) 1. **Fuel jet exits** the orifice with bulk velocity $u_0$ and diameter $d$. Initial Reynolds number sets whether flow is laminar or turbulent. 2. **Entrainment of ambient air** occurs at the jet shear layer. For round turbulent jets, the classic **Ricou-Spalding entrainment** gives constant entrainment coefficient $\alpha \approx 0.32$. 3. **Mixing layer grows linearly** with downstream distance $x$: the jet half-width $b(x) \propto x$. For a round turbulent jet, $b \approx 0.114\,x$. 4. **Centerline mean concentration** $\bar{C}(x)$ decays as $\bar{C} \propto d/x$ for round jets. The stoichiometric condition $\bar{C}_{stoich}$ is met at the **flame tip**. 5. **Set** $C(d, x=L_f) = C_{stoich}$ ⇒ $L_f \propto d \cdot (Y_{F,0}/Y_{stoich})$ for momentum jets, and $L_f \propto Q_H^{2/5}$ for buoyancy-dominated plumes (Zeldovich plume). 6. **Heat release rate scaling** for a round jet: $Q_H \propto \rho_0\, u_0\, d^{2}\, \Delta H_c\, Y_{F,0}$. Combining gives the Froude-type scaling. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these eight equations — they cover ~85% of KTU numerical questions on jet flames.** | # | Correlation Name | Equation | Regime / Use | |---|------------------|----------|--------------| | 1 | **Hawthorne (1949)** | $\dfrac{L_f}{d} \;=\; 21.6\,Fr^{0.21}$ | Momentum-dominated vertical jet, $10^{2} 0$ and visible reaction zone does not start at the nozzle — modifies radiation because the lower portion is unburnt fuel. - **Crosswind rule-of-thumb:** flame attaches to nozzle if $u_w/u_0 < 0.5$; fully lifted if $u_w/u_0 > 2$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Jet flame correlations are used in: - **Offshore platform layout** (ONGC, Reliance KG-D6) — minimum separation between gas vents and living quarters. - **LNG terminal design** (Kochi LNG, Petronet) — thermal radiation exclusion zones. - **Hydrogen refuelling stations** (NTPC pilot) — flame jet length on H₂ leaks (high $Fr$, long flames). - **Pesticide/fumigation plant safety audits** in Kerala — use Hawthorne and Kalghatgi to size fire-water spray radius. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Hawthorne Correlation (Momentum Round Jet) Starting point: a vertical, round, turbulent, momentum-dominated jet flame of exit velocity $u_0$, exit diameter $d$, in still air. Use the **turbulent jet similarity** solution (Schlichting, 1968). **Step 1 — Centerline mean velocity decay (round jet)** $$\frac{u_m}{u_0} \;=\; \frac{6.2\,d}{x} \quad \text{(for } x/d > 30\text{)}$$ **Step 2 — Centerline mass-fraction decay** $$\frac{Y_m}{Y_{F,0}} \;=\; \frac{5.0\,d}{x}$$ **Step 3 — Stoichiometric condition at flame tip** Set centerline mass fraction equal to stoichiometric value of fuel after mixing with air: $$Y_m(L_f) \;=\; Y_F \cdot r_s$$ where $r_s$ is the local stoichiometric mass ratio of fuel to (fuel+air). Substituting Step 2: $$\frac{5.0\,d}{L_f} \;=\; r_s \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; L_f \;=\; \frac{5.0\,d}{r_s}$$ This is the *pure-momentum, no-gravity* result, and gives a length proportional to $d$ only — independent of $Fr$. The empirical Hawthorne fit brings in buoyancy: **Step 4 — Introduce Froude correction** Replacing the constant 5.0 by an empirical Froude power-law fit: $$\frac{L_f}{d} \;=\; 21.6\,(Fr)^{0.21}$$ **Validation:** For methane at $u_0 = 50$ m/s, $d = 0.05$ m: $$Fr = \frac{50^2}{9.81 \times 0.05} \;=\; 5097$$ $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 21.6 \times (5097)^{0.21} = 21.6 \times 4.42 \approx 95.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; L_f \approx 4.78 \text{ m}$$ > ✅ Consistent with experimental data for subsonic methane jets. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Heskestad Crosswind Break-Point Height For a horizontal jet in cross-wind, the lift-off height $L_b$ is set by the **vertical velocity** $u_y$ that the jet acquires as it bends. The jet bends over the distance $L_b$ where buoyancy-generated vertical velocity becomes comparable to the horizontal wind-modulated velocity. **Step 1 — Buoyant vertical velocity at break-point** From Zeldovich plume theory at the point of break-over: $$u_y(L_b) \;\approx\; 1.9\,(g\,Q_H L_b / \rho_\infty \, c_p\, T_\infty)^{1/3}$$ **Step 2 — Heat release rate scaling** For a momentum jet of exit velocity $u_0$ and diameter $d$: $$Q_H \;\approx\; \rho_0\, u_0\, d^{2}\, \Delta H_c\, Y_{F,0}$$ **Step 3 — Equate $u_y$ to wind-modulated velocity at $L_b$** Heskestad (1981) correlated the resulting height with wind ratio: $$\frac{L_b}{d} \;=\; 29.6 \left(\frac{u_w}{u_0}\right)^{1.74}$$ **Numerical example (KTU-type):** Given $d = 0.05$ m, $u_0 = 80$ m/s, $u_w = 5$ m/s: $$\frac{u_w}{u_0} = 0.0625$$ $$\frac{L_b}{d} = 29.6 \times (0.0625)^{1.74} = 29.6 \times 0.00721 \approx 0.213$$ $$L_b \approx 0.0107 \text{ m} = 10.7 \text{ mm}$$ The flame lifts off the nozzle by about **1 cm** under these conditions — typical of a partially lifted turbulent jet. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Correlation (Decision Tree) | Condition | Regime | Use This Correlation | |-----------|--------|----------------------| | Vertical, no wind, $Fr>10^2$ | Momentum | **Hawthorne** or **Kalghatgi** | | Vertical, no wind, $Fr<5$ | Buoyant plume | **Zeldovich/Hawthorne buoyant** | | Cross-wind present, lift-off | Wind-dominated | **Heskestad** (break-point) | | Pressurized gas leak, refinery | Momentum + cross-wind | **API RP 521** | | Laminar jet ($Re<2000$) | Viscous | **Wohl** | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** Methane leaks from a 50 mm pipe on a Kochi refinery platform. The exit velocity is 60 m/s. The cross-wind is 6 m/s. Calculate (a) the momentum flame height using Kalghatgi, and (b) the break-point lift-off using Heskestad. Methane properties: $\nu = 1.5\times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Solution:** **(a) Kalghatgi flame height** $$Fr = \frac{60^{2}}{9.81 \times 0.05} = \frac{3600}{0.4905} = 7339$$ $$Re = \frac{60 \times 0.05}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$$ $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 55 \times (7339)^{0.067} \times (200000/2000)^{0.21}$$ Computing each term: $$(7339)^{0.067} = e^{0.067 \ln 7339} = e^{0.067 \times 8.901} = e^{0.5964} = 1.816$$ $$(100)^{0.21} = e^{0.21 \times \ln 100} = e^{0.21 \times 4.605} = e^{0.967} = 2.631$$ $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 55 \times 1.816 \times 2.631 \approx 262.7$$ $$L_f = 262.7 \times 0.05 = 13.13 \text{ m}$$ **(b) Heskestad break-point** $$\frac{u_w}{u_0} = \frac{6}{60} = 0.10$$ $$\frac{L_b}{d} = 29.6 \times (0.10)^{1.74} = 29.6 \times 0.0182 = 0.539$$ $$L_b = 0.539 \times 0.05 = 0.027 \text{ m} = 27 \text{ mm}$$ **Result:** A **13.1 m** vertical flame, lifted **27 mm** off the nozzle — full answer ready for 14-mark KTU valuation. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Jet Flame Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Storage
Pressurised Vessel] --> B[Orifice / Rupture
Diameter d] B --> C[Exit Jet
Velocity u0] C --> D[Entrainment Zone
Air Mixing] D --> E[Reaction Zone
Flame Sheet] E --> F[Buoyancy Lobe
Hot Products Rise] F --> G[Flame Tip
L_f measured here] H[Crosswind u_w] -.-> D I[Nozzle Exit] -.-> J[Lift-off Point L_lo] J -.-> E style A fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ecf0f1,color:#fff style B fill:#34495e,stroke:#ecf0f1,color:#fff style C fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#ecf0f1,color:#fff style D fill:#e67e22,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style E fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style F fill:#f39c12,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style G fill:#d35400,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style H fill:#16a085,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style I fill:#34495e,stroke:#ecf0f1,color:#fff style J fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Flame Regime Decision Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Release Event] --> B{Froude Number Fr} B -->|Fr is less than 5| C[Buoyant Plume
L proportional to Q to power 2 by 5] B -->|Fr between 5 and 1000| D[Transitional Jet
Hawthorne or Wohl] B -->|Fr greater than 1000| E[Momentum Jet
Kalghatgi dominant] D --> F{Crosswind Present?} E --> F C --> F F -->|Yes| G[Cross-flow Regime
Heskestad or API 521] F -->|No| H[Still-Air Regime
Kalghatgi or Hawthorne] style A fill:#34495e,color:#fff style B fill:#2980b9,color:#fff style C fill:#27ae60,color:#fff style D fill:#f39c12,color:#fff style E fill:#c0392b,color:#fff style F fill:#8e44ad,color:#fff style G fill:#d35400,color:#fff style H fill:#16a085,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Coordinate System for Crosswind Flame ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Geometry A[Nozzle origin O] --> B[Horizontal jet axis X] A --> C[Vertical axis Y] B --> D[Wind vector uw along X] C --> E[Buoyancy vector g along Y] D --> F[Break point at Lb along Y] F --> G[Flame tip Lf at angle theta] end H[Froude Number Fr] -.controls.-> F I[Wind Ratio uw divided by u0] -.controls.-> F style A fill:#2c3e50,color:#fff style B fill:#3498db,color:#fff style C fill:#3498db,color:#fff style D fill:#16a085,color:#fff style E fill:#e67e22,color:#fff style F fill:#c0392b,color:#fff style G fill:#d35400,color:#fff style H fill:#8e44ad,color:#fff style I fill:#8e44ad,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > The angle $\theta$ between flame centerline and the vertical is given by $\tan\theta \approx u_w/u_0$ for weak winds, and approaches 90° for strong winds (flame blown horizontal). This tilt angle is critical for **thermal detector placement** in fire-water spray systems on offshore platforms. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — 3-Mark Short Questions** **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define the term **jet flame height**. How does it differ from the **lift-off height** in a cross-wind scenario? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > *Jet flame height* ($L_f$) is the axial distance from the nozzle exit to the tip of the visible luminous flame where combustion is sustained. *Lift-off height* ($L_{lo}$) is the distance from the nozzle to the point where the reaction zone begins; in cross-wind this becomes the *break-point* ($L_b$). They differ because $L_f$ measures flame extent, while $L_{lo}$ marks where combustion starts. In still air they coincide; in cross-wind, $L_{lo} > 0$ and the flame is detached. **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` State the **Froude number** for a jet flame and explain its physical significance in identifying the flame regime. > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > $Fr = u_0^{2}/(gd)$. It is the ratio of **inertial momentum flux** to **gravitational buoyancy**. A low $Fr$ (less than 5) means buoyancy dominates, producing a short mushroom-like flame. A high $Fr$ (greater than $10^3$) means momentum dominates, producing a long pencil-like flame. It is the key parameter distinguishing buoyant plumes from momentum jets. --- ### **PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions** --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the **Hawthorne correlation** for a vertical round jet flame and discuss the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A natural gas leak occurs from a 100 mm diameter pipe on a process platform. The exit velocity is 90 m/s and ambient air kinematic viscosity is $1.5\times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the flame height using (i) the Hawthorne correlation, and (ii) the Kalghatgi correlation. Compare the results. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** > 1. Centerline velocity decay for round jet: $u_m = 6.2\,u_0\,d/x$. **(1 mark)** > 2. Centerline mass-fraction decay: $Y_m = 5.0\,Y_{F,0}\,d/x$. **(1 mark)** > 3. Set $Y_m(L_f) = r_s\,Y_{F,0}$ (stoichiometric condition). **(1 mark)** > 4. Obtain $L_f = 5.0\,d/r_s$ — pure momentum result. **(1 mark)** > 5. Empirical fit introducing $Fr^{0.21}$ to incorporate buoyancy correction: $L_f/d = 21.6\,Fr^{0.21}$. **(1 mark)** > 6. Assumptions: round, vertical, turbulent, top-hat profile, constant density air, no wind, fuel-rich stoichiometric condition. **(2 marks)** > > **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** > > Given: $d = 0.10$ m, $u_0 = 90$ m/s, $\nu = 1.5\times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s². > > **Froude number:** > > $$Fr = \frac{90^{2}}{9.81 \times 0.10} = \frac{8100}{0.981} = 8257$$ > > **Reynolds number:** > > $$Re = \frac{90 \times 0.10}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.0 \times 10^{5}$$ > > **(i) Hawthorne (2 marks):** > > $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 21.6 \times (8257)^{0.21}$$ > > $$(8257)^{0.21} = e^{0.21 \times 9.018} = e^{1.894} = 6.65$$ > > $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 21.6 \times 6.65 = 143.6 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; L_f = 14.36 \text{ m}$$ > > **(ii) Kalghatgi (2 marks):** > > $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 55 \times (8257)^{0.067} \times (600000/2000)^{0.21}$$ > > $$(8257)^{0.067} = e^{0.067 \times 9.018} = e^{0.604} = 1.83$$ > > $$(300)^{0.21} = e^{0.21 \times 5.704} = e^{1.198} = 3.31$$ > > $$\frac{L_f}{d} = 55 \times 1.83 \times 3.31 \approx 333$$ > > $$L_f = 333 \times 0.10 = 33.3 \text{ m}$$ > > **Comparison (1 mark):** Kalghatgi predicts a flame ~2.3× longer than Hawthorne because it accounts for $Re$ effects explicitly. For high-$Re$ turbulent jets, Kalghatgi is generally considered more accurate. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Heskestad crosswind break-point correlation**. Sketch a labelled diagram of a tilted jet flame in cross-wind and identify the break-point. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 75 mm diameter gas jet issues vertically at 70 m/s into a 4 m/s cross-wind. Compute (i) the break-point lift-off height using Heskestad, and (ii) the tilt angle of the flame centerline at the break-point. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Theory (7 marks):** > 1. Heskestad (1981) correlated the vertical distance from the nozzle to the break-point (where flame becomes vertical plume) with wind ratio $u_w/u_0$. **(2 marks)** > 2. Correlation: $L_b/d = 29.6\,(u_w/u_0)^{1.74}$. **(2 marks)** > 3. The break-point is the location where buoyancy-generated vertical velocity balances the wind-deflected horizontal velocity — beyond this point the flame becomes a vertical buoyant plume. **(2 marks)** > 4. Diagram showing nozzle, jet, tilted flame, break-point, vertical plume above. **(1 mark)** > > **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** > > Given: $d = 0.075$ m, $u_0 = 70$ m/s, $u_w = 4$ m/s. > > **Wind ratio:** > > $$\frac{u_w}{u_0} = \frac{4}{70} = 0.0571$$ > > **(i) Break-point (3 marks):** > > $$\frac{L_b}{d} = 29.6 \times (0.0571)^{1.74}$$ > > $$(0.0571)^{1.74} = e^{1.74 \times \ln 0.0571} = e^{1.74 \times (-2.864)} = e^{-4.984} = 0.00685$$ > > $$\frac{L_b}{d} = 29.6 \times 0.00685 = 0.2027$$ > > $$L_b = 0.2027 \times 0.075 = 0.0152 \text{ m} = 15.2 \text{ mm}$$ > > **(ii) Tilt angle (4 marks):** > > For weak cross-wind, the flame centreline at the break-point is approximately tilted by: > > $$\tan\theta = \frac{u_w}{u_0} = 0.0571$$ > > $$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0571) = 3.27^{\circ}$$ > > **Result:** The flame lifts off by ~15 mm and is tilted by only ~3.3° from vertical, characteristic of a *momentum-dominated* jet where wind effects are minor. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost students 2-3 marks each in KTU exams:** > > 1. **Mixing up $L_f$ with $L_{lo}$:** $L_f$ is *flame height* (tip to nozzle), $L_{lo}$ is *lift-off* (start of combustion to nozzle). They are NOT the same when the flame is detached. > 2. **Forgetting units in the Froude number:** Many students write $Fr = u_0^{2}/gd$ but plug in diameter in mm. Always convert **d to meters** before computing. > 3. **Using wrong correlation for the regime:** Do not use Hawthorne for cross-wind flames. Use Heskestad or API RP 521. > 4. **Ignoring the stoichiometric mass ratio** when deriving correlations. The constant 5.0 in the Hawthorne derivation is an empirical fit; replacing it with the actual $1/r_s$ changes the answer. > 5. **Exponent in Heskestad is 1.74, NOT 1.5** — frequently misquoted in answer sheets. > 6. **Kalghatgi uses $(Re/2000)^{0.21}$**, not $Re^{0.21}$ directly. The 2000 reference is critical for normalization. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Jet Flame Heights** - 🔥 **Jet flame height** ($L_f$) = nozzle to visible flame tip; **lift-off** ($L_{lo}$) = nozzle to flame base. - 📐 **Key dimensionless numbers:** $Fr = u_0^2/(gd)$, $Re = u_0 d/\nu$. - 📊 **Regime classification:** $Fr<5$ buoyant, $510^3$ momentum. - 📈 **Hawthorne (momentum):** $L_f/d = 21.6\,Fr^{0.21}$. - 📈 **Hawthorne (buoyant):** $L_f \propto Q^{2/5}$ (Zeldovich). - 📈 **Kalghatgi (universal):** $L_f/d = 55\,Fr^{0.067}\,(Re/2000)^{0.21}$. - 📈 **Heskestad (cross-wind break-point):** $L_b/d = 29.6\,(u_w/u_0)^{1.74}$. - 📈 **API RP 521 (unpressurized):** $L_f/d = 1000\sqrt{M_a}$. - ⚠️ **Tilt angle in cross-wind:** $\tan\theta \approx u_w/u_0$ for weak winds. - ⚠️ **Flame attaches to nozzle** if $u_w/u_0 < 0.5$; **lifts off** if $u_w/u_0 > 2$. - 🎯 **KTU 14-mark question pattern:** (a) Derivation of correlation (7 marks) + (b) Numerical application with given $d, u_0, u_w$ (7 marks). - 🎯 **For Kerala industrial applications:** Kochi LNG, Petronet, BPCL Kochi Refinery — use API RP 521 for pressurized gas leaks. - 🎯 **Radiation hazard:** flame height is the *line-source* length in point-source radiation models, so $L_f$ directly determines exclusion-zone radius.

topics.title: Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames — Section 1: Definition, Intuition & Syllabus Highlights

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flame (TJDF)** is a non-premixed, buoyancy-affected or momentum-dominated combustion system in which a gaseous (or vaporised liquid) fuel stream issues from a nozzle, orifice, or ruptured containment with a sufficiently high **Reynolds number** (typically $\mathrm{Re}_d > 2000$ for round jets) so that the issuing flow is **fully turbulent**. The fuel jet subsequently mixes with the surrounding quiescent ambient oxidiser by **entrainment** driven primarily by **Reynolds shear stresses**, **turbulent convection**, and (at large scales) **buoyancy-induced pressure gradients**. Combustion occurs in a thin, distributed, often **soot-laden reaction zone** where the local mixture fraction $\xi$ crosses the **stoichiometric value** $\xi_{st}$, separating a fuel-rich core from a fuel-lean outer annulus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Key Syllabus Keywords (Module 4)**
> Turbulent jet · diffusion flame · entrainment · self-similarity · conserved scalar (mixture fraction) · Favre averaging · Burke–Schumann limit · flamelet structure · Reynolds number criterion · Froude/Richardson effects · flame length correlations · soot formation in jet flames.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy & Intuitive Picture

Imagine lighting a domestic **LPG stove**. The fuel gas rushes out of a small circular port at high speed, dragging ambient air with it as it rises. Because the fuel jet is *momentum-driven* and fast, the flow becomes chaotic — you cannot predict the path of any individual fluid particle. The luminous yellow-orange flame you see is the **reaction zone**, where fuel vapour and entrained air meet in **stoichiometric proportions** and burn.

**Industrial analogues** of the same phenomenon include:
- A **pipe-rapture jet fire** in an oil refinery (high-momentum, methane/propane release).
- The towering **flare stack** of a petrochemical plant (buoyancy-dominated at large heights).
- A **pool fire** in a bunded tank farm (low-momentum, buoyancy-driven turbulent diffusion flame).
- A rocket engine plume at high altitude (supersonic momentum-dominated jet flame).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study this in Fire Engineering?** Almost all catastrophic industrial fires start as, or quickly transition to, **turbulent jet/pool diffusion flames**. Predicting their **flame length, thermal radiation footprint, soot yield, and likelihood of extinction or flash-back** is central to:
> - API 521 / API 537 flare design standards.
> - NFPA 30 (Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code) separation distances.
> - KTU-aligned outcomes on **fire hazard analysis (CO₃–CO₄)** and **passive fire protection sizing (CO₅)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Recall Box)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathrm{Re}_d$ | Nozzle-exit Reynolds number | $> 2000$ (laminar → turbulent transition) |
| $\mathrm{Fr}$ | Froude number at exit | $< 10^{-2}$ (buoyancy) to $> 10^{3}$ (momentum) |
| $\mathrm{Ri}$ | Local Richardson number | $< 0.1$ (jet) – $> 0.5$ (plume) |
| $\xi_{st}$ | Stoichiometric mixture fraction | $0.03$ ($\mathrm{CH_4}$–air) to $0.20$ ($\mathrm{C_3H_8}$–air) |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $L_f$ | Visible flame length | $0.1\,\mathrm{m} < L_f < 100\,\mathrm{m}$ |
| $\chi$ | Scalar dissipation rate | $10$ – $10^{3}\ \mathrm{s^{-1}}$ (flamelet regime) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames — Section 2: Deep Theory, Regimes & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Turbulent Jet Flames

A turbulent jet diffusion flame is classified along **two independent axes**:

**(a) Momentum-to-Buoyancy ratio** (governed by exit Froude number $\mathrm{Fr} = U_0^2/(g d)$):

$$\mathrm{Fr} \gg 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Momentum-dominated (forced) jet flame}$$

$$\mathrm{Fr} \ll 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Buoyancy-dominated (natural) plume flame}$$

**(b) Density ratio** between fuel and oxidiser:

$$S = \rho_{\infty}/\rho_0 \quad (\text{density ratio})$$

For $S \neq 1$, the **turbulent Reynolds analogy** must be corrected, and the jet becomes a **variable-density (or compressibility-corrected) jet**.

## 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a Round Turbulent Jet Flame

Along the centreline, four distinct regions can be identified:

1. **Initial Region (Potential Core)**: $0 \leq x \leq 5d$. Jet velocity is essentially uniform; little mixing.
2. **Transitional Region**: $5d \leq x \leq 30d$. Rapid growth of shear layer; centreline velocity begins to decay.
3. **Fully Developed (Self-Similar) Region**: $x \geq 30d$. Centreline velocity, concentration, and temperature obey **self-similar profiles**; the jet spreads linearly.
4. **Terminal/Buoyancy-Dominated Region**: When $\mathrm{Ri}_x = (g \Delta\rho_0 d)/(\rho_{\infty} U_0^2) \cdot (d/x) \gtrsim 0.5$, momentum is no longer conserved — **mass flux is conserved instead** (this is the **plume** regime).

## 2.3 The Conserved Scalar — Mixture Fraction ( $\xi$ or $Z$ )

The cornerstone of turbulent non-premixed combustion theory is the **conserved scalar** known as the **mixture fraction**:

$$\xi(\mathbf{x},t) \;=\; \frac{\text{mass of material that originated in the fuel stream}}{\text{total mass at point } (\mathbf{x},t)}$$

For a two-feed system (fuel stream $\xi=1$, oxidiser stream $\xi=0$):

$$\xi \in [0,1]$$

The reaction occurs at the **stoichiometric surface** $\xi = \xi_{st}$, where:

$$\xi_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + (\nu \, Y_{O,\infty}/Y_{F,0})}$$

with $\nu$ being the mass stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> Under the assumptions of (i) equal binary diffusivities of all species and (ii) one-step irreversible chemistry, **all thermochemical state variables become unique functions of the single scalar $\xi$**. This is the **Burke–Schumann limit** (Burke & Schumann, 1928) — the foundation of every modern TJDF theory.

## 2.4 Favre vs. Reynolds Averaging

Because turbulent scalars fluctuate strongly, two averaging frameworks coexist:

**Reynolds (time) average:**

$$\tilde{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \phi(\mathbf{x},t)\,dt$$

**Favre (mass-weighted) average:**

$$\hat{\phi} = \frac{\overline{\rho \phi}}{\bar{\rho}}$$

The **Favre-averaged** transport equation for the mixture fraction in a steady round jet reads:

$$\bar{\rho}\tilde{u}\,\frac{\partial \tilde{\xi}}{\partial x} + \bar{\rho}\tilde{v}\,\frac{\partial \tilde{\xi}}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\, \bar{\rho}\, \widetilde{D_t}\, \frac{\partial \tilde{\xi}}{\partial r}\right)$$

where $\widetilde{D_t}$ is the **turbulent diffusivity** (typically modelled as $\widetilde{D_t} \approx \mu_t/\bar{\rho}$ via a turbulent Schmidt number $\mathrm{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. Master the dimensionless forms and the empirical constants (Reynolds analogy constants: $K_u$, $K_\xi$, $\Lambda$, $K_e$).

| # | Formula | Description / Constant |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathrm{Re}_d = \dfrac{\rho_0 U_0 d}{\mu_0}$ | Nozzle Reynolds number (turbulent if $> 2000$) |
| 2 | $\mathrm{Fr} = \dfrac{U_0^2}{g\,d}$ | Froude number (forced/buoyant classification) |
| 3 | $\mathrm{Ri}_x = \dfrac{g\,\Delta\rho_0\, d}{\rho_\infty U_0^2} \cdot \dfrac{d}{x}$ | Local Richardson number at axial distance $x$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{u_m}{U_0} = \dfrac{K_u\, d}{x}, \quad K_u \approx 6.2$ | Centreline mean-velocity decay (round jet) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\xi_m}{\xi_0} = \dfrac{K_\xi\, d}{x}, \quad K_\xi \approx 5.6$ | Centreline mixture-fraction decay |
| 6 | $\dfrac{r_{1/2}}{x} = \tan\theta \approx 0.10\ \text{to}\ 0.13$ | Jet half-width spread rate |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_e = K_e\, \rho_\infty\, u_m\, r_{1/2} \cdot 2\pi x$ | Entrainment mass-flux rate; $K_e \approx 0.45$ |
| 8 | $\dot{m}(x) = \dot{m}_0\left[1 + K_e \left(\dfrac{x}{d} - K_{x_0}\right)\right]$ | Cumulative entrained mass in self-similar region |
| 9 | $\xi_{st} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\nu Y_{O,\infty}/Y_{F,0})}$ | Stoichiometric mixture fraction |
| 10 | $\chi = 2\, \widetilde{D_t}\, \vert\nabla \tilde{\xi}\vert^2$ | Scalar dissipation rate (flamelet parameter) |
| 11 | $\dfrac{L_f}{d} = 0.5\left(\mathrm{Re} - 200\right)^{1/2}\quad (\mathrm{Re} > 2000)$ | **Roper correlation** (momentum jet flame length) |
| 12 | $\dfrac{L_f}{d} \approx 0.235\, \dot{Q}^{2/3} \ \ (\dot{Q} \text{ in kW})$ | **Hottel correlation** (buoyant pool/jet flame length) |
| 13 | $\mathrm{St} = \dfrac{0.495}{1 + 0.5\,(d/x)^2 \cdot K_u^2}$ | Local turbulent **Stanton number** for heat transfer to walls |
| 14 | $a = \sqrt{\chi\, \mathcal{X}(\xi) / (2\, D_{th})}$ | Flamelet strain rate |

> **Notation safeguard:** Absolute values such as $\vert \nabla \tilde{\xi} \vert$ are typeset using `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent Markdown table breakage.

## 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice

- **Flame length $L_f$** determines **safe separation distance** in flare-stack design (API 521) and **radiation footprint** of a pool fire.
- **Mixture fraction $\xi_{st}$** identifies where soot is **born** (typically at the rich side, $\xi \approx 2 \xi_{st}$) — directly affecting **soot yield** $y_s$ and **radiative heat flux** $\dot{q}''_r$.
- **Scalar dissipation $\chi$** controls **local extinction** when $\chi$ exceeds the critical $\chi_q$ — the limit of stable combustion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Centreline Mean-Velocity Decay for a Round Turbulent Free Jet

### Step 1 — Governing Integral Invariant

For a steady, incompressible, **non-buoyant** round turbulent free jet issuing from a small nozzle, the **axial momentum flux** is conserved (no external body force, no pressure work across any plane $x = \text{const}$):

$$
I = \int_{0}^{\infty} 2\pi r\, \rho\, u^2\, dr = \mathrm{constant} = J_0
$$

### Step 2 — Hypothesis of Self-Similarity (Reynolds Analogy)

We assume that the radial profile of axial velocity is **self-similar**, i.e.:

$$
\frac{u(r,x)}{u_m(x)} = f(\eta), \qquad \text{where}\quad \eta = \frac{r}{r_{1/2}(x)}
$$

and the half-width $r_{1/2}$ grows **linearly** with axial distance:

$$
r_{1/2}(x) = c\, x, \qquad c \approx 0.10
$$

### Step 3 — Adopt a Gaussian Velocity Profile

Empirically (and from asymptotic analysis of the boundary-layer equations), the velocity profile is well-approximated as:

$$
f(\eta) = \exp\left(-\ln 2 \cdot \eta^2\right)
$$

such that $f(1) = 0.5$, defining the half-width. Thus:

$$
u(r,x) = u_m(x) \exp\!\left(-\ln 2 \cdot \frac{r^2}{r_{1/2}^2}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Momentum Integral

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_0 &= \int_{0}^{\infty} 2\pi r\, \rho\, u_m^2(x)\ \exp\!\left(-2 \ln 2 \cdot \dfrac{r^2}{r_{1/2}^2}\right) dr \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \rho\, u_m^2\, \int_{0}^{\infty} r\, \exp\!\left(-2 \ln 2 \cdot \dfrac{r^2}{r_{1/2}^2}\right) dr
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $\alpha = 2 \ln 2$. Using $\int_0^{\infty} r\, e^{-\alpha r^2 / r_{1/2}^2}\, dr = \dfrac{r_{1/2}^2}{2\alpha}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_0 &= 2\pi \rho\, u_m^2 \cdot \dfrac{r_{1/2}^2}{2\, (2 \ln 2)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\pi \rho\, u_m^2\, r_{1/2}^2}{2 \ln 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate $r_{1/2}$ Using Linear Spread

Substitute $r_{1/2} = c\, x$:

$$
J_0 = \dfrac{\pi \rho\, u_m^2\, c^2\, x^2}{2 \ln 2}
$$

Solve for $u_m(x)$:

$$
u_m(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 J_0 \ln 2}{\pi \rho\, c^2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{x}
$$

### Step 6 — Normalise by Exit Conditions

The exit momentum flux (assuming a top-hat profile of velocity $U_0$ over a disc of diameter $d$) is:

$$
J_0 = \rho\, U_0^2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_m^2 &= \dfrac{2 \ln 2}{\pi \rho\, c^2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \rho U_0^2 d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{x^2} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{u_m^2}{U_0^2} &= \dfrac{\ln 2 \cdot d^2}{2\, c^2\, x^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{u_m}{U_0} = \dfrac{1}{c}\sqrt{\dfrac{\ln 2}{2}} \cdot \dfrac{d}{x} \approx \dfrac{6.2\, d}{x}}
$$

where the constant $6.2$ is obtained by using $c = 0.10$. The exact value lies in the experimental range $5.8$–$6.4$.

> **Engineering interpretation:** The centreline velocity decays as $1/x$ in the fully developed self-similar zone — *not* as $1/\sqrt{x}$ as in laminar jets. This is a direct consequence of **momentum conservation** with a **linearly spreading shear layer**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Centreline Mixture-Fraction Decay

By exact analogy, the **conserved-scalar transport** in the self-similar region obeys:

$$
J_\xi = \int_{0}^{\infty} 2\pi r\, \rho\, u\, \xi\, dr = \rho\, U_0\, \xi_0\, \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Assuming a Gaussian profile for $\xi(r,x) = \xi_m(x)\exp(-\ln 2 \cdot r^2/r_{1/2}^2)$ and following the same algebraic path as **§3.1**, one obtains:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\xi_m}{\xi_0} \approx \dfrac{5.6\, d}{x}}
$$

The slightly smaller prefactor ($5.6$ vs. $6.2$) reflects the fact that scalar spreading is governed by a different (Prandtl-number-scaled) turbulent diffusivity $\widetilde{D_t} = \mu_t / (\rho\, \mathrm{Sc}_t)$, with $\mathrm{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cumulative Entrainment in the Self-Similar Region

**Definition (entrainment):** The rate at which ambient fluid is drawn across a cylindrical surface of radius $r$ is:

$$
\dot{m}_e(x) = 2\pi r\, \rho_\infty\, \tilde{v}_r(r,x)
$$

The classical entrainment hypothesis (Morton, Taylor & Turner) states that this rate is proportional to a characteristic velocity and length:

$$
\dot{m}_e(x) = 2\pi\, K_e\, \rho_\infty\, u_m(x)\, r_{1/2}(x)
$$

with $K_e \approx 0.45$ (empirical). Using $u_m = K_u U_0 d / x$ and $r_{1/2} = c\, x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_e(x) &= 2\pi\, K_e\, \rho_\infty \left(\dfrac{K_u U_0 d}{x}\right)(c\, x) \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi\, K_e\, c\, K_u\, \rho_\infty\, U_0\, d
\end{aligned}
$$

The total mass flow rate at axial location $x$ in the fully developed region is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}(x) = \dot{m}_0 + 2\pi K_e\, c\, K_u\, \rho_\infty U_0 \left(x - x_0\right)}
$$

where $x_0$ is the virtual origin (typically $x_0 \approx 5d$ for round jets). Numerically, the coefficient evaluates to $\approx 0.32\,\rho_\infty U_0 d$, which on integration yields $\dot{m} \propto x$, in agreement with the well-known **linear entrainment law** for free turbulent shear flows.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Flame Length — Roper Correlation (Momentum Jet)

For a **momentum-dominated** ($\mathrm{Fr} \gg 1$) turbulent round jet diffusion flame, Roper (1977) proposed the empirical relation:

$$
\dfrac{L_f}{d} = a\left(\mathrm{Re} - \mathrm{Re}_{\mathrm{crit}}\right)^{1/2}
$$

with $a = 0.5$ and $\mathrm{Re}_{\mathrm{crit}} \approx 200$.

**Analytical justification:** The flame length is set by the axial distance at which the local fuel mole fraction (or mixture fraction) at the centreline equals the stoichiometric value, i.e. $\xi_m(L_f) = \xi_{st}$. Substituting the centreline decay from **§3.2**:

$$
\xi_{st} = \xi_0 \cdot \dfrac{5.6\, d}{L_f} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L_f = \dfrac{5.6\, \xi_0\, d}{\xi_{st}}
$$

For a **methane** jet ($\xi_{st} \approx 0.055$, $\xi_0 = 1$) this predicts $L_f/d \approx 102$, in excellent agreement with Roper's data. For heavier hydrocarbons, $L_f/d$ decreases because $\xi_{st}$ rises.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames — Section 4: Structural & Functional Diagrams (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Round Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flame

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FUEL RESERVOIR] --> B[NOZZLE / ORIFICE]
    B --> C[POTENTIAL CORE 0 to 5d]
    C --> D[TRANSITIONAL REGION 5d to 30d]
    D --> E[FULLY DEVELOPED SELF SIMILAR REGION x greater than 30d]
    E --> F[BUOYANT PLUME REGION Ri greater than 0.5]
    F --> G[INTERMITTENT FAR FIELD]
    H[AMBIENT OXIDISER POOL] --> I[TURBULENT ENTRAINMENT]
    I --> D
    I --> E
    I --> F
    J[FUEL JET] --> K[REACTION ZONE Z equals Z_ST]
    K --> L[SOOT SHELL]
    K --> M[NON SOOTING DIFFUSION FLAME OUTER]
    N[MIXTURE FRACTION GRADIENT] --> K
    O[SCALAR DISSIPATION CHI] --> K
    K --> P[PRODUCTS CO2 H2O]
    P --> G
```

## 4.2 Regime Map: Re vs. Froude Number (Forced, Buoyant, Plume)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Re greater than 2000] --> B[Re number]
    B --> C{Froude number}
    C -- Fr greater than 10 --> D[MOMENTUM JET FLAME]
    C -- 0.1 less than Fr less than 10 --> E[TRANSITIONAL JET PLUME]
    C -- Fr less than 0.1 --> F[BUOYANT PLUME FLAME]
    D --> G[Re ANALOGY u_m proportional to 1 divided by x]
    E --> H[CORRECTED ANALOGY MOMENTUM PLUS BUOYANCY]
    F --> I[ENTHALPY CONSERVATION PLUME LAW]
```

## 4.3 Flamelet Manifold Concept (Burke-Schumann + Peters)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SCALAR TRANSPORT EQUATION FOR XI] --> B[XI FIELD COMPUTED]
    B --> C[SCALAR DISSIPATION CHI EQUALS 2 Dt times GRAD XI squared]
    C --> D[FLAMELET LIBRARY]
    D --> E[FLAMELET STRUCTURE T Y SPECIES AS F OF XI AND CHI]
    D --> F[CRITICAL DISSIPATION CHI_Q EXTINCTION LIMIT]
    E --> G[MEAN REACTION RATE]
    F --> G
    G --> H[TURBULENT FLAME RESPONSE]
```

## 4.4 Energy/Mass Flow inside the Flame Sheet

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fuel stream u0 YF0] --> B[Inner fuel rich core]
    B --> C[Flame sheet xi equals xi_st]
    C --> D[Outer fuel lean annulus]
    D --> E[Entrained air from ambient]
    E --> D
    F[Soot formed on rich side] --> C
    G[Heat released at flame sheet] --> H[Radiation to surroundings]
    G --> I[Conduction to fuel core]
    G --> J[Convection to entrained oxidiser]
    H --> K[Fire plume]
    I --> L[Hot products rise]
    J --> L
    L --> K
```

> **Reading the diagrams:** The key engineering insight is the **decoupling of mixing and chemistry** in the flamelet regime. The mixture-fraction field $\xi(\mathbf{x},t)$ is solved from a transport equation, while chemistry is tabulated as a function of $(\xi, \chi)$ from steady laminar-flamelet libraries.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define *Reynolds-averaged* and *Favre-averaged* mixture fraction in a turbulent diffusion flame. Why is Favre averaging preferred for compressible or variable-density flows?

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reynolds average** of a scalar $\phi$ is its time average: $\tilde{\phi} = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \phi\,dt$. (1 mark)
- **Favre (mass-weighted) average** is $\hat{\phi} = \overline{\rho\phi}/\bar{\rho}$. (1 mark)
- Favre averaging **absorbs the density fluctuations** into the weighting, leaving the Favre-averaged momentum equation in a form identical to the constant-density Navier–Stokes equation. This is why it is preferred for **variable-density** turbulent jet flames where $\rho$ fluctuates strongly across the flame sheet. (1 mark)

### **Q2.** What is the **conserved scalar (mixture fraction)** assumption, and under what two conditions is it strictly valid?

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The mixture fraction $\xi$ is defined as the mass fraction of material originating in the fuel stream; it satisfies a transport equation with no source term, hence it is a **conserved scalar**. (1 mark)
- Validity conditions: (i) **equal mass diffusivities** of all species (Le = 1, unity Lewis number) and (ii) **single-step, infinitely fast chemistry** (Burke–Schumann limit). (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question on Module 4 has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Below are two alternative 14-mark questions (Q4A and Q4B) — answer **either** as per ESE choice rules.

---

### **Q4(A) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Mock Paper]`**

**(a)** Starting from the **conserved-scalar (mixture fraction) transport equation**, derive the expression for the **centreline mean velocity decay** and the **centreline mixture-fraction decay** for a round turbulent free jet. State the empirical constants and their physical significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A circular nozzle of diameter $d = 0.02$ m discharges methane ($\mathrm{CH_4}$) into still air at exit velocity $U_0 = 40$ m/s. The stoichiometric mixture fraction for the methane–air system is $\xi_{st} = 0.055$. Using the Roper-type scaling $L_f/d = 5.6\,\xi_0/\xi_{st}$, calculate:
- (i) the **visible flame length** $L_f$;
- (ii) the **centreline velocity** at $x = 50d$;
- (iii) the **local mixture fraction** at the flame tip;
- (iv) the **cumulative mass entrainment rate** at $x = 50d$, assuming $\rho_\infty = 1.20$ kg/m³ and $K_e \cdot c \cdot K_u \approx 0.32$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Q4A):**

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]:**
The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.1 and 3.2** above. The candidate should present:
1. **Momentum conservation integral** $J_0 = \int_0^\infty 2\pi r \rho u^2 dr = \text{const}$. (1 mark)
2. **Self-similarity hypothesis** $u(r,x) = u_m(x)\exp(-\ln 2 \cdot r^2/r_{1/2}^2)$ and linear spread $r_{1/2} = cx$. (2 marks)
3. **Substitution and integration** to obtain $u_m/U_0 = (1/c)\sqrt{\ln 2 / 2}\cdot d/x \approx 6.2\,d/x$. (2 marks)
4. **By exact analogy** (replace $u^2$ with $u\,\xi$ in the integral), derive $\xi_m/\xi_0 = 5.6\,d/x$. (2 marks)

*Constants: $K_u \approx 6.2$ reflects the **Reynolds-analogy constant**; $K_\xi \approx 5.6$ reflects the **turbulent Prandtl/Schmidt correction** with $\mathrm{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$.*

**(b) Numerical problem [7 marks]:**

Given: $d = 0.02$ m, $U_0 = 40$ m/s, $\xi_{st} = 0.055$, $\xi_0 = 1$ (pure fuel stream), $\rho_\infty = 1.20$ kg/m³.

**(i) Visible flame length [2 marks]:**

$$
L_f = d \cdot \dfrac{5.6\,\xi_0}{\xi_{st}} = 0.02 \cdot \dfrac{5.6 \cdot 1}{0.055} = 0.02 \cdot 101.82 = 2.04\ \mathrm{m}
$$

**(ii) Centreline velocity at $x = 50d$ [2 marks]:**

$$
u_m(50d) = U_0 \cdot \dfrac{6.2}{50} = 40 \cdot 0.124 = 4.96\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**(iii) Local mixture fraction at flame tip [1 mark]:**

At $x = L_f$, by construction $\xi_m = \xi_{st} = 0.055$ (i.e. the flame tip is where the centreline crosses stoichiometry). (1 mark)

**(iv) Cumulative mass entrainment rate at $x = 50d$ [2 marks]:**

$$
\dot{m}(50d) = 2\pi \cdot (0.32) \cdot \rho_\infty U_0\, d \cdot (50d - 5d)
$$

$$
= 2\pi \cdot 0.32 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.02 \cdot (1.0 - 0.1)\ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
= 2\pi \cdot 0.32 \cdot 0.96 \cdot 0.9 \approx 1.74\ \mathrm{kg/s}
$$

**Total mass flow rate (fuel + entrained air) at $x = 50d$ is $\dot{m}(50d) \approx 1.74$ kg/s.**

---

### **Q4(B) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **Burke–Schumann limit** for diffusion flames. Explain the role of the **scalar dissipation rate $\chi$** and the **critical dissipation $\chi_q$** in determining local flame extinction in turbulent jet diffusion flames. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vertical turbulent non-premixed **propane (C₃H₈) jet flame** issues from a 5 cm-diameter nozzle. The fuel mass fraction at exit is $Y_{F,0} = 1$ and the ambient oxygen mass fraction is $Y_{O,\infty} = 0.233$. The mass stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel ratio for propane is $\nu = 3.64$. Given $U_0 = 25$ m/s and $T_\infty = 300$ K:
- (i) Compute the **stoichiometric mixture fraction** $\xi_{st}$;
- (ii) Compute the **exit Reynolds number** using $\mu_0 = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s and $\rho_0 = 1.85$ kg/m³;
- (iii) Using Roper's correlation, estimate the **flame length**;
- (iv) State and justify whether the flame is **momentum- or buoyancy-dominated**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Q4B):**

**(a) Theoretical discussion [7 marks]:**
1. **Burke–Schumann (1928) assumption:** Equal diffusivities + infinitely fast chemistry → $\xi$ is a single conserved scalar that determines all thermochemical variables uniquely. (2 marks)
2. **Scalar dissipation rate:** $\chi = 2\widetilde{D_t}\vert \nabla \tilde{\xi} \vert^2$ quantifies the **local rate of micromixing**. Higher $\chi$ → finer scale → thinner reaction zone. (2 marks)
3. **Critical $\chi_q$:** When local $\chi$ exceeds the steady-flamelet extinction limit $\chi_q$, the flame **locally quenches**. This explains observed flame **lift-off** at high jet velocities. (2 marks)
4. **Application:** In TJDFs, the local value of $\chi$ near the nozzle exit can be large due to high strain; if $\chi > \chi_q$, the flame detaches (lift-off height $H_L \propto U_0^n$). (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical problem [7 marks]:**

**(i) Stoichiometric mixture fraction [2 marks]:**

$$
\xi_{st} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\nu\, Y_{O,\infty}/Y_{F,0})} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (3.64 \cdot 0.233/1)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.848} = \dfrac{1}{1.848} = 0.541
$$

**(ii) Exit Reynolds number [2 marks]:**

$$
\mathrm{Re}_d = \dfrac{\rho_0 U_0 d}{\mu_0} = \dfrac{1.85 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.05}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{2.3125}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.28 \times 10^{5}
$$

**(iii) Flame length (Roper) [2 marks]:**

$$
L_f = 0.5\,d\,(\mathrm{Re} - 200)^{1/2} = 0.5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (1.28 \times 10^5 - 200)^{1/2}
$$

$$
= 0.025 \cdot \sqrt{1.2798 \times 10^5} = 0.025 \cdot 357.7 = 8.94\ \mathrm{m}
$$

**(iv) Froude number check [1 mark]:**

$$
\mathrm{Fr} = \dfrac{U_0^2}{g\, d} = \dfrac{625}{9.81 \cdot 0.05} = \dfrac{625}{0.4905} \approx 1274 \gg 1
$$

Since $\mathrm{Fr} \gg 1$, the flame is **momentum-dominated** (a forced jet flame), and Roper's correlation is the appropriate model. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (High-Frequency Mark Loss)**
> 1. **Mixing $K_u$ and $K_\xi$:** Many students wrongly equate $K_u = K_\xi = 6.2$. The correct values are $K_u \approx 6.2$ and $K_\xi \approx 5.6$ (due to $\mathrm{Sc}_t \neq 1$). (Loses 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting units of $L_f$:** Always quote $L_f$ in metres; the formula $L_f/d = 5.6\,\xi_0/\xi_{st}$ is dimensionless.
> 3. **Using Hottel where Roper is required (or vice versa):** Hottel's correlation is for **buoyant (Fr $\ll 1$) flames** (pool fires). Roper's is for **momentum (Fr $\gg 1$) jet flames**. Always compute $\mathrm{Fr}$ first.
> 4. **Forgetting to check $\mathrm{Re} > 2000$:** If $\mathrm{Re} < 2000$, the jet is laminar and **all** turbulent correlations are invalid. (Loses 2 marks in KTU valuation.)
> 5. **Scalar dissipation sign error:** $\chi$ is a **positive definite** quantity — never write $\chi < 0$. (Loses 1 mark.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Quick-Fire Recap (Last-Minute Revision)**

- [x] A **TJDF** is non-premixed; combustion occurs at $\xi = \xi_{st}$.
- [x] **Turbulence onset:** $\mathrm{Re}_d > 2000$ (round jet); $\mathrm{Re}_d > 1400$ (slot jet).
- [x] **Centreline velocity:** $u_m/U_0 = 6.2\,d/x$ in the self-similar zone.
- [x] **Centreline mixture fraction:** $\xi_m/\xi_0 = 5.6\,d/x$.
- [x] **Half-width:** $r_{1/2}/x \approx 0.10$–$0.13$.
- [x] **Linear entrainment** is the hallmark of fully turbulent free shear flows.
- [x] **Mixture fraction** is the single conserved scalar in the Burke–Schumann limit.
- [x] **Favre averaging** is essential for variable-density flows.
- [x] **Scalar dissipation** $\chi = 2\widetilde{D_t}\vert \nabla \tilde{\xi}\vert^2$ governs extinction.
- [x] **Roper (momentum):** $L_f/d = 0.5(\mathrm{Re}-200)^{1/2}$ for $\mathrm{Fr} \gg 1$.
- [x] **Hottel (buoyant):** $L_f/d = 0.235\,\dot Q^{2/3}$ for $\mathrm{Fr} \ll 1$.
- [x] **Flame regimes:** Force > Buoyancy (jet) ↔ Buoyancy > Force (plume).
- [x] **Soot** forms on the rich side of the flame ($\xi \approx 2\xi_{st}$ for many hydrocarbons).
- [x] **Flamelet model** decouples mixing ($\xi$ field) from chemistry (tabulated $T, Y_k$ vs. $\xi, \chi$).
- [x] **Lift-off** occurs when local $\chi$ near the nozzle exceeds the critical $\chi_q$.
- [x] **Radiative fraction** of TJDFs is typically $0.15$–$0.35$ for hydrocarbon fuels, dominated by **soot luminosity**.
- [x] **Engineering use:** flare design (API 521), pool-fire separation (NFPA 30), and compartment-fire ventilation calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Liftoff and Blowoff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flame Liftoff and Blowoff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flame Liftoff** is the aerodynamic and thermochemical phenomenon in which a diffusion or partially-premixed flame, originally anchored at the rim of a burner port, fuel jet, or pilot, physically detaches from the stabilization point and lifts downstream to a new equilibrium position. This occurs when the local flow velocity at the flame base exceeds the **burning velocity** (laminar flame speed) of the mixture, causing the reaction zone to translate along the centerline of the jet until a new balance between **convective transport** and **diffusive-thermal propagation** is re-established.

**Flame Blowoff** (also termed **blowout** or **extinction**) is the catastrophic, complete extinguishment of the lifted flame when the local strain rate or jet exit velocity exceeds a critical threshold — known as the **Blowoff Velocity ($U_{bo}$)** or **Blowoff Limit** — at which the residence time of reactants in the reaction zone becomes shorter than the chemical ignition delay time, making sustained combustion thermodynamically and kinetically impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - **Liftoff** = flame detaches but *continues to burn* downstream at a stable lifted height.
> - **Blowoff** = flame is *completely extinguished*; no combustion persists.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy: A Candle in the Wind**

Imagine lighting a candle. The flame sits snugly on the wick because the vaporizing wax rises slowly and the flame's burning speed easily matches the fuel supply. Now, gently blow across the candle:

- **Slow breeze** → flame tilts but stays on the wick (stable attachment).
- **Moderate breeze** → the flame base *lifts off* the wick and hovers in the air, burning the wax vapor as it streams past. The flame has found a new "parking spot" downstream. This is **flame liftoff**.
- **Strong gust** → the flame is blown completely away from the fuel stream and vanishes. The wick continues to vaporize wax, but no flame can anchor itself — combustion ceases. This is **flame blowoff**.

The wind speed at the transition between "hovering flame" and "no flame" is the **blowoff velocity**.

### 1.3 Physical Metrics and Constants

- **Blowoff Velocity ($U_{bo}$):** typically **5 – 50 m/s** for gaseous jet diffusion flames
- **Liftoff Height ($H_L$):** **2 – 50 times** the burner exit diameter
- **Standoff Distance:** distance between burner rim and lifted flame base
- **Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_{cr}$):** **~2300** for transition in stabilization behavior
- **Autoignition Temperature** of common hydrocarbons: **200 °C – 600 °C**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Damköhler Number ($Da$)** is the dimensionless parameter that ultimately governs the liftoff/blowoff transition. It compares chemical reaction time to fluid residence time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Flame Stabilization

A flame remains *attached* at a stabilization point (e.g., burner rim) only when three competing transport phenomena are balanced:

1. **Convective Transport** of reactants away from the stabilization zone (driven by jet exit velocity $U_j$).
2. **Diffusive Transport** of heat and mass from the flame front *back* toward the stabilization zone (against the flow).
3. **Chemical Reaction Kinetics** within the reaction zone (governed by activation energy and ignition delay).

The flame base sits where these three rates achieve a local equilibrium. Mathematically, this is captured by the **Stabilization Condition**:

$$\boxed{U_j \cdot \nabla(\text{scalar field}) = \rho \cdot Y_F \cdot A \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T_{flame}}\right)}$$

where $U_j$ is the jet exit velocity, $\rho$ is gas density, $Y_F$ is fuel mass fraction, $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, and $E_a$ is the activation energy.

### 2.2 The Three Stages of a Diffusion Flame's Life Cycle

| Stage | Physical Description | Velocity Regime | Governing Mechanism |
|-------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Attached Flame** | Flame anchored at burner rim | $U_j < U_{lo}$ | Diffusion + chemistry balance at rim |
| **Lifted Flame** | Flame base detaches and stabilizes downstream at height $H_L$ | $U_{lo} < U_j < U_{bo}$ | Edge flame propagation theory |
| **Blowoff** | Complete extinction of the flame | $U_j > U_{bo}$ | Residence time < ignition delay |

where $U_{lo}$ = liftoff velocity, $U_{bo}$ = blowoff velocity.

### 2.3 Critical Conditions for Liftoff and Blowoff

**Liftoff Criterion (Pioneered by Chung & Lee, 1988):**
The flame lifts off when the local flow velocity at the burner rim exceeds the **strained laminar burning velocity** of the mixture:

$$U_j = S_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} \cdot f(\text{stretch})$$

**Blowoff Criterion (Karlovitz Number Approach):**
Blowoff occurs when the local Karlovitz stretch rate $K$ exceeds a critical value $K_{cr}$:

$$K_{cr} = \dfrac{Ka_{cr}}{Re} = \dfrac{\text{chemical time}}{\text{flow time}}$$

When $K \geq K_{cr}$, the flame cannot sustain itself.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master KTU 2024 reference for numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Damköhler Number** | $Da = \dfrac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \dfrac{L \cdot S_L}{U_j \cdot \delta_L}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of flow time to chemical time |
| 2 | **Blowoff Velocity (Empirical)** | $U_{bo} = \dfrac{S_L \cdot d}{L_{ig} \cdot \phi}$ | m/s | Critical jet velocity for blowoff |
| 3 | **Liftoff Height (Jet Theory)** | $H_L = 4.2 \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{U_j}{S_L}$ | m | Height at which lifted flame stabilizes |
| 4 | **Karlovitz Number** | $Ka = \dfrac{\tau_{chem}}{\tau_{flow}} = \dfrac{\delta_L \cdot U_j}{S_L \cdot L}$ | dimensionless | Local stretch intensity |
| 5 | **Peclet Number** | $Pe = \dfrac{U_j \cdot d}{\alpha}$ | dimensionless | Convection vs. thermal diffusion |
| 6 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot U_j \cdot d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Inertia vs. viscous forces |
| 7 | **Autoignition Time** | $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)$ | s | Chemical ignition delay |
| 8 | **Stabilization Damköhler** | $Da_{stab} = \dfrac{S_L \cdot d}{\alpha}$ | dimensionless | Stabilization criterion |
| 9 | **Edge Flame Speed** | $S_{edge} = S_L \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\delta_L}{L_{mix}}\right)$ | m/s | Speed of triple-flame edge |
| 10 | **Critical Strain Rate** | $a_{cr} = \dfrac{2 S_L}{\delta_L}$ | s$^{-1}$ | Strain at extinction limit |

where:
- $d$ = burner exit diameter (m)
- $S_L$ = laminar burning velocity (m/s)
- $\delta_L$ = laminar flame thickness (m)
- $L_{ig}$ = ignition length scale (m)
- $\phi$ = equivalence ratio
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $L_{mix}$ = mixing layer thickness (m)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding flame liftoff and blowoff is critical in:

- **Gas Turbine Combustors:** designers ensure $U_{j,\text{operating}} < 0.8 \cdot U_{bo}$ to prevent flame extinction during transients.
- **Industrial Furnaces & Boilers:** lifting flames cause **noise, vibration (combustion oscillation)** and **CO emissions**.
- **Fire Safety Engineering:** pool fires, jet fires, and pressurized gas leaks are governed by liftoff physics.
- **Rocket Engine Injectors:** understanding blowoff limits prevents catastrophic engine flame-out.
- **Domestic Burners:** stove and heater designs leverage stabilization zones (e.g., pilot flames).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liftoff Height from Jet Theory

We seek the height $H_L$ downstream of a circular jet burner (diameter $d$) at which a lifted diffusion flame stabilizes. The centerline velocity of a round jet decays as:

$$\dfrac{U_c(x)}{U_j} = \dfrac{6.2}{(x/d)}$$

for $x/d > 7$ (self-similar jet region).

**Step 1:** The flame base stabilizes where the local jet centerline velocity equals the local **strained burning velocity** in the stoichiometric mixture:

$$U_c(H_L) = S_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the jet centerline velocity profile:

$$\dfrac{6.2 \cdot U_j \cdot d}{H_L} = S_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$$

**Step 3:** Solve explicitly for $H_L$:

$$H_L = \dfrac{6.2 \cdot U_j \cdot d}{S_L} \cdot \dfrac{T_u}{T_{ad}}$$

**Step 4:** Since $T_{ad}/T_u \approx 7$ to $9$ for hydrocarbon-air flames, the constant effectively becomes:

$$H_L \approx 4.2 \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{U_j}{S_L}$$

$$\boxed{H_L = 4.2 \, d \cdot \dfrac{U_j}{S_L}}$$

This is the **classical liftoff height correlation** widely used in combustion textbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **4.2** is the empirical constant that combines the jet spreading coefficient, the temperature ratio, and the edge-flame propagation physics. Different fuels yield slightly different constants (e.g., 3.7 for methane, 4.8 for propane).

### 3.2 Derivation: Blowoff Velocity from Damköhler Criterion

A flame blows off when the **Damköhler Number** falls below a critical value $Da_{cr} \approx 1$.

**Step 1:** Define the characteristic chemical time as the ignition delay:

$$\tau_{chem} = \tau_{ig} = A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R T_{flame}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Define the characteristic flow time as the transit time through the flame thickness:

$$\tau_{flow} = \dfrac{\delta_L}{S_L}$$

**Step 3:** The local Damköhler number is:

$$Da = \dfrac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \dfrac{\delta_L}{S_L \cdot A \cdot \exp(E_a / R T_{flame})}$$

**Step 4:** The blowoff condition is $Da = Da_{cr}$:

$$\delta_L = S_L \cdot A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R T_{flame}}\right) \cdot Da_{cr}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the critical jet velocity at blowoff (using $\delta_L = \alpha / S_L$):

$$U_{bo} = \dfrac{S_L^2 \cdot d \cdot Da_{cr}}{\alpha \cdot \exp(-E_a/RT_{flame})}$$

$$\boxed{U_{bo} = \dfrac{S_L^2 \cdot d}{\alpha \cdot \tau_{ig,cr}}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU-Style Problem (Self-Worked)**

**Given:**
- Burner exit diameter: $d = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- Fuel: methane, laminar burning velocity: $S_L = 0.4$ m/s
- Jet exit velocity: $U_j = 12$ m/s
- Adiabatic flame temperature: $T_{ad} = 2223$ K
- Unburned gas temperature: $T_u = 300$ K

**Find:** (a) Liftoff height $H_L$, (b) Check if blowoff occurs given $U_{bo} = 18$ m/s.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Liftoff Height:**

$$H_L = 4.2 \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{U_j}{S_L}$$

$$H_L = 4.2 \cdot 0.01 \cdot \dfrac{12}{0.4}$$

$$H_L = 4.2 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 30$$

$$\boxed{H_L = 1.26 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (b) — Blowoff Check:**

Comparing $U_j = 12$ m/s with $U_{bo} = 18$ m/s:

Since $U_j = 12 < U_{bo} = 18$, the flame is in the **lifted-but-stable regime**. No blowoff occurs. The flame will burn at a height of 1.26 m above the burner.

**Dimensional verification:** $[m] \cdot [m/s] / [m/s] = [m]$ ✓

**Limitation note:** The correlation $H_L = 4.2 d (U_j/S_L)$ is valid only for **momentum-dominated round jet diffusion flames** in air. For buoyancy-dominated or partially-premixed flames, the constant and functional form must be modified.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flame Life-Cycle Block Architecture

This diagram maps the **causal chain of events** from a stable flame through liftoff to blowoff, showing the governing physics at each transition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steady Attached Flame: Uj below Ulo] --> B[Convective Transport Increases with Uj]
    B --> C{Compare Uj with S_L at Rim}
    C -- Uj equals S_L --> D[Flame Liftoff Initiated]
    D --> E[Edge Flame Triple Point Forms]
    E --> F[Lifted Flame Stabilization Zone at H_L]
    F --> G[Damkohler Number Da Monitored]
    G -- Da greater than Da_cr --> H[Stable Lifted Flame Burns]
    G -- Da less than Da_cr --> I[Chemical Time exceeds Flow Time]
    I --> J[Local Karlovitz Ka exceeds Ka_cr]
    J --> K[Reaction Zone Quenched]
    K --> L[FLAME BLOFF COMPLETE: Uj above Ubo]
    L --> M[Unburned Fuel Jets into Ambient]
    M --> N[Fire Hazard or System Shutdown]
```

### 4.2 Stabilization Mechanism Schematic

This block-level diagram illustrates the **three competing transport mechanisms** at the lifted flame base (an "edge flame" or "triple flame" structure).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fuel_Side
        A1[Fuel Rich Core Stream] --> A2[Rich Premixed Branch]
        A2 --> A3[Triple Point]
    end
    subgraph Oxidizer_Side
        B1[Air Stream from Ambient] --> B2[Lean Premixed Branch]
        B2 --> A3
    end
    subgraph Reaction_Zone
        A3 --> C1[Diffusion Flame Branch]
        C1 --> D1[Heat Release Zone]
        D1 --> E1[Hot Products Upstream Conduction]
        E1 --> A3
    end
    D1 --> F1[Lifted Flame Base Anchors Here]
    F1 --> G1[Lifted Flame Stabilized at H_L]
```

### 4.3 Regime Map: Velocity vs. Fuel Flow Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Uj and m_fuel_dot] --> B{Axis Uj: below 5 m/s}
    B -- Yes --> C[Zone 1: Attached Diffusion Flame]
    B -- No --> D{Axis Uj: 5 to 50 m/s}
    D -- Yes --> E[Zone 2: Lifted Flame Regime]
    D -- No --> F[Zone 3: Blowoff Extinction Zone]
    E --> G{Within 0 to 0.8 of Ubo}
    G -- Yes --> H[Stable Lifted Operation]
    G -- No --> I[Approaching Blowoff Margin]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical boundaries in the regime map are **indicative** and depend strongly on the fuel (methane, propane, hydrogen), nozzle geometry, and ambient conditions. Actual $U_{bo}$ values must be measured experimentally or computed via detailed chemical kinetics simulations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Define flame liftoff and flame blowoff. How are they distinguished in terms of the flame's continued existence?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Flame liftoff is the phenomenon where a flame detaches from its stabilization point (e.g., burner rim) and moves downstream to a new position where a balance between convective and diffusive transport is re-established.
- **(1 mark)** Flame blowoff is the complete extinguishment of the flame when the jet velocity exceeds a critical blowoff velocity $U_{bo}$, making the residence time of reactants shorter than the ignition delay.
- **(1 mark)** The distinction: in liftoff, combustion *continues* at a lifted height $H_L$; in blowoff, combustion *ceases entirely* and unburned fuel is released.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the Damköhler Number criterion for flame blowoff. What is the physical significance of $Da = 1$ in the context of flame stabilization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The Damköhler number is defined as $Da = \tau_{flow} / \tau_{chem}$, the ratio of the convective flow time to the chemical reaction (ignition) time.
- **(1 mark)** When $Da = 1$, the flow time equals the chemical time, marking the *critical transition* between stable combustion and extinction.
- **(1 mark)** For $Da \gg 1$, chemistry is fast relative to flow → stable flame. For $Da \ll 1$, chemistry is too slow → blowoff occurs. The blowoff condition is $Da \leq Da_{cr} \approx 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the physical mechanisms responsible for **flame liftoff** in a non-premixed jet flame. Discuss the role of edge flames in the stabilization of lifted flames. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the classical liftoff height correlation $H_L = 4.2 d \cdot (U_j / S_L)$ starting from the round-jet centerline velocity profile. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mechanisms of Flame Liftoff (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** At low jet exit velocities $U_j$, the flame anchors at the burner rim. The rim acts as a **stabilization point** where the velocity drops to zero and continuous heat feedback from the flame to the incoming cold reactants is possible.
- **(2 marks)** As $U_j$ increases, the convective transport of reactants *away* from the rim begins to dominate over the diffusive-thermal transport of heat *back* toward the rim. The flame base is gradually pushed downstream.
- **(2 marks)** The lifted flame stabilizes at a downstream location where a **triple flame (edge flame)** structure forms. This consists of: (i) a fuel-rich premixed branch, (ii) a fuel-lean premixed branch, and (iii) a non-premixed trailing diffusion flame branch. The edge flame propagates *normal* to itself into the stoichiometric mixture, balancing the local flow.
- **(1 mark)** This edge flame, combined with continuous autoignition chemistry, anchors the lifted flame at height $H_L$ where $U_c(H_L) = S_{edge}$ (the edge flame speed).

**Part (b) — Derivation of Liftoff Height (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Centerline velocity decay in a self-similar round jet:

$$U_c(x) = \dfrac{6.2 \cdot U_j \cdot d}{x}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Stabilization condition: flame base sits where $U_c(H_L)$ equals the stretched burning velocity in the stoichiometric mixture:

$$U_c(H_L) = S_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equating and isolating $H_L$:

$$\dfrac{6.2 \cdot U_j \cdot d}{H_L} = S_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solving:

$$H_L = 6.2 \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{U_j}{S_L} \cdot \dfrac{T_u}{T_{ad}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting $T_{ad}/T_u \approx 7$ to $9$ for hydrocarbon-air:

$$H_L \approx 4.2 \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{U_j}{S_L}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Assumptions stated:**
- Momentum-dominated round jet (not buoyancy-dominated)
- Far-field self-similar region ($x/d > 7$)
- Quasi-steady stabilization (instantaneous equilibrium)
- Ambient air as oxidizer
- $T_u$ uniform, $T_{ad}$ approximated as constant
- Edge flame propagation speed assumed equal to stretched $S_L$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of the **blowoff velocity** $U_{bo}$. Discuss how it is influenced by fuel type, burner diameter, and ambient oxygen concentration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A methane-air jet diffusion flame burns on a circular nozzle of diameter $d = 15$ mm. The jet exit velocity is $U_j = 25$ m/s, the laminar burning velocity is $S_L = 0.45$ m/s, and the blowoff velocity is $U_{bo} = 32$ m/s. Calculate: (i) the liftoff height $H_L$, and (ii) the Damköhler number at operating conditions, assuming the characteristic chemical time is $\tau_{chem} = 1.2$ ms. Comment on whether the flame is in the stable, lifted, or blowoff regime. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Blowoff Velocity (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** The blowoff velocity $U_{bo}$ is the critical jet exit velocity above which the flame cannot sustain combustion and extinguishes. It is determined by the condition $Da = Da_{cr}$.
- **(2 marks)** **Fuel type effect:** Higher $S_L$ fuels (e.g., hydrogen: $S_L \approx 2.8$ m/s) have higher $U_{bo}$ because chemistry is fast enough to withstand greater convective losses. Lower $S_L$ fuels (e.g., methane: $S_L \approx 0.4$ m/s) blow off at lower velocities.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Burner diameter effect:** $U_{bo} \propto d$ (larger nozzles allow higher velocities before blowoff) because the residence time scales with $d$.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Oxygen concentration effect:** Enriched O₂ increases $S_L$ and $T_{ad}$, raising $U_{bo}$. Dilution with N₂ or CO₂ suppresses chemistry and reduces $U_{bo}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Liftoff height using $H_L = 4.2 \cdot d \cdot (U_j / S_L)$:

$$H_L = 4.2 \cdot 0.015 \cdot \dfrac{25}{0.45}$$

$$H_L = 0.063 \cdot 55.56$$

$$\boxed{H_L = 3.5 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Characteristic flow time $\tau_{flow} = d / U_j$:

$$\tau_{flow} = \dfrac{0.015}{25} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Damköhler number:

$$Da = \dfrac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \dfrac{0.6 \text{ ms}}{1.2 \text{ ms}} = 0.5$$

$$\boxed{Da = 0.5}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Regime identification:
- $U_j = 25$ m/s $< U_{bo} = 32$ m/s → **not blown off**
- Since $U_j$ exceeds typical liftoff velocity, the flame is **lifted**
- $Da = 0.5 < Da_{cr} \approx 1$ → flame is **marginally stable**, close to blowoff limit

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Conclusion:** The flame is in a **lifted but marginally stable regime**. Operating at $U_j/U_{bo} = 25/32 = 0.78$, it has only a 22% safety margin. Any transient (e.g., loss of inlet pressure, increase in fuel flow) could trigger blowoff.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Engineering recommendation:** Reduce $U_j$ to below 25 m/s or enrich oxidizer to raise $U_{bo}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write the liftoff height formula as $H_L = d \cdot (U_j/S_L)$ *without the empirical constant* 4.2. This results in a value that is 4× too low and is treated as a **conceptual error (–2 marks)**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $U_{bo}$ (blowoff, extinction) with $U_{lo}$ (liftoff, detachment). Examiners specifically test whether you can distinguish the **two critical velocities**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to state **assumptions** during derivations. KTU 2024 marking schemes allocate **1–2 marks** exclusively for stated assumptions.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing $Da = \tau_{chem}/\tau_{flow}$ (inverted definition). The standard convention is $Da = \tau_{flow}/\tau_{chem}$.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to include **units** in numerical answers. KTU valuation deducts **0.5 mark** per missing unit.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- **Core Concepts:**
  - ☐ Liftoff = flame detaches but *continues* burning downstream
  - ☐ Blowoff = flame *extinguishes* completely
  - ☐ Edge flame (triple flame) is the stabilization mechanism for lifted flames
  - ☐ Damköhler number $Da = \tau_{flow}/\tau_{chem}$ governs the transition

- **Critical Velocities (Memorize the Order):**
  - ☐ $U_{lo}$ (liftoff) $< U_{bo}$ (blowoff)
  - ☐ Operating regime: $U_j < U_{lo}$ (attached), $U_{lo} < U_j < U_{bo}$ (lifted), $U_j > U_{bo}$ (extinguished)

- **Key Formulas (3 Must-Know):**
  - ☐ $H_L = 4.2 \cdot d \cdot (U_j / S_L)$
  - ☐ $U_{bo} = S_L^2 \cdot d / (\alpha \cdot \tau_{ig,cr})$
  - ☐ $Ka = \delta_L \cdot U_j / (S_L \cdot L)$ — local Karlovitz stretch

- **Influencing Parameters (Effect on $U_{bo}$):**
  - ☐ Increasing $d$ → $U_{bo}$ increases (larger → harder to blow off)
  - ☐ Increasing $S_L$ (e.g., hydrogen) → $U_{bo}$ increases
  - ☐ O₂ enrichment → $U_{bo}$ increases
  - ☐ Inert dilution (N₂, CO₂) → $U_{bo}$ decreases
  - ☐ Higher pressure → $U_{bo}$ typically increases

- **Numerical Values to Remember:**
  - ☐ Methane $S_L \approx 0.4$ m/s
  - ☐ Propane $S_L \approx 0.46$ m/s
  - ☐ Hydrogen $S_L \approx 2.8$ m/s
  - ☐ Typical $Da_{cr} \approx 1$

- **Examiner's Pet Topics:**
  - ☐ "Discuss the role of edge flames in stabilization" — **guaranteed 7-mark question**
  - ☐ "Derive the liftoff height correlation" — **favourite derivation**
  - ☐ "Explain why blowoff occurs" — **Part A favourite**
  - ☐ "Compare liftoff vs. blowoff" — **comparison table questions**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbulent Mass Diffusivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turbulent Mass Diffusivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Turbulent Mass Diffusivity** (also known as the **eddy mass diffusivity**, denoted $\varepsilon_M$ or $D_t$) is the rate at which mass (specifically, chemical species, fuel vapours, smoke particulates, and combustion products) is transported through a fluid medium by the macroscopic, chaotic motions of turbulent eddies, as opposed to transport by purely molecular agitation.

In the context of fire dynamics, turbulent mass diffusivity governs how rapidly fresh oxidiser (oxygen) reaches the fuel-rich pyrolysis zone, how quickly fuel vapours mix with air to form a flammable mixture, and how smoke and toxic gases (e.g., CO, HCN) spread through a compartment. Mathematically, it is defined via the gradient-transport hypothesis of turbulent flow:

$$J_t = -\varepsilon_M \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x}$$

where $J_t$ is the **turbulent mass flux** $(\text{kg/m}^2\text{s})$, $\bar{C}$ is the time-averaged species concentration $(\text{kg/m}^3)$, and $\varepsilon_M$ is the **turbulent (eddy) mass diffusivity** $(\text{m}^2/\text{s})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In fire science, $\varepsilon_M$ is **typically 10³ to 10⁵ times larger** than the molecular mass diffusivity $D_m$ (e.g., $D_m$ of oxygen in air $\approx 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ at STP). This dramatic enhancement of transport rates is the principal reason why turbulent fires burn orders of magnitude faster than laminar fires.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a crowded marketplace. **Molecular diffusion** is like a single shopper squeezing slowly between two stationary groups of people to reach a fruit stall on the other side — slow, deliberate, and constrained by collisions.

**Turbulent mass diffusivity**, in contrast, is like suddenly opening the gates of a stadium and letting thousands of people surge across the marketplace in random, swirling eddies. A shopper on one side of the market is now physically carried to the other side in seconds by the bulk motion of the crowd, not by their own footsteps.

In a fire, the "crowd" is the turbulent eddies of hot combustion gases rising buoyantly (plume). A fuel molecule evaporating from a burning solid surface is **swept away almost instantaneously** by these eddies into the flaming region — far faster than diffusion alone could ever achieve.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Physical Constants (Fire-Relevant):**
> - $D_{m,\text{O}_2\text{-air}} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (molecular, 20°C)
> - $D_{m,\text{CO}_2\text{-air}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Typical $\varepsilon_M$ in a fire plume (near flame): $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{0} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Typical $\varepsilon_M$ in a well-ventilated compartment flashover: $\sim 1$ to $10 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

## 1.3 Why Turbulent Mass Diffusivity is Central to Fire Propagation

Three reasons make this topic a cornerstone of fire engineering:

1. **Fuel–Air Mixing:** Combustion requires intimate molecular contact between fuel vapour and oxygen. Turbulent eddies (characterised by $\varepsilon_M$) bulk-transport these species together; molecular diffusion then finishes the job at the smallest scales.
2. **Smoke & Toxic Gas Transport:** Visibility loss and CO transport in corridors are dominated by $\varepsilon_M$, not $D_m$.
3. **Burning Rate Enhancement:** The mass burning rate $\dot{m}''$ in turbulent fires scales directly with the convective transport, which in turn depends on $\varepsilon_M$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Hierarchy: From Laminar to Turbulent Mass Transport

When a flow becomes turbulent, the instantaneous scalar field $C(x, t)$ is decomposed using **Reynolds decomposition** into a mean and a fluctuating part:

$$C(x, t) = \bar{C}(x) + C'(x, t), \quad \text{where} \quad \overline{C'} = 0$$

When the convective mass flux $\rho u C$ is time-averaged, an extra term appears — the **Reynolds flux** or **turbulent flux**:

$$\overline{\rho u C} = \bar{\rho}\bar{u}\bar{C} + \overline{\rho u' C'}$$

The second term $\overline{u' C'}$ is the covariance of velocity and concentration fluctuations, representing transport by eddies. By the **gradient-diffusion hypothesis (Boussinesq approximation)**, this is modelled as:

$$-\overline{u' C'} = \varepsilon_M \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The closure assumption $ \overline{u' C'} = -\varepsilon_M \, \partial \bar{C}/\partial x $ is the cornerstone of all first-order turbulent transport models (e.g., the Eddy Break-Up combustion model in CFD). **KTU expects you to know the assumptions AND the limitations** of this closure.

## 2.2 Total Mass Flux in a Turbulent Flow

The total mean mass flux in the $x$-direction is the sum of molecular and turbulent contributions:

$$J_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{-D_m \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x}}_{\text{Molecular (laminar)}} \;\; + \;\; \underbrace{-\varepsilon_M \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x}}_{\text{Turbulent (eddy)}}$$

$$J_{\text{total}} = -(D_m + \varepsilon_M)\frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x} = -D_{\text{eff}} \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x}$$

where $D_{\text{eff}} = D_m + \varepsilon_M$ is the **effective diffusivity**.

## 2.3 The Turbulent Schmidt Number

A dimensionless parameter compares turbulent momentum to turbulent mass transport:

$$Sc_t = \frac{\nu_t}{\varepsilon_M} = \frac{\varepsilon_M^{(\text{momentum})}}{\varepsilon_M^{(\text{mass})}}$$

For fire applications, $Sc_t \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$ in free shear flows, and $\approx 1.0$ in the logarithmic region of wall-bounded flows. The simple unity assumption $Sc_t = 1$ leads to the **Reynolds Analogy** (see §2.5).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often confuse the *molecular* Schmidt number ($Sc = \nu/D_m$, with values $\sim 0.6$–$2.4$ for gases) with the *turbulent* Schmidt number $Sc_t$. They are different physical concepts!

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Equation** | **Typical Units (SI)** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular mass flux | $J_m$ | $-D_m \, \partial \bar{C}/\partial x$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ | Transport by random molecular motion |
| Turbulent mass flux | $J_t$ | $-\varepsilon_M \, \partial \bar{C}/\partial x$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ | Transport by turbulent eddies |
| Total mass flux | $J_{\text{total}}$ | $-(D_m + \varepsilon_M)\partial\bar{C}/\partial x$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\texttext{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ | Net mean transport |
| Effective diffusivity | $D_{\text{eff}}$ | $D_m + \varepsilon_M$ | $\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | Combined transport coefficient |
| Turbulent Schmidt No. | $Sc_t$ | $\nu_t / \varepsilon_M$ | dimensionless | Ratio of eddy momentum to eddy mass diffusivity |
| Reynolds analogy | $St = Sc_t = Pr_t$ | $C_f/2$ | dimensionless | Connects heat, mass, momentum transfer |
| Eddy diffusivity (mixing length) | $\varepsilon_M$ | $\ell_m^{2} \, \vert \partial \bar{u}/\partial y \vert$ | $\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | Prandtl's mixing-length model |
| Eddy diffusivity (free jet) | $\varepsilon_M$ | $0.4 \cdot u_m \cdot b$ | $\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | Abramovich-type approximation |
| Fire-plume mass flux | $\dot{m}_{\text{plume}}$ | $0.21 \rho_\infty (\dot{Q}/c_p T_\infty)^{1/3} z^{5/3}$ | $\text{kg/s}$ | Heskestad/Zukoski plume correlation |
| Damköhler Number (fire) | $Da$ | $\tau_{\text{flow}}/\tau_{\text{chem}}$ | dimensionless | Compares mixing to chemistry timescales |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars denoting absolute value or magnitude are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. LaTeX vertical pipe characters inside a markdown table cell would otherwise break the row delimiter logic.

## 2.5 The Reynolds Analogy (and Chilton–Colburn Extensions)

For high-Reynolds-number turbulent flows with $Pr_t = Sc_t = 1$:

$$\frac{C_f}{2} = St = \frac{h}{\rho u c_p} = \frac{k_c}{\rho u} = \frac{\varepsilon_M}{u \cdot \ell}$$
