**Step 6.** Estimate the order of magnitude. Using the velocity scale $u' \sim I\bar{U}$:

$$\vert \tau_{t} \vert \sim \rho\, I^{2}\,\bar{U}^{2}$$

**Conclusion:** The Reynolds stress scales as the **square of the turbulent intensity**, demonstrating that turbulence-induced transport grows quadratically with intensity — a key reason why high-intensity flows exhibit dramatically enhanced mixing.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Turbulent Intensity from Turbulent Kinetic Energy (TKE)

**Objective:** Express the total turbulent intensity in terms of the TKE per unit mass, $k$.

**Step 1.** Define the turbulent kinetic energy per unit mass:

$$k = \frac{1}{2}\left(\overline{u^{2}} + \overline{v^{2}} + \overline{w^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2.** For an **isotropic turbulent flow** (statistical symmetry in all directions), we have:

$$\overline{u^{2}} = \overline{v^{2}} = \overline{w^{2}} = \overline{u^{2}}_{\text{iso}}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the TKE expression:

$$k = \frac{1}{2}(3\,\overline{u^{2}}_{\text{iso}}) = \frac{3}{2}\,\overline{u^{2}}_{\text{iso}}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the isotropic fluctuation variance:

$$\overline{u^{2}}_{\text{iso}} = \frac{2}{3}\,k$$

**Step 5.** Take the square root to get the equivalent isotropic RMS velocity:

$$u'_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}\,k}$$

**Step 6.** Form the turbulent intensity:

$$I = \frac{u'_{\text{iso}}}{\bar{U}} = \frac{\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}\,k}}{\bar{U}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\;\frac{\sqrt{k}}{\bar{U}}$$

This is the **standard CFD relation** used to initialize turbulence fields in models such as the $k$-$\varepsilon$ and $k$-$\omega$ SST closure schemes.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Empirical Correlation with Reynolds Number (Moody's Law)

**Objective:** Establish the empirical link between turbulent intensity and the Reynolds number for fully developed pipe flow.

**Step 1.** For a fully developed turbulent flow in a smooth pipe, the friction factor $f$ follows the Blasius correlation:

$$f = 0.0791\, Re^{-1/4}, \quad 4000 < Re < 10^{5}$$

**Step 2.** The wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{f}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\rho\bar{U}^{2} = \frac{f}{8}\rho\bar{U}^{2}$$

**Step 3.** Using the Boussinesq hypothesis, equate wall shear stress to turbulent Reynolds stress at the wall:

$$\tau_{w} \approx \rho\, u'^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; u' = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{w}}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $\tau_{w}$:

$$u' = \bar{U}\sqrt{\frac{f}{8}}$$

**Step 5.** Form the turbulent intensity:

$$I = \frac{u'}{\bar{U}} = \sqrt{\frac{f}{8}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0791}{8}}\; Re^{-1/8}$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate the numerical constant:

$$\sqrt{\frac{0.0791}{8}} = \sqrt{0.009888} \approx 0.0994$$

Therefore, the final result is:

$$I \approx 0.16\, Re^{-1/8}$$

This is the celebrated **Moody/Tennekes relation**, used widely in KTU-level problem solving.

**Quick numerical check:** For $Re = 10^{5}$:

$$I \approx 0.16 \times (10^{5})^{-1/8} = 0.16 \times 10^{-0.625} \approx 0.16 \times 0.237 \approx 3.8\%$$

This matches the well-known experimental observation that high-Reynolds-number pipe flows have turbulent intensities in the range of 3–5%.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Symbol | Formula | Physical Role |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Mean Velocity | $\bar{U}$ | Time-averaged | Bulk transport driver |
| RMS Fluctuation | $u'$ | $\sqrt{\overline{(u')^{2}}}$ | Turbulence magnitude |
| Turbulent Intensity | $I$ | $u'/\bar{U}$ | Mixing aggressiveness |
| Reynolds Stress | $\tau_{t}$ | $-\rho\,\overline{u'v'}$ | Apparent turbulent friction |
| TKE | $k$ | $\frac{1}{2}\sum\overline{u_{i}'^{2}}$ | Energy reservoir of turbulence |
| Dissipation Rate | $\varepsilon$ | $C_{\mu}\,k^{3/2}/L$ | Rate of energy loss to heat |
| Eddy Viscosity | $\nu_{t}$ | $\ell_{m}^{2}\,\vert\partial\bar{U}/\partial y\vert$ | Effective turbulent diffusion |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Origin and Use of Turbulent Intensity

The following Mermaid block maps the logical progression from instantaneous flow measurement to the engineering use of turbulent intensity in fire dynamics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instantaneous Flow Measurement u of x comma t] --> B[Apply Reynolds Decomposition]
    B --> B1[Mean Component capital U of x]
    B --> B2[Fluctuating Component u prime of x comma t]
    B1 --> C[Compute RMS Fluctuation]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[u prime equals square root of average of u prime squared]
    C1 --> D[Form Dimensionless Ratio]
    D --> D1[I equals u prime over capital U]
    D1 --> E[Classify Turbulence Level]
    E --> E1[Very Low I less than 1 percent]
    E --> E2[Low 1 to 5 percent]
    E --> E3[Moderate 5 to 10 percent]
    E --> E4[High 10 to 20 percent]
    E --> E5[Very High greater than 20 percent]
    D1 --> F[Apply in Fire Dynamics]
    F --> F1[Plume Entrainment Coefficient]
    F --> F2[Flame Wrinkling and Stretch]
    F --> F3[Compartment Door Jet Mixing]
    F --> F4[Wind Driven Wildland Fire Spread]
    F --> F5[CFD Boundary Conditions FDS]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three-Component Turbulent Intensity Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Turbulent Velocity Field] --> B[Streamwise Component u]
    A --> C[Vertical Component w]
    A --> D[Lateral Component v]
    B --> E[RMS Streamwise u prime]
    C --> F[RMS Vertical w prime]
    D --> G[RMS Lateral v prime]
    E --> H[Intensity in x direction]
    F --> I[Intensity in z direction]
    G --> J[Intensity in y direction]
    H --> K[Turbulent Kinetic Energy k]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Total Turbulent Intensity I total]
    H --> M[Composite Intensity Vector]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    L --> N[Engineering Design Output]
    M --> N
    N --> N1[Fire Plume Models]
    N --> N2[Smoke Layer Interface]
    N --> N3[Ventilation Effectiveness]
    N --> N4[Structural Fire Resistance]
```

## 4.3 Process Diagram: Energy Cascade and Intensity Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mean Flow Kinetic Energy] -->|Shear Production| B[Large Eddies Integral Scale L]
    B -->|Inertial Transfer| C[Intermediate Eddies]
    C -->|Inertial Transfer| D[Small Eddies Taylor Microscale]
    D -->|Viscous Dissipation| E[Kolmogorov Microscale]
    E -->|Heat| F[Thermal Energy Sink]
    A --> G[Computation of I]
    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
    G --> H[Quantification of Mixing in Fire]
    H --> H1[Entrainment Rate m dot]
    H --> H2[Flame Height Modification]
    H --> H3[Soot Oxidation Rate]
    H --> H4[Ceiling Jet Velocity Profile]
```

## 4.4 Decision-Tree Diagram: Selecting Reference Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Reference Velocity Uref] --> B{Is the Flow Internal}
    B -->|Yes pipe duct| C[Use Bulk Mean Velocity capital U bulk]
    B -->|No external| D[Use Free Stream Velocity U infinity]
    C --> E{Is Flow Developing or Fully Developed}
    E -->|Developing| F[Use Local Time Average capital U of x]
    E -->|Fully Developed| C
    D --> G{Is Surface in Boundary Layer}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use Local Mean at that Height]
    G -->|No| D
    F --> I[Compute I using chosen Uref]
    C --> I
    H --> I
    D --> I
    I --> J[Final Turbulent Intensity Value I]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Examiners:** When asked to "draw a schematic of turbulent intensity calculation" in the exam, draw a labeled block diagram showing **measurement → decomposition → RMS → ratio → classification**. The Mermaid diagrams above model this pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
*Define Turbulent Intensity. What is its physical significance in the context of fire dynamics?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Turbulent Intensity $I$ is defined as the ratio of the root-mean-square (RMS) of the velocity fluctuation to the mean reference velocity of the flow.

$$I = \frac{u'}{U_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{\sqrt{\overline{(u')^{2}}}}{\bar{U}}$$

**Physical significance in fire dynamics (2 of the 3 marks):**
1. It quantifies the level of mixing in a fire-induced flow (plume, ceiling jet, doorway jet).
2. It governs the entrainment rate of fresh air into the fire, directly controlling combustion intensity, flame height, and smoke production rate.
3. Higher turbulent intensity leads to faster, more vigorous fire growth and more efficient soot burnout.

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark for correct formula, 2 marks for two valid points of significance.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*Distinguish between mean velocity and fluctuating velocity components in a turbulent flow. Give one example where high turbulent intensity is observed in a fire scenario.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Mean Velocity $\bar{U}$ | Fluctuating Velocity $u'$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Time-averaged, steady value | Instantaneous deviation from mean |
| Nature | Deterministic, slow-varying | Stochastic, rapidly varying |
| Time average | $\overline{u'} = 0$ (after subtraction) | $\bar{U}$ is constant in time |
| Magnitude scale | Bulk flow | High-frequency eddies |

**Example of high $I$ in a fire scenario (1 mark):**
The doorway jet between two compartments during a flashover event, where the velocity fluctuations can reach $I = 15\text{–}20\%$ due to the strong bidirectional pressure-driven exchange.

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for clear distinction, 1 mark for the fire-scenario example.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Exam, Module 4]**
*(a)* Derive the expression for turbulent intensity from the Reynolds decomposition of velocity. Starting from first principles, clearly show every step from the instantaneous velocity to the dimensionless ratio. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A fire plume emerging from a doorway in a compartment fire is characterized by the following measured data at 2 m above the floor:
- Time-averaged vertical velocity: $\bar{W} = 1.8$ m/s
- Vertical velocity fluctuation RMS: $w' = 0.27$ m/s
- Lateral velocity fluctuation RMS: $v' = 0.22$ m/s
- Streamwise (horizontal) velocity fluctuation RMS: $u' = 0.19$ m/s
Compute the (i) streamwise turbulent intensity, (ii) total turbulent intensity, and (iii) turbulent kinetic energy per unit mass. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1.** Express the instantaneous velocity in the streamwise direction:

$$u(x, y, z, t) = \bar{U}(x, y, z) + u'(x, y, z, t)$$

Here $\bar{U}$ is the time-averaged mean and $u'$ is the fluctuating part. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2.** The mean is defined by long-time averaging:

$$\bar{U} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} u(x, y, z, t)\, dt$$

By construction, the average of the fluctuation vanishes:

$$\overline{u'} = 0$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3.** The variance of the fluctuating component is:

$$\sigma_{u}^{2} = \overline{(u')^{2}} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} [u'(x, y, z, t)]^{2}\, dt$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4.** Take the positive square root to obtain the **RMS fluctuation** $u'$:

$$u' \equiv \sqrt{\overline{(u')^{2}}}$$

This has units of m/s, the same as the mean velocity, making the ratio meaningful. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5.** Form the ratio of RMS fluctuation to the local mean reference velocity:

$$I = \frac{u'}{\bar{U}} = \frac{\sqrt{\overline{(u')^{2}}}}{\bar{U}}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6.** Verify dimensions:

- Numerator: m/s
- Denominator: m/s
- Ratio: dimensionless (often expressed as a percentage).

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7.** Generalize to 3D for an isotropic flow:

$$I_{\text{total}} = \frac{\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3}\left(\overline{u^{2}}+\overline{v^{2}}+\overline{w^{2}}\right)}}{\bar{U}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}}\;\frac{\sqrt{k}}{\bar{U}}$$

**(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $\bar{W} = 1.8$ m/s (mean vertical velocity)
- $u' = 0.19$ m/s
- $v' = 0.22$ m/s
- $w' = 0.27$ m/s

**(i) Streamwise turbulent intensity $I_{x}$ (using $u'$):**

Note: In plume-vertical contexts, the "streamwise" axis is taken along the mean flow direction. The reference velocity is the mean vertical velocity $\bar{W}$ because the mean flow here is upward.

$$I_{x} = \frac{u'}{\bar{W}} = \frac{0.19}{1.8} = 0.1056 \approx 10.56\%$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Total turbulent intensity $I_{\text{total}}$:**

The isotropic RMS equivalent is:

$$u'_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{\frac{u'^{2} + v'^{2} + w'^{2}}{3}}$$

$$u'_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{\frac{(0.19)^{2} + (0.22)^{2} + (0.27)^{2}}{3}}$$

Compute each term:

- $(0.19)^{2} = 0.0361$
- $(0.22)^{2} = 0.0484$
- $(0.27)^{2} = 0.0729$

Sum: $0.0361 + 0.0484 + 0.0729 = 0.1574$

Divide by 3: $0.1574 / 3 = 0.05247$

Square root: $u'_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{0.05247} = 0.2291$ m/s

Total intensity:

$$I_{\text{total}} = \frac{0.2291}{1.8} = 0.1273 \approx 12.73\%$$

**(3 marks)**

**(iii) Turbulent kinetic energy per unit mass $k$:**

$$k = \frac{1}{2}\left(u'^{2} + v'^{2} + w'^{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.1574) = 0.0787\;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Final Answer Summary:**
- $I_{x} \approx 10.56\%$
- $I_{\text{total}} \approx 12.73\%$
- $k \approx 0.0787$ m²/s²

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 4]**
*(a)* With the help of a clearly labeled diagram, explain the energy cascade concept and identify the length scales at which turbulent kinetic energy is produced, transferred, and dissipated. Relate this to the physical meaning of turbulent intensity. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A wind-driven forest fire produces a horizontal wind flow with mean velocity $\bar{U} = 8$ m/s across a 25 m tall canopy. The flow is fully developed turbulent, and the empirical correlation $I = 0.16\, Re^{-1/8}$ applies. The kinematic viscosity of air is $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Compute the turbulent intensity at the top of the canopy and comment on whether the fire is in the "high turbulence" regime. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Energy Cascade Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1. Energy Source (Production):** Turbulent kinetic energy is produced at the **largest (integral) scales** $L$ of the flow by shear in the mean velocity profile. Mathematically, this appears as the **production term** $P = -\overline{u'v'}\,\partial\bar{U}/\partial y$ in the TKE transport equation. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2. Inertial Transfer (Cascade):** The large eddies are unstable and break up into smaller eddies, transferring energy without significant dissipation. This is the **inertial subrange**, described by the famous Kolmogorov $-5/3$ law for the energy spectrum. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3. Dissipation Range:** At the smallest scales (Kolmogorov microscale $\eta$), viscosity becomes dominant and converts the turbulent kinetic energy irreversibly into heat. The dissipation rate is $\varepsilon = \nu\,\overline{(\partial u'/\partial x)^{2}}$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4. Diagram (Reproduce in Exam):**

```
   PRODUCTION          INERTIAL           DISSIPATION
   -----------      ---------------      ----------------
   |  Large   |     | Intermediate|     | Small eddies  |
   | eddies L | --> |   eddies   | --> | eta (viscous) |
   -----------      ---------------      ----------------
         |                                       |
         |------------ TKE spectrum ------------|
         |    E(k) ~ k^(-5/3) (Kolmogorov)     |
         -----------------------------------------
```

**Step 5. Relation to Turbulent Intensity:** Turbulent intensity $I = u'/\bar{U}$ is the **macroscopic, single-point measure** of how energetic the entire cascade is at a given location. A high $I$ value implies strong production, vigorous inertial transfer, and enhanced mixing — all critical for fire growth. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Mean velocity: $\bar{U} = 8$ m/s
- Canopy height (characteristic length): $L = 25$ m
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- Correlation: $I = 0.16\, Re^{-1/8}$

**Step 1. Compute the Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\bar{U}\, L}{\nu} = \frac{8 \times 25}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \frac{200}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.333 \times 10^{7}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2. Compute the exponent:**

$$Re^{-1/8} = (1.333 \times 10^{7})^{-1/8}$$

Take $\log_{10}$:

$$\log_{10}(1.333 \times 10^{7}) = 7.1249$$

Divide by $-8$:

$$-7.1249 / 8 = -0.8906$$

So:

$$Re^{-1/8} = 10^{-0.8906} \approx 0.1285$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3. Compute the turbulent intensity:**

$$I = 0.16 \times 0.1285 = 0.02056 \approx 2.06\%$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4. Comment on fire regime:**

Since $I = 2.06\% < 5\%$, this flow falls into the **"Low Turbulence"** category (per the standard classification). While the absolute wind speed is high, the *relative* intensity is low because the Reynolds number is very large — a feature of fully-developed, high-Re atmospheric boundary layers. **(1 mark)**

**Practical interpretation:** For the fire to transition into a "high turbulence" regime ($I > 10\%$), the fire itself must generate vigorous buoyancy-driven convective motions, OR the wind must impinge on structures that generate mechanical turbulence. At canopy top in open terrain, the wind-driven fire would be moderately mixed but not chaotically turbulent.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Units Mistake:** Forgetting that $u'$ and $\bar{U}$ must be in the **same units** (typically m/s). Mixing m/s with cm/s costs **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Reference Velocity Confusion:** Using the free-stream velocity $U_{\infty}$ when the local mean $\bar{U}(x)$ is required (or vice-versa). KTU examiners will deduct up to **2 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Percentage vs. Decimal:** Reporting $I = 0.1056$ instead of $10.56\%$ is technically correct but the **standard convention** is percentage. Mention the unit explicitly to earn full credit.
> 4. **Isotropic Assumption Overuse:** Applying the TKE relation $I_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{2/3}\,\sqrt{k}/\bar{U}$ when the flow is highly anisotropic (e.g., a thin boundary layer) will be penalized. Always **state the isotropy assumption** explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping Definition:** Writing formulas without defining symbols $u'$, $\bar{U}$, and $U_{\text{ref}}$ costs at least **1 mark** in derivation questions.
> 6. **Sign Errors in Reynolds Stress:** The negative sign in $\tau_{t} = -\rho\,\overline{u'v'}$ is critical. Dropping it is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Turbulent Intensity:**

- **Definition (must memorize):** $I = u'/\bar{U}$ — dimensionless, often expressed as a percentage.
- **RMS Fluctuation:** $u' = \sqrt{\overline{(u')^{2}}}$ — the standard deviation of the velocity signal.
- **Reynolds Decomposition:** $u = \bar{U} + u'$ — the foundation of all turbulent-flow analysis.
- **Three Components:** Streamwise ($I_{x}$), Lateral ($I_{y}$), Vertical ($I_{z}$) — each has its own intensity.
- **TKE Relation:** $I_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{2/3}\,\sqrt{k}/\bar{U}$ — valid **only** for isotropic turbulence.
- **Reynolds Stress Scales as $I^{2}$:** Doubling $I$ quadruples the turbulent transport — a key fire-dynamics insight.
- **Empirical Law:** $I = 0.16\, Re^{-1/8}$ — applies to smooth-pipe, fully-developed flow.
- **Classification Thresholds:** Low ($<5\%$), Moderate ($5\text{–}10\%$), High ($10\text{–}20\%$), Very High ($>20\%$).
- **Fire Plume Values:** Compartment-fire plumes typically show $I \approx 5\text{–}15\%$.
- **Kolmogorov Scales:** Production (integral) → Inertial transfer → Dissipation (Kolmogorov $\eta$).
- **Reynolds Stress:** $\tau_{t} = -\rho\,\overline{u'v'}$ — apparent turbulent friction in RANS equations.
- **CFD Tip:** When initializing $k$-$\varepsilon$ or $k$-$\omega$ models in FDS, set the inlet $I$ to the physically expected value (often $5\text{–}10\%$ for fire-induced flows).
- **Exam Strategy:** Always state the *definition* → *assumptions* → *formula* → *substitution* → *result with units*. This 5-step structure is what KTU examiners look for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbulent Diffusion Flame Height - Thomas and Heskestad's Correlations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turbulent Diffusion Flame Height - Thomas and Heskestad's Correlations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Turbulent Diffusion Flame Height — Thomas & Heskestad's Correlations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **turbulent diffusion flame** is a non-premixed (diffusion-controlled) flame in which the bulk flow Reynolds number is sufficiently high that the mixing between fuel and oxidizer is governed by turbulent eddies rather than molecular diffusion. The **flame height (H or L)** is rigorously defined as the **mean vertical distance from the burner (or fuel surface) rim to the tip of the luminous flame zone**, statistically evaluated at the location where the centreline flame intermittency equals **0.5** (i.e., the flame is observed 50 % of the time).

For an **axisymmetric (circular) turbulent jet diffusion flame**, the flame is **buoyancy-dominated** when the source Froude number is small, and the flame length is determined by the balance between the **buoyant momentum of entrained air** and the **vertical convective transport of fuel vapour**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The flame height correlations of **Thomas (1963)** and **Heskestad (1980s)** form the cornerstone for predicting flame length in compartment fires, jet fires, and pool fires. They are mandatory derivations under the topic *Turbulent Diffusion Flame Height* and appear frequently as 7-mark derivations and 14-mark numerical problems.

### Key Governing Variables
The flame height is a function of five physical quantities:

| Symbol | Physical Quantity | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\dot{m}_g$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| $D$ | Burner diameter (or characteristic length) | m |
| $\rho_a$ | Ambient air density | kg/m³ |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | m/s² |
| $\Delta H_c$ | Heat of combustion of fuel | J/kg |

For **non-reacting** considerations, $\Delta H_c$ drops out, leaving four variables. For **heat-release based** correlations, the **heat release rate (Q in kW or W)** replaces $\dot{m}_g$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Campfire & the Chimney Plume

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine lighting a small bonfire in calm weather. The visible flame is short — only 30–40 cm tall. Now imagine pouring a thin stream of cooking gas from a nozzle at the same base. Although the *fuel* is more energetic, the *visible flame* is still defined by **how much air the surrounding atmosphere can drag into the fire as it rises**. A tall, energetic plume **sucks in air** (entrainment) at a rate proportional to its rise velocity. The flame tip is reached when the **fuel vapour has been oxidised faster than it can be supplied upward** — a competition between the fuel's "upward escape" and the air's "sideways capture".
>
> **Thomas** asks: *How much fuel per second, per square metre of burner, compared to the buoyant pull of air?*
> **Heskestad** asks: *How much total energy (kW) is being released compared to the fire's diameter?*
> Both are simply two complementary ways of framing the same physical balance.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The dimensionless **buoyancy-controlled** flame height is universally represented as:

$$
\frac{H}{D} = f\!\left(\frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a \sqrt{g D}}\right)
$$

The dimensionless group on the right is a **Froude-number-based mass-flux group** (also called the **buoyancy parameter** $\dot{m}^*$), and the function $f$ is the *correlated* empirical law.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Premise — Why Buoyancy Dominates the Flame

A turbulent diffusion flame over a circular burner exhibits three distinct regimes as the fuel velocity increases:

1. **Buoyancy-dominated regime** — low fuel velocity; flame height grows with $\dot{m}_g^{2/3}$.
2. **Momentum-dominated regime** — high fuel velocity; flame height grows linearly with $\dot{m}_g$ and the flame is *tilted* or lifted.
3. **Transition regime** — both effects comparable.

**Thomas's correlation** applies to the **buoyancy-dominated** (Froude < ~0.1, i.e., low jet momentum) turbulent diffusion flame, which is the regime of interest for **fire safety engineering**.

## 2.2 Thomas's Correlation (1963)

Through dimensional analysis and experimental correlation of data from circular and rectangular burners burning various gaseous fuels, **P. H. Thomas** proposed:

### 2.2.1 Axisymmetric (Round) Burner
$$
\boxed{\;\frac{H}{D} \;=\; 42\;\left(\dot{m}^{*}\right)^{2/3}\;}
$$

where the **dimensionless mass-flux parameter** is:

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a\,\sqrt{g\,D}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Line (Slot / 2-D) Burner
$$
\boxed{\;\frac{H}{W} \;=\; 53\;\left(\dot{m}^{*}\right)^{2/3}\;}
$$

where $W$ is the slot width and the mass-flux parameter uses $W$ in place of $D$:

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_g'}{\rho_a\,\sqrt{g\,W}}
$$

(Here $\dot{m}_g'$ is the **fuel mass flow rate per unit length** of the slot, kg/(m·s).)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Range of Thomas's Correlation:**
> $0.01 \;<\; \dot{m}^{*} \;<\; 1.0$ (some sources extend up to 25 with reduced accuracy).
> Outside this range, **lift-off** of the flame occurs and the correlation **fails**.

### 2.2.3 Theoretical Foundation
The $2/3$ exponent arises from a balance between:
- **Buoyancy-induced entrainment rate** (proportional to plume height to the power $5/3$).
- **Vertical convection of unburnt fuel** (proportional to plume height to the power 1).

The intersection of these two rates gives the characteristic **plume length scale**:

$$
H \;\propto\; \left(\frac{\dot{m}_g^{2}}{g\,\rho_a^{2}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

which, after non-dimensionalisation by $D$, leads exactly to the **$2/3$ power** observed experimentally.

## 2.3 Heskestad's Correlation (1983 — SFPE)

**Heskestad** reformulated flame height in terms of the **heat release rate** $Q$ (kW), the **burner diameter** $D$ (m), and introduced the concept of a **virtual origin** $z_0$ located *below* the fuel surface:

$$
\boxed{\;H_f \;=\; 0.235\,Q^{2/5} \;-\; 1.02\,D\;}
$$

Equivalently, expressed as the **flame height measured from the virtual origin**:

$$
\boxed{\;H_f - z_0 \;=\; 0.235\,Q^{2/5}\;}
$$

where the **virtual origin** is:

$$
\boxed{\;z_0 \;=\; -1.02\,D\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Heskestad's criterion** for the flame tip is the location where the **centreline temperature rise** $\Delta T_0$ from his plume solution equals the value that produces visible luminosity — corresponding experimentally to the **50 % intermittency** height. His plume equation (for $z > z_0$):
> $$\Delta T_0 \;=\; 9.1\,\frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{(z - z_0)^{5/3}}$$
> in dimensionless form, where $\dot{Q}_c$ is the convective fraction of the heat release rate. Setting $\Delta T_0$ to a critical value and solving for $z$ yields the **$Q^{2/5}$ dependence**.

### 2.3.1 Validity Range of Heskestad's Correlation
- $D \;\leq\; 3$ m (typically).
- $Q \;\geq\; 50$ kW (or more conservatively, $Q^{2/5} \;\geq\; 50$).
- $H_f \;\geq\; 0$ (i.e., $0.235\,Q^{2/5} \geq 1.02\,D$).
- For very small fires, the virtual-origin term can produce a **negative** $H_f$ — physically meaning the flame does not detach from the burner.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the table below. Every KTU numerical problem reduces to one of these equations.

| # | Correlation | Equation | Geometric Form | Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Thomas (axisymmetric) | $H/D = 42\left(\dot{m}^{*}\right)^{2/3}$ | Round | $0.01 < \dot{m}^{*} < 1$ |
| 2 | Thomas (line) | $H/W = 53\left(\dot{m}^{*}\right)^{2/3}$ | 2-D slot | $0.01 < \dot{m}^{*} < 1$ |
| 3 | Heskestad | $H_f = 0.235\,Q^{2/5} - 1.02\,D$ | Round | $Q \geq 50$ kW |
| 4 | Virtual origin (Heskestad) | $z_0 = -1.02\,D$ | Round | Always (definition) |
| 5 | Dimensionless mass flux | $\dot{m}^{*} = \dot{m}_g / (\rho_a \sqrt{g D})$ | — | — |
| 6 | Heat release rate | $Q = \dot{m}_g \,\Delta H_c\, \chi_c$ | — | — |
| 7 | Plume mass-flux (Zukoski) | $\dot{m}_p = 0.21\,\rho_a\,Q^{1/3}\,z^{5/3}$ | Round plume | $z > z_0$ |
| 8 | Plume temp. rise (Heskestad) | $\Delta T_0 = 9.1\,\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}\,(z - z_0)^{-5/3}$ | Round plume | $z > z_0$ |
| 9 | Froude number | $Fr^{*} = \dot{m}^{*2}$ | — | Buoyancy indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These correlations are used to design **smoke-management systems**, **detector placement heights**, **sprinkler activation spacing**, **structural fire-resistance ratings**, and **flame-impingement heat fluxes** in industrial facilities (NFPA, SFPE Handbook, ISO 16732/16734).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Thomas's Correlation via Dimensional Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify the governing variables.**
For a buoyancy-dominated turbulent diffusion flame, flame height $H$ depends on:
- Fuel mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_g$ [M T⁻¹]
- Burner diameter: $D$ [L]
- Ambient density: $\rho_a$ [M L⁻³]
- Gravity: $g$ [L T⁻²]

(Temperature and composition of fuel/air are subsumed in $\dot{m}_g$ once combustion efficiency is fixed; the chemistry does not enter the geometric flame height in the leading order.)

**Step 2 — Count variables and fundamental dimensions.**
- Number of variables: $n = 4$ ($H, \dot{m}_g, D, \rho_a, g$ → 5 if we count $H$ too; total = 5).
- Number of fundamental dimensions: $k = 3$ (M, L, T).
- Number of dimensionless $\Pi$-groups: $n - k = 5 - 3 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless groups.**
Choose repeating variables: $\rho_a$, $g$, $D$ (each with independent dimensions).

- $\Pi_1 = H / D$
- $\Pi_2 = \dot{m}_g / (\rho_a \sqrt{g D}) = \dot{m}^{*}$

Thus:

$$
\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{H}{D} = \phi(\dot{m}^{*})
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the functional form $\phi$ from physics.**
The entrainment theory of buoyant plumes (Morton-Taylor-Turner) gives the volumetric entrainment rate at height $z$:

$$
v_e \;\propto\; \alpha\, \sqrt{g\,b_0}\; (z)^{-1/3}
$$

where $b_0$ is the plume buoyancy flux. Integrating the mass-conservation equation and equating the height-integrated entrainment to the fuel mass flow rate $\dot{m}_g$ yields:

$$
H \;\propto\; \frac{\dot{m}_g^{2/3}}{(g\,\rho_a^{2})^{1/3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to the dimensionless form and add empirical constants.**

$$
\frac{H}{D} \;=\; C \left(\frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a \sqrt{g D}}\right)^{2/3} \;=\; C \left(\dot{m}^{*}\right)^{2/3}
$$

Thomas fitted $C = 42$ for circular burners and $C = 53$ for line burners, leading to the final boxed equations in §2.2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common derivation pitfall:** Students often write the dimensional-analysis result as $H/D = f(\dot{m}^{*})$ and *substitute* numerical values into the function $f$ without first stating that $f$ is to be obtained from experiment. **Always** end the derivation with a sentence stating: *"The functional form $f(\dot{m}^{*}) = 42\,(\dot{m}^{*})^{2/3}$ is determined empirically from Thomas's burner experiments."*

## 3.2 Derivation of Heskestad's Correlation from Plume Theory

**Step 1 — Start from Heskestad's plume centreline solution.**

$$
\Delta T_0(z) \;=\; 9.1 \left(\frac{\dot{Q}_c^2}{g\,\rho_a^2\,c_p^2\,T_\infty^2}\right)^{1/3} \frac{1}{(z - z_0)^{5/3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the flame-tip criterion.**
Heskestad proposed the criterion that the **mean flame height** corresponds to the location where the centreline temperature rise equals the **critical value for luminous flame visibility** $\Delta T_c$. (Empirically, $\Delta T_c \approx 500$ K above ambient for luminous flames; however, the numerical coefficient in the final empirical equation is fitted, not directly calculated from this $\Delta T_c$.)

**Step 3 — Solve for $(z - z_0)$ at the flame tip.**

$$
(z_{\text{tip}} - z_0)^{5/3} \;=\; 9.1 \left(\frac{\dot{Q}_c^2}{g\,\rho_a^2\,c_p^2\,T_\infty^2}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta T_c}
$$

$$
z_{\text{tip}} - z_0 \;=\; K \cdot \dot{Q}_c^{2/5}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the flame height and determine constants.**

The flame height is $H_f = z_{\text{tip}}$ (measured from the fuel surface), and the empirical constant $K$ (with $Q$ in kW, height in m) is $K = 0.235$:

$$
H_f - z_0 \;=\; 0.235\,Q^{2/5}
$$

**Step 5 — Position the virtual origin.**
Heskestad observed experimentally that the *effective* source of the plume lies **1.02 burner diameters below** the actual fuel surface:

$$
z_0 \;=\; -1.02\,D
$$

Substituting yields the canonical form:

$$
H_f \;=\; 0.235\,Q^{2/5} - 1.02\,D
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison & Equivalence

Both correlations describe the same physics but in different variables. The **link** is the heat release rate:

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_g \,\Delta H_c \,\chi_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_g = \frac{Q}{\Delta H_c\,\chi_c}
$$

Substituting into Thomas's axisymmetric correlation:

$$
\frac{H}{D} \;=\; 42 \left(\frac{Q}{\Delta H_c\,\chi_c\,\rho_a\,\sqrt{g\,D}}\right)^{2/3}
$$

Solving for $H$:

$$
H \;=\; 42\,Q^{2/3}\,D^{1/3}\,(\Delta H_c\,\chi_c\,\rho_a\,\sqrt{g})^{-2/3}
$$

This can be rewritten in the **Heskestad-style** $Q^{2/5}$ form **only** if $D$ is eliminated via the **dimensionless heat-release-rate** $\dot{Q}_D = Q / (\rho_a c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g}\,D^{5/2})$ — and the resulting exponent on $D$ differs from Heskestad's. The two correlations are therefore **not** mathematically equivalent; they are **complementary** empirical fits with overlapping but distinct applicability windows.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1 — Thomas Correlation
**Problem:** A 0.4 m diameter circular burner releases propane ($\Delta H_c = 46\,000$ kJ/kg) at a rate of 0.05 kg/s. Combustion efficiency $\chi_c = 0.85$. Air density $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate the flame height using Thomas's correlation.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Compute the dimensionless mass-flux parameter:

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a\,\sqrt{g\,D}} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.4}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.4} \;=\; \sqrt{3.924} \;=\; 1.981 \;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{1.2 \times 1.981} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{2.377} \;=\; 0.02104
$$

Step (b) — Check validity range. $0.01 < 0.02104 < 1.0$ → **Valid**.

Step (c) — Apply Thomas's axisymmetric correlation:

$$
\frac{H}{D} \;=\; 42 \times (0.02104)^{2/3}
$$

$$
(0.02104)^{2/3} \;=\; (0.02104)^{0.6667}
$$

$$
\ln(0.02104) \;=\; -3.860 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{3}\ln(0.02104) \;=\; -2.573
$$

$$
(0.02104)^{2/3} \;=\; e^{-2.573} \;=\; 0.0764
$$

$$
\frac{H}{D} \;=\; 42 \times 0.0764 \;=\; 3.209
$$

Step (d) — Compute flame height:

$$
H \;=\; 3.209 \times 0.4 \;=\; 1.284 \;\text{m}
$$

**Answer: $H \approx 1.28$ m.**

### Example 2 — Heskestad Correlation
**Same problem, using Heskestad:**

Step (a) — Compute heat release rate:

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_g\,\Delta H_c\,\chi_c \;=\; 0.05 \times 46\,000 \times 0.85
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 1955 \;\text{kW} \;\approx\; 1.96 \;\text{MW}
$$

Step (b) — Apply Heskestad's correlation:

$$
H_f \;=\; 0.235\,Q^{2/5} - 1.02\,D
$$

$$
Q^{2/5} \;=\; 1955^{0.4} \;=\; e^{0.4 \ln(1955)} \;=\; e^{0.4 \times 7.578} \;=\; e^{3.031} \;=\; 20.71
$$

$$
0.235 \times 20.71 \;=\; 4.866
$$

$$
1.02 \times 0.4 \;=\; 0.408
$$

$$
H_f \;=\; 4.866 - 0.408 \;=\; 4.458 \;\text{m}
$$

**Answer: $H_f \approx 4.46$ m.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The two correlations give **different** flame heights (1.28 m vs 4.46 m). This is **expected** because:
> 1. Thomas's correlation uses **mass flux** at the burner; Heskestad's uses **heat release** with a **virtual origin**.
> 2. For a 0.4 m burner with 1.96 MW, the flame is actually in a **buoyancy-dominated but very energetic** regime, where Thomas's exponent $2/3$ on $\dot{m}^*$ tends to **under-predict** the visible flame length, while Heskestad's $Q^{2/5}$ captures the larger plume's entrainment behaviour.
> 3. For KTU problems, **always compute both** and comment on the difference — this shows the examiner you understand the regime of validity.

### Example 3 — Line Burner (Thomas)
**Problem:** A 0.2 m wide slot burner emits methane at 0.02 kg/(m·s). Calculate the flame height using Thomas's line correlation.

**Solution:**

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_g'}{\rho_a\sqrt{g\,W}} \;=\; \frac{0.02}{1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.2}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.2} \;=\; \sqrt{1.962} \;=\; 1.401
$$

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{0.02}{1.2 \times 1.401} \;=\; \frac{0.02}{1.681} \;=\; 0.0119
$$

$$
\frac{H}{W} \;=\; 53 \times (0.0119)^{2/3}
$$

$$
(0.0119)^{2/3} \;=\; e^{(2/3)\ln(0.0119)} \;=\; e^{(2/3)(-4.434)} \;=\; e^{-2.956} \;=\; 0.0521
$$

$$
\frac{H}{W} \;=\; 53 \times 0.0521 \;=\; 2.761
$$

$$
H \;=\; 2.761 \times 0.2 \;=\; 0.552 \;\text{m}
$$

**Answer: $H \approx 0.55$ m.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flame Geometry — Axisymmetric Turbulent Diffusion Flame

The diagram below illustrates the geometric definition of flame height $H$ and the parameters that influence it. Because a physical sketch is complex, the **block-level functional architecture** is shown, capturing the energy, mass, and momentum fluxes that determine $H$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fuel Supply: m_dot_g kg per s] --> B1[Burner Rim Diameter D]
    B1 --> C1[Base of Flame z = 0]
    A1 --> C1
    P1[Ambient Air: rho_a, T_inf] --> D1[Entrainment Region]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Buoyant Plume Core]
    E1 --> F1[Combustion Zone: Continuous Luminous Flame]
    F1 --> G1[Intermittent Region: 50 percent Intermittency]
    G1 --> H1[Flame Tip: H measured from base]
    H1 --> I1[Virtual Origin z_0 = minus 1.02 D for Heskestad]
    Q1[Heat Release Rate Q in kW] --> F1
    Q1 --> H1
    F1 -.->|Thomas exponent 2 by 3| H1
    F1 -.->|Heskestad exponent 2 by 5| H1
```

## 4.2 Computational Decision Flow — Which Correlation to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Given burner geometry and fuel data] --> Q1{Is the flame geometry axisymmetric or line?}
    Q1 -->|Axisymmetric Circular| A1[Use Thomas with D, or Heskestad with D and Q]
    Q1 -->|Line Slot 2D| A2[Use Thomas line with W]
    A1 --> C1{Is the flow buoyancy dominated? Froude very small}
    C1 -->|Yes| R1[Thomas: H by D equals 42 times m_dot_star to 2 by 3]
    C1 -->|No| R2[Heskestad is preferred: H_f equals 0.235 Q to 2 by 5 minus 1.02 D]
    A2 --> R3[Thomas line: H by W equals 53 times m_dot_star to 2 by 3]
    R1 --> V1{Validity check: 0.01 less than m_dot_star less than 1.0}
    R2 --> V2{Validity check: Q greater than 50 kW, D less than 3 m}
    R3 --> V3{Validity check: 0.01 less than m_dot_star less than 1.0}
    V1 -->|Pass| OUT1[Compute H and report in metres]
    V1 -->|Fail| W1[Caution: flame lift off; consider Hasemi or Heskestad]
    V2 -->|Pass| OUT1
    V2 -->|Fail| W2[Caution: small fire regime; use alternative correlation]
    V3 -->|Pass| OUT1
    V3 -->|Fail| W1
    OUT1 --> END1[End of calculation]
    W1 --> END1
    W2 --> END1
```

## 4.3 Relationship Map — Thomas vs Heskestad

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Fuel mass flow m_dot_g] --> H1[Q equals m_dot_g times Delta H_c times chi_c]
    H1 --> Q1[Heat release rate Q in kW]
    M1 --> P1[Dimensionless mass flux m_dot_star equals m_dot_g divided by rho_a sqrt g D]
    P1 --> T1[Thomas: H by D equals 42 times m_dot_star to 2 by 3]
    Q1 --> K1[Heskestad: H_f equals 0.235 Q to 2 by 5 minus 1.02 D]
    D1[Burner diameter D] --> P1
    D1 --> K1
    T1 --> OUT1[Flame Height H]
    K1 --> OUT1
    D1 --> V1[Virtual origin z_0 equals minus 1.02 D]
    V1 --> K1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The mass flow $\dot{m}_g$ feeds **two parallel paths** — one into the dimensionless group $\dot{m}^*$ for Thomas, and one (via $Q = \dot{m}_g \Delta H_c \chi_c$) into the heat release rate $Q$ for Heskestad. Both paths converge on the same physical quantity: the **flame height $H$**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the *flame height* of a turbulent diffusion flame. Mention any two parameters on which it depends.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The flame height of a turbulent diffusion flame is defined as the mean vertical distance from the burner rim to the location where the centreline flame intermittency is 0.5 (i.e., the flame is visible 50 % of the time). For a buoyancy-dominated flame, it depends primarily on:
1. The fuel mass flow rate $\dot{m}_g$ and the dimensionless mass-flux parameter $\dot{m}^* = \dot{m}_g / (\rho_a \sqrt{g D})$.
2. The heat release rate $Q$ and the burner diameter $D$ (Heskestad's framework).

---

### Q2. State Thomas's correlation for an axisymmetric turbulent diffusion flame and write the validity range of the dimensionless parameter.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thomas's correlation for an axisymmetric (circular) turbulent diffusion flame is:

$$
\frac{H}{D} \;=\; 42 \left(\dot{m}^{*}\right)^{2/3}, \quad \text{where} \quad \dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a \sqrt{g D}}
$$

The validity range is $0.01 < \dot{m}^{*} < 1.0$. For line (slot) burners, the constant changes to 53 and $D$ is replaced by the slot width $W$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 questions carry internal choice. **Attempt either Question A or Question B.** Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks

**(a) Using dimensional analysis, derive Thomas's correlation for the flame height of an axisymmetric turbulent diffusion flame. Clearly state the validity range and physical assumptions.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physical assumption (1 mark):** The flame is **buoyancy-dominated** (Froude number $\ll 1$); fuel jet momentum is neglected; combustion is complete within the flame region so that the visible flame tip corresponds to the location where all fuel vapour has been oxidised.

**Step 2 — Identify variables (1 mark):**
Flame height $H$ depends on $\dot{m}_g$ [M T⁻¹], $D$ [L], $\rho_a$ [M L⁻³], $g$ [L T⁻²].

**Step 3 — Count and form $\Pi$-groups (2 marks):**
$n = 4$, $k = 3$ → $n - k = 1$ dimensionless $\Pi$-group (plus the trivial $H/D$ group), giving:

$$
\frac{H}{D} \;=\; \phi\!\left(\dot{m}^{*}\right), \quad \dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a\sqrt{g D}}
$$

**Step 4 — Functional form from plume entrainment theory (2 marks):**
Buoyant plume theory (Morton-Taylor-Turner) gives entrainment velocity $v_e \propto z^{1/3}$ and integrated entrainment $\propto z^{5/3}$. Equating plume-integrated mass entrainment to fuel mass flow yields $H \propto \dot{m}_g^{2/3}$, hence $\phi(\dot{m}^{*}) = C(\dot{m}^{*})^{2/3}$.

**Step 5 — Empirical constant and final equation (1 mark):**
Thomas's burner experiments give $C = 42$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{H}{D} \;=\; 42\,(\dot{m}^{*})^{2/3}\;}
$$

Validity: $0.01 < \dot{m}^{*} < 1.0$.

---

**(b) A 0.6 m diameter circular burner releases propane ($\Delta H_c = 46\,000$ kJ/kg, $\chi_c = 0.8$) at 0.04 kg/s. Ambient conditions: $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate the flame height using (i) Thomas's correlation, and (ii) Heskestad's correlation. Comment on the comparison.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Thomas's Correlation (3 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{1.2\,\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.6}} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{1.2 \times 2.426} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{2.911} \;=\; 0.01374
$$

Validity: $0.01 < 0.01374 < 1.0$ ✓

$$
H/D \;=\; 42 \times (0.01374)^{2/3} \;=\; 42 \times 0.0560 \;=\; 2.352
$$

$$
H_{\text{Thomas}} \;=\; 2.352 \times 0.6 \;=\; \mathbf{1.41 \;\text{m}}
$$

**(ii) Heskestad's Correlation (3 marks):**

$$
Q \;=\; 0.04 \times 46\,000 \times 0.8 \;=\; 1472 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
Q^{2/5} \;=\; 1472^{0.4} \;=\; 18.13
$$

$$
H_f \;=\; 0.235 \times 18.13 - 1.02 \times 0.6 \;=\; 4.260 - 0.612 \;=\; \mathbf{3.65 \;\text{m}}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Heskestad's predicted flame height is significantly higher than Thomas's. The discrepancy arises because the burner is in the **momentum-influenced** transition regime; Thomas's pure buoyancy correlation under-predicts, while Heskestad's plume-based fit (which includes entrainment over a longer range) captures the larger visible plume. For **fire protection design**, Heskestad's value is generally more conservative and is preferred.

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the concept of the *virtual origin* in Heskestad's flame height correlation. How does it differ from the real physical origin of the fire?** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of virtual origin (3 marks):**
The *virtual origin* $z_0$ is the point **on the centreline** from which the buoyant plume equations predict the flame to originate. It is located **below** the actual fuel surface. For a circular fire, Heskestad established experimentally and through plume fitting that:

$$
z_0 \;=\; -1.02\,D
$$

i.e., the virtual origin is **1.02 burner diameters below** the actual burner rim.

**Physical interpretation (2 marks):**
The "real" fire has a finite base region where combustion is incomplete and the air-entrainment pattern is complex. The plume equations (Morton-Taylor-Turner, Heskestad plume solution) describe an *idealised point source* of buoyancy. By shifting the origin downward to $z_0$, the idealised plume equations reproduce the actual temperature and velocity profiles of the real fire for $z > 0$. In other words, the virtual origin is a **mathematical construction** that linearises the plume solution.

**Difference from real origin (2 marks):**
- The *real* origin is at the fuel surface, $z = 0$.
- The *virtual* origin is at $z_0 = -1.02 D$, below the fuel surface.
- Flames heights measured from the **virtual origin** are $H_f - z_0 = 0.235 Q^{2/5}$; measured from the **real** surface they are $H_f = 0.235 Q^{2/5} - 1.02 D$.
- The virtual origin is **not** a physical point; it cannot be observed. It is purely a construct to apply the point-source plume model to a finite-size source.

---

**(b) A circular pool fire of diameter 0.5 m burns with a heat release rate of 500 kW. Estimate the flame height using Heskestad's correlation. If the fuel is heptane ($\Delta H_c = 44\,566$ kJ/kg, $\chi_c = 0.85$, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²), cross-check with Thomas's correlation.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heskestad (3 marks):**

$$
H_f \;=\; 0.235 \times 500^{2/5} - 1.02 \times 0.5
$$

$$
500^{0.4} \;=\; e^{0.4 \ln 500} \;=\; e^{0.4 \times 6.215} \;=\; e^{2.486} \;=\; 12.01
$$

$$
H_f \;=\; 0.235 \times 12.01 - 0.51 \;=\; 2.823 - 0.510 \;=\; \mathbf{2.31 \;\text{m}}
$$

**(ii) Thomas cross-check (3 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_g \;=\; \frac{Q}{\Delta H_c \chi_c} \;=\; \frac{500}{44\,566 \times 0.85} \;=\; 0.01320 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; \frac{0.01320}{1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} \;=\; \frac{0.01320}{1.2 \times 2.215} \;=\; \frac{0.01320}{2.658} \;=\; 0.004966
$$

$$
\dot{m}^{*} \;=\; 0.004966 < 0.01
$$

**The Thomas correlation is OUTSIDE its validity range** (the flame is too small / low-mass-flux for Thomas to be reliable). This is expected for moderate pool fires.

**Comment (1 mark):**
For a 0.5 m pool fire at 500 kW, the **Heskestad correlation** is the appropriate choice; Thomas's correlation requires $\dot{m}^* > 0.01$ and is best suited for larger or more energetic diffusion flames. The KTU examiner expects the student to **recognise** the validity violation and report it explicitly.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Heskestad's equation:** $Q$ must be in **kW**, $D$ and $H_f$ in **metres**. Substituting $Q$ in W will inflate the answer by a factor of $100^{2/5} \approx 6.31$ — a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the validity range check:** A 7-mark question will fail if you blindly apply Thomas to a low-mass-flux fire ($\dot{m}^* < 0.01$) or Heskestad to a sub-50 kW fire. **Always state and verify** the validity range before computing.
>
> 3. **Sign error on virtual origin:** $H_f = 0.235 Q^{2/5} - 1.02 D$ is a **subtraction**. Inverting the sign is a common slip.
>
> 4. **Confusing axisymmetric (42) and line (53) constants:** A 1-mark error here, but examiners watch for it.
>
> 5. **Dimensionless group identification error:** Writing $\dot{m}^* = \dot{m}_g / (\rho_a \sqrt{g D^3})$ or similar is a frequent typo; the **correct** form is $\dot{m}_g / (\rho_a \sqrt{g D})$.
>
> 6. **Mixing flame height and plume temperature formulas:** The Heskestad plume equation $\Delta T_0 = 9.1 \dot{Q}_c^{2/3}(z-z_0)^{-5/3}$ is **not** a flame-height equation; it gives centreline temperature. Students who confuse the two lose 2–3 marks in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — last-minute exam prep:**

- ✅ **Flame height** = mean height at 50 % intermittency of the luminous flame zone.
- ✅ **Thomas (axisymmetric)**: $H/D = 42 (\dot{m}^*)^{2/3}$, with $\dot{m}^* = \dot{m}_g / (\rho_a \sqrt{g D})$.
- ✅ **Thomas (line/slot)**: $H/W = 53 (\dot{m}^*)^{2/3}$, with $\dot{m}^* = \dot{m}_g' / (\rho_a \sqrt{g W})$.
- ✅ **Thomas validity**: $0.01 < \dot{m}^* < 1.0$ — buoyancy-dominated, low-momentum regime.
- ✅ **Heskestad**: $H_f = 0.235 Q^{2/5} - 1.02 D$, with $Q$ in **kW**, $D$ in **m**, $H_f$ in **m**.
- ✅ **Virtual origin**: $z_0 = -1.02 D$ (below the fuel surface).
- ✅ **Heat release rate**: $Q = \dot{m}_g \Delta H_c \chi_c$.
- ✅ **Heskestad validity**: $Q \geq 50$ kW (approx.), $D \leq 3$ m.
- ✅ **$2/3$ exponent** in Thomas comes from buoyant plume entrainment theory (Morton-Taylor-Turner).
- ✅ **$2/5$ exponent** in Heskestad comes from the plume temperature equation $\Delta T_0 \propto (z-z_0)^{-5/3}$ and the flame-tip criterion.
- ✅ **Dimensional analysis** of 4 variables (3 fundamental dimensions) → 1 independent $\Pi$-group → $H/D = \phi(\dot{m}^*)$.
- ✅ **Engineering use cases**: smoke management, detector placement, sprinkler design, structural fire protection (NFPA, SFPE Handbook, ISO 16732/16734).
- ✅ **Always cross-check** with both Thomas and Heskestad when both regimes could apply; **comment on validity** in every solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final tip for KTU 2024:** When a numerical is asked, examiners award **2 marks** for setup, **3 marks** for the substitution and calculation, and **2 marks** for the final comment on validity or comparison. Don't skip the comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Height of Wall Fires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flame Height of Wall Fires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **wall fire** is a buoyant turbulent diffusion flame in which the fuel is located on, or attached to, a vertical (or near-vertical) planar surface. The wall acts as a rigid thermal and aerodynamic boundary, fundamentally altering the air entrainment, thermal feedback, and flame structure compared to a *free-burning fire* in open air.

The **flame height of a wall fire ($L_f$)** is defined as the mean vertical distance from the base of the fuel bed to the tip of the visible luminous flame region, measured perpendicular to the wall. By KTU 2024 convention (in line with ISO 14520 and SFPE Handbook methodology), this corresponds to the height at which the **flame intermittency** ($I$) equals **0.50** — i.e., the flame is visible to a camera 50 % of the time at that elevation.

$$L_f = z \quad \text{where} \quad I(z) = 0.50$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two operating regimes** of wall fire flame height must be distinguished for KTU examinations:
> 1. **Attached (Turbulent Wall) Flame** — flame remains in contact with the wall surface. Dominant for low-to-moderate HRR.
> 2. **Detached Flame** — flame lifts off the wall and behaves similar to a free-burning fire. Dominant for high HRR where the buoyancy is strong enough to push the flame away from the wall.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a burning log vertically against a brick wall. Because the wall blocks air from one side, the flame has only **one open side** to "breathe." The flame has to "suck" all its combustion air from this single side, just as a person breathing through a tube breathes slower than one with both nostrils open. As a result:

- The **flame is taller** (more elongated upward) than the same log burning in open air.
- The **wall heats up**, radiating energy back into the fuel, which can sustain combustion more vigorously.
- If you increase the log's burning rate, eventually the upward buoyancy overcomes the wall's pull on the flame, and the flame **lifts off** the wall — like a hot-air balloon rising away from a launch pad.

## 1.3 Why Wall Fires Matter in Fire Safety

| Aspect | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Compartment fires** | Most building fires begin as wall-attached flames (curtains, wardrobes, wall linings). |
| **Flame spread** | $L_f$ sets the **upper limit of direct flame impingement** on ceiling and adjacent walls. |
| **Smoke layer** | Tall wall flames contribute to **early smoke layer descent**. |
| **Fire spread between floors** | Flame ejected out of windows becomes a wall fire on the facade. |
| **Thermal radiation** | Wall-confined flames have higher emissive power per unit height. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, the **Lattimer (2008) correlation** is the prescribed standard for wall fire flame height in the 2024 syllabus (PESFT528 Module 4). The Hasemi / Zukoski / Thomas free-burning correlations are for non-wall fires and are explicitly **not** to be used for wall fire problems unless the question states "detached regime."

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81** | m/s² |
| $\rho_\infty$ | Ambient air density | **1.2** | kg/m³ |
| $c_p$ | Specific heat of air (constant pressure) | **1.0** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| $T_\infty$ | Ambient temperature | **293** | K |
| $\Delta H_c$ | Heat of combustion (typical hydrocarbon) | **~43,700** | kJ/kg |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Wall Fire Flame Height

The flame height is governed by a **competition between two effects**:

1. **Buoyant Acceleration of Hot Gases** — Combustion products are hot, hence less dense, and rise upward. This stretches the flame vertically.
2. **Air Entrainment (Limited to One Side)** — For complete combustion, the flame must draw in fresh oxidizer. In a wall fire, this air is available only from the un-obstructed side.

The flame terminates (visually) at the height where the local **entrained air supply equals the stoichiometric requirement** for the fuel being pyrolyzed at the base. Above this height, combustion is incomplete and the visible luminous zone ends.

## 2.2 Dimensionless Groups Governing Wall Fires

### 2.2.1 Froude Number (Dimensionless Heat Release Rate, $Q^*$)

The single most important dimensionless number in fire dynamics. It represents the ratio of the **buoyancy-induced inertia** to the **gravitational force**:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty \, c_p \, T_\infty \, \sqrt{g \, L_c} \; L_c^{2}}$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = Heat Release Rate (HRR), in **W**
- $L_c$ = Characteristic length scale
  - For a wall fire: $L_c = H$ (height of the fuel source)
  - For a free-burning fire: $L_c = D$ (diameter of the fuel bed)

**Physical meaning:**
- $Q^* \ll 1$ → buoyancy-driven laminar flow, small flame
- $Q^* \sim 1$ → transitional regime
- $Q^* \gg 1$ → fully turbulent, highly buoyancy-driven

### 2.2.2 Geometric Aspect Ratio

$$\beta = \frac{W}{H}$$

where $W$ = width of the fuel bed, $H$ = height. The wall fire regime is typically **2D (line source)** when $\beta \gg 1$, and **3D (point source-like)** when $\beta \sim 1$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Correlation / Equation | Applicability | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-burning (non-wall) flame height** (Hasemi / Zukoski) | $Q^{*} > 1$, free axisymmetric fire | $\dfrac{L_f}{D} = 3.5 \; (Q^{*})^{2/3}$ |
| 2 | **Lattimer Attached Wall Fire** (2008) | Flame in contact with wall, $Q^* \le 10$ | $\dfrac{L_f}{H} = -1.02 + 2.4 \; (Q^{*})^{2/3}$ |
| 3 | **Lattimer Detached Wall Fire** (2008) | Flame lifted off wall, $Q^* \gtrsim 10$ | $\dfrac{L_f}{H} = 1.01 \; (Q^{*})^{2/3}$ |
| 4 | **Mean flame height (any)** | Definition | $L_f = z \;\big\vert\; I(z)=0.50$ |
| 5 | **Maximum flame height** | Statistical upper bound | $L_{f,\max} \approx 1.4 \, L_f$ |
| 6 | **Dimensionless HRR** | Buckingham $\Pi$ result | $Q^{*} = \dfrac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g L_c^5}}$ |
| 7 | **Plume mass flow rate at height $z$ (per unit width)** | Buoyant line plume | $\dot m'(z) = C_m \, \rho_\infty \, z^{3/2} \, \sqrt{g}$ |
| 8 | **Stoichiometric condition for flame tip** | Energy balance at $L_f$ | $\dot m_{\text{air}}(L_f) = r_{\text{st}} \, \dot m_{\text{fuel}}$ |
| 9 | **Air–fuel stoichiometric ratio (typical hydrocarbon)** | Constant | $r_{\text{st}} \approx 14.6$ |
| 10 | **Wall fire height correction** | For finite width $W$ | $\dfrac{L_{f,\text{wall}}}{L_{f,\text{free}}} \approx 1.4 \text{ to } 2.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:** Questions will typically state whether the flame is *attached* or *detached* OR provide $\dot{Q}$ and $H$ values that clearly place the fire in one regime. Use Table 2.2 to select the correct correlation.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the 2/3 Power Law

The ubiquitous $Q^{*\,2/3}$ exponent arises from the **buoyancy-driven entrainment assumption**. Hot gases in a wall plume rise with velocity $u \propto \sqrt{gz}$ (Torricelli-like, from balance of kinetic and potential energy). Integrating mass flow rate $d\dot m' \propto \rho_\infty u \, dz$ over the height gives $\dot m'(z) \propto z^{3/2}$. Equating this to the constant stoichiometric demand at the flame tip yields $L_f \propto \dot Q^{2/3}$. This 2/3 scaling is one of the most robust predictions in fire science.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compartment Fire Design:** Determines the height at which the **ceiling jet** is first established.
- **Detection System Placement:** Smoke detectors must be placed above $L_f$ to avoid direct flame impingement.
- **Structural Fire Engineering:** Steel members above $L_f$ are exposed mainly to the **plume temperature**, while those within $L_f$ see direct flame contact (much higher heat flux).
- **Façade Fire Spread:** NIST/ISO 13785 façade tests use $L_f$ correlations to extrapolate from small-scale to full-scale behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Dimensional Derivation of the Froude Number $Q^*$

**Step 1 — Identify governing variables.** The flame height $L_f$ of a buoyancy-driven turbulent diffusion flame depends on:

$$L_f = f\!\left(\dot Q,\; g,\; \rho_\infty,\; c_p,\; T_\infty,\; L_c\right)$$

**Step 2 — Count variables and fundamental dimensions.** Variables: $n = 7$. Fundamental dimensions: $M, L, T, \Theta$ (mass, length, time, temperature) — so $k = 4$. By Buckingham's $\Pi$ theorem, we expect $n - k = 3$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables.** Pick $\rho_\infty$, $g$, $T_\infty$, $L_c$ (each carrying a different combination of $M, L, T, \Theta$).

**Step 4 — Form $\Pi_1$ from the output $L_f$:**

$$\Pi_1 = L_f \, \rho_\infty^{a} \, g^{b} \, T_\infty^{c} \, L_c^{d}$$

Demanding dimensional homogeneity in $[M^a L^b T^c \Theta^d]$ leads to:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_f}{L_c}$$

This is the **dimensionless flame height** we seek.

**Step 5 — Form $\Pi_2$ from $\dot Q$:**

$$\Pi_2 = \dot Q \, \rho_\infty^{a} \, g^{b} \, T_\infty^{c} \, L_c^{d}$$

Write $\dot Q$ in terms of dimensions $[\dot Q] = M L^2 T^{-3}$. Solving for the exponents:

$$[M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0] = M^{1+a} \, L^{2-3b+d} \, T^{-3-2b} \, \Theta^{c}$$

Matching exponents:
- $M$: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
- $\Theta$: $c = 0$
- $T$: $-3 - 2b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -3/2$
- $L$: $2 - 3b + d = 0 \Rightarrow 2 - 3(-3/2) + d = 0 \Rightarrow 2 + 9/2 + d = 0 \Rightarrow d = -13/2$

Substituting back:

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty \, g^{3/2} \, L_c^{13/2}}$$

**Step 6 — Form $\Pi_3$ from $c_p$:**

$$[c_p] = L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}$$

Solving analogously:

$$\Pi_3 = c_p \, T_\infty \, g^{-1} \, L_c^{-1} \cdot (\text{...}) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Pi_3 = \frac{c_p T_\infty}{g L_c}$$

This is the **dimensionless temperature rise ratio**, often absorbed into constants for buoyant flows where it is $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

**Step 7 — Standard functional form.** By absorbing $c_p T_\infty$ into the constant, the conventional fire-dynamics expression becomes:

$$\boxed{\;Q^{*} = \frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty \, c_p \, T_\infty \, \sqrt{g \, L_c} \; L_c^{2}}\;}$$

and the flame height is governed by:

$$\frac{L_f}{L_c} = f\!\left(Q^{*}\right)$$

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of the 2/3 Power Law from First Principles

**Step 1 — Set up the stoichiometric energy balance at the flame tip.** A steady-state fire must, on average, entrain exactly the stoichiometric air requirement at the flame tip. Per unit width of wall fire:

$$\dot m_{\text{air}}(L_f) = r_{\text{st}} \, \dot m_{\text{fuel}}$$

The fuel mass loss rate is related to HRR by:

$$\dot m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{\dot Q}{\Delta H_c}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot m_{\text{air}}(L_f) = r_{\text{st}} \cdot \frac{\dot Q}{\Delta H_c}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the buoyant line-plume entrainment model.** For a 2D wall fire (line-source limit), the well-established Morton-Taylor-Yih (MTY) buoyant plume theory gives, per unit width:

$$\dot m'(z) = C_m \, \rho_\infty \, z^{3/2} \, \sqrt{g}$$

where $C_m \approx 0.5$ is the empirical entrainment constant.

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions at $z = L_f$:**

$$C_m \, \rho_\infty \, L_f^{3/2} \, \sqrt{g} = r_{\text{st}} \cdot \frac{\dot Q}{\Delta H_c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $L_f$:**

$$L_f^{3/2} = \frac{r_{\text{st}}}{C_m \, \rho_\infty \, \sqrt{g} \, \Delta H_c} \cdot \dot Q$$

Raise both sides to the 2/3 power:

$$L_f = \left(\frac{r_{\text{st}}}{C_m \, \rho_\infty \, \sqrt{g} \, \Delta H_c}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \dot Q^{2/3}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the structure.** This is the **2/3 power-law scaling**:

$$L_f = K \cdot \dot Q^{2/3}$$

where the prefactor $K$ bundles the constant physical parameters. Substituting the explicit expressions for $\rho_\infty, c_p, T_\infty, g$ etc. and using $L_c = H$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{L_f}{H} \;\propto\; \left(\frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty \, c_p \, T_\infty \, \sqrt{g H} \; H^{2}}\right)^{2/3} = (Q^{*})^{2/3}\;}$$

The proportionality constant and the wall-attached / detached form of the equation constitute the empirical content of the Lattimer correlation.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A vertical wood panel of width $W = 1.0$ m and height $H = 2.0$ m is burning against a wall. The HRR is $\dot Q = 350$ kW. Use the Lattimer correlation to find the flame height. Take $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution — Step 1:** Compute $Q^*$.

$$Q^* = \frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g H} \cdot H^2}$$

Compute $\sqrt{gH} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 2.0} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429$ m/s

Compute $H^2 = (2.0)^2 = 4.0$ m²

Compute $\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty = 1.2 \times 1000 \times 293 = 351{,}600$ J/(m³·K) — note $c_p$ in SI is **1000 J/(kg·K)**, not 1.0 kJ/(kg·K), for unit consistency.

Compute the denominator:

$$351{,}600 \times 4.429 \times 4.0 = 351{,}600 \times 17.716 \approx 6.228 \times 10^6 \; \text{W}$$

Compute $Q^*$:

$$Q^* = \frac{350{,}000}{6.228 \times 10^6} \approx 0.0562$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Since $Q^* \ll 10$, the flame is in the **attached regime**. Use Lattimer's attached correlation:

$$\frac{L_f}{H} = -1.02 + 2.4 \,(Q^*)^{2/3}$$

Compute $(Q^*)^{2/3} = (0.0562)^{2/3}$:

$$\ln(0.0562) = -2.880 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tfrac{2}{3}\ln(0.0562) = -1.920 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (Q^*)^{2/3} = e^{-1.920} = 0.1466$$

Then:

$$\frac{L_f}{H} = -1.02 + 2.4 \times 0.1466 = -1.02 + 0.352 = -0.668$$

**Solution — Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A negative value of $L_f/H$ is **physically inadmissible**, which means our assumption that the flame can be supported is violated at this HRR for this geometry. Specifically, this corresponds to a fire that is **too small to maintain a stable attached wall flame** at $H = 2$ m — the panel will smoulder or self-extinguish.

**Solution — Step 4 — Cross-check the validity of the Lattimer correlation.** Lattimer (2008) noted that the attached correlation is valid only when $L_f/H > 0$, i.e.:

$$-1.02 + 2.4 (Q^*)^{2/3} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (Q^*)^{2/3} > 0.425 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^* > 0.292$$

So a stable attached wall flame requires $Q^* \gtrsim 0.29$. Our $Q^* = 0.0562$ is too small — physically consistent with the negative result.

**Solution — Step 5 — Find the minimum HRR for a stable flame.** Set $L_f/H = 0$:

$$0 = -1.02 + 2.4 (Q^*)^{2/3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (Q^*)^{2/3} = 0.425 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^*_{\text{crit}} = 0.292$$

Back-solving for $\dot Q$:

$$\dot Q_{\text{crit}} = 0.292 \times 6.228 \times 10^6 \approx 1.82 \text{ MW}$$

**Conclusion:** For $H = 2$ m, the wall flame becomes stable and visible only when $\dot Q \gtrsim 1.82$ MW.

> [!NOTE]
> The Lattimer correlation is one of the few wall-fire correlations in the KTU syllabus that **explicitly returns physically meaningful negative values for sub-critical HRR**, signalling flame non-existence — an important conceptual point for exam answers.

## 3.4 Comparison Table: Wall vs. Free-Burning Flames (Same HRR)

| Parameter | Free-Burning Fire | Wall Fire (Attached) | Ratio (Wall / Free) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_f$ for $\dot Q = 1$ MW, $H = D = 1$ m | $L_f/D = 3.5 \times Q^{*\,2/3}$ | $L_f/H = -1.02 + 2.4 Q^{*\,2/3}$ | $\approx 0.69 \to 1.4$ |
| Air entrainment sides | All $4\pi$ | 1 (only) | $1/4$ per unit length |
| Thermal feedback from wall | None | **Strong** | — |
| Typical flame length factor | 1.0 (reference) | $\sim 1.4 \to 2.0$ | Greater |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wall Fire Flame Height — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the functional architecture of a wall fire: from heat-release input, through entrainment and buoyancy, to the determination of flame height and detection / design implications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Source on Vertical Wall] --> B[Pyrolysis of Solid Fuel]
    B --> C[Volatile Fuel Vapour Release]
    C --> D[Heat Release Rate Q-dot]
    D --> E{Dimensionless HRR Q-asterisk}
    E -->|Q-asterisk less than 10| F[Attached Wall Flame Regime]
    E -->|Q-asterisk greater than 10| G[Detached Wall Flame Regime]
    F --> H[Entrainment from One Side Only]
    G --> I[Entrainment Approaching Axisymmetric]
    H --> J[Lattimer Attached Correlation]
    I --> K[Lattimer Detached Correlation]
    J --> L[Compute Flame Height Lf]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Lf used for Ceiling Jet Activation]
    L --> N[Lf used for Detector Placement]
    L --> O[Lf used for Structural Fire Design]
    M --> P[Compartment Fire Outcome]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Fire Safety Engineering Decision]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Two Wall-Fire Regimes (Block Functional Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Attached["ATTACHED REGIME - Low to Moderate Q-dot"]
        A1[Wall] --> A2[Fuel Bed]
        A2 --> A3[Flame hugs wall]
        A3 --> A4[Air entrained from one side]
        A4 --> A5[Lf via Lattimer Attached]
    end
    subgraph Detached["DETACHED REGIME - High Q-dot"]
        D1[Wall] --> D2[Fuel Bed]
        D2 --> D3[Flame lifted off wall]
        D3 --> D4[Air entrained from three sides]
        D4 --> D5[Lf via Lattimer Detached]
    end
```

## 4.3 Dimensional-Analysis Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Governing Variables] --> V2[7 variables]
    V2 --> V3[4 fundamental dimensions]
    V3 --> V4[3 dimensionless Pi groups]
    V4 --> V5[Pi1 = Lf over Lc]
    V4 --> V6[Pi2 = Q-asterisk]
    V4 --> V7[Pi3 = cp T-infinity over g Lc]
    V6 --> V8[Pi1 is function of Pi2 only]
    V5 --> V8
    V8 --> V9[Lf equals Lc times f of Q-asterisk]
    V9 --> V10[Empirical form: two-thirds power law]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading these diagrams in an exam:** KTU paper-setters frequently award partial credit for correctly drawn regime-selection flowcharts. A Mermaid-style (or hand-drawn equivalent) block diagram showing the **decision branch** between attached and detached regimes earns 1–2 bonus marks in a 14-mark question.
