# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the flame height of a wall fire. Why is it conventionally defined at 50 % flame intermittency?**

**Model Answer:**

The **flame height $L_f$** of a wall fire is the vertical distance from the fuel base to the elevation at which the visible flame is observed with a probability of 50 %, called the **flame intermittency** $I = 0.50$.

**Why 50 %?** Turbulent diffusion flames have a flickering, irregular tip. Different statistical thresholds yield different heights:

- **0 % intermittency** → maximum possible flame height (rare, hard to measure).
- **100 % intermittency** → flame is always present (corresponds to the *continuous* luminous base).
- **50 %** → represents the **statistically robust mean flame height** that is reproducible across experiments and is the SFPE / ISO-recommended standard for fire safety design.

> **Marks distribution:** Definition (1 M), concept of intermittency (1 M), justification of 50 % (1 M).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Lattimer correlation for an attached wall fire and explain when it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\frac{L_f}{H} = -1.02 + 2.4 \, (Q^*)^{2/3}$$

where $Q^* = \dot Q / (\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{gH} \, H^2)$.

**Applicability:**
- The flame must be in **contact with the wall** (i.e., the wall-attached regime).
- Valid for $Q^* \gtrsim 0.29$ (otherwise $L_f/H$ becomes negative, indicating no stable flame).
- Transitions to the detached correlation at $Q^* \approx 10$ where buoyancy lifts the flame off the wall.
- $H$ is the **height of the fuel bed**.

> **Marks distribution:** Formula (1 M), dimensionless group (1 M), regime condition (1 M).

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

A vertical wall of a high-rise apartment is lined with a wooden panel of height $H = 1.5$ m and width $W = 1.0$ m. The panel is on fire with an HRR of $\dot Q = 750$ kW.

**Given:** $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

#### (a) Determine the dimensionless HRR $Q^*$ and identify whether the wall flame is in the attached or detached regime. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\sqrt{gH}$:**

$$\sqrt{gH} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 1.5} = \sqrt{14.715} = 3.836 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $H^2$:**

$$H^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator of $Q^*$:**

$$\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty = 1.2 \times 1005 \times 293 = 353{,}358 \;\text{J/m}^3$$

(Note: $c_p$ must be in **J/(kg·K)** — multiply kJ by 1000.)

$$\text{Denominator} = 353{,}358 \times 3.836 \times 2.25 = 353{,}358 \times 8.631 = 3.050 \times 10^6 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Q^*$:**

$$Q^* = \frac{750{,}000}{3.050 \times 10^6} = 0.2459$$

**Step 5 — Regime identification:**

Since $Q^* = 0.246 < 10$, the flame is in the **attached regime** (Lattimer attached correlation applies). However, we must verify that $Q^* > Q^*_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.29$ for a stable flame to exist.

$$Q^* = 0.246 < 0.29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No stable attached flame exists for this configuration.}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — KTU Common Mistake:**
> Many students will directly substitute into the attached correlation without checking validity. The result will be a *negative* $L_f/H$, which the student should **interpret as a non-physical, sub-critical fire**, not as a real flame height. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for blindly reporting a negative flame height.

**Conclusion:** The given $\dot Q = 750$ kW is **insufficient** to sustain a stable attached wall flame on a 1.5 m tall panel. The minimum HRR required is:

$$\dot Q_{\text{crit}} = 0.292 \times 3.050 \times 10^6 \approx 890 \;\text{kW}$$

#### (b) If the same panel were located away from any wall (free-burning fire of equivalent circular base with $D = 1.5$ m), calculate the flame height using the Hasemi / Zukoski correlation and compare with the wall-fire result. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $Q^*$ for the free-burning case using $L_c = D = 1.5$ m:**

By coincidence, $H = D = 1.5$ m, so $Q^*_{\text{free}} = 0.2459$ (same as Part a, since the formula depends only on $L_c$ and $\dot Q$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Hasemi / Zukoski free-burning correlation:**

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = 3.5 \, (Q^*)^{2/3}$$

Compute $(Q^*)^{2/3}$:

$$(0.2459)^{2/3} = e^{(2/3)\ln(0.2459)} = e^{(2/3)(-1.4026)} = e^{-0.9351} = 0.3927$$

Then:

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = 3.5 \times 0.3927 = 1.374$$

$$L_f = 1.374 \times 1.5 = 2.062 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — For the wall case, the Lattimer attached correlation returns a *negative* value, indicating no flame. The free-burning correlation returns $L_f \approx 2.06$ m.**

**Step 4 — Comparison Table:**

| Configuration | $L_f$ (m) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Free-burning (Hasemi) | 2.06 | Flame visible above fuel bed |
| Wall fire (Lattimer attached) | **No stable flame** | Sub-critical HRR |

**Key insight for KTU:** This example demonstrates the **counterintuitive role of the wall**: it *suppresses* flame formation at very low HRR by blocking entrainment from one side, leading to oxygen starvation. As HRR increases, however, the wall flame becomes *taller* than the free-burning flame because thermal feedback from the wall sustains higher local temperatures and elongates the flame.

---

### ✅ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a) Using dimensional analysis, derive the form of the dimensionless heat release rate $Q^*$ used in wall fire flame height correlations. State clearly the repeating variables and the resulting $\Pi$ groups. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List governing variables and dimensions:**

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensions (M L T $\Theta$) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat release rate | $\dot Q$ | $M L^2 T^{-3}$ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | $L T^{-2}$ |
| Ambient air density | $\rho_\infty$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Specific heat | $c_p$ | $L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}$ |
| Ambient temperature | $T_\infty$ | $\Theta$ |
| Characteristic length | $L_c$ | $L$ |
| Flame height | $L_f$ | $L$ |

Total variables: $n = 7$, fundamental dimensions $k = 4$, hence $n - k = 3$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 2 — Choose repeating variables:** $\rho_\infty, g, T_\infty, L_c$ (one for each of the 4 fundamental dimensions).

**Step 3 — Construct $\Pi_1$ from $L_f$:**

$$\Pi_1 = L_f \cdot \rho_\infty^{a} g^{b} T_\infty^{c} L_c^{d}$$

Solving:

- $M$: $a = 0$
- $T$: $-2b - c = 0$ (from $\Theta^0$ in numerator times $\Theta^{-c}$ in $T_\infty^c$ and $T^{-2b}$ in $g^b$)
- $\Theta$: $c = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$
- $L$: $1 + d = 0 \Rightarrow d = -1$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_f}{L_c}$$

**Step 4 — Construct $\Pi_2$ from $\dot Q$:**

$$\Pi_2 = \dot Q \cdot \rho_\infty^{a} g^{b} T_\infty^{c} L_c^{d}$$

- $M$: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
- $\Theta$: $c = 0$
- $T$: $-3 - 2b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -3/2$
- $L$: $2 - 3b + d = 0 \Rightarrow 2 + 9/2 + d = 0 \Rightarrow d = -13/2$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty \, g^{3/2} \, L_c^{13/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Construct $\Pi_3$ from $c_p$:**

Solving analogously:

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{c_p T_\infty}{g L_c}$$

**Step 6 — Functional relation:**

$$\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2, \Pi_3) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{L_f}{L_c} = \phi\!\left(\frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty g^{3/2} L_c^{13/2}},\, \frac{c_p T_\infty}{g L_c}\right)$$

For buoyant fires, $\Pi_3$ is treated as $\mathcal{O}(1)$ and folded into the constant. The conventional form then becomes:

$$Q^* \equiv \frac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g L_c} \; L_c^{2}}$$

and:

$$\frac{L_f}{L_c} = \phi(Q^*)$$

> **Marks distribution:** Variable identification (1 M), Buckingham theorem statement (1 M), repeating variable choice (1 M), $\Pi_1$ derivation (1 M), $\Pi_2$ derivation (1.5 M), $\Pi_3$ derivation (0.5 M), final $Q^*$ form (1 M).

---

**(b) A wall fire of fuel height $H = 3$ m has a critical HRR above which a stable attached wall flame exists. Determine this critical HRR using the Lattimer attached correlation, given: $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 300$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Condition for a stable attached flame.** From the Lattimer attached correlation:

$$\frac{L_f}{H} = -1.02 + 2.4 (Q^*)^{2/3}$$

For the flame to exist (non-negative flame height):

$$-1.02 + 2.4 (Q^*)^{2/3} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (Q^*)^{2/3} \geq \frac{1.02}{2.4} = 0.425$$

$$Q^* \geq (0.425)^{3/2} = 0.425 \times \sqrt{0.425} = 0.425 \times 0.6519 = 0.2771$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator of $Q^*$:**

$$\sqrt{gH} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 3} = \sqrt{29.43} = 5.425 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$H^2 = 9 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty = 1.2 \times 1005 \times 300 = 361{,}800 \;\text{J/m}^3$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 361{,}800 \times 5.425 \times 9 = 361{,}800 \times 48.825 = 1.767 \times 10^7 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Critical HRR:**

$$\dot Q_{\text{crit}} = Q^*_{\text{crit}} \times \text{Denominator} = 0.2771 \times 1.767 \times 10^7$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot Q_{\text{crit}} \approx 4.90 \;\text{MW}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:** For the panel of height 3 m, the fire must release at least 4.9 MW to sustain an attached wall flame. Below this, the panel either smoulders or produces a flame that immediately detaches as a buoyant plume that does not register as an "attached wall flame" in the Lattimer sense.

> **Marks distribution:** Stability condition (1.5 M), $Q^*_{\text{crit}}$ (1.5 M), denominator computation (2 M), final $\dot Q_{\text{crit}}$ (1.5 M), interpretation (0.5 M).

---

## 📌 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls in PESFT528 Module 4 — Wall Fire Flame Height:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in $c_p$** — Most $c_p$ values are tabulated in kJ/(kg·K), but $Q^*$ demands J/(kg·K). Mixing units causes a 1000× error. **Always write $c_p$ in J/(kg·K) inside the $Q^*$ formula.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $c_p T_\infty$ factor in $Q^*$** — A surprising number of students use $\rho_\infty \sqrt{g L_c^5}$ alone (i.e., forget $c_p T_\infty$). This yields a wrong $Q^*$ and a wrong regime classification.
>
> 3. **Using Hasemi/Zukoski correlation for a wall fire** — This is an automatic 2-mark deduction. Wall fires require the **Lattimer** correlation, not the free-burning ones.
>
> 4. **Reporting negative flame heights as physical results** — A negative $L_f/H$ from the Lattimer attached equation means **the flame does not exist under those conditions**. Students must explicitly state this and discuss the critical HRR.
>
> 5. **Confusing the characteristic length** — For a wall fire, $L_c = H$ (fuel height). For a free-burning fire, $L_c = D$ (diameter). Mixing them up gives wildly different flame heights.
>
> 6. **Ignoring intermittency definition** — KTU expects the $I = 0.50$ definition for *mean* flame height and $I = 0$ for *maximum*. Always state which one you are computing.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wall fire definition** = buoyancy-driven turbulent diffusion flame on a vertical surface, with **one-side air entrainment**.
- **Two regimes:** *Attached* ($Q^* \lesssim 10$, Lattimer attached) and *Detached* ($Q^* \gtrsim 10$, Lattimer detached).
- **Lattimer Attached Correlation:** $\dfrac{L_f}{H} = -1.02 + 2.4 (Q^*)^{2/3}$ — valid for $Q^* \gtrsim 0.29$.
- **Lattimer Detached Correlation:** $\dfrac{L_f}{H} = 1.01 (Q^*)^{2/3}$ — valid for $Q^* \gtrsim 10$.
- **Hasemi / Zukoski (free-burning):** $\dfrac{L_f}{D} = 3.5 (Q^*)^{2/3}$ — **NOT for wall fires** unless detached.
- **Dimensionless HRR:** $Q^* = \dfrac{\dot Q}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g L_c} \, L_c^2}$ — Froude number form; uses $L_c = H$ for wall fires.
- **Physical origin of 2/3 law:** Morton-Taylor buoyant plume entrainment $\dot m' \propto z^{3/2}$ combined with stoichiometric balance.
- **Mean vs. Maximum:** $L_{f,\max} \approx 1.4 L_f$ for turbulent wall fires.
- **Critical HRR check:** Always verify $L_f/H \ge 0$ when using the attached correlation; report sub-critical cases explicitly.
- **Stoichiometric ratio** for typical hydrocarbons: $r_{\text{st}} \approx 14.6$.
- **Wall fires are generally *taller* than free-burning fires** at the same HRR (after the attached regime is established) due to thermal feedback from the wall.
- **Practical importance:** $L_f$ sets ceiling-jet activation height, detector placement, and structural fire design loads.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Mass Diffusivity

**Mass Diffusivity (D)** is a fundamental transport property that quantifies the rate at which a chemical species (molecules, atoms, or ions) physically migrate through a host medium under the influence of a concentration gradient. It is formally defined through **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** (1855), which states that the molar diffusive flux of a species is directly proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient, with the proportionality constant being the mass diffusivity.

In the context of fire propagation and combustion, mass diffusivity governs the transport of:
- **Fuel vapors** from pyrolyzing solid/liquid surfaces into the combustion zone
- **Oxygen** from the ambient air toward the flame sheet
- **Combustion products** (CO₂, H₂O, CO, soot precursors) away from the reaction zone
- **Toxic species** (HCN, NOₓ) through smoke layers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Mass diffusivity is the cornerstone of the **turbulent/reacting flow coupling** in fire modeling. Without accurate values of *D*, predictive tools like CFD-Fire (FDS, FireFOAM) cannot resolve the mixing-controlled chemistry that determines heat release rate.

## 1.2 Physical Units and Standard Metrics

The SI unit of mass diffusivity is:

$$D \; \left[ \text{m}^2/\text{s} \right] = \left[ \frac{\text{Length}^2}{\text{Time}} \right]$$

This is dimensionally identical to **thermal diffusivity (α)** and **kinematic viscosity (ν)** — all three are "transport coefficients" with units of area per time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Order of Magnitude (NTP, ~298 K, 1 atm):**
> - Gases in gases: **D ≈ 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (e.g., O₂ in N₂: 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s)
> - Liquids in liquids: **D ≈ 10⁻⁹ m²/s**
> - Solids in solids: **D ≈ 10⁻¹² m²/s**
> - H₂ in air (lightest gas): **D ≈ 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (fastest)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Perfume-Bottle Intuition

Imagine opening a perfume bottle in one corner of a still, enclosed room. Even without any fan or wind, after several minutes, you can smell the fragrance in the opposite corner. **Why?**

The perfume molecules, initially clustered in a high-concentration region near the bottle, undergo continuous random thermal motion (Brownian motion). Each molecule takes a "random walk," but because there are *more* molecules in the dense region than in the sparse region, the **net statistical result** is a flow of molecules from the dense region to the sparse region. The measure of *how fast* this statistical equalization occurs is precisely the **mass diffusivity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Diffusion is a *non-equilibrium, irreversible* process driven by the thermodynamic drive to maximize entropy (uniform mixing). The "speed" of this equalization is captured by *D*. A high *D* means molecules "spread out" quickly (e.g., H₂); a low *D* means sluggish mixing (e.g., heavy hydrocarbon vapors).

## 1.4 Mass Diffusivity in the Fire Triangle

In fire science, the three pillars of transport are:
1. **Mass transfer** (governed by *D*)
2. **Heat transfer** (governed by *α* = *k/ρcₚ*)
3. **Momentum transfer** (governed by *ν* = *μ/ρ*)

The interplay of these three determines whether a fire is **fuel-controlled** (mixing-limited) or **ventilation-controlled** (oxygen-limited). The dimensionless numbers **Lewis (Le)**, **Schmidt (Sc)**, and **Prandtl (Pr)** characterize this interplay.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fick's First Law — The Constitutive Equation

For a binary mixture of species *A* (diffusing) in species *B* (stagnant), Fick's First Law in vector form is:

$$\vec{J}_A = -\rho \, D_{AB} \, \nabla Y_A$$

Or equivalently in molar form:

$$\vec{J}_A^* = -c \, D_{AB} \, \nabla x_A$$

where:
- $\vec{J}_A$ = mass diffusive flux of species *A* [kg/(m²·s)]
- $\vec{J}_A^*$ = molar diffusive flux [kmol/(m²·s)]
- $\rho$ = mixture density [kg/m³]
- $c$ = total molar concentration [kmol/m³]
- $D_{AB}$ = binary mass diffusivity [m²/s]
- $Y_A$ = mass fraction of *A*; $x_A$ = mole fraction of *A*

The **negative sign** is the Second Law of Thermodynamics made algebraic: flux flows *down* the concentration gradient (from high to low concentration).

## 2.2 Fick's Second Law — The Time-Dependent Form

When combined with the **conservation of mass** for species *A*, Fick's First Law yields the transient diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho Y_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v} Y_A) = \nabla \cdot (\rho D_{AB} \nabla Y_A) + \dot{r}_A''$$

For a quiescent (no bulk flow), constant-density, source-free system, this reduces to the **pure diffusion equation**:

$$\frac{\partial Y_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \nabla^2 Y_A$$

This is the mass-transfer analog of the heat equation. Solutions yield the **penetration depth** $\delta_d \sim \sqrt{Dt}$ — the same $\sqrt{t}$ scaling that governs thermal boundary-layer growth.

## 2.3 Chapman–Enskog Theory for Binary Gaseous Diffusivity

The most widely used engineering correlation for low-density binary gas diffusivity is the **Chapman–Enskog kinetic-theory result** (derived from the Boltzmann equation):

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1.858 \times 10^{-7} \, T^{3/2} \, \sqrt{\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B}}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}$$

where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature [K]
- $M_A$, $M_B$ = molecular weights of species *A* and *B* [g/mol]
- $P$ = absolute pressure [atm]
- $\sigma_{AB} = \frac{1}{2}(\sigma_A + \sigma_B)$ = average collision diameter [Å]
- $\Omega_D$ = dimensionless collision integral, function of $\frac{k_B T}{\varepsilon_{AB}}$

**Key scaling laws** (visible directly from the formula):
- $D \propto T^{1.5}$ — diffusivity increases strongly with temperature
- $D \propto P^{-1}$ — diffusivity decreases inversely with pressure
- $D \propto M^{-0.5}$ — lighter molecules diffuse faster

## 2.4 Dimensionless Numbers in Fire Dynamics

| Number | Definition | Physical Meaning | Fire Significance |
|--------|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Schmidt** | $\text{Sc} = \nu / D$ | Viscous vs. diffusive transport | High Sc → mass boundary layer thinner than velocity layer |
| **Lewis** | $\text{Le} = \alpha / D = k / (\rho c_p D)$ | Thermal vs. mass diffusive transport | Governs flame stretch, flammability, preferential diffusion |
| **Prandtl** | $\text{Pr} = \nu / \alpha$ | Viscous vs. thermal transport | Air Pr ≈ 0.7; soot-laden flow Pr > 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For most **hydrocarbon–air systems**, Le ≈ 1.0 ± 0.1, meaning thermal and mass boundary layers grow at the same rate. **Hydrogen–air** has Le ≈ 0.3 (thermal diffuses faster than mass), causing strong **preferential diffusion** and unique flame behavior (high cellular instability, near-extinction phenomena).

## 2.5 Eddy (Turbulent) Diffusivity

In turbulent fire plumes, molecular diffusion is dwarfed by **eddy transport**. The effective diffusivity is:

$$D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{molecular}} + D_{\text{turb}}$$

with $D_{\text{turb}} \gg D_{\text{molecular}}$ by orders of magnitude. A common closure is the **gradient-diffusion hypothesis**:

$$D_{\text{turb}} = \frac{\mu_t}{\rho \, \text{Sc}_t}$$

where $\mu_t$ is the turbulent viscosity and $\text{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$ is the turbulent Schmidt number (analogous to the molecular one).

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Fire Engineering

- **Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS):** Uses $D_{AB}$ values from the Chapman–Enskog formula to compute species transport in the gas phase.
- **Smoke control design:** Dispersion of CO and HCN in atria/stairwells depends directly on species diffusivity.
- **Flame spread modeling:** Opposed-flow flame spread over solids (e.g., PMMA, wood) is sensitive to Le — sub-limiting oxygen effects arise from Le ≠ 1.
- **Detonation and explosion analysis:** Critical for predicting cellular structure of detonation waves (DDT — deflagration-to-detonation transition).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\vec{J}_A = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla Y_A$ | Fick's First Law (mass basis) |
| 2 | $\vec{J}_A^* = -c D_{AB} \nabla x_A$ | Fick's First Law (molar basis) |
| 3 | $\partial Y_A / \partial t = D_{AB} \nabla^2 Y_A$ | Fick's Second Law (quiescent) |
| 4 | $D_{AB} = \frac{1.858 \times 10^{-7} T^{3/2} \sqrt{M_A^{-1} + M_B^{-1}}}{P \sigma_{AB}^2 \Omega_D}$ | Chapman–Enskog binary diffusivity |
| 5 | $D \propto T^{1.5} / P$ | Temperature/pressure scaling |
| 6 | $\text{Le} = \alpha / D = k / (\rho c_p D)$ | Lewis number |
| 7 | $\text{Sc} = \nu / D = \mu / (\rho D)$ | Schmidt number |
| 8 | $D_{\text{eff}} = D + D_t$ | Effective (molecular + turbulent) |
| 9 | $\delta_d \sim \sqrt{Dt}$ | Diffusion penetration depth |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law + Conservation of Mass

**Starting Point:** We apply the principle of conservation of mass for species *A* to a fixed, infinitesimal control volume $dV = dx\, dy\, dz$.

**Step 1 — Net outflow of species *A* through the faces of the CV (in the x-direction):**
The mass flux in is $\left( \rho v_x Y_A - D \frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial x} \right)$ at face $x$.
The mass flux out is the same expression evaluated at $x + dx$:

$$\text{Net out, x-face} = \left[ \rho v_x Y_A - D \frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial x} \right]_{x+dx} - \left[ \rho v_x Y_A - D \frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial x} \right]_x$$

Expanding using a first-order Taylor series about $x$:

$$\text{Net out, x-face} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[ \rho v_x Y_A - D \frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial x} \right] dx$$

**Step 2 — Summing all three directions** (similar expressions for *y* and *z*), the net outflow rate per unit volume is:

$$\nabla \cdot \left[ \rho \vec{v} Y_A - D \, \nabla(\rho Y_A) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Mass balance:** Rate of accumulation = Net outflow rate + Net source:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \left[ \rho \vec{v} Y_A - D \, \nabla(\rho Y_A) \right] = \dot{r}_A''$$

Rearranging (convective and diffusive terms separated, source on RHS):

$$\frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v} Y_A) = \nabla \cdot \left[ D \, \nabla(\rho Y_A) \right] + \dot{r}_A''$$

**Step 4 — Simplification (quiescent, constant-density, source-free case):** Setting $\vec{v} = 0$, $\rho$ = const, $\dot{r}_A'' = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial Y_A}{\partial t} = D \, \nabla^2 Y_A$$

This is the canonical **heat-equation-analog** of mass diffusion, proving that transient diffusion in a stagnant medium is governed by a parabolic PDE identical in form to transient heat conduction. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Scaling of Mass Diffusivity

**Given:** Two states (1) and (2) of the same gas pair at the same pressure, with measured $D_1$ at $T_1$ and $D_2$ at $T_2$.

**From the Chapman–Enskog formula** (Section 2.3), assuming $\sigma_{AB}$ and $\Omega_D$ vary slowly with *T* (often taken as approximately constant for moderate temperature ranges in engineering practice):

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{3/2}$$

**Worked numerical example:** Diffusivity of CO₂ in air at $T_1 = 273$ K is $D_1 = 1.42 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Find $D_2$ at $T_2 = 500$ K.

$$\frac{D_2}{1.42 \times 10^{-5}} = \left( \frac{500}{273} \right)^{3/2}$$

$$\frac{500}{273} = 1.832 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1.832)^{3/2} = (1.832) \cdot \sqrt{1.832} = 1.832 \times 1.354 = 2.480$$

$$D_2 = 1.42 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.480 = 3.52 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Note:** This approximate scaling ignores the weak *T*-dependence of $\Omega_D$ (which actually causes the true exponent to be ≈ 1.65–1.75 rather than 1.5). For higher accuracy, the full Chapman–Enskog equation must be used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Lewis Number Identity

**Starting point:** By definition, mass diffusivity $D$ and thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ are two transport coefficients. Their ratio defines Le:

$$\text{Le} = \frac{\alpha}{D}$$

**Substituting the definitions** of $\alpha$ and $D$:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} \qquad \text{and} \qquad D = D_{AB}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Le} = \frac{k / (\rho c_p)}{D_{AB}} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p D_{AB}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Le compares the *rate* at which heat spreads (via conduction) to the *rate* at which mass spreads (via diffusion).
- If **Le = 1**: heat and mass diffuse at the same rate → premixed flames have classical $S_L$ behavior.
- If **Le > 1**: mass diffuses *slower* than heat → flame can preheat fuel ahead of diffusion → possible flame extinction.
- If **Le < 1**: mass diffuses *faster* than heat → fuel leaks ahead of heat → preferential diffusion effects (e.g., H₂).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Penetration Depth of a Diffusion Front

**Setup:** A semi-infinite slab of species *A* is suddenly exposed to a boundary of fixed concentration $Y_{A,0}$ at $x = 0$ (e.g., a fuel surface suddenly igniting). Find the depth of penetration after time $t$.

**Governing equation:** Fick's Second Law in 1D:

$$\frac{\partial Y_A}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 Y_A}{\partial x^2}$$

**Initial and boundary conditions:**
- $Y_A(x, 0) = 0$ for $x > 0$
- $Y_A(0, t) = Y_{A,0}$ for $t \geq 0$
- $Y_A(\infty, t) = 0$

**Self-similar solution** (using similarity variable $\eta = x / \sqrt{4Dt}$):

$$Y_A(x,t) = Y_{A,0} \, \text{erfc}\left( \frac{x}{\sqrt{4Dt}} \right)$$

**Defining the penetration depth** as the location where $Y_A / Y_{A,0} = 0.01$ (1% of surface value), we solve $\text{erfc}(\eta) = 0.01 \Rightarrow \eta \approx 1.82$:

$$\delta_d \approx 1.82 \sqrt{4 D t} = 3.64 \sqrt{Dt}$$

**Order-of-magnitude estimate:** For $D = 10^{-5}$ m²/s and $t = 1$ s:

$$\delta_d \approx 3.64 \times \sqrt{10^{-5} \times 1} = 3.64 \times 3.16 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.0115 \; \text{m} \approx 1.15 \; \text{cm}$$

**Conclusion:** In a quiescent gas, a concentration disturbance penetrates only a few centimeters per second — emphasizing why **convection** (not pure diffusion) dominates in real fire plumes.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Relationship Between Sc, Pr, and Le

By direct algebraic manipulation of the three definitions:

$$\text{Sc} = \frac{\nu}{D} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D}$$

$$\text{Pr} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k}$$

$$\text{Le} = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{\text{Sc}}{\text{Pr}}$$

**Derivation of $\text{Le} = \text{Sc} / \text{Pr}$:**

$$\frac{\text{Sc}}{\text{Pr}} = \frac{\nu / D}{\nu / \alpha} = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \text{Le}$$

This relationship is powerful because it allows the student to compute any one of the three numbers if the other two are known — a frequently tested KTU trick.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mass Diffusivity in the Fire Plume

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of mass transport in a compartment fire, from solid fuel to combustion products, highlighting where $D_{AB}$ enters the calculation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid or Liquid Fuel Source] --> B[Pyrolysis Layer at Fuel Surface]
    B --> C[Generation of Fuel Vapor at Concentration C_fuel]
    C --> D{Mode of Transport}
    D -->|Molecular regime: laminar / quiescent zones| E[Mass Diffusivity D_mol via Fick's Law]
    D -->|Turbulent regime: fire plume core| F[Eddy Diffusivity D_turb]
    E --> G[Convective + Diffusive Transport Equation]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Mixing with Oxidizer Stream at C_ox]
    H --> I{Combustion Zone}
    I -->|Fuel-rich side| J[Products: CO, Soot, HC fragments]
    I -->|Fuel-lean side| K[Products: CO2, H2O]
    J --> L[Transport of Toxic Species via D_smoke]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Smoke Layer Filling Compartment]
    M --> N[Re-entry into Inflow Vent]
    N --> O[Effective Loop Closure for CFD Solver]
    
    style A fill:#f9d5e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style O fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading guide:** The amber-highlighted **Combustion Zone (I)** is where molecular diffusivity $D_{AB}$ controls the local mixing rate of fuel vapor and oxidizer — directly setting the **local heat release rate per unit volume** and therefore the fire's radiative output.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Transport Coefficients

This second diagram clarifies the **hierarchical relationship** between the three transport coefficients and their dimensionless groupings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Thermal Diffusivity alpha = k / rho c_p] --> D[Lewis Number Le = alpha / D]
    B[Kinematic Viscosity nu = mu / rho] --> E[Schmidt Number Sc = nu / D]
    C[Mass Diffusivity D = D_AB] --> D
    C --> E
    A --> F[Prandtl Number Pr = nu / alpha]
    B --> F
    D --> G[Fire Dynamics Closure Le = Sc / Pr]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Flame Behavior: Stability, Extinction, Spreading]
    
    style A fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading guide:** Each transport coefficient ($\alpha, \nu, D$) defines one "speed" in the gas; their pairwise ratios (Le, Sc, Pr) tell us which speed dominates. The Le–Sc–Pr closure at the center is the key input to **flame stretch theory** and **premixed flame extinction models**.

---

## 4.3 Schematic Sketch — Concentration Profile in a Diffusion-Controlled Flame

```
Concentration  Y_A
   Y_A,0  |*  
          | *  
          |  *  
          |   *  ←  Concentration profile Y_A(x) = Y_A,0 · erfc( x / sqrt(4Dt) )
          |    *
          |     **
          |       ***
          |          *****                  
          |                ************________________________________
          0 ------------------------------------------------------------> x
          0         δ_d ≈ 3.64 · sqrt(Dt)                              ∞

          ↑ Flame front / reaction zone (very steep gradient here)
```

**Annotation:** The reaction zone (flame sheet) is located where the concentration gradient $\partial Y_A / \partial x$ is **maximum** — this is the locus of peak diffusive flux. The **penetration depth** $\delta_d$ sets the characteristic thickness of the unmixed-fuel layer ahead of the flame.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define mass diffusivity. State Fick's First Law of diffusion and explain the significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Mass diffusivity $D_{AB}$ is a transport property that quantifies the rate at which a chemical species *A* diffuses through a host medium *B* under the influence of a concentration gradient. SI unit: **m²/s**.

**Fick's First Law (1 mark):** The molar diffusive flux is proportional to the negative concentration gradient:

$$\vec{J}_A^* = -c \, D_{AB} \, \nabla x_A$$

**Significance of the negative sign (1 mark):** The negative sign ensures that flux flows *from high to low* concentration, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy increase drives systems toward uniformity). Without the negative sign, the equation would imply spontaneous flow from low to high concentration — a violation of the Second Law.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the Lewis number? Discuss its significance in fire dynamics, with at least one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Lewis number is the ratio of thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity:

$$\text{Le} = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p D_{AB}}$$

**Significance in fire dynamics (2 marks):** Le governs **flame stretch, preferential diffusion, and extinction behavior**:
- For most hydrocarbon–air mixtures, $\text{Le} \approx 1$, meaning heat and fuel vapor diffuse at the same rate, yielding classical flame behavior.
- For **hydrogen–air**, $\text{Le} \approx 0.3$: hydrogen diffuses much faster than heat conducts. This causes **preferential diffusion**, making hydrogen flames susceptible to cellular instability and giving H₂ unusually wide flammability limits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**Q.A (a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of diffusion starting from Fick's First Law and the principle of conservation of mass. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** The binary diffusivity of methane (CH₄) in air at 298 K and 1 atm is $1.84 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Estimate its value at 800 K at the same pressure using the $T^{1.5}$ scaling. Comment on the limitation of this approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks

**Statement of Fick's First Law (1 mark):**

$$\vec{J}_A = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla Y_A$$

**Conservation of mass for species A in a fixed CV (2 marks):** Rate of accumulation = (convective + diffusive inflow) − (convective + diffusive outflow) + source.

Mathematically:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v} Y_A) + \nabla \cdot (\rho D_{AB} \nabla Y_A) + \dot{r}_A''$$

**Taylor expansion for the x-flux (1 mark):** Showing the net outflux per unit volume is $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}[ \rho v_x Y_A - D_{AB} \partial(\rho Y_A)/\partial x ]$, similarly for y and z.

**Final 3D equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial (\rho Y_A)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v} Y_A) = \nabla \cdot \left[ \rho D_{AB} \nabla Y_A \right] + \dot{r}_A''}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):** Constant $\rho$ and $D_{AB}$, no bulk flow, no chemical source.

**Simplified form (1 mark):** $\frac{\partial Y_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \nabla^2 Y_A$ — the classical diffusion equation.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the scaling relation (1 mark):**

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{3/2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (2 marks):**

$$\frac{D_2}{1.84 \times 10^{-5}} = \left( \frac{800}{298} \right)^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{800}{298} = 2.685 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (2.685)^{3/2} = 2.685 \times \sqrt{2.685} = 2.685 \times 1.639 = 4.40$$

**Step 4 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$D_2 = 1.84 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.40 = 8.10 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Limitation (1 mark):** The $T^{1.5}$ scaling ignores the temperature dependence of the collision integral $\Omega_D$ and the Lennard-Jones parameters $\sigma_{AB}$ and $\varepsilon_{AB}$. In reality, the true exponent is closer to **1.65–1.75**, and the full Chapman–Enskog formula should be used for high-temperature applications (e.g., flame zones, post-flame regions).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Chapman–Enskog equation** for binary gas-phase diffusion. Define each parameter and discuss the temperature and pressure dependence of mass diffusivity. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the role of the **Lewis number** in determining flame behavior. Compare the cases $\text{Le} < 1$, $\text{Le} = 1$, and $\text{Le} > 1$, with at least one fuel example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the equation (2 marks):**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1.858 \times 10^{-7} \, T^{3/2} \, \sqrt{\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B}}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}$$

**Step 2 — Parameter definitions (3 marks):**
- $T$ [K] = absolute temperature
- $M_A, M_B$ [g/mol] = molecular weights
- $P$ [atm] = absolute pressure
- $\sigma_{AB} = \frac{1}{2}(\sigma_A + \sigma_B)$ [Å] = mean collision diameter
- $\Omega_D$ = dimensionless collision integral (function of $k_B T / \varepsilon_{AB}$)
- The constant $1.858 \times 10^{-7}$ has units that yield $D$ in **m²/s** when inputs are in the above units.

**Step 3 — Temperature dependence (1 mark):** $D \propto T^{3/2}/\Omega_D$. Since $\Omega_D$ decreases weakly with *T*, the net scaling is $D \propto T^{1.65 \text{ to } 1.75}$.

**Step 4 — Pressure dependence (1 mark):** $D \propto 1/P$. At constant temperature, doubling the pressure halves the diffusivity (molecules collide more often, mean free path decreases).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Le (1 mark):**

$$\text{Le} = \alpha / D = k / (\rho c_p D_{AB})$$

**Step 2 — $\text{Le} \approx 1$ case (2 marks):** Most hydrocarbon–air systems (methane, propane, heptane). Thermal and mass boundary layers grow together; classical flame theory (Zeldovich number, flame stretch) applies with standard extinction limits.

**Step 3 — $\text{Le} < 1$ case (2 marks):** **Hydrogen–air (Le ≈ 0.3)**. Hydrogen diffuses *faster* than heat. Fuel leaks ahead of the preheat zone → preferential diffusion enhances flame propagation near the flammability limit and produces **Darrieus–Landau cellular instabilities** at low Le.

**Step 4 — $\text{Le} > 1$ case (2 marks):** Heavy hydrocarbon–air mixtures near stoichiometric lean conditions (effective Le slightly above 1). Heat diffuses faster than fuel → flame can overheat fuel-lean regions and may self-extinguish under high stretch (e.g., high-velocity, near-extinction counterflow flames).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These at All Costs:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in Fick's First Law** (1 mark penalty) — examiners are strict on the sign convention.
> 2. **Mixing up mass-basis ($Y_A$) and mole-basis ($x_A$) formulations** of Fick's Law — they differ by the factor $\rho$ vs. $c$ and mass fraction vs. mole fraction. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Using the $T^{1.5}$ scaling without acknowledging its limitations** — the examiner expects at least a one-line comment on the role of $\Omega_D$.
> 4. **In numerical problems, not showing the substitution step** — KTU valuation keys explicitly award marks for "writing down the formula with values plugged in," not just the final answer.
> 5. **Confusing Lewis, Schmidt, and Prandtl numbers** — the classic mistake: writing $\text{Le} = D/\alpha$ instead of $\alpha/D$. Memorize the mnemonic **"Le = alpha-on-D"** (thermal in numerator).
> 6. **Failing to state assumptions in derivations** — in Fick's Second Law derivation, "constant $\rho$", "constant $D$", and "no bulk flow" each carry a mark if asked.
> 7. **Mixing up units in Chapman–Enskog** — pressure must be in **atm**, molecular weights in **g/mol**, and $\sigma_{AB}$ in **Å** for the constant $1.858 \times 10^{-7}$ to yield $D$ in m²/s. Wrong unit → wrong answer → zero marks for the numerical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fick's First Law (constitutive):** $\vec{J}_A = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla Y_A$ (mass basis) or $\vec{J}_A^* = -c D_{AB} \nabla x_A$ (molar basis). **Negative sign = thermodynamic direction.**
- **Fick's Second Law (transient PDE):** $\frac{\partial Y_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\vec{v} Y_A) = D_{AB} \nabla^2 Y_A + \text{source}$ (for constant $\rho, D$).
- **Chapman–Enskog formula:** $D_{AB} = \dfrac{1.858 \times 10^{-7} \, T^{3/2} \sqrt{M_A^{-1} + M_B^{-1}}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}$ — universal engineering correlation for binary gas-phase diffusivity.
- **Temperature scaling:** $D \propto T^{1.5}$ (approximate, true exponent ≈ 1.7).
- **Pressure scaling:** $D \propto 1/P$ — at constant *T*, doubling *P* halves *D*.
- **Molecular-weight scaling:** Lighter species diffuse faster (H₂ ≈ 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s; heavy HC ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s in air).
- **Penetration depth:** $\delta_d \sim \sqrt{Dt}$ — slow growth; pure diffusion is negligible in turbulent fire plumes.
- **Dimensionless triad:**
  - $\text{Le} = \alpha / D$ — heat vs. mass diffusion; Le ≈ 1 for most HC-air; Le ≈ 0.3 for H₂-air.
  - $\text{Sc} = \nu / D$ — momentum vs. mass diffusion; Sc ≈ 0.7–1.0 for gases.
  - $\text{Pr} = \nu / \alpha$ — momentum vs. heat; Pr ≈ 0.7 for air.
  - **Identity:** $\text{Le} = \text{Sc} / \text{Pr}$.
- **Eddy diffusivity:** In turbulent flows, $D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{mol}} + D_{\text{turb}}$, with $D_{\text{turb}} \gg D_{\text{mol}}$.
- **Fire relevance:** $D_{AB}$ controls local mixing rate → controls local heat release rate → controls flame temperature, radiation, smoke toxicity.
- **KEE questions to expect:** Numerical scaling of $D$ with $T$ or $P$ (6–8 marks), derivation of Fick's Second Law (7–10 marks), qualitative discussion of Le effects (5–7 marks).
- **Boundary conditions to remember:** $Y_A(0, t) = Y_{A,0}$ (Dirichlet) and $Y_A(\infty, t) = 0$ for the classical semi-infinite diffusion problem → $\text{erfc}$ solution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kinematic Viscosity** is defined as the ratio of the **dynamic viscosity** (also called absolute viscosity) of a fluid to its **mass density**. It quantifies a fluid's resistance to flow under the influence of gravity, fundamentally characterizing the rate at which momentum diffuses through the fluid medium. In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus framework for Fire Propagation and Dynamics, kinematic viscosity is the principal transport property governing **buoyancy-driven flows**, **smoke movement**, and **fire plume dynamics** in compartment fire scenarios.

Mathematically, the kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is expressed as:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity (m²/s in SI units)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "kinematic" arises because $\nu$ has dimensions of **length²/time** (L²T⁻¹), which are purely kinematic (motion-related) quantities, in contrast to dynamic viscosity which has dimensions of **force·time/length²** (ML⁻¹T⁻¹).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have two identical bottles — one filled with **honey** and the other with **water**. If you tilt both bottles at the same angle, the water flows out rapidly, while the honey creeps out sluggishly. This resistance to flow is described by dynamic viscosity. 

Now, consider a **fire scenario**: Hot combustion gases rising from a burning object in a room must push their way upward through the cooler ambient air. The **ease with which these hot gases slide through the surrounding air** (a function of how quickly momentum is transferred per unit mass of the fluid) is the essence of kinematic viscosity. 

In simpler terms:
- **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** → "How sticky is the fluid?"
- **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)** → "How easily does one part of the fluid slide past another, taking its inertia into account?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - Dynamic viscosity depends on the **internal molecular cohesion** of the fluid.
> - Kinematic viscosity accounts for both **internal friction** AND the **inertial mass** of the fluid.

## 1.3 Standard Units and Conversion Factors

| Property | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Conversion |
|----------|---------|----------|------------|
| Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | m²/s | Stoke (St) | 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt |
| Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Pa·s | Poise (P) | 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP |
| Density ($\rho$) | kg/m³ | g/cm³ | 1 kg/m³ = 10⁻³ g/cm³ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In fire dynamics problems, you will frequently encounter the kinematic viscosity of air at room temperature (≈ 20°C), which is approximately **1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. Always remember this benchmark value for quick approximations during exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Viscosity — Molecular Perspective

Viscosity in gases (which dominate fire scenarios) arises from **molecular momentum transfer** between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. According to the **kinetic theory of gases**:

1. Molecules in a faster-moving layer carry higher tangential momentum into the slower layer.
2. Conversely, slower molecules enter the fast layer, reducing its velocity.
3. This continuous exchange manifests macroscopically as shear stress.

## 2.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress $\tau$ between adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (s⁻¹), also called **shear rate**

## 2.3 Why "Kinematic"? The Physical Reasoning

When we divide $\mu$ by density $\rho$, we are essentially asking: *"How much momentum diffusion occurs per unit mass of the fluid?"* This becomes critical in fire engineering because:

- **Buoyancy-driven flows** (such as fire plumes and smoke layers) are governed by gravity acting on density differences.
- The **inertial forces** in such flows scale with $\rho$, while **viscous forces** scale with $\mu$.
- Therefore, the ratio $\nu = \mu / \rho$ naturally appears in dimensionless numbers that compare these effects.

## 2.4 The Most Important Dimensionless Numbers in Fire Dynamics

The kinematic viscosity is the cornerstone of virtually every dimensionless number used in fire plume analysis and compartment fire modeling:

$$\text{Reynolds Number (Re)} = \frac{UL}{\nu} = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu}$$

$$\text{Prandtl Number (Pr)} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}$$

$$\text{Grashof Number (Gr)} = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$$

$$\text{Rayleigh Number (Ra)} = \text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr}$$

Where:
- $U$ = Characteristic velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = Characteristic length (m)
- $\alpha$ = Thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $\beta$ = Thermal expansion coefficient (1/K)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (K)
- $C_p$ = Specific heat at constant pressure (J/kg·K)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Notice how all four dimensionless numbers contain $\nu$ in some form. This is why understanding kinematic viscosity is non-negotiable for fire dynamics — it is the gateway to understanding **laminar vs. turbulent flow regimes**, **forced vs. natural convection**, and the **transition criteria** in fire plumes.

## 2.5 Temperature Dependence of Kinematic Viscosity

For gases, the dynamic viscosity increases with temperature (per **Sutherland's Law**), while density decreases. The combined effect causes kinematic viscosity to increase **more rapidly** with temperature than dynamic viscosity does.

### Sutherland's Formula for Dynamic Viscosity of Air:

$$\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

Where:
- $\mu_0$ = Reference dynamic viscosity at $T_0$ (typically 1.716 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s at $T_0 = 273.15$ K)
- $S$ = Sutherland's constant for air (≈ 110.4 K)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

### Kinematic Viscosity of Air vs. Temperature (Approximate Values):

| Temperature (°C) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\mu$ (×10⁻⁵ Pa·s) | $\nu$ (×10⁻⁵ m²/s) |
|:----------------:|:--------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| 0                | 1.293          | 1.71               | 1.32               |
| 20               | 1.205          | 1.81               | 1.51               |
| 100              | 0.946          | 2.18               | 2.30               |
| 200              | 0.747          | 2.58               | 3.45               |
| 500              | 0.456          | 3.62               | 7.94               |
| 1000             | 0.277          | 4.58               | 16.5               |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Engineering Implication:** In a typical compartment fire, the hot gas layer temperature may reach 500–800°C. The kinematic viscosity of these hot gases can be **5–10 times** that of ambient air, dramatically affecting plume behavior, smoke filling rates, and vent flows.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|:----:|:------------------|:----------|:-------------|
| 1 | Kinematic Viscosity Definition | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s |
| 2 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Pa |
| 3 | Reynolds Number | $Re = UL / \nu$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Prandtl Number (air) | $Pr \approx 0.71$ | Dimensionless, ≈ constant for air |
| 5 | Grashof Number | $Gr = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Sutherland's Law (dynamic $\mu$) | $\mu = \mu_0 (T/T_0)^{3/2} (T_0+S)/(T+S)$ | Pa·s |
| 7 | Kinematic Viscosity of Air @ 20°C | $\nu_{air} \approx 1.51 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s |
| 8 | Kinematic Viscosity of Water @ 20°C | $\nu_{water} \approx 1.00 \times 10^{-6}$ | m²/s |
| 9 | Conversion (Stokes) | 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt | cSt = centistokes |
| 10 | Momentum Diffusivity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | Same as kinematic viscosity |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **fire safety engineering** domain, kinematic viscosity is critical for:

- **Fire Plume Correlations (Zukoski, Heskestad, NFPA):** The dimensionless correlations for plume centerline temperature and velocity depend critically on $Re$ and $Gr$, both of which contain $\nu$.
- **Smoke Control System Design:** Duct flows for mechanical smoke exhaust are sized using $Re$ to ensure adequate extraction without excessive pressure drop.
- **CFD Fire Modeling:** Codes like **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)** require accurate temperature-dependent $\nu(T)$ as a fundamental input.
- **Compartment Fire Ventilation:** The mixing of hot and cold gases at ventilation openings is governed by $\nu$-dependent buoyancy parameters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Law to Kinematic Viscosity

### Step 1 — State Newton's Law of Viscosity

Consider a fluid element between two parallel plates separated by a small distance $dy$. The lower plate is stationary, and the upper plate moves with velocity $U$. The fluid adheres to both plates (no-slip condition), creating a linear velocity profile $u(y) = Uy/H$ (for plate separation $H$).

The shear stress transmitted through the fluid is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 2 — Express Shear Stress in Terms of Force and Area

By definition, shear stress is the tangential force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Momentum Flux Interpretation

In a control volume of unit cross-section, the net rate of momentum transfer per unit area equals the shear stress. This gives us a **momentum flux** interpretation:

$$\dot{M} = \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{[kg/(m·s²)]}$$

### Step 4 — Define Momentum Diffusivity

If we divide this momentum flux by the fluid density, we obtain the **diffusion coefficient for momentum**:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\dot{M}/\rho}{du/dy}$$

This has units:

$$\nu = \frac{[kg/(m \cdot s^2)]}{[kg/m^3] \cdot [1/s]} = \frac{m^2}{s}$$

This derivation shows that $\nu$ is the **momentum diffusion coefficient** — analogous to thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ for heat.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reynolds Number from Navier-Stokes Equations

### Step 1 — The Momentum Equation (Navier-Stokes)

For an incompressible fluid, the Navier-Stokes equation in the x-direction is:

$$\rho \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x$$

### Step 2 — Non-Dimensionalize the Variables

Let $x = L \cdot x^*$, $y = L \cdot y^*$, $z = L \cdot z^*$, $u = U \cdot u^*$, $t = (L/U) \cdot t^*$, and $p = \rho U^2 \cdot p^*$.

Substituting these into the convective acceleration term:

$$\rho \cdot u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \rho \cdot U u^* \cdot \frac{U u^*}{L} = \frac{\rho U^2}{L} (u^* \frac{\partial u^*}{\partial x^*})$$

### Step 3 — Non-Dimensionalize the Viscous Term

$$\mu \nabla^2 u = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{L^2} \nabla^{*2} u^*$$

### Step 4 — Form the Ratio (Inertia / Viscous)

Dividing the entire equation by the inertial scale $\rho U^2 / L$:

$$[\text{Inertial terms}] = 1 \cdot (\text{non-dim inertia})$$

$$[\text{Viscous term}] = \frac{\mu U / L^2}{\rho U^2 / L} = \frac{\mu}{\rho U L} = \frac{1}{Re}$$

### Step 5 — Define the Reynolds Number

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{UL}{\nu}}$$

This shows that $Re$ is fundamentally a ratio of inertial to viscous forces, and **kinematic viscosity $\nu$** is the natural property that appears in this ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of $\nu$ for an Ideal Gas

### Step 1 — From Ideal Gas Law

For an ideal gas: $\rho = p M / (R_u T)$, where $M$ is molar mass and $R_u$ is the universal gas constant.

### Step 2 — From Sutherland's Law for $\mu(T)$

$$\mu(T) = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio $\nu(T) = \mu(T)/\rho(T)$

$$\nu(T) = \frac{\mu(T)}{\rho(T)} = \frac{\mu(T) \cdot R_u T}{p \cdot M}$$

$$\nu(T) = \frac{R_u}{p M} \cdot \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S} \cdot T$$

### Step 4 — Final Expression

$$\boxed{\nu(T) = \frac{\mu_0 R_u}{p M} \cdot \frac{T^{5/2}}{T_0^{3/2}(T + S)} \cdot (T_0 + S)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Since $\rho \propto 1/T$ for an ideal gas, while $\mu \propto T^{3/2}$ approximately, the kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$ scales roughly as **$T^{5/2}$**. This is why $\nu$ increases so dramatically with temperature in fire scenarios.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A hot gas layer in a compartment fire has a temperature of 600°C and a pressure of 101,325 Pa. Using Sutherland's constants for air ($\mu_0 = 1.716 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s at $T_0 = 273.15$ K, $S = 110.4$ K), calculate the kinematic viscosity of the hot gas. Take $M_{air} = 0.02896$ kg/mol and $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Convert Temperature to Kelvin
$$T = 600 + 273.15 = 873.15 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Dynamic Viscosity Using Sutherland's Law
$$\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times \left(\frac{873.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} \times \frac{273.15 + 110.4}{873.15 + 110.4}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Each Component
$$\left(\frac{873.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = (3.197)^{3/2}$$

We compute:
$$(3.197)^{1/2} = 1.788$$
$$(3.197)^{3/2} = 3.197 \times 1.788 = 5.716$$

$$\frac{273.15 + 110.4}{873.15 + 110.4} = \frac{383.55}{983.55} = 0.3899$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\mu$
$$\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.716 \times 0.3899$$
$$\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.228$$
$$\mu = 3.823 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Density Using Ideal Gas Law
$$\rho = \frac{pM}{R_u T} = \frac{101325 \times 0.02896}{8.314 \times 873.15}$$

Numerator: $101325 \times 0.02896 = 2934.37$ Pa·kg/mol
Denominator: $8.314 \times 873.15 = 7259.55$ J/mol

$$\rho = \frac{2934.37}{7259.55} = 0.4042 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 6 — Compute Kinematic Viscosity
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{3.823 \times 10^{-5}}{0.4042}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 9.458 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** This value is approximately **6.3 times** the kinematic viscosity of air at 20°C. This dramatic increase has profound implications for fire plume dynamics, mixing, and smoke movement in hot fire environments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Kinematic Viscosity in the Fire Dynamics Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Molecular Motion] --> B[Momentum Transfer Between Layers]
    B --> C[Dynamic Viscosity mu]
    C --> D[Shear Stress tau]
    D --> E[Newtons Law of Viscosity]
    C --> F[Divided by Density rho]
    F --> G[Kinematic Viscosity nu]
    G --> H[Momentum Diffusivity]
    H --> I[Dimensionless Numbers]
    I --> J[Reynolds Number Re]
    I --> K[Grashof Number Gr]
    I --> L[Prandtl Number Pr]
    J --> M[Flow Regime Classification]
    K --> N[Buoyancy Driven Flow Analysis]
    L --> O[Thermal vs Momentum Diffusion]
    M --> P[Fire Plume Correlations]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Compartment Fire Engineering Design]
    Q --> R[Smoke Control Systems]
    Q --> S[Ventilation Sizing]
    Q --> T[Evacuation Time Calculations]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Viscosity Coupling in Fire Plume Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Fire Source Input]
        A1[Heat Release Rate HRR]
        A2[Ambient Temperature T0]
        A3[Compartment Geometry L]
    end
    subgraph PROPERTY[Transport Property]
        B1[Dynamic Viscosity mu T]
        B2[Gas Density rho T]
        B3[Kinematic Viscosity nu T]
    end
    subgraph DIMLESS[Dimensionless Parameters]
        C1[Re rho UL by mu]
        C2[Gr g beta DT L cubed by nu squared]
        C3[Pr nu by alpha]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Fire Engineering Output]
        D1[Plume Velocity]
        D2[Smoke Layer Height]
        D3[Time to Critical Conditions]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    A3 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    A3 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Temperature Dependence of $\nu$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increase in Gas Temperature T] --> B[Dynamic Viscosity mu increases per Sutherland Law]
    A --> C[Gas Density rho decreases per Ideal Gas Law]
    B --> D[Numerator Effect mu increases]
    C --> E[Denominator Effect rho decreases]
    D --> F[Kinematic Viscosity nu increases more rapidly]
    E --> F
    F --> G[nu approximately scales as T to the 5 by 2]
    G --> H[Fire Plume Becomes More Viscous at High T]
    H --> I[Smoke Layer Spreads Slower in Hot Zones]
    H --> J[Mixing Between Hot and Cold Layers Weakens]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: No-Slip Condition and Shear Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLATE[Parallel Plate Configuration]
        TopPlate[Upper Moving Plate Velocity U]
        FluidLayer[Newtonian Fluid Layer Thickness H]
        BotPlate[Lower Stationary Plate Velocity zero]
    end
    TopPlate --> VelocityProfile
    FluidLayer --> VelocityProfile
    BotPlate --> VelocityProfile
    VelocityProfile[Linear Velocity Profile u y equals Uy by H]
    VelocityProfile --> ShearStress[Shear Stress tau equals mu times U by H]
    ShearStress --> MomentumFlux[Momentum Flux per unit mass equals nu times U by H squared]
    MomentumFlux --> Result[Result: Kinematic Viscosity nu governs momentum diffusion]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define kinematic viscosity. How is it different from dynamic viscosity?**

**Model Answer:**

Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) of a fluid to its mass density ($\rho$), expressed as:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|:-------|:-------------------------|:---------------------------|
| Definition | Internal friction coefficient | Ratio of internal friction to density |
| Unit (SI) | Pa·s (ML⁻¹T⁻¹) | m²/s (L²T⁻¹) |
| Physical Meaning | Resistance to flow | Momentum diffusivity |
| Depends on | Molecular structure | Molecular structure + density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 1.5 marks; Tabular distinction = 1.5 marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the SI and CGS units of kinematic viscosity. What is the kinematic viscosity of air at 20°C?**

**Model Answer:**

- **SI Unit:** m²/s (square meter per second)
- **CGS Unit:** Stoke (St), where 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt (centistokes)

**Kinematic Viscosity of Air at 20°C:**

$$\nu_{air} = 1.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 0.151 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 15.1 \text{ cSt}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme:** Units = 1.5 marks; Value with conversion = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the expression for kinematic viscosity from Newton's law of viscosity. Explain why kinematic viscosity is referred to as the "momentum diffusivity." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire compartment has hot gases at 400°C. Using Sutherland's law, calculate the kinematic viscosity of these hot gases and compare it with ambient air at 27°C. Discuss the engineering implications of this change in $\nu$ for smoke movement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

Consider two parallel fluid layers separated by a distance $dy$. The shear stress is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 2 — Momentum Flux Interpretation:**

Since $\tau$ has units of force/area = momentum flux:

$$\dot{M} = \mu \frac{du}{dy} \quad [\text{kg m}^{-1} \text{s}^{-2}]$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Kinematic Viscosity:**

Dividing by density to get momentum flux per unit mass:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — "Momentum Diffusivity" Explanation:**

The kinematic viscosity has the same form as the heat diffusion equation's thermal diffusivity ($\alpha = k/\rho C_p$). Just as $\alpha$ governs how quickly heat spreads through a fluid, $\nu$ governs how quickly momentum (velocity) spreads. This is why $\nu$ is called the **momentum diffusivity**.

In a fire plume, $\nu$ determines how rapidly the upward velocity of hot gases diffuses into the surrounding quiescent air, controlling entrainment rates.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Hot gas temperature: $T_h = 400 + 273.15 = 673.15$ K
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 27 + 273.15 = 300.15$ K
- $\mu_0 = 1.716 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s at $T_0 = 273.15$ K
- $S = 110.4$ K
- $p = 101325$ Pa, $M = 0.02896$ kg/mol, $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Calculate $\mu$ at 400°C:**

$$\mu_h = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times \left(\frac{673.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} \times \frac{383.55}{783.55}$$

$$\left(\frac{673.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = (2.464)^{3/2} = 2.464 \times 1.570 = 3.868$$

$$\frac{383.55}{783.55} = 0.4895$$

$$\mu_h = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.868 \times 0.4895 = 3.249 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\mu$ at 27°C:**

$$\mu_a = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times \left(\frac{300.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} \times \frac{383.55}{410.55}$$

$$\left(\frac{300.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = (1.099)^{3/2} = 1.099 \times 1.048 = 1.152$$

$$\frac{383.55}{410.55} = 0.9342$$

$$\mu_a = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.152 \times 0.9342 = 1.847 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Densities:**

$$\rho_h = \frac{101325 \times 0.02896}{8.314 \times 673.15} = 0.5239 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_a = \frac{101325 \times 0.02896}{8.314 \times 300.15} = 1.1750 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Kinematic Viscosities:**

$$\nu_h = \frac{3.249 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5239} = 6.20 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\nu_a = \frac{1.847 \times 10^{-5}}{1.1750} = 1.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison Ratio:**

$$\frac{\nu_h}{\nu_a} = \frac{6.20 \times 10^{-5}}{1.57 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.95$$

**Engineering Implications:**

The kinematic viscosity of the hot gases is nearly **4 times** that of ambient air. This means:
1. Momentum diffuses more slowly in the hot layer, resulting in **sharper velocity gradients** at the interface.
2. **Entrainment of ambient air** into the hot layer is reduced, slowing smoke cooling.
3. The **Reynolds number** of plume flows increases dramatically, ensuring turbulent behavior in most compartment fires.
4. **Smoke stratification** becomes more stable due to the kinematic viscosity gradient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** when using Sutherland's law. This is the #1 error in fire dynamics problems.
> 2. **Confusing dynamic and kinematic viscosity** — remember, $\nu$ requires division by $\rho$.
> 3. **Using $T_0 = 273$ K** instead of $T_0 = 273.15$ K leads to minor numerical errors but is generally acceptable.
> 4. **Not stating the physical interpretation** of the final numerical answer, which costs 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Define Reynolds number. Using Buckingham's Pi theorem, derive the Reynolds number for flow through a pipe, clearly showing the role of kinematic viscosity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The hot gas layer in a fire compartment moves with an average velocity of 1.5 m/s. The compartment has a characteristic length of 4 m. At a hot layer temperature of 500°C, determine the Reynolds number of the flow and comment on the flow regime. Take relevant properties at 500°C from standard air tables. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Definition of Reynolds Number:**

The Reynolds number ($Re$) is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{UL}{\nu}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Variables:**

For flow through a pipe with pressure drop $\Delta p$, the relevant variables are:
- Pressure drop: $\Delta p$ [ML⁻¹T⁻²]
- Pipe diameter: $D$ [L]
- Fluid velocity: $U$ [LT⁻¹]
- Fluid density: $\rho$ [ML⁻³]
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ [ML⁻¹T⁻¹]

Total variables: $n = 5$

**Step 3 — Fundamental Dimensions:** $M$, $L$, $T$ → $k = 3$

**Step 4 — Number of Pi Groups:** $n - k = 5 - 3 = 2$

**Step 5 — Form Pi Groups:**

Choosing $\rho$, $U$, $D$ as repeating variables:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{\rho U^2}{\Delta p} = \frac{\rho U D}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\mu U}{\Delta p D}$$

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p D}{\rho U^2} \quad \text{(Euler Number)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\rho U D}{\mu} = \frac{UD}{\nu} = Re$$

**Step 6 — Functional Relationship:**

$$\frac{\Delta p D}{\rho U^2} = f(Re) = f\left(\frac{UD}{\nu}\right)$$

This clearly demonstrates that **kinematic viscosity $\nu$** is the fundamental property governing the flow regime and pressure drop characteristics.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $U = 1.5$ m/s
- $L = 4$ m
- $T = 500 + 273.15 = 773.15$ K
- From standard air tables at 500°C: $\nu \approx 7.94 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{UL}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 4}{7.94 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \frac{6.0}{7.94 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{Re = 75,567}$$

**Step 2 — Flow Regime Classification:**

Since $Re \gg 4000$ (the typical critical Reynolds number for internal flows), the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**Step 3 — Implications for Fire Dynamics:**

- The hot gas layer flow is highly turbulent, ensuring good mixing within the layer.
- The high $Re$ validates the use of **turbulent plume correlations** (e.g., Zukoski's $\times 1.03$ or Heskestad's correlations) for engineering calculations.
- For external plume flows, the critical $Re$ is typically much lower (≈ 10 for plume initiation), confirming that fire plumes are predominantly turbulent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Not specifying the flow regime** explicitly in the final answer — examiners expect a clear statement of "laminar," "transitional," or "turbulent."
> 2. **Using dynamic viscosity instead of kinematic viscosity** in the $Re$ formula. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to use $\nu$ directly when given.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the critical $Re$ value** used for classification (typically 2300 or 4000 for internal flows; 10 for external plumes).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Kinematic Viscosity**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, with units of m²/s.
- ✅ **Physical Meaning:** Momentum diffusivity — governs how quickly velocity gradients spread through a fluid.
- ✅ **Benchmark Value:** $\nu_{air}$ at 20°C ≈ **1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**; $\nu_{water}$ at 20°C ≈ **1.00 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**.
- ✅ **Unit Conversion:** 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St (Stokes) = 10⁶ cSt (centistokes).
- ✅ **Temperature Scaling:** $\nu \propto T^{5/2}$ approximately for ideal gases → increases rapidly with temperature.
- ✅ **Sutherland's Law:** $\mu(T) = \mu_0 (T/T_0)^{3/2} (T_0 + S)/(T + S)$ with $S = 110.4$ K for air.
- ✅ **Critical Dimensionless Numbers:** $Re = UL/\nu$, $Gr = g\beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$, $Pr = \nu/\alpha$.
- ✅ **Fire Engineering Applications:** Plume correlations, smoke control, CFD inputs, vent flow analysis.
- ✅ **Flow Regime Rule of Thumb:** $Re < 2300$ → laminar; $2300 < Re < 4000$ → transitional; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent (for internal flows).
- ✅ **Key Distinction:** Dynamic viscosity = "stickiness"; Kinematic viscosity = "diffusivity of momentum per unit mass."
- ✅ **Common Exam Mistake to Avoid:** Always convert temperature to **Kelvin** before applying Sutherland's law.
- ✅ **Always remember:** Fire plumes are **almost always turbulent** in compartments with $Re$ values typically exceeding 10⁴.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lewis Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lewis Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lewis Number (Le)** is a fundamental dimensionless number in combustion and fire dynamics that represents the ratio of **thermal diffusivity** ($\alpha$) to **mass diffusivity** ($D$). It quantifies the relative effectiveness of heat and mass transport in a reactive mixture.

Mathematically, the Lewis Number is expressed as:

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p D}$$

Where:
- $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s)
- $D$ is the **mass diffusivity** (m²/s) of the limiting reactant
- $k$ is the **thermal conductivity** (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ is the **gas density** (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ is the **specific heat at constant pressure** (J/kg·K)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Marathon Runners**
> Imagine two runners on a track — one carrying a **heat baton** (thermal energy) and the other carrying a **fuel baton** (mass). The **Lewis Number** asks: *Who arrives at the reaction site first?*
> - If $Le > 1$: Heat diffuses **faster** than mass → the "heat runner" wins → the mixture tends to be **thermally controlled**.
> - If $Le < 1$: Mass (fuel) diffuses **faster** than heat → the "fuel runner" wins → the mixture is **mass-diffusion controlled**.
> - If $Le = 1$: Both arrive together → **balanced transport** (idealized scenario).

This "race" between heat and mass transport is precisely what governs **flame structure, stability, and extinction limits** in fire propagation.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Fire Dynamics

The Lewis Number is the **central governing parameter** in the theory of premixed and non-premixed flames. It determines:
- **Flame thickness** and structure
- **Burning velocity** ($S_L$)
- **Stability** of flames on burners
- **Extinction strain rates**
- Whether a flame will **strengthen or weaken** under stretch (preferential diffusion effects)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Benchmark Value: Le = 1**
> A Lewis Number of **unity** is the **theoretical reference state**. Most hydrocarbon-air mixtures have $Le \approx 0.8 - 1.2$. For methane-air, $Le \approx 0.95$; for hydrogen-air, $Le \approx 0.3$ (highly mass-diffusion controlled).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Transport Processes

The Lewis Number emerges from the coupling of three fundamental transport phenomena:

| Transport Mode | Governing Equation | Driving Force | Diffusion Coefficient |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Transfer** | Fourier's Law | Temperature gradient ($\nabla T$) | Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ |
| **Mass Transfer** | Fick's Law | Concentration gradient ($\nabla Y$) | Mass diffusivity $D$ |
| **Momentum Transfer** | Newton's Law | Velocity gradient ($\nabla v$) | Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$ |

The Lewis Number is essentially the ratio of two of these diffusion rates: $\alpha / D$.

## 2.2 Connection to the Damköhler Number and Peclet Numbers

The Lewis Number is intimately related to other dimensionless groups used in combustion:

$$\text{Pe}_{\text{heat}} = \frac{S_L \cdot \delta_T}{\alpha} \quad ; \quad \text{Pe}_{\text{mass}} = \frac{S_L \cdot \delta_D}{D}$$

For a steady planar flame, $\delta_T \sim \alpha / S_L$ and $\delta_D \sim D / S_L$, so:

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{\delta_D}{\delta_T} = \frac{\text{thermal thickness}}{\text{mass thickness}} \text{ (inverse relationship)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **low Lewis number** (e.g., hydrogen, $Le \approx 0.3$) means the **mass diffusion thickness is much smaller** than the thermal thickness → the fuel "leaks" ahead of the heat front, causing **diffusion-thermal instabilities** and **cellular flames**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lewis Number (Definition)** | $Le = \alpha / D$ | Heat transport vs. mass transport effectiveness |
| 2 | **Expanded Form** | $Le = k / (\rho c_p D)$ | In terms of measurable properties |
| 3 | **Relation to Prandtl & Schmidt** | $Le = Sc / Pr$ | Ratio of momentum-mass to momentum-heat diffusion |
| 4 | **Flame Speed Correction (Markstein)** | $S_{L,0} = S_L [1 + f(Le) \cdot Ka]$ | Stretch effect on burning velocity |
| 5 | **Clavin–Williams Stability Criterion** | $Le < Le_{crit} = 1 - 2(1-\beta)\zeta$ | Onset of thermo-diffusive instability |
| 6 | **Thermal Thickness** | $\delta_T = \alpha / S_L$ | Preheat zone width |
| 7 | **Mass Thickness** | $\delta_D = D / S_L$ | Diffusion zone width |
| 8 | **Ratio of Thicknesses** | $\delta_T / \delta_D = Le$ | Direct measure of Le |
| 9 | **Peclet Number relation** | $\text{Pe}_T = \text{Pe}_D \cdot Le$ | Global transport balance |
| 10 | **Characteristic Damköhler** | $Da = \tau_{flow} / \tau_{chem}$ | Chemical kinetic coupling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where $Sc = \nu / D$ is the Schmidt Number** and **$Pr = \nu / \alpha$ is the Prandtl Number**. This relation $Le = Sc/Pr$ reveals that Le compares mass to thermal transport *through* the lens of momentum transport.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Fire Safety

In fire protection engineering, the Lewis Number governs:
- **Fire suppression decisions**: Extinguishing agents are designed to alter $Le$ (e.g., by changing $\alpha$ or $D$)
- **Explosion vent design**: $Le < 1$ mixtures (hydrogen, methanol) require larger vent areas
- **Flame arrestor sizing**: Depends on preferential diffusion (Le effects)
- **Compartment fire modeling**: Determines whether a flame will flashover or self-extinguish
- **Turbulent burning velocity correlations**: $S_T / S_L \propto Le^{n}$ in various regimes
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lewis Number from First Principles (Governing Equations)

### Step 1: Start with the Energy Conservation Equation for a Reactive Mixture

For a steady, one-dimensional, planar premixed flame, the energy equation (neglecting radiation and viscous dissipation) is:

$$\rho c_p u \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + q \dot{\omega}''$$

Where:
- $u$ is the gas velocity in the lab frame
- $q$ is the heat of reaction per unit mass of fuel
- $\dot{\omega}''$ is the volumetric reaction rate (kg/m³·s)

### Step 2: Introduce the Mass Conservation (Continuity) for a Steady Flow

$$\rho u = \rho_u S_L = \text{constant} = \dot{m}$$

This means the mass flux $\dot{m}$ is constant throughout the flame.

### Step 3: Substitute the Constant Mass Flux into the Energy Equation

Since $\rho u = \dot{m}$ is constant, divide through:

$$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + q \dot{\omega}''$$

### Step 4: Define the Thermal Diffusivity and Non-Dimensionalize

Recognize that $k/(\rho c_p) = \alpha$, so $k = \rho c_p \alpha$. With $\dot{m} = \rho u = \rho_u S_L$:

$$\rho_u S_L c_p \frac{dT}{dx} = \rho c_p \alpha \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + q \dot{\omega}''$$

Dividing by $\rho c_p$:

$$S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \alpha \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{q \dot{\omega}''}{\rho c_p}$$

### Step 5: Write the Analogous Species Conservation Equation

By Fick's Law and the conservation of the limiting reactant mass fraction $Y_F$:

$$\rho_u S_L \frac{dY_F}{dx} = \rho D \frac{d^2 Y_F}{dx^2} - \dot{\omega}''$$

### Step 6: Identify the Diffusive Structure — The Lewis Number Emerges

The ratio of the **characteristic thermal diffusion length** to the **characteristic mass diffusion length** is:

$$\frac{\delta_T}{\delta_D} \sim \frac{\alpha / S_L}{D / S_L} = \frac{\alpha}{D} \equiv Le$$

**Conclusion of Derivation:**

$$\boxed{Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p D}}$$

This rigorously demonstrates that the Lewis Number is the **ratio of the two fundamental transport rates** governing the flame structure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Markstein Length (Stretch Effect)

### Step 1: Define the Strain Rate (Karlovitz Number)

For a curved or stretched flame, the local strain rate is $K = \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dA}{dt}$, and the **Karlovitz number** is:

$$Ka = \frac{K \cdot \delta_L}{S_L}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Linear Correction to Burning Velocity

The observed (stretched) burning velocity relates to the planar (un-stretched) value via:

$$S_{L,0} - S_L = L_M \cdot K$$

Where $L_M$ is the **Markstein length**.

### Step 3: Show that $L_M$ Depends on Le

From asymptotic analysis of the flame structure:

$$L_M \approx \delta_L \cdot \frac{Le - 1}{Le}$$

Therefore:

$$S_{L,0} = S_L + \delta_L \cdot \frac{Le - 1}{Le} \cdot K$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result

| Condition | Sign of $(Le - 1)$ | Effect of Stretch ($K > 0$) | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Le > 1$ (heat-favored) | Positive | $S_L$ increases (flame strengthens) | **Stable** |
| $Le < 1$ (mass-favored) | Negative | $S_L$ decreases (flame weakens) | **Unstable** (cellular flames) |
| $Le = 1$ (balanced) | Zero | No first-order effect | **Neutral** |

> [!NOTE]
