> This is the theoretical foundation of **thermo-diffusive instability** and explains why **lean hydrogen-air flames** ($Le \approx 0.3$) spontaneously form **cellular patterns**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | Steps |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compute $Le$ | $k, \rho, c_p, D$ | Dimensionless $Le$ | $Le = k/(\rho c_p D)$ | 3-step substitution |
| Flame thickness ratio | $Le, S_L, \alpha$ | $\delta_T / \delta_D$ | $\delta_T/\delta_D = Le$ | Direct ratio |
| Stability analysis | $Le, \beta$ (Zeldovich no.) | $Le_{crit}$ | $Le_{crit} = 1 - 2(1-\beta)\zeta$ | Clavin-Williams |
| Markstein correction | $Le, S_L, K$ | $S_{L,0}$ | $S_{L,0} = S_L [1 + (Le-1)Ka/Le]$ | Substitution + solve |
| Extinction strain | $Le, Da$ | $K_{ext}$ | $K_{ext} \cdot \tau_{chem} = f(Le)$ | Iterative/Da-limit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Transport Processes and Lewis Number Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Reacting Mixture] --> B[Transport Phenomena]
    B --> C[Heat Conduction]
    B --> D[Mass Diffusion]
    B --> E[Momentum Diffusion]
    C --> C1[Thermal Diffusivity alpha]
    D --> D1[Mass Diffusivity D]
    E --> E1[Kinematic Viscosity nu]
    C1 --> F[Lewis Number Le = alpha / D]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> G[Prandtl Pr = nu / alpha]
    E1 --> H[Schmidt Sc = nu / D]
    F --> I[Flame Structure]
    F --> J[Flame Stability]
    F --> K[Burning Velocity Correction]
    G --> L[Le = Sc / Pr]
    H --> L
    I --> M[Thermal Thickness delta_T]
    I --> N[Reaction Zone]
    J --> O[Le less than 1: Unstable Cellular]
    J --> P[Le greater than 1: Stable]
    J --> Q[Le equals 1: Neutral]
    K --> R[Markstein Length Effect]
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic Flow for Le-Based Flame Stability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Compute Le for Mixture] --> Q1{Is Le greater than 1.0?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Stable[Stable Flame - Heat-Favored]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Le less than 0.7?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Unstable[Highly Unstable - Cellular Formation]
    Q2 -->|No| Marginal[Marginally Stable - Curvature Sensitive]
    Stable --> App1[Industrial burners, methane]
    Unstable --> App2[Hydrogen, methanol flames]
    Marginal --> App3[Lean propane, weak flames]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Le in Fire Dynamics Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Parameters]
        I1[Thermal Conductivity k]
        I2[Density rho]
        I3[Specific Heat cp]
        I4[Mass Diffusivity D]
    end
    subgraph Compute[Computation Engine]
        C1[alpha = k / rho cp]
        C2[Le = alpha / D]
        C3[Sc = nu / D]
        C4[Pr = nu / alpha]
    end
    subgraph Output[Engineering Outputs]
        O1[Flame Stability Index]
        O2[Markstein Length]
        O3[Extinction Strain]
        O4[Fire Suppression Strategy]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C1
    I4 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> O1
    C2 --> O2
    C2 --> O3
    C2 --> O4
    C3 --> C2
    C4 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The Lewis Number sits at the **central computational node**, receiving transport property inputs and feeding all major fire dynamics outputs. This architectural view emphasizes its role as a **master parameter** in fire engineering analysis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define the Lewis Number. What is its physical significance in premixed flame theory? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Lewis Number is defined as the ratio of thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity:

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p D}$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):** It represents the **relative rates of heat and mass transport** in a reacting mixture. It governs:
1. The **relative thickness** of the preheat and reaction zones
2. The **flame stability** under stretch (preferential diffusion)
3. The **Markstein effect** on burning velocity

For $Le = 1$, heat and mass diffuse at the same rate (neutral). For $Le < 1$, mass diffuses faster (unstable, cellular flames possible, e.g., hydrogen). For $Le > 1$, heat diffuses faster (stable, e.g., heavy hydrocarbons).

---

### Question A2
**Q: The thermal diffusivity of a methane-air mixture is $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and the mass diffusivity of methane is $3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the Lewis Number and comment on flame stability. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.833$$

**Stability Comment (2 marks):** Since $Le < 1$, the methane-air mixture is **slightly mass-diffusion controlled**. The flame is **marginally stable** and may exhibit weak cellular instabilities at very lean conditions. The Markstein length will be negative, meaning flame stretch **reduces** burning velocity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Q: Derive the expression for the Lewis Number from the governing conservation equations of a steady, one-dimensional, planar premixed flame. Using the Markstein theory, explain how $Le$ controls flame stability and cellular flame formation. A stoichiometric propane-air flame has $\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $D = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, and burning velocity $S_L = 0.46$ m/s. Compute (a) the Lewis Number, (b) the thermal and mass-diffusion thicknesses, and (c) the Markstein length using $L_M = \delta_L (Le-1)/Le$, where $\delta_L = \alpha/S_L$. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derivation

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Steady 1D energy equation for premixed flame:

$$\rho c_p u \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + q \dot{\omega}''$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Continuity gives $\rho u = \rho_u S_L = \text{const}$. Substitute and divide by $\rho c_p$:

$$S_L \frac{dT}{dx} = \alpha \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{q \dot{\omega}''}{\rho c_p}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Species conservation (Fick's Law):

$$S_L \frac{dY_F}{dx} = D \frac{d^2 Y_F}{dx^2} - \frac{\dot{\omega}''}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Characteristic lengths: thermal $\delta_T \sim \alpha/S_L$ and mass $\delta_D \sim D/S_L$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Ratio:

$$\frac{\delta_T}{\delta_D} = \frac{\alpha/S_L}{D/S_L} = \frac{\alpha}{D} = Le$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final expression and interpretation.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Markstein stability theory — discuss $L_M \propto (Le-1)/Le$, and the cellular flame onset for $Le < Le_{crit}$.

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Computation

**(i) Lewis Number (2 marks):**

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.222$$

**(ii) Thermal Thickness (2 marks):**

$$\delta_T = \frac{\alpha}{S_L} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.46} = 4.783 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 47.83 \text{ μm}$$

Mass-diffusion thickness:

$$\delta_D = \frac{D}{S_L} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{0.46} = 3.913 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 39.13 \text{ μm}$$

**(iii) Markstein Length (3 marks):**

$$L_M = \delta_T \cdot \frac{Le - 1}{Le} = (4.783 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot \frac{1.222 - 1}{1.222}$$

$$L_M = (4.783 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot \frac{0.222}{1.222} = (4.783 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 0.1817$$

$$L_M = 8.69 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 8.69 \text{ μm}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Since $Le > 1$ and $L_M > 0$, the propane-air flame is **thermo-diffusively stable** — flame stretch **increases** burning velocity, suppressing cellular instabilities.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**Q: (a) Define Prandtl, Schmidt, and Lewis numbers. Establish the relation $Le = Sc/Pr$. Discuss with a phase-plane diagram how the relative magnitudes of $Pr$, $Sc$, and $Le$ classify transport regimes in combustion. (b) For a hydrogen-air mixture, $k = 0.18$ W/m·K, $\rho = 0.85$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K, $D = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Compute $Le$, $\alpha$, and the ratio of flame zone thicknesses. Comment on the fire safety implications for hydrogen as a fuel. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Definitions and Relation

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Definitions:
- **Prandtl Number:** $Pr = \nu / \alpha = c_p \mu / k$ — ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity
- **Schmidt Number:** $Sc = \nu / D$ — ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity
- **Lewis Number:** $Le = \alpha / D$ — ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive $Le = Sc/Pr$:

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{\nu/D}{\nu/\alpha} = \frac{Sc}{Pr}$$

**Step 3 (3.5 marks):** Phase-plane classification table/diagram:

| Regime | Pr | Sc | Le | Physical Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gases (typical) | 0.7 | 0.7 | 1.0 | All transports equal | Reference state |
| Liquid metals | $\ll 1$ | high | $\gg 1$ | Heat dominates | Molten metals |
| Hydrogen flames | ~0.7 | ~0.2 | ~0.3 | Mass dominates | H₂-air |
| Heavy hydrocarbons | ~0.7 | ~1.0 | ~1.3 | Heat slightly favored | Propane-air |
| Liquids (water) | ~7 | ~500 | ~70 | Mass strongly suppressed | Pool fires |

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Hydrogen-Air Computation

**(i) Compute $\alpha$ (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{0.18}{0.85 \times 1050} = \frac{0.18}{892.5} = 2.017 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Compute $Le$ (2 marks):**

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D} = \frac{2.017 \times 10^{-4}}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.689$$

Wait — the actual hydrogen-air Le is known to be **less than 1** because of the very high mass diffusivity of H₂. The given numerical values here yield $Le > 1$, but for **validation purposes** we use the given data. In practice, hydrogen's molecular weight being very small gives extremely high $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Note:** The problem values yield $Le = 2.69$ mathematically. In real practice, hydrogen-air has $Le \approx 0.3$ because $D_{H_2}$ in air is exceptionally high ($\sim 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s) while $\alpha$ is moderate. Students should always cross-check with literature values.

**(iii) Thickness Ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\delta_T}{\delta_D} = Le = 2.689$$

The thermal zone is **2.69 times wider** than the diffusion zone.

**(iv) Fire Safety Implications (1 mark):**
- If $Le < 1$ (real H₂): **Highly unstable**, prone to **cellular flames** and **deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT)**
- Requires **larger explosion vents**, **flame arrestors** with finer gaps
- **Detonation cell size** is much smaller — confinement hazards are severe
- Hydrogen fire detection must rely on **flame detection**, not smoke

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common error:** Forgetting to convert $c_p$ from kJ to J in numerical problems — this gives a wrong $\alpha$ by factor 1000.
> 2. **Second pitfall:** Confusing $Le$ with $Pr$ or $Sc$ — remember $Le = \alpha/D$, $Pr = \nu/\alpha$, $Sc = \nu/D$.
> 3. **Stability sign error:** Students often write that $Le < 1$ is "more stable" — it is actually **less stable** (negative Markstein length).
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always ensure $D$ and $\alpha$ are in the **same units** (m²/s) before computing $Le$.
> 5. **Definition accuracy:** Do not define Le as "ratio of heat to mass transfer" — that is qualitative. The **rigorous definition** is ratio of **diffusivities**, not the fluxes themselves.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition (memorize verbatim):** $Le = \alpha / D = k / (\rho c_p D)$ — **dimensionless**
- **Three benchmark values:**
  - $Le = 1$ → balanced transport (neutral stability, reference state)
  - $Le > 1$ → heat-favored → **positive** Markstein length → **stable**
  - $Le < 1$ → mass-favored → **negative** Markstein length → **unstable** (cellular flames)
- **Key relations to remember:**
  - $Le = Sc / Pr$ (cross-relation of three dimensionless groups)
  - $\delta_T = \alpha / S_L$ (thermal thickness)
  - $\delta_D = D / S_L$ (mass thickness)
  - $\delta_T / \delta_D = Le$
  - $L_M = \delta_L (Le - 1)/Le$ (Markstein length)
- **Typical values:** Methane-air $Le \approx 0.95$; Propane-air $Le \approx 1.2$; Hydrogen-air $Le \approx 0.3$
- **Physical meaning:** $Le$ governs the **competition** between heat conduction ahead of the flame and fuel/oxidizer diffusion toward the flame.
- **Engineering implications:**
  - Low-Le fuels (H₂) → small detonation cells → high explosion risk
  - High-Le fuels (heavy HC) → stable laminar flames, easier to control
  - Fire suppression alters $Le$ by changing $\alpha$ or $D$
- **Clavin-Williams stability criterion:** $Le_{crit} = 1 - 2(1 - \beta)\zeta$ where $\beta$ is Zeldovich number and $\zeta$ is a non-dimensional activation energy term.
- **Examination tip:** Always state **both** the value of $Le$ **and** the **implication** (stable / unstable / neutral) — partial marks are lost when only the number is given.
- **Common confusion to avoid:** $Le$ is **NOT** the ratio of heat flux to mass flux — it is the ratio of **diffusivities** (kinematic quantities), which has dimensions of [m²/s] / [m²/s] = dimensionless.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prandtl Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prandtl Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Prandtl Number (Pr)** is a fundamental **dimensionless number** in convective heat transfer and fluid dynamics that quantifies the ratio of **momentum diffusivity** (kinematic viscosity, $\nu$) to **thermal diffusivity** ($\alpha$). It essentially measures the relative effectiveness of momentum and energy transport by molecular diffusion in a moving fluid.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$Pr = \frac{\text{Momentum Diffusivity}}{\text{Thermal Diffusivity}} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$$

Where the individual parameters are:
- $\nu$ = **Kinematic Viscosity** [m²/s]
- $\alpha$ = **Thermal Diffusivity** [m²/s]
- $\mu$ = **Dynamic Viscosity** [kg/(m·s)] or [Pa·s]
- $C_p$ = **Specific Heat at constant pressure** [J/(kg·K)]
- $k$ = **Thermal Conductivity** [W/(m·K)]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Prandtl Number is a pure **fluid property** (not a flow property). It depends ONLY on the physical state and composition of the fluid, NOT on the geometry or flow velocity. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU examinations.

### Context in Fire Propagation & Dynamics

In fire science, the Prandtl number controls the **relative thickness of the thermal and velocity boundary layers** that develop over burning surfaces, hot smoke layers, and fire-driven flows. Since most fires occur in air (Pr ≈ 0.7), and flame chemistry involves combustion gases (Pr ≈ 0.65–0.75), this number directly governs how rapidly heat diffuses away from a fire compared to how fast the hot gases move.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded swimming pool (representing fluid molecules):

- **High Prandtl Number (Pr ≫ 1)** — Like **honey or engine oil**: Heat (energy) diffuses very slowly through the fluid compared to how fast momentum (motion) spreads. The thermal boundary layer is **very thin** and hugs the hot surface closely, while the velocity layer is thick. Think of a drop of food coloring in honey — the color spreads (momentum) much faster than heat would.

- **Low Prandtl Number (Pr ≪ 1)** — Like **liquid metals (mercury, sodium)**: Heat diffuses **extremely rapidly** compared to momentum. The thermal boundary layer is **much thicker** than the velocity boundary layer. Heat "outruns" the flow.

- **Prandtl Number near unity (Pr ≈ 1)** — Like **air and most gases**: Momentum and heat diffuse at **comparable rates**. The velocity and thermal boundary layers grow at nearly the same rate. This is the regime of most fire-related applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Pr = **P**ropagation of **r**elative **d**iffusivities — a high Pr means **d**ominance of **m**omentum diffusion over thermal diffusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Physical Interpretation: The "Why" and "How"

The Prandtl number is best understood by examining the governing partial differential equations for a flowing fluid:

1. **Momentum Transport (Navier-Stokes equation)** — governed by kinematic viscosity $\nu$. This dictates how fast velocity gradients (shear) smooth out across the flow.

2. **Energy Transport (Heat equation)** — governed by thermal diffusivity $\alpha$. This dictates how fast temperature gradients (hot/cold layers) equalize.

Both processes occur simultaneously via **molecular motion**, but at different rates depending on the fluid's molecular structure. The Prandtl number simply **ratios these two molecular transport rates**.

### The Boundary Layer Connection (Critical for Fire Dynamics)

When a hot surface (e.g., a burning wall, smoke layer interface) heats a flowing fluid:

- The **velocity boundary layer** thickness scales as $\delta_v \sim \sqrt{\nu \, t}$
- The **thermal boundary layer** thickness scales as $\delta_t \sim \sqrt{\alpha \, t}$

Their ratio gives:

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} \sim \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\nu}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{Pr}}$$

This produces three critical regimes:

| Regime | Prandtl Value | Boundary Layer Behavior | Example Fluids |
|--------|---------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| Very Low Pr | Pr < 0.1 | $\delta_t \gg \delta_v$ (thermal layer far thicker) | Liquid metals (Hg, Na, K) |
| Low Pr | 0.1 < Pr < 1.0 | $\delta_t > \delta_v$ | Air (0.7), Combustion gases |
| Pr ≈ 1 | Pr ≈ 1.0 | $\delta_t \approx \delta_v$ | Light gases, some refrigerants |
| High Pr | Pr > 1.0 | $\delta_t < \delta_v$ | Water (~7), Engine oil |
| Very High Pr | Pr > 100 | $\delta_t \ll \delta_v$ (thermal layer very thin) | Heavy oils, glycerin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Fire Science:** The Prandtl number is the key input to all convective heat transfer correlations (Nusselt number correlations) of the form $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$. It determines whether the **fire plume's thermal field** is dominated by conduction (low Pr) or convection (high Pr).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units of Result |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Pr = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Primary definition (ratio of diffusivities) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$ | Working definition (measurable properties) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity relation | m²/s |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, C_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity relation | m²/s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{Pr}}$ | Boundary layer thickness ratio | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, u \, L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (companion to Pr) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $Pe = Re \cdot Pr = \dfrac{u \, L}{\alpha}$ | Peclet number (combined convection) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Nu = \dfrac{h \, L}{k}$ | Nusselt number (heat transfer driver) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $Pr_{air} \approx 0.71$ | Standard value for air at 300 K | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $Pr_{water} \approx 7.0$ | Standard value for water at 293 K | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Never write the absolute value bars `|x|` inside KTU table cells. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to preserve the markdown table structure.

### Dimensional Verification

A frequent KTU question is "Prove that Pr is dimensionless." Here is the dimensional check:

$$[Pr] = \frac{[\mu][C_p]}{[k]} = \frac{\left(\frac{kg}{m \cdot s}\right)\left(\frac{J}{kg \cdot K}\right)}{\left(\frac{W}{m \cdot K}\right)}$$

Since $J = W \cdot s$, the numerator becomes $\frac{W}{m \cdot K}$, which cancels exactly with the denominator. Therefore, **[Pr] = 1** (dimensionless).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Equivalence of the Two Forms of Pr

**Step 1 — Start from the kinematic-to-thermal diffusivity definition.**

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard definitions of $\nu$ and $\alpha$.**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, C_p}$$

**Step 3 — Plug both into the Prandtl expression.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu / \rho}{k / (\rho \, C_p)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \cdot \frac{\rho \, C_p}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the density term $\rho$.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$$

This proves the equivalence of the two common forms. QED.

### Derivation 2: Boundary Layer Thickness Ratio

**Step 1 — Recall the diffusion length scale for each process.**

For a parcel of fluid in contact with a surface for time $t$:

$$\delta_v \propto \sqrt{\nu \, t} \quad \text{(velocity BL)}$$

$$\delta_t \propto \sqrt{\alpha \, t} \quad \text{(thermal BL)}$$

This follows from dimensional analysis of the unsteady diffusion equation.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of thicknesses.**

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha \, t}}{\sqrt{\nu \, t}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common time term $t$.**

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\nu}}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the ratio is the inverse square root of Pr.**

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{Pr}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{Pr}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.** 

For air at 300 K, $Pr = 0.71$:

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.71}} \approx 1.19$$

This means the thermal boundary layer is about **19% thicker** than the velocity boundary layer — a key input for modeling ceiling jet flows and fire plumes.

### Derivation 3: Dimensional Homogeneity Proof

**Step 1 — Expand each variable into its base SI dimensions.**

$$[\mu] = M L^{-1} T^{-1}, \quad [C_p] = L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}, \quad [k] = M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Form the numerator dimensions.**

$$[\mu \cdot C_p] = (M L^{-1} T^{-1}) \cdot (L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}) = M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with denominator dimensions.**

$$[k] = M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.**

$$[Pr] = \frac{M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}}{M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}} = 1$$

The dimensions cancel completely, confirming **Pr is dimensionless**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Boundary Layer Regimes by Prandtl Number

This flowchart maps the physical regimes of thermal-versus-momentum diffusion based on the Prandtl number value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Prandtl Number Pr"] --> B{"Compare Pr with 1.0"}
    B -- "Pr much less than 1" --> C["LOW Pr REGIME\nPr less than 0.1\nLiquid Metals Mercury Sodium"]
    C --> C1["Thermal BL much thicker than velocity BL\nHeat outruns momentum diffusion"]
    B -- "Pr approximately 1" --> D["UNITY REGIME\nPr between 0.1 and 1.0\nAir Combustion Gases Pr 0.7"]
    D --> D1["Thermal BL and velocity BL comparable\nMost fire and atmospheric flows"]
    B -- "Pr much greater than 1" --> E["HIGH Pr REGIME\nPr greater than 1.0\nWater Oils Glycerin"]
    E --> E1["Thermal BL much thinner than velocity BL\nMomentum outruns heat diffusion"]
    C1 --> F["Engineering Implication\nChoose Nusselt correlation carefully"]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G["Apply in fire plume\nand smoke layer analysis"]
```

### Diagram 2: Functional Block Architecture of the Prandtl Number

This diagram shows the hierarchical "block" relationship between Prandtl number, its parent dimensionless groups, and its role in convective heat transfer correlations used in fire engineering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MOL FLUID PROPERTIES\nINPUT BLOCK"] --> A1["Dynamic Viscosity mu\nkg per m s"]
    A --> A2["Specific Heat Cp\nJ per kg K"]
    A --> A3["Thermal Conductivity k\nW per m K"]
    A1 --> B["PRANDTL NUMBER Pr\nDimensionless Group Block"]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4["Density rho\nkg per m cubed"] --> B
    A4 --> C["REYNOLDS NUMBER Re\nDimensionless Group Block"]
    A5["Velocity u m per s\nLength L m"] --> C
    C --> D["PECLET NUMBER Pe\nDimensionless Combined Block"]
    B --> D
    D --> E["NUSSELT NUMBER Nu\nHeat Transfer Block"]
    A3 --> E
    A5 --> E
    E --> F["CONVECTIVE HEAT FLUX q\nOUTPUT BLOCK\nW per m squared"]
    B --> G["BOUNDARY LAYER RATIO\ndelta t over delta v equals 1 over square root Pr"]
    G --> H["FIRE PLUME MODEL\nSmoke Layer Analysis"]
    E --> H
```

### Diagram 3: Dimensional Reduction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["mu Cp"] --> B["Numerator: M L T^-3 Theta^-1"]
    C["k"] --> D["Denominator: M L T^-3 Theta^-1"]
    B --> E["Ratio Pr equals 1"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["DIMENSIONLESS CONFIRMED\nSI Base Units Cancel Completely"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PESFT528]**
**Define the Prandtl Number. State its physical significance in the context of convective heat transfer during fire propagation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Prandtl Number (Pr) is defined as the ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity of a fluid:

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$$

**Physical Significance in Fire Propagation:**
- It is a dimensionless fluid property that indicates the **relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers** in fire-driven flows.
- For typical fire scenarios in air (Pr ≈ 0.7), the thermal boundary layer is slightly thicker than the velocity boundary layer ($\delta_t / \delta_v \approx 1.19$).
- This ratio controls the rate of heat transfer from burning surfaces, flame sheets, and hot smoke layers to the surrounding environment.
- It is a key input to convective heat transfer correlations (Nusselt number equations) used in fire plume and ceiling jet modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks distribution: Definition (1.5) + Physical significance (1.5) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Prove that the Prandtl Number is dimensionless. List any two fluids with their approximate Prandtl Number values at standard conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dimensional Proof:**

$$[Pr] = \frac{[\mu][C_p]}{[k]} = \frac{\left(\frac{kg}{m \cdot s}\right)\left(\frac{J}{kg \cdot K}\right)}{\left(\frac{W}{m \cdot K}\right)}$$

Since $1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ W} \cdot \text{s}$, the numerator reduces to $\frac{W}{m \cdot K}$, which is dimensionally identical to the denominator. Hence $[Pr] = 1$, confirming it is dimensionless.

**Sample Fluid Values:**
- **Air at 300 K: Pr ≈ 0.71**
- **Water at 293 K: Pr ≈ 7.0**
- (Also acceptable: Engine Oil Pr ≈ 100–1000; Liquid Mercury Pr ≈ 0.024)

> [!NOTE]
> Marks distribution: Proof (2.0) + Two fluid values (1.0) = **3 marks**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the velocity and thermal boundary layer thicknesses in terms of the Prandtl Number. (7 marks)

**(b)** For air at 300 K, the following data is given: $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.0263$ W/(m·K). Calculate the Prandtl Number and comment on the dominant diffusion mechanism. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a fluid parcel in contact with a hot surface for time $t$. By dimensional analysis of the unsteady diffusion equations:

- Velocity boundary layer thickness: $\delta_v \propto \sqrt{\nu \, t}$
- Thermal boundary layer thickness: $\delta_t \propto \sqrt{\alpha \, t}$

Forming the ratio:

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha \, t}}{\sqrt{\nu \, t}} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\nu}}$$

Recognizing that $Pr = \nu / \alpha$:

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta_v} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{Pr}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{Pr}}$$

This is the required relationship. **(7 marks — full step-wise allocation: 2 for stating BL scalings, 3 for ratio manipulation, 2 for final result)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s); $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K); $k = 0.0263$ W/(m·K).

**Step 1 — Apply the working definition of Pr.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$Pr = \frac{(1.846 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1005)}{0.0263}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$(1.846 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1005) = 1.85523 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$Pr = \frac{1.85523 \times 10^{-2}}{0.0263} \approx 0.705$$

**Step 5 — Comment on dominant mechanism.**

Since Pr < 1 (specifically Pr ≈ 0.705), **momentum diffusivity is slightly less than thermal diffusivity**, meaning heat diffuses marginally faster than momentum. The thermal boundary layer is approximately 19% thicker than the velocity boundary layer. This is characteristic of air and most combustion gases, and is the standard regime for fire plume analysis. **(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for evaluation, 3 for interpretation)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the Prandtl Number in classifying fluids for heat transfer applications. Give one example each of low-Pr, unity-Pr, and high-Pr fluids. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a fire scenario, smoke flows over a hot ceiling at 1.5 m/s. The ceiling is 10 m long. The smoke properties are: $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $C_p = 1100$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.030$ W/(m·K). Compute the Reynolds Number, Prandtl Number, and Peclet Number. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Classification and Examples (7 marks):**

The Prandtl Number classifies fluids based on the relative rates of momentum and thermal diffusion, which is critical in selecting appropriate convective heat transfer correlations.

- **Low-Pr Fluids (Pr < 0.1):** Heat diffuses much faster than momentum. Thermal boundary layer is much thicker. **Example: Liquid Mercury (Pr ≈ 0.024)** — used in nuclear reactor coolants and specialized high-heat-flux applications.

- **Unity-Pr Fluids (0.1 < Pr < 1):** Thermal and momentum diffusivities are comparable. **Example: Air (Pr ≈ 0.7)** — the dominant regime for fire, atmospheric, and HVAC flows.

- **High-Pr Fluids (Pr > 1):** Momentum diffuses faster than heat. Thermal boundary layer is thin and tightly bound to the hot surface. **Example: Water (Pr ≈ 7.0)** — used in fire sprinkler cooling, quench tanks, and water-mist suppression.

This classification determines whether conduction or convection dominates heat transfer from a fire-heated surface. **(7 marks: 3 for classification logic, 4 for examples)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $u = 1.5$ m/s; $L = 10$ m; $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³; $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s); $C_p = 1100$ J/(kg·K); $k = 0.030$ W/(m·K).

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, u \, L}{\mu} = \frac{(1.2)(1.5)(10)}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{18.0}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.0 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Prandtl Number.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(1100)}{0.030} = \frac{0.022}{0.030} \approx 0.733$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Peclet Number.**

$$Pe = Re \times Pr = (9.0 \times 10^{5}) \times (0.733) \approx 6.60 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The very high Peclet number (Pe ≫ 1) indicates that **convective transport dominates over molecular (conductive) diffusion** in this ceiling jet scenario. The Prandtl number near 0.73 confirms smoke behaves like air in terms of boundary layer ratios. **(7 marks: 2 for Re, 2 for Pr, 2 for Pe, 1 for interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently omit units in intermediate steps. Always carry units explicitly until the final dimensionless result.
> 2. **Forgetting the $C_p$ term (–2 marks):** A common mistake is using $Pr = \mu/k$ (which is INCORRECT). The full expression $Pr = \mu C_p / k$ must be used.
> 3. **Confusing Pr with Pe or Nu (–2 marks):** Remember — Pr is a **fluid property only**; Pe and Nu depend on flow geometry and velocity.
> 4. **Missing "physical significance" (–1 to –3 marks):** In 3-mark and 14-mark questions, ALWAYS conclude with a physical interpretation. A numerical answer without a comment on boundary layer behavior loses significant marks.
> 5. **Boundary Layer Direction Confusion (–2 marks):** Low Pr → thermal BL is THICKER (not thinner). High Pr → thermal BL is THINNER. Memorize: $\delta_t / \delta_v = 1/\sqrt{Pr}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Prandtl Number:**

- [x] **Definition:** $Pr = \nu / \alpha = \mu C_p / k$ — **dimensionless**, pure fluid property.
- [x] **Physical meaning:** Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
- [x] **Key boundary layer result:** $\delta_t / \delta_v = 1 / \sqrt{Pr}$.
- [x] **Air at 300 K:** $Pr \approx 0.71$ — the canonical fire engineering value.
- [x] **Water:** $Pr \approx 7$ — high-Pr fluid used in fire suppression.
- [x] **Liquid metals:** $Pr \ll 1$ — very low, used in high-heat-flux cooling.
- [x] **Companion numbers:** $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$ (Peclet) and $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$ (Nusselt).
- [x] **Dimensional check:** $\mu C_p$ has units of W/(m·K), identical to $k$ — confirming dimensionless nature.
- [x] **For fire plumes and smoke layers:** Always assume air properties unless otherwise stated ($Pr \approx 0.7$).
- [x] **Common KTU pitfall:** Never confuse Pr (fluid property) with Re or Pe (flow properties).
- [x] **Boundary layer rule:** Pr < 1 → thick thermal layer; Pr > 1 → thin thermal layer.
- [x] **Engineering role:** Pr is the **bridge parameter** linking momentum transport to heat transport in all convective fire calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmidt Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Schmidt Number ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Schmidt Number (Sc)** is a fundamental **dimensionless number** in transport phenomena and fire dynamics that quantifies the relative effectiveness of **momentum transport** (viscous diffusion) compared to **mass transport** (molecular diffusion) in a flowing fluid. It is formally defined as the ratio of the **kinematic viscosity** ($\nu$) to the **mass diffusivity** ($D_{AB}$): $$\text{Sc} = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{\mu / \rho}{D_{AB}}$$ Where: - $\nu$ = **kinematic viscosity** [m²/s] — momentum diffusivity - $D_{AB}$ = **mass diffusivity** of species A in B [m²/s] - $\mu$ = **dynamic viscosity** of the fluid [Pa·s or kg/(m·s)] - $\rho$ = **density** of the fluid [kg/m³] > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Schmidt number is the **mass-transfer analog** of the Prandtl number (which compares momentum to thermal diffusivity). In fire science, it is the **governing parameter** that controls the rate at which fuel vapors, oxygen, smoke particles, and combustion products spread through the buoyant fire plume. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Momentum vs. Mass Memory" Picture Imagine a crowd of people jogging through a foggy park: - The **momentum diffusivity** ($\nu$) is like how quickly the joggers' running pattern (speed, direction) evens out across the crowd. If a fast runner passes by, the surrounding joggers gradually pick up the pace — this is momentum diffusion. - The **mass diffusivity** ($D_{AB}$) is like how quickly the **smell of the jogger's perfume** spreads outward through the fog. Even if the jogger has stopped moving, the scent molecules still wander and mix. The **Schmidt Number** asks the question: *"Which spreads faster — the tendency of the fluid to smooth out its motion, or the tendency of its chemical species to spread out?"* - If **Sc ≫ 1** → momentum diffuses much faster than mass → species tend to remain concentrated in thin filaments (smoke trails linger in fire plumes). - If **Sc ≪ 1** → mass diffuses much faster than momentum → species spread almost uniformly throughout the flow. - If **Sc ≈ 1** → momentum and mass diffuse at comparable rates (e.g., most gases in air at room temperature, where Sc ≈ **0.6 – 0.8**). > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics for Fire Gases:** > - For most **gas-phase species in air** at standard conditions: **Sc ≈ 0.7** (commonly used in fire CFD codes like FDS). > - For **smoke aerosols/particulates** (e.g., soot, ash): **Sc ranges from 0.5 to 2.0**, often taken as **Sc = 1** in many fire engineering models. > - For **liquids** (e.g., fuel pool evaporation): **Sc can be very high (100 – 10,000)** because mass diffusivity in liquids is tiny. ### 1.3 KTU Board-Critical Definition Callout > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Standard Definition (verbatim memorization required):** > *"The Schmidt number is the ratio of the momentum diffusivity (kinematic viscosity) to the mass diffusivity, and represents the ratio of the rate of momentum transfer by molecular motion to the rate of mass transfer by molecular motion in a fluid system."* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Significance — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown The Schmidt number emerges naturally from the dimensionless form of the **convective-diffusive transport equation** for a chemical species. The reasoning proceeds as follows: 1. **Transport by Convection:** Fluid parcels carry species with their bulk motion — characterized by velocity $U$ and length scale $L$. 2. **Transport by Diffusion (Mass):** Molecular agitation causes species to spread — characterized by $D_{AB}$ and gradient $\partial C / \partial x$. 3. **Dimensionless Analysis:** The species conservation equation, when non-dimensionalized, contains a ratio of the **convective time scale** ($L/U$) to the **diffusive time scale** ($L^2 / D_{AB}$), yielding the **Peclet number** for mass transfer, $Pe_m = UL/D_{AB}$. 4. **Role of Sc:** Since $Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc$, the Schmidt number is the missing link that ties Reynolds number (inertial vs. viscous) to mass transport. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Equation / Parameter** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **Typical Range in Fire** | |---|---|---|---| | Schmidt Number (definition) | $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ | Momentum / Mass diffusivity ratio | Gases: **0.6 – 0.8** | | In terms of viscosity | $Sc = \mu / (\rho \cdot D_{AB})$ | Direct measurable form | Aerosols: **0.5 – 2.0** | | Mass Peclet Number | $Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc$ | Convection vs. mass diffusion | $Pe_m = UL/D_{AB}$ | | Reynolds Number | $Re = UL/\nu$ | Inertia vs. viscous forces | Plume: $Re \sim 10^3 - 10^5$ | | Lewis Number | $Le = \alpha / D_{AB}$ | Thermal / mass diffusivity ratio | Gases: $Le \approx 1$ | | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \nu / \alpha$ | Momentum / thermal diffusivity | Gases: $Pr \approx 0.7$ | | Sc–Pr–Le Relation | $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$ | Triangular coupling | $0.7 \approx 0.7 \cdot 1$ | | Mass Transfer Coefficient (analog to Nu) | $Sh = (f/8)(Re - 1000) \cdot Sc \cdot [1 + (d/L)^{2/3}]$ | Chilton–Colburn for forced convection | Engineering tool | | Sherwood Number | $Sh = h_m L / D_{AB}$ | Convective / diffusive mass flux | Dimensionless flux | | Kinematic Viscosity (air, 300 K) | $\nu \approx 1.57 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s | Momentum diffusivity of air | Standard atmospheric | | Mass Diffusivity (typical gas in air) | $D_{AB} \approx 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s | Smoke/vapor diffusivity | Standard atmospheric | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Triangular Coupling Theorem:** In fire dynamics, the three numbers $Sc$, $Pr$, and $Le$ are not independent. The product relation $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$ is a **mandatory result** that students must reproduce in Part B derivations. It is the "Rosetta Stone" linking momentum, heat, and mass transfer. ### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Fire Protection In real-world fire engineering, the Schmidt number is not an academic curiosity — it is a **directly measurable and tabulated input** for: - **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) codes** such as **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)**: A Sc value must be assigned to every smoke/aerosol species to compute turbulent mass transport correctly. - **Smoke detector placement**: Smoke spread rates through corridors depend on the effective Sc of the aerosol. - **Ventilation design**: Mass transfer of CO, HCN, and soot through openings is Sc-dependent. - **Flame spread over solids**: Fuel vapor diffusion from the pyrolysis zone is governed by the local Sc. - **Pool fire evaporation**: Liquid fuel vaporization (high Sc regime) controls burning rate. ### 2.4 Why Schmidt Number Matters in Fire Plumes A buoyant fire plume is a **buoyancy-driven, turbulent, reacting flow**. Three transport mechanisms compete: 1. **Buoyancy** (drives the flow vertically due to hot gases being lighter). 2. **Momentum** (entrainment of surrounding air — characterized by $\nu$). 3. **Mass transfer** (smoke, unburned fuel, oxygen — characterized by $D_{AB}$). The **Sc number controls the third process** relative to the second. If Sc is high, the plume is "sharp" — concentrated fuel and smoke cores with sharp edges. If Sc is low, the plume is "diffuse" — well-mixed smoke and air. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Schmidt Number from the Species Conservation Equation The starting point is the steady-state, one-dimensional convective-diffusive species equation for species A: $$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 1:** Introduce dimensionless variables to make the equation parameter-free. Let $$\tilde{x} = \frac{x}{L}, \quad \tilde{C}_A = \frac{C_A}{C_0}, \quad \tilde{u} = \frac{u}{U}$$ where $L$ is a characteristic length, $C_0$ is a reference concentration, and $U$ is a reference velocity. **Step 2:** Substitute these into the original equation: $$\frac{U C_0}{L} \tilde{u} \frac{\partial \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}} = \frac{D_{AB} C_0}{L^2} \frac{\partial^2 \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}^2}$$ **Step 3:** Divide through by the convective coefficient $(U C_0 / L)$: $$\tilde{u} \frac{\partial \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}} = \frac{D_{AB}}{U L} \frac{\partial^2 \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}^2}$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the right-hand coefficient as the inverse of the **mass Peclet number**: $$Pe_m = \frac{U L}{D_{AB}}$$ **Step 5:** The mass Peclet number can be split using the Reynolds number identity: $$Pe_m = \frac{U L}{D_{AB}} = \frac{U L}{\nu} \cdot \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = Re \cdot Sc$$ **Step 6:** Therefore, by direct identification: $$\boxed{Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}}$$ **Interpretation:** Sc is the **proportionality constant** that, when multiplied by the Reynolds number, gives the mass Peclet number — i.e., the ratio of convective to diffusive mass transport. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Triangular Relation $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$ This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that students are routinely asked to produce. **Step 1:** Start from the definitions of the three numbers: $$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}, \quad Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}, \quad Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}$$ where $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity**. **Step 2:** Multiply Prandtl and Lewis numbers: $$Pr \cdot Le = \left(\frac{\nu}{\alpha}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}\right)$$ **Step 3:** The $\alpha$ terms cancel out: $$Pr \cdot Le = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$$ **Step 4:** By the definition of the Schmidt number, the right-hand side is exactly $Sc$: $$\boxed{Sc = Pr \cdot Le}$$ **Physical Meaning:** This identity is profound — it shows that **momentum, heat, and mass transport are not three independent processes**. If you know any two of $(Sc, Pr, Le)$, the third is fixed. For most combustion gases, $Pr \approx Le \approx 0.7$, giving $Sc \approx 0.49$ to $0.7$ depending on the species. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Calculation of Sc for Smoke Aerosols (Chilton-Colburn Analogy) For a fire engineering calculation of smoke deposition, the **Chilton–Colburn analogy** is used: $$j_H \approx j_M, \quad \text{where} \quad j_M = \frac{Sc^{2/3}}{Re}$$ **Step 1:** The mass transfer coefficient $h_m$ relates to the Sherwood number: $$Sh = \frac{h_m L}{D_{AB}}$$ **Step 2:** For turbulent flow inside a duct (e.g., smoke-filled corridor), the Dittus-Boelter type correlation for mass transfer is: $$Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{0.4}$$ **Step 3:** For air at 300 K, take $\nu = 1.57 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and $D_{AB} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s for smoke particulates: $$Sc = \frac{1.57 \times 10^{-5}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.785$$ **Step 4:** Therefore, the Schmidt number for smoke in air under standard fire conditions is: $$\boxed{Sc_{\text{smoke}} \approx 0.78 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$ This value is the **default input** in FDS for most smoke transport simulations. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style **Problem:** A flame spreading over a liquid fuel pool has a characteristic velocity of 0.05 m/s, a length scale of 0.02 m, and the fuel vapor has a mass diffusivity of $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s in air. The kinematic viscosity of the hot gas is $4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the **Schmidt number** and the **mass Peclet number**. **Step 1:** Calculate the Schmidt number: $$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.0$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the Reynolds number: $$Re = \frac{U L}{\nu} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.02}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 25$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the mass Peclet number: $$Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc = 25 \times 4.0 = 100$$ **Step 4:** Physical interpretation: - $Sc = 4 > 1$ → momentum diffuses 4 times faster than the fuel vapor. The vapor trails are concentrated. - $Pe_m = 100 \gg 1$ → convection dominates mass transport by 100:1. The vapor is carried downstream before it can diffuse much. This is the regime characteristic of **diffusion flames** in fire spread over liquid pools. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Coupling of Momentum, Heat, and Mass Transfer ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Fluid Parcel
carrying fuel vapor,
heat, and momentum"] --> B{"Transport
Mechanism?"} B -- "Bulk motion
carries everything" --> C["Convective Transport
U * grad C"] B -- "Molecular agitation
spreads quantities" --> D["Diffusive Transport"] D --> E["Momentum
Diffusivity = nu"] D --> F["Thermal
Diffusivity = alpha"] D --> G["Mass
Diffusivity = D_AB"] E --> H["Pr = nu / alpha
Prandtl Number"] F --> I["Le = alpha / D_AB
Lewis Number"] G --> J["Sc = nu / D_AB
Schmidt Number"] H --> K["Coupled Transport
Identity"] I --> K J --> K K --> L["Sc = Pr x Le
Triangular Theorem"] L --> M["Fire Plume Behavior
Sharp vs Diffuse Smoke"] L --> N["CFD Input
FDS Solver"] L --> O["Smoke Detector
Siting"] L --> P["Ventilation
Design"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Dimensionless Numbers in Fire Dynamics ```mermaid graph TD Root["Dimensionless Numbers
in Fire Dynamics"] --> Mom["Momentum Transport"] Root --> Heat["Heat Transport"] Root --> Mass["Mass Transport"] Mom --> Re["Reynolds Number Re
Inertia / Viscous"] Mom --> Fr["Froude Number Fr
Inertia / Buoyancy"] Heat --> Pr["Prandtl Number Pr
Momentum / Thermal"] Heat --> Nu["Nusselt Number Nu
Convection / Conduction"] Mass --> Sc["Schmidt Number Sc
Momentum / Mass"] Mass --> Sh["Sherwood Number Sh
Convection / Diffusion"] Mass --> Le["Lewis Number Le
Thermal / Mass"] Pr --> Identity["Triangular Identity
Sc = Pr x Le"] Le --> Identity Sc --> Identity Identity --> Eng["Engineering Output
Smoke spread, flame
geometry, toxicity"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Schmidt Number in the Fire Plume ```mermaid flowchart LR Plume["Buoyant Fire Plume"] --> M1["Hot gases rise
Buoyancy driven"] Plume --> M2["Air entrainment
Driven by velocity"] Plume --> M3["Smoke aerosol
transport"] M1 --> Q1["Governed by Fr"] M2 --> Q2["Governed by Re"] M3 --> Q3["Governed by Sc"] Q3 --> R1["Sc >> 1
Sharp smoke boundaries
Filamentary mixing"] Q3 --> R2["Sc approx 1
Gases in air
Diffuse smoke"] Q3 --> R3["Sc << 1
Unusual regime
Instant mixing"] R1 --> Out["FDS default Sc = 0.7
or 1.0 for aerosols"] R2 --> Out R3 --> Out ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These flow maps emphasize that the **Schmidt number is the bridge** between momentum (Re) and mass (Sh) transport. Without it, no closed-form fire model can be solved. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Define the Schmidt number. State its mathematical expression and physical significance in fire dynamics.** **Model Answer:** The **Schmidt number (Sc)** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of **momentum diffusivity (kinematic viscosity, $\nu$)** to **mass diffusivity ($D_{AB}$)** of a chemical species in a fluid: $$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$$ **Physical significance in fire dynamics:** It quantifies the relative rates at which **momentum** and **mass** are transported by molecular motion in a fire plume or compartment flow. A high Sc (e.g., for liquid fuel vapors) indicates that momentum spreads faster than mass, producing **sharp, concentrated smoke and fuel vapor trails**. A low Sc (Sc ≈ 0.7 for gases in air) implies momentum and mass diffuse at similar rates, producing **diffuse, well-mixed smoke**. It is a critical input parameter in CFD fire codes like **FDS** for accurate simulation of smoke spread, fuel vapor dispersion, and toxic species transport. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **State and prove the relation between Schmidt, Prandtl, and Lewis numbers.** **Model Answer:** The **triangular relation** is: $$Sc = Pr \cdot Le$$ **Proof:** $$Pr \cdot Le = \left(\frac{\nu}{\alpha}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}\right) = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = Sc$$ Hence, $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$ (proved). This shows that momentum, heat, and mass transport in a fire flow are not independent — knowing any two dimensionless numbers fixes the third. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the Schmidt number starting from the convective-diffusive species conservation equation. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a compartment fire scenario, the following data are given: - Kinematic viscosity of hot smoke layer: $\nu = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - Mass diffusivity of CO in air: $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - Characteristic velocity: $U = 0.4$ m/s - Characteristic length (compartment height): $L = 3.0$ m Calculate the **Schmidt number**, **Reynolds number**, and **mass Peclet number**. Comment on the dominant transport regime. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Derivation:** Starting from the steady, 1-D convective-diffusive species equation: $$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$ Introduce dimensionless variables: $\tilde{x} = x/L$, $\tilde{C}_A = C_A / C_0$, $\tilde{u} = u/U$. Substituting: $$\frac{U C_0}{L} \tilde{u} \frac{\partial \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}} = \frac{D_{AB} C_0}{L^2} \frac{\partial^2 \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}^2}$$ Dividing both sides by the convective coefficient $(U C_0 / L)$: $$\tilde{u} \frac{\partial \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}} = \frac{D_{AB}}{U L} \frac{\partial^2 \tilde{C}_A}{\partial \tilde{x}^2}$$ The right-hand side coefficient is the **inverse mass Peclet number** $1/Pe_m$, where $Pe_m = UL / D_{AB}$. Splitting $Pe_m$: $$Pe_m = \frac{UL}{D_{AB}} = \frac{UL}{\nu} \cdot \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = Re \cdot Sc$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}}$$ The Schmidt number thus emerges as the link between Re (momentum) and $Pe_m$ (mass transport). **(Full marks: 7)** --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Schmidt number:** $$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.4$$ **Step 2 — Reynolds number:** $$Re = \frac{UL}{\nu} = \frac{0.4 \times 3.0}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.2}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Mass Peclet number:** $$Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc = 20{,}000 \times 2.4 = 48{,}000$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** - $Sc = 2.4 > 1$ → momentum diffuses 2.4 times faster than CO mass → **smoke and CO are concentrated** in narrow zones. - $Re = 20{,}000$ → flow is **fully turbulent**. - $Pe_m = 48{,}000 \gg 1$ → **convection dominates mass transport by a factor of 48,000**. The CO is carried by bulk flow far faster than it can diffuse. This is the regime of a **smoke-logged compartment** — the bulk hot layer moves as a coherent slab, with very little molecular CO mixing out of it. **(Full marks: 7)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]` **(a)** State the definitions of Schmidt, Prandtl, and Lewis numbers. Derive the triangular identity relating them. Discuss the physical meaning of the identity in the context of fire propagation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A diffusion flame over a heptane pool has the following parameters: - Air kinematic viscosity: $\nu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - Heptane vapor mass diffusivity in air: $D_{AB} = 0.7 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - Thermal diffusivity of the combustion gases: $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - Characteristic flame height: $L = 0.5$ m - Plume velocity: $U = 1.2$ m/s Compute the **Schmidt number**, **Lewis number**, **Prandtl number**, and **mass Peclet number**. Comment on the implications for the diffusion flame. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Definitions and Derivation:** - **Schmidt number:** $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ — ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity. - **Prandtl number:** $Pr = \nu / \alpha$ — ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity. - **Lewis number:** $Le = \alpha / D_{AB}$ — ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity. **Triangular identity:** $$Pr \cdot Le = \left(\frac{\nu}{\alpha}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}\right) = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = Sc$$ **Physical meaning in fire propagation:** The identity establishes that in any fire flow, the three transport processes (momentum, heat, mass) are **mutually coupled**. The Schmidt number cannot be tuned independently of Pr and Le. For a typical diffusion flame, $Pr \approx Le \approx 0.7$, giving $Sc \approx 0.5$. This coupling implies that buoyancy-driven flows (which carry momentum) also automatically drive heat and mass transport in a fixed proportion. The flame shape, smoke pattern, and flame height all derive their structure from this underlying triangular coupling. **(7 marks)** --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Schmidt number:** $$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{0.7 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.57$$ **Step 2 — Lewis number:** $$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.7 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.57$$ **Step 3 — Prandtl number:** $$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.72$$ **Step 4 — Verify triangular identity:** $$Pr \cdot Le = 0.72 \times 3.57 = 2.57 \approx Sc \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Reynolds number:** $$Re = \frac{UL}{\nu} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.5}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 33{,}333$$ **Step 6 — Mass Peclet number:** $$Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc = 33{,}333 \times 2.57 = 85{,}666$$ **Step 7 — Implications for the diffusion flame:** - $Sc = 2.57 > 1$ → heptane vapor trails are **concentrated**; the fuel jet is sharp. - $Le = 3.57 > 1$ → heat diffuses much faster than fuel vapor → the flame is **buoyancy-dominated** and elongated. - $Pr = 0.72 \approx 0.7$ → standard value for air — consistent with textbook values. - $Pe_m \sim 10^5$ → **convection strongly dominates** — this is a **non-premixed (diffusion) flame** with a thin reaction zone where vapor and air meet. The flame structure is therefore **lifted and elongated** (Le > 1 means heat outruns fuel), typical of large hydrocarbon pool fires. **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in the Board Exam:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently.** If $\nu$ is in cm²/s and $D_{AB}$ is in m²/s, the Sc calculation will be off by a factor of 10,000. Always use **SI units** (m²/s for both). > > 2. **Confusing Sc with Le or Pr.** Students routinely write $Sc = \alpha/D_{AB}$ (which is actually $Le$) or $Sc = \nu/\alpha$ (which is $Pr$). Memorize the **anchors**: "**S**chmidt = **S**pecies/mass; momentum/mass" → $Sc = \nu/D_{AB}$. > > 3. **Skipping the derivation of the triangular identity.** This is the **most-asked Part B sub-question** in KTU Module 4. The two-line proof $Pr \cdot Le = (\nu/\alpha)(\alpha/D_{AB}) = \nu/D_{AB} = Sc$ must be shown explicitly. > > 4. **Forgetting to add a physical interpretation.** Numerical answers without comments on "what does this mean for the fire?" lose up to 2 marks per sub-part. > > 5. **Wrong direction of identity.** Some students write $Pr = Sc \cdot Le$ instead of $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$. The board key explicitly demands the form $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ — ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity. *(Always SI units.)* - **Typical values:** Gases in air → **Sc ≈ 0.7**; smoke aerosols → **Sc ≈ 0.5 – 2.0**; liquids → **Sc ≫ 1**. - **Triangular identity (HIGH PRIORITY):** $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$ — must be memorizable and derivable in under 30 seconds. - **Coupling to mass Peclet number:** $Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc$ — links inertia, viscosity, and mass transport. - **CFD significance:** Sc is a direct input to **FDS** for smoke/aerosol transport — wrong Sc → wrong smoke spread prediction. - **Fire plume regimes:** Sc > 1 → sharp smoke; Sc ≈ 1 → diffuse smoke; Sc < 1 → near-instant mixing. - **Dimensionless consistency:** $Sc$, $Pr$, $Le$ are all **dimensionless** — a unit mismatch in their components (m²/s, m²/s, m²/s) always cancels. - **Chilton–Colburn analogy:** $j_H \approx j_M$ relates heat and mass transfer through $Pr^{2/3} \approx Sc^{2/3}$ at equal $Re$. - **Sherwood vs Nusselt:** $Sh = h_m L / D_{AB}$ is the **mass-transfer analog** of $Nu = h L / k$ — both appear in fire heat/mass correlations. - **The $Sc = Pr \cdot Le$ identity is non-negotiable** — it has appeared in **every KTU Dec/May exam since 2021** for this module.

topics.title: Chemical Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Chemical Time (τ_chem)** is a fundamental characteristic time scale in combustion and fire dynamics that quantifies the temporal duration required for the chemical reactions (primarily oxidation of fuel by an oxidizer) to reach completion within a given reaction zone. It is formally defined as the ratio of the characteristic chemical reaction zone thickness ($\delta_r$) to the characteristic reaction propagation speed (typically the **laminar burning velocity**, $S_L$):

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{\delta_r}{S_L}$$

In the broader framework of fire propagation and flame structure analysis, chemical time defines the intrinsic rate at which the molecular-level chemistry proceeds, independent of the macroscopic flow or transport phenomena. It serves as one of the three critical time scales in combustion theory, alongside the **flow (residence) time** ($\tau_{flow}$) and the **diffusion (mixing) time** ($\tau_{diff}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chemical Time is the foundational metric used to construct dimensionless numbers such as the **Damköhler Number (Da)** and **Karlovitz Number (Ka)**, which collectively classify the regime of combustion — whether it is mixing-controlled or chemistry-controlled.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cooking Analogy"

Imagine you are cooking a dish (the chemical reaction) in a kitchen:

- The **recipe duration** (how long the dish takes to cook properly) is analogous to **chemical time**.
- The **time you actually have available** before the food burns (or the time your guest is willing to wait) is the **flow/residence time**.
- The **time it takes to stir the ingredients uniformly** is the **diffusion/mixing time**.

If you have a slow recipe (long $\tau_{chem}$) but stir quickly (short $\tau_{diff}$) and serve fast (short $\tau_{flow}$), the dish will be under-cooked — the chemistry cannot keep up. Conversely, if the recipe is fast but the stirring or serving is slow, the dish is well-cooked but its quality is limited by your stirring speed. This interplay is captured by the **Damköhler number**, the ratio of flow time to chemical time.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Fire Scenario) |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar Burning Velocity | $S_L$ | **0.1 – 1.0 m/s** (hydrocarbons) |
| Reaction Zone Thickness | $\delta_r$ | **0.1 – 1.0 mm** |
| Chemical Time | $\tau_{chem}$ | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ s** |
| Activation Energy (Methane) | $E_a$ | **~202 kJ/mol** |
| Pre-exponential Factor | $B$ | **~1.0 × 10¹⁰ s⁻¹** |

> [!NOTE]
> Chemical times in fires are **orders of magnitude shorter** than typical flow or mixing times, which is why most fire scenarios are considered **chemistry-controlled at the micro-scale** but **mixing-controlled at the macro-scale**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: The Three Time Scales in Combustion
Fire and combustion phenomena are characterized by the competition between three distinct temporal processes:

1. **Chemical Time ($\tau_{chem}$)** — Time for fuel and oxidizer molecules to react and form products at the molecular level.
2. **Flow/Residence Time ($\tau_{flow}$)** — Time a fluid parcel spends within the reaction zone.
3. **Diffusion/Mixing Time ($\tau_{diff}$)** — Time required for fuel and oxidizer to become molecularly mixed through molecular and turbulent diffusion.

### Step 2: Why Chemical Time Matters
The magnitude of $\tau_{chem}$ relative to the other two time scales determines the **combustion regime**:
- If $\tau_{chem} \gg \tau_{flow}$: **Flamelet regime** — chemistry is slow, the local mixture is in quasi-equilibrium.
- If $\tau_{chem} \ll \tau_{flow}$: **Well-stirred reactor / mixed regime** — chemistry is fast, mixing limits the overall rate.
- If $\tau_{chem} \approx \tau_{flow}$: **Finite-rate chemistry** — both mixing and kinetics are important.

### Step 3: The Arrhenius Foundation
The chemical time is intrinsically linked to the global reaction rate $\dot{\omega}$ given by Arrhenius kinetics:

$$\dot{\omega} = B \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right) \cdot C_f^a \cdot C_o^b$$

The inverse of the maximum reaction rate (at peak temperature) provides an order-of-magnitude estimate of the chemical time:

$$\tau_{chem} \approx \frac{1}{B \cdot \exp\left(-E_a / R_u T_{peak}\right)}$$

### Step 4: Connection to Flame Structure
In a **premixed flame**, the reaction zone has a characteristic thickness $\delta_r$, and the flame propagates through it at the laminar burning velocity $S_L$. Thus, the local time a fluid parcel spends inside the reaction zone is:

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{\delta_r}{S_L}$$

For a **diffusion (non-premixed) flame**, chemistry occurs at the interface where fuel and oxidizer meet. Here, $\tau_{chem}$ is governed by the local stoichiometry and the local Damköhler number.

### Step 5: Damköhler Number — The Master Classifier
The Damköhler number $Da$ is the dimensionless ratio of flow time to chemical time:

$$Da = \frac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}}$$

- $Da \gg 1$ — Fast chemistry (mixing-limited combustion)
- $Da \ll 1$ — Slow chemistry (kinetically-limited combustion)
- $Da \approx 1$ — Balanced regime (finite-rate)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{chem} = \delta_r / S_L$ | Chemical time from flame structure | s |
| 2 | $\tau_{chem} \approx 1/\dot{\omega}_{max}$ | Chemical time from peak reaction rate | s |
| 3 | $\dot{\omega} = B \cdot \exp(-E_a/R_uT) \cdot C_f^a C_o^b$ | Arrhenius reaction rate | kmol/m³·s |
| 4 | $Da = \tau_{flow} / \tau_{chem}$ | Damköhler Number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Ka = \tau_{chem} / \tau_{Kolmogorov}$ | Karlovitz Number | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\tau_{flow} = L / u$ | Flow time (length/velocity) | s |
| 7 | $\tau_{diff} = L^2 / D$ | Diffusion time (L²/Diffusivity) | s |
| 8 | $L_e = \alpha / D$ | Lewis Number (thermal/mass diffusivity) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\delta_L = \alpha / S_L$ | Laminar flame thickness (thermal) | m |
| 10 | $\delta_r \approx \delta_L / \sigma$ | Reaction zone thickness (with $\sigma$ = density ratio) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The chemical time framework is the foundation of computational combustion codes (e.g., **CFD-Fire simulation tools like FDS — Fire Dynamics Simulator**). Engineers use it to predict ignition delays, extinction limits, and flame stability in fire safety design of buildings, tunnels, and industrial facilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Chemical Time from Flame Structure

**Starting Premise:** Consider a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic, steady premixed flame propagating into an unburned gas mixture at the laminar burning velocity $S_L$. The reaction zone is bounded by the preheat zone and the fully burned gas region.

**Step 1:** Define the reaction zone thickness $\delta_r$ as the spatial extent over which the net chemical reaction rate $\dot{\omega}$ is non-negligible (conventionally, where $\dot{\omega} \geq 0.1 \cdot \dot{\omega}_{max}$).

**Step 2:** In the steady flame-fixed coordinate system, a fluid parcel traverses the reaction zone in a time equal to the zone thickness divided by the convective velocity (which equals $S_L$ in a stationary flame).

**Step 3:** Therefore, the chemical time is:

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{\delta_r}{S_L}$$

**Step 4:** Using the Zeldovich–Frank-Kamenetskii analysis for a global one-step reaction, the laminar flame thickness can be expressed in terms of the thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ and the laminar burning velocity:

$$\delta_L = \frac{\alpha}{S_L}$$

**Step 5:** The reaction zone thickness $\delta_r$ is typically a fraction of the total flame thickness. For many hydrocarbons:

$$\delta_r \approx \frac{\delta_L}{\sigma}$$

where $\sigma = \rho_u / \rho_b$ is the density ratio across the flame (typically $\sigma \approx 5$–$8$ for hydrocarbon-air flames).

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{\delta_r}{S_L} = \frac{\delta_L}{\sigma \cdot S_L} = \frac{\alpha}{\sigma \cdot S_L^2}$$

**Resulting Form:**

$$\boxed{\tau_{chem} = \frac{\alpha}{\sigma \cdot S_L^2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Chemical Time from Arrhenius Kinetics

**Starting Premise:** Consider a global one-step irreversible reaction: $\text{Fuel} + \nu \, \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Products}$, with rate given by Arrhenius kinetics.

**Step 1:** Write the reaction rate expression:

$$\dot{\omega} = B \cdot C_f^a \cdot C_o^b \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The chemical time is defined as the time required to consume the fuel at the rate of peak reaction. In a well-mixed reactor of characteristic length $L$, the molar consumption rate per unit volume is $\dot{\omega}$, and the initial fuel concentration is $C_{f,0}$. The chemical time (e-folding time) is:

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{C_{f,0}}{\dot{\omega}_{max}}$$

**Step 3:** At the peak reaction temperature $T_{max}$ (approximately the adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$ for a premixed flame), the maximum reaction rate is:

$$\dot{\omega}_{max} = B \cdot C_{f,0}^a \cdot C_{o,0}^b \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** For a first-order global reaction ($a = 1, b = 0$, valid for fuel-lean mixtures), this simplifies to:

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{1}{B \cdot \exp\left(-E_a / R_u T_{ad}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Expressing it in the form of a useful closed-form result:

$$\boxed{\tau_{chem} \approx \frac{1}{B} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}}\right)}$$

This is the **Zeldovich expression for chemical time** and explicitly shows that $\tau_{chem}$ is exponentially sensitive to the adiabatic flame temperature — a small increase in $T_{ad}$ causes a dramatic decrease in chemical time.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Methane-Air Premixed Flame

**Given:**
- Laminar burning velocity: $S_L = 0.4$ m/s
- Thermal diffusivity of unburned mixture: $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- Density ratio: $\sigma = \rho_u / \rho_b = 7.5$
- Activation energy: $E_a = 202$ kJ/mol
- Pre-exponential factor: $B = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ s⁻¹
- Adiabatic flame temperature: $T_{ad} = 2225$ K
- Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Part (a): Calculate $\tau_{chem}$ from flame structure.**

Using $\tau_{chem} = \alpha / (\sigma \cdot S_L^2)$:

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{7.5 \times (0.4)^2} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{7.5 \times 0.16} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{chem} = 2.083 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 20.83 \, \mu s}$$

**Part (b): Calculate $\tau_{chem}$ from Arrhenius kinetics.**

Using $\tau_{chem} = (1/B) \cdot \exp(E_a / R_u T_{ad})$:

$$\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{ad}} = \frac{202{,}000}{8.314 \times 2225} = \frac{202{,}000}{18{,}498.65} = 10.92$$

$$\exp(10.92) = 5.51 \times 10^4$$

$$\tau_{chem} = \frac{1}{1.0 \times 10^{10}} \times 5.51 \times 10^4 = 5.51 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{chem} \approx 5.51 \, \mu s}$$

**Part (c): Order-of-magnitude consistency check.**
Both methods yield $\tau_{chem}$ in the range of **5 – 25 μs**, which is physically consistent with values reported in combustion literature for methane-air flames. The two-method discrepancy (~4×) is expected because the global one-step Arrhenius model is a simplification.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Regime Classification

| Regime | $\tau_{chem}$ vs $\tau_{flow}$ | Damköhler $Da$ | Physical Behavior | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mixing-controlled (Fast chemistry)** | $\tau_{chem} \ll \tau_{flow}$ | $Da \gg 1$ | Combustion rate limited by fuel-oxidizer mixing | Turbulent diffusion flames, pool fires |
| **Finite-rate (Balanced)** | $\tau_{chem} \approx \tau_{flow}$ | $Da \approx 1$ | Both mixing and kinetics matter | Methanol pool fires, low-Re flames |
| **Kinetically-controlled (Slow chemistry)** | $\tau_{chem} \gg \tau_{flow}$ | $Da \ll 1$ | Combustion rate limited by chemical kinetics | Ignition processes, blow-off limits |
