```mermaid
    D3 --> D4[Stoichiometric Surface in Space]
    D4 --> D5[Examples: Pool Fire, Candle, Wood Fire]

    A --> ED[Damkohler Number Da Evaluation]
    ED --> ED1{Da Comparison to 1}
    ED1 -->|Da much less than 1| C
    ED1 -->|Da much greater than 1| D
    ED1 -->|Da approximately 1| MX[Mixed Control Regime]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Transport-Controlled Pool Fire Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PoolAssembly
        LIQ[Liquid Fuel Pool]
        SURF[Pool Surface T_boil]
    end

    subgraph BoundaryLayer
        VAP[Fuel Vapor Rising]
        DIFF[Conduction-Convection Zone]
    end

    subgraph FlameZone
        BURNF[Burke-Schumann Flame Sheet]
        ENT[Buoyant Plume Entrainment]
        AIR[Ambient Air Entrained]
    end

    subgraph CombustionOutputs
        HRR[Heat Release Rate]
        PROD[Combustion Products]
        RAD[Re-radiation to Surface]
    end

    LIQ --> SURF
    SURF -->|Mass flux m_doubleprime| VAP
    VAP --> DIFF
    DIFF -->|Heat flux q_doubleprime| SURF
    AIR --> ENT
    ENT --> BURNF
    VAP --> BURNF
    BURNF -->|Spalding B| HRR
    BURNF --> PROD
    BURNF --> RAD
    RAD --> SURF

    RAD -.Re-radiation feedback.-> SURF
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Kinetic-vs-Transport Control Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Classify Fire] --> Q1{Reactants premixed?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Da less than 1?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Transport Controlled Default]

    Q2 -->|Yes| KC[Kinetically Controlled]
    Q2 -->|No| MC[Mixed Control Kinetics-Transport]

    Q3 --> Q4{Froude-scaled HRR high?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| TCB[Strong Buoyancy-Driven Plume]
    Q4 -->|No| TCW[Weak Momentum-Entrainment]

    KC --> K1[Use Arrhenius + S_L]
    MC --> M1[Use combined Arrhenius-Spalding]
    TCB --> T1[Use Heskestad Zukoski correlations]
    TCW --> T2[Use forced-convection correlation]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Spalding Mass Burning Rate Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Inputs: T_boil, T_flame, T_amb, k, c_p, delta, L_v, H_v, nu] --> S1[Compute Spalding B]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Log term]
    S2 --> S3[Compute m_doubleprime]
    S3 --> S4[Multiply by Pool Area]
    S4 --> S5[Total Mass Loss Rate]
    S5 --> S6[Multiply by Delta H_c]
    S6 --> OUT[Heat Release Rate Q_dot]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the Damköhler number. Using it, distinguish between a kinetically controlled fire and a transport controlled fire.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Damköhler number is defined as the ratio of the characteristic residence (flow) time to the characteristic chemical reaction time:

$$
Da = \frac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{(L/V)}{(δ_f/S_L)}
$$

It is dimensionless. When **Da ≪ 1**, the chemical reaction is much slower than the residence time, so fuel and oxidizer leave the system unreacted if not given enough time — the **chemistry (kinetics) is rate-limiting**, and the fire is **kinetically controlled**. When **Da ≫ 1**, the chemistry is essentially instantaneous and mixing is the bottleneck, so the fire is **transport controlled**. Most accidental fires are transport controlled (Da ≫ 1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sessional II]**
*State the Spalding mass burning rate equation for a liquid pool fire. What does the parameter B represent physically?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Spalding mass burning flux for a liquid pool is:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{k}{c_p \cdot δ} \cdot \ln(1 + B)
$$

where $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the gas boundary layer, $c_p$ is the specific heat, $δ$ is the boundary layer thickness, and $B$ is the **Spalding transfer number**:

$$
B = \frac{m_o \, c_p \, (T_{flame} - T_{boil}) + m_{fg} H_v}{m_o \, L_v}
$$

Physically, $B$ represents the **ratio of the chemical energy released per unit mass of oxidizer (corrected for re-radiation and fuel vaporization) to the energy required to vaporize the fuel**. A larger $B$ corresponds to a more energetic flame and a higher mass burning rate. The presence of $k$, $c_p$, and $δ$ — and the absence of any Arrhenius kinetic constants — confirms that this correlation describes a **transport-controlled** (diffusion-limited) combustion regime.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define the Damköhler number and explain, with the aid of a labeled sketch, the structural difference between a premixed (kinetically controlled) flame and a non-premixed (transport controlled) diffusion flame. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A methane-air premixed flame has a laminar burning velocity $S_L = 0.4$ m/s, a reaction zone thickness $δ_f = 1.0$ mm, and burns in a tube of length $L = 0.5$ m at an inlet velocity $V = 5$ m/s. Compute the Damköhler number and classify the regime. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The Damköhler number is the ratio of residence time to chemical reaction time:

$$
Da = \frac{L/V}{δ_f/S_L} = \frac{L \cdot S_L}{V \cdot δ_f}
$$

**Premixed (kinetically controlled) flame:** Fuel and oxidizer are mixed *upstream* of the flame. The flame is a thin reaction sheet propagating into the unburned mixture at the laminar burning velocity $S_L$. The rate is governed by the Arrhenius reaction rate. Structure: preheat zone → reaction zone → product zone, all in series along the flow.

**Non-premixed (transport controlled) diffusion flame:** Fuel and oxidizer enter from separate streams. They mix by molecular diffusion and/or turbulent entrainment *at* the flame. The flame is pinned at the **stoichiometric surface** (Burke-Schumann) where $Y_F / Y_{O_2} = 1/\nu$. The rate is governed by the mixing rate. A typical example is a candle: wax vapor diffuses out, oxygen diffuses in, and they meet at the flame sheet.

> **Labeled schematic (text representation, since drawing on paper):**
> ```
>   PREMIXED (kinetically controlled)         NON-PREMIXED (transport controlled)
>   ──────────────────────────────             ─────────────────────────────────
>   ┌───────────────────────────┐             ┌──────────────────────────────┐
>   │ Unburned F + O2 mixture   │             │      Oxidizer (air) stream   │
>   │ (premixed upstream)       │             │   O2 ←───                    │
>   └─────────────┬─────────────┘             │                              │
>                 ▼                           │                              │
>   ┌───────────────────────────┐             │   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~  ←flame sheet│
>   │ Reaction zone (thin)      │             │   ~~~~~~ Fuel F vapor ~~~~~~│
>   │ T = T_ad, chemistry       │             │   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~              │
>   └─────────────┬─────────────┘             │                              │
>                 ▼                           │   Fuel stream (F vapor)      │
>   ┌───────────────────────────┐             └──────────────────────────────┘
>   │ Combustion products (T_ad)│
>   └───────────────────────────┘
> ```

> **Mark distribution (7 marks):** Da definition (1) + premixed description (1.5) + diffusion flame description (1.5) + sketch (2) + classification criterion (1).

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 0.5$ m
- $V = 5$ m/s
- $S_L = 0.4$ m/s
- $δ_f = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 1 — Residence (flow) time:**

$$
\tau_{flow} = \frac{L}{V} = \frac{0.5}{5} = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Chemical reaction time:**

$$
\tau_{chem} = \frac{δ_f}{S_L} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Damköhler number:**

$$
Da = \frac{\tau_{flow}}{\tau_{chem}} = \frac{0.1}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 40
$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**

Since $Da = 40 \gg 1$, the residence time is much larger than the chemical reaction time. The reaction is **40 times faster** than the transport; therefore the fire is **transport controlled**.

> **Mark distribution (7 marks):** Writing Da formula (1) + computing residence time (1.5) + computing chemical time (1.5) + computing Da (1.5) + classification with justification (1.5).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative]

**(a)** With suitable derivations, obtain the Spalding mass burning rate equation for a liquid pool fire. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular heptane pool fire of diameter $D = 1.0$ m is burning in still air. The following data are given: heptane boiling temperature $T_b = 371$ K, flame temperature $T_{flame} = 1500$ K, ambient temperature $T_\infty = 300$ K, latent heat of vaporization $L_v = 320$ kJ/kg, heat of vaporization correction $H_v = 0.5 \, L_v$, stoichiometric air-fuel ratio $\nu = 15.1$, $k = 0.025$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K), boundary layer thickness $\delta = 0.05$ m. Estimate the mass burning rate, total mass loss rate, and heat release rate (take $\Delta H_c = 44,000$ kJ/kg for heptane). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

*(The full derivation is presented in §3.1 of Section 3 above. Here is the concise KTU exam-style writeup.)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, axisymmetric, quiescent ambient.
2. One-dimensional heat and mass transfer in the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$.
3. Constant thermophysical properties ($k$, $c_p$).
4. **Transport controlled regime** — chemistry is infinitely fast; reaction occurs at the Burke-Schumann flame sheet.
5. Quasi-laminar; conduction is the dominant heat transfer mode from flame to pool.

**Derivation:**

Energy balance at the flame sheet: the heat conducted downward from the flame vaporizes the incoming fuel:

$$
k \cdot \frac{T_{flame} - T_s}{\delta} = \dot{m}'' \cdot L_v \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

Introducing the **Spalding transfer number**:

$$
B = \frac{m_o \, c_p \, (T_{flame} - T_b) + m_{fg} H_v}{m_o \, L_v} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

Integrating the boundary-layer energy equation with the source term $-\dot{m}'' c_p \, dT/dx$ across the layer gives:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{k}{c_p \, \delta} \cdot \ln(1 + B) \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

This is the **Spalding mass burning flux**. (Full intermediate steps with all substitutions as shown in §3.1.)

> **Mark distribution (7 marks):** Assumptions (2) + energy balance (2) + definition of B (1) + final equation (2).

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the Spalding B-number.**

Given: $T_b = 371$ K, $T_{flame} = 1500$ K, $T_\infty = 300$ K, $L_v = 320$ kJ/kg, $H_v = 0.5 \times 320 = 160$ kJ/kg, $\nu = 15.1$ (stoichiometric air-fuel ratio by mass).

For a diffusion flame, the relevant ratio inside $B$ uses the **mass of oxidizer per unit mass of fuel vaporized**. We take $m_o / m_{fg} = \nu = 15.1$ (air-fuel ratio), and we can simplify the Spalding B for a heptane pool (using the standard form for liquid pools):

$$
B = \frac{c_p (T_{flame} - T_b) + (H_v / \nu)}{L_v}
$$

(where the division by $\nu$ is the standard lumping used in pool-fire correlations to convert the B-number into a fuel-based form).

Compute the numerator term by term:

$$
c_p (T_{flame} - T_b) = 1.0 \times (1500 - 371) = 1129 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\frac{H_v}{\nu} = \frac{160}{15.1} = 10.60 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1129 + 10.60 = 1139.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
B = \frac{1139.6}{320} = 3.56
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass burning flux.**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{k}{c_p \, \delta} \cdot \ln(1 + B)
$$

Convert units: $k = 0.025$ W/(m·K) = 0.025 J/(s·m·K) = 0.025 × 10⁻³ kJ/(s·m·K); $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K); $\delta = 0.05$ m.

$$
\frac{k}{c_p \, \delta} = \frac{0.025 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 0.05} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

$$
\ln(1 + B) = \ln(1 + 3.56) = \ln(4.56) = 1.518
$$

$$
\dot{m}'' = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.518 = 7.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total mass loss rate.**

Pool area:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0^2}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = \dot{m}'' \times A_p = 7.59 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.7854 = 5.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat release rate.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{total} \times \Delta H_c = 5.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 44{,}000 = 26.2 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Final answers:**
> - Mass burning flux: $\dot{m}'' = 7.59 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/(m²·s)
> - Total mass loss rate: $\dot{m}_{total} = 5.96 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s
> - Heat release rate: $\dot{Q} \approx 26.2$ kW

> **Mark distribution (7 marks):** Computing B (2) + m_doubleprime (1.5) + pool area and total mass (1.5) + HRR (1) + final boxed answer (1).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Spalding equation (–2 to –3 marks).** The most common error is plugging $k$ in W/(m·K) directly with $c_p$ in kJ/(kg·K) without unit conversion. Always convert $k$ to kW or use $c_p$ in J/(kg·K). A factor-of-1000 error in the final answer is the hallmark of this mistake.
>
> 2. **Confusing Da's two definitions (–1 mark).** Some students write $Da = \tau_{chem}/\tau_{flow}$ instead of the textbook definition. The correct one is $Da = \tau_{flow}/\tau_{chem}$, so that **large Da ⇒ transport control** and **small Da ⇒ kinetic control**. State the definition explicitly on the first line.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $\ln(1+B)$ term and writing $\dot{m}'' = k B / (c_p \delta)$ (–2 marks).** The Spalding formula is a **logarithmic** form, not linear. Linearizing the logarithm is only valid for $B \ll 1$, which is rarely the case for hydrocarbon pool fires.
>
> 4. **Mixing up the B-number forms.** There are at least three equivalent-looking forms (oxidizer-based, fuel-based, lumped with $\nu$). Pick **one** at the start of the solution, define the symbols, and stick to it throughout. Examiners will not chase your units if you flip definitions mid-solution.
>
> 5. **Omitting the assumption list in derivation questions.** KTU valuation keys mandate that the first 2 marks of any 7-mark derivation go to the **physical assumptions** (steady, 1-D, constant properties, transport control). Skipping this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Claiming "all fires are transport controlled" without qualification (–1 mark).** Premixed systems (gas stoves, Bunsen burners, IC engines, premixed gas explosions) are kinetically controlled. The blanket statement loses nuance marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 4 — Kinetically Controlled & Transport Controlled Fires**
>
> - **Damköhler number**: $Da = \tau_{flow}/\tau_{chem}$. Da ≫ 1 ⇒ transport; Da ≪ 1 ⇒ kinetic.
> - **Premixed flames** = kinetically controlled. Arrhenius. $S_L$ is the rate.
> - **Diffusion flames** = transport controlled. Spalding + Burke-Schumann. Mixing is the rate.
> - **Spalding B-number**: $B = [c_p(T_{flame} - T_b) + H_v/\nu]/L_v$ (pool form). Higher B ⇒ more energetic flame.
> - **Spalding mass flux**: $\dot{m}'' = (k/c_p\delta)\ln(1+B)$. No kinetic constants appear.
> - **Most accidental fires are transport controlled.** Premixed fires are the exception.
> - **Froude-scaled HRR** $\dot{Q}^*$ is the standard non-dimensional fire size used in plume correlations.
> - **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$. $\phi = 1$ stoichiometric; $\phi < 1$ lean; $\phi > 1$ rich.
> - **Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for hydrocarbons**: ≈ 14.7 (gasoline), 17.2 (methane), 15.1 (heptane).
> - **Activation energy $E_a$ is the fingerprint of kinetic control.** If the answer involves $E_a$, you are in kinetic-control territory.
> - **Re-radiation reduces B by ~10–20%** in real pool fires — examiners may ask for the corrected form.
> - **Boiling temperature $T_b$ of the liquid** is the pool surface temperature (Vapor-liquid equilibrium); use the saturation curve, not 25 °C.
> - **Flame temperature** in the Spalding correlation is the **gas-phase temperature just above the pool**, often approximated as $T_{ad}$ for adiabatic flames.
> - **Burke-Schumann flame sheet**: stoichiometric surface where $Y_F / Y_{O_2} = 1/\nu$.
> - **Wall fires (Hasemi)**: $\dot{Q}^* = 0.025(y^*)^{1.10}$ for $0.05 \le y^* \le 0.4$.
> - **Heskestad ceiling jet**: $\Delta T_{cj} = 16.9 \dot{Q}^{2/3}/H^{5/3}$.
> - **For KTU exam**: always list assumptions first in any derivation, always box the final numerical answer, always quote units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Liquid Fuels Based on Flash Point Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Liquid Fuels Based on Flash Point Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Flash Point

The **Flash Point** of a liquid fuel is defined as the **lowest temperature** at which the vapours of the liquid, when mixed with air in a specific proportion (typically near the lower flammable limit), will ignite momentarily (flash) when an external ignition source is applied. This is a critical physical-chemical property that governs the **fire hazard classification, storage requirements, transportation regulations, and handling protocols** of all combustible liquids.

Mathematically, the flash point is the temperature $T_f$ at which the vapour pressure of the liquid $P_v(T_f)$ equals a threshold pressure sufficient to produce a flammable mixture with air at the lower explosive limit (LEL):

$$P_v(T_f) = P_{LEL}$$

where $P_v$ is the saturation vapour pressure of the liquid fuel and $P_{LEL}$ corresponds to the partial pressure of fuel vapour at the lower flammable limit concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flash point is *not* the temperature at which the liquid itself ignites — that temperature is the **Fire Point** (typically 5–10 °C higher than the flash point), at which sustained combustion occurs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a boiling pot of water. Water boils at 100 °C, but you can see *vapours* rising from the surface well before that temperature is reached. In a similar way, **every flammable liquid releases invisible vapours at room temperature** — petrol/gasoline famously produces flammable vapours even on a cold morning.

The **Flash Point** is the exact temperature at which these escaping vapours become "thick enough" to catch fire from a passing spark — like a thin fog that suddenly becomes ignitable. Below the flash point, the vapour concentration in the air is too lean; above it, the mixture is in the flammable range. This is why **flash point is the single most important number used by fire safety engineers** to decide where a fuel can be stored, transported, and used.

### 1.3 Why Classification is Necessary

Different liquid fuels behave very differently when exposed to heat:

- Some ignite at temperatures well below **0 °C** (e.g., diethyl ether, gasoline).
- Others need to be heated to over **100 °C** before they can flash (e.g., heavy fuel oil, lubricating oil).

Without a systematic classification, engineers, fire inspectors, and insurance assessors cannot reliably predict fire risk. Therefore, standards bodies such as **NFPA (National Fire Protection Association, USA)**, **OSHA (Occupational Safety & Health Administration)**, and the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS / IS 1446)** have established formal classification systems based on flash point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The standard reference flash point test methods are:
> - **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** — IS 1446 / ASTM D93
> - **Tag Closed Cup (TCC)** — ASTM D56
> - **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** — ASTM D92
> 
> Closed-cup values are typically **5–10 °C lower** than open-cup values for the same liquid.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classical NFPA / OSHA Classification (Globally Used)

The most widely adopted classification of flammable liquids is the **NFPA 30 / OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106** system, which divides all combustible liquids into three classes based on flash point and boiling point.

The classification logic is as follows:

- **Step 1:** Measure the **closed-cup flash point** of the liquid.
- **Step 2:** Compare it against the threshold temperatures of **22.8 °C (73 °F)** and **93.3 °C (200 °F)**.
- **Step 3:** Assign the corresponding hazard class.
- **Step 4:** For borderline liquids (Class IB), boiling point is also used as a secondary discriminator.

The three classes of **flammable liquids** are:

| Class | Flash Point Range | Boiling Point | Fire Hazard Level | Typical Examples |
|-------|------------------|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Class IA** | $T_f < 22.8 \text{ °C}$ (73 °F) | $T_b < 37.8 \text{ °C}$ (100 °F) | **Extreme** | Diethyl ether, pentane, acetaldehyde |
| **Class IB** | $T_f < 22.8 \text{ °C}$ (73 °F) | $T_b \geq 37.8 \text{ °C}$ (100 °F) | **High** | Gasoline (petrol), acetone, methanol, ethanol, benzene, toluene |
| **Class IC** | $22.8 \text{ °C} \leq T_f < 37.8 \text{ °C}$ | Any | **High** | Xylene, isopropanol, turpentine |

The single class of **combustible liquid** is:

| Class | Flash Point Range | Fire Hazard Level | Typical Examples |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Class II** | $37.8 \text{ °C} \leq T_f < 60 \text{ °C}$ (100–140 °F) | **Moderate** | Kerosene, diesel (light), fuel oil No. 1 |
| **Class IIIA** | $60 \text{ °C} \leq T_f < 93.3 \text{ °C}$ (140–200 °F) | **Low–Moderate** | Diesel (heavy), fuel oil No. 2, domestic heating oil |
| **Class IIIB** | $T_f \geq 93.3 \text{ °C}$ (200 °F) | **Low** | Lubricating oils, hydraulic fluids, bitumen, transformer oil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction in NFPA Terminology:** Liquids with flash point **< 37.8 °C** are called **"Flammable Liquids"**; liquids with flash point **≥ 37.8 °C** are called **"Combustible Liquids"**. This is a regulatory, not scientific, distinction.

### 2.2 Indian Standard (IS 1446) Classification

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** classifies flammable liquids under **IS 1446:2002** for the purpose of fire safety, especially in storage depots and oil refineries. India broadly follows a three-tier system:

| IS Class | Indian Term | Flash Point (Closed Cup) | Examples |
|----------|-------------|-------------------------|----------|
| **Class A** | Highly flammable / "Dangerously flammable" | $T_f < 23 \text{ °C}$ | Petrol (gasoline), acetone, benzene, methanol, ethanol, ether |
| **Class B** | Flammable / "Red label" petroleum | $23 \text{ °C} \leq T_f \leq 65 \text{ °C}$ | Kerosene, diesel, naphtha, fuel oil No. 1 |
| **Class C** | Combustible / "Moderately flammable" | $T_f \geq 65 \text{ °C}$ | Heavy fuel oil, lubricating oil, bitumen, tar |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Red Label"** classification in India (under the Petroleum Act 1934 and Rules 1976) corresponds to **Class B** petroleum, which historically had to carry a red label on containers — this is the famous "red label kerosene" used by poor households in India for decades.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Unit / Application |
|---|--------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $P_v(T_f) = P_{LEL}$ | $P_v$ = vapour pressure at flash point, $P_{LEL}$ = partial pressure at Lower Explosive Limit | Pa or atm |
| 2 | $\ln P_v = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ (Clausius–Clapeyron) | $\Delta H_{vap}$ = enthalpy of vaporisation, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Derives flash point from vapour pressure data |
| 3 | $\log P_v = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ (Antoine form) | $A$, $B$ are Antoine constants (fuel-specific) | Used in flash point calculators |
| 4 | $LFL \text{ (vol \%)} = \dfrac{P_{LEL}}{P_{atm}} \times 100$ | $LFL$ = Lower Flammable Limit in volume % | For mixture calculations |
| 5 | Fire Point $\approx$ Flash Point $+ (5 \text{ to } 10) \text{ °C}$ | Empirical | Used in fire safety design |
| 6 | Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) correlates inversely with Flash Point | RVP at 37.8 °C | Used for gasoline classification |
| 7 | Vapour Density $= \dfrac{M_{fuel}}{29}$ (relative to air) | $M_{fuel}$ = molar mass | Determines whether vapour settles low or rises |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For hydrocarbon fuels, a **lower flash point** ⇒ **higher fire hazard**. Roughly, every **10 °C decrease** in flash point **doubles the evaporation rate** and **significantly increases** the probability of a flammable vapour cloud forming at ambient temperature.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The flash point classification is the cornerstone of:

- **Storage tank design** — Class I liquids require floating-roof tanks, vapour-recovery systems, and explosion-relief vents.
- **Container labelling** — UN / IMDG / IATA shipping codes (UN 1170 for ethanol, UN 1203 for petrol) use flash point as a primary classification parameter.
- **Electrical zoning** — Class I liquids mandate **explosion-proof (Ex-Proof) electrical fittings** in any area where vapours may accumulate.
- **Fire extinguisher selection** — Class A/B/C extinguishers and foam suppression systems are selected based on the fuel's hazard class.
- **Building codes and insurance** — warehouse construction, separation distances, and insurance premiums all depend on the flash point class of stored liquids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vapour Pressure at Flash Point from Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

The flash point is a thermodynamic equilibrium property. To derive its analytical relation to vapour pressure, we begin with the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**, which describes how the saturation vapour pressure of a pure liquid varies with absolute temperature.

**Starting form** (two-state equilibrium of pure liquid and its vapour):

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

**Assumption 1:** The molar volume of the liquid $V_l$ is negligible compared to the molar volume of the vapour $V_v$ at low pressures, so:

$$\Delta V = V_v - V_l \approx V_v$$

**Assumption 2:** The vapour behaves as an **ideal gas**, so $V_v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T} \cdot \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P}{R T^{2}}$$

Rearranging to separate variables $P$ and $T$:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Assumption 3:** $\Delta H_{vap}$ is approximately constant over the small temperature range between ambient and flash point (a standard textbook assumption for narrow ranges). Integrating both sides from a reference state $(T_0, P_0)$ to $(T_f, P_f)$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P_f} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_0}^{T_f} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

The left side gives a natural logarithm; the right side integrates to a reciprocal temperature:

$$\ln \left( \frac{P_f}{P_0} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)$$

Rearranging into the standard linear form used in practice:

$$\ln P_f = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R \cdot T_f} + C$$

where $C = \ln P_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T_0}$ is a constant for a given fuel.

Converting from natural log to base-10 (Antoine form), let $A = C / 2.303$ and $B = \Delta H_{vap} / (2.303 R)$:

$$\boxed{\log_{10} P_f = A - \frac{B}{T_f}}$$

This is the famous **Antoine equation**, used in every fire-engineering and chemical-engineering handbook to compute the vapour pressure of a fuel at its flash point.

**Applying the flash-point condition** $P_v(T_f) = P_{LEL}$:

$$\log_{10} P_{LEL} = A - \frac{B}{T_f}$$

Solving explicitly for the flash point $T_f$:

$$\boxed{T_f = \frac{B}{A - \log_{10} P_{LEL}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $P_{LEL}$ for most hydrocarbons is on the order of **0.01 to 0.05 atm** (i.e., 1%–5% by volume in air at 1 atm), the value of $\log_{10} P_{LEL}$ is **negative** (between –2 and –1.3). This pushes $T_f$ to a finite, measurable value in the range 250–500 K (–23 °C to 227 °C), which matches real-world flash points.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Flash Point of n-Heptane

**Given:** Antoine constants for n-heptane: $A = 6.876$, $B = 1171.5$ (with $P$ in mmHg and $T$ in K). Lower Explosive Limit partial pressure $P_{LEL} = 30 \text{ mmHg}$ (≈ 1.0 vol% in air).

**Step 1:** Apply the flash-point formula:

$$T_f = \frac{1171.5}{6.876 - \log_{10}(30)}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(30) = \log_{10}(3 \times 10) = 0.4771 + 1 = 1.4771$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the denominator:

$$A - \log_{10} P_{LEL} = 6.876 - 1.4771 = 5.3989$$

**Step 4:** Compute the temperature:

$$T_f = \frac{1171.5}{5.3989} = 216.96 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Celsius:

$$T_f \text{ (°C)} = 216.96 - 273.15 = -56.19 \text{ °C}$$

This sub-zero result is consistent with n-heptane being a **highly volatile** component of gasoline. Real measured closed-cup flash point of n-heptane is approximately **−4 °C**, indicating that the simplified Antoine calculation gives a *lower bound* — a common and pedagogically useful result.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Flash Points of Common Fuels

| Fuel | Molecular Formula | Closed-Cup Flash Point (°C) | NFPA Class | IS Class | Hazard Rating |
|------|-------------------|------------------------------|------------|----------|---------------|
| Diethyl ether | C₄H₁₀O | **−45** | IA | A | Extreme |
| n-Pentane | C₅H₁₂ | **−40** | IA | A | Extreme |
| Acetone | C₃H₆O | **−20** | IB | A | Very High |
| Gasoline (Petrol) | C₄–C₁₂ mix | **−43** (typical) | IB | A | Very High |
| Methanol | CH₃OH | **11** | IB | A | High |
| Ethanol | C₂H₅OH | **13** | IB | A | High |
| Toluene | C₇H₈ | **4** | IB | A | High |
| Xylene | C₈H₁₀ | **27** | IC | A | High |
| Kerosene | C₁₀–C₁₆ | **38–72** | II | B | Moderate |
| Diesel (No. 2) | C₁₂–C₁₈ | **52–96** | IIIA | B / C | Low–Moderate |
| Fuel oil No. 6 (Heavy) | C₂₀+ | **>100** | IIIB | C | Low |
| Lubricating oil | C₂₀–C₃₅ | **150–230** | IIIB | C | Very Low |
| Bitumen (Asphalt) | C₂₅+ | **>200** | IIIB | C | Negligible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (NFPA + IS Combined)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Liquid Fuel Sample] --> B[Measure Closed-Cup Flash Point Tf]
    B --> C{Tf < 22.8 °C?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Boiling Point < 37.8 °C?}
    C -- No --> E{22.8 °C <= Tf < 37.8 °C?}
    D -- Yes --> F1[NFPA Class IA / IS Class A]
    D -- No --> F2[NFPA Class IB / IS Class A]
    E -- Yes --> F3[NFPA Class IC / IS Class A]
    E -- No --> G{37.8 °C <= Tf < 60 °C?}
    G -- Yes --> F4[NFPA Class II / IS Class B]
    G -- No --> H{60 °C <= Tf < 93.3 °C?}
    H -- Yes --> F5[NFPA Class IIIA / IS Class B or C]
    H -- No --> F6[NFPA Class IIIB / IS Class C]
    F1 --> Z[Storage: Explosion-Proof, Vapour Recovery]
    F2 --> Z
    F3 --> Z
    F4 --> Y[Storage: Sealed Drums, Foam Suppression]
    F5 --> Y
    F6 --> X[Storage: Open Drums OK, Standard Precautions]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Factors Influencing Flash Point Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Liquid Fuel Inputs]
        A1[Chemical Structure]
        A2[Molecular Weight]
        A3[Vapour Pressure at 20 °C]
    end
    subgraph TestMethod[Standard Test Methods]
        B1[Tag Closed Cup]
        B2[Pensky-Martens Closed Cup]
        B3[Cleveland Open Cup]
    end
    subgraph Property[Derived Property]
        C1[Closed-Cup Flash Point Tf]
    end
    subgraph Classification[Classification Output]
        D1[NFPA Class IA / IB / IC]
        D2[NFPA Class II / IIIA / IIIB]
        D3[IS Class A / B / C]
    end
    subgraph Engineering[Engineering Application]
        E1[Tank Design]
        E2[Vent Sizing]
        E3[Fire Extinguisher Selection]
        E4[Electrical Zoning]
    end
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
    D1 --> E4
```

### 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Closed-Cup vs Open-Cup Flash Point

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClosedCup[Closed-Cup Method - PMCC / TCC]
        CC1[Sealed test cup] --> CC2[Vapour accumulates above liquid]
        CC2 --> CC3[Lower vapour pressure needed to reach LEL]
        CC3 --> CC4[Lower measured flash point]
    end
    subgraph OpenCup[Open-Cup Method - COC]
        OC1[Open test cup] --> OC2[Vapour disperses into air]
        OC2 --> OC3[Higher liquid temperature needed to reach LEL]
        OC3 --> OC4[Higher measured flash point]
    end
    CC4 --> Diff[Difference typically 5-10 °C]
    OC4 --> Diff
    Diff --> Reg[Regulatory rule: Use CLOSED-CUP value for classification]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define flash point. How is it different from fire point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Flash Point (1 mark):** Flash point is the *lowest temperature* at which the vapours of a liquid fuel, when mixed with air in a flammable proportion, will ignite momentarily (flash) on application of an external ignition source, but the combustion does **not** sustain.
- **Fire Point (1 mark):** Fire point is the *lowest temperature* at which the vapours ignite and continue to burn (sustained combustion) for at least 5 seconds after removal of the ignition source.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Fire point is always **5–10 °C higher** than the flash point. At the flash point, the vapour evolution rate is sufficient only for a momentary flash; at the fire point, it is sufficient to sustain continuous combustion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**Classify the following fuels as per IS 1446: petrol, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oil. State the criterion used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The IS 1446 classification is based on the **closed-cup flash point** of the liquid:

| Fuel | Flash Point (°C) | IS 1446 Class |
|------|------------------|----------------|
| Petrol | ≈ −43 | **Class A** ($T_f < 23 \text{ °C}$) |
| Kerosene | 38–72 | **Class B** ($23 \leq T_f \leq 65 \text{ °C}$) |
| Diesel | 52–96 | **Class B / Class C** (borderline, often Class C) |
| Lubricating Oil | 150–230 | **Class C** ($T_f > 65 \text{ °C}$) |

(1 mark for criterion, 1 mark for correct classes, 1 mark for tabulation and reasoning.)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]

**Q.A. (a) Explain the NFPA classification of flammable and combustible liquids. How is it different from the IS 1446 classification? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **National Fire Protection Association (NFPA 30)** classifies all flammable and combustible liquids into **six categories** based on **closed-cup flash point** and **boiling point**.

**(i) Flammable Liquids (Flash Point < 37.8 °C):**

- **Class IA:** $T_f < 22.8 \text{ °C}$ AND $T_b < 37.8 \text{ °C}$ — e.g., diethyl ether, pentane.
- **Class IB:** $T_f < 22.8 \text{ °C}$ AND $T_b \geq 37.8 \text{ °C}$ — e.g., gasoline, acetone, methanol.
- **Class IC:** $22.8 \text{ °C} \leq T_f < 37.8 \text{ °C}$ — e.g., turpentine, xylene.

**(ii) Combustible Liquids (Flash Point ≥ 37.8 °C):**

- **Class II:** $37.8 \text{ °C} \leq T_f < 60 \text{ °C}$ — e.g., kerosene.
- **Class IIIA:** $60 \text{ °C} \leq T_f < 93.3 \text{ °C}$ — e.g., diesel, light fuel oil.
- **Class IIIB:** $T_f \geq 93.3 \text{ °C}$ — e.g., lubricating oil, bitumen.

**Comparison with IS 1446 (4 marks for the comparison portion):**

| Aspect | NFPA 30 (USA) | IS 1446 (India) |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Number of classes | **6** (IA, IB, IC, II, IIIA, IIIB) | **3** (A, B, C) |
| Reference temperature | Uses °F (73 °F = 22.8 °C, 200 °F = 93.3 °C) | Uses °C (23 °C, 65 °C) |
| Boiling point used? | **Yes**, for Class IA vs IB distinction | **No** distinction by boiling point |
| Test method | Closed-cup (Pensky-Martens) | Closed-cup (PMCC) |
| Scope | Storage, fire code, insurance | Storage depots, Petroleum Act compliance |

The IS system is **simpler** (only three classes), reflecting the original Indian need for **petroleum storage and red-label** regulations, while the NFPA system is **more granular**, designed for the **broader US chemical and petrochemical industry**.

---

**Q.A. (b) Using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, derive the Antoine equation for vapour pressure. A liquid fuel has Antoine constants $A = 7.0$ and $B = 1500$ (with $T$ in K, $P$ in mmHg). If the lower explosive limit partial pressure $P_{LEL} = 40 \text{ mmHg}$, determine its flash point in °C. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the Clausius–Clapeyron equation applied to liquid–vapour equilibrium at constant $\Delta H_{vap}$:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P}{R T^{2}}$$

Separating variables and integrating:

$$\int \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int \frac{dT}{T^{2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln P = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm and defining Antoine constants:

$$A = \frac{C}{\ln 10}, \;\;\; B = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R \ln 10}$$

$$\boxed{\log_{10} P = A - \frac{B}{T}}$$

**Computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** At the flash point, $P_v(T_f) = P_{LEL} = 40 \text{ mmHg}$. Substituting:

$$\log_{10}(40) = 7.0 - \frac{1500}{T_f}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $\log_{10}(40)$:

$$\log_{10}(40) = \log_{10}(4 \times 10) = \log_{10}(4) + 1 = 0.6021 + 1 = 1.6021$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $T_f$:

$$1.6021 = 7.0 - \frac{1500}{T_f} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{1500}{T_f} = 7.0 - 1.6021 = 5.3979$$

**Step 4:**

$$T_f = \frac{1500}{5.3979} = 277.88 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Celsius:

$$T_f \text{ (°C)} = 277.88 - 273.15 = \boxed{4.73 \text{ °C}}$$

**Interpretation:** The fuel is a **Class IB** flammable liquid (flash point below 22.8 °C), placing it in the same category as **methanol (11 °C) and ethanol (13 °C)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]

**Q.B. (a) Discuss the experimental determination of flash point using the Pensky-Martens Closed Cup apparatus. List the advantages and limitations of this method. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Description (3 marks):**

The **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) apparatus** consists of:

1. A **brass test cup** of standard capacity (≈ 70 mL) with a **sliding lid** containing three openings: one for a stirrer, one for an ignition source (small test flame / electric hot wire), and one for introducing the test flame into the cup.
2. A **stirring mechanism** to maintain uniform temperature within the sample.
3. A **temperature-controlled heating bath** surrounding the test cup, with a thermometer accurate to ±0.5 °C.
4. A **shutter mechanism** that dips the test flame into the cup at controlled intervals.

**Procedure (2 marks):**

1. The liquid sample is filled in the test cup up to a marked level.
2. The cup is sealed with the lid; the sample is stirred continuously.
3. The temperature is raised at a **rate of 5–6 °C per minute**, with continuous stirring.
4. At every **1 °C** rise, the test flame is introduced for about **2.5 seconds** through the shutter.
5. The temperature at which a **distinct flash** is observed inside the cup is recorded as the **flash point**.
6. The test is repeated with a **fresh sample** at the expected flash point temperature to confirm the value.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Vapour-air mixture is contained, giving a **more realistic, lower** flash point value.
- Suitable for liquids with **viscous residues** (e.g., diesel, fuel oil).
- Results are highly **reproducible** and form the basis of most regulatory standards.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Not suitable for liquids with very low flash points (< 10 °C) without auxiliary cooling.
- Cannot be used for liquids that form **surface films** (e.g., contaminated or emulsified fuels).
- The closed cup can lead to **vapour accumulation** and false readings if the lid is not perfectly sealed.

---

**Q.B. (b) A refinery stores three liquids with flash points −40 °C, 45 °C, and 110 °C. Classify each as per NFPA and IS 1446. For each, recommend the type of storage tank and fire-extinguishing medium. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Liquid | Flash Point | NFPA Class | IS Class |
|--------|-------------|------------|----------|
| Liquid 1 | −40 °C | **Class IA** ($T_f < 22.8$ °C, $T_b$ likely < 37.8 °C) | **Class A** |
| Liquid 2 | 45 °C | **Class II** (37.8 ≤ $T_f$ < 60) | **Class B** (since 23 ≤ 45 ≤ 65) |
| Liquid 3 | 110 °C | **Class IIIB** ($T_f \geq 93.3$ °C) | **Class C** ($T_f > 65$ °C) |

**Storage Tank Recommendations (2 marks):**

- **Liquid 1 (Class IA, IS-A):** **Floating-roof tank** with **vapour-recovery system**, explosion-relief vents, inert-gas blanketing (N₂), and located in an **earthen-bunded** dyke. Storage in **underground** caverns is preferred.
- **Liquid 2 (Class II, IS-B):** **Cone-roof fixed tank** with **flame arrestors** on vents and foam-chamber provision. Above-ground installation with **bunding capacity of 110%** of tank volume.
- **Liquid 3 (Class IIIB, IS-C):** **Cone-roof tank** with normal venting. Standard above-ground storage, no vapour recovery needed.

**Fire-Extinguishing Media (2 marks):**

- **Liquid 1:** **Alcohol-resistant AFFF foam** (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam) + **dry chemical powder (ABC)** + **CO₂** for small spills. **Water spray cooling** of adjacent tanks. **No water jet** directly on the burning liquid.
- **Liquid 2:** **AFFF foam** (regular) + **dry chemical powder (BC)** + **water spray** for cooling.
- **Liquid 3:** **Foam** (regular AFFF) + **water spray**. If the fire is small, even a **water mist** may suffice. **CO₂** is also effective.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall:** Students frequently:
> - Confuse **flash point with fire point** and lose 2–3 marks.
> - Forget to specify the **closed-cup vs open-cup** test method (closed-cup is the *regulatory* standard).
> - Mix up **NFPA Classes II and IIIA**, missing the **60 °C boundary**.
> - In derivations, drop the constant of integration or fail to convert K → °C in the final answer.
> - In the Indian system, miss the **"red-label"** terminology that examiners love to test.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Flash Point Definition:** Lowest temperature at which vapours flash (momentary ignition) but do not sustain combustion.
- **Fire Point:** Flash Point + (5 to 10) °C; sustained combustion occurs.
- **NFPA Total Classes:** 6 — three "Flammable" (IA, IB, IC) and three "Combustible" (II, IIIA, IIIB).
- **IS 1446 Classes:** 3 — A (< 23 °C), B (23–65 °C), C (> 65 °C).
- **Key Thresholds to Memorise:** 22.8 °C (73 °F), 37.8 °C (100 °F), 60 °C, 93.3 °C (200 °F).
- **Closed-cup values are LOWER than open-cup** by about 5–10 °C; closed-cup is used for legal classification.
- **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** is *inversely* related to flash point — high RVP ⇒ low flash point ⇒ high fire hazard.
- **Petrol (gasoline)** is the classic **Class IB / IS-A** liquid; **lubricating oil** is the classic **Class IIIB / IS-C** liquid.
- **Antoine Equation** is the engineering shortcut to compute flash point from vapour-pressure data.
- **Higher flash point ⇒ safer storage, cheaper tanks, lower insurance premiums, smaller separation distances.**
- **"Red label" kerosene** in India = IS Class B petroleum (23 °C ≤ $T_f$ ≤ 65 °C).
- Always state the **test method** (PMCC, TCC, COC) when reporting a flash point value in KTU answers.
- For any *flammable* liquid storage, mandatory design features are: **bund walls, foam pourers, flame arrestors, vapour recovery, explosion-relief panels, and earthing/bonding**.
- **Boiling point** is used *only* in the NFPA system to distinguish **Class IA from Class IB**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Fuel Evaporation - Steady Evaporation using Stefan's Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Liquid Fuel Evaporation - Steady Evaporation using Stefan's Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Stefan's Problem** (also known as the **Stefan Evaporation Problem** or **Stefan Diffusion Problem**) is a classical steady-state, one-dimensional mass transfer problem describing the diffusion of a vapor **A** (generated by evaporation of a liquid fuel) through a stagnant (quasi-quiescent) layer of gas **B** (typically air) to a free stream. It was originally formulated by **Josef Stefan (1871)** in connection with the rate of evaporation of water into air. In the KTU fire science context, Stefan's problem provides the **fundamental analytical benchmark** for predicting the **mass burning rate** of a quiescent (no-wind) **pool fire** or a liquid fuel layer prior to ignition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The model assumes: (1) quasi-steady diffusion, (2) a stagnant boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ above the liquid surface, (3) constant total molar concentration (low mass transfer rate approximation), and (4) liquid-vapor interface at the saturation condition $y_{A,s} = y_{A,1}$.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **scented perfume bottle** opened in a still room. The fragrance molecules drift upward very slowly even when the air is completely calm. This is **not** because of wind — it happens because the molecules themselves, by random motion, *push* the air out of the way as they escape from the surface. This **self-induced bulk flow** is called **Stefan flow**.

In a pool fire situation:
- The liquid fuel (say, n-heptane) sits in a pan.
- Its vapor pressure pushes molecules upward into the air just above.
- These molecules must diffuse through a thin layer of still air before reaching the ambient atmosphere.
- The diffusion of vapor **drags** the air with it (the **Stefan flow**), modifying the concentration profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Interest (highlighted in bold for KTU):**
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P = 101325 \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{101.3 \text{ kPa}}$
> - **Binary diffusion coefficient (fuel vapor–air):** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ at $T = 298 \text{ K}$
> - **Saturation mole fraction at fuel surface** (from **Clausius–Clapeyron**): $y_{A,1} = P_{sat}(T_s)/P$

---

### Physical Setup (Schematic Description)

A horizontal pool of liquid fuel occupies the plane $z = 0$. Above it lies a **stagnant gas boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ (or a control layer up to $z = L$). The configuration is:

- At $z = 0$ (liquid-vapor interface): Mole fraction of fuel vapor $y_A(0) = y_{A,1}$ (saturation value at liquid surface temperature $T_s$).
- At $z = L$ (top of layer / ambient): Mole fraction $y_A(L) = y_{A,2} \approx 0$ (in fresh air far above, or the value set by the bulk flow / flame).
- The non-condensable gas **B** (air) is **stagnant** in the lab frame at the upper boundary, so $N_B = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Framework — The Logic Stack

The Stefan evaporation problem is solved by combining **Fick's Law** with the **bulk-motion correction** that arises because the evaporating molecules carry mass, momentum, and induce a convective (Stefan) flow. The reasoning proceeds in the following steps:

1. **Step 1 — Define molar fluxes.** Let $N_A$ and $N_B$ be the molar fluxes (mol/m²·s) of vapor and air, respectively.
2. **Step 2 — Apply the stagnant-gas condition.** Since air cannot escape through the upper boundary, $N_B = 0$ in the stationary (lab) frame.
3. **Step 3 — Write Fick's law with bulk motion correction.** For a binary mixture:

   $$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A(N_A + N_B)$$

4. **Step 4 — Set $N_B = 0$ and simplify.** The convective term becomes $y_A \cdot N_A$, which is the **Stefan flow correction**.

   $$N_A(1 - y_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

5. **Step 5 — Express concentrations in mole fractions.** For an ideal gas at constant $P$ and $T$, $C_A = y_A \cdot C_{tot}$ where $C_{tot} = P/(RT)$.
6. **Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate** over the boundary layer $0 \to L$ with B.C.s $y_A(0) = y_{A,1}$ and $y_A(L) = y_{A,2}$.
7. **Step 7 — Solve for $N_A$.** This yields the **Stefan flux** — a logarithmically weighted driving force.
8. **Step 8 — Convert molar flux to mass flux** $\dot{m}''_A = N_A \cdot M_A$ for use in fire calculations (e.g., burning rate $\dot{m}''_f$).
9. **Step 9 — Link to the fire problem.** For a quiescent pool fire with negligible heat feedback from flame, the **evaporation rate = mass burning rate** and Stefan analysis gives a baseline $\dot{m}''$.
10. **Step 10 — Apply the Clausius–Clapeyron equation** to compute $y_{A,1}$ as a function of $T_s$ — because the surface temperature itself depends on the heat balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the logarithm?** The factor $\ln[(1-y_{A,2})/(1-y_{A,1})]$ arises because the **effective diffusion length** is reduced in regions of high vapor concentration. The denominator $(1 - y_A)$ is small near the surface (vapor-rich), so $D_{AB}$ is "less effective" there.

### Engineering Significance in Fire Dynamics

| Fire Scenario | Role of Stefan's Result |
|---|---|
| **Small / thin pool fire** | Gives the **lower bound** on mass burning rate (pure diffusion limit) |
| **Spill / leak of LNG/LPG** | Predicts vapor cloud formation rate on still water/ground |
| **Cryogenic fuel storage** | Used to estimate boil-off / flash evaporation of LNG into air |
| **Pre-ignition vapor accumulation** | Determines time to reach Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) |
| **Wind-still forest floor fuel beds** | Baseline for dead-needle / leaf-litter moisture evaporation |

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total molar concentration | $C_{tot} = P/(RT)$ | mol/m³ | Ideal gas law |
| 2 | Stefan molar flux (vapor) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_{tot}}{L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$ | mol/(m²·s) | **Core KTU formula** |
| 3 | Stefan mass flux | $\dot{m}''_A = N_A \cdot M_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P\,M_A}{R\,T\,L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$ | kg/(m²·s) | Used as $\dot{m}''_f$ in fire |
| 4 | Driving-force form | $\dot{m}''_A = \dfrac{\rho\,D_{AB}}{L}\ln(1+B_M)$ | kg/(m²·s) | Where $B_M = y_{A,1} - y_{A,2}$ is the **Spalding mass transfer number** |
| 5 | Sherwood number (stagnant) | $Sh = \dfrac{k_c \cdot L}{D_{AB}} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Equals $2$ when $y_{A,1} \ll 1$ |
| 6 | Clausius–Clapeyron | $\ln(P_{sat}) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_s}-\dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$ | Pa | Gives $y_{A,1} = P_{sat}/P$ |
| 7 | Film temperature | $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ | K | Used when temperature varies across layer |
| 8 | Effective diffusivity (T-dep) | $D_{AB} = D_{AB,0}\!\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{1.75}\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)$ | m²/s | Fuller-Schettler-Giddings correlation |
| 9 | Spalding mass transfer No. | $B_M = \dfrac{y_{A,1}-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}$ | dimensionless | Couples to $B_T$ via Lewis number |
| 10 | Heat–mass transfer coupling | $\dot{m}''_A = \dfrac{h}{c_p}\ln(1+B_M) = \dfrac{h}{c_p B_T}\dot{Y}_{O,\infty}$ | kg/(m²·s) | Used in pool-fire thermal models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always quote formula (2) or (3) in the *logarithmic* form. The linear approximation $N_A \approx (D_{AB}\,C_{tot}/L)(y_{A,1}-y_{A,2})$ is only valid for $y_A \ll 1$ and is a common mistake.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Stefan Evaporation Flux

We consider a quiescent layer of thickness $L$ above a liquid fuel surface, with the coordinate $z$ measured upward from the liquid-vapor interface.

**Starting point — Molar flux with bulk motion correction:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A(N_A + N_B)$$

**Step 1.** Apply the **stagnant-air condition** at the upper boundary. Because air is not consumed or produced, the *net* molar flux of air must vanish in the lab frame: $N_B = 0$.

**Step 2.** Substitute $N_B = 0$:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A\,N_A$$

**Step 3.** Move the convective term to the left-hand side:

$$N_A(1 - y_A) = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4.** For an ideal gas at constant pressure and temperature, $C_A = y_A \cdot C_{tot}$, so $dC_A/dz = C_{tot}\,dy_A/dz$. Substituting:

$$N_A(1 - y_A) = -D_{AB}\,C_{tot}\,\frac{dy_A}{dz}$$

**Step 5.** Steady state implies $N_A$ is independent of $z$ (no accumulation). Separate variables:

$$N_A\,dz = -D_{AB}\,C_{tot}\,\frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A}$$

**Step 6.** Integrate across the layer from $z = 0$ (where $y_A = y_{A,1}$) to $z = L$ (where $y_A = y_{A,2}$):

$$N_A\int_0^L dz = -D_{AB}\,C_{tot}\int_{y_{A,1}}^{y_{A,2}}\frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A}$$

**Step 7.** The right-hand integral evaluates to $-\ln(1 - y_A)$. Therefore:

$$N_A \cdot L = D_{AB}\,C_{tot}\,\bigl[-\ln(1-y_{A,2}) + \ln(1-y_{A,1})\bigr]$$

**Step 8.** Combine the logarithms:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_{tot}}{L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$$

**Step 9.** Replace $C_{tot} = P/(RT)$ for an ideal gas:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$$

**Step 10.** Convert to mass flux (kg/m²·s) by multiplying by molar mass $M_A$:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}''_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P\,M_A}{R\,T\,L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)\;}$$

This is the **classical Stefan (1871) result** for steady evaporation of a liquid into a stagnant gas.

---

### 3.2 Limiting Cases (Validation of the Result)

**Case (a): Highly dilute vapor, $y_{A,1} \ll 1$ and $y_{A,2} \to 0$.**

Use $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$N_A \approx \frac{D_{AB}\,C_{tot}}{L}\,(y_{A,1} - y_{A,2})$$

This recovers **Fick's law** (no Stefan-flow correction) — the limit of infinite dilution. *Interpretation:* when fuel vapor is highly diluted in air, bulk motion is negligible.

**Case (b): Pure A evaporating into B, $y_{A,1} \to 1$.**

The log term $\ln[(1-y_{A,2})/(1-y_{A,1})] \to \infty$. Physically, evaporation becomes *unbounded* because the surface is pure liquid A and there is no air resistance — all transport is A itself. *Interpretation:* The Stefan formula correctly blows up, indicating the model breaks down (it assumes $y_A < 1$).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem.** A pool of n-heptane ($C_7H_{16}$, $M_A = 100.2$ g/mol) sits in a tray at surface temperature $T_s = 298$ K. Ambient air is at $T_\infty = 298$ K, with fuel vapor mole fraction $y_{A,2} = 0$ far from the surface. The saturation vapor pressure of n-heptane at 298 K is $P_{sat} = 6.13$ kPa. Binary diffusion coefficient $D_{AB} = 7.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. The stagnant boundary-layer thickness is $L = 0.01$ m. Total pressure $P = 101.325$ kPa. Compute the steady mass evaporation flux.

**Step 1.** Compute the saturation mole fraction at the surface:

$$y_{A,1} = \frac{P_{sat}}{P} = \frac{6130}{101325} = 0.0605$$

**Step 2.** Verify the dilute limit: $y_{A,1} \approx 0.06 \ll 1$, so we can use either the exact logarithmic or the linear form. We use the exact Stefan form for completeness.

**Step 3.** Compute the log driving force:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1-0}{1-0.0605}\right) = -\ln(0.9395) = 0.0624$$

**Step 4.** Compute $C_{tot}$:

$$C_{tot} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.89 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 5.** Compute molar flux:

$$N_A = \frac{7.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 40.89}{0.01} \times 0.0624 = 1.786 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 6.** Convert to mass flux:

$$\dot{m}''_A = N_A \cdot M_A = 1.786 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1002 = 1.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}''_A \approx 1.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} \approx 0.64 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{hr)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** The experimentally measured mass burning rate of n-heptane in a small quiescent pool is $\approx 0.7$–$0.8$ kg/(m²·min) $\approx 0.012$ kg/(m²·s), about 60× higher. The reason: the experimental value includes **conductive heat feedback from the flame** that raises $T_s$ above ambient, dramatically increasing $P_{sat}$. Stefan's pure-diffusion result is the **no-feedback lower bound** — a key conceptual point in KTU Module 4.

---

### 3.4 Coupling to Heat Transfer — Temperature-Dependent Form

When the surface temperature $T_s$ and ambient $T_\infty$ differ, the layer is non-isothermal. A **film-temperature** approximation replaces $T$ by:

$$T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$$

and uses the diffusivity evaluated at $T_f$:

$$\dot{m}''_A = \frac{D_{AB}(T_f)\,P\,M_A}{R\,T_f\,L}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$$

For more accuracy, the temperature profile $T(z)$ is integrated with the Arrhenius-type dependence $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.75}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Setup Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Liquid Fuel Pool n heptane] --> A2[Vapor Liquid Interface z equals 0]
    A2 --> A3[Stagnant Boundary Layer thickness L or delta]
    A3 --> A4[Ambient Air Bulk z equals L yA2 about 0]
    A2 -->|saturation condition| A5[yA1 equals Psat of T divided by P]
    A4 -->|bulk condition| A6[yA2 equals 0 or far field value]
    A5 --> A7[Stefan Equation molar flux NA]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Mass flux m double prime A equals NA times MA]
    A8 --> A9[Mass Burning Rate m double prime f in pool fire]
    A8 --> A10[Vapor Cloud Generation Rate pre ignition]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Stefan's Analysis (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Step 1 Define geometry and BCs] --> B2[Step 2 Apply Fick law with bulk motion]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3 Set NB equals 0 stagnant air]
    B3 --> B4[Step 4 Convert to mole fraction form]
    B4 --> B5[Step 5 Separate and integrate over L]
    B5 --> B6[Step 6 Obtain Stefan flux equation]
    B6 --> B7[Step 7 Apply Clausius Clapeyron for yA1]
    B7 --> B8[Step 8 Compute m double prime A]
    B8 --> B9[Step 9 Couple to heat transfer to find Ts]
    B9 --> B10[Step 10 Iterate to closure of fire model]
```

### 4.3 Concentration Profile Schematic (ASCII Illustration)

```
y_A (vapor mole fraction)
   │
y_A,1 ●─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─  ← Fuel surface (z=0)
      │\
      │ \         <-- Concave profile (Stefan flow)
      │  \            (logarithmic, not linear)
      │   \
      │    \
      │     \
      │      \
y_A,2 ●─ ─ ─ ┴ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─  ← Bulk (z=L)
      │      │
      └──────┴──────────── z
            z=0       z=L
```

> The **concavity toward the surface** reflects the *Stefan flow*: near the surface, vapor concentration is high, the gradient $dy_A/dz$ is gentle (less effective driving), and far from the surface the gradient is steep. This is why the **integral is logarithmic** rather than linear.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Stefan's problem of liquid evaporation. Why is the resulting flux expression logarithmic rather than linear in the driving concentration difference?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Stefan's problem is the steady, one-dimensional diffusion of vapor A from a liquid surface through a stagnant layer of gas B to the bulk. The bulk motion of gas (Stefan flow) caused by the addition of A reduces the effective driving gradient as $y_A \to 1$. Mathematically, this appears as the factor $1/(1-y_A)$ in the integrated Fick equation, leading to the natural logarithm. When $y_A \ll 1$ (dilute limit), the log reduces to the linear Fick form.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What physical condition justifies setting $N_B = 0$ in Stefan's problem, and what is the consequence of removing this assumption?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> $N_B = 0$ is justified by the **stagnant-gas condition**: the non-condensable gas B cannot leave the system across the upper boundary, so its net molar flux in the lab frame is zero. If this assumption is removed (e.g., with bulk air flow), the equation generalises to the **convective Stefan flow** form with both $N_A$ and $N_B$ nonzero, and the Sherwood number becomes a function of the Reynolds and Schmidt numbers rather than the constant $Sh = 2$ of pure diffusion.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A — Stefan's Problem in Pool Fire (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive, starting from Fick's first law with the bulk-motion correction, the expression for the **steady-state molar flux** of fuel vapor evaporating from a quiescent liquid pool into a stagnant layer of air of thickness $L$. Clearly state the boundary conditions and the physical meaning of the logarithmic driving-force term. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A small pool of acetone ($M_A = 58.08$ g/mol) at $T_s = 293$ K evaporates into still air at $P = 101.325$ kPa. Given: $P_{sat}(293\text{ K}) = 24.5$ kPa, $D_{AB} = 1.05 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $L = 5$ mm, and $y_{A,2} = 0$. Compute (i) the **molar flux** $N_A$, (ii) the **mass evaporation rate per unit area** $\dot{m}''_A$, and (iii) comment on whether Stefan's pure-diffusion model overestimates or underestimates the actual mass burning rate of a real acetone pool fire. **\[7 marks\]**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
> - State assumptions: 1-D, steady, ideal gas, constant $P$ and $T$, stagnant air. **\[1 mark\]**
> - Write Fick's law with bulk motion correction:
>   $$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A(N_A + N_B)$$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - Apply $N_B = 0$, rearrange:
>   $$N_A(1 - y_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - Substitute $C_A = y_A C_{tot}$ and separate variables:
>   $$N_A\,dz = -D_{AB}\,C_{tot}\,\frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A}$$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - Apply B.C.s $y_A(0) = y_{A,1}$, $y_A(L) = y_{A,2}$ and integrate:
>   $$N_A \cdot L = D_{AB}\,C_{tot}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$$ **\[1.5 marks\]**
> - Replace $C_{tot} = P/(RT)$, give final result and explain log term physically (Stefan flow correction). **\[1.5 marks\]**
>
> **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**
> - $y_{A,1} = P_{sat}/P = 24500/101325 = 0.2418$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - $\ln[(1-0)/(1-0.2418)] = -\ln(0.7582) = 0.2768$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - $C_{tot} = 101325/(8.314 \times 293) = 41.58$ mol/m³ **\[1 mark\]**
> - $N_A = (1.05\times10^{-5} \times 41.58 / 0.005) \times 0.2768 = 2.42 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/(m²·s) **\[1.5 marks\]**
> - $\dot{m}''_A = 2.42\times10^{-2} \times 0.05808 = 1.41 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m²·s) **\[1 mark\]**
> - **Comment:** Stefan's model **underestimates** real burning rate because it ignores conductive/radiative heat feedback from the flame that raises $T_s$, increasing $P_{sat}$ and hence $\dot{m}''$. **\[1.5 marks\]**

---

#### Question B — Limiting Cases and Mass-Transfer Coupling (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Starting from the Stefan flux equation, derive (i) the **dilute-limit (Fick's law)** approximation valid for $y_{A,1} \ll 1$, and (ii) the expression for the **Sherwood number** for a stagnant boundary layer. State the value of $Sh$ in the dilute limit and explain why it represents the lower limit of convection-enhanced mass transfer. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Using the **Spalding mass-transfer number** $B_M$ form of the Stefan equation,
$$\dot{m}''_A = \frac{\rho\,D_{AB}}{L}\ln(1 + B_M), \quad B_M = \frac{y_{A,1} - y_{A,2}}{1 - y_{A,1}},$$
compute $B_M$ and $\dot{m}''_A$ for **methanol** ($M_A = 32.04$ g/mol) at $T_s = 290$ K, given $P_{sat} = 12.97$ kPa, $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³, $D_{AB} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $L = 0.008$ m, $y_{A,2} = 0$. Discuss how the result would change if a **wind of $u_\infty = 2$ m/s** were imposed over the pool, and name the appropriate non-dimensional correlation that would replace the stagnant-film result. **\[7 marks\]**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Limiting-case derivation (7 marks):**
> - Take the Stefan flux: $N_A = (D_{AB}\,C_{tot}/L)\ln[(1-y_{A,2})/(1-y_{A,1})]$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - For $y_A \ll 1$, use $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$, $1-y_A \approx 1$:
>   $$N_A \approx \frac{D_{AB}\,C_{tot}}{L}(y_{A,1} - y_{A,2})$$ **\[2 marks\]**
> - This is **Fick's first law** with effective diffusivity $D_{AB}$. **\[0.5 mark\]**
> - Define $k_c = D_{AB}/L$ as the mass transfer coefficient; then $Sh = k_c L/D_{AB} = 1$ for the dilute case, but the **log form** gives:
>   $$Sh = \ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A,2}}{1-y_{A,1}}\right)$$ **\[2 marks\]**
> - In the dilute limit, $Sh \to (y_{A,1}-y_{A,2})$, which equals the difference in mole fractions; for $y_{A,1} \to 0$, $Sh \to 0$ but a common convention is $Sh = 2$ for the **stagnant-film** (analogous to $Nu = 2$ for conduction from an isothermal plate in still fluid). **\[1 mark\]**
> - **Discussion:** Convection (e.g., wind) enhances $Sh$ via correlations like $Sh \propto Re^m Sc^n$ with $m,n > 0$. Hence $Sh = 2$ is the lower bound. **\[0.5 mark\]**
>
> **(b) Numerical & discussion (7 marks):**
> - $y_{A,1} = 12970/101325 = 0.1280$ **\[1 mark\]**
> - $B_M = (0.1280 - 0)/(1 - 0.1280) = 0.1468$ **\[1.5 marks\]**
> - $\dot{m}''_A = 1.20 \times 1.6\times10^{-5}/0.008 \times \ln(1.1468) = 2.4\times10^{-3} \times 0.1369 = 3.28 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/(m²·s) **\[2 marks\]**
> - **Wind effect discussion:** Imposing $u_\infty = 2$ m/s thins the boundary layer (effective $L$ reduces), increases $k_c$, and hence $\dot{m}''_A$ rises significantly. The appropriate correlation for a **flat plate with forced convection** is the **Sherwood–Reynolds–Schmidt** form:
>   $$Sh_x = 0.332\,Re_x^{1/2}\,Sc^{1/3}$$ **\[2 marks\]**
> - For the average over a plate of length $L$: $\bar{Sh}_L = 0.664\,Re_L^{1/2}\,Sc^{1/3}$. **\[0.5 mark\]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1 - y_A$ factor in the integral.** Writing $\int dy_A$ instead of $\int dy_A/(1-y_A)$ is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using linear Fick's law without justification.** The Stefan logarithmic form is required whenever $y_{A,1} > 0.05$. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the stagnant-gas condition $N_B = 0$** at the start of the derivation. Examiners look for it explicitly. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign errors in molar-flux-to-mass-flux conversion.** Always check $M_A$ in kg/mol, not g/mol. Common 0.5–1 mark error.
> 5. **Confusing Sherwood with Nusselt.** $Sh$ uses $D_{AB}$ (mass diffusivity), not $\alpha$ (thermal diffusivity). Loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert pressure to Pa** when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Loss: 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Stefan's problem** = steady, 1-D evaporation of liquid A into stagnant gas B.
- **Key assumption:** $N_B = 0$ at the upper boundary (stagnant air, no bulk flow).
- **Governing equation:** $N_A(1-y_A) = -D_{AB}\,dC_A/dz$ (Fick with Stefan-flow correction).
- **Core result (molar flux):** $N_A = (D_{AB}\,P/ R\,T\,L)\,\ln[(1-y_{A,2})/(1-y_{A,1})]$.
- **Core result (mass flux):** $\dot{m}''_A = (D_{AB}\,P\,M_A/ R\,T\,L)\,\ln[(1-y_{A,2})/(1-y_{A,1})]$.
- **Spalding form:** $\dot{m}''_A = (\rho D_{AB}/L)\ln(1 + B_M)$.
- **Dilute limit ($y_A \ll 1$):** reduces to Fick's law $N_A = (D_{AB}\,C_{tot}/L)(y_{A,1} - y_{A,2})$.
- **Clausius–Clapeyron** gives $y_{A,1} = P_{sat}(T_s)/P$ — required to close the problem.
- **Sherwood number (stagnant):** $Sh = \ln[(1-y_{A,2})/(1-y_{A,1})] \to 2$ in the dilute limit.
- **Fire interpretation:** Stefan's result is the **no-feedback lower bound** on the mass burning rate of a pool fire.
- **Convection enhancement:** With wind, $L$ is replaced by $\delta \propto (L/\sqrt{Re})$, and $Sh$ rises as $Re^m Sc^n$.
- **Heat–mass transfer coupling:** $\dot{m}''_A \propto h/c_p$ with $B_M$ — links to convective heat transfer at the pool surface.
- **Order of magnitude:** For n-heptane at 298 K, $\dot{m}'' \sim 10^{-4}$ kg/(m²·s) from Stefan; real pool fires give $\sim 10^{-2}$ kg/(m²·s) due to flame heat feedback.
- **Limitation:** Stefan model assumes constant $T$ and $P$, ideal gas, and one-way diffusion; fails at high $y_A$ (near-boiling conditions).
- **Engineering use:** LNG/LPG spill modelling, cryogenic fuel boil-off, vapor cloud dispersion pre-ignition, pool-fire baseline calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fick's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fick's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fick's Law of Diffusion** is a constitutive equation that quantitatively describes the transport of mass (species, vapors, smoke particulates) through a medium under the driving force of a **concentration gradient**. In the context of fire propagation and dynamics, Fick's Law governs the **molecular diffusion of fuel vapors, oxidizers, combustion products, and thermal energy** across spatial domains, forming the mathematical foundation of mass-transfer analysis in fire science.

The law exists in two complementary mathematical statements:
- **Fick's First Law** (steady-state, non-equilibrium): Relates the diffusive mass flux directly to the spatial concentration gradient.
- **Fick's Second Law** (transient, time-dependent): Predicts how the concentration profile itself evolves in time as a consequence of the diffusive process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PESFT528, Module 4):** Students must be able to *derive, state, and apply* both Fick's First and Second Laws, identify the diffusion coefficient $D$ and its physical dimensions, and interpret diffusion phenomena in fire-relevant scenarios such as fuel vapor migration, smoke layer formation, and species transport in compartment fires.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Drop of Ink in Water"**
> Imagine placing a single drop of dark ink into a still glass of clear water. Initially, the ink is concentrated in one small region. With time, the dark color *spreads* outward, becoming gradually fainter near the drop and slightly darker farther away, until eventually the entire glass is uniformly tinted. The ink molecules move from a region of **high concentration** to a region of **low concentration**, driven purely by random molecular motion.
>
> - The **rate of spreading** depends on how agitated the molecules are (temperature) and how easily they slip past one another (medium properties). 
> - In a *fire*, the same principle governs how **fuel vapor** leaks out of a burning solid into the surrounding air, and how **smoke particles** disperse through a room. Fick's Law is the mathematical translation of this everyday observation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

The following table introduces the foundational quantities used throughout the topic. All quantities must be expressed in **SI units** in KTU examinations.

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $J$ | Diffusive mass flux (per unit area per unit time) | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| $D$ | Mass diffusivity (diffusion coefficient) | $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| $C$ | Species concentration (mass per unit volume) | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$ |
| $x$ | Spatial coordinate along the diffusion direction | $\text{m}$ |
| $t$ | Time | $\text{s}$ |
| $M$ | Molar mass | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constant:** For gases at standard conditions (e298 K, 1 atm), the **mass diffusivity $D$** typically has an order of magnitude of **$\mathbf{\sim 10^{-5} \ m^2 \cdot s^{-1}}$**. This is a frequently tested benchmark value in KTU 2024 Scheme questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fick's First Law — Conceptual Breakdown

Fick's First Law is a **constitutive postulate** (analogous to Fourier's Law for heat conduction or Newton's Law for viscosity). It asserts that the **diffusive mass flux** is proportional to the negative of the **concentration gradient**.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- Molecules in a region of higher concentration undergo *more frequent intermolecular collisions* than molecules in a lower-concentration region.
- On a macroscopic scale, this *statistical imbalance* manifests as a *net flow* of mass from high- to low-concentration regions.
- The proportionality constant linking the flux to the gradient is the **diffusion coefficient $D$**, which encapsulates all micro-scale molecular interaction physics into a single macroscopic parameter.
- The **negative sign** is a mathematical encoding of the physical directionality: mass flows *down* the gradient (from high to low), so the gradient is positive while the flux is negative.

**One-dimensional scalar form:**
$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

**General three-dimensional vector form:**
$$\vec{J} = -D \, \nabla C$$

For anisotropic media, $D$ becomes a second-order tensor; in fire-engineering problems, the medium is almost always treated as **isotropic** (scalar $D$).

### 2.2 Fick's Second Law — Conceptual Breakdown

Fick's First Law tells us *how much* mass flows at a given instant. Fick's Second Law tells us *how the concentration field changes in time* as a result of that flow.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- Consider a small control volume of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area.
- Mass entering the left face minus mass exiting the right face equals the rate of accumulation inside.
- Applying the **principle of conservation of mass** to this control volume yields a partial differential equation (PDE) in both $x$ and $t$.
- Substituting Fick's First Law for the flux and assuming $D$ is spatially uniform (constant diffusivity), the resulting PDE is the **diffusion equation**.

**One-dimensional form (constant $D$):**
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**General three-dimensional form:**
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \nabla^2 C$$

### 2.3 Boundary and Initial Conditions (Standard KTU Problem-Solving Toolkit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems almost always require a complete mathematical specification including the following conditions. Memorize all four standard forms.

| Condition Type | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dirichlet (Type I) | Concentration prescribed at boundary | $C(0,t) = C_s$ |
| Neumann (Type II) | Flux prescribed at boundary | $-D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x=0} = J_0$ |
| Robin / Mixed (Type III) | Convective exchange at boundary | $-D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x=0} = h_m (C_s - C_\infty)$ |
| Initial Condition (IC) | Concentration profile at $t=0$ | $C(x,0) = f(x)$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Fick's Law" cheat sheet below is the single most-tested set of equations in this module. Replicate it verbatim in the examination hall.**

| Equation | Name | Physical Meaning | Key Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law (1-D) | Flux proportional to gradient | Negative sign = flow from high to low $C$ |
| $\vec{J} = -D \, \nabla C$ | Fick's First Law (3-D vector) | Generalized form | $D$ scalar for isotropic media |
| $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (1-D, constant $D$) | Time evolution of $C$ | PDE, requires IC + BCs to solve |
| $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (D \, \nabla C)$ | Fick's Second Law (variable $D$) | Generalized transient form | Reduces to above if $D$ = constant |
| $C(x,t) = C_s \, \text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Semi-infinite solution | Diffusion into a deep medium | $\text{erfc}$ is the complementary error function |
| $\delta \sim \sqrt{Dt}$ | Characteristic diffusion length | Depth of penetration at time $t$ | Critical for estimating fire-spread timescales |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Fire Dynamics

- **Fuel Vapor Transport:** Solid and liquid fuels (e.g., polyurethane, gasoline pools) release flammable vapors that migrate through the air by molecular diffusion. Fick's Law predicts the vapor concentration profile around the fuel and determines whether the mixture has reached the **lower flammable limit (LFL)**.
- **Smoke and Toxic Gas Spread:** In a compartment fire, the migration of CO, HCN, and soot particles away from the fire source follows Fickian diffusion superimposed on bulk convective flow.
- **Heat-Mass Transfer Analogy:** The mathematical form of Fick's Second Law is *identical* to the heat conduction equation (Fourier's Second Law). This is the **Sherwood–Nusselt analogy** used to transfer empirical correlations between heat and mass transfer.
- **Char-Layer Oxidation and Pyrolysis Fronts:** In-depth solid-phase diffusion of oxygen into char controls the **afterglow and deep-seated smoldering** behavior of fires.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law (Constant Diffusivity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a guaranteed 5-to-7 mark question in any KTU 2024 ESE on this module.** Master the logical steps and the algebraic transitions in sequence.

**Starting Premise:** Fick's First Law provides the relationship between mass flux and concentration gradient:
$$J(x,t) = -D \frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1 — Set up a differential control volume.**
Consider a one-dimensional slice of the medium between positions $x$ and $x + dx$, with a unit cross-sectional area ($A = 1 \, \text{m}^2$). The volume of this slice is:
$$dV = A \cdot dx = 1 \cdot dx = dx$$

**Step 2 — Compute the net rate of mass accumulation inside the slice.**
Mass enters through the left face at rate $J(x,t)$ (in $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$) and leaves through the right face at rate $J(x+dx,t)$. The **net accumulation rate** is therefore:
$$\text{Net in} - \text{out} = J(x,t) \cdot A - J(x+dx,t) \cdot A = \left[ J(x,t) - J(x+dx,t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Law of Conservation of Mass.**
The net mass accumulation rate must equal the rate of change of mass stored inside the slice:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl[C(x,t) \cdot dV\bigr] = J(x,t) - J(x+dx,t)$$
