Since the volume element $dx$ is constant in time, the left side simplifies to:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \, dx = J(x,t) - J(x+dx,t)$$

**Step 4 — Taylor-expand the right-hand side flux term.**
Expand $J(x+dx,t)$ about the point $x$:
$$J(x+dx,t) = J(x,t) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 J}{\partial x^2}(dx)^2 + \dots$$

Retaining only the first-order term (valid for an infinitesimal slice):
$$J(x+dx,t) \approx J(x,t) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Taylor expansion back into the conservation statement.**
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,dx = J(x,t) - \left[ J(x,t) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx \right]$$

The $J(x,t)$ terms cancel exactly:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,dx = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx$$

Dividing both sides by the non-zero slice thickness $dx$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Fick's First Law for the flux.**
Replace $J$ with $-D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the spatial derivative (constant $D$ assumption).**
The two negatives cancel. Since $D$ is treated as spatially uniform, it can be factored out of the derivative:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

This is the canonical **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion in one dimension**, derived rigorously from the first law and the principle of mass conservation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extension to 3-D Cartesian Coordinates:** By repeating the argument along $x$, $y$, and $z$ and adding the contributions (since the operators are linear and isotropic), one obtains:
> $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\left(\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}\right) = D\,\nabla^2 C$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Characteristic Diffusion Depth in a Smoldering Char Layer

**Problem Statement:** Oxygen diffuses into a porous char layer with mass diffusivity $D = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. Estimate the depth $\delta$ to which oxygen has penetrated after $t = 600 \, \text{s}$ (10 minutes) of smoldering.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing scaling law.**
For diffusion into a semi-infinite medium, the characteristic penetration depth scales as:
$$\delta \sim \sqrt{D\,t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$\delta \sim \sqrt{(2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 600}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the argument of the square root.**
$$(2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 600 = 1200 \times 10^{-5} = 1.2 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**
$$\delta \sim \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-2}} = \sqrt{0.012} \approx 0.1095 \, \text{m} \approx 11 \, \text{cm}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** After 10 minutes, oxygen has diffused roughly 11 cm into the char. This depth is the order of magnitude of the smoldering reaction zone thickness and is essential for designing fire-retardant barriers and predicting smoldering-to-flaming transitions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Fourier's and Ohm's Laws

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative table is a frequent KTU short-answer requirement. Examiners explicitly test the student's ability to recognize the **unified structure of transport phenomena**.

| Phenomenon | Driving "Gradient" | Flux Quantity | Proportionality Constant | Governing Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat conduction | Temperature gradient $\nabla T$ | Heat flux $\vec{q}$ | Thermal conductivity $k$ | Fourier's Law: $\vec{q} = -k\,\nabla T$ |
| Mass diffusion | Concentration gradient $\nabla C$ | Mass flux $\vec{J}$ | Diffusivity $D$ | **Fick's Law: $\vec{J} = -D\,\nabla C$** |
| Electric conduction | Potential gradient $\nabla V$ | Current density $\vec{i}$ | Conductivity $\sigma$ | Ohm's Law: $\vec{i} = -\sigma\,\nabla V$ |
| Fluid momentum | Velocity gradient $\nabla u$ | Shear stress $\tau$ | Viscosity $\mu$ | Newton's Law: $\tau = \mu\,\nabla u$ |

The same mathematical form across all four laws is the basis of the **analogy principle** that allows empirical heat-transfer correlations (e.g., Nusselt number relations) to be transferred directly to mass-transfer problems (Sherwood number).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fickian Diffusion in a Fire Compartment

The mermaid diagram below maps the functional architecture of how Fick's Law operates as a *sub-system* within a compartment fire scenario. It traces the inputs, transformations, and outputs of the diffusion process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([FIRE COMPARTMENT INITIAL STATE]) --> A1{Fuel Source Active?}
    A1 -- YES --> A2[Generation of Fuel Vapor at High C]
    A1 -- NO --> A3[No Vapor Flux Q1 equals 0]
    A2 --> A4[Concentration Gradient dC by dx forms]
    A4 --> A5[Ficks First Law Computes Flux J equals minus D times dC by dx]
    A5 --> A6{D crosses LFL Threshold?}
    A6 -- YES --> A7[Ignitable Mixture at Q2]
    A6 -- NO --> A8[Sub-LFL Plume Q3]
    A7 --> A9[Mass Conservation in Control Volume]
    A8 --> A9
    A3 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Ficks Second Law Solves dC by dt equals D d2C by dx2]
    A10 --> A11[Concentration Profile C at x comma t Updates]
    A11 --> A12{Steady State Reached?}
    A12 -- YES --> A13[dC by dt equals 0 Outputs Steady Profile Q4]
    A12 -- NO --> A14[Transient Profile Evolves Q5]
    A14 --> A12
    A13 --> A15([FINAL FUEL DISTRIBUTION MAP])
    A14 --> A15
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **$Q_1$:** Trivial flux output when no fuel source is present.
- **$Q_2$:** Ignitable mixture state — critical safety concern; triggers combustion if an ignition source is encountered.
- **$Q_3$:** Sub-LFL state — vapor is present but not yet flammable; useful for ventilation engineering.
- **$Q_4$:** Steady concentration profile — reached when $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = 0$, the time derivative vanishes and Fick's Second Law collapses to a simple ordinary differential equation.
- **$Q_5$:** Transient profile — the time-evolving concentration field governed by the full PDE.

### 4.2 Diffusion Profile Visualization: Spatial Concentration Decay

The next diagram visualizes the spatial profile of a typical Fickian diffusion scenario — fuel vapor concentration as a function of position $x$ at successive time instants $t_1 < t_2 < t_3$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROFILE_EVOLUTION
        P1[Concentration at t1] -- DECAYS TO --> P2[Concentration at t2]
        P2 -- DECAYS TO --> P3[Concentration at t3]
        P1 -- PEAK AT SURFACE --> N1[Cs Source Concentration]
        P2 -- PEAK AT SURFACE --> N1
        P3 -- PEAK AT SURFACE --> N1
    end
    N1 --> X_AXIS[Position x increasing away from source]
    P1 --> O_AXIS[Concentration C x t decreasing with x]
    P2 --> O_AXIS
    P3 --> O_AXIS
    P1 -. CHARACTERISTIC LENGTH .-> L1[delta1 proportional to sqrt D t1]
    P2 -. CHARACTERISTIC LENGTH .-> L2[delta2 proportional to sqrt D t2]
    P3 -. CHARACTERISTIC LENGTH .-> L3[delta3 proportional to sqrt D t3]
```

**Engineering Takeaway from the Visualization:**
- The surface concentration $C_s$ remains anchored (a Dirichlet boundary condition imposed by the fuel).
- Far from the source, the concentration asymptotically approaches the ambient value $C_\infty \to 0$.
- The **characteristic diffusion length $\delta$** grows as $\sqrt{Dt}$ — this square-root dependence is the universal fingerprint of Fickian diffusion and is the most-quoted scaling law in fire science.

### 4.3 Cross-Modal Block Diagram: Coupling of Fick's First and Second Laws

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([INPUT C x 0 Initial Profile]) --> S1[Set Up IC and BCs]
    S1 --> S2[Apply Ficks First Law to Obtain J x t]
    S2 --> S3[Apply Conservation of Mass to Control Volume]
    S3 --> S4[Derive Ficks Second Law PDE]
    S4 --> S5[Choose Solution Method Analytical or Numerical]
    S5 -- ANALYTICAL --> S6[Error Function Solution erfc for Semi Infinite]
    S5 -- NUMERICAL --> S7[Finite Difference Time Domain Discretization]
    S6 --> S8([OUTPUT C x t Concentration Field])
    S7 --> S8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the physical significance of the negative sign in the equation.**

**Model Answer:**

Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the **diffusive mass flux** of a species is directly proportional to the negative of the **concentration gradient** of that species:

$$J = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

The proportionality constant $D$ is the **mass diffusivity** (units: $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$).

**Significance of the negative sign:** The concentration gradient $\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ is positive in the direction of *increasing* concentration. However, physical diffusion always transports mass *from* regions of high concentration *to* regions of low concentration, i.e., the flux is directed *opposite* to the gradient. The negative sign mathematically encodes this directional opposition, ensuring the flux $J$ is positive when mass moves toward decreasing $C$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Fick's First Law and Fick's Second Law. State the typical order of magnitude of gas-phase mass diffusivity at standard conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fick's First Law | Fick's Second Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Form | Algebraic / constitutive | Partial differential equation (PDE) |
| Variables | $J$ and $C$ at a single instant | $C$ as a function of $x$ and $t$ |
| Physical meaning | Rate of mass transport at a point | Time evolution of concentration field |
| Typical equation | $J = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ |

**Order of magnitude of gas-phase mass diffusivity at standard conditions:**
$$D \sim 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** For every 14-mark question, students are offered **Module Internal Choice**. Two fully independent options are provided below; answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion in one dimension from Fick's First Law and the principle of conservation of mass. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** The medium is one-dimensional and isotropic.
**Assumption 2:** The diffusion coefficient $D$ is **constant** in space and time.
**Assumption 3:** No internal sources or sinks of mass exist inside the control volume.
**Assumption 4:** The only mode of mass transport is molecular diffusion (no bulk convection).

**Step 1 — Control Volume Definition:**
Consider a thin slab between $x$ and $x + dx$ with unit cross-sectional area. The slab volume is $dV = dx$.

**Step 2 — Mass Conservation Statement:**
The rate of mass accumulation inside the slab equals the difference between inflow and outflow:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,dx = J(x,t) - J(x+dx,t)$$

**Step 3 — Taylor Expansion of the Outflow:**
$$J(x+dx,t) \approx J(x,t) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify:**
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,dx = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Fick's First Law** $J = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}}$$

This is Fick's Second Law for one-dimensional diffusion with constant $D$. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** A semi-infinite solid fuel slab initially contains no fuel vapor. At time $t = 0$, the surface concentration is suddenly raised to $C_s$ and held constant. Using the standard semi-infinite diffusion solution:
$$C(x,t) = C_s\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$
**(i)** Calculate the concentration at depth $x = 5\,\text{mm}$ after $t = 60\,\text{s}$ if $D = 4 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ and $C_s = 0.5\,\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$.
**(ii)** Estimate the depth $\delta$ at which the concentration drops to $5\%$ of $C_s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Concentration Calculation:**

Compute the argument of the error function:
$$\xi = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{0.005}{2\sqrt{(4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 60}}$$

The denominator:
$$2\sqrt{(4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 60} = 2\sqrt{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \times 0.04899 = 0.09798\,\text{m}$$

Therefore:
$$\xi = \frac{0.005}{0.09798} \approx 0.0510$$

Consult standard tables (or a calculator) for the complementary error function:
$$\text{erfc}(0.0510) \approx 0.9422$$

Therefore:
$$C(0.005, 60) = 0.5 \times 0.9422 = 0.4711\,\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$$

**Part (ii) — Depth at 5% of $C_s$:**

We require $C/C_s = 0.05$, i.e., $\text{erfc}(\xi) = 0.05$. Inverting the erfc function:
$$\xi = \text{erfc}^{-1}(0.05) \approx 1.3863$$

Using the characteristic scaling:
$$\delta = 2\xi\sqrt{Dt} = 2 \times 1.3863 \times \sqrt{(4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 60}$$
$$\delta = 2.7726 \times 0.04899 = 0.1358\,\text{m} \approx 13.6\,\text{cm}$$

$$\boxed{C(0.005, 60) \approx 0.471\,\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}, \qquad \delta \approx 0.136\,\text{m}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a one-dimensional differential control volume analysis, show that the time-dependent diffusion of a scalar species in an isotropic, constant-property medium is governed by Fick's Second Law. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** Consider a Cartesian control volume of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ centered at position $(x, y, z)$. Assume the species mass flux is purely one-dimensional in the $x$-direction (a common simplification when concentration varies predominantly in one direction).

**Conservation of Mass Statement:**
Rate of accumulation of mass inside the CV = Net mass inflow through all faces:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,(dx\,dy\,dz) = \left[J_x - \left(J_x + \frac{\partial J_x}{\partial x}dx\right)\right](dy\,dz)$$

The flux contributions in the $y$- and $z$-directions are zero by the one-dimensional assumption. The right side reduces to:
$$\text{Net inflow} = -\frac{\partial J_x}{\partial x}(dx\,dy\,dz)$$

Dividing through by the volume $(dx\,dy\,dz)$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_x}{\partial x}$$

**Apply Fick's First Law** in one dimension: $J_x = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$. With $D$ constant:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

This PDE is the desired Fick's Second Law. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** Methane vapor leaks from a pipeline at $C_s = 0.8\,\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$ into still air. The diffusivity of methane in air is $D = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. Determine: **(i)** the diffusive mass flux at the surface at $t = 0^+$, and **(ii)** the time required for the methane concentration at $x = 2\,\text{cm}$ to reach $30\%$ of the surface value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Initial Diffusive Mass Flux at the Surface:**

For a semi-infinite medium with constant surface concentration, the analytical solution is:
$$C(x,t) = C_s\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

The flux is given by Fick's First Law:
$$J(x,t) = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = -D \cdot C_s \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

Using the chain rule and the derivative identity $\frac{d}{du}\text{erfc}(u) = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}e^{-u^2}$:
$$J(x,t) = D \cdot C_s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D t}}\,e^{-x^2/(4Dt)}$$

At the surface ($x = 0$) and in the limit $t \to 0^+$ (which we treat as the "initial" instant):
$$J(0, 0^+) = D \cdot C_s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} = C_s\,\sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}}$$

Numerically (taking $t \to 0^+$ as a limiting behavior, evaluated at the very small instant $t = 1\,\text{s}$ for an indicative value):
$$J(0, 1) = 0.8 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 1}} = 0.8 \cdot \sqrt{7.00 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$J(0, 1) = 0.8 \times 2.647 \times 10^{-3} = 2.118 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Part (ii) — Time to Reach 30% of $C_s$ at $x = 2\,\text{cm}$:**

We require $C/C_s = 0.30$, i.e., $\text{erfc}(\xi) = 0.30$. Inverting:
$$\xi = \text{erfc}^{-1}(0.30) \approx 0.7329$$

From the definition $\xi = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$, solve for $t$:
$$t = \frac{x^2}{4 D \xi^2} = \frac{(0.02)^2}{4 \times (2.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (0.7329)^2}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$4 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5371 = 4.727 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

Therefore:
$$t = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{4.727 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 8.46\,\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{J(0, 0^+) \approx C_s\sqrt{D/(\pi t)};\quad t \approx 8.46\,\text{s to reach 30\% at 2 cm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in Fick's First Law: Worth 1 mark by itself, but loses physical-interpretation marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $D$ units:** Writing $D$ in $\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ in one part and $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ in another within the same problem — KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 marks for unit inconsistency.
> 3. **Skipping the IC and BC statement** in a derivation: A 7-mark derivation question that omits the initial condition $C(x,0) = f(x)$ and boundary conditions $C(0,t) = C_s$, $C(\infty,t) = 0$ will lose at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Treating $D$ as a vector:** $D$ is a *scalar* in isotropic media. Marking it as $\vec{D}$ in the equation is a common conceptual error.
> 5. **Numerical $\text{erfc}$ values:** Examiners expect students to either quote standard table values (e.g., $\text{erfc}(1) = 0.1573$, $\text{erfc}(0.5) = 0.4795$) or show an explicit numerical inversion step. Computing $\text{erfc}^{-1}$ without showing the lookup method is penalized.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation Aid)**

- **Fick's First Law (1-D):** $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$. **Memorize the negative sign** — it is the most-tested single feature.
- **Fick's First Law (3-D):** $\vec{J} = -D\,\nabla C$. Treat $D$ as a **scalar** for isotropic media.
- **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial C/\partial t = D\,\partial^2 C/\partial x^2$ (1-D, constant $D$). General form: $\partial C/\partial t = \nabla \cdot (D\,\nabla C)$.
- **Diffusivity Benchmark:** $D \sim 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ for gases at NTP. Liquids: $D \sim 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. Solids: $D \sim 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ or smaller.
- **Characteristic Length:** $\delta \sim \sqrt{Dt}$. **Square-root dependence on time** is the universal signature of Fickian diffusion.
- **Semi-Infinite Solution:** $C(x,t) = C_s\,\text{erfc}\!\left(x/(2\sqrt{Dt})\right)$ — must be quoted verbatim in any diffusion-penetration numerical.
- **Initial Condition (IC):** $C(x,0) = f(x)$ — always required for transient solutions.
- **Boundary Conditions (BCs):** Dirichlet (fixed $C$), Neumann (fixed flux), Robin (convective $h_m$). All three forms have appeared in KTU past papers.
- **Analogy Principle:** Fick's Law has the same mathematical structure as Fourier's Law (heat), Ohm's Law (electricity), and Darcy's Law (fluid flow). Cross-referencing analogies earns easy marks in KTU short-answer questions.
- **Engineering Applications in Fire Science:** (i) fuel vapor transport to flame zone, (ii) smoke dispersal, (iii) char oxidation, (iv) afterglow in smoldering, (v) LFL determination, (vi) fire-retardant barrier design, (vii) compartment fire ventilation analysis.
- **Limitation:** Fick's Law assumes *binary* diffusion (only two species interacting). For multi-component mixtures in fire (typically N$_2$, O$_2$, CO$_2$, H$_2$O, CO, soot), the **Stefan–Maxwell equations** are the more general framework, but Fick's approximation is the *industry-standard simplification* tested at KTU B.Tech level.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Burning of Solid Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT528 - THEORY OF FIRE PROPAGATION AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Burning of Solid Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Burning of Solid Fuels** is the thermo-chemical process by which condensed-phase combustible materials (such as wood, polymers, coal, char, and cellulosic solids) undergo thermal decomposition, volatile release, and heterogeneous surface oxidation to liberate heat, light, and combustion products. In the framework of **fire dynamics**, solid-fuel burning is treated as a coupled, multi-stage phenomenon involving:

1. **Heat-up (preheating) phase** – conductive and radiative heating of the virgin solid to its decomposition temperature.
2. **Pyrolysis (thermal degradation) phase** – endothermic decomposition of the solid matrix into volatile gases and a carbonaceous **char** residue.
3. **Flaming combustion phase** – mixing of the released pyrolysates with oxidizer and homogeneous gas-phase combustion in the diffusion flame above the solid.
4. **Char (smoldering) combustion phase** – heterogeneous, surface-mediated oxidation of the residual solid when volatiles are exhausted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic establishes the foundational mass-loss and energy-release models (e.g., the *quasi-steady gasification* assumption, the *B-number criterion*, and *flame-spread theories*) upon which later modules on fire growth and compartment fires are built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **campfire log** 🔥. Before flames appear, the log's surface first darkens, then cracks, releasing a yellow, smoky stream of gases. The log itself is *not* what is burning in the flame — the gases escaping from it are. Only after the volatiles are driven off does the residual black *charcoal* glow red and burn slowly from the outside in. Solid-fuel burning is therefore best visualised as a **"two-stage relay race"**: the gas-phase flame runs first (using the pyrolysates as fuel), and the char oxidation finishes the race.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Most solids do **not** burn directly. They decompose thermally into combustible gases that burn in the gas phase. The rate-limiting step is usually the heat flowing *into* the solid, not the chemistry of the flame above it.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_s$ | Solid density | kg/m³ |
| $k_s$ | Solid thermal conductivity | W/(m·K) |
| $c_s$ | Solid specific heat | kJ/(kg·K) |
| $\Delta h_g$ | Heat of gasification | kJ/kg (≈ 1800 for wood) |
| $T_p$ | Pyrolysis temperature | 300 – 600 °C |
| $\dot{q}''_{cr}$ | Critical heat flux for ignition | 10 – 25 kW/m² (piloted) |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** |
| $B$ | Spalding mass-transfer number | dimensionless |
| $\chi_r$ | Radiative fraction | 0.2 – 0.6 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Sub-Processes of Solid-Fuel Burning

**A. Solid Heat-Up (Pre-heating Stage)**
- External heat flux $\dot{q}''_e$ is absorbed at the exposed surface.
- Conduction into the depth of the solid raises the sub-surface temperature.
- The thermal wave penetrates on a characteristic time scale

$$
t_p \sim \frac{k_s \rho_s c_s \, L^2}{(\dot{q}''_e)^2}
$$

where $L$ is the thickness of the virgin solid.

**B. Pyrolysis (Thermal Degradation)**
- Once the surface reaches $T_p$, the material begins to decompose.
- Reaction typically follows an **Arrhenius** form:

$$
k = A \, e^{-E_a / R T}
$$

- Decomposition is endothermic — energy is consumed (latent-heat-like), characterized by $\Delta h_g$.
- Two kinetic regimes: **kinetically-controlled** (low temperature, slow chemistry) and **heat-transfer-controlled** (high flux, ablation-like).

**C. Flaming Combustion of Volatiles**
- Pyrolysates mix with air and burn in a diffusion flame standing off from the surface.
- Heat is fed back by conduction, convection, and **reradiation** from the flame to the surface.
- Flame structure: a thin blue reaction sheet for soot-poor fuels (e.g., methanol), a thick luminous yellow zone for sooty fuels (e.g., polystyrene, polyurethane).

**D. Char Combustion (Heterogeneous)**
- Remaining carbon reacts directly with O₂ at the surface:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}
$$

- Surface temperature can reach 800 – 1200 °C.
- Rate governed by both **diffusion of O₂ to the surface** and the **Arrhenius surface reaction**.

### 2.2 Why Heat-Flux-Controlled Gasification? (The "Why & How")

- Pyrolysis is highly endothermic; once the surface reaches $T_p$, any additional heat supplied is consumed as latent heat of gasification.
- This makes the surface temperature nearly **constant** at $T_p$ during sustained burning.
- Consequently, the **mass loss rate** becomes a simple linear function of the **net heat feedback**:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{q}''_{net}}{\Delta h_g}
$$

This is the *quasi-steady solid gasification* assumption, the single most useful relation in solid-fire modeling.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{\dot{q}''_{net}}{\Delta h_g}$ | Mass loss rate per unit area (kg/m²·s) |
| 2 | $\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{q}''_e - \dot{q}''_{re} - \dot{q}''_{loss}$ | Energy balance at the burning surface |
| 3 | $\dot{q}''_{re} = \varepsilon_s \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Reradiation loss (W/m²) |
| 4 | $\dot{q}''_{cr} = \sigma (T_{ig}^4 - T_\infty^4) \Big/ \text{(view factor)}$ | Critical ignition heat flux (W/m²) |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{Y_O \, \Delta h_c \, \nu}{c_p (T_\infty - T_s)}$ | **Spalding mass-transfer number** (dimensionless) |
| 6 | $B_o = \dfrac{\Delta h_c \, Y_{O,\infty} \, \nu}{\Delta h_g}$ | Oxidizer–fuel coupling number |
| 7 | $\tau_{ig} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} k_s \rho_s c_s \Big(\dfrac{T_{ig} - T_\infty}{\dot{q}''_e}\Big)^2$ | Time to ignition (semi-infinite slab) |
| 8 | $V_f = \dfrac{\lambda_g \,\delta_g / \rho_s c_s \, \delta T}{\delta T}$ | de Ris opposed-flow flame-spread velocity |
| 9 | $\dot{q}''_{flame} = k_g \, \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{\delta_g}$ | Conductive feedback from flame to surface |
| 10 | $\dot{m}''_{char} = \dfrac{h_m Y_{O,\infty}}{1 + B_{ox}}$ | Heterogeneous char-burning rate (diffusion-limited form) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder for KTU Board Exams:** All $\dot{q}''$ terms are in **W/m² or kW/m²**; $\Delta h_g$ and $\Delta h_c$ in **kJ/kg**; $\dot{m}''$ in **kg/m²·s**; $T$ in **K** or **°C** (be consistent within a single problem).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance-Based Fire Design (ISO 16732, NFPA 101):** Wall and ceiling lining materials are tested in cone calorimeters to determine $\dot{q}''_{cr}$, $\Delta h_g$, and $\chi_r$ — these are the inputs to zone and CFD models.
- **Forest-Fire Modelling:** Wildland fuels are classified by their **fuel-bed bulk density** and **heat of gasification**; the Rothermel and Balbi fire-spread models are direct descendants of the de Ris framework.
- **Fire-Retardant Design:** Sublimation-type retardants increase $\Delta h_g$, thereby reducing $\dot{m}''$ for a given heat flux — the central lever of intumescent coatings.
- **Compartment Fire Calculation:** In a fully-developed room fire, the post-flashover burning rate is set by the **ventilation factor** $A_v \sqrt{H_v}$, which is itself derived from the solid-fuel gasification relation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Loss Rate from Energy Balance at a Burning Surface

Consider a semi-infinite solid of density $\rho_s$ and specific heat $c_s$, subjected to a net surface heat flux $\dot{q}''_{net}$. We assume the surface is held at the pyrolysis temperature $T_p$ throughout the gasification process (quasi-steady assumption).

**Step 1 — Energy balance at the receding surface**

The heat arriving at the surface is consumed as: (a) the latent heat to gasify the solid, and (b) the energy stored in raising the in-depth virgin material to $T_p$. The first term dominates for thick fuels, giving the standard result.

$$
\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta h_g
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for mass-loss rate**

Rearranging:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{q}''_{net}}{\Delta h_g}
$$

**Step 3 — Explicit expansion of $\dot{q}''_{net}$**

For a flame-heated surface with external flux $\dot{q}''_e$, flame feedback $\dot{q}''_{flame}$, and reradiation loss $\dot{q}''_{re}$:

$$
\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{q}''_e + \dot{q}''_{flame} - \varepsilon_s \sigma \left(T_s^4 - T_\infty^4\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back**

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{q}''_e + \dot{q}''_{flame} - \varepsilon_s \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)}{\Delta h_g}\;}
$$

> This is the working equation of every KTU board numerical on solid-fuel burning.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Time to Ignition for a Semi-Infinite Solid

We model a thermally-thick slab (Biot number → 0), initially at $T_\infty$, exposed to a constant surface flux $\dot{q}''_e$. We seek the time $t_{ig}$ at which the surface temperature reaches the ignition temperature $T_{ig}$.

**Step 1 — Solution of the 1-D conduction equation (constant surface flux)**

The textbook solution for a semi-infinite solid under a constant flux $\dot{q}''_e$ is:

$$
T(x,t) - T_\infty = \frac{2 \,\dot{q}''_e \sqrt{\alpha_s t / \pi}}{k_s} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4 \alpha_s t}\right) - \frac{\dot{q}''_e x}{k_s} \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{\alpha_s t}}\right)
$$

where $\alpha_s = k_s / (\rho_s c_s)$ is the thermal diffusivity.

**Step 2 — Surface temperature (set $x = 0$)**

The complementary-error-function term vanishes at $x = 0$, leaving:

$$
T_s(t) - T_\infty = \frac{2 \,\dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi k_s \rho_s c_s}} \sqrt{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Set $T_s = T_{ig}$ and solve for $t_{ig}$**

$$
T_{ig} - T_\infty = \frac{2 \,\dot{q}''_e}{\sqrt{\pi k_s \rho_s c_s}} \sqrt{t_{ig}}
$$

Square both sides and isolate $t_{ig}$:

$$
t_{ig} = \frac{\pi \, k_s \rho_s c_s}{4 \,(\dot{q}''_e)^2}\,(T_{ig} - T_\infty)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Final canonical form**

$$
\boxed{\;t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} k_s \rho_s c_s \left(\frac{T_{ig} - T_\infty}{\dot{q}''_e}\right)^2\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU numericals:** $t_{ig} \propto 1/(\dot{q}''_e)^2$ — doubling the external flux reduces the ignition time to **one-quarter**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Spalding's B-Number for a Diffusion Flame over a Solid

A stoichiometric diffusion flame of pyrolysates burns in air above the solid. By Spalding's classical transfer-number argument, the energy released by combustion of unit mass of fuel is partitioned between heating the products and re-radiating/conducting back to the surface. The dimensionless **mass-transfer number** $B$ is defined as:

$$
B = \frac{Y_{O,\infty}\,\Delta h_{c,eff}\,\nu}{c_p (T_\infty - T_s)}
$$

**Step 1 — Mass flux of pyrolysates from a quiescent surface**

If we treat the flame as a thin sheet a small distance $\delta$ above the surface, the conductive heat flux to the surface is:

$$
\dot{q}''_{flame} = \frac{\lambda_g (T_f - T_s)}{\delta}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate to the energy needed to gasify the fuel at rate $\dot{m}''$**

$$
\dot{m}'' \, \Delta h_g = \dot{q}''_{flame} = \frac{\lambda_g (T_f - T_s)}{\delta}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $\delta$ in terms of $\dot{m}''$ via continuity and species balance**

The stoichiometric requirement gives a flame-standoff $\delta \propto \lambda_g / \dot{m}'' c_p$. Substituting and rearranging yields the B-number form:

$$
\dot{m}'' \propto \frac{\lambda_g}{c_p \, \delta}\, B
$$

**Step 4 — The closed-form Spalding result**

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}'' = \frac{\lambda_g}{c_p \, \delta}\, \ln(1 + B) \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_g}{c_p \, \delta} \, B \quad \text{(for } B \gg 1\text{)}\;}
$$

For most condensed fuels $B \approx 2$–$5$, so the log form is preferred at the **board-exam level**.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A horizontal PMMA slab, $\rho_s = 1190$ kg/m³, $c_s = 1.46$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 25$ °C, is exposed to $\dot{q}''_e = 30$ kW/m². Its ignition temperature is $T_{ig} = 320$ °C. Compute the time to piloted ignition. Take $k_s = 0.20$ W/(m·K).

**Step 1 — Convert all units to SI**

$\dot{q}''_e = 30{,}000$ W/m², $T_{ig} - T_\infty = 295$ K, $c_s = 1460$ J/(kg·K), $k_s = 0.20$ W/(m·K).

**Step 2 — Compute $k_s \rho_s c_s$**

$$
k_s \rho_s c_s = 0.20 \times 1190 \times 1460 = 3.4748 \times 10^{5}\ \text{W²·s/(m⁴·K²)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $t_{ig}$ equation**

$$
t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(3.4748 \times 10^{5}) \left(\frac{295}{30{,}000}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation**

$$
\left(\frac{295}{30{,}000}\right)^{2} = (9.833 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 9.669 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s·m²/W}
$$

$$
t_{ig} = 0.7854 \times 3.4748 \times 10^{5} \times 9.669 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
t_{ig} \approx 0.7854 \times 33.61 \approx 26.4\ \text{s}
$$

**Result:** $t_{ig} \approx \mathbf{26\ \text{seconds}}$ — consistent with published PMMA cone-calorimeter data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Solid-Fuel Burning Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Heat Flux q_doubleprime_e] --> B[Pre-Heat Phase]
    B --> C{T_s Greater Than T_p?}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Pyrolysis Zone]
    D --> E[Volatile Gases Rise]
    E --> F[Diffusion Flame Sheet]
    F -- Flame Feedback q_doubleprime_flame --> B
    F -- Radiative Loss q_doubleprime_re --> G[Ambient]
    D --> H[Char Residue]
    H --> I[Char Oxidation C plus O2]
    I -- Surface Oxidation Flux --> F
    I -- Re-radiation --> G
    H --> J{Volatiles Depleted?}
    J -- No --> F
    J -- Yes --> K[Smoldering Combustion]
    K --> L[Ash Residue]
```

### 4.2 Process-Stage Interaction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Virgin Solid] --> step2[Heat Absorption]
    step2 --> step3[Thermal Decomposition]
    step3 --> step4[Volatile Release]
    step4 --> step5[Gas-Phase Mixing]
    step5 --> step6[Diffusion Flame]
    step6 -- Feedback --> step2
    step3 --> step7[Char Layer Formation]
    step7 --> step8[Surface O2 Attack]
    step8 --> step9[Heterogeneous Reaction]
    step9 -- Radiant Heat --> step6
    step9 --> step10[Final Ash]
```

### 4.3 Energy Partition at a Burning Solid Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Qe[External Flux q_doubleprime_e] --> Net[Net Heat at Surface]
    Qf[Flame Conduction q_doubleprime_flame] --> Net
    Net --> Gasif[Heat of Gasification m_doubleprime times Delta h_g]
    Net --> ReRad[Reradiation Loss epsilon sigma Ts to the fourth]
    Net --> Conv[Convective Loss to Ambient]
    Gasif --> Pyro[Pyrolysis Product]
    Pyro --> Flame[Gas-Phase Combustion]
    Flame --> Qf
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:**
> 1. The **central cycle** (Pre-Heat ↔ Flame ↔ Pyrolysis ↔ Volatiles) is *self-sustaining* — that is *steady burning*.
> 2. The **char branch** is *secondary* and only dominates after volatiles are exhausted.
> 3. The **reradiation arrow** is the *only* loss term in the *quasi-steady* analysis — every other arrow is a gain or recycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the heat of gasification of a solid fuel and state a typical value for wood.**

**Model Answer:**
The **heat of gasification** ($\Delta h_g$) of a solid fuel is the total energy required to convert a unit mass of the virgin solid at ambient temperature into combustible pyrolytic gases at the pyrolysis temperature. It is the sum of the sensible heat to raise the solid to $T_p$ and the endothermic heat of the decomposition reaction.
Typical value for dry wood: $\Delta h_g \approx 1800$ kJ/kg (range 1500 – 2500 kJ/kg depending on moisture and species). **[3 marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between piloted and unpiloted ignition of a solid, citing the role of critical heat flux.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Piloted ignition** occurs when an external pilot (spark, hot wire) is present; it requires only that the fuel–air mixture above the heated solid reaches its *flame (flammability) limit*. Required surface temperature and critical heat flux are **lower**.
- **Unpiloted (auto) ignition** requires the pyrolysate–air mixture to reach the *auto-ignition* temperature by self-heating; it demands a much higher surface temperature and a much higher **critical heat flux** $\dot{q}''_{cr}$.
- For PMMA, $\dot{q}''_{cr,piloted} \approx 14$ kW/m² while $\dot{q}''_{cr,unpiloted} \approx 28$ kW/m². **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the quasi-steady mass-loss rate relation $\dot{m}'' = \dot{q}''_{net}/\Delta h_g$ for a thermally-thick solid fuel undergoing pyrolysis. Clearly state the assumptions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wood panel ($\rho_s = 500$ kg/m³, $c_s = 1.6$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta h_g = 1800$ kJ/kg, $\varepsilon_s = 0.9$, $T_p = 350$ °C) is exposed to a flame giving $\dot{q}''_{flame} = 40$ kW/m² in still air at $T_\infty = 25$ °C. Compute the steady mass-loss rate per unit area. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Alternative)*

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **de Ris** opposed-flow flame-spread mechanism over a solid fuel. State the controlling non-dimensional group and the dependence of spread velocity on opposed-flow velocity. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal PMMA slab, $k_s \rho_s c_s = 3.5 \times 10^5$ W²·s/(m⁴·K²), is exposed to $\dot{q}''_e = 25$ kW/m². The ignition temperature is 320 °C and ambient is 25 °C. Estimate (i) the time to piloted ignition, and (ii) the critical heat flux below which piloted ignition will not occur. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Q(A)(a) — Derivation of $\dot{m}'' = \dot{q}''_{net}/\Delta h_g$**

**Assumptions (write these first for full marks):**
1. Solid is thermally thick (semi-infinite during burning).
2. Surface temperature is constant at $T_p$ (quasi-steady gasification).
3. One-dimensional heat conduction; no in-depth chemical reaction before reaching $T_p$.
4. Heat of gasification $\Delta h_g$ is constant.

**Derivation:**
At the receding surface, an energy balance per unit area per unit time gives:

$$
\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{m}'' \, \Delta h_g
$$

where $\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{q}''_{flame} + \dot{q}''_e - \varepsilon_s \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$. Solving:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{q}''_{flame} + \dot{q}''_e - \varepsilon_s \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)}{\Delta h_g}}
$$

**[7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for boxed form]**

---

#### **Solution to Q(A)(b) — Numerical Mass-Loss Rate**

**Step 1 — Write the net flux equation**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{\dot{q}''_{flame} - \varepsilon_s \sigma (T_p^4 - T_\infty^4)}{\Delta h_g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute reradiation loss**

$T_p = 350 + 273 = 623$ K; $T_\infty = 298$ K.

$$
\dot{q}''_{re} = 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times \left(623^4 - 298^4\right)
$$

$623^4 = 1.504 \times 10^{11}$, $298^4 = 7.886 \times 10^9$.

$$
\dot{q}''_{re} = 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1.504 \times 10^{11} - 7.89 \times 10^9)
$$

$$
\dot{q}''_{re} = 5.103 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.425 \times 10^{11} \approx 7273\ \text{W/m}^2 = 7.27\ \text{kW/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Net flux**

$$
\dot{q}''_{net} = 40 - 7.27 = 32.73\ \text{kW/m}^2 = 32{,}730\ \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Mass loss rate**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \frac{32{,}730}{1{,}800{,}000} \approx 0.0182\ \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

**Result:** $\dot{m}'' \approx \mathbf{0.018\ \text{kg/m}^2\text{·s}}$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q(B)(a) — de Ris Opposed-Flow Spread**

**Sketch description (board-friendly):** A horizontal fuel surface burns with a diffusion flame inclined against an opposing oxidizer flow $V_\infty$. Heat conducted upstream preheats the unburnt fuel; the pyrolysis front propagates in the direction opposite to $V_\infty$.

**Controlling parameter:** the **thermal thickness ratio** $\tau = \sqrt{\alpha_s t_{ig}} / \delta_g$, where $\delta_g$ is the boundary-layer thickness.

**Velocity dependence:** The de Ris solution (1973) gives

$$
V_f \propto \frac{1}{V_\infty}
$$

i.e., **spread velocity decreases with increasing opposed flow**, in contrast to concurrent (wind-aided) flow where $V_f$ *increases* with flow. This is the basis of the classic "**flashover vs backdraft**" interpretation. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q(B)(b) — Ignition Time and Critical Flux**

**(i) Time to piloted ignition:**

$$
t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} k_s \rho_s c_s \left(\frac{T_{ig} - T_\infty}{\dot{q}''_e}\right)^2
$$

$$
t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 3.5 \times 10^{5} \times \left(\frac{320 - 25}{25{,}000}\right)^2
$$

$$
t_{ig} = 2.749 \times 10^{5} \times (1.18 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 2.749 \times 10^{5} \times 1.3924 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{ig} \approx 38.3\ \text{seconds}}
$$

**(ii) Critical heat flux:**

The critical condition corresponds to $\dot{q}''_e = \dot{q}''_{re}$ at $T = T_{ig}$ (steady-state radiation balance):

$$
\dot{q}''_{cr} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{ig}^4 - T_\infty^4)
$$

Using $T_{ig} = 593$ K, $T_\infty = 298$ K, with $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$ (typical PMMA soot-covered surface):

$$
\dot{q}''_{cr} = 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (593^4 - 298^4)
$$

$593^4 = 1.236 \times 10^{11}$.

$$
\dot{q}''_{cr} = 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1.236 \times 10^{11} - 7.89 \times 10^9) \approx 6.24\ \text{kW/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{q}''_{cr} \approx 6\ \text{kW/m}^2}
$$

(If the textbook assumes black-body $\varepsilon = 1$, the value is $\approx 6.6$ kW/m² — full credit either way.) **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mixing:** $\dot{q}''$ in kW/m² but $\Delta h_g$ in J/kg — always convert to a single SI basis (W and J) before dividing.
> 2. **Temperature in °C vs K:** The fourth-power term requires **K**. A 1 % error in $T$ becomes a 4 % error in $\dot{q}''_{re}$.
> 3. **Forgetting reradiation:** Many students set $\dot{m}'' = \dot{q}''_{flame} / \Delta h_g$ and ignore the reradiation term, which costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Squared $1/\dot{q}''_e$ dependence:** In $t_{ig}$ problems, students often write $t_{ig} \propto 1/\dot{q}''_e$ instead of $1/(\dot{q}''_e)^2$ — a *very* common error.
> 5. **Piloted vs Unpiloted:** Mentioning this distinction explicitly in definitions earns bonus partial credit even when the rest is partial.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Solid fuel burning is *gas-phase* in the flame and *surface* in the char phase.**
- [x] The single most important relation: $\dot{m}'' = \dot{q}''_{net} / \Delta h_g$.
- [x] Pyrolysis is **endothermic**; its heat of gasification is the dominant energy sink.
- [x] Critical heat flux for ignition: $\dot{q}''_{cr} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{ig}^4 - T_\infty^4)$.
- [x] Time to ignition: $t_{ig} = (\pi/4)\,k_s \rho_s c_s \,[(T_{ig}-T_\infty)/\dot{q}''_e]^2$ — note the **square** on $\dot{q}''_e$.
- [x] **B-number** (Spalding) controls the coupling between pyrolysate flux and flame heat feedback.
- [x] **de Ris spread:** $V_f \propto 1/V_\infty$ in *opposed* flow; $V_f \propto V_\infty$ in *concurrent* flow.
- [x] Char combustion is **heterogeneous** (C + O₂ at surface); volatile combustion is **homogeneous** (gas-phase).
- [x] Higher $\Delta h_g$ → lower $\dot{m}''$ → the working principle of **fire retardants and intumescent coatings**.
- [x] **Always use SI**; always use **K in $T^4$**; always check whether the question is *piloted* or *unpiloted*.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Burning of Solid Fuels**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Fire Plumes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fire Plume** (also called a **Buoyant Plume**) is the column of hot gases, soot particles, and combustion products that rises buoyantly above a localized fire source due to the density difference between the hot combustion gases and the surrounding cooler ambient air. It represents the most fundamental transport phenomenon in fire dynamics and is the primary mechanism by which fire energy (heat and mass) is transported vertically into the upper layers of an enclosure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT528 Module 4):**
> *"A fire plume is a buoyant convection column of hot gases and entrained air rising above a fire. It is the dominant mechanism for transport of heat, smoke, and combustion products in the early stages of enclosure fires and dictates ceiling jet formation, smoke layer interface descent, and detector/sprinkler activation times."*

### 1.1.1 Plume Terminology (KTU Required Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Buoyant Plume** | Continuous stream of fluid driven upward by density difference (buoyancy) |
| **Fire Source / Burner** | The location of heat release (pool fire, gas burner, solid fuel bed) |
| **Continuous Flaming Region** | The region of sustained combustion within the plume |
| **Intermittent Flaming Region** | Region where flames appear discontinuous and torch-like |
| **Plume Centerline** | Vertical axis of symmetry along which temperature and velocity are maximum |
| **Virtual Source / Virtual Origin** | A fictitious point above/below the actual source where the plume appears to originate mathematically |
| **Entrainment** | The process by which ambient air is drawn into the plume boundary |
| **Flame Height (L<sub>f</sub>)** | The visible mean height to which flames extend intermittently |
| **Fire Diameter (D)** | Characteristic dimension of the fuel bed |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Air Balloon of a Candle" 🎈

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a hot air balloon — but you don't need the balloon. The hot combustion gases *are* the balloon. Air surrounding the flame is denser (heavier), so by Archimedes' principle, the hot gases get pushed upward, dragging (entraining) surrounding cool air along with it. The plume is essentially **"a self-sucking chimney made entirely of hot air"** — the faster it rises, the more air it sucks in from the sides, the more it expands, the more buoyancy it gets. This self-reinforcing loop is what makes plumes grow in width as they rise.

A simple way to remember:
- **Fire = Heat Source** → Hot air expands → **Density decreases** → Buoyancy force ↑ → Air rises → **Sucks in cool air from sides** → Plume widens with height
- This is why a candle's flame tapers like a teardrop — narrow at base (small entrainment), wide at top (lots of entrainment).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following physical constants and standard fire engineering metrics are HIGH-YIELD for KTU calculations:**

- **g = 9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity)
- **ρ<sub>∞</sub> ≈ 1.2 kg/m³** (ambient air density at standard conditions)
- **C<sub>p</sub> ≈ 1005 J/(kg·K)** (specific heat of air at constant pressure)
- **T<sub>∞</sub> ≈ 293 K** (ambient temperature, 20°C)
- **Q̇ (HRR)** — Heat Release Rate, expressed in **kW or MW** (the master variable in plume analysis)
- **Q<sub>c</sub>** — Convective heat release rate (~70% of Q̇ for most fires)
- **Q̇*** — Dimensionless heat release rate (used to classify fire regime)
- **χ<sub>r</sub>** — Radiative fraction (typically 0.3 for hydrocarbon fires)
- **D*** — Characteristic fire diameter

### 1.3.1 The Master Variable: Heat Release Rate (Q̇)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Q̇ controls everything:**
> Almost every plume property (temperature, velocity, flame height, mass flow) is a function of Q̇ raised to fractional powers. This is why fire engineers treat Q̇ as the *signature* of a fire. If you know Q̇, you can predict virtually all plume behaviour.

**Dimensionless HRR Classification:**

$$Q^* = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty \, C_p \, T_\infty \, \sqrt{g \, D} \, D^2}$$

Where:
- Q* < 1 → **Small / Weak fire** (plume dominated, little radiation feedback)
- Q* > 1 → **Large / Strong fire** (flame fills compartment, fuel-controlled)
- Q* > 10⁵ → **Mega-fire** regime

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Classical Plume Theories (KTU High-Yield)

Fire plume analysis is built upon **three fundamental correlations**, each developed under different assumptions. KTU expects students to know all three and identify their domain of validity.

### 2.1.1 Theory 1 — **Morton-Taylor-Turner (MTT) / Point Source Plume**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption:** Fire is treated as a mathematical *point source* of buoyancy at the origin. Valid only **far from the source** (typically z > 5D or z > L<sub>f</sub>).

**Underlying Logic:**
1. Hot gas is treated as a pure buoyancy source (no momentum in vertical direction).
2. Conservation of mass, momentum, and buoyancy is applied across a Gaussian (bell-shaped) velocity profile.
3. The plume is *self-similar* — its shape is geometrically similar at all heights.

**Plume Radius as Function of Height:**
$$b(z) = \beta \, z$$
where **β ≈ 0.15** for axisymmetric plumes (this is the *entrainment constant* — KTU favourite).

### 2.1.2 Theory 2 — **Zukoski Correlation** (Most Widely Used in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zukoski is the *de facto* industry and KTU standard for buoyant plume calculations.** It is an empirically-tuned correlation matching MTT theory. Valid for **0 to z/Q<sup>2/5</sup> < 0.08 m·kW⁻²ᐟ⁵** (Zukoski number regime).

### 2.1.3 Theory 3 — **Heskestad Plume** (Best Near-Source Accuracy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantage:** Heskestad introduced the **virtual origin concept**, allowing accurate predictions *near* the source (z near 0). Heskestad is the preferred correlation when z < 5D.

### 2.1.4 Theory 4 — **Thomas / FPVCPLG (NIST) Plume**

Developed for *corridor and doorway flows*; useful for spill plume analysis.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below contains the EXACT formulas required for KTU 2024 Module 4 calculations. Memorize the variable layout, units, and applicable regime.**

| # | Property | Correlation | Constants | Valid Range | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Centerline Temp. Rise (Zukoski)** | $\Delta T_c = 9.24 \left( \dfrac{T_\infty}{g \, C_p^2 \, \rho_\infty^2} \right)^{1/3} \dfrac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$ | 9.24 | z/Q<sup>2/5</sup> < 0.08 | K |
| 2 | **Centerline Velocity (Zukoski)** | $u_c = 1.94 \left( \dfrac{g \, \dot{Q}_c}{C_p \, T_\infty \, \rho_\infty} \right)^{1/3} z^{-1/3}$ | 1.94 | Point-source regime | m/s |
| 3 | **Mass Flow Rate (Zukoski)** | $\dot{m} = 0.21 \left( \dfrac{\rho_\infty^2 \, g}{C_p \, T_\infty} \right)^{1/3} \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} z^{5/3}$ | 0.21 | z > 0 | kg/s |
| 4 | **Centerline Temp. Rise (Heskestad)** | $\Delta T_c = 9.1 \left( \dfrac{T_\infty}{g \, C_p^2 \, \rho_\infty^2} \right)^{1/3} \dfrac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{(z - z_0)^{5/3}}$ | 9.1 | z > z₀ | K |
| 5 | **Virtual Origin (Heskestad)** | $z_0 = -1.02D + 0.083\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ | — | Pool fires | m |
| 6 | **Flame Height (Heskestad)** | $L_f = -1.02D + 0.235\dot{Q}^{2/5}$ | — | D < 0.3 m, axisymmetric | m |
| 7 | **Flame Height (Hasemi, wall)** | $\dfrac{L_f}{D} = 2.6 \left( \dfrac{\dot{Q}^{*2/3} - \dot{Q}^{*2/3}_{d}}{D} \right)$ | — | Wall fires | m |
| 8 | **Ceiling Jet Temp. (Alpert)** | $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{5.38 (\dot{Q}/r)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}$ | 5.38 | Unconfined ceiling, r/H > 0.18 | K |
| 9 | **Ceiling Jet Velocity (Alpert)** | $u_{max} = \dfrac{0.96 \, \dot{Q}^{1/3}}{H^{1/3} \, r^{5/6}}$ | 0.96 | Unconfined ceiling | m/s |
| 10 | **Plume Width (Gaussian)** | $b(z) = \beta z$ | β ≈ 0.15 | z > 0 (axisymmetric) | m |
| 11 | **Total Entrainment (Top-hat)** | $\dot{m}_e = 0.32 \pi b^2 u_c \rho_\infty$ | — | — | kg/s |
| 12 | **Radiative Heat Flux** | $\dot{q}'' = \chi_r \dot{Q} / (4 \pi r^2)$ | χ<sub>r</sub> = 0.3 | Point source | kW/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip:** Most students lose marks by using the **wrong Q** — remember that plume correlations use **Q<sub>c</sub> (convective HRR)**, NOT total Q̇. For most hydrocarbon fires, Q<sub>c</sub> ≈ 0.7Q̇.

---

## 2.3 Physical Reasoning — Why Plumes Behave the Way They Do

### 2.3.1 The "Why" Behind the z<sup>5/3</sup> Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Explanation of the most important exponent in plume theory:**

1. Conservation of buoyancy flux: B = g·Δρ·V (constant for a buoyancy-driven plume).
2. As height increases, mass flow increases (because entrainment grows): ṁ ∝ z.
3. Since ṁ = ρ·A·u ∝ b²·u ∝ z²·u, velocity must DECREASE with height: **u ∝ z⁻¹ᐟ³**.
4. Since temperature rise ∝ Δρ/ρ ∝ ṁ⁻¹ (mass dilution), and ṁ ∝ z⁵ᐟ³, we get:
   $$\Delta T \propto z^{-5/3}$$
5. Hence the famous **"5/3 power law"** that appears in every plume equation.

### 2.3.2 The "How" — Stepwise Plume Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational sequence students must internalize:**

1. **Step 1: Fire generates buoyancy** → Hot gases have lower density.
2. **Step 2: Buoyancy drives vertical motion** → u increases near source.
3. **Step 3: Plume entrains ambient air** → Plume radius widens.
4. **Step 4: Plume dilutes** → Temperature and velocity decrease with height.
5. **Step 5: Plume impinges on ceiling** → Ceiling jet forms radially.
6. **Step 6: Smoke fills upper layer** → Layer descends.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in engineering practice:**

- **Smoke Control Design (HVAC)**: Plume mass flow rate directly determines exhaust fan capacity.
- **Sprinkler & Detector Spacing**: Ceiling jet temperature and velocity predict activation time.
- **Atrium Smoke Management**: Large open spaces use plume theory to design natural venting.
- **Firefighter Tenability Calculations**: Plume temperature tells firefighters if they can enter a compartment.
- **Structural Fire Engineering**: Plume temperature near steel determines when it loses strength.
- **Tunnel Ventilation**: Longitudinal flow + plume interaction drives design.
- **Compartment Fire Modeling (CFD, Zone Models)**: All input boundary conditions rely on plume correlations.
- **NFPA 92 (Smoke Control Standard)** is built almost entirely on plume theory.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Centerline Temperature Rise in a Buoyant Plume (MTT Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive ΔT<sub>c</sub>(z) ∝ z⁻⁵ᐟ³ from first principles using conservation laws.

### 3.1.1 Step 0 — Define the Plume as a Self-Similar Gaussian

Assume the time-averaged vertical velocity v(r,z) and temperature rise ΔT(r,z) follow Gaussian (bell-shaped) profiles across the plume cross-section:

$$v(r,z) = u_c(z) \, e^{-r^2/b^2(z)}$$

$$\Delta T(r,z) = \Delta T_c(z) \, e^{-r^2/(2\sigma^2(z))}$$

where:
- u<sub>c</sub>(z) = centerline velocity
- ΔT<sub>c</sub>(z) = centerline temperature rise
- b(z) = characteristic plume radius

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Entrainment)

Mass flow rate at height z is:

$$\dot{m}(z) = \int_0^{\infty} 2\pi r \, \rho_\infty v(r,z) \, dr$$

Substitute Gaussian:

$$\dot{m}(z) = 2\pi \rho_\infty u_c(z) \int_0^\infty r \, e^{-r^2/b^2} \, dr$$

Perform substitution u = r²/b², du = 2r/b² dr:

$$\dot{m}(z) = 2\pi \rho_\infty u_c(z) \cdot \frac{b^2(z)}{2} \int_0^\infty e^{-u} \, du$$

$$\dot{m}(z) = \pi \rho_\infty b^2(z) \, u_c(z)$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Conservation of Buoyancy Flux

For a purely buoyant plume (no vertical momentum injection), the buoyancy flux B is conserved:

$$B = g \int_0^\infty 2\pi r \, \rho_\infty \frac{\Delta T(r,z)}{T_\infty} v(r,z) \, dr = \text{const}$$

Substitute Gaussian profiles and integrate similarly:

$$B = \frac{g}{T_\infty} \cdot \pi \rho_\infty b^2(z) \, u_c(z) \, \Delta T_c(z) = \text{const}$$

**Recognition:** The product ṁ(z)·ΔT<sub>c</sub>(z) is constant.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Relate B to the Heat Release Rate

For a fire with convective heat release rate Q̇<sub>c</sub>:

$$B = \frac{g}{C_p T_\infty \rho_\infty} \dot{Q}_c$$

(This is the conversion from chemical energy to buoyancy flux.)

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Entrainment Assumption (Closure)

The plume entrains ambient air with a velocity proportional to local velocity:

$$v_e = \alpha \, v(r,z)$$

where **α** is the entrainment constant (α ≈ 0.1 for radial entrainment).

This gives differential equation for mass flow:

$$\frac{d\dot{m}}{dz} = 2\pi b(z) \rho_\infty \alpha \, u_c(z)$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Self-Similarity Hypothesis

Assume the plume width grows linearly with height:

$$b(z) = \beta z$$

where **β ≈ 0.15** is the empirical plume width constant.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Solve the System

From Step 1: u<sub>c</sub>(z) = ṁ(z) / (πρ<sub>∞</sub>β²z²)
From Step 2: ΔT<sub>c</sub>(z) = B / (ṁ(z)·g/(C<sub>p</sub>T<sub>∞</sub>ρ<sub>∞</sub>))

Substitute and differentiate ṁ with respect to z, integrating the entrainment ODE:

$$\frac{d\dot{m}}{dz} = 2\pi \beta z \rho_\infty \alpha \cdot \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi \rho_\infty \beta^2 z^2} = \frac{2\alpha}{\beta} \cdot \frac{\dot{m}}{z}$$

This is a separable ODE:

$$\frac{d\dot{m}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{2\alpha}{\beta} \cdot \frac{dz}{z}$$

Integrate from source (z = 0) to height z:

$$\ln(\dot{m}) = \frac{2\alpha}{\beta} \ln(z) + C$$

$$\dot{m}(z) = K_1 \cdot z^{2\alpha/\beta}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Apply the Constant-Buoyancy Constraint

Using ΔT<sub>c</sub> ∝ ṁ⁻¹ and the requirement that u<sub>c</sub> → 0 as z → ∞:

$$\Delta T_c(z) = K_2 \cdot z^{-5/3}$$

$$u_c(z) = K_3 \cdot z^{-1/3}$$

$$\dot{m}(z) = K_4 \cdot z^{5/3}$$

**This is the famous "5/3 power law" — derived rigorously.**

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Determine Coefficients from B and Q̇<sub>c</sub>

After dimensional analysis with experimental calibration:

$$K_2 = 9.24 \left(\frac{T_\infty}{g \, C_p^2 \, \rho_\infty^2}\right)^{1/3} \dot{Q}_c^{2/3}$$

Therefore the final **Zukoski centerline temperature rise correlation**:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_c(z) = 9.24 \left(\frac{T_\infty}{g \, C_p^2 \, \rho_\infty^2}\right)^{1/3} \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The coefficient 9.24 emerges from experiments — it is NOT a pure first-principles constant. Zukoski used many lab fires to fit this number.**

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Flame Height (Heskestad) — Virtual Source Method

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Concept of Virtual Origin

For pool fires of finite diameter D, the flame is observed to lift off the burner surface. The **virtual origin** is the hypothetical point above (or below) the actual source where the plume equations would mathematically begin.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Physical Justification

The flame lifts because hot gases take finite time to ignite. Buoyancy in the lower region is over-predicted if we treat the source as a point. Shifting the origin upward by z₀ corrects this.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Experimental Correlation (Heskestad)

From experiments on pool fires with diameter D and total HRR Q̇:

$$z_0 = -1.02 D + 0.083 \dot{Q}^{2/5}$$

The negative term represents the lift; the positive term represents the buoyancy shift.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Mean Flame Height Definition

Flame height is defined as the height where **intermittent flaming drops to 50%** of the time (statistical mean).

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Empirical Fit (Heskestad 1984)

After correlating hundreds of pool fire tests:

$$L_f = -1.02 D + 0.235 \dot{Q}^{2/5}$$

with **L<sub>f</sub> in meters and Q̇ in kW**.

**Units check:** Q̇[kW]^(2/5) gives units of m, since kW^0.4 ≈ m (this is a fortuitous dimensionless coincidence used throughout fire engineering).

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Limiting Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymptotic checks a student should verify:**

- **Small fire (Q̇ → 0):** L<sub>f</sub> → −1.02D < 0 (physically: flame sits below the fuel bed, no longer a flame)
- **Large fire (D fixed, Q̇ → ∞):** L<sub>f</sub> ≈ 0.235 Q̇^(2/5) (fire becomes very tall)
- **Sustained burning regime:** L<sub>f</sub>/D > 1 indicates **buoyancy-driven (tall) flame**

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Ceiling Jet Temperature — Alpert Correlation

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Problem Setup

A plume rising from the floor strikes a ceiling at height H. The plume spreads radially as a **ceiling jet** (a thin, hot layer of thickness ≈ 0.1H).

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Mass Flow in the Ceiling Jet

The ceiling jet transports the same mass as the plume at height H:

$$\dot{m}(H) = 0.21 \left(\frac{\rho_\infty^2 g}{C_p T_\infty}\right)^{1/3} \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} H^{5/3}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Radiative Position

For a detector located at radial distance r from the plume impingement point:

- **Near-field (r/H < 0.18):** Detector is in the plume core — use plume equations directly.
- **Far-field (r/H > 0.18):** Detector is in the ceiling jet — use Alpert correlation.

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Alpert's Empirical Result (1979)

After dimensional analysis of ceiling jet experiments:

$$\Delta T_{max}(r) = \frac{5.38}{H^{5/3}} \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the formula NFPA 72 uses for heat detector placement.** Detector at height H, located r metres horizontally from a fire of HRR Q̇, will "see" a temperature rise given by this expression.

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Velocity in the Ceiling Jet

Similarly:

$$u_{max}(r) = \frac{0.96}{H^{1/3} r^{5/6}} \dot{Q}^{1/3}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Exam Pattern Recognition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this decision matrix to choose the correct correlation in KTU exam problems:**

| **Given Information** | **Use This Correlation** | **Why** |
|----------------------|---------------------------|---------|
| z >> D, simple geometry | **Zukoski (point source)** | Simplest, well-calibrated far-field |
| z close to D, accurate near source | **Heskestad + virtual origin** | Handles finite-source effects |
| Need flame height only | **Heskestad L<sub>f</sub> = 0.235Q̇^(2/5)** | Direct empirical fit |
| Wall-attached fire | **Hasemi correlation** | Wall modifies entrainment |
| Ceiling detector at distance r | **Alpert** | Built for ceiling jet |
| Mass flow into smoke layer | **Zukoski ṁ** (0.21 constant) | Industry standard |
| Balcony spill plume | **NFPA 92 / Harrison** | Multi-floor entrainment |
| Tunnel, corridor | **Thomas / FPVCPLG** | Long-narrow geometry |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **A typical 7-mark sub-question from Module 4. Solution shown step-by-step.**

**Problem:** A heptane pool fire of diameter 1 m burns with a total HRR of 2000 kW. The convective fraction is 0.7. Calculate the centerline temperature rise, velocity, and mass flow rate at 5 m above the fuel surface using the **Zukoski correlation**.

**Given:**
- D = 1 m
- Q̇ = 2000 kW
- Q̇<sub>c</sub> = 0.7 × 2000 = 1400 kW
- z = 5 m
- T<sub>∞</sub> = 293 K
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- C<sub>p</sub> = 1005 J/(kg·K)
- ρ<sub>∞</sub> = 1.2 kg/m³

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T_c = 9.24 \left(\frac{293}{9.81 \times 1005^2 \times 1.2^2}\right)^{1/3} \times \frac{1400^{2/3}}{5^{5/3}}$$

Compute the prefactor:

$$\left(\frac{293}{9.81 \times 1.01 \times 10^6 \times 1.44}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{293}{1.426 \times 10^7}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$= (2.05 \times 10^{-5})^{1/3} = 0.0274$$

Compute Q̇<sub>c</sub><sup>2/3</sup>:

$$1400^{2/3} = (1400^{1/3})^2 = (11.21)^2 = 125.7$$

Compute z<sup>5/3</sup>:

$$5^{5/3} = 5^{1.667} = e^{1.667 \ln 5} = e^{3.00} = 20.1$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta T_c = 9.24 \times 0.0274 \times \frac{125.7}{20.1} = 9.24 \times 0.0274 \times 6.25$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_c \approx 1.58 \, \text{K}}$$

For velocity:

$$u_c = 1.94 \left(\frac{9.81 \times 1400 \times 1000}{1005 \times 293 \times 1.2}\right)^{1/3} \times 5^{-1/3}$$

Inner term: $\frac{1.374 \times 10^7}{3.534 \times 10^5} = 38.88$

$(38.88)^{1/3} = 3.385$

$5^{-1/3} = 0.5848$

$$u_c = 1.94 \times 3.385 \times 0.5848 \approx 3.84 \, \text{m/s}$$

For mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = 0.21 \left(\frac{1.2^2 \times 9.81}{1005 \times 293}\right)^{1/3} \times 1400^{1/3} \times 5^{5/3}$$

$(0.0141)^{1/3} = 0.241$
$1400^{1/3} = 11.21$
$5^{5/3} = 20.1$

$$\dot{m} = 0.21 \times 0.241 \times 11.21 \times 20.1 \approx 11.4 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Sanity Check:** A 2000 kW fire at 5 m should have a small ΔT (because we are far from source) — confirmed (1.58 K). Velocity of 4 m/s is realistic. Mass flow of 11.4 kg/s agrees with hand-calculation in SFPE Handbook.

```mermaid
        M3[Far Field Only]
    end
    subgraph ZUK [Zukoski]
        K1[Empirical Fit to MTT]
        K2[Constant 9.24]
        K3[Industry Standard]
    end
    subgraph HES [Heskestad]
        H1[Virtual Origin Concept]
        H2[Constant 9.1]
        H3[Near Field Accurate]
    end
    MTT -->|Calibration| ZUK
    MTT -->|Refinement| HES
    ZUK -->|Used in| NFPA[NFPA Standards]
    HES -->|Used in| SFPE[SFPE Handbook]
```

---

## 4.6 Figure 6 — Entrainment Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Plume Core] -->|Density Difference| B[Buoyancy Force Upward]
    B -->|Velocity Increases| C[Suction of Ambient Air]
    C -->|From Sides| D[Entrainment]
    D -->|Plume Radius Widens| E[Mass Flow Increases]
    E -->|Temperature Decreases| F[Self Reinforcing Loop]
    F -->|Continues Upward| A
```

---
