## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Part B - Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q.** (a) A 6-storey office building has a floor plate of 30 m × 20 m. The Occupant Load Factor is **4.5 m²/person** as per NBC 2016. Calculate:
   (i) The **Occupant Load** per floor.
   (ii) The **minimum number of exits** required per floor.
   (iii) The **total stair width** required per floor if stairs are sized for one exit being blocked.

**(b)** Explain with sketches the **active and passive fire protection systems** used in high-rise buildings. Compare their effectiveness in terms of **reliability, response time, and life safety**.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Floor plate area: $A = 30 \times 20 = 600$ m²
- Occupant Load Factor (OLF): $4.5$ m²/person
- Number of floors: 6 (irrelevant for per-floor calculation)

**Step 1 — Occupant Load per floor (2 marks):**

$$ \text{OL} = \frac{A}{\text{OLF}} = \frac{600}{4.5} = 133.33 $$

$$ \text{OL} \approx 134 \text{ persons per floor} \quad (\text{round up}) $$

**Step 2 — Minimum Number of Exits (2 marks):**

Per NBC 2016, for OL between 51–300: **2 exits** are required.
Since 134 falls in this range, we need **2 exits per floor**.

**Step 3 — Total Stair Width (3 marks):**

Stairs must be sized for the worst case: **one exit is blocked**, so the remaining stair must handle the full load.

$$ \text{Persons per stair (worst case)} = \frac{\text{OL}}{n - 1} = \frac{134}{2 - 1} = 134 \text{ persons} $$

Using unit exit width factor $U_f = 0.5$ m/person:

$$ W_{\text{per stair}} = 134 \times 0.5 = 67 \text{ m} $$

**Result:** Each of the **2 staircases** must be at least **67 meters wide**, which is impractical. Hence, in real design, **horizontal exits, refuge floors, and phased evacuation** are adopted. The required width suggests a **high-rise evacuation challenge** requiring performance-based design.

##### Part (b) — Descriptive Solution (7 marks)

**Active Fire Protection Systems (3.5 marks):**

These systems require **activation** (mechanical, electrical, or manual) to function.

| System | Function | Response Time | Reliability |
|--------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Sprinklers | Suppression | 1–3 min | 96% (NFPA) |
| Fire Hydrants | Manual suppression | 5–10 min | High |
| Gaseous Suppression (FM-200) | Clean agent for IT rooms | <10 s | Very High |
| Smoke Management | Smoke control | 30 s | Moderate |
| Fire Alarm Systems | Detection + alert | 30 s | High |

**Passive Fire Protection Systems (3.5 marks):**

These systems function **without activation** and rely on inherent material properties.

| System | Function | Response Time | Reliability |
|--------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Fire-rated walls | Compartmentation | Immediate | Very High |
| Intumescent coatings | Steel protection | 30 min to 2 hr | High |
| Fire doors | Maintain compartment | Immediate | High |
| Firestop sealants | Penetration sealing | Immediate | High |
| Fire-resistant glazing | Maintain integrity | 30 min to 2 hr | High |

**Comparison:**

- **Active systems** are more effective for **direct fire suppression** but depend on detection and power.
- **Passive systems** are more **reliable** (no moving parts) but limited to **containment**, not suppression.
- A combination of both is **mandatory** for high-rise buildings per **NBC 2016, Part 4**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse** $U_f$ for stairs (0.5 m/person) with $U_f$ for doors/corridors (typically different). Read the question carefully.
> - **Do not** calculate total occupant load for the building if only per-floor is asked.
> - **Do not** forget the "+1" rule: if 2 exits are required, each must handle the load of 2 - 1 = 1 exit being blocked.

---

### Part B - Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q.** (a) Differentiate between **Active and Passive Fire Protection** systems. List **four examples** of each, with one **advantage and disadvantage** for each category.

**(b)** A factory has a fire load of **5 × 10⁵ MJ** distributed over a floor area of **250 m²**. The ventilation factor $F_v$ is **0.025**. Using **IS 1642** empirical relation, calculate the **required fire resistance rating** of the structural elements. State the **implications for the choice of structural material** (RCC vs. steel).

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Conceptual Solution (7 marks)

**Differences Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Fire Protection | Passive Fire Protection |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Activation** | Requires trigger (smoke, heat, manual) | Inherent in design, no activation |
| **Power** | Needs electricity/water supply | No external power needed |
| **Function** | Detect, suppress, control | Contain, resist, insulate |
| **Maintenance** | High (weekly/monthly testing) | Low (periodic inspection) |
| **Cost** | High initial + recurring | High initial, low recurring |
| **Examples** | Sprinklers, alarms, extinguishers | Fire walls, intumescent paint, fire doors |

**Active Systems (2 marks):**

1. **Automatic Sprinklers** — *Advantage:* Highly effective (96% control rate). *Disadvantage:* Water damage to property.
2. **Fire Alarm Systems** — *Advantage:* Early warning saves lives. *Disadvantage:* False alarms cause evacuation disruption.
3. **Smoke Management Systems** — *Advantage:* Maintains tenable conditions. *Disadvantage:* High installation cost.
4. **Gaseous Suppression (FM-200)** — *Advantage:* No residue, safe for electronics. *Disadvantage:* Limited cylinder capacity.

**Passive Systems (2 marks):**

1. **Fire-rated Walls** — *Advantage:* Reliable compartmentation. *Disadvantage:* Cannot suppress fire.
2. **Intumescent Coatings** — *Advantage:* Aesthetic, protects steel. *Disadvantage:* Expensive for large areas.
3. **Fire Doors** — *Advantage:* Maintains compartment integrity. *Disadvantage:* Must be kept closed (often propped open).
4. **Firestop Sealants** — *Advantage:* Prevents fire spread through penetrations. *Disadvantage:* Difficult to inspect.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate Fire Load Density (2 marks):**

$$ q = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{250} = 2000 \text{ MJ/m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Determine Constants from IS 1642 (2 marks):**

For $F_v = 0.025$ (moderate ventilation), from IS 1642 empirical charts:
- $k_1 = 0.001$ hours·m²/MJ
- $k_2 = 0.5$ hours

**Step 3 — Calculate Fire Resistance Rating (2 marks):**

$$ \text{FRR} = k_1 \cdot q + k_2 $$

$$ \text{FRR} = (0.001 \times 2000) + 0.5 $$

$$ \text{FRR} = 2.0 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 4 — Material Implications (1 mark):**

- **RCC (Reinforced Concrete):** A 200 mm thick slab with 25 mm cover to steel provides ~2.5 hours of fire resistance. RCC is naturally fire-resistant.
- **Steel:** Bare steel loses strength at 550°C. To achieve 2.5 hours, **intumescent coating** (1.5–2 mm) or **encasement** in concrete is required.
- **Recommendation:** RCC is the preferred material for this fire load. If steel is used (for long spans), it must be protected with intumescent paint or sprayed vermiculite.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the units** of fire load density (MJ/m²). Always include them.
> - **Confusing** $F_v$ (ventilation factor) with $A_v$ (area of vents). These are not the same.
> - **Not stating material implications** is a common reason for losing 2–3 marks. Always end the question with a **practical recommendation**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Fire Safety** = umbrella term covering prevention, detection, suppression, compartmentation, and egress.
- ✅ **Five Layers of Defense** = Prevention → Detection → Suppression → Compartmentation → Egress.
- ✅ **Occupant Load** = $\text{OL} = A / \text{OLF}$ (in persons).
- ✅ **Exit Width** = $W_e = \text{OL} \times U_f$, with $U_f = 0.5$ m/person for stairs.
- ✅ **Number of Exits**: ≤50 = 1, 51–300 = 2, 301–500 = 3, >500 = 4.
- ✅ **Travel Distance** is **occupancy-dependent** (15 m to 45 m as per NBC 2016).
- ✅ **Fire Load Density** determines **Fire Resistance Rating** via IS 1642.
- ✅ **ASET > RSET** is the fundamental performance-based design criterion.
- ✅ **RSET** = $t_d + t_n + t_r + t_{\text{travel}}$.
- ✅ **Active Systems** = require activation (sprinklers, alarms).
- ✅ **Passive Systems** = inherent in design (fire walls, intumescent coatings).
- ✅ **Sprinkler Design Density**: LH = 4.1, OH1 = 5.0, OH2 = 5.0, EH1 = 7.7, EH2 = 12.5 L/min/m² (NFPA 13).
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model** = multiple layers with offset weaknesses.
- ✅ **Defence-in-Depth** = no single point of failure.
- ✅ **Performance-based design** is increasingly preferred for complex buildings.
- ✅ **NBC 2016, Part 4** is the primary Indian code reference.
- ✅ **NFPA 13, 72, 101, SFPE Handbook** are international references.
- ✅ **Two-stage evacuation** is used in high-rise buildings.
- ✅ **Refuge floors** are mandatory every 20–25 floors in Indian high-rises.
- ✅ **Voice communication systems** are required for assembly and high-rise occupancies.
- ✅ **Three E's of Prevention** = Engineering, Education, Enforcement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** When answering **14-mark questions** in the KTU exam, always structure your answer as: (1) Definition/Concept (2 marks), (2) Theory/Explanation (4 marks), (3) Diagram/Table (4 marks), (4) Numerical/Application (4 marks). This shows the examiner a balanced answer covering all cognitive levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).
